topics.title: Injection Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Injection Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Injection Moulding** is a **cyclic**, high-volume manufacturing process used to produce discrete plastic parts by forcing (injecting) a measured quantity of molten polymer into a closed, temperature-controlled mould cavity under high pressure, where it solidifies by cooling (or curing for thermosets) and is subsequently ejected as a finished component.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPOT503 Module 1)** terminology, injection moulding is classified as a **primary plastics processing technique** under the category of **pressure-based shaping methods**, distinct from compression moulding, transfer moulding, blow moulding, and extrusion. The process is governed by the **Course Outcome CO1**: *"Understand the working principles of major polymer processing machineries."*

### Key Terminology (KTU Glossary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Shot** | The quantity of polymer melt required to fill one mould cavity and compensate for shrinkage |
| **Plasticising** | The process of converting solid polymer granules into a homogeneous melt |
| **Injection Stroke** | The linear travel of the screw/ram to deliver the shot into the cavity |
| **Hold-on Pressure** | Pressure maintained during packing stage to compensate volumetric shrinkage |
| **Clamp Tonnage** | The clamping force (in **tonnes**) required to keep the mould closed against injection pressure |
| **Cycle Time** | Total time for one complete moulding cycle (close → inject → cool → open → eject) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Polymer Syringe"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of injection moulding like a giant medical syringe injecting molten plastic into a metal cookie cutter.**

Imagine a **giant industrial syringe** filled with hot, melted cheese (the polymer melt). You press the plunger (the **injection ram/screw**), and the molten material is forced through a narrow nozzle into a closed, water-cooled metal mould (your "cookie cutter"). After a few seconds, the cheese hardens inside, you open the mould halves, and a perfect shaped part pops out — just like ejecting a finished plastic component with ejector pins.

In this analogy:
- **Molten polymer** → hot cheese
- **Screw/Ram** → syringe plunger
- **Mould cavity** → cookie cutter
- **Cooling channels** → refrigerator
- **Ejector pins** → your fingers pushing the cookie out

This mental image immediately clarifies *why* injection moulding excels at **high-volume, repeatable, complex-geometry** part production.

## 1.3 Historical Context & Industrial Significance

Injection moulding traces its origins to **1872** when the **Hyatt brothers** patented the first injection moulding machine for celluloid. Modern reciprocating-screw machines were commercialized in the **1950s**, revolutionizing mass production. Today, the global injection moulding market exceeds **USD 350 billion**, with applications spanning automotive (40%), packaging (20%), electronics (15%), medical (10%), and consumer goods (15%).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industrial Metric:** Injection moulding machines are rated by their **clamping force (tonnes)** ranging from **5 tonnes** (micro-moulding) to **5500+ tonnes** (large automotive panels). Machine selection is dictated by the projected area of the part and the cavity pressure (**typically 20–70 MPa**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Injection Moulding Machines

Injection moulding machines are classified by their **plasticising mechanism** and **clamping mechanism**.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Plasticising Unit

1. **Plunger (Ram) Type** — *Obsolete but conceptually important*
   - Uses a plunger to push pre-heated (but not melted) granules into a heated spreader chamber
   - Limited melt homogeneity; **rarely used today**
   - Mentioned in KTU syllabus for **historical/analytical comparison**

2. **Reciprocating Screw Type** — *Industry standard*
   - A single rotating screw both **plasticises** (during rotation) and **injects** (during axial translation)
   - Combines melting, mixing, metering, and injection in one unit
   - **Screw L/D ratio: typically 18:1 to 24:1**
   - **Compression ratio: 2:1 to 3:1**

3. **Pre-plasticising (Two-Stage) Type**
   - Uses a separate **plasticiser (extruder)** feeding melt into an **injection cylinder**
   - Used for **very large shots** or **PVC** (heat-sensitive)
   - Higher melt consistency, but higher cost

### 2.1.2 Classification by Clamping Unit

| Clamp Type | Mechanism | Typical Tonnage |
|---|---|---|
| **Toggle (Mechanical) Clamp** | Four-bar linkage converts linear motion to high force | 50–2000 tonnes |
| **Hydraulic Clamp** | Direct-acting hydraulic cylinder | 100–5500 tonnes |
| **Hydromechanical (Toggle-Hydraulic Hybrid)** | Combines toggle speed with hydraulic accuracy | 200–4000 tonnes |
| **Electric (Servo-Driven) Clamp** | Servo motors + ball screws (no hydraulics) | 50–1500 tonnes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Toggle vs Hydraulic matters in KTU exams:** Toggle clamps are *faster* (high dry-cycle speed) but lose tonnage as the mould opens; hydraulic clamps are *slower* but maintain constant tonnage throughout the stroke.

## 2.2 The Complete Injection Moulding Cycle

The injection moulding cycle is a **closed-loop sequential operation** consisting of the following stages:

### Stage 1 — Mould Closing & Clamping (t_close)
- The **moving platen** travels to close the mould halves
- Clamp force builds up to **rated tonnage**
- Safety interlocks confirm mould closure

### Stage 2 — Injection (Filling Phase) (t_inject)
- Screw advances axially at **high velocity (50–200 mm/s)**
- Melt enters the cavity through the **sprue, runner, and gate**
- Filling ends when **95–98%** of the cavity is filled
- **Injection pressure: 70–140 MPa**
- **Injection speed: critical for surface finish**

### Stage 3 — Packing / Hold-on Phase (t_pack)
- Pressure switches from **injection pressure** to a lower **hold pressure (50–70% of injection pressure)**
- Compensates for **volumetric shrinkage** (typically 1–3%)
- Additional melt is packed into the cavity
- Critical for **sink marks** and **voids**

### Stage 4 — Cooling / Solidification (t_cool)
- Cooling channels circulate water (or oil) at controlled temperature (**20–80 °C**)
- This is the **longest phase** (typically 50–80% of cycle time)
- Part must be dimensionally stable before ejection
- **Cooling time** depends on **section thickness** and **thermal diffusivity**

### Stage 5 — Plasticising (Metering) (t_plasticise)
- Screw rotates and retracts, drawing in fresh granules
- Concurrently cools the melt to the set temperature
- Done **during cooling** to save cycle time (overlapping)

### Stage 6 — Mould Opening & Part Ejection (t_eject)
- Moving platen retracts
- **Ejector pins** push the solidified part out
- Part drops into a conveyor/bin

### Total Cycle Time
The **Total Cycle Time (T_c)** is the sum of all individual phases:

$$T_c = t_{close} + t_{inject} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{plasticise} + t_{open} + t_{eject}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **exam-critical** for KTU 2024 Scheme calculations.

### 2.3.1 Clamping Force Calculations

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \cdot A_{proj}$ | Clamp force = Cavity pressure × Projected area |
| $P_{cav} = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{A_{proj}}$ | Cavity pressure (resolving for pressure) |
| $A_{proj} = n \cdot A_{part} + A_{runner}$ | Total projected area = (parts × part area) + runner area |
| $T_{req} = \dfrac{P_{cav} \cdot A_{proj}}{g \cdot 10^3}$ | Required clamp tonnage (in tonnes) |

### 2.3.2 Shot Volume & Shot Weight

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $V_{shot} = V_{part} + V_{runner} + V_{sprue}$ | Total shot volume |
| $m_{shot} = V_{shot} \cdot \rho_{melt}$ | Shot weight (melt density) |
| $\rho_{melt} = \dfrac{\rho_{solid}}{1 - S_{v}}$ | Melt density from solid density and volumetric shrinkage |
| $S_{v} = \dfrac{\rho_{solid} - \rho_{melt}}{\rho_{solid}}$ | Volumetric shrinkage fraction |

### 2.3.3 Cooling Time (Simplified)

$$t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \cdot \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_{melt} - T_{mould}}{T_{eject} - T_{mould}}\right)$$

Where:
- $s$ = maximum section thickness (m)
- $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity of polymer (m²/s)
- $T_{melt}$ = melt temperature (°C)
- $T_{mould}$ = mould surface temperature (°C)
- $T_{eject}$ = ejection temperature (°C, usually $T_g + 20$ for amorphous polymers)

### 2.3.4 Injection Pressure (Bernoulli-style simplification)

$$\Delta P_{inj} = \dfrac{128 \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot Q}{\pi \cdot D^4}$$

Where:
- $\mu$ = melt viscosity (Pa·s)
- $L$ = flow length (m)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $D$ = channel diameter (m)

### 2.3.5 Plasticising Capacity

$$Q_{plast} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot D_s^2 \cdot N \cdot h \cdot \sin\phi \cdot \cos\phi}{2} - \dfrac{Q_{leak}}{2}$$

### 2.3.6 Part Shrinkage Allowance

$$D_{mould} = D_{part} \cdot (1 + S)$$

Where $S$ is the linear shrinkage (typically 0.3–3% for most thermoplastics).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Injection moulding is the **workhorse of plastics manufacturing** for:

- **Automotive:** Bumpers, dashboards, interior trims (PP, ABS, PC/ABS)
- **Medical:** Syringes, IV components, surgical器械 housings (PP, PE, PC)
- **Packaging:** Bottle preforms, closures, thin-wall containers (PET, PP, PE)
- **Electronics:** Connectors, housings, switchgear (PBT, LCP, PA)
- **Consumer goods:** Toys, kitchenware, appliances (PS, ABS, PP)

The process enables **net-shape or near-net-shape** production, **tight tolerances (±0.05 mm typical)**, and **minimal post-processing**, making it economically dominant for runs exceeding **~5,000 parts**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Clamping Force Requirement

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A mould has 4 cavities, each producing a part of projected area **50 cm²**. The runner system projects **30 cm²**. If the cavity pressure is **40 MPa**, calculate the required clamping force and clamp tonnage. Take **g = 9.81 m/s²**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the total projected area:**

$$A_{proj} = n \cdot A_{part} + A_{runner}$$

Substituting the values:

$$A_{proj} = (4 \times 50 \times 10^{-4}) + (30 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$A_{proj} = (200 + 30) \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$A_{proj} = 230 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2 = 0.023\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert all units to SI:**
- Cavity pressure: $P_{cav} = 40 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa}$
- Projected area: $A_{proj} = 0.023\ \text{m}^2$

**Step 3 — Apply the clamping force formula:**

$$F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \cdot A_{proj}$$

$$F_{clamp} = (40 \times 10^6) \times (0.023)$$

$$F_{clamp} = 920{,}000\ \text{N} = 9.2 \times 10^5\ \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to tonnage:**

$$T_{req} = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{g \cdot 10^3}$$

$$T_{req} = \dfrac{9.2 \times 10^5}{9.81 \times 10^3}$$

$$T_{req} = 93.78\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 5 — Apply safety factor (KTU standard: 1.15–1.25):**

$$T_{selected} = 93.78 \times 1.20 = 112.5\ \text{tonnes}$$

$$\boxed{T_{selected} \approx 115\ \text{tonnes (next standard machine)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cooling Time Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A **PP (polypropylene)** part has a maximum wall thickness of **3.0 mm**. The melt temperature is **230 °C**, the mould surface temperature is **40 °C**, and the part is ejected at **90 °C**. Given the thermal diffusivity of PP is **$\alpha = 9.4 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$**, calculate the cooling time.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — List the parameters:**
- $s = 3.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$
- $\alpha = 9.4 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- $T_{melt} = 230\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- $T_{mould} = 40\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- $T_{eject} = 90\ ^\circ\text{C}$

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature ratios:**

Numerator: $T_{melt} - T_{mould} = 230 - 40 = 190\ ^\circ\text{C}$
Denominator: $T_{eject} - T_{mould} = 90 - 40 = 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$

$$\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{190}{50} = 3.8$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithmic term:**

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \times 3.8\right) = \ln\!\left(0.8106 \times 3.8\right)$$

$$= \ln(3.080) = 1.125$$

**Step 4 — Compute $s^2 / (\pi^2 \alpha)$:**

$$s^2 = (3 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 9 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$$

$$\pi^2 \cdot \alpha = 9.8696 \times 9.4 \times 10^{-8} = 9.278 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} = \dfrac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{9.278 \times 10^{-7}} = 9.701\ \text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**

$$t_{cool} = 9.701 \times 1.125 = 10.91\ \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{cool} \approx 11\ \text{seconds}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Shot Weight from Mould Volume

**Problem Statement:** A single-cavity mould has a part volume of **60 cm³**, a runner volume of **8 cm³**, and a sprue volume of **2 cm³**. The solid density of the polymer is **0.905 g/cm³** (PP), and volumetric shrinkage is **2.5%**. Calculate the shot weight.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total shot volume:**

$$V_{shot} = 60 + 8 + 2 = 70\ \text{cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Melt density (accounting for shrinkage):**

$$\rho_{melt} = \dfrac{\rho_{solid}}{1 - S_v}$$

$$\rho_{melt} = \dfrac{0.905}{1 - 0.025} = \dfrac{0.905}{0.975}$$

$$\rho_{melt} = 0.9282\ \text{g/cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Shot weight:**

$$m_{shot} = V_{shot} \times \rho_{melt}$$

$$m_{shot} = 70 \times 0.9282 = 64.97\ \text{g}$$

$$\boxed{m_{shot} \approx 65\ \text{g}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Defects, Causes & Remedies

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often state *only* the defect and remedy but forget the **root cause**. Always structure as: **Defect → Root Cause → Remedy**.

| Defect | Visual Symptom | Root Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sink Marks** | Depressions on thick sections | Insufficient hold pressure / short cooling | ↑ Hold pressure, ↑ cooling time, ↓ wall thickness |
| **Weld Lines** | Visible line where fronts meet | Low melt temperature, poor venting | ↑ Melt temp, ↑ injection speed, add vents |
| **Flash (Burrs)** | Excess material at parting line | Insufficient clamp force, worn mould | ↑ Clamp tonnage, clean mould surfaces |
| **Voids / Bubbles** | Internal air pockets | Trapped air, insufficient packing | Add vents, ↑ hold pressure, redesign gate |
| **Short Shot** | Incomplete filling | Low melt temp, low pressure, high flow length | ↑ Melt temp, ↑ injection pressure, redesign gate location |
| **Warping** | Distortion after ejection | Uneven cooling, residual stress | Balance cooling, ↑ mould temp, design uniformity |
| **Burn Marks** | Black/discoloured streaks | Trapped air igniting, overheating | Improve venting, lower injection speed |
| **Silver Streaking** | Silver-coloured lines | Moisture in resin, degradation | Dry resin thoroughly, lower melt temp |
| **Flow Lines** | Wavy patterns on surface | Low melt temp, slow injection speed | ↑ Melt temp, ↑ injection speed, ↑ mould temp |
| **Ejector Pin Marks** | Visible pin indentations | Insufficient cooling, poor surface finish | ↑ Cooling time, polish pins, relocate pins |

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Plunger vs. Reciprocating Screw

| Parameter | Plunger Type | Reciprocating Screw |
|---|---|---|
| **Melt Homogeneity** | Poor (uneven heating) | Excellent (shear mixing) |
| **Shot Size Range** | Limited (~25 g) | Wide (1 g to 25 kg) |
| **Cycle Time** | Longer | 20–40% shorter |
| **Residence Time Control** | Difficult | Precise |
| **Material Wastage** | Higher | Lower |
| **Color/Masterbatch Mixing** | Difficult | Excellent |
| **Modern Usage** | Nearly obsolete | ~98% of installed base |
| **Cost** | Lower initial | Higher initial |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Injection Moulding Machine Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of a modern reciprocating-screw injection moulding machine, showing all major subsystems and their functional interactions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hopper and Feed Throat] --> B[Heated Barrel with Screw]
    B --> C[Reciprocating Screw Plasticising Unit]
    C --> D[Nozzle and Shut Off Valve]
    D --> E[Fixed Platen with Sprue Bush]
    E --> F[Two Plate Mould with Cavity and Core]
    F --> G[Moving Platen with Support Plate]
    G --> H[Toggle or Hydraulic Clamping Unit]
    H --> I[Ejector Plate with Ejector Pins]
    I --> J[Conveyor and Finished Part Bin]

    K[Hydraulic Power Unit] --> C
    K --> H
    L[Electronic Controller PLC] --> C
    L --> H
    L --> M[Heater Bands and PID Temperature Control]
    M --> B
    N[Cooling Water Circulation Unit] --> F

    O[Material Granules In] --> A
    J --> P[Finished Moulded Parts Out]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Injection Moulding Cycle Phases

This sequence diagram maps the **chronological interaction** between the controller, plasticising unit, mould, and clamp across one complete cycle.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Ctrl as Machine Controller PLC
    participant Clamp as Clamping Unit
    participant Inj as Injection Unit Screw
    participant Mould as Mould Cavity
    participant Cool as Cooling System
    participant Ej as Ejector System

    Ctrl->>Clamp: Activate close and build tonnage
    Clamp->>Mould: Mould halves fully closed and locked
    Ctrl->>Inj: Initiate injection stroke
    Inj->>Mould: Inject molten polymer under high pressure
    Ctrl->>Inj: Switch to hold on pressure
    Inj->>Mould: Maintain packing pressure to compensate shrinkage
    Ctrl->>Cool: Activate cooling water circulation
    Note over Mould,Cool: Cooling phase overlaps with plasticising
    Ctrl->>Inj: Rotate screw for plasticising new shot
    Inj-->>Inj: Meter next shot into barrel front
    Ctrl->>Clamp: Release tonnage and open mould
    Clamp->>Mould: Moving platen retracts
    Ctrl->>Ej: Activate ejector plate
    Ej->>Mould: Ejector pins push finished part out
    Mould-->>Ej: Part drops onto conveyor
    Ctrl->>Clamp: Begin next cycle
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mould Cooling Channel Architecture

This diagram depicts the **conformal cooling channel layout** typical of a mould for a rectangular box-shaped part.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Cooling Water Inlet Manifold] --> B[Cavity Side Cooling Circuit]
    A --> C[Core Side Cooling Circuit]
    B --> D[Baffle Cooler near Gate]
    B --> E[Drilled Channel along Cavity Wall]
    C --> F[Bubble Cooler around Core Pin]
    C --> G[Drilled Channel along Core Wall]
    E --> H[Cooling Water Outlet Manifold]
    G --> H
    D --> H
    F --> H
    H --> I[Return to Chiller Unit]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Flowchart — Process Parameter Selection Logic

This flowchart represents the **decision logic** an injection moulding engineer follows when setting up machine parameters.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Material Selection] --> B{Polymer Data Sheet Available}
    B -->|Yes| C[Set Melt Temp from Data Sheet]
    B -->|No| D[Use Generic Range e.g. PP 220 to 260 C]
    C --> E[Calculate Projected Area and Shot Volume]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute Required Clamp Tonnage]
    F --> G[Select Machine with Higher Rated Tonnage]
    G --> H[Set Injection Speed based on Wall Thickness]
    H --> I[Set Hold Pressure at 60 to 70 percent of Inj Pressure]
    I --> J[Run First Shot and Inspect for Short Shot or Flash]
    J --> K{Defects Present}
    K -->|Short Shot| L[Increase Inj Pressure or Speed or Melt Temp]
    K -->|Flash| M[Reduce Inj Pressure or Increase Clamp Tonnage]
    K -->|No Defects| N[Lock Parameters and Begin Production Run]
    L --> J
    M --> J
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Two-Plate vs Three-Plate Mould Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph TwoPlateMould[Two Plate Mould]
        A1[Fixed Half] --> A2[Sprue and Runner on Parting Line]
        A2 --> A3[Cavity and Core]
        A3 --> A4[Moving Half with Ejector]
    end

    subgraph ThreePlateMould[Three Plate Mould]
        B1[Fixed Half] --> B2[Runner Plate in Middle]
        B2 --> B3[Cavity Plate]
        B3 --> B4[Core Plate with Ejector]
    end

    Note1[Single Parting Line] -.-> A2
    Note2[Two Parting Lines for Automatic Degating] -.-> B2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These questions model the **KTU University Exam Part A** pattern: concise, definition- or concept-oriented, answerable in **2–3 sentences plus a diagram**.

### Question 1 (Module 1)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 pattern]**
*With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a reciprocating screw injection moulding machine.*

**Model Answer:**
A reciprocating screw injection moulding machine consists of three major units: **plasticising unit, clamping unit, and control unit**. The plasticising unit houses a rotating screw inside a heated barrel that simultaneously melts, mixes, and meters the polymer. During injection, the screw acts as a plunger and pushes the melt through the nozzle into the closed mould. The clamping unit holds the mould halves together against the cavity pressure. The cycle includes plasticising, injection, packing, cooling, and ejection.

### Question 2 (Module 1)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 pattern]**
*Distinguish between plunger-type and reciprocating-screw-type injection moulding machines.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Plunger Type | Reciprocating Screw Type |
|---|---|---|
| Plasticising | Done in spreader, not in barrel | Done directly by rotating screw |
| Melt uniformity | Poor | Excellent |
| Shot size | Limited | Wide range |
| Cycle time | Long | Short |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks** as per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, with internal choice. Structure: typically **7 + 7 marks** across two sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]*

**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the construction and working of a **reciprocating screw injection moulding machine**. Discuss the functions of the **plasticising unit, clamping unit, and hydraulic system**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **complete injection moulding cycle** with a temperature–time and pressure–time profile. Discuss the significance of each stage in determining part quality. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Construction:** The machine comprises:
1. **Hopper** — feeds granules into the barrel
2. **Barrel** — heated externally by bands; houses the screw
3. **Screw** — L/D ratio 18–24:1, compression ratio 2–3:1; flights melt and convey polymer
4. **Nozzle and shut-off valve** — controls melt flow into mould
5. **Clamping unit** — toggle or hydraulic; holds mould halves together
6. **Hydraulic system** — powers screw rotation, injection, and clamp movement
7. **Control system** — PLC with PID loops for temperature, pressure, position

**Working:**
- Screw rotates, conveying and melting polymer (plasticising phase).
- Screw retracts to meter the exact shot.
- Screw advances axially (acting as plunger) to inject melt.
- Hold-on pressure compensates shrinkage.
- Cooling solidifies the part.
- Mould opens; ejector pins release the part.

#### Model Solution for (b):

The injection moulding cycle has the following phases (see Section 4.2 sequence diagram):

1. **Mould closing and clamping** — fast closing, slow tonnage build-up
2. **Filling** — high velocity, pressure rises
3. **Packing/Hold-on** — pressure held at lower value, additional melt packed in
4. **Cooling** — water/oil circulation; longest phase; solidification
5. **Plasticising** — screw rotates during cooling to meter next shot
6. **Mould opening** — moving platen retracts
7. **Ejection** — ejector pins push part out

**Temperature–time profile:** Melt enters at high T, cools through T_g or T_m, stabilises near ejection T.
**Pressure–time profile:** Pressure rises during fill, plateaus during hold, drops sharply at gate freeze.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** A mould produces **6 identical parts** per shot. Each part has a projected area of **35 cm²**, and the runner system projects **40 cm²**. If the cavity pressure is **45 MPa**, calculate the **required clamping tonnage**. Use a safety factor of **1.2**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **weld lines, sink marks, and flash** as common injection moulding defects. For each, explain the **root cause and at least two remedies**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Given:**
- $n = 6$, $A_{part} = 35\ \text{cm}^2$, $A_{runner} = 40\ \text{cm}^2$, $P_{cav} = 45\ \text{MPa}$, $SF = 1.2$

**Step 1 — Total projected area:**

$$A_{proj} = (6 \times 35) + 40 = 210 + 40 = 250\ \text{cm}^2$$

Converting to m²: $A_{proj} = 250 \times 10^{-4} = 0.025\ \text{m}^2$

**Step 2 — Clamp force:**

$$F_{clamp} = 45 \times 10^6 \times 0.025 = 1{,}125{,}000\ \text{N} = 1.125 \times 10^6\ \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Tonnage:**

$$T_{req} = \dfrac{1.125 \times 10^6}{9.81 \times 10^3} = 114.68\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 4 — With safety factor:**

$$T_{selected} = 114.68 \times 1.2 = 137.6\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Answer:** A machine of at least **140 tonnes** clamping capacity should be selected.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Weld Lines:** Visible lines where two flow fronts meet (e.g., around inserts).
- *Cause:* Low melt temperature, low injection speed, poor venting at weld location.
- *Remedies:* Increase melt and mould temperature; increase injection speed; add vents near weld; relocate gate.

**Sink Marks:** Depressions on the surface of thick sections.
- *Cause:* Insufficient hold pressure; insufficient cooling time; non-uniform wall thickness.
- *Remedies:* Increase hold pressure and time; redesign for uniform wall thickness; lower melt temperature slightly.

**Flash (Burrs):** Excess material escaping at the parting line.
- *Cause:* Insufficient clamp force; worn or damaged mould surfaces; excessive injection pressure.
- *Remedies:* Increase clamping tonnage; repair mould surfaces; reduce injection pressure; check parting line cleanliness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Failing to convert cm² to m² when calculating clamping force (loses 2–3 marks).
> 2. **Ignoring safety factor:** Examiner expects a standard safety factor of 1.15–1.25; skipping it loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Cause-remedy mismatch:** Students often write generic remedies (e.g., "increase temperature") without identifying the specific defect mechanism.
> 4. **Missing labelled diagrams:** A 7-mark question without a proper labelled diagram is capped at ~5 marks even with correct text.
> 5. **Skipping cycle overlap:** Examiners expect students to note that plasticising overlaps with cooling to optimise cycle time.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Injection Moulding**

- ✅ **Definition:** Cyclic, pressure-based process; inject melt → cool → eject
- ✅ **Three units:** Plasticising + Clamping + Control/Hydraulic
- ✅ **Plunger vs Screw:** Screw type is the modern industry standard
- ✅ **Toggle vs Hydraulic clamp:** Toggle = fast, Hydraulic = constant tonnage
- ✅ **Clamp force formula:** $F = P_{cav} \cdot A_{proj}$ (units: Pa × m² → N)
- ✅ **Cycle phases:** Close → Inject → Pack → Cool → Plasticise → Open → Eject
- ✅ **Cooling is the longest phase** (50–80% of cycle time)
- ✅ **Plasticising overlaps with cooling** for cycle time optimisation
- ✅ **Hold-on pressure = 50–70% of injection pressure**; compensates shrinkage
- ✅ **Shot volume = part + runner + sprue** volumes
- ✅ **Cavity pressure** typically 20–70 MPa; **clamp tonnage** 5–5500 tonnes
- ✅ **Volumetric shrinkage** for PP ≈ 1.5–2.5%, for PA66 ≈ 1.5–2.0%
- ✅ **Common defects:** Sink marks, weld lines, flash, short shot, warping, voids, burn marks, silver streaking
- ✅ **L/D ratio** of screw = 18:1 to 24:1; **Compression ratio** = 2:1 to 3:1
- ✅ **Ejection temperature** for amorphous polymers = $T_g + 20\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- ✅ **Always apply safety factor** (1.15–1.25) when selecting clamp tonnage
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terminology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Terminology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1: TERMINOLOGY IN POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 🔷 What is "Terminology" in Polymer Processing?

In the context of **Polymer Machinery & Product Manufacturing (PCPOT503)**, the term **"Terminology"** refers to the standardized vocabulary, definitions, and nomenclature that engineers, technologists, and operators must master to communicate precisely about polymer materials, processing equipment, process parameters, and finished product characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Terminology in polymer processing encompasses the systematic set of technical words, phrases, and standardized expressions used to describe polymers, their processing behaviour, the machinery employed, and the resulting manufactured products. It forms the foundational language required for studying extrusion, injection moulding, blow moulding, thermoforming, calendaring, and other conversion processes.*

---

### 🔷 Why Terminology Matters — The Conceptual Analogy

**🎯 Real-World Analogy — "The Doctor's Language":**

Imagine you visit a doctor. If you say "I have a headache," the doctor understands. But if you cannot say "I have **migraine with aura**," or "I have **temporal arteritis**," the doctor cannot prescribe the right treatment. Similarly, in polymer manufacturing, if an engineer cannot distinguish between **"melt flow rate"** and **"melt flow index,"** or between **"shot weight"** and **"clamp tonnage,"** catastrophic machine failures or product defects occur.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Five Pillars of Polymer Terminology (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Material Terminology** — Words describing polymers themselves (thermoplastic, thermoset, elastomer, copolymer, etc.)
> 2. **Processing Terminology** — Words describing what happens during processing (plasticization, melt homogenization, shear, etc.)
> 3. **Machine Terminology** — Words describing equipment components (barrel, screw, die, platen, etc.)
> 4. **Process Parameter Terminology** — Words describing operating conditions (temperature, pressure, screw speed, etc.)
> 5. **Product/Defect Terminology** — Words describing finished product quality (weld line, sink mark, flash, warpage, etc.)

---

### 🔷 Fundamental Polymer Material Terminology

#### A. Classification of Polymers

| Term | Formal Definition | Key Property |
|------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Thermoplastic** | A polymer that softens upon heating and hardens upon cooling, with the process being **reversible** and **repeatable**. | Recyclable, reprocessable |
| **Thermoset** | A polymer that undergoes **irreversible chemical crosslinking** during curing; cannot be remelted. | High heat resistance, rigid |
| **Elastomer** | A polymer exhibiting **elastic behaviour** with the ability to return to original shape after deformation. | High elongation, rubbery |
| **Homopolymer** | A polymer made from a **single type of monomer**. | Uniform properties |
| **Copolymer** | A polymer made from **two or more different monomers**. | Tailored properties |
| **Polymer Blend** | A physical mixture of **two or more polymers** without covalent bonding. | Combined properties |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:**
> - **Thermoplastic** = "Plastic that can be melted again" (reversible physical change)
> - **Thermoset** = "Plastic that sets permanently" (irreversible chemical change)
> - **Elastomer** = "Plastic that bounces back" (high elastic recovery)

#### B. Molecular Weight Terminology

The **molecular weight** of a polymer is arguably the single most important property affecting its processing behaviour and end-use performance.

- **Number Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M_n}$):** The total weight of all molecules divided by the total number of molecules.

$$\overline{M_n} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$$

- **Weight Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M_w}$):** Weighted by the weight fraction of each molecular species.

$$\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

- **Polydispersity Index (PDI):** Ratio indicating molecular weight distribution breadth.

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> - For **monodisperse** polymers (theoretical): **PDI = 1.0**
> - For **typical commercial polymers**: **PDI = 2.0 to 5.0**
> - Higher PDI = broader molecular weight distribution = easier processing but weaker mechanical properties.

---

### 🔷 Polymer Processing Terminology — Core Definitions

#### A. The Concept of "Plasticization"

**Plasticization** is the process of converting a polymer from a **solid granular/powder state** into a **homogeneous molten state** suitable for shaping. This is achieved through the combined action of **heat** (external heaters) and **mechanical shear** (screw rotation) in the presence of **residence time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:**
> *Plasticization is the combined thermal and mechanical process by which polymer granules are converted into a uniform, void-free, temperature-homogeneous melt suitable for further forming operations such as extrusion, injection, or blow moulding.*

#### B. The Concept of "Melt"

The **melt** refers to the polymer in its molten (liquid) state during processing. Key melt properties include:
- **Melt Temperature** — Actual temperature of molten polymer (°C)
- **Melt Pressure** — Hydrostatic pressure of melt in barrel/die (MPa or bar)
- **Melt Viscosity** — Resistance of melt to flow (Pa·s)
- **Melt Density** — Mass per unit volume of molten polymer (kg/m³)

#### C. The Concept of "Shear"

**Shear** is the relative sliding motion between adjacent layers of polymer melt. It is quantified by the **shear rate** ($\dot{\gamma}$) measured in **reciprocal seconds (s⁻¹)**.

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{dv}{dy}$$

where $v$ is velocity and $y$ is the distance perpendicular to flow direction.

#### D. The Concept of "Shear Rate in Processing"

| Process | Typical Shear Rate (s⁻¹) |
|---------|--------------------------|
| Compression Moulding | $10^0$ to $10^1$ |
| Calendering | $10^1$ to $10^2$ |
| Extrusion | $10^2$ to $10^3$ |
| Injection Moulding | $10^3$ to $10^5$ |

---

### 🔷 Machine Component Terminology

#### A. Injection Moulding Machine Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Barrel** | The heated cylindrical chamber where polymer melting occurs. |
| **Screw** | The rotating helical shaft that conveys, compresses, melts, and meters the polymer. |
| **Hopper** | The funnel-shaped feeder through which polymer granules enter the barrel. |
| **Nozzle** | The tapered outlet that connects the barrel to the mould. |
| **Platen** | The large steel plate that holds the mould half. |
| **Clamp Unit** | The mechanism (toggle or hydraulic) that opens/closes and locks the mould. |
| **Injection Unit** | The assembly (barrel + screw + nozzle) responsible for plasticization and injection. |
| **Shot** | The total volume of melt injected in one cycle. |
| **Clamp Tonnage** | The maximum clamping force (in **tonnes** or **kN**) the machine can apply. |
| **Mould Cavity** | The hollow space inside the mould that defines the part shape. |

#### B. Extrusion Machine Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Die** | The shaped opening that gives the extrudate its profile. |
| **Breaker Plate** | A perforated plate supporting the screen pack at the barrel exit. |
| **Screen Pack** | A set of wire mesh screens filtering contaminants from the melt. |
| **Feed Zone** | The first screw section (constant depth) where solids are conveyed. |
| **Compression Zone** | The middle section where the screw depth decreases (melting occurs). |
| **Metering Zone** | The final section (shallowest depth) that pumps melt at constant rate. |
| **L/D Ratio** | Length-to-Diameter ratio of the screw. |

---

### 🔷 Process Parameter Terminology

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Unit | Meaning |
|-----------|--------|--------------|---------|
| **Barrel Temperature** | $T_b$ | °C | Set temperature of barrel heater zones |
| **Melt Temperature** | $T_m$ | °C | Actual temperature of polymer melt |
| **Mould Temperature** | $T_{mold}$ | °C | Temperature of the mould cavity surface |
| **Injection Pressure** | $P_{inj}$ | MPa or bar | Pressure applied to inject melt |
| **Holding Pressure** | $P_{hold}$ | MPa or bar | Pressure during packing phase |
| **Back Pressure** | $P_{back}$ | MPa or bar | Pressure in front of screw during plasticization |
| **Screw Speed** | $N$ | rpm | Rotational speed of the screw |
| **Injection Speed** | $v_{inj}$ | mm/s | Speed of screw forward motion |
| **Cycle Time** | $t_c$ | seconds | Total time for one complete moulding cycle |
| **Cooling Time** | $t_{cool}$ | seconds | Time allowed for part solidification |

---

### 🔷 Product/Defect Terminology

| Defect | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **Flash** | Excess material that escapes from the mould parting line. |
| **Sink Mark** | Surface depression caused by internal shrinkage. |
| **Weld Line** | Visible line where two melt fronts meet. |
| **Warpage** | Distortion of part from intended geometry. |
| **Short Shot** | Incomplete filling of mould cavity. |
| **Voids** | Internal air bubbles in thick sections. |
| **Burn Marks** | Black/brown streaks from overheating or trapped air. |
| **Flow Marks** | Wavy patterns on part surface. |
| **Knit Line** | Similar to weld line, formed by flow around an obstruction. |
| **Silver Streaks** | Thin silver lines on surface from moisture or degraded material. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** Students must be able to **identify, define, AND state the cause AND remedy** for each defect to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 1.2 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔷 Systematic Classification of Polymer Processing Terminology

The KTU 2024 scheme organizes polymer processing terminology into **five functional categories**. Understanding this classification is essential for both exam answers and industrial practice.

---

### A. Material State Terminology — The Four States of a Polymer

Throughout any polymer manufacturing process, the polymer exists in one of **four distinct states**:

1. **Solid State (Granular/Powder)** — Virgin polymer as supplied.
2. **Transition State (Sintering/Sticky)** — Beginning of melting, partially fused.
3. **Melt State (Viscous Liquid)** — Fully molten, ready for shaping.
4. **Solidified State (Finished Part)** — Cooled and dimensionally stable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Each state has different **viscosity**, **density**, **specific heat capacity**, and **thermal conductivity**. Misidentifying the state leads to wrong temperature settings and part defects.

---

### B. Rheological Terminology — The Language of Flow

**Rheology** is the science of flow and deformation of matter. In polymer processing, the key rheological terms are:

| Term | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|------|--------|------------|------|
| **Viscosity** | $\eta$ | Resistance of fluid to flow | Pa·s |
| **Shear Stress** | $\tau$ | Force per unit area acting tangentially | Pa |
| **Shear Rate** | $\dot{\gamma}$ | Velocity gradient perpendicular to flow | s⁻¹ |
| **Newtonian Fluid** | — | Fluid with constant viscosity | — |
| **Non-Newtonian Fluid** | — | Fluid whose viscosity changes with shear rate | — |
| **Shear-Thinning** | — | Viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate | — |
| **Shear-Thickening** | — | Viscosity increases with increasing shear rate | — |
| **Thixotropy** | — | Time-dependent viscosity reduction under constant shear | — |

The fundamental relationship:

$$\eta = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Polymer melts are NON-NEWTONIAN and SHEAR-THINNING.** This means their viscosity is not constant — it decreases as the shear rate increases. This is why the same polymer flows more easily at high injection speeds.

---

### C. The Power Law (Ostwald-de Waele) Model

For polymer melts, viscosity is commonly modelled as:

$$\eta = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

where:
- $K$ = **consistency index** (Pa·sⁿ)
- $n$ = **power law index** (dimensionless)
- $n = 1$ → Newtonian; $n < 1$ → Shear-thinning (typical for polymers); $n > 1$ → Shear-thickening

---

### D. Screw Geometry Terminology — Critical for Extrusion & Injection

The **screw** is the heart of every plasticizing machine. Its key geometric parameters are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| **Screw Diameter** | $D$ | Outer diameter of screw flight |
| **Screw Length** | $L$ | Total length of the screw |
| **L/D Ratio** | $L/D$ | Length-to-diameter ratio (typically **18:1 to 24:1** for injection) |
| **Flight Depth (Feed)** | $H_f$ | Channel depth in feed zone |
| **Flight Depth (Metering)** | $H_m$ | Channel depth in metering zone |
| **Compression Ratio** | $CR$ | $H_f / H_m$ (typically **2:1 to 4:1**) |
| **Lead** | $l$ | Axial distance per full flight turn |
| **Flight Width** | $e$ | Width of the helical flight |
| **Helix Angle** | $\theta$ | Angle of flight relative to plane perpendicular to screw axis |
| **Radial Clearance** | $\delta$ | Gap between screw flight tip and barrel inner wall |

---

### E. Mould Terminology (Injection Moulding)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Cavity** | The hollow space defining the part's external shape. |
| **Core** | The protruding feature defining internal shape. |
| **Parting Line** | The line where two mould halves meet. |
| **Gate** | The narrow entry point connecting runner to cavity. |
| **Runner** | The channel distributing melt from sprue to gate. |
| **Sprue** | The main channel from nozzle to runner system. |
| **Ejector Pin** | A pin that pushes the solidified part out of the mould. |
| **Cooling Channel** | Internal channel through which coolant circulates. |
| **Bubbler** | A cooling channel that brings coolant close to cavity surface. |
| **Baffle** | A cooling channel diverter for uniform heat removal. |

---

### F. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Printable for Exam)

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $\eta = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | Definition of viscosity | Pa·s |
| 2 | $\eta = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Power law model | Pa·s |
| 3 | $\overline{M_n} = \sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i$ | Number average MW | g/mol |
| 4 | $\overline{M_w} = \sum N_i M_i^2 / \sum N_i M_i$ | Weight average MW | g/mol |
| 5 | $\text{PDI} = \overline{M_w} / \overline{M_n}$ | Polydispersity index | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dot{\gamma} = dv / dy$ | Shear rate definition | s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $Q = \alpha \cdot N - \beta \cdot P / \eta$ | Screw output (extrusion) | m³/s |
| 8 | $P_{clamp} \geq A_{proj} \times P_{cavity}$ | Clamp tonnage requirement | tonnes |
| 9 | $t_{cool} = (s^2 / \pi^2 \alpha) \cdot \ln(8/\pi^2) \cdot (T_m - T_e)/(T_{mold} - T_e)$ | Cooling time (approx.) | seconds |
| 10 | $L/D = L / D$ | Screw L/D ratio | dimensionless |
| 11 | $CR = H_f / H_m$ | Compression ratio | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\text{Cycle Time} = t_{inj} + t_{hold} + t_{cool} + t_{eject}$ | Total cycle time | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Warning:** You will NOT be asked to derive these formulas directly. Instead, questions will be **"Define the term and state its significance"** or **"Explain the relationship between viscosity, shear rate, and processability."** Memorize definitions + units + industrial significance.

---

### G. Real-World Engineering Utility

**Why Terminology Mastery is Critical in Industry:**

1. **Safety:** Misunderstanding "decomposition temperature" vs. "melting temperature" can cause toxic fume release.
2. **Quality:** Confusing "weld line" vs. "knit line" leads to wrong defect remedies.
3. **Communication:** International suppliers and customers use standardized terms (ISO 1043, ASTM D883).
4. **Process Control:** Each term maps to a measurable parameter on the machine HMI.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard References (No Hallucination):**
> - **ASTM D883** — Standard Terminology Relating to Plastics
> - **ISO 1043** — Plastics — Symbols and Abbreviations
> - **ISO 472** — Plastics — Vocabulary
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 1.3 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔷 Derivations of Foundational Relationships in Polymer Processing Terminology

While "Terminology" is primarily a **definitional topic**, the KTU 2024 examiner often asks derivations that establish **why** specific terms are defined the way they are. Below are the exhaustive derivations of the most important relationships.

---

### 🔷 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Screw Output (Q) and Process Variables

**Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for the volumetric output ($Q$) of a single-screw extruder in terms of screw speed ($N$), pressure ($P$), and melt viscosity ($\eta$).

**Conceptual Basis:** The output of a single-screw extruder arises from **two competing mechanisms**:
- **Drag Flow** ($Q_d$): Forward pumping caused by the relative motion of the barrel over the screw channel.
- **Pressure Flow** ($Q_p$): Backward leakage caused by the pressure gradient at the die.

The net output is:

$$Q = Q_d - Q_p$$

**Step 1 — Drag Flow Component:**

Consider the screw channel as an "unwrapped" flat surface. The barrel moves relative to the screw at velocity:

$$V_b = \pi D N \cos\theta$$

where:
- $D$ = screw diameter
- $N$ = screw rotational speed (rev/s)
- $\theta$ = helix angle

For a Newtonian fluid in a rectangular channel of width $W$ and depth $H$, the drag flow rate is:

$$Q_d = \frac{1}{2} V_b \cdot W \cdot H$$

**Step 2 — Pressure Flow Component:**

The pressure flow in a rectangular channel (analogous to flow between parallel plates) is given by the Poiseuille equation:

$$Q_p = \frac{W H^3}{12 \eta} \cdot \left(-\frac{dP}{dz}\right)$$

where $dP/dz$ is the pressure gradient along the channel.

**Step 3 — Net Output:**

$$Q = \frac{1}{2} V_b W H - \frac{W H^3}{12 \eta} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L_{ch}}$$

where $L_{ch}$ is the channel length in the down-channel direction.

**Step 4 — Simplification Using Geometric Constants:**

Define shape factors $\alpha$ and $\beta$ that account for the actual screw geometry (helix angle, flight width effects):

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{2} W H \cdot \pi D \cos\theta$$

$$\beta = \frac{W H^3 \sin^2\theta}{12 \eta}$$

**Step 5 — Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{Q = \alpha N - \frac{\beta P}{\eta}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- If die is **open** ($P = 0$): $Q = \alpha N$ (pure drag flow, maximum output).
- If die is **closed** ($Q = 0$): Maximum pressure $P_{max} = \alpha \eta N / \beta$.
- The output is a **linear function of screw speed** $N$ and a **decreasing function of die pressure** $P$.

---

### 🔷 Derivation 2: Clamp Tonnage Requirement

**Problem Statement:** Derive the relationship between clamp tonnage, projected area, and cavity pressure for an injection moulding machine.

**Conceptual Basis:** The clamp force must exceed the **mould opening force** created by melt pressure acting on the projected area of the part.

**Step 1 — Force on the Part:**

The melt inside the cavity exerts pressure $P_{cavity}$ on the mould walls. The force trying to open the mould is:

$$F_{open} = P_{cavity} \times A_{projected}$$

where $A_{projected}$ is the projected area of the part perpendicular to the mould opening direction.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium Condition:**

For the mould to remain closed, the clamp force $F_{clamp}$ must satisfy:

$$F_{clamp} \geq F_{open}$$

**Step 3 — Conversion to Tonnage:**

Since clamp tonnage is measured in **tonnes** (1 tonne ≈ 9.81 kN):

$$T_{clamp} = \frac{P_{cavity} \times A_{projected}}{9.81 \times 10^3} \quad \text{[in tonnes]}$$

**Step 4 — Final Expression:**

$$\boxed{T_{clamp} \geq \frac{P_{cavity} \times A_{projected}}{9.81 \times 10^3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Typical cavity pressures are **30 to 70 MPa**. For safety, a factor of **1.2 to 1.5** is applied to the calculated tonnage.

---

### 🔷 Derivation 3: Number Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M_n}$)

**Step 1 — Definition:**

Consider a polymer sample containing $N_i$ molecules of molecular weight $M_i$. The **total mass** of all molecules is:

$$m_{total} = \sum_{i} N_i M_i$$

**Step 2 — Total Number of Molecules:**

$$N_{total} = \sum_{i} N_i$$

**Step 3 — Number Average Molecular Weight (by definition):**

$$\overline{M_n} = \frac{\text{Total mass}}{\text{Total number of molecules}}$$

**Step 4 — Final Expression:**

$$\boxed{\overline{M_n} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}}$$

**Why "Number Average"?** Each molecule is **counted once**, regardless of its size. Thus, small molecules dominate this average.

---

### 🔷 Derivation 4: Weight Average Molecular Weight ($\overline{M_w}$)

**Step 1 — Weight Fraction of Species $i$:**

$$w_i = \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum N_i M_i}$$

**Step 2 — Weighted Average Definition:**

$$\overline{M_w} = \sum w_i M_i = \sum \frac{N_i M_i}{\sum N_i M_i} \cdot M_i$$

**Step 3 — Simplification:**

$$\boxed{\overline{M_w} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}}$$

**Why "Weight Average"?** Each molecule is **weighted by its own mass** (squared term). Thus, **larger molecules dominate** this average. This is why $\overline{M_w} \geq \overline{M_n}$ always.

---

### 🔷 Derivation 5: Polydispersity Index (PDI)

**Step 1 — Combine the Two Averages:**

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\overline{M_w}}{\overline{M_n}}$$

**Step 2 — Substituting the Expressions:**

$$\text{PDI} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2 / \sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i}$$

**Step 3 — Final Compact Form:**

$$\boxed{\text{PDI} = \frac{\sum N_i M_i^2 \cdot \sum N_i}{\left(\sum N_i M_i\right)^2}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- $\text{PDI} = 1$ → All molecules have the same weight (monodisperse, theoretical only).
- $\text{PDI} > 1$ → A distribution of molecular weights exists (polydisperse, real polymers).
- $\text{PDI} \rightarrow \infty$ → Extremely broad distribution.

---

### 🔷 Analytical Matrix — Term-to-Concept Mapping

| Term | Belongs To Category | Related Process | Common Confusion |
|------|---------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Plasticization** | Process | Injection/Extrusion | vs. "Plasticization (chemical)" of PVC |
| **Shear Rate** | Rheology | All flow processes | vs. Shear Stress |
| **Clamp Tonnage** | Machine | Injection Moulding | vs. Injection Force |
| **Compression Ratio** | Screw Geometry | Extrusion/Injection | vs. Compression Moulding |
| **Sink Mark** | Defect | Injection Moulding | vs. Void (internal vs. surface) |
| **Weld Line** | Defect | Injection Moulding | vs. Knit Line |
| **Melt Flow Index** | Material Property | All | vs. Melt Flow Rate (ASTM vs. ISO terminology) |
| **Residence Time** | Process | Extrusion | vs. Cycle Time |
| **Shot Weight** | Machine | Injection Moulding | vs. Part Weight |
| **Die Swell** | Process | Extrusion | vs. Extrudate Swell (same thing) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 1.4 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🔷 Diagram 1: Functional Architecture of Polymer Processing Terminology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Polymer Processing Terminology]
    A --> B[Material Terminology]
    A --> C[Process Terminology]
    A --> D[Machine Terminology]
    A --> E[Product Terminology]

    B --> B1[Thermoplastic]
    B --> B2[Thermoset]
    B --> B3[Elastomer]
    B --> B4[Homopolymer]
    B --> B5[Copolymer]
    B --> B6[Polymer Blend]
    B --> B7[Molecular Weight]
    B --> B8[MFI MFR]

    C --> C1[Plasticization]
    C --> C2[Melt Homogenization]
    C --> C3[Shear Mixing]
    C --> C4[Cooling Solidification]
    C --> C5[Curing Crosslinking]

    D --> D1[Injection Machine]
    D --> D2[Extrusion Machine]
    D --> D3[Blow Moulding Machine]
    D --> D4[Compression Moulding]

    D1 --> D1a[Barrel]
    D1 --> D1b[Screw]
    D1 --> D1c[Nozzle]
    D1 --> D1d[Clamp Unit]
    D1 --> D1e[Mould]

    D2 --> D2a[Hopper]
    D2 --> D2b[Barrel]
    D2 --> D2c[Screw]
    D2 --> D2d[Die]
    D2 --> D2e[Haul Off]

    E --> E1[Flash]
    E --> E2[Sink Mark]
    E --> E3[Weld Line]
    E --> E4[Warpage]
    E --> E5[Short Shot]
    E --> E6[Voids]
```
