## 1.5 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 🔷 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition Type)

---

#### ❓ Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Define the following with their significance in polymer processing:**
**(a) Plasticization**
**(b) Melt Flow Index (MFI)**

**✅ Model Answer:**

**(a) Plasticization:**
Plasticization is the combined thermal and mechanical process by which polymer granules are converted into a homogeneous, void-free melt suitable for shaping operations such as injection moulding or extrusion. It involves three simultaneous mechanisms: **conduction of heat** from barrel heaters, **generation of frictional heat** from screw rotation (shear heating), and **mechanical mixing** to eliminate temperature and composition gradients. The degree of plasticization directly affects product quality — incomplete plasticization causes unmelted particles (gels) and inconsistent flow.

**(b) Melt Flow Index (MFI):**
Melt Flow Index, also called Melt Mass-Flow Rate (MFR), is defined as the **mass of polymer (in grams) extruded in 10 minutes** through a standard capillary die (typically 2.095 mm diameter) under a specified load (usually 2.16 kg) at a specified temperature (specified for each polymer). MFI is measured using a **Melt Flow Indexer** as per **ASTM D1238 / ISO 1133**. A **higher MFI indicates lower viscosity** and therefore easier processability. It is a critical parameter for material selection in injection moulding and extrusion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
> - (a) Definition = 2 marks; Significance = 1 mark.
> - (b) Definition + standard reference = 2 marks; Significance = 1 mark.

---

#### ❓ Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]

**Differentiate between:**
**(a) Thermoplastic and Thermoset polymers**
**(b) Weld Line and Knit Line**

**✅ Model Answer:**

**(a) Thermoplastic vs. Thermoset:**

| Property | Thermoplastic | Thermoset |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| Molecular Structure | Linear/Branched chains | Crosslinked 3D network |
| Behaviour on Heating | Softens, melts (reversible) | Decomposes (irreversible) |
| Reprocessability | Recyclable, remeltable | Non-recyclable by remelting |
| Examples | PE, PP, PVC, PS, PET, Nylon | Phenolic, Epoxy, Melamine |
| Mechanical Strength | Moderate | High |
| Chemical Resistance | Lower | Higher |

**(b) Weld Line vs. Knit Line:**

- **Weld Line:** Formed when **two or more melt fronts** meet after flowing around **opposite sides** of an obstruction (e.g., a hole or insert) inside the mould. The molecular chains at the meeting point have **random orientation**, leading to weaker bonding.
- **Knit Line:** Formed when **two melt fronts** meet after flowing from the **same direction** (e.g., from two gates). The molecular chains may have **some parallel alignment**, resulting in slightly better bonding than a weld line.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often state "weld line and knit line are the same." They are **NOT identical** — both the *cause* (flow path geometry) and the *strength* differ. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.

---

### 🔷 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Long Answer / Module Internal Choice)

---

#### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q. (a) Define the term "viscosity" in the context of polymer melts. Explain the difference between Newtonian and non-Newtonian behaviour with suitable examples and rheological models. (7 marks)**

**(b) List and explain any five common defects encountered in injection-moulded plastic products. For each defect, state at least one cause and one remedy. (7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (a) — Viscosity & Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Behaviour (7 marks)

**Definition of Viscosity (2 marks):**
Viscosity ($\eta$) is the measure of a fluid's internal resistance to flow. Mathematically, it is defined as the ratio of shear stress ($\tau$) to shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$):

$$\eta = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$

The SI unit of viscosity is **Pascal-second (Pa·s)**, and the CGS unit is **Poise (P)** where 1 Pa·s = 10 P.

**Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Behaviour (3 marks):**

| Property | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
|----------|----------------|---------------------|
| Definition | Viscosity is constant, independent of shear rate | Viscosity changes with shear rate |
| Equation | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ (linear) | $\tau = \eta(\dot{\gamma}) \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ (non-linear) |
| Examples | Water, air, low-MW oils, simple hydrocarbons | Polymer melts, polymer solutions, blood, ketchup |
| Viscosity vs. Shear Rate | Constant | Decreases (shear-thinning) or increases (shear-thickening) |

**Rheological Models (2 marks):**

**1. Newton's Law (for Newtonian fluids):**
$$\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$$
where $\eta$ is a constant.

**2. Power Law Model (Ostwald-de Waele, for non-Newtonian fluids):**
$$\eta = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$
where $K$ is the consistency index and $n$ is the power law index.
- If $n = 1$ → Newtonian behaviour
- If $n < 1$ → Shear-thinning (pseudoplastic) — most polymer melts
- If $n > 1$ → Shear-thickening (dilatant) — rare

**Significance in Polymer Processing:**
Understanding viscosity behaviour is critical for **die design**, **mould filling simulation**, **process parameter optimization**, and **defect prediction**. Polymer melts being shear-thinning means that increasing injection speed reduces viscosity, improving flow but potentially causing **jetting** or **flash**.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (b) — Five Common Injection Moulding Defects (7 marks)

**1. Flash (Thin Excess Material at Parting Line) (1.4 marks):**
- **Description:** Thin layer of plastic escapes from the mould cavity along the parting line.
- **Cause:** Insufficient clamp tonnage, excessive injection pressure, worn mould surfaces, or high melt temperature.
- **Remedy:** Increase clamp force, reduce injection pressure, repair mould surfaces, or lower melt temperature.

**2. Sink Mark (Surface Depression) (1.4 marks):**
- **Description:** Localized depression on the part surface.
- **Cause:** Inadequate holding pressure/holding time leading to internal shrinkage as the part cools.
- **Remedy:** Increase holding pressure, extend holding/cooling time, or redesign with uniform wall thickness.

**3. Weld Line (Visible Line from Flow Front Reunion) (1.4 marks):**
- **Description:** A visible seam where two melt fronts meet after flowing around an obstacle.
- **Cause:** Low melt temperature, low injection speed, or poor mould design (multiple obstructions).
- **Remedy:** Increase melt and mould temperature, increase injection speed, or relocate the gate.

**4. Warpage (Distortion of Part Geometry) (1.4 marks):**
- **Description:** Part deviates from intended flat/curved geometry after ejection.
- **Cause:** Non-uniform cooling, differential shrinkage due to fibre orientation, or unbalanced mould design.
- **Remedy:** Optimize cooling channel design, increase cooling time, or anneal the part post-moulding.

**5. Short Shot (Incomplete Cavity Filling) (1.4 marks):**
- **Description:** The mould cavity is only partially filled with melt.
- **Cause:** Insufficient injection pressure/speed, low melt temperature, or inadequate venting.
- **Remedy:** Increase injection pressure and speed, raise barrel/melt temperature, or improve mould venting.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Definition is missing or vague** (−1 mark per defect)
> 2. **Cause OR remedy not stated** (−0.5 mark per defect)
> 3. **No diagram or schematic** for the defect (−0.5 mark overall)
> 4. **Confusing weld line and knit line** (−1 mark)
> 5. **Using layman terms instead of standard terminology** (e.g., "bubble" instead of "void") (−0.5 mark)
> **Pro Tip:** Always state the defect in the format: **Definition + Cause + Remedy + Diagram (if time permits).**

---

#### 📘 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q. (a) Define the term "screw" as used in plastic processing. With a neat sketch, explain the three functional zones of a single-screw extruder and their role in polymer processing. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the following process parameter terms with their typical values and units: (i) Back Pressure, (ii) Cooling Time, (iii) Mould Temperature, (iv) Holding Pressure. (7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (a) — Screw and Its Three Zones (7 marks)

**Definition of Screw (2 marks):**
A **screw** is a rotating helical shaft installed inside the heated barrel of an injection moulding machine or extruder. Its primary function is to **convey, compress, melt, mix, and meter** the polymer from the hopper to the nozzle/die. The screw is characterized by its **diameter (D)**, **length (L)**, **L/D ratio**, **compression ratio (CR)**, and **helix angle (θ)**.

**Three Functional Zones (5 marks):**

| Zone | Location | Depth | Function |
|------|----------|-------|----------|
| **1. Feed Zone** | Near hopper (length ≈ 50% of L) | Constant, deep channel | Conveys solid polymer granules from hopper into the barrel via drag-induced conveying. |
| **2. Compression (Transition) Zone** | Middle section (length ≈ 25-30% of L) | Depth decreases gradually | Compresses, melts, and mixes the polymer using combined heat conduction and shear heating. Solid-bed conversion to melt occurs here. |
| **3. Metering Zone** | Near nozzle (length ≈ 20-25% of L) | Constant, shallow channel | Pumps the homogenized melt at a constant rate to the nozzle, ensuring steady output and uniform pressure. |

**Diagrammatic Representation (Mermaid-based):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hopper] --> B[SOLID BED]
    B --> C[Feed Zone Deep Constant]
    C --> D[Compression Zone Depth Decreases]
    D --> E[Metering Zone Shallow Constant]
    E --> F[Nozzle Die]
    C -.Conduction Heat.-> D
    D -.Shear Heat.-> E
    style C fill:#FFD700
    style D fill:#FF6347
    style E fill:#90EE90
```

**Significance of Zones (1 mark):**
Each zone is essential. If the **feed zone is too short**, granules will not be conveyed properly. If the **compression zone is inadequate**, melting will be incomplete. If the **metering zone is too short**, output will pulsate and pressure will be unstable.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION (b) — Process Parameter Definitions (7 marks)

**(i) Back Pressure (1.75 marks):**
Back pressure is the **resistance pressure** maintained in front of the screw tip during the plasticization (screw rotation) phase of an injection moulding cycle. It is created by partially restricting the screw's backward motion using hydraulic pressure. Typical values range from **0.5 to 5 MPa (5 to 50 bar)**. Higher back pressure improves melt homogeneity and removes trapped air, but lengthens cycle time.

**(ii) Cooling Time (1.75 marks):**
Cooling time is the **duration** for which the mould remains closed (after holding phase ends) to allow the part to solidify sufficiently for ejection. Typical values range from **5 to 60 seconds** depending on wall thickness and material. It is often the **longest phase** of the cycle and directly affects productivity.

**(iii) Mould Temperature (1.75 marks):**
Mould temperature is the **controlled surface temperature** of the mould cavity, maintained by circulating coolant (water or oil) through internal channels. Typical values range from **20°C to 120°C** depending on polymer (e.g., 20-60°C for PP, 80-120°C for PC). Higher mould temperatures improve surface finish, reduce residual stress, and minimize sink marks, but increase cycle time.

**(iv) Holding Pressure (1.75 marks):**
Holding pressure (also called packing pressure) is the **sustained pressure** applied to the melt in the cavity **after the cavity is filled** but before the gate freezes, to compensate for volumetric shrinkage due to cooling. Typical values range from **30 to 100 MPa**. Proper holding pressure eliminates voids and sink marks; insufficient holding pressure causes short shots and excessive shrinkage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often **forget the units** when defining process parameters. Always write "**Cooling time is measured in seconds**" or "**Mould temperature is in °C**" — examiners award **0.5 marks** specifically for the unit.

---

### 🔷 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This Before Every Exam:**

✅ **Five Categories of Terminology:**
1. Material (polymer types, MW, MFI)
2. Process (plasticization, melt, shear)
3. Machine (barrel, screw, die, clamp)
4. Process Parameters (T, P, N, time)
5. Product/Defects (flash, sink, weld, warp)

✅ **Three Polymer Types:**
- Thermoplastic (reversible, recyclable)
- Thermoset (irreversible, crosslinked)
- Elastomer (high elasticity)

✅ **Three Screw Zones:**
- Feed (deep, constant) → Convey
- Compression (decreasing) → Melt
- Metering (shallow, constant) → Pump

✅ **Key Molecular Weight Formulas:**
- $\overline{M_n} = \sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i$
- $\overline{M_w} = \sum N_i M_i^2 / \sum N_i M_i$
- $\text{PDI} = \overline{M_w} / \overline{M_n} \geq 1$

✅ **Five Critical Defects (with causes):**
1. Flash → low clamp force
2. Sink Mark → low holding pressure
3. Weld Line → low melt temp / obstruction
4. Warpage → non-uniform cooling
5. Short Shot → insufficient fill

✅ **Three Key Material Properties:**
- MFI (ease of flow, g/10 min)
- PDI (distribution breadth, ≥ 1)
- Viscosity (flow resistance, Pa·s)

✅ **Process Parameters Always Include Units:**
- T in °C, P in MPa/bar, N in rpm, time in seconds

✅ **Standards to Remember:**
- ASTM D883 (plastics terminology)
- ISO 1043 (symbols)
- ISO 1133 / ASTM D1238 (MFI)

✅ **Examiner's Golden Rule:**
> "Definition + Significance + Units + Example = Full Marks"

✅ **Most Common Exam Topics (by frequency):**
1. Plasticization definition
2. Screw zones with diagram
3. Weld line vs. knit line
4. MFI definition
5. Defect causes and remedies
6. Newtonian vs. non-Newtonian behaviour
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Process Description # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Polymer Process Description** refers to the systematic, step-by-step engineering characterization of the unit operations employed to convert raw polymeric materials (resins, granules, powders, melts, or solutions) into finished or semi-finished products of defined geometry, mechanical integrity, and surface quality. Under the KTU 2024 PCPOT503 syllabus, Module 1 classifies these into **Primary Forming Processes** (where the shape is established from the polymer melt/feed) and **Secondary Forming Processes** (where the already-shaped article is re-shaped or finished). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to describe the operating sequence, identify the machine elements, list the process variables, and explain the inter-relationship of heat–pressure–time for each of the major shaping operations: *Injection Molding, Extrusion, Blow Molding, Compression Molding, Thermoforming, Rotational Molding, Calendaring, and Casting*. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Polymer Kitchen" Imagine a modern industrial bakery: | Bakery Element | Polymer Processing Equivalent | |---|---| | Mixing dough ingredients (flour, water, yeast) | **Compounding** – blending polymer resin with additives, fillers, stabilizers | | Heating the dough to make it pliable | **Plasticization / Melting** – raising the polymer above its melting ($T_m$) or glass-transition ($T_g$) temperature | | Pushing dough into a shaped mould to bake a cake | **Injection / Compression / Blow Molding** – forcing the melt into a defined cavity | | Pushing dough through a nozzle to make noodles | **Extrusion** – continuous forcing of melt through a die | | Letting the cake cool and solidify before demoulding | **Cooling / Solidification** – removing sensible heat to freeze the part geometry | | Decorating / cutting the cooled cake | **Secondary Operations** – trimming, printing, machining, welding | In every polymer process, the **four universal drivers** are: *Heat (Q)*, *Pressure (P)*, *Time (t)*, and *Flow (Q̇)*. Mastering how these four variables interact defines the *process description* for any operation. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metrics Used Throughout KTU Polymer Processing:** > - **Melt Temperature (${T_m}^{melt}$):** typically **180 °C – 320 °C** for thermoplastics > - **Mold / Surface Temperature (${T}_{mold}$):** typically **20 °C – 120 °C** > - **Injection / Forming Pressure (P):** typically **35 MPa – 140 MPa** > - **Cycle Time (${t}_{cycle}$):** typically **10 s – 120 s** > - **Clamping Tonnage (F):** typically **50 tonnes – 4000 tonnes** ## 1.3 Classification of Polymer Processes ``` POLYMER PROCESSING ├── 1. Primary Forming (Shape Generation) │ ├── 1.1 Injection Molding (IM) │ ├── 1.2 Extrusion (EXT) │ ├── 1.3 Blow Molding (BM) │ ├── 1.4 Compression Molding (CM) │ ├── 1.5 Transfer Molding (TM) │ ├── 1.6 Thermoforming (TF) │ ├── 1.7 Rotational Molding (RM) │ ├── 1.8 Calendaring │ ├── 1.9 Casting │ └── 1.10 Reaction Injection Molding (RIM) └── 2. Secondary Forming (Re-shaping / Finishing) ├── 2.1 Welding (Hot plate, Ultrasonic, Vibration) ├── 2.2 Machining & Trimming ├── 2.3 Surface Decoration (Printing, Plating) └── 2.4 Annealing / Conditioning ``` > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid for KTU Viva:** "**I Eat Big Chapatis To Remain Calm**" → **I**njection, **E**xtrusion, **B**low, **C**ompression, **T**hermoforming, **R**otational, **C**alendaring, **C**asting. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Big Four" — Process-by-Process Operational Logic ### 2.1.1 Injection Molding (IM) — The Workhorse **Why it dominates:** ~32 % of all plastics processed worldwide pass through an injection molding machine (IMM). It produces net-shape, high-tolerance parts at high repeatability. **How it works — Six-step Cycle:** 1. **Feeding & Plasticization:** Granules from the hopper fall into the heated barrel; a rotating **screw** (acting as an Archimedes screw) conveys, compresses, shears, and melts the polymer. Melt accumulates in front of the screw, pushing it back (this phase is called *metering*). 2. **Injection (Filling):** The screw acts as a plunger and rams the melt through the nozzle into the closed mould cavity at high velocity. ~95 % of the cavity volume is filled during this step. 3. **Packing / Holding:** The screw continues to push a small amount of additional melt (5–10 % by volume) to compensate for volumetric shrinkage as the part cools. Pressure falls from peak to hold pressure. 4. **Cooling:** No material flows; heat is conducted out through the mould wall (water/oil channels). The part solidifies from the wall inward. 5. **Screw Recovery:** Concurrently with cooling, the screw rotates, plasticizing the next shot. This is why IMM cycle times are so short. 6. **Mould Opening & Ejection:** The mould halves separate; ejector pins push the part out; mould closes, clamp tonnage re-applied → cycle repeats. ### 2.1.2 Extrusion — Continuous Profile Formation **Why it matters:** All plastic films, sheets, pipes, profiles, wire coatings, and even the raw feed for IM machines are made by extrusion. **How it works:** 1. **Feeding (Solid Conveying):** Polymer granules are drawn from the hopper into the screw channel by *drag flow* between the rotating screw and stationary barrel wall. 2. **Melting / Plasticization:** The compression zone (where the channel depth decreases) generates shear heat plus barrel heat, melting the polymer. 3. **Metering (Melt Pumping):** The shallow, constant-depth metering zone behaves as a *positive displacement pump* — outputting a steady, pressurized melt stream. 4. **Die Forming:** The melt is forced through a *die* that defines the cross-section of the product (a circular annulus → pipe; a flat slit → sheet; a complex profile → window frame). 5. **Calibration & Cooling:** The extrudate (still hot and pliable) passes through a **calibrator** (vacuum sizing tank or sleeve) that fixes dimensions, then through a water bath/air cooling tunnel. 6. **Haul-off & Cutting:** Puller rolls (caterpillar or belt) draw the profile at controlled speed, and an in-line cutter (for pipes) or winder (for films) finishes the product. ### 2.1.3 Blow Molding — Hollow-Article Champion **Why it matters:** Bottles, jerry cans, fuel tanks, toys — all hollow — are blow molded. Used for ~10 % of global polymer production. **Two principal variants:** - **Extrusion Blow Molding (EBM):** A continuous tube of melt (**parison**) is extruded downward between two open mould halves. Mould closes, pinching one end; a **blow pin** injects compressed air (~0.5–1.0 MPa), inflating the parison against the cold mould walls. After cooling, mould opens and the flash (excess) is trimmed. - **Injection Blow Molding (IBM):** A preform is injection-molded on a steel core (like a small test tube), transferred to a blow station, blown, and ejected. Used for high-clarity PET bottles. ### 2.1.4 Compression Molding — The Thermoset Specialist **Why it matters:** Ideal for thermosetting plastics (phenolic, melamine, unsaturated polyester with glass fiber — *SMC/BMC*), rubber products, and large-area parts. **How it works:** 1. A measured charge of molding compound (powder, granule, or preform) is placed in the **open, heated lower mould cavity**. 2. The **upper mould (force plug)** descends, closing the mould under high clamping force (50–2000+ tonnes). 3. Under combined *heat + pressure*, the material flows, fills the cavity, and simultaneously undergoes a *cross-linking* (curing) chemical reaction in thermosets. 4. After the cure time, mould opens and the part is ejected (often with assistance, as thermosets cannot remelt). ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master Table — All Equations a KTU Examiner Can Ask in Module 1** > (For exam: units in **SI** unless noted; use $\vert x \vert$ only as absolute value if needed.) | # | Formula | Description / Use | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\boxed{V_{shot} = \dfrac{m_{part}}{\rho_{melt}} \times n_{cav}}$ | Shot volume from part mass, density, and number of cavities | $\text{cm}^3$ | | 2 | $\boxed{F_{clamp} \ge P_{cav} \times A_{proj}}$ | Minimum clamping tonnage (Stefan Rumscheidt rule of thumb) | $\text{N (or tonnes)}$ | | 3 | $\boxed{Q_{IM} = \dfrac{3.6 \times n \times V_{shot}}{t_{cycle}}}$ | Injection molding throughput | $\text{kg/h}$ | | 4 | $\boxed{Q_{EXT} = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}}$ | Extruder output (simplified drag-flow only) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | | 5 | $\boxed{t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8(T_m - T_w)}{\pi^2 (T_{eject} - T_w)}\right)}$ | Analytical cooling time for a slab of thickness $h$ | $\text{s}$ | | 6 | $\boxed{P_{cav} \approx P_{inj} \times 10^{-p \cdot s}}$ | Pressure drop through runner/gate, $p$ = pressure loss coefficient | $\text{Pa}$ | | 7 | $\boxed{\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\pi D N}{h}}$ | Apparent shear rate in extruder screw channel | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | | 8 | $\boxed{LDR = \dfrac{L_{max}}{h_{avg}}}$ | Length-to-Diameter (L/D) ratio of an extruder screw — typical **20–30** | dimensionless | | 9 | $\boxed{\eta_{app} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}}$ | Power-law (Ostwald-de Waele) viscosity for pseudoplastic melt | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ | | 10 | $\boxed{P_{blow} = \dfrac{2 \sigma t}{R}}$ | Hoop stress relation for an inflated parison (thin shell) | $\text{Pa}$ | | 11 | $\boxed{\eta_{MFI} \propto \dfrac{1}{MFI}}$ | Melt Flow Index is *inversely* related to melt viscosity | $\text{g/10 min}$ | | 12 | $\boxed{C_{p} \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.5 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}}$ | Specific heat of polymer melt (range for design calcs) | $\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ | | 13 | $\boxed{Q_{heat} = \dot{m} \, C_p \, \Delta T}$ | Heat required to raise polymer from feed to melt temperature | $\text{W}$ | | 14 | $\boxed{T_{g} \approx \dfrac{2}{3} T_{m} \;(K)}$ | Beaman-Boyer rule of thumb for many crystalline polymers | $\text{K}$ | | 15 | $\boxed{A_{proj} = W \times L_{proj}}$ | Projected area for clamping force calculation | $\text{m}^2$ | | 16 | $\boxed{S_{flash} = P_{cav} \times A_{flash}}$ | Clamping force contribution from flash land | $\text{N}$ | ## 2.3 Process Variables — The Q-P-T Triangle Every polymer process can be uniquely described by the interaction of three controllable variables: - **Q (Heat input / Energy):** supplied by electric band heaters, shear dissipation, and (in some cases) induction. - **P (Pressure / Mechanical Force):** generated by the screw (extruder), plunger (IM), or hydraulic press (CM). - **t (Time / Residence):** residence time distribution, cycle time, cooling time. A *fourth*, often neglected variable is **F (Flow / Velocity)** — the velocity of the melt front, critical for surface replication and weld-line strength. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Injection molding parameter windows are usually represented as a *processing window* (melt temperature vs. mould temperature vs. injection speed vs. hold pressure). Setting parameters outside the window leads to **short shots** (too cold/too fast), **flash** (too much clamp tonnage deficit), **warpage** (uneven cooling), or **weld lines** (premature freeze-off at flow fronts). The KTU Module-1 question on IM expects you to name these four defects and link each to a *root cause in Q, P, t, or F*. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Minimum Clamping Tonnage in Injection Molding **Objective:** Derive the rule-of-thumb formula for the minimum clamping force required to keep the mould closed during the *packing* phase. ### Step 1 — Identify the pressure acting on the cavity The hydraulic pressure in the cavity ($P_{cav}$) acts on every square meter of mould surface that is in contact with melt. The most demanding area, in terms of trying to push the mould open, is the *projected area* ($A_{proj}$) — the silhouette of the part when viewed in the direction of mould opening. $$ P_{total} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj} $$ **Logic:** By Newton's third law, the melt pushes on the cavity walls with force $P_{cav} \cdot A_{proj}$ in the opening direction. The clamp must oppose exactly this force (plus a safety factor). ### Step 2 — Add the flash-land contribution A small portion of the cavity pressure acts on the *flash land* (the thin gap between mould halves that allows air to escape but stops melt). The flash area is $A_{flash}$ and the pressure there is also $P_{cav}$. $$ F_{flash} = P_{cav} \times A_{flash} $$ **Logic:** The flash land is the most likely escape route for the melt, so it must be considered. ### Step 3 — Total opening force (Fo) $$ F_{open} = P_{cav} (A_{proj} + A_{flash}) $$ ### Step 4 — Equate to clamp force with safety margin The clamping force $F_{clamp}$ provided by the tie-bar / toggle system must be at least equal to $F_{open}$. In practice, a safety factor of **1.1 to 1.3** is added to allow for process fluctuations: $$ F_{clamp} = k \cdot P_{cav} (A_{proj} + A_{flash}), \quad k = 1.1 \text{ to } 1.3 $$ ### Step 5 — Final boxed result $$ \boxed{\,F_{clamp} = k \, P_{cav} \, A_{proj} \, \left(1 + \dfrac{A_{flash}}{A_{proj}}\right) \approx k \, P_{cav} \, A_{proj}\,} $$ **Practical use:** A part with $A_{proj} = 250 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.025 \text{ m}^2$ and a cavity pressure of $50 \text{ MPa} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$ requires a clamp of: $$ F_{clamp} = 1.2 \times 50 \times 10^6 \times 0.025 = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 153 \text{ tonnes} $$ This is the standard "tier" of an IMM a KTU examiner expects you to recognize. --- ## 3.2 Derivation — Cooling Time for a Polymer Slab (IM Part Wall) **Objective:** Derive the analytical cooling time for a uniform-thickness polymer slab cooled from both sides — the most common scenario for an IM wall. ### Step 1 — Set up the 1-D transient heat conduction Consider a slab of half-thickness $L$ (so the full thickness is $h = 2L$) initially at uniform temperature $T_0 = T_m$ (melt temperature), and both surfaces suddenly quenched to wall temperature $T_w$. The governing PDE is: $$ \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} $$ with $\alpha = k / (\rho C_p)$ = thermal diffusivity (m²/s). ### Step 2 — Apply boundary & initial conditions $$ T(x,0) = T_0, \qquad T(\pm L, t) = T_w $$ ### Step 3 — Solve by separation of variables The series solution is the classical Fourier series for a slab with Biot → ∞ (perfect quench): $$ \dfrac{T(x,t) - T_w}{T_0 - T_w} = \dfrac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^n}{2n+1} \cos\!\left[\dfrac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2L}\right] \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 \alpha t}{4L^2}\right] $$ ### Step 4 — Take only the first term for engineering accuracy For $t > 0.2 \, h^2 / \alpha$, retaining only the $n = 0$ term gives < 1 % error: $$ \dfrac{T_{centre}(t) - T_w}{T_0 - T_w} \approx \dfrac{4}{\pi} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 \alpha t}{4L^2}\right) $$ ### Step 5 — Solve for the time at which the centre reaches ejection temperature $T_{ej}$ Rearrange by taking the natural log of both sides: $$ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{\pi (T_0 - T_w)}{4 (T_{ej} - T_w)}\right) = -\dfrac{\pi^2 \alpha t_{cool}}{4 L^2} $$ ### Step 6 — Solve for $t_{cool}$ and express in terms of full thickness $h = 2L$ Since $L = h/2$, we have $L^2 = h^2/4$: $$ t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4 (T_0 - T_w)}{\pi (T_{ej} - T_w)}\right) $$ ### Step 7 — Final boxed result (KTU textbook form) $$ \boxed{\;t_{cool} \approx \dfrac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8\,(T_m - T_w)}{\pi^2 (T_{ej} - T_w)}\right)\;} $$ **Logic summary:** Cooling time grows with the **square** of wall thickness. Doubling the wall makes the cycle **4× longer** — a *key driver* of KTU design problems asking you to minimize cycle time by thinning walls. > [!TIP] > **Quick numerical check:** For polypropylene with $h = 3 \text{ mm}$, $T_m = 220 \,°C$, $T_w = 25 \,°C$, $T_{ej} = 90 \,°C$, $\alpha \approx 1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$: $$ t_{cool} = \dfrac{(0.003)^2}{\pi^2 \times 10^{-7}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8 \times 195}{\pi^2 \times 65}\right) \approx 8.7 \text{ s} $$ This matches the **10–15 s** cycle-time target for a 3 mm PP wall — confirming the formula. --- ## 3.3 Derivation — Extruder Output (Simplified Newtonian Drag Flow) **Objective:** Derive the volumetric output of a single-screw extruder treating the melt as Newtonian in the metering zone. ### Step 1 — Geometry of the metering channel Unwrap the screw channel: a rectangular channel of width $W = \pi D \sin\phi - e \approx \pi D \sin\phi$, depth $H$, with the bottom (screw flight) moving at velocity $V_b = \pi D N$ at angle $\phi$ to the channel axis. ### Step 2 — Drag flow (the only contribution in this simplified model) For a Newtonian fluid in Couette flow between a moving plate (velocity $V_{bx}$) and a stationary plate (the barrel), the velocity profile is linear: $$ v_z(y) = V_{bx} \dfrac{y}{H} $$ The volumetric flow rate per unit width is: $$ q = \int_0^H v_z(y) \, dy = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{bx} H $$ ### Step 3 — Multiply by channel width $W$ $$ Q = W \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} V_{bx} H $$ ### Step 4 — Substitute geometric identities $V_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\phi$, $W = \pi D \sin\phi - e \approx \pi D \sin\phi$, $H = h$: $$ Q = \dfrac{1}{2} (\pi D \sin\phi)(\pi D N \cos\phi) h $$ ### Step 5 — Final boxed result $$ \boxed{\;Q_{drag} = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}\;} $$ **Logic summary:** Output is proportional to $D^2$ (screw diameter squared), to $N$ (rpm), to $h$ (channel depth), and to $\sin\phi \cos\phi$ (maximized at $\phi = 45°$, but real screws use $\phi \approx 17.5°$ for mechanical strength). This is the most-tested extruder equation in KTU module exams. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Injection Molding Cycle — Process State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> MouldClose MouldClose --> Clamp : Toggle / Hydraulic Lock Clamp --> Injection : Screw Ram Forward Injection --> Packing : 95% Cavity Filled Packing --> Cooling : Pressure Drop Cooling --> ScrewRecovery : Concurrent Melt Plastication ScrewRecovery --> MouldOpen : Cycle Timer Trigger MouldOpen --> Ejection : Ejector Pins Forward Ejection --> MouldClose : Ejector Pins Retract Ejection --> [*] : Part Drops / Robot Pick ``` ## 4.2 Single-Screw Extruder — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR hopperA[Hopper
Resin Storage] --> feedZoneA[Feed Zone
Solid Conveying
Length 25% L] feedZoneA --> compZoneA[Compression Zone
Melting + Shear
Length 50% L] compZoneA --> meterZoneA[Metering Zone
Melt Pumping
Length 25% L] meterZoneA --> adapterA[Adapter
Breaker Plate + Screen Pack] adapterA --> dieA[Die
Cross-Section Forms Profile] dieA --> calA[Calibrator
Vacuum Sizing] calA --> coolA[Cooling Bath
Water Spray / Immersion] coolA --> pullA[Haul-Off Unit
Caterpillar Belts] pullA --> cutA[Cutting Station
Saw / Fly Knife] cutA --> stackA[Stacking / Winding] heaterA[Band Heaters] -.controls heat.-> feedZoneA heaterA -.controls heat.-> compZoneA heaterA -.controls heat.-> meterZoneA motorA[Drive Motor + Gearbox] -.turns.-> feedZoneA ``` ## 4.3 Comparison Matrix of the "Big Four" Primary Processes ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IM["INJECTION MOLDING (IM)"] IM_shape[Shape: 3D complex, net-shape] IM_mat[Material: Thermoplastics, some TS] IM_cycle[Cycle: 10 - 120 s] IM_press[Pressure: 35 - 140 MPa] end subgraph EXT["EXTRUSION (EXT)"] EXT_shape[Shape: Continuous profile, 2D cross-section] EXT_mat[Material: Thermoplastics, elastomers] EXT_cycle[Cycle: Continuous throughput] EXT_press[Pressure: 5 - 35 MPa] end subgraph BM["BLOW MOLDING (BM)"] BM_shape[Shape: Hollow 3D bottles, tanks] BM_mat[Material: PE, PP, PET, PVC] BM_cycle[Cycle: 15 - 60 s] BM_press[Pressure: 0.4 - 1.0 MPa air] end subgraph CM["COMPRESSION MOLDING (CM)"] CM_shape[Shape: Large area, moderate complexity] CM_mat[Material: Thermosets, rubber, SMC BMC] CM_cycle[Cycle: 60 - 600 s cure] CM_press[Pressure: 10 - 70 MPa] end ``` ## 4.4 Decision Logic — Choosing the Right Process ```mermaid flowchart TD startA{Is the part continuous
profile or discrete 3D?} startA -- Continuous --> EXTA[Extrusion] startA -- Discrete 3D --> startB{Is the part hollow?} startB -- Yes --> BMA[Blow Molding] startB -- No --> startC{Material thermoplastic
or thermoset?} startC -- Thermoplastic --> startD{Annual volume?} startD -- High > 10k --> IMA[Injection Molding] startD -- Low < 1k --> TFA[Thermoforming from sheet] startC -- Thermoset / Rubber --> CMA[Compression Molding] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper] > *Define the term "cycle time" in injection molding. List the four major sub-phases of an IMM cycle.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Cycle time (${t}_{cycle}$) is the total time elapsed between two successive repetitions of the same step in an injection molding machine — i.e., the time from the start of mould closing to the next start of mould closing. It is measured in seconds and is the inverse-multiplier of machine throughput. - **Four sub-phases (2 marks):** 1. **Injection (Filling)** – melt is rammed into the cavity. 2. **Packing / Holding** – additional melt compensates for shrinkage. 3. **Cooling** – the part solidifies against the cold mould walls. 4. **Mould opening & Ejection** – the part is removed and the mould closes for the next cycle. --- > **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] > *Distinguish between Extrusion Blow Molding (EBM) and Injection Blow Molding (IBM). State two products made by each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | EBM | IBM | |---|---|---| | Parison source | Continuously extruded tube | Injection-molded preform on a core | | Mould stations | Single (extrude + blow) | Two (inject, then transfer to blow) | | Neck finish | Trimming needed; less accurate | No trimming; high accuracy | | Typical products (1 mark each pair) | Detergent bottles, jerry cans, HDPE drums | PET water/soft-drink bottles, pharmaceutical vials | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > ### Question B — Option A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Modified from Dec 2022]** > *(a)* Describe the injection molding process with a neat block diagram. List the major machine elements and explain the function of the **plasticizing screw** in detail. **(7 marks)** > > *(b)* A polypropylene part is to be injection molded with a total projected area of $300 \text{ cm}^2$ and a wall thickness of $2.5 \text{ mm}$. The cavity pressure during packing is $55 \text{ MPa}$. Calculate: (i) the **minimum clamping tonnage** (use safety factor $k = 1.25$), and (ii) the **approximate cooling time** if the relevant data are: $T_m = 230 \,°C$, $T_w = 30 \,°C$, $T_{ej} = 100 \,°C$, and $\alpha = 9 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. **(7 marks)** ### Solution to B-A (a) — Injection Molding Process Description (7 marks) 1. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the schematic in **Section 4.1** — show the six-step cycle: Mould Close → Clamp → Injection → Packing → Cooling (concurrent Screw Recovery) → Mould Open → Ejection. 2. **Major machine elements (2 marks):** - **Hopper** (feed reservoir) - **Barrel + Heater bands** (melting chamber) - **Screw + Drive unit** (plasticizer and injector) - **Nozzle + Sprue bushing** (melt delivery) - **Mould (cavity + core + cooling channels + ejectors)** - **Clamping unit (toggle or hydraulic tie bars)** - **Hydraulic / Electric power pack + Controller (PLC)** 3. **Plasticizing screw function (3 marks):** A plasticizing screw performs **three** roles in *rotation* — solid conveying (Archimedes drag), plasticating (compression + shear + conduction melting), and metering (positive-displacement pumping). In its *axial* role (when it acts as a plunger), it injects the accumulated shot. The screw's three geometric zones are: **Feed (L/D ~ 8–10)**, **Compression/Transition (L/D ~ 5–8)**, and **Metering (L/D ~ 5–8)**, with **compression ratio** typically **2:1 to 4:1** for thermoplastics. The non-return valve (NRV) at the screw tip prevents back-flow during injection. ### Solution to B-A (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks) **Given:** $A_{proj} = 300 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.0300 \text{ m}^2$, $P_{cav} = 55 \text{ MPa} = 55 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$, $k = 1.25$, $h = 2.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0025 \text{ m}$, $T_m = 230 \,°C$, $T_w = 30 \,°C$, $T_{ej} = 100 \,°C$, $\alpha = 9 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. **(i) Minimum Clamping Force (3 marks):** $$ F_{clamp} = k \, P_{cav} \, A_{proj} $$ $$ F_{clamp} = 1.25 \times 55 \times 10^6 \times 0.0300 $$ $$ F_{clamp} = 2.0625 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \approx 210.3 \text{ tonnes-force} $$ **Answer:** $F_{clamp} \approx 210 \text{ tonnes}$ (round to 2.06 MN). **(ii) Approximate Cooling Time (4 marks):** $$ t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8\,(T_m - T_w)}{\pi^2 (T_{ej} - T_w)}\right) $$ Numerator argument: $T_m - T_w = 230 - 30 = 200$; $T_{ej} - T_w = 100 - 30 = 70$. $$ \dfrac{8 \times 200}{\pi^2 \times 70} = \dfrac{1600}{690.8} = 2.317 $$ $$ \ln(2.317) = 0.840 $$ $$ t_{cool} = \dfrac{(0.0025)^2}{\pi^2 \times 9 \times 10^{-8}} \times 0.840 = \dfrac{6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{8.882 \times 10^{-7}} \times 0.840 $$ $$ t_{cool} = 7.037 \times 0.840 = 5.91 \text{ s} $$ **Answer:** $t_{cool} \approx 5.9 \text{ s}$ (realistic for a 2.5 mm PP wall). --- > ### Question B — Option B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Modified from May 2023]** > *(a)* Describe the **single-screw extrusion process** with a labelled block diagram. Explain the three functional zones of the extruder screw. **(7 marks)** > > *(b)* A 60 mm diameter extruder screw with a lead of 60 mm, channel depth of 4 mm in the metering zone, and a helix angle of 17.5° rotates at 80 rpm. Treating the melt as Newtonian, estimate: (i) the **drag-flow output** in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, and (ii) the **hourly mass throughput** in $\text{kg/h}$ for a polymer with melt density $\rho = 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)** ### Solution to B-B (a) — Single-Screw Extrusion Description (7 marks) 1. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.2** — Hopper → Feed zone → Compression zone → Metering zone → Breaker plate → Die → Calibrator → Cooling → Haul-off → Cut. 2. **Three zones (5 marks):** - **Solid Conveying / Feed Zone (~25 % of L, L/D ≈ 5–8):** The depth of the screw channel is at its *maximum*. Polymer granules are dragged forward by friction between the rotating flight and the barrel wall (drag flow). The forward movement is governed by the *solid conveying angle* (θ), and the throughput scales with $Q_s \propto \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N h_s \sin\phi\cos\phi$ where $h_s$ is the channel depth here. - **Compression / Melting Zone (~50 % of L, L/D ≈ 8–10):** The channel depth *tapers* from $h_s$ to $h_m$. Polymer is compacted, air is squeezed back toward the hopper, and melting occurs by a combination of **barrel conduction** and **shear dissipation** in the thin melt film between the solid bed and the barrel. The *Maddock* melting model gives the melt-pool profile. - **Metering Zone (~25 % of L, L/D ≈ 5–8):** Channel depth is at its *minimum* and constant. The melt acts as a **Newtonian / power-law fluid** in Couette flow. Output is steady and pressure-stable. This is where the $Q_{drag}$ formula is most applicable. ### Solution to B-B (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks) **Given:** $D = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m}$, $h = 4 \text{ mm} = 0.004 \text{ m}$, $\phi = 17.5°$, $N = 80 \text{ rpm} = 80/60 \text{ rps} = 1.333 \text{ Hz}$, $\rho = 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(i) Drag-flow Output (4 marks):** $$ Q_{drag} = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} $$ Compute $\sin(17.5°) = 0.3007$, $\cos(17.5°) = 0.9537$. $$ Q_{drag} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \times (0.060)^2 \times 1.333 \times 0.004 \times 0.3007 \times 0.9537}{2} $$ $$ Q_{drag} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 0.0036 \times 1.333 \times 0.004 \times 0.2868}{2} $$ $$ Q_{drag} = \dfrac{9.8696 \times 0.0036 \times 1.333 \times 0.001147}{2} $$ Step-by-step accumulation: - $9.8696 \times 0.0036 = 0.035530$ - $0.035530 \times 1.333 = 0.047361$ - $0.047361 \times 0.001147 = 5.432 \times 10^{-5}$ - $/2 = 2.716 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ $$ Q_{drag} \approx 2.72 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **(ii) Hourly Mass Throughput (3 marks):** $$ \dot{m} = Q_{drag} \times \rho = 2.716 \times 10^{-5} \times 750 = 0.02037 \text{ kg/s} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{hourly} = 0.02037 \times 3600 = 73.3 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Answer:** $Q_{drag} \approx 2.72 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ and $\dot{m} \approx 73 \text{ kg/h}$. (Real machines would give ~80–90 % of this, since the formula neglects pressure and leakage flows.) --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 1 Process Description Questions:** > 1. **Confusing "shot weight" with "shot volume":** $m_{part} = V_{shot} \times \rho_{melt}$ — many students drop the density or use the *solid* density instead of *melt* density (melt density of PP ≈ 0.74 g/cm³ vs. solid 0.91 g/cm³). > 2. **Forgetting units in clamping-force problems:** $A_{proj}$ must be in $\text{m}^2$ and $P_{cav}$ in $\text{Pa}$ to get Newtons. Converting 300 cm² to 0.03 m² is a free 1-mark trap. > 3. **Mixing up Compression vs. Injection Molding pressures:** Compression molding uses **7–70 MPa**, Injection Molding uses **35–140 MPa**. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > 4. **Leaving the "Newtonian" disclaimer off extrusion derivations:** If you quote the drag-flow formula without mentioning "Newtonian approximation, metering zone, no leakage, no pressure flow", you lose **1 mark** for assumptions. > 5. **Skipping the screw L/D ratio:** A perfect process-description answer must include **L/D = 20–30** and **compression ratio = 2:1–4:1** as standard IMM/extruder design rules. > 6. **In thermoforming questions, students forget to mention vacuum vs. pressure vs. mechanical assist** — these are the three sub-variants a KTU examiner looks for. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **Module 1 — Process Description: Must-Know Bullets for KTU Exam** > - ☐ Polymer processing = conversion of raw polymer → finished article using **Heat, Pressure, Time, Flow**. > - ☐ Eight major processes: **IM, EXT, BM, CM, TF, RM, Calendaring, Casting**. > - ☐ **Injection Molding:** Six-step cycle (Close → Clamp → Inject → Pack → Cool → Open/Eject); screw L/D = 18–22; clamp tonnage formula $F = k P A_{proj}$. > - ☐ **Extrusion:** Three zones — Feed (solid drag), Compression (melting), Metering (pump); die shapes product; drag-flow output $Q \propto D^2 N h \sin\phi\cos\phi$. > - ☐ **Blow Molding:** Two types — EBM (extruded parison) and IBM (injection preform); air pressure ~0.5–1 MPa. > - ☐ **Compression Molding:** Used for **thermosets and rubber**; cure time dominates cycle; pressure 10–70 MPa; cannot remelt. > - ☐ **Thermoforming:** Secondary process starting from a *sheet*; three variants — vacuum, pressure, mechanical. > - ☐ **Cooling time scales with $h^2$** — wall thickness is the dominant cycle-time driver. > - ☐ **MFI is inversely related to viscosity** — higher MFI ⇒ easier flow, lower molecular weight. > - ☐ **Melt temperature** for most thermoplastics: 180–320 °C; **Mold temperature**: 20–120 °C. > - ☐ **Specific heat of polymer melt** ≈ 1.5–2.5 kJ/(kg·K); **thermal diffusivity** ≈ $10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. > - ☐ **Defects to remember** (IM): short shot, flash, warpage, weld line, sink mark, jetting, burn marks. > - ☐ **Projected area × cavity pressure = minimum clamp force** (always use a safety factor of 1.1–1.3). > - ☐ **Compression ratio** of an extruder screw = feed-zone channel depth / metering-zone channel depth = 2:1 to 4:1 for thermoplastics. > - ☐ **L/D ratio** for an extruder screw = total screw length / screw diameter = 20–30 (vented: 36+). > - ☐ **Beverly–Beaman rule:** $T_g / T_m \approx 2/3$ (in Kelvin) for many crystalline polymers.

topics.title: Moulding Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Moulding Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Moulding Cycle** in polymer processing (specifically injection moulding) is defined as the **complete, sequential series of operations** executed by an injection moulding machine to produce **one single moulded part**, measured from the instant the mould closes until it closes again for the next shot.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCPOT503, the moulding cycle is a closed-loop **time-temperature-pressure** dependent process, governed by the rheological behaviour of the polymer melt, the thermodynamic properties of the mould, and the kinematic constraints of the machine. The standard industry benchmark is **15–60 seconds per cycle**, depending on part thickness, material grade, and cooling efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> The moulding cycle is the **repetitive sequence of filling, packing, holding, cooling, plastication, mould opening, part ejection, and mould closing** events that together transform polymer granules into a finished, dimensionally-stable product.

The total cycle time is universally denoted as:

$$ t_{cycle} = t_{fill} + t_{pack/hold} + t_{cool} + t_{plasticate} + t_{open} + t_{eject} + t_{close} $$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Syringe and Ice Cube Tray" Intuition

Imagine you have a hot syringe filled with molten chocolate, and you are injecting it into an ice cube tray to make shaped chocolates:

| Real Injection Moulding Step | Home Analogy (Chocolate Mould) |
|---|---|
| **Mould closes** | Closing the ice cube tray lid |
| **Injection (Fill)** | Squirting hot chocolate into each cavity |
| **Packing/Holding** | Pressing the syringe to top up shrinking chocolate |
| **Cooling** | Waiting for chocolate to solidify in the fridge |
| **Plasticating (Melt prep)** | Heating the next batch of chocolate on the stove |
| **Mould opens** | Opening the ice cube tray |
| **Ejection** | Popping the chocolate cubes out |
| **Mould closes** | Closing the tray again for the next batch |

The **time it takes to do ALL of these steps ONCE** is the moulding cycle. The moulding machine's job is to perform this entire sequence **automatically, thousands of times per day**, with millisecond precision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the moulding cycle matters in industry:**
> - Determines **production rate** (parts/hour)
> - Governs **energy consumption** per part
> - Influences **part quality** (warpage, sink marks, residual stress)
> - Directly impacts **machine economic viability**

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics (Bold Constants)

| Metric | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Cavity pressure (P)** | 50 (amorphous) / 80 (semi-crystalline) | **MPa** |
| **Cycle time benchmark** | 15 – 60 | **seconds** |
| **Ejection temperature (Tₑ)** | 30 – 90 (depends on Tg) | **°C** |
| **Mould wall temperature (T_w)** | 25 – 120 | **°C** |
| **Injection velocity (v)** | 10 – 200 | **mm/s** |
| **Holding pressure** | 30 – 70 % of injection pressure | **MPa** |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Phases of the Moulding Cycle — Operational Breakdown

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mandates understanding each phase, the associated **pressure–time–temperature profile**, and its **effect on final part quality**.

### Phase 1: Mould Closing and Clamping
- The stationary platen and moving platen close at high speed, then switch to a slow approach speed to avoid crushing trapped air or debris.
- The **clamp tonnage** must overcome the cavity pressure acting on the projected area of the mould to prevent flash.
- Clamp force formula:

$$ F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \times A_{projected} $$

Where:
- $F_{clamp}$ = clamping force in **kN**
- $P_{cavity}$ = maximum cavity pressure in **MPa** (N/mm²)
- $A_{projected}$ = projected area of the cavity + runner in **mm²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A safety factor of 1.1 to 1.3** is typically multiplied to the calculated clamp tonnage in industrial practice.

---

### Phase 2: Filling (Injection)
- The screw (acting as a plunger) drives the molten polymer into the closed mould cavity at **injection velocity (v_i)**.
- Velocity-controlled: the machine maintains a set ram speed regardless of pressure rise.
- The mould cavity fills typically in **0.5 to 5 seconds**.
- Key control parameter: **V/P (Velocity-to-Pressure) switchover point** — switches from velocity control to pressure control when ~95–99 % of cavity is filled.

---

### Phase 3: Packing / Holding
- Once the cavity is ~98 % full, the machine switches to **pressure control mode**.
- Additional melt is packed in to compensate for **volumetric shrinkage** (1–4 % for amorphous, 1–20 % for semi-crystalline polymers).
- Pressure drops gradually from peak to holding pressure, then holds at a constant level.
- **Duration** = 1–10 seconds, depending on part wall thickness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality linkage:** Insufficient packing → sink marks and voids. Excessive packing → flash, internal stress, and part sticking.

---

### Phase 4: Cooling (Solidification)
- The dominant time-consuming phase — typically **50–80 % of total cycle time**.
- Heat is removed from the polymer melt through the mould walls, conducted away by cooling channels.
- The polymer must cool from **melt temperature (T_m)** down to the **ejection temperature (T_e)**, which is above the glass transition temperature (T_g) for amorphous polymers to prevent distortion.
- Cooling time formula (analytical solution from transient heat conduction):

$$ t_{cool} = \frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left[\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right] $$

Where:
- $s$ = **half wall thickness** in mm
- $\alpha$ = **thermal diffusivity** of polymer in mm²/s
- $T_m$ = melt temperature
- $T_w$ = mould wall temperature
- $T_e$ = ejection temperature

---

### Phase 5: Plasticating (Metering / Screw Recovery)
- Occurs **simultaneously with cooling** (overlapping operation) in most modern machines.
- The screw rotates, drawing in fresh granules from the hopper, melting, and metering the next shot's volume in front of the screw tip.
- The required **plasticizing capacity** is calculated as:

$$ \dot{m}_{p} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot s \cdot N \cdot \rho \cdot \eta \times \frac{1}{3600} $$

Where:
- $D$ = screw diameter in **m**
- $s$ = screw stroke in **m**
- $N$ = screw rotational speed in **rpm**
- $\rho$ = melt density in **kg/m³**
- $\eta$ = conveying efficiency (0.2–0.4)

---

### Phase 6: Mould Opening, Ejection, and Mould Closing
- After plasticating is complete, cooling ends, and the mould opens.
- The part is ejected by **ejector pins** driven by an hydraulic cylinder.
- The mould then closes again — and the cycle repeats.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Board Exams)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Cycle Time** | $t_c = t_f + t_{ph} + t_{cool} + t_{plast} + t_{open} + t_{eject} + t_{close}$ | All in **seconds** | Production rate calculation |
| 2 | **Clamp Tonnage** | $F = P \cdot A$ | $F$ in N, $P$ in MPa, $A$ in mm² | Machine selection |
| 3 | **Cooling Time** | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right]$ | $s$ in m, $\alpha$ in m²/s, $T$ in K | Cycle optimization |
| 4 | **Plasticating Capacity** | $\dot{m}_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^2 s N \rho \eta / 3600$ | All in SI | Shot weight verification |
| 5 | **Production Rate** | $n = \dfrac{3600}{t_{cycle}}$ | $n$ in parts/hour | Plant capacity planning |
| 6 | **Shear Rate** | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\pi D N}{h}$ | $D$ in m, $N$ in 1/s, $h$ in m | Melt flow check |
| 7 | **Volumetric Shrinkage Compensation** | $\Delta V_{pack} = V_{cavity} \times S$ | $S$ = shrinkage fraction | Packer volume design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Do NOT use vertical bars $\vert x \vert$ directly in markdown tables — always use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid table breakage.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The moulding cycle is the **backbone of plastic part manufacturing economics**. Optimization of cycle time directly determines whether a product is profitable. In industries like automotive (bumpers, dashboards), medical (syringes), and packaging (caps, containers), a **1-second cycle time reduction** can yield **millions of rupees in annual savings** for high-volume production. Modern KTU-aligned engineers must balance **cycle speed, part quality, and machine wear** using Process Capability Indices (Cpk) and Design of Experiments (DoE) on cycle parameters.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cooling Time Equation

The cooling time equation is derived from the **one-dimensional transient heat conduction** in a polymer slab of half-thickness $s$, with symmetric cooling on both sides. The governing PDE is:

$$ \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} $$

**Boundary Conditions:**
- At $x = 0$ (centreline), $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x} = 0$ (symmetry)
- At $x = s$ (mould wall), $T(s, t) = T_w$ (constant wall temperature)
- Initial condition: $T(x, 0) = T_m$ (uniform melt temperature)

**Step 1:** Assume a solution of the form using **separation of variables**. Let $\theta(x,t) = T(x,t) - T_w$, which converts the boundary to $\theta(s,t) = 0$.

$$ \theta(x,t) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} A_n \cos\!\left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2s}\right) \exp\!\left(-\alpha \frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2}{4s^2} t\right) $$

**Step 2:** Apply the initial condition $\theta(x, 0) = T_m - T_w$ and use orthogonality of cosine terms:

$$ A_n = \frac{4(-1)^n}{\pi(2n+1)} (T_m - T_w) $$

**Step 3:** Evaluate temperature at the centreline ($x = 0$), since this is the hottest point in the cavity:

$$ \theta(0,t) = (T_m - T_w) \cdot \frac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha (2n+1)^2 \pi^2 t}{4s^2}\right) $$

**Step 4:** Approximate the infinite series by retaining only the first term ($n=0$), which dominates for large $t$:

$$ \theta(0,t) \approx \frac{4}{\pi}(T_m - T_w) \exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha \pi^2 t}{4s^2}\right) $$

**Step 5:** Set $\theta(0, t_{cool}) = T_e - T_w$ (centreline reaches ejection temperature):

$$ T_e - T_w = \frac{4}{\pi}(T_m - T_w) \exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha \pi^2 t_{cool}}{4s^2}\right) $$

**Step 6:** Take natural logarithm on both sides:

$$ \ln\!\left(\frac{T_e - T_w}{T_m - T_w}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{4}{\pi}\right) - \frac{\alpha \pi^2 t_{cool}}{4s^2} $$

**Step 7:** Re-arrange to solve for $t_{cool}$:

$$ t_{cool} = \frac{4s^2}{\alpha \pi^2} \ln\!\left(\frac{4/\pi}{(T_e - T_w)/(T_m - T_w)}\right) = \frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left[\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right] $$

**Final Derived Result:**

$$ \boxed{t_{cool} = \frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left[\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right]} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Cooling time scales with the **square of wall thickness** — doubling the wall thickness **quadruples** the cooling time. This is the #1 KTU board exam takeaway.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1: Cooling Time Calculation

**Problem:** A polypropylene (PP) moulded part has a half-thickness $s = 2 \text{ mm}$. Given:
- $T_m = 220 °C = 493 \text{ K}$
- $T_w = 30 °C = 303 \text{ K}$
- $T_e = 90 °C = 363 \text{ K}$
- $\alpha = 0.1 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$

**Find:** Cooling time $t_{cool}$.

**Step 1:** Substitute values into the equation.

$$ t_{cool} = \frac{(2)^2}{\pi^2 (0.1)} \ln\!\left[\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{493 - 303}{363 - 303}\right] $$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the numerator: $s^2 = 4 \text{ mm}^2$

$$ t_{cool} = \frac{4}{0.987} \ln\!\left[\frac{8}{9.87} \cdot \frac{190}{60}\right] $$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the inner fraction:

$$ \frac{8}{9.87} = 0.811, \quad \frac{190}{60} = 3.167 $$

**Step 4:** Multiply inside the logarithm:

$$ 0.811 \times 3.167 = 2.567 $$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the natural log:

$$ \ln(2.567) = 0.943 $$

**Step 6:** Final division:

$$ t_{cool} = 4.052 \times 0.943 = 3.82 \text{ seconds} $$

**Answer:** $t_{cool} \approx 3.82$ seconds.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2: Clamp Tonnage Calculation

**Problem:** A 4-cavity mould has each cavity projecting 50 cm². The runner area is 10 cm². Maximum cavity pressure is 60 MPa. Calculate the required clamp tonnage (with 1.2 safety factor).

**Step 1:** Calculate total projected area.

$$ A_{projected} = (4 \times 50) + 10 = 210 \text{ cm}^2 = 21000 \text{ mm}^2 $$

**Step 2:** Apply clamp force formula.

$$ F_{clamp} = P \times A = 60 \times 21000 = 1{,}260{,}000 \text{ N} $$

**Step 3:** Convert to tonnes-force (1 tonne-force = 9806.65 N).

$$ F_{clamp} = \frac{1{,}260{,}000}{9806.65} = 128.5 \text{ tonnes} $$

**Step 4:** Apply safety factor.

$$ F_{required} = 128.5 \times 1.2 = 154.2 \text{ tonnes} $$

**Answer:** Select a machine with **≥ 160 tonnes clamp force**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Parameters on Cycle Time

| Parameter ↑ Increases | Effect on $t_{cool}$ | Effect on Part Quality | KTU 2024 Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wall thickness $s$ | Increases (quadratically) | Higher sink risk | High |
| Mould wall temp $T_w$ | Increases | Better surface finish | Medium |
| Melt temperature $T_m$ | Increases | Lower viscosity, better flow | High |
| Ejection temp $T_e$ | Decreases (higher $T_e$ = less cooling needed) | Risk of deformation if too high | Medium |
| Thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ | Decreases (faster cooling) | Internal stress may rise | Low |
| Cavity pressure $P$ | Minimal direct effect | Higher density, less shrinkage | High |
