# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define moulding cycle. List any four major phases of the injection moulding cycle.**

**Model Answer:**
The moulding cycle is the **complete sequence of operations** performed by an injection moulding machine to produce a single finished plastic part, measured from mould closing to the next mould closing event.

The four major phases are:
1. **Filling (Injection)** — Molten polymer is injected into the closed cavity.
2. **Packing/Holding** — Pressure is maintained to compensate for volumetric shrinkage.
3. **Cooling** — Heat is removed via cooling channels; the longest phase.
4. **Plastication** — Screw rotates to melt and meter the next shot (overlaps with cooling).

> [!TIP]
> Examiners award full 3 marks only if the **definition is verbatim from the textbook** and **all four phases** are correctly named.

---

### **Question A2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is the significance of the V/P switchover point in the injection moulding cycle?**

**Model Answer:**
The V/P (Velocity-to-Pressure) switchover point is the moment during the filling phase when the machine control transitions from **velocity-controlled** filling to **pressure-controlled** packing. It is typically set at **95–99 % of the cavity being filled**.

**Significance:**
- Prevents over-packing and flash formation
- Ensures consistent part weight and dimensions
- Switches at a precise screw position, hydraulic pressure, or time, depending on machine setup
- Incorrect switchover leads to **short shots** (too early) or **flash and stress** (too late)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the different phases of the injection moulding cycle. Discuss the role of cooling time in determining the total cycle time.

**Model Answer:**

The injection moulding cycle consists of **six sequential phases**:

1. **Mould Closing and Clamping** — The two mould halves are closed and locked by the clamping unit. The clamp tonnage must exceed the force generated by cavity pressure to prevent flash. $F = P \times A_{projected}$.

2. **Filling (Injection)** — The screw acts as a plunger, injecting the melt under **velocity control** at 10–200 mm/s. The cavity fills in 0.5–5 s. The filling phase ends at the V/P switchover point.

3. **Packing/Holding** — Under **pressure control**, additional melt is forced in to compensate shrinkage. Holding pressure is typically 30–70 % of injection pressure. Duration: 1–10 s.

4. **Cooling** — Heat is removed through mould walls. The polymer cools from $T_m$ to $T_e$. **Cooling consumes 50–80 % of the total cycle time**, making it the bottleneck. Optimized mould cooling directly improves productivity.

5. **Plasticating (Screw Recovery)** — The screw rotates, melting fresh granules for the next shot. This phase **overlaps with cooling** in modern machines, hiding its time within the cooling window.

6. **Mould Opening and Ejection** — Mould halves separate; ejector pins push the part out. Mould then re-closes.

**Role of cooling time:**
Cooling time governs the **minimum possible cycle time** because it is the longest non-overlapping phase. Any reduction in cooling time (via better cooling channel design, higher thermal conductivity mould inserts, or lower ejection temperature) directly reduces $t_{cycle}$ and increases the production rate $n = 3600 / t_{cycle}$.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A polystyrene part has a half-thickness of 3 mm. The melt temperature is 230 °C, the mould wall temperature is 35 °C, and the ejection temperature is 95 °C. The thermal diffusivity of polystyrene is 0.08 mm²/s. Calculate the cooling time.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Convert all temperatures to Kelvin.
- $T_m = 230 + 273 = 503$ K
- $T_w = 35 + 273 = 308$ K
- $T_e = 95 + 273 = 368$ K

**Step 2:** Apply the cooling time formula.

$$ t_{cool} = \frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left[\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right] $$

**Step 3:** Substitute $s = 3$ mm, $\alpha = 0.08$ mm²/s.

$$ t_{cool} = \frac{(3)^2}{\pi^2 \times 0.08} \ln\!\left[\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{503 - 308}{368 - 308}\right] $$

**Step 4:** Calculate numerator: $s^2 = 9$ mm². Denominator: $\pi^2 \times 0.08 = 0.7896$ mm²/s.

$$ t_{cool} = \frac{9}{0.7896} \ln\!\left[\frac{8}{9.87} \cdot \frac{195}{60}\right] $$

**Step 5:** Inside the logarithm:

$$ \frac{8}{9.87} = 0.811, \quad \frac{195}{60} = 3.25 $$

$$ 0.811 \times 3.25 = 2.636 $$

**Step 6:** $\ln(2.636) = 0.969$.

**Step 7:** Final calculation.

$$ t_{cool} = 11.40 \times 0.969 = 11.05 \text{ seconds} $$

**Answer:** $t_{cool} \approx 11.05$ seconds.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of clamp tonnage in injection moulding. A mould has a total projected area of 250 cm². If the cavity pressure is 70 MPa, determine the required clamp force (in tonnes) with a safety factor of 1.25.

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Clamp Tonnage:**
The clamp tonnage is the **maximum locking force** that the machine's clamping unit can apply to keep the mould closed against the internal cavity pressure during injection. If the clamp force is insufficient, the mould halves separate slightly, causing **flash** (excess thin material) on the part.

The basic formula is:

$$ F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \times A_{projected} $$

Where $A_{projected}$ is the total shadow area of the cavities and runner system as seen from the parting line. A **safety factor** of 1.1 to 1.3 is applied to account for pressure spikes, uneven filling, and machine wear.

**Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert projected area to mm².
$$ A = 250 \text{ cm}^2 = 250 \times 100 = 25000 \text{ mm}^2 $$

**Step 2:** Calculate base clamp force.
$$ F_{base} = 70 \text{ MPa} \times 25000 \text{ mm}^2 = 1{,}750{,}000 \text{ N} $$

**Step 3:** Convert to tonnes-force.
$$ F_{base} = \frac{1{,}750{,}000}{9806.65} = 178.45 \text{ tonnes} $$

**Step 4:** Apply safety factor.
$$ F_{required} = 178.45 \times 1.25 = 223.06 \text{ tonnes} $$

**Answer:** A machine with **at least 225 tonnes** clamp force must be selected.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a pressure–time and temperature–time diagram, explain the dynamics of the injection moulding cycle. Discuss the importance of holding pressure in determining part quality.

**Model Answer:**

**Pressure–Time Profile:**
- **Phase 1 (Filling):** Pressure rises rapidly from 0 to peak injection pressure as the screw forces melt into the cavity. The peak is typically 70–140 MPa.
- **Phase 2 (Packing):** Pressure drops slightly to the **holding pressure** (30–70 % of peak) and is held constant for 1–10 s. The cavity continues to fill as the polymer shrinks.
- **Phase 3 (Cooling):** Pressure decays asymptotically toward zero as the gate freezes. After gate freeze, the cavity is sealed, and pressure inside drops naturally.
- **Phase 4 (Ejection):** Pressure is zero; mould opens and the part is ejected.

**Temperature–Time Profile:**
- At start: $T = T_m$ uniformly throughout cavity.
- During filling: thin skins near mould walls solidify instantly (T → T_w), while the centreline remains hot.
- During cooling: centreline temperature drops from $T_m$ to $T_e$ following the analytical cooling equation.
- At ejection: the part has reached $T_e$, just above Tg for amorphous polymers.

**Importance of Holding Pressure:**
The holding pressure phase **compensates for volumetric shrinkage** of the polymer as it cools. If holding pressure is too low:
- Sink marks appear on thick sections
- Voids form internally
- Part weight is low and dimensions are inaccurate

If holding pressure is too high:
- Flash forms at the parting line
- Ejection becomes difficult (part sticks in mould)
- High residual stresses may cause warpage

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **holding pressure** with **holding time**. The pressure is the *magnitude* (MPa), and the time is the *duration* (s). Both must be specified separately. Marks are deducted if the answer uses the terms interchangeably.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Moulding cycle** = complete sequence from one mould-close to the next mould-close.
- **Six phases**: Closing → Filling → Packing → Cooling → Plastication → Opening/Ejection.
- **Total cycle time**: $t_c = t_f + t_{ph} + t_{cool} + t_{plast} + t_{open} + t_{eject} + t_{close}$
- **Cooling dominates**: 50–80 % of cycle time.
- **Cooling time formula**: $t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha}\ln\!\left[\dfrac{8}{\pi^2}\cdot\dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right]$
- **Cooling time scales with $s^2$** — the most tested KTU concept.
- **Clamp tonnage**: $F = P \times A_{projected}$; apply safety factor 1.1–1.3.
- **V/P switchover** at 95–99 % cavity fill; controls part quality.
- **Packing** compensates shrinkage; **insufficient packing = sinks**, **excessive = flash**.
- **Plasticating overlaps with cooling** in modern machines.
- **Pressure profile**: rises → drops to holding → decays to zero at gate freeze.
- **Temperature profile**: uniform $T_m$ → centreline cools last → reaches $T_e$ at ejection.
- **For semi-crystalline polymers**, use $P_{cavity} = 80$ MPa; for **amorphous**, use $P_{cavity} = 50$ MPa.
- **Cavity pressure × projected area = clamp force** — a 2-mark direct question every KTU exam.
- **Wall thickness** is the #1 design variable affecting cycle time and quality.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Moulds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Moulds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Mould** (in polymer product manufacturing) is a precision-engineered tooling assembly that provides the **negative geometry (cavity and core)** into which a polymer melt (or prepolymer) is injected, compressed, transferred, blown, or cast, and subsequently solidified to retain the desired shape of the finished plastic product.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPOT503)**, moulds are systematically classified based on:
1. The **polymer processing technique** (Injection, Compression, Transfer, Blow, Rotational, Thermoforming).
2. The **constructional architecture** (Two-plate, Three-plate, Hot-runner, Cold-runner, Stack, Family, etc.).
3. The **number of impressions** (Single-cavity vs. Multi-cavity).
4. The **runner system topology** (Cold runner, Hot runner, Insulated runner).
5. The **level of automation** (Manual, Semi-automatic, Fully automatic).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** A mould is NOT a single block of metal. It is an *assembly* of precision components — cavities, cores, runners, gates, ejectors, cooling channels, and guiding elements — designed to survive millions of cycles under **high pressure (typically 700 bar to 1400 bar cavity pressure)** and elevated temperature (**up to 300 °C** for engineering plastics).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are making **chocolates at home**:
- The chocolate melt is your **polymer melt**.
- The plastic tray with shaped depressions (the cavity) is your **mould**.
- When you pour the melt in and let it cool, it takes the shape of the depression.
- If the tray has **1 cavity**, you get **1 chocolate** per pour. If it has **12 cavities**, you get **12 chocolates** — that is the difference between a **single-cavity** and a **multi-cavity mould**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Insight:** In industry, a mould is often called a "**tool**" or "**die**". A *mould* is generally used for plastics/rubber processing, while a *die* is used for metals/extrusion. However, in polymer product manufacturing, the terms **mould**, **tool**, and **die** are used interchangeably depending on regional industrial practice (e.g., American English prefers "mold/tool"; German engineering uses "Werkzeug").

---

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters in KTU Examinations

The classification is **not arbitrary** — it dictates:
- The **mould cost** (a hot-runner mould is 2×–3× costlier than a cold-runner mould).
- The **cycle time** and **production rate**.
- The **part quality** (weld line position, gate vestige, sink marks).
- The **machine compatibility** (clamp tonnage, shot capacity, platen size).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Highlight:** Industrial moulds are typically hardened to **HRC 58–62** (Rockwell Hardness C scale) and manufactured from **P-20, H-13, S-7, or DIN 1.2738 (P20+Ni)** tool steels to withstand cyclic thermal and pressure loading.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Classification — Based on the Polymer Process

This is the **most fundamental and KTU-favoured classification axis**.

### 2.1.1 Injection Mould
- **Working principle:** Molten polymer is injected under high pressure (700–1400 bar) into a closed mould cavity through a **gate**, then cooled and ejected.
- **Components:** Cavity, Core, Sprue, Runner, Gate, Ejector pins, Cooling channels, Guide pillars/bushes.
- **Sub-classes:** Two-plate mould, Three-plate mould, Hot-runner mould, Stack mould, Family mould, Insert mould.

### 2.1.2 Compression Mould
- **Working principle:** A measured charge of thermoset/Elastomer is placed in the open cavity. The press closes, and **heat + pressure** simultaneously cure the material.
- **Sub-classes:** Flash mould, Positive mould, Semi-positive mould, Landing mould.

### 2.1.3 Transfer Mould
- **Working principle:** The polymer (thermoset) is preheated in a **transfer pot**, then forced through a runner system into the closed cavity via a plunger.
- **Sub-classes:** Pot-type transfer mould, Plunger-type transfer mould, Integral transfer mould.

### 2.1.4 Blow Mould
- **Working principle:** A molten **parison** (tube of polymer) is inflated by compressed air against the walls of a cooled cavity to form hollow parts (bottles, tanks).
- **Sub-classes:** Extrusion blow mould, Injection blow mould, Stretch blow mould, Dip blow mould.

### 2.1.5 Rotational Mould
- **Working principle:** Powder/liquid polymer is placed in a closed mould that rotates biaxially inside a heated oven. Centrifugal force distributes the melt, which fuses and forms a hollow part.
- **Sub-classes:** Rock-and-roll, Clamshell, Carousel (3-arm/4-arm), Shuttle, Vertical wheel.

### 2.1.6 Thermoforming Mould
- **Working principle:** A thermoplastic sheet is heated to its softening point, then forced against a mould surface by vacuum, pressure, or mechanical means.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** Examiners often ask for a **comparison table** between Injection and Compression moulds, or to "explain the working of a transfer mould with a neat sketch."

---

## 2.2 Secondary Classification — Based on Constructional Architecture (Injection Mould Context)

| **Mould Type** | **Distinctive Feature** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Two-Plate Mould** | One parting plane; runner is on the parting line. | Simple parts, single gate. |
| **Three-Plate Mould** | Two parting planes; runner system is on a separate plate. | Multi-cavity moulds, balanced filling. |
| **Hot-Runner Mould** | Runner is heated; no solidified runner scrap. | High-volume production, no gate trimming. |
| **Cold-Runner Mould** | Runner solidifies with the part (and is reground). | Cost-sensitive, low-budget production. |
| **Stack Mould** | Two moulds stacked back-to-back; double the cavities per machine. | High-cavitation, multi-impression. |
| **Family Mould** | Produces **different** parts in the same cycle. | Small-batch product families. |
| **Insert Mould** | A pre-formed metal insert is overmoulded. | Electrical/threaded components. |

---

## 2.3 Tertiary Classification — Based on Cavity Count

| **Type** | **Definition** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Cavity Mould** | Only **1 cavity impression**; produces 1 part per cycle. | Large parts, prototype runs, high-precision components. |
| **Multi-Cavity Mould** | **2 or more identical or different** impressions; produces multiple parts per cycle. | Mass production, small parts (caps, closures). |

---

## 2.4 Quaternary Classification — Based on Runner System

- **Cold Runner Mould:** Runner is at room temperature and solidifies with each cycle. The runner is then **recycled (reground)**.
- **Hot Runner Mould:** Runner is **actively heated** (by heater bands or internally heated manifolds) to keep the polymer in a molten state. **Zero runner waste.**
- **Insulated Runner Mould:** A *hybrid* where the central channel is large enough to retain heat and remain molten, while the outer surface freezes.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are the **most-tested numerical problems** in PCPOT503 University Examinations. Master them.

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description / Notation** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n = \dfrac{Q \times t_{c}}{H \times 3600}$ | **Number of cavities**, where $Q$ = annual production (parts), $t_c$ = cycle time (s), $H$ = working hours/year. |
| 2 | $F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \times k$ | **Clamping force**, where $P_{cav}$ = cavity pressure (bar), $A_{proj}$ = projected area (cm²), $k$ = safety factor (1.1–1.3). |
| 3 | $P_{cav} = n \cdot \tau \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{t}\right)^{n+1}$ | **Cavity pressure** from rheology (Power-law fluid), $n$ = power-law index, $\tau$ = shear stress, $L$ = flow length, $t$ = thickness. |
| 4 | $D_{s} = D_{cav} \times (1 + S)$ | **Shrinkage allowance**, where $D_{s}$ = mould dimension, $D_{cav}$ = part dimension, $S$ = volumetric shrinkage. |
| 5 | $Q_{cool} = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T$ | **Cooling heat removal rate** (W), $\dot{m}$ = mass flow, $c_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T$ = coolant temperature rise. |
| 6 | $T_{cycle} = t_{fill} + t_{cool} + t_{eject} + t_{reset}$ | **Total cycle time decomposition** (s). |
| 7 | $V_{shot} = n \cdot V_{part} + V_{runner} + V_{sprue}$ | **Total shot volume** (cm³). |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, all absolute values and conditionals (such as $\vert x \vert$) are rendered using the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` command (or their equivalent). The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is **NOT** typed directly as `\vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Number of cavities (Eq. 1)** is the **first decision** any mould designer makes — it determines the entire machine selection, clamp tonnage, platen size, and economics.
- **Clamping force (Eq. 2)** determines the **injection moulding machine size**. If $F_{clamp}$ exceeds the machine's clamp tonnage, the mould will **open (flash)** during injection.
- **Shrinkage allowance (Eq. 4)** is applied when machining the cavity, so that the *moulded* part matches the *designed* dimension after cooling.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Number of Cavities ($n$)

This derivation is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in KTU examinations when paired with a production data set.

### 3.1.1 Given Data (Assumed)
- Annual production requirement, $Q = 1{,}000{,}000$ parts/year.
- Working hours per year, $H = 240$ days × 8 h/day = **1920 h/year**.
- Cycle time, $t_c = 30$ s.
- Number of working days excludes Sundays and holidays.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define production rate per cavity per year.**

If a single cavity produces **1 part per cycle**, then in one hour, it produces:

$$\text{Parts per hour per cavity} = \dfrac{3600}{t_c}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parts per hour per cavity} &= \dfrac{3600 \text{ s/h}}{30 \text{ s/cycle}} \\
&= 120 \text{ parts/hour/cavity}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by total available working hours.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parts per year per cavity} &= \dfrac{3600}{t_c} \times H \\
&= 120 \times 1920 \\
&= 230{,}400 \text{ parts/year/cavity}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate to annual demand and solve for $n$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= n \times \dfrac{3600 \times H}{t_c} \\
n &= \dfrac{Q \times t_c}{3600 \times H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000 \times 30}{3600 \times 1920} \\
&= \dfrac{30{,}000{,}000}{6{,}912{,}000} \\
&\approx 4.34
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Round up to the next whole integer.**

Since a cavity cannot be fractional, we always round **UP**:

$$
\boxed{n = 5 \text{ cavities}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** A designer would then check if **5 cavities fit on the platen** of the proposed machine. If not, two alternatives exist: (a) choose a larger machine, or (b) split production across two moulds of 3 and 2 cavities.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Clamping Force ($F_{clamp}$)

### 3.2.1 Given Data
- Cavity pressure, $P_{cav} = 700 \text{ bar} = 700 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 = 70 \text{ MPa}$.
- Projected area per part, $A_{part} = 25 \text{ cm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$.
- Number of cavities, $n = 5$.
- Safety factor, $k = 1.2$.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Total projected area.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{proj} &= n \times A_{part} \\
&= 5 \times 25 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 0.0125 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the clamp force equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{clamp} &= P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \times k \\
&= (70 \times 10^6) \times 0.0125 \times 1.2 \\
&= 1{,}050{,}000 \text{ N} \\
&= 1050 \text{ kN} \\
&\approx 107 \text{ tonnes-force (T)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Machine selection.**

A standard KTU 2024 industry benchmark is $1 \text{ T} \approx 10 \text{ kN}$. Thus the minimum machine size is **~107 T clamping force**. Designers select the next standard size: **130 T or 150 T**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Many students forget the unit conversion ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2 = 0.1 \text{ MPa}$). Always convert $\text{bar} \to \text{Pa}$ *before* substituting.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Shrinkage Allowance ($D_{s}$)

### 3.3.1 Given Data
- Nominal part dimension, $D_{cav} = 100 \text{ mm}$.
- Volumetric shrinkage (typical for PP), $S = 0.020$ (i.e., 2 %).

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{s} &= D_{cav} \times (1 + S) \\
&= 100 \times (1 + 0.020) \\
&= 100 \times 1.020 \\
&= 102.0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The mould cavity must be machined **2 % larger** than the part's nominal dimension. So the steel block is cut to **102.0 mm**, and after the polymer cools and shrinks, the moulded part will be **100.0 mm**, exactly matching the design.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Highly Tested in KTU)

| **Parameter** | **Injection Mould** | **Compression Mould** | **Transfer Mould** | **Blow Mould** | **Rotational Mould** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure** | 700–1400 bar | 100–400 bar | 400–700 bar | 4–10 bar (air) | < 1 bar (centrifugal) |
| **Material** | Thermoplastic | Thermoset, Rubber | Thermoset | Thermoplastic (hollow) | PE, PVC (powder) |
| **Curing** | Cooling (physical) | Heating (chemical) | Heating (chemical) | Cooling (physical) | Heating (chemical) |
| **Cycle Time** | 5–60 s | 60–600 s | 60–300 s | 10–60 s | 600–3600 s |
| **Part Geometry** | Solid, complex | Simple to moderate | Moderate, intricate | Hollow (bottles) | Large hollow (tanks) |
| **Wall Thickness** | Uniform, thin | Variable | Variable | Controlled by parison | Controlled by rotation |
| **Scrap / Waste** | Runner + sprue | Flash (minimal) | Runner + cull | Tail + flash | Negligible |
| **Mould Cost** | High | Low | Medium | Medium–High | Low |
| **Production Volume** | Very High | Medium | Medium | Very High | Low–Medium |
| **KTU Weightage** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Mermaid)

The following **block-level functional architecture** maps the entire classification of moulds covered in Module 1. This is the **single most important diagram** for KTU 14-mark essay questions.

```mermaid

    A[MOULD CLASSIFICATION]:::root --> B[Process Based]

    C --> C1[Two Plate Mould]
    C --> C2[Three Plate Mould]

    B1 --> B1a[Sub Gate]
```
