```mermaid
    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0b1d33,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef process fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#0b1d33
    classDef sub fill:#f4f4f4,stroke:#888,color:#222
```

---

## 4.2 Component Architecture of a Generic Two-Plate Injection Mould

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FIXED_HALF[Fixed Platen Side]
        A1[Clamping Plate] --> A2[Retainer Plate]
        A2 --> A3[Cavity Insert A]
        A2 --> A4[Cavity Insert B]
        A2 --> A5[Guide Bushing]
        A2 --> A6[Sprue Bushing]
    end

    subgraph MOVING_HALF[Moving Platen Side]
        B1[Support Plate] --> B2[Core Insert]
        B1 --> B3[Guide Pillar]
        B1 --> B4[Cooling Channel]
        B1 --> B5[Ejector Pin]
        B1 --> B6[Return Pin]
        B1 --> B7[Runner]
    end

    FIXED_HALF --- PARTING[PARTING LINE] --- MOVING_HALF

    A6 --> A1
    B2 --> PARTING
    B7 --> PARTING

    classDef fixed fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b1d33,color:#000
    classDef moving fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6 fixed
    class B1,B2,B3,B4,B5,B6,B7 moving
```

---

## 4.3 Process Flow Diagram — Injection Moulding Cycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Mould Closes] --> S2[Step 2: Injection Phase]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Packing and Holding]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Cooling]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Mould Opens]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Ejection of Part]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Mould Resets and Closes Again]
    S7 --> S2

    classDef step fill:#e2f0d9,stroke:#385723,color:#000
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7 step
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** Each **cycle step** above corresponds to a time interval in $T_{cycle}$. For a 30 s cycle: Injection (2 s) + Packing (4 s) + Cooling (18 s) + Open/Eject/Close (6 s) = 30 s. Cooling is **always the dominant** term — that is why mould cooling design is the single most-studied topic in Polymer Machinery.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between a two-plate mould and a three-plate mould."**

**Model Answer:**

A **two-plate mould** has **one parting plane** and a single-delivery system. The runner and gate are located on the same parting line, and the part is ejected along with the runner. In contrast, a **three-plate mould** has **two parting planes** — the runner system is sandwiched between a stationary plate and a movable plate (the third plate). When the mould opens, the runner is automatically separated from the part by a separate plate motion, allowing **automatic degating**.

| **Feature** | **Two-Plate** | **Three-Plate** |
|---|---|---|
| Parting planes | 1 | 2 |
| Gate types supported | Edge, tab, submarine | Pin-point, film |
| Runner removal | Manual / robotic | Automatic |
| Mould cost | Lower | Higher |
| Cavity balance | Moderate | Excellent |

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for table, 1 mark for application. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"What is a hot-runner mould? State any two advantages."**

**Model Answer:**

A **hot-runner mould** (also called a runnerless mould) is an injection mould in which the runner system is **actively heated** (using heater bands or internally heated manifolds) to keep the polymer in a **molten state** throughout the cycle, eliminating the formation of solid runner scrap.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Zero runner waste** — material saving of 5–30 % depending on part-to-runner ratio.
2. **Shorter cycle time** — no need to cool and re-melt the runner; only the part is solidified.
3. *(Bonus, optional)* Better part quality due to consistent pressure and temperature at the gate.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for advantages. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) Classify moulds used in polymer processing. Explain any three types in detail with neat sketches. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Classification Heading (2 marks)**

Moulds used in polymer processing are classified into the following major types:
1. Injection Mould
2. Compression Mould
3. Transfer Mould
4. Blow Mould
5. Rotational Mould
6. Thermoforming Die

**Step 2 — Detailed Explanation of Three Types (5 marks, distributed as ~1.7 marks each)**

**Type 1 — Injection Mould:**
A mould in which molten thermoplastic is injected under high pressure (700–1400 bar) into a closed cavity, held under pressure during cooling, and ejected as a solid part. The mould consists of two halves (cavity side and core side), a sprue, runner, gate, cooling channels, ejector system, and guide elements.

**Type 2 — Compression Mould:**
Used for thermosetting plastics and rubber. A pre-measured charge of material is placed in the open cavity. The press closes, applying heat and pressure simultaneously. The material cures (cross-links) and is then ejected. Types include **flash mould**, **positive mould**, and **semi-positive mould**.

**Type 3 — Transfer Mould:**
The thermoset is preheated in a **transfer pot** above the cavity, then forced by a plunger through a runner into the closed cavity. Advantages include less flash, better dimensional control, and ability to mould intricate parts with delicate inserts.

**Step 3 — Sketch (Optional but Recommended):**
A simple block diagram of an injection mould showing cavity, core, sprue, runner, gate, and ejector pins can be drawn for full marks.

---

**(b) A company needs to produce 5,00,000 plastic parts per year. Each mould cycle takes 25 seconds. The factory works 8 hours/day, 300 days/year. Calculate the number of cavities required. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Given Data (1 mark)**

- $Q = 5{,}00{,}000$ parts/year
- $t_c = 25$ s
- $H = 8 \times 300 = 2400$ hours/year

**Step 2 — Apply the Number of Cavities Formula (2 marks)**

$$
n = \dfrac{Q \times t_c}{3600 \times H}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Solve (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000 \times 25}{3600 \times 2400} \\
&= \dfrac{1{,}25{,}00{,}000}{86{,}40{,}000} \\
&\approx 1.446
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Round Up and Conclude (2 marks)**

Since $n$ must be a whole number, round up:

$$
\boxed{n = 2 \text{ cavities}}
$$

A **2-cavity mould** is selected. Each cycle produces 2 parts, and 2 cavities × 144 parts/hour × 2400 h/year = **6,91,200 parts/year**, comfortably meeting the 5,00,000 target with a safety margin of ~38 %.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) Compare injection moulding and compression moulding on the basis of (i) process principle, (ii) pressure, (iii) cycle time, (iv) material type, (v) part complexity, and (vi) wastage. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Tabular — 7 marks distributed as ~1 mark per row + 1 mark for introduction):**

| **Parameter** | **Injection Moulding** | **Compression Moulding** |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Principle** | Melt injected into closed mould under pressure. | Charge placed in open mould, then heat + pressure applied. |
| **Pressure** | 700–1400 bar | 100–400 bar |
| **Cycle Time** | 5–60 s | 60–600 s |
| **Material Type** | Thermoplastics | Thermosets, Rubber |
| **Part Complexity** | High (thin walls, intricate features) | Moderate (simpler geometry) |
| **Wastage** | Runner + sprue waste | Flash (minimal) |
| **Curing Mechanism** | Physical cooling | Chemical cross-linking |

**Conclusion (Bonus mark):** Injection moulding is favoured for high-volume, complex thermoplastic parts; compression moulding is preferred for thermoset parts where cross-link curing is required.

---

**(b) Explain the construction and working of a **rotational moulding** machine with a neat sketch. List any two applications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark)**
Rotational moulding is a process used to produce **hollow plastic parts** by rotating a closed mould (containing polymer powder or liquid) **biaxially** (around two perpendicular axes) inside a heated chamber.

**Step 2 — Construction (2 marks)**
- **Mould:** Hollow, split-cavity shell made of aluminium or mild steel.
- **Heating chamber:** Oven with gas/electric/oil heating.
- **Cooling chamber:** Air or water spray zone.
- **Rotating arm / spindle:** Carries the mould and provides biaxial rotation.
- **Drive system:** Electric motor + gear assembly for controlled rotation speed (typically 1–20 rpm on each axis).

**Step 3 — Working (2 marks)**
1. A pre-weighed charge of polymer powder is placed inside the open mould.
2. The mould is closed and transferred into the heating chamber.
3. The mould rotates biaxially — centrifugal force coats the inner mould surface with the melting polymer.
4. After full fusion, the mould is transferred to the cooling chamber.
5. Solidified part is removed, deflashed, and finished.

**Step 4 — Applications (2 marks)**
1. **Storage tanks** (500 L to 50,000 L water/chemical tanks).
2. **Kayaks, canoes, and playground equipment.**
3. *(Bonus)* Road barriers, traffic cones, and large containers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Mould Classification Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "mould" with "die":** In KTU valuation, students lose 1 mark if they say "die" when the question is about polymer moulds. Reserve "die" for extrusion.
> 2. **Unit Conversion Error in $n$ calculation:** Always convert $t_c$ from seconds to hours by multiplying the denominator by 3600, OR by using 3.6 ks/h. Writing $H$ in hours without the 3600 factor loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Rounding Down Instead of Up:** Always round $n$ **UP** to the next integer. A fractional cavity is impossible.
> 4. **Forgetting the safety factor in $F_{clamp}$:** The factor $k = 1.1$–$1.3$ is **non-negotiable** in industry and earns a full 1 mark in the KTU key.
> 5. **Confusing "Three-plate" with "Stack":** A three-plate mould is for runner separation; a stack mould is two moulds back-to-back. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this confusion.
> 6. **Not stating the process pressure:** If a question asks to "explain injection moulding" and you don't mention the **700–1400 bar pressure range**, you lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- **Mould** = precision tooling with negative geometry for shaping polymer.
- **Classification Axes:** Process / Construction / Cavity / Runner / Automation.
- **Injection Mould Pressure:** 700–1400 bar cavity pressure.
- **Compression Mould Pressure:** 100–400 bar.
- **Transfer Mould:** Polymer preheated in a **pot**, then forced into the cavity via a **plunger**.
- **Blow Mould:** Hollow parts, parison inflated by 4–10 bar compressed air.
- **Rotational Mould:** Biaxial rotation, < 1 bar effective pressure, large hollow parts.
- **Two-Plate Mould:** 1 parting plane, edge/submarine gates.
- **Three-Plate Mould:** 2 parting planes, pin-point gates, auto runner separation.
- **Hot-Runner Mould:** No runner waste, higher mould cost.
- **Cold-Runner Mould:** Runner solidifies, recycled, lower mould cost.
- **Single-Cavity:** Large or precision parts.
- **Multi-Cavity:** Mass production of small parts.
- **Number of Cavities Formula:** $n = \dfrac{Q \times t_c}{3600 \times H}$ — **always round UP.**
- **Clamping Force:** $F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \times k$.
- **Shrinkage Allowance:** $D_{s} = D_{cav} \times (1 + S)$.
- **Cycle Time:** $T_{cycle} = t_{fill} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{eject} + t_{reset}$.
- **Standard Mould Steel Hardness:** HRC 58–62.
- **Common Mould Steels:** P-20, H-13, S-7, DIN 1.2738.
- **Cooling channel diameter:** typically **8–12 mm**, with coolant flow **turbulent** (Re > 10,000).
- **Always draw a labelled sketch** in 14-mark questions; 2 marks are reserved for the diagram alone.
- **Cavity pressure is NOT the same as injection pressure** — cavity pressure is what the part experiences *inside* the mould (lower than injection pressure due to pressure losses in the runner).
- **Use the unit $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$** rigorously in numerical problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 2-Plate and 3-Plate Moulds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** 2-Plate and 3-Plate Moulds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Injection Moulds

Injection moulding is the most widely used polymer processing technique for the mass production of complex plastic parts. The **heart of the injection moulding process is the mould (tool)**, which determines the final shape, dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and production economics of the moulded article.

A mould essentially consists of two halves that open and close to allow mould filling, cooling, and part ejection. The classification of moulds into **2-plate** and **3-plate** systems is based on the number of parting lines, plates, and the mechanism of part ejection.

---

## 1.2 2-Plate Mould — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A **2-plate mould** is an injection mould construction consisting of **two primary plates** — the **cavity plate (A-side / fixed half)** and the **core plate (B-side / moving half)** — separated by a single parting line. The moulded part remains on the core side and is ejected through a single parting line using an ejection system (ejector pins, sleeves, or stripper plate).

### Structural Composition (High-Yield)

The 2-plate mould contains exactly **two main moulding plates** plus the support plates, ejector plates, and a single runner system that lies on the parting plane.

| Component Plate | Standard KTU Name | Function |
|----------------|-------------------|----------|
| Cavity Plate | **Fixed Half (A-Side)** | Houses the cavity impression; fixed to the stationary platen |
| Core Plate | **Moving Half (B-Side)** | Houses the core impression; attached to the moving platen |
| Support Plate | Backup Plate | Provides rigidity and houses cooling channels |
| Ejector Plate | Knock-out System | Pushes the solidified part out of the core |

---

## 1.3 3-Plate Mould — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A **3-plate mould** (also called a **three-plate mould** or **double-parting mould**) consists of **three primary plates** — the **cavity plate**, the **runner plate (middle plate)**, and the **core plate**. It employs **two parting lines**: one between the cavity and runner plate, and another between the runner plate and core plate. The part is ejected via the lower parting line, while the runner system is automatically separated at the upper parting line and ejected through a dedicated runner ejection system.

### Structural Composition (High-Yield)

| Component Plate | Standard KTU Name | Function |
|----------------|-------------------|----------|
| Cavity Plate | **Stationary Plate** | Forms the cavity impression |
| Runner Plate | **Middle / Stripper Plate** | Houses the runner and gate channels |
| Core Plate | **Moving Plate** | Forms the core impression and holds the moulded part |

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

### Analogy for 2-Plate Mould — *The Sandwich Press*

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a commercial sandwich toaster:** You place bread and filling on the lower plate, close the upper heated plate, the contents are "moulded" into shape, then you open the device and remove the finished sandwich from one side. The sandwich toaster has **two plates only** — exactly like a 2-plate mould. The molten polymer is the "filling", and the ejector pins are like a small spatula that lifts the sandwich off.

### Analogy for 3-Plate Mould — *The Multi-Tier Cake Pan*

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine baking a layered cake with a removable middle tier:** You have three layers — the bottom baking tray, a perforated middle tray that holds the frosting decoration (the runner), and the top mould that shapes the cake top. When you separate the pans, the **cake (part) drops off the lower plate** while the **decorative piping (runner) stays on the middle plate** and is removed separately. This double-separation action is precisely how a 3-plate mould works — the **part** drops at the **lower parting line**, while the **runner system** drops at the **upper parting line**.

---

## 1.5 When to Use Which Mould — Decision Logic

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Examinations:**
> - **Use 2-Plate Mould** when: Single cavity, single gate location acceptable, runner can be on the parting line, simpler parts, lower tooling cost required.
> - **Use 3-Plate Mould** when: Multi-cavity production required, **centre gating** is needed for symmetric filling, runner system must be automatically separated from the part, hot runner systems are not used, and high production rates are mandatory.

### Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Standard Cavity Pressure (P):** **30 – 70 MPa** (typically **40 MPa** for general-purpose polymers)
- **Projected Area Calculation Unit:** **mm²**
- **Standard Mould Steel:** P20, H13, S50C (varying with production volume)
- **Typical Polymers Used:** PP, PE, ABS, PS, PA6, POM, PMMA

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence — 2-Plate Mould

The injection cycle of a 2-plate mould follows a deterministic sequence:

1. **Mould Closing:** The moving platen advances, closing the cavity plate against the core plate with a clamping force sufficient to resist injection pressure.
2. **Injection Phase:** Molten polymer is injected through a **single gate** located on the parting line. The material flows through a runner (located on the parting plane) into the cavity.
3. **Packing & Holding:** Additional material is forced into the cavity to compensate for volumetric shrinkage during cooling.
4. **Cooling Phase:** The part solidifies inside the mould while heat is extracted via cooling channels (water/oil circulation).
5. **Mould Opening:** The moving platen retracts, opening the mould at the single parting line. The solidified part remains on the core (due to part shrinkage around the core).
6. **Ejection:** The ejector plate advances, pushing the part off the core using ejector pins, sleeves, or stripper plates.
7. **Runner Removal:** The runner (which is connected to the part at the gate) must be **manually trimmed** or **automatically cut** in a secondary operation.

### Engineering Limitations of 2-Plate Mould

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In a 2-plate mould, the **runner is attached to the part** at the gate location. This is acceptable for single-cavity moulds but **uneconomical for multi-cavity moulds** because the runner represents wasted material and requires post-moulding trimming.

---

## 2.2 Operational Sequence — 3-Plate Mould

1. **Mould Closing:** All three plates close, creating a sealed cavity system.
2. **Injection:** Molten polymer enters through a **centre gate** (or pin gate) located on the runner plate.
3. **Packing & Cooling:** Same as 2-plate mould, but the runner is in a separate plate.
4. **First Opening — Runner Separation:** The mould opens first at the **upper parting line** (between cavity plate and runner plate). The **runner system is retained** in the runner plate and later ejected.
5. **Second Opening — Part Ejection:** The mould then opens at the **lower parting line** (between runner plate and core plate). The **part remains on the core**.
6. **Ejection:** Two separate ejection systems operate:
   - **Part Ejection:** Ejector pins push the part off the core.
   - **Runner Ejection:** A separate runner ejector system pushes the solidified runner out of the runner plate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The double-opening mechanism in a 3-plate mould requires a **sequence control system** (often using **springs**, **hydraulic cylinders**, or **limit switches**) to ensure the upper parting line opens first, then the lower.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable under Module 1 of PCPOT503. Memorize with units.**

### Table 2.1: Clamping Force, Projected Area & Shot Weight

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Formula | Units (SI) | Application |
|--------|-----------|---------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | Projected Area (rectangular part) | $A_{proj} = L \times W$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Clamping force calculation |
| 2 | Projected Area (circular part) | $A_{proj} = \dfrac{\pi \times D^2}{4}$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Clamping force for cylindrical parts |
| 3 | Total Projected Area (multi-cavity) | $A_{total} = A_{proj} \times n_{cav} + A_{runner}$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | Total clamping load |
| 4 | Clamping Force | $F_{clamp} = A_{total} \times P_{cavity}$ | $\text{N}$ | Selecting moulding machine tonnage |
| 5 | Required Machine Tonnage | $T = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{10^4}$ | $\text{Tonnes (T)}$ | Convert N → Tonnes |
| 6 | Volume of Rectangular Part | $V_{part} = L \times W \times T_{h}$ | $\text{mm}^3$ | Shot weight calculation |
| 7 | Shot Weight (single cavity) | $W_{shot} = V_{part} \times \rho_{polymer}$ | $\text{g}$ | Mass of polymer per shot |
| 8 | Shot Weight (multi-cavity) | $W_{shot} = (n_{cav} \times V_{part} + V_{runner}) \times \rho_{polymer}$ | $\text{g}$ | Including runner mass |
| 9 | Cycle Time | $t_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{cool} + t_{eject} + t_{close}$ | $\text{s}$ | Production rate |
| 10 | Production Rate | $N = \dfrac{3600}{t_{cycle}}$ | $\text{shots/hr}$ | Hourly output |

### Table 2.2: Standard Symbols & Constants

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| $P_{cavity}$ | Average cavity pressure | **30 – 70 MPa** |
| $\rho_{polymer}$ | Polymer melt density | PP: 0.91 g/cm³, ABS: 1.05 g/cm³, PA6: 1.14 g/cm³ |
| $L, W, T_h$ | Length, Width, Thickness of part | mm |
| $n_{cav}$ | Number of cavities | dimensionless |
| $D$ | Diameter | mm |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Polymer Engineering

- **2-Plate Moulds** dominate the production of **household goods, automotive interior trim, packaging containers, and toys** due to their low tooling cost and simple construction.
- **3-Plate Moulds** are extensively used in the production of **multi-cavity thin-walled packaging (caps, lids, syringe components), high-precision medical devices, and consumer electronics** where balanced centre gating is essential for uniform filling.
- The choice directly affects **tooling cost (2-plate is 20–40% cheaper)**, **cycle time**, and **material wastage**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Clamping Force for 2-Plate Mould

### Statement
For a single rectangular moulded part of dimensions $L \times W \times T_h$, derive the expression for the clamping force required to keep the mould closed during the injection phase.

### Given Data
- Part length: $L$ (in mm)
- Part width: $W$ (in mm)
- Average cavity pressure: $P_{cavity}$ (in MPa = N/mm²)
- Number of cavities: $n_{cav}$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the projected area.**
The projected area is the shadow of the moulded part when viewed perpendicular to the parting line.

$$A_{proj} = L \times W$$

**Step 2: Compute the total projected area for the entire mould.**
This includes all cavity projections plus the runner system.

$$A_{total} = (L \times W) \times n_{cav} + A_{runner}$$

**Step 3: Apply Newton's Third Law (Equilibrium Condition).**
The clamping force must balance the internal cavity pressure acting on the projected area.

$$\sum F_{x} = 0$$

$$F_{clamp} - P_{cavity} \times A_{total} = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve for clamping force.**

$$F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \times A_{total}$$

**Step 5: Substitute the projected area expression.**

$$F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \times \left[(L \times W) \times n_{cav} + A_{runner}\right]$$

**Step 6: Convert to standard machine tonnage.**

$$T = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{9.81 \times 10^3} \approx \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{10^4} \quad \text{(in Tonnes)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Each 1 MPa of cavity pressure applied over a projected area of 1 cm² (i.e., 100 mm²) generates **100 N** of opening force on the mould. A typical moulding machine operates between **50 to 1000 tonnes** of clamping force.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shot Weight for Multi-Cavity 3-Plate Mould

### Statement
For a 3-plate mould producing $n_{cav}$ identical rectangular parts with a runner system of volume $V_{runner}$, derive the total shot weight required per cycle.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Volume of a single moulded part.**

$$V_{part} = L \times W \times T_h$$

**Step 2: Total volume of all cavities.**

$$V_{cavities} = n_{cav} \times V_{part} = n_{cav} \times L \times W \times T_h$$

**Step 3: Total moulded volume (cavities + runner).**

$$V_{total} = V_{cavities} + V_{runner} = (n_{cav} \times L \times W \times T_h) + V_{runner}$$

**Step 4: Multiply by polymer density to obtain mass.**

$$W_{shot} = V_{total} \times \rho_{polymer}$$

**Step 5: Final expanded expression.**

$$W_{shot} = \left[(n_{cav} \times L \times W \times T_h) + V_{runner}\right] \times \rho_{polymer}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Production Rate (Hourly Output)

### Statement
Derive the production rate in shots/hour for a mould with cycle time $t_{cycle}$ seconds.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Convert one hour into seconds.**

$$1 \text{ hour} = 3600 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2: Number of shots produced in 3600 seconds.**

$$N = \dfrac{3600}{t_{cycle}}$$

**Step 3: Mass produced per hour.**

$$M_{hourly} = N \times W_{shot} = \dfrac{3600 \times W_{shot}}{t_{cycle}}$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (3-Plate Mould)

> [!TIP]
> **Solve this example as a KTU Part B practice problem.**

**Problem:** A 3-plate mould produces **6 identical rectangular parts** per cycle. Each part has dimensions: $L = 80$ mm, $W = 50$ mm, $T_h = 3$ mm. The runner volume is $V_{runner} = 4 \times 10^4$ mm³. The polymer used is **Polypropylene (PP)** with $\rho = 0.91$ g/cm³. The average cavity pressure is **40 MPa**. Calculate:
(a) Total shot weight per cycle.
(b) Required clamping force (neglecting runner projected area).
(c) Hourly production rate if cycle time is **15 seconds**.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Shot Weight:**

$$V_{part} = 80 \times 50 \times 3 = 12000 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$V_{cavities} = 6 \times 12000 = 72000 \text{ mm}^3$$

Convert to cm³: $V_{cavities} = 72$ cm³

$$V_{runner} = 4 \times 10^4 \text{ mm}^3 = 40 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$V_{total} = 72 + 40 = 112 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$W_{shot} = 112 \times 0.91 = 101.92 \text{ g}$$

**Part (b) — Clamping Force:**

$$A_{proj} = 80 \times 50 = 4000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{total} = 6 \times 4000 = 24000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$F_{clamp} = 40 \times 24000 = 960000 \text{ N} = 960 \text{ kN}$$

$$T = \dfrac{960000}{10^4} = 96 \text{ Tonnes}$$

**Part (c) — Production Rate:**

$$N = \dfrac{3600}{15} = 240 \text{ shots/hr}$$

$$M_{hourly} = 240 \times 101.92 = 24460.8 \text{ g/hr} \approx 24.46 \text{ kg/hr}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | 2-Plate Mould | 3-Plate Mould | KTU Exam Insight |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|------------------|
| Number of Plates | 2 | 3 | Most asked definition question |
| Parting Lines | 1 | 2 | Frequently tested |
| Gate Type | Edge / Submarine / Tunnel | Centre / Pin | Critical design difference |
| Runner Location | On parting line | Inside runner plate | Distinguishes construction |
| Runner Ejection | Manual trimming | Automatic (separate system) | 3-plate advantage |
| Multi-cavity Suitability | Limited | Excellent | 3-plate preferred |
| Tooling Cost | Lower (baseline) | 20–40% higher | Trade-off question |
| Cycle Time | Slightly lower | Slightly higher | Due to two openings |
| Material Wastage | Higher (runner on part) | Lower (runner separated) | Economic consideration |
| Centre Gating | Not possible | Possible | Critical for symmetric filling |
| Construction Complexity | Simple | Complex | KTU short-note favourite |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — 2-Plate Mould

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Fixed Platen Mounting Plate] --> A2[Cavity Plate A-Side]
    A2 --> A3[Single Parting Line PL1]
    A3 --> A4[Core Plate B-Side]
    A4 --> A5[Support Backup Plate]
    A5 --> A6[Ejector Retainer Plate]
    A6 --> A7[Ejector Plate with Ejector Pins]
    A7 --> A8[Moving Platen Mounting Plate]
    B1[Sprue Bushing] --> A2
    B2[Runner Channel on Parting Line] --> A3
    B3[Gate at Cavity Entry] --> A3
    C1[Cooling Channel A-Side] -.-> A2
    C2[Cooling Channel B-Side] -.-> A4
    D1[Ejector Pin] --> A7
    D2[Return Pin] --> A7
    E1[Moulded Part on Core] --> A4
    E2[Runner Attached to Part] --> A3
    F1[Injection Machine Fixed Platen] --> A1
    F2[Injection Machine Moving Platen] --> A8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow proceeds vertically from the **fixed platen (top)** through the **cavity plate**, across the **single parting line (PL1)**, through the **core plate**, and terminates at the **ejector system (bottom)**, which is mounted on the **moving platen**. Cooling channels (dotted lines) and the runner system (attached to the part) are critical auxiliary systems.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — 3-Plate Mould

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Fixed Platen Mounting Plate] --> P2[Cavity Plate Stationary]
    P2 --> P3[Upper Parting Line UPL]
    P3 --> P4[Runner Plate Middle with Sprue and Runner]
    P4 --> P5[Lower Parting Line LPL]
    P5 --> P6[Core Plate Moving]
    P6 --> P7[Support Backup Plate]
    P7 --> P8[Part Ejector Plate]
    P8 --> P9[Runner Ejector Plate]
    P9 --> P10[Moving Platen Mounting Plate]
    Q1[Sprue Bushing Top Entry] --> P2
    Q2[Centre Gate Pin Gate] --> P4
    Q3[Runner Channel Network] --> P4
    R1[Part Ejector Pins] --> P8
    R2[Runner Ejector Pins] --> P9
    S1[Cooling Channel Stationary] -.-> P2
    S2[Cooling Channel Runner Plate] -.-> P4
    S3[Cooling Channel Core Side] -.-> P6
    T1[Moulded Part Drops at LPL] --> P6
    T2[Runner System Drops at UPL] --> P4
    U1[Sequence Control Springs or Hydraulic Cylinders] -.-> P3
    U2[Sequence Control Springs or Hydraulic Cylinders] -.-> P5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Note the **two parting lines (UPL and LPL)** with the **runner plate sandwiched between** them. The sequence control system (dotted) ensures the **upper parting line opens first** to release the runner, followed by the **lower parting line** to release the part. Two independent ejector systems handle part and runner ejection separately.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Flow — 2-Plate vs 3-Plate Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start of Cycle] --> B[Mould Closing]
    B --> C[Injection of Melt]
    C --> D[Packing and Holding]
    D --> E[Cooling Phase]
    E --> F{Plate Type}
    F -->|2-Plate| G1[Single Opening at PL1]
    F -->|3-Plate| G2[First Opening at UPL Runner Released]
    G2 --> H2[Second Opening at LPL Part Released]
    G1 --> I1[Part and Runner Ejected Together on Core]
    H2 --> I2[Runner Ejected Separately from Runner Plate]
    I1 --> J1[Manual Trimming of Runner Required]
    I2 --> J2[No Manual Runner Trimming Required]
    J1 --> K[Next Cycle]
    J2 --> K[Next Cycle]
```

---

## 4.4 Component Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IA[Injection Assembly]
        IA1[Sprue Bushing]
        IA2[Nozzle of Machine]
    end
    subgraph CP[Cavity Plate Stationary]
        CP1[Cavity Impression]
        CP2[Cooling Channels]
    end
    subgraph RP[Runner Plate Middle]
        RP1[Runner Network]
        RP2[Centre Gate or Pin Gate]
        RP3[Runner Ejector]
    end
    subgraph CORE[Core Plate Moving]
        CORE1[Core Impression]
        CORE2[Cooling Channels]
        CORE3[Part Ejector Pins]
    end
    subgraph EM[Ejection Mechanism]
        EM1[Part Ejector Plate]
        EM2[Return Pins]
        EM3[Guide Pins and Bushings]
    end
    IA2 --> IA1
    IA1 --> CP1
    CP1 --> RP2
    RP2 --> RP1
    RP1 --> CORE1
    CORE1 --> EM1
    RP1 --> RP3
    EM1 --> CORE3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]**

**Define a 2-plate mould. List its main components with their functions.**

#### Model Answer

A **2-plate mould** is an injection mould construction consisting of **two primary plates** — the **cavity plate (A-side)** and the **core plate (B-side)** — separated by a **single parting line**. The moulded part is ejected from the core side using an ejector system.

**Main Components & Functions (1.5 marks each):**

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Cavity Plate (A-Side)** | Houses the cavity impression; attached to the fixed platen of the moulding machine |
| **Core Plate (B-Side)** | Houses the core impression; attached to the moving platen |
| **Ejector Plate Assembly** | Pushes the solidified part off the core using ejector pins |
| **Sprue Bushing** | Provides entry point for molten polymer from the machine nozzle |
| **Runner Channel** | Located on the parting line; distributes melt to the cavity |
| **Gate** | Restricts flow and separates part from runner |
| **Cooling Channels** | Circulate water/oil to extract heat and solidify the part |

*(Word limit as per KTU norms: ~80–100 words for 3-mark answer)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – December 2023 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]**

**Differentiate between 2-plate and 3-plate moulds based on parting lines, gate type, runner ejection, and multi-cavity suitability.**

#### Model Answer

| Feature | 2-Plate Mould | 3-Plate Mould |
|---------|---------------|---------------|
| **Parting Lines** | Single parting line (PL1) | Two parting lines (UPL and LPL) |
| **Gate Type** | Edge gate, submarine gate, tunnel gate | Centre gate or pin gate |
| **Runner Ejection** | Manual trimming required | Automatic ejection via runner plate |
| **Multi-cavity Suitability** | Limited | Excellent (balanced filling) |
| **Number of Plates** | 2 main plates | 3 main plates |
| **Tooling Cost** | Lower | Higher |

*(Total 6 distinctions × 0.5 mark each = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 | Module 1 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **3-plate mould** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations over a 2-plate mould. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-plate mould produces **8 rectangular parts** per cycle. Each part measures $L = 100$ mm, $W = 60$ mm, $T_h = 4$ mm. The runner volume is $5 \times 10^4$ mm³. The polymer is **ABS** with density $\rho = 1.05$ g/cm³. The average cavity pressure is **35 MPa**. Calculate:
  (i) Total shot weight per cycle
  (ii) Required clamping force in Tonnes
  (iii) Hourly production rate if cycle time = 20 seconds **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction of 3-Plate Mould (3 Marks):**

A 3-plate mould consists of three primary plates:
1. **Cavity Plate (Stationary):** Houses the cavity impression; fixed to the stationary platen.
2. **Runner Plate (Middle):** Houses the runner channel and centre/pin gates; it is the middle plate that separates during mould opening.
3. **Core Plate (Moving):** Houses the core impression; the moulded part remains on this plate.

Auxiliary components: sprue bushing, runner ejector system, part ejector pins, cooling channels, guide pins, return pins, and a sequence control mechanism (springs or hydraulic cylinders).

**Working Cycle (2 Marks):**
- Mould closing → Injection → Packing → Cooling → First opening at upper parting line (runner separates from cavity) → Second opening at lower parting line (part separates from runner plate) → Part ejection via ejector pins → Runner ejection via runner ejector system → Mould closing for next cycle.

**Advantages over 2-plate mould (1 Mark):**
- Centre gating possible → balanced multi-cavity filling.
- Automatic runner separation → no manual trimming.
- Suitable for symmetric, thin-walled parts.

**Limitations (1 Mark):**
- Higher tooling cost (20–40% more).
- More complex construction and maintenance.
- Slightly longer cycle time due to double opening.

---

#### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $n_{cav} = 8$, $L = 100$ mm, $W = 60$ mm, $T_h = 4$ mm
- $V_{runner} = 5 \times 10^4$ mm³ = **50 cm³**
- $\rho = 1.05$ g/cm³, $P_{cavity} = 35$ MPa, $t_{cycle} = 20$ s

**(i) Shot Weight (3 Marks):**

$$V_{part} = 100 \times 60 \times 4 = 24000 \text{ mm}^3 = 24 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$V_{cavities} = 8 \times 24 = 192 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$V_{total} = 192 + 50 = 242 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$W_{shot} = 242 \times 1.05 = 254.1 \text{ g}$$

**(ii) Clamping Force (2 Marks):**

$$A_{proj} = 100 \times 60 = 6000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{total} = 8 \times 6000 = 48000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$F_{clamp} = 35 \times 48000 = 1680000 \text{ N}$$

$$T = \dfrac{1680000}{10^4} = 168 \text{ Tonnes}$$

**(iii) Production Rate (2 Marks):**

$$N = \dfrac{3600}{20} = 180 \text{ shots/hr}$$

$$M_{hourly} = 180 \times 254.1 = 45738 \text{ g/hr} = 45.74 \text{ kg/hr}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – December 2023 | Module 1 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the **constructional features of a 2-plate mould**. Explain the function of sprue, runner, gate, and ejector system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 2-plate mould produces **4 identical circular discs** per cycle. Each disc has diameter $D = 80$ mm and thickness $T_h = 5$ mm. The runner projected area is $1200$ mm². The cavity pressure is **45 MPa**. The polymer is **Polypropylene (PP)** with $\rho = 0.91$ g/cm³. Calculate:
  (i) Total projected area and clamping force in Tonnes
  (ii) Shot weight per cycle (assuming runner volume = 20 cm³)
  (iii) Cycle time if the hourly output target is **250 shots/hr** **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Labelled Diagram (3 Marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the functional block diagram.

**Constructional Features (2 Marks):**
A 2-plate mould consists of:
- **Cavity plate (fixed half)** mounted on the stationary platen.
- **Core plate (moving half)** mounted on the moving platen.
- **Single parting line** between the two plates.
- **Sprue bushing, runner channel, gate** all lie on the parting plane.
- **Ejector assembly** mounted behind the core plate.
- **Cooling channels** in both plates.
- **Guide pins and bushings** for alignment.

**Function of Key Components (2 Marks):**
- **Sprue:** Direct entry passage for molten polymer from the machine nozzle into the mould.
- **Runner:** Distributes the melt from the sprue to each cavity.
- **Gate:** Restricts flow, controls fill rate, and allows part separation from runner.
- **Ejector System:** Pushes the solidified part off the core after mould opening.

---

#### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $n_{cav} = 4$, $D = 80$ mm, $T_h = 5$ mm
- $A_{runner} = 1200$ mm², $P_{cavity} = 45$ MPa
- $\rho = 0.91$ g/cm³, $V_{runner} = 20$ cm³
- Target: 250 shots/hr

**(i) Clamping Force (3 Marks):**

$$A_{disc} = \dfrac{\pi \times 80^2}{4} = 5026.55 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{cavities} = 4 \times 5026.55 = 20106.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{total} = 20106.2 + 1200 = 21306.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$F_{clamp} = 45 \times 21306.2 = 958779 \text{ N}$$

$$T = \dfrac{958779}{10^4} = 95.88 \approx 96 \text{ Tonnes}$$

**(ii) Shot Weight (2 Marks):**

$$V_{part} = 5026.55 \times 5 = 25132.7 \text{ mm}^3 = 25.13 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$V_{cavities} = 4 \times 25.13 = 100.53 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$V_{total} = 100.53 + 20 = 120.53 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$W_{shot} = 120.53 \times 0.91 = 109.68 \text{ g}$$

**(iii) Cycle Time (2 Marks):**

$$t_{cycle} = \dfrac{3600}{250} = 14.4 \text{ s}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically LOSE MARKS on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (–2 marks):** Students forget to convert mm³ → cm³ before multiplying by density in g/cm³. Always convert volume to cm³.
> 2. **Neglecting Runner Area/Volume (–1 to –2 marks):** When the problem states "runner area = X mm²", students forget to add it to the total projected area. Always read carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting N → Tonnes Conversion (–1 mark):** Clamping force in Newtons must be divided by **10⁴** to obtain Tonnes. Writing F in kN without conversion loses marks.
> 4. **Confusing Plate Names:** Mixing up A-side / B-side or stationary / moving plate definitions. Memorize: **A-side = Cavity = Fixed**, **B-side = Core = Moving**.
> 5. **Drawing the Diagram WITHOUT Labels:** A neat diagram without labels fetches only 1 mark out of 3–4. Always label **sprue, runner, gate, parting line, ejector pins, cooling channels**.
> 6. **Skipping the Sequence Control Mechanism:** For 3-plate moulds, KTU examiners specifically look for mention of **springs/hydraulic sequence control** to ensure upper parting line opens first.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — 2-Plate & 3-Plate Moulds (Module 1)**
>
> - **2-Plate Mould:** 2 main plates, **1 parting line**, runner on parting plane, gate is edge/submarine/tunnel type, manual runner trimming.
> - **3-Plate Mould:** 3 main plates, **2 parting lines**, runner inside middle plate, gate is **centre/pin** type, **automatic runner ejection**.
> - **Clamping Force Formula:** $F_{clamp} = A_{total} \times P_{cavity}$ (always in Newtons).
> - **Tonnage Conversion:** $T = F_{clamp} / 10^4$ Tonnes.
> - **Shot Weight Formula:** $W_{shot} = (n_{cav} \times V_{part} + V_{runner}) \times \rho$.
> - **Production Rate:** $N = 3600 / t_{cycle}$ shots/hr.
> - **Standard Cavity Pressure Range:** **30 – 70 MPa** (use 40 MPa for default problems).
> - **3-Plate Advantages:** Centre gating, balanced filling, automatic runner separation, multi-cavity efficiency.
> - **3-Plate Disadvantages:** Higher cost, complex construction, longer cycle.
> - **2-Plate Advantages:** Simple, cheap, faster cycle for single-cavity production.
> - **Sequence Control:** Essential in 3-plate moulds — ensures upper parting line opens first via springs or hydraulic cylinders.
> - **Part Retention:** In both moulds, the **part remains on the core** due to shrinkage around the core.
> - **Cooling Time:** Usually **50–70% of total cycle time**; dominates productivity.
> - **Tooling Cost Premium for 3-Plate:** Typically **20–40%** higher than 2-plate.
> - **Always state units explicitly** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Types of Gates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Different Types of Gates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GATE (Injection Molding Context):** A *gate* is a precisely engineered constriction or restricted passage machined into a mold that connects the runner system to the mold cavity. It is the **final control point** through which the molten polymer (melt) enters the cavity, and its geometry, size, and location directly govern the **flow rate, shear rate, pressure drop, fill pattern, packing efficiency, weld line position, and the cosmetic/structural quality** of the finished plastic part.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PCPOT503, Module 1), gates are classified as a sub-system of the **feeding system** (hot tip → sprue → runner → **gate** → cavity). The gate is the **smallest cross-section** in the entire flow path, and therefore it is also the region where **maximum shear rate** and **maximum heat generation** (viscous dissipation) occur. Once the part is ejected, the gate leaves a vestigial mark (gate vestige) that must be subsequently removed, hidden, or designed to be functionally invisible.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a gate as the **nozzle of a pressure cooker or a shower head**. Imagine you have a water tank (the barrel/cylinder) connected to a wide pipe (runner) which then suddenly narrows to a small outlet (gate) just before entering a mold-shaped basin (cavity). That narrow outlet:
> 1. **Speeds up the water (increases velocity and shear)**
> 2. **Generates a pressure drop**
> 3. **Decides where the water hits the basin first** (gate location)
> 4. **Can be opened or closed quickly** (gate freeze-off)
>
> The plastic melt behaves exactly like this, except the melt *freezes solid* when the gate cools, sealing the part inside the cavity for proper packing before the mold opens.

## 1.2 Primary Functional Roles of a Gate

A gate in polymer injection molding performs **five simultaneous, interlinked engineering functions**:

| Sl. No. | Function | Engineering Significance |
|:-:|:--|:--|
| 1 | **Flow control** | Restricts melt flow; creates the necessary shear heating to keep melt fluid. |
| 2 | **Pressure transmission** | Transfers injection pressure from the runner into the cavity for packing. |
| 3 | **Gate freeze-off** | Solidifies first when injection stops; prevents back-flow of melt. |
| 4 | **Cavity fill sequencing** | Determines *where* and *how fast* the melt front advances. |
| 5 | **Part separation / cosmetic** | Acts as the natural *weak point* for part de-gating after molding. |

## 1.3 General Classification of Gates

Gates are broadly classified under **two super-categories** in the KTU syllabus:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A. Cold Runner Gates (Conventional / Three-Plate Molds):** The gate solidifies with the part and is removed/torn off.
> **B. Hot Runner Gates (Hot Tip / Hot Sprue / Valve Gate Systems):** The gate region is kept molten; no solidified gate vestige remains on the part.

We will now analyze **every individual type** in detail in Section 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Types of Gates — Engineering Analysis

Below is a comprehensive, examiner-grade analysis of **every gate type** that a KTU 2024 student must be able to draw, define, and compare.

### 2.1.1 Sprue Gate (Direct Gate)

- **Geometry:** The melt enters the cavity *directly* from the sprue. There is **no runner** between the sprue and the cavity.
- **Diameter:** Typically **d = 0.8 – 1.5 × part wall thickness** (most often equal to wall thickness).
- **Where used:** Single-cavity molds, thick-walled parts, large center-gated items (buckets, tubs).
- **Advantages:** Simplest design; shortest flow path; minimum pressure drop; low scrap.
- **Disadvantages:** Leaves a **large, prominent gate vestige** that must be machined/trimmed; gate freeze-off is slow → high residual stress and sink marks near the gate.
- **Shear rate peak:** Very high because the gate cross-section is small.

### 2.1.2 Edge Gate (Side Gate / Rectangular Gate)

- **Geometry:** A rectangular slot machined at the **parting line**; the runner lies on one half of the mold, the cavity on the other.
- **Cross-section:** $A = w \times t$ (width × thickness).
- **Typical ratio:** $w : t = 2 : 1$ to $5 : 1$.
- **Where used:** Most common gate for general purpose parts; two-plate molds; multi-cavity molds.
- **Advantages:** Easy to machine; gate vestige at parting line is easy to trim; flexible positioning.
- **Disadvantages:** Generates **weld lines** if placed opposite another gate; weld/meld line forms in the cavity mid-way.

### 2.1.3 Fan Gate

- **Geometry:** A fan-shaped land that tapers from the runner into a wide, thin rectangular gate.
- **Where used:** For **large, thin, flat parts** (e.g., lids, covers, panels) where uniform flow across the width is critical.
- **Advantages:** **Equal flow distribution** across the entire width; low jetting; uniform filling.
- **Disadvantages:** More expensive to machine; runner length is wasted material.

### 2.1.4 Tab Gate

- **Geometry:** A small **tab** (stub) of material is added to the part at the gate location, so the gate is cut off *with the tab* and the part itself is unmarked.
- **Where used:** Cosmetic parts (transparent housings, lens covers) where no gate mark is permitted on the visible surface.
- **Advantages:** Gate mark is hidden on the tab; cleaner cosmetic appearance.
- **Disadvantages:** Extra material (tab) is waste; extra machining in mold.

### 2.1.5 Pin Gate (Tunnel Gate)

- **Geometry:** A small **circular tunnel** (typically 0.5 – 1.5 mm diameter) machined at an angle (often 30°–45°) from the parting line into the cavity wall.
- **Where used:** **Three-plate molds**; multi-cavity molds; small parts; parts needing automatic de-gating.
- **Advantages:** Automatically de-gates as the part is ejected; small gate vestige; small pressure drop per gate.
- **Disadvantages:** High shear rate (small diameter); prone to **jetting**; expensive tooling; not suitable for highly filled or glass-fibre-reinforced materials.

### 2.1.6 Submarine Gate (Sub-Gate / Tunnel Gate Sub-type)

- **Geometry:** The gate tunnel passes *underneath* the cavity surface (submerged) and opens into the cavity through a sub-surface channel.
- **Where used:** When a small, clean gate mark is needed and the part permits a hole in a non-critical location.
- **Advantages:** Hidden, sub-surface; automatic de-gating.
- **Disadvantages:** More complex machining; small diameter → high shear.

### 2.1.7 Diaphragm Gate

- **Geometry:** The melt enters through an **annular ring** around a central core, then flows radially outward to fill a **cylindrical/hollow part** (e.g., a cup, pipe fitting).
- **Where used:** Cylindrical parts requiring **uniform wall thickness** and concentric flow; eliminates weld lines completely.
- **Advantages:** No weld line; uniform wall; balanced filling.
- **Disadvantages:** High material waste; runner becomes a disc of scrap; restricted to round/cylindrical parts.

### 2.1.8 Ring Gate

- **Geometry:** Similar to diaphragm gate but the annular opening is on the **outside** of a cylindrical part, encircling the core pin.
- **Where used:** Externally cylindrical parts with internal features.
- **Advantages:** Balanced flow; no weld lines.
- **Disadvantages:** Material wastage; difficult to balance flow in asymmetric parts.

### 2.1.9 Disk Gate (Film Gate / Wafer Gate)

- **Geometry:** A very **thin, wide rectangular gate** covering a large area of the part surface.
- **Where used:** **Thin, flat, large-area parts** (e.g., compact discs, lenses, nameplates).
- **Advantages:** Uniform filling across width; minimal jetting; very small vestige.
- **Disadvantages:** High pressure drop due to thin gate; difficult to machine; slow freeze.

### 2.1.10 Cashew Gate (Banana Gate / Curved Tab Gate)

- **Geometry:** A **curved tab** gate used when a straight tab would interfere with a feature.
- **Where used:** Cosmetics, decorative parts.
- **Advantages:** Aesthetically pleasing gate mark placement.
- **Disadvantages:** Difficult machining; expensive.

### 2.1.11 Flash Gate (Flash-Type Edge Gate)

- **Geometry:** A wide, thin land where the cavity is intentionally over-filled and excess flashes out at the parting line.
- **Where used:** Very thin, flat parts (films, gaskets) where shear control is paramount.
- **Advantages:** Excellent for thin-wall parts; very uniform flow.
- **Disadvantages:** Excessive flash; high scrap; mould maintenance critical.

### 2.1.12 Hot Runner Gates (Hot Tip / Heated Sprue)

- **Geometry:** The runner and gate region are heated by **internal heater bands**; melt never freezes in the runner.
- **Sub-types:**
  - **Open Hot Runner Gate** — no shut-off.
  - **Valve Gate (Mechanical Shut-off)** — a pneumatically/hydraulically actuated needle closes the gate after packing.
- **Where used:** High-volume production, technical polymers, large multi-cavity molds.
- **Advantages:** **Zero runner waste**; consistent gate quality; faster cycle time; lower pressure drop.
- **Disadvantages:** Very expensive tooling; complex temperature control; not for PVC (thermal degradation).

### 2.1.13 Insulated Runner

- **Geometry:** A **large-diameter** runner that remains molten during the cycle due to its low surface-area-to-volume ratio and the insulating skin layer that forms on the inside.
- **Where used:** Lower-cost alternative to hot runner; commodity polymers.
- **Advantages:** No runner scrap; simple.
- **Disadvantages:** Difficult start-up; colour changeover is slow.

### 2.1.14 Self-Decoupling (Automatic) Gate vs Manual Gate

| Feature | Self-Decoupling | Manual |
|:--|:--|:--|
| De-gating | Part is pulled off during ejection | Operator trims gate |
| Speed | Fast (no labour) | Slow |
| Cost of tooling | Higher | Lower |
| Vestige | Small | Often larger |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **core derivations** a KTU examiner will expect a student to recall and apply for gate design.

### 2.2.1 Gate Diameter — Sprue Gate (Diameter Equation)

$$d_{g} = \sqrt{\frac{W_{s}}{\rho_{m} \cdot v_{g} \cdot t_{f}}}$$

Where:
- $d_{g}$ = gate diameter (m)
- $W_{s}$ = mass of the shot (kg)
- $\rho_{m}$ = density of the melt (kg/m³)
- $v_{g}$ = average velocity in the gate (m/s)
- $t_{f}$ = fill time (s)

### 2.2.2 Apparent Shear Rate in a Circular Gate (Pin / Sprue Gate)

$$\dot{\gamma}_{a} = \frac{32 \cdot Q}{\pi \cdot d_{g}^{3}}$$

Where $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s), and $d_{g}$ is the gate diameter. This is the **most-tested formula** in KTU Module 1 problems.

### 2.2.3 Apparent Shear Rate in a Rectangular (Edge) Gate

$$\dot{\gamma}_{a} = \frac{6 \cdot Q}{w \cdot t^{2}}$$

Where $w$ = gate width, $t$ = gate thickness (m).

### 2.2.4 Pressure Drop Across a Circular Gate (Poiseuille Flow)

$$\Delta P_{g} = \frac{128 \cdot \eta \cdot L_{g} \cdot Q}{\pi \cdot d_{g}^{4}}$$

Where $L_{g}$ = gate land length, $\eta$ = melt viscosity (Pa·s).

### 2.2.5 Gate Freeze Time (Approximate, assuming 1-D heat conduction)

$$t_{f,g} = \frac{d_{g}^{2}}{\pi^{2} \cdot \alpha_{m}} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \frac{T_{m} - T_{w}}{T_{g,mat} - T_{w}}\right)^{2}$$

Where:
- $\alpha_{m}$ = thermal diffusivity of polymer (m²/s)
- $T_{m}$ = melt temperature (K)
- $T_{w}$ = mould wall temperature (K)
- $T_{g,mat}$ = glass transition (or melting) temperature (K)

### 2.2.6 Volume of a Part (used in shot size / gate diameter problems)

$$V_{part} = \frac{W_{s}}{\rho_{m}}$$

### 2.2.7 Power Dissipation (Viscous Heating) in the Gate

$$\dot{W}_{visc} = \Delta P_{g} \cdot Q$$

This is the heat that keeps the gate region fluid during injection.

### 2.2.8 Summary Table — All Equations in One Place

| # | Equation | Application |
|:-:|:--|:--|
| 1 | $d_{g} = \sqrt{\dfrac{W_{s}}{\rho_{m} v_{g} t_{f}}}$ | Sprue / circular gate diameter |
| 2 | $\dot{\gamma}_{a} = \dfrac{32Q}{\pi d_{g}^{3}}$ | Shear rate in circular gate |
| 3 | $\dot{\gamma}_{a} = \dfrac{6Q}{w t^{2}}$ | Shear rate in rectangular gate |
| 4 | $\Delta P_{g} = \dfrac{128 \eta L_{g} Q}{\pi d_{g}^{4}}$ | Pressure drop across gate |
| 5 | $t_{f,g} = \dfrac{d_{g}^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha_{m}} \ln^{2}\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{m} - T_{w}}{T_{g,mat} - T_{w}}\right)$ | Gate freeze time |
| 6 | $\dot{W}_{visc} = \Delta P_{g} \cdot Q$ | Viscous heating power |
| 7 | $V_{part} = \dfrac{W_{s}}{\rho_{m}}$ | Part volume from shot mass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When solving numerical problems, always carry units and **convert mm → m** before substituting. The $d^{4}$ and $d^{3}$ terms make a tiny dimensional error catastrophically wrong. Use **5–6 significant figures** in intermediate steps.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Sprue Gate Diameter for a Polystyrene Part

**Problem Statement (KTU 2019-style adapted):**
> An injection moulding machine is to produce a Polystyrene (PS) cup. The shot mass required is **45 g**. The melt density of PS is $\rho_{m} = 950 \,\text{kg/m}^{3}$. The allowable average gate velocity is $v_{g} = 4 \,\text{m/s}$ and the fill time is $t_{f} = 0.6 \,\text{s}$. Calculate the **diameter of the sprue gate**.

**Step 1 — Identify given data and target variable.**

$$W_{s} = 45 \,\text{g} = 0.045 \,\text{kg}$$
$$\rho_{m} = 950 \,\text{kg/m}^{3}$$
$$v_{g} = 4 \,\text{m/s}$$
$$t_{f} = 0.6 \,\text{s}$$
$$\text{Target: } d_{g} = ?$$

**Step 2 — Apply the gate diameter equation.**

$$d_{g} = \sqrt{\frac{W_{s}}{\rho_{m} \cdot v_{g} \cdot t_{f}}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$d_{g} = \sqrt{\frac{0.045}{950 \times 4 \times 0.6}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator first.**

$$950 \times 4 = 3800$$
$$3800 \times 0.6 = 2280$$

**Step 5 — Compute the fraction under the root.**

$$\frac{0.045}{2280} = 1.9737 \times 10^{-5} \,\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Take the square root.**

$$d_{g} = \sqrt{1.9737 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.443 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to mm for practical readability.**

$$d_{g} = 4.443 \,\text{mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A sprue gate of diameter ≈ **4.5 mm** is required. This is consistent with the rule-of-thumb: $d_{g} \approx 0.8 - 1.5 \times$ wall thickness (assuming wall ≈ 3–5 mm).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Shear Rate & Pressure Drop in a Pin Gate

**Problem Statement:**
> A polypropylene (PP) part is filled through a circular pin gate of diameter $d_{g} = 1.2 \,\text{mm}$ and land length $L_{g} = 2.5 \,\text{mm}$. The volumetric flow rate is $Q = 5 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ and the melt viscosity at the gate shear rate is $\eta = 250 \,\text{Pa \cdot s}$. Calculate (a) the **apparent shear rate** in the gate, and (b) the **pressure drop** across the gate.

**Step 1 — Convert dimensions to SI units (metres).**

$$d_{g} = 1.2 \,\text{mm} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m}$$
$$L_{g} = 2.5 \,\text{mm} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Part (a): Apply the shear rate formula for a circular gate.**

$$\dot{\gamma}_{a} = \frac{32 \cdot Q}{\pi \cdot d_{g}^{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $d_{g}^{3}$.**

$$d_{g}^{3} = (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^{3} = 1.728 \times 10^{-9} \,\text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve.**

$$\dot{\gamma}_{a} = \frac{32 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times 1.728 \times 10^{-9}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerator evaluation.**

$$32 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 — Denominator evaluation.**

$$\pi \times 1.728 \times 10^{-9} = 5.4287 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Step 7 — Divide.**

$$\dot{\gamma}_{a} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-4}}{5.4287 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.948 \times 10^{4} \,\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 8 — Final shear rate.**

$$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_{a} \approx 29{,}480 \,\text{s}^{-1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This shear rate of ~3 × 10⁴ s⁻¹ is well within the typical processing window of PP (10³ – 10⁵ s⁻¹). Shear thinning keeps effective viscosity low, allowing fill with moderate pressure.

**Step 9 — Part (b): Apply the Hagen–Poiseuille pressure drop equation.**

$$\Delta P_{g} = \frac{128 \cdot \eta \cdot L_{g} \cdot Q}{\pi \cdot d_{g}^{4}}$$

**Step 10 — Evaluate $d_{g}^{4}$.**

$$d_{g}^{4} = (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^{4} = 2.0736 \times 10^{-12} \,\text{m}^{4}$$

**Step 11 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$\Delta P_{g} = \frac{128 \times 250 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times 2.0736 \times 10^{-12}}$$

**Step 12 — Numerator evaluation.**

$$128 \times 250 = 32000$$
$$32000 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 80$$
$$80 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 13 — Denominator evaluation.**

$$\pi \times 2.0736 \times 10^{-12} = 6.5142 \times 10^{-12}$$

**Step 14 — Final division.**

$$\Delta P_{g} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{6.5142 \times 10^{-12}} = 6.141 \times 10^{7} \,\text{Pa}$$

**Step 15 — Convert to MPa.**

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{g} \approx 61.4 \,\text{MPa}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A pressure drop of 61 MPa is **very high** and indicates the gate is too small (or flow rate too high). The remedy is to enlarge $d_{g}$ slightly, since $\Delta P \propto 1/d_{g}^{4}$.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All Gate Types

| Gate Type | Gate Cross-Section | Typical Size | Shear Rate | Pressure Drop | Gate Vestige | Best For | Cost |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| Sprue / Direct | Circular | d = 0.8–6 mm | High | Low–Med | Large, visible | Single cavity, thick parts | Low |
| Edge / Side | Rectangular | 0.8–3 mm t | Medium | Medium | Visible at PL | General purpose | Low |
| Fan | Tapered rectangular | 0.5–2 mm t | Medium | Low | Thin line | Flat, wide parts | Medium |
| Tab | Rectangular on tab | 1–3 mm t | Low | Low | Hidden on tab | Cosmetics | Medium |
| Pin | Circular | d = 0.5–1.5 mm | **Very High** | High | Tiny dot | 3-plate, multi-cavity | High |
| Submarine | Circular | d = 0.5–1.2 mm | **Very High** | High | Sub-surface | Small cosmetic parts | High |
| Diaphragm | Annular | 0.3–1 mm land | High | Medium | On central core | Cylindrical parts | Medium |
| Ring | Annular | 0.3–1 mm land | High | Medium | Internal | Cylindrical parts | Medium |
| Disk / Film | Rectangular (wide) | 0.2–0.6 mm t | Low | Low | Minimal | Discs, lenses | High |
| Cashew | Curved tab | 1–2 mm t | Low | Low | Hidden | Cosmetics | High |
| Hot Runner | Heated land | d = 1–4 mm | Controlled | Low | None | High volume, technical | **Very High** |
| Valve Gate | Heated + needle | d = 1–4 mm | Controlled | Low | None | Premium cosmetics | **Very High** |
| Insulated | Large circular | d = 6–12 mm | Very low | Very low | None | Commodity polymers | Medium |

---

## 3.4 Design Rules of Thumb (KTU High-Yield Points)

> [!NOTE]
> 1. **Gate thickness ≈ 50–80 % of part wall thickness** (for edge gates).
> 2. **Gate land length ≈ 0.5–1.0 × gate thickness** (avoid long land — it causes premature freeze).
> 3. **Maximum shear rate in gate** should not exceed **50,000 s⁻¹** for most amorphous polymers and **30,000 s⁻¹** for semi-crystalline polymers.
> 4. **Gate should be located at the thickest section** of the part to minimize sink marks and voids.
> 5. For **multi-cavity molds**, all gates must be **geometrically balanced** so each cavity fills in the same time.
> 6. The **gate is always the smallest cross-section** in the flow path.

---
