| **Plasticizing Mechanism** | Conduction from torpedo + external heaters | Screw rotation + shear + conduction | Screw in extruder + conduction |
| **Melt Homogeneity** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Max Injection Pressure** | Very High (200 MPa) | High (100 – 180 MPa) | Very High (250 MPa) |
| **Shot Capacity Range** | Small (≤ 100 g) | Small to Large | Very Large (multi-kg) |
| **Residence Time Uniformity** | Poor | Good (with check valve) | Best (separate melting) |
| **Plasticizing Rate** | Low | High | Very High |
| **Cycle Time for Large Parts** | Slow | Moderate | Fast (parallel plast. + inj.) |
| **Modern Industrial Use** | Rare / Obsolete | Dominant (> 90 %) | Specialized (large parts) |
| **Complexity / Cost** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Heat-Sensitive Polymer Suitability** | Poor | Moderate | Best (short residence) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 📊 Diagram 1: Hierarchical Classification of Injection Units

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INJECTION UNIT]:::root --> B[Plunger Ram Type]:::old
    A --> C[Screw Type]:::main
    A --> D[Hybrid Plunger plus Screw Pre-plasticizer]:::old

    B --> B1[Solid Ram driven hydraulically]:::old
    B --> B2[Torpedo or Spreader in barrel]:::old
    B --> B3[External band heaters only]:::old

    C --> C1[In Line Screw Single Stage]:::main
    C --> C2[Reciprocating Screw Most Common]:::main
    C --> C3[Two Stage Screw plus Plunger Accumulator]:::main

    C1 --> C11[Screw plastici]:::main
    C12[Separate plunger injects]:::main

    C2 --> C21[Screw rotates to melt]:::main
    C22[Screw translates to inject]:::main
    C23[Check valve at screw tip]:::main

    C3 --> C31[Extruder screw melts continuously]:::main
    C32[Accumulator stores shot]:::main
    C33[Plunger injects fast]:::main

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef main fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef old fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#b45309,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The blue path (right side) shows modern industry-dominant designs; the orange path (left side) represents legacy/obsolete designs that KTU examiners still ask about for **comparative/contrast questions**.

---

## 📊 Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Reciprocating Screw Injection Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Hopper Granule Feed]:::feed --> B[Heated Barrel 3 to 5 Zones]:::main
    B --> S[Reciprocating Screw]:::main
    S --> CV[Non Return Check Valve]:::main
    CV --> N[Nozzle]:::main
    N --> M[Mold Cavity]:::main

    HM[Hydraulic Motor]:::driver --> S
    HC[Hydraulic Cylinder]:::driver --> S

    B --> HTR1[Heater Band Zone 1]:::heat
    B --> HTR2[Heater Band Zone 2]:::heat
    B --> HTR3[Heater Band Zone 3]:::heat

    TC[Thermocouples and PID]:::ctrl --> HTR1
    TC --> HTR2
    TC --> HTR3

    classDef feed fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#065f46,color:#065f46
    classDef main fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
    classDef driver fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff
    classDef heat fill:#f97316,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctrl fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#78350f,color:#78350f
```

### Functional Flow Explained

1. **Feed Stage:** Granules enter from the **hopper** by gravity.
2. **Plasticizing Stage:** The **rotating screw** conveys, compresses, shears, and melts the polymer. **Heater bands** provide external thermal energy; **thermocouples** maintain temperature via PID control.
3. **Metering Stage:** The **metering zone** delivers homogeneous melt at constant rate behind the screw tip.
4. **Injection Stage:** Hydraulic pressure drives the screw forward; the **non-return valve** prevents back-flow; melt is forced through the **nozzle** into the **mold cavity**.

---

## 📊 Diagram 3: Screw Zone Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[FEED ZONE\nDeep Channel\nConveys Solids]:::fz --> C[COMPRESSION ZONE\nTapering Channel\nMelts and Mixes]:::cz
    C --> M[METERING ZONE\nShallow Channel\nHomogenizes Melt]:::mz
    M --> TIP[Screw Tip\nNon Return Valve\nInjection Point]:::tip

    classDef fz fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#92400e
    classDef cz fill:#fb923c,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    classDef mz fill:#ef4444,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    classDef tip fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why tapering matters:** As polymer moves from the feed to the metering zone, the channel depth reduces from $h_f$ to $h_m$. This compresses the solid bed against the barrel wall (melting) and finally establishes a melt pool in the metering zone. The ratio $h_f / h_m$ is the **Compression Ratio (CR)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a plunger type and a reciprocating screw type injection unit with respect to melt homogeneity, plasticizing mechanism, and shot capacity.**

**📗 Model Answer (3 Marks – ~80 words):**

| Criterion | Plunger Type | Reciprocating Screw Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Plasticizing Mechanism** | Conduction from heater bands and torpedo surface only | Screw rotation generates **shear + conduction**, providing internal heating |
| **Melt Homogeneity** | Poor — only thin melt film around torpedo | Excellent — screw channels mix continuously |
| **Shot Capacity** | Small (< 100 g), limited by barrel volume | Small to very large; screw stroke defines shot |

> Hence the reciprocating screw has **virtually replaced** the plunger type in modern injection molding.

*(1 mark per correctly filled row – examiner pattern)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define L/D ratio and Compression Ratio of an injection molding screw. State their typical ranges.**

**📗 Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **L/D Ratio:** Ratio of the **effective screw length** to the **outer screw diameter**.

$$
\dfrac{L}{D} = \dfrac{\text{Effective Length of Screw}}{\text{Outer Diameter of Screw}}
$$

Typical range: **18:1 to 22:1** (modern screws may go up to 25:1 for better melt homogeneity).

- **Compression Ratio (CR):** Ratio of **feed zone channel depth** to **metering zone channel depth**.

$$
CR = \dfrac{h_f}{h_m}
$$

Typical range: **2:1 to 4:1**.

*(1.5 marks each definition with values)*

---

## 📘 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**With neat sketches, classify the different types of injection units used in injection molding machines. Compare any two types with respect to (i) construction (ii) plasticizing mechanism and (iii) typical applications. Justify why the reciprocating screw injection unit is the most preferred in modern industry.**

#### (a) Classification of Injection Units (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Injection units are broadly classified into **three major categories**:

**1. Plunger (Ram) Type Injection Unit**
- Uses a solid plunger to push polymer through a heated barrel containing a torpedo.
- Plasticizing depends on **external heat conduction only**.

**2. Screw Type Injection Unit**
- **(a) In-Line Screw Type:** Screw rotates for melting; a separate plunger injects.
- **(b) Reciprocating Screw Type:** Screw itself rotates for melting and translates for injection.
- **(c) Two-Stage Screw Pre-plasticizing Type:** Separate extruder screw melts continuously into an accumulator; plunger injects.

**3. Hybrid (Plunger + Screw Pre-plasticizer)**
- Combines continuous screw melting with plunger injection.

*(3 marks for classification; 4 marks for neat sketches – examiner pattern)*

---

#### (b) Comparative Analysis of Plunger vs Reciprocating Screw (7 Marks)

| Feature | Plunger Type | Reciprocating Screw Type |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Construction** | Solid plunger + torpedo + heater bands | Helical screw inside heated barrel; check valve at tip |
| **(ii) Plasticizing Mechanism** | Conduction only — thin melt film | Screw rotation: shear + conduction → bulk melting |
| **(iii) Typical Applications** | Simple commodity products, low-precision items | Engineering plastics, thin-wall, high-precision parts, automotive |

**Justification for Reciprocating Screw Dominance (3 marks):**

The reciprocating screw is preferred because:

1. **Superior melt homogeneity** due to continuous shear mixing → consistent part quality.
2. **Shortens cycle time** — melting and injection share the same screw.
3. **Higher plasticizing rate** with self-generated shear heating → energy efficient.
4. **Better shot weight accuracy** due to precise metering in the metering zone.
5. **Compact design** reduces machine footprint and cost.

*(2 marks comparison table + 3 marks justification)*

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / 2024 Scheme Reference]`

**Explain the working of a reciprocating screw injection molding unit with a neat sketch. Derive the expressions for (i) theoretical shot capacity and (ii) plasticizing rate. A machine has screw diameter 40 mm, screw stroke 100 mm, screw speed 100 rpm, metering depth 3 mm, melt density 0.8 g/cm³, and volumetric efficiency 0.65. Calculate the shot weight and plasticizing rate. Assume square-pitch single-flight screw.**

#### (a) Working of Reciprocating Screw Injection Unit (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:** The unit consists of a **hopper**, **heated barrel**, **reciprocating screw**, **hydraulic motor**, **hydraulic cylinder**, **non-return (check) valve** at the screw tip, and a **nozzle**.

**Working — Two Phases:**

**Phase 1 — Plasticizing (Rotational Phase):**
- The screw rotates while the hydraulic cylinder allows it to **retract slowly**.
- Polymer granules are conveyed from feed → compression → metering zones.
- Shear + external heat melts the polymer; melt accumulates in front of the check valve.

**Phase 2 — Injection (Translational Phase):**
- Screw rotation stops.
- Hydraulic cylinder drives the screw forward axially.
- Check valve closes, preventing back-flow.
- Melt is forced through the nozzle into the mold cavity under high pressure.
- Pressure-hold (packing) phase follows to compensate for shrinkage.

*(3 marks for sketch + 4 marks for working)*

---

#### (b) Derivations and Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)

**Derivation of Theoretical Shot Capacity (3 marks):**

As derived in Section 3:

$$
V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_s^2 \cdot L_{stroke}
$$

**Derivation of Plasticizing Rate (2 marks):**

As derived in Section 3:

$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_s^2 \cdot L_{lead} \cdot N \cdot \rho_{melt} \cdot \eta_v
$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given:
- $D_s = 40 \text{ mm} = 4 \text{ cm}$
- $L_{stroke} = 100 \text{ mm} = 10 \text{ cm}$
- $N = 100 \text{ rpm}$
- $h_m = 3 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ cm}$ *(used for $L_{lead} \approx D_s$ alternative — here we apply the metering depth approximation per KTU convention)*
- $\rho_{melt} = 0.8 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $\eta_v = 0.65$

**Shot Volume:**

$$
V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (4)^2 \cdot 10 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 16 \cdot 10 = 40\pi \approx 125.66 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Shot Weight:**

$$
m_{shot} = V_s \cdot \rho_{melt} = 125.66 \cdot 0.8 \approx 100.53 \text{ g}
$$

**Plasticizing Rate (using metering depth approximation $L_{lead} \approx D_s$ in KTU simplified formula):**

$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_s^2 \cdot h_m \cdot N \cdot \rho_{melt} \cdot \eta_v
$$

Substituting:

$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (4)^2 \cdot (0.3) \cdot 100 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.65
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 16 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 100 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.65
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.7854 \cdot 16 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 52 = 0.7854 \cdot 16 \cdot 15.6 \approx 196.0 \text{ g/min}
$$

Converting to kg/hr:

$$
\dot{m} \approx 196 \cdot \dfrac{60}{1000} = 11.76 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Final Answer:** Shot weight ≈ **100.5 g**; Plasticizing rate ≈ **11.76 kg/hr**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Consistency:** Marks are deducted heavily if mm is mixed with cm without conversion. Always convert to a single SI unit before substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting $\eta_v$:** Students often write the geometric formula only and miss the volumetric efficiency factor — examiners allocate **dedicated marks** for $\eta_v$.
> 3. **Confusion between Screw OD ($D_s$) and Metering Depth ($h_m$):** These are two distinct dimensions — $D_s$ is the outer screw diameter; $h_m$ is the channel depth in the metering zone.
> 4. **Sketch Quality:** A labelled sketch of the reciprocating screw unit (showing hopper, barrel, screw, check valve, nozzle, hydraulic cylinder) is worth **at least 2–3 marks** in Part B; omitting the sketch costs significant marks.
> 5. **Plunger vs Screw confusion:** Examiners test whether students understand that a **torpedo is not a screw** — it is a static spreader.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 🔹 **Injection Unit** = plasticizing + injection of polymer melt; it is the **heart of an injection molding machine**.
- 🔹 **Plunger Type** uses a **solid ram + torpedo**; relies on conduction heating → **poor melt homogeneity** → largely obsolete.
- 🔹 **Reciprocating Screw** is the **industry-dominant design** — screw rotates for melting, translates for injection.
- 🔹 **In-Line Screw** uses a **separate plunger** for injection after the screw has melted.
- 🔹 **Two-Stage (Screw + Plunger Accumulator)** is used for **large shot volumes + high-speed injection**.
- 🔹 **L/D Ratio:** typically **18:1 to 22:1** for injection molding screws.
- 🔹 **Compression Ratio (CR):** typically **2:1 to 4:1**.
- 🔹 **Screw Zones:** Feed (deep) → Compression (tapering) → Metering (shallow).
- 🔹 **Key Formula 1:** $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_s^2 \cdot L_{stroke}$
- 🔹 **Key Formula 2:** $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D_s^2 \cdot L_{lead} \cdot N \cdot \rho_{melt} \cdot \eta_v$
- 🔹 **Key Formula 3:** $P_{melt} = P_{hyd} \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_{ram}}{D_s}\right)^2$ — pressure intensification principle.
- 🔹 **Non-return (check) valve** at screw tip is essential in reciprocating screws to prevent melt back-flow during injection.
- 🔹 **Shear heating** (internal) + **conduction heating** (external bands) together melt polymer efficiently in screw units.
- 🔹 Always mention **volumetric efficiency ($\eta_v = 0.4$–$0.7$)** for injection screws in numericals.
- 🔹 Two-stage units enable **parallel plasticizing + injection** → shortest cycle time for large parts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Screw
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Screw

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Screw (Extruder Screw):** A precisely machined, long, rotating metallic shaft with a helical flight (ridge) wound around its core, which is the central component of a plastics extruder. It performs **three critical functions** simultaneously: (1) conveying solid polymer pellets, (2) compressing and melting (plastication) the polymer through shear and external heating, and (3) metering (pumping) the homogeneous melt at a controlled rate and pressure toward the die.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, the screw is treated as a **transport device** that converts rotational mechanical energy into thermal energy via viscous dissipation and pressure energy for downstream shaping. Its geometry directly governs the **melt quality, output rate, and stability of the extrusion process**.

## 1.2 The Three Functional Zones of a Screw

Every conventional extrusion screw is divided into **three distinct zones** along its length:

| Zone | Length (Typical) | Function | Channel Depth Behavior |
|------|------------------|----------|------------------------|
| **Feed Zone** (Solids Conveying) | 25–30 % of L | Compacts and conveys solid pellets by friction against barrel | Deepest channel |
| **Compression / Transition Zone** (Melting) | 40–50 % of L | Melts polymer via shear + conduction; air is excluded | Gradually decreasing |
| **Metering Zone** (Melt Pumping) | 20–25 % of L | Homogenizes melt, builds die pressure, controls output | Shallowest channel |

Where **L** is the total effective screw length and **D** is the screw outside diameter. The ratio **L/D** is a standard design metric that typically ranges from **20:1 to 30:1** for single-screw extruders.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architectural Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the screw like a long spiral staircase inside a hollow pipe (the barrel):**
> - The **stairs (flight)** push the polymer upward (or forward) as the screw rotates.
> - The **steps get shallower as you go up** — that narrowing represents the **compression zone** that squeezes and melts the polymer.
> - The **landing at the top** is the **metering zone** — a narrow, controlled channel that pushes the molten polymer steadily out the door (the die).
> - If the staircase has one set of rails → **single-flight screw**; two sets → **double-flight screw**.

This analogy makes it intuitive why **channel depth must decrease** to build pressure: just like squeezing the end of a toothpaste tube forces paste out at a steady rate.

## 1.4 Standard Design Metrics (Bold)

The following metrics are the **primary design parameters** for any screw classification:

- **Outside Diameter (D)** — typically **25 mm to 300 mm**
- **L/D Ratio** — typically **20:1, 24:1, 30:1**
- **Compression Ratio (CR)** — typically **2:1 to 4:1** for thermoplastics
- **Helix Angle (θ)** — typically **17.65°** (which makes lead = D)
- **Flight Clearance** — typically **0.05 % to 0.25 % of D**
- **Pitch (t)** — axial distance between flight crests
- **Lead (s)** — axial advance per screw revolution
- **Root Diameter (D_r)** — diameter of the screw core at its deepest section

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** A screw with **Lead = D** has a helix angle of exactly **17.65°**, which is the standard "square-cut" geometry used in most commercial extruders because it gives an optimal balance between drag and thrust conveying.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Philosophy of Classification

The KTU 2024 scheme classifies screws along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **By Channel Geometry** (How compression is generated)
2. **By Flight/Thread Configuration** (Single, double, barrier, etc.)
3. **By Process Application** (Extrusion, injection, mixing, venting)

## 2.2 Classification Axis 1 — Based on Method of Compression

> [!NOTE]
> The **compression ratio (CR)** is defined as the ratio of channel volume in the **feed zone** to the channel volume in the **metering zone**, per unit axial length.

### Type A: Constant Pitch — Variable Channel Depth
- **Pitch (t)** is held constant along the entire screw length.
- Compression is achieved by **gradually reducing the root diameter (D_r)**, i.e., making the channel shallower.
- **Most common type** in industry (≈ 90 % of commercial screws).
- **Advantage:** Simple to manufacture; predictable shear distribution.
- **Disadvantage:** Shear rate spikes at the metering zone due to shallow channel.

### Type B: Variable Pitch — Constant Channel Depth
- Channel depth (h) is kept uniform.
- Compression is obtained by **decreasing the pitch (t)** along the screw.
- Reduces shear intensity in the metering zone.
- Used for **heat-sensitive polymers** (e.g., rigid PVC).

### Type C: Variable Pitch — Variable Channel Depth
- Both pitch and depth vary.
- Allows independent tuning of **compression rate** and **shear rate**.
- Used in **specialty compounding screws**.

## 2.3 Classification Axis 2 — Based on Flight Configuration

| Sub-Type | Description | Application |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Single-Flight Screw** | One continuous helical flight | Standard extrusion |
| **Double-Flight (Twin-Start)** | Two parallel helical flights | Higher output, lower shear |
| **Triple-Flight** | Three parallel flights | Very high output |
| **Barrier Flight Screw** | Secondary flight ("barrier flight") separates solid bed from melt pool | High-output, high-quality extrusion |
| **Pulsating / Pin Screw** | Pins or interruptions on the flight | Mixing enhancement |

## 2.4 Classification Axis 3 — Based on Process Application

- **Extruder Screw** — Continuous melt delivery for profile/sheet/film
- **Injection Molding Screw (Reciprocating Screw)** — Plugs, accumulates, injects
- **Mixing Screw** — Saxton, Maddock, pineapple, pin mixers
- **Venting / Two-Stage Screw** — Decompression zone for devolatilization
- **Reactive Processing Screw** — Long L/D (> 40:1) for reactive extrusion

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Critical for Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **the most frequently tested** in KTU University Exams for Module 1.

**Table 2.1 — Master Equation Sheet for Screw Geometry**

| # | Equation | Symbol Definition | Typical Units |
|---|----------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $CR = \dfrac{V_f}{V_m} = \dfrac{h_f}{h_m}$ (when D_r is constant) | $V_f, V_m$ = Feed/Meter channel vol; $h_f, h_m$ = feed/meter depth | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $CR = \dfrac{D^2 - h_f^2}{D^2 - h_m^2}$ (general form) | D = outer diameter; h = channel depth | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $s = \pi D \tan(\theta)$ | s = lead; D = screw OD; θ = helix angle | mm, degrees |
| 4 | $t = s / N_{starts}$ | t = pitch; N = number of flight starts | mm |
| 5 | $Q_{drag} = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N d \sin(\theta) \cos(\theta)$ | Q = drag flow; N = rpm; d = channel depth | m³/s |
| 6 | $Q_{pressure} = -\dfrac{d^3 \sin^2(\theta)}{12 \eta} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L_m}$ | ΔP = pressure rise; η = melt viscosity; L_m = metering length | m³/s |
| 7 | $Q_{net} = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure}$ | Net output of extruder | m³/s |
| 8 | $\dot{\gamma}_{apparent} = \dfrac{\pi D N}{h}$ | Apparent wall shear rate | s⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\tau_w = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Wall shear stress | Pa |
| 10 | $\dot{W}_{visc} = \tau_w \cdot \dot{\gamma} \cdot V_{meter}$ | Viscous dissipation rate | W |

**Boundary Conditions and Design Limits:**

- $17.65° \leq \theta \leq 20°$ (Standard extruder range)
- $2 \leq CR \leq 5$ (Thermoplastics)
- $1.05 \leq CR \leq 1.5$ (Heat-sensitive / thermosets)
- $20 \leq L/D \leq 30$ (Single-stage); up to **44:1** for two-stage venting

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- A **High-CR (4–5)** screw is chosen for **polyolefins** (PE, PP) to ensure complete melting.
- A **Low-CR (1.05–1.5)** screw is mandatory for **PVC** to avoid thermal degradation and HCl release.
- **Barrier screws** dominate in **film and pipe extrusion** where melt homogeneity is critical.
- **Two-stage venting screws** are essential for processing **recycled or moisture-laden polymers**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *"CR is high for Happy Polymers (PE/PP); CR is Low for Sensitive (PVC/PS)."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Compression Ratio (General Form)

The **compression ratio (CR)** is the single most tested derivation in this module. We derive it rigorously.

**Geometric Setup:**

Consider a single-flight screw with outer diameter **D** and root diameter **D_r**. The channel cross-section is an annulus, and per axial length **dx**, the channel volume per turn is:

$$
dV = \left(\dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)\left(D^2 - D_r^2\right) \cdot t
$$

Where **t** is the pitch (axial advance per turn). The channel depth **h** is defined as:

$$
h = \dfrac{D - D_r}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_r = D - 2h
$$

**Step 1 — Express Volume in Terms of Channel Depth:**

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left[D^2 - (D - 2h)^2\right] \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the Binomial:**

$$
(D - 2h)^2 = D^2 - 4Dh + 4h^2
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Simplify:**

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left[D^2 - D^2 + 4Dh - 4h^2\right] \cdot t = \pi t h (D - h)
$$

This is the **fundamental channel volume equation** for a single-flight screw. Note the elegant simplification: $V = \pi t h (D - h)$.

**Step 4 — Compute Compression Ratio (Feed ÷ Meter):**

$$
CR = \dfrac{V_{feed}}{V_{meter}} = \dfrac{\pi t_f h_f (D - h_f)}{\pi t_m h_m (D - h_m)}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Constant-Pitch Condition ($t_f = t_m = t$):**

$$
CR = \dfrac{h_f (D - h_f)}{h_m (D - h_m)}
$$

**Step 6 — Special Case (Shallow Channels, $h \ll D$):**

When $h \ll D$, the term $(D - h) \approx D$, giving the much-simplified:

$$
CR \approx \dfrac{h_f}{h_m}
$$

This is the **classic result** memorized by students. It is valid when channel depth is small compared to the screw diameter.

**Step 7 — Numerical Verification:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (KTU Typical):** A **60 mm** screw has a feed depth of **7.5 mm** and a meter depth of **2.5 mm**.

**Exact CR:**
$$
CR = \dfrac{7.5 \times (60 - 7.5)}{2.5 \times (60 - 2.5)} = \dfrac{7.5 \times 52.5}{2.5 \times 57.5} = \dfrac{393.75}{143.75} = 2.74
$$

**Approximate CR (shallow channel):**
$$
CR_{approx} = \dfrac{7.5}{2.5} = 3.00
$$

**Percentage Error:**
$$
\% \, \text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert 3.00 - 2.74 \vert}{2.74} \times 100 = 9.49 \, \%
$$

**Conclusion:** For typical screws where $h_f/D > 0.10$, the **exact formula** must be used for accurate exam answers.

## 3.2 Derivation of Helix Angle from Lead and Diameter

The helix angle **θ** is the angle between the flight and a plane perpendicular to the screw axis. Given a screw of outer diameter **D** and axial lead **s**:

**Step 1 — Unroll One Turn of the Flight:**

Imagine cutting the screw cylinder along an axial line and unrolling it. The flight becomes a straight line forming a right triangle with base = πD (circumference) and height = s (axial advance per turn).

**Step 2 — Apply Trigonometry:**

$$
\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \dfrac{s}{\pi D}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Helix Angle:**

$$
\theta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{s}{\pi D}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Lead:**

$$
s = \pi D \tan(\theta)
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Verification:**

For a **D = 45 mm** screw with the standard square-cut geometry (s = D = 45 mm):
$$
\theta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{45}{\pi \times 45}\right) = \arctan\left(\dfrac{1}{\pi}\right) = \arctan(0.3183) = 17.65°
$$

**Confirmed — the standard helix angle of 17.65° yields lead = D.**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Screw Type

| Polymer | Recommended CR | Helix Angle θ | L/D | Special Features |
|---------|----------------|---------------|-----|------------------|
| **HDPE / LDPE** | 3.5 – 4.5 | 17.65° | 24–30 | Standard single-flight |
| **PP (Polypropylene)** | 3.0 – 4.0 | 17.65° | 24–30 | Barrier flight optional |
| **PVC (Rigid)** | 1.5 – 2.5 | 17.65° | 22–24 | Bimodal, low shear |
| **PS / HIPS** | 2.0 – 3.0 | 17.65° | 20–24 | No barrier |
| **Nylon (PA 6,6)** | 3.0 – 3.5 | 17.65° | 24–28 | Dry vent required |
| **PMMA / Acrylics** | 2.5 – 3.0 | 20° | 20–24 | Variable pitch preferred |
| **Heat-Sensitive (CPVC)** | 1.05 – 1.5 | 17.65° | 22–26 | Constant depth, variable pitch |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[EXTRUDER SCREW] --> B[Classification by Compression]
    A --> C[Classification by Flights]
    A --> D[Classification by Application]
    A --> E[Classification by L over D Ratio]

    B --> B1[Constant Pitch Variable Depth]
    B --> B2[Constant Depth Variable Pitch]
    B --> B3[Variable Pitch Variable Depth]
    B --> B4[Barrier Flight Screw]

    C --> C1[Single Flight]
    C --> C2[Double Flight Twin Start]
    C --> C3[Multi Flight]
    C --> C4[Pin Mixer Screw]

    D --> D1[Extrusion Screw]
    D --> D2[Reciprocating IM Screw]
    D --> D3[Two Stage Venting Screw]
    D --> D4[Reactive Processing Screw]

    E --> E1[Short L over D 20 to 1]
    E --> E2[Standard L over D 24 to 1]
    E --> E3[Long L over D 30 to 1]
    E --> E4[Very Long L over D 40 to 1 plus]

    B1 --> B1a[Most Common in Industry]
    B1 --> B1b[CR controlled by root diameter]

    B4 --> B4a[Separates melt from solids]
    B4 --> B4b[High output film extrusion]

    D2 --> D2a[Acts as plunger]
    D2 --> D2b[Plasticizing plus injection]

    D3 --> D3a[Decompression zone present]
    D3 --> D3b[Used for devolatilization]
```

## 4.2 Screw Functional Zones — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BARREL[BARREL]
        direction LR
        F[FEED ZONE\nDeep Channel\nSolid Conveying] --> C[COMPRESSION ZONE\nTapered Channel\nMelting plus Mixing]
        C --> M[METERING ZONE\nShallow Channel\nMelt Pumping]
        M --> D[DIE\nShape Forming]
    end

    subgraph HOPPER[HOPPER INPUT]
        H[Pellets] --> F
    end

    subgraph DRIVE[DRIVE SYSTEM]
        MOTOR[Electric Motor] --> GB[Gearbox] --> SCREW[Screw Shaft]
        SCREW -.rotates.-> F
    end

    subgraph CONTROLS[THERMAL CONTROLS]
        HTR1[Heater Band 1] -.-> F
        HTR2[Heater Band 2] -.-> C
        HTR3[Heater Band 3] -.-> M
    end
```

## 4.3 Geometry Annotation Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    SCR[Screw Cross Section] --> OD[Outside Diameter D]
    SCR --> RD[Root Diameter D_r]
    SCR --> CH[Channel Depth h]
    SCR --> FW[Flight Width e]
    SCR --> CLR[Barrel Clearance delta]

    SCR --> LEAD[Lead s equals pi D tan theta]
    SCR --> PITCH[Pitch t equals s divided by N starts]
    SCR --> HA[Helix Angle theta equals 17.65 degrees standard]
    SCR --> CR[Compression Ratio equals V_feed divided by V_meter]
```

## 4.4 Compression-Generation Methods Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[COMPRESSION GENERATION METHODS] --> B[Method 1: Variable Root Diameter]
    A --> C[Method 2: Variable Pitch]
    A --> D[Method 3: Variable Flight Angle]

    B --> B1[Constant t Constant theta Varying D_r]
    B --> B2[Most widely used]
    B --> B3[Simple taper profile]

    C --> C1[Constant h Constant D_r Varying t]
    C --> C2[Reduces shear in metering]
    C --> C3[Used for heat sensitive PVC]

    D --> D1[Constant h Constant t Varying theta]
    D --> D2[Uncommon but precise]
    D --> D3[Specialty compounding only]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading Tip for Students:** When revising, always trace a polymer's path from the **HOPPER → FEED → COMPRESSION → METER → DIE** diagram above. This single mental map covers **80 % of descriptive questions** in the KTU exam.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define the following terms with respect to an extruder screw:**
- **(a)** Compression Ratio
- **(b)** L/D Ratio
- **(c)** Helix Angle

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(a) Compression Ratio (1 mark):** It is the ratio of the volume of the feed channel to the volume of the metering channel, calculated per screw turn.

$$
CR = \dfrac{V_{feed}}{V_{meter}} = \dfrac{h_f (D - h_f)}{h_m (D - h_m)}
$$

**(b) L/D Ratio (1 mark):** The ratio of the effective screw length (L) to the screw outside diameter (D). It indicates the residence time and melting capacity. Typical value: **24:1**.

**(c) Helix Angle (1 mark):** The angle made by the flight with a plane perpendicular to the screw axis. For standard commercial screws, **θ = 17.65°**, which makes the lead numerically equal to the screw diameter.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Differentiate between a single-flight screw and a double-flight screw in terms of construction, shear, and output.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Single-Flight Screw | Double-Flight Screw |
|---------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Construction** | One continuous helical flight along the core | Two parallel helical flights, offset by 180° |
| **Shear Rate** | High (longer residence per turn) | Lower (higher output per revolution) |
| **Output** | Lower | ≈ 1.6 to 1.8 × higher |
| **Application** | General purpose | High-output extrusion, thin films |
| **Mixing** | Moderate | Better distributive mixing |

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Classify extruder screws based on:
  - (i) Method of generating compression (3 marks)
  - (ii) Flight configuration (2 marks)
  - (iii) Process application (2 marks)

**(b)** A **45 mm diameter** single-screw extruder has:
  - Feed channel depth: **8.0 mm**
  - Metering channel depth: **3.0 mm**
  - Screw rpm: **80**
  - Helix angle: **17.65°**
  
  Calculate:
  - (i) Exact Compression Ratio (3 marks)
  - (ii) Lead of the screw (2 marks)
  - (iii) Drag flow rate (assuming metering zone only) (2 marks)

### Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **three functional zones** of a conventional extrusion screw and state the design equations governing each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A two-stage venting screw has an **L/D of 36:1**. The first stage has CR = 3.0 and the second stage has CR = 1.5. Justify the design and explain the role of the **venting zone**. (7 marks)

---

### DETAILED MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Classification (7 marks)

**(i) Based on Method of Generating Compression (3 marks):**

**Type 1 — Constant Pitch, Variable Depth (1 mark):**
Pitch is held constant; channel depth decreases along the screw by increasing the root diameter. This is the **most common industrial design** because it gives a linear taper profile, easy machining, and predictable shear. The compression ratio is governed solely by the channel depth ratio.

**Type 2 — Constant Depth, Variable Pitch (1 mark):**
Channel depth is held constant; pitch decreases from feed to meter. This generates compression without increasing shear in the metering zone. It is preferred for **heat-sensitive polymers** like rigid PVC where excessive shear causes degradation.

**Type 3 — Variable Depth and Variable Pitch (1 mark):**
Both parameters vary along the screw. It offers maximum design flexibility but is **expensive to manufacture** and used only in specialty compounding screws.

**(ii) Based on Flight Configuration (2 marks):**

- **Single-flight screw:** One continuous helical flight — general purpose, simple.
- **Multi-flight screw (double/triple):** Multiple parallel flights — higher output at lower rpm.
- **Barrier flight screw:** A secondary flight (barrier) separates the solid bed from the melt pool, dramatically improving melting efficiency.
- **Pulsating / pin screw:** Pins interrupt the flow for enhanced distributive mixing.

**(iii) Based on Process Application (2 marks):**

- **Extrusion screw:** Continuous flow, metering-dominant.
- **Injection molding (reciprocating) screw:** Acts as both plasticator and plunger.
- **Venting (two-stage) screw:** Has a decompression zone for devolatilization.
- **Mixing screws:** Saxton, Maddock, pineapple — incorporate dedicated mixing sections.
- **Reactive processing screw:** Very high L/D (> 40:1) for chemical reactions in the melt.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 45 \text{ mm}$, $h_f = 8.0 \text{ mm}$, $h_m = 3.0 \text{ mm}$, $N = 80 \text{ rpm}$, $\theta = 17.65°$

**(i) Exact Compression Ratio (3 marks):**

Using the general formula:
$$
CR = \dfrac{h_f (D - h_f)}{h_m (D - h_m)}
$$

Substituting:
$$
CR = \dfrac{8.0 \times (45 - 8.0)}{3.0 \times (45 - 3.0)} = \dfrac{8.0 \times 37.0}{3.0 \times 42.0} = \dfrac{296}{126}
$$

$$
\boxed{CR = 2.35}
$$

**Comment:** Since $h_f/D = 0.178 > 0.10$, we cannot use the approximate formula $CR = h_f/h_m$. (1 mark for correct identification of formula choice.)

**(ii) Lead of the Screw (2 marks):**

$$
s = \pi D \tan(\theta) = \pi \times 45 \times \tan(17.65°)
$$

$$
\tan(17.65°) = 0.3183
$$

$$
s = \pi \times 45 \times 0.3183 = 3.1416 \times 14.32
$$

$$
\boxed{s = 45.0 \text{ mm}}
$$

This confirms the **square-cut geometry**: for θ = 17.65°, lead equals the diameter.

**(iii) Drag Flow Rate (2 marks):**

The drag flow equation for the metering zone:
$$
Q_{drag} = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N d \sin(\theta) \cos(\theta)
$$

Where $d = h_m = 3.0 \text{ mm} = 0.003 \text{ m}$, $N = 80/60 = 1.333 \text{ rev/s}$.

$$
\sin(17.65°) = 0.3032, \quad \cos(17.65°) = 0.9529
$$

$$
Q_{drag} = 0.5 \times \pi^2 \times (0.045)^2 \times 1.333 \times 0.003 \times 0.3032 \times 0.9529
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:
- $\pi^2 = 9.8696$
- $(0.045)^2 = 0.002025$
- $0.5 \times 9.8696 \times 0.002025 = 0.009993$
- $0.009993 \times 1.333 = 0.013320$
- $0.013320 \times 0.003 = 3.996 \times 10^{-5}$
- $0.3032 \times 0.9529 = 0.2889$
- $3.996 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.2889 = 1.155 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

$$
\boxed{Q_{drag} = 1.155 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 41.6 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}}
$$

---

### DETAILED MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Three Functional Zones (7 marks)

**Diagram:**
```
[========HOPPER========]
   |
   |_____FEED ZONE (L_f = 25-30% L)_______|  h = h_f (max)
   |
   |___COMPRESSION/MELTING ZONE (L_c = 40-50% L)___|  h : h_f → h_m
   |
   |__METERING ZONE (L_m = 20-25% L)__|  h = h_m (min)
   |
   [===========DIE==========]
```

**Description:**

**Feed Zone — Solids Conveying (2 marks):**
The polymer pellets are conveyed forward by **friction drag** between the barrel and pellets. A higher barrel friction and lower screw-root friction promote forward movement. The governing equation for solids conveying rate:
$$
G_s = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N d_f \sin(\theta) \cos(\theta) \cdot \rho_{bulk}
$$

Where $\rho_{bulk}$ is the bulk density of the pellets.

**Compression Zone — Melting (3 marks):**
Polymer is melted by a combination of **conduction** (from the heated barrel) and **viscous shear dissipation** from the rotating flight. The **Tadmor Model** treats the melting as a continuous process with a solid bed of thickness $X$ decreasing along the screw. The melting rate is given by:
$$
\omega_m = \left(\dfrac{V_{bx} \cdot X \cdot \rho_s \cdot \lambda \cdot k}{2 \Delta T}\right)^{0.5} \cdot \sqrt{W_{channel}}
$$

Where $\lambda$ is the heat of fusion, k is thermal conductivity, $\Delta T$ is temperature difference, and $W_{channel}$ is the channel width.

**Metering Zone — Melt Pumping (2 marks):**
The melt behaves as a **non-Newtonian power-law fluid**. The drag flow is:
$$
Q_{drag} = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N d_m \sin(\theta) \cos(\theta)}{2}
$$

The pressure flow (opposing) is:
$$
Q_{pressure} = \dfrac{d_m^3 \sin^2(\theta) \Delta P}{12 \eta L_m}
$$

Net output: $Q_{net} = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure}$.

---

#### Part (b) — Two-Stage Venting Screw (7 marks)

**Justification for L/D = 36:1 (3 marks):**

A **two-stage venting screw** consists of two metering zones separated by a **decompression (vent) zone**. The total L/D must accommodate:
- First metering zone: L/D ≈ 12:1
- Decompression zone: L/D ≈ 4:1
- Second metering zone: L/D ≈ 12:1
- Plus feed and compression: L/D ≈ 8:1

Total: $12 + 4 + 12 + 8 = 36:1$. **Hence the design is justified.**

**Stage-wise Analysis (2 marks):**

- **Stage 1 (CR = 3.0):** Melts and pressurizes the polymer. The high CR ensures complete melting of the incoming polymer.
- **Vent Zone (deep channel):** Sudden expansion drops pressure below atmospheric. **Volatiles (moisture, monomers, solvents) escape** through the vent port.
- **Stage 2 (CR = 1.5):** Re-pressurizes the devolatilized melt to deliver stable output to the die.

**Role of the Venting Zone (2 marks):**
- Removes moisture, air, and trapped volatiles that would otherwise cause **die-lip drool, bubbles, or surface defects**.
- Essential for **recycled polymers, hygroscopic materials (nylon, PC, PMMA), and emulsion PVC**.
- Prevents **hydrolytic degradation** during processing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong Formula Choice:** Using $CR = h_f/h_m$ when $h_f/D > 0.10$ loses **2 of 3 marks**. Always default to the exact formula $CR = h_f(D - h_f) / [h_m(D - h_m)]$ unless the question explicitly says "shallow channel."
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Drag Flow:** $Q_{drag}$ must be in m³/s — students often mix cm³/s without unit conversion, losing **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing Pitch and Lead:** Pitch is axial distance per *thread*; Lead is axial advance per *revolution*. For single-start screws they are equal, but the examiner will deduct marks for not stating this distinction.
> 4. **Skipping Diagram in Zone Description:** A 7-mark "explain the zones" question without a labeled sketch loses up to **2 marks** automatically.
> 5. **Mixing L/D with Compression Ratio:** L/D governs residence time; CR governs compression. They are independent design parameters.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision**

- **Screw = helical flight on a rotating shaft** inside a heated barrel.
- **Three Zones:** Feed (solid convey) → Compression (melt) → Meter (pump).
- **L/D Ratio:** Range 20:1 to 30:1 for single-stage; up to 44:1 for two-stage venting.
- **Compression Ratio (CR):** $CR = V_{feed}/V_{meter}$.
  - Exact: $CR = h_f(D - h_f) / [h_m(D - h_m)]$
  - Approximate (shallow channel): $CR = h_f/h_m$
- **Classification by Compression Method:** Constant Pitch Variable Depth (most common) | Constant Depth Variable Pitch (PVC) | Variable Both (specialty).
- **Classification by Flights:** Single | Double | Multi | Barrier | Pin.
- **Standard Helix Angle:** $\theta = 17.65°$ (square-cut, s = D).
- **Lead:** $s = \pi D \tan(\theta)$.
- **Pitch:** $t = s / N_{starts}$.
- **Drag Flow:** $Q_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N d \sin\theta \cos\theta$.
- **Pressure Flow:** $Q_{pressure} = d^3 \sin^2\theta \cdot \Delta P / (12\eta L)$.
- **Net Output:** $Q_{net} = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure}$.
- **Apparent Shear Rate:** $\dot\gamma = \pi D N / h$.
- **High CR (3.5–5)** for PE/PP; **Low CR (1.05–2.5)** for PVC/CPVC.
- **Barrier screws** separate melt from solid bed → high output + good melt quality.
- **Two-stage venting screws** have decompression zone for devolatilization.
- **Reciprocating screw** used in injection molding — also acts as plunger.
- **Saxton, Maddock, Pineapple mixers** are common mixing-screw variants.

**Memory Hook:** *"Feed depth high, Meter depth low — that's how polymers flow."*

---

**End of Notes — Classification of Screw (Module 1, PCPOT503)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Screw Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Screw Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In polymer extrusion technology, the **Extruder Screw** is the single most critical rotating component mounted inside the barrel of a single-screw extruder. It serves four simultaneous engineering functions: **solids conveying, plasticating (melting), melt conveying (metering/pumping), and devolatilization/mixing**. Screw design, therefore, is the systematic engineering exercise of determining the screw's geometric parameters—diameter ($D$), length ($L$), length-to-diameter ratio ($L/D$), flight depths ($H$), helix angle ($\theta$), lead ($S$), flight width ($W$), and compression ratio ($CR$)—to achieve the required throughput, melt quality, melt temperature uniformity, and pressure development for a given polymer and downstream die.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PCPOT503, Module 1):** *Screw design – features of conventional screws, screw geometry, screw speed, channel depth in feed and metering zones, compression ratio, barrel-screw clearances, barrier screws, and effect of screw design on output.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Architectural Analogy"

Think of an extruder screw as a **self-propelled Archimedes screw that also cooks the material as it travels**. Picture a long, rotating metal "auger" inside a heated cylindrical tube (barrel). Polymer granules enter one end like dry rice entering a corkscrew conveyor; the rotating screw *drags* them forward against the barrel wall, compresses them, melts them under combined heat and shear, and pumps the resulting molten plastic out the other end at high, steady pressure—much like a sausage stuffer, but capable of generating **>35 MPa** of die pressure with melt temperature precision of $\pm 2\,^{\circ}\text{C}$.

If the screw is the *heart* of the extruder, the **screw geometry is the genetic code**—it dictates how the polymer behaves at every millimeter of its journey.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metrics (KTU Board Favourites):**
> - Typical **L/D** for single-screw extruders: **20 to 30** (modern high-performance: 36–40)
> - Typical **compression ratio (CR)**: **2.0 to 4.0**
> - Standard **helix angle ($\theta$)**: **17.7°** (corresponds to $S = D$, i.e., square pitch)
> - Screw speeds (**N**): **20 to 150 rpm** (high-speed: up to 300–600 rpm)
> - Clearance between screw flight tip and barrel: **0.05 to 0.13 mm** (per side, for $D$ ≤ 63 mm)

## 1.3 Classification of Extruder Screws

| Type | Geometry | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Conventional (metering) screw** | Constant lead, constant OD, varying channel depth | General-purpose polyolefins, PVC |
| **Barrier flight screw** (e.g., Maddock) | Auxiliary barrier flight separates melt from solids | Polyolefins, high-output lines |
| **Mixing screws** (Saxton, pineapple, pin) | Disruptive elements in metering zone | Colour masterbatch, fillers, alloys |
| **Two-stage screws** | Vacuum port between two metering zones | Devolatilization, PET sheet |
| **Grooved-feed (high-performance) screws** | Axial grooves in feed zone of barrel | High-output polyolefin extrusion |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 2024:** Always state that the *conventional metering screw* has three geometrically distinct zones: **Feed (solids conveying) zone → Compression (melting/plasticating) zone → Metering (melt-pumping) zone**, occupying typically **50%, 25–30%, and 20–25%** of total screw length respectively.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Screw Geometry — Parametric Breakdown

The screw is a helical surface wound around a cylindrical core. Unwrapping one flight produces a **rectangular channel** of width $W_c$ and depth $H$, oriented at the helix angle $\theta$ relative to the plane perpendicular to the screw axis.

### Core Geometric Parameters

1. **Screw Diameter ($D$)** — outer (major) diameter of the screw root-to-tip. Determines machine capacity.
2. **Screw Length ($L$)** — total axial length. Combined with $D$ into the $L/D$ ratio, which governs residence time, melting capacity, and mixing.
3. **Length-to-Diameter Ratio ($L/D$)** — dimensionless number; the single most quoted extruder specification. $L/D$ values:
   - 20: short, low-residence, simple profile extrusion
   - 24–28: general purpose
   - 30–36: high-output, compounding
4. **Channel Depth ($H$)** — radial distance from screw root to barrel inner wall. Varies axially along the screw:
   - $H_f$ = channel depth in the **feed zone** (deep, typically $0.10D$–$0.15D$)
   - $H_m$ = channel depth in the **metering zone** (shallow, typically $0.02D$–$0.06D$)
5. **Flight Width ($W$)** — the helical ridge that confines the polymer. The axial (projected) width is conventionally denoted $e$.
6. **Lead ($S$)** — axial advance per turn of the flight. For a *square-pitch* (single-flighted) screw, $S = D$.
7. **Helix Angle ($\theta$)** — angle between the flight and the plane normal to the screw axis.
8. **Compression Ratio ($CR$)** — ratio of feed-zone channel volume to metering-zone channel volume. Two equivalent definitions:
   - Volume-based: $CR = \dfrac{V_f}{V_m}$
   - Depth-based (for constant lead and constant flight width): $CR = \dfrac{H_f}{H_m}$

### Why These Parameters Matter — The "How" Behind Each Choice

- **Higher $L/D$** → longer residence time → better melting and mixing → lower specific energy, but more torque demand and increased screw deflection.
- **Higher $CR$** → deeper feed zone relative to metering zone → accommodates the high bulk density of solid granules, compacts them progressively, and develops a homogeneous melt at the metering end. For low-bulk-density polymers (e.g., PP fluff), higher $CR$ is preferred; for shear-sensitive polymers (e.g., rigid PVC), lower $CR$ ($\approx 2.0$–$2.5$) avoids degradation.
- **Shallower metering depth $H_m$** → higher shear rate → higher melt pressure but also higher melt temperature; risk of degradation for heat-sensitive polymers.
- **Greater helix angle $\theta$** → more efficient solids conveying but reduces axial thrust and lowers the forward axial component of drag flow.
- **Larger flight clearance ($\delta$)** → more leakage flow (back-flow) → reduced output and increased residence-time dispersion.

## 2.2 The Three Functional Zones — Mechanism of Operation

| Zone | Length (typical) | Function | Governing Physics |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Feed (Solids Conveying)** | 50% of $L$ | Compacts and conveys solid granules | Friction (solid–solid vs. solid–metal) |
| **Compression (Melting/Plasticating)** | 25–30% of $L$ | Melts polymer via conduction + shear | Tadmor's melting model (drag-induced melt pool) |
| **Metering (Melt Pumping)** | 20–25% of $L$ | Homogenizes and pumps melt at constant pressure | Viscous drag + pressure flow in a shallow channel |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations below are **universally tested** in KTU university examinations on PCPOT503. Memorize the symbols, the units, and the limiting-case behaviour.

| # | Equation | Symbol Legend | Units / Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $S = \pi D \tan\theta$ | $S$: lead; $D$: OD; $\theta$: helix angle | $S$ in m, $D$ in m |
| 2 | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{S}{\pi D}\right)$ | — | radians or degrees |
| 3 | $CR = \dfrac{H_f}{H_m}$ | $H_f$, $H_m$: feed & metering depths | dimensionless |
| 4 | $CR = \dfrac{V_f}{V_m} = \dfrac{\text{feed ch. volume}}{\text{metering ch. volume}}$ | Volume basis | dimensionless |
| 5 | Channel width (developed): $W_c = \dfrac{S \cos\theta - e}{\cos\theta}$ | $e$: axial flight width | For $e \ll S$, $W_c \approx S$ |
| 6 | **Drag flow:** $Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta$ | $N$: screw speed; $H$: local depth | $Q_d$ in m³/s |
| 7 | **Pressure flow:** $Q_p = -\dfrac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta}{12 \eta}\, \Delta P$ | $\eta$: melt viscosity; $\Delta P$: pressure rise | $Q_p$ opposes $Q_d$ |
| 8 | **Net (Newtonian) throughput:** $Q = Q_d - Q_p$ | Total extrusion rate | m³/s |
| 9 | **Leakage flow (flight clearance):** $Q_L = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N \delta^2 \tan\theta}{2 E}$ | $\delta$: radial flight clearance; $E$: flight width perpendicular to flight | Often neglected for first design |
| 10 | **Power required (approx.):** $P = Q \cdot \Delta P$ | $Q$ in m³/s; $\Delta P$ in Pa | Watts (W) |
| 11 | **Taper line equation (tapered compression zone):** $\dfrac{H(z)}{D} = A - B z$ | Linear taper; $z$: axial coordinate | $A$, $B$ chosen from $H_f$ and $H_m$ |
| 12 | **Specific energy:** $E_{sp} = \dfrac{P}{Q \rho}$ | $\rho$: melt density | J/kg |
| 13 | **Shear rate in metering zone (approx.):** $\dot\gamma = \dfrac{\pi D N}{H_m}$ | High-shear corner | s⁻¹ |
| 14 | **Residence time (approx.):** $\bar{t} = \dfrac{V_{free}}{Q} \approx \dfrac{\pi D L H_m \cos\theta}{Q}$ | Plug-flow estimate | seconds |

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Substitutions in KTU Problems:**
> - $1\,\text{rpm} = \dfrac{2\pi}{60}\,\text{rad/s} \approx 0.1047\,\text{rad/s}$
> - Viscosity $\eta$ of typical polyolefin melt at $200\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $\dot\gamma \approx 100\,\text{s}^{-1}$: $\eta \approx 500$–$1500\,\text{Pa\cdot s}$.
> - **Limiting case:** If die is wide-open ($\Delta P = 0$), $Q = Q_d$ (pure drag flow). If die is closed ($\Delta P \to \infty$), $Q \to 0$ (no net output).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Screw design directly controls:
- **Product quality**: melt temperature uniformity, gel content, colour homogeneity.
- **Throughput**: a $63\,\text{mm}$ extruder with $L/D = 24$ and $N = 100\,\text{rpm}$ typically delivers **50–200 kg/h** of LDPE.
- **Energy efficiency**: poor design wastes 20–40% of motor input as heat that must be removed.
- **Polymer versatility**: a properly designed screw can process HDPE, LDPE, PP, PS, PA, and PET with only minor geometric adjustments—L/D and CR are the primary tuning variables.

> [!NOTE]
> The screw design equations above are *first-order Newtonian* approximations. For *rigorous* non-Newtonian (power-law) analysis, replace $\eta$ with the apparent viscosity $\eta_a = K \dot\gamma^{n-1}$ where $K$ is the consistency index and $n$ is the power-law index (typical $n$ for polymer melts: 0.3–0.6).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Helix Angle from Lead and Screw Diameter

**Statement:** For a single-flighted screw, the helix angle $\theta$ at the screw root (or at the OD, geometrically identical) is the angle the flight makes with the plane normal to the screw axis.

**Setup:** Unwrap one complete flight of the screw onto a flat plane. The horizontal (circumferential) distance covered in one turn is the circumference $\pi D$. The axial advance in one turn is the lead $S$. The flight now forms the hypotenuse of a right-angled triangle with legs $\pi D$ (circumference) and $S$ (axial).

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{By definition of }\tan\theta &= \frac{\text{Opposite side (axial lead)}}{\text{Adjacent side (circumference)}} \\[4pt]
\tan\theta &= \frac{S}{\pi D} \\[6pt]
\therefore \theta &= \arctan\!\left(\frac{S}{\pi D}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical illustration (KTU-style):**
Given $D = 60\,\text{mm}$ and $S = 60\,\text{mm}$ (square pitch):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\theta &= \frac{60}{\pi \times 60} = \frac{1}{\pi} = 0.3183 \\[4pt]
\theta &= \arctan(0.3183) = 17.66^{\circ} \approx 17.7^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The conventional $17.7^{\circ}$ helix angle is a *compromise* — it maximizes the axial component of drag flow for a *square-pitch* screw ($S = D$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Compression Ratio from Channel Depths

**Statement:** For a screw with constant lead $S$, constant flight width $e$, and constant outer diameter $D$, the compression ratio equals the ratio of feed-zone channel depth to metering-zone channel depth.

**Setup:** The volume of the helical channel in any zone is (per unit length):

$$
V_{\text{channel}} = \pi D \, H \cos\theta - \text{volume occupied by flight core}
$$

For the conventional (ignoring flight core volume, which is constant along the length for a uniform flight cross-section), the volume per unit length is proportional to $H$. Hence:

$$
\frac{V_f}{V_m} = \frac{H_f}{H_m}
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{V_f}{V_m} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi D H_f \cos\theta}{\pi D H_m \cos\theta} \quad \text{(per unit length, $D$ and $\theta$ cancel)} \\[6pt]
\therefore CR &= \frac{H_f}{H_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical illustration:**
$H_f = 8\,\text{mm}$, $H_m = 3\,\text{mm}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{8}{3} = 2.67
\end{aligned}
$$

This lies in the typical 2.0–4.0 design range and suits general-purpose polyolefin processing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Net Throughput in the Metering Zone (Newtonian Melt)

**Statement:** In the metering zone, the polymer behaves as a highly viscous Newtonian fluid. The total axial output is the sum of two superimposed flows in the unwrapped rectangular channel: a **drag flow** driven by relative motion of the barrel over the stationary screw, and a **pressure flow** driven by the pressure gradient $\Delta P/L$ that opposes the drag flow.

**Setup:** Unwrap the metering zone channel of width $W_c$ and depth $H_m$. Set up a 2-D Cartesian frame: $x$ along the channel (down-channel direction), $y$ across the depth (from screw root at $y=0$ to barrel at $y=H_m$).

The barrel moves at velocity $V_b = \pi D N$ relative to the stationary screw, with a down-channel component $V_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ and a cross-channel component $V_{by} = \pi D N \sin\theta$.

**Governing equation:** Steady, fully-developed, laminar, incompressible Newtonian flow. Continuity: $\dfrac{\partial v_x}{\partial x} = 0$ → $v_x = v_x(y)$ only. Momentum (x-direction):

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \eta \frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2}
$$

**Step-by-step solution:**

Integrate twice with boundary conditions $v_x(0)=0$ (no-slip at screw root) and $v_x(H_m)=V_{bx}$ (no-slip at barrel):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2} &= \frac{1}{\eta}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = \frac{\Delta P}{\eta L_m} \quad \text{(constant pressure gradient)} \\[4pt]
\frac{dv_x}{dy} &= \frac{\Delta P}{\eta L_m} y + C_1 \\[4pt]
v_x(y) &= \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L_m} y^2 + C_1 y + C_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply $v_x(0)=0$ → $C_2 = 0$. Apply $v_x(H_m) = V_{bx}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bx} &= \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L_m} H_m^2 + C_1 H_m \\[4pt]
C_1 &= \frac{V_{bx}}{H_m} - \frac{\Delta P \, H_m}{2\eta L_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
v_x(y) = V_{bx} \frac{y}{H_m} - \frac{\Delta P \, y}{2\eta L_m}\,(H_m - y)
$$

**Volumetric flow rate (per unit width) — integrate over depth:**

$$
q = \int_0^{H_m} v_x(y)\, dy
$$

Substituting and evaluating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \int_0^{H_m} \left[V_{bx} \frac{y}{H_m} - \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L_m}\,y(H_m - y)\right] dy \\[4pt]
&= \frac{V_{bx} H_m}{2} - \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L_m}\int_0^{H_m}(H_m y - y^2)\, dy \\[4pt]
&= \frac{V_{bx} H_m}{2} - \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L_m}\!\left[\frac{H_m^3}{2} - \frac{H_m^3}{3}\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{V_{bx} H_m}{2} - \frac{\Delta P H_m^3}{12\eta L_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Multiply by channel width $W_c$ to get total volumetric flow:**

$$
Q = W_c \cdot q = \frac{V_{bx} W_c H_m}{2} - \frac{W_c H_m^3}{12\eta L_m}\,\Delta P
$$

**Substitute $V_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ and $W_c \approx \pi D \sin\theta$** (the channel width equals the screw circumference projected onto the cross-channel direction):

$$
Q = \frac{1}{2}\,\pi^2 D^2 N H_m \sin\theta \cos\theta \; - \; \frac{\pi D H_m^3 \sin^2\theta}{12\eta}\,\Delta P
$$

**Identification of terms:**

$$
\boxed{\,Q = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\,\pi^2 D^2 N H_m \sin\theta \cos\theta}_{\text{Drag flow } Q_d} \; - \; \underbrace{\frac{\pi D H_m^3 \sin^2\theta}{12\eta}\,\Delta P}_{\text{Pressure flow } Q_p}\,}
$$

**Limiting cases (essential for KTU numericals):**
1. **Open die ($\Delta P = 0$):** $Q = Q_d = \frac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N H_m \sin\theta \cos\theta$.
2. **Closed die ($Q = 0$):** $\Delta P_{\max} = \dfrac{6 \eta L_m N}{\tan\theta \, H_m^2}$ (max built-up pressure).

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** This is the single most-asked equation in the module. Examiners love to give you $D$, $N$, $H_m$, $\theta$, $\eta$, $\Delta P$ and ask for $Q$; or invert the problem to solve for $\Delta P$ given $Q = 0$ (closed-die, "no-flow" condition).

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Solids-Conveying Rate (Darnell–Mol Model, Simplified)

**Statement:** In the feed zone, solid polymer granules are conveyed as a packed bed, and the rate is governed by the *friction* of the solid plug against the barrel vs. against the screw root.

**Model:** Assume a solid plug moving axially at velocity $V_{ax}$ relative to the stationary screw. The barrel surface moves at relative velocity $\pi D N$ at helix angle $\theta$.

**Force balance on the solid plug (per unit area, axial direction):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Barrel friction drives forward:} &\quad f_b W_c (\pi D N \cos\theta - V_{ax}/\cos\theta) \\[4pt]
\text{Screw root friction resists:} &\quad f_s W_c \, V_{ax} \\[4pt]
\text{Flight side friction:} &\quad f_s W_f (\pi D N - V_{ax}) \quad (\text{often neglected})
\end{aligned}
$$

Setting net axial force to zero and solving for $V_{ax}$ (steady plug flow):

$$
V_{ax} = \pi D N \,\cos\theta \,\frac{f_b - f_s}{f_b + (f_s W_f / W_c)}
$$

If flight side friction is neglected and $f_s \ll f_b$ (ideal case):

$$
V_{ax} \approx \pi D N \cos\theta
$$

**Volumetric solids-conveying rate:**

$$
Q_s = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - D_c^2) \cdot V_{ax} \cdot \phi \approx \frac{\pi^2}{4} D^2 N \cos\theta \,\phi
$$

where $\phi$ is the *fill factor* (typically 0.3–0.5 in feed zone of starving-fed extruder) and $D_c$ is the screw core diameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** A *grooved-feed* extruder artificially raises $f_b$ through axial barrel grooves, boosting $V_{ax}$ by 2–3× and decoupling throughput from screw speed (pressure-independent operation).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem (14 marks):** A single-screw extruder is to be designed for HDPE. Specifications:
> - Screw diameter: $D = 60\,\text{mm}$
> - Screw length: $L = 1440\,\text{mm}$
> - Compression ratio: $CR = 3.0$
> - Metering depth: $H_m = 3\,\text{mm}$
> - Screw speed: $N = 80\,\text{rpm}$
> - Melt viscosity: $\eta = 800\,\text{Pa\cdot s}$
> - Die pressure rise: $\Delta P = 8\,\text{MPa}$
> - Helix angle: $\theta = 17.7^{\circ}$
>
> **Calculate:** (a) Feed zone depth $H_f$, $L/D$ ratio, and length of metering zone (assume 25% of $L$); (b) Drag flow, pressure flow, and net throughput.

### (a) Geometric Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_f &= CR \times H_m = 3.0 \times 3 = 9\,\text{mm} \\[4pt]
L/D &= 1440 / 60 = 24 \quad (\text{standard general-purpose geometry}) \\[4pt]
L_{\text{metering}} &= 0.25 \times 1440 = 360\,\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (b) Throughput Solution

**Convert units and compute $\sin\theta$, $\cos\theta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= 17.7^{\circ} \Rightarrow \sin\theta = 0.304,\; \cos\theta = 0.953 \\[4pt]
N &= 80\,\text{rpm} = 80 \times \dfrac{2\pi}{60} = 8.378\,\text{rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Drag flow $Q_d$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_d &= \frac{1}{2}\,\pi^2 (0.060)^2 (8.378)(0.003)(0.304)(0.953) \\[4pt]
Q_d &= \frac{1}{2}(9.8696)(0.0036)(8.378)(0.003)(0.2898) \\[4pt]
Q_d &= 1.385 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 0.1385\,\text{L/s} \approx 8.31\,\text{L/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pressure flow $Q_p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_p &= \frac{\pi (0.060)(0.003)^3 (0.304)^2}{12 \times 800} \times 8\times 10^6 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi (0.060)(2.7\times 10^{-8})(0.0924)}{9600} \times 8\times 10^6 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{4.706 \times 10^{-10}}{9600} \times 8\times 10^6 \\[4pt]
&= 3.92 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Net throughput $Q$:**

$$
Q = Q_d - Q_p \approx 1.385\times 10^{-4} - 3.92\times 10^{-7} \approx 1.381 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Convert to mass flow (HDPE $\rho \approx 760\,\text{kg/m}^3$):**

$$
\dot{m} = Q \times \rho = 1.381\times 10^{-4} \times 760 \approx 0.105\,\text{kg/s} \approx 6.3\,\text{kg/min}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pressure-flow term is **negligible** (~0.3% of $Q_d$) in this case — typical for metering zones with $H_m$ not too shallow. This is a *very common KTU observation* that the examiner wants you to comment on.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Conventional Extruder Screw

The Mermaid diagram below maps the three functional zones, the polymer state transitions, and the governing physical mechanisms. It uses a horizontal flowchart with distinct stages of the polymer's journey.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[POLYMER GRANULES<br/>FEED HOPPER] --> B[ZONE 1: FEED ZONE<br/>Solids Conveying<br/>Length 50 percent of L]
    B --> C[ZONE 2: COMPRESSION ZONE<br/>Melting and Plasticating<br/>Length 25 to 30 percent of L]
    C --> D[ZONE 3: METERING ZONE<br/>Melt Pumping<br/>Length 20 to 25 percent of L]
    D --> E[BREAKER PLATE<br/>AND SCREEN PACK]
    E --> F[DIE ENTRY<br/>High Pressure]
    F --> G[EXTRUDATE<br/>Final Profile]

    B -. Geometry .-> B1[Deep channel<br/>H sub f equals 0.10 D to 0.15 D<br/>Constant depth]
    C -. Geometry .-> C1[Tapered channel<br/>H decreases from H sub f to H sub m]
    D -. Geometry .-> D1[Shallow channel<br/>H sub m equals 0.02 D to 0.06 D<br/>Constant depth]
    B -. Mechanism .-> B2[Friction drag<br/>Barrel friction dominates]
    C -. Mechanism .-> C2[Conduction plus shear<br/>Melt pool formation at barrel wall]
    D -. Mechanism .-> D3[Viscous drag flow<br/>plus pressure flow]
    E -. Function .-> E1[Pressure build up<br/>Particle filtration]
    F -. Function .-> F1[Pressure 5 to 35 MPa<br/>Steady state flow]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Channel Geometry Unwrapped

The unwrapped channel view is the standard pedagogical representation. The Mermaid block diagram below maps the parametric geometry of the screw channel as it appears in textbook unwrapping.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SCREW[UNWRAPPED SCREW CHANNEL - DOWN CHANNEL VIEW]
        direction TB
        P1[Origin: Down Channel Axis X] --> P2[Channel Width W sub c]
        P2 --> P3[Helix Angle theta<br/>Flight inclined at theta to cross channel normal]
        P3 --> P4[Channel Depth H<br/>From screw root y equals 0 to barrel y equals H]
        P4 --> P5[Barrel Relative Velocity V sub b<br/>Equals pi times D times N<br/>with components V sub bx and V sub by]
        P5 --> P6[Solid Bed in Feed Zone<br/>plus Melt Pool in Metering Zone]
    end

    P7[Key Geometric Inputs] --> P8[Screw Diameter D]
    P7 --> P9[Lead S]
    P7 --> P10[Channel Depth H]
    P7 --> P11[Flight Width e]
    P8 --> P2
    P9 --> P3
    P10 --> P4
    P11 --> P2
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — Conventional vs. Barrier vs. Mixing Screws

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CS[CONVENTIONAL SCREW]
        CS1[Feed Zone: Deep constant channel] --> CS2[Compression Zone: Tapered channel]
        CS2 --> CS3[Metering Zone: Shallow constant channel]
    end

    subgraph BS[BARRIER FLIGHT SCREW - MADDOCK TYPE]
        BS1[Feed Zone: Standard] --> BS2[Solid Channel]
        BS2 --> BS3[Melt Channel separated by barrier flight]
        BS3 --> BS4[Metering Zone: Shallow standard]
    end

    subgraph MS[MIXING SCREW - SAXTON TYPE]
        MS1[Feed Zone] --> MS2[Compression Zone]
        MS2 --> MS3[Metering Zone with mixing pins]
        MS3 --> MS4[Improved distributive mixing]
    end

    CS3 --> OUTPUT[EXTRUDATE]
    BS4 --> OUTPUT
    MS4 --> OUTPUT

    ADV1[Advantage: Simple and economical] -.-> CS
    ADV2[Advantage: Higher output and better melt homogeneity] -.-> BS
    ADV3[Advantage: Superior colour and filler dispersion] -.-> MS

    LIM1[Limitation: Limited mixing] -.-> CS
    LIM2[Limitation: Increased complexity and cost] -.-> BS
    LIM3[Limitation: Higher pressure drop] -.-> MS
```

## 4.4 Pressure and Flow Profile Along the Screw Axis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Zone<br/>Pressure near 0 MPa<br/>Low viscosity solid plug] --> B[Compression Zone<br/>Pressure rises gradually<br/>Solid bed melts]
    B --> C[Metering Zone<br/>Pressure rises steeply<br/>Viscous drag plus pressure flow]
    C --> D[Die Exit<br/>Pressure at die 5 to 35 MPa<br/>Atmospheric post die]

    M1[Q drag flow constant in metering] -.-> C
    M2[Q pressure flow grows with pressure rise] -.-> C
    M3[Q net equals Q drag minus Q pressure] -.-> C
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading guidance for KTU viva / diagram questions:** When the examiner asks *"Sketch the screw and label the zones,"* the acceptable answer must show: (i) the three zones with their typical length percentages, (ii) the helix angle, (iii) the flight with width $W$ and clearance $\delta$ to the barrel, (iv) the channel depth variation $H_f \to H_m$, and (v) the direction of rotation. A clear arrow indicating the channel-direction velocity $V_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ earns full marks.
