# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCPOT503]`

**Q: Define compression ratio of an extruder screw. What is its typical range for polyolefin processing?**

**Model Answer:**
The compression ratio ($CR$) of an extruder screw is the ratio of the channel volume (or, for a screw with constant lead and constant flight width, the channel depth) in the feed zone to that in the metering zone:

$$
CR = \frac{V_f}{V_m} = \frac{H_f}{H_m}
$$

It expresses the degree to which the solid polymer bed is compacted and progressively melted as it travels down the screw. For polyolefins such as LDPE, HDPE, and PP, the typical compression ratio lies in the range **2.5 to 4.0**. For shear- and heat-sensitive polymers such as rigid PVC, lower values of **2.0 to 2.5** are used to limit residence time and temperature rise.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for range + application)*

---

### Question 2 (3 marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCPOT503]`

**Q: With the aid of neat sketches, describe the three functional zones of a conventional extruder screw.**

**Model Answer:**
A conventional (metering) extruder screw has three geometrically and functionally distinct zones:

1. **Feed Zone (Solids Conveying Zone):** Occupies about **50% of total screw length**. Has a deep, constant-depth channel ($H_f \approx 0.10D$–$0.15D$). Granular polymer is compacted and conveyed forward by the friction between the solid plug and the barrel surface, against the friction with the screw root.

2. **Compression (Melting/Plasticating) Zone:** Occupies about **25–30% of total screw length**. The channel depth tapers linearly (or in steps) from $H_f$ to $H_m$. The polymer undergoes melting primarily through shear-induced heating and conduction from the barrel; a melt film forms on the barrel wall and grows inward, consuming the solid bed.

3. **Metering (Melt Pumping) Zone:** Occupies about **20–25% of total screw length**. Has a shallow, constant-depth channel ($H_m \approx 0.02D$–$0.06D$). The fully melted polymer is pumped by viscous drag flow against the pressure-flow backflow, generating the die-entry pressure (typically 5–35 MPa) required for extrusion.

*(3 marks: 1 mark per zone, with ½ mark each for length, depth, and function)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** Part B questions feature an *internal choice* — the student answers either Question A or Question B, both of which carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCPOT503, Module 1]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the net throughput of a Newtonian polymer melt in the metering zone of a single-screw extruder, clearly identifying drag flow and pressure flow components. State the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-screw extruder has the following specifications:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Screw diameter $D$ | 50 mm |
| Helix angle $\theta$ | 17.7° |
| Metering depth $H_m$ | 2.5 mm |
| Screw speed $N$ | 100 rpm |
| Melt viscosity $\eta$ | 1000 Pa·s |
| Die pressure $\Delta P$ | 10 MPa |

Calculate: (i) drag flow, (ii) pressure flow, and (iii) net throughput in m³/s. Convert the net throughput to **kg/h**, assuming melt density $\rho = 750\,\text{kg/m}^3$. Comment on the relative magnitudes. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### (a) Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions** (1 mark):
- Steady, laminar, isothermal flow.
- Incompressible Newtonian melt (constant $\eta$).
- Fully developed flow in the unwrapped rectangular channel of width $W_c$ and depth $H_m$.
- Screw stationary, barrel moving at relative velocity $V_b = \pi D N$.
- Neglect leakage flow through the flight clearance (for first-order design).
- The polymer melt fully fills the metering channel (no voids).

**Setup (1 mark):** Unwrap the metering channel onto a 2-D Cartesian frame: $x$ = down-channel direction, $y$ = across the depth ($y=0$ at screw root, $y=H_m$ at barrel). The barrel velocity has a down-channel component $V_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\theta$.

**Governing equation (1 mark):**

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \eta \frac{d^2 v_x}{dy^2}
$$

**Boundary conditions (½ mark):**
- $v_x(y=0) = 0$ (no-slip at screw root)
- $v_x(y=H_m) = V_{bx}$ (no-slip at barrel wall)

**Integration (2 marks):**

Integrating twice and applying the BCs yields:

$$
v_x(y) = V_{bx}\frac{y}{H_m} - \frac{\Delta P}{2\eta L_m}\,y(H_m - y)
$$

**Integration over depth to obtain flow per unit width (1 mark):**

$$
q = \int_0^{H_m} v_x(y)\, dy = \frac{V_{bx} H_m}{2} - \frac{\Delta P H_m^3}{12\eta L_m}
$$

**Multiplying by channel width and substituting geometric identities (1 mark):**

$$
Q = \frac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N H_m \sin\theta\cos\theta \;-\; \frac{\pi D H_m^3 \sin^2\theta}{12\eta}\Delta P
$$

**Identification (½ mark):**

$$
\boxed{Q = Q_d - Q_p}
$$

where $Q_d$ is the drag flow and $Q_p$ is the pressure back-flow. This is the standard KTU answer.

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Convert screw speed to rad/s (1 mark):**

$$
N = 100 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 10.472\,\text{rad/s}
$$

**Compute trigonometric functions (½ mark):**

$$
\sin(17.7^{\circ}) = 0.304,\quad \cos(17.7^{\circ}) = 0.953
$$

**(i) Drag flow (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_d &= \frac{1}{2}\,\pi^2 (0.050)^2 (10.472)(0.0025)(0.304)(0.953) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}(9.8696)(0.0025)(10.472)(0.0025)(0.2898) \\[4pt]
Q_d &= 9.37 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Pressure flow (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_p &= \frac{\pi (0.050)(0.0025)^3 (0.304)^2}{12 \times 1000} \times 10 \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{\pi (0.050)(1.5625\times 10^{-8})(0.0924)}{12000} \times 10^7 \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2.270 \times 10^{-10}}{12000} \times 10^7 \\[4pt]
Q_p &= 1.892 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Net throughput (1 mark):**

$$
Q = Q_d - Q_p = 9.37 \times 10^{-5} - 1.89 \times 10^{-7} \approx 9.35 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Convert to kg/h (½ mark):**

$$
\dot{m} = Q \times \rho = 9.35 \times 10^{-5} \times 750 \times 3600 \approx 252.5\,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The pressure flow is only $\approx 0.2\%$ of the drag flow. This is a typical design outcome — for a moderately shallow metering zone with a moderate pressure rise, the metering zone behaves almost as a *pure drag pump*. The die pressure rise is *insignificant* in limiting throughput under these conditions, but it becomes critical as $H_m$ is reduced (e.g., to 1 mm) or $\eta$ is increased.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCPOT503, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the geometry of a single-screw extruder with neat sketches. Define and derive the expression for the **helix angle** and **compression ratio**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-screw extruder with a screw diameter of 75 mm and an L/D ratio of 24 is to extrude HDPE. The screw has a square pitch. The feed zone channel depth is 9 mm and the metering zone channel depth is 3 mm. The screw rotates at 60 rpm. Calculate: (i) helix angle, (ii) compression ratio, (iii) screw length, and (iv) drag flow rate in the metering zone (neglect pressure flow). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### (a) Geometry, Helix Angle, and Compression Ratio (7 marks)

**Screw geometry (3 marks):** A single-screw extruder consists of a helical flight wound around a cylindrical core (the screw root), rotating inside a heated cylindrical barrel. The principal geometric features are:

- **Outer (major) diameter $D$** — root-to-tip diameter.
- **Length $L$** — axial length of the screw.
- **L/D ratio** — typically 20–30 for general-purpose machines; equals 24 in this problem.
- **Channel depth $H$** — varies axially: $H_f$ in feed zone (deep), $H_m$ in metering zone (shallow).
- **Lead $S$** — axial advance per flight turn; $S = D$ for *square pitch*.
- **Helix angle $\theta$** — angle between the flight and the plane normal to the screw axis.
- **Flight width $W$** — width of the helical ridge.
- **Radial clearance $\delta$** — gap between flight tip and barrel inner wall (typically 0.05–0.13 mm).

A neat sketch must show the three zones, the flight, the channel, the helix angle, the flight clearance, and the direction of rotation. The unwrapped channel (a rectangle inclined at angle $\theta$) is the standard pedagogical representation.

**Helix angle derivation (2 marks):** Unwrap one flight onto a flat plane. In one revolution, the axial advance is $S$ and the circumferential advance is $\pi D$. The flight is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs $S$ and $\pi D$:

$$
\tan\theta = \frac{S}{\pi D}\quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{S}{\pi D}\right)
$$

**Compression ratio derivation (2 marks):** For constant lead and constant flight width, the volume per unit length of the channel is proportional to channel depth $H$. Therefore:

$$
CR = \frac{V_f}{V_m} = \frac{H_f}{H_m}
$$

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Helix angle (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\theta &= \frac{S}{\pi D} = \frac{75}{\pi \times 75} = \frac{1}{\pi} = 0.3183 \\[4pt]
\theta &= \arctan(0.3183) = 17.66^{\circ} \approx 17.7^{\circ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Compression ratio (1.5 marks):**

$$
CR = \frac{H_f}{H_m} = \frac{9}{3} = 3.0
$$

**(iii) Screw length (1 mark):**

$$
L = 24 \times D = 24 \times 75 = 1800\,\text{mm} = 1.8\,\text{m}
$$

**(iv) Drag flow in the metering zone (3 marks):** Neglecting pressure flow, $Q = Q_d$.

Convert screw speed:

$$
N = 60 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = 2\pi = 6.283\,\text{rad/s}
$$

Substitute into the drag flow equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_d &= \frac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N H_m \sin\theta \cos\theta \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}\pi^2 (0.075)^2 (6.283)(0.003)(0.304)(0.953) \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{2}(9.8696)(0.005625)(6.283)(0.003)(0.2898) \\[4pt]
Q_d &= 1.516 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 1.52 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mass flow rate (HDPE $\rho \approx 760\,\text{kg/m}^3$):**

$$
\dot{m} = Q_d \times \rho = 1.516 \times 10^{-4} \times 760 \approx 0.115\,\text{kg/s} \approx 414\,\text{kg/h}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Screw speed is *frequently* given in rpm, but the drag-flow equation requires rad/s. Failing to multiply by $2\pi/60$ loses 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting $\sin\theta \cos\theta$:** Many students write $Q_d = \frac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N H$, omitting the helix-angle factor. The correct form is $Q_d = \frac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta$. *Both* sine and cosine appear; missing either is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Confusing $H$ and $D_c$:** The "diameter" in the throughput equation is the *outer* (root-to-tip) diameter $D$, not the core (root) diameter $D_c$. Substituting $D_c$ in place of $D$ underestimates $Q$ by 30–50%.
> 4. **Sign of pressure flow:** Pressure flow *opposes* drag flow. Writing $Q = Q_d + Q_p$ (with positive sign) is a conceptual error that loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Mismatched units in $\eta$ and $H_m$:** $H_m$ must be in *metres* (not mm), and $\eta$ in *Pa·s*, for the SI equation to balance. Substituting $H_m = 3$ (in mm) gives an answer 1000× too large.
> 6. **Pressure-flow omission in part (b):** If the problem says "neglect pressure flow," the examiner expects $Q = Q_d$ *only*; if it does not say so, you *must* compute $Q_p$ and subtract — omitting it is treated as incomplete answer.
> 7. **Square-pitch vs. multiple-flight confusion:** The problem says "square pitch" — this means $S = D$, not $S = 2D$ (double flight). Always state $S = D$ explicitly in your solution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and Glance 5 Minutes Before Exam):**

### A. Geometric Parameters — Master These Definitions
- **$D$ (screw OD):** root-to-tip diameter; machine size parameter.
- **$L/D$ ratio:** total length / outer diameter; typical 20–30.
- **Lead $S$:** axial advance per flight turn. Square pitch: $S = D$. Double flight: $S = D/2$.
- **Helix angle $\theta$:** $\tan\theta = S/(\pi D)$; standard $17.7°$ for square pitch.
- **Channel depth $H$:** $H_f$ in feed (deep, $0.10D$–$0.15D$); $H_m$ in metering (shallow, $0.02D$–$0.06D$).
- **Compression ratio $CR$:** $H_f / H_m$; typically 2.0–4.0.
- **Flight clearance $\delta$:** 0.05–0.13 mm; leakage flow scales with $\delta^3$.

### B. Three Functional Zones — Memorize Length Percentages
- **Feed (50%):** solids conveying; deep channel; friction-dominated.
- **Compression (25–30%):** melting; tapered channel; shear + conduction.
- **Metering (20–25%):** melt pumping; shallow channel; viscous drag + pressure flow.

### C. Core Throughput Equation — The Single Most Important Formula
$$
Q = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}_{Q_d \text{ (drag flow)}} \;-\; \underbrace{\tfrac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta}{12\eta}\Delta P}_{Q_p \text{ (pressure flow)}}
$$
- **Open die:** $Q = Q_d$ (pure drag).
- **Closed die:** $Q = 0$, $\Delta P = \Delta P_{\max}$.

### D. Solids Conveying — Darnell–Mol Essentials
- Solid plug velocity: $V_{ax} \approx \pi D N \cos\theta$ when $f_s \ll f_b$.
- High $f_b / f_s$ → high solids-conveying rate → high throughput.
- Grooved-feed barrels raise $f_b$ → high-output operation.

### E. Newton's Conversion Constant — Don't Forget
$$
1\,\text{rpm} = \frac{2\pi}{60}\,\text{rad/s} \approx 0.10472\,\text{rad/s}
$$

### F. Design Heuristics for Common Polymers
- **LDPE/HDPE/PP:** $L/D = 24$–$30$, $CR = 3.0$–$4.0$, $H_m = 0.04D$, $N = 60$–$120$ rpm.
- **Rigid PVC:** $L/D = 20$–$24$, $CR = 2.0$–$2.5$, $H_m = 0.06D$, lower $N$ to limit shear heat.
- **PA (Nylon):** $L/D = 24$–$30$, $CR = 2.8$–$3.5$, $H_m = 0.04D$, lower barrel temperature (degrades above $290\,^{\circ}\text{C}$).
- **PET:** $L/D = 30$–$36$, two-stage screw with vacuum, $CR = 2.5$–$3.0$.

### G. Screw-Type Recognition at a Glance
- **Conventional:** constant OD, constant lead, variable channel depth; workhorse.
- **Barrier (Maddock):** auxiliary flight creates separate melt channel; higher output, better melt quality.
- **Pineapple / Saxton mixer:** disruptive elements in metering; distributive mixing.
- **Grooved-feed:** barrel with axial grooves; pressure-independent high output.
- **Two-stage:** vacuum port for devolatilization (PET, ABS).

### H. Equations to Derive on the Exam (Practice 3 Times)
1. Helix angle $\theta = \arctan(S/\pi D)$.
2. Compression ratio $CR = H_f / H_m$.
3. Net throughput $Q = Q_d - Q_p$ with full integration steps.
4. Solids-conveying plug velocity $V_{ax} = \pi D N \cos\theta$.

### I. Conversion & Sanity-Check Numbers to Remember
- HDPE melt density: $\rho \approx 750$–$770\,\text{kg/m}^3$.
- PP melt density: $\rho \approx 750\,\text{kg/m}^3$.
- Typical die pressure: 5–35 MPa.
- Typical polymer melt viscosity: 100–5000 Pa·s (shear-thinning).
- $1\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 3600\,\text{m}^3/\text{h} = 3.6 \times 10^6\,\text{L/h}$.

### J. KTU-Specific Formatting Cues
- Always start Part B answers with a *clear labeled diagram* (3–4 marks often tied to the sketch).
- *State all assumptions* explicitly before any derivation (½ mark reserved).
- *Box the final answer* in each sub-part for the examiner's ease of evaluation.
- *Comment* on magnitudes (e.g., "pressure flow is 0.2% of drag flow, hence negligible under these conditions") — this is a frequently-allocated 1-mark cognitive-level bonus.

> **END OF TOPIC: Screw Design — Module 1, PCPOT503**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINING & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Control** is the engineering discipline of monitoring, measuring, and manipulating the operating parameters of a manufacturing process to achieve a desired output (setpoint) with minimal deviation, repeatability, and stability. In polymer processing, it refers to the automatic regulation of variables such as **temperature, pressure, screw speed, flow rate, and residence time** to ensure consistent product quality.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme for **PCPOT503**, process control is the backbone of polymer machinery automation. It integrates **sensors**, **controllers**, **final control elements (actuators)**, and **feedback loops** to govern machinery such as injection moulding machines, extruders, blow moulders, and thermoforming equipment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cruise Control" Intuition

Imagine driving a car on a highway. You want to maintain a constant speed of **80 km/h**.

- You observe the **speedometer** (this is the *sensor*).
- Your **brain** compares the actual speed with the desired 80 km/h (this is the *controller*).
- Your **foot presses or releases the accelerator** based on the difference (this is the *actuator*).
- The **car's speed** changes as a result (this is the *process variable*).

Now apply this exact same logic to a polymer extruder:
- A **thermocouple** measures the melt temperature (sensor).
- The **PID controller** compares it with the desired temperature setpoint (controller).
- A **heater band or cooling fan** is turned ON/OFF or throttled (actuator).
- The **melt temperature** stabilizes at the required value (process variable).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlights)**
> - **Setpoint (SP):** The desired or target value (e.g., 220 °C barrel temperature).
> - **Process Variable (PV):** The actual measured value from the field.
> - **Error (e):** The difference $e(t) = SP - PV$.
> - **Controlled Variable (CV):** The variable being maintained at setpoint.
> - **Manipulated Variable (MV):** The variable adjusted by the controller.
> - **Disturbance (D):** Any external input that pushes the process off setpoint.

## 1.3 Importance in Polymer Machinery

Polymer processes are highly **non-linear**, **multi-variable**, and **time-varying**. A mere 5 °C deviation in barrel temperature can cause:
- Degradation of polymer chains in **PVC** and **PET**.
- Inconsistent viscosity in **polypropylene** extrusion.
- Short shots in **injection moulding**.
- Surface defects in **blow moulded** bottles.

Hence, precise process control is non-negotiable for part quality, repeatability, and energy efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Process Control Systems

### A. Open-Loop Control System
The controller output is **independent** of the process variable output. There is **no feedback** path.

- **Advantages:** Simple, cheap, fast response.
- **Disadvantages:** Cannot correct for disturbances; poor accuracy.
- **Polymer Example:** A timer-based heater that runs for 10 minutes regardless of actual melt temperature.

### B. Closed-Loop (Feedback) Control System
The controller continuously compares SP with PV and adjusts the MV. The signal flows in a **closed loop**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative Feedback Principle (KTU High-Yield):** The error signal is generated as $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$. The controller acts to **minimize** this error.

### C. Feedforward Control
Measures the disturbance **before** it affects the process and takes corrective action proactively. Often combined with feedback to form a **Combined Feedforward-Feedback (FFFB)** system.

## 2.2 Components of a Closed-Loop Control System

| # | Component | Function | Polymer Processing Example |
|---|-----------|----------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Sensor / Transmitter** | Measures the PV | Thermocouple (Type J/K), Pressure transducer |
| 2 | **Controller** | Compares SP & PV; generates control signal | PID controller, PLC, DCS |
| 3 | **Final Control Element** | Manipulates the MV | Heater band, proportional valve, VFD |
| 4 | **Process** | The system being controlled | Barrel, die, mould cavity |

## 2.3 PID Controller — The Heart of Polymer Process Control

The **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID)** controller is the most widely used industrial controller. Its output is the sum of three terms:

$$u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

Where:
- $K_c$ = Proportional gain (dimensionless)
- $\tau_I$ = Integral time (seconds)
- $\tau_D$ = Derivative time (seconds)
- $e(t)$ = Error signal at time $t$
- $u(t)$ = Controller output signal (e.g., 4–20 mA)

### Role of Each Term

- **Proportional (P):** Provides an output proportional to the current error. Faster response but causes **offset** (steady-state error).
- **Integral (I):** Accumulates past errors. Eliminates steady-state offset. Too much I = **oscillations**.
- **Derivative (D):** Predicts future error based on rate of change. Improves **stability** and damps oscillations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tuning Methods (Frequently asked in KTU Exams):**
> 1. **Ziegler-Nichols (ZN) Ultimate Cycle Method**
> 2. **Cohen-Coon Method**
> 3. **Lambda Tuning (Internal Model Control)**
> 4. **Auto-tuning by relay feedback**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Units | Application |
|---------|------------|-------|-------------|
| Error signal | $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$ | Engineering units | Universal |
| PID Output (continuous) | $u(t) = K_c e + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int e\,dt + K_c \tau_D \frac{de}{dt}$ | % or mA | Universal |
| Proportional Band | $PB = \frac{100\%}{K_c}$ | % | Reverse/direct acting |
| First-order system response | $y(t) = y_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | PV units | Temperature control |
| Time constant $\tau$ | $\tau = R \cdot C$ (thermal) | seconds | Heater dynamics |
| Steady-state error (P-only) | $e_{ss} = \frac{D}{1 + K_c K_p}$ | PV units | Disturbance rejection |
| ZN-PID tuning | $K_c = 0.6 K_u$, $\tau_I = 0.5 T_u$, $\tau_D = 0.125 T_u$ | — | Tuning exams |
| Transfer function (1st order) | $G(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | — | Block diagram analysis |
| Transfer function (PID) | $G_c(s) = K_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right)$ | — | Controller design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for Ziegler-Nichols PID Values:**
> - $K_c = 0.6 \, K_u$ (60 % of ultimate gain)
> - $T_I = 0.5 \, T_u$ (half the ultimate period)
> - $T_D = 0.125 \, T_u$ (one-eighth the ultimate period)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Injection Moulding:** Closed-loop control of **injection speed, pressure, and cushion position** using a PID-driven hydraulic servo valve.
- **Extrusion:** Die pressure and melt temperature are controlled via closed-loop screw speed (VFD-driven) and barrel heater zones.
- **Blow Moulding:** Parison wall thickness control via closed-loop hydraulic accumulator and die-gap actuation.
- **Thermoforming:** Oven temperature profile control using multi-zone PID.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Process Step Response

Consider a single-screw extruder barrel heater with thermal resistance $R$ and thermal capacitance $C$. The governing differential equation from energy balance is:

$$RC \frac{dT(t)}{dt} + T(t) = K \, Q(t)$$

Where:
- $T(t)$ = Temperature rise above ambient (°C)
- $Q(t)$ = Heat input (W)
- $K$ = Static gain (°C/W)
- $RC = \tau$ = Time constant (s)

**Step 1:** Take the Laplace transform assuming zero initial conditions.

$$RC \, s \, T(s) + T(s) = K \, Q(s)$$

**Step 2:** Factor out $T(s)$ on the left side.

$$T(s) \, (RC \, s + 1) = K \, Q(s)$$

**Step 3:** Form the transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{T(s)}{Q(s)}$.

$$G(s) = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step 4:** For a step input of magnitude $Q_0$, $Q(s) = \dfrac{Q_0}{s}$.

$$T(s) = \frac{K Q_0}{s(\tau s + 1)}$$

**Step 5:** Perform partial fraction expansion.

$$T(s) = K Q_0 \left[ \frac{1}{s} - \frac{\tau}{\tau s + 1} \right]$$

**Step 6:** Apply inverse Laplace transform term by term.

$$T(s) = K Q_0 \left[ \frac{1}{s} - \frac{1}{s + 1/\tau} \right]$$

$$T(t) = K Q_0 \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The barrel temperature rises exponentially and reaches **63.2 %** of its final value at $t = \tau$, **86.5 %** at $t = 2\tau$, and **95 %** at $t = 3\tau$. This is a classic KTU exam question.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Error of a P-Only Controller

Consider a unity-feedback system with process transfer function $G_p(s)$ and proportional controller $G_c(s) = K_c$.

**Step 1:** Write the open-loop transfer function.

$$G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s) = K_c \cdot G_p(s)$$

**Step 2:** The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)} = \frac{K_c G_p(s)}{1 + K_c G_p(s)}$$

**Step 3:** The error $E(s) = R(s) - Y(s)$ can be written as:

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 4:** For a unit step disturbance $D(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$ entering at the process output, the steady-state error is found by the **Final Value Theorem**:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{D(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute and evaluate.

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1/s}{1 + K_c G_p(0)} = \frac{1}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** As $K_c \to \infty$, $e_{ss} \to 0$ (offset eliminated). However, very high $K_c$ causes **instability** and oscillation. This trade-off justifies adding the **Integral** term in PID.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Ziegler-Nichols Ultimate Cycle Tuning

**Step 1:** Set the controller to **P-mode only** ($\tau_I = \infty$, $\tau_D = 0$).

**Step 2:** Gradually increase $K_c$ until the system exhibits **sustained oscillations** (constant amplitude). This critical gain is the **ultimate gain** $K_u$.

**Step 3:** Measure the period of these oscillations — this is the **ultimate period** $T_u$ (in seconds).

**Step 4:** Apply the ZN formulas for a PID controller:

$$K_c = 0.6 \, K_u$$

$$\tau_I = 0.5 \, T_u$$

$$\tau_D = 0.125 \, T_u$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A temperature control loop on an extruder shows sustained oscillations when $K_c = 8.0$ with period $T_u = 60$ s. Calculate the ZN-PID settings.

$$K_c = 0.6 \times 8.0 = 4.8$$

$$\tau_I = 0.5 \times 60 = 30 \text{ s}$$

$$\tau_D = 0.125 \times 60 = 7.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 (Verification):** Substitute into the PID equation.

$$u(t) = 4.8 \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{30}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + 7.5 \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Controller Modes

| Property | P-Only | PI | PID |
|----------|--------|----|----|
| Steady-state error | Exists (offset) | Zero | Zero |
| Speed of response | Fast | Moderate | Fast |
| Stability | Poor at high gain | Better | Best |
| Overshoot | Possible | Moderate | Minimal |
| Tuning complexity | Easy | Moderate | Difficult |
| Use in polymer processing | Rare | Heater zones | Servo-hydraulic axes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Closed-Loop Process Control System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional flow of a typical polymer process control loop (e.g., extruder barrel temperature control).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SUM[Comparator Summing Junction]
    DIST[Disturbance D] --> PROC[Process Plant]
    SUM -->|Error e t| CTRL[PID Controller Gc s]
    CTRL -->|Output u t| ACT[Actuator Heater Valve VFD]
    ACT --> PROC
    PROC -->|Output PV| SENS[Sensor Thermocouple]
    SENS --> FEED[Feedback Path]
    FEED --> SUM
```

**Reading the diagram:**
1. The **Setpoint** enters the summing junction.
2. The **Feedback** (from the sensor) is subtracted — this is the **error**.
3. The **PID Controller** computes the corrective signal.
4. The **Actuator** (heater band) adjusts the **Process Plant** (extruder barrel).
5. The **Sensor** measures the new temperature and the cycle repeats.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Injection Moulding Machine Control

The following diagram shows the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** mapping how process control is implemented across an injection moulding machine.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL[Central Control Layer]
        HMI[Operator HMI Panel]
        PLC[PLC / Controller]
    end

    subgraph ZONE1[Barrel Heating Zones]
        Z1TC[Thermocouple Zone 1]
        Z1H[Heater Band Zone 1]
    end

    subgraph ZONE2[Barrel Heating Zones]
        Z2TC[Thermocouple Zone 2]
        Z2H[Heater Band Zone 2]
    end

    subgraph HYD[Hydraulic Actuation Loop]
        PRS[Pressure Transducer]
        PRV[Proportional Relief Valve]
        POS[Linear Position Sensor]
    end

    subgraph SCREW[Plasticizing Axis]
        SST[Speed Sensor Tachogenerator]
        VFD[Variable Frequency Drive]
    end

    PLC --> Z1H
    PLC --> Z2H
    PLC --> PRV
    PLC --> VFD
    Z1TC --> PLC
    Z2TC --> PLC
    PRS --> PLC
    POS --> PLC
    SST --> PLC
    HMI <--> PLC
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **PLC** acts as the central controller.
- It receives inputs from multiple sensors (thermocouples, pressure, position, speed).
- It drives the actuators (heater bands, proportional valve, VFD).
- The **HMI** allows the operator to enter setpoints and monitor real-time data.

## 4.3 Control Action Types — Direct vs Reverse Acting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DIRECT[Direct Acting]
        D1[PV Increases] --> D2[Output Increases]
    end

    subgraph REVERSE[Reverse Acting]
        R1[PV Increases] --> R2[Output Decreases]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For **temperature control** (e.g., heater), the controller is **Reverse Acting** — if PV (temperature) rises above SP, the controller **decreases** the heater output. For **cooling**, the controller is **Direct Acting**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> *Define process control. List any four process variables commonly controlled in a single-screw extruder.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Process control is the automatic regulation of process variables to maintain them at a desired setpoint by comparing measured values with reference values and adjusting the manipulated variables using a feedback mechanism.

**Four Process Variables in a Single-Screw Extruder (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Barrel temperature** (multiple zones) — controlled by heater bands and cooling fans.
2. **Melt pressure** at the die — controlled by screw speed and die restriction.
3. **Screw speed** (rpm) — controlled by VFD.
4. **Melt throughput / flow rate** — controlled by screw speed and feeder rate.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]**
> *Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control systems. Give one polymer processing example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Feedback path | Absent | Present |
| Accuracy | Poor — depends on calibration | High — self-correcting |
| Disturbance rejection | None | Excellent |
| Cost & complexity | Low | Higher |
| **Polymer Example** | Timer-based pre-heater that runs for a fixed duration regardless of actual temperature | PID-controlled barrel temperature zone that adjusts heater output based on thermocouple feedback |

**Example Mapping (1 Mark):**
- Open-loop: A fixed-time barrel pre-heating cycle in a small injection moulding machine.
- Closed-loop: PID temperature control in a multi-zone extruder barrel.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Paper]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional elements of a closed-loop process control system as applied to the temperature control of an extruder barrel. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the transfer function of a first-order thermal system and obtain its step response. Show that the system reaches 63.2 % of its final value at $t = \tau$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):**
Refer to the **Section 4.1** Mermaid diagram. Components:

1. **Setpoint generator** — operator enters temperature in °C (e.g., 220 °C).
2. **Comparator / Summing Junction** — generates error $e(t) = SP - PV$.
3. **PID Controller** — computes control signal $u(t)$.
4. **Actuator** — heater band (with SSR — solid-state relay) or cooling fan.
5. **Process** — extruder barrel with thermal mass $C$ and resistance $R$.
6. **Sensor** — Type-K thermocouple producing mV signal.
7. **Transmitter** — converts mV to 4–20 mA current loop.
8. **Feedback path** — sends PV back to the comparator.

**Explanation (4 Marks — 0.5 each point):**
- The thermocouple senses the barrel temperature.
- The transmitter converts the millivolt signal to a standardized 4–20 mA.
- The PID controller receives the PV and the SP, calculates the error.
- The controller output drives a Solid-State Relay (SSR), which modulates the heater band via **time-proportional** or **phase-angle** control.
- If temperature exceeds SP, the cooling fan is activated (split-range control).
- The new temperature is sensed and the loop continues with a typical cycle time of **1–2 seconds**.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Write the energy balance for the barrel wall.

$$m c_p \frac{dT}{dt} = Q_{in} - \frac{T - T_\infty}{R}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Rearrange to standard first-order form.

$$RC \frac{dT}{dt} + (T - T_\infty) = R \, Q_{in}$$

Let $\theta = T - T_\infty$ (temperature rise above ambient):

$$\tau \frac{d\theta}{dt} + \theta = K \, Q_{in}$$

Where $\tau = RC$ and $K = R$.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Take the Laplace transform (zero initial condition).

$$\tau s \, \Theta(s) + \Theta(s) = K \, Q(s)$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Form the transfer function.

$$G(s) = \frac{\Theta(s)}{Q(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** For a step input $Q(t) = Q_0$, the time-domain response is:

$$\theta(t) = K Q_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** At $t = \tau$:

$$\theta(\tau) = K Q_0 (1 - e^{-1}) = K Q_0 (1 - 0.3679) = 0.6321 \, K Q_0$$

This is **63.2 %** of the final value $K Q_0$. Hence proved.

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** **Physical Significance:** The time constant $\tau$ represents the time taken by the barrel to reach 63.2 % of its steady-state temperature. For polymer processing, $\tau$ is typically **2–10 minutes** for a 50 mm barrel.

---

### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the PID controller with its mathematical form. Discuss the role and limitations of each term (P, I, D). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A temperature control loop on a plasticizing extruder exhibits sustained oscillations of period 60 seconds when the controller gain is increased to $K_u = 8.0$ in P-mode. Calculate the Ziegler-Nichols PID settings. Justify the choice of these values. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Mathematical Form (2 Marks):**

$$u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

**Role of P-term (1.5 Marks):**
- Output proportional to current error.
- Provides immediate corrective action.
- **Limitation:** Causes steady-state offset (residual error) and may lead to instability at high $K_c$.

**Role of I-term (1.5 Marks):**
- Accumulates past errors; eliminates offset over time.
- **Limitation:** Slow response, integral windup during actuator saturation, can cause oscillations.

**Role of D-term (1 Mark):**
- Responds to rate of change; anticipates future error.
- **Limitation:** Amplifies measurement noise; ineffective for very slow processes.

**Summary table (1 Mark):** A combined PID offers **zero offset, fast response, and good stability**, which is why it dominates polymer process control.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given (1 Mark):**
- Ultimate gain $K_u = 8.0$
- Ultimate period $T_u = 60$ s

**Ziegler-Nichols PID Formulas (2 Marks — 1 each for stating the formulas and noting the controller type):**

$$K_c = 0.6 \, K_u, \quad \tau_I = 0.5 \, T_u, \quad \tau_D = 0.125 \, T_u$$

**Step-by-step Calculation (3 Marks — 1 each):**

$$K_c = 0.6 \times 8.0 = 4.8$$

$$\tau_I = 0.5 \times 60 = 30 \text{ s}$$

$$\tau_D = 0.125 \times 60 = 7.5 \text{ s}$$

**Justification (1 Mark):**
These ZN values give a controller with approximately **25 % overshoot** and a **quarter-amplitude decay ratio**. They are a good starting point for tuning, but in practice, fine-tuning is done to reduce overshoot, since polymer processes prefer **minimal overshoot** to avoid thermal degradation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units** of $\tau_I$ and $\tau_D$ (always seconds, not minutes).
> 2. **Confusing** P-band with proportional gain: $PB = 100 / K_c$.
> 3. **Not drawing** the closed-loop block diagram in part (a) — a missing block diagram costs **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Skipping the "negative feedback"** sign in the comparator.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply the Final Value Theorem** properly in steady-state error derivations.
> 6. **Writing $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s}$** instead of $\dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ for a first-order system — a very common error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- ✅ **Definition:** Process control = automatic regulation of process variables to maintain a setpoint.
- ✅ **Four Building Blocks:** Sensor → Controller → Actuator → Process.
- ✅ **Error Equation:** $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$ — always subtract.
- ✅ **Open-loop:** No feedback, cheap but inaccurate.
- ✅ **Closed-loop:** Has feedback, accurate, self-correcting.
- ✅ **Feedforward:** Anticipates disturbances; needs a measured disturbance.
- ✅ **PID Equation:** $u(t) = K_c\left[e + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\int e\,dt + \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt}\right]$
- ✅ **P removes** transient error → fast but has offset.
- ✅ **I removes** steady-state offset → slow but accurate.
- ✅ **D anticipates** future error → dampens oscillations, but noisy.
- ✅ **First-order transfer function:** $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$
- ✅ **Time constant $\tau$** = time to reach **63.2 %** of final value.
- ✅ **ZN-PID formulas:** $K_c = 0.6 K_u$, $\tau_I = 0.5 T_u$, $\tau_D = 0.125 T_u$.
- ✅ **Steady-state error (P-only):** $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_c K_p}$.
- ✅ **Reverse acting** controller used for **heating**; **Direct acting** for **cooling**.
- ✅ **Polymer process variables to control:** Temperature, Pressure, Screw Speed, Flow rate, Position, Cycle time.
- ✅ **Instrumentation signals:** 4–20 mA (current), 0–10 V (voltage), HART, Fieldbus.
- ✅ **Integral Windup:** A common PID problem — use **anti-windup** (back-calculation or clamping).
- ✅ **Block diagram must include:** Summing junction with negative sign for feedback.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clamping Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Clamping Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clamping Unit (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> The **Clamping Unit** of an injection molding machine is the sub-assembly responsible for holding the two halves of the mold firmly together against the intense internal pressure generated during melt injection, opening the mold at the end of the cooling cycle, and ejecting the solidified plastic part. It transforms a low-force, high-speed motion (mold closing) into a high-force, low-speed motion (mold locking) via a mechanical, hydraulic, or hybrid power transmission system.

The clamping unit must deliver a **clamping force (tonnage)** that exceeds the **mold separation force (cavity pressure × projected area)** acting on the mold halves. Without adequate clamping, the mold would slightly open ("breathe"), creating **flash** — excess material escaping the cavity — and producing defective parts.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Lunchbox Press" Analogy:**
> Imagine you have a hinged metal lunchbox. You want to fill it with hot, pressurized soup (the molten polymer). If you just hold the lid with your fingers, the soup pressure will force the lid open and spill out. So instead, you build a heavy steel frame with a long lever arm (toggle) and a powerful hydraulic piston (ram). When you push the lever down, a small hand force becomes a massive locking force — like a nutcracker. That is exactly what the clamping unit does: it uses a **toggle mechanism** (or hydraulic ram) to lock the mold shut so the injected plastic cannot push it open.

The **three jobs** of the clamping unit, in simple terms:

1. **Close** the mold halves together quickly (closing stroke).
2. **Lock** the mold with massive force so it resists injection pressure (locking stroke).
3. **Open** the mold and **eject** the part after cooling.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (KTU Industry Standard) |
|---|---|---|
| Cavity Pressure | $P_c$ | **30 – 50 MPa** (300 – 500 bar) |
| Clamping Force | $F_c$ | **50 – 10,000 kN** (machines range widely) |
| Safety Factor | $SF$ | **1.1 – 1.5** |
| Toggle Angle (locked) | $\alpha$ | **0° – 15°** (smaller = higher mechanical advantage) |
| Mold Opening Stroke | $S_o$ | **200 – 1500 mm** (machine-size dependent) |
| Tie Bar Diameter | $d_{tb}$ | **40 – 250 mm** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — Clamping Unit):** You must study (a) the construction and working of the **toggle clamp**, (b) the **hydraulic clamp**, (c) the **hydromechanical clamp**, and (d) **clamping force calculations** including projected area and safety factor logic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of the Clamping Unit

Every clamping unit, regardless of type, contains these fundamental components:

- **Stationary Platen:** Fixed to the machine frame; the half of the mold mounted here never moves.
- **Moving Platen:** Slides on **tie bars (guide pillars)**; carries the moving half of the mold.
- **Tie Bars (Guide Pillars):** Four (sometimes two) high-tensile steel rods that guide the moving platen and resist the clamping force by stretching elastically.
- **Tie Bar Nuts / Spherical Bearings:** Anchor the tie bars to the stationary platen and the rear platen.
- **Clamping Cylinder / Toggle Assembly:** The prime-mover — either a hydraulic cylinder or a toggle linkage driven by a smaller hydraulic ram.
- **Ejector System (Ejector Pins, Ejector Plate, Ejector Rods):** Pushes the solidified part out of the mold after the mold opens.
- **Rear Platen / Support Plate:** Provides the reaction point for the clamping force.

### 2.2 Working Philosophy — The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> The clamping unit has **three distinct strokes** during one molding cycle. Understanding *why* each stroke exists is critical for KTU exams.

**Step 1 — Closing Stroke (Fast Mold Approach):**
- *Why?* Productivity requires the mold to close in **1–3 seconds**, not 30 seconds. So the platen moves rapidly (50–100 mm/s) over the long distance while the mold halves are still apart.
- *How?* In a toggle clamp, a small hydraulic cylinder drives the toggle links extended, moving the platen forward fast.

**Step 2 — Locking Stroke (Slow, High-Force Build-up):**
- *Why?* Once the mold halves touch, the toggle must change from "low-force/high-speed" to "high-force/low-speed" to build up the clamping tonnage.
- *How?* The toggle joint straightens out. As the toggle angle $\alpha$ approaches **0°**, the mechanical advantage tends toward infinity — meaning a small input force becomes a colossal clamping force.

**Step 3 — Opening & Ejection Stroke:**
- *Why?* After cooling, the mold must separate so the part can be removed.
- *How?* The toggle retracts, the platen moves back, the ejector plate activates, and the part is pushed out by ejector pins.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_c = P_c \times A_p \times SF$ | Clamping Force required | $\text{N}$ |
| 2 | $A_p = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_i \times D_i)$ | Projected Area (sum over all cavities) | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 3 | $F_{toggle} = \dfrac{F_{cyl}}{2 \tan(\alpha)}$ | Mechanical advantage of symmetric toggle | $\text{N}$ |
| 4 | $MA = \dfrac{1}{2 \tan(\alpha)}$ | Mechanical Advantage ratio (dimensionless) | — |
| 5 | $\sigma_{tb} = \dfrac{F_c}{n \times A_{tb}}$ | Stress in tie bars (n = number of tie bars) | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 6 | $A_{tb} = \dfrac{\pi \times d_{tb}^2}{4}$ | Cross-sectional area of one tie bar | $\text{mm}^2$ |
| 7 | $\delta_{tb} = \dfrac{F_c \times L_{tb}}{n \times A_{tb} \times E}$ | Tie-bar elastic elongation | $\text{mm}$ |
| 8 | $V_{inj} \propto t_{fill} \times Q_{inj}$ | Fill-time relation (linked to clamp response) | — |
| 9 | $t_{cycle} = t_{close} + t_{fill} + t_{cool} + t_{open}$ | Total cycle time | $\text{s}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU:**
> - $P_c$ maximum is taken as **49 MPa** (≈ 500 bar) for unfilled thermoplastics, and **70 MPa** for glass-filled engineering plastics.
> - $SF \geq 1.1$ is **mandatory** to absorb pressure spikes and avoid flash.
> - Toggle angle $\alpha$ at lock is typically **10°–15°** to prevent self-unlocking under reverse load.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The clamping force selection directly affects:
- **Part Quality:** Insufficient force → flash, dimensional variation, sink marks adjacent to parting line.
- **Machine Capital Cost:** Clamping tonnage scales machine cost almost linearly (a 1000-ton press costs ~5× a 200-ton press).
- **Energy Efficiency:** Over-clamping wastes energy and stresses tie bars.
- **Mold Life:** Excessive clamping force accelerates platen/mold wear and may deflect the platen, causing flash on thin-wall parts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Clamping Force from First Principles

> **Problem Setup:** A 2-cavity mold produces two identical rectangular products. Each cavity has projected dimensions $W \times D = 80\,\text{mm} \times 60\,\text{mm}$. The polymer is polypropylene with a peak cavity pressure $P_c = 35\,\text{MPa}$. A safety factor of $1.2$ is mandated. Determine the required clamping force $F_c$.

**Step 1 — Identify the fundamental governing equation:**

The clamping force must counteract the **mold separation force**, which equals the cavity pressure integrated over the projected area of the cavities on the parting plane.

$$
F_c = P_c \times A_p \times SF
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the projected area of a single cavity:**

$$
A_1 = W \times D
$$

$$
A_1 = 80\,\text{mm} \times 60\,\text{mm}
$$

$$
A_1 = 4800\,\text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the total projected area for all cavities:**

$$
A_p = n \times A_1
$$

$$
A_p = 2 \times 4800\,\text{mm}^2
$$

$$
A_p = 9600\,\text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Convert area to SI units for force calculation:**

$$
A_p = 9600 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2
$$

$$
A_p = 9.6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the clamping force equation:**

$$
F_c = 35 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa} \times 9.6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^2 \times 1.2
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate step by step:**

$$
35 \times 10^6 \times 9.6 \times 10^{-3} = 33.6 \times 10^4 = 3.36 \times 10^5\,\text{N}
$$

$$
F_c = 3.36 \times 10^5 \times 1.2
$$

$$
\boxed{F_c = 4.032 \times 10^5\,\text{N} \approx 403.2\,\text{kN} \approx 41\,\text{tonnes}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on unit "tonne":** In injection molding industry, 1 tonne = 10 kN (not 9.81 kN). Hence 403.2 kN ≈ **40 tonnes** of clamping force is required.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Toggle Mechanical Advantage

> **Problem Setup:** A toggle clamp has a driving hydraulic cylinder force $F_{cyl} = 25\,\text{kN}$ and the toggle angle at lock $\alpha = 12°$. Determine the clamping force delivered by the toggle.

**Step 1 — Recall the toggle mechanical advantage formula:**

The symmetric four-bar toggle, when actuated by a single hydraulic cylinder, gives:

$$
F_{toggle} = \dfrac{F_{cyl}}{2 \tan(\alpha)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\tan(\alpha)$:**

$$
\tan(12°) = 0.2126
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
F_{toggle} = \dfrac{25 \times 10^3\,\text{N}}{2 \times 0.2126}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$
2 \times 0.2126 = 0.4252
$$

**Step 5 — Divide:**

$$
F_{toggle} = \dfrac{25000}{0.4252}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{toggle} \approx 58{,}800\,\text{N} \approx 58.8\,\text{kN}}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

A small 25 kN cylinder force becomes 58.8 kN at the toggle output — a mechanical advantage of **2.35×**. As the toggle closes fully ($\alpha \to 0°$), this advantage approaches infinity in the limit, which is precisely why a small cylinder can build up hundreds of tonnes of clamping force.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Tie Bar Stress and Elongation

> **Problem Setup:** The above machine has 4 tie bars, each of diameter $d_{tb} = 80\,\text{mm}$ and length $L_{tb} = 2000\,\text{mm}$. Steel modulus $E = 210\,\text{GPa}$. Find (a) tie-bar stress, and (b) elastic elongation at full clamp.

**(a) Stress:**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of one tie bar:**

$$
A_{tb} = \dfrac{\pi \times d_{tb}^2}{4}
$$

$$
A_{tb} = \dfrac{\pi \times (80)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 6400}{4} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55\,\text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Convert $F_c$ to Newtons (using 40 tonnes = 400 kN as round figure):**

$$
F_c = 4.0 \times 10^5\,\text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Stress per tie bar (load shared equally):**

$$
\sigma_{tb} = \dfrac{F_c}{n \times A_{tb}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{tb} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^5}{4 \times 5026.55} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^5}{20106.2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{tb} \approx 19.89\,\text{MPa}}
$$

This is well below the **yield strength** of typical tie-bar steel (~900 MPa), confirming the design is safe.

**(b) Elongation:**

$$
\delta_{tb} = \dfrac{F_c \times L_{tb}}{n \times A_{tb} \times E}
$$

$$
\delta_{tb} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^5 \times 2000}{4 \times 5026.55 \times 210 \times 10^3}
$$

$$
\delta_{tb} = \dfrac{8.0 \times 10^8}{4.222 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_{tb} \approx 0.189\,\text{mm}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This tiny elongation (≈ 0.2 mm) is precisely why tie bars must be pre-stretched during manufacture — to remove the initial elastic "give" and ensure the platen does not drift during the hold phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram type:** Mermaid cannot render an actual mechanical drawing of a toggle linkage with pivots. A functional block architecture faithfully represents the energy flow, decision logic, and force build-up — which is what examiners test.

### 4.4 Description of Toggle Clamp Working (For Sketch-Based Questions)

In KTU exams, you are often asked to *describe with a neat sketch* the working of a toggle clamp. The key elements to label on your sketch:

1. Stationary platen (left, fixed)
2. Moving platen (right, slides on tie bars)
3. Four tie bars (top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right)
4. Toggle links (two: upper and lower) joined at a central **knee joint**
5. Crosshead (yoke) connected to the rear platen
6. Clamping cylinder with piston rod
7. Hydraulic hoses (P and T ports)

The three angles to mark on the sketch:
- $\alpha$ = toggle angle (knee to horizontal) — typically marked as 0°–15° at full lock
- $\beta$ = half-angle between toggle and tie bar axis

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*Define the term "Clamping Force" in an injection molding machine. Why is a safety factor applied when calculating the required clamping force?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Clamping force is the total compressive force exerted by the clamping unit to keep the mold halves firmly closed against the **mold separation force** that arises from the cavity pressure acting on the projected area of the molded part. It is measured in **kN or tonnes** (1 tonne = 10 kN).

A safety factor ($SF = 1.1$ to $1.5$) is applied because:
- Cavity pressure can surge **instantaneously** above the steady-state value (pressure spikes).
- Process variations (melt temperature, injection speed) cause pressure fluctuations.
- Wear of the toggle linkage reduces effective clamping force over machine lifetime.
- Mechanical tolerances mean the actual delivered force is slightly less than theoretical.

Without $SF$, even a momentary pressure spike would open the mold and cause **flash defects**. **(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for SF reasoning.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*With the help of a labeled sketch, explain the function of the "tie bars" in a toggle-type clamping unit.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Tie bars (also called guide pillars or tie rods) are **four high-tensile steel rods** (sometimes two, on smaller machines) that:
1. **Guide** the moving platen in a straight line during mold open/close.
2. **Resist the clamping force** by stretching elastically — they transmit the reactive load from the moving platen to the rear platen, putting the tie bars in **tension**.
3. **Maintain alignment** of the two mold halves to prevent flash and parting-line mismatch.

They are pre-stressed during assembly to eliminate the initial slack. The stress in each tie bar is given by $\sigma_{tb} = \dfrac{F_c}{n \times A_{tb}}$ and must be well below the yield strength of the steel (typically $< 200\,\text{MPa}$ for a safety factor on yield). **(1 mark each for guiding, force resisting, alignment.)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Each Part B question is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Below are TWO full 14-mark questions — you must answer only ONE in the exam.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **toggle-type clamping unit** in an injection molding machine. Clearly explain the role of the **toggle angle** $\alpha$ in clamping force generation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-cavity mold produces a circular disk of diameter $120\,\text{mm}$ from ABS plastic. The peak cavity pressure is $40\,\text{MPa}$. A safety factor of $1.25$ is required. Calculate:
  (i) The required clamping force in kN.
  (ii) The number of **40 mm diameter** tie bars needed if the allowable tensile stress in tie-bar steel is $100\,\text{MPa}$.
  (iii) The mechanical advantage of the toggle if the driving cylinder delivers $F_{cyl} = 30\,\text{kN}$ at a lock angle of $\alpha = 10°$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Working (7 marks):**

The toggle clamping unit consists of:
- A **stationary platen** (fixed) and a **moving platen** (slides on tie bars).
- Four **tie bars** connecting the stationary platen to a rear platen, all passing through the moving platen.
- Two **toggle links** forming a four-bar linkage: one end pinned to the moving platen, the other to a **crosshead (yoke)**, with a central **knee joint**.
- A **clamping cylinder** with a piston rod attached to the knee joint.
- A **hydraulic power pack** with directional control valves.

**Working cycle (3 marks):**
1. **Closing stroke:** Oil enters the rear of the cylinder, extending the piston rod, rotating the toggle links from a folded position. The platen moves forward at high speed (~50 mm/s).
2. **Locking stroke:** As the mold halves touch, the toggle continues to straighten. The toggle angle $\alpha$ (between the link and the tie-bar axis) decreases from ~90° toward 0°.
3. **Force build-up:** The mechanical advantage $MA = 1/(2\tan\alpha)$ rises dramatically. A small cylinder force becomes a massive clamping force.
4. **Hold phase:** The valve closes; the mold resists injection pressure.
5. **Opening stroke:** Oil reverses, the toggle folds, the platen retracts.

**Role of toggle angle (2 marks):** At $\alpha \to 0°$, $\tan(\alpha) \to 0$, so $MA \to \infty$. This is why a compact 30 kN cylinder can deliver 3000 kN of clamping force. However, $\alpha$ at full lock is kept at **10°–15°** to prevent the toggle from "self-unlocking" backwards if cavity pressure tries to push the mold open.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Clamping Force:**

$$
A_p = \dfrac{\pi \times D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (120)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 14400}{4} = 3600\pi \approx 11{,}310\,\text{mm}^2
$$

Convert to m²: $A_p = 11{,}310 \times 10^{-6} = 1.131 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{m}^2$

$$
F_c = P_c \times A_p \times SF = 40 \times 10^6 \times 1.131 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.25
$$

$$
F_c = 40 \times 10^6 \times 1.41375 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_c = 5.655 \times 10^5\,\text{N} \approx 565.5\,\text{kN} \approx 56.6\,\text{tonnes}}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Number of Tie Bars:**

Area of one 40 mm tie bar:

$$
A_{tb} = \dfrac{\pi \times (40)^2}{4} = 400\pi \approx 1256.64\,\text{mm}^2
$$

Allowable force per tie bar:

$$
F_{tb,allow} = \sigma_{allow} \times A_{tb} = 100 \times 1256.64 = 125{,}664\,\text{N} \approx 125.66\,\text{kN}
$$

Number of tie bars (round up to next integer):

$$
n = \left\lceil \dfrac{F_c}{F_{tb,allow}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{565.5}{125.66} \right\rceil = \lceil 4.50 \rceil = 5
$$

$$
\boxed{n = 5 \text{ tie bars (4 is insufficient since 4×125.66 = 502.6 kN < 565.5 kN)}}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Mechanical Advantage:**

$$
MA = \dfrac{1}{2 \tan(\alpha)} = \dfrac{1}{2 \tan(10°)} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 0.1763} = \dfrac{1}{0.3526}
$$

$$
\boxed{MA \approx 2.836}
$$

Verification: $F_{toggle} = MA \times F_{cyl} = 2.836 \times 30 = 85.08\,\text{kN}$. **(2 marks + 1 mark for verification)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, Module 1]**

**(a)** Compare the **Toggle (Mechanical)**, **Direct Hydraulic**, and **Hydromechanical** clamping systems in terms of construction, working principle, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-cavity mold produces ABS containers. Each cavity has projected area $25\,\text{cm}^2$. Injection pressure at the cavity is $35\,\text{MPa}$. The machine is fitted with a toggle clamp whose drive cylinder exerts $F_{cyl} = 50\,\text{kN}$ at full lock. The toggle angle is $\alpha = 8°$. The tie bars are 4 in number, each of diameter $90\,\text{mm}$ and length $1800\,\text{mm}$ (Steel $E = 210\,\text{GPa}$). Calculate:
  (i) Required clamping tonnage.
  (ii) Toggle clamping force delivered.
  (iii) Tie-bar elongation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Feature | Toggle (Mechanical) | Direct Hydraulic | Hydromechanical |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prime Mover** | Small hydraulic cylinder + toggle links | Large hydraulic cylinder directly behind platen | Small cylinder + toggle for close, large cylinder for lock |
| **Force Mechanism** | Mechanical advantage of toggle | Pascal's law, direct pressure on piston | Combined: toggle for partial lock, hydraulic for full lock |
| **Speed of Close** | Fast (toggle) | Slower (limited by oil flow) | Fastest |
| **Energy at Lock** | Low (mechanical lock, no oil flow) | High (continuous oil pressure to hold) | Medium |
| **Force Control** | Stepless via position, but not as precise | Very precise, stepless | Precise |
| **Wear** | Toggle knuckle joint wears | Minimal (cylinder only) | Both systems wear |
| **Use Case** | Most common — general purpose | Large tonnage machines (>2000 t) | High-speed packaging industry |
| **Limitation** | Joint wear, complex geometry | Noisy, high oil temp, slow | Most expensive, complex controls |

**(1 mark per row × 5 rows = 5 marks + 2 marks for synthesis/explanation.)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Required Clamping Tonnage:**

$$
A_p = n \times A_1 = 4 \times 25\,\text{cm}^2 = 100\,\text{cm}^2 = 10{,}000\,\text{mm}^2 = 10^{-2}\,\text{m}^2
$$

Assume $SF = 1.2$ (standard):

$$
F_c = 35 \times 10^6 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.2 = 4.2 \times 10^5\,\text{N} = 420\,\text{kN}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_c = 420\,\text{kN} = 42\,\text{tonnes}}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Toggle Clamping Force:**

$$
F_{toggle} = \dfrac{F_{cyl}}{2\tan(\alpha)} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{2 \times \tan(8°)} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{2 \times 0.1405} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{0.2810}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{toggle} \approx 177{,}936\,\text{N} \approx 177.9\,\text{kN} = 17.8\,\text{tonnes}}
$$

**Note:** This is less than the required 420 kN — the machine is under-clamped for this mold. The student should comment on this. **(2 marks + 1 mark for interpretation.)**

**(iii) Tie-bar Elongation:**

$$
A_{tb} = \dfrac{\pi \times (90)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 8100}{4} = 2025\pi \approx 6361.7\,\text{mm}^2
$$

$$
\delta_{tb} = \dfrac{F_c \times L_{tb}}{n \times A_{tb} \times E} = \dfrac{4.2 \times 10^5 \times 1800}{4 \times 6361.7 \times 210 \times 10^3}
$$

$$
\delta_{tb} = \dfrac{7.56 \times 10^8}{5.344 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_{tb} \approx 0.1415\,\text{mm}}
$$

**(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Forgetting to convert mm² → m² when computing $F_c$ in Newtons. Always verify your final force is in **kN or tonnes** before writing the answer.
> 2. **"1 tonne = 10 kN" rule:** Many students wrongly use 9.81 kN/tonne. In injection molding industry, the convention is **1 tonne = 10 kN** for simplicity.
> 3. **Projected area = 2D shadow:** Examiners often give a 3D part (e.g., a box) and students wrongly sum the four side walls. Only the **shadow on the parting plane** counts.
> 4. **Safety factor forgotten:** A very common 1-mark loss. Always multiply by $SF \geq 1.1$.
> 5. **Toggle angle in degrees vs radians:** Trigonometric functions in calculators default to DEGREES, but if you accidentally enter radians you'll get an answer off by a factor of ~57. Always check the mode.
> 6. **Self-locking condition:** In descriptive questions, students forget to mention that $\alpha$ at full lock must be **> 0°** (typically 10°–15°) to prevent the toggle from being pushed back open by cavity pressure.

---

### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Clamping Unit Function:** Holds mold closed, opens mold, ejects part.
- ✅ **Clamping Force Equation:** $F_c = P_c \times A_p \times SF$ (master this!).
- ✅ **Typical $P_c$:** 30–50 MPa (unfilled), up to 70 MPa (filled plastics).
- ✅ **Safety Factor:** $SF = 1.1$ to $1.5$ — **never omit it**.
- ✅ **Three Strokes:** Closing (fast) → Locking (slow, high force) → Opening + Ejection.
- ✅ **Toggle Mechanical Advantage:** $MA = 1 / (2\tan\alpha)$ — diverges as $\alpha \to 0°$.
- ✅ **Lock Angle:** Held at **10°–15°** to prevent self-unlocking.
- ✅ **Tie Bars:** Resist clamping force via **tension**; area $A_{tb} = \pi d^2/4$.
- ✅ **Tie-bar Stress Limit:** $< 200\,\text{MPa}$ (industry standard with safety on yield).
- ✅ **Tie-bar Elongation:** Small (<0.5 mm) but pre-stretched at manufacture.
- ✅ **Clamp Types:** Toggle (most common), Direct Hydraulic (large tonnage), Hydromechanical (high speed), All-Electric (clean, efficient).
- ✅ **1 tonne = 10 kN** (molding industry convention).
- ✅ **Flash Defect Cause:** $F_c < P_c \times A_p$ — direct consequence of insufficient clamp.
- ✅ **Cycle Time:** $t_{cycle} = t_{close} + t_{fill} + t_{cool} + t_{open}$.
- ✅ **KTU Frequently Asked:** (1) Toggle sketch with labels, (2) Compare clamp types, (3) Calculate $F_c$ from projected area, (4) Derive mechanical advantage, (5) Tie-bar stress check.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Machine Hydraulics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Machine Hydraulics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine Hydraulics (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):**
> Machine Hydraulics refers to the engineered system of **liquids under pressure** used to generate, transmit, and control **force, motion, and power** in polymer processing machinery. In the context of **PCPOT503**, machine hydraulics specifically classifies the hydraulic circuits, pumps, valves, and actuators that drive the functional movements of injection molding machines (IMM), blow molding machines, hydraulic presses, and auxiliary polymer equipment.

The **hydraulic system** operates on **Pascal's Law** (1663), which states that pressure applied to a confined incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid. In polymer machinery, this principle is exploited to generate the **clamping tonnage (ranging from 50 kN to 12,000 kN)**, the **injection pressure (typically 1,000 bar to 2,500 bar)**, and the **ejection force** required for repeatable plastic part production.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Muscular System" of a Machine

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a polymer machine as a human body:**
> - The **hydraulic pump** = the **heart** (pumps the lifeblood, i.e., oil).
> - The **hydraulic oil** = the **blood** (transmits force and energy).
> - The **valves** = the **brain's nerve signals** (direct flow to where it is needed).
> - The **hydraulic cylinder** = the **muscle** (converts fluid energy into linear motion).
> - The **reservoir (tank)** = the **lungs** (stores and cools the fluid).

Just as a body cannot lift a heavy object without coordinated muscle action, an **injection molding machine cannot close a 1,000-ton mold** without a precisely classified and synchronized hydraulic circuit. The "classification" essentially tells us **what type of muscle action** is being used: a quick jab (high-speed low-pressure), a slow crush (low-speed high-pressure), or a balanced grip (proportional control).

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters in Polymer Machinery

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Standard Operating Pressure Ranges (KTU-referenced values):**
> - **Low-Pressure Hydraulics:** Up to **50 bar (5 MPa)** — used in clamping circuits and cooling lines.
> - **Medium-Pressure Hydraulics:** **50 bar – 250 bar (5–25 MPa)** — used in general machine tool hydraulics.
> - **High-Pressure Hydraulics:** **Above 250 bar, up to 2,500 bar (25–250 MPa)** — used in **injection units** of IMMs.

Without a clear classification framework, an engineer cannot select the correct pump, valve, or actuator for a specific polymer process. For example, using a low-pressure gear pump in a high-pressure injection circuit would result in **cavitation, pressure ripple, and catastrophic seal failure** within hours of operation.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Pascal's Law** | Pressure in a confined fluid acts equally in all directions. |
| **Hydrostatic Transmission** | Power transmission using fluid under pressure (no moving fluid, only pressure). |
| **Hydrodynamic Transmission** | Power transmission using fluid momentum (e.g., torque converters). |
| **Open-Center Circuit** | Pump flow returns to tank when valves are in neutral position. |
| **Closed-Center Circuit** | Pump flow is blocked when valves are neutral; requires a pressure-compensated pump. |
| **Load-Sensing (LS) System** | Pump delivers only the flow required by the load, saving energy. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Machine Hydraulics

The classification of machine hydraulics in polymer machinery is **multi-axial** — meaning a single system can be classified under several criteria simultaneously. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes classification along **four primary axes**:

### Axis 1: Classification by Energy Conversion Function

- **Pump (Hydraulic Generator):** Converts mechanical energy (from an electric motor) into hydraulic energy (pressure × flow). The standard efficiency ranges from **85% to 95%** for fixed-displacement units and **90% to 98%** for variable-displacement units.
- **Motor (Hydraulic Actuator-Rotary):** Converts hydraulic energy back into rotary mechanical energy. Used in **screw drives** of some specialized IMMs and in **hydraulic clamping rotary tables**.
- **Cylinder (Hydraulic Actuator-Linear):** Converts hydraulic energy into linear force and stroke. Used in **clamping rams, injection rams, ejection rams, and core-pull cylinders**.

### Axis 2: Classification by Circuit Topology

- **Open-Center (Open-Loop) Circuit:** Pump continuously delivers flow; unused oil returns to tank through the center position of the directional valve. Simple, cheap, but **30% less energy efficient** than closed-center.
- **Closed-Center (Closed-Loop) Circuit:** Pump output is connected directly to actuators; oil returns from actuators to the pump inlet (not to the tank). Used in **high-response injection circuits** where fast acceleration of the screw is critical.
- **Load-Sensing (LS) Circuit:** A pressure-feedback signal from the load adjusts the pump displacement to match demand. **Saves up to 60–80%** of energy compared to fixed displacement systems.
- **Servo-Proportional Circuit:** Uses electronic proportional valves with closed-loop position/pressure feedback. Standard in modern **all-electric-hybrid IMMs**.

### Axis 3: Classification by Working Pressure Range

- **Low-Pressure Systems (≤ 50 bar):** Cooling water hydraulics, clamping safety circuits, lubrication.
- **Medium-Pressure Systems (50 – 250 bar):** Mold clamping, ejection, core-pulling.
- **High-Pressure Systems (250 – 2,500 bar):** Plastic injection, melt accumulation, accumulator-assisted ejection.

### Axis 4: Classification by Component Type

| Sub-class | Component | Typical Polymer-Machinery Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Positive Displacement Pumps** | Gear, Vane, Piston | Main hydraulic power unit (HPU) |
| **Directional Control Valves** | 4/3, 4/2 solenoid valves | Mold open/close, injection forward/reverse |
| **Pressure Control Valves** | Relief, Sequence, Reducing | Pressure limiting, sequence logic |
| **Flow Control Valves** | Throttle, Pressure-compensated, Proportional | Speed control of clamp & injection |
| **Hydraulic Accumulators** | Bladder, Piston, Diaphragm | Energy storage for peak injection demands |
| **Filters & Coolers** | Return-line, Pressure-line | Fluid cleanliness (target ISO 4406: 18/16/13) |

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Classification Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Logic of Classification:**

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Function:** Is the system generating force (cylinder), rotation (motor), or pressure (intensifier)? This places it in **Actuator vs. Generator** category.
2. **Step 2 — Identify the Pressure Range:** Check the maximum working pressure. This dictates **pump type** (gear for low pressure, piston for high pressure).
3. **Step 3 — Identify the Control Philosophy:** Is the system open-loop (simple) or closed-loop (precise)? Modern IMMs prefer **servo-proportional closed-loop** for repeatability within **±0.01 mm**.
4. **Step 4 — Identify the Energy Efficiency Requirement:** For high-tonnage machines (>1,000 tons), **load-sensing variable pumps** are mandatory to keep energy consumption below **0.4 kWh/kg** of processed polymer.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Examinations** (all symbols are board-exam standard):

| Equation No. | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure = Force / Area | $P$ in Pa, $F$ in N, $A$ in m² |
| 2 | $F = P \times A$ | Force generated by cylinder | N |
| 3 | $Q = A \times v$ | Volumetric flow rate | m³/s |
| 4 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Actuator velocity | m/s |
| 5 | $P_{hyd} = \Delta p \times Q$ | Hydraulic power | Watts |
| 6 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 7 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{theoretical}}$ | Mechanical efficiency | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 8 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}$ | Overall pump efficiency | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 9 | $V_d = \dfrac{Q}{n}$ | Pump displacement volume | m³/rev |
| 10 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{A_{platen}}$ | Clamping pressure | Pa |
| 11 | $E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \Delta p \times V$ | Accumulator energy | Joules |
| 12 | $T = \dfrac{\Delta p \times V_d}{2\pi}$ | Hydraulic motor torque | N·m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Absolute Values:** In all derived expressions, absolute values are written using `\vert` (e.g., $P = \vert F \vert / A$) to prevent markdown table parsing conflicts. Examiners prefer **SI units throughout** — never mix bar, psi, and MPa in a single answer.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Injection Molding Machines (IMMs):** The clamping unit uses **medium-pressure hydraulics (140–200 bar)**; the injection unit uses **high-pressure hydraulics (1,400–2,500 bar)** intensifiers.
- **Hydraulic Presses for Compression Molding:** Use **low-pressure, high-flow circuits** (50–100 bar) for thermoset molding of phenol-formaldehyde and melamine parts.
- **Blow Molding Machines:** Use **proportional hydraulics** for parison thickness control, with **accumulator-assisted peak flows** up to **600 L/min**.
- **Sheet Extrusion Lines:** Use **closed-loop hydraulic screen changers** that maintain constant melt pressure (typically **150–250 bar**) during filter indexing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Force Generated by a Hydraulic Cylinder

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Derive the relationship between hydraulic pressure, piston area, and generated clamping force — the most frequently asked derivation in KTU PCPOT503 exams.

### Given
- A double-acting hydraulic cylinder with piston diameter $D$ (m).
- A rod diameter $d$ (m) on the rod side.
- Supply pressure $P$ (Pa).

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the Effective Areas:**

The piston has two effective areas because the rod displaces oil on one side.

$$
A_{full} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2}
$$

$$
A_{annulus} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times \left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Express Force on the Piston (Cap-End Side):**

When pressurized oil enters the cap-end (full bore) side, the force pushes the piston outward.

$$
F_{extend} = P \times A_{full} = P \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Express Force on the Piston (Rod-End Side):**

When pressurized oil enters the rod-end, the effective area is the annulus (because the rod occupies part of the bore).

$$
F_{retract} = P \times A_{annulus} = P \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times \left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Determine the Force Ratio (Critical for Clamp Cylinder Selection):**

$$
\frac{F_{retract}}{F_{extend}} = \frac{D^{2} - d^{2}}{D^{2}} = 1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{2}
$$

This ratio is **always less than 1**, meaning a cylinder produces **less force during retraction** than during extension. For KTU problems, typical rod-to-bore ratio is $\dfrac{d}{D} \approx 0.5$ to $0.7$, giving a retraction efficiency of **51% to 75%**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pump Flow Rate from Motor Speed and Displacement

> [!NOTE]
> **Objective:** Show how theoretical and actual pump flows are related — used in accumulator sizing and cycle-time calculations.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Theoretical Flow Definition:**

For a positive-displacement pump, each revolution displaces a fixed volume $V_d$ of oil. With $n$ revolutions per second, the theoretical flow is:

$$
Q_{th} = V_d \times n
$$

**Step 2 — Account for Volumetric Losses:**

Due to internal leakage (clearance between gears, vanes, or pistons), the actual flow is lower:

$$
Q_{act} = Q_{th} \times \eta_{vol}
$$

**Step 3 — Express Volumetric Efficiency Explicitly:**

$$
\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{leak}}{V_d \times n}
$$

Where $Q_{leak}$ is the leakage flow rate, typically proportional to pressure (higher pressure → more leakage → lower efficiency).

**Step 4 — Pressure-Dependent Efficiency Model (Linear Approximation):**

For exam purposes, a common linear model is:

$$
\eta_{vol} = \eta_{vol,\,0} - k \times \Delta p
$$

Where $\eta_{vol,\,0}$ is the no-load volumetric efficiency (close to 1.0) and $k$ is the leakage coefficient.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hydraulic Power Transmission Chain

**Step 1 — Input Mechanical Power to Pump:**

$$
P_{mech,\,in} = T_{shaft} \times \omega = T_{shaft} \times 2\pi n
$$

**Step 2 — Hydraulic Power Generated:**

$$
P_{hyd} = \Delta p \times Q_{act}
$$

**Step 3 — Output Mechanical Power at Cylinder/Motor:**

$$
P_{mech,\,out} = F \times v \quad \text{(for cylinder)}
$$

$$
P_{mech,\,out} = T \times \omega \quad \text{(for motor)}
$$

**Step 4 — Overall Transmission Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{mech,\,out}}{P_{mech,\,in}} = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Examples (Worked-Out KTU Style)

### Example 1: Clamping Force Calculation

**Problem:** A 500-ton IMM uses a hydraulic cylinder with piston diameter $D = 250 \text{ mm}$ and rod diameter $d = 140 \text{ mm}$. The supply pressure is $P = 160 \text{ bar}$. Calculate the maximum clamping force and the retraction force.

**Solution — Step 1 — Convert Units:**

$$
D = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.250 \text{ m}, \quad d = 140 \text{ mm} = 0.140 \text{ m}, \quad P = 160 \text{ bar} = 16 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}
$$

**Solution — Step 2 — Full Bore Area:**

$$
A_{full} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.250)^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0625 = 0.04909 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Solution — Step 3 — Extension Force:**

$$
F_{extend} = 16 \times 10^{6} \times 0.04909 = 7.85 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} = 785 \text{ kN}
$$

**Solution — Step 4 — Annulus Area:**

$$
A_{annulus} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times \left((0.250)^{2} - (0.140)^{2}\right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.0625 - 0.0196) = 0.03373 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Solution — Step 5 — Retraction Force:**

$$
F_{retract} = 16 \times 10^{6} \times 0.03373 = 5.40 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} = 540 \text{ kN}
$$

**Solution — Step 6 — Ratio Check:**

$$
\frac{F_{retract}}{F_{extend}} = \frac{540}{785} = 0.688 \approx 1 - (0.56)^{2} = 0.6864 \;\; \checkmark
$$

### Example 2: Pump Sizing for Cycle Time

**Problem:** An injection cylinder with bore $D = 80 \text{ mm}$ and stroke $L = 250 \text{ mm}$ must complete injection in $t = 0.5 \text{ s}$. Calculate the required pump flow rate.

**Solution — Step 1 — Cylinder Volume:**

$$
V_{cyl} = A \times L = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.080)^{2} \times 0.250 = 1.257 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Solution — Step 2 — Required Flow:**

$$
Q = \frac{V_{cyl}}{t} = \frac{1.257 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5} = 2.51 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} = 151 \text{ L/min}
$$

**Solution — Step 3 — Account for Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_{vol} = 0.95$):**

$$
Q_{pump} = \frac{Q}{\eta_{vol}} = \frac{151}{0.95} = 159 \text{ L/min}
$$
