### Example 3: Accumulator Sizing for Peak Flow

**Problem:** A machine requires a peak injection flow of $Q_{peak} = 300 \text{ L/min}$ for $t = 3 \text{ s}$. The pump supplies only $Q_{pump} = 200 \text{ L/min}$. The pressure drop in the accumulator is from $P_1 = 210 \text{ bar}$ to $P_2 = 175 \text{ bar}$. Size the accumulator.

**Solution — Step 1 — Deficit Flow:**

$$
Q_{deficit} = Q_{peak} - Q_{pump} = 300 - 200 = 100 \text{ L/min} = 1.667 \text{ L/s}
$$

**Solution — Step 2 — Volume of Oil Released by Accumulator (Isothermal Process for Hydraulic Oil):**

$$
V_{oil} = Q_{deficit} \times t = 1.667 \times 3 = 5.0 \text{ L}
$$

**Solution — Step 3 — Apply Boyle's Law (Pre-charged with Nitrogen at $P_0 = 0.9 \times P_1$):**

$$
P_0 \times V_0 = P_2 \times (V_0 - V_{oil}) \quad \text{(mass conservation)}
$$

Where $P_0 = 0.9 \times 210 = 189 \text{ bar}$ (standard pre-charge rule).

**Solution — Step 4 — Solve for $V_0$:**

$$
V_0 = \frac{P_2 \times V_{oil}}{P_0 - P_2} = \frac{175 \times 5.0}{189 - 175} = \frac{875}{14} = 62.5 \text{ L}
$$

**Solution — Step 5 — Final Answer:** A **62.5 L nitrogen pre-charged bladder accumulator** is required.

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Gear Pump | Vane Pump | Piston Pump |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure range | 100–200 bar | 150–210 bar | 350–700 bar |
| Typical $\eta_{vol}$ | 0.85–0.92 | 0.90–0.95 | 0.95–0.98 |
| Noise level | High | Medium | Low |
| Cost (relative) | 1× | 2× | 4× |
| Best suited for | Clamping circuit | General machine tool | Injection circuit |
| KTU exam answer hint | "Low cost, low pressure" | "Moderate, low noise" | "High pressure, high efficiency" |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchical Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[MACHINE HYDRAULICS] --> B1[By Energy Function]
    A --> B2[By Circuit Topology]
    A --> B3[By Pressure Range]
    A --> B4[By Component Type]

    B1 --> C1[Pump - Generator]
    B1 --> C2[Motor - Rotary Actuator]
    B1 --> C3[Cylinder - Linear Actuator]

    B2 --> D1[Open Center Circuit]
    B2 --> D2[Closed Center Circuit]
    B2 --> D3[Load Sensing LS Circuit]
    B2 --> D4[Servo Proportional Circuit]

    B3 --> E1[Low Pressure up to 50 bar]
    B3 --> E2[Medium Pressure 50 to 250 bar]
    B3 --> E3[High Pressure above 250 bar]

    B4 --> F1[Gear Pump]
    B4 --> F2[Vane Pump]
    B4 --> F3[Piston Pump]
    B4 --> F4[DCV Directional Valve]
    B4 --> F5[PCV Pressure Control Valve]
    B4 --> F6[FCV Flow Control Valve]
    B4 --> F7[Accumulator]
    B4 --> F8[Filter and Cooler]

    C1 --> G1[Gear Vane Piston]
    C3 --> G2[Single Acting Double Acting]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Polymer Machine Hydraulic System

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph POWER_UNIT
        MTR[Electric Motor] --> PMP[Variable Displacement Piston Pump]
        PMP --> RSV[Reservoir Tank]
        RSV --> PMP
    end

    subgraph CONTROL_BLOCK
        PMP --> PRV[Pressure Relief Valve]
        PRV --> DCV1[4 by 3 Directional Valve - Clamp]
        DCV1 --> FCV1[Flow Control Valve - Clamp Speed]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATION_BLOCK
        FCV1 --> CLM[Clamping Cylinder]
        DCV2[4 by 3 Directional Valve - Injection] --> INJ[Injection Cylinder]
        ACC[Bladder Accumulator] --> DCV2
        DCV3[4 by 3 Directional Valve - Eject] --> EJC[Ejection Cylinder]
    end

    subgraph FEEDBACK_BLOCK
        CLM --> PS1[Linear Position Sensor]
        INJ --> PS2[Linear Position Sensor]
        EJC --> PS3[Linear Position Sensor]
        PS1 --> ECU[Machine Controller PLC]
        PS2 --> ECU
        PS3 --> ECU
        ECU --> DCV1
        ECU --> DCV2
        ECU --> DCV3
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Open-Center vs Closed-Center Circuit Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph OPEN_CENTER_SYSTEM
        OC_PMP[Pump] -->|continuous flow| OC_DCV[DCV - Center to Tank]
        OC_DCV -->|neutral returns oil| OC_TANK[Reservoir]
        OC_DCV -->|actuated position| OC_CYL[Cylinder]
    end

    subgraph CLOSED_CENTER_SYSTEM
        CC_PMP[Variable Pump] -->|zero flow at neutral| CC_DCV[DCV - Center Blocked]
        CC_DCV -->|actuated position| CC_CYL[Cylinder]
        CC_CYL -->|return line| CC_PMP
        CC_LS[Load Sensing Signal] --> CC_PMP
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pressure Stage Classification with Typical Components

```mermaid
graph TD
    SYS[Machine Hydraulic System] --> LP[Low Pressure Stage - 50 bar]
    SYS --> MP[Medium Pressure Stage - 200 bar]
    SYS --> HP[High Pressure Stage - 2500 bar]

    LP --> LP_APP[Application: Clamp Safety Cooling]
    LP --> LP_PMP[Gear Pump]

    MP --> MP_APP[Application: Clamping Ejection]
    MP --> MP_PMP[Vane Pump]
    MP --> MP_VAL[Directional and Flow Valves]

    HP --> HP_APP[Application: Injection Melt Accumulation]
    HP --> HP_PMP[Radial Piston Pump]
    HP --> HP_VAL[Servo Proportional Valve]
    HP --> HP_ACC[Hydraulic Intensifier]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Injection Cycle Hydraulic Operation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Operator
    participant PLC as Machine Controller
    participant PumpVar as Variable Pump
    participant InjDCV as Injection DCV
    participant InjCyl as Injection Cylinder
    participant Sensor as Pressure Sensor

    Operator->>PLC: Start Injection Command
    PLC->>PumpVar: Set Pressure 1800 bar
    PLC->>InjDCV: Energize Extend Position
    InjDCV->>InjCyl: High Pressure Oil Flow
    InjCyl->>Sensor: Pressure Feedback 1750 bar
    Sensor->>PLC: Pressure OK Signal
    PLC->>InjDCV: Hold Position V-Position
    PLC->>InjDCV: Energize Retract After Hold
    InjDCV->>InjCyl: Low Pressure Return
    InjCyl-->>PLC: Position Zero Reached
    PLC->>Operator: Cycle Complete Signal
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]

**Q: Classify machine hydraulics used in polymer processing machinery based on working pressure range. Give two examples of components used in each category.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Machine hydraulics in polymer machinery are classified into three pressure categories:

1. **Low-Pressure Hydraulics (≤ 50 bar / 5 MPa):** Used in clamping safety circuits, cooling lines, and lubrication systems. **Examples:** Gear pumps, return-line filters. (1 Mark)

2. **Medium-Pressure Hydraulics (50 – 250 bar / 5 – 25 MPa):** Used in mold clamping, ejection, and core-pulling operations. **Examples:** Vane pumps, 4/3 directional control valves, flow control valves. (1 Mark)

3. **High-Pressure Hydraulics (> 250 bar / 25 MPa, up to 2,500 bar):** Used in plastic injection, melt accumulation, and accumulator-assisted ejection. **Examples:** Radial piston pumps, servo-proportional valves, hydraulic accumulators. (1 Mark)

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** Always state the **upper limit in bar AND MPa** — examiners look for unit clarity. Avoid writing "psi" in KTU answers.

### Question A.2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]

**Q: Differentiate between an open-center and a closed-center hydraulic circuit. State one polymer-machinery application where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Open-Center Circuit | Closed-Center Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| Pump type | Fixed displacement | Variable displacement |
| Neutral position | Oil returns to tank | Pump unloads (zero delivery) |
| Energy efficiency | Low (continuous flow) | High (no flow at neutral) |
| Application | Clamping circuits in small IMMs | High-speed injection circuits in large IMMs |

Open-center circuits are preferred in **small-capacity injection molding machines** where the cost of a variable pump is not justified. (1 Mark)

Closed-center circuits are preferred in **high-tonnage (>1,000 ton) IMMs** where the injection unit requires fast response and high energy efficiency. (1 Mark)

The third mark is awarded for the clear comparative statement. (1 Mark)

## 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### CHOICE 1: QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question Dec 2024]**

> **Q: (a)** Explain in detail the classification of hydraulic pumps used in polymer processing machinery. Compare gear, vane, and piston pumps with respect to working pressure, volumetric efficiency, noise, and typical application. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q: (b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder of bore 200 mm and rod diameter 110 mm is connected to a hydraulic supply at 140 bar. Calculate (i) the extension force, (ii) the retraction force, and (iii) the velocity of extension if the pump flow is 120 L/min. Assume $\eta_{vol} = 0.95$. **(7 Marks)**

### MODEL ANSWER (a) — 7 Marks

**Classification of Hydraulic Pumps (1 Mark):**
Hydraulic pumps used in polymer machinery are classified as **positive displacement pumps**, which deliver a fixed volume of fluid per revolution. The three main sub-classes are:

1. **Gear Pumps (External & Internal):** 2 Marks
2. **Vane Pumps (Fixed & Variable):** 2 Marks
3. **Piston Pumps (Axial & Radial):** 2 Marks

**Comparison Table (built into the narrative):**

**Gear Pumps:** Use two meshing gears to displace oil. Simple, cheap, robust. Working pressure: 100–200 bar. $\eta_{vol}$: 0.85–0.92. Noise level: high. Typical use: clamping circuits of small IMMs.

**Vane Pumps:** Use a slotted rotor with sliding vanes. Smoother flow, moderate pressure capability. Working pressure: 150–210 bar. $\eta_{vol}$: 0.90–0.95. Noise level: medium. Typical use: medium-tonnage IMMs, hydraulic presses.

**Piston Pumps:** Use reciprocating pistons in a cylinder block. Highest pressure and efficiency. Working pressure: 350–700 bar. $\eta_{vol}$: 0.95–0.98. Noise level: low. Typical use: injection units of high-tonnage IMMs, accumulator charging circuits.

### MODEL ANSWER (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
$D = 0.200 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.110 \text{ m}, \quad P = 140 \text{ bar} = 14 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}, \quad Q_{pump} = 120 \text{ L/min}, \quad \eta_{vol} = 0.95$

**(i) Extension Force (2 Marks):**

$$
A_{full} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.200)^{2} = 0.03142 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
F_{extend} = P \times A_{full} = 14 \times 10^{6} \times 0.03142 = 4.40 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} = 440 \text{ kN}
$$

**(ii) Retraction Force (2 Marks):**

$$
A_{annulus} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times \left((0.200)^{2} - (0.110)^{2}\right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.0400 - 0.0121) = 0.02195 \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
F_{retract} = 14 \times 10^{6} \times 0.02195 = 3.07 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} = 307 \text{ kN}
$$

**(iii) Extension Velocity (3 Marks):**

$$
Q_{act} = Q_{pump} \times \eta_{vol} = 120 \times 0.95 = 114 \text{ L/min} = 1.90 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

$$
v_{extend} = \frac{Q_{act}}{A_{full}} = \frac{1.90 \times 10^{-3}}{0.03142} = 0.0605 \text{ m/s} = 60.5 \text{ mm/s}
$$

### CHOICE 2: QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question May 2024]**

> **Q: (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a hydraulic power unit used in an injection molding machine. Identify the role of each block. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q: (b)** A hydraulic accumulator pre-charged with nitrogen at 90 bar is used to deliver a peak flow of 150 L/min for 4 seconds. The system pressure drops from 200 bar to 160 bar during the discharge. Assuming isothermal conditions, calculate (i) the volume of oil delivered, and (ii) the size of the accumulator (nitrogen pre-charge volume). **(7 Marks)**

### MODEL ANSWER (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram Description (4 Marks):**

The functional architecture of an IMM hydraulic system consists of four main blocks:

1. **Power Unit Block:** Contains the electric motor, variable-displacement pump, and reservoir. Converts electrical energy into hydraulic energy.
2. **Control Block:** Contains pressure relief valves, directional control valves (4/3), and flow control valves. Directs the oil to the required actuator at the required pressure and flow rate.
3. **Actuation Block:** Contains the clamping cylinder, injection cylinder, ejection cylinder, and accumulator. Converts hydraulic energy into mechanical motion/force.
4. **Feedback Block:** Contains pressure sensors, position sensors, and the PLC controller. Provides closed-loop control for repeatability.

**Identification of Roles (3 Marks):** Each block's function is mapped to a specific polymer process — e.g., the clamping cylinder generates the tonnage to hold the mold closed against injection pressure; the accumulator provides peak flow during the injection phase; the PLC coordinates the entire sequence within a cycle time of typically 15–60 seconds.

### MODEL ANSWER (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $P_0 = 90 \text{ bar}$ (pre-charge), $P_1 = 200 \text{ bar}$ (max), $P_2 = 160 \text{ bar}$ (min), $Q_{peak} = 150 \text{ L/min}$, $t = 4 \text{ s}$.

**(i) Volume of Oil Delivered (3 Marks):**

$$
V_{oil} = Q_{peak} \times t = 150 \times \frac{4}{60} = 10 \text{ L}
$$

**(ii) Accumulator Size (4 Marks):**

Apply Boyle's Law for isothermal nitrogen compression:

$$
P_0 \times V_0 = P_2 \times (V_0 - V_{oil})
$$

Where $V_0$ is the nitrogen pre-charge volume (accumulator size).

Substituting:

$$
90 \times V_0 = 160 \times (V_0 - 10)
$$

$$
90 \times V_0 = 160 \times V_0 - 1600
$$

$$
1600 = 70 \times V_0
$$

$$
V_0 = \frac{1600}{70} = 22.86 \text{ L}
$$

**Result:** A **23-liter nitrogen pre-charged accumulator** is required.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU PCPOT503 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap (–2 marks typical):** Writing $F = P \times A$ with $P$ in bar and $A$ in mm² without unit conversion. Always convert: **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa**, and area must be in **m²**.
> 2. **Confusing $A_{full}$ and $A_{annulus}$ (–1 to –2 marks):** The extension force uses the **full bore area**; the retraction force uses the **annulus area**. Many students swap them.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply $\eta_{vol}$ (–1 mark):** When asked for "actual" flow or "actual" force, always multiply by the efficiency.
> 4. **Pre-charge Pressure Rule (–2 marks):** For nitrogen pre-charge in accumulators, the rule of thumb is **$P_0 \approx 0.9 \times P_{min}$** (i.e., 90% of the minimum system pressure). Students often use $0.9 \times P_{max}$ by mistake.
> 5. **Missing the "classification" keyword (–1 mark):** When asked to "classify," the answer must explicitly state the **classification criteria (axes)** first, then list the categories. A mere listing of components without a classification framework loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Mixing Imperial and SI Units:** KTU answers **must use SI units** (Pa, m³/s, N, m, s). Mentioning "psi" or "gpm" will be penalized.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Classification of Machine Hydraulics**

- ☐ Machine hydraulics in polymer machinery operate on **Pascal's Law** (1663).
- ☐ Classification is done along **four axes**: function, topology, pressure, and component type.
- ☐ **Pressure categories:** Low (≤50 bar), Medium (50–250 bar), High (>250 bar, up to 2,500 bar).
- ☐ **Gear pump** = low cost, low pressure, high noise, used in clamping.
- ☐ **Vane pump** = moderate pressure (150–210 bar), medium noise, used in general IMM circuits.
- ☐ **Piston pump** = high pressure (350–700 bar), low noise, used in injection units.
- ☐ **Open-center circuit** has continuous flow and is less energy-efficient.
- ☐ **Closed-center circuit** uses a variable pump and unloads at neutral — preferred for high-tonnage IMMs.
- ☐ **Load-sensing (LS) circuit** saves **60–80% energy** by matching pump output to load demand.
- ☐ **Pascal's equation:** $P = F / A$, so $F = P \times A$.
- ☐ **Cylinder extension force** uses the full bore area: $A_{full} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2}$.
- ☐ **Cylinder retraction force** uses the annulus area: $A_{annulus} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (D^{2} - d^{2})$.
- ☐ **Retraction force is always less than extension force** (typically 50–75% of extension).
- ☐ **Pump flow equation:** $Q = V_d \times n$; **actual flow** is $Q \times \eta_{vol}$.
- ☐ **Hydraulic power:** $P_{hyd} = \Delta p \times Q$.
- ☐ **Accumulator pre-charge rule:** $P_0 \approx 0.9 \times P_{min}$ (for isothermal nitrogen).
- ☐ **Accumulator sizing equation:** $V_0 = \dfrac{P_2 \times V_{oil}}{P_2 - P_0}$ (isothermal).
- ☐ **Volumetric efficiency $\eta_{vol}$** decreases with increasing pressure due to internal leakage.
- ☐ **Overall efficiency** of a hydraulic system is the product of volumetric and mechanical efficiencies: $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}$.
- ☐ **Standard KTU exam pressure units:** always bar or MPa (Pa for derivation); **never** use psi.
- ☐ **Target fluid cleanliness for modern IMMs:** ISO 4406 code **18/16/13** (equivalent to NAS 7).
- ☐ **Typical clamping tonnage range:** 50 kN (micro-IMM) to **12,000 kN** (large automotive IMM).
- ☐ **Typical injection pressure range:** 1,000 bar to **2,500 bar** for engineering plastics.
- ☐ **Energy benchmark:** modern servo-hydraulic IMMs target **0.4 kWh/kg** of processed polymer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ancillary Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ancillary Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of polymer processing machinery, **Ancillary Equipment** (also termed *Auxiliary Equipment* or *Peripheral Equipment*) refers to the suite of secondary, supporting machines and devices that are coupled to a primary processing unit—such as an injection moulding machine, an extruder, a blow moulder, or a thermoforming line—to ensure the smooth, efficient, and consistent conversion of raw polymeric feedstock into finished products.

These units do not, by themselves, perform the primary shaping operation (i.e., they do not plasticize, inject, extrude, or blow the melt). Instead, they perform critical *pre-processing* (e.g., drying, colouring, conveying), *in-process support* (e.g., temperature control, robotic handling), and *post-processing* (e.g., granulation, trimming, stacking) tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Ancillary equipment is a foundational topic because the *quality of the final moulded/extruded article is only as good as the weakest link in the auxiliary chain*. For example, insufficient drying of nylon will cause hydrolysis and silver streaks even in a perfectly tuned injection machine.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen" Analogy

Think of a polymer processing plant as a large, modern commercial **kitchen**:
- The **primary processing machine** (e.g., injection moulding machine or extruder) is the *Chef* — it does the actual cooking.
- The **ancillary equipment** is everything *else* that makes cooking possible: the *refrigerator* (raw material storage/conditioning), the *water purifier* (material dryer/dehumidifier), the *gas pipeline and regulator* (compressed air system and pressure regulator), the *mixer-grinder* (colour mixer/blender), the *serving plate warmer* (mould temperature controller), the *dishwasher* (chiller/cooling tower), and the *waiter* (robotic take-out arm/conveyor).

If the refrigerator fails, the chef cannot cook fresh ingredients. If the gas pressure fluctuates, the dish is ruined. Similarly, in polymer processing, a failure in any ancillary unit immediately translates to a defect in the final product.

## 1.3 Standard Classification of Ancillary Equipment (KTU Framework)

For examination purposes, ancillary equipment in polymer processing is broadly classified into **seven functional families**:

| Sl. No. | Functional Family | Typical Equipment | Primary Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Material Storage & Conveying | Silos, hopper loaders, vacuum conveyors | Bulk storage and dust-free transfer of granules/powder |
| 2 | Drying & Dehumidification | Hot-air dryers, desiccant dehumidifying dryers | Removal of surface and bound moisture from hygroscopic polymers |
| 3 | Metering, Mixing & Blending | Volumetric/Gravimetric feeders, colour dosers, mixers | Accurate proportioning of polymer + masterbatch + additives |
| 4 | Temperature Control | Mould temperature controllers (MTCs), chillers, oil heaters | Maintaining precise mould/roller temperature |
| 5 | Granulation / Recycling | Beside-the-press granulators, central granulators, agglomerators | Reprocessing of runners, sprues, and rejected parts |
| 6 | Robotics & Handling | Sprue pickers, traverse robots, conveyors, stackers | Automated part extraction, deflashing, and stacking |
| 7 | Compressed Air & Utilities | Compressors, air dryers, pressure regulators, cooling towers | Plant-wide pneumatic and thermal utility supply |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units and Conventions:** Throughout this module, we use the **SI** system. Key metrics include:
> - **Throughput:** kilograms per hour ($\text{kg/h}$)
> - **Dew Point:** degrees Celsius ($^\circ\text{C}$), typically $-40^\circ\text{C}$ for engineering plastics
> - **Drying Temperature:** degrees Celsius ($^\circ\text{C}$)
> - **Mould Temperature:** degrees Celsius ($^\circ\text{C}$)
> - **Cooling Capacity:** kilowatts ($\text{kW}$) or tons of refrigeration (TR), where $1\ \text{TR} = 3.517\ \text{kW}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Working of the Major Ancillary Units

### 2.1.1 Desiccant Dehumidifying Dryer (For Hygroscopic Polymers)

Polymers such as **PA 6, PA 6.6, PC, PMMA, PET, PBT, and ABS** are *hygroscopic*—they absorb moisture from the atmosphere. Processing wet polymer causes:
- Hydrolytic degradation (chain scission)
- Surface defects (splay, silver streaks, bubbles)
- Poor mechanical properties (low tensile and impact strength)

A **desiccant dryer** operates on the principle of adsorption. A desiccant bed (typically molecular sieves, Type 4A) is regenerated in one chamber while the other chamber dries process air, which is then heated and passed over the polymer in the drying hopper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU High-Yield):**
> - Drying temperature for **PA 6.6** = $80\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for $4\text{–}6\ \text{hours}$
> - Drying temperature for **PC** = $120\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for $3\text{–}4\ \text{hours}$
> - Required dew point = $\leq -40\ ^\circ\text{C}$

### 2.1.2 Mould Temperature Controller (MTC)

The MTC circulates a heat-transfer fluid (water for temperatures up to $95\ ^\circ\text{C}$, or thermal oil for temperatures up to $200\ ^\circ\text{C}$) through channels machined into the mould. It consists of:
- A **reservoir tank**
- A **pump** (centrifugal, typically $5\text{–}60\ \text{L/min}$)
- A **heater** (immersion type, often $3 \times 3\ \text{kW}$ banks)
- A **PID controller** with thermocouple input
- **Inlet and outlet manifolds** with non-return valves and Y-strainers

The MTC directly affects: cycle time, surface finish, warpage, internal stress, and dimensional accuracy.

### 2.1.3 Chillers

A chiller removes heat from the process water (which itself cools the mould via a secondary loop, or directly cools the barrel/extruder). The two principal types are:
- **Air-cooled chiller:** uses ambient air to condense the refrigerant.
- **Water-cooled chiller:** uses a cooling tower loop for condensation—more efficient in hot climates.

### 2.1.4 Granulators

Granulators are rotary cutting mills used to reduce runners, sprues, and rejected parts into reusable granules. The two configurations are:
- **Beside-the-press (BTP) granulator:** mounted adjacent to the IMM for immediate re-feeding.
- **Central granulator:** high-throughput unit serving an entire shop floor, fitted with conveyors and fines separators.

The key design parameter is the **screen size** (typically $4\text{–}8\ \text{mm}$ openings), which controls the granulate size and bulk density.

### 2.1.5 Gravimetric (Loss-in-Weight) Feeder

A gravimetric feeder is a closed-loop control device that continuously measures the mass of material in a hopper and adjusts a screw speed to deliver a precise mass flow rate. It is essential for:
- Co-extrusion layer ratio control
- Masterbatch dosing
- Continuous compounding lines

### 2.1.6 Material Conveying Systems

- **Vacuum loader (single-shot):** a vacuum pump sucks granules from a drum into a receiver, then discharges to the machine hopper.
- **Central vacuum conveying system:** a central pump station serves multiple machines via a piped network, with diverter valves directing material to specific destinations.

### 2.1.7 Sprue Picker / Robot

For injection moulding, a **sprue picker** is a simple pneumatic swing arm that extracts the moulded part plus runner system. A **traverse robot** (servo-driven, 3-axis) handles more complex parts, inserts, and downstream operations such as degating, printing, and stacking.

## 2.2 The "Why and How" Logic Chain

- **Why dry hygroscopic polymers?** Because water at processing temperature ($>200\ ^\circ\text{C}$) vaporizes explosively, causing voids and degradation.
- **How does a desiccant remove water?** Through **physical adsorption**—water molecules adhere to the vast internal surface area of the molecular sieve.
- **Why control mould temperature?** A cold mould freezes the skin prematurely, causing sink marks and high internal stress; a hot mould yields better surface finish but increases cycle time.
- **How is throughput calculated for a granulator?** It is governed by the **rotor diameter, number of rotating knives, screen area, and the bulk density of the feed**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Units | Application / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cooling capacity of a chiller | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $\text{kW}$ | Used to size a chiller |
| Heat removed by mould cooling | $Q_{\text{mould}} = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}})$ | $\text{kW}$ | $c_{p,w} \approx 4.186\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ |
| Conversion: Ton of Refrigeration | $1\ \text{TR} = 3.517\ \text{kW} = 3.024 \times 10^3\ \text{kcal/h}$ | — | Frequently asked in KTU |
| Drying time (Fick's Law based) | $t \approx \frac{x^2}{2D}$ (approx.) | hours | $x$ = half-thickness, $D$ = diffusivity |
| Screw feeder throughput (volumetric) | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot N \cdot \eta_v$ | $\text{kg/h}$ | $A$ = cross-section, $N$ = rpm, $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency |
| Pressure drop in conveying line | $\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | $\text{Pa}$ | $f$ = Darcy friction factor |
| Air volume for vacuum conveying | $V_{\text{air}} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\mu \cdot \rho_{\text{bulk}}}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ | $\mu$ = loading ratio (typically 5–10) |
| Dew point relation | $\text{Dew Point} \approx T_{\text{dry bulb}} - (100 - \text{RH})/5$ | $^\circ\text{C}$ | Approximate (Magnus formula simplification) |
| Cycle time effect of MTC | $t_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\text{fill}} + t_{\text{cool}} + t_{\text{plast}} + t_{\text{open/close}}$ | $\text{s}$ | MTC affects $t_{\text{cool}}$ directly |
| Pump head for MTC | $H = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} + h_f$ | $\text{m}$ | $\Delta P$ across mould channels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real plant design, these formulas are used by the process engineer to (a) specify the dryer capacity in $\text{kg/h}$ matching the IMM shot weight and cycle time, (b) size the chiller in $\text{kW}$ to match the mould heat load, and (c) select the correct vacuum pump capacity in $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ for a given conveying distance.

## 2.4 Selection Criteria for Ancillary Equipment

A KTU favourite sub-question is: *"What factors govern the selection of an ancillary unit?"* The principal criteria are:

1. **Polymer type and grade** (hygroscopic vs. non-hygroscopic)
2. **Throughput requirement** (kg/h)
3. **Required accuracy** (volumetric vs. gravimetric dosing)
4. **Floor space and layout** (beside-the-press vs. central)
5. **Energy efficiency and operating cost**
6. **Level of automation required** (manual vs. robotic)
7. **Maintenance and clean-down frequency** (e.g., medical grades)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Sizing a Chiller for a Mould

A typical KTU numerical problem asks: *"A 200-tonne injection moulding machine requires a chiller. The mould is cooled by water flowing at $30\ \text{L/min}$, entering at $20\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and leaving at $28\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate the heat to be removed by the chiller."*

### Step 1 — Identify the given parameters
- Volumetric flow rate, $\dot{V} = 30\ \text{L/min}$
- Inlet temperature, $T_{\text{in}} = 20\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Outlet temperature, $T_{\text{out}} = 28\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Specific heat of water, $c_p = 4.186\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Density of water, $\rho \approx 1\ \text{kg/L}$

### Step 2 — Convert volumetric flow to mass flow
The mass flow rate is the product of density and volumetric flow.

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$\dot{m} = 1\ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{L}} \times 30\ \frac{\text{L}}{\text{min}} = 30\ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{min}}$$

Converting to $\text{kg/s}$ for SI consistency:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{30}{60}\ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} = 0.5\ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the temperature rise
The temperature difference is the outlet temperature minus the inlet temperature.

$$\Delta T = T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}}$$

$$\Delta T = 28\ ^\circ\text{C} - 20\ ^\circ\text{C} = 8\ ^\circ\text{C} = 8\ \text{K}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the heat transfer equation
The heat to be removed by the chiller is:

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Substituting all the values:

$$Q = 0.5\ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} \times 4.186\ \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg·K}} \times 8\ \text{K}$$

$$Q = 16.744\ \text{kJ/s} = 16.744\ \text{kW}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Tonnes of Refrigeration (TR)
Since chiller capacities in India are commonly specified in TR, we convert using the conversion factor $1\ \text{TR} = 3.517\ \text{kW}$.

$$Q_{\text{TR}} = \frac{Q}{3.517}$$

$$Q_{\text{TR}} = \frac{16.744}{3.517} \approx 4.76\ \text{TR}$$

### Step 6 — Apply a safety factor
Industry practice (and KTU marking schemes) require adding a 20% safety margin:

$$Q_{\text{design}} = 1.20 \times 4.76 \approx 5.71\ \text{TR}$$

A standard **6 TR chiller** is therefore specified.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-step explanation of the conversion logic:** The water absorbs heat from the hot mould (which is in turn heated by the polymer melt). The amount of heat picked up by the water is calculated by the simple first-law energy balance. We then convert to refrigeration tons because commercial chiller catalogues are universally indexed in TR in India and the Middle East.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Ancillary Unit vs. Process Variable

| Ancillary Equipment | Primary Process Variable Controlled | Common Defect If Unit Fails | Recommended Specification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dehumidifying Dryer | Moisture content (ppm) | Splay, silver streaks, voids | Dew point $\leq -40\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for PA/PC |
| Mould Temperature Controller | Mould surface temperature | Sink marks, warpage, poor gloss | $\pm 1\ ^\circ\text{C}$ accuracy |
| Chiller | Cooling water temperature | Long cycle time, part distortion | Match heat load in kW + 20% margin |
| Volumetric Feeder | Additive ratio (by volume) | Inconsistent colour, property drift | $\pm 1\%$ dosing accuracy |
| Gravimetric Feeder | Additive ratio (by mass) | Same as above, but more accurate | $\pm 0.5\%$ |
| Granulator | Regrind particle size | Poor feeding, feeding bridging | Screen size $4\text{–}8\ \text{mm}$ |
| Sprue Picker | Cycle time, labour | Manual handling injury, contamination | 1.0–1.5 s pick time |
| Vacuum Loader | Material availability | Starve feeding, colour variation | Throughput in kg/h |

## 3.3 Worked Example: Drying Time for PA 6.6

A KTU short problem may state: *"PA 6.6 granules of diameter 3 mm are to be dried. The diffusion coefficient of water in PA 6.6 at $80\ ^\circ\text{C}$ is $1.5 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Estimate the minimum drying time."*

### Step 1 — Identify the diffusion path
For a sphere, the characteristic diffusion path $x$ is taken as the radius of the granule.

$$r = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{3\ \text{mm}}{2} = 1.5\ \text{mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the approximate diffusion time formula
For diffusion-dominated drying, a simplified Fickian estimate is:

$$t \approx \frac{r^2}{2D}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the values
$$t \approx \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$t \approx \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-6}}{3.0 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$t \approx 0.75 \times 10^{5}\ \text{s}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to hours
$$t \approx \frac{0.75 \times 10^{5}}{3600} \approx 20.8\ \text{hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step explanation:** Fick's second law describes how a concentration gradient drives mass diffusion through a medium. For a sphere, the radius is the longest distance a water molecule must travel to reach the surface and evaporate. The $r^2$ dependence explains why **smaller granules dry faster** and why **thick sections of reground material require longer drying**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: Drying–Conveying–Moulding Loop

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the various ancillary units interact with a primary processing machine (an injection moulding machine) to form a closed-loop, continuous production system. This is the standard layout taught in PCPOT503.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Material Silo] --> B[Vacuum Loader]
    B --> C[Desiccant Dehumidifying Dryer]
    C --> D[Material Hopper of IMM]
    D --> E[Injection Moulding Machine]
    E --> F[Cooled Mould with MTC]
    F --> E
    E --> G[Sprue Picker / Robot]
    G --> H[Conveyor Belt]
    H --> I[Beside the Press Granulator]
    I --> J[Regrind Silo]
    J --> B
    E --> K[Chiller Loop]
    F --> K
    K --> L[Cooling Tower]
    L --> K
    C --> M[Compressed Air Supply]
    G --> M
    E --> M
```

### Reading the Diagram

- The **central backbone** is the material flow path: Silo $\rightarrow$ Loader $\rightarrow$ Dryer $\rightarrow$ IMM $\rightarrow$ Granulator $\rightarrow$ Regrind Silo $\rightarrow$ back to Loader.
- The **MTC and Chiller Loop** form a closed thermal circuit: chilled water leaves the chiller, enters the mould, absorbs heat, and returns to the chiller; waste heat is rejected via the cooling tower.
- The **Compressed Air Supply** is a *utility manifold* feeding the dryer regeneration valves, the sprue picker pneumatic cylinders, and the IMM clamping unit.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture: Material Dosing & Mixing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Polymer Resin Bag or Silo] --> B1[Volumetric Feeder 1]
    A2[Masterbatch Container] --> B2[Volumetric Feeder 2]
    A3[Additive Hopper] --> B3[Gravimetric Feeder]
    B1 --> C1[Static Mixer]
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Hopper Loader]
    D1 --> E1[Processing Machine Inlet]
    style C1 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### Reading the Diagram

- Three independent feeders handle the polymer, the colour masterbatch, and the performance additive.
- The **static mixer** is a motionless in-line device containing helical elements that homogenize the blend by repeatedly dividing and recombining the flow stream.
- For high-precision colour or additive dosing, a **gravimetric feeder** replaces the volumetric feeder for the additive, while the bulk polymer may be dosed volumetrically.

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: Granulation Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Beside the Press
        R1[Runner and Rejected Part] --> G1[Beside Press Granulator]
        G1 --> F1[Fines Separator]
        F1 --> RP1[Regrind Collection Bin]
    end
    subgraph Central Granulator Station
        R2[Central Collection of Rejects] --> G2[Central Granulator]
        G2 --> F2[Fines Separator]
        F2 --> RP2[Regrind Silo]
    end
    RP1 --> RP2
    RP2 --> LO[Vacuum Loader]
    LO --> DRY[Dryer]
    DRY --> IMM[Injection Moulding Machine]
```

### Reading the Diagram

- The **beside-the-press** unit handles small, frequent volumes (runners and sprues) generated by a single IMM.
- The **central granulator** processes larger or mixed rejects from multiple machines.
- Both feed a common regrind silo, which is then re-introduced to the upstream drying–conveying–moulding loop at a controlled ratio (typically 10–30% virgin replacement).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*What are the functions of a desiccant dehumidifying dryer in polymer processing? Why is it mandatory for polyamides and polycarbonates?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A desiccant dehumidifying dryer removes surface and bound moisture from hygroscopic polymer granules by passing low-dew-point hot air (dew point $\leq -40\ ^\circ\text{C}$) over the polymer bed. The desiccant bed (typically molecular sieve 4A) is periodically regenerated. Drying is mandatory for **polyamides (PA 6, PA 6.6)** and **polycarbonates (PC)** because these polymers absorb atmospheric moisture, and processing wet material causes hydrolytic degradation, reduced molecular weight, silver streaks, voids, and poor mechanical properties. Typical drying parameters: PA 6.6 at $80\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for $4\text{–}6\ \text{h}$ and PC at $120\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for $3\text{–}4\ \text{h}$. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*Distinguish between a volumetric feeder and a gravimetric feeder. State one application where a gravimetric unit is essential.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **volumetric feeder** doses material based on the swept volume of a rotating screw; its accuracy is affected by bulk density variation (typically $\pm 1\text{–}2\%$). A **gravimetric (loss-in-weight) feeder** continuously weighs the hopper contents and adjusts screw speed to maintain a set mass flow rate, achieving accuracies of $\pm 0.25\text{–}0.5\%$. Gravimetric feeders are essential in **co-extrusion** (where the layer ratio must be held to within $\pm 1\%$) and for dosing expensive additives or masterbatches. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a vacuum conveying system used to feed polymer granules to an injection moulding machine. Explain the role of the receiver, the vacuum pump, and the discharge valve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and briefly explain five important factors governing the selection of a granulator for a polymer processing plant. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

A **vacuum conveying system** pneumatically transports polymer granules from a storage drum to the machine hopper using negative pressure. The major components are:

1. **Vacuum pump** (typically a regenerative blower or a rotary vane pump) — generates the suction, typically at $-0.4$ to $-0.6\ \text{bar}$ gauge.
2. **Receiver** — a stainless-steel or aluminium vessel fitted with a top-mounted filter sock (to separate granules from air) and a discharge valve at the base.
3. **Suction line** — a flexible hose or rigid pipe fitted with a *pickup wand* inserted into the material drum.
4. **Discharge valve** — a butterfly or slide gate actuated by compressed air; it opens when the vacuum cycle ends, allowing granules to fall into the machine hopper.
5. **Control panel** — sets the loading cycle time and triggers the discharge.

**Working cycle:** During the *load phase*, the vacuum pump evacuates the receiver, drawing granules from the drum up the suction line. Granules collect on the filter sock while clean air passes through the pump. When the preset time is reached (or a level sensor is triggered), the vacuum is closed and **compressed air** is admitted to the filter, blowing the cake of granules into the receiver body. The discharge valve then opens, dropping the granules into the IMM hopper. The cycle repeats. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

Five key factors for granulator selection are:

1. **Throughput requirement (kg/h):** A small beside-the-press unit handles $5\text{–}30\ \text{kg/h}$, while a central granulator handles $200\text{–}1000\ \text{kg/h}$.
2. **Screen size and granulate geometry:** A smaller screen gives finer regrind but reduces throughput and increases fines; the screen must match the virgin pellet size.
3. **Rotor design:** *Open rotor* for soft polymers (PE, PP); *cross-shaped* or *staggered* for hard, glass-filled, or thick-walled parts.
4. **Material of construction:** Hardened tool-steel knives for filled or glass-reinforced polymers to resist abrasive wear.
5. **Ease of clean-down and accessibility:** Important for frequent colour changes or medical-grade production. Quick-access screens, removable hoppers, and easy knife-adjustment features are preferred. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**(a)** Describe the construction and working of a mould temperature controller (MTC). Explain how the choice of heat transfer fluid (water vs. oil) is made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mould is cooled by water flowing at $40\ \text{L/min}$, entering at $22\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and leaving at $32\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate (i) the heat removed in $\text{kW}$ and (ii) the equivalent cooling capacity in Tonnes of Refrigeration (TR). Take $c_p$ of water = $4.186\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ and $1\ \text{TR} = 3.517\ \text{kW}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

A **Mould Temperature Controller (MTC)** is a closed-loop thermal unit that circulates a heat-transfer fluid through the mould's cooling/heating channels to maintain the mould at a precise set-point temperature.

**Construction:**
- **Reservoir tank:** typically $30\text{–}200\ \text{L}$ stainless-steel tank.
- **Pump:** centrifugal, flow rates $5\text{–}60\ \text{L/min}$, head $2\text{–}4\ \text{bar}$.
- **Heater bank:** usually three $3\ \text{kW}$ immersion heaters (total $9\ \text{kW}$).
- **Cooling coil:** a copper or stainless coil through which chilled water from a chiller or cooling tower is circulated; an automated three-way mixing valve modulates cooling.
- **PID controller:** with J- or K-type thermocouple input from the mould or the return line.
- **Safety features:** flow switch (trips heater on no-flow), high-temperature cut-off, low-level cut-off, Y-strainer.

**Working:** The pump circulates the fluid from the reservoir to the mould. The thermocouple reads the return-line temperature; the controller modulates the heater and the cooling valve to maintain the set point.

**Choice of fluid:**
- **Water** is used for mould temperatures up to $95\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (atmospheric boiling point). It is cheap and has high heat capacity.
- **Thermal oil** (e.g., synthetic heat-transfer fluid) is used for temperatures between $100\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and $200\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (e.g., for PET preform moulds, PC optical moulds). Oil is selected because water would boil and lose its heat-transfer capability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $\dot{V} = 40\ \text{L/min}$
- $T_{\text{in}} = 22\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- $T_{\text{out}} = 32\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- $c_p = 4.186\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $\rho = 1\ \text{kg/L}$

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate**
$\dot{m} = 1 \times 40 = 40\ \text{kg/min} = 0.6667\ \text{kg/s}$

**Step 2 — Temperature rise**
$\Delta T = 32 - 22 = 10\ ^\circ\text{C} = 10\ \text{K}$

**Step 3 — Heat removed in kW**
$Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.6667 \times 4.186 \times 10 \approx 27.91\ \text{kW}$

**Step 4 — Convert to TR**
$Q_{\text{TR}} = \dfrac{27.91}{3.517} \approx 7.93\ \text{TR}$

**Step 5 — Apply 20% safety margin (KTU best practice)**
$Q_{\text{design}} = 1.20 \times 7.93 \approx 9.52\ \text{TR}$; hence a **10 TR chiller** is specified. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** In numericals on chiller sizing, the most common mistake is to forget to convert $\text{L/min}$ to $\text{kg/s}$ before multiplying with $c_p$ in $\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$. Always show the conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Dew Point Confusion:** Students often write "the dryer should be at $-40\ ^\circ\text{C}$" without clarifying that it is the *dew point of the process air*, not the air temperature. This loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Vague Block Diagrams:** Mermaid/flowchart diagrams without labels on the arrows (e.g., "hot dry air", "water flow") are penalised in KTU. Always annotate the connecting lines.
> 4. **Skipping the Safety Factor:** A chiller calculation that omits the $20\%$ design margin loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing Polymer Names:** Writing "polypropylene is hygroscopic" is a factual error (PP is non-hygroscopic). Only PA, PC, PET, PBT, PMMA, ABS and PLA are hygroscopic.
> 6. **Ignoring Internal Choice Logic:** KTU Part B always offers an internal choice; ensure you attempt the option you are most prepared for.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] Ancillary equipment supports the primary polymer processing machine in pre-, in-, and post-processing tasks.
- [x] Seven major families: storage/conveying, drying, dosing/mixing, temperature control, granulation, robotics, utilities.
- [x] **Desiccant dryers** are mandatory for **hygroscopic polymers** (PA, PC, PET, PBT, PMMA, ABS).
- [x] Required **dew point** for engineering plastics: $\leq -40\ ^\circ\text{C}$.
- [x] Drying temperature and time: PA 6.6 = $80\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for $4\text{–}6\ \text{h}$; PC = $120\ ^\circ\text{C}$ for $3\text{–}4\ \text{h}$.
- [x] **MTC** uses water for $<95\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and **thermal oil** for $95\text{–}200\ ^\circ\text{C}$.
- [x] Chiller capacity formula: $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$.
- [x] Conversion: $1\ \text{TR} = 3.517\ \text{kW} = 3.024 \times 10^3\ \text{kcal/h}$.
- [x] Always add a $20\%$ safety margin to chiller size.
- [x] **Gravimetric feeder** accuracy ($\pm 0.5\%$) $>$ **Volumetric feeder** accuracy ($\pm 1\text{–}2\%$).
- [x] Beside-the-press granulators handle small volumes; central granulators handle high volumes.
- [x] Vacuum conveying uses **negative pressure**; pressure conveying uses **positive pressure**.
- [x] Compressed air, cooling water, and chilled water are the three plant utilities.
- [x] Fick's law: drying time $\propto$ granule radius squared.
- [x] Smaller granules and higher temperatures accelerate drying.
- [x] MTC affects surface finish, cycle time, warpage, and internal stress.
- [x] Sprue pickers and robots improve safety, consistency, and cycle time.
- [x] KTU 14-mark questions are split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.
- [x] KTU 3-mark questions expect a crisp 3–4 sentence answer with the key formula or principle.
- [x] Always annotate diagrams with arrow labels and units.
- [x] Always state the **SI units** of every numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computer Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Computer Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Polymer Machinery & Product Manufacturing**, *Computer Operation* refers to the systematic execution of hardware, software, firmware, and network resources to monitor, control, optimize, and document the conversion of polymeric raw materials into finished products. It is the backbone of modern **Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)**, **Computer-Aided Design (CAD)**, **Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAM)**, and **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)** systems deployed across injection molding, extrusion, blow molding, thermoforming, and compounding lines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim style):**
> "Computer operation in a polymer processing plant is the coordinated functioning of the Central Processing Unit (CPU), memory units, input/output peripherals, control algorithms, and communication interfaces to drive actuators, acquire sensor feedback, and maintain process parameters within specified tolerances."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a polymer manufacturing plant as a **human body**:

| Polymer Plant | Human Body Analogy |
|---------------|--------------------|
| CPU (Microcontroller / PLC) | Brain |
| RAM / ROM | Short-term & long-term memory |
| Sensors (T°, Pressure, Flow) | Eyes, ears, skin |
| Actuators (Heaters, Motors, Valves) | Muscles and limbs |
| HMI / Monitor | Voice and face |
| Software / PLC Program | Thoughts and decisions |

Just as your brain reads signals from your senses and tells your muscles what to do, the **computer operation** in a polymer plant reads temperature, pressure, and screw speed, then tells heaters and drives how to respond — thousands of times per second.

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Core Functions of a Computer in Polymer Manufacturing (POSM Model):**
> 1. **P**rocessing — execute control algorithms (PID, fuzzy logic, neural nets)
> 2. **O**peration — drive heaters, screws, hydraulics in real time
> 3. **S**torage — retain recipes, historical trends, machine calibrations
> 4. **M**easurement — read analog/digital signals from sensors
> 5. **C**ommunication — talk to MES, ERP, and cloud platforms

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Sampling Rate (Nyquist):** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component of the process signal
- **Control Loop Update Time:** typically **10 ms – 100 ms** in injection molding
- **Memory Hierarchy Speed:** Register → Cache → RAM → HDD → Tape (in ascending latency)
- **Standard Voltage Levels:** **0 V – 5 V (TTL)**, **0 V – 10 V**, **4 mA – 20 mA** (current loop for industry)
- **Communication Protocols (mandatory in polymer plants):** **OPC-UA, Modbus RTU/TCP, Profibus, EtherCAT, CAN bus**
- **IEEE 754 Floating Point Standard** for numerical computation
- **Industrial Ambient Standard:** **0 °C – 55 °C** operating temperature for control cabinets

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Computer Operation Cycle

Every computer operation in a polymer process follows the **IPO Cycle (Input → Process → Output)**:

### Stage 1 — Input Stage
- Receives analog signals from **thermocouples, pressure transducers, flow meters, position encoders, optical sensors**.
- These continuous signals are converted to digital form using **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADC)**.
- Resolution of ADC matters: a **12-bit ADC** gives $2^{12} = 4096$ discrete steps.
- Quantization error = $\frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$

### Stage 2 — Processing Stage
- The **CPU** runs:
  - Sequential control logic (Boolean / ladder logic for PLCs)
  - Feedback algorithms (PID with $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$)
  - Data acquisition routines
  - Recipe management
- Math co-processors / DSP chips handle complex matrix math used in **CAE simulation**.

### Stage 3 — Output Stage
- **Digital-to-Analog Converters (DAC)** generate control signals.
- Signals amplified by **power electronics** drive heaters, hydraulic valves, and servo motors.
- **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is widely used for temperature and speed control.

## 2.2 Real-Time Operating Constraints

A polymer process is **hard real-time**: a missed deadline means a defective product (or worse, a damaged machine).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deadline requirement for injection molding closed-loop control:** control action must complete within one **mold cycle slice** ($\sim 50\,\text{ms}$). Missing it = scrapped shot.

## 2.3 Memory Hierarchy — The Speed Pyramid

```
    Registers       ← ~1 ns access, smallest
    Cache (L1/L2/L3)
    Main RAM        ← ~100 ns
    SSD             ← ~100 µs
    HDD / NAS       ← ~10 ms
    Cloud / ERP     ← seconds
```

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Engineering Use in Polymer Mfg. |
|---|---------------------|--------|------|---------------------------------|
| 1 | Sampling Frequency (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | ADC sampling for barrel T° |
| 2 | ADC Quantization Error | $E_q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$ | V | Pick ADC bit-depth for ±0.5 °C accuracy |
| 3 | ADC Resolution | $\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | V | Smallest detectable voltage change |
| 4 | PID Control Law | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i\!\int\! e\,dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$ | — | Melt temperature / pressure control |
| 5 | Ziegler-Nichols Ultimate Gain | $K_u = \frac{1}{d \cdot \omega_u}$ | — | Initial PID tuning |
| 6 | Bit Rate of ADC | $R = f_s \cdot n \cdot c$ | bits/s | Data pipeline bandwidth |
| 7 | Throughput | $\text{TP} = \frac{3600 \cdot m_{shot}}{t_{cycle}}$ | kg/h | Production rate estimation |
| 8 | Hydraulic Power | $P = \frac{p \cdot Q}{600}$ | kW | Clamp force computer control |
| 9 | Heater Energy | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | J | Mold heating control |
| 10 | Shear Rate | $\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\pi D N}{h}$ | s⁻¹ | Extruder screw RPM control |
| 11 | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours | Reliability planning |
| 12 | Availability | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | — | Uptime KPI of CNC/CIM |
| 13 | Cycle Time | $t_c = t_{inj} + t_{cool} + t_{eject}} + t_{idle}$ | s | Process monitoring |
| 14 | Baud Rate | $R_b = \frac{1}{T_b}$ | bits/s | Fieldbus communication |
| 15 | CPU Utilization | $U = \frac{t_{busy}}{t_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Real-time headroom check |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU problems, remember that **the ADC resolution formula** is the most commonly asked. A 16-bit ADC over a 10 V full-scale gives $\Delta V = 10/(2^{16}-1) \approx 152.6\,\mu\text{V}$ — small enough to detect thermocouple microvoltages after amplification.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive polymer parts**: Real-time control ensures ±0.02 mm dimensional tolerance.
- **Medical tubing extrusion**: Laser micrometer feedback at **2 kHz** sampling keeps diameter within ±0.01 mm.
- **Bottle blow molding**: Computer vision systems reject defective preforms at line speed > 60,000 bottles/hour.
- **Smart factories**: OPC-UA links PLCs with MES, enabling OEE dashboards in real time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required ADC Bit Depth for Temperature Control

**Problem Setup:** A polymer barrel has temperature range **0 °C – 400 °C**. The Type-J thermocouple produces **±5 V** after amplification. We need to resolve **±0.5 °C**. Find the minimum ADC bit depth.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert temperature resolution to voltage resolution.**

The full-scale voltage range is:

$$
V_{FS} = V_{max} - V_{min} = 5 - (-5) = 10\,\text{V}
$$

The temperature range corresponds to:

$$
\Delta T_{range} = 400 - 0 = 400\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

Voltage per degree Celsius:

$$
V_{/^\circ C} = \frac{V_{FS}}{\Delta T_{range}} = \frac{10}{400} = 0.025\,\text{V/}^\circ\text{C} = 25\,\text{mV/}^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert required temperature tolerance to voltage tolerance.**

For $\Delta T = 0.5\,^\circ\text{C}$:

$$
\Delta V_{req} = 0.025 \times 0.5 = 0.0125\,\text{V} = 12.5\,\text{mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply ADC resolution formula.**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} \;\Rightarrow\; 2^n - 1 \geq \frac{V_{FS}}{\Delta V_{req}}
$$

$$
2^n - 1 \geq \frac{10}{0.0125} = 800
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for n.**

$$
2^n \geq 801
$$

$$
n \geq \log_2(801) \approx 9.65
$$

**Step 5 — Round up to next integer.**

$$
n_{min} = 10\,\text{bits}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, engineers select **12-bit** or **16-bit** ADCs to provide noise margin and oversampling headroom.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cycle Time & Throughput for an Injection Molding Cell

A computer-controlled injection molding machine produces a part of mass **$m_{shot} = 120\,\text{g}$**. Cycle times are: injection **2 s**, cooling **18 s**, ejection **1 s**, robot transfer **1.5 s**.

**Step 1 — Compute total cycle time.**

$$
t_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{cool} + t_{eject} + t_{robot} = 2 + 18 + 1 + 1.5 = 22.5\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute parts per hour.**

$$
N_{parts} = \frac{3600}{t_{cycle}} = \frac{3600}{22.5} = 160\,\text{parts/hour}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute mass throughput.**

$$
\text{TP} = N_{parts} \cdot m_{shot} = 160 \cdot 0.120 = 19.2\,\text{kg/hour}
$$

**Step 4 — Daily throughput (assuming 24/7 with 90% OEE).**

$$
\text{TP}_{day} = 19.2 \times 24 \times 0.90 = 414.72\,\text{kg/day}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PID Tuning via Ziegler-Nichols (Ultimate Cycle Method)

A temperature loop exhibits sustained oscillation at $K_u = 8$ and $T_u = 40$ s when the integral and derivative actions are disabled.

**Step 1 — Use Ziegler-Nichols PID tuning table.**

$$
K_p = 0.60 \cdot K_u = 0.60 \times 8 = 4.8
$$

$$
T_i = 0.50 \cdot T_u = 0.50 \times 40 = 20\,\text{s}
$$

$$
T_d = 0.125 \cdot T_u = 0.125 \times 40 = 5\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to standard PID gains.**

$$
K_i = \frac{K_p}{T_i} = \frac{4.8}{20} = 0.24\,\text{s}^{-1}
$$

$$
K_d = K_p \cdot T_d = 4.8 \times 5 = 24\,\text{s}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Manual Operation | Relay-Based | PLC / Computer Operation |
|-----------|------------------|-------------|---------------------------|
| Response time | seconds | 100 ms | **<10 ms** |
| Repeatability | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Recipe storage | Paper | DIP switches | **Non-volatile memory** |
| Data logging | None | Limited | **Continuous (years)** |
| Cost (initial) | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Scalability | Poor | Limited | **Excellent (OPC-UA)** |
| Diagnostics | Operator | LED panel | **HMI + remote** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Computer Operation in a Polymer Processing Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENS["SENSOR LAYER Field Level"]
        TC["Thermocouple Type J or K"]
        PT["Pressure Transducer 0 to 200 bar"]
        FT["Flow Meter Melt Flow Index"]
        PE["Position Encoder Screw Stroke"]
        LE["Laser Extensometer Wall Thickness"]
    end

    subgraph SIGCON["SIGNAL CONDITIONING"]
        AMP["Amplifier Gain 100x"]
        FILT["Low Pass Filter 100 Hz"]
        ISO["Isolator Galvanic 1500 V"]
    end

    subgraph IO["I/O MODULES"]
        ADC["ADC 16 bit 1 MSPS"]
        DI["Digital Inputs 24 V DC"]
        DO["Digital Outputs Relay / SSR"]
        DAC["DAC 16 bit"]
    end

    subgraph CPU["COMPUTE LAYER"]
        PLC["PLC / Industrial PC IEC 61131-3"]
        PID["PID Controller Block"]
        REC["Recipe Manager Database"]
        ALM["Alarm and Event Logger"]
        HIST["Historical Trending"]
    end

    subgraph HMI["HMI / SCADA LAYER"]
        MON["Operator Monitor Touchscreen"]
        TREND["Live Trend Charts"]
        REP["Production Reports"]
    end

    subgraph ACT["ACTUATOR LAYER"]
        HTR["Band Heaters PID Controlled"]
        MOT["Servo Motor Injection Axis"]
        VALV["Proportional Hydraulic Valve"]
        ROB["6 Axis Robot Pick and Place"]
    end

    subgraph NET["NETWORK LAYER"]
        FBUS["Fieldbus EtherCAT or Profinet"]
        ETH["Industrial Ethernet"]
        MES["MES / ERP Interface OPC UA"]
        CLOUD["Cloud Analytics IIoT"]
    end

    TC --> AMP
    PT --> AMP
    FT --> AMP
    PE --> DI
    LE --> ADC
    AMP --> FILT --> ISO --> ADC
    ADC --> PLC
    DI --> PLC

    PLC --> PID
    PLC --> REC
    PLC --> ALM
    PLC --> HIST

    PLC --> MON
    PLC --> TREND
    PLC --> REP

    PLC --> DAC
    PLC --> DO
    DAC --> HTR
    DAC --> MOT
    DO --> VALV
    PLC --> ROB

    PLC --> FBUS --> ETH --> MES --> CLOUD
```

## 4.2 Information Flow Sequence (IPO Model)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant HMI as HMI Panel
    participant PLC as PLC CPU
    participant SENS as Sensors
    participant ACT as Actuators
    participant MES as MES Server

    Op->>HMI: Select Recipe "ABS Black 220C"
    HMI->>PLC: Download Recipe Parameters
    PLC->>ACT: Activate Heaters Setpoint 220C
    loop Every 50 ms Control Cycle
        SENS->>PLC: Read Temperature Pressure Position
        PLC->>PLC: Run PID Compute
        PLC->>ACT: Adjust Heater Power Valve Position
    end
    ACT->>SENS: Physical Response
    SENS->>PLC: New Feedback Values
    PLC->>HMI: Update Live Trend
    PLC->>MES: Send Cycle Data OEE Counter
    MES-->>PLC: Acknowledge and Store
```

## 4.3 Computer Memory Hierarchy in Process Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CPU Registers\n1 ns\nBytes"] --> B["L1 Cache\n2 ns\nKB"]
    B --> C["L2 Cache\n10 ns\nMB"]
    C --> D["Main RAM DDR4\n100 ns\nGB"]
    D --> E["SSD NVMe\n100 us\nTB"]
    E --> F["HDD NAS Archive\n10 ms\nPB"]
    F --> G["Cloud Cold Storage\nseconds\nEB"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a polymer plant, **recipe data resides in RAM**, **historical trends in SSD**, **batch records in NAS**, and **multi-plant analytics in cloud** — this tiered architecture balances speed, cost, and durability.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**Define Computer Operation. List any three functional components involved in computer-based control of a polymer processing machine.**

**Model Answer:**

Computer operation, in a polymer manufacturing context, is the coordinated execution of hardware, software, and communication resources to monitor and control the conversion of polymers into finished products.

Three functional components are:
1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU / PLC)** — executes control algorithms.
2. **Input/Output Modules (ADC, DAC, Digital I/O)** — interface with sensors and actuators.
3. **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** — allows operator interaction and recipe management.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

**State and explain Nyquist Sampling Theorem with reference to polymer barrel temperature measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

The Nyquist Sampling Theorem states that the sampling frequency $f_s$ must be at least twice the highest frequency $f_{max}$ present in the signal to allow accurate reconstruction:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{max}
$$

In polymer barrel temperature control, the heater dynamics and sensor noise can have frequency components up to about 5 Hz. Hence, a sampling rate of at least 10 Hz (preferably 50–100 Hz with oversampling) is used to accurately capture the temperature profile.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a computer-controlled injection molding machine. Label all major blocks and describe the function of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 12-bit ADC is used to digitize the output of a melt pressure transducer with full-scale output of **0 V – 10 V**. The transducer reads **75 bar** out of a 200 bar full scale. Calculate (i) ADC resolution in volts, (ii) the digital output code, and (iii) the quantization error as a percentage. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

The architecture has five major layers:

1. **Sensor Layer** — thermocouples (Type J/K), pressure transducers, position encoders.
2. **Signal Conditioning** — amplification, filtering, isolation.
3. **I/O Interface** — ADC, DAC, digital I/O modules.
4. **Compute Layer (CPU/PLC)** — runs PID, recipe manager, alarms.
5. **Actuator Layer** — band heaters, servo motors, hydraulic valves, robots.
6. **HMI/SCADA Layer** — operator display, trending, reports.
7. **Network Layer** — Fieldbus, Industrial Ethernet, MES/ERP connectivity.

Each block is connected through standardized voltage/current levels (0–10 V or 4–20 mA) and protocols (OPC-UA, EtherCAT).

#### Solution to (b):

**Step (i) — ADC resolution:**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{10}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{10}{4095} = 2.442 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} = 2.442\,\text{mV}
$$

**Step (ii) — Digital output code:**

Voltage at 75 bar:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{75}{200} \times 10 = 3.75\,\text{V}
$$

Digital code:

$$
D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{out}}{\Delta V}\right) = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{3.75}{0.002442}\right) = 1536
$$

In hex: $1536_{10} = 0x0600$.

**Step (iii) — Quantization error:**

Maximum quantization error:

$$
E_q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}} = \frac{10}{2^{13}} = 1.221\,\text{mV}
$$

As percentage of full scale:

$$
\%E = \frac{E_q}{V_{FS}} \times 100 = \frac{0.001221}{10} \times 100 = 0.0122\%
$$

As percentage of reading:

$$
\%E_{read} = \frac{0.001221}{3.75} \times 100 = 0.0326\%
$$

---

### Question B — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the PID control algorithm as applied to barrel temperature control in an extruder. Derive the digital PID equation suitable for implementation in a PLC. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A computer-controlled extruder produces polymer film at a rate of **50 kg/h**. The screw diameter is **30 mm**, the flight depth is **3 mm**, and the screw rotates at **60 RPM**. The polymer melt has density **950 kg/m³**. Verify whether the throughput is consistent with the screw geometry. If not, propose the corrective computer action. **(7 Marks)**

#### Solution to (a):

The continuous-time PID law is:

$$
u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

Discretizing for a sampling period $T$:

$$
u_k = K_p e_k + K_i T \sum_{j=0}^{k} e_j + \frac{K_d}{T}(e_k - e_{k-1})
$$

This is the **position form** suitable for direct PLC implementation. The **velocity form** (more robust against integral windup) is:

$$
\Delta u_k = u_k - u_{k-1} = K_p(e_k - e_{k-1}) + K_i T e_k + \frac{K_d}{T}(e_k - 2e_{k-1} + e_{k-2})
$$

For barrel temperature control:
- $K_p$ corrects present error (heater power).
- $K_i$ eliminates steady-state offset.
- $K_d$ damps overshoot during heating ramp.
- Anti-windup clamping is added to prevent integral saturation during machine start-up.

#### Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute volumetric throughput from screw geometry.**

The screw channel volume per revolution:

$$
V_{rev} = \pi D h L \quad (\text{but here only }V \text{ per revolution is needed})
$$

For a metering section (approximate):

$$
Q_{geom} = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 h N \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}
$$

Assuming helix angle $\phi = 17.65°$ ($\sin\phi\cos\phi \approx 0.25$):

$$
Q_{geom} = \frac{\pi^2 (0.030)^2 (0.003) (60/60) \times 0.25}{2} \times \frac{1}{2}
$$

Simplified form (volumetric displacement per second):

$$
Q_v = \pi D h \cdot \frac{N}{60} \cdot \sin\phi \cos\phi
$$

$$
Q_v = \pi \cdot 0.030 \cdot 0.003 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.25 = 7.069 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to mass throughput.**

$$
\dot{m}_{geom} = Q_v \cdot \rho = 7.069 \times 10^{-5} \times 950 = 0.0672\,\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with given throughput.**

Given: $50\,\text{kg/h} = 50/3600 = 0.01389\,\text{kg/s}$.

**Step 4 — Discrepancy analysis.**

$$
\frac{\dot{m}_{geom}}{\dot{m}_{given}} = \frac{0.0672}{0.01389} \approx 4.84
$$

The screw can theoretically deliver about 4.8× more than the measured throughput. The shortfall is due to **back-flow leakage, pressure flow, and drag-flow inefficiency**.

**Step 5 — Corrective computer action.**
- Increase screw RPM via $N \uparrow$ (until $\dot{m}$ matches setpoint).
- Reduce die restriction (back pressure) by adjusting gear pump speed.
- Log the deviation in SCADA and alert maintenance for screw/barrel wear check.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **ADC Formula Mistakes:** Using $2^n$ instead of $2^n - 1$ in the denominator loses 1–2 marks. The "–1" reflects that 0 is one of the codes.
> 2. **Sampling Theorem Misapplication:** Writing $f_s \geq f_{max}$ instead of $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ is a classic error.
> 3. **PID Discretization Confusion:** Mixing position form and velocity form, or forgetting the anti-windup clause, costs marks.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing seconds and milliseconds, kg/h and kg/s — always state conversions.
> 5. **No Diagram in Long Answers:** A missing block diagram in a 7-mark architecture question can deduct up to 2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting "−1" in $2^n-1$:** Many students write $V_{FS}/2^n$ and lose credit for ADC resolution.
> 7. **Neglecting Real-Time Constraint:** Not mentioning hard real-time deadlines in process control loses application-level marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Computer operation = hardware + software + I/O + network executing polymer process control.
- **Five Core Functions:** POSM — Processing, Operation, Storage, Measurement, Communication.
- **IPO Cycle:** Input (sensors) → Process (CPU) → Output (actuators).
- **Key Formula Set:**
  - ADC resolution: $\Delta V = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$
  - Quantization error: $E_q = V_{FS}/2^{n+1}$
  - Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$
  - PID: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \!\int\! e\,dt + K_d\,de/dt$
  - Throughput: $\text{TP} = 3600 m_{shot}/t_{cycle}$
- **Standard Interfaces:** 4–20 mA, 0–10 V, OPC-UA, EtherCAT, Modbus, Profibus.
- **Hard Real-Time Rule:** Closed-loop control must finish within one control slice (≤ 50 ms typical).
- **Memory Pyramid:** Register → Cache → RAM → SSD → NAS → Cloud.
- **Industrial PC vs PLC:** PLC for deterministic logic, IPC for SCADA + advanced analytics.
- **CAD/CAM/CAE Integration:** Computer operation links part design (CAD) → mold flow (CAE) → CNC/robot path (CAM).
- **OEE Metric:** $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$ — central to computer-aided reliability.
- **Cybersecurity Note (2024 Scheme emphasis):** Always reference **IEC 62443** for industrial network security in KTU answers on Industry 4.0 topics.
- **Exam Heuristic:** Always draw a labeled block diagram for any 7-mark "explain the architecture" question — examiners reward visual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trouble Shooting of Injection Moulding
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Injection Moulding Troubleshooting** is the **systematic, scientific methodology** employed to identify, diagnose, analyse, and rectify the **processing defects**, **mould-related faults**, and **material anomalies** that occur during the thermoplastic injection moulding cycle. It involves a cause-and-effect correlation between **defect symptoms** (visible/inferred), **process parameters** (pressure, temperature, time), **mould design variables** (gating, cooling, ejection), and **material behaviour** (rheology, thermal properties, moisture content).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, troubleshooting is examined under the broader domain of **"Process Control & Quality Assurance in Polymer Processing"** and forms a high-weightage segment in Module 1 of *PCPOT503 – Polymer Machinery & Product Manufacturing*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis (Module 1):** The university expects students to clearly map each defect to its root cause (categorized under *Material*, *Machine*, *Method*, *Mould*, and *Manpower* — the classic **4M/5M analysis**). Memorising both the defect name and at least three causes is mandatory to score above 70% marks in ESE.

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor-Patient" Approach

Think of injection moulding troubleshooting the way a **doctor diagnoses a patient**:

| Medical Analogy | Injection Moulding Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient comes with **symptoms** (fever, cough) | A defective part exhibits **visual symptoms** (flash, sink marks, warpage) |
| Doctor orders **blood tests / X-ray** | Engineer inspects **process logs, pressure curves, and mould conditions** |
| Diagnosis based on **cause-effect logic** | Root-cause analysis using **Ishikawa (Fishbone) diagram** or **5-Why technique** |
| Treatment via **medicine/lifestyle change** | Remedies via **parameter adjustment / mould modification / material drying** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Intuition:** If a part comes out bad, do not blame the machine first. Follow the hierarchy: **Material → Mould → Machine → Method**. A KTU topper once said: *"The plastic never lies — every defect tells a story if you know how to read it."*

## The Five Pillars of Injection Moulding (4M + 1M Framework)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to classify every defect under one or more of these five categories:

1. **Material (M)** — polymer grade, moisture, contamination, MFI, additives
2. **Machine (M)** — barrel/screw condition, heater band failure, hydraulic system
3. **Method (M)** — injection speed, pressure profile, cycle time, cooling
4. **Mould (M)** — gating, venting, cooling channels, surface finish, ejection
5. **Manpower (M)** — operator error, incorrect setting, lack of training

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Quick Recall:** ***"My Mould Makes Many Mistakes"*** → **M**aterial, **M**ould, **M**achine, **M**ethod, **M**anpower.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Systematic Troubleshooting Methodology

Troubleshooting in injection moulding is **never random**. KTU examiners expect a **structured 6-step approach**:

### Step 1 — Defect Identification (Symptom Recognition)
- Visual inspection, dimensional measurement (CMM/caliper), weight comparison
- Classification into: *surface defect*, *internal defect*, *dimensional defect*, *mechanical defect*

### Step 2 — Frequency & Consistency Analysis
- **Random defect** → points to *material inconsistency* or *operator error*
- **Consistent defect (part-to-part)** → points to *machine drift* or *mould wear*
- **Cycle-dependent defect** → points to *thermal/cooling imbalance*

### Step 3 — Process Parameter Audit
- Review the **injection moulding cycle**:
  - Plastication (screw rotation, back pressure, melt temperature)
  - Injection (ram velocity, peak pressure, switch-over point)
  - Holding/Packing (holding pressure, holding time)
  - Cooling (coolant flow, temperature, time)
  - Ejection (ejector speed, stroke)

### Step 4 — 5-Why / Fishbone (Ishikawa) Root-Cause Analysis
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When asked *"List the causes of sink marks"*, examiners want answers under **3–4 categories** (Material, Mould, Method, Machine). A single-line answer will fetch only 1–2 marks out of 7. Use the Ishikawa structure.

### Step 5 — Trial Correction (DOE Approach)
- Change **one parameter at a time** (the *OFAT method*: One Factor At a Time)
- Use **Design of Experiments (DoE)** for advanced troubleshooting

### Step 6 — Validation & Standardisation
- Run 50+ cycles to confirm the fix
- Update the **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)** and **process control charts**

---

## 2.2 Major Defects — Causes & Remedies Matrix (KTU High-Yield Table)

| # | Defect | Description | Material Causes | Mould Causes | Method/Process Causes | Remedies |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Flash / Burr** | Excess material at parting line or ejector pin | Low viscosity melt, excess material | Worn parting surfaces, insufficient clamp tonnage, damaged parting line, poor venting | High injection pressure, low clamp force, worn mould | ↓ injection pressure, ↑ clamp tonnage, repair mould, ↓ melt temperature |
| 2 | **Short Shot** | Incomplete filling of cavity | Insufficient material, high MFI, contamination | Insufficient gates, poor venting, thin wall, long flow path | Low injection pressure, low melt temp, low injection speed, premature freeze-off | ↑ pressure, ↑ melt temp, ↑ injection speed, improve venting, add gates |
| 3 | **Sink Marks** | Depressions on thick sections | High shrinkage polymer (PP, PE, PA) | Thick wall sections, insufficient cooling, poor gate location | Insufficient holding pressure, short holding time, high melt temp | ↑ holding pressure/time, ↓ melt temp, redesign wall thickness, add cooling |
| 4 | **Voids (Internal)** | Air pockets inside thick walls | High shrinkage material | Thick sections, single-side cooling | Short holding time, low holding pressure, high melt temp | ↑ packing, redesign gate, add cooling, use nucleating agents |
| 5 | **Warpage** | Distortion / bending of part | Anisotropic shrinkage (fibre-filled) | Asymmetric cooling, uneven wall thickness | High mould temp differential, low holding pressure, ejection too early | Balance cooling, ↑ holding time, redesign part, use uniform wall thickness |
| 6 | **Weld / Meld Lines** | Visible line where flow fronts meet | Contaminated material, low MFI | Poor gate location, multi-cavity imbalance | Low melt temp, low injection speed, mould release agent | ↑ melt temp, ↑ injection speed, dry material, vent weld-line region |
| 7 | **Burn Marks (Diesel Effect)** | Black/brown streaks at end of fill | Degraded material | Poor venting, trapped air compression | Excessive injection speed, high melt temp | ↓ injection speed, improve venting, ↓ melt temp, clean vents |
| 8 | **Silver Streaking / Splay** | Silver-white marks on surface | Wet/moist material, contamination, degraded polymer | Overheating near gate, poor mould surface | Excessive shear, high back pressure, residence time too long | Dry material (3–4 hr at 80–120 °C), ↓ back pressure, clean barrel |
| 9 | **Flow Marks** | Wavy or ripple patterns on surface | Low MFI resin | Poor surface polish, cold mould | Low melt temp, low injection speed | ↑ melt temp, ↑ injection speed, polish cavity |
| 10 | **Jetting** | Snake-like flow pattern from gate | High MFI, low viscosity | Small gate, incorrect gate location | High injection speed | ↓ injection speed, redesign gate (fan/gate relocation) |
| 11 | **Sticking in Cavity** | Part remains in cavity | High shrinkage, no release agent | Insufficient draft angle, rough surface, no ejector pins | Overpacking, high holding pressure, slow cooling | Add draft (1–2°), polish cavity, ↑ ejection, ↓ holding pressure |
| 12 | **Brittleness / Cracking** | Part breaks easily | Wet material, wrong grade, degraded resin | Sharp corners, stress concentration | Excessive shear, high melt temp causing degradation | Dry material, redesign corners (fillet), ↓ melt temp |
| 13 | **Delamination** | Layer peeling on surface | Contaminated material, incompatible regrind | Mould release excess | Low melt temp, high injection speed | Clean material, ↓ release agent, ↑ melt temp |
