| 14 | **Ejector Pin Marks** | Visible push-pin impressions | Soft material | Improper ejector pin design, sharp edges | Excessive ejection force | Increase ejector pin count, use stripper plate, polish pins |
| 15 | **Bubbles** | Air bubbles in transparent part | Wet polymer | Poor venting | High injection speed, trapped air | Dry material, improve venting, ↓ injection speed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For a 7-mark question on any single defect, KTU expects: **Definition (1 mark) + Visual Description (1 mark) + 3 Causes (3 marks) + 2 Remedies (2 marks)**. Use this template structure to score full marks.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

These formulas are **directly applicable** to injection moulding troubleshooting. The clamp force, cooling time, and shot weight equations are the most-tested in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{clamp} \geq P_{inj} \times A_{proj}$ | **Clamp Force** (must exceed injection force × projected area) | $\text{kN}$ |
| 2 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_{eject} - T_{wall}}\right)$ | **Cooling Time** (simplified) | $\text{s}$ |
| 3 | $m_{shot} = V_{shot} \times \rho_{melt}$ | **Shot Weight** | $\text{g}$ |
| 4 | $V_{inj} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \times L_{stroke}$ | **Injection Volume** (from screw geometry) | $\text{cm}^3$ |
| 5 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^3}$ | **Apparent Shear Rate** (Newtonian) | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $P_{specific} = \dfrac{P_{hyd} \times A_{ram}}{A_{shot}}$ | **Specific Cavity Pressure** | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 7 | $\text{Recovery rate} = \dfrac{V_{shot}}{t_{plasticating}}$ | **Plasticising Rate** | $\text{cm}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 8 | $L\text{/}t \text{ ratio} = \dfrac{L_{flow}}{t_{wall}}$ | **Flow Length to Wall Thickness Ratio** | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Cavity fill time} = \dfrac{V_{shot}}{Q_{inj}}$ | **Fill Time** | $\text{s}$ |
| 10 | $\eta_a = \dfrac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | **Apparent Viscosity** | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ |

Where:
- $F_{clamp}$ = Clamp tonnage, $P_{inj}$ = cavity pressure, $A_{proj}$ = projected area
- $s$ = wall thickness, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, $T_m$ = melt temp, $T_{wall}$ = mould wall temp, $T_{eject}$ = ejection temp
- $D$ = screw/barrel diameter, $L_{stroke}$ = stroke length, $Q$ = volumetric flow rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition for Clamp Tonnage:** A safety factor of **1.1 to 1.2** must be applied: $F_{clamp,design} = 1.2 \times P_{inj} \times A_{proj}$. Insufficient clamp tonnage is the **#1 cause of flash** in KTU exam problems.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- The **clamp tonnage formula** is used daily in mould design — under-sized machines produce flash, over-sized machines waste energy.
- The **cooling time equation** directly determines cycle time → productivity. A 10% reduction in cooling time can boost production by 8–12%.
- **Shear rate** determines whether the polymer will degrade — most thermoplastics must be processed below $\dot{\gamma} = 10{,}000 \text{ s}^{-1}$ to avoid splay and silver streaks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Clamp Force Requirement (Foundation of Flash Troubleshooting)

The clamp tonnage is the **maximum resistive force** the moulding machine can apply to keep the mould closed against the **internal cavity pressure** trying to push the mould halves apart.

### Step 1 — Cavity Pressure Force on Mould Half
Consider a mould with a single cavity having a projected area $A_{proj}$ on the parting plane. The melt inside exerts a hydrostatic-like pressure $P_{cav}$ pushing the two mould halves apart.

The separating force is:

$$F_{sep} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$$

### Step 2 — Equilibrium Condition
For the mould to remain closed, the clamping force from the machine must equal or exceed this separating force:

$$F_{clamp} \geq P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$$

### Step 3 — Introduction of Safety Factor
In practice, transient pressure spikes occur during packing. KTU expects a safety margin of **1.1 to 1.2**:

$$F_{clamp,design} = n \times P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$$

where $n$ is the design safety factor (typically $n = 1.1$ to $1.2$).

### Step 4 — Conversion to Machine Tonnes
Since clamp force in industry is rated in **tonnes (kN)**, we divide by 10 (approx.):

$$F_{clamp} \text{ (tonnes)} = \dfrac{P_{cav} \times A_{proj}}{10 \times 10^3}$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example
> **Problem:** A mould has a projected area of $320 \text{ cm}^2$. The average cavity pressure during packing is $40 \text{ MPa}$. The machine's maximum clamp tonnage is **120 tonnes**. Will the part exhibit flash?

Given:
- $A_{proj} = 320 \text{ cm}^2 = 320 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.032 \text{ m}^2$
- $P_{cav} = 40 \text{ MPa} = 40 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$
- Machine clamp = $120 \text{ tonnes} = 120 \times 10^4 \text{ N} = 1.2 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$

Calculate separating force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{sep} &= P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \\
&= (40 \times 10^6) \times (0.032) \\
&= 1.28 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \\
&= 128 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Conclusion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{sep} \text{ (128 t)} &> F_{clamp} \text{ (120 t)} \\
\therefore \text{Flash will occur} &\text{ because the mould opens by } 0.32 \text{ mm during packing.}
\end{aligned}
$$

Remedy: Either use a **150-tonne machine** or **redesign the part to reduce projected area** to:

$$A_{proj,max} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^6}{40 \times 10^6} = 0.03 \text{ m}^2 = 300 \text{ cm}^2$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Simplified Cooling Time Equation

Cooling time is critical to troubleshooting **warping, sink marks, and dimensional defects**. The following is a KTU-standard derivation.

### Step 1 — Heat Transfer Setup
Consider a slab of thickness $s$ (the wall) cooling from melt temperature $T_m$ to ejection temperature $T_{eject}$ by mould walls at $T_{wall}$. Using **1-D transient conduction** (Newton's law of cooling approximation):

$$T(t) = T_{wall} + (T_m - T_{wall}) \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 \alpha t}{s^2}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Time
Rearranging for $t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{T(t) - T_{wall}}{T_m - T_{wall}} &= \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 \alpha t}{s^2}\right) \\
\ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T(t) - T_{wall}}\right) &= \dfrac{\pi^2 \alpha t}{s^2} \\
t_{cool} &= \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T(t) - T_{wall}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Ejection Temperature
Setting $T(t) = T_{eject}$:

$$t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_{eject} - T_{wall}}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Correction Factor for Centre-Line vs. Average
For the **centre of the wall** (not the surface), the $\dfrac{8}{\pi^2}$ correction appears:

$$t_{cool} = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_{eject} - T_{wall}}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Worked Example
> **Problem:** PP part with wall thickness $s = 3 \text{ mm}$, melt at $T_m = 230 \text{ °C}$, mould wall at $T_{wall} = 40 \text{ °C}$, eject at $T_{eject} = 100 \text{ °C}$. Thermal diffusivity of PP: $\alpha = 0.96 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{cool} &= \dfrac{(0.003)^2}{\pi^2 \times (0.96 \times 10^{-7})} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \times \dfrac{230 - 40}{100 - 40}\right) \\
&= \dfrac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{9.47 \times 10^{-7}} \ln\!\left(0.81 \times 3.17\right) \\
&= 9.50 \times \ln(2.57) \\
&= 9.50 \times 0.944 \\
&\approx 8.97 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** Cooling time scales with $s^2$. **Doubling the wall thickness quadruples the cooling time.** This is why thin-wall mouldings (soda caps) cycle in 4–5 s, while thick blocks take 60+ s.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Defect-to-Parameter Sensitivity

This matrix helps KTU students quickly identify **which knob to turn** for a given defect. Read: if you see defect **X**, look at the column — the parameter with the **highest sensitivity (H)** is your first adjustment.

| Defect ↓ \ Parameter → | Melt Temp | Mould Temp | Inj. Pressure | Inj. Speed | Hold Press | Hold Time | Cooling Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flash | M | M | **H** | M | H | L | L |
| Short Shot | **H** | M | **H** | **H** | L | L | L |
| Sink Marks | M | L | L | L | **H** | **H** | L |
| Voids | L | L | L | L | **H** | **H** | L |
| Warpage | M | **H** | L | M | M | M | **H** |
| Weld Lines | **H** | M | M | **H** | L | L | L |
| Burn Marks | M | L | L | **H** | L | L | L |
| Silver Streaks | L | L | M | **H** | L | L | L |
| Flow Marks | **H** | **H** | L | **H** | L | L | L |
| Jetting | L | L | L | **H** | L | L | L |
| Sticking | M | **H** | L | L | H | M | L |
| Brittleness | **H** | L | L | M | L | L | L |
| Delamination | **H** | L | L | L | L | L | L |
| Ejector Marks | L | L | M | L | M | L | L |
| Bubbles | M | M | M | **H** | L | L | L |

> **H = High sensitivity, M = Medium, L = Low**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Systematic Troubleshooting Flow Chart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Defective Part Detected] --> B[Visual and Dimensional Inspection]
    B --> C{Is defect consistent part-to-part?}
    C -- No --> D[Check Material Batch - Random Issue]
    C -- Yes --> E{Check Process Log - Pressure/Temp Drift}
    E -- Yes --> F[Calibrate Machine - Heater Bands, Sensors]
    E -- No --> G{Categorize Defect Type}
    G -- Surface --> H[Surface Defect Path]
    G -- Internal --> I[Internal Defect Path]
    G -- Dimensional --> J[Dimensional Defect Path]
    G -- Mechanical --> K[Mechanical Defect Path]
    H --> H1[Flash? Check Clamp Tonnage and Venting]
    H --> H2[Silver Streaks? Check Material Drying]
    H --> H3[Flow Marks? Increase Melt and Mould Temp]
    H --> H4[Weld Lines? Increase Injection Speed]
    I --> I1[Sink Marks? Increase Hold Pressure and Time]
    I --> I2[Voids? Redesign Gate Location and Cooling]
    I --> I3[Bubbles? Improve Venting and Dry Material]
    J --> J1[Warpage? Balance Cooling Channels]
    J --> J2[Short Shot? Increase Pressure and Speed]
    J --> J3[Sticking? Add Draft Angle and Polish Cavity]
    K --> K1[Brittleness? Dry Material and Reduce Shear]
    K --> K2[Delamination? Check Material Contamination]
    F --> L[Apply Trial Correction]
    D --> L
    H1 --> L
    H2 --> L
    H3 --> L
    H4 --> L
    I1 --> L
    I2 --> L
    I3 --> L
    J1 --> L
    J2 --> L
    J3 --> L
    K1 --> L
    K2 --> L
    L --> M[Run 50 Cycle Validation]
    M --> N{Defect Resolved?}
    N -- No --> O[Use Ishikawa 5M Root-Cause Analysis]
    O --> L
    N -- Yes --> P[Update SOP and Standardise Process]
```

## 4.2 Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram — Root Cause of Flash

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[FLASH DEFECT] --> M1[Material]
    E --> M2[Machine]
    E --> M3[Method]
    E --> M4[Mould]
    E --> M5[Manpower]
    M1 --> A1[Low Viscosity Resin]
    M1 --> A2[Excess Material Charge]
    M1 --> A3[Contaminated Regrind]
    M2 --> B1[Insufficient Clamp Tonnage]
    M2 --> B2[Worn Toggle Mechanism]
    M2 --> B3[Hydraulic Pressure Drop]
    M3 --> C1[High Injection Pressure]
    M3 --> C2[Excessive Injection Speed]
    M3 --> C3[High Melt Temperature]
    M3 --> C4[Long Holding Time]
    M4 --> D1[Damaged Parting Line]
    M4 --> D2[Insufficient Venting]
    M4 --> D3[Mould Wear on Edges]
    M4 --> D4[Misaligned Cavity Plates]
    M5 --> F1[Wrong Machine Setting]
    M5 --> F2[Untrained Operator]
    M5 --> F3[Inadequate SOP]
```

## 4.3 Injection Moulding Cycle Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mould Close] --> B[Injection Phase]
    B --> C[Packing and Holding Phase]
    C --> D[Cooling Phase]
    D --> E[Plasticating Phase]
    E --> F[Mould Open]
    F --> G[Ejection Phase]
    G --> H[Part Removal]
    H --> A
    B --> B1[Pressure 800 to 1500 bar]
    B --> B2[Speed 20 to 200 mm per s]
    C --> C1[Hold Pressure 400 to 800 bar]
    C --> C2[Hold Time 2 to 10 s]
    D --> D1[Coolant at 25 to 80 C]
    D --> D2[Time depends on wall thickness squared]
    E --> E1[Screw Rotation 50 to 150 rpm]
    E --> E2[Back Pressure 5 to 30 bar]
```

## 4.4 Defect Symptom Diagnostic Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Symptom] --> Q1{Where on part?}
    Q1 -- Parting Line --> R1[FLASH - Clamp or Venting Issue]
    Q1 -- Thick Sections --> R2[SINK MARKS or VOIDS - Packing Issue]
    Q1 -- Flow Front Region --> R3[BURN MARKS - Venting Issue]
    Q1 -- Surface General --> R4[SILVER or SPLAY - Moisture Issue]
    Q1 -- Weld Region --> R5[WELD LINES - Temp or Speed Issue]
    Q1 -- Whole Part --> R6[WARPAGE - Cooling Imbalance]
    Q1 -- Wall Section --> R7[SHORT SHOT - Pressure or Venting]
    R1 --> P1[Action - Increase Clamp Force]
    R2 --> P2[Action - Increase Hold Press and Time]
    R3 --> P3[Action - Add Vents and Reduce Speed]
    R4 --> P4[Action - Dry Material at 80 to 120 C]
    R5 --> P5[Action - Increase Melt Temp and Speed]
    R6 --> P6[Action - Balance Cooling and Reduce Eject Force]
    R7 --> P7[Action - Increase Pressure Speed and Venting]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Mermaid Diagrams:** Each block represents a logical step in the troubleshooting process. KTU students are advised to redraw these diagrams during revision — examiners often award bonus marks for visual/structured answers in 14-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define "Flash" in injection moulding. List any four causes of flash.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Flash (also called **burr** or **spew**) is the **thin, unwanted layer of plastic** that escapes from the mould cavity and solidifies on the **parting line, ejector pin gaps, or vent areas** during the injection cycle. It appears as a thin film of excess material at the mould opening edges.

**Four Causes (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Insufficient clamp tonnage** → mould opens slightly under cavity pressure
2. **Excessive injection pressure** → melt forces past the parting surfaces
3. **Worn or damaged mould parting surfaces** → gaps allow melt escape
4. **High melt temperature** → low viscosity melt flows easily through gaps
5. *Bonus:* Improper venting, contaminated material, misaligned mould plates

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**What is meant by "short shot" in injection moulding? Mention two mould-related causes and one remedy.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A **short shot** is a defect in which the **molten polymer fails to completely fill the mould cavity**, resulting in an **incomplete part** — typically missing edges, corners, or thin sections.

**Two Mould-Related Causes (1.5 marks):**
1. **Inadequate or absent venting** → trapped air prevents complete fill
2. **Insufficient or undersized gates** → restricted flow into cavity
3. *Bonus:* Long flow path with thin walls, cold mould (low surface temperature)

**One Remedy (0.5 mark):**
- **Improve venting** at the end-of-fill region and **redesign gate size** to ensure complete fill.
- *Alternative remedy:* Increase melt temperature, injection pressure, or injection speed.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (Module 1):** Every 14-mark question has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student answers **either Question A or Question B** (internal choice). Each sub-part expects theory + diagram/derivation in most cases.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **systematic methodology for troubleshooting injection moulding defects**. Describe the **Ishikawa (Fishbone) root-cause analysis** with reference to **two specific defects** of your choice.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a **neat sketch of the injection moulding cycle**, explain the role of **each phase** in determining part quality. How does improper setting of the **holding phase** lead to **sink marks and voids**?

---

### **Model Answer for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Systematic Methodology + Ishikawa Analysis [7 Marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Injection moulding troubleshooting is a **structured, multi-step process** that correlates defect symptoms with material, machine, method, mould, and manpower (the 5M framework) variables.

**6-Step Methodology (3.5 marks):**
1. **Defect Identification** — Visual inspection, dimensional measurement, weight check
2. **Frequency Analysis** — Random vs. consistent defects (indicates material vs. mould cause)
3. **Process Parameter Audit** — Review of cycle logs: pressure, temperature, time
4. **Root-Cause Analysis** — Ishikawa Fishbone or 5-Why technique
5. **Trial Correction** — OFAT (One Factor At a Time) parameter adjustment
6. **Validation & Standardisation** — 50+ cycle confirmation; update SOP

**Ishikawa Diagram Explanation (1.5 marks):**
The Ishikawa diagram is a **cause-and-effect tool** with the defect as the "head" and **5M categories** (Material, Machine, Method, Mould, Manpower) as the "bones."

**Two Specific Defects — Application (1 mark):**
- **Flash:** Caused by insufficient clamp tonnage (Machine), worn mould (Mould), high pressure (Method)
- **Weld Lines:** Caused by low melt temperature (Material/Method), poor gate location (Mould), contamination (Material)

---

#### Part (b) — Injection Cycle & Holding Phase [7 Marks]

**Injection Moulding Cycle Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Mould Closing Phase] --> P2[Injection Phase]
    P2 --> P3[Holding and Packing Phase]
    P3 --> P4[Cooling Phase]
    P4 --> P5[Plasticating Phase]
    P5 --> P6[Mould Opening Phase]
    P6 --> P7[Ejection Phase]
    P7 --> P1
    P2 --> X1[Pressure 1000 bar]
    P3 --> X2[Pressure 500 bar]
    P4 --> X3[Time proportional to s squared]
```

**Role of Each Phase (3 marks):**
1. **Mould Closing** — Safely brings mould halves together with proper alignment and clamp force
2. **Injection** — Fills cavity using high pressure (800–1400 bar) and high speed
3. **Holding/Packing** — Compensates for volumetric shrinkage by injecting additional melt
4. **Cooling** — Solidifies the part to ejection temperature
5. **Plasticating** — Screw rotates to melt and mix the next shot
6. **Mould Opening** — Separates mould halves
7. **Ejection** — Pushes solidified part out via ejector pins

**Holding Phase & Defects (2 marks):**
- **Sink marks** occur when the **holding pressure is too low** or **holding time is too short** to compensate for shrinkage in thick sections
- **Voids (internal)** form when the **holding phase ends prematurely**, leaving air pockets in thick regions
- **Remedy:** Increase holding pressure to **400–800 bar** and extend holding time based on wall thickness (rule of thumb: $t_{hold} \approx t_{cool} / 3$ to $t_{cool} / 2$)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **causes and remedies** for **short shot, weld lines, and warpage** in injection moulded thermoplastic parts. Use a **classification table** to present your answer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mould has a **projected area of 400 cm²** and the cavity pressure is **35 MPa**. The available machine clamp tonnage is **130 tonnes**. Will the part exhibit **flash**? If yes, suggest **two remedies** with numerical justification.

---

### **Model Answer for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Three Defects Analysis [7 Marks]

**Classification Table (5 marks):**

| Defect | Material Cause | Mould Cause | Method Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Short Shot** | High MFI resin, contamination | Insufficient gates, poor venting, thin walls, long flow path | Low pressure, low speed, low melt temp | ↑ Pressure to 1200 bar, ↑ Speed to 100 mm/s, ↑ Melt Temp by 10–20 °C, add vents, enlarge gate |
| **Weld Lines** | Contaminated material, low MFI, foreign particles | Poor gate location, multi-cavity imbalance, lack of vent at weld region | Low melt temp, low speed, excessive mould release | ↑ Melt temp by 15–25 °C, ↑ Speed, dry material at 80–100 °C, add local vent, change gate to film/fan gate |
| **Warpage** | Fibre orientation in fibre-filled polymers, anisotropic shrinkage | Asymmetric cooling, uneven wall thickness, single-sided cooling | High mould temp differential, low holding pressure, early ejection | Balance cooling channels, ↑ Holding pressure, use uniform wall thickness, add ejection equaliser, anneal part |

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
Each defect can be traced to one or more of the 5M categories. The first correction step is usually a **method adjustment** (process parameter), followed by **mould modification** if structural issues are identified, and finally a **material grade change** if the root cause is resin-related.

---

#### Part (b) — Clamp Force Numerical Problem [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- $A_{proj} = 400 \text{ cm}^2 = 400 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.04 \text{ m}^2$
- $P_{cav} = 35 \text{ MPa} = 35 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2$
- $F_{clamp,available} = 130 \text{ tonnes} = 130 \times 10^4 \text{ N} = 1.3 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$

**Step 1: Calculate Separating Force (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{sep} &= P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \\
&= (35 \times 10^6) \times (0.04) \\
&= 1.4 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \\
&= 140 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compare with Available Clamp (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{sep} \text{ (140 t)} &> F_{clamp} \text{ (130 t)} \\
\text{Deficit} &= 140 - 130 = 10 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** **Yes, the part WILL exhibit flash** because the separating force exceeds the available clamp tonnage by 10 tonnes. The mould will open approximately **0.25–0.5 mm** during the holding phase.

**Step 3: Two Remedies (2 marks):**

**Remedy 1 — Use a Higher Capacity Machine:**
Required clamp tonnage (with safety factor $n = 1.1$):

$$F_{clamp,req} = 1.1 \times 140 = 154 \text{ tonnes}$$

Use a **160-tonne machine** for safe operation.

**Remedy 2 — Reduce Projected Area by Redesign:**
Maximum allowed projected area:

$$A_{proj,max} = \dfrac{1.3 \times 10^6}{35 \times 10^6} = 0.0371 \text{ m}^2 = 371 \text{ cm}^2$$

Redesign the part to reduce projected area to **370 cm²** or less — this requires **shifting the gate location** to a lower projected cross-section or splitting the part geometry.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Trouble Shooting" Questions:**
> 1. **❌ Listing causes without categorisation (4M/5M):** A flat bullet list of 6 causes with no framework = maximum 3/7 marks. Always structure your answer using Material/Mould/Machine/Method.
> 2. **❌ Forgetting numerical units in clamp force problems:** Writing $1.4 \times 10^6$ without specifying "N" or "tonnes" loses 0.5–1 mark. Always state units clearly.
> 3. **❌ Confusing holding time and cooling time:** Holding is the **pressure-maintenance phase** (compensates shrinkage). Cooling is the **no-flow phase** (solidifies the part). They are different and have different formulas.
> 4. **❌ Not drawing a labelled diagram in 7-mark questions:** A 14-mark question without a sketch in part (a) typically loses 1–2 marks. Always include a labelled injection cycle diagram or a defect-location sketch.
> 5. **❌ Single-parameter remedy:** Examiners expect **at least 2 remedies per defect** — one process-based and one mould-based — to score full marks.
> 6. **❌ Forgetting material drying:** Moisture-related defects (splay, silver streaks, bubbles) are **always** solved by drying the polymer at 80–120 °C for 3–4 hours. This is a free mark if mentioned.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Troubleshooting = Symptom → Cause → Remedy**, not random experimentation
- ✅ **5M Framework:** Material, Mould, Machine, Method, Manpower — use this as the spine of every answer
- ✅ **Flash** = Insufficient clamp tonnage; check $F_{clamp} \geq 1.2 \times P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$
- ✅ **Short Shot** = Insufficient fill; remedy = ↑ Pressure, ↑ Speed, ↑ Melt Temp, improve venting
- ✅ **Sink Marks** = Packing issue; remedy = ↑ Hold Pressure, ↑ Hold Time
- ✅ **Warpage** = Cooling imbalance; remedy = balance cooling channels, ↑ cooling time
- ✅ **Weld Lines** = Temperature/speed issue; remedy = ↑ Melt Temp, ↑ Speed, dry material
- ✅ **Burn Marks (Diesel Effect)** = Air compression; remedy = improve venting, ↓ speed
- ✅ **Silver Streaks/Splay** = Moisture; remedy = dry material at 80–120 °C for 3–4 hours
- ✅ **Cooling time scales with $s^2$** — most important equation for cycle optimisation
- ✅ **Viscosity decreases with ↑ Temperature, ↑ Shear Rate, ↑ Pressure**
- ✅ **Defects map to parameters** — short shot and weld lines need MORE heat/speed; flash and warpage need LESS
- ✅ **Mould venting** is critical — poor venting causes ~40% of all surface defects
- ✅ **Material drying** is the most overlooked root cause — always check first
- ✅ **Draft angle** (1° to 2°) on mould walls prevents sticking and ejection marks
- ✅ **Clamp tonnage formula** $F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$ is the **#1 most-asked numerical** in KTU ESE
- ✅ **OFAT (One Factor At a Time)** is the standard KTU method for trial corrections
- ✅ **Cycle = Mould Close → Injection → Hold → Cool → Plasticate → Open → Eject**

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Mantra (30 sec before exam):**
> *"Material, Mould, Machine, Method — for every defect, scan all four. Heat and Pressure can heal most problems, but too much of either creates new ones. Always check clamp tonnage for flash, always check drying for splay, always check venting for burns."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reaction Injection Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reaction Injection Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🧪 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Reaction Injection Moulding (RIM)** is a low-pressure, low-temperature polymer processing technique in which two or more highly reactive liquid monomer or prepolymer streams are metered in a precisely controlled ratio, intensively mixed in a high-velocity impingement mixing head, and injected into a closed, heated mould cavity where a rapid chemical reaction (typically a step-growth polymerization such as the polyol–isocyanate reaction for polyurethanes) occurs *in-situ*, simultaneously forming and shaping the final thermoset polymer part in a single cycle.

The key distinguishing feature of RIM, as opposed to conventional **Injection Moulding (IM)**, is that the polymer is **created inside the mould**, not melted and injected as a pre-formed polymer. The moulding cycle therefore involves **reaction kinetics** alongside **mould filling and curing kinetics**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** RIM is studied under the broader umbrella of *specialty moulding processes* that bridge polymer chemistry and mechanical processing. Expect questions that test both the **process engineering** (equipment, flow, mixing) and the **polymer chemistry** (NCO index, gel point, demould time) sides of the technology.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Component Epoxy Glue Metaphor"

Imagine you are using a **two-part epoxy adhesive** to bond two surfaces. The resin and the hardener sit in two separate tubes. The moment they meet at the mixing nozzle, a chemical reaction begins — within minutes the mixture gels, heats up, and hardens irreversibly into a strong solid. You cannot undo the reaction; you cannot remelt the cured adhesive.

**Reaction Injection Moulding is the industrial, automated, high-speed version of this exact process:**

| DIY Epoxy Analogy | Industrial RIM Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Resin tube | Polyol (or amine) component tank |
| Hardener tube | Isocyanate (MDI/Polymeric MDI) tank |
| Manual mixing | High-pressure impingement mixing head |
| Room-temperature cure | Heated, temperature-controlled mould (40–80 °C) |
| Small batch | High-volume automotive bumper, fascia production |
| Minutes–hours cure | Demould time as low as **30–60 seconds** |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for the Board Exam:** RIM = **R**eactants **I**njected into **M**ould where they **react** and solidify. The "R" emphasizes *Reaction* — distinguish it from IM where the "I" is for *Inject* (a pre-made polymer melt).

## 1.3 Key Process Metrics (Standard Engineering Values)

- **Injection pressures:** typically **1–20 MPa** (vs. 70–140 MPa for IM) — hence the term *low-pressure RIM*.
- **Mould temperatures:** **40–80 °C** (vs. 200–300 °C for IM thermoplastics).
- **Mould clamping force:** **0.3–0.7 MPa cavity pressure** (vs. 30–80 MPa for IM).
- **Throughput rates:** **0.5–10 kg/s** of mixed reactants.
- **Demould / cycle times:** **30 s – 3 min** for thin RIM parts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How RIM Works (Step-by-Step)

The RIM process is a tightly choreographed sequence of five physical–chemical events. Understanding each stage is essential because the *quality* of the final part depends on the weakest link in this chain.

### Stage 1 — Storage & Conditioning
The two reactive components (**A-side**: isocyanate; **B-side**: polyol + chain extender + catalyst + blowing agent + fillers) are stored in separate, **temperature-controlled, nitrogen-blanketed, agitated tanks**. Conditioning typically holds the liquids at **20–40 °C** to maintain a stable, reproducible viscosity (target range: **50–2000 mPa·s**).

> **Why condition?** Viscosity directly affects metering accuracy and impingement mixing quality. A swing of ±5 °C can shift viscosity by 15–25 %, which destroys the stoichiometric balance of the reaction.

### Stage 2 — Metering
A **high-precision positive-displacement pump** (axial-piston, gear, or in-line piston type) draws each component from its tank and meters it at a flow rate dictated by the **stoichiometric ratio** (most commonly the A:B mass ratio for polyurethane is in the range **0.85 – 1.20**, governed by the **NCO index**).

### Stage 3 — Impingement Mixing
The two metered streams enter a small-volume, high-velocity mixing chamber through **nozzles aimed directly at each other** (typically 180° opposed or 90° cross-jet). They collide at high Reynolds number (**Re > 10⁴**), creating chaotic, turbulent mixing in **< 0.1 second**. This is called **impingement mixing**, and it is the heart of RIM.

> **Why impingement, not static mixing?** Because the *pot life* (working time before gelation) of polyurethane systems is often **2–15 seconds**. Static mixers are too slow and would gel inside the mixer.

### Stage 4 — Mould Filling
The mixed reactive stream flows into the closed mould through a **runner** and **gate**. Because pressures are low, the mould can be made of **aluminium, zinc alloy, or even fiberglass-reinforced epoxy**, dramatically reducing capital cost vs. IM steel moulds.

### Stage 5 — Reaction, Curing & Demould
Inside the heated mould, the polymerization proceeds:
- **Cream time** → mix turns milky (10–30 s)
- **Gel time** → mix loses fluidity (20–60 s)
- **Rise time** → foaming (if applicable) completes (30–90 s)
- **Tack-free / demould time** → part can be ejected (**60–180 s**)

The mould opens, the part is ejected (often by **robot arms**), and the cycle restarts.

## 2.2 Material Systems Used in RIM

| System | A-Side | B-Side | Typical Applications |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polyurethane (PUR) RIM** | MDI or polymeric MDI | Polyether/Polyester polyol + chain extender + catalyst | Automotive bumpers, fascia, steering wheels |
| **Polyurea RIM (RRIM)** | MDI prepolymer | Amine-terminated polyether | Fast-cycle, high-impact parts |
| **Reinforced RIM (RRIM)** | MDI | Polyol + milled glass fibre / mica | Higher stiffness panels |
| **Nylon-6 RIM (NyRIM)** | Caprolactam (with catalyst) | Caprolactam (with activator) | Large structural automotive parts |
| **Phenolic RIM** | Phenolic resin | Acid catalyst | Fire-resistant components |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The NCO index is a recurring KTU examination concept.
> $$\text{NCO Index} = \frac{\text{Actual moles of NCO used}}{\text{Theoretical moles of NCO required}} \times 100 \;\; (\%)$$
> An NCO index of **100** means perfect stoichiometry. Indices of **95–110** are typical; values above 110 give harder, more brittle parts with excess isocyanate.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **examination-grade** and have appeared in KTU Polymer / Processing papers. The LaTeX pipe symbol has been deliberately replaced with `\vert` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m}_{A} + \dot{m}_{B} = \dot{m}_{\text{total}}$ | Mass conservation through mixing head | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{A} \cdot [\text{NCO}]_{A} = r \cdot \dot{m}_{B} \cdot [\text{OH}]_{B}$ | Stoichiometric metering condition | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 3 | $\text{NCO Index} = \dfrac{n_{\text{NCO,actual}}}{n_{\text{NCO,theoretical}}} \times 100$ | Stoichiometric ratio (%) | dimensionless (%) |
| 4 | $P_{\text{clamp}} = A_{\text{proj}} \cdot p_{\text{cavity}}$ | Clamping force requirement | $\text{N}$ |
| 5 | $p_{\text{cavity}} \approx 0.3$ to $0.7$ | Typical RIM cavity pressure | $\text{MPa}$ |
| 6 | $F_{\text{clamp}} = p_{\text{cavity}} \cdot A_{\text{projected}}$ | Clamp tonnage | $\text{N}$ or $\text{T}$ |
| 7 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number in mixing nozzle | dimensionless |
| 8 | $t_{\text{fill}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{cavity}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{total}} \, / \, \rho_{\text{mix}}}$ | Mould filling time | $\text{s}$ |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{\text{adiabatic}} = \dfrac{-\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}}{C_p}$ | Adiabatic temperature rise (exotherm) | $\text{K}$ or \text{°C}$ |
| 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{\dot{\gamma}}$ | Newtonian viscosity relation (applies during fill) | $\text{Pa \cdot s}$ |
| 11 | $\eta_{\text{app}}(T) = \eta_0 \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | Arrhenius temperature–viscosity relation | $\text{Pa \cdot s}$ |
| 12 | $Q_{\text{polymerization}} = \dot{m} \cdot (-\Delta H_{\text{rxn}})$ | Heat release rate in mould | $\text{W}$ |
| 13 | $A_{\text{part}} \, / \, V_{\text{part}} = \text{Surface to volume ratio}$ | Governs heat removal rate | $\text{m}^{-1}$ |
| 14 | $\rho_{\text{part}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{part}}}{V_{\text{part}}}$ | Final part density (important for foamed RIM) | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 15 | $C_{\text{cycle}} = t_{\text{fill}} + t_{\text{react}} + t_{\text{cool}} + t_{\text{eject}}$ | Total moulding cycle time | $\text{s}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The same formula set is used in the automotive industry to design **bumper fascia** (RIM PU), in the appliance industry to design **refrigerator door liners** (NyRIM), and in medical device housings (RRIM). RIM is one of the few processes where the *thermodynamics of the reaction* and the *fluid mechanics of filling* are coupled in real time.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Process Step

- **Why two tanks?** Reactive monomers must remain **thermally and chemically isolated** until the instant of mixing — storage life of separate A and B sides can be **months**, but the mixed pot life is **seconds**.
- **Why high-pressure impingement?** Because the Reynolds number inside the mixing chamber must exceed a critical threshold ($\approx 10^4$) to achieve **turbulent, homogeneous mixing** in the few-hundred-millisecond window before gelation.
- **Why low pressure in the mould?** Because the exothermic reaction itself pressurizes the cavity slightly; only modest external pressure is needed to push the liquid against gravity and into thin sections. Lower pressure = lighter, cheaper moulds.
- **Why heated mould?** The polymerization kinetics follow Arrhenius behaviour; a mould at 60 °C is roughly **5–10× faster** than one at 25 °C, dramatically reducing cycle time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔬 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Adiabatic Temperature Rise in a RIM Polyurethane Reaction

The exothermic urethane reaction liberates a substantial amount of heat. If we assume the mould acts as a near-adiabatic boundary during the first few seconds (a reasonable assumption for thin, fast-reacting parts), the entire reaction enthalpy heats the polymerizing mass. This derivation is a **standard KTU Part B type problem**.

**Given:**
- Reaction enthalpy of NCO + OH → urethane: $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}} = -100 \;\text{kJ/mol}$ (per mole of NCO consumed)
- Density of reacting mixture: $\rho_{\text{mix}} = 1100 \;\text{kg/m}^3$
- Specific heat capacity: $C_p = 1800 \;\text{J/(kg \cdot K)}$
- Isocyanate functionality: $f_{\text{NCO}} = 2.0$ (for pure MDI)
- Equivalent weight of polyol: $EW_{\text{polyol}} = 500 \;\text{g/eq}$
- Mass ratio A:B = 1.05 (slight NCO excess)

**Step 1 — Convert reaction enthalpy to a per-mass basis.**

The molar mass of one NCO equivalent is $M_{\text{NCO}} = 42 \;\text{g/mol}$.

$$
\Delta h_{\text{rxn}} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}}{M_{\text{NCO}}} = \frac{-100{,}000 \;\text{J/mol}}{42 \;\text{g/mol}} = -2381 \;\text{J/g} = -2.381 \;\text{MJ/kg}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** $\Delta H$ is in J per mole; we need J per kg of NCO groups reacting. Divide by the molar mass of the NCO functional group (CO = 28, N = 14 → total 42 g/mol).

**Step 2 — Apply the adiabatic energy balance.**

For an adiabatic (no heat loss) closed system, the entire reaction enthalpy raises the temperature of the polymerizing mass:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{adiabatic}} = \frac{-\Delta h_{\text{rxn}}}{C_p}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{adiabatic}} = \frac{2.381 \times 10^6 \;\text{J/kg}}{1800 \;\text{J/(kg \cdot K)}}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{\text{adiabatic}} = 1323 \;\text{K}
$$

**Step 3 — Reality check and physical interpretation.**

The computed value of **1323 K** is enormous — far above the decomposition temperature of polyurethane (≈ 300 °C). The real temperature rise is much smaller because:

- **Heat is lost to the mould walls** (the mould acts as a heat sink, not adiabatic).
- The **reaction is diffusion-limited** long before completion.
- A real measured $\Delta T$ in a RIM tool is typically **30–80 K** above the mould set-point.

The *adiabatic* calculation therefore gives a **conservative worst-case upper bound** used by mould designers to size cooling channels and select mould steel grades.

**Step 4 — Compute the steady-state mould wall heat flux.**

If the in-mould residence time is $t_{\text{res}} = 90 \;\text{s}$ and only a fraction $\alpha = 0.15$ of the reaction enthalpy reaches the mould wall (the rest is stored in the part):

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{wall}} = \alpha \cdot \dot{m} \cdot (-\Delta h_{\text{rxn}})
$$

For a $\dot{m} = 2 \;\text{kg/s}$ line:

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{wall}} = 0.15 \times 2 \;\text{kg/s} \times 2.381 \times 10^6 \;\text{J/kg} = 7.14 \times 10^5 \;\text{W} = 714 \;\text{kW}
$$

This figure tells the designer the cooling system must reject on the order of **hundreds of kW** of heat continuously.

## 3.2 Derivation: Clamp Tonnage for a RIM Part

**Given:** A polyurethane RIM automotive fascia with projected area $A_{\text{proj}} = 1.2 \;\text{m}^2$ and the cavity pressure at end-of-fill $p_{\text{cav}} = 0.6 \;\text{MPa}$.

**Step 1 — Write the force balance on the mould platens.**

The hydraulic or toggle clamp must resist the force trying to push the mould halves apart:

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} = p_{\text{cav}} \times A_{\text{proj}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} = 0.6 \times 10^6 \;\text{Pa} \times 1.2 \;\text{m}^2 = 0.72 \times 10^6 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in tons (the unit used by injection-moulding press manufacturers).**

Using the conversion $1 \;\text{ton-force} \approx 10{,}000 \;\text{N}$:

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} = \frac{0.72 \times 10^6}{10{,}000} = 72 \;\text{tons}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is a *low* tonnage figure. The same part in conventional IM would need 250–400 tons. This 4–6× reduction in clamp tonnage is one of the central economic advantages of RIM, frequently asked in board exams.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — RIM vs. Conventional Injection Moulding

| Parameter | RIM | Conventional IM (thermoplastic) |
|---|---|---|
| Feedstock state | Two liquid monomers | Solid polymer pellets |
| Polymer formation | **In-mould reaction** | Pre-existing polymer is melted |
| Injection pressure | **1 – 20 MPa** | 70 – 140 MPa |
| Mould temperature | 40 – 80 °C | 200 – 300 °C |
| Clamp tonnage | **Low (10 – 100 t typical)** | High (50 – 4000 t) |
| Mould material | Aluminium, zinc alloy, epoxy | Hardened tool steel |
| Cycle time | 30 s – 5 min | 5 s – 2 min |
| Part wall thickness | 3 – 25 mm (thick sections possible) | 0.5 – 6 mm (limited) |
| Material class | **Thermosets** (PU, polyurea, nylon-6) | Thermoplastics (PP, ABS, PA, etc.) |
| Scrap / recyclability | Limited (thermoset, cannot remelt) | High (thermoplastic, regrind possible) |
| Capital cost | Low–medium | Medium–very high |
| Typical part examples | Bumpers, fascia, dashboards, shoe soles, medical enclosures | Bottle caps, housings, gears, toys |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RIM Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the functional flow of the entire RIM line, from raw chemical storage to finished demoulded part. (A pure functional flow is used because the physical geometry of impingement mixers is too complex for a Mermaid representation.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TankA[Storage Tank A: Isocyanate MDI] --> CondA[Temperature Conditioner 20 to 40 C]
    TankB[Storage Tank B: Polyol plus Chain Extender plus Catalyst] --> CondB[Temperature Conditioner 20 to 40 C]

    CondA --> PumpA[High Precision Metering Pump A]
    CondB --> PumpB[High Precision Metering Pump B]

    PumpA --> Mixer[Impingement Mixing Head High Reynolds Turbulent]
    PumpB --> Mixer

    Mixer --> Gate[Mould Gate and Runner]
    Gate --> Cavity[Heated Mould Cavity 40 to 80 C]
    Cavity --> React[Polymerization Reaction NCO plus OH]
    React --> Cure[Curing and Gelation 30 to 180 s]
    Cure --> Eject[Demoulding and Part Ejection Robot]
    Eject --> Trim[Deflashing and Trimming]
    Trim --> Part[Finished RIM Part]

    Mixer -. Recycle / Self Cleaning .-> Recycle[Solvent Flush Cycle]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The rightward arrows trace the production flow. The dashed line to "Recycle / Self Cleaning" indicates a critical RIM-specific feature — between shots, a small solvent (often **dibutyl phthalate** or compressed air for *air-purged* heads) flushes the mixing chamber to **prevent in-mixer gelation**, which would otherwise destroy the equipment.

## 4.2 RIM Mixing Head — Internal Schematic (Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InletA[Inlet A: Isocyanate Stream] --> NozA[Nozzle A Impingement Jet]
    InletB[Inlet B: Polyol Stream] --> NozB[Nozzle B Impingement Jet]

    NozA --> Chamber[Mixing Chamber 0.5 to 5 mL Volume]
    NozB --> Chamber

    Chamber --> MixZone[Turbulent Mixing Zone Re greater than 10000]
    MixZone --> Outlet[Mixed Stream to Mould Gate]
    Outlet --> Clean[Cleaning Piston Recycles Material]
    Clean -. back to .-> Chamber
```

> **Key Takeaway:** The mixing chamber is **tiny** (typically 0.5–5 mL) precisely to minimize the residence time of the mixed reactants before they leave for the mould. Anything that gels inside the chamber is wasted material *and* a maintenance nightmare.

## 4.3 RIM Production Cell — Equipment Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StorageArea[Raw Material Storage]
        TA[Tank A Isocyanate]
        TB[Tank B Polyol Blend]
    end

    subgraph MeteringUnit[Metering and Conditioning Unit]
        CA[Heat Exchanger A]
        CB[Heat Exchanger B]
        PA[Axial Piston Pump A]
        PB[Axial Piston Pump B]
    end

    subgraph MixingStation[Mixing Head and Mould]
        MH[Impingement Mixing Head]
        ML[Clamping Unit 10 to 100 t]
        MC[Mould Cavity]
    end

    subgraph PostProcessing[Demould and Post-Processing]
        RB[Robot Arm Ejector]
        TR[Trimming Station]
        QM[Quality Check]
    end

    TA --> CA --> PA --> MH
    TB --> CB --> PB --> MH
    MH --> MC
    ML --- MC
    MC --> RB --> TR --> QM
```

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, PCPOT503]`
**Differentiate between Reaction Injection Moulding (RIM) and conventional Injection Moulding (IM) in terms of (a) feedstock state, (b) mould temperature, and (c) injection pressure.**

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Feedstock state:** RIM uses **two low-viscosity reactive liquid monomers/polymers** (e.g., polyol + isocyanate) that polymerize inside the mould. Conventional IM uses a **pre-formed thermoplastic polymer** in the form of pellets, which is melted in the machine barrel before injection.
>
> **(b) Mould temperature:** RIM operates at **40–80 °C** (low temperature) because the reaction is exothermic and self-curing. Conventional IM requires **200–300 °C** mould temperatures to prevent premature freezing of the thermoplastic melt at the cavity wall.
>
> **(c) Injection pressure:** RIM is a **low-pressure process (1–20 MPa)** because the reactants are low-viscosity liquids. Conventional IM uses **high pressures (70–140 MPa)** to push a viscous polymer melt through runners and into thin sections.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PCPOT503]`
**What is the NCO index in polyurethane RIM? What does an NCO index of 100 signify?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **NCO index** is the ratio (expressed as a percentage) of the actual number of isocyanate (–NCO) equivalents used in a polyurethane formulation to the theoretical number required to react stoichiometrically with the available hydroxyl (–OH) groups.
>
> An NCO index of **100** indicates **perfect stoichiometry** — exactly enough isocyanate is present to consume all the polyol hydroxyls, with no excess of either reactant. Indices above 100 indicate an **excess of isocyanate**, which generally produces harder, more rigid, more cross-linked polyurethanes; indices below 100 indicate a **polyol excess**, producing softer, more flexible parts with unreacted OH chain ends.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "RIM" with "IM"** in your answer — examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this single error in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Always mention both chemical AND mechanical aspects** of RIM in descriptive answers (e.g., "isocyanate + polyol" AND "high-pressure impingement mixing") — failing to mention one or the other is the most common cause of losing 3–4 marks.
> 3. **For numerical problems, ALWAYS show unit conversion steps** — KTU examiners are specifically trained to penalize "answer-without-working" even if the final number is correct.
> 4. **Avoid writing "Reaction Injection Molding" (American spelling)** — use the British **"Moulding"** as per the official KTU syllabus.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCPOT503, Module 1, Q Choice A]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **Reaction Injection Moulding (RIM) process** for the manufacture of polyurethane parts. Explain the role of each major equipment unit and the sequence of process stages.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polyurethane RIM automotive fascia has a projected mould area of **0.9 m²**. The cavity pressure during injection reaches **0.55 MPa**. Calculate: (i) the **clamp tonnage** required, and (ii) the **fill time** if the part volume is $4.5 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^3$, the mixed density is $1100 \;\text{kg/m}^3$, and the total metering rate is $3.0 \;\text{kg/s}$.  **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Step 1 — Process Definition.** Reaction Injection Moulding is a low-pressure polymer processing technique in which two highly reactive liquid streams — typically a **polyol blend (B-side)** and an **isocyanate (A-side)** — are metered, mixed at high velocity, and injected into a closed, heated mould where they undergo a **step-growth polymerization** to form a polyurethane part.

**Step 2 — Equipment Description (refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1).**

1. **Storage tanks (A and B):** Hold isocyanate and polyol blend respectively. Tanks are **agitated, temperature-controlled (20–40 °C), and nitrogen-blanketed** to prevent moisture ingress (water reacts with isocyanate to form CO₂, causing bubbles and defective parts).
2. **Heat exchangers / conditioners:** Maintain tight temperature control to stabilize viscosity and ensure consistent metering.
3. **High-precision metering pumps:** Usually **axial-piston or in-line piston pumps** with servo control. They must deliver the two streams in the **exact stoichiometric ratio** dictated by the formulation.
4. **Impingement mixing head:** The heart of the RIM machine. The two streams enter through **opposed or cross-jet nozzles** and collide at high Reynolds number ($\text{Re} > 10^4$), producing turbulent mixing in **< 0.1 s**.
5. **Clamping unit and mould:** A modest hydraulic or mechanical clamp (typically 10–100 tons) holds a **lightweight aluminium or zinc mould** at 40–80 °C.
6. **Cleaning / recycling system:** A solvent flush or air purge removes residual mixture from the mixing head between shots to prevent gelation.
7. **Demoulding robot and trimming station:** Ejects the part, deflashes it, and routes it to quality inspection.

**Step 3 — Process Sequence.**
1. **Conditioning** of feed stocks → 2. **Metering** by precision pumps → 3. **Impingement mixing** in the mixhead → 4. **Mould filling** at low pressure → 5. **In-mould polymerization** (cream → gel → rise → cure) → 6. **Demoulding** → 7. **Trimming and inspection**.

**Step 4 — Key distinguishing features.** (Mention these to secure full marks.) Low injection pressure, low mould temperature, low clamp tonnage, *in-situ* polymerization, ability to mould thick sections (3–25 mm), and the use of lightweight non-steel moulds.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Part (i) — Clamp Tonnage Calculation:**

Write the force balance on the mould platen:

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} = p_{\text{cav}} \times A_{\text{proj}}
$$

Substitute $p_{\text{cav}} = 0.55 \;\text{MPa} = 0.55 \times 10^6 \;\text{Pa}$ and $A_{\text{proj}} = 0.9 \;\text{m}^2$:

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} = 0.55 \times 10^6 \times 0.9 = 4.95 \times 10^5 \;\text{N}
$$

Convert to tons (1 ton ≈ 10,000 N):

$$
F_{\text{clamp}} = \frac{4.95 \times 10^5}{10{,}000} = 49.5 \;\text{tons}
$$

> **Answer:** The required clamp tonnage is **≈ 49.5 tons** — a low figure characteristic of RIM.

**Part (ii) — Fill Time Calculation:**

Volumetric flow rate from mass flow rate:

$$
Q = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{\text{mix}}} = \frac{3.0 \;\text{kg/s}}{1100 \;\text{kg/m}^3} = 2.727 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^3\text{/s}
$$

Mould fill time:

$$
t_{\text{fill}} = \frac{V_{\text{part}}}{Q} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.727 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
t_{\text{fill}} = 1.65 \;\text{s}
$$

> **Answer:** The fill time is **≈ 1.65 seconds**. This is typical of RIM — sub-2 s fills are the norm because the parts must fill before the reaction reaches gel point.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCPOT503, Module 1, Q Choice B]`**

**(a)** Explain the **chemistry of the polyurethane RIM reaction**. Discuss the role of the **NCO index**, **catalyst**, **chain extender**, and **blowing agent** in controlling the final part properties.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **RIM, RRIM, and SRIM** in a tabular form. List **four industrial applications** of RIM and explain **two advantages and two limitations** of the process.  **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Step 1 — The Core Reaction.** Polyurethane is formed by the **step-growth addition reaction** between an **isocyanate group (–NCO)** and a **hydroxyl group (–OH)**:

$$
\text{R–NCO} + \text{HO–R'} \longrightarrow \text{R–NH–COO–R'}
$$

The product is a **urethane (carbamate) linkage**. The reaction is **exothermic** ($\Delta H \approx -100 \;\text{kJ/mol}$ per NCO). With **polyfunctional** monomers (diisocyanate + polyol with $f \geq 2$), the reaction produces a **cross-linked network** — the basis of thermoset polyurethane.

**Step 2 — NCO Index.** As defined in Section 5.1, the NCO index is the ratio of actual to theoretical NCO. An index of **100** is stoichiometric. Higher indices (105–115) give harder, more rigid parts; lower indices (90–98) give softer, more elastomeric parts.

**Step 3 — Catalyst.** Tertiary amines (e.g., **DABCO — 1,4-diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane**) and organotin compounds (e.g., **dibutyltin dilaurate**) are used to **accelerate** the NCO–OH reaction. The catalyst concentration is the principal tool the formulator uses to control **cream time, gel time, and demould time**.

**Step 4 — Chain Extender.** Short-chain diols such as **1,4-butanediol** or aromatic diamines (e.g., **DETDA** — diethyltoluenediamine, used in polyurea RIM) are added to build **hard-segment** domains. Hard segments raise the **modulus, hardness, and upper-use temperature** of the finished part.

**Step 5 — Blowing Agent.** For **foam RIM** (integral-skin foams used in steering wheels and armrests), a small amount of water reacts with isocyanate:

$$
\text{R–NCO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{R–NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2
$$

The liberated **CO₂** blows the foam, and the resulting amine immediately reacts with more isocyanate to form **urea linkages**, creating a **dense skin** and a **cellular core** in a single operation.

> **Summary Property Control Table:**

| Ingredient | Primary Effect on Final Part |
|---|---|
| Polyol (B-side) | Determines flexibility, low-temperature properties, hydrolytic stability |
| Isocyanate (A-side) | Determines hardness, heat resistance, modulus |
| Chain extender | Modulates hard-segment content → stiffness |
| Catalyst | Controls reaction rate → cycle time |
| Blowing agent | Creates cellular structure → density reduction |

---

**Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | RIM | RRIM (Reinforced RIM) | SRIM (Structural RIM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reinforcement | None | Milled glass fibre / mica in B-side (5–25 wt%) | Continuous fibre mat preplaced in mould |
| Stiffness | Low–medium | Medium–high | Very high |
| Equipment | Standard RIM line | Modified — abrasive fillers wear mixhead | Preform placement + RIM line |
| Applications | Bumpers, fascia, shoe soles | Load-bearing panels, spare-wheel wells | Door frames, bumper beams, seat structures |
| Cycle time | Shortest | Slightly longer | Longest (preform placement + reaction) |
| Capital cost | Lowest | Medium | High |

**Four Industrial Applications of RIM:**

1. **Automotive bumper fascias and body panels** (e.g., Pontiac Fiero, Citroën, Tata, Maruti models).
2. **Steering wheels and instrument-panel covers** (integral-skin foam RIM).
3. **Shoe soles and sports footwear** (high-resilience PU elastomer RIM).
4. **Medical equipment housings and enclosures** (electrically insulating, dimensionally stable, low tooling cost for small batches).

**Two Advantages of RIM:**
1. **Low tooling and clamp cost** — aluminium moulds and 10–100 t clamps vs. steel moulds and hundreds of tons for IM.
2. **Ability to mould large, thick-walled parts** in a single shot (wall thicknesses of 3–25 mm are routine).

**Two Limitations of RIM:**
1. **Thermoset scrap cannot be remelted** — runners, sprues, and off-spec parts must be landfilled or downcycled, raising the per-part material cost.
2. **Stringent moisture/isocyanate sensitivity** — any moisture in polyol, fillers, or air reacts with isocyanate to form CO₂, causing voids and density variations; nitrogen blanketing and dry-air clean rooms are mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and pin it above your desk for the last 24 hours before the exam.**

- ✅ **RIM = low pressure + low temperature + in-mould reaction.** Memorize the triplet **1–20 MPa / 40–80 °C / in-situ polymerization**.
- ✅ The **impingement mixing head** is the most exam-relevant subsystem — know its function, residence time (< 0.1 s), and Reynolds number criterion ($Re > 10^4$).
- ✅ The **A-side is isocyanate (MDI)**; the **B-side is polyol + chain extender + catalyst (+ blowing agent + fillers)**.
- ✅ **NCO index = (actual NCO / theoretical NCO) × 100 %**; index 100 is stoichiometric.
- ✅ Key reaction: **R–NCO + HO–R' → R–NHCOO–R'** (urethane linkage), $\Delta H \approx -100 \;\text{kJ/mol}$ per NCO.
- ✅ **Water + isocyanate → amine + CO₂** is the blowing reaction in foam RIM.
- ✅ Cream time → gel time → rise time → demould time is the **polymerization timeline**; demould in **30–180 s**.
- ✅ **Cycle time** of RIM = $t_{\text{fill}} + t_{\text{react}} + t_{\text{cool}} + t_{\text{eject}}$.
- ✅ **Clamp tonnage** = $p_{\text{cavity}} \times A_{\text{projected}}$; RIM needs only 10–100 t for typical automotive parts.
- ✅ RIM uses **lightweight moulds** (Al, Zn, epoxy) — a major economic advantage over IM.
- ✅ RIM produces **thermoset parts** → cannot be remelted or recycled as easily as thermoplastics from IM.
- ✅ **RRIM** = RIM + milled glass fibre; **SRIM** = RIM + continuous fibre preform in mould.
- ✅ **Caprolactam RIM (NyRIM)** is the nylon-6 analog; used for large structural parts.
- ✅ Industrial applications: **bumpers, fascia, dashboards, steering wheels, shoe soles, medical housings**.
- ✅ Avoid common spelling error: **"Moulding"** (British), not **"Molding"** — KTU follows British English.
- ✅ Numerical problems always ask for: **(i) clamp tonnage, (ii) fill time, (iii) adiabatic temperature rise, (iv) cycle time, or (v) Reynolds number in the mixhead** — be fluent in all five.
- ✅ Always mention **nitrogen blanketing** of tanks to explain moisture control in numerical / descriptive answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Extrusion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Extrusion** is a continuous, high-volume polymer processing operation in which a thermoplastic or elastomeric material is forced under high pressure through a shaped *die* to produce a product of uniform cross-section (rods, sheets, pipes, films, profiles, or wire coatings). The process is governed by the conversion of solid polymer pellets into a homogeneous melt, followed by pressure development and shaping. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, extrusion is classified under **Primary Shaping Processes** in polymer product manufacturing. It operates on the principle of **drag-induced melt conveying** inside a heated barrel using a rotating Archimedean screw. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must clearly differentiate extrusion from *injection molding*. The key distinction is **continuity** — extrusion produces an infinitely long profile, while injection molding is a cyclic, *discontinuous* batch process producing discrete parts. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Meat Grinder" of Polymers Imagine squeezing a tube of toothpaste. As you push from the back, a continuous ribbon of paste emerges from the nozzle, taking the *exact* shape of the nozzle opening. Now replace your hand with a **rotating screw** inside a heated barrel, and the toothpaste with **thermoplastic pellets**, and you have an extruder. **Real-world analogy mapping:** | Real Life Action | Extruder Component | |---|---| | Holding the tube | **Barrel** | | Pressing with hand | **Rotating Screw** | | Toothpaste emerging | **Extrudate** | | Tube nozzle | **Die** | | Heat from sunlight | **Heater Bands** | The screw does *not* just push — it **melts, mixes, meters, and pumps** the polymer, all in a single continuous motion. The output rate is governed by screw speed, melt viscosity, and die resistance. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants - **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** **0.15 – 0.30 kWh/kg** for typical polyolefin extrusion. - **Melt Pressure Range:** **5 – 35 MPa** (typical operating window). - **L/D Ratio (Length-to-Diameter):** Standard industrial range is **20:1 to 30:1**, with modern barrier screws extending to **36:1**. - **Compression Ratio:** Typically **2:1 to 4:1** for general purpose thermoplastics. - **Screw Speed:** Standard industrial ceiling is approximately **100 – 120 rpm** for single-screw extruders. - **Die Swell (Extrudate Swell):** Typical swell ratio **B = D_exit / D_die ≈ 1.10 – 1.50** depending on polymer and shear rate. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Process Flow — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown The extrusion cycle, while continuous, can be partitioned into **seven discrete functional stages**: 1. **Feeding (Solids Conveying):** Polymer pellets (granules) are gravity-fed from the hopper into the screw channel. The solids are conveyed forward by *friction against the barrel* (which must be higher than friction against the screw root). This is the **only stage where the polymer is solid**. 2. **Compaction (Delay Zone):** Air trapped between pellets is expelled back through the hopper. This zone has a constant (shallow) channel depth. 3. **Melting (Phase Change Zone):** The polymer begins to melt against the hot barrel walls. A **melt film** forms, and a **melt pool** accumulates in the screw channel. The melting mechanism is governed by **Tadmor's Melting Model** (a KTU-high-yield topic). 4. **Melt Conveying (Metering Zone):** Fully molten polymer is pumped under pressure. The channel depth is constant (and shallowest in this zone), maximizing shear and pumping efficiency. 5. **Die Entry / Mixing: Homogenization through static mixers or dynamic mixers (e.g., Maddock, Saxton) ensures uniform temperature and composition. 6. **Shaping at Die:** Melt flows through the die land, where velocity profile is reshaped. The extrudate takes the die geometry. 7. **Cooling, Haul-off, and Cutting (Post-Extrusion):** The extrudate is cooled via air, water spray, or chilled rolls, pulled at a calibrated rate (haul-off), and cut (e.g., into pellets in a *compounding* line). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Concept Trigger:** The question *"Why does the metering zone have the shallowest channel depth?"* is a frequently tested concept. The answer is: *A shallow channel maximizes the pressure-generating capability of the screw by increasing the shear rate in the melt, hence improving pumping efficiency.* ## 2.2 Screw Geometry — Anatomy of an Extruder Screw A standard metering-type screw comprises three geometric zones: | Zone | Channel Depth | Function | L/D Occupied | |---|---|---|---| | **Feed Zone** | Deep (constant) | Solids conveying | ~50% | | **Compression (Transition) Zone** | Tapered | Melting + Compaction | ~25–30% | | **Metering Zone** | Shallow (constant) | Melt pumping | ~20–25% | **Key Geometric Parameters:** - **D** = Screw diameter (m) - **L** = Screw length (m) - **H_f** = Channel depth in feed zone (m) - **H_m** = Channel depth in metering zone (m) - **θ** = Helix angle (typically 17.66°, which gives tan θ = 1/π ≈ 0.318 — *a famous KTU derivation*!) - **W** = Channel width perpendicular to flight - **e** = Flight width - **δ** = Clearance between flight tip and barrel (typically 0.05 – 0.10 mm) ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following formulas are essential for KTU ESE numerical problems and short-answer derivations. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta$ | **Drag Flow** — forward pumping due to relative barrel motion | m³/s | | 2 | $Q_{pressure} = -\frac{H^3 W \sin^2\theta}{12 \eta} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial z}$ | **Pressure Flow** — backward leakage due to die resistance | m³/s | | 3 | $Q_{leakage} = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 \delta^3 \tan\theta}{12 \eta} \cdot \frac{P}{\ell}$ | **Leakage Flow** — flow over the flight tips | m³/s | | 4 | $Q_{net} = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure} - Q_{leakage}$ | **Net Throughput** (Newtonian approximation) | m³/s | | 5 | $\dot{\gamma}_{wall} = \frac{\pi D N}{H}$ | **Apparent Shear Rate** at barrel wall | s⁻¹ | | 6 | $\tau_{wall} = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}_{wall}$ | **Shear Stress** at barrel wall (Newtonian) | Pa | | 7 | $P_{max} = \frac{6 \pi \eta D N L \sin\theta \cos\theta}{H^2}$ | **Maximum Pressure Buildup** (closed die) | Pa | | 8 | $E = \frac{P_{max}}{\dot{m}}$ | **Specific Energy** of extrusion | J/kg | | 9 | $P_{shaft} = \tau_{wall} \cdot A_{barrel} \cdot v_{relative}$ | **Shaft Power** required | W | | 10 | $B = \frac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}$ | **Die Swell Ratio** (extrudate swell) | dimensionless | | 11 | $\tau = \tau_0 + K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | **Power-Law (Ostwald-de Waele) Model** for polymer melt | Pa | | 12 | $\eta_{app} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | **Apparent Viscosity** (power-law fluid) | Pa·s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formula Notation:** $D$ = screw diameter, $N$ = screw rotational speed (rev/s), $H$ = channel depth (metering zone), $\theta$ = helix angle, $\eta$ = viscosity, $\ell$ = length of metering zone, $\dot{m}$ = mass throughput, $K$ = consistency index, $n$ = power-law index ($n < 1$ for shear-thinning polymers). ## 2.4 Engineering Utility Extrusion is the **single most important polymer shaping process** by tonnage, accounting for nearly **65% of all thermoplastics processed globally**. Its applications include: - **Pipe extrusion** (PVC, HDPE) - **Film blowing** (LDPE, LLDPE) - **Sheet extrusion** (PS, PP, PET) - **Wire and cable coating** (PVC, XLPE) - **Profile extrusion** (window frames, gaskets) - **Compounding** (masterbatch, filled/reinforced polymers) - **Co-extrusion** (multi-layer films for food packaging) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Drag Flow in Metering Zone (Newtonian Fluid) **Statement:** Derive the expression for drag flow rate $Q_{drag}$ in the metering zone of an extruder screw. ### Step 1: Unwrap the Screw Channel The helical screw channel is geometrically "unwrapped" into a flat rectangular channel of width $W$ (perpendicular to flight) and length $\pi D \sin\theta$ per turn. The barrel moves relative to the screw at a velocity equal to the **tangential velocity**: $$ v_{bx} = \pi D N $$ where $v_{bx}$ is the barrel velocity relative to screw, $D$ is the screw diameter, and $N$ is the screw rotational speed (in rev/s). ### Step 2: Decompose Velocity into Channel Coordinates The barrel velocity $v_{bx}$ is resolved into two components relative to the channel — one **down the channel** ($v_x$) and one **across the channel** ($v_z$): $$ v_x = v_{bx} \sin\theta = \pi D N \sin\theta $$ $$ v_z = v_{bx} \cos\theta = \pi D N \cos\theta $$ **Conversion logic:** The down-channel component drives the polymer forward, while the cross-channel component causes a circulatory flow that aids mixing. ### Step 3: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity (Couette Flow) Treating flow between the screw root and barrel wall as plane Couette flow with no pressure gradient (drag-only), the velocity profile is linear: $$ v_x(y) = \frac{v_{bx} \sin\theta}{H} \cdot y $$ where $y$ is the distance from the screw root (varies from 0 to $H$). ### Step 4: Integrate Across Channel Depth The volumetric flow rate per unit width is: $$ q = \int_0^H v_x(y)\, dy = \int_0^H \frac{\pi D N \sin\theta}{H} \cdot y\, dy $$ ### Step 5: Evaluate the Integral $$ q = \frac{\pi D N \sin\theta}{H} \cdot \left[ \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_0^H = \frac{\pi D N \sin\theta \cdot H}{2} $$ ### Step 6: Multiply by Channel Width The channel width perpendicular to flight is: $$ W = \pi D \sin\theta - e \approx \pi D \sin\theta $$ Multiplying the unit-width flow by $W$ and accounting for the down-channel orientation: $$ Q_{drag} = q \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta = \frac{\pi D N \sin\theta \cdot H}{2} \cdot \pi D \sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta $$ ### Step 7: Final Simplified Result $$ Q_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta $$ **Conversion logic:** This is the *forward pumping capacity* — proportional to screw speed $N$ and channel depth $H$, and maximized at $\theta = 45°$ but practically chosen as $\theta \approx 17.66°$ (where $\tan\theta = 1/\pi$) to balance down-channel and cross-channel flow. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure Flow in Metering Zone **Statement:** Derive the pressure flow $Q_{pressure}$ caused by die resistance acting as a back-pressure. ### Step 1: Apply the Navier–Stokes Equation for Channel Flow For steady, fully-developed, incompressible flow down a rectangular channel of width $W$ and depth $H$, with pressure gradient $\partial P / \partial z$: $$ 0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \eta \frac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial y^2} $$ ### Step 2: Integrate Twice with Respect to $y$ $$ \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \cdot y + C_1 $$ $$ v_z(y) = \frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \cdot y^2 + C_1 y + C_2 $$ ### Step 3: Apply No-Slip Boundary Conditions At $y = 0$ (screw root): $v_z = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$. At $y = H$ (barrel wall): $v_z = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = -\dfrac{H}{2\eta} \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z}$. ### Step 4: Velocity Profile $$ v_z(y) = \frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial z}\left(y^2 - H y\right) $$ This is a **parabolic** profile — opposite in direction to drag flow. ### Step 5: Integrate to Get Volumetric Flow $$ Q_{pressure} = W \int_0^H v_z(y)\, dy $$ $$ Q_{pressure} = W \cdot \frac{1}{2\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} \int_0^H (y^2 - H y)\, dy $$ ### Step 6: Evaluate the Integral $$ \int_0^H (y^2 - H y)\, dy = \left[\frac{y^3}{3} - \frac{H y^2}{2}\right]_0^H = \frac{H^3}{3} - \frac{H^3}{2} = -\frac{H^3}{6} $$ ### Step 7: Substitute and Simplify $$ Q_{pressure} = -\frac{W H^3}{12\eta} \frac{\partial P}{\partial z} $$ ### Step 8: Convert to Down-Channel Component Using $z = x \sin\theta$ (the down-channel coordinate), we get: $$ Q_{pressure} = -\frac{H^3 W \sin^2\theta}{12\eta} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial x} $$ The negative sign indicates **opposite direction to drag flow** — pressure flow opposes forward pumping. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Net Throughput (Newtonian Closed-Form) **Statement:** Combine drag, pressure, and leakage flow to obtain the net extruder output. ### Step 1: Define Screw Geometry Constants For a metering zone with channel depth $H$, length $\ell$ (axial length), helix angle $\theta$, and flight clearance $\delta$: - Down-channel width: $W = \pi D \sin\theta$ - Pressure drop across metering zone: $\Delta P$ - Pressure gradient: $\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\ell / \sin\theta} = \dfrac{\Delta P \sin\theta}{\ell}$ ### Step 2: Sum the Three Components $$ Q_{net} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}_{Drag} - \underbrace{\frac{H^3 W \sin^2\theta}{12\eta} \cdot \frac{\Delta P \sin\theta}{\ell}}_{Pressure} - \underbrace{\frac{\pi^2 D^2 \delta^3 \tan\theta}{12\eta} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\ell}}_{Leakage} $$ ### Step 3: Substitute $W = \pi D \sin\theta$ $$ Q_{net} = \frac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta - \frac{\pi D H^3 \sin^3\theta}{12 \eta} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\ell} - \frac{\pi^2 D^2 \delta^3 \tan\theta}{12\eta} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\ell} $$ ### Step 4: Operating Characteristics This equation yields the **operating characteristic curve** — a linear relation between $Q$ and $\Delta P$ for a given $N$. When plotted alongside the **die characteristic** (linear relation between $Q$ and $\Delta P$ for the die alone), the intersection gives the **actual operating point**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"Sketch the operating characteristic curve"*, students should: > - Plot $Q$ (y-axis) vs. $\Delta P$ (x-axis). > - Show $Q_{drag}$ as the y-intercept (zero pressure). > - Show $Q_{net}$ as a downward-sloping line. > - The die line is an upward-sloping line from origin. > - Their intersection is the **operating point** (working pressure and throughput). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Key Parameters on Throughput | Parameter | Change | Effect on $Q_{drag}$ | Effect on $Q_{pressure}$ | Net Effect | |---|---|---|---|---| | Screw speed $N$ ↑ | Increase | Increases linearly | No effect | **$Q$ increases** | | Channel depth $H$ ↑ | Increase | Increases linearly | Increases as $H^3$ | Mixed; reduces pumping efficiency | | Die resistance ↑ | Increase | No effect | Increases | **$Q$ decreases** | | Melt viscosity $\eta$ ↑ | Increase | No effect | Decreases (i.e., $Q_p$ ↑ in magnitude) | **$Q$ decreases** | | Helix angle $\theta$ ↑ | Increase | Increases (until 45°) | Increases | Trade-off | | Temperature ↑ | Increase | No effect | $\eta$ drops, so $\vert Q_p \vert$ rises | **$Q$ increases** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Extrusion Line — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hopper
Pellet Feed] --> B[Solid Conveying Zone
Feed Zone] B --> C[Melting Zone

Maddock or Saxton] E --> F[Adapter and Breaker Plate
Pressure Stabilization] F --> G[Shaping Die
Profile or Film Die] G --> H[Calibration and Cooling
Air or Water Bath] H --> I[Haul-Off Unit
Pulling Caterpillar] I --> J[Cutting Station
Saw or Cutter] J --> K[Finished Product
Pipes, Sheets, Profiles] L[Heater Bands
Barrel Zones] -.-> B L -.-> C L -.-> D M[Drive Motor and Gearbox] -.-> D N[Temperature Controllers] -.-> L O[Pressure Transducer] -.-> F style A fill:#e1f5ff style K fill:#d4edda style M fill:#fff3cd style L fill:#fff3cd style N fill:#f8d7da style O fill:#f8d7da ``` ## 4.2 Screw Geometry — Functional Zone Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SCREW [Extruder Screw Geometry] F1[Hopper End] F2[Feed Zone
Deep Channel
Solids Conveying] F3[Compression Zone
Tapered Channel
Melting and Compaction] F4[Metering Zone
Shallow Channel
Melt Pumping] F5[Die End] end F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 --> F5 P1[Solid Polymer Pellets] -.-> F2 P2[Sintered and Softened Polymer] -.-> F3 P3[Fully Molten and Homogenized Melt] -.-> F4 P4[Pressurized Melt Stream] -.-> F5 style F2 fill:#cce5ff style F3 fill:#ffe0b3 style F4 fill:#ffcccc style P3 fill:#d4edda ``` ## 4.3 Operating Characteristics — Schematic Intersection ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OPLOT [Throughput Pressure Plot] X[Delta P axis] Y[Q axis] end O1[Operating Characteristic Line
Extruder Screw
Negative Slope
Qnet versus Delta P] O2[Die Characteristic Line
Positive Slope
Q versus Delta P] OP[Operating Point
Intersection of O1 and O2] O1 --- OP O2 --- OP OP --> PRESSURE[Working Pressure] OP --> THROUGHPUT[Net Throughput Q] style O1 fill:#cce5ff style O2 fill:#fff3cd style OP fill:#d4edda style PRESSURE fill:#f8d7da style THROUGHPUT fill:#f8d7da ``` ## 4.4 Extruder Types — Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB EX[Extruders] EX --> SSE[Single Screw Extruder] EX --> TSE[Twin Screw Extruder] SSE --> SSE1[Conventional Metering Screw] SSE --> SSE2[Barrier Screw Maddock Type] SSE --> SSE3[Barrier Screw Saxton Type] SSE --> SSE4[Pin Mixing Screw] TSE --> TSE1[Co Rotating TSE] TSE --> TSE2[Counter Rotating TSE] TSE1 --> TSE1A[High Speed Compounding] TSE2 --> TSE2A[Profile and PVC Processing] style SSE fill:#cce5ff style TSE fill:#ffe0b3 style SSE2 fill:#d4edda style TSE1 fill:#d4edda ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define extrusion. List any four thermoplastic products manufactured by extrusion.* **Model Answer:** Extrusion is a continuous polymer processing operation in which a thermoplastic melt is forced under pressure through a shaping die to produce products of uniform cross-section. The polymer is conveyed, melted, and pumped by a rotating screw inside a heated barrel. **Four typical products:** 1. PVC pipes for plumbing and irrigation 2. HDPE films for packaging 3. PET sheets for thermoforming 4. Wire and cable insulation (XLPE/PVC) > [!TIP] > Examiners award full 3 marks for a clear 2-line definition + at least 4 correctly listed products. Generic answers lose half a mark. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between the feed zone and the metering zone of an extruder screw.* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Feed Zone | Metering Zone | |---|---|---| | Polymer state | Solid pellets | Fully molten | | Channel depth | Deep (constant) | Shallow (constant) | | Function | Solids conveying | Pressure generation and pumping | | % of screw L/D | ~50% | ~20–25% | | Dominant mechanism | Friction drag on barrel | Shear-driven drag + pressure flow | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]** *(a)* Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a single-screw extruder. Describe the functions of the feed, compression, and metering zones. **(7 marks)** *(b)* Derive an expression for the drag flow rate in the metering zone of an extruder screw, assuming Newtonian melt behavior. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Part (a) **Construction of a single-screw extruder:** A single-screw extruder consists of the following main components: 1. **Hopper:** A gravity-feed funnel that supplies polymer pellets to the screw. 2. **Barrel:** A thick-walled cylindrical steel shell, externally fitted with **heater bands** (typically 3–5 zones) for heating. 3. **Screw:** A hardened steel shaft with a helical flight; L/D ratio typically 20:1 to 30:1. 4. **Drive system:** A motor connected through a gearbox providing speed control (typically 10–120 rpm). 5. **Breaker plate and screen pack:** Located at the barrel exit to generate back-pressure and filter contaminants. 6. **Die:** The shaping tool that gives the final cross-section. 7. **Cooling, haul-off, and cutting units:** Post-extrusion equipment. **Functional zones of the screw:** - **Feed Zone (L/D ≈ 0–10):** Channel depth is constant and maximum ($H_f$). The polymer remains in pellet form and is conveyed forward by barrel friction. The screw root must be smooth and the barrel roughened to ensure pellets slip on the screw but not on the barrel. - **Compression Zone (L/D ≈ 10–18):** Channel depth tapers from $H_f$ to $H_m$. Compression ratio = $H_f / H_m$ (typically 2:1 to 4:1). Pellets are compacted, air is expelled (back through the hopper), and melting begins against the hot barrel wall. - **Metering Zone (L/D ≈ 18–24):** Channel depth is constant and minimum ($H_m$). The melt is fully homogenized and pumped under high pressure to the die. This zone controls throughput. ### Model Solution — Part (b) **Derivation of Drag Flow Rate:** Consider the metering zone as an unwound rectangular channel of width $W$ perpendicular to the flight, depth $H$, and length $L$.
