The barrel moves relative to the screw at tangential velocity: $$ v_{bx} = \pi D N $$ This is resolved into down-channel and cross-channel components: $$ v_x = \pi D N \sin\theta $$ $$ v_z = \pi D N \cos\theta $$ Treating the drag flow as simple Couette flow (no pressure gradient), the velocity profile between screw root ($y = 0$) and barrel wall ($y = H$) is linear: $$ v_x(y) = \frac{\pi D N \sin\theta}{H} \cdot y $$ The volumetric flow rate per unit channel width: $$ q = \int_0^H v_x(y)\, dy = \frac{\pi D N \sin\theta \cdot H}{2} $$ With channel width $W = \pi D \sin\theta$, the total drag flow is: $$ Q_{drag} = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2} $$ $$ \boxed{Q_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently: > - Forget to multiply by **$\cos\theta$** when converting to volumetric flow rate (loses 1 mark). > - Use $N$ in rpm instead of rev/s (loses 2 marks for dimensional inconsistency). > - Confuse the down-channel width $W$ with the axial pitch (loses 1 mark). > - Forget to mention the **Newtonian assumption** (loses 0.5 mark but significant in examiner's eyes). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]** *(a)* With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a breaker plate, screen pack, and die in a single-screw extruder. Discuss the role of die swell in product quality. **(7 marks)** *(b)* A 60 mm diameter single-screw extruder has a metering zone channel depth of 4 mm and a helix angle of 17.66°. The screw rotates at 80 rpm and extrudes a Newtonian polymer melt of viscosity 250 Pa·s. Calculate: (i) drag flow rate, (ii) maximum pressure buildup at closed die, and (iii) apparent shear rate at the barrel wall. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Part (a) **Breaker plate and screen pack:** The breaker plate is a thick steel plate (50–100 mm) with many parallel holes, located between the screw tip and the die. It serves three functions: 1. Converts **rotational (swirling) flow** into **axial (linear) flow**. 2. Supports the **screen pack** — a stack of wire meshes (typically 20/40/60/80/100 mesh from coarse to fine) that filter contaminants and generate uniform back-pressure. 3. Provides structural support to the screen pack against melt pressure. **Die construction:** The die is the shaping tool and comprises: - **Die body:** Heavy steel housing. - **Die land:** The parallel channel just before exit, which shapes and stabilizes the extrudate. - **Die entry:** Convergent or divergent transition from breaker plate to die land. - **Heater bands:** Maintain melt temperature above solidification point. **Die Swell (Extrudate Swell):** When polymer melt exits the die, the extrudate diameter is **greater** than the die diameter due to **recoverable elastic strain** (memory effect). The swell ratio: $$ B = \frac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}} $$ Causes of die swell: 1. **Elastic recovery** of the melt upon exiting high shear region. 2. **Velocity profile rearrangement** from parabolic (in die) to uniform (in air). Effects on product quality: - Dimensional inaccuracy → must be compensated during die design (e.g., pre-shrink). - Surface roughness if swelling is non-uniform. - Critical in film blowing and pipe extrusion where concentricity matters. ### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** - $D = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m - $H = 4$ mm $= 0.004$ m - $\theta = 17.66° \Rightarrow \sin\theta \approx 0.3035$, $\cos\theta \approx 0.9528$, $\sin\theta\cos\theta \approx 0.2893$ - $N = 80$ rpm $= 80/60 = 1.333$ rev/s - $\eta = 250$ Pa·s - Metering zone length: assume $L = 8D = 0.48$ m (standard assumption when not given) **(i) Drag Flow Rate:** $$ Q_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta $$ $$ Q_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \times \pi^2 \times (0.060)^2 \times 1.333 \times 0.004 \times 0.2893 $$ $$ Q_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \times 9.8696 \times 0.0036 \times 1.333 \times 0.004 \times 0.2893 $$ $$ Q_{drag} = \frac{1}{2} \times 9.8696 \times 0.0036 \times 0.001543 $$ $$ Q_{drag} \approx 2.74 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 27.4 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} $$ **(ii) Maximum Pressure Buildup (Closed Die):** $$ P_{max} = \frac{6 \pi \eta D N L \sin\theta \cos\theta}{H^2} $$ $$ P_{max} = \frac{6 \pi \times 250 \times 0.060 \times 1.333 \times 0.48 \times 0.2893}{(0.004)^2} $$ Numerator: $$ 6 \times 3.1416 \times 250 \times 0.060 \times 1.333 \times 0.48 \times 0.2893 \approx 130.9 $$ Denominator: $$ (0.004)^2 = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ P_{max} \approx \frac{130.9}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 8.18 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 8.18 \text{ MPa} $$ **(iii) Apparent Shear Rate at Barrel Wall:** $$ \dot{\gamma}_{wall} = \frac{\pi D N}{H} $$ $$ \dot{\gamma}_{wall} = \frac{\pi \times 0.060 \times 1.333}{0.004} = \frac{0.2513}{0.004} \approx 62.83 \text{ s}^{-1} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly lose marks for: > - **Not converting rpm to rev/s** — this single error cascades into all three answers (loses up to 3 marks). > - **Wrong assumption for metering zone length** — when not specified, use $L = 8D$ or $L = (L/D)_{metering} \times D$. State the assumption explicitly. > - **Forgetting units in final answer** — answers without units are penalized 0.5 mark each. > - **Incorrect trigonometry** at $\theta = 17.66°$: $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \dfrac{1}{\pi+2}$ is a frequently used identity; verify numerically. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme:** - **Extrusion is a CONTINUOUS** process — injection molding is **DISCONTINUOUS**. - **Single-screw extruder has THREE zones:** Feed (deep, solid), Compression (tapered, melting), Metering (shallow, pumping). - **Drag flow is PROPORTIONAL** to $N$, $H$, and $D^2$ — drives output forward. - **Pressure flow is PROPORTIONAL** to $H^3$ and die resistance — opposes output. - **Leakage flow is usually NEGLIGIBLE** ($< 5\%$) but matters at high pressure. - **Operating point** = intersection of screw characteristic (negative slope) and die characteristic (positive slope). - **Helix angle for max down-channel velocity** is $\theta = 45°$, but $\theta = 17.66°$ ($\tan\theta = 1/\pi$) balances down-channel vs. cross-channel flow. - **Compression ratio** = $H_f / H_m$ — typically 2:1 to 4:1. - **L/D ratio** — typically 20:1 to 30:1 for single-screw extruders. - **Die swell** is due to **elastic recovery** of the polymer melt (memory effect), not thermal expansion. - **Twin-screw extruders** offer better mixing — used in compounding, PVC, and reactive extrusion. - **Newtonian assumption** is valid for **small molecules and low shear rates**; for polymers use **power-law model** $\tau = K\dot{\gamma}^n$ with $n < 1$. - **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** for extrusion = **0.15–0.30 kWh/kg** — KTU expects this benchmark. - **Breaker plate + screen pack** convert swirl to axial flow AND generate back-pressure for homogenization. - **Co-extrusion** = multiple extruders feeding one die → multi-layer products (food packaging films). - **Operating envelope:** Barrel **170–230°C**, Melt pressure **5–35 MPa**, Screw speed **10–120 rpm** for general thermoplastics. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonics:** > - "**F**eed → **C**ompress → **M**eter" = "**F**riends **C**all **M**e" (zones in order). > - Drag = **Drives** forward; Pressure = **Pulls back**; Leakage = **Slips away** over flights. > - Higher $H$ → More drag but **MORE** pressure flow loss (cube effect) → **Net drop** in throughput efficiency.

topics.title: Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Principle # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **PCPOT503 – Polymer Machinery & Product Manufacturing**, the term **"Principle"** in Module 2 refers to the **fundamental working principles of primary polymer processing machinery**, which transform raw polymer feedstock (granules, powder, melt, or dispersion) into finished or semi-finished products of controlled geometry, density, and surface quality. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2 Focus):** > The *principle* of a polymer processing machine is the **physical, thermal, and mechanical basis** upon which the machine transforms a polymeric material from one state (solid/liquid/dispersion) to a shaped product. It encompasses the heat transfer, pressure development, flow mechanics, and solidification (or curing) cycle that define the process. The four governing principles used across virtually all polymer machinery are: 1. **Melt Processing Principle** – Polymer is melted (thermoplastics) and shaped under pressure (e.g., Injection Moulding, Extrusion). 2. **Melt-Forming Principle** – A polymer melt is continuously shaped and solidified (e.g., Film Blowing, Sheet Extrusion, Blow Moulding). 3. **Softening-Forming Principle** – A polymer sheet is softened (below melt) and mechanically formed (e.g., Thermoforming). 4. **Solid-Stage Forming Principle** – Polymer is shaped in the solid or semi-solid state under heat and pressure (e.g., Compression Moulding, Calendaring). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Polymer Kitchen":** > Think of polymer processing machinery as a **highly specialized industrial kitchen**: > > - **Extruder** → like a giant **meat grinder / pasta maker**: it pushes material forward while heating it. > - **Injection Moulding Machine** → like a **syringe injecting hot chocolate into a mould** to make shaped chocolates. > - **Blow Moulding** → like **blowing a glass bottle from molten glass**, but using compressed air and plastic. > - **Compression Moulding** → like a **waffle iron** — the material is placed between two heated plates and squeezed into shape. > - **Thermoforming** → like shaping **heated plastic wrap over a vacuum-formed cup**. Each of these analogies captures the **Principle**: a *combination of heat, pressure, time, and shaping* that converts raw polymer into a finished article. ## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU High-Yield) | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Melt Temperature | $T_m$ | **150 – 320** | °C | | Mould Temperature | $T_{mould}$ | **20 – 200** | °C | | Injection Pressure | $P_{inj}$ | **35 – 140** | MPa | | Clamping Force | $F_{clamp}$ | **50 – 10,000** | kN | | Screw L/D Ratio | $L/D$ | **15 – 30** | — | | Compression Ratio | $CR$ | **2 – 4** | — | | Shear Rate in Extrusion | $\dot{\gamma}$ | **10 – 10,000** | $s^{-1}$ | > [!TIP] > **Board Exam Tip:** Always state the **units** explicitly. KTU examiners frequently deduct ½ mark for missing units in numerical or descriptive answers. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Universal Stages of Polymer Processing Every polymer processing machine — regardless of its principle — operates through three sequential stages. KTU examiners frequently test this: ### Stage 1 — **Preparation / Plasticization** - Solid polymer (granules, powder, sheet) is **heated, melted (or softened), and homogenized**. - Heat is supplied by **external heaters (band heaters)** and **internal shear heating (viscous dissipation)**. - Achieved in an **extruder screw, plasticizing chamber, or heated platen**. ### Stage 2 — **Forming / Shaping / Moulding** - The molten or softened polymer is **forced into a shaping device** (die, mould, cavity, rolls). - Pressure is applied by a **plunger, screw, ram, rolls, or vacuum/pressure differential**. - The principle of **momentum transfer (fluid flow under pressure)** governs this stage. ### Stage 3 — **Solidification / Curing / Cooling** - The shaped polymer is **cooled below its melting ($T_m$) or glass transition ($T_g$) temperature** for thermoplastics. - For **thermosets**, the polymer is **cured by cross-linking** (chemical solidification). - The product is then **ejected, stripped, or wound up**. ## 2.2 Principle of Plasticization (Melt Generation) Polymer melting inside an extruder is governed by two simultaneous mechanisms: $$q_{total} = q_{conduction} + q_{viscous\,dissipation}$$ Where: - $q_{conduction}$ = heat from **external band heaters** (W/m³) - $q_{viscous\,dissipation}$ = heat generated by **shear within the polymer melt** (W/m³) The viscous dissipation term is given by: $$q_{viscous} = \tau \cdot \dot{\gamma} = \eta(\dot{\gamma}) \cdot \dot{\gamma}^2$$ Where: - $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa) - $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate ($s^{-1}$) - $\eta(\dot{\gamma})$ = viscosity at the operating shear rate (Pa·s) ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Following Table is the Most Tested Formula Set in KTU PCPOT503 Module 2 — memorize it thoroughly.** | S.No | Formula | Description | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{inj} = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{A_{proj}}$ | Injection pressure = Clamping force / Projected area | Injection Moulding | | 2 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = \rho \cdot v \cdot A$ | Mass flow rate from volumetric flow | Extrusion | | 3 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D^3 N \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} \cdot f_d$ | Drag flow in extruder (Newtonian) | Extruder output | | 4 | $P_{back} = \dfrac{12 \eta Q L}{w h^3}$ | Pressure flow in extruder die | Die design | | 5 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_eject} - T_w\right)$ | Cooling time in mould | Injection Moulding | | 6 | $\tau = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | Newton's law of viscosity | General rheology | | 7 | $\eta = \eta_0 \cdot e^{E/RT}$ | Arrhenius viscosity model | Melt temperature effect | | 8 | $CR = \dfrac{D_f^2 - D_c^2}{D_c^2}$ | Compression ratio of screw | Screw design | | 9 | $L/D = $ screw length / screw diameter | Geometric ratio | Screw/barrel design | | 10 | $t_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{cool} + t_{eject} + t_{open/close}$ | Total cycle time | Production rate | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe Symbol in Tables:** Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value to prevent markdown table breakage. ## 2.4 The Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry Sector | Process Used | Principle Applied | |---|---|---| | **Packaging** | Blown Film, Injection Moulding | Melt extrusion + blow forming | | **Automotive** | Injection Moulding, Blow Moulding | High-pressure melt forming | | **Pipes & Profiles** | Single-Screw Extrusion | Continuous drag-flow extrusion | | **Houseware** | Compression, Injection Moulding | Bulk shaping | | **Medical Devices** | Injection Moulding, Extrusion | Precision melt forming | | **Textiles** | Melt Spinning | Continuous drawing from melt | ## 2.5 Principle of Pressure Generation Pressure in polymer machinery is generated by **three primary mechanisms**: 1. **Positive Displacement (Plunger/Ram)** — used in **ram extruders** and **transfer moulding**. 2. **Screw-Driven Flow** — used in **single-screw and twin-screw extruders** and **injection moulding screws**. 3. **External Compressed Gas or Vacuum** — used in **blow moulding** and **thermoforming**. The pressure-flow relationship in a simple channel of width $w$, height $h$, and length $L$ is: $$\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \eta Q L}{w h^3} \quad \text{(for rectangular channel, Newtonian fluid)}$$ This equation is the **bedrock of die design** and is heavily tested in KTU Module 2. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Drag Flow in a Single-Screw Extruder (The Famous KTU Derivation) ### Problem Statement Derive the expression for the **drag flow rate ($Q_d$)** in a single-screw extruder, considering the screw channel as an unwound flat plate. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Unwrap the screw channel** The helical screw channel of a single-screw extruder can be "unwound" into a flat rectangular channel. The barrel (which rotates or is stationary) drags the polymer melt forward. **Assumptions:** - Newtonian fluid - Steady, laminar, isothermal flow - No leakage over flights - Barrel surface moves at velocity $V_b = \pi D N$ **Step 2 — Velocity of barrel relative to channel** The velocity component along the channel direction (down-channel) is: $$V_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\phi$$ **Step 3 — Apply Couette flow equation** For a fluid in a rectangular channel of depth $H$, with the top plate moving at velocity $V_{bx}$: $$v_z(y) = V_{bx} \cdot \dfrac{y}{H}$$ **Step 4 — Integrate to get volumetric flow rate** The volumetric drag flow rate is obtained by integrating the velocity profile over the channel cross-section: $$Q_d = \int_0^H v_z(y) \cdot w \, dy$$ Substituting the velocity expression: $$Q_d = \int_0^H \left(V_{bx} \cdot \dfrac{y}{H}\right) w \, dy$$ $$Q_d = \dfrac{V_{bx} \cdot w}{H} \cdot \int_0^H y \, dy$$ $$Q_d = \dfrac{V_{bx} \cdot w}{H} \cdot \dfrac{H^2}{2}$$ $$Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \, V_{bx} \, w \, H$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $V_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\phi$ and $w = \pi D \sin\phi - e$ (channel width)** $$Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2} \, \pi D N \cos\phi \cdot (\pi D \sin\phi - e) \cdot H$$ **Step 6 — Final drag flow equation (neglecting flight width $e$)** $$\boxed{Q_d = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}}$$ This is the **classical Newtonian drag flow equation** for a single-screw extruder. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cooling Time in an Injection Mould ### Problem Statement Derive the **cooling time** required for a plastic part of thickness $h$ to solidify in a mould at temperature $T_w$, given the mould surface temperature $T_w$ and ejection temperature $T_e$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Heat conduction equation in the mould** Using 1-D unsteady-state heat conduction with **lumped capacitance + Fourier series solution**: $$\dfrac{T - T_w}{T_m - T_w} = \dfrac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{(-1)^n}{2n+1} \exp\left(-\dfrac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 \alpha t}{4 h^2}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Use only the first term of the series (sufficient accuracy for long cooling times)** $$\dfrac{T_e - T_w}{T_m - T_w} \approx \dfrac{4}{\pi} \exp\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 \alpha t}{4 h^2}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $t$ (cooling time)** $$\exp\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 \alpha t}{4 h^2}\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{T_e - T_w}{T_m - T_w}$$ Taking natural logarithm: $$-\dfrac{\pi^2 \alpha t}{4 h^2} = \ln\left(\dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{T_e - T_w}{T_m - T_w}\right)$$ $$t = \dfrac{4 h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left(\dfrac{4}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Final equation** $$\boxed{t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left[\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right]}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical meaning:** Cooling time is **proportional to $h^2$** — doubling the wall thickness **quadruples** the cooling time. This is why thick plastic parts take disproportionately long to cool. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Principle-to-Process Mapping | S.No | Principle (Working Basis) | Governing Physics | Primary Process | State of Polymer | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Drag + Pressure Flow** | Drag flow + Pressure flow in channel | **Single-Screw Extrusion** | Molten | | 2 | **Positive Displacement** | Ram-driven flow | **Ram Extrusion, Transfer Moulding** | Molten / Softened | | 3 | **High-Pressure Injection** | Screw plunger + Clamp force | **Injection Moulding** | Molten | | 4 | **Pneumatic Forming** | Compressed air expansion | **Blow Moulding** | Molten (Parison) | | 5 | **Conduction + Squeeze Flow** | Heat + Clamp pressure | **Compression Moulding** | Solid / Pre-heated | | 6 | **Vacuum / Pressure Forming** | Atmospheric pressure differential | **Thermoforming** | Softened Sheet | | 7 | **Rotational Drag** | Counter-rotating rolls | **Calendaring** | Molten | | 8 | **Solution Casting** | Solvent evaporation | **Cast Film** | Solution | | 9 | **Reaction Injection** | Polymerization in mould | **RIM (Reaction Injection Moulding)** | Liquid Monomer | | 10 | **Melt Drawing** | Tensile attenuation | **Fibre Spinning** | Molten Filament | --- ## 3.4 Derivation 3: Pressure Flow in a Rectangular Die ### Problem Statement Derive the **pressure flow** component in a rectangular slit die. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Apply Navier-Stokes for fully developed flow between parallel plates** For a Newtonian fluid in a slit of half-height $H$: $$\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} = \eta \dfrac{\partial^2 v_z}{\partial y^2}$$ **Step 2 — Integrate twice and apply no-slip at walls ($y = \pm H$)** $$v_z(y) = \dfrac{1}{2\eta} \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} (y^2 - H^2)$$ **Step 3 — Integrate over cross-section to get flow rate (for full slit of width $w$ and height $2H$)** $$Q_p = -\dfrac{2 w H^3}{3 \eta} \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$$ **Step 4 — Express in terms of total slit height $h = 2H$** $$\boxed{Q_p = -\dfrac{w h^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L}}$$ This is the **pressure flow** in a slit die — fundamental for die design problems in KTU exams. --- ## 3.5 Combined Net Flow Equation The total (net) flow in a single-screw extruder is: $$Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \dfrac{w h^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$$ > [!TIP] > **Operational Insight:** When $Q_p = 0$ (open discharge, no die), $Q_{net} = Q_d$ (maximum output). When $\Delta P$ is very high, $Q_p = Q_d$ and $Q_{net} = 0$ (extruder is stalled). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Universal Polymer Processing Principle ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Polymer Feed
Granules Powder Sheet] --> B[Stage 1 Plasticization
Heating Melting Mixing] B --> C[Stage 2 Forming Shaping
Pressure Die Mould Vacuum] C --> D[Stage 3 Solidification
Cooling Curing Crosslinking] D --> E[Finished Part Product
Ejected Wound Cut] B --> B1[Heat Source
Band Heaters Shear Heating] B --> B2[Conveying Mechanism
Screw Ram Plunger] C --> C1[Shaping Tool
Die Mould Cavity Rolls] C --> C2[Driving Force
Pressure Vacuum Pneumatic] D --> D1[Cooling System
Water Oil Air Channels] D --> D2[Solidification Trigger
Tg Tm Crystallization Reaction] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style C fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style D fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style E fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f style B1 fill:#fff59d style B2 fill:#fff59d style C1 fill:#ffcc80 style C2 fill:#ffcc80 style D1 fill:#a5d6a7 style D2 fill:#a5d6a7 ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Single-Screw Extruder ```mermaid flowchart LR HOPPER[Hopper
Polymer Feed] --> FEED[Feed Zone
Solid Conveying] FEED --> COMP[Compression Zone
Melting Compaction] COMP --> METER[Metering Zone
Melt Pumping] METER --> DIE[Die
Shaping] DIE --> COOL[Cooling Bath
Calibrator] COOL --> HAUL[Hauloff Unit] HAUL --> CUT[Cutter Winder] BARREL[Barrel Heater Bands] -.-> FEED BARREL -.-> COMP BARREL -.-> METER SCREW[Rotating Screw
Driven by Motor] -.-> METER style HOPPER fill:#bbdefb style FEED fill:#fff9c4 style COMP fill:#ffe0b2 style METER fill:#ffccbc style DIE fill:#c8e6c9 style COOL fill:#b3e5fc style HAUL fill:#f8bbd0 style CUT fill:#d1c4e9 style BARREL fill:#fff59d style SCREW fill:#fff59d ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture of an Injection Moulding Machine ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INJ[Injection Unit] H1[Hopper] --> S1[Screw Plasticizer] S1 --> NOZ[Nozzle] end subgraph CLAMP[Clamping Unit] FP[Fixed Platen] --- MLD[Mould Cavity] MLD --- MP[Moving Platen] TK[Toggle Clamp Mechanism] end NOZ --> MLD S1 -.Heats.-> S1 MLD -.Cooling Water.-> MLD TK -.Clamping Force.-> MP style H1 fill:#bbdefb style S1 fill:#fff9c4 style NOZ fill:#ffe0b2 style FP fill:#c8e6c9 style MLD fill:#c8e6c9 style MP fill:#c8e6c9 style TK fill:#f8bbd0 ``` ## 4.4 Process Selection Decision Tree (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Select Polymer Processing Principle] --> Q1{Continuous or Discrete?} Q1 -->|Continuous| Q2{Melt or Solution?} Q1 -->|Discrete| Q3{Part Geometry?} Q2 -->|Melt| EXT[Single Twin Screw Extrusion] Q2 -->|Solution| CAST[Solution Casting] Q3 -->|3D Complex| Q4{Thermo Plastic or Set?} Q3 -->|Hollow Bottle| BLOW[Blow Moulding] Q3 -->|Sheet 2.5D| THERMO[Thermoforming] Q4 -->|Thermoplastic| INJ[Injection Moulding] Q4 -->|Thermoset| COMP[Compression Moulding] style START fill:#e1f5ff style EXT fill:#fff9c4 style CAST fill:#fff9c4 style INJ fill:#c8e6c9 style COMP fill:#c8e6c9 style BLOW fill:#f8bbd0 style THERMO fill:#f8bbd0 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** ### Q1. Define the "Principle" of polymer processing. List the three universal stages involved. **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The **principle of polymer processing** refers to the fundamental physical, thermal, and mechanical basis by which raw polymer is transformed into a finished product. It governs the heat transfer, pressure development, and flow mechanics inside a processing machine. The **three universal stages** are: 1. **Plasticization (Preparation)** – melting/softening the polymer 2. **Forming (Shaping)** – forcing the melt into a shaping tool 3. **Solidification (Curing/Cooling)** – solidifying the shaped part > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write *stages* but fail to mention **heat + pressure + time** as the governing trio. Always state all three parameters. --- > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** ### Q2. Differentiate between the principles of **Injection Moulding** and **Compression Moulding**. **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Injection Moulding | Compression Moulding | |---|---|---| | Material State | Molten polymer injected | Pre-heated solid charge placed | | Pressure Source | Screw + plunger (high pressure 35–140 MPa) | Hydraulic press (clamping) | | Cycle Time | Short (5–60 s) | Long (60–600 s) | | Part Geometry | Complex, thin-walled | Simple, thick-walled | | Process Principle | **Melt injection + clamping** | **Heat + squeeze flow** | --- ## 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, May 2024]** ### Question A: **(14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the **principle of working of a single-screw extruder** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the three functional zones. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the **drag flow rate** in a single-screw extruder. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Answer for Question A #### (a) Principle of Working of a Single-Screw Extruder (7 Marks) A single-screw extruder operates on the principle of **drag-induced melt flow** combined with **pressure flow**. The polymer is conveyed, melted, and pumped along a heated barrel by a rotating screw. The barrel is stationary, and the screw rotates inside it. **Three Functional Zones:** 1. **Feed Zone (Solid Conveying):** Granules are conveyed forward by **friction against the barrel**. The depth of the channel is maximum, and the polymer remains solid. Friction between the polymer and barrel must be high; friction between the polymer and screw root must be low. 2. **Compression (Melt) Zone:** The channel depth decreases gradually, **compacting and melting the polymer**. The melting occurs due to: - Conduction from **external barrel heaters** - **Viscous dissipation** (shear heating) as the polymer is dragged along the screw. 3. **Metering Zone:** The channel depth is minimum. The polymer is fully melted and acts as a **Newtonian/non-Newtonian fluid**. The screw acts as a **positive displacement pump**, generating pressure to push the melt through the die. **Block Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes. --- #### (b) Derivation of Drag Flow Rate (7 Marks) **Refer to the complete derivation in Section 3.1.** **Assumptions:** - Steady, laminar, isothermal flow - Newtonian fluid behaviour - No leakage over flights - Barrel surface moves at $V_b = \pi D N$ - Flight width is negligible **Final Result:** $$Q_d = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}$$ Where: - $D$ = screw diameter - $N$ = screw rotational speed - $H$ = channel depth - $\phi$ = helix angle **Discussion:** - $Q_d$ is **directly proportional to $D^2, N, H$** — doubling screw speed doubles output. - The helix angle $\phi$ gives a **maximum at $\phi = 45°$**, but practical screws use $\phi = 17°$–$20°$ for optimum pumping efficiency. --- ### Question B (Alternative): **(14 Marks)** **(a)** Discuss the **principle of plasticization** in polymer processing. Explain the role of **viscous dissipation** with a neat sketch. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A **single-screw extruder** has the following specifications: - Screw diameter $D = 50$ mm - Channel depth at metering zone $H = 3$ mm - Screw speed $N = 60$ rpm - Helix angle $\phi = 20°$ - Melt viscosity $\eta = 500$ Pa·s - Die width $w = 100$ mm, Die length $L = 30$ mm, Die slit height $h = 4$ mm Calculate the **drag flow rate ($Q_d$)** and the **pressure flow rate ($Q_p$)** in the die. Comment on the **net output**. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Answer for Question B #### (a) Principle of Plasticization (7 Marks) **Plasticization** is the process of converting solid polymer (granules, powder) into a homogeneous, flowable melt. It involves two simultaneous mechanisms: 1. **Conduction Heating:** Heat is supplied by **electrical band heaters** wrapped around the barrel. This heat is conducted through the barrel wall into the polymer. 2. **Viscous Dissipation:** As the polymer is sheared between the rotating screw and the stationary barrel, the mechanical work done against viscous resistance is converted into heat: $$q_{visc} = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}^2$$ **Total heat input to the polymer:** $$q_{total} = q_{cond} + q_{visc}$$ At high screw speeds, **viscous dissipation dominates** over conduction — this is why **high-speed extrusion** can sometimes run with reduced external heating. However, for **temperature-sensitive polymers (PVC)**, controlled conduction heating is essential to avoid degradation. **Sketch:** A screw rotating inside a heated barrel with arrows showing heat flow inward and shear-induced heat generation in the melt film. --- #### (b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** - $D = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m - $H = 3$ mm $= 0.003$ m - $N = 60$ rpm $= 1$ rev/s - $\phi = 20°$, $\sin 20° = 0.342$, $\cos 20° = 0.940$ - $\eta = 500$ Pa·s - $w = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m - $L = 30$ mm $= 0.03$ m - $h = 4$ mm $= 0.004$ m **Step 1 — Drag Flow Rate ($Q_d$):** $$Q_d = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}$$ Substituting values: $$Q_d = \dfrac{(3.1416)^2 \cdot (0.05)^2 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.003 \cdot 0.342 \cdot 0.940}{2}$$ Calculating the numerator step by step: - $\pi^2 = 9.8696$ - $D^2 = 0.0025$ - $N = 1$ - $H = 0.003$ - $\sin\phi \cos\phi = 0.342 \times 0.940 = 0.3215$ - Numerator = $9.8696 \times 0.0025 \times 1 \times 0.003 \times 0.3215 = 2.379 \times 10^{-5}$ - $Q_d = 2.379 \times 10^{-5} / 2 = 1.189 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s $$\boxed{Q_d = 1.189 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 11.89 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}}$$ **Step 2 — Pressure Flow Rate ($Q_p$):** For a slit die, $Q_p$ is: $$Q_p = \dfrac{w h^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$$ For the open-discharge case (no die restriction, $\Delta P \to 0$): $$Q_p = 0 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **But for the die's internal pressure drop**, we need to assume a $\Delta P$. In a typical exam, this is often computed when a back pressure is specified. For demonstration, assume $\Delta P = 1$ MPa $= 10^6$ Pa: $$Q_p = \dfrac{(0.1)(0.004)^3 (10^6)}{12 (500)(0.03)}$$ $$Q_p = \dfrac{(0.1)(6.4 \times 10^{-8})(10^6)}{180}$$ $$Q_p = \dfrac{6.4 \times 10^{-3}}{180} = 3.556 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Net Output:** $$Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p = 1.189 \times 10^{-5} - 3.556 \times 10^{-5} = -2.37 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ The **negative** value indicates the assumed $\Delta P = 1$ MPa is **too high** for stable extrusion with these parameters. In practice, the operating $\Delta P$ would be: $$\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \eta L Q_d}{w h^3} = \dfrac{12 \times 500 \times 0.03 \times 1.189 \times 10^{-5}}{0.1 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-8}}$$ $$\Delta P = 3.34 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \approx 0.334 \text{ MPa}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often **forget to convert rpm to rev/s** (divide by 60). Always check unit consistency. > **Examiner's Pitfall #2:** In the drag-flow equation, ensure $\sin\phi$ and $\cos\phi$ are used in **radians-compatible** form (i.e., the standard formula uses the degree values directly for sine/cosine). Do NOT convert angles to radians for trig functions. > **Examiner's Pitfall #3:** Sign convention in $Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p$ is critical. Pressure flow **opposes** drag flow, hence the minus sign. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Principles of Polymer Processing** ### Core Principles (Must Know) - ☐ Polymer processing = **Heat + Pressure + Time** acting on polymer - ☐ The **3 Universal Stages** = Plasticization → Forming → Solidification - ☐ Thermoplastics → **cool to solidify**; Thermosets → **cure to solidify** ### Process Principles (Must Know) - ☐ **Extrusion** principle = Drag flow + Pressure flow - ☐ **Injection Moulding** principle = Melt injection + Clamping - ☐ **Blow Moulding** principle = Pneumatic expansion of parison - ☐ **Compression Moulding** principle = Heat + Squeeze flow - ☐ **Thermoforming** principle = Vacuum/pressure differential on softened sheet - ☐ **Calendaring** principle = Counter-rotating rolls for sheet forming - ☐ **RIM** principle = Polymerization inside mould ### Key Formulas (Must Memorize) - ☐ $Q_d = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}$ (Drag flow) - ☐ $Q_p = \dfrac{w h^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$ (Pressure flow in slit die) - ☐ $t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left[\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right]$ (Cooling time) - ☐ $P_{inj} = F_{clamp} / A_{proj}$ (Injection pressure) - ☐ $q_{visc} = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}^2$ (Viscous dissipation) - ☐ $\eta = \eta_0 \cdot e^{E/RT}$ (Arrhenius viscosity) - ☐ $CR = D_f^2 - D_c^2 / D_c^2$ (Compression ratio) ### Geometric Concepts (Frequently Tested) - ☐ **L/D ratio** for screws: typically **15:1 to 30:1** - ☐ **Compression ratio (CR)**: typically **2:1 to 4:1** - ☐ **Helix angle** $\phi$ for optimum pumping ≈ **17° to 20°** - ☐ **Clamping force** must exceed the **mould cavity pressure × projected area** ### Numerical Safety Net - ☐ Always convert **mm → m** before substituting in SI equations - ☐ Always convert **rpm → rev/s** (divide by 60) for screw speed - ☐ Always state the **unit** of the final answer explicitly - ☐ Use $\sin\phi \cos\phi = \dfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\phi$ identity to simplify at $\phi = 45°$ ### Common Examiner Traps - ☐ Forgetting **assumptions** in derivations (cost: 1–2 marks) - ☐ Confusing **drag flow** vs **pressure flow** directions - ☐ Missing the **sign convention** in net flow equation - ☐ Writing $T_m$ when meaning **mould temperature** vs **melt temperature** — clarify with subscripts - ☐ Forgetting the **"screw vs barrel" motion convention** (most extruders have rotating screw, stationary barrel — but the velocity of the barrel *relative to the screw* drives the flow) > [!TIP] > **Final Board Exam Strategy:** > 1. Always start numerical answers with a **"Given"** section listing all data with units. > 2. Always end derivations with a **boxed final answer** and a short **physical interpretation**. > 3. Always include a **neat block diagram** for any 7-mark descriptive question — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for labelled diagrams.

topics.title: Types of Extruders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Extruders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — TYPES OF EXTRUDERS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Extruder (KTU Definition):**
> An **extruder** is a continuous, high-throughput polymer processing machine that converts solid thermoplastic polymer (granules, pellets, powder, or flakes) into a homogeneous, pressure-developing melt, and forces (extrudes) this melt through a shaped **die** to produce a continuous profile of fixed cross-section (e.g., pipe, sheet, film, wire-coating, or filament).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPOT503)** syllabus, extruders are classified primarily by the **mechanism used to generate melt pressure and pumping action**:

1. **Screw Extruders** (dominant industrial class)
   - Single-Screw Extruder (SSE)
   - Multi-Screw Extruder (Twin-Screw — co-rotating & counter-rotating)
2. **Ram / Plunger Extruders** (batch-type, historical & specialty)
3. **Disk / Drum Extruders** (specialty)
4. **Gear / Melt Pumps** (auxiliary)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toothpaste Tube" Intuition

Imagine a giant **industrial toothpaste tube**:

- The **barrel** is the rigid outer tube.
- Inside, a rotating **screw** acts like a giant "auger" — like the screw of a meat-grinder or a concrete-mixer screw.
- As the screw turns, the polymer pellets are grabbed, **conveyed forward**, **compressed**, **sheared**, and **melted** by friction and heater bands wrapped around the barrel.
- The molten polymer is then pushed out through a shaped opening (**die**) — exactly like squeezing toothpaste out of a tube in a continuous ribbon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):**
> Out of all types, the **Single-Screw Extruder** is the *workhorse* of the plastics industry (~**80–85%** of all extrusion lines). The **Twin-Screw Extruder** is preferred for **compounding, devolatilization, and PVC processing** due to its positive conveying and self-wiping action.

### 1.3 Industrial Significance

| Metric | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|
| Throughput (Single-Screw) | **50 – 5000 kg/hr** |
| Screw L/D ratio (modern) | **24 : 1 to 36 : 1** |
| Screw Speed (N) | **20 – 300 rpm** |
| Operating Pressure | **5 – 35 MPa** |
| Barrel Temperature | **150 – 300 °C** |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Tree of Extruders

```
EXTRUDERS
├── 1. Screw Extruders
│   ├── 1.1 Single-Screw Extruder (SSE)
│   │       ├── Conventional (3-zone)
│   │       ├── Barrier-type (Maddock / Grooved-feed)
│   │       └── Vented / Two-stage (with vacuum port)
│   │
│   └── 1.2 Multi-Screw Extruders
│           ├── Twin-Screw — Counter-Rotating
│           │       ├── Intermeshing
│           │       └── Non-intermeshing
│           ├── Twin-Screw — Co-Rotating (Intermeshing)
│           └── Ring / Multi-Screw (>2)
│
├── 2. Ram / Plunger Extruders (Reciprocating)
├── 3. Disk Extruders (Rotating-disk plasticator)
└── 4. Gear-Pump / Melt-Pump Extruders
```

## 2.2 Single-Screw Extruder — Functional Zones

The screw is the heart of any screw extruder. The **KTU textbook (Rauwendaal, Tadmor & Gogos)** divides the screw into three (or four) functional zones:

| Zone | Function | Channel Depth | Typical Length |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Feed (Solid Conveying)** | Transports solid pellets forward | Deep | ~10–25 % of L |
| **Compression / Melting (Transition)** | Compacts & melts polymer via drag-heating + heaters | Tapered | ~50–60 % of L |
| **Metering (Melt Pumping)** | Develops pressure; ensures uniform throughput | Shallow | ~20–25 % of L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compression Ratio (CR):** Ratio of feed-zone channel depth to metering-zone channel depth.
> $$\text{CR} = \frac{H_f}{H_m} \quad \text{(typical: 2 : 1 to 4 : 1)}$$

## 2.3 Twin-Screw Extruder — Sub-Classification

| Feature | Counter-Rotating (CR) | Co-Rotating (CO) |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotation** | Two screws rotate in **opposite** directions | Both screws rotate in the **same** direction |
| **Wiping action** | Calendrical — flights wipe each other | Intermeshing self-wiping — flight of one screw wipes root of other |
| **Conveying** | **Positive displacement** (gear-like) | **Friction-dependent** (drag-flow) |
| **Best for** | **PVC pipe, profile** (gentle shear, low residence time) | **Compounding, masterbatch, devolatilization, reactive extrusion** |
| **Stagnation** | Some stagnation possible | Self-wiping → minimal stagnation |
| **Throughput control** | Independent of viscosity & screw speed | Depends on screw speed & fill |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used:** $D$ = screw diameter; $N$ = screw speed (rev/s); $\varphi$ = helix angle at barrel wall; $H$ = channel depth; $W$ = channel width (≈ $\pi D \sin\varphi - e$, $e$ = flight width); $L$ = axial length; $\mu$ = melt viscosity; $\Delta P$ = pressure rise along channel.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Drag flow** (Newtonian, rectangular channel) | $Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2}\, v_{bx}\, W\, H = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N\, H\, \sin\varphi \cos\varphi}{2}$ | $\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | **Pressure flow** (Newtonian, rectangular channel) | $Q_p = -\dfrac{W H^{3}}{12\, \mu} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = -\dfrac{\pi D H^{3} \sin^{2}\varphi}{12\, \mu L} \cdot \Delta P$ | $\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ |
| 3 | **Net / actual throughput** (Newtonian) | $Q = Q_d - Q_p = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H \sin\varphi \cos\varphi}{2} - \dfrac{\pi D H^{3} \sin^{2}\varphi}{12\, \mu L}\,\Delta P$ | $\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | **Maximum throughput** (when $\Delta P = 0$, open discharge) | $Q_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H \sin\varphi \cos\varphi}{2}$ | $\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ |
| 5 | **Maximum pressure** (when $Q = 0$, closed die) | $\Delta P_{\max} = \dfrac{6\, \pi\, \mu\, L\, N\, D}{H^{2}\, \tan\varphi}$ | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 6 | **Specific throughput** $q$ (per rev) | $q = \dfrac{Q}{N} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} H \sin\varphi \cos\varphi}{2} - \dfrac{\pi D H^{3} \sin^{2}\varphi}{12\, \mu L N}\,\Delta P$ | $\text{m}^{3}$ |
| 7 | **Solids conveying rate** (Damköhler / Darnell–Mol) | $m_s = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N\, \rho_{b}\, \tan\varphi\, (1-\varepsilon)\, \tan\theta}{1 + \tan^{2}\theta} \cdot f_s$ | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 8 | **Power required to drive screw** | $P = 2\pi N\, T$ | $\text{W}$ |
| 9 | **Residence time (avg.)** | $\bar{t} = \dfrac{V_{\text{channel}}}{Q} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2} H L/4}{Q}$ | $\text{s}$ |
| 10 | **Newtonian shear rate in channel** | $\dot\gamma = \dfrac{\pi D N}{H}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — The pipe-vs-channel model:**
> The classical formulas above use the **"unwound channel"** approximation (rectangular slit of width $W$, depth $H$, length $L$, tilted at helix angle $\varphi$). This is the standard KTU-board model. Students often confuse **barrel wall velocity** $v_b = \pi D N$ with the **down-channel velocity** $v_{bx} = v_b \sin\varphi$ and the **cross-channel velocity** $v_{bz} = v_b \cos\varphi$. **Remember the 2D decomposition at the barrel wall!**

## 2.5 Why These Formulas Matter (Engineering Utility)

- **Sizing a die**: Knowing $Q$ and the die land length, you select die dimensions so that the **die pressure drop equals the extruder's pressure-generation capability** $\Delta P_{\max}$.
- **Scale-up rule**: For geometrically similar screws, $Q \propto D^{2.5}$ to $D^{3}$ — a **"cube law"** used to scale lab extruders to production.
- **Mixing & residence time**: Critical for **reactive extrusion** (e.g., PA-6 anionic polymerization) and for **thermal degradation control** of PVC.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Drag Flow $Q_d$ in an Unwound Screw Channel

We model the screw channel as a **rectangular slit of width $W$ and depth $H$**, with the **barrel moving at velocity $v_b$ at angle $\varphi$** relative to the channel axis.

**Step 1 — Establish the geometry.**

Unwrap the helical channel onto a flat plane. Set:
- $x$-axis → down-channel (along flight length $L$)
- $z$-axis → cross-channel (perpendicular to flights)
- $y$-axis → normal to barrel wall (depth, $0 \le y \le H$)

The **barrel wall moves** at $v_b = \pi D N$ in the direction of the **flight helix**. Decompose:
$$v_{bx} = v_b \sin\varphi \quad (\text{down-channel component})$$
$$v_{bz} = v_b \cos\varphi \quad (\text{cross-channel component})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the no-slip boundary condition.**

At the barrel wall ($y = H$): $v_x = v_{bx}$.
At the screw root ($y = 0$): $v_x = 0$ (screw is stationary).
At the side walls ($z = 0, W$): $v_x = 0$ (no cross-flow leakage assumed).

**Step 3 — Solve the simplified Navier–Stokes for fully-developed, steady, incompressible Newtonian flow with $\partial P/\partial z = 0$ and $\partial v_x / \partial x = 0$:**

$$\mu \frac{\partial^{2} v_x}{\partial y^{2}} = \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

This is a standard 1-D shear-driven + pressure-driven flow (planar Poiseuille + Couette).

**Step 4 — Integrate twice with boundary conditions.**

The general solution is:
$$v_x(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu} \frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\, y^{2} + C_1 y + C_2$$

Apply BCs:
- At $y = 0$: $v_x = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$
- At $y = H$: $v_x = v_{bx} \Rightarrow C_1 = \dfrac{v_{bx}}{H} - \dfrac{1}{2\mu} \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} H$

So:
$$v_x(y) = \frac{v_{bx}}{H}\, y - \frac{H^{2}}{2\mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \cdot \frac{y}{H}\!\left(1 - \frac{y}{H}\right) \quad \text{(after algebra)}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate over the channel cross-section to get volumetric flow.**

$$Q = \int_{0}^{H}\!\!\int_{0}^{W} v_x(y)\, dz\, dy = W \int_{0}^{H} v_x(y)\, dy$$

Carrying out the integration:
$$Q = \frac{1}{2} v_{bx} W H \;-\; \frac{W H^{3}}{12 \mu}\,\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the two physical contributions.**

- The first term is **drag flow** $Q_d$ (purely due to the moving barrel, $\partial P/\partial x = 0$):
$$Q_d = \frac{1}{2} v_{bx}\, W H$$

- The second term is **pressure flow** $Q_p$ (opposes $Q_d$ when $\partial P/\partial x < 0$):
$$Q_p = -\frac{W H^{3}}{12 \mu}\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

**Step 7 — Convert back to screw geometry using $W = \pi D \sin\varphi - e \approx \pi D \sin\varphi$ for thin flights, and $L/\cos\varphi = $ axial length along the barrel:**

$$Q_d = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H \sin\varphi \cos\varphi}{2} \qquad \text{(screw drag flow)}$$

$$Q_p = -\frac{\pi D H^{3} \sin^{2}\varphi}{12 \mu L}\,\Delta P \qquad \text{(screw pressure flow)}$$

**Step 8 — Net throughput (Newtonian approximation):**

$$Q = Q_d - Q_p = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H \sin\varphi \cos\varphi}{2} - \frac{\pi D H^{3} \sin^{2}\varphi}{12 \mu L}\,\Delta P$$

**Step 9 — Limiting cases (important for KTU problems).**

| Limit | Condition | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Open discharge (no die) | $\Delta P = 0$ | $Q = Q_d = Q_{\max}$ |
| Closed discharge (die blocked) | $Q = 0$ | $\Delta P = \Delta P_{\max} = \dfrac{6\pi \mu L N D}{H^{2}\tan\varphi}$ |
| Optimum helix angle | $dQ_d/d\varphi = 0$ | $\varphi_{\text{opt}} = 45^{\circ}$ (gives $Q_{d,\max} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H}{4}$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** Real extruders operate with **non-Newtonian** polymers (pseudoplastic). For a **power-law fluid** ($\eta = m \dot\gamma^{n-1}$), the integral above becomes non-linear; the *order* of the resulting flow equation is **$2n+1$** (e.g., $n = 0.33 \Rightarrow 7^{\text{th}}$ order). Examiners love asking "why is the Newtonian assumption a simplification?"

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (typical KTU 14-mark style):**

> A **Single-Screw Extruder** has the following specifications:
> Screw diameter $D = 60$ mm, channel depth in metering zone $H = 3$ mm,
> screw speed $N = 60$ rpm = **1 rev/s**, helix angle $\varphi = 17.65^{\circ}$ (typical),
> screw length in metering zone $L = 10D = 0.6$ m,
> melt viscosity of LDPE at processing conditions $\mu = 500$ Pa·s.
> A die gives a pressure rise $\Delta P = 5$ MPa across the metering zone.
> Calculate:
> (a) Drag flow rate $Q_d$
> (b) Pressure flow rate $Q_p$
> (c) Net throughput $Q$
> (d) Maximum possible throughput $Q_{\max}$

**Solution:**

Given:
$D = 0.06$ m, $H = 0.003$ m, $N = 1$ rev/s,
$\varphi = 17.65^{\circ} \Rightarrow \sin\varphi = 0.3033,\ \cos\varphi = 0.9529,\ \sin\varphi\cos\varphi = 0.2890,\ \sin^{2}\varphi = 0.0920$,
$L = 0.6$ m, $\mu = 500$ Pa·s, $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^{6}$ Pa.

**Part (a) — Drag flow:**
$$Q_d = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H \sin\varphi \cos\varphi}{2}$$

$$Q_d = \frac{\pi^{2}\,(0.06)^{2}\,(1)\,(0.003)\,(0.2890)}{2}$$

Compute numerator piece by piece:
- $\pi^{2} \approx 9.8696$
- $D^{2} = 0.0036$
- $D^{2} N H = 0.0036 \times 1 \times 0.003 = 1.08 \times 10^{-5}$
- Times $\sin\varphi\cos\varphi = 0.2890 \Rightarrow 3.1212 \times 10^{-6}$
- Divide by 2: $\boxed{Q_d \approx 1.56 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 5.62\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{hr}}$

**Part (b) — Pressure flow:**
$$Q_p = \frac{\pi D H^{3} \sin^{2}\varphi\, \Delta P}{12\, \mu\, L}$$

- $H^{3} = (0.003)^{3} = 2.7 \times 10^{-8}$
- $D\,H^{3} = 0.06 \times 2.7\times10^{-8} = 1.62 \times 10^{-9}$
- Times $\sin^{2}\varphi = 0.0920 \Rightarrow 1.4904 \times 10^{-10}$
- Times $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^{6} \Rightarrow 7.452 \times 10^{-4}$
- Denominator: $12 \times 500 \times 0.6 = 3600$
- $Q_p = 7.452 \times 10^{-4} / 3600 \approx 2.07 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 0.745\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{hr}$

$$\boxed{Q_p \approx 2.07 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}}$$

**Part (c) — Net throughput:**
$$Q = Q_d - Q_p = (1.56 - 0.207) \times 10^{-6} \approx 1.353 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 1.35 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 4.88\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{hr}}$$

**Part (d) — Maximum throughput** (open discharge, $\Delta P = 0$):
$$Q_{\max} = Q_d = 1.56 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

If the LDPE density at melt state is $\rho \approx 770\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$, then **mass throughput**:
$$\dot{m} = Q \cdot \rho = 1.35 \times 10^{-6} \times 770 \approx 1.04 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/s} \approx 62.4\ \text{kg/hr}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** With this 60-mm screw, an industrial LDPE line producing ~62 kg/hr is realistic. Pressure flow reduces throughput by ~**13.3 %** from the open-discharge maximum — a typical KTU-board question asks the student to comment on **"leakage flow"** and **"Newtonian-vs-non-Newtonian correction"**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Single-Screw Extruder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HOPPER["HOPPER\nSolid Polymer Pellets"]
    THROAT["FEED THROAT\nGravity Entry"]
    FZ["FEED ZONE\nSolid Conveying\nDeep Channel"]
    CZ["COMPRESSION ZONE\nMelting + Mixing\nTapered Channel"]
    MZ["METERING ZONE\nMelt Pumping\nShallow Channel"]
    BRRL["BARREL\nExternal Heater Bands 150 to 300 C"]
    SCREW["ROTATING SCREW\nDriven by Motor via Gearbox"]
    SCREEN["BREAKER PLATE\n+ SCREEN PACK"]
    DIE["SHAPING DIE\nProfile Outlet"]
    PROD["FINAL EXTRUDATE\nContinuous Profile"]
    VENT["VENT PORT\nVented Extruder Only"]

    HOPPER --> THROAT --> FZ --> CZ --> MZ --> SCREEN --> DIE --> PROD
    BRRL -. heats .- FZ
    BRRL -. heats .- CZ
    BRRL -. heats .- MZ
    SCREW -. rotates inside .- FZ
    SCREW -. rotates inside .- CZ
    SCREW -. rotates inside .- MZ
    VENT -. removes volatiles .- CZ
```

## 4.2 Twin-Screw Extruder — Counter-Rotating vs Co-Rotating Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CR["COUNTER ROTATING TWIN SCREW"]
        CR1["SCREW A\nClockwise"]
        CR2["SCREW B\nCounter Clockwise"]
        CRNOTE["Positive displacement gear like flow\nBest for PVC pipe and profile\nCalendrical wiping action"]
    end
    subgraph CO["CO ROTATING TWIN SCREW INTERMESHING"]
        CO1["SCREW A\nClockwise"]
        CO2["SCREW B\nAlso Clockwise"]
        CONOTE["Self wiping Friction drag flow\nBest for compounding and devolatilization\nStaged addition of fillers and additives"]
    end
    CRNOTE --> CR1
    CONOTE --> CO1
```

## 4.3 Classification of Extruders (One-View Block Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    EXT["EXTRUDER FAMILY"]
    EXT --> SCREW["SCREW EXTRUDER"]
    EXT --> RAM["RAM EXTRUDER"]
    EXT --> DISK["DISK EXTRUDER"]
    EXT --> GEAR["GEAR / MELT PUMP"]

    SCREW --> SSE["SINGLE SCREW EXTRUDER"]
    SCREW --> TSE["TWIN SCREW EXTRUDER"]
    SCREW --> MSE["MULTI SCREW RING TYPE"]

    SSE --> SSE1["Conventional 3 zone"]
    SSE --> SSE2["Barrier flight Maddock type"]
    SSE --> SSE3["Vented two stage"]

    TSE --> TSE1["Counter Rotating Intermeshing"]
    TSE --> TSE2["Counter Rotating Non Intermeshing"]
    TSE --> TSE3["Co Rotating Intermeshing"]

    RAM --> RAM1["Batch type high pressure\nUsed for PTFE ram extrusion\nof rods and billets"]

    DISK --> DISK1["Rotating disk plasticator\nSpecialty for heat sensitive PVC"]
```

## 4.4 Screw Geometry — Unwound Channel Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BARREL["BARREL WALL\nmoving at v_b = pi D N\nat helix angle phi"]
    ROOT["SCREW ROOT\nstationary"]
    H_DEPTH["Channel depth H"]
    W_WIDTH["Channel width W"]
    HELIX["Helix unwrapped onto flat plane"]
    BARREL --> HELIX
    ROOT --> HELIX
    H_DEPTH --> HELIX
    W_WIDTH --> HELIX
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway from the diagrams:** The **unwound-channel model** is the bridge between screw geometry and the simple shear-flow formulas derived in §3. The same drag-flow + pressure-flow framework applies to **all** screw extruders — single, twin, ring, or barrier — only the geometry $H, W, L, \varphi$ changes.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Differentiate between co-rotating and counter-rotating twin-screw extruders with respect to rotation, conveying mechanism and typical applications."** (3 marks)

**Model Answer (3 key points × 1 mark):**
1. **Rotation:** Co-rotating — both screws rotate in the **same** direction. Counter-rotating — screws rotate in **opposite** directions.
2. **Conveying:** Co-rotating — **drag (friction) flow**; depends on screw speed and viscosity. Counter-rotating — **positive displacement** (gear-like, calendar nip between flights).
3. **Applications:** Co-rotating — **compounding, masterbatch, devolatilization, reactive extrusion**. Counter-rotating — **PVC pipe, profile extrusion** (gentle shear, short residence time, low melt temperature).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Define (a) compression ratio of an extruder screw, and (b) L/D ratio. State typical values used in industry."** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
- **(a) Compression Ratio (CR)** = $H_f / H_m$ = ratio of feed-zone channel depth to metering-zone channel depth. Typical: **2 : 1 to 4 : 1**.
- **(b) L/D ratio** = axial screw length ÷ screw diameter. Typical modern values: **24 : 1 to 36 : 1** (vented extruders up to 36:1 or higher).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice)

> Each 14-mark question is split into **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Single-Screw Extruder Focus)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a single-screw extruder**. Label all three functional zones of the screw and state the physical processes occurring in each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the **unwound-channel model**, derive the expressions for **drag flow, pressure flow, and net throughput** for a Newtonian polymer in a single-screw extruder. State clearly all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Construction** (sketch: hopper, barrel with heater bands, screw, breaker plate, die):
- **Hopper** — gravity feed of solid polymer
- **Barrel** — thick steel cylinder, electrically heated (3–5 zones)
- **Screw** — fluted steel shaft, root diameter $D_r$, flight diameter $D$, helix angle $\varphi$
- **Drive** — motor + reduction gearbox (typically 10:1 to 20:1)
- **Breaker plate + screen pack** — holds back contaminants, builds back-pressure
- **Die** — gives final shape

**Three Functional Zones:**

| Zone | Process | Geometry |
|---|---|---|
| **Feed Zone** | Solid conveying by drag against barrel | Deep constant channel |
| **Compression Zone** | Compaction, melting via conduction + viscous dissipation | Tapered (decreasing H) |
| **Metering Zone** | Homogenization and pressure development | Shallow constant channel |

**Working principle:** Screw rotation drags polymer forward, melts it via conduction from heaters + viscous dissipation, and pumps the melt through the die.

#### Model Solution — (b)

The full derivation is **exactly as shown in §3.1 of these notes**, with these **assumptions** (each 1 mark):

1. **Unwound channel** — screw geometry unwrapped into a flat rectangular slit.
2. **Steady, fully-developed, laminar** flow.
3. **Isothermal** in the metering zone.
4. **Newtonian** viscosity.
5. **Thin flights** (flight width $e \ll W$), no leakage over flights.
6. **No slip** at barrel and screw root.
7. **Incompressible** melt.
8. **Constant channel depth $H$** and **constant helix angle $\varphi$** in metering zone.

Final boxed results:
$$Q_d = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H \sin\varphi \cos\varphi}{2}, \quad Q_p = -\frac{\pi D H^{3} \sin^{2}\varphi\, \Delta P}{12\, \mu\, L}$$
$$Q = Q_d - Q_p$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **−1 mark** if you forget to state **all 8 assumptions**.
> - **−2 marks** if you confuse $\sin\varphi\cos\varphi$ with $\sin^{2}\varphi$ in the drag-flow term.
> - **−1 mark** if you forget to multiply $W$ by $\int dy$ in the volumetric integration step.
> - Many students write the "rectangular-slit" form $Q_d = \tfrac{1}{2}v_{bx}WH$ but **fail to convert** $v_{bx}$ and $W$ into screw parameters $D, N, \varphi$. The examiner specifically wants the **screw-geometry form** for full marks.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Twin-Screw & Ram Extruder Focus)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative Module 2 Question]**

**(a)** Compare the **co-rotating and counter-rotating intermeshing twin-screw extruders** in terms of (i) rotation direction, (ii) conveying mechanism, (iii) self-wiping action, (iv) typical applications, and (v) effect on residence time distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **construction, working principle, advantages, and limitations of a Ram (plunger) extruder**. Indicate **two industrial applications** where a ram extruder is preferred over a screw extruder. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

A **comparison table** of co-rotating (CO) vs counter-rotating (CR) intermeshing twin-screw extruders (each row ≈ 1.4 marks):

| Parameter | Co-Rotating (CO) | Counter-Rotating (CR) |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Rotation | Both screws rotate **same direction** | Rotating in **opposite** directions |
| (ii) Conveying | **Drag flow** (friction-dependent) | **Positive displacement** (calendar nip, gear-like) |
| (iii) Self-wiping | **Yes** — flight of one screw wipes root of the other → no stagnation | Partial — calendrical wiping only; some stagnation possible |
| (iv) Applications | Compounding, masterbatch, reactive extrusion, devolatilization, PVC dry-blend | **PVC pipe & profile**, wood-plastic composites |
| (v) Residence time distribution (RTD) | **Narrower** RTD → uniform quality | Even narrower RTD (close to plug flow) |

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Construction of a Ram Extruder:**
- A heated **barrel** (horizontal or vertical)
- A **reciprocating plunger / ram** driven hydraulically
- A **die** at the discharge end
- **Heating elements** wrapped around the barrel
- No rotating screw

**Working Principle:** Polymer (usually in powder/granule form) is **batch-fed** into the heated barrel. The hydraulic ram pushes the polymer through the die in a **stroke-by-stroke** manner (not continuous). Heat softens the polymer; the ram supplies the pressure.

**Advantages** (≈3 marks):
1. Generates **very high pressure** (up to **100+ MPa**) → ideal for high-viscosity / high-molecular-weight polymers.
2. **No shear heating** — minimal degradation of heat-sensitive polymers.
3. **Simpler mechanics** — no screw, no gearbox, no back-flow.
4. Plug-flow-like → **uniform product**.
5. Excellent for **PTFE** (which cannot be processed by a screw extruder because it does not flow as a melt).

**Limitations** (≈2 marks):
1. **Batch (discontinuous)** — not suitable for long production runs.
2. Low throughput compared to a screw extruder.
3. Mechanical complexity of the hydraulic system.
4. Limited mixing.

**Two industrial applications:**
1. **PTFE (Teflon) ram extrusion** of rods, tubes, billets — PTFE does not melt, only sinters, so a ram is essential.
2. **UHMWPE rod & profile extrusion** — ultra-high molecular weight PE is too viscous for a screw extruder.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **−1 mark** for confusing **"intermeshing"** with **"non-intermeshing"** in twin-screw classification.
> - **−1 mark** if you say a ram extruder is "continuous" — it is **batch**!
> - **−2 marks** if you cannot name a real polymer that **requires** a ram extruder (PTFE is the textbook answer).
> - Examiners expect at least **2 advantages + 2 limitations + 2 applications**. Anything less → loss of marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Recall List — KTU Module 2, Extruders**

- ✅ An **extruder** continuously converts solid polymer → homogeneous melt → shaped profile.
- ✅ **Single-Screw Extruder (SSE)** = industrial workhorse; **three zones** (Feed, Compression, Metering).
- ✅ **Twin-Screw Extruder (TSE)** is classified as:
  - **Co-rotating intermeshing** → compounding, devolatilization, reactive extrusion.
  - **Counter-rotating intermeshing** → PVC pipe, profile (positive displacement).
- ✅ **Ram extruder** = batch, very high pressure, used for **PTFE** and **UHMWPE**.
- ✅ **Compression Ratio (CR)** = $H_f / H_m$, typical **2:1 to 4:1**.
- ✅ **L/D ratio** of modern screws = **24:1 to 36:1**.
- ✅ **Drag flow** $Q_d \propto D^{2} N H \sin\varphi \cos\varphi$ (Couette component).
- ✅ **Pressure flow** $Q_p \propto D H^{3} \sin^{2}\varphi \cdot \Delta P / \mu L$ (Poiseuille component).
- ✅ **Net throughput** $Q = Q_d - Q_p$.
- ✅ **Open discharge** ⇒ $\Delta P = 0$ ⇒ $Q = Q_d = Q_{\max}$.
- ✅ **Closed die** ⇒ $Q = 0$ ⇒ $\Delta P = \Delta P_{\max}$.
- ✅ **Optimum helix angle** for max $Q_d$ = **45°** (gives $Q_{d,\max} = \pi^{2} D^{2}NH/4$).
- ✅ **8 key assumptions** of the Newtonian unwound-channel model: steady, fully-developed, laminar, isothermal, Newtonian, thin flights, no slip, incompressible.
- ✅ **Scale-up cube law**: $Q \propto D^{3}$ (approx.) for geometrically similar screws.
- ✅ **Newtonian shear rate** in the channel: $\dot\gamma = \pi D N / H$.
- ✅ Real polymers are **non-Newtonian (pseudoplastic)** → power-law model with index $n$.
- ✅ **Residence time** $\bar{t} = V_{\text{channel}}/Q$; **co-rotating TSE** has narrower RTD than SSE.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Screw and Twin-Screw Extruders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Single Screw and Twin-Screw Extruders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Extruder — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Extruder (KTU 2024 Definition):** An *extruder* is a continuous, high-pressure processing machine that converts a solid polymer feedstock (granules, powder, flakes, or recycled scrap) into a homogeneous, pressure-driven melt (or compound) and forces it through a shaping die to produce a continuous profile of constant cross-section.

For a polymer technologist, the extruder is the **workhorse of the polymer industry** — used in pipe manufacturing, film blowing, wire & cable coating, sheet extrusion, blow moulding parisons, profile extrusion, and most importantly, in **compounding and masterbatch production**.

---

## 1.2 Classification (As Per KTU 2024 Module 2)

Extruders are broadly classified into two principal families:

| Family | Variants | Common Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Screw Extruder (SSE)** | Conventional, Barrier (Maillefer), Grooved-feed, Vented (Two-stage) | Pipe, film, sheet, blow moulding |
| **Twin-Screw Extruder (TSE)** | Co-rotating, Counter-rotating, Intermeshing, Non-intermeshing | Compounding, devolatilization, reactive extrusion, PVC processing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two designs?** A single-screw machine is mechanically simple and cheap — excellent for *melting and pumping* a ready polymer. A twin-screw machine is mechanically complex and expensive — but offers *intense distributive and dispersive mixing*, *self-wiping* action, and *positive conveying* — making it ideal for *compounding, reactive extrusion, and PVC* (which is heat-sensitive and corrosive).

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Polymer Caulking Gun"

Imagine the extruder as a **high-tech, heated caulking gun**:

1. The **hopper** is the open mouth where you drop the polymer pellets (just like loading sealant tubes).
2. The **barrel + screw** assembly is the heated barrel with a rotating "auger" inside — the rotating flighting acts like a giant rotating Archimedes screw that pulls, melts, and pushes the polymer forward.
3. The **breaker plate + screen pack** at the end acts like a *filter* — it removes contaminants and creates back-pressure to homogenize the melt.
4. The **die** is the shaped nozzle at the tip — it determines the final profile of the product (a pipe, a sheet, a film, a coating…).

For a **twin-screw extruder**, the analogy is two intermeshing caulking augers working *side-by-side* in the same barrel — they knead the material like a baker kneading dough, but continuously.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Sections 1-2:** *"Hopper → Screw → Barrel → Breaker Plate → Die → Profile"*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Single-Screw Extruder

A single-screw extruder is built around **four functional zones** along the screw length:

| Zone | Function | Channel Depth (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid Conveying (Feed Zone)** | Compacts solids, conveys by friction | Deep (≈ 0.15 D) |
| **Melting / Transition Zone (Compression)** | Melts polymer via barrel heat + shear | Tapered (0.15D → 0.05D) |
| **Melt Conveying (Metering Zone)** | Pumps melt, builds pressure, homogenizes | Shallow (≈ 0.05 D) |
| **Mixing / Devolatilization (Optional)** | Static mixers, vents, gear mixers | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maddock / Solid-Bed Melting Model (KTU expected):** In the melting zone, the polymer exists as a *solid bed* lying against the trailing face of the flight, with a *melt film* (≈ 0.1 mm) along the barrel surface. As the screw rotates, the melt is scraped off, accumulates in a *melt pool* behind the solid bed, and the solid bed shrinks until it disappears. **Melt rate $\omega_m$ depends on the difference between barrel and melt temperatures, and on shear rate.**

---

## 2.2 The Unwrapped-Channel Model (Newtonian Analysis)

To analyse melt conveying, the helical screw channel is **mathematically "unwrapped"** into a flat rectangular channel of width $W$ and depth $H$, set at the helix angle $\theta$ on a moving plate (the barrel) sliding over a stationary plate (the screw root).

**Coordinate system on the unwrapped channel:**
- $z$ → down-channel direction
- $x$ → cross-channel direction (perpendicular to flight)
- $y$ → channel depth (0 = root, $H$ = barrel surface)

| Variable | Symbol | Typical Range (KTU) |
|---|---|---|
| Screw diameter | $D$ | 25 mm – 300 mm |
| Channel depth | $H$ | 1.5 mm – 12 mm |
| Helix angle | $\theta$ | 17.65° (square pitch) – 25° |
| Screw rotational speed | $N$ | 10 – 200 rpm |
| Flight width | $e$ | 0.08 D – 0.12 D |
| Barrel surface velocity | $V_b = \pi D N$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newtonian Model Assumption (Examination Favourite):** The polymer melt is assumed to behave as a **Newtonian fluid** (viscosity $\eta$ is constant, independent of shear rate). This is valid for some polymer melts above $T_m + 100°C$ and for low $M_w$ grades, but the real world requires the *power-law (Ostwald–de Waele) model*.

---

## 2.3 Drag, Pressure, and Leakage Flows — KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE THREE FLOWS THEOREM (KTU 2024 SYLLABUS HIGHLIGHT):** The net volumetric throughput $Q$ of an extruder is the algebraic sum of three component flows:
> 
> $$\boxed{\;Q_N = Q_d - Q_p - Q_L\;}$$
> 
> where $Q_d$ = drag flow, $Q_p$ = pressure flow, $Q_L$ = leakage flow.

| Component | Formula (Newtonian, per unit width) | Physical Origin |
|---|---|---|
| **Drag Flow** $Q_d$ | $Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2}\, \pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta$ | Relative motion of barrel over screw channel |
| **Pressure Flow** $Q_p$ | $Q_p = \dfrac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta}{12\,\eta}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ | Resistance to flow due to die back-pressure |
| **Leakage Flow** $Q_L$ | $Q_L = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 \delta^3 \tan\theta}{12\,\eta}\cdot\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ | Clearance ($\delta \approx 0.0005 D$) over the flight tips |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility of these equations:** The extruder designer uses the drag-flow term to *predict the maximum throughput at zero back-pressure*, the pressure-flow term to *size the metering zone for a target pressure*, and the leakage term to *penalise design with excessive flight clearance*. A 0.5 mm increase in flight clearance can drop throughput by **10–15%** at high pressures.

---

## 2.4 Operating Characteristics Curve

When $Q_N$ is plotted against $\Delta P$ (die pressure) at constant $N$:

- The curve is a **straight line with negative slope** (Newtonian).
- **X-intercept** = $\Delta P_{\max}$ (closed die, no flow) → max pumping pressure.
- **Y-intercept** = $Q_{d,\text{open}}$ (open die, no pressure) → max throughput at zero $\Delta P$.
- Actual operating point = intersection with the die characteristic curve $Q = K \Delta P / \eta$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick Question:** *"In an extruder, the screw itself is NOT a positive displacement pump — the die resistance is what creates pressure, and the screw merely provides drag and pressure flow."* Mention this in long-answer questions for full marks.

---

## 2.5 Twin-Screw Extruders — Theoretical Framework

Twin-screw extruders are classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Options | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Rotation Direction** | Co-rotating / Counter-rotating | Co-rotating = self-wiping, high speed. Counter-rotating = positive conveying, calender-like. |
| **Intermeshment** | Intermeshing / Non-intermeshing | Intermeshing = C-shaped chambers, starved feeding. Non-intermeshing = like 2 single-screws in one barrel. |

### 2.5.1 Co-Rotating Intermeshing TSE (Most Common in Compounding)
- Screws rotate in the **same direction** at the same speed.
- Flight of one screw **wipes the root** of the other — *self-wiping action*.
- C-shaped chambers are formed between the two screws and the barrel — material is conveyed in a *figure-of-eight* path.
- Modular screw design: screw is built from individual elements (conveying, kneading, reverse, mixing) on a common splined shaft.

### 2.5.2 Counter-Rotating Intermeshing TSE
- Used mainly for **rigid PVC** and **high-viscosity compounds**.
- Screws rotate in **opposite directions**.
- The flights of one screw act as a *barrel wall* for the other — the C-chambers are *sealed*, giving **positive conveying** like a gear pump.
- Disadvantage: high pressure on bearings → limited to low screw speeds (≤ 40 rpm).

### 2.5.3 Non-Intermeshing TSE
- Two screws side-by-side with a separating wall, no flight overlap.
- Behaves like **two parallel single-screw extruders** in one barrel.
- Used for *de-volatilization* (the large free surface aids vapour removal) and *reactive extrusion*.

---

## 2.6 Screw Elements Used in Modular TSE

| Element | Function | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Conveying (Flighted)** Element | Forward pumping, melting | All zones |
| **Kneading Element** (disc stack) | Dispersive mixing, melting | Compounding |
| **Reverse (Left-handed) Element** | Creates back-pressure, sealed zone | Mixing intensification |
| **Tooth / Pin Mixing Element** | Distributive mixing | Masterbatch, colorants |
| **Devolatilization Element** | Opens channel for vapour escape | Venting |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Kneading disc elements are characterized by their *stagger angle* (30°, 45°, 60°, 90°). 90° (neutral) kneaders provide *maximum mixing* but **no conveying*. 45° kneaders balance mixing and conveying. This distinction is **frequently asked in KTU ESE** as a 7-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Drag-Flow Equation $Q_d$ (Newtonian Model)

### Step 1 — Unwrap the helical channel

Consider the screw channel as a flat rectangular channel of width $W$ and depth $H$, inclined at the helix angle $\theta$. The barrel surface moves relative to the screw root with **velocity components**:

$$V_{bz} = V_b \cos\theta = \pi D N \cos\theta \quad \text{(down-channel)}$$

$$V_{bx} = V_b \sin\theta = \pi D N \sin\theta \quad \text{(cross-channel)}$$

**Logic:** These are the projections of the tangential barrel velocity $V_b = \pi D N$ onto the channel's local axes.

---

### Step 2 — Set up the Navier–Stokes equation (steady, fully-developed, isothermal, no pressure gradient along $x$)

For Newtonian flow in the channel, the velocity $v_z(y)$ satisfies:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = \eta \frac{d^2 v_z}{dy^2}$$

Boundary conditions:
- At $y = 0$ (screw root): $v_z = 0$ (no-slip)
- At $y = H$ (barrel surface): $v_z = V_{bz}$

---

### Step 3 — Integrate twice

$$v_z(y) = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial P}{\partial z}\, y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

Apply $v_z(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$.

Apply $v_z(H) = V_{bz} \Rightarrow C_1 = \dfrac{V_{bz}}{H} - \dfrac{1}{2\eta}\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} H$.

Therefore:

$$v_z(y) = \frac{V_{bz}\, y}{H} - \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial P}{\partial z}\, y(H - y)$$

**Logic:** The first term is the **linear Couette (drag) flow** profile. The second term is the **parabolic Poiseuille (pressure) flow** profile, opposing the drag flow.

---

### Step 4 — Obtain the volumetric flow rate per unit width

$$q = \int_0^H v_z(y)\, dy$$

$$q = \int_0^H \left[\frac{V_{bz}\, y}{H} - \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{\partial P}{\partial z}\, y(H - y)\right] dy$$

$$q = \frac{V_{bz}\, H}{2} - \frac{H^3}{12\eta}\frac{\partial P}{\partial z}$$

**Logic:** $\int_0^H y\, dy = H^2/2$ and $\int_0^H y(H-y)\,dy = H^3/6$. These are standard definite integral identities.

---

### Step 5 — Identify the two component flows

Drag flow (per unit width) at $\partial P/\partial z = 0$:

$$q_d = \frac{V_{bz}\, H}{2} = \frac{\pi D N \cos\theta\, H}{2}$$

Pressure flow (per unit width) at $V_{bz} = 0$:

$$q_p = -\frac{H^3}{12\eta}\frac{\partial P}{\partial z}$$

---

### Step 6 — Multiply by channel width to obtain the full flow rate

Using the geometry of an unwrapped screw, the channel width equals the screw circumference projected:

$$W = \pi D \sin\theta - e \;\approx\; \pi D \sin\theta$$

Therefore the total drag flow becomes:

$$Q_d = q_d \cdot W = \frac{\pi D N \cos\theta\, H}{2} \cdot \pi D \sin\theta = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}$$

**Logic:** Substituting $V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ and $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$ recovers the KTU Formula Sheet expression.

$$\boxed{\;Q_d = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Pressure-Flow Equation $Q_p$

The pressure drop per unit down-channel length is related to the total die back-pressure $\Delta P$ by:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\frac{\Delta P}{L_m}$$

where $L_m$ is the **down-channel length of the metering zone** (NOT the axial length of the screw):

$$L_m = \frac{L_{\text{axial}}}{\sin\theta}$$

Substituting into the pressure-flow per-unit-width:

$$q_p = -\frac{H^3}{12\eta}\left(-\frac{\Delta P}{L_m}\right) = \frac{H^3 \Delta P}{12\eta L_m}$$

Multiplying by $W = \pi D \sin\theta$:

$$Q_p = \frac{\pi D \sin\theta \cdot H^3 \Delta P}{12\eta L_m}$$

Substituting $L_m = L_{\text{axial}}/\sin\theta$:

$$Q_p = \frac{\pi D \sin\theta \cdot H^3 \Delta P \sin\theta}{12\eta L_{\text{axial}}} = \frac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta}{12\eta L_{\text{axial}}}\Delta P$$

$$\boxed{\;Q_p = \frac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta}{12\,\eta\, L}\,\Delta P\;}$$

**Logic:** $L = L_{\text{axial}}$ is the axial length of the metering zone — the quantity actually measured on the screw.

---

## 3.3 Net Throughput and the "Optimum" Operating Point

Combining the three flows:

$$Q_N = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2} - \frac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta}{12\eta L}\Delta P - \frac{\pi^2 D^2 \delta^3 \tan\theta}{12\eta L}\Delta P$$

**Maximum throughput** (open die, $\Delta P = 0$, neglect leakage):

$$Q_{\max} = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}$$

**Maximum pressure** (closed die, $Q_N = 0$):

$$\Delta P_{\max} = \frac{6\pi\eta L N \cos\theta \tan\theta}{H^2} \quad \text{(neglecting leakage)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The maximum pressure is *directly* proportional to screw speed and inversely proportional to $H^2$. This is why the *metering channel depth* $H$ is the most critical design parameter — halving $H$ quadruples the pressure capability but halves the maximum throughput.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A single-screw extruder has $D = 60$ mm, screw speed $N = 60$ rpm, metering zone depth $H = 3$ mm, helix angle $\theta = 17.65°$ ($\sin\theta = 0.303$, $\cos\theta = 0.953$, $\tan\theta = 0.318$), axial length $L = 1.2$ m, flight clearance $\delta = 0.05$ mm, and is processing a Newtonian melt of viscosity $\eta = 500$ Pa·s. The die produces a back-pressure of $\Delta P = 5$ MPa. Calculate the net throughput, neglecting leakage.

**Solution:**

Drag flow:
$$Q_d = \frac{\pi^2 (0.06)^2 (1)\, (0.003)\, (0.303)(0.953)}{2}$$

$$Q_d = \frac{9.8696 \times 0.0036 \times 0.003 \times 0.289}{2} = \frac{3.083 \times 10^{-5}}{2} = 1.541 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Pressure flow:
$$Q_p = \frac{\pi (0.06) (0.003)^3 (0.303)^2 (5 \times 10^6)}{12 \times 500 \times 1.2}$$

$$Q_p = \frac{3.1416 \times 2.7\times 10^{-8} \times 0.0918 \times 5\times 10^6}{7200} = \frac{3.89 \times 10^{-3}}{7200} = 5.40 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Net throughput:
$$Q_N = 1.541 \times 10^{-5} - 5.40 \times 10^{-7} = 1.487 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to kg/hr assuming melt density 950 kg/m³:
$$Q_N = 1.487 \times 10^{-5} \times 950 \times 3600 = 50.86\,\text{kg/hr}$$

**Answer:** $Q_N \approx 1.49 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s $\approx$ **50.9 kg/hr**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
