# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Polytechnic Equivalent]**
**Define the terms "drag flow" and "pressure flow" in a single-screw extruder and state the net throughput equation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Drag Flow ($Q_d$):** The volumetric flow rate generated purely by the **relative motion of the barrel surface over the screw channel**, in the absence of any pressure gradient. It is mathematically defined as $Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta\cos\theta$.

**Pressure Flow ($Q_p$):** The **reverse volumetric flow rate** generated in response to the die back-pressure $\Delta P$. The die resistance forces the melt to flow back up the channel, opposing the drag flow. It is given by $Q_p = \dfrac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta}{12\eta L}\Delta P$.

**Net Throughput Equation:**

$$Q_N = Q_d - Q_p - Q_L$$

where $Q_L$ is the leakage flow over the flight tips.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between co-rotating and counter-rotating twin-screw extruders.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Co-rotating TSE | Counter-rotating TSE |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Both screws rotate in the **same direction** | Screws rotate in **opposite directions** |
| Wiping | **Self-wiping** action between screws | No self-wiping; flights form a *calender nip* |
| Conveying | Partially **starved**, *figure-of-eight* path | **Positive conveying** via sealed C-chambers |
| Screw Speed | High (up to **1200 rpm**) | Limited (≤ 40 rpm) |
| Application | Compounding, masterbatch, reactive extrusion | **Rigid PVC**, heat-sensitive compounds |
| Wear | Higher on kneading elements | Higher on bearings |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each 14-mark question contains **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. You are expected to attempt **either full Question A or full Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] With the help of a neat sketch, describe the working of a single-screw extruder. Explain the four functional zones along the screw.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, KTU Sample Paper 2024 Module 2]**

**Model Solution:**

A single-screw extruder consists essentially of a **hopper**, a **barrel** heated by external electric/induction heaters, a **rotating screw** driven by a variable-speed motor through a gearbox, a **breaker plate with screen pack** at the discharge end, and a **shaping die**.

**Working Principle:** Polymer pellets fall from the hopper into the rotating screw channel. The relative motion of the heated barrel over the screw flights creates a viscous drag that conveys, melts, mixes, and pressurizes the polymer into a continuous melt stream, which is forced through the die to form a profile of fixed cross-section.

**Four Functional Zones (each is a 1-mark item; complete sketch worth 3 marks):**

1. **Solid Conveying (Feed) Zone:** The deep-channel section just below the hopper. Polymer pellets are preheated by conduction and barrel heat, compacted by the rotation, and conveyed forward by *solid friction conveying* — driven by the difference between the barrel/solid friction coefficient (high) and screw/solid friction coefficient (low). Typical channel depth $\approx 0.15 D$.

2. **Melting / Compression / Transition Zone:** The channel depth is *tapered* (typically 3:1 to 4:1 compression ratio). The combination of decreasing channel volume, external heat, and viscous dissipation in the thin melt film melts the polymer. According to **Maddock's solid-bed melting model**, a *melt film* forms at the barrel surface and is scraped off into a *melt pool*, while the *solid bed* shrinks progressively.

3. **Melt Conveying (Metering) Zone:** A shallow constant-depth channel section that *pumps* the melt, builds *die back-pressure*, and provides limited *distributive mixing*. This is the zone where the *Newtonian drag-flow/pressure-flow* analysis is valid. Typical depth $\approx 0.05 D$.

4. **Mixing / Devolatilization Zone (Optional):** Special screw sections like **Saxton, pineapple, or Union Carbide (UC) mixers** are added to improve *distributive mixing*. For *vented extruders*, a second decompression zone is incorporated to remove volatiles, moisture, or residual monomer.

#### **(b) [7 Marks] Derive the drag-flow and pressure-flow expressions for a Newtonian fluid in the metering zone of a single-screw extruder, and hence obtain the net throughput equation.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

Refer to **Section 3.1 to 3.3** of these notes for the complete derivation. Key steps to write in the examination:

1. Unwrap the helical channel of width $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$ and depth $H$ at helix angle $\theta$.
2. Identify the relative barrel velocity $V_b = \pi D N$ and resolve into $V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos\theta$ and $V_{bx} = \pi D N \sin\theta$.
3. Write the Navier–Stokes equation in the $z$-direction: $\partial P/\partial z = \eta\, d^2v_z/dy^2$.
4. Apply no-slip boundary conditions at $y=0$ and $y=H$.
5. Integrate twice to obtain $v_z(y) = (V_{bz}\, y/H) - (y/2\eta)(\partial P/\partial z)(H - y)$.
6. Integrate over the channel depth to obtain $q = V_{bz}H/2 - (H^3/12\eta)(\partial P/\partial z)$.
7. Multiply by channel width and substitute $\partial P/\partial z = -\Delta P/L$.
8. Final result:
   $$Q_N = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2} - \frac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta}{12\eta L}\Delta P$$
9. Mention that $Q_L$ is the leakage flow (small) and the full equation is $Q_N = Q_d - Q_p - Q_L$.
10. Note: $Q_d$ is independent of viscosity, $Q_p$ is viscosity-dependent.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Classify twin-screw extruders. Explain the construction and working of a co-rotating intermeshing twin-screw extruder used in compounding.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 2 Important]**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification (2 marks):** Twin-screw extruders are classified along two orthogonal axes — **rotation direction** (co-rotating vs counter-rotating) and **degree of intermeshment** (intermeshing vs non-intermeshing). This gives **four families**: co-rotating intermeshing, counter-rotating intermeshing, co-rotating non-intermeshing, and counter-rotating non-intermeshing.

**Construction of Co-Rotating Intermeshing TSE (3 marks):**
- Two **parallel intermeshing screws** mounted on **splined shafts** inside a figure-of-eight cross-section barrel.
- The **modular screw design** — each screw is assembled from individual elements (conveying, kneading, mixing, reverse) on a common shaft, allowing the screw profile to be customized for the polymer system.
- The screws are driven through a **reduction gearbox** with an internal spline coupling.
- A **side or top hopper** feeds material into the entry zone of the screws.
- A **vacuum vent** is provided for devolatilization.

**Working (2 marks):**
- The two screws rotate in the **same direction** at identical speed.
- As the screws intermesh, the **flight of one screw wipes the root of the other** — this is the *self-wiping* action that prevents polymer hang-up and stagnation.
- The intermeshing creates **C-shaped chambers** between the two screws, which **seal** the material and convey it forward in a *figure-of-eight* path.
- In the kneading zone, **kneading disc elements** with various stagger angles (30°–90°) apply **intense shear and elongational flow**, achieving both *dispersive* (breaking agglomerates) and *distributive* (spatially uniform) mixing.
- The TSE is typically **starve-fed** (the metering of feed from the hopper is rate-limited), so the **degree of fill** in each zone is controlled — this is fundamentally different from a single-screw extruder, which is *flood-fed*.

#### **(b) [7 Marks] Explain with a sketch the solid-bed (Maddock) melting model in a single-screw extruder. How does the melting mechanism differ in a TSE?** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, May 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Maddock / Solid-Bed Melting Model — Single-Screw Extruder (5 marks):**

The melting zone is conceptually divided into the following features:
- A thin **melt film** of thickness $\delta_f \approx 0.1$ mm forms on the *barrel surface* due to conduction and viscous dissipation.
- The rotating flight scrapes this melt film and deposits it into a **melt pool** that grows at the *trailing face* of the flight.
- A **solid bed** of un-melted pellets lies against the *pushing flight face*.
- The interface between the solid bed and the melt pool is the **melting front**, whose location is determined by the energy balance.

**Governing Equation (Maddock–Tadmor Model):**

$$\omega_m = \Phi\, \sqrt{\frac{V_b\, \rho_m\, k_m\, (T_b - T_m)}{\delta_f}}$$

where $\omega_m$ = melt rate per unit length, $V_b$ = barrel velocity, $k_m$ = melt thermal conductivity, $\rho_m$ = melt density, $T_b$ = barrel temperature, $T_m$ = polymer melting point, and $\Phi$ is a geometric factor.

**Key Insight:** The solid bed *thickness* decreases linearly along the melting zone, until the bed is fully consumed at the *end of the melting zone* (Tadmor's linear melting assumption).

**Melting in TSE (2 marks):**
- In a TSE, melting is dominated by **external heat input** (conducted from the barrel) and not viscous dissipation, because the screws are *starve-fed* with low fill ratio and low pressure.
- Melting occurs primarily in the **conveying elements** by conduction from the barrel wall.
- **Kneading elements** are placed *after* melting is complete, so their role is *mixing*, not melting.
- The **specific energy input (SEI)** in a TSE is much lower than in a single-screw extruder, making TSEs ideal for **heat-sensitive polymers like PVC**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU ESE — Module 2 Extruders:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to unwrap the helix** — students often write $Q_d = \pi D N H/2$ which is *wrong by a factor of $\sin\theta \cos\theta$*. The unwrapped-channel model is mandatory.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $L$ (axial length) with $L_m$ (down-channel length)** — the pressure-flow denominator uses **axial** length $L$ in the KTU formula; if you use $L_m = L/\sin\theta$ and forget to invert, you lose 2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Writing $Q_d$ as the net throughput** — always write the full $Q_N = Q_d - Q_p - Q_L$; missing the leakage term is a recurring 1-mark deduction.
> 
> 4. **Conflating kneading disc stagger angles with kneading effectiveness** — state explicitly that 90° kneaders are *neutral* (no forward flow), 45° are *forward-conveying*, and reverse (left-handed) elements *seal* a section.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting to mention starve-feeding in TSE** — every TSE operating-characteristics answer must clarify that TSEs are *rate-limited at the feed throat*, unlike single-screw extruders which are *flood-fed*.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up barrier-flight and Maddock terms** — the barrier screw (Maillefer type) is a *design modification*, not a melting model. Don't conflate the two.
> 
> 7. **Missing the unit of $Q_d$** — always state m³/s. Marks are lost if units are omitted.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Extruder** = continuous, high-pressure, polymer-melt pumping machine.
- **Single-Screw Extruder (SSE):** simplest, cheapest, used for melting & pumping. Flood-fed. Four zones: **Feed → Melting → Metering → (Optional Mixing)**.
- **Twin-Screw Extruder (TSE):** modular, self-wiping, starve-fed, used for compounding, reactive extrusion, devolatilization, PVC.
- **TSE configurations:** *co-rotating intermeshing* (most common), *counter-rotating intermeshing* (PVC), *non-intermeshing* (devolatilization).
- **Three-flows equation (KTU gold formula):** $Q_N = Q_d - Q_p - Q_L$.
- **Drag flow** $Q_d = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}$ — *independent of viscosity*.
- **Pressure flow** $Q_p = \dfrac{\pi D H^3 \sin^2\theta}{12\eta L}\Delta P$ — *inversely proportional to viscosity*.
- **Leakage flow** is over the flight tips, depends on clearance $\delta$ (typically $0.0005\,D$).
- **Newtonian model** is the *first-approximation* analysis — real polymers are *power-law fluids* (Ostwald–de Waele), which gives higher $Q_d$ and lower $Q_p$ exponents.
- **Maddock melting model:** solid bed against flight, melt film at barrel, melt pool grows, solid bed shrinks.
- **Maillefer barrier screw:** a *secondary flight* separates solid bed and melt pool — increases melting rate.
- **Operating curve:** $Q_N$ vs $\Delta P$ is a *straight line with negative slope*; operating point is intersection with die curve.
- **Kneading discs** stagger angles: 30° (mild mixing), 45° (forward conveying), 60° (medium mixing), 90° (neutral, max mixing), reverse (sealing).
- **Starve feeding** in TSE → degree of fill is an *independent process variable*; in SSE, fill is always ~100%.
- **L/D ratio:** SSE 20–30 (compounding) to 40 (film), TSE 32–48 (compounding) up to 60 (reactive extrusion).
- **Compression ratio** (SSE): ratio of feed-zone depth to metering-zone depth, typically 2.5:1 to 4:1.
- **Default $L = L_{\text{axial}}$** in the pressure-flow formula (KTU convention).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Metering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Metering

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Polymer Machinery and Product Manufacturing (PCPOT503)**, the term **"Metering"** refers to the controlled, metered delivery of a precisely known, uniform quantity of polymer melt (or compound) per unit time from the processing machine to the die, mould, or downstream equipment. It is the **final, quality-determining functional stage** of any continuous or cyclic polymer processing operation.

In a **single-screw extruder**, metering specifically describes the function of the **metering zone (also called the metering section or pumping zone)** — the terminal shallow-channel section of the extruder screw whose sole purpose is to:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> *Metering is the third and final functional zone of an extruder screw, located after the feed and compression/melting zones, where the fully plasticated, homogenized melt is pressurized and delivered at a steady, predictable, pulsation-free volumetric output rate to the die, governed by Newtonian/Non-Newtonian drag-flow and pressure-flow mechanics.*

The metering zone is bounded by:
- **Upstream boundary:** The end of the compression/melting zone (where melt pool is **100 %**).
- **Downstream boundary:** The screw tip / breaker plate assembly / die entry.

The metering zone **must** satisfy three engineering requirements simultaneously:
1. **Steady-state mass output** (constant throughput $Q$ independent of small die pressure perturbations).
2. **Melt homogeneity** (uniform temperature and composition — no solid polymer residue).
3. **Pressure build-up** to overcome die resistance without excessive screw-shaft torque.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Tapering Garden Hose Analogy

Imagine a long cylindrical **garden hose** filled with water, lying horizontally on a gentle slope. Now imagine pushing a coiled spring (the screw) through it from the back.

- The **feed section** is where you pour water in (water enters a wide opening).
- The **compression zone** is where the hose gradually narrows — water gets compressed.
- The **metering zone** is the **final, narrow, constant-diameter section** of the hose. Here, the cross-section is fixed, the water (melt) is fully liquid, and whatever amount you push in from behind per second comes out from the front per second — exactly like a **precise kitchen measuring cup** ("1 cup of water every second, no more, no less").

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** "Meter" comes from the Latin *metiri* (to measure). The metering zone is the machine's **measuring cup** — it ensures that for every revolution of the screw, exactly a known volume of melt is delivered.

## 1.3 Why Metering is the Most Critical Zone (KTU High-Yield)

Most polymer product defects — **die-lip surging, sharkskin, output fluctuation, dimensional drift, gauge bands** — originate in **poorly designed or improperly operated metering zones**. For this reason, KTU examiners and industry process engineers treat metering as the **"heart of the extruder"**.

| Property | Numerical Indicator (Typical Range) |
|---|---|
| Metering zone length $L_m$ | **20 % – 30 %** of total screw length $L$ |
| Metering channel depth $H_m$ | **1 mm – 3 mm** (small for thermoplastics) |
| Compression ratio $CR$ | **2.5 – 4.0** (typical for LDPE/HDPE/PP) |
| Metering pressure | **5 MPa – 35 MPa** (typical extrusion range) |
| Metering melt temperature | **180 °C – 280 °C** (polymer-dependent) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Metering Zone

The metering zone of a single-screw extruder is the **shallow, constant-depth helical channel** carved by the flight of the screw. To understand metering mathematically, we must first define its geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Geometric Vocabulary (Memorize for Exams)**
> Let $D$ = screw outer diameter, $L$ = axial length, $\theta$ = helix angle (measured at the flight land to the plane perpendicular to the screw axis).

### 2.1.1 Fundamental Screw Geometry

The helical flight, when "unwound" onto a flat plane, becomes a rectangular channel of width $W$ and depth $H_m$, inclined at the helix angle $\theta$. The relationships are:

$$
W = \pi D \sin(\overline{\theta}) - e
$$

where $\overline{\theta}$ is the **average helix angle** between root and tip, and $e$ is the flight width (typically $e \approx 0.08D$ to $0.12D$).

The **lead** of the screw (axial advance per turn) is:

$$
\ell = \pi D \tan(\theta)
$$

For a **square-pitched** screw (most common), $\theta \approx 17.65°$ giving $\ell = D$.

### 2.1.2 Unwound-Channel Model (Tadmor & Klein / Classical)

The metering channel is "unwound" and laid flat. The bottom is the screw root (moving with velocity $V_b = \pi D N$ in the down-channel direction), the side is the stationary flight land. This gives a **Couette-like flow** superimposed with a **Poiseuille-like back flow** due to pressure.

## 2.2 The Two Flow Components in the Metering Zone

The metering zone flow is classically decomposed (Newtonian approximation) into:

### 2.2.1 Drag Flow ($Q_d$) — The "Forward" Pumping Component

Caused by the relative motion of the screw root against the stationary barrel. The screw root drags melt forward (down-channel).

For a Newtonian fluid in a shallow rectangular channel of depth $H_m$ and width $W$:

$$
Q_d = \frac{1}{2} V_{bz} \, W \, H_m
$$

where the down-channel velocity of the barrel (relative to screw) is:

$$
V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos(\theta)
$$

and $N$ is the screw rotational speed in revolutions per second (rev/s).

### 2.2.2 Pressure Flow ($Q_p$) — The "Backward" Leakage Component

Caused by the pressure gradient $\partial p / \partial z$ set up against the die. It opposes drag flow:

$$
Q_p = -\frac{W H_m^{3}}{12 \mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}
$$

where $\mu$ is the **Newtonian viscosity** of the melt.

### 2.2.3 Net Down-Channel Flow

$$
Q = Q_d + Q_p = \frac{1}{2} V_{bz} W H_m - \frac{W H_m^{3}}{12 \mu} \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}
$$

### 2.2.4 Cross-Channel (Circulation) Flow

A circulatory secondary flow arises in the plane normal to the flight. Its magnitude (peak down-channel velocity):

$$
v_{z,\max} = \frac{V_{bz}}{2} + \frac{H_m^{2}}{8 \mu}\left(-\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right)
$$

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every equation a KTU student is expected to recognize** for the metering topic. Memorize the symbol meaning and the units.

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units / Typical Value |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $Q_d = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{bz} \, W \, H_m$ | Drag (forward) flow rate | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos(\theta)$ | Barrel velocity component along channel | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 3 | $Q_p = -\dfrac{W H_m^{3}}{12 \mu}\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial z}$ | Pressure (back) flow rate | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | $Q = Q_d + Q_p$ | Net metering zone throughput | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 5 | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2} \alpha \, \pi^{2} D^{2} N H_m \sin(\theta)\cos(\theta) - \dfrac{\beta \, \pi D H_m^{3} \sin^{2}(\theta)}{12 \mu}\Delta p$ | Net throughput (Newtonian, with shape factors $\alpha, \beta$) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 6 | $Q_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \pi^{2} D^{2} N H_m \sin(\theta)\cos(\theta)$ | Open-discharge flow ($\Delta p = 0$) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{\alpha \pi^{2} D^{2} N H_m \sin(\theta)\cos(\theta)}{2}\left[1 - \dfrac{\beta \, H_m^{2} \tan(\theta)}{6 \alpha \mu \pi D N \cos(\theta)}\Delta p\right]$ | General metering equation | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 8 | $V_{bz}^{'} = \pi D N \cos(\theta)$ | Cross-channel relative velocity (barrel vs root) | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 9 | $\dot{\gamma}_{av} = \dfrac{\pi D N}{H_m}$ | Average shear rate in metering zone | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 10 | $\Delta p_{\max} = \dfrac{6 \mu \pi D N L_m \cos(\theta)}{H_m^{2} \tan(\theta)}$ | Max pressure developed at $Q = 0$ (shut-off) | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 11 | $\eta_{m} = \dfrac{m \exp(a/T)}{1 + (b/T) \cdot \gamma^{n-1}}$ | Modified Cross model (non-Newtonian viscosity) | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| 12 | $P_{\text{shaft}} = 2 \pi N \cdot T_{\text{shaft}}$ | Mechanical power absorbed by screw | $\text{W}$ |
| 13 | $Q_{\text{mass}} = \rho \cdot Q$ | Mass throughput (production rate) | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 14 | $CR = \dfrac{H_f}{H_m}$ | Compression ratio ($H_f$ = feed depth) | dimensionless |
| 15 | $\dot{m} = \rho \, \pi^{2} D^{2} N H_m \sin(\theta)\cos(\theta) / 2$ | Mass output, open discharge | $\text{kg/s}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Newtonian Reality:** Real polymer melts obey the **Power Law** $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$ or **Cross model**. The Newtonian metering equation is **only** valid when $n = 1$. For $n < 1$ (shear-thinning), the true throughput is approximately **1.2 – 1.5 times** the Newtonian prediction. This is a frequent KTU exam trap.

## 2.4 Operating Characteristics Curve (KTU Favourite Question)

Plotting $Q$ versus $\Delta p$ at constant $N$ gives a **straight line** (Newtonian case) called the **screw characteristic curve** or **operating line**:

$$
Q(\Delta p) = Q_{\max} - K_p \, \Delta p
$$

where:

$$
Q_{\max} = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^{2} D^{2} N H_m \sin(\theta)\cos(\theta), \quad K_p = \dfrac{\beta \pi D H_m^{3}\sin^{2}(\theta)}{12 \mu}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> The **intersection point** of the screw characteristic curve and the die characteristic curve (die: $Q = K_d \, \Delta p^{1/n}$) determines the actual **operating point** of the extruder. KTU examiners commonly ask students to plot both curves and locate the operating point graphically.

## 2.5 Why Metering Depth $H_m$ Matters Enormously

Because $Q \propto H_m$ (drag term) and $Q \propto -H_m^{3}$ (pressure term), the metering zone depth is a **delicately balanced design parameter**:

- **Shallow metering zone** ($H_m \downarrow$): Less throughput, but **higher pressure capability** and better melt homogenization. Used for high-viscosity engineering plastics (PC, PEEK, PPS).
- **Deep metering zone** ($H_m \uparrow$): Higher throughput, but lower pressure capability and risk of solid-bed carry-over. Used for polyolefins (LDPE, PP).

## 2.6 Metering Pumps (Auxiliary Metering Devices)

Beyond the extruder screw, **metering pumps** (usually **gear pumps**) are placed between the extruder and die for ultra-precise throughput. They decouple screw pulsation from die output:

$$
Q_{\text{gear}} = \frac{\pi}{4} w \left(D_o^{2} - D_i^{2}\right) N_g - \text{clearance leakage}
$$

where $w$ = gear width, $D_o, D_i$ = tip and root diameters, $N_g$ = gear rotation speed.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Net Throughput of the Metering Zone (Newtonian Melt)

This derivation is a **KTU board-exam favourite** and appears in almost every polymer processing module question paper.

### Step 1 — Unwinding the Helical Channel

The helical channel of the metering zone is "unwrapped" onto a flat rectangular slab. The slab has:
- Length along down-channel direction $z$: equal to $L_m / \sin(\theta)$
- Width across the channel: $W = \pi D \sin(\overline{\theta}) - e$
- Depth (normal to barrel): $H_m$

### Step 2 — Boundary Conditions

The two parallel walls are:
- The **barrel** at $y = H_m$ — stationary ($v_z = 0$).
- The **screw root** at $y = 0$ — moving in $+z$ direction with velocity $V_{bz}$.

### Step 3 — Equation of Motion (Simplified Navier–Stokes)

For fully-developed, steady, incompressible flow in $z$-direction with negligible inertia (creeping flow, $\text{Re} \ll 1$):

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu \frac{\partial^{2} v_z}{\partial y^{2}}
$$

### Step 4 — First Integration with Respect to $y$

Integrate once:

$$
\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial y} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\, y + C_1
$$

### Step 5 — Second Integration

$$
v_z(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\, y^{2} + C_1 y + C_2
$$

### Step 6 — Apply Boundary Conditions

- **At $y = 0$ (screw root):** $v_z = V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos(\theta)$, so $C_2 = V_{bz}$.
- **At $y = H_m$ (barrel):** $v_z = 0$, so:

$$
0 = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\, H_m^{2} + C_1 H_m + V_{bz}
$$

Solving for $C_1$:

$$
C_1 = -\frac{V_{bz}}{H_m} - \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\, H_m
$$

### Step 7 — Final Velocity Profile

$$
v_z(y) = \frac{V_{bz}\, y}{H_m} - \frac{V_{bz}\, y^{2}}{2 H_m \cdot \ldots}
$$

Substituting carefully:

$$
v_z(y) = V_{bz}\left(\frac{y}{H_m}\right) - \frac{H_m^{2}}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\left[\frac{y}{H_m} - \left(\frac{y}{H_m}\right)^{2}\right]
$$

The complete expression is:

$$
v_z(y) = V_{bz}\,\frac{y}{H_m} - \frac{H_m^{2}}{2\mu}\left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right)\left[\frac{y}{H_m} - \left(\frac{y}{H_m}\right)^{2}\right]
$$

### Step 8 — Volumetric Flow Rate (Integration Across Channel Depth)

$$
Q = W \int_{0}^{H_m} v_z(y)\, dy
$$

$$
Q = W\int_{0}^{H_m}\!\left[V_{bz}\frac{y}{H_m} - \frac{H_m^{2}}{2\mu}\!\left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right)\!\left(\frac{y}{H_m} - \frac{y^{2}}{H_m^{2}}\right)\right]dy
$$

Evaluating the integrals one by one:

$$
\int_{0}^{H_m}\!\frac{y}{H_m}\,dy = \frac{H_m}{2}
$$

$$
\int_{0}^{H_m}\!\left(\frac{y}{H_m} - \frac{y^{2}}{H_m^{2}}\right)dy = \frac{H_m}{2} - \frac{H_m}{3} = \frac{H_m}{6}
$$

### Step 9 — Final Net Flow Equation

Substituting:

$$
Q = W\left[\frac{V_{bz} H_m}{2} - \frac{H_m^{2}}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\cdot\frac{H_m}{6}\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q = \frac{V_{bz} W H_m}{2} - \frac{W H_m^{3}}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\,}
$$

This is the **governing Newtonian metering equation**. ✓ Verified.

## 3.2 Derivation: Open-Discharge Maximum Throughput

In **open discharge** (no die resistance, $\Delta p = 0$):

$$
Q_{\max} = \frac{V_{bz} W H_m}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\pi D N\cos(\theta)\cdot \pi D\sin(\theta)\cdot H_m
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{\max} = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H_m \sin(\theta)\cos(\theta)}{2}\,}
$$

For a square-pitched screw ($\theta = 17.65°$, $\sin\theta\cos\theta \approx 0.25$):

$$
Q_{\max} \approx 0.4\,\pi^{2} D^{2} N H_m \approx 4.0\, D^{2} N H_m \;\; (\text{empirical})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** At **open discharge**, the metering zone behaves as a pure drag pump — output is **independent of viscosity**. This is why throughput measurement at open discharge is a standard calibration check.

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum (Shut-off) Pressure

At **shut-off** ($Q = 0$), all the drag flow is cancelled by back pressure flow:

$$
\frac{V_{bz} W H_m}{2} = \frac{W H_m^{3}}{12\mu}\frac{\Delta p_{\max}}{L_m/\sin\theta}
$$

$$
\frac{V_{bz}}{2} = \frac{H_m^{2}}{12\mu}\frac{\Delta p_{\max}\sin\theta}{L_m}
$$

$$
\Delta p_{\max} = \frac{6\mu V_{bz} L_m}{H_m^{2} \sin\theta}
$$

Substituting $V_{bz} = \pi D N \cos\theta$:

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta p_{\max} = \frac{6 \mu \pi D N L_m \cos\theta}{H_m^{2} \sin\theta} = \frac{6 \mu \pi D N L_m}{H_m^{2} \tan\theta}\,}
$$

This pressure is the **maximum the extruder can develop** for given melt viscosity, screw geometry, and speed. In practice, the **operating pressure** is 30–60 % of $\Delta p_{\max}$.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard)

**Problem Statement:**
A 60 mm diameter single-screw extruder has a metering zone of length $L_m = 600$ mm and depth $H_m = 2$ mm. The screw has a square pitch and rotates at $N = 60$ rev/min. A Newtonian melt of viscosity $\mu = 5 \times 10^{3}$ Pa·s flows against a die pressure of $\Delta p = 8$ MPa.

**Required:**
(a) Calculate the maximum theoretical throughput at open discharge.
(b) Calculate the actual throughput at the given die pressure.
(c) Calculate the maximum pressure the extruder can develop.
(d) Compare mass output (melt density $\rho = 750$ kg/m³).

### Solution

**Given Data:**
- $D = 0.060$ m
- $L_m = 0.600$ m
- $H_m = 0.002$ m
- $N = 60 \text{ rpm} = 1.0$ rev/s
- $\mu = 5 \times 10^{3}$ Pa·s
- $\Delta p = 8 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $\theta = 17.65°$ (square pitch)
- $\sin\theta = 0.303$, $\cos\theta = 0.953$, $\tan\theta = 0.318$
- $\rho = 750$ kg/m³

### Part (a) — Open Discharge Throughput

$$
Q_{\max} = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H_m \sin\theta\cos\theta}{2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q_{\max} = \frac{\pi^{2} (0.060)^{2}(1.0)(0.002)(0.303)(0.953)}{2}
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
\pi^{2} \approx 9.8696
$$

$$
D^{2} = (0.060)^{2} = 3.6\times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^{2}
$$

$$
\pi^{2}D^{2} = 9.8696 \times 3.6\times 10^{-3} = 3.553\times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
\times N H_m = \times(1.0)(0.002) = \times 2.0\times 10^{-3} \;\Rightarrow\; 7.106\times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\times \sin\theta\cos\theta = \times(0.303)(0.953) = \times 0.2888 \;\Rightarrow\; 2.053\times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
\div 2 = 1.026\times 10^{-5}\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{\max} \approx 1.03 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 36.9\;\text{L/hr}\,}
$$

### Part (b) — Actual Throughput

Use the full equation with pressure term:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N H_m \sin\theta\cos\theta}{2} - \frac{\pi D H_m^{3} \sin^{2}\theta}{12\mu}\Delta p
$$

Drag term: $1.026 \times 10^{-5}$ (already computed).

Pressure term:

$$
\frac{\pi D H_m^{3} \sin^{2}\theta}{12\mu}\Delta p = \frac{\pi(0.060)(0.002)^{3}(0.303)^{2}}{12 \times 5000}\times 8\times 10^{6}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
\pi(0.060) = 0.1885
$$

$$
\times H_m^{3} = \times(8\times 10^{-9}) = 1.508\times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
\times \sin^{2}\theta = \times(0.0918) = 1.385\times 10^{-10}
$$

$$
\times \Delta p = \times 8\times 10^{6} = 1.108\times 10^{-3}
$$

Divide by $12 \times 5000 = 60000$:

$$
\frac{1.108\times 10^{-3}}{6\times 10^{4}} = 1.847\times 10^{-8}\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q = 1.026\times 10^{-5} - 1.847\times 10^{-8} \approx 1.008\times 10^{-5}\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{\text{actual}} \approx 1.01\times 10^{-5}\;\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}\,}
$$

The pressure drop reduces throughput by only **~1.8 %** because $\Delta p$ is small relative to $\Delta p_{\max}$.

### Part (c) — Maximum (Shut-off) Pressure

$$
\Delta p_{\max} = \frac{6\mu \pi D N L_m \cos\theta}{H_m^{2} \tan\theta}
$$

Compute:

$$
6\mu = 6 \times 5000 = 3\times 10^{4}
$$

$$
\pi D N L_m \cos\theta = \pi(0.060)(1.0)(0.600)(0.953) = 0.1078
$$

Numerator: $3\times 10^{4} \times 0.1078 = 3234$ (Pa·s/m × m³/s × m = Pa·m²/s × m = Pa·m³/s — we need Pa)

Actually, dimensional check: $\mu$ [Pa·s] × $D$ [m] × $N$ [1/s] × $L_m$ [m] / ($H_m^{2}$ [m²] × $\tan\theta$ [—]) → Pa.

Denominator: $H_m^{2}\tan\theta = (0.002)^{2}(0.318) = 1.272\times 10^{-6}$

$$
\Delta p_{\max} = \frac{3234}{1.272\times 10^{-6}} = 2.543\times 10^{9}\;\text{Pa} = 2543\;\text{MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** This enormous theoretical $\Delta p_{\max}$ is **never** achieved because (i) the melt becomes non-Newtonian at high shear, (ii) the screw shaft would yield torsionally, and (iii) the breaker plate would rupture. Realistic maximum is **~30 – 50 MPa**.

### Part (d) — Mass Throughput

$$
\dot{m} = \rho Q_{\text{actual}} = 750 \times 1.008\times 10^{-5} = 7.56\times 10^{-3}\;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\dot{m} \approx 7.56\times 10^{-3}\;\text{kg/s} \approx 27.2\;\text{kg/hr}\,}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Newtonian Prediction | Real (Non-Newtonian) Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Throughput vs Speed | Linear $Q \propto N$ | Slightly super-linear due to shear-thinning |
| Throughput vs Viscosity | $Q$ independent of $\mu$ at $Q_{\max}$ | True at $Q_{\max}$, but $\mu$ itself depends on $\dot{\gamma}$ |
| $\Delta p_{\max}$ | Very high (~GPa theoretical) | Limited to ~30 – 50 MPa |
| Shape factor correction | $\alpha = 0.5$, $\beta = 1$ (Newtonian) | $\alpha \approx 0.4$, $\beta \approx 0.7$ for shear-thinning |
| Effect of flight clearance | Often neglected | **Significant**: leakage flow $\propto H_c^{3}/\mu$ |
