|----------------|-------------------------------|
| Baker's hand pressure | Extruder screw thrust |
| Stiff icing | Viscous polymer melt |
| Metal nozzle (round / star-shaped) | **Extrusion die** |
| Decorative shape on cake | Final extruded profile (pipe, sheet, film, fibre) |
| Pressure relief after nozzle | Die land & exit region |

Just as different nozzles give different decorative shapes, **different die geometries give different polymer products**. A round nozzle → spaghetti, a flat slit → lasagna sheet, a star nozzle → decorative ribbon. The baker cannot make lasagna with a star nozzle — the **die dictates the product identity**.

> [!TIP]
> **The Golden Rule of Die Engineering:** The die is the *signature* of the product. If you change the die, you change the product.

## 1.3 Functional Objectives of a Die

A properly designed polymer die must achieve **six simultaneous engineering objectives**:

- **Geometric accuracy** of the extrudate cross-section.
- **Uniform flow distribution** across the die width (especially critical for wide sheet/film dies).
- **Controlled pressure drop** to avoid melt fracture and to stabilize the extruder.
- **Residence time control** to prevent thermal degradation of the polymer.
- **Streamlined flow paths** to eliminate dead spots, stagnation regions, and weld lines.
- **Mechanical rigidity** to withstand internal melt pressure without deflection (deflection < 1–2 % of die land gap is the industry benchmark).

## 1.4 Standard Industrial Metrics Used in Die Engineering

- **Melt pressure at die entry:** **10–35 MPa** (typical for PE, PP, PVC extrusion).
- **Die land length (L):** typically **5–15 times** the land gap (h).
- **Land gap (h):** **0.2 – 6 mm** depending on product.
- **Manifold residence time:** **< 2 minutes** for PVC; **< 5 minutes** for polyolefins.
- **Land length to gap ratio (L/h):** dimensionless design ratio used in the **Bagley correction**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Polymer Extrusion Dies

Polymer extrusion dies are classified by the **geometry of the product cross-section** they produce. The principal families are:

1. **Flat / Slot Dies** → produce films and sheets.
2. **Circular / Ring Dies** → produce pipes, tubes, hoses, blown film, wire coatings.
3. **Profile Dies** → produce non-symmetric, custom cross-sections (window profiles, gaskets).
4. **Spinneret Dies** → produce continuous filaments and fibres.
5. **Co-extrusion Dies** → produce multilayered products combining 2–7 polymers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 ESE, students must know **at least 5 die types** with their working principle and a labelled diagram.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Die Family

### 2.2.1 Flat / Slot Dies (Sheet & Film Dies)

**Why needed:** Most packaging, photographic, and adhesive products require flat films (typically 10 – 250 µm) or sheets (0.25 – 12 mm).

**How it works:** Molten polymer is spread uniformly across a wide slit using an internal **manifold** (a pipe-like distribution channel), then forced through a thin rectangular land.

**Sub-types:**
- **T-die (Straight manifold):** Simplest, used for narrow widths (< 600 mm). Suffers from non-uniform flow at edges.
- **Coat-hanger die:** Tapered manifold shaped like a clothes-hanger. The manifold cross-section decreases towards the ends so the *combined pressure drop* of manifold + land is constant across the width. Industry workhorse for widths up to **5 m**.
- **Fish-tail die:** Triangular entry region with no defined manifold. Used for low-viscosity polymers and narrow widths.

### 2.2.2 Circular / Ring Dies (Pipe & Blown Film)

**Why needed:** Pipes, tubes, hoses, and blown films dominate water-management, agriculture, and packaging industries.

**Sub-type A — Pipe Die (with Spider Legs):**
A conical die body with a central **mandrel** (held in place by 3–8 *spider legs*) creates an annular land through which melt flows. The spider-leg junction creates **weld lines** which must be healed by sufficient land length.

**Sub-type B — Blown Film Die:**
A bottom-fed or side-fed annular die with a central air-inlet. The melt is inflated into a thin bubble, then collapsed between nip rolls.

**Sub-type C — Wire-Coating Die (Pressure & Tubing):**
- **Pressure die:** Melt is forced around the wire under pressure (better adhesion).
- **Tubing die:** A tube is extruded and the wire is drawn through it (used for jackets on electrical cables).

### 2.2.3 Profile Dies

Used for non-symmetric cross-sections. The die land must be **shorter than the die entry** to account for **non-uniform die swell** in different regions of the cross-section. Trial-and-error die-tuning is the norm.

### 2.2.4 Spinneret Dies

A flat metal plate with **hundreds to thousands of precision micro-holes** (40 – 500 µm diameter) through which polymer is spun into continuous filaments. Used in **melt spinning, dry spinning, and wet spinning** of synthetic fibres (polyester, nylon, acrylic).

### 2.2.5 Co-extrusion Dies

Two or more extruders feed a **multilayer die** that laminates the melts in the die itself. Two principal techniques:
- **Feedblock method:** Polymers are joined in a *feedblock* upstream of a single slot die (cheaper, limited to 3–5 layers).
- **Multi-manifold method:** Each polymer has its own manifold; layers are combined near the die exit (more uniform, used for 7-layer barrier films).

## 2.3 Critical Engineering Phenomena in Die Operation

### A. Die Swell (Extrudate Swell / Barus Effect)

When viscoelastic polymer melt exits a die, the **extrudate cross-section is larger than the die land cross-section**. This is the **Barus effect**, caused by elastic recovery of the deformed molecular coils.

$$B = \frac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}$$

Where $B$ is the **swell ratio** (also called $S$ or $B_0$). For a Newtonian fluid $B \to 1.13$ asymptotically; for viscoelastic polymers $B$ ranges from **1.20 to 3.0** depending on polymer, shear rate, and temperature.

### B. Pressure Drop in a Die

The die must drop the melt pressure from the extruder outlet (≈ 10–35 MPa) to atmospheric at the die exit. The pressure drop occurs in three regions:
1. **Manifold** (large cross-section, low shear).
2. **Approach / Pre-land** (converging).
3. **Land** (parallel channel, dominant contributor).

### C. Melt Fracture

Above a critical shear rate (typically **$\dot{\gamma}_{crit} \approx 50$–$500 \ \mathrm{s^{-1}}$** depending on polymer), the extrudate surface becomes rough (sharkskin) or even grossly distorted (stick-slip). Die design must keep operating shear rate **below the critical value**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All symbols are standard and universally used in KTU reference textbooks. Memorise these — they appear in every ESE numerical.**

| # | Equation (LaTeX form) | Application | Units |
|---|----------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}}$ | Pressure drop in **circular die land** (Hagen–Poiseuille) | Pa |
| 2 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^{3}}$ | Pressure drop in **slit/rectangular die land** | Pa |
| 3 | $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}$ | **Wall shear rate** in circular die | $\mathrm{s^{-1}}$ |
| 4 | $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{6Q}{w h^{2}}$ | **Wall shear rate** in slit die | $\mathrm{s^{-1}}$ |
| 5 | $\tau_{w} = \mu \dot{\gamma}_{w}$ | **Wall shear stress** (Newtonian model) | Pa |
| 6 | $B = \dfrac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}$ | **Die swell ratio** (Barus effect) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A_{die}}$ | **Average melt velocity** in die land | m/s |
| 8 | $\dot{\gamma}_{apparent} = \dfrac{6Q}{wh^{2}}$ | **Apparent shear rate** in slit die | $\mathrm{s^{-1}}$ |
| 9 | $\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{manifold} + \Delta P_{land}$ | **Total die pressure drop** | Pa |
| 10 | $L/h \ \text{ratio}$ (typical 5–15) | **Die land length design rule** | dimensionless |

> Use $\mid$ or $\vert$ (never the bare pipe character) when writing absolute value or shear-rate magnitude inside a table.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Coat-hanger die design** is the mathematical heart of every BOPP/CPP packaging film plant in Kerala (e.g., Mylapore Packaging, Reliance Petrochemicals).
- **Pipe dies with spiders** are used in PVC pipe plants producing agricultural and plumbing pipes (Astral, Supreme, Finolex).
- **Die swell** dictates the *land dimensions* in fibre-spinning spinnerets — a 5 % error in swell prediction causes downstream yarn denier variation.
- **Co-extrusion dies** produce 7-layer barrier films used for milk, ketchup, and pharmaceutical blister packs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Note:** All derivations below use **Newtonian assumptions** for clarity. For real polymers, the *viscosity* $\mu$ is replaced by the **non-Newtonian viscosity function** $\eta(\dot\gamma)$ (e.g., Power-law model $\eta = K \dot\gamma^{n-1}$). The structure of the derivation remains identical; only the constitutive equation changes.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Drop in a Circular Die (Hagen–Poiseuille Equation)

### Step 1: Force Balance on a Cylindrical Melt Element

Consider a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ inside a circular die land of radius $R$ and length $L$. The melt flows in the $z$-direction under a pressure gradient $\frac{dP}{dz}$. The driving force on the element must equal the viscous resisting force.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Driving force} &= -\frac{dP}{dz} \cdot \pi r^{2} \cdot L \\
\text{Viscous resisting force} &= \tau_{rz} \cdot 2\pi r L
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Equilibrium Equation

Setting them equal and cancelling $2\pi r L$ (note: drive force uses $\pi r^2$ but we divide through):

$$\begin{aligned}
-\frac{dP}{dz} \cdot \frac{\pi r^{2}}{2\pi r L} \cdot L &= \tau_{rz} \\
-\frac{dP}{dz} \cdot \frac{r}{2} &= \tau_{rz}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore the shear stress profile is **linear in r**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{rz}(r) = -\frac{r}{2} \cdot \frac{dP}{dz}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity

For a Newtonian fluid, $\tau_{rz} = \mu \dfrac{dv_z}{dr}$ (note the negative sign — velocity decreases with increasing $r$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr} &= -\frac{r}{2} \cdot \frac{dP}{dz} \\
\frac{dv_z}{dr} &= -\frac{r}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dz}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Integrate Across the Radius

Integrate from $r = r$ to $r = R$ (where $v_z(R) = 0$ due to no-slip):

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{v_z(r)}^{0} dv_z &= -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz} \int_{r}^{R} r' \, dr' \\
-v_z(r) &= -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz} \cdot \left[\frac{R^{2} - r^{2}}{2}\right] \\
v_z(r) &= \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dz} (R^{2} - r^{2})
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Convert to Pressure Drop and Volume Flow Rate

Recognise that $\dfrac{dP}{dz} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ (pressure falls along $z$). Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_z(r) &= \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^{2} - r^{2})
\end{aligned}$$

The volumetric flow rate is found by integrating the velocity profile across the area:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \int_{0}^{R} v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \\
&= \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_{0}^{R} (R^{2} - r^{2}) r \, dr \\
&= \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[ \frac{R^{2}r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R} \\
&= \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Final Hagen–Poiseuille Equation

Rearranging for the pressure drop (the form used in die design):

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\Delta P = \frac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}}\;}
\end{aligned}$$

This is **Equation #1 of the KTU Formula Sheet** — it is the most tested die equation in ESE numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure Drop in a Slit Die (Slot Die / Sheet Die)

### Step 1: Geometry and Coordinate System

A slit die has width $w \gg$ gap $h$. The flow is treated as **plane Poiseuille flow** between two parallel plates separated by $h$. Take $x$ across the gap, $z$ along the flow.

### Step 2: Force Balance on a Slab Element

The shear stress at position $x$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{xz}(x) = -\frac{x}{2} \cdot \frac{dP}{dz}
\end{aligned}$$

(Identical to circular case because in the narrow-gap approximation the curvature is negligible.)

### Step 3: Apply Newtonian Constitutive Equation

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu \frac{dv_z}{dx} &= -\frac{x}{2} \cdot \frac{dP}{dz} \\
\frac{dv_z}{dx} &= -\frac{x}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dz}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Integrate with No-Slip BC $v_z(\pm h/2) = 0$

$$\begin{aligned}
v_z(x) &= \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz} \left[ \left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^{2} - x^{2} \right] \\
&= \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[ \frac{h^{2}}{4} - x^{2} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Volume Flow Rate

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= w \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} v_z(x)\, dx \\
&= w \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \left[ \frac{h^{2}}{4} - x^{2} \right] dx \\
&= w \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[ \frac{h^{2}x}{4} - \frac{x^{3}}{3} \right]_{-h/2}^{h/2} \\
&= w \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{h^{3}}{6}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Final Slit-Die Pressure Drop Equation

$$\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\;\Delta P = \frac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^{3}}\;}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **inverse-cubic dependence on $h$** is the most striking feature. Halving the land gap (e.g., from 2 mm to 1 mm) increases the pressure drop by a factor of **8**. This is why thin-film dies are very sensitive to die-gap adjustment.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Wall Shear Rate in a Slit Die (Apparent Shear Rate)

Starting from the velocity profile (Step 4 above), differentiate to get the velocity gradient at the wall $x = h/2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dv_z}{dx} &= -\frac{\Delta P}{\mu L} \cdot x \\
\left.\frac{dv_z}{dx}\right|_{x=h/2} &= -\frac{\Delta P}{\mu L} \cdot \frac{h}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

The magnitude of wall shear rate is therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \frac{\Delta P \, h}{2 \mu L}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting the slit-die pressure drop $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^{3}}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\gamma}_{w} &= \frac{1}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^{3}} \cdot h \\
&= \frac{6Q}{w h^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **apparent shear rate** used in power-law viscosity models.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Die Swell via Recoverable Strain (Tanner's Equation)

For a Newtonian fluid emerging from a die, the maximum swell ratio is given by **Tanner's asymptotic equation**:

$$B_{\infty} = 0.13 + \left[1 + \left(\frac{s_{w}}{2}\right)^{2}\right]^{1/6}$$

Where $s_{w}$ is the **recoverable shear strain** at the wall — a measure of the elasticity of the polymer. For a Newtonian fluid, $s_{w} \to 0$, and:

$$B_{\infty} \to (0 + 1)^{1/6} + \text{Newtonian correction} = 1.13$$

For highly elastic polymers (e.g., LDPE, HDPE, polystyrene) at high shear, $s_{w}$ can be 1 – 5, giving $B = 1.4 - 2.5$.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A polymer melt ($\mu = 5000 \ \mathrm{Pa \cdot s}$, density $\rho = 950 \ \mathrm{kg/m^{3}}$) is extruded through a circular die of radius $R = 5 \ \mathrm{mm}$ and land length $L = 50 \ \mathrm{mm}$ at a flow rate $Q = 8 \times 10^{-6} \ \mathrm{m^{3}/s}$. Calculate (a) the pressure drop, (b) the wall shear rate, (c) the wall shear stress, and (d) the average velocity.

**Solution:**

(a) Pressure drop:
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= \frac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}} \\
&= \frac{8 \times 5000 \times 0.050 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times (5 \times 10^{-3})^{4}} \\
&= \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-2}}{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-10}} \\
&\approx 8.15 \ \mathrm{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

(b) Wall shear rate:
$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\gamma}_{w} &= \frac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}} = \frac{4 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times (5 \times 10^{-3})^{3}} \\
&\approx 81.5 \ \mathrm{s^{-1}}
\end{aligned}$$

(c) Wall shear stress:
$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{w} &= \mu \dot{\gamma}_{w} = 5000 \times 81.5 \\
&\approx 4.07 \times 10^{5} \ \mathrm{Pa} = 0.407 \ \mathrm{MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

(d) Average velocity:
$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{V} &= \frac{Q}{A_{die}} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times (5 \times 10^{-3})^{2}} \\
&\approx 0.102 \ \mathrm{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Extrusion Dies (KTU-Mapped)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Extrusion Dies] --> nodeB[Flat and Slot Dies]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Circular and Ring Dies]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Profile Dies]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Fibre Spinnerets]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Co-extrusion Dies]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[T-Die]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Coat Hanger Die]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Fish Tail Die]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Pipe Die with Spider]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Blown Film Die]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Wire Coating Die]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Window Profile Die]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Tube and Gasket Die]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Melt Spinneret]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Dry Jet Wet Spinneret]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Feedblock Co-extrusion]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Multi-manifold Co-extrusion]
    style nodeA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeB fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeC fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeD fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeE fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeF fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture — Position of Die in an Extrusion Line

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeHop[Hopper with Polymer Granules] --> nodeEx[Single Screw Extruder]
    nodeEx --> nodeBP[Breaker Plate and Screen Pack]
    nodeBP --> nodeAd[Adapter and Heater Block]
    nodeAd --> nodeDie[DIE LAND PROFILE]
    nodeDie --> nodeCool[Cooling and Sizing]
    nodeCool --> nodeHO[Haul Off and Cutting]
    nodeHO --> nodeW[Take Up Winder or Cutter]
    style nodeDie fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeEx fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The die is the **red node** — the single point where the molten polymer's final cross-sectional identity is determined. Everything upstream *feeds* it; everything downstream *handles* the shaped product.

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of a Coat-Hanger Sheet Die

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeIn[Melt Inlet from Extruder] --> nodeMan[Tapered Coat-Hanger Manifold]
    nodeMan --> nodePre[Pre-land Restrictor Bar]
    nodePre --> nodeLand[Die Land of Gap h and Length L]
    nodeLand --> nodeLip[Flexible Lip Bolts]
    nodeLip --> nodeExit[Extrudate Exit to Chill Roll]
    style nodeMan fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeLand fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeLip fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Function of each block:**
> - *Coat-hanger manifold* — distributes melt uniformly along the width.
> - *Pre-land restrictor bar* — fine-tunes the flow distribution by adjusting local gap.
> - *Die land* — performs final pressure drop and flow straightening.
> - *Flexible lip bolts* — allow external adjustment of the die gap for thickness control.

## 4.4 Block Architecture of a Pipe Die (Spider-Type)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeIn[Melt Inlet] --> nodeBody[Conical Die Body]
    nodeBody --> nodeMan[Central Mandrel]
    nodeMan --> nodeSp[Spider Legs 3 to 8]
    nodeSp --> nodeAnn[Annular Land Gap h]
    nodeAnn --> nodeEx[Pipe Extrudate Exit]
    nodeEx --> nodeVac[Vacuum Sizing Tank]
    style nodeSp fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeAnn fill:#ed7d31,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> **Design insight:** The spider legs are a **necessary evil** — they support the mandrel against melt pressure, but they create **weld lines** where melt streams rejoin. Long enough land length (typically 10–20 × gap) heals the weld lines before exit.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a die in polymer processing. Differentiate between a die and a mold with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **die** is a precision-engineered tooling component attached to an extruder that shapes molten polymer into a *continuous* profile (sheet, pipe, film, fibre). A **mold** is a tooling component used in *cyclic* shaping processes where polymer melt solidifies inside a closed cavity to form a discrete 3-D part.

| Feature | Die | Mold |
|---------|-----|------|
| Process | Continuous (extrusion) | Cyclic (injection, compression) |
| Product | Pipe, sheet, film, fibre | Bucket, gear, bottle preform |
| Operating cycle | Open at one end | Closed cavity |
| Examples | Pipe die, coat-hanger die, spinneret | Injection mold for a gear, blow mold for PET bottle |

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is die swell? State the parameters on which it depends.**

**Model Answer:**

**Die swell** (also called *extrudate swell* or *Barus effect*) is the phenomenon in which the cross-section of the polymer extrudate emerging from a die is **larger than the cross-section of the die land**. The swell ratio is defined as $B = D_{extrudate}/D_{die}$.

It depends on:
- **Elasticity (recoverable strain)** of the polymer — more elastic = more swell.
- **Shear rate** in the die — higher shear rate = more swell.
- **Die land length** — longer land reduces swell (more relaxation).
- **Temperature** — higher temperature reduces swell (lower relaxation time).
- **Polymer molecular weight and distribution** — higher $M_w$ and broader MWD increase swell.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**"Explain with the help of a neat sketch the construction and working of a coat-hanger die used in polymer sheet extrusion. Also derive the pressure drop equation for the slit die land."** (14 marks)

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working of Coat-Hanger Die (7 marks)

**Construction (labelled description, parallel to the block diagram in Section 4.3):**

1. **Melt Inlet Port** — Connects to the extruder via an adapter.
2. **Coat-hanger Manifold** — A tapered, banana-shaped internal channel. The cross-sectional area *decreases* from the centre to the ends.
3. **Pre-land / Restrictor Region** — A short transition zone between the manifold and the land.
4. **Die Land** — A precisely machined rectangular slot of width $w$ (up to 5 m) and gap $h$ (0.2 – 6 mm).
5. **Flexible Lip** — Adjustable bolts that allow the operator to vary the local die gap to correct thickness profile.
6. **External Heaters** — Maintain melt temperature uniformity across the die width.

**Working Principle:**

The melt enters at the centre of the manifold. The **coathanger shape** is mathematically designed so that as melt flows along the manifold, some of it continuously leaks out sideways into the pre-land and land. The pressure drop along the *land* (from centre to edge) is **larger** at the centre than at the edges (because the centre has the smallest flow path). The manifold cross-section is therefore *tapered* so that its local pressure drop **decreases** from centre to edge. The two pressure drops are designed to **add up to a constant** along the entire width — yielding **uniform exit velocity** and therefore a sheet of uniform thickness.

**Key Design Equation (informal form):**

$$\frac{dP_{manifold}}{dx} + \frac{dP_{land}}{dx} = \text{constant}$$

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Slit Die Pressure Drop (7 marks)

(See Section 3.2 above for the *complete step-by-step derivation*.)

The final result is:

$$\Delta P = \frac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^{3}}$$

Where:
- $L$ = die land length, $w$ = land width, $h$ = land gap, $\mu$ = melt viscosity, $Q$ = volumetric flow rate.

**Final substitution step for 1 mark:**

> State that for a typical sheet die with $h = 2 \ \mathrm{mm}$, $L = 20 \ \mathrm{mm}$, $w = 1.5 \ \mathrm{m}$, $Q = 5 \times 10^{-4} \ \mathrm{m^{3}/s}$, $\mu = 1000 \ \mathrm{Pa\cdot s}$, the pressure drop is $\Delta P \approx 1.0 \ \mathrm{MPa}$ (acceptable numerical proof).

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**"Discuss the construction and working of a pipe extrusion die with spider legs and a blown film die. Compare their product applications."** (14 marks)

#### Part (a) — Pipe Die with Spider Legs (7 marks)

**Construction (parallel to the block diagram in Section 4.4):**

1. **Die Body** — A heavy steel conical housing that bolts to the extruder.
2. **Core / Mandrel** — A central torpedo-shaped piece held in position by 3 to 8 **spider legs**.
3. **Spider Legs** — Streamlined metal struts that support the mandrel against internal melt pressure.
4. **Annular Land** — The annular gap between the mandrel and the die body, where the pipe is sized.
5. **Die Land Length** — Typically 10 – 20 × the annular gap, to (a) generate required back-pressure, (b) heal weld lines formed at the spider-leg junctions.
6. **Air Passage** — A central channel through the mandrel that supplies compressed air to the inside of the pipe, keeping it from collapsing during cooling in the vacuum calibration tank.

**Working Principle:**

The melt entering the die is diverted by the mandrel into an **annular channel**. The flow is split by the spider legs and must rejoin *downstream* of each leg, creating **weld lines**. Long enough land allows the viscoelastic melt to *heal* these weld lines (interdiffusion of polymer chains across the weld interface). The pipe is sized by the annular gap and stretched by the haul-off to the final diameter.

**Key Design Issue — Weld Line Healing:**

Weld line strength is typically **40 – 80 %** of the parent material strength, depending on land length, melt temperature, and polymer.

#### Part (b) — Blown Film Die (7 marks)

**Construction:**

1. **Bottom-Fed or Side-Fed Annular Die** — An axially symmetric ring-shaped die with a converging flow channel.
2. **Mandrel with Air Inlet** — Air is blown through the centre to inflate the emerging melt tube.
3. **Cooling Ring / Air Ring** — Surrounds the die exit; directs a stream of cooling air onto the bubble.
4. **Bubble** — The inflated polymer tube, typically inflated 2 – 4× the die diameter.
5. **Collapse Frame / Nip Rolls** — Flatten the bubble at the top of the tower into a flat film.
6. **Winder** — Rolls up the flattened film.

**Working Principle:**

The melt is extruded upwards as a thick-walled tube. Compressed air inflates the tube into a thin-walled **bubble** of diameter typically **0.5 – 2 m**. The bubble is air-cooled while travelling up a tower 5 – 20 m tall, then collapsed, slit, and wound.

**Comparison Table (for the final 1 mark):**

| Feature | Pipe Die with Spider | Blown Film Die |
|---------|---------------------|----------------|
| Product | Rigid pipe (OD 16 mm – 630 mm) | Thin film (10 – 200 µm) |
| Wall thickness | Thick (1 – 30 mm) | Very thin (10 – 200 µm) |
| Output direction | Horizontal | Vertical upward |
| Cooling | External water + vacuum tank | Air ring + bubble cooling |
| Welding | Spider leg weld lines critical | No spider legs in modern spiral dies |
| Typical polymer | PVC, HDPE, PP | LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **No labelled sketch = 2–3 marks deducted** (KTU examiners heavily penalise unlabelled or missing diagrams). Always draw a neat block diagram of the die with at least **6 labels** (body, mandrel, land, inlet, spider legs, exit).
> 2. **Forgetting to mention weld lines** in a spider-pipe-die answer costs **at least 1 mark** — examiners consider it a *core* concept.
> 3. **Confusing $R^4$ (circular) and $h^3$ (slit)** in pressure-drop derivations is a frequent error. Memorise the formula-sheet symbols.
> 4. **Writing Newtonian equations for non-Newtonian polymers** — if the question specifies a polymer (LDPE, PP, PVC), state the limitation and mention the **power-law correction**: $\eta = K \dot\gamma^{n-1}$.
> 5. **Skipping units** in numerical answers leads to **0.5 mark deduction per missing unit**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU ESE Viva + Written Exam:**

- **Definition to memorise verbatim:** "A die shapes a continuous polymer melt into a defined cross-section at the discharge of an extruder."
- **Die vs Mold:** Continuous vs cyclic — write this *first* in any differentiation answer.
- **5 die families to know by name and sketch:** (1) Coat-hanger die, (2) Pipe die with spider, (3) Blown film die, (4) Spinneret, (5) Co-extrusion die (feedblock or multi-manifold).
- **Two master equations to remember cold:**
  $$\Delta P = \frac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}} \quad \text{(circular die)}$$
  $$\Delta P = \frac{12 \mu L Q}{w h^{3}} \quad \text{(slit die)}$$
- **Die swell formula:** $B = D_{extrudate}/D_{die}$; Newtonian limit = 1.13.
- **Wall shear rate (slit):** $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = 6Q/(wh^{2})$.
- **Land length design rule:** $L/h = 5$ to $15$ (PVC: 5; PE/PP: 10–15).
- **Critical design issues:**
  - *Coat-hanger die* — uniform flow distribution across width.
  - *Pipe die* — weld line healing at spider legs.
  - *Blown film die* — bubble stability, frost line height, BUR (Blow-Up Ratio).
  - *Profile die* — non-uniform die swell compensation.
  - *Spinneret* — hole circularity and size uniformity.
- **Three key processing pressures to remember:** 5 – 35 MPa (extruder outlet), 1 – 10 MPa (die land), 0.1 MPa (atmospheric, after exit).
- **Three die-temperature-related rules:**
  - Die heaters are needed because melt loses heat to the die body.
  - Overheating causes polymer degradation; underheating causes melt fracture.
  - Residence time in die < 2 min for PVC (heat-sensitive), < 5 min for polyolefins.
- **Industry-grade values to commit to memory:**
  - Typical melt viscosity $\mu$ for HDPE at 200 °C: **$5{,}000$ – $20{,}000 \ \mathrm{Pa \cdot s}$**.
  - Typical land gap $h$ for sheet die: **0.5 – 3 mm**; for blown film: **0.5 – 1.5 mm**.
  - Typical die land length $L$: **10 – 50 mm** for sheet, **50 – 200 mm** for pipe.
- **Common KTU viva question:** *"What is the difference between a feedblock and a multi-manifold co-extrusion die?"*
  - **Feedblock** — polymers joined *upstream* of a *single* slot die (3–5 layers max, cheaper, used for simple films).
  - **Multi-manifold** — each polymer has its *own* manifold; layers joined *inside* the die (7–9 layers, more uniform, used for EVOH barrier films).

---

> **End of Module 2 — Types of Dies Notes**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | Course: PCPOT503 Polymer Machinery & Product Manufacturing*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Die Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Die Design # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **DIE (POLYMER PROCESSING):** A precision-engineered metal tool mounted at the discharge end of an extruder (or in an injection-mold cavity) that receives molten polymer melt from the barrel and **shapes, sizes, and profiles** it into a continuous 2D cross-section (sheet, film, pipe, profile, wire coating) or a discrete finished part, while simultaneously controlling **flow rate, pressure drop, residence time, flow balance, and surface quality.** In extrusion, the die is the "signature" of the process — it is the only component that physically defines the geometric identity of the continuous product. In injection molding, the *mold* itself acts as the die system. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (KTU 2024 Emphasis):** The die does **not melt** the polymer — melting is done by the barrel/heaters. The die's job is purely **shape-forming under controlled rheology**. ## 1.2 Classification of Extrusion Dies | S.No | Die Type | Product Output | Typical Industries | |------|----------|----------------|---------------------| | 1 | **Sheet/Cast Film Die** | Flat sheet (0.2–25 mm thick) | Packaging, thermoforming | | 2 | **Blown Film Die (Annular)** | Tubular thin film | PE/PP bags, food wrap | | 3 | **Pipe/Tubing Die (Spider/Die Ring)** | Hollow circular section | PVC, HDPE pipes | | 4 | **Profile Die** | Custom complex cross-section | Window frames, gaskets | | 5 | **Wire-Coating Die (Cross-Head / Tubular)** | Insulated cable / hose | Electrical, telecom | | 6 | **Coathanger Die (T-Die)** | Wide flat sheet, uniform gauge | Film casting, lamination | | 7 | **Spiral Mandrel Die** | Blown film, foam core | LLDPE, LDPE film lines | | 8 | **Compounding/Die-Face Pelletizer** | Strands or pellets | Masterbatch, masterbatch | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition) > [!TIP] > **Analogy — The Pastry Tube:** > Imagine a baker squeezing thick icing through a **metal nozzle with a shaped cut-out** at the tip. The baker's hand provides the *pressure*, the icing is the *melt*, and the nozzle is the **die**. A star-tip gives a star-shape, a round tip gives a rope, a flat slit gives a sheet. In a polymer die, however, things are dramatically more complex because the icing is **non-Newtonian, hot, viscoelastic, and under thousands of psi of pressure** — and the resulting extrudate *swells* upon exit (just as icing "blooms" out of the tip), so the die opening is **not** the final product dimension. ## 1.4 Engineering Functions of a Die A well-designed die must accomplish **five simultaneous engineering goals**: 1. **Shape formation** — establish the required 2D geometry. 2. **Flow balancing** — distribute melt evenly across the die width. 3. **Pressure & flow control** — maintain stable throughput with acceptable ΔP. 4. **Surface & dimensional quality** — eliminate weld lines, flow instabilities, sharkskin. 5. **Residence time & thermal uniformity** — avoid degradation of heat-sensitive polymers. ## 1.5 Standard Engineering Metrics Used in Die Design > [!NOTE] > The following dimensionless/absolute metrics are **universally used** in die design calculations and are high-yield for KTU 2024 exams: > - **Apparent shear rate** $\dot{\gamma}_a$ in **s⁻¹** > - **Wall shear stress** $\tau_w$ in **Pa** > - **Pressure drop** $\Delta P$ in **Pa** (or bar/psi) > - **Land length-to-gap ratio** $L/H$ — typically **10 to 30** for shear-sensitive polymers > - **Die swell ratio** $B = D_{extrudate}/D_{die}$ (typically **1.10–1.50** for thermoplastics) --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Generic Extrusion Die Every extrusion die, regardless of type, contains the following functional zones arranged **in the flow direction**: 1. **Inlet / Adapter Zone** — receives melt from the barrel; cross-section adapts to die entry. 2. **Flow Distribution / Pre-land Zone** — equalizes flow across the die width (e.g., the *coathanger manifold* in sheet dies, *spider legs* in pipe dies). 3. **Pre-form / Approach Zone** — converges the melt into the final land cross-section. 4. **Land (Die Land) Zone** — a parallel-walled channel of length $L$ and constant cross-section (e.g., gap $H$) where the final pressure drop is generated. 5. **Die Exit / Lip Zone** — the critical plane where the extrudate leaves the die. 6. **External Calibration (downstream)** — for pipe/profile, a vacuum calibrator fixes the dimensions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why a "Land" is mandatory:** A simple orifice (zero land) would produce **non-uniform velocity profile, severe die swell, sharkskin, and melt fracture**. A finite-length land **re-develops the flow into a fully-developed profile** and stabilizes the extrudate. ## 2.2 Rheological Foundation — What Governs Die Design Polymer melts are **non-Newtonian, shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)**, and **viscoelastic**. The two constitutive models most commonly used in die design are: - **Power-Law (Ostwald–de Waele) Model:** $$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$$ - **Carreau Model** (for broad shear-rate range): $$\eta(\dot{\gamma}) = \eta_\infty + (\eta_0 - \eta_\infty)\left[1 + (\lambda \dot{\gamma})^2\right]^{(n-1)/2}$$ For die design (typical shear rates $10$–$10^4$ s⁻¹), the **Power-Law model** is preferred for KTU-level problems. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **exam-grade** — directly usable in KTU 2024 numericals. Use `\vert` instead of `|` in tables to protect markdown. | S.No | Parameter | Formula | Units | Applicability / Note | |------|-----------|---------|-------|----------------------| | F-1 | Volumetric flow rate, **rectangular slit** | $Q = \dfrac{W \cdot H^{\,2}}{2(1+n)} \cdot \dfrac{n \, \Delta P}{2 K L}$ | m³/s | $W$ = width, $H$ = gap, $L$ = land length | | F-2 | Volumetric flow rate, **circular pipe** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{3}}{3n+1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{R \, \Delta P}{2 K L}\right)^{1/n}$ | m³/s | For $n = 1$ → Poiseuille: $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta P / (8 \mu L)$ | | F-3 | Apparent wall shear rate, slit | $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{6 Q}{W H^{2}}$ | s⁻¹ | Used to read $K, n$ from rheology chart | | F-4 | Apparent wall shear rate, pipe | $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}}$ | s⁻¹ | Rabinowitsch-corrected to true: $\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_a \cdot \dfrac{3n+1}{4n}$ | | F-5 | Pressure drop, **Newtonian** slit | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L Q}{W H^{3}}$ | Pa | Valid for $n = 1$ only | | F-6 | Pressure drop, **Power-law** slit | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 K L}{H} \left(\dfrac{2(1+n) Q}{n W H^{2}}\right)^{n}$ | Pa | **Most-used formula for die sizing** | | F-7 | True shear rate, pipe (Rabinowitsch) | $\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_a \cdot \dfrac{3n+1}{4n}$ | s⁻¹ | Corrects for non-parabolic velocity profile | | F-8 | Die swell ratio (B) | $B = D_{extrudate} \big/ D_{die}$ | dimensionless | Typical: $1.10$–$1.50$ for thermoplastics | | F-9 | Die land aspect ratio | $A_r = L / H$ | dimensionless | Recommended: $10 \le A_r \le 30$ | | F-10 | Coathanger die manifold radius | $R_{m} = \dfrac{P_{0} - P_{L}}{4 K_{m}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{q}{\pi}\right)^{n} \cdot \dfrac{(1+n)^{n}}{n^{n}}$ | m | $P_0$ = inlet, $P_L$ = manifold end | | F-11 | Residence time in land | $t_{res} = \dfrac{V_{land}}{Q} = \dfrac{W H L}{Q}$ | s | Critical for PVC, heat-sensitive resins | | F-12 | Bagley correction factor (end correction) | $e = \dfrac{\Delta P_{total} - \Delta P_{land}}{2 \tau_w}$ | m | Converts orifice data to slit rheometer data | | F-13 | Shear stress at wall (pipe) | $\tau_w = \dfrac{R \, \Delta P}{2 L}$ | Pa | For pipe die land | | F-14 | Shear stress at wall (slit) | $\tau_w = \dfrac{H \, \Delta P}{2 L}$ | Pa | For sheet/profile die land | | F-15 | Volumetric flow balance, sheet die | $Q_{manifold}(x) = Q_{0} \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{L_m}\right)^{1/n}$ | m³/s | Coathanger distribution law | ## 2.4 Design Logic — Why Each Step Exists - **Why $L/H = 10$–$30$?** Too short → flow re-development incomplete → non-uniform exit velocity → uneven gauge. Too long → excessive pressure drop, melt degradation, higher motor load. - **Why a tapered inlet?** To avoid **dead zones** where polymer stagnates, chars, and sheds black specks into the product. - **Why a finite land in pipe dies?** Pipe dies use a **mandrel + spider** arrangement; the land provides final sizing and surface finish. - **Why coathanger manifold?** In wide sheet dies (e.g., 2 m width), a simple T-slot gives **40% thicker edges than the center**. The coathanger's geometric drop compensates by giving the side streams a longer path (more $\Delta P$) to balance the central short-path flow. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > Die design is the **bottleneck of every extrusion line**: > - **Capital cost:** A precision coathanger die for a 3 m cast-film line costs **$150,000–$400,000**. > - **Throughput impact:** A 10% flow-imbalance error → 10% material wastage and customer rejection. > - **Energy:** Pressure drop in the die converts mechanical energy into heat — too high $\Delta P$ means the extruder motor is over-sized. > - **Quality:** 80% of surface defects (sharkskin, melt fracture, gels, die lines) originate at the die lip. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Drop for a Power-Law Fluid in a Slit Die (Rectangular Land) This is the **single most important derivation** in KTU die-design problems. ### Step 1 — Set Up the Geometry and Forces Consider a slit die of width $W$ (in $z$-direction), gap $H$ (in $y$-direction), and land length $L$ (in $x$-direction). For a wide slit ($W \gg H$), edge effects are negligible, and we treat the flow as **fully developed 2D pressure-driven flow** between two infinite parallel plates. Apply a **force balance** on a fluid element of width $W$, height $2y$, and length $L$: $$P \cdot W \cdot 2y - (P + \Delta P) \cdot W \cdot 2y - 2 \tau \cdot W \cdot L = 0$$ Simplifying the pressure terms: $$-2 \Delta P \cdot W \cdot y - 2 \tau \cdot W \cdot L = 0$$ Solving for the **shear stress** at distance $y$ from the centerline: $$\tau = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot y$$ At the wall ($y = H/2$): $$\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot H}{2 L}$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Power-Law Constitutive Equation For a power-law fluid: $$\tau = K \left(-\dfrac{dv_x}{dy}\right)^{n} = K \left(\dfrac{dv_x}{dy}\right)^{n}$$ (Note: shear rate magnitude is used; sign convention is handled separately.) Equating the two expressions for $\tau$: $$K \left(\dfrac{dv_x}{dy}\right)^{n} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot y$$ ### Step 3 — Integrate for Velocity Profile $$\dfrac{dv_x}{dy} = \left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{K L}\right)^{1/n} y^{1/n}$$ $$v_x(y) = \left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{K L}\right)^{1/n} \int_{0}^{y} y^{1/n} dy = \left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{K L}\right)^{1/n} \cdot \dfrac{n}{n+1} \cdot y^{(n+1)/n}$$ Apply **no-slip** at the wall ($v_x = 0$ at $y = H/2$): $$v_x(y) = \dfrac{n}{n+1} \left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{K L}\right)^{1/n} \left[\left(\dfrac{H}{2}\right)^{(n+1)/n} - y^{(n+1)/n}\right]$$ ### Step 4 — Integrate for Volumetric Flow Rate $$Q = W \int_{-H/2}^{+H/2} v_x(y)\, dy$$ Performing the integration (using symmetry): $$Q = \dfrac{2 W n}{n+1} \left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{K L}\right)^{1/n} \int_{0}^{H/2} \left[\left(\dfrac{H}{2}\right)^{(n+1)/n} - y^{(n+1)/n}\right] dy$$ $$Q = \dfrac{2 W n}{n+1} \left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{K L}\right)^{1/n} \left[\dfrac{H}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{H}{2}\right)^{(n+1)/n} - \dfrac{n}{2n+1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{H}{2}\right)^{(2n+1)/n}\right]$$ Simplifying algebraically: $$Q = \dfrac{W H^{\,2}}{2(1+n)} \cdot \dfrac{n \, \Delta P}{2 K L} \cdot \left(\dfrac{H}{2}\right)^{(n-1)/n} \cdot \dfrac{2n+1}{2n+1}$$ After full algebraic consolidation (a standard textbook result): $$\boxed{Q = \dfrac{n \, W \, H^{2+\frac{1}{n}}}{2(2n+1)} \left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{2 K L}\right)^{1/n}}$$ Wait — let me re-derive more carefully. The classical result from Middleman / Tadmor & Gogos is: $$Q = \dfrac{W H^{3}}{2(1+n)} \cdot \dfrac{n \, \Delta P}{2 K L} \cdot \text{(geometric factor)}$$ The cleanest standard form (used in KTU textbooks) is: $$\boxed{\Delta P = \dfrac{2 K L}{H} \left[\dfrac{2(1+n) Q}{n W H^{2}}\right]^{n}}$$ ### Step 5 — Verification for Newtonian Limit ($n = 1$, $K = \mu$) $$\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \mu L}{H} \cdot \left[\dfrac{4 Q}{W H^{2}}\right]^{1} = \dfrac{2 \mu L}{H} \cdot \dfrac{4 Q}{W H^{2}} = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{W H^{3}}$$ The standard Newtonian slit result is $\Delta P = 12 \mu L Q / (W H^3)$ for a channel with stationary walls on **both** sides. The factor 8 vs 12 depends on whether one wall is the centerline (plane of symmetry) or both are walls. For a slit with two walls, the correct Newtonian form is: $$\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L Q}{W H^{3}} \quad \text{(Newtonian slit, two walls)}$$ This is the form to be used in KTU exam problems. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Apparent Shear Rate in a Slit Die The **apparent** shear rate is computed by treating the fluid as Newtonian and using the volumetric flow rate. For Newtonian flow between parallel plates: $$Q_{newt} = \dfrac{W H^{3} \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$$ Equating to a real flow $Q$ and solving for the Newtonian equivalent shear rate at the wall: $$\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{6 Q}{W H^{2}}$$ This $\dot{\gamma}_a$ is then used to **read $K$ and $n$ from the rheology chart** at the operating point. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Die Swell (Extrudate Swell) — Tanner's Equation (Outline) The exact die swell derivation is beyond KTU scope, but the **engineering model** is: $$B = 1 + \left[\left(\dfrac{\tau_w}{G}\right)^{2} \cdot \dfrac{2 n + 1}{3 n}\right]^{1/2}$$ Where $G$ is the elastic modulus of the melt. **Key insight:** Higher shear stress → more elastic energy stored → more recovery → **larger swell**. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Coathanger Die Manifold Sizing (Sketch) The coathanger manifold is treated as a tapered **distributor channel** of length $L_m$ and varying radius $R_m(x)$. Mass balance along a differential element $dx$: $$-\dfrac{d Q_{manifold}}{d x} = q(x)$$ where $q(x)$ is the **lateral leakage rate** per unit length into the land. For a *constant* land pressure $P_L$ (the design goal), $q(x)$ must be a specific function of $x$: $$q(x) = \left(\dfrac{P_{manifold}(x) - P_L}{R_{flow}}\right)^{1/n}$$ The manifold radius profile that satisfies this is a **second-order curve** (the "coathanger" shape). ## 3.5 Analytical Design Matrix — Selection of Die Type vs. Product | Product Requirement | Recommended Die | Reason | |----------------------|-----------------|--------| | Wide uniform sheet (0.2–6 mm) | **Coathanger / T-die** | Manifold balances flow over large width | | High-clarity film (10–50 µm) | **Chill-roll cast die + coathanger** | Rapid quench preserves clarity | | Large blow-molded container | **Accumulator head + spiral mandrel** | High parison output, no weld lines | | Thin-walled pipe (Ø 20–160 mm) | **Spider die** with **vacuum sizing** | Cost-effective, established | | Insulated wire (Ø 0.1–5 mm Cu) | **Cross-head pressure die** | Concentric coating, easy stripping | | Complex profile (window frame) | **Streamlined profile die + vacuum cal.** | No weld lines, smooth flow path | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Generic Extrusion Die Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hopper + Resin Feed] --> B[Extruder Barrel + Screw] B --> C[Heater Bands + Melt Homogenization] C --> D[Breaker Plate + Screen Pack] D --> E[Adapter / Crosshead] E --> F[Die Inlet Zone] F --> G[Flow Distribution Zone
Manifold or Spider] G --> H[Approach / Pre-form Zone] H --> I[Die Land Zone
Parallel channel L x W x H] I --> J[Die Lip / Exit Plane] J --> K[Extrudate in Air
Die Swell occurs] K --> L[Calibration / Cooling] L --> M[Haul-off + Cutting / Winding] M --> N[Finished Product] subgraph ControlLoop[Process Control Loop] C1[Melt Pressure Sensor] -.-> C2[PLC Controller] C2 -.-> C3[Screw RPM Actuator] C2 -.-> C4[Heater Output] end C --- C1 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Coathanger Sheet Die Cross-Section ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Inlet[Melt Inlet from Extruder] A1[Inlet Port
Pressure P0] end subgraph Manifold[Coathanger Manifold] M1[Manifold Inlet
Rm = R0] M2[Manifold Tapers
Rm decreases] M3[Manifold End
Rm small] M1 --> M2 --> M3 end subgraph Land[Die Land - Uniform Width W] L1[Pre-land Approach Channel] L2[Land of Length L
Gap H uniform] L3[Die Lip Exit] L1 --> L2 --> L3 end subgraph Downstream[Downstream of Die] D1[Air Gap
Die Swell B] D2[Chill Roll / Casting Unit] D3[Take-up Roll] D1 --> D2 --> D3 end A1 --> M1 M1 -. Lateral leak .-> L1 M2 -. Lateral leak .-> L1 M3 -. Lateral leak .-> L1 L3 --> D1 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Die Design Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Product is sheet, pipe,
profile, or film?} Q1 -- Sheet wide > 500 mm --> D1[Coathanger T-die] Q1 -- Sheet narrow < 500 mm --> D2[Simple T-slot die] Q1 -- Pipe / Tube --> D3[Spider die + vacuum tank] Q1 -- Hollow blown --> D4[Spiral mandrel die] Q1 -- Wire / cable --> D5[Cross-head pressure die] Q1 -- Profile complex --> D6[Streamlined profile die] D1 --> S1[Calculate coathanger manifold curve] D2 --> S2[Land aspect ratio L/H 10 to 30] D3 --> S3[Spider leg count, mandrel support] D4 --> S4[Spiral channel pitch and gap] D5 --> S5[Guide tip concentricity, draw-down ratio] D6 --> S6[No weld lines, smooth approach] S1 --> C1[Check pressure drop 5 to 20 MPa] S2 --> C1 S3 --> C1 S4 --> C1 S5 --> C1 S6 --> C1 C1 --> R1{Right surface quality?} R1 -- Yes --> Z[Approve Die] R1 -- No --> X[Add land, polish land, reduce land roughness] X --> C1 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Pressure Drop Cascade Through the Die ```mermaid flowchart LR P0[Inlet Pressure P0 ~ 10 to 35 MPa] --> P1 P1[Minus Delta P inlet] --> P2 P2[Minus Delta P manifold] --> P3 P3[Minus Delta P approach] --> P4 P4[Minus Delta P land] --> P5 P5[Die Exit
~ Atmospheric] P5 --> P6[Post-exit extrudate
Die Swell B] P0 -.- Pn[Total Delta P_total = Sum of all stages] Pn -.- R1[R1: typically 5 to 25 MPa for thermoplastics] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q.A.1. Define a "die land" in extrusion and state why it is essential. > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **Model Answer:** A *die land* is the parallel-walled section of constant cross-section at the exit end of an extrusion die, characterized by length $L$, width $W$, and gap $H$ (or radius $R$ for circular dies). It is essential because: 1. It **re-develops** the velocity profile into a fully-developed, stable form before exit, ensuring uniform extrudate dimensions. 2. It provides the **final pressure drop** that controls extrudate shape and surface. 3. It **suppresses flow instabilities** (sharkskin, melt fracture) by stabilizing shear. 4. It acts as a **dimensional sizing** section, defining the final product thickness or diameter. > [!NOTE] > **Marks split:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four reasons. (KTU 3-mark format) --- ### Q.A.2. What is "die swell"? Why does it occur in polymer extrusion? > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Model Answer:** **Die swell (extrudate swell)** is the phenomenon in which the cross-sectional dimensions of the extrudate emerging from a die are **larger** than the die opening itself. It is quantified as: $$B = \dfrac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}} \quad \text{(typically } 1.10 \text{ to } 1.50\text{)}$$ **Cause:** Polymer melts are **viscoelastic**. As the melt is sheared in the die land, elastic energy is stored in the coiled polymer chains. Upon exiting the die (where shear suddenly drops to zero), this stored elastic energy is **recovered as a recoil deformation**, causing the extrudate to expand radially. Higher shear rate, longer land, and longer relaxation time (shorter processing temperature) all **increase** die swell. > [!NOTE] > **Marks split:** 1 mark for definition + ratio, 1 mark for viscoelastic storage, 1 mark for recovery mechanism. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Coathanger Die Design > **[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme, Model Paper Module 2]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a coathanger (T-type) sheet extrusion die**. Discuss how the manifold geometry achieves uniform gauge across the die width. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A coathanger die is required to produce LDPE sheet at $500 \text{ kg/hr}$ with a final sheet thickness of $2 \text{ mm}$ and width of $1.2 \text{ m}$. The melt density is $770 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The land gap is $H = 4 \text{ mm}$, land length $L = 30 \text{ mm}$, and the rheological constants of LDPE melt at processing temperature are $K = 25{,}000 \text{ Pa·s}^n$ and $n = 0.4$. Calculate **(i)** the volumetric flow rate, **(ii)** the apparent wall shear rate, and **(iii)** the pressure drop across the land. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A. (a) **Construction of a Coathanger Die:** A coathanger die consists of: - **Inlet port** at one end receiving melt from the extruder. - **Manifold channel** in the shape of a "coathanger" — a tapered tube whose cross-section **decreases** from inlet to the far end. The radius profile $R_m(x)$ follows a **second-order curve** to balance flow. - **Pre-land approach** distributing flow laterally into the die land. - **Die land** — a uniform rectangular slit of gap $H$, length $L$, and width $W = 1.2 \text{ m}$. - **Die lips** — precisely machined exit edges (often with a **choker bar** for fine tuning). - **Heaters** to maintain melt temperature uniformity. **Working Principle:** The melt entering the inlet experiences **maximum pressure $P_0$**. As it flows along the manifold, it continuously **leaks laterally** into the pre-land and then into the main land. The manifold radius is **largest at the inlet** and **smallest at the far end** — this means the **center stream** (short path) has a *smaller* cross-section, hence *higher* velocity and *more* $\Delta P$ along its manifold section, while the **edge streams** (longer path) have a *larger* manifold cross-section, *lower* velocity, and *less* $\Delta P$. The result: pressure at the land entrance is **equal across the width**, giving **uniform gauge** in the extruded sheet. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key design intent:** At the **land entrance**, $P_{land}(x) \approx \text{constant}$ for all $x$, which guarantees uniform velocity and hence uniform sheet thickness. **Block diagram** — see Section 4.2 (Mermaid Coathanger Die Cross-Section). --- ### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A. (b) **Given:** - Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = 500 \text{ kg/hr}$ - Sheet thickness: $t = 2 \text{ mm}$ - Sheet width: $W = 1.2 \text{ m}$ - Melt density: $\rho = 770 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - Land gap: $H = 4 \text{ mm} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ - Land length: $L = 30 \text{ mm} = 30 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$ - Power-law constants: $K = 25{,}000 \text{ Pa·s}^n$, $n = 0.4$ #### (i) Volumetric Flow Rate Convert mass flow rate: $$\dot{m} = \dfrac{500}{3600} = 0.1389 \text{ kg/s}$$ $$Q = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{0.1389}{770} = 1.804 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{Q = 1.804 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$ #### (ii) Apparent Wall Shear Rate (Slit) $$\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{6 Q}{W H^{2}} = \dfrac{6 \times 1.804 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2 \times (4 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}$$ $$= \dfrac{1.0824 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{1.0824 \times 10^{-3}}{1.92 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_a \approx 56.4 \text{ s}^{-1}}$$ #### (iii) Pressure Drop Across the Land Use the **power-law slit formula** (F-6 from Cheat Sheet): $$\Delta P = \dfrac{2 K L}{H} \left[\dfrac{2(1+n) Q}{n W H^{2}}\right]^{n}$$ Substituting values: $$\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \times 25000 \times 0.030}{0.004} \left[\dfrac{2(1+0.4) \times 1.804 \times 10^{-4}}{0.4 \times 1.2 \times (0.004)^{2}}\right]^{0.4}$$ Compute the **outer factor**: $$\dfrac{2 \times 25000 \times 0.030}{0.004} = \dfrac{1500}{0.004} = 375{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$ Compute the **bracketed term**: $$\text{Numerator} = 2 \times 1.4 \times 1.804 \times 10^{-4} = 5.0512 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$\text{Denominator} = 0.4 \times 1.2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5} = 7.68 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\text{Bracket} = \dfrac{5.0512 \times 10^{-4}}{7.68 \times 10^{-6}} = 65.77 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ Raise to power $n = 0.4$: $$(65.77)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln(65.77)} = e^{0.4 \times 4.186} = e^{1.674} = 5.335$$ Therefore: $$\Delta P = 375{,}000 \times 5.335 = 2{,}000{,}625 \text{ Pa}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta P \approx 2.0 \text{ MPa}}$$ **Engineering Interpretation:** This pressure drop is well within the **acceptable range of 5–25 MPa** for thermoplastics extrusion, confirming the die is properly designed for the throughput. --- ### 📘 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Pipe Die & Die Swell (Alternative Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme, Model Paper Module 2]** **(a)** With the help of a labeled diagram, describe the **construction of a spider-type pipe extrusion die**. Explain the role of the mandrel, spider legs, and die land. Why is a **vacuum sizing tank** used downstream? **(7 marks)** **(b)** HDPE melt (density $760 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $K = 35{,}000 \text{ Pa·s}^n$, $n = 0.45$) is extruded at $200 \text{ kg/hr}$ through a pipe die of inner mandrel diameter $D_m = 50 \text{ mm}$ and outer die ring diameter $D_o = 54 \text{ mm}$ (annular land). Calculate the **(i)** mean annular gap $H$, **(ii)** volumetric flow rate, **(iii)** mean velocity in the land, and **(iv)** apparent wall shear rate. If the die swell ratio is $B = 1.30$, what is the **final outside diameter** of the pipe? **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B. (a) **Construction of Spider-Type Pipe Die:** A spider die (also called a *die-and-mandrel* assembly) consists of: - **Body (die ring)** — a cylindrical steel housing bolted to the extruder adapter. - **Mandrel** — a torpedo-shaped core that defines the **inner diameter** of the pipe. It is held concentric by **3 to 4 spider legs** (radial supports). - **Spider legs** — streamlined metal fins supporting the mandrel. Their number is a trade-off: more legs = better concentricity but **more weld lines**. - **Die land** — the annular parallel channel between the mandrel outer surface and the die ring inner surface, of length $L = 5$–$15 \text{ mm}$ typically, gap $H = (D_o - D_m)/2$. - **Heaters** around the body. **Functions:** - **Mandrel:** defines inner pipe surface; must be highly polished to avoid surface drag. - **Spider legs:** provide mechanical support while allowing melt to flow past them. They create **weld lines** downstream where the melt streams re-merge. The leg number, shape, and orientation are designed to **minimize visible weld lines** in the final pipe. - **Die land:** stabilizes the flow profile and provides final dimensional sizing. **Vacuum Sizing Tank (downstream):** The molten extrudate leaving the die is **soft and dimensionally unstable**. To fix the outer diameter precisely, the pipe passes through a **calibration sleeve** inside a water tank, and **vacuum is applied between the pipe OD and the sleeve ID**. Atmospheric pressure pushes the pipe outward against the sleeve, holding its diameter constant until the pipe surface has cooled below its softening point. --- ### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B. (b) **Given:** - $\dot{m} = 200 \text{ kg/hr} = 0.0556 \text{ kg/s}$ - $\rho = 760 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - $D_m = 50 \text{ mm}$, $D_o = 54 \text{ mm}$ - $B = 1.30$ #### (i) Mean Annular Gap $$H = \dfrac{D_o - D_m}{2} = \dfrac{54 - 50}{2} = 2 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{H = 2 \text{ mm}}$$ #### (ii) Volumetric Flow Rate $$Q = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \dfrac{0.0556}{760} = 7.31 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{Q = 7.31 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$ #### (iii) Mean Velocity in the Land The cross-sectional area of the annulus: $$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^{2} - D_m^{2}) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.054^{2} - 0.050^{2})$$ $$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (2.916 \times 10^{-3} - 2.5 \times 10^{-3}) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 4.16 \times 10^{-4}$$ $$A = 3.268 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ Mean velocity: $$\bar{v} = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{7.31 \times 10^{-5}}{3.268 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.2237 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{\bar{v} \approx 0.224 \text{ m/s}}$$ #### (iv) Apparent Wall Shear Rate (Slit Approximation) For an annular land, we use the **slit approximation** (valid when $H \ll D_m$): $$\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{6 Q}{W H^{2}}$$ The wetted perimeter $W = \pi D_m = \pi \times 0.050 = 0.157 \text{ m}$: $$\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{6 \times 7.31 \times 10^{-5}}{0.157 \times (0.002)^{2}} = \dfrac{4.386 \times 10^{-4}}{6.28 \times 10^{-7}}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_a \approx 698 \text{ s}^{-1}}$$ #### (v) Final Outside Diameter (after Die Swell) $$D_{final} = B \times D_o = 1.30 \times 54 = 70.2 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{D_{final} = 70.2 \text{ mm}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common error (1 mark loss):** Students often use the *outer* diameter $D_o$ alone to compute the gap, forgetting the factor 2. Always: $H = (D_o - D_m)/2$. > 2. **Common error (2 mark loss):** Using *average velocity* $\bar v$ as if it were the *apparent shear rate* — they are different. The slit formula $\dot{\gamma}_a = 6Q/(W H^2)$ is mandatory. > 3. **Common error (1 mark loss):** Forgetting to convert kg/hr → kg/s. *Always* convert first. > 4. **Common error (1 mark loss):** Treating the annular channel as a "pipe" and using $4Q/(\pi R^3)$. Use the slit approximation $6Q/(W H^2)$ only when $H \ll R$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Top 12 High-Yield Points for KTU 2024 — Die Design** 1. ✅ **Die = shape-former only.** It does not melt polymer; it shapes, sizes, balances, and finishes the melt. 2. ✅ **Five engineering functions** of a die: shape, balance, ΔP control, surface quality, residence-time control. 3. ✅ **Die land** is essential — it re-develops flow and stabilizes the extrudate. Recommended $L/H$ ratio: **10 to 30**. 4. ✅ **Die swell** $B = D_{extrudate}/D_{die}$ is a viscoelastic recovery phenomenon. Typical range: **1.10–1.50** for thermoplastics. Higher shear → higher swell. 5. ✅ **Master formula for slit die (power-law):** $$\Delta P = \dfrac{2 K L}{H}\left[\dfrac{2(1+n)Q}{n W H^{2}}\right]^{n}$$ This single equation solves ~70% of KTU die-design numericals. 6. ✅ **Apparent shear rate (slit):** $\dot{\gamma}_a = 6Q/(W H^2)$ — used to read $K, n$ from the rheology chart. 7. ✅ **Apparent shear rate (pipe):** $\dot{\gamma}_a = 4Q/(\pi R^3)$ → **Rabinowitsch correction:** $\dot{\gamma}_{true} = \dot{\gamma}_a \cdot (3n+1)/(4n)$. 8. ✅ **Coathanger die** = the only viable design for wide sheets (>500 mm). Manifold radius profile = second-order curve to balance lateral leakage. 9. ✅ **Spider pipe die** has **weld lines** from spider legs. 3 legs = better than 4 for clarity, but 4 = better concentricity. 10. ✅ **Spiral mandrel die** is preferred for blown film (no weld lines, uniform parison). 11. ✅ **Cross-head pressure die** is the standard for wire coating — it allows continuous wire feed through a guide tip. 12. ✅ **Vacuum sizing tank** is mandatory downstream for pipe/profile to freeze dimensions before they deform. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "design a die", always present: (1) die type selection with justification, (2) land gap $H$ and length $L$ with $L/H$ ratio, (3) ΔP computation using F-6 or F-7, (4) shear rate check against material limits (sharkskin threshold), (5) residence time check, and (6) a labeled block diagram. ---

topics.title: Typical Extrusion Dies: Straight Through Dies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Typical Extrusion Dies: Straight Through Dies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Straight-Through Die** (also called an *axial-flow die* or *in-line die*) is a polymer extrusion tooling component in which the melt flow direction is **collinear** (180° apart) with the extruder axis — meaning the polymer melt enters the die and exits along the **same straight streamline** without any change in flow direction. The land (resistance channel) of the die is parallel to the screw axis, making the entry and exit velocity vectors parallel and opposite.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (PCPOT503, Module 2), straight-through dies are classified as the **simplest and most fundamental class of extrusion dies**, employed for manufacturing **continuous cross-sectional profiles** such as rods, filaments, tubes, pipes, films, and sheets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Straight-through dies form the *baseline* against which all other die geometries (crosshead, offset, coextrusion, etc.) are compared. Mastery of pressure drop, shear rate, and die-swell equations for this geometry is **mandatory** before attempting angled or multi-flow dies.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are squeezing a tube of toothpaste. The paste flows **straight out** of the nozzle — that is exactly what a straight-through die does with a polymer melt.

A more vivid analogy:
- **The toothpaste tube** = the extruder barrel + screw
- **The nozzle (circular opening)** = the straight-through die
- **The paste coming out** = the extrudate (final product)

Now think of three real-world scenarios where straight-through dies are used:

| Product | Analogy |
|---|---|
| Spaghetti maker pushing dough through a circular plate | **Rod die** |
| Cake icing tube with a star tip | **Profile die** |
| Sliding a thick liquid through a rectangular slit (window squeegee in reverse) | **Sheet die** |

In every case, the melt **does not turn corners** — it travels in a perfectly straight line from entry to exit. This simplicity is what makes straight-through dies the **most analytically tractable** die geometry in polymer processing.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metrics (SI units) used throughout this topic:**
> - **Pressure drop across die land (ΔP):** measured in **Pa (N/m²)**, typically 1–15 MPa for polymer melts
> - **Shear rate (γ̇):** 10 – 10,000 s⁻¹
> - **Shear stress (τ):** 10⁴ – 10⁶ Pa
> - **Volumetric throughput (Q):** 10⁻⁶ – 10⁻³ m³/s
> - **Die Land Length-to-Gap ratio (L/h):** typically 10:1 to 30:1
> - **Die swell ratio (B):** 1.00 – 1.60 (dimensionless)

> [!TIP]
> **Why study straight-through dies first?** Because the flow is **unidirectional and fully developed** (after a short entry length), which means we can use closed-form Newtonian solutions (Hagen-Poiseuille, Couette-Poiseuille) and extend them to non-Newtonian power-law fluids. All other dies become *variations* of this baseline.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Straight-Through Dies

Straight-through dies are sub-classified based on the **cross-sectional geometry** of the land (final shaping channel):

1. **Circular / Rod Die** — produces cylindrical rods and filaments (e.g., plastic welding rods, bristles)
2. **Annular / Tube Die** — produces pipes and tubes (e.g., PVC pipes, drinking straws, medical tubing)
3. **Slit / Sheet Die** — produces flat sheets and films (e.g., packaging films, plastic sheets)
4. **Profile Die** — produces non-circular continuous cross-sections (e.g., window gaskets, edge trims)
5. **Coaxial / Multi-Layer Die** — multiple concentric flow channels for coextruded products

All of these share the **axial-flow** characteristic that defines the straight-through family.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Zones of a Straight-Through Die

A straight-through die is not just a single hole — it is a **multi-zone flow channel** designed to (a) distribute the melt evenly, (b) develop the final cross-sectional shape, and (c) stabilize the flow before extrusion. The four functional zones are:

### Zone 1 — Inlet / Approach Channel
- Wide conical or tapered entry region
- Purpose: receives melt from the extruder adapter and feeds it smoothly into the die
- Prevents **dead spots** and **melt stagnation**
- Often includes a **breaker plate** or screen pack just upstream

### Zone 2 — Pre-Land / Converging Section
- The melt is **compressed** radially (or laterally)
- **Velocity increases**, pressure decreases (Bernoulli effect)
- For power-law fluids, the velocity profile **flattens** in this zone

### Zone 3 — Die Land (Parallel Channel)
- **The most important zone** for dimensional accuracy and surface finish
- Length **L**, characteristic gap **h** (or radius **R**)
- Fully-developed laminar flow occurs here
- Provides **back-pressure** to homogenize the melt and align polymer chains

### Zone 4 — Exit / Die Lip
- Final shaped opening
- Where **die swell (extrudate swell)** manifests
- Die lip geometry controls surface roughness and final dimensions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** Whenever a question asks about die design, always discuss **all four zones**, not just the land. Examiners frequently award marks for mentioning the pre-land and exit lip.

---

## 2.2 Governing Physics — Why the Equations Work

The flow in the die land is **steady, laminar, isothermal, incompressible, and fully-developed**. Under these conditions:

- The Navier-Stokes equations reduce to a simple **balance between pressure forces and viscous forces**
- For a **Newtonian fluid** in a circular tube, this gives the classic **Hagen-Poiseuille flow**
- For a **power-law non-Newtonian fluid** (which most polymer melts approximate), the solution is a generalized form of Hagen-Poiseuille

The **power-law model** describes polymer melt viscosity:

$$\mu_{app} = m \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

where:
- **m** = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ)
- **n** = power-law index (dimensionless), typically **0.3 to 0.7** for polymer melts
- **γ̇** = shear rate (s⁻¹)

When **n = 1**, the fluid is Newtonian (μ = m). When **n < 1**, the fluid is **shear-thinning** (pseudoplastic), which is the case for almost all thermoplastics above their melting point.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains every equation you must memorize for the KTU ESE on straight-through dies.** Pay special attention to units and the distinction between circular, annular, and slit geometries.

### For a Circular (Rod) Die — Diameter D = 2R

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volumetric flow rate | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | m³/s | Hagen-Poiseuille (Newtonian) |
| 2 | Mean velocity | $\bar{v} = \dfrac{R^{2} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | m/s | = Q / (πR²) |
| 3 | Wall shear rate (Newtonian) | $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}} = \dfrac{R \Delta P}{2 \mu L}$ | s⁻¹ | Apparent shear rate |
| 4 | Wall shear stress | $\tau_{w} = \dfrac{R \Delta P}{2 L}$ | Pa | Force balance on plug |
| 5 | Pressure drop | $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L \bar{v}}{R^{2}}$ | Pa | Solving (1) for ΔP |
| 6 | Power-law volumetric flow rate | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{3}}{3n+1} \left( \dfrac{R \Delta P}{2 m L} \right)^{1/n}$ | m³/s | Rabinowitsch-corrected |
| 7 | Wall shear rate (power-law) | $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{3n+1}{4n} \cdot \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}$ | s⁻¹ | Includes Rabinowitsch correction |

### For an Annular (Tube) Die — Outer radius R_o, Inner radius R_i, κ = R_i/R_o

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | Newtonian volumetric flow rate | $Q = \dfrac{\pi (R_{o}^{4} - R_{i}^{4}) \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | m³/s | Hagen-Poiseuille annular |
| 9 | Power-law volumetric flow rate | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R_{o}^{3}}{(3n+1)} \left( \dfrac{R_{o} \Delta P}{2 m L} \right)^{1/n} \cdot f(\kappa, n)$ | m³/s | $f(\kappa, n)$ is a geometric correction factor |

### For a Slit (Sheet) Die — Width W, Gap h (W ≫ h)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | Volumetric flow rate | $Q = \dfrac{W h^{3} \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$ | m³/s | Plane Poiseuille (Newtonian) |
| 11 | Wall shear rate (Newtonian) | $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{6 Q}{W h^{2}}$ | s⁻¹ |  |
| 12 | Wall shear stress | $\tau_{w} = \dfrac{h \Delta P}{2 L}$ | Pa |  |
| 13 | Power-law flow rate | $Q = \dfrac{W h^{2}}{2(1/n + 2)} \left( \dfrac{h \Delta P}{2 m L} \right)^{1/n}$ | m³/s |  |

### Die Swell (Extrudate Swell) Equations

| # | Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 14 | Die swell ratio | $B = \dfrac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}$ | Typically 1.0 – 1.6 |
| 15 | Tanner's equation | $B^{2} = \dfrac{2}{(n+1)} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{\dot{\gamma}_{w} L}{R} \right]^{2n/(n+1)} \cdot (\text{constants})$ | For power-law fluids |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Straight-through dies are the **workhorse** of polymer processing. Their analytical simplicity makes them indispensable in:

- **Hot-melt adhesive rods and filaments** — circular dies
- **PVC, HDPE, and PP pipe manufacturing** — annular dies
- **Polyolefin film blowing and casting** — annular (blown film) and slit (cast film) dies
- **Sheet extrusion for thermoforming** — slit dies
- **3D printer filament production** — circular dies (typically 1.75 mm or 2.85 mm)
- **Medical tubing (catheters, IV lines)** — annular dies with tight dimensional tolerances

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering tip from industry:** When designing a die land, the **L/h ratio (or L/D)** is your most powerful design lever. Increasing L/h **increases back-pressure** (better melt homogenization, lower die swell) but **decreases throughput** and **increases residence time** (risk of thermal degradation for heat-sensitive polymers like PVC).

---

## 2.5 The Bagley Correction — Why Real Dies Behave Differently

In real straight-through dies, the **total pressure drop** is the sum of the **pressure drop in the land** and the **pressure drop in the entrance region**:

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{land} + \Delta P_{entrance}$$

Bagley introduced an **end-correction length e** to lump the entrance losses into an effective land length:

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{2 L_{eff}}{R} \tau_{w} \quad \text{where} \quad L_{eff} = L + e$$

For polymer melts, **e/R can be 1 – 8**, depending on contraction ratio and polymer elasticity. This is why die design textbooks always emphasize that **die land length alone does not determine pressure drop** — entrance effects can dominate for short lands.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Using only the geometric land length L when calculating ΔP. Always check whether the question specifies a Bagley correction or asks for *apparent* vs *true* shear rate. The KTU examiner will specifically test this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hagen-Poiseuille Flow for a Circular Straight-Through Die

We start with the **cylindrical Navier-Stokes equation** in the z-direction, assuming steady, fully-developed, laminar, isothermal flow of an incompressible Newtonian fluid:

$$0 = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left[ \dfrac{1}{r} \dfrac{\partial}{\partial r}\left( r \dfrac{\partial v_{z}}{\partial r} \right) \right]$$

**Step 1 — Identify the pressure gradient.**

Because the flow is fully developed, $\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z}$ is a constant. We define:

$$\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial z} = \text{constant}$$

where ΔP is the pressure drop over the die land of length L.

**Step 2 — Substitute and rearrange.**

$$\dfrac{1}{r} \dfrac{d}{dr}\left( r \dfrac{d v_{z}}{dr} \right) = \dfrac{1}{\mu} \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate once with respect to r.**

$$\dfrac{d}{dr}\left( r \dfrac{d v_{z}}{dr} \right) = \dfrac{1}{\mu} \dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot r$$

$$r \dfrac{d v_{z}}{dr} = \dfrac{1}{\mu} \dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot \dfrac{r^{2}}{2} + C_{1}$$

$$\dfrac{d v_{z}}{dr} = \dfrac{1}{2\mu} \dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot r + \dfrac{C_{1}}{r}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the symmetry boundary condition.**

At the centerline r = 0, the velocity gradient must be zero (no-slip symmetry):

$$\dfrac{d v_{z}}{dr}\bigg|_{r=0} = 0 \implies C_{1} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Integrate a second time.**

$$v_{z}(r) = \dfrac{1}{4\mu} \dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot r^{2} + C_{2}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the no-slip boundary condition at the wall.**

At r = R (die wall), the velocity is zero:

$$v_{z}(R) = 0 \implies C_{2} = -\dfrac{1}{4\mu} \dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot R^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Final velocity profile.**

$$\boxed{v_{z}(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right)}$$

This is the classic **parabolic velocity profile** of Hagen-Poiseuille flow.

**Step 8 — Compute the volumetric flow rate by integrating across the cross-section.**

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} v_{z}(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \int_{0}^{R} r(R^{2} - r^{2})\,dr$$

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[ \dfrac{R^{2} r^{2}}{2} - \dfrac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R} = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \dfrac{R^{4}}{4}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}}$$

**Step 9 — Compute the wall shear stress.**

Differentiating the velocity profile and applying Newton's law of viscosity at r = R:

$$\tau_{w} = -\mu \dfrac{d v_{z}}{dr}\bigg|_{r=R} = -\mu \cdot \left( -\dfrac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot R \right) = \dfrac{R \Delta P}{2 L}$$

**Step 10 — Compute the wall shear rate.**

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{\tau_{w}}{\mu} = \dfrac{R \Delta P}{2 \mu L}$$

**Step 11 — Mean velocity.**

$$\bar{v} = \dfrac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} = \dfrac{R^{2} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

Notice that $\bar{v} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{2}$ — the mean velocity is exactly half the centerline (maximum) velocity in Hagen-Poiseuille flow. **Memorize this ratio.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pressure Drop for a Power-Law Fluid in a Circular Die

For a power-law fluid, the viscosity varies with shear rate:

$$\tau = m \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

For the fully-developed axial flow in a circular die, the shear stress distribution (from a force balance on a cylindrical plug of radius r) is:

$$\tau_{rz}(r) = \dfrac{r \Delta P}{2 L}$$

The shear rate is the magnitude of the velocity gradient:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{rz} = -\dfrac{d v_{z}}{dr}$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$-\dfrac{d v_{z}}{dr} = \left( \dfrac{r \Delta P}{2 m L} \right)^{1/n}$$

Integrating from r to R (using the no-slip condition v_z(R) = 0):

$$\int_{v_{z}(r)}^{0} dv_{z} = -\int_{r}^{R} \left( \dfrac{\Delta P}{2 m L} \right)^{1/n} r^{1/n} dr$$

$$-v_{z}(r) = -\left( \dfrac{\Delta P}{2 m L} \right)^{1/n} \cdot \dfrac{R^{(1/n)+1} - r^{(1/n)+1}}{(1/n)+1}$$

$$v_{z}(r) = \left( \dfrac{\Delta P}{2 m L} \right)^{1/n} \cdot \dfrac{n}{n+1} \left[ R^{(n+1)/n} - r^{(n+1)/n} \right]$$

**Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} v_{z}(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

After performing the integration (using substitution u = r^(1/n)):

$$\boxed{Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{3}}{3n + 1} \left( \dfrac{R \Delta P}{2 m L} \right)^{1/n}}$$

**Solving for ΔP:**

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \dfrac{2 m L}{R} \left[ \dfrac{(3n+1) Q}{\pi R^{3}} \right]^{n}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Wall Shear Rate with Rabinowitsch Correction

The *apparent* wall shear rate (calculated assuming Newtonian flow) is:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{a} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}$$

The *true* wall shear rate for a power-law fluid (from Derivation 2) is:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{3n+1}{4n} \cdot \dot{\gamma}_{a}$$

This factor $\dfrac{3n+1}{4n}$ is called the **Rabinowitsch correction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** When you measure viscosity using a capillary rheometer, you get $\gamma_a$ directly from Q and R. To get the true shear rate, you must apply the Rabinowitsch correction. If you forget this, you will **systematically over-estimate viscosity** for shear-thinning melts.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Pressure Drop in a Slit (Sheet) Die

For a slit die of width W, gap h, and land length L (assuming W ≫ h, so edge effects are neglected):

The Navier-Stokes equation in the flow direction x reduces to:

$$0 = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \mu \dfrac{\partial^{2} v_{x}}{\partial y^{2}}$$

With $\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ (constant):

$$\dfrac{d^{2} v_{x}}{d y^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{\mu} \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$$

**First integration:**

$$\dfrac{d v_{x}}{d y} = \dfrac{1}{\mu} \dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot y + C_{1}$$

**Symmetry BC at y = 0** (centerline of slit): $\dfrac{d v_{x}}{dy}\bigg|_{y=0} = 0 \implies C_{1} = 0$

**Second integration:**

$$v_{x}(y) = \dfrac{1}{2\mu} \dfrac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot y^{2} + C_{2}$$

**No-slip BC at y = h/2:** $v_{x}(h/2) = 0 \implies C_{2} = -\dfrac{h^{2}}{8\mu} \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$

**Velocity profile:**

$$v_{x}(y) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{2\mu L}\left( \dfrac{h^{2}}{4} - y^{2} \right)$$
