<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wire Coating** is a continuous polymer extrusion process in which a molten thermoplastic (or curable thermoset/elastomer) compound is uniformly applied as a concentric insulating or protective layer over a moving metallic conductor (copper, aluminium, or tinned wire) using a **crosshead extrusion die** aligned transverse to the wire travel direction.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, wire coating is classified as a **secondary extrusion / profile-coating operation**, belonging to the family of **continuous polymer processing techniques** (akin to film blowing and pipe extrusion, but distinguished by the presence of an internal solid substrate that acts as the forming mandrel).

The three mandatory functional outcomes of any wire coating line are:
1. **Concentric deposition** of the polymer sheath with eccentricity < 10% for Class-1 insulation.
2. **Adhesion control** between metal and polymer (either bonded or strippable, depending on end-use).
3. **Dimensional stability** of the final insulated wire (outer diameter tolerance typically $\pm \ 0.05 \ \text{mm}$ for thin wires).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a **fountain pen nib writing on a moving page**, but in reverse. The page (the wire) is moving continuously through the machine, and instead of ink, a thick molten polymer "ink" is squeezed in a perfect ring around it. As the wire exits, the ring solidifies into a uniform plastic sleeve — that's wire coating!

A more practical real-world analogy is the **candle-making dipping process**, but far more controlled. The wire is the "wick" and the polymer melt bath is the "wax," except here the polymer is pressure-injected around the wire inside a precision-machined metal die rather than relying on gravity dipping.

Another useful mental picture: imagine covering a steel rod with a tight rubber sleeve. You push the rod through a donut-shaped puddle of molten plastic. The plastic freezes the moment it clears the puddle, locking itself around the rod. Wire coating does exactly this at industrial speeds of **200 to 2000 m/min**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Industrial Values)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Wire diameter ($d_w$) | 0.20 – 4.00 | mm |
| Final insulated diameter ($D_f$) | 0.50 – 25.00 | mm |
| Line speed ($v_L$) | 50 – 2000 | m/min |
| Melt temperature ($T_m$) | 140 – 220 (PVC); 200 – 280 (PE) | °C |
| Coating thickness ($t_c$) | 0.15 – 3.00 | mm |
| Throughput ($Q$) | 5 – 500 | kg/h |

## 1.4 Materials Commonly Used for Wire Coating

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The polymer selection is dictated by the **voltage class**, **temperature rating**, **flexibility requirement**, and **flame-retardancy** specification of the cable.

- **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)** — most widely used; cheap, flame-retardant, easy to colour.
- **Low/Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE/LLDPE)** — used in telephone and data cables.
- **Crosslinked Polyethylene (XLPE)** — for high-voltage power cables (crosslinked post-extrusion).
- **Polyamide (Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6)** — jacketing layer for abrasion resistance.
- **Fluoropolymers (PTFE, FEP, ETFE)** — aerospace, high-temperature, and chemical-resistant wiring.
- **Thermoplastic Elastomers (TPE/TPU)** — flexible cords, USB cables, medical leads.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Classification — The Three Industrial Methods

Wire coating is implemented in industry through three principal methods, each with distinct die geometry and material-flow behaviour:

### 2.1.1 Pressure Coating (T-Head / Crosshead Die Method)
- The polymer melt is forced under pressure into a **crosshead die** oriented at 90° to the wire travel direction.
- The melt flows around a **guide tip** (torpedo/mandrel) and exits as a continuous annular sheath.
- The die land length creates back-pressure, ensuring intimate contact and adhesion.
- **Used for**: thin insulation, tight tolerances, adhesion-type coatings (PVC house wires).

### 2.1.2 Sleeve / Tubing (Collapsing) Method
- Polymer is extruded as a tube **larger than the wire diameter**.
- A vacuum is drawn downstream in a collapsing chamber, sucking the tube onto the wire.
- **Used for**: large cables, jacketing, strippable insulation, when zero adhesion is desired.

### 2.1.3 Electrostatic Coating (Powder Method)
- Fine polymer powder (e.g., PE powder) is fluidised and given an electrostatic charge.
- The grounded wire attracts the powder, which is then melted in a curing oven.
- **Used for**: large HV cables, XLPE insulation.

## 2.2 Why and How — The Governing Physics

> [!NOTE]
> Three simultaneous transport phenomena control the process: (1) **polymer melt flow** through the die, (2) **wire motion** through the die, and (3) **heat transfer** from melt → wire → surroundings. Successful coating requires all three to be in balance.

### Step 1 — Wire Pre-heating
- Cold wire entering the die causes a steep temperature gradient, leading to **shrink marks**, **blow holes**, and **neck-down**.
- Wire is typically pre-heated to **80 – 120 °C** to reduce thermal shock and improve adhesion.

### Step 2 — Melt Conveying and Pressure Generation
- A single-screw extruder (L/D = 20:1 to 30:1) homogenises and pressurises the melt.
- Typical melt pressure at the die entry: **5 – 25 MPa**.

### Step 3 — Crosshead Die Flow
- The melt is split by the wire guide, flows around the mandrel, recombines on the downstream side at the **wire-melt interface**.
- The **land length ($L_{land}$)** typically equals **5 × to 10 ×** the coating thickness.

### Step 4 — Cooling and Sizing
- The coated wire enters a **water trough** (length 6 – 20 m) maintained at **15 – 60 °C**.
- Cooling rate must be controlled to avoid **amorphous orientation** and internal stresses.

### Step 5 — Haul-off and Quality Inspection
- A **dual-wheel capstan** (with caterpillar belts) pulls the wire at constant speed.
- In-line spark testers (≥ 3 kV for thin wires) and laser OD gauges ensure defect detection.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> This is the high-yield formula bank for KTU University Exam problems. Memorise these and their units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \rho \cdot v_L \cdot A_c$ | Mass throughput = density × line speed × annular cross-section | kg/h |
| 2 | $A_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( D_f^2 - d_w^2 \right)$ | Annular area of the polymer sheath | mm² |
| 3 | $t_c = \dfrac{D_f - d_w}{2}$ | Radial coating thickness | mm |
| 4 | $DDR = \dfrac{D_{die}}{D_f}$ | Draw Down Ratio (die exit OD ÷ final OD) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $DDR_{turk} = \dfrac{D_{die}^2 - d_{tip}^2}{D_f^2 - d_w^2}$ | Turk's draw-down ratio accounting for mandrel | dimensionless |
| 6 | $v_{melt} = v_L \cdot DDR$ | Average melt velocity at die exit | m/min |
| 7 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{6 Q}{W \cdot H^2}$ | Shear rate in a rectangular die land (slit approximation) | s⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \ \mu \ Q \ L_{land}}{W \cdot H^3}$ | Pressure drop across die land (Poiseuille flow) | Pa |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{D_f - d_w}{D_f} \times 100$ | Eccentricity / insulation thickness uniformity (%) | % |
| 10 | $P_{haul} = \mu_b \cdot F_{normal}$ | Haul-off force balance (friction-based) | N |
| 11 | $Bi = \dfrac{h \cdot D_f}{2 k_p}$ | Biot number for cooling (h = HTC, $k_p$ = polymer conductivity) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{\rho_p c_p t_c (T_m - T_{water})}{h (T_{surface} - T_{water})}$ | Approximate cooling time | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRTICAL NOTATION NOTE** — In the KTU Formula Sheet above, the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ (absolute value) has been deliberately avoided inside markdown tables. For absolute-value expressions, write it as $\lvert x \rvert$ in your answer scripts to prevent table-formatting breakage during PDF generation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Wire coating is the **backbone of the global electrical infrastructure**. The process is used in:
- **House wiring & building cables** (PVC insulated, 1.5 – 6 mm² copper).
- **Automotive harnesses** (thin-wall PVC, crosslinked PE).
- **Underwater and submarine HV cables** (XLPE with metallic armoring).
- **Data and telecom cables** (foamed PE dielectric for low signal loss).
- **Aerospace & defence wiring** (PTFE, ETFE for high-temperature tolerance).
- **Medical device leads** (TPE / silicone-coated for biocompatibility).

> [!NOTE]
> The global wire and cable market exceeds **USD 200 billion annually**, with PVC and XLPE together accounting for over **70%** of polymeric insulation volume.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Throughput Equation (Mass Balance)

### Problem Statement
Derive the relation between extruder throughput, line speed, and final insulation dimensions for a wire-coating line.

### Given
- Final insulated wire outer diameter: $D_f$
- Bare wire diameter: $d_w$
- Line (haul-off) speed: $v_L$
- Polymer melt density at processing temperature: $\rho$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** In steady-state, the volumetric flow of polymer exiting the die must equal the annular volume of coating produced per unit time (conservation of mass, incompressible melt).

$$Q_{vol} = v_L \cdot A_c$$

where $A_c$ is the cross-sectional area of the polymer sheath.

**Step 2:** The annular cross-section is the area of the outer circle minus the area of the wire (the wire displaces the polymer at its exact diameter):

$$A_c = \frac{\pi D_f^2}{4} - \frac{\pi d_w^2}{4} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_f^2 - d_w^2 \right)$$

**Step 3:** Substituting back:

$$Q_{vol} = v_L \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_f^2 - d_w^2 \right)$$

**Step 4:** To convert volumetric flow to mass flow, multiply by density $\rho$:

$$Q_{mass} = \rho \cdot Q_{vol} = \rho \cdot v_L \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_f^2 - d_w^2 \right)$$

This is the **master throughput equation** for wire coating.

### Numerical Worked Example

**Given:** $D_f = 2.50 \ \text{mm}$, $d_w = 1.00 \ \text{mm}$, $v_L = 500 \ \text{m/min}$, $\rho = 1100 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ (PVC at 180 °C).

**Compute annular area:**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( 2.50^2 - 1.00^2 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( 6.25 - 1.00 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 5.25 = 4.124 \ \text{mm}^2$$

**Convert to SI:**

$$A_c = 4.124 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$v_L = \frac{500}{60} = 8.333 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Mass flow:**

$$Q_{mass} = 1100 \times 8.333 \times 4.124 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0378 \ \text{kg/s} = 136.0 \ \text{kg/h}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Draw Down Ratio (Turk's Method)

### Step 1
Define the geometric Draw Down Ratio (DDR) as the ratio of the die exit outer diameter to the final coated wire diameter:

$$DDR = \frac{D_{die}}{D_f}$$

**Step 2 — Turk's Correction (KTU High-Yield):**
The simple DDR ignores the mandrel (guide tip) diameter. Turk introduced a corrected ratio that includes the tip:

$$DDR_{Turk} = \frac{D_{die}^2 - d_{tip}^2}{D_f^2 - d_w^2}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Significance:**
- If $DDR_{Turk} > 1.0$: polymer is being **stretched** (neck-down possible).
- If $DDR_{Turk} = 1.0$: no stretching; equilibrium flow.
- If $DDR_{Turk} < 1.0$: polymer is being **accumulated** (die swell dominant, swelling visible at die exit).
- Practical range: $DDR_{Turk} = 1.0$ to $1.5$.

### Numerical Example
Given: $D_{die} = 6.0 \ \text{mm}$, $d_{tip} = 2.0 \ \text{mm}$, $D_f = 2.5 \ \text{mm}$, $d_w = 1.0 \ \text{mm}$.

$$DDR_{Turk} = \frac{6.0^2 - 2.0^2}{2.5^2 - 1.0^2} = \frac{36 - 4}{6.25 - 1.00} = \frac{32}{5.25} = 6.095$$

> [!WARNING]
> A DDR of 6.095 is **excessively high** and will cause excessive draw-down, surface fibrillation, and residual orientation stresses. Reduce $D_{die}$ or increase the wire diameter to bring DDR into the 1.0 – 1.5 range.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Drop in the Die Land (Poiseuille Flow Model)

### Step 1
Treat the die land as a thin rectangular slit of width $W$ (circumference of the die) and gap $H$ (radial die land clearance).

$$W \approx \pi \cdot D_{mean} = \pi \left( D_{die} + d_{tip} \right) / 2$$

**Step 2:** For fully developed, isothermal, laminar flow of a Newtonian melt (zero-order approximation; suitable for KTU numerical problems), the Hagen-Poiseuille equation applies:

$$\Delta P = \frac{12 \ \mu \ Q \ L_{land}}{W \cdot H^3}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example:**
Given: $\mu = 500 \ \text{Pa.s}$ (PVC melt), $Q = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $L_{land} = 0.020 \ \text{m}$, $W = 0.025 \ \text{m}$, $H = 0.001 \ \text{m}$.

$$\Delta P = \frac{12 \times 500 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.020}{0.025 \times (0.001)^3}$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-11}} = 4.8 \times 10^{7} \ \text{Pa} = 48 \ \text{MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, this 48 MPa far exceeds typical die-pressure ratings (15 MPa). Either reduce $Q$, increase $H$, or shorten $L_{land}$. This illustrates why die designers iteratively balance these four variables.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cooling Time Estimate

### Step 1
Heat balance on the coating (assuming lumped capacitance and 1-D radial conduction from outside to inside):

$$q = h \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s - T_{water})$$

**Step 2:** Heat to be removed to solidify the polymer (sensible + latent, for semi-crystalline materials):

$$q = \rho_p \cdot V \cdot [c_p (T_m - T_{water}) + \Delta H_f]$$

**Step 3:** Equating and simplifying for the cooling time:

$$t_{cool} = \frac{\rho_p \ c_p \ t_c \ (T_m - T_{water})}{h \ (T_{s,avg} - T_{water})}$$

### Numerical Example
PVC coating: $\rho_p = 1400 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1500 \ \text{J/kg.K}$, $t_c = 0.75 \ \text{mm} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}$, $T_m = 180 \ °C$, $T_{water} = 25 \ °C$, $h = 300 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{.K}$, $T_{s,avg} = 110 \ °C$.

$$t_{cool} = \frac{1400 \times 1500 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \times (180 - 25)}{300 \times (110 - 25)}$$

$$t_{cool} = \frac{1400 \times 1500 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 155}{300 \times 85}$$

$$t_{cool} = \frac{243.9}{25500} = 9.56 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{s} \approx 10 \ \text{ms}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This calculation is for the **surface-to-water** conduction only. Total trough length is determined by integrating cooling rate along the wire travel — typically requiring 6 – 20 m trough length depending on line speed.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Defect → Cause → Remedy

> [!TIP]
> High-yield table for KTU viva and short-answer questions. Memorise the **mechanism**, not just the symptom.

| Defect | Primary Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| Eccentric coating (off-centre) | Misaligned guide tip, worn die | Realign torpedo, replace die |
| Surface roughness / melt fracture | Excessive shear rate ($\dot{\gamma} > 500 \ \text{s}^{-1}$) | Increase melt temperature, reduce line speed |
| Blow holes / voids | Moisture in polymer, volatile additives | Pre-dry polymer (e.g., 80 °C, 4 h for PA) |
| Neck-down / thinning | DDR too high | Increase die land, reduce haul-off speed |
| Poor adhesion | Wire surface contamination, cold wire | Clean & pre-heat wire to > 80 °C |
| Die-lip buildup (drool) | Insufficient land length, low viscosity | Polish die lips, add land length |
| Sharkskin | Wall slip at high shear | Add process-aid, lower throughput |
| Sink marks on insulation | Non-uniform cooling | Adjust water trough temperature gradient |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Line Architecture — Wire Coating Extrusion Line

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **functional block-level architecture** of a complete wire-coating line, from pay-off reel to take-up spool. It is rendered in Mermaid so it can be regenerated in any compatible viewer (GitHub, VS Code preview, Obsidian, etc.).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Pay-off Reel] --> A2[Wire Tension Control]
    A2 --> A3[Wire Pre-heater]
    A3 --> A4[Crosshead Die with Extruder]
    A4 --> A5[Primary Cooling Trough]
    A5 --> A6[Diameter Gauge Spark Tester]
    A6 --> A7[Dual Capstan Haul-off]
    A7 --> A8[Secondary Cooling Trough]
    A8 --> A9[Spark Tester HV]
    A9 --> A10[Take-up Spool]
    A4 --- E1[Hopper with Drying Unit]
    E1 --- E2[Screw Extruder L over D 25 to 1]
    E2 --- E3[Melt Pump and Filter]
    E3 --- A4
```

## 4.2 Crosshead Die — Internal Melt Flow Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block diagram shows the **internal melt path** inside a pressure-type crosshead die. The wire enters axially and the melt enters at 90° — this is the defining geometric feature of "crosshead" dies.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[Inlet Polymer Melt from Extruder 90 deg entry] --> B2[Distributor Manifold Channel]
    B2 --> B3[Conical Guide Tip Torpedo]
    B3 --> B4[Wire Inlet along axis]
    B4 --> B5[Melt flows around guide tip]
    B5 --> B6[Recombination point wire meets melt]
    B6 --> B7[Die Land annular channel]
    B7 --> B8[Die Exit coated wire]
    B8 --> B9[Cooling Trough]
    B2 -.annular flow.-> B7
```

## 4.3 Tubing (Sleeve) Coating Die — Collapsing Method Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Wire Inlet straight pass] --> C2[Crosshead Die extrudes larger tube]
    C2 --> C3[Air Gap sizing zone]
    C3 --> C4[Vacuum Sizing Chamber collapses tube onto wire]
    C4 --> C5[Collapsed Coating shrinks onto wire]
    C5 --> C6[Water Cooling Trough]
    C6 --> C7[Capstan Pull]
    C7 --> C8[Take-up Spool]
```

## 4.4 Control and Instrumentation Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> A modern wire-coating line has **closed-loop control** on line speed, melt temperature, melt pressure, and outer diameter. The block diagram below shows the feedback architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1[Setpoint: Line Speed Diameter] --> D2[PLC Controller]
    D2 --> D3[Capstan Drive VFD]
    D3 --> D4[Wire Motion]
    D4 --> D5[Laser OD Gauge Sensor]
    D5 --> D6[Feedback Signal]
    D6 --> D2
    D2 --> D7[Heater Bands]
    D7 --> D8[Melt Temperature]
    D8 --> D9[Thermocouple]
    D9 --> D6
```

## 4.5 Defect-Detection Subsystem — Spark Tester Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Insulated Wire Passes Through Beaded Chain Electrode] --> F2[High Voltage AC 3 to 30 kV]
    F2 --> F3{Pin-hole or Thin Spot present}
    F3 -- Yes --> F4[Spark Discharge Current Spike]
    F4 --> F5[Alarm and Mark Reject]
    F3 -- No --> F6[Normal Pass-through]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!TIP]
> These questions are calibrated to the **revised Bloom's Taxonomy** cognitive levels used in the KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE). Marks indicated in brackets correspond to ESE 2024 Scheme weightage.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PCPOT503]**
*Compare the pressure-coating method and the tubing (sleeve) method of wire coating with respect to die geometry, adhesion, and typical applications.* (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pressure Coating | Tubing / Sleeve |
|---|---|---|
| Die geometry | Crosshead, tight land | Crosshead, larger OD, vacuum |
| Polymer-wire contact | Forced pressure contact | Vacuum-induced collapse |
| Adhesion to wire | High (bonded) | Low (strippable) |
| Insulation thickness | Tight tolerance | Slightly looser |
| Typical applications | House wiring (PVC), signal cables | Cable jacketing, HV XLPE |

> **Marking allocation:** 1 mark for die geometry, 1 for adhesion difference, 1 for application example.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PCPOT503]**
*What is the function of a crosshead die in a wire coating line? Why is the wire pre-heated before entering the die?* (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **crosshead die** orients the polymer melt entry at 90° to the wire travel direction. The melt is split by a guide tip (mandrel), flows around the wire, recombines on the downstream side, and exits as a concentric annular coating. The die land controls back-pressure, thickness, and surface finish.

The wire is pre-heated to **80 – 120 °C** to:
1. **Prevent thermal shock** between the hot melt and cold metal, which causes voids, blow holes, and neck-down.
2. **Improve melt adhesion** by activating the metal surface and reducing viscosity at the wire-melt interface.
3. **Reduce differential shrinkage stresses** during cooling.

> **Marking allocation:** 1.5 marks for die function, 1.5 marks for the three pre-heating reasons.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, Module 2 carries a mandatory **internal choice** of two questions (a) and (b) — both 14 marks. We present TWO full alternative questions so you can practise both.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Describe with a neat schematic the construction and working of a crosshead (T-head) die used for pressure-type wire coating. Explain the role of the guide tip, die land, and die lip.*

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Construction (3 marks):** A crosshead die consists of a die body, an inlet port aligned perpendicular to the wire path, a conical guide tip (torpedo/mandrel), an annular die land of length $L_{land}$, and a die exit lip. The wire is fed axially through the centre of the die.

2. **Working (2 marks):** Molten polymer from the extruder enters the die inlet, flows through a distribution channel around the guide tip, and is forced under pressure around the moving wire. The polymer recombines at the **weld line** (also called the "knit line") on the far side of the guide tip and is drawn out as a concentric sleeve.

3. **Role of components (2 marks):**
   - **Guide tip (mandrel):** splits the melt flow, ensures concentricity, sets the inner diameter of the coating.
   - **Die land:** creates back-pressure, improves melt homogeneity, removes weld-line weakness by forcing polymer re-mixing.
   - **Die lip:** controls final surface finish; sharp lip = glossy surface; recessed lip = matt finish.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*For a PVC wire coating line, the following data is given: bare copper wire diameter = 1.20 mm, final insulated wire diameter = 2.40 mm, line speed = 600 m/min, melt density of PVC at 180 °C = 1080 kg/m³. Calculate: (i) the coating thickness, (ii) the volumetric throughput in cm³/s, and (iii) the mass throughput in kg/h.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $d_w = 1.20 \ \text{mm}$, $D_f = 2.40 \ \text{mm}$, $v_L = 600 \ \text{m/min}$, $\rho = 1080 \ \text{kg/m}^3$.

**(i) Coating thickness:**

$$t_c = \frac{D_f - d_w}{2} = \frac{2.40 - 1.20}{2} = \frac{1.20}{2} = 0.60 \ \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Volumetric throughput:**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_f^2 - d_w^2 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( 2.40^2 - 1.20^2 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( 5.76 - 1.44 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 4.32 = 3.393 \ \text{mm}^2$$

$$Q_{vol} = v_L \times A_c = \frac{600 \ \text{m/min}}{60} \times 3.393 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2 = 10 \ \text{m/s} \times 3.393 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2 = 3.393 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to cm³/s:

$$Q_{vol} = 3.393 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s} \times 10^6 \ \text{cm}^3/\text{m}^3 = 33.93 \ \text{cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**(iii) Mass throughput:**

$$Q_{mass} = \rho \times Q_{vol} = 1080 \ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \times 3.393 \times 10^{-5} \ \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}} = 0.03665 \ \text{kg/s}$$

Convert to kg/h:

$$Q_{mass} = 0.03665 \times 3600 = 131.9 \ \text{kg/h}$$

> **Final Answers:** $t_c = 0.60 \ \text{mm}$, $Q_{vol} = 33.93 \ \text{cm}^3/\text{s}$, $Q_{mass} \approx 132 \ \text{kg/h}$.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Explain the various types of defects encountered in polymer-insulated wires. Discuss their causes and remedies in detail.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Use the analytical matrix from Section 3.5 as the structural backbone of this answer.

1. **Eccentricity (off-centre insulation):** Caused by misaligned guide tip, worn die, or wire wandering in the capstan. *Remedy:* Realign torpedo, replace die, install dancer roller for tension control.

2. **Blow holes / voids:** Moisture in the polymer, or trapped air at the wire-melt interface. *Remedy:* Pre-dry the polymer (80 °C × 4 h for PA), use vented extruder, apply vacuum at die.

3. **Surface roughness / melt fracture:** Shear rate exceeds the critical value (typically $\dot{\gamma} > 500 \ \text{s}^{-1}$ for PVC). *Remedy:* Increase melt temperature, lower line speed, use processing aid.

4. **Neck-down / diameter reduction:** Draw Down Ratio too high; melt is over-stretched. *Remedy:* Increase die land, decrease haul-off speed, raise melt temperature.

5. **Poor adhesion:** Wire surface contamination, low melt temperature, insufficient pre-heat. *Remedy:* Clean wire (solvent wipe), pre-heat to > 80 °C, raise melt temperature.

6. **Sharkskin / stick-slip:** Wall slip at high shear stress; common in LLDPE. *Remedy:* Add fluoroelastomer process aid, lower throughput.

7. **Sink marks on insulation:** Non-uniform cooling or excessive post-extrusion crystallisation. *Remedy:* Gradual cooling trough (40 °C → 25 °C), reduce cooling rate.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*A wire coating line uses polyethylene of melt density 760 kg/m³ at 200 °C. The bare aluminium wire diameter is 0.80 mm and the final insulated diameter is 1.60 mm. The line speed is 800 m/min. If the DDR (Turk's method) is 1.20 and the guide tip diameter is 1.10 mm, calculate: (i) the die exit diameter, (ii) the mass throughput in kg/h, and (iii) the average melt velocity at the die exit.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $d_w = 0.80 \ \text{mm}$, $D_f = 1.60 \ \text{mm}$, $v_L = 800 \ \text{m/min}$, $\rho = 760 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, $DDR_{Turk} = 1.20$, $d_{tip} = 1.10 \ \text{mm}$.

**(i) Die exit diameter ($D_{die}$):**

$$DDR_{Turk} = \frac{D_{die}^2 - d_{tip}^2}{D_f^2 - d_w^2}$$

$$1.20 = \frac{D_{die}^2 - 1.10^2}{1.60^2 - 0.80^2} = \frac{D_{die}^2 - 1.21}{2.56 - 0.64} = \frac{D_{die}^2 - 1.21}{1.92}$$

$$D_{die}^2 - 1.21 = 1.20 \times 1.92 = 2.304$$

$$D_{die}^2 = 2.304 + 1.21 = 3.514$$

$$D_{die} = \sqrt{3.514} = 1.875 \ \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Mass throughput:**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_f^2 - d_w^2 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 1.92 = 1.508 \ \text{mm}^2 = 1.508 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$v_L = \frac{800}{60} = 13.33 \ \text{m/s}$$

$$Q_{vol} = 13.33 \times 1.508 \times 10^{-6} = 2.011 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_{mass} = 760 \times 2.011 \times 10^{-5} = 0.01528 \ \text{kg/s} = 55.0 \ \text{kg/h}$$

**(iii) Average melt velocity at die exit:**

$$v_{melt} = v_L \times DDR_{Turk} = 13.33 \times 1.20 = 16.0 \ \text{m/s}$$

> **Final Answers:** $D_{die} = 1.875 \ \text{mm}$, $Q_{mass} = 55.0 \ \text{kg/h}$, $v_{melt} = 16.0 \ \text{m/s}$.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **1. Unit conversion trap (most common):** Students often forget to convert mm² to m² when computing volumetric flow. Always convert **before** multiplying. The examiner deducts **1 – 2 marks** for unit inconsistency even if the final answer is numerically correct.
>
> **2. DDR confusion:** Some students use the simple DDR $\frac{D_{die}}{D_f}$ instead of Turk's corrected form. If the question explicitly states "Turk's method" or provides $d_{tip}$, you **must** use the corrected formula. Penalty: **2 marks**.
>
> **3. Density-temperature distinction:** Wire coating exam problems sometimes provide density at room temperature. If not stated as "melt density," use room-temperature density (e.g., $\rho_{PVC} = 1400 \ \text{kg/m}^3$). Always read the **state of the material** in the problem.
>
> **4. Knock-out marking in PART B:** If your schematic is missing, expect a flat deduction of **2 – 3 marks** in part (a) of either Question A or B. Always draw a labelled crosshead die with all four key parts: body, inlet, guide tip, die land, die lip.
>
> **5. Eccentricity vs neck-down:** These are NOT the same defect. Eccentricity = non-uniform thickness around the circumference. Neck-down = uniform thinning along the wire length. Examiners test this distinction.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision checklist — read this twice before the exam.**

- ✅ Wire coating is a **continuous secondary extrusion process** using a **crosshead die**.
- ✅ Three industrial methods: **Pressure (T-head), Tubing (Sleeve/Collapsing), and Electrostatic** powder coating.
- ✅ The **guide tip (mandrel/torpedo)** splits the melt and sets the inner diameter of the coating.
- ✅ The **die land** creates back-pressure; typical length = **5 × to 10 × coating thickness**.
- ✅ Master throughput equation: $Q = \rho \ v_L \ \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_f^2 - d_w^2 \right)$.
- ✅ Coating thickness: $t_c = \frac{D_f - d_w}{2}$.
- ✅ **Turk's DDR**: $DDR_{Turk} = \frac{D_{die}^2 - d_{tip}^2}{D_f^2 - d_w^2}$ — preferred over simple DDR.
- ✅ **Pre-heating the wire** (80 – 120 °C) prevents blow holes and improves adhesion.
- ✅ **Cooling** is done in a water trough (15 – 60 °C), trough length 6 – 20 m.
- ✅ **Haul-off** uses dual-wheel capstan with caterpillar belts for slip-free pulling.
- ✅ **Quality control**: in-line laser OD gauge + spark tester (≥ 3 kV for thin wires).
- ✅ **Common defects**: eccentricity, blow holes, melt fracture, neck-down, poor adhesion, sharkskin, sink marks.
- ✅ **Common materials**: PVC (most), PE, XLPE, Nylon, PTFE, FEP, TPE/TPU.
- ✅ **Typical line speeds**: 50 – 2000 m/min; **typical extruder L/D**: 20:1 to 30:1.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme hot topics**: crosshead die schematic, throughput calculation, DDR (Turk's), defect-cause-remedy, comparison of pressure vs. tubing method.
- ✅ **Always convert units first**, label every numerical answer with units, and use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ notation (not pipes) for absolute values in any table.

---

*End of Module 2 – Wire Coating | PCPOT503 | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Dies for Tubular Film
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dies for Tubular Film

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tubular Film Die:** A precision-engineered annular extrusion tooling component used in *blown film extrusion* lines to convert a converging polymer melt stream into a uniform, concentric molten tube (parison), which is subsequently inflated, drawn, cooled, and collapsed into a flat lay-flat film.

The die forms the **heart** of the blown film process. It takes the rotating, swirling, often non-homogeneous melt arriving from the extruder screw and re-distributes it into a **perfectly axisymmetric, velocity-uniform annular flow**. The molten tube emerges vertically upward, is inflated by internal air, cooled by external air rings, and solidified at the **frost line** before being flattened by nip rolls.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A die for tubular film is essentially a *flow-redistribution device* — its design quality determines film gauge uniformity (tolerance ±2–5%), optical clarity, and mechanical isotropy. Any non-uniform velocity at the die lip is "frozen" into the final film as thickness variation.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Think of the die as a **pastry bag with a ring-shaped nozzle** used by a baker. The baker squeezes dough (polymer melt) through a star-shaped or smooth opening, and air blown into the centre puffs it up like a balloon. If the dough comes out faster on one side than the other, the balloon becomes lopsided — exactly what happens with a poor tubular film die. Good die design is like a well-engineered, perfectly balanced pastry tip that delivers the dough at the same speed all the way around the circle.

**Key physical constants and standard metrics in practice:**

- **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR):** typically **1.5 – 4.0**
- **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR):** typically **5 – 30**
- **Die Diameter:** **50 – 1500 mm** (commercial range)
- **Die Lip Gap (h):** **0.5 – 2.5 mm**
- **Land Length (L):** **5 × h to 15 × h**
- **Frost Line Height (FLH):** **3 – 8 × D<sub>d</sub>**
- **Operating Pressure Drop:** **5 – 25 MPa**
- **Shear Rate at Die Lip:** **50 – 500 s⁻¹**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Melt Conveyance** — The extruder screw delivers a swirling, helical melt flow into the die inlet.
2. **Flow Redistribution** — Internals (spider legs or spiral mandrel) convert this rotating flow into **purely axial, uniform annular flow**.
3. **Converging Section** — The melt is compressed radially inward (or outward) to form a thin, concentric annulus.
4. **Land (Parallel Section)** — A short, straight cylindrical channel of length *L* and gap *h* imparts final velocity profile uniformity and adds back-pressure (important for shear uniformity).
5. **Die Lip Exit** — Melt exits at velocity *V<sub>o</sub>*. Due to viscoelasticity, it swells (extrudate swell) by a factor *B*.
6. **Inflation & Drawing** — Internal air bubble inflates the tube to BUR, while take-up rolls stretch it axially (DDR).
7. **Cooling & Solidification** — External air ring quenches the bubble; frost line marks solidification.
8. **Collapsing & Winding** — Nip rolls flatten the tube into lay-flat film and wind it.

### 2.2 Types of Tubular Film Dies

| Die Type | Construction | Advantages | Disadvantages | Typical Use |
|----------|--------------|------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Spider (Mandrel) Die** | Central mandrel held by 3–8 radial legs ("spider arms") | Simple, cheap, easy to clean | Weld lines (one per leg), stagnation points | LDPE, general purpose |
| **Spiral (Spiderless) Die** | Helical grooves on a conical mandrel distribute melt | No weld lines, excellent gauge uniformity | Complex machining, hard to purge | LLDPE, mLLDPE, HDPE, barrier films |
| **Side-Fed Die** | Melt enters tangentially on the side | Self-cleaning, low residence time | Asymmetric flow, gauge variation | PVC, cross-linked PE |
| **Center-Fed Die** | Melt enters axially from below, divides around a torpedo | Symmetric flow, good for low pressure | Limited die sizes | Small-scale, lab extruders |

> [!NOTE]
> **Spider legs create "weld lines" (also called "mandrel lines" or "leg lines")** — these are cosmetic and mechanical weak spots. Spiral dies eliminate this by feeding the melt in a continuous helical stream.

### 2.3 Critical Design Parameters

- **Lip Land Length (L):** Sufficient to remove transverse velocity non-uniformity, but not so long as to cause melt degradation or excessive pressure drop. Rule of thumb: **L/h = 5 to 15**.
- **Lip Angle (α):** Typically **15°–45°**. Sharper angles reduce stagnation but increase stress.
- **Converging Angle:** Cone half-angle of **15°–30°** is standard.
- **Die Gap (h):** Small gap → higher shear → better mixing but higher pressure drop and melt fracture risk.
- **Surface Finish:** Lip surfaces polished to **Ra < 0.2 μm** to prevent hang-up and streaks.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Flow rate (Newtonian, thin-gap annular die) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D_d h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$ | m³/s | Thin-gap (h ≪ D) approximation |
| 2 | Flow rate (exact annular, Newtonian) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{8 \mu L} \left[ R_o^4 - R_i^4 - \dfrac{(R_o^2 - R_i^2)^2}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} \right]$ | m³/s | Full solution of annular Poiseuille |
| 3 | Wall Shear Rate | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{6 Q}{\pi D_d h^2}$ | s⁻¹ | For thin annular slit |
| 4 | Wall Shear Stress | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot h}{2 L}$ | Pa | Thin-gap assumption |
| 5 | Mean Exit Velocity | $V_o = \dfrac{Q}{\pi D_d h}$ | m/s | Through annular cross-section |
| 6 | Blow-Up Ratio (BUR) | $\mathrm{BUR} = \dfrac{D_b}{D_d}$ | — | D<sub>b</sub> = bubble diameter |
| 7 | Draw-Down Ratio (DDR) | $\mathrm{DDR} = \dfrac{V_{tu}}{V_o}$ | — | V<sub>tu</sub> = take-up velocity |
| 8 | Final Film Thickness | $t_f = \dfrac{h}{\mathrm{BUR} \times \mathrm{DDR}}$ | m | Mass-conservation result |
| 9 | Die Swell (Tanner, Newtonian) | $B \approx \left( 2 \dot{\gamma}_w \tau_w L / D_d \right)^{1/6}$ | — | Approximate; for power-law, more complex |
| 10 | Power-Law Pressure Drop | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 L}{h} \left( \dfrac{Q \cdot 2m+1}{\pi D_d h m} \right)^n \cdot \dfrac{1}{m K}$ | Pa | K, n from power-law model τ = K γ̇ⁿ |
| 11 | Frost Line Height (empirical) | $\mathrm{FLH} \approx (3 \text{ to } 8) D_d$ | m | Correlates with cooling rate |
| 12 | Specific Output (productivity) | $q = \dfrac{\rho \pi D_d h V_o}{A_{film}}$ | kg/h·m | Per unit film width |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipe `|` is used inside the table cells above** — absolute value is rendered as plain text or implied by context to preserve the markdown table syntax. Use `\vert` only inside LaTeX math blocks, never inside the table delimiter.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Tubular film dies are used industrially for producing:
- **Food packaging films** (LDPE, LLDPE)
- **Stretch films and shrink films** (mLLDPE, EVA)
- **Agricultural mulch films** (LDPE, LLDPE)
- **Barrier films** (multi-layer via coextrusion dies)
- **Medical sterilization films** (Tyvek-type)
- **Geomembranes and construction films** (HDPE)

A well-designed die directly improves **production tonnage**, **scrap rate**, and **film optical properties (gloss, haze)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Flow Through a Thin Annular Die (Newtonian Melt)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation treats the thin annular gap between the mandrel and die body as a **slit of width πD<sub>d</sub>** and gap *h*. This is the standard engineering approximation because *h* ≪ *D<sub>d</sub>* in most commercial dies.

**Starting point — Navier–Stokes for steady, incompressible, fully-developed flow in the z-direction:**

$$\frac{dp}{dz} = \mu \frac{d^2 v_z}{dr^2}$$

**Step 1:** Integrate once with respect to r:

$$\frac{dp}{dz} = \mu \frac{d^2 v_z}{dr^2} \Rightarrow \frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dz} r + C_1$$

**Step 2:** Integrate again:

$$v_z(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dp}{dz} r^2 + C_1 r + C_2$$

**Step 3:** Apply no-slip boundary conditions on both walls (mandrel radius R<sub>i</sub>, outer radius R<sub>o</sub>):

At $r = R_i$: $v_z = 0$ and at $r = R_o$: $v_z = 0$.

**Step 4:** Solving the simultaneous equations for C₁ and C₂ (detailed algebra omitted for brevity but standard in any polymer processing textbook) yields the parabolic profile:

$$v_z(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu}\left(-\frac{\Delta P}{L}\right) \left[ r^2 - R_i^2 - \left(\frac{R_o^2 - R_i^2}{\ln(R_o/R_i)}\right)\ln(r/R_i) \right]$$

**Step 5:** Integrate across the annulus to obtain volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

**Step 6:** Performing the integration produces the **exact annular Hagen–Poiseuille result:**

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{8 \mu L} \left[ R_o^4 - R_i^4 - \frac{(R_o^2 - R_i^2)^2}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} \right]$$

**Step 7:** Now apply the *thin-gap* approximation: let $D_d = R_o + R_i \approx 2R$ and $h = R_o - R_i \ll D_d$. Using a Taylor expansion of the bracketed term for small $h/D_d$:

$$\left[ R_o^4 - R_i^4 - \frac{(R_o^2 - R_i^2)^2}{\ln(R_o/R_i)} \right] \approx \frac{D_d \, h^3}{6}$$

**Step 8:** Substituting back gives the **practical thin-gap formula** (the most-used in KTU problems):

$$Q = \frac{\pi D_d h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$$

**Step 9:** Wall shear rate from the velocity gradient at $r = R_o$:

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = -\left.\frac{dv_z}{dr}\right|_{r=R_o} = \frac{6Q}{\pi D_d h^2}$$

**Step 10:** Wall shear stress from force balance on a die element:

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \cdot h}{2L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For power-law fluids** (τ = K γ̇ⁿ), repeat the same procedure substituting a non-Newtonian viscosity. The result is the **generalized Hagen–Poiseuille equation**, used in Problem 3 below.

### 3.2 Derivation: Film Thickness from Mass Conservation

At the die exit, the cross-sectional area of the melt annulus is $A_d = \pi D_d h$. After inflation (BUR) and drawing (DDR), the bubble cross-section becomes a flattened tube of thickness $t_f$ and width $w_f = \pi D_b / 2$ (half-circumference).

Mass conservation per unit time:

$$\rho Q = \rho \, A_d \, V_o = \rho \, t_f \, w_f \, V_{tu}$$

Substituting $D_b = \mathrm{BUR} \cdot D_d$ and $V_{tu} = \mathrm{DDR} \cdot V_o$:

$$\pi D_d h \, V_o = t_f \cdot \pi D_b \, V_{tu} = t_f \cdot \pi \, \mathrm{BUR} \cdot D_d \cdot \mathrm{DDR} \cdot V_o$$

Cancelling common terms and solving for $t_f$:

$$t_f = \frac{h}{\mathrm{BUR} \times \mathrm{DDR}}$$

This is the single most important design relation for setting up a blown film line.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem Style)

**Problem:** A tubular film die has D<sub>d</sub> = 200 mm, land length L = 25 mm, lip gap h = 1.2 mm. Operating at ΔP = 8 MPa on a polymer with apparent viscosity μ = 1500 Pa·s.

**Required:** (a) Volumetric flow rate; (b) Wall shear rate; (c) Exit velocity; (d) Final film thickness for BUR = 2.5 and DDR = 12.

**Solution (a):**

$$Q = \frac{\pi (0.200)(0.0012)^3 (8 \times 10^6)}{12 \times 1500 \times 0.025}$$

Computing numerator and denominator separately:

$$\text{Numerator} = \pi \times 0.200 \times 1.728 \times 10^{-9} \times 8 \times 10^6 = 8.68 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 18000 \times 0.025 = 450$$

$$Q = \frac{8.68 \times 10^{-3}}{450} = 1.928 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 69.4 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{6 \times 1.928 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times 0.200 \times (0.0012)^2} = \frac{1.157 \times 10^{-4}}{9.05 \times 10^{-7}} = 127.8 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Solution (c):**

$$V_o = \frac{Q}{\pi D_d h} = \frac{1.928 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times 0.200 \times 0.0012} = 0.0256 \text{ m/s} = 2.56 \text{ cm/s}$$

**Solution (d):**

$$t_f = \frac{1.2 \text{ mm}}{2.5 \times 12} = \frac{1.2}{30} = 0.040 \text{ mm} = 40 \text{ μm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> All steps shown above use the **standard KTU valuation key pattern** — full equation, substituted values, and final boxed/marked answer. Students who skip the unit conversions lose 1–2 marks per sub-part.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Blown Film Line — Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Extruder Screw] --> B[Screen Pack and Breaker Plate]
    B --> C[Adapter and Inlet Pipe]
    C --> D[Tubular Film Die]
    D --> D1[Spider Legs or Spiral Distributor]
    D1 --> D2[Converging Section]
    D2 --> D3[Land and Lip]
    D3 --> E[Molten Bubble Parison]
    E --> F[Internal Air for Inflation]
    E --> G[External Air Ring Cooling]
    G --> H[Frost Line Solidification]
    H --> I[Collapsing Frame and Nip Rolls]
    I --> J[Lay Flat Film]
    J --> K[Haul Off and Winder]

    step1[Step 1 Conveyance] -.-> A
    step2[Step 2 Redistribution] -.-> D1
    step3[Step 3 Shaping] -.-> D3
    step4[Step 4 Inflation] -.-> F
    step5[Step 5 Quench] -.-> G
    step6[Step 6 Solidify] -.-> H
    step7[Step 7 Collapse] -.-> I
    step8[Step 8 Wind] -.-> K
```

### 4.2 Die Types — Functional Comparison (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TYPE1[Spider Mandrel Die]
        SP1[Melt Inlet] --> SP2[Central Mandrel]
        SP2 --> SP3[Spider Arms 3 to 8]
        SP3 --> SP4[Annular Land and Lip]
        SP4 --> SP5[Weld Lines Form Here]
    end

    subgraph TYPE2[Spiral Spiderless Die]
        SL1[Melt Inlet Tangential] --> SL2[Conical Mandrel]
        SL2 --> SL3[Helical Grooves]
        SL3 --> SL4[Spiral Channels Merge]
        SL4 --> SL5[Annular Land and Lip]
        SL5 --> SL6[No Weld Lines]
    end

    subgraph TYPE3[Side Fed Die]
        SF1[Melt Enters Side] --> SF2[Asymmetric Flow Path]
        SF2 --> SF3[Die Body with Deflector]
        SF3 --> SF4[Lip Exit]
    end
```

### 4.3 Velocity and Pressure Profile Along the Die

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Inlet High Pressure P1] --> A2[Converging Pressure Falls]
    A2 --> A3[Land Constant Pressure Drop]
    A3 --> A4[Exit Low Pressure Atmosphere]
    B1[Inlet Low Velocity] --> B2[Converging Velocity Rises]
    B2 --> B3[Land Velocity Stable]
    B3 --> B4[Exit Velocity Peaks then Swells]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Die-swell occurs at the exit** because the viscoelastic melt "remembers" its larger cross-section. The *Tanner equation* relates swell to recoverable shear strain. Excessive swell causes **edge bead thickening** and **bubble instability**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define: (i) Blow-Up Ratio, (ii) Draw-Down Ratio, (iii) Frost Line Height, as applicable to tubular film extrusion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
- (i) **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR):** The ratio of the final bubble diameter to the die diameter, $\mathrm{BUR} = D_b / D_d$. It controls transverse (TD) orientation.
- (ii) **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR):** The ratio of the take-up velocity to the melt exit velocity, $\mathrm{DDR} = V_{tu} / V_o$. It controls machine-direction (MD) orientation.
- (iii) **Frost Line Height (FLH):** The axial distance from the die exit to the point where the bubble becomes opaque (crystallization or solidification front). For LLDPE, FLH ≈ (3–5) D<sub>d</sub>.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**List any three differences between a spider die and a spiral die used in blown film extrusion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Feature | Spider Die | Spiral Die |
|---------|-----------|------------|
| Melt distribution | Mandrel held by radial arms | Helical grooves on mandrel |
| Weld lines | Present, one per spider leg | Absent |
| Gauge uniformity | Moderate (±5–8%) | Excellent (±1–3%) |
| Best suited for | LDPE, simple films | LLDPE, mLLDPE, barrier films |
| Cleaning / purging | Easy | Difficult, long spiral paths |
| Cost | Low | High |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In 14-mark die-design questions, the most common mistakes are:
> 1. **Mixing up D and R** (diameter vs. radius) in the annular flow formula — costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mm → m** consistently — costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Using Newtonian viscosity for a polymer** when the problem specifies power-law — costs **3–4 marks**.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions** (steady, isothermal, thin-gap, no-slip) — costs **1 mark**.
> 5. **Skipping the final boxed answer** — costs **0.5 mark** (yes, half-marks exist in KTU).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Design and Flow Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample QP, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, describe the **construction and working of a spiral mandrel die** for tubular film extrusion. Compare its performance with a **conventional spider die** in terms of weld lines, gauge uniformity, and residence time distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tubular film die has die diameter 150 mm, land length 20 mm, and lip gap 0.9 mm. The polymer behaves as a power-law fluid with K = 8000 Pa·sⁿ and n = 0.45. The mass flow rate is 35 kg/h and density is 920 kg/m³. Compute the **(i) wall shear rate, (ii) wall shear stress, (iii) pressure drop across the land.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Construction (3 marks):** A spiral die consists of:
- A **tangential inlet port** feeding the melt into a conical or cylindrical body.
- A **conical mandrel** with **shallow helical grooves** machined on its surface.
- A **grooved die body** that interlocks with the mandrel to form continuous spiral channels.
- An **annular land** at the exit terminating in a sharp die lip.

**Working (2 marks):** Melt enters tangentially, divides into multiple spiral streams that flow helically along the mandrel. Adjacent spirals gradually merge as the channels taper, producing a uniform, weld-line-free annular flow at the exit.

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Spider Die | Spiral Die |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Weld lines | Yes (per leg) | None |
| Gauge tolerance | ±5–8% | ±1–3% |
| Residence time distribution | Broad, with stagnation | Narrow, plug-flow like |
| Cost & complexity | Low | High |
| Suitable polymers | LDPE | LLDPE, mLLDPE, HDPE |

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** D<sub>d</sub> = 0.150 m; L = 0.020 m; h = 0.0009 m; K = 8000 Pa·sⁿ; n = 0.45; ṁ = 35 kg/h = 0.00972 kg/s; ρ = 920 kg/m³.

**Convert to volumetric flow:**

$$Q = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho} = \frac{0.00972}{920} = 1.057 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Wall shear rate (Newtonian form, then check applicability) — using the power-law shear-rate expression for thin annular slit:**

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{2n+1}{n} \cdot \frac{6Q}{\pi D_d h^2}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{2(0.45)+1}{0.45} \cdot \frac{6 \times 1.057 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times 0.150 \times (0.0009)^2}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{1.9}{0.45} \cdot \frac{6.342 \times 10^{-5}}{3.817 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.222 \times 166.16 = 701.6 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Wall shear stress (power-law):**

$$\tau_w = K \dot{\gamma}_w^n = 8000 \times (701.6)^{0.45}$$

$$\tau_w = 8000 \times 19.96 = 1.597 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 0.160 \text{ MPa}$$

**Pressure drop across land (force balance on die element):**

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 L \tau_w}{h} = \frac{2 \times 0.020 \times 1.597 \times 10^5}{0.0009}$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{6388}{0.0009} = 7.098 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 7.1 \text{ MPa}$$

**Final boxed answers (must appear in valuation copy):**
- (i) $\dot{\gamma}_w \approx 702 \text{ s}^{-1}$
- (ii) $\tau_w \approx 0.16 \text{ MPa}$
- (iii) $\Delta P \approx 7.1 \text{ MPa}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice (Internal)*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample QP, Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for **volumetric flow rate of a Newtonian polymer melt through a thin annular die**. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A blown film line operates with die diameter 250 mm, lip gap 1.5 mm, BUR = 3.0, and DDR = 15. If the take-up speed is 30 m/min, calculate: **(i) the final film thickness, (ii) the melt exit velocity, (iii) the volumetric output in kg/h for a polymer of density 0.92 g/cm³.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** *(See full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes. Key assumptions to state: steady, isothermal, laminar, fully-developed, Newtonian, incompressible, no-slip, thin gap h ≪ D<sub>d</sub>, axial flow only.)*

Final derived result:

$$Q = \frac{\pi D_d h^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$$

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** D<sub>d</sub> = 0.250 m; h = 0.0015 m; BUR = 3.0; DDR = 15; V<sub>tu</sub> = 30 m/min = 0.500 m/s; ρ = 920 kg/m³.

**(i) Final film thickness:**

$$t_f = \frac{h}{\mathrm{BUR} \times \mathrm{DDR}} = \frac{0.0015}{3.0 \times 15} = \frac{0.0015}{45} = 3.33 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 33.3 \text{ μm}$$

**(ii) Melt exit velocity (using DDR definition):**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{tu}}{\mathrm{DDR}} = \frac{0.500}{15} = 0.0333 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) Volumetric output in kg/h:**

First, lay-flat film width:

$$w_f = \pi D_b / 2 = \pi \times \mathrm{BUR} \times D_d / 2 = \pi \times 3.0 \times 0.250 / 2 = 1.178 \text{ m}$$

Mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot t_f \cdot w_f \cdot V_{tu} = 920 \times 3.33 \times 10^{-5} \times 1.178 \times 0.500$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.01804 \text{ kg/s} = 64.95 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Final boxed answers:**
- (i) $t_f = 33.3 \text{ μm}$
- (ii) $V_o = 0.0333 \text{ m/s}$
- (iii) $\dot{m} \approx 65 \text{ kg/h}$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency KTU Recap — Must-Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ The thin-gap approximation is **valid only when h/D<sub>d</sub> < 0.1**; otherwise use the exact annular Hagen–Poiseuille formula.
- ✅ **Spider dies have weld lines; spiral dies do not.** This is the most-tested 1-mark fact.
- ✅ **BUR controls TD properties**; **DDR controls MD properties**; their product is inversely proportional to final film thickness.
- ✅ **Wall shear stress formula:** $\tau_w = \Delta P \cdot h / (2L)$ — applicable to thin annular slit.
- ✅ **Wall shear rate formula:** $\dot{\gamma}_w = 6Q / (\pi D_d h^2)$ (Newtonian) or $(2n+1)/n \cdot 6Q/(\pi D_d h^2)$ (power-law).
- ✅ **Land length rule of thumb:** L/h = 5 to 15. Too short → poor uniformity; too long → polymer degradation.
- ✅ **Frost line height** ≈ (3 to 8) × D<sub>d</sub> depending on cooling air velocity and polymer.
- ✅ **Die swell** is a viscoelastic phenomenon; quantified by **Tanner's equation** for Newtonian and extended forms for viscoelastic fluids.
- ✅ **Always convert mm → m** before substituting into SI formulas — most common arithmetic error in KTU scripts.
- ✅ For **multi-layer barrier films**, use **co-extrusion tubular dies** with concentric annular channels.
- ✅ **Pressure drop** across the die land is typically 5–25 MPa in commercial operations.
- ✅ Critical die design equation to memorize: $t_f = h / (\mathrm{BUR} \times \mathrm{DDR})$.
- ✅ **Coextrusion dies** extend single-layer dies with multiple concentric melt channels.
- ✅ The **stagnation points** in spider dies cause localized degradation — one reason spiral dies dominate in modern lines.
- ✅ **Tangential (side-fed) inlets** are preferred for thermally sensitive polymers due to short, uniform residence time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flat Film Dies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flat Film Dies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flat Film Die**: A flat film die is a polymer extrusion die assembly designed to convert a converging cylindrical melt stream from a single-screw extruder into a uniform, planar, rectangular web of molten polymer of controlled thickness across the entire die width. It is the most widely used die geometry for producing plastic films, sheets, and laminates in continuous extrusion lines.

The flat film die family is characterised by a **slit-type discharge geometry**, where the final flow cross-section emerging from the die has a width $W$ (in the transverse direction, typically 0.5 m to 5 m) and a lip gap $H$ (in the thickness direction, typically 0.2 mm to 6 mm). The polymer melt must be redistributed laterally from a single central inlet port into a uniform sheet across hundreds of millimetres of width.

### Classification of Flat Film Dies (KTU Syllabus)

| Type | Manifold Geometry | Typical Application |
|------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Straight Manifold (T-Die / Slot Die)** | Cylindrical, constant diameter | Coatings, low-viscosity polymers, narrow widths |
| **Coat-Hanger Die** | Tapered (trumpet-shaped) manifold | General-purpose films, packaging |
| **Fish-Tail Die** | Triangular expanding channel | Moderate width sheets, simple films |

### Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Bakery Icing Nozzle**
> Imagine a baker with a piping bag that has a single round hole at the tip. If the baker squeezes icing, it comes out as a thick rope. Now suppose the baker wants to ice a cake in one smooth, *uniformly thick* sheet of width 50 cm. The baker cannot just enlarge the hole — the icing would pile up in the middle and starve the edges.
>
> To solve this, the baker attaches a *fan-shaped metal spreader* (a manifold) that redistributes the icing sideways *while* forcing it through a thin adjustable slit at the bottom (the die lip). The spreader is shaped (tapered) so that the pressure pushing icing sideways gradually decreases, exactly compensating for the fact that less icing remains to be spread as we move toward the edges.
>
> A **flat film die** does precisely this with molten polymer: a tapered manifold distributes the melt sideways, and a precision-controlled slit (lip) lays down a uniform sheet.

### Engineering Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Flat film dies are the **workhorse of the plastics converting industry**. A single flat film line can produce 200 to 2000 kg/hour of film ranging from 10 µm (cling film) to 6 mm (thermoforming sheet). Die quality directly determines:
> - **Thickness uniformity** (±2% is commercial target, ±0.5% is premium)
> - **Residence time** (critical for heat-sensitive polymers like PVC and PET)
> - **Surface finish** (gloss, haze)
> - **Weld line / streak defects** (from poor manifold design)

### Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

- **Polymer melt viscosity** at processing temperature: $\mu = 10^2$ to $10^4$ Pa·s
- **Operating pressure** at die inlet: $\Delta P = 5$ to $30$ MPa
- **Wall shear rate** in land: $\dot{\gamma}_w = 10$ to $1000$ s$^{-1}$
- **Standard film gauge**: 20 µm to 500 µm
- **Maximum line speed**: up to 500 m/min in modern cast film lines
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Functional Anatomy of a Flat Film Die

A flat film die is composed of **five sequential functional zones** through which the polymer melt passes:

1. **Inlet / Adapter Zone** — receives melt from extruder, often includes a breaker plate and screen pack to remove gels and pressure fluctuations.
2. **Manifold Zone** — distributes the melt laterally across the die width. Geometry determines flow uniformity.
3. **Pre-Land (Approach) Zone** — converging region between manifold and land; reduces flow disturbances.
4. **Die Land (Final Channel)** — the narrow rectangular channel that determines final flow uniformity and pressure drop.
5. **Lip / Exit Zone** — precision-machined final opening; may include flexible (decarbamate) or rigid (fixed) lip geometry.

### Operating Principle: Why a Coat-Hanger Manifold?

The fundamental design problem is **flow uniformity**. At the centre of the die (directly below the inlet), the melt has the longest path to travel sideways before exiting, while at the edges it has a much shorter path. If a uniform manifold diameter were used (as in a **T-die**), then:

- Centre flow exits with **too much residual pressure** → over-thickness at centre
- Edge flow exits with **excessive pressure loss along the manifold** → under-thickness at edges

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solution**: In a **coat-hanger die**, the manifold cross-section is *progressively reduced* from centre to edges so that the pressure driving the melt *outward* through the manifold is exactly balanced by the pressure driving the melt *downward* through the land at every lateral position. This guarantees that the volumetric flow per unit width $q(x)$ is constant across the die width $W$.

### Restrictor Bar (Choker Bar)

A **choker bar** is a metallic bar of adjustable height inserted into the die land. It locally restricts the flow channel cross-section, increasing local pressure drop. Operators adjust the choker bar profile to **fine-tune** thickness distribution, compensating for:
- Material viscosity variations
- Worn or warped die lips
- Edge effects (edge beads)

### Engineering Justification / Real-World Utility

The coat-hanger die principle is used in:
- **Cast film extrusion** (PP, PE, PET for food packaging)
- **Sheet extrusion** (HIPS, ABS, PVC for thermoforming)
- **Extrusion coating** (PE coated onto paper, aluminium foil, fabric)
- **Multilayer coextrusion** (5-, 7-, 9-layer dies for barrier packaging)
- **Solar PV backsheet and frontsheet manufacturing**

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **mandatory equations** for solving flat film die problems in the KTU ESE. All symbols use SI units unless otherwise stated.

| Parameter | Formula | Engineering Use |
|-----------|---------|-----------------|
| Volumetric flow rate through slit (Newtonian) | $Q = \dfrac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$ | Determines throughput |
| Wall shear rate in slit | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{6 Q}{W H^2} = \dfrac{H \Delta P}{2 \mu L}$ | Die lip design, melt fracture check |
| Wall shear stress in slit | $\tau_w = \dfrac{H \Delta P}{2 L}$ | Viscous heating, polymer degradation |
| Hagen-Poiseuille flow (circular manifold) | $\Delta P_m = \dfrac{8 \mu L_m Q_m}{\pi R^4}$ | Manifold pressure drop |
| Coathanger manifold radius profile | $R(x) = R_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{x}{L}\right)^{1/4}$ | Manifold taper design |
| Die swell ratio | $B = \dfrac{v_{exit}}{\bar{v}}$ | Lip gap correction |
| Final film thickness (mass balance) | $t_f = \dfrac{Q}{W \cdot v_{haul}}$ | Output film gauge |
| Drawdown ratio | $DDR = \dfrac{v_{haul}}{v_{exit}}$ | Orientation, neck-in |
| Residence time | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{die}}{Q}$ | Thermal degradation check |
| Reynolds number in slit | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \bar{v} D_h}{\mu}$ | Flow regime check |
| Newtonian power-law relation | $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Non-Newtonian viscosity model |

**Notation**: $W$ = die width, $H$ = lip gap (height), $L$ = land length, $\mu$ = viscosity, $\Delta P$ = pressure drop, $K$ = consistency index, $n$ = power-law index, $D_h = 2H$ = hydraulic diameter for wide slit.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Volumetric Flow Rate Through a Rectangular Die Land (Newtonian Fluid)

**Problem Setup**: Consider a wide rectangular slit of width $W$ (into the page) and gap $H$, length $L$. The fluid is incompressible, isothermal, Newtonian, with viscosity $\mu$. The pressure drop across the land is $\Delta P$. Assume $W \gg H$ so that side-wall effects are negligible (this is the "wide slit" approximation, valid for $W/H > 10$).

**Governing Equation (Navier-Stokes, simplified for fully developed, unidirectional flow along $z$-axis):**

$$
0 = -\frac{dP}{dz} + \mu \frac{d^2 v_z}{dy^2}
$$

where $y$ is the gap-direction coordinate (0 to $H$), and $v_z$ is the local velocity along the channel.

**Step 1 — Integrate twice with no-slip boundary conditions $v_z(0) = v_z(H) = 0$:**

$$
\frac{d^2 v_z}{dy^2} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}
$$

$$
v_z(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\, y^2 + C_1 y + C_2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions:**

$$
v_z(0) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_2 = 0
$$

$$
v_z(H) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_1 = -\frac{H}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}
$$

**Step 3 — Velocity profile (parabolic — Poiseuille flow):**

$$
v_z(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dz}\left(y^2 - H y\right)
$$

Since $\frac{dP}{dz}$ is negative (pressure drops along the flow), let $\Delta P = P_{in} - P_{out}$ over length $L$, so $\frac{dP}{dz} = -\frac{\Delta P}{L}$.

$$
v_z(y) = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L}\, y (H - y)
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum velocity at $y = H/2$:**

$$
v_{max} = \frac{\Delta P\, H^2}{8 \mu L}
$$

**Step 5 — Average velocity:**

$$
\bar{v} = \frac{1}{H}\int_0^H v_z(y)\, dy = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{1}{H}\int_0^H y(H - y)\, dy
$$

$$
\int_0^H y(H - y)\, dy = \left[\frac{H y^2}{2} - \frac{y^3}{3}\right]_0^H = \frac{H^3}{2} - \frac{H^3}{3} = \frac{H^3}{6}
$$

$$
\bar{v} = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{H^2}{6} = \frac{\Delta P\, H^2}{12 \mu L}
$$

**Step 6 — Volumetric flow rate $Q = \bar{v} \cdot W \cdot H$:**

$$
Q = \frac{\Delta P\, H^2}{12 \mu L} \cdot W \cdot H = \frac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **fundamental KTU equation** for Newtonian flow through a wide slit die land. Note the strong cubic dependence on gap $H$: doubling the lip gap increases flow by $2^3 = 8 \times$.

---

### Derivation 2: Coat-Hanger Die Manifold Radius Profile

**Problem Setup**: At any lateral position $x$ along the manifold (where $x$ is measured from the centre of the die), a portion of melt flows outward through the manifold (carrying flow $Q_m(x)$) while the remainder exits downward through the land.

**Step 1 — Mass balance along manifold:**

At position $x$, the flow remaining in the manifold equals the total flow minus what has exited through the land up to that point. For uniform flow per unit width $q = Q_{total}/W$ exiting through the land:

$$
Q_m(x) = Q_{total} - q \cdot 2x = Q_{total}\left(1 - \frac{2x}{W}\right)
$$

where $W$ is the full die width and $x$ ranges from 0 (centre) to $W/2$ (edge).

**Step 2 — Pressure gradient along manifold (Hagen-Poiseuille for a circular tube):**

$$
\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{8\mu Q_m(x)}{\pi R(x)^4}
$$

**Step 3 — Design condition for uniform film thickness:**

The pressure drop across the land must be **constant** at every $x$, so the pressure along the manifold must decrease **linearly** from centre to edge:

$$
\frac{dP}{dx} = -\text{constant} = -\frac{\Delta P_{manifold}}{W/2}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for $R(x)$:**

$$
\frac{8\mu Q_m(x)}{\pi R(x)^4} = \frac{2\Delta P_{manifold}}{W}
$$

$$
R(x)^4 = \frac{4\mu W\, Q_m(x)}{\pi \Delta P_{manifold}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $Q_m(x) = Q_{total}(1 - 2x/W)$ and define $R_0$ at $x=0$:**

$$
R(0)^4 = \frac{4\mu W Q_{total}}{\pi \Delta P_{manifold}} \equiv R_0^4
$$

Therefore:

$$
R(x)^4 = R_0^4 \left(1 - \frac{2x}{W}\right)
$$

Defining half-width $L = W/2$ and position variable $x \in [0, L]$:

$$
\boxed{R(x) = R_0 \left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right)^{1/4}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **quarter-power law** is the design rule for coathanger dies. At $x = L$ (the edge), $R = 0$ mathematically; in practice, the manifold terminates in a finite-radius end cap.

---

### Derivation 3: Final Film Thickness After Haul-Off

**Problem Setup**: After the melt exits the die lip, it is drawn down by chill rolls moving at velocity $v_{haul}$. The film width contracts slightly (neck-in) and the thickness decreases.

**Step 1 — Mass conservation between die exit and solidified film:**

$$
\rho\, Q = \rho\, W_f\, t_f\, v_{haul}
$$

where $W_f$ is the film width (slightly less than $W$ due to neck-in), $t_f$ is the final film thickness, and $v_{haul}$ is the haul-off speed.

**Step 2 — Assume width contraction is negligible** ($W_f \approx W$ for engineering design):

$$
t_f = \frac{Q}{W \cdot v_{haul}}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of die conditions:**

Substituting $Q = \frac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$:

$$
t_f = \frac{H^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L \cdot v_{haul}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This shows that **film thickness is controlled primarily by haul-off speed**, not by die gap. In commercial practice, the die gap is set to approximately 1.2 to 2× the desired final thickness, and the haul-off speed is the primary control knob.

---

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Given**: A flat film die with $W = 1.2$ m, $H = 2.5$ mm, $L = 40$ mm. The polymer melt has viscosity $\mu = 4000$ Pa·s at processing temperature. The pressure drop across the land is $\Delta P = 4$ MPa.

**Required**: (a) Volumetric flow rate $Q$, (b) Wall shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_w$, (c) Final film thickness if haul-off speed is $v_{haul} = 30$ m/min.

**Solution:**

**(a) Volumetric flow rate:**

$$
Q = \frac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L} = \frac{1.2 \times (0.0025)^3 \times 4 \times 10^6}{12 \times 4000 \times 0.04}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{1.2 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-8} \times 4 \times 10^6}{1920}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{0.075}{1920} = 3.906 \times 10^{-5}\; \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 39.06 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(b) Wall shear rate:**

$$
\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{6Q}{W H^2} = \frac{6 \times 3.906 \times 10^{-5}}{1.2 \times (0.0025)^2}
$$

$$
\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{2.344 \times 10^{-4}}{7.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 31.25 \;\text{s}^{-1}
$$

**(c) Final film thickness** ($v_{haul} = 30$ m/min $= 0.5$ m/s):

$$
t_f = \frac{Q}{W \cdot v_{haul}} = \frac{3.906 \times 10^{-5}}{1.2 \times 0.5} = 6.51 \times 10^{-5}\; \text{m} = 65.1\;\mu\text{m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification**: Die gap $H = 2500$ µm, final film $t_f = 65.1$ µm. Drawdown ratio $DDR = 2500/65.1 \approx 38.4$. This is typical for cast film lines producing ~60 µm film for packaging.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define a flat film die. List the three main types of flat film dies and state one distinguishing feature of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **flat film die** is an extrusion die that converts a cylindrical melt stream from a single-screw extruder into a uniform planar web of molten polymer through a rectangular slit geometry. It is used to manufacture plastic films, sheets, and extrusion coatings.

The three main types are:

1. **T-Die (Straight Manifold Die)** — Uses a cylindrical manifold of constant diameter across the die width. Distinguishing feature: simplest construction but suffers from non-uniform flow distribution, with thicker film at the centre and thinner at the edges.

2. **Fish-Tail Die** — Uses a triangular, linearly tapering manifold. Distinguishing feature: provides intermediate flow uniformity improvement over the T-die at moderate cost, commonly used for narrow-to-medium width sheets.

3. **Coat-Hanger Die** — Uses a trumpet-shaped manifold whose radius decreases according to $R(x) = R_0(1 - x/L)^{1/4}$. Distinguishing feature: the quarter-power taper maintains constant pressure drop across the die land, producing excellent thickness uniformity, and is the industry standard for wide film production.

*(Award 1 mark each for the definition and the three types with their features.)*

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**What is the function of a choker bar (restrictor bar) in a flat film die? How does it affect film thickness?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **choker bar** (also called a restrictor bar) is a precision-machined metallic bar inserted into the die land, parallel to the flow direction, whose vertical position can be locally adjusted along the die width.

**Function:** It locally restricts the cross-sectional area of the die land, thereby increasing the local pressure drop and reducing the local volumetric flow rate. By adjusting its profile (height along the die width), the operator compensates for non-uniformities in flow arising from material property variations, wear, temperature gradients, or edge effects.

**Effect on film thickness:** Where the choker bar is lowered (closer to the land surface), the flow is restricted and the film becomes thinner; where it is raised, the flow increases and the film becomes thicker. In modern automated dies, the choker bar profile is controlled by **thermal expansion bolts** that respond to die-zone temperature, giving closed-loop thickness control within ±1–2%.

*(Award 1.5 marks for function, 1.5 marks for effect on film thickness.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **coat-hanger die** for flat film extrusion. (7 marks)

**(b)** A flat film die has the following specifications:
- Die width: $W = 1.5$ m
- Lip gap (land height): $H = 2.0$ mm
- Land length: $L = 35$ mm
- Polymer melt viscosity: $\mu = 6000$ Pa·s
- Pressure drop across the land: $\Delta P = 5$ MPa

Calculate the:
1. Volumetric flow rate $Q$ in m³/s
2. Wall shear rate $\dot{\gamma}_w$ in s$^{-1}$
3. Final film thickness if the haul-off speed is $v_{haul} = 25$ m/min (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of a Coat-Hanger Die (7 marks):**

**Construction:** A coat-hanger die consists of the following parts:

1. **Die Body** — A massive steel block with internal channels machined by CNC and electro-discharge machining. The body is typically chrome-plated and polished on internal surfaces.

2. **Inlet Port** — A circular entry at the top centre where melt from the extruder enters.

3. **Manifold (Trumpet-shaped channel)** — The heart of the die. The manifold cross-section radius decreases from a maximum $R_0$ at the centre to zero at the edges, following $R(x) = R_0(1-x/L)^{1/4}$. This shape gives the die its name, as the side profile resembles a coat-hanger.

4. **Pre-Land** — A converging region between manifold and the main land that smoothly accelerates the flow.

5. **Die Land** — A narrow rectangular slit of constant gap $H$ and length $L$ (typically 25–50 mm). It is the final flow channel that determines pressure drop.

6. **Choker Bar (Restrictor Bar)** — An adjustable bar in the land cavity for fine flow control.

7. **Upper and Lower Lips** — Precision-machined final openings. The upper lip may be flexible (decarbamate type) for thermal/automatic gauge adjustment.

8. **Heaters** — Band or cartridge heaters with multiple zones (typically 5–9 zones across width) for temperature uniformity.

**Working Principle:** Polymer melt entering the inlet port encounters a manifold whose cross-sectional area decreases progressively from centre to edge. The volumetric flow remaining in the manifold decreases linearly with $x$, since flow is continuously being bled off downward through the land. By tapering the manifold radius as $R(x) \propto (1-x/L)^{1/4}$, the pressure gradient driving the melt sideways is maintained constant, which in turn maintains a constant pressure drop across the land at every lateral position. Consequently, the volumetric flow per unit width exiting through the land is uniform, and a film of constant thickness is produced across the entire die width.

*(Award marks: 2 for sketch description, 2 for components, 1 for principle, 1 for quarter-power taper formula, 1 for working.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$$W = 1.5 \text{ m}, \quad H = 2.0 \text{ mm} = 0.002 \text{ m}, \quad L = 35 \text{ mm} = 0.035 \text{ m}$$
$$\mu = 6000 \text{ Pa·s}, \quad \Delta P = 5 \text{ MPa} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 1 — Volumetric Flow Rate (3 marks):**

$$
Q = \frac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{1.5 \times (0.002)^3 \times 5 \times 10^6}{12 \times 6000 \times 0.035}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{1.5 \times 8 \times 10^{-9} \times 5 \times 10^6}{2520}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{0.060}{2520} = 2.381 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Wall Shear Rate (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{6 Q}{W H^2} = \frac{6 \times 2.381 \times 10^{-5}}{1.5 \times (0.002)^2}
$$

$$
\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{1.4286 \times 10^{-4}}{6 \times 10^{-6}} = 23.81 \;\text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Final Film Thickness (2 marks):**

Convert haul-off speed: $v_{haul} = 25$ m/min $= 25/60 = 0.4167$ m/s

$$
t_f = \frac{Q}{W \cdot v_{haul}} = \frac{2.381 \times 10^{-5}}{1.5 \times 0.4167}
$$

$$
t_f = \frac{2.381 \times 10^{-5}}{0.625} = 3.81 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m} = 38.1 \;\mu\text{m}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $Q = 2.38 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s (≈ 23.8 cm³/s)
- $\dot{\gamma}_w = 23.8$ s$^{-1}$
- $t_f = 38.1$ µm

---

#### QUESTION B [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of **T-die** and **fish-tail die**. Compare their performance with the coat-hanger die. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for the volumetric flow rate of a Newtonian polymer melt through the rectangular land of a flat film die. State clearly all assumptions. A die has land length $L = 25$ mm and gap $H = 1.5$ mm. If the wall shear stress is $\tau_w = 1.5 \times 10^5$ Pa, calculate the pressure drop $\Delta P$ across the land and the volumetric flow rate per unit width. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) T-Die, Fish-Tail Die and Comparison (7 marks):**

**T-Die (Straight Manifold / Slot Die):**
- **Construction:** Simplest flat die design. A cylindrical manifold of constant diameter extends across the full die width. Below the manifold is a rectangular slot (land) that forms the final opening.
- **Working:** Polymer melt enters the manifold centrally, then flows outward toward both edges, finally exiting through the slit. However, the pressure drop along the manifold from centre to edge is significant, so the driving pressure for downward flow at the centre is much higher than at the edges. This produces a film that is **thick at the centre and thin at the edges**.
- **Limitations:** Limited to narrow widths (typically < 600 mm) and low-viscosity polymers. Used primarily for coating applications and polymer processing of low-viscosity melts.

**Fish-Tail Die:**
- **Construction:** Manifold has a triangular (wedge-shaped) cross-section that decreases linearly from centre to edges.
- **Working:** The linearly tapering cross-section provides partial compensation for the centre-to-edge pressure drop, producing better uniformity than a T-die but not as good as a coat-hanger.
- **Applications:** Moderate width films and sheets, typically up to 1.5 m wide.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | T-Die | Fish-Tail Die | Coat-Hanger Die |
|---------|-------|---------------|-----------------|
| Manifold shape | Cylindrical | Linear taper | Quarter-power taper |
| Flow uniformity | Poor | Moderate | Excellent |
| Max practical width | ~600 mm | ~1500 mm | > 5000 mm |
| Cost | Low | Moderate | High |
| Residence time | Long (poor purging) | Moderate | Short |
| Common use | Coatings | Narrow sheets | Wide films, packaging |

*(Award 2 marks each for T-die and fish-tail die explanation, 3 marks for comparison table.)*

---

**(b) Derivation and Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):** The full derivation was given in Section 3 above. Recap of the key result:

For a Newtonian fluid in a wide rectangular slit with width $W$, gap $H$, and length $L$, assuming steady, incompressible, isothermal, fully developed laminar flow with $W \gg H$ and no-slip at walls:

$$
\boxed{Q = \frac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}}
$$

**Assumptions (must be stated for full marks):**
1. Steady-state flow
2. Incompressible melt
3. Newtonian viscosity (constant $\mu$)
4. Isothermal conditions (no viscous heating effect on $\mu$)
5. Fully developed flow
6. Wide-slit approximation ($W \gg H$), side-wall effects neglected
7. No-slip at die walls
8. Laminar flow (creeping flow, $Re \ll 2100$)

**Numerical Part (3 marks):**

**Given:** $L = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m, $H = 1.5$ mm $= 0.0015$ m, $\tau_w = 1.5 \times 10^5$ Pa.

**Step 1 — Pressure drop from wall shear stress:**

The wall shear stress in a slit die is given by:

$$
\tau_w = \frac{H \Delta P}{2 L}
$$

Solving for $\Delta P$:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{2 L \tau_w}{H} = \frac{2 \times 0.025 \times 1.5 \times 10^5}{0.0015}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \frac{7500}{0.0015} = 5 \times 10^6 \;\text{Pa} = 5 \;\text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate per unit width** ($Q/W$):

Using the alternative expression (substituting $\Delta P = 2 L \tau_w / H$ into the flow equation):

$$
\frac{Q}{W} = \frac{H^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L} = \frac{H^3}{12 \mu L} \cdot \frac{2 L \tau_w}{H} = \frac{H^2 \tau_w}{6 \mu}
$$

This eliminates $L$, but since $\mu$ is not given, use the original form. Alternatively, since the wall shear rate is:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{\tau_w}{\mu}
$$

For Newtonian flow:
$$
Q = \frac{W H^2 \dot{\gamma}_w}{6}
$$

However, since $\mu$ is not provided, express $Q/W$ using $\Delta P$:

$$
\frac{Q}{W} = \frac{H^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}
$$
