To find $\mu$, use the relation $\tau_w = \mu \dot{\gamma}_w$ — but $\dot{\gamma}_w$ is also not given. Therefore, present the result in terms of the known quantities:

$$
\frac{Q}{W} = \frac{H^2 \tau_w}{6 \mu} \quad \text{(requires } \mu\text{)}
$$

Using the pressure-drop form:
$$
\frac{Q}{W} = \frac{(0.0015)^3 \times 5 \times 10^6}{12 \mu \times 0.025}
$$

$$
\frac{Q}{W} = \frac{1.6875 \times 10^{-2}}{0.3 \mu} = \frac{0.05625}{\mu}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\Delta P = 5$ MPa
- $\dfrac{Q}{W} = \dfrac{0.05625}{\mu}$ m²/s (or equivalently, $\dfrac{Q}{W} = \dfrac{H^2 \tau_w}{6 \mu}$, requires $\mu$ to evaluate)

*(Examiner's note: a fully evaluable numerical answer for $Q/W$ requires $\mu$. The student's solution correctly identifies the equation and expresses the dependency on $\mu$.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the wide-slit assumption** $W \gg H$ — must be explicitly stated (loses 1 mark).
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** — most common. $H$ in mm must be converted to m; $\Delta P$ in MPa to Pa. Sloppy conversion loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing shear rate with shear stress** — these are dimensionally and physically different; check whether the formula uses $\tau_w$ or $\dot{\gamma}_w$.
> 4. **Forgetting the cubic dependence on $H$** — when asked "what happens if $H$ is doubled?", the answer is **flow increases 8×**, not 2×.
> 5. **Not deriving the quarter-power law** in coathanger design questions — you must show the $R(x) = R_0(1-x/L)^{1/4}$ derivation, not just state it.
> 6. **Mistaking die gap for final film thickness** — the die gap is 1.2–2× the final thickness due to drawdown.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Points for KTU ESE Quick Revision:**

- ✅ Flat film dies convert cylindrical melt stream into a uniform rectangular web; used for films, sheets, and coatings.
- ✅ Three main types: **T-die** (straight manifold, poor uniformity), **Fish-Tail die** (linear taper, moderate), **Coat-hanger die** (quarter-power taper, best uniformity).
- ✅ **Coathanger manifold radius profile:** $R(x) = R_0(1 - x/L)^{1/4}$ — derived from requirement of constant pressure drop across the die land at every lateral position.
- ✅ **Fundamental Newtonian flow equation in slit land:** $Q = \dfrac{W H^3 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$ — note the cubic dependence on gap $H$.
- ✅ **Wall shear rate in slit:** $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{6Q}{W H^2} = \dfrac{H \Delta P}{2 \mu L}$ — critical for checking against melt fracture and degradation limits.
- ✅ **Wall shear stress in slit:** $\tau_w = \dfrac{H \Delta P}{2 L}$ — drives viscous heating.
- ✅ **Final film thickness:** $t_f = \dfrac{Q}{W v_{haul}}$ — controlled primarily by haul-off speed, not die gap.
- ✅ **Drawdown ratio (DDR):** typically 20 to 80 for cast film, 5 to 20 for blown film; affects orientation and mechanical properties.
- ✅ **Choker bar** is an adjustable restrictor used to fine-tune thickness distribution; automated dies use thermal bolts for closed-loop control.
- ✅ **Assumptions for slit flow derivation:** steady, incompressible, Newtonian, isothermal, fully developed, $W \gg H$, no-slip, laminar.
- ✅ **Die land length:** typically 25 to 50 mm — long enough to even out flow, short enough to avoid excessive pressure drop and residence time.
- ✅ **Lip gap setting:** 1.2 to 2× the desired final film thickness.
- ✅ **Reynolds number** in the land should remain below ~2100 (laminar) for predictable flow; typical operating $Re$ is well below this.
- ✅ **Industrial applications:** food packaging films, shrink film, stretch film, lamination films, photo-quality film, solar PV sheet, thermoforming sheet, extrusion coating.
- ✅ **Multilayer coextrusion** uses a combination of flat dies in feedblock or multi-manifold configurations for barrier packaging (e.g., 7-layer EVOH barrier films).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sheet Dies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sheet Dies # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) A **Sheet Die** is a precision-engineered extrusion tooling device that converts a cylindrical melt stream emerging from a single-screw extruder into a **flat, uniformly thick, wide polymer sheet** of controlled width and gauge (typically **0.25 mm to 25 mm** thickness and up to **3 m** width). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > A sheet die is a polymer processing die used to produce flat sheet products with uniform thickness distribution, comprising a **manifold** (primary distribution channel), a **preland** (secondary distribution region), and a **land** (final parallel lip region) through which the melt is forced before emerging as a flat extrudate. The fundamental engineering challenge in sheet die design is achieving **uniform velocity profile** across the entire die exit width so that the resulting sheet has **constant thickness and uniform gauge distribution**, despite the melt entering as a single cylindrical stream from the extruder. ## 1.2 Constituent Components of a Sheet Die | Component | Function | Typical Specification | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Manifold** | Distributes melt laterally across the die width | Circular cross-section, tapered | | **Preland** | Equalises flow velocity and pressure across width | Rectangular, gradual taper | | **Land (Die Lip)** | Final shaping and flow homogenisation | Parallel, length 5–15 × gap | | **Choker Bar** | Restricts flow in slow regions, balances velocity | Adjustable restrictor plate | | **Die Lip Bolts** | Fine adjustment of exit gap thickness | ~50–100 thermal adjustment bolts | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Showerhead" Concept > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy:** > Imagine pouring water from a single bucket into a long, narrow horizontal trough with a slit at the bottom. The water at the centre arrives first with high pressure, while water at the ends trickles in slowly — you get a **non-uniform flow**. Now imagine reshaping the trough into a curved pipe (manifold) that gradually narrows from the centre to the ends, combined with adjustable flow restrictors — this balances the output across the entire slit. **This is exactly what a coat-hanger sheet die does for molten polymer.** The manifold is designed with a **larger cross-section at the centre** (where melt enters) and **progressively smaller cross-section toward the ends** (where less flow is needed), ensuring the **residual pressure** at every point along the manifold drives an equal amount of flow through the preland to the die exit. ## 1.4 Applications of Sheet Dies in Industry - **Thermoforming feedstock** (PP, HIPS, PET, PVC sheets) - **Packaging sheets** (multi-layer, food-grade) - **Stationery products** (transparent PVC, PP folders) - **Construction sheets** (corrugated roofing, wall cladding) - **Automotive interior panels** (ABS, PP composites) - **Geomembranes & pond liners** (HDPE, LLDPE) > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** Always remember — the **uniformity of exit velocity** is the single most important design parameter. A 5% non-uniformity in exit velocity translates directly to a **5% thickness variation** in the final sheet, which is often unacceptable. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Types of Sheet Dies ### 2.1.1 T-Slot Die - Simplest, oldest design. - A flat rectangular channel (slit) machined into the die body. - Melt enters from one end, exits across the full width. - **Limitation:** Severe flow imbalance — pressure at the entry end is much higher than at the dead-end. - **Use:** Only for very short, narrow sheets or low-viscosity polymers. ### 2.1.2 Fishtail Die - The flow channel diverges from a circular inlet to a wide rectangular outlet in a "fishtail" shape. - Two flat plates form the diverging channel. - **Improvement over T-slot:** Better lateral flow distribution due to the diverging geometry. - **Limitation:** Velocity profile is still parabolic; centre flow is faster than edge flow. ### 2.1.3 Coat-Hanger Die (Most Common Industrial Design) - Combines a **primary circular manifold** with a **secondary tapered preland**. - Named for its characteristic **"hanger-shaped" cross-section** when viewed from the side. - Manifold radius typically **0.6 – 1.5 × sheet width**. - Achieves **±2–3% thickness uniformity** across the width — the industry benchmark. ### 2.1.4 Curved Manifold Die - Manifold is curved (banana-shaped) rather than straight. - Used for **very wide sheets** (>2 m) where a straight manifold would be impractically long. - The curved geometry compensates for thermal expansion and mechanical flex. > [!NOTE] > **Why Coat-Hanger Dominates:** The combination of a **pressure-feeding manifold** (high pressure at centre) and a **restrictive preland** (creates back-pressure) allows the designer to tune the cross-section of the manifold so that the **exit velocity becomes constant** along the entire width. ## 2.2 Design Objectives — The Five Engineering Goals A properly designed sheet die must simultaneously achieve: 1. **Uniform exit velocity** across the die width (tolerance typically **±2–5%**). 2. **Minimum pressure drop** to prevent shear-induced degradation of heat-sensitive polymers (e.g., PVC, CPVC). 3. **Minimum residence time** to avoid thermal degradation — residence time distribution (RTD) must be narrow. 4. **Uniform shear history** — every fluid element should experience similar shear strain. 5. **Mechanical rigidity** and ease of disassembly for cleaning (especially for PVC and other corrosive polymers). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they are the most frequently tested in KTU Module 2 questions.** | # | Parameter | Equation | Variables & Units | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1** | **Pressure drop in preland (rectangular channel)** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12\,\mu\,L\,Q}{w\,h^{3}}$ | $\mu$ = viscosity [Pa·s], $L$ = preland length [m], $Q$ = volumetric flow rate [m³/s], $w$ = channel width [m], $h$ = channel gap [m] | | **2** | **Average velocity in preland** | $\bar{v} = \dfrac{Q}{w\,h}$ | $Q$ [m³/s], $w$ [m], $h$ [m] | | **3** | **Wall shear rate in preland** | $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{6\,Q}{w\,h^{2}}$ | [s⁻¹] | | **4** | **Shear stress at wall** | $\tau_{w} = \mu\,\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{6\,\mu\,Q}{w\,h^{2}}$ | [Pa] | | **5** | **Pressure drop in circular manifold (Hagen–Poiseuille)** | $\Delta P_{m} = \dfrac{8\,\mu\,L_{m}\,Q_{m}}{\pi\,R^{4}}$ | $R$ = manifold radius [m], $L_{m}$ = manifold length [m] | | **6** | **Power-law fluid pressure drop (generalisation)** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\,L\,K}{h}\left(\dfrac{6Q}{wh^{2}}\right)^{n}\left(\dfrac{2n+1}{3n}\right)^{n}$ | $K$ = consistency index, $n$ = power-law index | | **7** | **Die swell ratio** | $B = \dfrac{h_{\text{sheet}}}{h_{\text{die lip}}}$ | Dimensionless, $B > 1$ for viscoelastic melts | | **8** | **Approximate die swell (Tanner's equation)** | $B \approx \left[1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{N_{1}}{2\tau_{w}}\right)^{2}\right]^{1/6}$ | $N_{1}$ = first normal stress difference [Pa] | | **9** | **Leakage flow per unit preland length** | $q(x) = \dfrac{w\,h^{3}}{12\,\mu\,L}\bigl(P_{m}(x) - P_{a}\bigr)$ | [m³/s per m] | | **10** | **Manifold area profile (ideal coat hanger)** | $A(x) = A_{0}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{k\,x^{2}}{2}\right)$ | Gaussian taper, $k$ is a design constant | | **11** | **Manifold length (empirical)** | $L_{m} \approx 0.6\,W$ to $1.2\,W$ | $W$ = half die width | | **12** | **Reynolds number (polymer melt)** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho\,\bar{v}\,D_{h}}{\mu}$ | $D_{h}$ = hydraulic diameter [m] | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Equations (1)–(4) are used for **preland sizing**. Equation (5) governs **manifold pressure analysis**. Equation (7) is critical because **die swell determines the relationship between die lip gap and final sheet thickness** — a die cut at 2 mm may produce a 2.3 mm sheet due to viscoelastic recovery. ## 2.4 Shear Rate Considerations — Polymer Degradation Limit For most commercial polymers: - **Polyethylene (LDPE):** $\dot{\gamma}_{w}$ max $\approx$ **100–1000 s⁻¹** - **PVC (rigid):** $\dot{\gamma}_{w}$ max $\approx$ **40–100 s⁻¹** (shear-sensitive, degrades to HCl) - **Polycarbonate:** $\dot{\gamma}_{w}$ max $\approx$ **1000–5000 s⁻¹** - **PMMA:** $\dot{\gamma}_{w}$ max $\approx$ **100–1000 s⁻¹** > [!WARNING] > **Critical Design Rule:** Always verify that the calculated wall shear rate does not exceed the thermal/shear degradation threshold of the polymer. The rule of thumb used in industry is to keep $\Delta P$ across the die below **5–10 MPa** for shear-sensitive polymers. ## 2.5 Residence Time Analysis $$\bar{t}_{res} = \frac{V_{die}}{Q}$$ where $V_{die}$ is the total internal volume of the die (manifold + preland + land). For thermal-sensitive polymers, the target is typically $\bar{t}_{res} \leq$ **60–300 seconds** at processing temperature. The coat-hanger die is preferred over T-slot precisely because its smaller internal volume yields shorter residence time. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Pressure Drop Across the Prel and (Newtonian Melt) This is the most critical derivation for KTU examinations. ### Setup Consider the preland as a **rectangular slit channel** of: - Width $w$ (lateral direction, much larger than height) - Height $h$ (gap between die plates) - Length $L$ (flow direction) The melt is assumed **Newtonian** with viscosity $\mu$, **isothermal**, and the flow is **steady, laminar, fully-developed, and incompressible**. ### Governing Equation Starting from the Navier–Stokes equation for steady, fully-developed flow in the $x$-direction (neglecting inertia and gravity): $$\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = \mu\left(\frac{\partial^{2} v_{x}}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} v_{x}}{\partial z^{2}}\right)$$ For a wide slit ($w \gg h$), the velocity gradient in the $z$-direction (width) is much smaller than in the $y$-direction (gap), so: $$\frac{\partial^{2} v_{x}}{\partial z^{2}} \ll \frac{\partial^{2} v_{x}}{\partial y^{2}}$$ Hence the equation simplifies to: $$\frac{dP}{dx} = \mu\,\frac{d^{2} v_{x}}{dy^{2}}$$ ### Step 1: Integrate Once $$\frac{d v_{x}}{dy} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\,y + C_{1}$$ ### Step 2: Apply Symmetry Boundary Condition At the centreline ($y = 0$), by symmetry, the velocity gradient is zero: $dv_{x}/dy = 0$. Therefore $C_{1} = 0$, giving: $$\frac{d v_{x}}{dy} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\,y$$ ### Step 3: Integrate Again $$v_{x}(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\,y^{2} + C_{2}$$ ### Step 4: Apply No-Slip at the Wall At $y = h/2$, $v_{x} = 0$: $$0 = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(\frac{h}{2}\right)^{2} + C_{2}$$ $$C_{2} = -\frac{h^{2}}{8\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$ ### Step 5: Velocity Profile (Parabolic) $$v_{x}(y) = -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(\frac{h^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)$$ ### Step 6: Volumetric Flow Rate Integrate the velocity across the gap: $$Q = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} v_{x}(y)\,w\,dy$$ $$Q = -\frac{w}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\int_{-h/2}^{+h/2}\left(\frac{h^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)dy$$ The integral evaluates to: $$\int_{-h/2}^{+h/2}\left(\frac{h^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)dy = \frac{h^{3}}{6}$$ ### Step 7: Final Pressure Drop Equation Substituting: $$Q = -\frac{w\,h^{3}}{12\,\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$ Since $\Delta P = P_{0} - P_{L} = -L\,(dP/dx)$ (assuming linear pressure drop), we obtain the **canonical sheet-die preland equation**: $$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{12\,\mu\,L\,Q}{w\,h^{3}}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Wall Shear Rate and Stress Differentiating the velocity profile and evaluating at the wall ($y = h/2$): $$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \left|\frac{dv_{x}}{dy}\right|_{y=h/2} = \frac{h}{2\mu}\left|\frac{dP}{dx}\right|$$ Substituting $dP/dx = -\Delta P / L$: $$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \frac{h\,\Delta P}{2\mu\,L}$$ Using $\Delta P = 12\mu L Q/(wh^{3})$: $$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \frac{h}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{12\mu L Q}{wh^{3}} = \frac{6\,Q}{w\,h^{2}}$$ $$\tau_{w} = \mu\,\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \frac{6\,\mu\,Q}{w\,h^{2}}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Prel and Pressure Drop > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU-style numerical problem.** **Given:** A coat-hanger sheet die preland has the following dimensions and operating conditions: - Prel and width: $w = 500$ mm $= 0.5$ m - Prel and gap: $h = 3$ mm $= 3 \times 10^{-3}$ m - Prel and length: $L = 50$ mm $= 0.05$ m - Melt viscosity: $\mu = 1500$ Pa·s - Volumetric flow rate: $Q = 7.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s - Melt density: $\rho = 950$ kg/m³ **Required:** Calculate (a) the pressure drop across the preland, (b) the average velocity, (c) the wall shear rate, and (d) verify if the melt flow is laminar. ### Solution **(a) Pressure drop across the preland:** $$\Delta P = \frac{12\,\mu\,L\,Q}{w\,h^{3}}$$ Substituting: $$\Delta P = \frac{12 \times 1500 \times 0.05 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.5 \times (3 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}$$ **Numerator:** $$12 \times 1500 \times 0.05 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0675$$ **Denominator:** $$0.5 \times (3 \times 10^{-3})^{3} = 0.5 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-8} = 1.35 \times 10^{-8}$$ $$\Delta P = \frac{0.0675}{1.35 \times 10^{-8}} = 5.0 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta P = 5.0\ \text{MPa}}$$ **(b) Average velocity:** $$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{w\,h} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.5 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.05\ \text{m/s}$$ $$\boxed{\bar{v} = 50\ \text{mm/s}}$$ **(c) Wall shear rate:** $$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \frac{6\,Q}{w\,h^{2}} = \frac{6 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.5 \times (3 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}$$ $$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-4}}{4.5 \times 10^{-6}} = 100\ \text{s}^{-1}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_{w} = 100\ \text{s}^{-1}}$$ **(d) Flow regime check:** Hydraulic diameter for rectangular channel: $$D_{h} = \frac{4\,A}{P_{wetted}} = \frac{4\,w\,h}{2(w+h)} = \frac{2\,w\,h}{w+h}$$ $$D_{h} = \frac{2 \times 0.5 \times 0.003}{0.5 + 0.003} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{0.503} \approx 5.96 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$$ Reynolds number: $$Re = \frac{\rho\,\bar{v}\,D_{h}}{\mu} = \frac{950 \times 0.05 \times 5.96 \times 10^{-3}}{1500}$$ $$Re = \frac{0.2831}{1500} \approx 1.89 \times 10^{-4}$$ Since $Re \ll 2100$, the flow is **deeply laminar** ✓. > [!TIP] > **Exam Strategy:** Always show all 4 steps (numerator, denominator, substitution, final answer) explicitly. KTU examiners award partial marks for correctly identifying the equation and unit conversions. ## 3.4 Derivation: Manifold Profile for Uniform Exit Velocity The classical coat-hanger manifold analysis assumes **uniform exit velocity** $v_{e}$ from the die. Let $q(x)$ be the leakage flow per unit length into the preland from the manifold. **Flow conservation in manifold:** $$-\frac{dQ_{m}(x)}{dx} = q(x) = v_{e} \cdot h$$ Therefore: $$Q_{m}(x) = Q_{0} - v_{e} \cdot h \cdot x$$ where $x$ is the distance from the centre (inlet). At $x = 0$, $Q_{m} = Q_{0}$ (total flow). At $x = W$ (half-width), $Q_{m} = 0$ (closed end). **Pressure-driven flow in preland:** The pressure at position $x$ in the manifold must overcome the preland pressure drop: $$P_{m}(x) - P_{a} = \frac{12\,\mu\,L\,q(x)}{h^{3}} = \frac{12\,\mu\,L\,v_{e}}{h^{2}}$$ This is **constant** if $v_{e}$ is uniform — implying $P_{m}(x)$ should be constant along the manifold, which is impossible in a real flow. **Compromise (Carley–Smith approach):** Assume the manifold cross-section $A(x)$ varies such that the **residual pressure at every point** is just enough to drive the local preland flow. Using Hagen–Poiseuille for the manifold: $$\frac{dP_{m}}{dx} = -\frac{8\mu Q_{m}(x)}{\pi\,A(x)^{2}} \cdot k_{shape}$$ Combining with flow conservation and integrating yields the **ideal manifold taper**, often approximated as an **exponential or Gaussian profile**: $$\boxed{A(x) = A_{0}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{\alpha\,x^{2}}{L_{m}^{2}}\right)}$$ where $\alpha$ is a dimensionless design constant depending on the rheology. This is the mathematical basis for the "coat-hanger shape." # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sheet Die ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Extruder Adapter
Round to Slit Transition] --> B[Primary Manifold
Circular Tapered Channel] B --> C[Prel and Region
Rectangular Tapered Slot] C --> D[Land Region
Parallel Die Lip] D --> E[Die Exit
Flat Sheet Formation] F[Choker Bar
Adjustable Flow Restrictor] -.-> C G[Die Lip Bolts
50 to 100 Thermal Bolts] -.-> D H[Heater Bands
Cartridge Heaters] -.-> B H -.-> C H -.-> D I[Pressure Transducer] -.-> B J[Thermocouple Sensors] -.-> C E --> K[Sizing Cooling Rolls
Chrome Plated] K --> L[Pull Roll Stack
Haul Off System] L --> M[Slitting and Winding
Finished Sheet Roll] ``` ## 4.2 Coat-Hanger Die — Side View Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SH[COAT HANGER DIE SIDE VIEW] direction TB A1[Inlet from Extruder] --> B1[Manifold Inlet High Pressure] B1 --> C1[Tapered Manifold Radius Decreases] C1 --> D1[Prel and Gap Tapers from h1 to h2] D1 --> E1[Land Region Constant Gap] E1 --> F1[Die Exit Flat Sheet] end style SH fill:#f0f8ff,stroke:#003366 style A1 fill:#ffe4b5 style F1 fill:#90ee90 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Comparison — Sheet Die Types ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Sheet Die Family] --> B[T Slot Die] A --> C[Fishtail Die] A --> D[Coat Hanger Die] A --> E[Curved Manifold Die] B --> B1[Simple Flat Channel] B --> B2[Poor Uniformity] B --> B3[Low Cost Narrow Width] C --> C1[Diverging Plates] C --> C2[Moderate Uniformity] C --> C3[Medium Width] D --> D1[Circular Manifold Plus Prel and] D --> D2[Excellent Uniformity] D --> D3[Industry Standard] E --> E1[Banana Shaped Manifold] E --> E2[Wide Sheets Above 2 m] E --> E3[Specialty Applications] ``` ## 4.4 Sheet Die Process Flow (Downstream Equipment) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sheet Die Exit] --> B[Air Knife or Vacuum Box] B --> C[Primary Cooling Roll] C --> D[Secondary Cooling Roll] D --> E[Tertiary Cooling Roll] E --> F[Edge Trimmer] F --> G[Thickness Gauge] G --> H[Haul Off Pull Rolls] H --> I[Tension Control] I --> J[Windup Station] ``` ## 4.5 Pressure and Velocity Profiles Across a Coat-Hanger Die ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Inlet Center
High Pressure] --> B[Manifold Mid
Medium Pressure] B --> C[Manifold End
Low Pressure] C --> D[Prel and Equalization
Uniform Exit Velocity] style A fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff style B fill:#ffa500,color:#fff style C fill:#ffd93d,color:#000 style D fill:#6bcf7f,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** The **red → green** colour transition represents the **pressure decay** along the manifold (red = high pressure at inlet centre, green = controlled low pressure at die exit). The goal of the tapered manifold is to ensure that **all points along the die exit (D) achieve the same green colour** — i.e., uniform exit velocity. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q: Define a coat-hanger die. List any two advantages it has over a T-slot die for sheet extrusion.** **Model Answer:** A **coat-hanger die** is a sheet extrusion die in which a **tapered primary manifold** (curved pipe of progressively decreasing cross-section) feeds a **tapered preland** (rectangular slot) which then delivers melt to a parallel land region before the die exit. The combination produces a flat, uniformly thick polymer sheet. **Two advantages over T-slot die:** 1. **Better thickness uniformity (±2–3%)** — The tapered manifold compensates for the pressure drop along the preland, ensuring uniform exit velocity across the entire width. A T-slot die typically gives ±10–20% non-uniformity. 2. **Lower residence time and reduced polymer degradation** — The compact manifold-and-preland geometry has smaller internal volume than the long T-slot channel, critical for heat-sensitive polymers like rigid PVC and CPVC. *(Accept also: Adjustable choker bar; lower pressure drop for the same output; easier scale-up to wide widths.)* --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: What is die swell? Name two factors that influence the die swell ratio in polymer extrusion.** **Model Answer:** **Die swell** (also called **extrudate swell** or **B-extrusion swell**) is the phenomenon in which the cross-sectional dimensions of a polymer extrudate emerging from a die become **larger** than the corresponding die dimensions, due to the **recovery of elastic (recoverable) shear strain** stored in the viscoelastic melt during flow through the die. Mathematically, the die swell ratio is: $$B = \frac{h_{\text{after}}}{h_{\text{die lip}}}$$ where $B > 1$ for polymer melts. **Two factors influencing die swell:** 1. **Shear rate** — Higher shear rates in the die land produce more elastic energy storage, hence higher die swell. Empirically, $B \propto \dot{\gamma}^{0.2}$ to $\dot{\gamma}^{0.5}$. 2. **Residence time in the die** — Longer residence time allows partial stress relaxation, slightly reducing swell. Also, the **land length** affects swell: longer land → more relaxation → lower swell. *(Accept also: Melt temperature, polymer molecular weight and distribution, polymer elasticity $N_1/\tau_w$ ratio, flow convergence angle, and the die entrance geometry.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Students answer **ONE full question** (14 marks) by selecting between the two alternatives given below. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a coat-hanger sheet die. List its industrial applications.** **Model Answer:** **Construction:** A coat-hanger die consists of the following components (as shown in Section 4.2 diagram): 1. **Adapter Section** — Connects the extruder outlet (circular) to the die inlet via a smooth transition that minimises flow separation and dead spots. 2. **Manifold** — A circular cross-section channel of **progressively decreasing radius** from the centre to the ends. Typically fabricated from a single piece of drilled metal or by welding a curved pipe. 3. **Prel and Region** — A rectangular channel of gradually decreasing depth (or constant depth with varying width) that connects the manifold to the land. The taper is designed to **equalise pressure** along the length. 4. **Land Region** — A short parallel section (length typically 5–15 × gap) at the die exit. 5. **Choker Bar** — An **adjustable restrictor plate** placed at the junction of manifold and preland, used to fine-tune flow balance. 6. **Die Lip Bolts** — A series of **50–100 thermal or mechanical bolts** distributed across the width for fine thickness adjustment. 7. **Heating System** — Cartridge heaters and bands for temperature control. **Working Principle:** - Molten polymer enters the die through the central inlet at **high pressure** ($P_0$). - The **manifold** distributes the melt laterally (perpendicular to the extruder axis) across the die width. - The **tapered manifold cross-section** ensures that the **pressure drop rate** along the manifold matches the preland requirement at every point. - The **preland** further equalises the velocity profile. - Melt emerges from the **die lip** as a **flat, uniform sheet** at a controlled velocity $v_e$. - Downstream **cooling rolls, pull rolls, edge trimmers, and winders** complete the process. **Industrial Applications:** - Packaging sheets (PP, PET, PS, PVC) - Thermoforming feedstock (HIPS, PP, PET) - Construction sheets (corrugated PP, HDPE) - Stationery and consumer goods - Automotive interior panels (ABS, PP) #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: A coat-hanger die preland has dimensions: width 400 mm, gap 4 mm, length 60 mm. The melt (LDPE) has viscosity 2000 Pa·s and the volumetric flow rate is $1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s. Calculate the pressure drop, average velocity, and wall shear rate in the preland.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $w = 400$ mm $= 0.4$ m - $h = 4$ mm $= 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m - $L = 60$ mm $= 0.06$ m - $\mu = 2000$ Pa·s - $Q = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s **Step 1: Pressure drop across the preland** $$\Delta P = \frac{12\,\mu\,L\,Q}{w\,h^{3}}$$ $$\Delta P = \frac{12 \times 2000 \times 0.06 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.4 \times (4 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}$$ Numerator: $$12 \times 2000 \times 0.06 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4} = 1.44$$ Denominator: $$0.4 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-8} = 2.56 \times 10^{-8}$$ $$\Delta P = \frac{1.44}{2.56 \times 10^{-8}} = 5.625 \times 10^{7}\ \text{Pa}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta P = 56.25\ \text{MPa}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall #1:** A common error is forgetting to convert mm to m. Using $h = 4$ mm directly gives wrong $\Delta P$ by a factor of $10^{9}$. Always convert all dimensions to SI units (metres, seconds) before substituting. **Step 2: Average velocity** $$\bar{v} = \frac{Q}{w\,h} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.4 \times 4 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.25 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m/s}$$ $$\boxed{\bar{v} = 62.5\ \text{mm/s}}$$ **Step 3: Wall shear rate** $$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \frac{6\,Q}{w\,h^{2}} = \frac{6 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{0.4 \times (4 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}$$ $$\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-4}}{6.4 \times 10^{-6}} = 93.75\ \text{s}^{-1}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_{w} \approx 93.75\ \text{s}^{-1}}$$ **Step 4: Shear stress at wall** $$\tau_{w} = \mu\,\dot{\gamma}_{w} = 2000 \times 93.75 = 1.875 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Pa} = 0.1875\ \text{MPa}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Many students calculate wall shear rate as $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \bar{v}/h$ (which is INCORRECT — this gives a maximum velocity, not wall shear rate). The correct relation is $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = 6Q/(wh^{2})$, which is **three times** the erroneous value. **Conclusion:** The pressure drop of 56.25 MPa is on the higher side — for production use, the preland length or gap should be increased to bring $\Delta P$ below 10 MPa, which is the recommended upper limit for LDPE. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Q: Explain in detail the different types of sheet dies used in polymer processing, with neat diagrams. Compare their relative performance.** **Model Answer:** Sheet dies are classified into four major types based on the flow distribution principle. The diagrams are provided in Section 4.3. **1. T-Slot Die (Slit Die):** - **Construction:** A simple rectangular slit of constant cross-section machined into a die body. Melt enters from one end and flows in a single direction. - **Flow pattern:** Strongly non-uniform — high pressure at inlet, dead end at the far end. - **Uniformity:** Poor (typically ±15–25% thickness variation). - **Applications:** Low-viscosity polymers, narrow sheets (<300 mm), laboratory use. **2. Fishtail Die:** - **Construction:** Two flat plates diverging from a circular inlet to a wide rectangular exit, forming a triangular (fishtail) shape. - **Flow pattern:** Diverging flow leads to lateral spreading; better than T-slot but velocity profile is still parabolic. - **Uniformity:** Moderate (±5–10% thickness variation). - **Applications:** Medium-width sheets (300–800 mm), short production runs. **3. Coat-Hanger Die:** - **Construction:** Combines a **circular tapered manifold** with a **rectangular tapered preland** and parallel land. The manifold radius decreases from centre to end. - **Flow pattern:** Uniform exit velocity achieved by matching manifold pressure profile to preland requirement. - **Uniformity:** Excellent (±2–3% thickness variation, industry benchmark). - **Applications:** Most commercial sheet extrusion lines, widths up to 3 m. **4. Curved Manifold Die:** - **Construction:** The manifold is curved (banana or arc-shaped) rather than straight, to accommodate very wide sheets within a compact die. - **Flow pattern:** Similar to coat-hanger but with curved geometry. - **Uniformity:** Excellent (±2–4%). - **Applications:** Very wide sheets (>2 m), such as geomembranes and large thermoforming stock. **Performance Comparison Table:** | Die Type | Uniformity | Max Width | Cost | Polymer Suitability | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | T-Slot | Poor | <300 mm | Very Low | Low-viscosity only | | Fishtail | Moderate | 300–800 mm | Low | General purpose | | Coat-Hanger | Excellent | Up to 3 m | High | Universal, incl. heat-sensitive | | Curved Manifold | Excellent | >2 m | Very High | Specialty wide sheets | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Coat-hanger die is the most commonly used** in industry because it offers the best trade-off between **uniformity, residence time, pressure drop, and cost**. It is the preferred choice for over 85% of commercial sheet extrusion lines. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Q: Derive the expression for the pressure drop across a rectangular preland for a Newtonian polymer melt. List the assumptions clearly.** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. The polymer melt is **Newtonian** (viscosity $\mu$ is constant, independent of shear rate). 2. The flow is **steady, laminar, and fully-developed**. 3. The flow is **isothermal** (no temperature variation across the die). 4. The melt is **incompressible** (constant density). 5. The channel is a **wide rectangular slit** ($w \gg h$), so end effects in the width direction are negligible. 6. The fluid adheres to the **no-slip condition** at the walls. 7. **Inertial and gravitational forces** are negligible compared to viscous forces (low Reynolds number). 8. The flow is **unidirectional** along the channel length (no secondary flows or recirculation). 9. The pressure gradient $\frac{dP}{dx}$ is **constant** along the channel length. **Derivation (as detailed in Section 3.1):** Starting from the Navier–Stokes equation simplified for the wide-slit assumption: $$\frac{dP}{dx} = \mu\,\frac{d^{2} v_{x}}{dy^{2}}$$ **Step 1 — Integrate once with respect to $y$:** $$\frac{d v_{x}}{dy} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\,y + C_{1}$$ **Step 2 — Apply symmetry BC at $y = 0$ (centreline), $dv_x/dy = 0$:** $$C_{1} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d v_{x}}{dy} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\,y$$ **Step 3 — Integrate again:** $$v_{x}(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\,y^{2} + C_{2}$$ **Step 4 — Apply no-slip BC at $y = h/2$, $v_x = 0$:** $$C_{2} = -\frac{h^{2}}{8\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$ So the velocity profile is: $$v_{x}(y) = -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(\frac{h^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Calculate volumetric flow rate:** $$Q = \int_{-h/2}^{+h/2} v_{x}(y)\,w\,dy = -\frac{w}{2\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\int_{-h/2}^{+h/2}\left(\frac{h^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)dy$$ The integral equals $\frac{h^{3}}{6}$: $$Q = -\frac{w\,h^{3}}{12\,\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$ **Step 6 — Solve for pressure drop:** Using $\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{\Delta P}{L}$: $$Q = \frac{w\,h^{3}}{12\,\mu\,L}\,\Delta P$$ Rearranging: $$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{12\,\mu\,L\,Q}{w\,h^{3}}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** Students often **omit the assumptions** in this question. KTU examiners typically deduct **1–2 marks** for missing the list of assumptions. Always state them explicitly before starting the derivation. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #4:** In numerical problems, students frequently confuse the **centreline** (where $y = 0$ and shear rate is zero) with the **wall** (where $y = h/2$ and shear rate is maximum). The wall shear rate formula $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = 6Q/(wh^{2})$ is for the **wall**, not the centre. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Sheet Die Questions:** > > 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Using $h$ in mm instead of m in the pressure drop equation. This single error causes $\Delta P$ to be off by **9 orders of magnitude**. > > 2. **Confusing wall and centreline shear rate** — Wall shear rate is **3× the average** gradient (factor of 6, not 2). > > 3. **Forgetting assumptions in derivations** — Always list 6–9 explicit assumptions. Marks are reserved for this in every derivation question. > > 4. **Misidentifying the die type** — A fishtail die is **NOT** the same as a coat-hanger die. The presence of a **circular manifold** is the key distinguishing feature. > > 5. **Ignoring die swell** — When asked for the **die lip gap** to produce a sheet of given thickness, students forget to divide by the swell ratio. For a 2 mm final sheet with $B = 1.15$, the die gap must be $h_{die} = 2/1.15 = 1.74$ mm. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Sheet Dies (Module 2):** - [x] **Sheet die** converts cylindrical melt to flat sheet; goal is **uniform exit velocity** across width. - [x] Four main types: **T-Slot, Fishtail, Coat-Hanger, Curved Manifold** — in increasing order of uniformity and cost. - [x] **Coat-hanger die** is the industry standard; uses a **tapered circular manifold + tapered rectangular preland**. - [x] Components: **adapter, manifold, preland, land, choker bar, die lip bolts, heaters, sensors**. - [x] **Prel and pressure drop (Newtonian):** $\Delta P = \dfrac{12\mu L Q}{w h^{3}}$ — the most-tested equation. - [x] **Wall shear rate:** $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{6Q}{w h^{2}}$; **average velocity:** $\bar{v} = \dfrac{Q}{w h}$. - [x] **Wall shear stress:** $\tau_{w} = \dfrac{6\mu Q}{w h^{2}}$. - [x] **Manifold pressure drop (Hagen-Poiseuille):** $\Delta P_{m} = \dfrac{8\mu L_{m} Q_{m}}{\pi R^{4}}$. - [x] **Die swell ratio:** $B = h_{sheet}/h_{die} > 1$ for viscoelastic melts. - [x] **Design limits:** $\Delta P \leq 5$–$10$ MPa for shear-sensitive polymers; $\bar{t}_{res} \leq 60$–$300$ s for thermal-sensitive polymers. - [x] **Tapered manifold** is needed to match pressure profile along preland and produce uniform exit velocity. - [x] **Choker bar** is the adjustable flow restrictor used to fine-tune velocity balance. - [x] **Die lip bolts** (50–100 bolts) provide fine thickness adjustment (often thermal push-pull bolts). - [x] **Sheet thickness control** = die lip gap × die swell; downstream **gauges** monitor actual thickness. - [x] Always check **Reynolds number** to confirm laminar flow ($Re \ll 2100$). - [x] **Coat-hanger cross-section shape** comes from Gaussian/exponential manifold profile $A(x) = A_0 \exp(-\alpha x^{2}/L_{m}^{2})$. --- > **End of Module 2 — Sheet Dies Notes** | *PCPOT503 | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Tube Dies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tube Dies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Tube Die** (also called a **Tubing Die** or **Pipe Die**) is a specialized polymer extrusion die designed to continuously produce hollow, tubular cross-sectioned plastic products such as rigid PVC pipes, flexible hoses, drinking straws, medical catheters, and inflatable films. It achieves this by forcing a thermoplastic melt to flow around a central, torpedo-shaped body called a **mandrel** (or torpedo) that is held in coaxial alignment within an outer die sleeve called the **die body** or **die land**. The resulting annular cross-section of the extrudate determines the **outer diameter (OD)** and **inner diameter (ID)** of the final tube, while **vacuum calibration** or **internal pressure (air)** downstream is used to fix the final dimensions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPOT503 – Module 2):**
> Tube dies are studied as a critical subset of **profile extrusion dies**. A student must be able to (a) label the anatomy of the die, (b) distinguish between **spider, basket, spiral, and cross-head dies**, and (c) relate die geometry to **flow balance, die swell, and land length**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Definition (verbatim expected answer):**
> *"A tube die is a circular extrusion die in which the polymer melt is divided and re-united as it flows around a centrally supported mandrel, leaving the die as a continuous hollow tube whose wall thickness and diameter are governed by the annular gap, the land length, and the downstream calibration system."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **toothpaste tube** being squeezed from the closed end. The flat paste coming out of the nozzle is actually a **thin-walled hollow cylinder** – the toothpaste contacts the outside of the nozzle pin and the inside of the cap, exiting as a ring. Now imagine that pin is **anchored in the middle of a circular channel** by 2 or 3 thin metal arms (the "spiders") and you continuously push **melted plastic** instead of paste. The plastic flows around the spiders, rejoins on the far side, and exits as a continuous seamless tube.

Another everyday analogy is a **cake-decorating piping nozzle** that extrudes cream in a ring shape. The **outer cone** of the nozzle sets the outer wall of the cream rope, and the **inner cone** sets the inner wall. The cream flowing between the two cones forms a hollow tube that collapses under its own weight.

- **The annulus (gap) between mandrel and die body** → controls wall thickness
- **The land length (length of the parallel cylindrical section)** → controls surface finish and dimensional stability
- **The spiders / flow distributors** → keep the mandrel centered and split the flow evenly
- **Vacuum sizing tank (downstream)** → "freezes" the final diameter and roundness

> [!TIP]
> **Why does the melt rejoin cleanly without a visible seam?**
> The high **land length-to-gap ratio** (typically **L/h ≥ 10** for polyolefins) and the **welding pressure** at the spider junctions cause **stress-induced molecular diffusion (autohesion)** across the weld lines, restoring mechanical strength.

## 1.3 Key Metrics, Physical Constants & Standard Nomenclature

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range / Standard | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D_o$ | Die outlet outer diameter | **20 – 800** | mm |
| $D_i$ | Mandrel outer diameter (at tip) | $D_i = D_o - 2t$ | mm |
| $t$ | Die gap (annular wall thickness) | **0.5 – 30** | mm |
| $L$ | Die land length | $L = (10\text{ to }40)\times t$ | mm |
| $\Delta P$ | Pressure drop across die | **5 – 15** | MPa |
| $Q$ | Volumetric flow rate | $10^{-5}\text{ to }10^{-1}$ | m³/s |
| $\mu$ | Melt viscosity (non-Newtonian) | $10^2\text{ to }10^5$ | Pa·s |
| $B$ | Die swell ratio | **1.00 – 1.30** | – |
| $n$ | Power-law index | **0.2 – 0.8** | – |
| $\dot{\gamma}$ | Apparent shear rate | $10^1\text{ to }10^4$ | s⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> The melt is **non-Newtonian** and follows a **power-law (Ostwald–de Waele) viscosity model**:
> $$\eta(\dot{\gamma}) \;=\; K\,\dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$$
> where $K$ is the **consistency index** and $n$ is the **power-law index**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

A tube die is not a single uniform channel – it is a **three-zone system**. Understanding the function of each zone is the key to the KTU Module 2 expected answer.

### Zone 1 – Flow Distribution / Manifold Zone
- The melt enters from the extruder (single circular stream).
- It must be **split and re-distributed** into a perfectly **axisymmetric annular flow**.
- Components: **breaker plate, screen pack, manifold, spider legs, or spiral distributor**.
- Pressure drop here is intentionally high (≈ **30 – 50 %** of total ΔP) to achieve flow uniformity.

### Zone 2 – Converging / Pre-land Zone (Approach Region)
- Cross-section gradually shrinks from a thick annular ring → final thin annulus.
- The melt is **accelerated**, and **elastic energy** is stored.
- This is the primary site of **die swell** generation because the elastic strain is recovered at the exit.

### Zone 3 – Land / Parallel Zone
- A short, **straight cylindrical channel** of constant gap $t$ and length $L$.
- Functions: (i) re-aligns flow streamlines, (ii) erases residual weld lines from the spiders, (iii) imposes a fully developed **Hagen–Poiseuille-type** velocity profile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Spider Weld Line Problem:**
> When the melt passes around the spider legs, the flow is split. After re-uniting, a **stagnation line** (weld line) is formed where the streams meet head-on. If the **land length L** is too short, the extrudate will show a visible **weld mark**, lower tensile strength, and possible bursting under pressure (critical for pressure pipes – **PE-100, PVC-U, PEX**).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,(D_o - D_i)\,t^{3}\,\Delta P}{12\,\mu\,L}$ | Simplified **thin-gap Hagen–Poiseuille** for tube; used in elementary board problems |
| 2 | $Q \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,\Delta P}{8\,\mu\,L}\!\left[\,R_{o}^{4} - R_{i}^{4} - \dfrac{(R_{o}^{2}-R_{i}^{2})^{2}}{\ln(R_o/R_i)}\,\right]$ | **Exact annular Hagen–Poiseuille** flow (Newtonian); accurate for thick walls |
| 3 | $\dot{\gamma}_w \;=\; \dfrac{6\,Q}{w\,t^{2}}\;=\;\dfrac{6\,Q}{\pi\,(D_o + D_i)\,t^{2}}$ | **Apparent wall shear rate** in a thin annular gap of wetted perimeter $w$ |
| 4 | $\eta(\dot{\gamma}) \;=\; K\,\dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$ | **Power-law viscosity model** (Ostwald–de Waele) |
| 5 | $\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{2\,K\,L}{t}\!\left(\dfrac{6Q}{\pi(D_o+D_i)t^{2}}\right)^{n}\!\!\left(\dfrac{2n+1}{3n}\right)^{n}$ | **Pressure drop for a power-law fluid in a thin slit** (adapted to annulus) |
| 6 | $B \;=\; \dfrac{D_{\text{extrudate}}}{D_{\text{die}}}$ | **Die swell ratio**; $B > 1$ for viscoelastic melts |
| 7 | $L_{\text{min}} \;=\; (10\text{ to }40)\,t$ | **Minimum land length** to suppress weld lines |
| 8 | $\tau_w \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P\,t}{2L}$ | **Wall shear stress** in thin-gap approximation |
| 9 | $\dot{V}_{\text{calibration}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4}\!\left(D_{\text{final}}^{\,2} - D_{\text{inner}}^{\,2}\right) v_{\text{pull}}$ | **Volumetric continuity** for haul-off speed $v_{\text{pull}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING NOTE (K–T–U Cheat Sheet Rule):**
> Never embed raw vertical bars `|` inside the table cells (they break Markdown rendering). Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value-style notation, e.g. $\vert \dot{\gamma} \vert$, as shown in the equations above.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Tube Die | Critical Design Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Plumbing / Civil** | PVC-U, HDPE pressure pipes, PPR hot-water pipes | Pressure rating (PN 10 / PN 16), weld-line strength |
| **Medical** | IV tubes, catheters, blood bags | Biocompatibility, ultra-tight OD tolerance (±0.05 mm) |
| **Packaging** | Drinking straws, toothpaste tubes, cosmetic sleeves | Print registration, thin wall (down to **0.1 mm**) |
| **Automotive** | Fuel lines, wiring harness conduits | Chemical resistance, dimensional stability at 120 °C |
| **Agriculture** | Drip irrigation laterals, lay-flat hoses | UV resistance, emitter punching on moving tube |
| **Industrial** | Pneumatic tubing, spiral-reinforced hoses | Burst pressure, concentricity > **95 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Concentricity** is the ratio $\dfrac{\text{min wall thickness}}{\text{max wall thickness}} \times 100\%$. KTU problems frequently ask: *“If the mandrel is offset by $\delta$, what is the percentage wall-thickness variation?”*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thin-Gap Hagen–Poiseuille for an Annular Tube Die

This is the **single most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Module 2. We derive it from the **Navier–Stokes equation** simplified for steady, fully developed, laminar, isothermal, Newtonian flow in a thin annular gap.

**Step 1 — Geometric Simplification of the Annulus**

We *un-wrap* the thin annulus into a flat rectangular slit of:
- width $w$ = mean circumference = $\pi\,(D_o + D_i)$
- thickness $h = t = (D_o - D_i)/2$
- length = $L$ (the land length)

> **Justification:** When $t \ll D_m$ (true for almost all extrusion tube dies, since $D_m/t > 10$), the curvature is negligible and the slit approximation is accurate to within 2 – 3 %.

**Step 2 — Set up the Navier–Stokes Equation in the Slit**

For flow along the $x$-direction (axial) with velocity $u(z)$ where $z$ is the cross-slit (gap) coordinate, the steady, fully-developed momentum equation reduces to:

$$0 \;=\; -\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} \;+\; \mu\,\frac{d^{2}u}{dz^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Twice with No-Slip Boundary Conditions**

$$\frac{d^{2}u}{dz^{2}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\mu}\,\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

**First integration:**

$$\frac{du}{dz} \;=\; \frac{1}{\mu}\,\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\,z \;+\; C_1$$

**Second integration:**

$$u(z) \;=\; \frac{1}{2\mu}\,\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\,z^{2} \;+\; C_1 z \;+\; C_2$$

Applying **no-slip** at the two walls $z = \pm t/2$:

$$u\!\left(\pm\frac{t}{2}\right) \;=\; 0$$

By symmetry, the velocity profile is **parabolic** and symmetric, hence $C_1 = 0$. Then:

$$0 \;=\; \frac{1}{2\mu}\,\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{t}{2}\right)^{2} + C_2 \;\Longrightarrow\; C_2 \;=\; -\frac{1}{8\mu}\,\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\,t^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Velocity Profile**

$$u(z) \;=\; -\frac{1}{2\mu}\,\frac{\partial P}{\partial x}\!\left[\left(\frac{t}{2}\right)^{2} - z^{2}\right]$$

The **pressure gradient is negative** (pressure drops in the flow direction), so for clarity we set $\Delta P / L \;=\; -\,\partial P / \partial x$:

$$u(z) \;=\; \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L}\!\left[\left(\frac{t}{2}\right)^{2} - z^{2}\right]$$

The **maximum velocity** is at $z = 0$:

$$u_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\Delta P\,t^{2}}{8\mu L}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate to get the Volumetric Flow Rate**

$$Q \;=\; w\!\int_{-t/2}^{+t/2} u(z)\,dz \;=\; w\!\int_{-t/2}^{+t/2} \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L}\!\left[\left(\frac{t}{2}\right)^{2} - z^{2}\right]dz$$

Evaluating the integral (the integrand is even):

$$\int_{-t/2}^{+t/2}\!\left[\left(\frac{t}{2}\right)^{2} - z^{2}\right]dz \;=\; \frac{t^{3}}{6}$$

Therefore:

$$Q \;=\; \frac{w\,t^{3}\,\Delta P}{12\,\mu L}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Wetted Perimeter of the Annulus**

$w \;=\; \pi\,(D_o + D_i)$, yielding the **K–T–U board equation**:

$$\boxed{\,Q \;=\; \frac{\pi\,(D_o - D_i)\,t^{3}\,\Delta P}{12\,\mu L}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation of Each Variable:**
> - $Q$ scales as $t^{3}$ → a 2× increase in wall gap produces an **8× increase in flow**.
> - $Q$ scales as $1/L$ → doubling the land length **halves** the flow at the same pressure.
> - $Q$ scales as $1/\mu$ → at higher shear rates (and therefore lower viscosity) the throughput rises sharply — this is why **output is highly temperature-sensitive**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pressure Drop for a Power-Law Fluid (Non-Newtonian Correction)

Because polymer melts are **shear-thinning** ($n < 1$), the Newtonian result above overestimates $\Delta P$. We use the **Ostwald–de Waele** model: $\tau \;=\; K\,\dot{\gamma}^{\,n}$.

**Step 1 — Wall Shear Stress (Mechanical Equilibrium)**

For a slit channel of width $w$, thickness $t$, length $L$:

$$\tau_w \;=\; \frac{\Delta P\,t}{2L}$$

**Step 2 — Apparent Wall Shear Rate**

For a power-law fluid in a slit:

$$\dot{\gamma}_w \;=\; \left(\frac{2n+1}{3n}\right)\frac{6Q}{w\,t^{2}}$$

> **Note:** The bracketed term is a correction factor that equals 1 for $n=1$ (Newtonian) and rises to **1.5** for $n=0.5$ (typical LDPE).

**Step 3 — Power-Law Stress at the Wall**

$$\tau_w \;=\; K\,\dot{\gamma}_w^{\,n}$$

**Step 4 — Combine to Solve for $\Delta P$**

Substituting $\tau_w$:

$$\frac{\Delta P\,t}{2L} \;=\; K\!\left[\left(\frac{2n+1}{3n}\right)\frac{6Q}{w\,t^{2}}\right]^{n}$$

Solving for $\Delta P$:

$$\boxed{\,\Delta P \;=\; \frac{2KL}{t}\!\left[\left(\frac{2n+1}{3n}\right)\frac{6Q}{w\,t^{2}}\right]^{n}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **circular annulus** of mean diameter $D_m = D_o - t$, replace the slit wetted perimeter $w$ with $\pi D_m$ and the slit thickness $t$ with the same $t$. This gives the KTU working equation for **non-Newtonian tube-die design**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Die Swell (Extrudate Swell) Phenomenological Estimate

A rigorous first-principles model of die swell is complex, but the **KTU expected board-level answer** is the **Tanner–Bagley phenomenological correlation**:

$$B \;=\; 1 \;+\; \left[\left(\frac{\tau_w}{G}\right)^{2} - \frac{\tau_w}{G}\right]^{1/6}$$

where:
- $B = D_{\text{extrudate}}/D_{\text{die}}$ is the swell ratio
- $\tau_w$ is the wall shear stress in the die
- $G$ is the **elastic modulus** of the melt (a material property; typical $G$ for LDPE ≈ **0.1 – 0.3 MPa**, for HDPE ≈ **0.5 – 1.0 MPa**)

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. As $\tau_w \to 0$ (creeping flow), $B \to 1$ → no swell.
2. As $\tau_w \to G$, $B \to 1.26$ (the famous **"1/3 power law"** limit, $B \propto \tau_w^{1/3}$).
3. In practice, $B$ for a tube die is measured after the extrudate cools to a stress-free state; for **HDPE pipes at industrial throughputs**, $B \approx$ **1.10 – 1.20**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Design implication:** To produce a tube of **final OD = 100 mm**, the die must be machined to $D_o = 100 / B \approx$ **83 – 91 mm**. This is called the **die-backsizing** calculation, and KTU frequently asks: *“If the swell ratio of HDPE is 1.15, what die diameter is required to obtain a 110 mm pipe?”*

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A tube die has $D_o = 50\,\text{mm}$, $D_i = 44\,\text{mm}$, land length $L = 60\,\text{mm}$. The melt viscosity (treated as Newtonian for simplicity) is $\mu = 5 \times 10^{3}\,\text{Pa·s}$. If the pressure drop across the die is $\Delta P = 8\,\text{MPa}$, calculate (a) the wall gap $t$, (b) the volumetric flow rate $Q$, and (c) the average velocity of the extrudate at the die exit.

**Given:**

$$D_o = 50 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}, \quad D_i = 44 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$$

$$L = 60 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}, \quad \mu = 5 \times 10^{3}\,\text{Pa·s}, \quad \Delta P = 8 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa}$$

**Part (a) — Wall gap:**

$$t \;=\; \frac{D_o - D_i}{2} \;=\; \frac{(50-44)\times 10^{-3}}{2}$$

$$t \;=\; 3 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} \;=\; 3\,\text{mm}$$

**Part (b) — Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q \;=\; \frac{\pi\,(D_o - D_i)\,t^{3}\,\Delta P}{12\,\mu L}$$

Numerator:

$$N \;=\; \pi \times (6 \times 10^{-3}) \times (3 \times 10^{-3})^{3} \times (8 \times 10^{6})$$

$$N \;=\; \pi \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times 27 \times 10^{-9} \times 8 \times 10^{6}$$

$$N \;=\; \pi \times 1296 \times 10^{-6} \;\approx\; 4.071 \times 10^{-3}$$

Denominator:

$$D \;=\; 12 \times 5 \times 10^{3} \times 60 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 12 \times 5 \times 60 \;=\; 3600$$

$$Q \;=\; \frac{4.071 \times 10^{-3}}{3600} \;\approx\; 1.131 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$Q \;\approx\; 1.13\,\text{cm}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Part (c) — Average exit velocity:**

$$A_{\text{annulus}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\!\left(D_o^{2} - D_i^{2}\right) \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\!\left(50^{2} - 44^{2}\right)\times 10^{-6}$$

$$A_{\text{annulus}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\times (2500 - 1924) \times 10^{-6} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4}\times 576 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$A_{\text{annulus}} \;\approx\; 4.524 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{2}$$

$$v_{\text{avg}} \;=\; \frac{Q}{A} \;=\; \frac{1.131 \times 10^{-6}}{4.524 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{\,v_{\text{avg}} \;\approx\; 2.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m/s} \;=\; 2.5\,\text{mm/s}\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** A haul-off speed of about **10 – 15 mm/s** is typical for such a die because the **draw-down ratio** (haul-off / die-exit velocity) is normally **3 – 6** to give a **swell-compensated, dimensionally stable pipe**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Tube Die Extrusion Line

This diagram is a **block-level functional architecture** showing how every component of the tube-die system interacts. It is used in Module 2 to teach students the *system view* of the process.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[EXTRUDER\nHOPPER AND BARREL] --> B[BREAKER PLATE\nAND SCREEN PACK]
    B --> C[ADAPTER\nAND TRANSITION BLOCK]
    C --> D[DIE MANIFOLD\nSPIRAL OR CHESTNUT]
    D --> E[SPIDER LEGS\n3 OR 4 MANDREL SUPPORTS]
    E --> F[CONVERGING\nAPPROACH ZONE]
    F --> G[DIE LAND\nPARALLEL ANNULUS]
    G --> H[DIE EXIT\nTUBE EXTRUDATE]
    H --> I[VACUUM SIZING TANK\nCALIBRATION SLEEVE]
    I --> J[COOLING TROUGH\nSPRAY OR IMMERSION]
    J --> K[HAUL OFF UNIT\nCATERPULLER OR BELT]
    K --> L[COUTING SAW\nOR GUILLOTINE]
    L --> M[STACKING TABLE\nFINISHED PIPE]

    subgraph AFTERDIE[POST DIE PROCESSING]
        I
        J
        K
        L
        M
    end

    subgraph INTERNAL[INTERNAL AIR LINE]
        N[COMPRESSED AIR\nINLET THROUGH MANDREL] --> O[AIR PLUG\nINTERNAL PRESSURE]
        O --> H
    end
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Classification of Tube Dies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[TUBE DIE\nCLASSIFICATION] --> S1[AXIAL TUBE DIE\nIN LINE FLOW]
    R --> S2[CROSS HEAD TUBE DIE\n90 DEGREE FLOW]
    R --> S3[OFFSET TUBE DIE\nCOEXTRUSION JACKET]

    S1 --> T1A[SPIDER TYPE\n3 OR 4 SUPPORT ARMS]
    S1 --> T1B[BASKET TYPE\nCURVED MANDREL BASKET]
    S1 --> T1C[SPIRAL MANDREL\nDISTRIBUTOR]
    S1 --> T1D[COATHANGER\nMODIFIED FOR CIRCLE]

    S2 --> T2A[USED FOR\nWIRE CABLE COATING]
    S2 --> T2B[USED FOR\nRUBBER HOSE]

    S3 --> T3A[USED FOR\nFOAM INSULATION]
    S3 --> T3B[USED FOR\nSTEEL PIPE LINING]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Matrix — Spider vs. Basket vs. Spiral Tube Dies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SD[SPIDER DIE]
        SDA[LAND LENGTH LONG\nWELD LINES PRESENT] --> SDB[FLOW DISTRIBUTION\nPOOR TO MODERATE]
    end

    subgraph BD[BASKET DIE]
        BDA[NO WELD LINES\nSMOOTH INNER SURFACE] --> BDB[USED FOR\nFLEXIBLE TUBING]
    end

    subgraph SPD[SPIRAL MANDREL DIE]
        SPDA[BEST FLOW UNIFORMITY\nNO STAGNATION] --> SPDB[USED FOR\nLARGE DIAMETER PIPES]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this as follows:** A *spider die* has long land to mask weld lines but flow is uneven; a *basket die* has no spiders (the basket itself is a perforated shell that smooths flow) and is preferred for medical tubing; a *spiral mandrel die* uses helical channels that distribute flow uniformly and is the workhorse for large-bore HDPE and PVC pipes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Define a tube die. With a neat sketch, list its main components and state the function of the *land*.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **tube die** is an extrusion tooling that produces a continuous hollow plastic tube by forcing melt around a centrally located **mandrel** supported by **spider legs** within a circular die body. *(1 mark)*

**Main components:** *(1 mark)*
1. Breaker plate and screen pack
2. Manifold / flow distributor
3. Mandrel (torpedo) with spider supports
4. Converging approach zone
5. Land (parallel cylindrical section)
6. Outer die body (die ring)

**Function of the land:** *(1 mark)*
The land is a straight, parallel cylindrical section of length $L = (10 \text{ to } 40) t$ that re-develops a uniform velocity profile, **erases weld lines** formed at the spider junctions, controls **die swell**, and provides a **smooth surface finish**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain *die swell* in tube extrusion. Why is the die diameter always *less* than the desired final tube diameter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Die swell** (or **extrudate swell**) is the phenomenon in which the diameter and wall thickness of the extrudate increase *after* leaving the die due to the **recovery of elastic (stored) strain** in the viscoelastic polymer melt. *(1 mark)*

It is characterized by the swell ratio $B = D_{\text{extrudate}} / D_{\text{die}} > 1$. *(1 mark)*

The die diameter is therefore intentionally machined **smaller** than the target final diameter. For example, to obtain a 110 mm HDPE pipe with a typical swell ratio $B = 1.15$, the die exit diameter is set to $D_o = 110/1.15 \approx$ **95.6 mm**. The final diameter is then locked in by the **vacuum calibration tank**. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 marks each)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, 14 marks]

**Q.A (a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of a **spider-type tube die**. Discuss the role of the spider legs and the land length in determining the quality of the extrudate. *(7 marks)*

**Q.A (b)** Derive an expression for the **volumetric flow rate** of a Newtonian polymer melt through a thin annular tube die. A tube die has $D_o = 60$ mm, $D_i = 52$ mm, and land length $L = 80$ mm. The melt viscosity is $\mu = 4 \times 10^{3}$ Pa·s and the pressure drop is $\Delta P = 10$ MPa. Calculate the volumetric flow rate and the average exit velocity. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 marks]

1. **Construction (3 marks):** The spider-type tube die consists of:
   - **Outer die body** (a thick cylindrical steel block with a central bore).
   - **Mandrel (torpedo)** coaxial with the bore, held in place by **3 or 4 thin metal "spider legs"** that radiate from the rear of the die.
   - **Breaker plate and screen pack** at the inlet to remove spirality and pressure fluctuations.
   - **Land** – a parallel cylindrical section of length $L$ before the exit.

   *(A neat sketch with the die body, mandrel, spider legs, land, and the annular exit must accompany the answer.)*

2. **Working (2 marks):** Polymer melt enters axially, hits the breaker plate, flows through the screen pack, and divides around the mandrel by passing over the spider legs. The streams **re-unite on the downstream side of each leg**, forming **weld lines**. The melt then accelerates through the converging zone, fully develops its velocity profile in the **land**, and exits as a continuous tube. The tube is drawn into a **vacuum calibration tank** for sizing and cooling.

3. **Role of spider legs (1 mark):** Spider legs *support the mandrel against the hydrodynamic pressure* of the melt, but they *create weld lines*. For pressure-pipe applications (PE-100, PVC-U) the spider leg region must be followed by an adequate land length to **anneal the welds**.

4. **Role of land length (1 mark):** Land length must satisfy $L \geq 10\,t$ to **(i) erase weld lines**, **(ii) ensure fully developed flow**, and **(iii) suppress die swell** by giving the elastic strain time to relax. Long lands increase pressure drop, however, so designers balance $L$ against $\Delta P$.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 marks]

**Derivation (4 marks):** Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete derivation. The final board-ready expression is:

$$Q \;=\; \frac{\pi\,(D_o - D_i)\,t^{3}\,\Delta P}{12\,\mu L}$$

**Calculation (3 marks):**

- Wall gap: $t = (60 - 52)/2 = 4\,\text{mm} = 4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- $D_o - D_i = 8 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- Numerator:

$$N \;=\; \pi \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \times (4 \times 10^{-3})^{3} \times 10 \times 10^{6}$$

$$N \;=\; \pi \times 8 \times 64 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \;=\; 16088 \times 10^{-3} \;\approx\; 16.09$$

- Denominator: $D = 12 \times 4 \times 10^{3} \times 80 \times 10^{-3} = 3840$

$$Q \;\approx\; \frac{16.09}{3840} \;\approx\; 4.19 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \;\approx\; 4.2\,\text{L/s}$$

- Annular area: $A = \frac{\pi}{4}(60^2 - 52^2)\times 10^{-6} = \frac{\pi}{4}\times 896 \times 10^{-6} \approx 7.04 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{2}$
- Average exit velocity:

$$v_{\text{avg}} \;=\; \frac{Q}{A} \;=\; \frac{4.19 \times 10^{-3}}{7.04 \times 10^{-4}} \;\approx\; 5.95\,\text{m/s}$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 marks]

**Q.B (a)** Compare **spider, basket, and spiral-mandrel tube dies** with neat sketches. State two industrial applications of each. *(7 marks)*

**Q.B (b)** A non-Newtonian power-law melt with $K = 5 \times 10^{3}$ Pa·s$^n$ and $n = 0.4$ is extruded through a tube die of mean diameter $D_m = 80$ mm, annular gap $t = 5$ mm, and land length $L = 100$ mm. The volumetric flow rate is $Q = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s. Calculate the **pressure drop** and the **wall shear rate** across the die. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 marks]

| Feature | Spider Die | Basket Die | Spiral Mandrel Die |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Construction** *(2 marks)* | Mandrel held by 3–4 metal arms | Mandrel replaced by a *perforated basket*; no discrete supports | Mandrel replaced by a *spiral channel distributor* |
| **Weld lines** *(1 mark)* | Present (3 or 4 weld lines) | None – smooth flow | None – helical flow avoids stagnation |
| **Land length needed** *(1 mark)* | Long ($L \geq 20 t$) to mask welds | Short ($L \geq 5 t$) | Moderate |
| **Best for** *(1 mark)* | Rigid PVC, HDPE pressure pipes | Medical tubing, IV lines, drinking straws | Large-bore HDPE/PVC sewer & water pipes |
| **Applications** *(1 mark)* | PN-16 water pipes, conduit | Blood bags, catheters | 800 mm OD sewer mains |
| **Cost** *(1 mark)* | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |

*(Sketches expected: a cross-section showing the spider arms, a basket shell with perforations, and a spiral channel wrapped around the mandrel.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $K = 5 \times 10^{3}$ Pa·s$^n$, $n = 0.4$, $D_m = 0.08$ m, $t = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $L = 0.1$ m, $Q = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s.

**Step 1 — Wall shear rate (1 mark):**

$$\dot{\gamma}_w \;=\; \left(\frac{2n+1}{3n}\right)\frac{6Q}{\pi D_m t^{2}}$$

- $(2n+1)/(3n) = (0.8 + 1)/(1.2) = 1.8/1.2 = 1.5$
- $6Q = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$
- $\pi D_m t^{2} = \pi \times 0.08 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} = 6.283 \times 10^{-6}$

$$\dot{\gamma}_w \;=\; 1.5 \times \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{6.283 \times 10^{-6}} \;\approx\; 286.5\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Pressure drop using the power-law slit analogy (5 marks):**

$$\Delta P \;=\; \frac{2KL}{t}\left(\dot{\gamma}_w\right)^{n}$$

$$\Delta P \;=\; \frac{2 \times 5 \times 10^{3} \times 0.1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \times (286.5)^{0.4}$$

- Prefactor: $2 \times 5 \times 10^{3} \times 0.1 / 5 \times 10^{-3} = 2 \times 10^{5}$
- $(286.5)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 286.5} = e^{0.4 \times 5.658} = e^{2.263} \approx 9.61$

$$\Delta P \;\approx\; 2 \times 10^{5} \times 9.61 \;\approx\; 1.92 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa} \;=\; 1.92\,\text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Wall shear stress (1 mark):**

$$\tau_w \;=\; \frac{\Delta P\,t}{2L} \;=\; \frac{1.92 \times 10^{6} \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 0.1} \;\approx\; 4.8 \times 10^{4}\,\text{Pa} \;=\; 48\,\text{kPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $D_m$ and $D_o$:** The mean diameter $D_m = D_o - t$ is the correct reference for annular flow; using $D_o$ in the wetted-perimeter formula $\pi D$ gives the wrong answer by a factor of $(D_o/D_m)$. *(Lose 1 mark.)*
> 2. **Forgetting the correction factor** $(2n+1)/(3n)$: For $n=0.4$ it is 1.5, not 1.0. *(Lose 1 mark.)*
> 3. **Using the Newtonian formula for a power-law melt:** Will give $\Delta P$ off by an order of magnitude. *(Lose 2 marks.)*
> 4. **Not converting units:** A common error is to mix mm and m in $t$ and $D$. Always convert to SI before substituting. *(Lose 1 mark.)*
> 5. **Missing the land-length discussion in part (a):** "Weld lines", "die swell", and "vacuum calibration" are the *three* highest-frequency keywords in tube-die theory. Their absence is penalized.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 15 things to remember before the KTU exam on Tube Dies:**

1. **Three zones of a tube die:** Manifold / distributor → Converging approach → Parallel land.
2. **Weld lines** are formed at the spider legs; they are eliminated by adequate **land length** and by using **basket** or **spiral-mandrel** designs.
3. **Land length rule:** $L = (10 \text{ to } 40)\,t$ — too short gives visible welds; too long wastes pressure.
4. **Hagen–Poiseuille for thin annulus:** $Q = \pi (D_o - D_i) t^{3} \Delta P / (12 \mu L)$.
5. **Power-law pressure drop:** $\Delta P = (2KL/t) [(2n+1)/(3n) \cdot 6Q/(\pi D_m t^2)]^n$.
6. **Die swell ratio:** $B = D_{\text{ext}}/D_{\text{die}}$; die must be **back-sized** by $1/B$.
7. **Wall shear rate:** $\dot{\gamma}_w = 6Q/(\pi D_m t^{2})$ with the $(2n+1)/(3n)$ correction for non-Newtonian.
8. **Wall shear stress:** $\tau_w = \Delta P\,t/(2L)$.
9. **Vacuum sizing tank** fixes the final dimensions; the die only sets a rough shape.
10. **Concentricity** = (min wall)/(max wall) × 100 %; targets **≥ 95 %** for pressure pipes.
11. **Mandrel offset** $\delta$ causes wall-thickness variation $\Delta t / t = 2\delta/t$.
12. **Haul-off ratio** (draw-down) typically **3 – 6** to control orientation and final dimensions.
13. **Material-typical swell:** LDPE 1.20 – 1.30; HDPE 1.10 – 1.20; PVC 1.02 – 1.08.
14. **Melt temperature uniformity** across the circumference is the #1 cause of ovality in pipes.
15. **KTU board keywords to use:** *mandrel, spider leg, weld line, land length, die swell, back-sizing, calibration, haul-off, concentricity, power-law index*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elastic Properties and Die Swell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Elastic Properties and Die Swell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Elastic Properties of Polymer Melts

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
*Elastic properties* of a polymer melt refer to the **solid-like, recoverable** response exhibited by the melt under deformation. Although polymer melts flow like liquids, their long, entangled macromolecular chains store a portion of the deformation energy elastically, and this energy can be recovered when the stress is removed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Viscoelastic Nature of Polymers**
> A polymer melt is neither a pure liquid (Newtonian viscous) nor a pure solid (Hookean elastic). It is **viscoelastic** — meaning it simultaneously exhibits viscous flow AND elastic recovery. The elastic character dominates at **high shear rates**, **short times**, and **low temperatures**.

### Key Elastic Manifestations
- **Die Swell (Barus Effect)** – expansion of extrudate on leaving a die
- **Melt Fracture** – unstable, distorted extrudate surface
- **Sharkskin** – small-amplitude, high-frequency surface roughness
- **Weissenberg Effect (Rod Climbing)** – melt climbs up a rotating shaft
- **Frozen-in Orientation** – residual anisotropic properties in finished parts

---

## 1.2 Die Swell (Extrudate Swell / Barus Effect)

**Formal Definition:**
*Die swell* is the phenomenon in which the cross-sectional area (or diameter) of a polymer extrudate, immediately after emerging from a die, becomes **larger** than the cross-sectional area of the die itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Die Swell Ratio (B)**
> $$B = \frac{D_e}{D_0}$$
> where $D_e$ = extrudate diameter, $D_0$ = die diameter. The ratio can also be expressed on an area basis: $B_A = A_e / A_0$.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy 🧠

> [!TIP]
> **Think of squeezing toothpaste out of a tube.**
> When you press toothpaste (a viscoelastic paste) through the nozzle, it does **not** retain the thin nozzle shape. Instead, the ribbon that comes out is **fatter** than the nozzle. The reason: the molecules were forced to deform and align under pressure inside the tube, and once they exit into free space, they "remember" their original un-deformed state and **spring back** — exactly the same principle that governs die swell in polymer extrusion.

**Plain English Summary:**
- A polymer is like a **bundle of entangled rubber bands soaked in honey**.
- The "honey" part flows (viscous).
- The "rubber bands" part wants to recoil (elastic).
- Inside the die → chains are stretched and aligned.
- On leaving the die → chains recoil, swelling the extrudate.

---

## 1.4 The Two Governing Causes of Die Swell

| # | Cause | Mechanism |
|---|-------|-----------|
| 1 | **Elastic Recovery** | Stored elastic energy in oriented chains is released on leaving the die |
| 2 | **Velocity Profile Rearrangement** | Parabolic velocity profile inside the die → flattened profile outside, causing radial expansion |

> [!WARNING]
> The **elastic recovery** component typically contributes **70–90%** of the total die swell, while velocity rearrangement accounts for the remaining **10–30%** for most polymer melts. This is a frequently tested KTU point.

---

## 1.5 The Significance of Elastic Properties in Processing

- **Dimensional control:** Die swell determines the actual size of extruded profiles, films, sheets, and fibers.
- **Quality control:** Uncontrolled swell leads to wavy edges, thickness variation, and rejection.
- **Process window selection:** Dictates allowable throughput rates.
- **Surface defects:** Strongly related to sharkskin and melt fracture.
- **Molecular characterization:** Die swell is used to estimate **relaxation time** and **molecular weight distribution**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Viscoelastic Framework

A polymer melt under flow stores elastic energy through the orientation and stretching of molecular chains. When the constraint (die wall) is removed, this energy drives the **elastic recovery** of the extrudate.

### 2.1.1 Normal Stress Differences

When a polymer melt is sheared between parallel plates, the stress state is not purely viscous. Three **normal stress differences** arise:

$$
N_1 = \sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22} \quad \text{(Primary normal stress difference)}
$$

$$
N_2 = \sigma_{22} - \sigma_{33} \quad \text{(Secondary normal stress difference)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For almost all polymer melts, $N_1 > 0$ and $N_2 < 0$, with $\vert N_2 \vert \ll N_1$. The **primary normal stress difference $N_1$** is the principal driver of die swell.

### 2.1.2 The Primary Normal Stress Coefficient

$$
\Psi_1 = \frac{N_1}{\dot{\gamma}^{\,2}}
$$

where $\dot{\gamma}$ is the shear rate. $\Psi_1$ has units of **Pa·s²** and is a strong function of shear rate and temperature.

### 2.1.3 Recoverable Shear Strain (Elastic Strain)

$$
\gamma_R = \frac{\sigma_{12}}{G} = \frac{\tau}{G} = \dot{\gamma}\,\lambda
$$

where:
- $\sigma_{12}$ = shear stress
- $G$ = shear modulus of the melt
- $\lambda$ = characteristic **relaxation time** (s)
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)

> [!TIP]
> $\gamma_R$ is the **most important dimensionless parameter** for die swell — it is the ratio of elastic to viscous response. A high $\gamma_R$ → high die swell.

### 2.1.4 Dimensionless Groups

$$
\text{Weissenberg Number: } \quad N_W = \dot{\gamma}\,\lambda
$$

$$
\text{Deborah Number: } \quad D_e = \frac{\lambda}{t_{\text{process}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $N_W \ll 1$ → viscous behavior dominates → low die swell.
> When $N_W \gg 1$ → elastic behavior dominates → high die swell.

---

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Die Swell — The Engineering Matrix

| # | Factor | Effect on Die Swell | Why? |
|---|--------|---------------------|------|
| 1 | ↑ Shear rate ($\dot{\gamma}$) | **Increases** | More orientation stored, faster than relaxation |
| 2 | ↑ Residence time (longer die) | **Decreases** | More time for relaxation inside die |
| 3 | ↑ Temperature (T) | **Decreases** | Relaxation time $\lambda$ drops sharply with T |
| 4 | ↑ Molecular weight (M) | **Increases** | Longer chains → more entanglements → more stored elastic energy |
| 5 | ↑ Molecular weight distribution (MWD) | **Increases** | High-M tail gives more elastic memory |
| 6 | ↑ Die L/D ratio | **Decreases** | Greater time for relaxation before exit |
| 7 | ↓ Die entrance angle | **Decreases** | Smoother entry → less extensional deformation |
| 8 | ↑ Filler / Plasticizer | **Decreases** | Dilutes elastic network, reduces $\lambda$ |
| 9 | Crosslinked polymer (thermoset) | **Very low** | Permanent network restricts recovery |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 🧾

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $B = D_e / D_0$ | Die swell ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\gamma_R = \dot{\gamma}\,\lambda$ | Recoverable shear strain | dimensionless |
| 3 | $N_W = \dot{\gamma}\,\lambda$ | Weissenberg number | dimensionless |
| 4 | $N_1 = \Psi_1\,\dot{\gamma}^{\,2}$ | Primary normal stress difference | Pa |
| 5 | $\Psi_1 = N_1 / \dot{\gamma}^{\,2}$ | Primary normal stress coefficient | Pa·s² |
| 6 | $B \approx (\gamma_R / 2)^{1/6}$ | **Tanner's equation** (small swell) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $B \approx (2\,N_W)^{1/6}$ | Tanner's equation (alternative form) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $E = 3G$ | Young's modulus from shear modulus (incompressible) | Pa |
| 9 | $G' = G(\omega\,\lambda)^{2}/(1+(\omega\,\lambda)^{2})$ | Storage modulus (Maxwell) | Pa |
| 10 | $G'' = G(\omega\,\lambda)/(1+(\omega\,\lambda)^{2})$ | Loss modulus (Maxwell) | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examination Tip:** Tanner's equation $B = (2 N_W)^{1/6}$ is the **single most-tested formula** for die swell problems in KTU examinations. Memorize it, its derivation, and the assumptions behind it.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Profile Extrusion** (window frames, gaskets): Die swell dictates die-design compensation and haul-off speed.
- **Film Blowing:** Die swell determines the **blow-up ratio** and final film thickness uniformity.
- **Fiber Spinning:** Controls fiber **denier** and dye uptake uniformity.
- **Blow Molding (Parison Control):** Parison programming compensates for sagging AND die swell.
- **Rheological Characterization:** Swell measurement is a quick way to estimate relaxation time without an oscillatory rheometer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tanner's Equation for Die Swell

> **Objective:** To derive a relationship between the die swell ratio $B$ and the recoverable shear strain $\gamma_R$.

### Step 1 — Statement of the Problem
A polymer melt is sheared in a long capillary of radius $R_0$ under steady-state shear. The total elastic energy stored per unit volume is:

$$
W_{el} = \int_0^{\gamma_R} \sigma_{12}(\gamma')\,d\gamma' = \int_0^{\gamma_R} G\,\gamma_R\,d\gamma' = \frac{1}{2}\,G\,\gamma_R^{\,2}
$$

We need to find how much of this stored energy is converted into radial expansion on leaving the die.

### Step 2 — Energy Released on Free Recovery
On exiting the die, the melt is stress-free. The stored elastic energy $W_{el}$ is converted into surface energy of the new (larger) cylindrical surface and bulk elastic strain energy of the swelling extrudate.

The increase in surface energy (per unit length of extrudate) for a swell from $R_0$ to $R_e$ is:

$$
\Delta W_{surf} = 2\pi R_e \cdot \sigma_{sg} - 2\pi R_0 \cdot \sigma_{sg} = 2\pi \sigma_{sg}(R_e - R_0)
$$

where $\sigma_{sg}$ is the surface tension of the polymer melt (typically $0.02$ to $0.04$ N/m).

### Step 3 — Equilibrium of Forces (Radial Direction)
For an incompressible elastic cylinder undergoing radial expansion, the elastic hoop stress $\sigma_{rr}$ is related to the radial strain $\varepsilon_r$ by:

$$
\sigma_{rr} = \frac{E}{2}\left[\left(\frac{R_e}{R_0}\right)^2 - 1\right]
$$

For an incompressible material, $E = 3G$:

$$
\sigma_{rr} = \frac{3G}{2}\left[B^2 - 1\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Force Balance on the Swelling Neck
