Balancing the elastic hoop stress against the surface tension at the die exit (perimeter $2\pi R_0$):

$$
\sigma_{rr} \cdot \pi R_0^{\,2} = \sigma_{sg} \cdot 2\pi R_0
$$

Substituting the expression for $\sigma_{rr}$ and solving for $B$:

$$
\frac{3G}{2}\left[B^2 - 1\right] \cdot R_0 = 2\sigma_{sg}
$$

### Step 5 — Identifying the Strain Energy Equivalence
For a *viscoelastic* melt in fast recovery (neglecting surface tension for the bulk of the polymer volume), the stored elastic energy is fully converted into recoverable strain:

$$
W_{el} = \frac{1}{2}G\,\gamma_R^{\,2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad G = \frac{2 W_{el}}{\gamma_R^{\,2}}
$$

Substituting back and simplifying for the case of **large $\gamma_R$** (where the quadratic term dominates):

$$
B^{\,2} - 1 \approx B^{\,2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad B \approx \left(\frac{4}{3}\,\gamma_R^{\,2}\right)^{1/6}
$$

### Step 6 — Final Form (Tanner's Equation)
After including the proper numerical prefactor from Tanner's original analysis of the recovery kinematics:

$$
\boxed{\,B \;=\; \left(1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\gamma_R^{\,2}\right)^{1/6} \;\approx\; \left(\tfrac{\gamma_R}{2}\right)^{1/6} \;\approx\; (2\,N_W)^{1/6}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exponent **1/6** is a fingerprint of Tanner's theory. It comes from the **birefringence-strain coupling** in the recovery of a viscoelastic cylinder. A derivation with more rigorous treatment of the convected Maxwell model yields exactly the same exponent.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-style)

> **Q:** A polypropylene (PP) melt is extruded through a capillary die of diameter $D_0 = 2$ mm at a shear rate $\dot{\gamma} = 500\ \text{s}^{-1}$. The relaxation time of the melt at the processing temperature is $\lambda = 0.2$ s. Calculate the **die swell ratio** using Tanner's equation.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the Weissenberg number.

$$
N_W = \dot{\gamma} \cdot \lambda = 500 \times 0.2 = 100
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Tanner's equation.

$$
B = (2 N_W)^{1/6} = (2 \times 100)^{1/6} = (200)^{1/6}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate numerically.

$$
\ln B = \tfrac{1}{6}\ln(200) = \tfrac{1}{6}(5.298) = 0.883
$$

$$
B = e^{0.883} \approx 2.418
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the extrudate diameter.

$$
D_e = B \cdot D_0 = 2.418 \times 2\ \text{mm} = 4.836\ \text{mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The extrudate is **~142%** larger than the die diameter. In a real extrusion line, the die must be **undersized** by the inverse of $B$ to deliver the desired final product dimension.

---

## 3.3 Analytic Summary Table — Mechanism → Parameter → Effect

| Mechanism | Governing Parameter | Formula Tie | Effect on $B$ |
|-----------|--------------------|-------------|---------------|
| Elastic chain recoil | $\gamma_R$ | $B \propto \gamma_R^{\,1/6}$ | ↑ as $\gamma_R$ ↑ |
| Relaxation during flow | $\lambda$ | $B \propto \lambda^{1/6}$ | ↑ as $\lambda$ ↑ |
| Molecular weight | $M$ (via $\lambda \propto M^{3.4}$) | $B \propto M^{0.57}$ | ↑ as M ↑ |
| Temperature | $T$ (via $\lambda \propto e^{E/RT}$) | $B \propto e^{E/(6RT)}$ | ↓ as T ↑ |
| Die residence | $L/D$ | $B \propto (L/D)^{-1/6}$ | ↓ as L/D ↑ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Die Swell Phenomenon

Below is a **functional block diagram** that maps the **inputs, internal mechanics, and observable outputs** of die swell in a polymer extrusion process.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Polymer Melt Reservoir] --> B[High Pressure Pump / Extruder Screw]
    B --> C[Converging Die Entry Region]
    C --> D[Capillary Die Land Length L]
    D --> E[Stress State: Shear + Extensional + Normal]
    E --> F[Stored Elastic Energy W = 0.5 G gamma_R squared]
    F --> G[Die Exit: Constraint Removed]
    G --> H[Elastic Recovery: Chain Recoil]
    G --> I[Velocity Profile Rearrangement]
    H --> J[OUTPUT: Swollen Extrudate D_e]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Measurement: B = D_e divided by D_0]
    K --> L[Feedback to Die Design Compensation]

    M[FACTOR: Shear Rate dot_gamma] --> E
    N[FACTOR: Relaxation Time lambda] --> F
    O[FACTOR: Temperature T] --> F
    P[FACTOR: Die L over D ratio] --> D
    Q[FACTOR: Molecular Weight M] --> F
    R[FACTOR: MWD] --> F
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Causal Hierarchy of Viscoelastic Response

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Molecular Structure: M, MWD, Chain Branching] --> IN2[Relaxation Spectrum lambda_i]
    IN2 --> IN3[Processing Inputs: dot_gamma, T, L over D]
    IN3 --> IN4[In-Die Stress State]
    IN4 --> IN5[Stored Elastic Energy]
    IN5 --> OUT1[Die Swell B]
    IN4 --> OUT2[Normal Stress N_1]
    IN4 --> OUT3[Melt Fracture]
    IN4 --> OUT4[Sharkskin]
    IN5 --> OUT1
```

---

## 4.3 Process-Level Schematic: From Melting to Swelled Extrudate

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Hopper: Solid Polymer Granules] --> S2[Feed Zone: Compaction]
    S2 --> S3[Compression Zone: Melting]
    S3 --> S4[Metering Zone: Homogenization]
    S4 --> S5[Die Adapter: Pressure Build-up]
    S5 --> S6[Capillary Die: Shear Orientation]
    S6 --> S7[Exit Plane: Constraint Released]
    S7 --> S8[Free Extrudate: Swollen D_e greater than D_0]
    S8 --> S9[Cooling and Solidification]
    S9 --> S10[Final Dimensional Inspection]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in your answer script:** Start at the input, follow the arrow chain, and at every step annotate which **rheological parameter** is being modified. KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for **labelled, well-drawn flow diagrams** in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define die swell. List any four factors that affect it.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):**
> Die swell (also called extrudate swell or Barus effect) is the phenomenon in which the cross-section of a polymer extrudate emerging from a die becomes **larger** than the cross-section of the die, expressed as $B = D_e / D_0$.

> **Four factors (2 Marks — ½ Mark each):**
> 1. **Shear rate** — Higher shear rate → more chain orientation → higher swell.
> 2. **Temperature** — Higher T → lower $\lambda$ → lower swell.
> 3. **Die L/D ratio** — Higher L/D → more relaxation → lower swell.
> 4. **Molecular weight (and MWD)** — Higher M / broader MWD → more elastic memory → higher swell.

---

### **Q2. Define primary normal stress difference. State its significance in polymer processing.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):**
> The primary normal stress difference $N_1$ is the difference between the normal stress in the flow direction and the normal stress in the perpendicular direction:
> $$N_1 = \sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22}$$

> **Significance (2 Marks):**
> 1. **Die Swell Driver:** $N_1$ is directly responsible for the elastic recovery that causes extrudate swell.
> 2. **Weissenberg Effect:** Non-zero $N_1$ causes polymer melt to climb a rotating rod.
> 3. **Melt Fracture Onset:** Critical $N_1$ triggers flow instabilities.
> 4. **Process Simulation:** $N_1$ data is required as input for viscoelastic CFD codes.

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> ⚠️ **Each 14-mark question in KTU ESE consists of two sub-parts, (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You are expected to attempt ONE choice fully.**

---

### 🟦 QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### **(a) Explain in detail the various factors affecting die swell in polymer extrusion.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Die swell is governed by the **recoverable elastic strain** $\gamma_R = \dot{\gamma}\,\lambda$ stored during flow. Anything that increases $\gamma_R$ increases $B$.

1. **Shear Rate (1 Mark):** Higher $\dot{\gamma}$ directly increases $\gamma_R$. Typical relationship: $B \propto \dot{\gamma}^{\,1/6}$ (Tanner). At very high $\dot{\gamma}$ the swell saturates and may even decline slightly due to non-Newtonian shear-thinning.

2. **Temperature (1 Mark):** Relaxation time follows an Arrhenius-type law $\lambda \propto \exp(E/RT)$. Even a 10 °C rise can halve $\lambda$ and reduce $B$ by 10–15 %.

3. **Residence Time / Die L/D (1 Mark):** During flow through the die, partial relaxation occurs. Longer land length gives more time for disentanglement. $B$ decreases as $(L/D)^{1/6}$ approximately.

4. **Molecular Weight and MWD (1 Mark):** Since $\lambda \propto M^{3.4}$, higher $M$ sharply raises $\gamma_R$ and hence $B$. A broad MWD gives a long high-$M$ tail that retains elasticity for longer.

5. **Die Entry Geometry (1 Mark):** Sharp-edged entries generate strong **extensional (vorticity-free) flow** that stretches chains. Tapered entries (angle $\leq 30°$) reduce this and lower $B$.

6. **Polymer Composition (1 Mark):** Branching (e.g., LDPE vs. HDPE), fillers, and plasticizers alter $\lambda$ and $G$. LDPE, with long-chain branches, swells considerably more than HDPE at the same $\dot{\gamma}$.

7. **Surface Tension (0.5 Mark):** Counteracts swelling at very low $B$; negligible for engineering polymers at typical shear rates.

8. **Wall Slip and Compressibility (0.5 Mark):** In fluoropolymer or highly filled systems, wall slip can reduce the effective $\dot{\gamma}$ and lower $B$.

---

#### **(b) Derive Tanner's equation for die swell.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*(See the full derivation in Section 3.1. Outline the key steps here.)*

1. **Step 1 (1 Mark):** Define the stored elastic energy per unit volume:
   $W_{el} = \tfrac{1}{2} G \gamma_R^{\,2}$.

2. **Step 2 (1 Mark):** Write the radial (hoop) stress in the swelling cylinder:
   $\sigma_{rr} = \tfrac{3G}{2}(B^2 - 1)$ using $E = 3G$ for incompressible material.

3. **Step 3 (1 Mark):** Apply force balance at the die exit (elastic stress vs. surface tension).

4. **Step 4 (1 Mark):** Identify the equivalence between stored energy and recoverable strain.

5. **Step 5 (1.5 Marks):** Combine and simplify for large $\gamma_R$ to obtain:
   $B = (1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma_R^{\,2})^{1/6}$.

6. **Step 6 (1 Mark):** State the alternative form:
   $B \approx (2 N_W)^{1/6}$ with $N_W = \dot{\gamma}\lambda$.

7. **Step 7 (0.5 Mark):** Note Tanner's key assumptions: incompressible melt, fast recovery relative to flow, $N_2$ negligible.

---

### 🟩 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*

#### **(a) Discuss the elastic properties of polymer melts with reference to normal stress differences.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Viscoelastic Origin (1 Mark):** Polymer melts are viscoelastic. The elastic component arises from the entropic recoil of oriented, entangled macromolecular chains.

2. **Why Elastic? (1 Mark):** On short timescales (or high $\dot{\gamma}$), chains behave like rubber — they store energy elastically. On long timescales, they slide past each other and flow viscously.

3. **Definition of Normal Stresses (1.5 Marks):**
   $N_1 = \sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22}$ and $N_2 = \sigma_{22} - \sigma_{33}$.

4. **Sign and Magnitude (1 Mark):** For most polymer melts, $N_1 > 0$ and scales as $\dot{\gamma}^2$ at low shear rates. $N_2 < 0$ and is small ($\vert N_2 \vert \approx 0.1 N_1$).

5. **Phenomenological Consequences (1.5 Marks):**
   - Die swell
   - Weissenberg (rod-climbing) effect
   - Melt fracture
   - Frozen-in orientation

6. **Normal Stress Coefficient $\Psi_1$ (1 Mark):** Defined as $\Psi_1 = N_1 / \dot{\gamma}^2$, with units Pa·s². Decreases with increasing $\dot{\gamma}$ and $T$.

---

#### **(b) An HDPE melt is extruded through a die of diameter 2 mm at 200 °C. The shear rate is 100 s⁻¹ and the relaxation time is 0.5 s. Calculate the die swell ratio and the resulting extrudate diameter using Tanner's equation.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given (1 Mark):**
$D_0 = 2$ mm, $\dot{\gamma} = 100\ \text{s}^{-1}$, $\lambda = 0.5$ s, $T = 200\ ^\circ\text{C}$.

**Step 1 — Weissenberg number (1.5 Marks):**

$$
N_W = \dot{\gamma}\,\lambda = 100 \times 0.5 = 50
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Tanner's equation (1.5 Marks):**

$$
B = (2 N_W)^{1/6} = (2 \times 50)^{1/6} = (100)^{1/6}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation (1.5 Marks):**

$$
\log_{10} B = \tfrac{1}{6} \log_{10}(100) = \tfrac{1}{6} \times 2 = 0.333
$$

$$
B = 10^{0.333} = 2.154
$$

**Step 4 — Extrudate diameter (1 Mark):**

$$
D_e = B \cdot D_0 = 2.154 \times 2\ \text{mm} = 4.308\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation (0.5 Mark):**
The extrudate is **115 % larger** than the die. To obtain a product of 2 mm final diameter, the die must be sized as $D_0 = D_e / B = 2 / 2.154 = 0.928$ mm.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on Die Swell questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $B$ with $B_A$** — Die swell is a *linear* ratio ($D_e/D_0$), not an area ratio. Writing $B = A_e/A_0$ is a common **1-mark deduction**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{6}$ exponent** — Students often write $B = 2 N_W$ or $B = (2 N_W)^{1/2}$. The exponent 1/6 is non-negotiable for Tanner's model.
>
> 3. **Mixing up $N_1$ and $N_2$** — $N_1$ is the **primary** normal stress difference; it is the dominant one for die swell. Writing $N_2$ as the cause of die swell will be marked wrong.
>
> 4. **Ignoring units of $\Psi_1$** — $\Psi_1$ has units of **Pa·s²** (or equivalently kg·m⁻¹). Writing it as just "Pa" loses 0.5 marks.
>
> 5. **Not labelling diagrams** — In 7-mark theory questions, an unlabelled flow diagram yields **0 marks** for the diagram component.
>
> 6. **Treating die swell as purely viscous** — Die swell is fundamentally an **elastic recovery** phenomenon. Any answer attributing it solely to "viscous flow" or "velocity profile" will be marked incomplete.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Die swell** = expansion of extrudate on leaving the die; ratio $B = D_e / D_0$.
- ✅ Also called **Barus effect**.
- ✅ **Two causes:** (i) elastic recovery of oriented chains (dominant, 70–90 %), (ii) velocity profile rearrangement (10–30 %).
- ✅ **Primary normal stress difference** $N_1 = \sigma_{11} - \sigma_{22}$ is the key elastic parameter.
- ✅ **Recoverable shear strain** $\gamma_R = \dot{\gamma}\,\lambda$ is the controlling variable.
- ✅ **Weissenberg number** $N_W = \dot{\gamma}\,\lambda$ is the dimensionless group.
- ✅ **Tanner's equation:** $B = (1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma_R^{\,2})^{1/6} \approx (2 N_W)^{1/6}$ — exponent **1/6** is critical.
- ✅ **Higher $B$ occurs when:** shear rate ↑, T ↓, M ↑, MWD ↑, L/D ↓, sharp entry angle ↑.
- ✅ **Lower $B$ occurs when:** T ↑, L/D ↑, tapered entry, plasticizer / filler ↑, crosslinked polymer.
- ✅ **Polymer melts are viscoelastic** — they exhibit both viscous flow and elastic recovery.
- ✅ **Engineering implications:** die design compensation, product tolerance, surface defects, parison control in blow molding.
- ✅ **Other elastic manifestations to know:** Weissenberg effect (rod climbing), melt fracture, sharkskin, frozen-in orientation.
- ✅ **Units to memorize:** $\Psi_1$ in Pa·s² ; $\lambda$ in seconds ; $\dot{\gamma}$ in s⁻¹ ; $B$ dimensionless.
- ✅ **Comparison to remember:** LDPE (branched) swells **more** than HDPE (linear) at the same $\dot{\gamma}$ due to long-chain branches.
- ✅ **Order of magnitude:** Typical industrial die swell ratios for polyolefins range from **1.2 to 2.5**; for highly elastic elastomers, $B$ can exceed **3.0**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Manufacturing of Pipes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Manufacturing of Pipes # 🏭 MANUFACTURING OF POLYMER PIPES — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Polymer Pipe Manufacturing** is an industrial continuous-extrusion process wherein a thermoplastic polymer melt is forced through a specially designed annular die, calibrated to a precise outer diameter and wall thickness, then dimensionally stabilized (sized), cooled below its glass transition temperature $T_g$ or crystalline melting point $T_m$, and finally cut to specified lengths to produce pipes used for fluid transport, structural, or protective applications. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Pipe manufacturing is a flagship topic in polymer processing. The most commercially dominant process is **Single-Screw Extrusion of Thermoplastic Pipes**, which accounts for **>90%** of all plastic pipes produced globally (PVC, HDPE, MDPE, LDPE, PP-R, PEX, PB). The governing standard terminology uses: - **OD (Outer Diameter)** — measured in **mm** (ISO 3126 metric system) - **Wall Thickness ($s$)** — measured in **mm** - **SDR (Standard Dimension Ratio)** — a key design parameter - **PN (Pressure Nominal)** — pressure rating in **bar** The pipe is characterized by the **SDR (Standard Dimension Ratio)**: $$ SDR = \frac{OD}{s} $$ And the **Pressure Rating (PN)** at $20^\circ C$ for a given material design stress $\sigma$: $$ PN = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma \cdot s}{OD - s} = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma}{SDR - 1} $$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Dough Press for Plastic" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Pastry/Icing Press in a Bakery** > Imagine squeezing soft icing from a pastry bag fitted with a **ring-shaped nozzle**. As you squeeze: > - The icing (molten polymer) flows out in a **hollow tube shape** (the pipe). > - The nozzle (pipe die) decides the **outer shape and wall thickness**. > - A fan (sizing & cooling unit) blows cool air, hardening the icing (polymer solidifies). > - A long conveyor belt (haul-off) pulls the tube forward at steady speed. > - A pair of scissors (cutter) chops it into uniform lengths. > - A stacker places the finished lengths into a pile (storage/inspection). > > This is *exactly* how a polymer pipe line works — only the materials, pressures ($50$–$200 \text{ bar}$), and temperatures ($160$–$230^\circ C$) are far more extreme than baking! ## 1.3 Why Polymer Pipes Dominate Modern Infrastructure | Property | Polymer Pipe | Metal Pipe (Steel/Cu) | |----------|--------------|------------------------| | **Density** | $0.9$–$1.5 \text{ g/cm}^3$ | $7.8$–$8.9 \text{ g/cm}^3$ | | **Corrosion resistance** | Excellent | Poor (needs coating) | | **Installation cost** | Low (lightweight) | High (heavy lifting) | | **Service life** | $50$–$100$ years | $15$–$30$ years | | **Thermal conductivity** | $0.2$–$0.4 \text{ W/m·K}$ | $45$–$400 \text{ W/m·K}$ | | **Flexibility** | High (PE) | Low (rigid) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The low density of polymers (around $0.95 \text{ g/cm}^3$ for HDPE) makes pipes **7–8 times lighter** than equivalent steel pipes, dramatically reducing transportation and installation costs — a critical factor in modern water and gas distribution networks. # ⚙️ MANUFACTURING OF POLYMER PIPES — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Pipe Extrusion Line — Functional Architecture A modern polymer pipe extrusion line is a **continuous, integrated, multi-stage system**. Each subsystem performs a defined physical transformation on the polymer: | Stage | Subsystem | Physical Transformation | Key Output | |-------|-----------|-------------------------|------------| | **1** | **Gravimetric/Volumetric Feeder** | Solid pellets → metered feed | Constant mass flow $Q_m$ | | **2** | **Single-Screw Extruder** | Solid → Melt (plastication) | Homogeneous melt at $T_m + 30$ to $50^\circ C$ | | **3** | **Screen Pack & Breaker Plate** | Filtration of contaminants | Pressure build-up, melt filtration | | **4** | **Pipe Die (Die Head)** | Melt flow → Annular profile | Shaped molten pipe | | **5** | **Sizing Unit (Calibration)** | Dimension fixing | Correct OD, roundness | | **6** | **Cooling Tank (Water Bath/Spray)** | Solidification | Crystalline/glassy pipe | | **7** | **Haul-Off (Caterpillar/Belt)** | Tension-controlled drawing | Constant line speed | | **8** | **Cutting Unit (Saw/Planetary)** | Length-wise cutting | Pipe to specified length | | **9** | **Stacking & Marking** | Packaging | Ready-to-ship pipes | ## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Subsystem ### Stage 1: Feeding System - **Volumetric feeders** use a screw rotating at fixed RPM to deliver pellets. - **Gravimetric (loss-in-weight) feeders** continuously weigh and adjust to maintain constant throughput, compensating for bulk density variations. - Typical throughput range: **$50$ to $2000 \text{ kg/hr}$** depending on pipe diameter. ### Stage 2: Single-Screw Extruder The extruder has **three functional zones**: - **Feed Zone (Solids Conveying):** Pellets are conveyed forward by friction against the barrel. - **Compression/Metering Zone:** Polymer melts via conduction from barrel heaters ($160$–$230^\circ C$) and mechanical shear. The screw compression ratio is typically $3:1$ to $4:1$. - **Metering Zone:** Homogenized melt is pumped at constant pressure ($50$–$200 \text{ bar}$). The **specific output $q$** (output per unit RPM) for a metering-type screw is: $$ q = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot D^2 \cdot N \cdot h \cdot \sin\phi \cdot \cos\phi}{2} - \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot h^3 \cdot \sin^2\phi \cdot \Delta P}{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L} $$ where $D$ = screw diameter, $N$ = RPM, $h$ = channel depth, $\phi$ = helix angle, $\Delta P$ = pressure rise, $\eta$ = melt viscosity, $L$ = metering length. ### Stage 3: Pipe Die — The Heart of Pipe Manufacturing > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Hot Topic:** Die design determines the **melt flow distribution, weld line quality, and final pipe dimensions**. The three main industrial pipe die designs are: #### 2.2.1 Spider Die (Most Common for Small/Medium Pipes) - Melt from extruder enters a **manifold** and is split by **$3$ to $6$ "spider legs"** (radial support arms). - The split streams reunite downstream, forming a **"weld line"** at the point of reunion. - Weld line quality is critical — it can be a **mechanical weak point** if not properly designed. - Suitable for: PVC, small HDPE pipes up to **$160 \text{ mm}$ OD**. #### 2.2.2 Basket Die (For Larger Pipes) - The mandrel is supported by a **porous sintered metal "basket"** that looks like a wire mesh. - Provides **better flow distribution** with shorter, more uniform weld lines. - Suitable for: PE, PP pipes from **$50$ to $400 \text{ mm}$ OD**. #### 2.2.3 Spiral Die (High-Performance, Large Diameter) - Melt is distributed through **spiral channels** machined into a die body. - Eliminates weld lines almost entirely. - Used for very large diameter pipes **$400$ to $1600 \text{ mm}$ OD** (sewer, industrial). ### Stage 4: Sizing (Calibration) — Critical for Dimensional Accuracy > [!NOTE] > **Why sizing is needed:** After exiting the die, the molten pipe is **soft and deformable**. Without external constraint, it would sag, ovalize, and bulge due to gravity and internal stresses. Sizing fixes the **outer diameter** to within tight tolerances (typically $\pm 0.5\%$). **Three industrial sizing methods:** 1. **Vacuum Sizing (Most Common for PE/PP):** - Pipe passes through a **water-cooled stainless steel sleeve** under vacuum. - Vacuum ($0.3$–$0.8 \text{ bar}$ absolute) holds the pipe against the cooled inner wall. - Cooling water circulates at $15$–$25^\circ C$. 2. **Pressure Sizing (For PVC):** - Pipe is internally pressurized with air ($0.5$–$2 \text{ bar}$) and pulled against a chilled external sleeve. 3. **Sleeve Sizing (Internal Calibration):** - A mandrel is pulled inside the pipe; water spray externally cools it. ### Stage 5: Cooling - **Spray tank** (for larger pipes): water sprayed on the pipe surface. - **Immersion bath** (for small/medium pipes): pipe submerged in water at controlled temperature. - For PE pipes, controlled cooling prevents **internal stress buildup** — critical for long-term performance. ### Stage 6: Haul-Off Unit - **Caterpillar-type** (most common): two endless rubber belts gripping the pipe from top and bottom. - **Belt-type** (for very large/sensitive pipes): multiple belt contact. - Pulling speed $v = 0.5$ to $20 \text{ m/min}$ depending on pipe size. ### Stage 7: Cutting - **Flying saw:** cuts pipe while in motion (continuous production). - **Planetary cutter:** rotates around the pipe (for very large diameters). ## 2.3 Key Engineering Relationships ### Wall Thickness Control The wall thickness $s$ of the pipe is related to the **haul-off speed $v$** and **die gap $\delta$** as: $$ s = \delta \cdot \left(\frac{v_{die}}{v_{haul}}\right) = \delta \cdot DR $$ where $DR$ = draw-down ratio. Higher haul-off speed → thinner wall. > [!WARNING] > **Excessively high $DR$ causes orientation-induced residual stress** and reduces long-term pressure performance. Typical $DR$ limit: **$1.5:1$ to $3:1$** for pressure pipes. ### Throughput (Output) Relationship The mass output $Q_m$ of a pipe line is: $$ Q_m = \rho_m \cdot \pi \cdot s \cdot (OD - s) \cdot v_{haul} $$ where $\rho_m$ = melt density (typically $700$–$950 \text{ kg/m}^3$). ### Cooling Time Estimate The minimum cooling time $t_{cool}$ for a thick pipe wall: $$ t_{cool} \approx \frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \cdot \alpha} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{8 \cdot (T_{melt} - T_{cool})}{\pi \cdot (T_{surface} - T_{cool})}\right) $$ where $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity (typically $1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ for polymers). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbols | Units / Notes | |---|------------------|---------|---------------| | 1 | $SDR = OD \div s$ | Standard Dimension Ratio | Dimensionless | | 2 | $PN = \dfrac{2 \sigma}{SDR - 1}$ | Pressure Nominal | bar | | 3 | $s = \delta \cdot (v_{die} / v_{haul})$ | Wall thickness | mm | | 4 | $Q_m = \rho_m \cdot \pi \cdot s \cdot (OD - s) \cdot v_{haul}$ | Mass throughput | kg/s | | 5 | $Q_v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot \Delta P \cdot R^4}{8 \eta L}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille (Newtonian) | m³/s | | 6 | $q = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \dfrac{\pi D h^3 \sin^2\phi \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$ | Screw output | m³/s | | 7 | $t_{cool} \approx \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \cdot \ln\left[\dfrac{8 (T_{melt} - T_{cool})}{\pi (T_{surface} - T_{cool})}\right]$ | Cooling time | seconds | | 8 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{4 Q_v}{\pi R^3}$ | Shear rate in pipe | 1/s | | 9 | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 L}$ | Wall shear stress | Pa | | 10 | $v_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R^2}{4 \eta L}$ | Max velocity in pipe (Newtonian) | m/s | ## 2.5 Real-World Applications - **Water supply:** HDPE, MDPE, PVC-U, PP-R pipes (ISO 4427, EN 12201). - **Gas distribution:** MDPE pipes (yellow color) — must meet ISO 4437. - **Sewage/Drainage:** PVC-U, PP, HDPE structured-wall pipes. - **Industrial:** PVDF, PP-H, PEEK for chemicals. - **Hot & cold water plumbing:** PEX, PP-R (cross-linked polyethylene). - **Cable protection:** HDPE ducts in telecom/power. # 🔬 MANUFACTURING OF POLYMER PIPES — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Pressure Rating (PN) from Geometry The relationship between pressure rating, wall thickness, and material stress comes from **hoop stress analysis** of a thin-walled cylinder. ### Step 1: Free Body Analysis Consider a pipe segment of length $L$ and OD, subjected to internal pressure $P$. The internal force acting on the projected area is balanced by hoop stress $\sigma_h$ acting on the wall cross-section. $$ \text{Force on projected area} = P \cdot OD \cdot L $$ $$ \text{Resisting force from walls (2 walls)} = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot s \cdot L $$ ### Step 2: Equilibrium Equation Setting the two forces equal for static equilibrium: $$ P \cdot OD \cdot L = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot s \cdot L $$ ### Step 3: Solve for Hoop Stress $$ \sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot OD}{2 \cdot s} $$ ### Step 4: Introduce Design Stress In practice, the material's design stress $\sigma$ is the **long-term hydrostatic strength** (e.g., $8.0 \text{ MPa}$ for PE 80, $10.0 \text{ MPa}$ for PE 100) divided by a **safety factor** (typically $1.25$ for water at $20^\circ C$). ### Step 5: Solve for PN $$ PN = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma \cdot s}{OD - s} $$ ### Step 6: Express in Terms of SDR Substituting $s = OD \div SDR$: $$ PN = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma \cdot (OD / SDR)}{OD - (OD / SDR)} = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma \cdot OD / SDR}{OD \cdot (1 - 1/SDR)} = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma}{SDR - 1} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Form:** $PN = \dfrac{2\sigma}{SDR - 1}$ — This equation is essential for KTU problems on pipe classification. For PE 100 ($\sigma = 10 \text{ MPa}$) and $SDR = 11$: $PN = 20/10 = 2 \text{ MPa} = 20 \text{ bar}$. ## 3.2 Derivation: Mass Throughput from Geometric Parameters Given a pipe of outer diameter $OD$ and wall thickness $s$, the **cross-sectional area of polymer** is the difference between the outer circle and the inner circle: $$ A_{polymer} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot OD^2 - \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (OD - 2s)^2 $$ Expanding $(OD - 2s)^2$: $$ (OD - 2s)^2 = OD^2 - 4 \cdot OD \cdot s + 4s^2 $$ So: $$ A_{polymer} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[ OD^2 - (OD^2 - 4 \cdot OD \cdot s + 4s^2) \right] = \frac{\pi}{4} (4 \cdot OD \cdot s - 4s^2) $$ $$ A_{polymer} = \pi \cdot s \cdot (OD - s) $$ The mass throughput at haul-off speed $v$: $$ Q_m = \rho_m \cdot A_{polymer} \cdot v $$ $$ Q_m = \rho_m \cdot \pi \cdot s \cdot (OD - s) \cdot v $$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Example:** HDPE pipe with $OD = 110 \text{ mm}$, $s = 10 \text{ mm}$, $v = 2 \text{ m/min} = 0.0333 \text{ m/s}$, $\rho_m = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$: > > $Q_m = 850 \times \pi \times 0.010 \times (0.110 - 0.010) \times 0.0333$ > > $Q_m = 850 \times 3.1416 \times 0.010 \times 0.100 \times 0.0333$ > > $Q_m = 0.889 \text{ kg/s} = 53.3 \text{ kg/min} = 3200 \text{ kg/hr}$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Melt Flow in a Pipe Die Annulus The annular die is modeled as flow between two concentric cylinders. For a **Newtonian melt** of viscosity $\eta$, the volumetric flow $Q_v$ in a slit of width $w$ and gap $\delta$ is: $$ Q_v = \frac{w \cdot \delta^3 \cdot \Delta P}{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L} $$ Treating the annular die as a slit of width $w = \pi \cdot (OD + ID)$ and gap $\delta = (OD - ID)/2$: $$ Q_v = \frac{\pi \cdot (OD + ID) \cdot \left(\frac{OD - ID}{2}\right)^3 \cdot \Delta P}{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L} $$ Simplifying with $R_{avg} = (OD + ID)/4$ and gap $h = (OD - ID)/2$: $$ Q_v \approx \frac{2 \pi R_{avg} \cdot h^3 \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L} = \frac{\pi R_{avg} h^3 \Delta P}{6 \eta L} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Real polymer melts are **non-Newtonian** (pseudoplastic/shear-thinning). The power-law model $\eta = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ should ideally be used. For KTU problems, the Newtonian form is acceptable unless explicitly stated otherwise. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Problem Types | Problem Type | Given | Find | Method | |--------------|-------|------|--------| | Pipe Classification | $OD$, $s$, Material | $SDR$, $PN$ | $SDR = OD/s$, $PN = 2\sigma/(SDR-1)$ | | Throughput | $OD$, $s$, $v$, $\rho$ | $Q_m$ | $Q_m = \rho \pi s(OD-s)v$ | | Wall Thickness | $\delta$, $v_{die}$, $v_{haul}$ | $s$ | $s = \delta \cdot (v_{die}/v_{haul})$ | | Draw Ratio | $s_{die}$, $s_{pipe}$ | $DR$ | $DR = s_{die}/s_{pipe}$ | | Die Pressure | $Q_v$, $\eta$, geometry | $\Delta P$ | $\Delta P = 12 \eta L Q_v / (w \delta^3)$ | # 🗺️ MANUFACTURING OF POLYMER PIPES — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Complete Pipe Extrusion Line — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Material Hopper

The **color-coded blocks** in Diagram 4.1 represent different functional zones (feeding, plastication, shaping, sizing, cooling, cutting, quality control). # 📝 MANUFACTURING OF POLYMER PIPES — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📌 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **What is SDR in pipe manufacturing? How is it related to the pressure rating (PN)?** **Model Answer:** SDR stands for **Standard Dimension Ratio**, defined as the ratio of outer diameter to wall thickness: $$ SDR = \frac{OD}{s} $$ The relationship with pressure rating $PN$ is derived from hoop stress equilibrium: $$ PN = \frac{2\sigma}{SDR - 1} $$ where $\sigma$ is the design stress. For example, PE 100 pipes ($\sigma = 10 \text{ MPa}$) with $SDR = 11$ have $PN = 20/10 = 2 \text{ MPa} = 20 \text{ bar}$. A **lower SDR means a thicker wall and higher pressure rating**. *(3 marks — full credit for correct formulas with sample calculation)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **List the main components of a polymer pipe extrusion line in correct sequence.** **Model Answer:** The pipe extrusion line sequence is: 1. **Raw material feeder** (volumetric/gravimetric) 2. **Single-screw extruder** (feeding, compression, metering zones) 3. **Screen pack and breaker plate** (melt filtration) 4. **Pipe die head** (spider/basket/spiral type) 5. **Sizing/calibration unit** (vacuum sizing tank) 6. **Cooling system** (spray tank + immersion bath) 7. **Haul-off unit** (caterpillar/belt type) 8. **Cutting unit** (flying saw/planetary cutter) 9. **Stacking and quality control** (OD/PN testing) *(3 marks — sequence must be logically correct; missing one stage = −0.5 mark)* --- ## 📌 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a **spider-type pipe die** used in polymer extrusion. Discuss the function of spider legs, mandrel, and die land. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **single-screw extruder** has a screw diameter $D = 90 \text{ mm}$, channel depth $h = 6 \text{ mm}$ in the metering zone, helix angle $\phi = 17.65^\circ$, screw speed $N = 60 \text{ RPM}$, and metering length $L = 24 D$. The polymer melt has viscosity $\eta = 500 \text{ Pa·s}$ and the pressure rise across the metering zone is $\Delta P = 80 \text{ bar} = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$. Calculate: (i) drag flow, (ii) pressure flow, (iii) net output in $\text{kg/hr}$ (take $\rho_m = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$). **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):** **Spider Pipe Die Construction:** A spider die consists of: - **Die body (housing):** heavy steel block with heater bands maintaining melt temperature. - **Mandrel:** central torpedo-shaped core that defines the **inner diameter** of the pipe. - **Spider legs (3 to 6):** radial support arms connecting the mandrel to the die body. They hold the mandrel centered but obstruct flow. - **Manifold (resin pool):** converging region where melt from the extruder is distributed around the mandrel. - **Die land:** final cylindrical section of fixed length that establishes the final wall profile and smooths flow. **Working Principle:** 1. Melt enters the die from the extruder at the rear. 2. It flows into the **manifold** surrounding the mandrel. 3. Spider legs split the flow into 3–6 streams; the mandrel is held concentric. 4. The split streams **reunite downstream** of the spider → forming a **weld line**. 5. The melt passes through the **die land**, which controls the final wall thickness and gives uniform velocity profile. 6. The shaped annular extrudate exits the die, where it is taken up by the sizing and cooling system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Weld line quality is critical** — poorly designed spiders create weak weld lines that can rupture under pressure. Quality is enhanced by: (a) high land length, (b) high land temperature, (c) tapered spider legs, (d) surface polishing. *(Full 7 marks awarded for schematic + 5 functional parts + working)* #### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):** **Given:** - $D = 0.090 \text{ m}$, $h = 0.006 \text{ m}$, $\phi = 17.65^\circ$, $N = 60 \text{ RPM} = 1 \text{ rev/s}$, $L = 24D = 2.16 \text{ m}$, $\eta = 500 \text{ Pa·s}$, $\Delta P = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$, $\rho_m = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Trigonometric values:** - $\sin(17.65^\circ) = 0.303$ - $\cos(17.65^\circ) = 0.953$ - $\sin^2(17.65^\circ) = 0.0918$ **(i) Drag Flow $Q_d$:** $$ Q_d = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot D^2 \cdot N \cdot h \cdot \sin\phi \cdot \cos\phi}{2} $$ $$ Q_d = \frac{(3.1416)^2 \cdot (0.090)^2 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.006 \cdot 0.303 \cdot 0.953}{2} $$ $$ Q_d = \frac{9.8696 \cdot 0.0081 \cdot 0.006 \cdot 0.2887}{2} $$ $$ Q_d = \frac{0.0001386}{2} = 6.93 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **(ii) Pressure Flow $Q_p$ (negative, since it opposes drag):** $$ Q_p = -\frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot h^3 \cdot \sin^2\phi \cdot \Delta P}{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L} $$ $$ Q_p = -\frac{3.1416 \cdot 0.090 \cdot (0.006)^3 \cdot 0.0918 \cdot 8 \times 10^6}{12 \cdot 500 \cdot 2.16} $$ $$ Q_p = -\frac{3.1416 \cdot 0.090 \cdot 2.16 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 7.344 \times 10^5}{12960} $$ $$ Q_p = -\frac{4.494 \times 10^{-2}}{12960} = -3.47 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **(iii) Net Output $Q$ and Mass Throughput:** $$ Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p = 6.93 \times 10^{-5} - 3.47 \times 10^{-6} = 6.58 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ $$ Q_m = Q_{net} \cdot \rho_m = 6.58 \times 10^{-5} \times 900 = 0.0592 \text{ kg/s} $$ $$ Q_m = 0.0592 \times 3600 = 213 \text{ kg/hr} $$ *(7 marks — full marks for all three sub-parts with units; show every step)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]** **(a)** Explain the **vacuum sizing and cooling system** used in HDPE pipe extrusion. Discuss the role of vacuum, water circulation, and length of sizing tank. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An HDPE pipe has $OD = 110 \text{ mm}$, wall thickness $s = 10 \text{ mm}$, melt density $\rho_m = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, and is produced at a haul-off speed of $1.5 \text{ m/min}$. The die exit velocity is $0.6 \text{ m/min}$ and the die gap is $14 \text{ mm}$. Determine: (i) the actual wall thickness of the pipe, (ii) the draw-down ratio, (iii) the mass throughput in $\text{kg/hr}$, and (iv) the SDR and PN rating (use $\sigma = 8 \text{ MPa}$ for PE 80). **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):** **Vacuum Sizing System:** After leaving the die, the molten HDPE pipe is **soft, deformable, and prone to ovality** under gravity. The vacuum sizing system fixes the outer diameter precisely. **Construction:** - A **stainless steel sizing sleeve** (length $1$–$4 \text{ m}$) with internal diameter slightly larger than the desired pipe OD. - The sleeve is enclosed in a **water-filled tank**. - A **vacuum pump** maintains low pressure ($0.3$–$0.8 \text{ bar absolute}$) inside the sleeve. - Water at $15$–$25^\circ C$ circulates through the tank and around the sleeve. **Working:** 1. The molten pipe enters the sizing sleeve while still hot. 2. Vacuum inside the sleeve **pulls the pipe wall outward**, pressing it firmly against the cooled inner wall. 3. Water circulation **extracts heat** from the pipe, solidifying it dimensionally. 4. A thin water film between pipe and sleeve acts as both a **coolant and a lubricant**. 5. The pipe exits the tank dimensionally stable at the calibration OD. **Key Parameters:** - **Sleeve length** determines the time available for cooling — longer = better dimensional accuracy. - **Vacuum level** controls the contact pressure and thus cooling rate. - **Water temperature gradient** (in multi-stage tanks) prevents thermal shock. > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Practice:** For HDPE pipes, the sizing is typically followed by **3 to 4 successive cooling tanks** to bring the pipe gradually from $200^\circ C$ to $30^\circ C$ without inducing internal stress. *(7 marks awarded for full process description, diagram, and parameter explanation)* #### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):** **Given:** - $OD = 110 \text{ mm} = 0.110 \text{ m}$, $\delta = 14 \text{ mm}$, $s = 10 \text{ mm}$, $\rho_m = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $v_{haul} = 1.5 \text{ m/min}$, $v_{die} = 0.6 \text{ m/min}$, $\sigma = 8 \text{ MPa}$ **(i) Actual Wall Thickness:** The wall thickness is related to die gap and velocity ratio: $$ s_{pipe} = \delta \cdot \left(\frac{v_{die}}{v_{haul}}\right) $$ $$ s_{pipe} = 14 \text{ mm} \times \left(\frac{0.6}{1.5}\right) = 14 \times 0.4 = 5.6 \text{ mm} $$ **(ii) Draw-Down Ratio (DR):** $$ DR = \frac{v_{haul}}{v_{die}} = \frac{1.5}{0.6} = 2.5 $$ Alternatively, based on thickness: $$ DR = \frac{\delta}{s_{pipe}} = \frac{14}{5.6} = 2.5 $$ **(iii) Mass Throughput:** $$ Q_m = \rho_m \cdot \pi \cdot s \cdot (OD - s) \cdot v_{haul} $$ Convert units: $s = 0.0056 \text{ m}$, $v_{haul} = 1.5/60 = 0.025 \text{ m/s}$ $$ Q_m = 850 \times 3.1416 \times 0.0056 \times (0.110 - 0.0056) \times 0.025 $$ $$ Q_m = 850 \times 3.1416 \times 0.0056 \times 0.1044 \times 0.025 $$ $$ Q_m = 0.0390 \text{ kg/s} = 0.0390 \times 3600 = 140.4 \text{ kg/hr} $$ **(iv) SDR and PN:** $$ SDR = \frac{OD}{s} = \frac{110}{5.6} = 19.64 $$ $$ PN = \frac{2 \cdot \sigma}{SDR - 1} = \frac{2 \times 8}{19.64 - 1} = \frac{16}{18.64} = 0.858 \text{ MPa} = 8.58 \text{ bar} $$ *(7 marks — full marks for all 4 sub-parts with units and formulas)* --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S PITFALL CALLOUT — COMMON MARK-LOSING MISTAKES:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm with m in the same equation (e.g., diameter in mm, velocity in m/s). Always convert to SI units **before** substituting. > 2. **Sign Convention for Pressure Flow:** Forgetting that pressure flow **opposes** drag flow in the extruder; many students add them instead of subtracting. > 3. **Forgetting the $SDR - 1$ Denominator:** A frequent error is to write $PN = 2\sigma/SDR$ instead of $PN = 2\sigma/(SDR - 1)$. > 4. **Die Gap vs. Wall Thickness:** The die gap $\delta$ is **not** equal to the final wall thickness $s$. It is related through the draw-down ratio: $s = \delta \cdot (v_{die}/v_{haul})$. > 5. **No Unit for $\sigma$:** Always convert $\sigma$ to the same units as $PN$ (typically $\text{MPa} = \text{N/mm}^2$, and $1 \text{ MPa} = 10 \text{ bar}$). > 6. **Skipping the Mass Throughput Final Unit:** Marks are lost if $Q_m$ is left in $\text{kg/s}$ without converting to $\text{kg/hr}$ when the question demands it. --- ## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST ### ✅ Core Concepts (MUST remember) - ☐ Pipe manufacturing is a **continuous extrusion process** — 9 sequential stages. - ☐ The **pipe die** is the heart: spider (small), basket (medium), spiral (large) pipes. - ☐ **SDR** = Outer Diameter / Wall Thickness (dimensionless). - ☐ **PN** = $2\sigma / (SDR - 1)$ — pressure rating in bar. - ☐ **Wall thickness** is controlled by draw-down ratio: $s = \delta \cdot (v_{die} / v_{haul})$. - ☐ **Mass throughput:** $Q_m = \rho_m \cdot \pi \cdot s \cdot (OD - s) \cdot v_{haul}$. ### ✅ Subsystem Functions - ☐ **Feeder:** Constant mass flow to extruder. - ☐ **Extruder:** 3 zones — feed (solid conveying), compression (melting), metering (pumping). - ☐ **Screen pack:** Filtration of contaminants; pressure build-up. - ☐ **Pipe die:** Forms annular profile; **weld line** forms in spider/basket dies. - ☐ **Sizing tank:** Fixes OD via vacuum + cooled sleeve. - ☐ **Cooling tank:** Brings pipe below $T_g$ or $T_m$. - ☐ **Haul-off:** Maintains constant line speed; provides tension. - ☐ **Cutter:** Flying saw for in-line cutting; planetary for large pipes. ### ✅ Common Materials & Temperatures - ☐ **PVC-U:** $160$–$190^\circ C$ (rigid, water pipes) - ☐ **HDPE (PE 100):** $180$–$220^\circ C$ (water/gas, $\sigma = 10 \text{ MPa}$) - ☐ **MDPE:** $180$–$210^\circ C$ (gas distribution) - ☐ **PP-R:** $200$–$230^\circ C$ (hot water plumbing) - ☐ **PEX:** $180$–$210^\circ C$ (cross-linked, hot/cold water) ### ✅ KTU Hot Topics (Most Frequently Asked) - ☐ Differences between spider, basket, and spiral dies. - ☐ Vacuum sizing system — construction and working. - ☐ Calculation of SDR, PN, and mass throughput (numerical). - ☐ Draw-down ratio and its effect on pipe quality. - ☐ Screw extruder output formula (drag + pressure flow). ### ✅ Quality Control Tests - ☐ **OD measurement** (laser gauge or Pi tape) - ☐ **Wall thickness** (ultrasonic) - ☐ **Hydrostatic pressure test** (long-term creep) - ☐ **Burst pressure test** (short-term) - ☐ **Density / MFI testing** (raw material) ### ✅ Standards (Industry References) - ☐ **ISO 4427** — HDPE pipes for water supply - ☐ **ISO 4437** — PE pipes for gas distribution - ☐ **EN 12201** — Plastic piping systems for water - ☐ **ASTM D3035** — PE pipe specifications - ☐ **IS 4985** — Indian Standard for PVC pipes > 🎯 **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** Always **draw a labelled schematic** for long-answer questions. Examiners award 1–2 marks for a clear, well-labelled diagram even before the explanation.

topics.title: Cables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔌 POLYMER CABLES — SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cables (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **cable** is an assembly of one or more **electrical conductors** (typically copper or aluminum) mechanically bonded together with **polymeric insulation, bedding, fillers, and protective sheathing/jacketing**, designed for the **efficient, safe, and reliable transmission of electrical power, signals, or data** in accordance with specified voltage, current, temperature, and environmental conditions.

In the context of **Polymer Product Manufacturing (PCPOT503 – Module 2)**, cables are essentially **multi-layered polymer-composite products** in which polymers provide:
- **Electrical insulation** (primary function)
- **Mechanical protection** (tensile, abrasion, impact resistance)
- **Environmental shielding** (moisture, chemicals, UV, flame, oil)
- **Thermal stability** (over a defined temperature rating, e.g., **70 °C, 90 °C, 105 °C**)

The polymer layers are typically **extruded continuously** over the conductor using **crosshead dies** on **wire and cable extrusion lines**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation (Real-World Analogy):** Think of a polymer cable as the **"layered skin of a modern skyscraper."**
> - The **copper/aluminum conductor** is the **structural steel core** (carries the load — here, electrical load).
> - The **inner polymer insulation** is the **interior drywall** (isolates, prevents leakage).
> - The **bedding and fillers** are the **service ducts and insulation batts** (give roundness, fill gaps).
> - The **armour (steel wire/tape)** is the **reinforced concrete outer wall** (mechanical protection).
> - The **outer sheath (PVC/PE/HFFR)** is the **raincoat + weather-shield façade** (environmental barrier, UV, flame, moisture).
>
> Just as a building's skin is engineered in **multiple layers** for different functions, a cable is engineered in **concentric extruded/laminated layers** of polymer compounds — each layer performing a unique role in the overall system integrity.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Numerical Highlights

The following metrics are **industry-standard benchmarks** used globally in cable manufacturing:

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Rated Voltage (U₀/U)** | **600/1000 V, 1.9/3.3 kV, 6.35/11 kV, 11/11 kV, 33/33 kV** | Defines insulation thickness per IS 7098 / IEC 60502 |
| **Conductor Resistivity (Cu)** | **1.724 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m at 20 °C** | IACS standard |
| **Conductor Resistivity (Al)** | **2.654 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m at 20 °C** | 61% IACS |
| **Temperature Rating (PVC)** | **70 °C (continuous), 160 °C (short-circuit)** | IS 5831 |
| **Temperature Rating (XLPE)** | **90 °C (continuous), 250 °C (short-circuit)** | IS 7098 |
| **Temperature Rating (EPR)** | **90 °C – 105 °C** | IS 6380 |
| **Flame Retardancy (Oxygen Index)** | **≥ 28 %** (FR) , **≥ 32 %** (FRLS) | ASTM D2863 |
| **Smoke Density (Ds max)** | **≤ 60 %** for FRLS-H | ASTM D2843 |

## 1.4 Historical Context (Brief)

The evolution of polymer cables mirrors the evolution of polymer science:
1. **Pre-1950s** — Varnished cambric, rubber, gutta-percha insulation.
2. **1950s–1970s** — **PVC** (Polyvinyl Chloride) became dominant due to its flame retardancy and low cost.
3. **1970s–1990s** — **XLPE** (Cross-linked Polyethylene) revolutionized medium/high-voltage cables due to its **higher temperature rating** and **lower dielectric loss**.
4. **2000s onward** — **LSZH/HFFR** (Low Smoke Zero Halogen / Halogen-Free Flame Retardant) compounds emerged for **fire-safety-critical** applications (metros, hospitals, data centers).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ POLYMER CABLES — SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Polymer Cables (KTU Syllabus Domain)

Polymer cables are classified based on **function, voltage rating, and construction**:

### A. By Function
- **Power Cables** — Energy transmission (LV, MV, HV, EHV)
- **Control Cables** — Signalling, instrumentation
- **Communication Cables** — Coaxial, telephone, Ethernet (UTP/STP)
- **Optical Fiber Cables** — Data via glass/polymer fiber
- **Specialty Cables** — Fire-survival, submarine, nuclear, aerospace

### B. By Voltage Rating
| Class | Voltage (U₀/U) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Low Voltage (LV)** | **≤ 1000 V AC / 1500 V DC** | Building wiring, appliances |
| **Medium Voltage (MV)** | **1 kV – 33 kV** | Distribution networks |
| **High Voltage (HV)** | **33 kV – 230 kV** | Transmission |
| **Extra-High Voltage (EHV)** | **> 230 kV** | Bulk power transmission |

### C. By Construction (Cores, Layers)
- **Single-core**
- **Multi-core (2-core, 3-core, 4-core)**
- **Armoured (SWA — Steel Wire Armoured, STA — Steel Tape Armoured)**
- **Unarmoured**
- **Screened / Unscreened**

## 2.2 Constructional Anatomy of a Typical Power Cable

A standard **multi-core LV PVC/XLPE power cable** has the following concentric layers (from inside outward):

1. **Conductor** — Cu (Class 1, 2, 5, 6) or Al (Class 1, 2)
2. **Conductor Screen** (semi-conductive tape/polymer) — for MV/HV only
3. **Insulation** — PVC / XLPE / EPR (extruded)
4. **Insulation Screen** (semi-conductive) — for MV/HV
5. **Metallic Screen / Drainage** — Copper tape / wire (optional)
6. **Inner Sheath / Bedding** — PVC / PE (extruded)
7. **Filler** — Non-hygroscopic PP yarn / jute
8. **Armour** — Steel wire (SWA) or steel tape (STA)
9. **Outer Sheath** — PVC / PE / LSZH (extruded, with optional **graphite coating** for sliding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Focus:** The **sequence of layers** and the **material grade** of each layer is a **high-frequency 7-mark question** in Module 2 of PCPOT503.

## 2.3 Polymer Materials Used in Cables

| Cable Layer | Common Polymer | Key Property Required | Standards |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Insulation (LV)** | **PVC** (general purpose) | Dielectric strength ≥ 30 kV/mm | IS 5831 |
| **Insulation (MV/HV)** | **XLPE** | Low tan δ, high TD strength | IS 7098 (Pt-I, Pt-II) |
| **Insulation (flexible cords)** | **EPR / Silicone rubber** | Flexibility + heat resistance | IS 6380 |
| **Bedding / Inner Sheath** | **PVC Type ST1/ST2** | Mechanical cushioning | IS 5831 |
| **Outer Sheath** | **PVC Type ST3 / ST4 / FR / FRLS** | Weather, oil, flame, UV | IS 5831 |
| **Outer Sheath (Hazardous)** | **LSZH / HFFR** | Low smoke, halogen-free | IEC 60754, IEC 61034 |
| **Optical Fiber Coating** | **UV-cured acrylate** | Cure speed, low shrinkage | Telcordia GR-20 |

### 2.3.1 Why XLPE for MV/HV?
- **Lower Dielectric Loss:** tan δ (XLPE) ≈ **0.0005** vs. tan δ (PVC) ≈ **0.08**.
- **Higher Continuous Operating Temperature:** **90 °C** vs. **70 °C** → **29%** higher current carrying capacity.
- **Superior Thermal Endurance:** Activation energy ~ 1.3 eV.
- **Moisture Resistance:** Cross-linking reduces free-volume diffusion.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **exam-essential** for KTU PCPOT503 Module 2 (Cables).

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Key Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DC Resistance of Conductor** | $R_{DC} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$ | Ω | ρ = resistivity (Ω·m) |
| 2 | **AC Resistance Correction** | $R_{AC} = R_{DC} \cdot (1 + y_p + y_s)$ | Ω | y_p = proximity effect, y_s = skin effect |
| 3 | **Skin Effect Factor (y_s)** | $y_s = \dfrac{x_s^4}{192 + 0.8 \cdot x_s^4}$ | — | $x_s^2 = \dfrac{8 \pi f}{R_{DC}} \cdot 10^{-7} \cdot k_s$ |
| 4 | **Proximity Effect Factor (y_p)** | $y_p = \dfrac{x_p^4}{192 + 0.8 \cdot x_p^4} \cdot \left(\dfrac{d_c}{s}\right)^2 \cdot \left[0.312 \cdot \left(\dfrac{d_c}{s}\right)^2 + \dfrac{1.18}{\dfrac{x_p^4}{192+0.8x_p^4}+0.27}\right]$ | — | Multi-core correction |
| 5 | **Insulation Resistance** | $R_i = \dfrac{\rho_i}{2\pi L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D}{d}\right)$ | Ω | D = outer insulation dia, d = conductor dia |
| 6 | **Capacitance (Single-core)** | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot 2\pi L}{\ln(D/d)}$ | F | ε_r = relative permittivity |
| 7 | **Dielectric Loss (Charging Current)** | $W_d = 2\pi f C \cdot V^2 \cdot \tan\delta$ | W | Critical for MV/HV |
| 8 | **Current Carrying Capacity (steady-state)** | $I = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta\theta \cdot (T_1 + n \cdot T_2 + T_3) - W_d}{R_{AC}}}$ | A | IEC 60287 |
| 9 | **Conductor Cross-section (A)** | $A = \dfrac{n \cdot I \cdot \rho \cdot L \cdot 2 \cdot 10^{-3}}{V_d}$ | mm² | Voltage-drop method |
| 10 | **Insulation Thickness (empirical)** | $t_i = k \cdot V_{rated} + 0.5$ (mm) | mm | Empirical, IS 7098 |
| 11 | **Power Loss in Conductor** | $P_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R_{AC}$ | W | Per phase |
| 12 | **Efficiency of Transmission** | $\eta = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi}{V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi + I^2 R_{AC} + W_d}$ | — | — |
| 13 | **Skin Depth (δ)** | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{(2\pi f)(\mu_0 \mu_r)}}$ | m | Frequency-dependent |
| 14 | **Conductor Mass (per km)** | $m = A \cdot \rho_m \cdot 1000$ | kg/km | ρ_m = mass density |

### 2.4.1 Quick-Reference Constants (in **bold** as required)

- **Resistivity of Copper ρ_Cu = 1.724 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m at 20 °C**
- **Resistivity of Aluminum ρ_Al = 2.654 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m at 20 °C**
- **Permittivity of XLPE ε_r ≈ 2.3**
- **Permittivity of PVC ε_r ≈ 4.0 – 8.0**
- **Dielectric strength of XLPE ≈ 22 – 30 kV/mm**
- **Dielectric strength of PVC ≈ 30 – 50 kV/mm**
- **Dielectric loss factor (tan δ) of XLPE ≈ 0.0005 – 0.001**
- **Dielectric loss factor (tan δ) of PVC ≈ 0.05 – 0.10**

## 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Significance

- **Power utilities:** XLPE cables form the **backbone of urban distribution** networks — they allow underground transmission without corona losses.
- **Building services:** PVC-insulated wires (e.g., **FR PVC**) are the default choice for residential and commercial buildings.
- **Data centers:** **LSZH/HFFR sheathed** Cat 6/6A cables are mandatory for fire safety (NFPA 262 / IEC 60332).
- **Railways & Metros:** **Fire-survival cables** (mica-tape + XLPE + LSZH) maintain circuit integrity for 90 min at 950 °C.
- **Renewable Energy:** **XLPE DC cables** rated for 1.5 kV DC are used in solar farms.
- **Oil & Gas:** **Lead-sheathed + steel-armoured** cables with EPR insulation resist H₂S and hydrocarbons.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 POLYMER CABLES — SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — DC Resistance of a Conductor

**Statement:** Show that the DC resistance of a uniform conductor of length *L* and cross-sectional area *A* is given by

$$R_{DC} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$

**Step-by-step Derivation:**

Consider a cylindrical conductor of:
- Length = $L$ (m)
- Uniform cross-sectional area = $A$ (m²)
- Material resistivity = $\rho$ (Ω·m)

The **Ohm's law** in differential form is:

$$\vec{J} = \sigma \cdot \vec{E}$$

where $J$ is current density (A/m²) and $\sigma$ is conductivity (S/m).

The **resistance** is defined as:

$$R = \dfrac{V}{I} = \dfrac{\int \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l}}{\int \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{A}}$$

For a uniform field inside a straight conductor:

$$\vec{E} = \dfrac{V}{L} \hat{l} \quad \text{and} \quad \vec{J} = \dfrac{I}{A} \hat{l}$$

Substituting into the integral form:

$$R = \dfrac{(V/L) \cdot L}{(I/A) \cdot A} = \dfrac{V}{I}$$

Now, $V = E \cdot L$ and $I = J \cdot A = \sigma E \cdot A = \dfrac{E \cdot A}{\rho}$.

Therefore:

$$R = \dfrac{E \cdot L}{\dfrac{E \cdot A}{\rho}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}$$

$$\boxed{\,R_{DC} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{A}\,}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Resistance is **directly proportional** to length and **inversely proportional** to area — the reason why **long-distance power cables use large cross-section** conductors (typically **185 mm², 240 mm², 400 mm²**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Capacitance and Charging Current of a Single-Core Cable

**Statement:** A single-core cable has a conductor of diameter *d*, insulation outer diameter *D*, and relative permittivity *ε_r*. Derive the **capacitance per unit length** and the **charging current**.

**Step-by-step Derivation:**

Assume a **cylindrical capacitor** model (conductor = inner plate, outer screen = outer plate).

By **Gauss's Law**, at a radius *r* (where $d/2 \leq r \leq D/2$) inside the insulation, the electric flux density is:

$$D(r) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi r L}$$

where $Q$ is the charge per unit length (C/m) and $L$ is the length.

The electric field is:

$$E(r) = \dfrac{D(r)}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot r L}$$

The **potential difference** between the conductor surface ($r = d/2$) and the insulation outer surface ($r = D/2$) is:

$$V = \int_{d/2}^{D/2} E(r) \, dr = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L} \int_{d/2}^{D/2} \dfrac{dr}{r}$$

Integrating:

$$V = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{D}{d}\right)$$

By definition, $C = Q/V$:

$$C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(D/d)}$$

Per unit length ($L = 1$ m):

$$\boxed{\,C_0 = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\ln(D/d)}\, \text{ (F/m)}\,}$$

**Charging Current** at supply frequency *f* and voltage *V*:

$$I_c = \dfrac{V}{X_c} = 2\pi f C_0 \cdot V$$

$$\boxed{\,I_c = 2\pi f C_0 V = \dfrac{2\pi f \cdot 2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot V}{\ln(D/d)}\,}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU type):** A single-core cable has $d = 10$ mm, $D = 30$ mm, $\varepsilon_r = 2.3$ (XLPE), $V = 11$ kV, $f = 50$ Hz.

$$C_0 = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 2.3}{\ln(30/10)} = \dfrac{1.279 \times 10^{-10}}{1.0986} \approx 1.164 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F/m}$$

$$I_c = 2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 1.164 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 11000 \approx 0.402 \text{ A per phase}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dielectric Loss (W_d) in a Cable

**Statement:** Show that the **dielectric loss per phase** in an AC cable is given by $W_d = \omega C V^2 \tan\delta$.

**Step-by-step Derivation:**

In a real dielectric, the **phasor relationship** between current density $\vec{J}$ and electric field $\vec{E}$ involves a **loss component** characterized by $\tan\delta$:

$$\vec{J}_{total} = \vec{J}_{capacitive} + \vec{J}_{resistive}$$

$$\vec{J}_{total} = j \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \vec{E} + \sigma \vec{E} = j \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \vec{E} (1 - j \tan\delta)$$

where $\tan\delta = \dfrac{\sigma}{\omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$ is the **loss tangent**.

The **complex power** per unit volume dissipated in the dielectric is:

$$P_{vol} = \vec{E} \cdot \vec{J}^* = \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot \vert E \vert^2 \cdot \tan\delta$$

Integrating over the dielectric volume between radii $d/2$ and $D/2$ and length $L$:

$$W_d = \int_{d/2}^{D/2} \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \cdot \tan\delta \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{r \ln(D/d)}\right)^2 \cdot 2\pi r L \cdot dr$$

$$W_d = \dfrac{2\pi \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \tan\delta \cdot V^2 L}{\ln^2(D/d)} \int_{d/2}^{D/2} \dfrac{dr}{r}$$

$$W_d = \dfrac{2\pi \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \tan\delta \cdot V^2 L}{\ln(D/d)}$$

Recognizing that $C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(D/d)}$:

$$\boxed{\,W_d = \omega C V^2 \tan\delta = 2\pi f C V^2 \tan\delta\, \text{ (W per phase)}\,}$$

**Engineering Significance:** For **long EHV AC cables**, $W_d$ can be **3 – 5% of transmitted power** — this is why **HVDC XLPE cables** are preferred for distances **> 80 km** (no charging current, no dielectric loss).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Cable vs. Voltage Level

| Voltage Class | Conductor Size (typical) | Insulation Material | Insulation Thickness | Sheath Material | Armour |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1.1 kV (LV)** | 1.5 – 400 mm² | PVC / XLPE | 0.6 – 2.0 mm | PVC / LSZH | SWA (optional) |
| **3.3 kV (MV)** | 16 – 400 mm² | XLPE | 2.0 – 3.4 mm | PVC / LSZH | SWA/STA |
| **11 kV (MV)** | 25 – 630 mm² | XLPE | 3.4 – 4.5 mm | PVC / LSZH | SWA/STA |
| **33 kV (HV)** | 120 – 1000 mm² | XLPE | 8.0 – 10.0 mm | PVC / LSZH | SWA/STA |
| **66 kV+** | 300 – 2500 mm² | XLPE / EPR | > 12 mm | Lead + LSZH | Optional |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU ESE Pattern)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 11 kV, 50 Hz, 5 km long, 3-core XLPE cable has a conductor diameter of 10 mm, insulation thickness of 4 mm, and a relative permittivity of 2.3. Calculate:
1. Capacitance per phase
2. Charging current per phase
3. Total dielectric loss if tan δ = 0.001

**Given:** $d = 10$ mm, $t_i = 4$ mm, $D = d + 2t_i = 18$ mm, $V_{phase} = 11000/\sqrt{3} = 6350.85$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $\varepsilon_r = 2.3$, $\tan\delta = 0.001$.

**Step 1 — Capacitance per phase:**

$$C_0 = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 2.3}{\ln(18/10)}$$

$$C_0 = \dfrac{1.279 \times 10^{-10}}{\ln(1.8)} = \dfrac{1.279 \times 10^{-10}}{0.5878} \approx 2.176 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F/m}$$

Total capacitance for 5 km:

$$C_{total} = 2.176 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 5000 = 1.088 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F} = 1.088 \,\mu\text{F}$$

**Step 2 — Charging current per phase:**

$$I_c = 2\pi f \cdot C_{total} \cdot V_{phase} = 2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 1.088 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 6350.85$$

$$I_c \approx 2.171 \text{ A per phase}$$

**Step 3 — Dielectric loss (per phase):**

$$W_d = 2\pi f \cdot C_{total} \cdot V_{phase}^2 \cdot \tan\delta$$

$$W_d = 2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 1.088 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (6350.85)^2 \cdot 0.001$$

$$W_d \approx 13.79 \text{ W per phase}$$

Total 3-phase dielectric loss: $3 \times 13.79 \approx 41.4$ W.

$$\boxed{\,C_{total} \approx 1.088 \,\mu F, \quad I_c \approx 2.17 \text{ A}, \quad W_{d,3\phi} \approx 41.4 \text{ W}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The low value of dielectric loss (~41 W for 5 km of 11 kV cable) clearly demonstrates the **techno-economic superiority of XLPE** over PVC for MV applications.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ POLYMER CABLES — SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Polymer Cable Extrusion Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pay1[Payoff Reel] --> draw[Drawn Copper Conductor]
    draw --> anneal[Inductive Annealer]
    anneal --> acc1[Accumulator]
    acc1 --> extr1[Crosshead Extruder - Insulation]
    extr1 --> ccv1[CCV Curing Tube]
    ccv1 --> cap1[Capstan 1]
    cap1 --> extr2[Crosshead Extruder - Inner Sheath]
    extr2 --> cap2[Capstan 2]
    cap2 --> lay[Laying Up Machine - 3 Core]
    lay --> extr3[Crosshead Extruder - Bedding]
    extr3 --> arm[Armouring Machine SWA STA]
    arm --> extr4[Crosshead Extruder - Outer Sheath]
    extr4 --> print[Ink Jet Printing]
    print --> cap3[Take Up Capstan]
    cap3 --> take[Take Up Reel]
    ccv1 --> spark1[Spark Tester HV]
    spark1 --> cap1
    cap2 --> spark2[Sieve Tester]
    spark2 --> lay
    extr4 --> spark3[Sheath Spark Tester]
    spark3 --> print
```

**Functional Block Descriptions:**

| Block | Function | Key Polymer Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Payoff Reel** | Unwinds incoming conductor | None |
| **Inductive Annealer** | Softens Cu for bending | Thermal |
| **Crosshead Extruder – Insulation** | Extrudes XLPE / PVC over conductor | **Polymer Melt Processing** |
| **CCV Curing Tube** | Cross-links PE under steam/nitrogen pressure | **CV Curing** |
| **Capstan** | Pulls cable at controlled speed | Mechanical |
| **Spark Tester** | Detects insulation pinholes via HV | QC |
| **Laying Up** | Twists 3 cores with fillers | Mechanical |
| **Armouring** | Wraps steel wire/tape | Mechanical |
| **Outer Sheath Extruder** | Extrudes outer PVC/LSZH jacket | **Polymer Coating** |
| **Ink Jet Printer** | Prints cable identification | Surface Marking |
| **Take Up Reel** | Winds finished cable | None |

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Concentric Layer Architecture of a Polymer Cable

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Conductor Cu or Al] --> L1[Conductor Screen Optional Semi Conductive]
    L1 --> L2[Primary Insulation XLPE or PVC or EPR]
    L2 --> L3[Insulation Screen Optional Semi Conductive]
    L3 --> L4[Metallic Screen Cu Tape or Wire]
    L4 --> L5[Inner Sheath PVC Bedding]
    L5 --> L6[Filler PP Yarn or Jute]
    L6 --> L7[Armour SWA or STA]
    L7 --> L8[Outer Sheath PVC or PE or LSZH]
    L8 --> L9[Optional Graphite Coating Anti Stick]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Process Selection Logic for Cable Polymers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Cable Design Start] --> volt{Rated Voltage}
    volt -->|Less than 1 kV| lv[Use PVC or XLPE Insulation]
    volt -->|1 kV to 33 kV| mv[Use XLPE Insulation]
    volt -->|Above 33 kV| hv[Use XLPE with Metallic Screen Lead Sheath]
    lv --> env{Environment}
    mv --> env
    hv --> env
    env -->|Indoor General| pvc1[PVC Sheath]
    env -->|Fire Safety Critical| lz[LSZH or HFFR Sheath]
    env -->|Outdoor UV Exposed| pe1[PE Sheath with UV Stabilizer]
    env -->|Oil Chemical Exposure| csp[Chlorosulfonated PE Hypalon Sheath]
    pvc1 --> mech{Armour Required}
    lz --> mech
    pe1 --> mech
    csp --> mech
    mech -->|Yes Direct Burial| swa[SWA Armour]
    mech -->|No Indoor Tray| ua[Unarmoured]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Quality Control Station Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Raw Material Inward QC] --> comp[Compounding Hot Melt Mix]
    comp --> rheo[Online Rheometer]
    rheo --> ext[Extrusion Line]
    ext --> spark[In Line Spark Tester]
    spark --> cap[Capstan]
    cap --> ink[Ink Jet Printer]
    ink --> lab[Off Line Lab Testing]
    lab --> tens[Tensile and Elongation]
    lab --> hot[Hot Set Test for XLPE]
    lab --> oi[Oxygen Index Test for FR]
    lab --> td[Tan Delta and Capacitance]
    lab --> hvdc[HVDC Pressure Test for HV]
    hvdc --> ship[Final Reel and Despatch]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Hot Set Test (IEC 60811-2-1)** is the **definitive QC test** for XLPE cross-linking — a properly cross-linked XLPE must withstand **200% elongation at 200 °C** under **20 N/cm² load** for **15 minutes** without breaking. This single test, in the KTU 2024 lab record, earns **5 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 POLYMER CABLES — SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (3 Marks)**
**Define a polymer cable. List any four polymer materials commonly used in cable construction with their primary function.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A polymer cable is an assembly of one or more electrical conductors insulated and protected by one or more layers of polymeric materials, designed for the safe and efficient transmission of electrical power, signals, or data.

| Polymer Material | Primary Function |
|---|---|
| **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride)** | Insulation for LV cables; general-purpose sheathing |
| **XLPE (Cross-linked Polyethylene)** | Insulation for MV/HV cables; higher temperature rating |
| **EPR (Ethylene Propylene Rubber)** | Flexible insulation for trailing cables and welding leads |
| **LSZH / HFFR (Low Smoke Zero Halogen / Halogen-Free Flame Retardant)** | Outer sheath in fire-safety-critical environments (metros, hospitals) |

*(Any four valid polymer–function pairs = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (3 Marks)**
**Differentiate between PVC and XLPE as cable insulation with respect to (i) maximum continuous operating temperature, (ii) dielectric loss (tan δ), and (iii) typical application voltage range.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | PVC | XLPE |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Max Continuous Operating Temperature** | **70 °C** | **90 °C** |
| **(ii) Dielectric Loss Factor (tan δ)** | **0.05 – 0.10** (high) | **0.0005 – 0.001** (very low) |
| **(iii) Typical Application Voltage** | **LV (≤ 1.1 kV)** | **LV, MV, HV, EHV (up to 500 kV)** |

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice — 1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**

**a)** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, describe the **constructional features of a 3-core 11 kV XLPE armoured power cable**. Identify each layer and state the **material of construction** for each. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A single-core 11 kV, 50 Hz cable has a conductor diameter of 12 mm and an insulation thickness of 5 mm. The relative permittivity of the insulation is 2.3. Calculate **(i)** the capacitance per km, **(ii)** the charging current per phase, and **(iii)** the total dielectric loss per phase if tan δ = 0.001. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The 3-core 11 kV XLPE armoured power cable has the following concentric layers (from inside outward):

| Layer | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Conductor (×3)** | Stranded Copper (Class 2) or Aluminum | Carries the current |
| **2. Conductor Screen** | Semi-conductive XLPE compound | Uniformizes electric field |
| **3. Insulation** | **XLPE** (Cross-linked Polyethylene) | Primary dielectric barrier |
| **4. Insulation Screen** | Semi-conductive XLPE compound | Symmetric electric stress |
| **5. Metallic Screen** | Copper tape / wire (0.5 – 1.0 mm² equiv.) | Carries fault current, earthing |
| **6. Fillers** | Non-hygroscopic PP yarn or jute | Maintains circular cross-section |
| **7. Inner Sheath (Bedding)** | PVC Type ST1 | Binds cores, cushion under armour |
| **8. Armour** | Steel Wire (SWA) or Steel Tape (STA) | Mechanical protection |
| **9. Outer Sheath** | PVC Type ST3 / FRLS-PVC / LSZH | Moisture, chemical, flame, UV protection |

*(Neat diagram with 9 layers, each labeled with material = 4 marks; 3 marks for correct description of functions.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $d = 12$ mm, $t_i = 5$ mm
- $D = d + 2 t_i = 12 + 10 = 22$ mm
- $V_{phase} = 11000 / \sqrt{3} = 6350.85$ V
- $f = 50$ Hz
- $\varepsilon_r = 2.3$, $\tan\delta = 0.001$
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- $L = 1000$ m

**Step 1 — Capacitance per km:**

$$C_0 = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot L}{\ln(D/d)}$$

$$C_0 = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 2.3 \cdot 1000}{\ln(22/12)}$$

$$C_0 = \dfrac{1.279 \times 10^{-7}}{\ln(1.8333)} = \dfrac{1.279 \times 10^{-7}}{0.6061} \approx 2.110 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F} = 0.211 \,\mu F/\text{km}$$

**Step 2 — Charging current per phase:**

$$I_c = 2\pi f \cdot C_0 \cdot V_{phase}$$

$$I_c = 2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 2.110 \times 10^{-7} \cdot 6350.85$$

$$I_c \approx 0.421 \text{ A per phase per km}$$

**Step 3 — Dielectric loss per phase:**

$$W_d = 2\pi f \cdot C_0 \cdot V_{phase}^2 \cdot \tan\delta$$

$$W_d = 2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot 2.110 \times 10^{-7} \cdot (6350.85)^2 \cdot 0.001$$

$$W_d \approx 2.674 \text{ W per phase per km}$$

**Final Results:**

$$\boxed{\,C_0 \approx 0.211 \,\mu F/km, \quad I_c \approx 0.421 \text{ A/phase/km}, \quad W_d \approx 2.67 \text{ W/phase/km}\,}$$

*(3 marks each for parts i, ii, iii; 1 mark for the final boxed answer; deductions if units missing.)*

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]**

**a)** Explain in detail the **differences between PVC, XLPE, and EPR** as cable insulation materials. Compare them on the basis of **(i) chemical structure, (ii) temperature rating, (iii) dielectric loss, (iv) flexibility, and (v) typical applications.** **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A 1.1 kV, single-core PVC insulated copper cable is to be used to carry a load of 200 A over 100 m. The maximum permissible voltage drop is 3% of the rated voltage. The resistivity of copper is **1.724 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m** at 20 °C. Calculate the **required cross-sectional area of the conductor** and the **conduction (copper) loss** at full load. Take 1 m cable resistance per km per mm² as 0.01724 Ω. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Property | **PVC** | **XLPE** | **EPR** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Chemical Structure** | Polymer of vinyl chloride, contains ~57% Cl | Cross-linked polyethylene, thermoset | Copolymer of ethylene + propylene (EPM) or with diene (EPDM), thermoset |
| **(ii) Temperature Rating** | **70 °C** | **90 °C** | **90 – 105 °C** |
| **(iii) Dielectric Loss (tan δ)** | **0.05 – 0.10** (high) | **0.0005 – 0.001** (very low) | **0.005 – 0.015** (low) |
| **(iv) Flexibility** | Moderate | Poor (rigid) | Excellent (rubbery) |
| **(v) Typical Applications** | Building wires, appliance cords, LV control cables | MV/HV power transmission, distribution | Flexible trailing cables, motor leads, welding cables, marine |

*(1.4 marks per row, 7 marks total; 1 mark extra for concluding statement on selection criterion.)*

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $V = 1.1 \text{ kV} = 1100 \text{ V}$ (single-phase, line-to-line)
- $I = 200 \text{ A}$
- $L = 100 \text{ m}$
- Permissible voltage drop $V_d = 3\% \text{ of } 1100 = 33 \text{ V}$
- $R_{per \, km \, per \, mm^2} = 0.01724 \, \Omega$

**Step 1 — Resistance of cable run (2-way, so total conductor length = 2L = 200 m):**

For a single-phase circuit, the loop length is $2L$.

$$R_{total} = 0.01724 \cdot \dfrac{2L \text{ (in km)}}{A \text{ (in mm}^2\text{)}} = 0.01724 \cdot \dfrac{0.2}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Apply voltage-drop equation:**

$$V_d = I \cdot R_{total} = I \cdot 0.01724 \cdot \dfrac{0.2}{A}$$

$$33 = 200 \cdot 0.01724 \cdot \dfrac{0.2}{A}$$

$$33 = 0.6896 / A$$

$$A = \dfrac{0.6896}{33} = 0.02089 \, \text{mm}^2$$

> **Result is impractically small** — this indicates the cable is **massively oversized** for the voltage-drop criterion alone. Select the **nearest standard size** from IS 3961 / IEC 60228:

**Next standard size: 16 mm², 25 mm², 35 mm², 50 mm², 70 mm², 95 mm², 120 mm², 150 mm², 185 mm², 240 mm², 300 mm², 400 mm², 500 mm², 630 mm².**

**Selection Criterion:** The current-carrying capacity for **1.1 kV PVC single-core Cu cable (clipped direct, 70 °C rating, single circuit)** ≈ **382 A for 95 mm², 451 A for 120 mm², 504 A for 150 mm²** (from IS 3961, Table 4).

Hence the **nearest standard size based on current rating of 200 A** is **50 mm² (rated ~237 A)** or **70 mm² (rated ~291 A)**. We select **70 mm²** for a 30% design margin.

$$\boxed{\,A_{selected} = 70 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ (standard size, governing criterion: current carrying capacity)}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Copper (conduction) loss at full load:**

$$R_{70} = 0.01724 \cdot \dfrac{200}{70} = 0.04926 \, \Omega$$

$$P_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R_{70} = 200^2 \cdot 0.04926 = 40000 \cdot 0.04926 \approx 1970 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{loss} \approx 1.97 \text{ kW} \text{ (at full load of 200 A, 100 m run, 70 mm}^2\text{ Cu cable)}\,}$$

*(2 marks for voltage-drop formula setup; 1 mark for A calculation; 1 mark for current-based selection; 3 marks for copper-loss calculation with units.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — PITFALL CALLOUT**
> **Common mistakes in cable-related questions (each = 1 mark loss):**
> 1. **Forgetting the loop factor (2L)** in single-phase voltage-drop problems — the formula uses **2L, not L**.
> 2. **Using line voltage instead of phase voltage** in capacitance and dielectric-loss problems — always use $V_{phase} = V_{L-L} / \sqrt{3}$ for 3-phase.
> 3. **Confusing DC and AC resistance** — at 50 Hz and large conductors (>185 mm²), skin effect adds **2 – 8%** to DC resistance.
> 4. **Forgetting to apply design margin** — standard practice is **1.25 to 1.5×** the calculated current for safety.
> 5. **Selecting non-standard conductor sizes** — must choose from IS 3961 / IEC 60228 standard tables (16, 25, 35, 50, 70, 95, 120, 150, 185, 240, 300, 400, 500, 630 mm²).
> 6. **Misidentifying cable layers in diagrams** — common confusion between **conductor screen, insulation screen, and metallic screen**. The **insulation is between the two screens** (semi-conductive layers).
> 7. **Wrong units in tan δ problems** — tan δ is **dimensionless**, but the formula $W_d = \omega C V^2 \tan\delta$ yields **watts only if V is in volts and C in farads.**

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]

| # | Key Fact | Memory Hook |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PVC temp rating = 70 °C** | "**P**VC = **P**altry 70" |

| 9 | **SWA = Steel Wire Armour** (direct burial) | "Wire for dig-in" |
