topics.title: Wire Coating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wire Coating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wire Coating** is a continuous polymer extrusion process in which a molten thermoplastic (or curable thermoset/elastomer) compound is uniformly applied as a concentric insulating or protective layer over a moving metallic conductor (copper, aluminium, or tinned wire) using a **crosshead extrusion die** aligned transverse to the wire travel direction.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, wire coating is classified as a **secondary extrusion / profile-coating operation**, belonging to the family of **continuous polymer processing techniques** (akin to film blowing and pipe extrusion, but distinguished by the presence of an internal solid substrate that acts as the forming mandrel).

The three mandatory functional outcomes of any wire coating line are:
1. **Concentric deposition** of the polymer sheath with eccentricity < 10% for Class-1 insulation.
2. **Adhesion control** between metal and polymer (either bonded or strippable, depending on end-use).
3. **Dimensional stability** of the final insulated wire (outer diameter tolerance typically $\pm \ 0.05 \ \text{mm}$ for thin wires).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a **fountain pen nib writing on a moving page**, but in reverse. The page (the wire) is moving continuously through the machine, and instead of ink, a thick molten polymer "ink" is squeezed in a perfect ring around it. As the wire exits, the ring solidifies into a uniform plastic sleeve — that's wire coating!

A more practical real-world analogy is the **candle-making dipping process**, but far more controlled. The wire is the "wick" and the polymer melt bath is the "wax," except here the polymer is pressure-injected around the wire inside a precision-machined metal die rather than relying on gravity dipping.

Another useful mental picture: imagine covering a steel rod with a tight rubber sleeve. You push the rod through a donut-shaped puddle of molten plastic. The plastic freezes the moment it clears the puddle, locking itself around the rod. Wire coating does exactly this at industrial speeds of **200 to 2000 m/min**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Industrial Values)

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Wire diameter ($d_w$) | 0.20 – 4.00 | mm |
| Final insulated diameter ($D_f$) | 0.50 – 25.00 | mm |
| Line speed ($v_L$) | 50 – 2000 | m/min |
| Melt temperature ($T_m$) | 140 – 220 (PVC); 200 – 280 (PE) | °C |
| Coating thickness ($t_c$) | 0.15 – 3.00 | mm |
| Throughput ($Q$) | 5 – 500 | kg/h |

## 1.4 Materials Commonly Used for Wire Coating

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The polymer selection is dictated by the **voltage class**, **temperature rating**, **flexibility requirement**, and **flame-retardancy** specification of the cable.

- **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)** — most widely used; cheap, flame-retardant, easy to colour.
- **Low/Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE/LLDPE)** — used in telephone and data cables.
- **Crosslinked Polyethylene (XLPE)** — for high-voltage power cables (crosslinked post-extrusion).
- **Polyamide (Nylon 6, Nylon 6,6)** — jacketing layer for abrasion resistance.
- **Fluoropolymers (PTFE, FEP, ETFE)** — aerospace, high-temperature, and chemical-resistant wiring.
- **Thermoplastic Elastomers (TPE/TPU)** — flexible cords, USB cables, medical leads.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Classification — The Three Industrial Methods

Wire coating is implemented in industry through three principal methods, each with distinct die geometry and material-flow behaviour:

### 2.1.1 Pressure Coating (T-Head / Crosshead Die Method)
- The polymer melt is forced under pressure into a **crosshead die** oriented at 90° to the wire travel direction.
- The melt flows around a **guide tip** (torpedo/mandrel) and exits as a continuous annular sheath.
- The die land length creates back-pressure, ensuring intimate contact and adhesion.
- **Used for**: thin insulation, tight tolerances, adhesion-type coatings (PVC house wires).

### 2.1.2 Sleeve / Tubing (Collapsing) Method
- Polymer is extruded as a tube **larger than the wire diameter**.
- A vacuum is drawn downstream in a collapsing chamber, sucking the tube onto the wire.
- **Used for**: large cables, jacketing, strippable insulation, when zero adhesion is desired.

### 2.1.3 Electrostatic Coating (Powder Method)
- Fine polymer powder (e.g., PE powder) is fluidised and given an electrostatic charge.
- The grounded wire attracts the powder, which is then melted in a curing oven.
- **Used for**: large HV cables, XLPE insulation.

## 2.2 Why and How — The Governing Physics

> [!NOTE]
> Three simultaneous transport phenomena control the process: (1) **polymer melt flow** through the die, (2) **wire motion** through the die, and (3) **heat transfer** from melt → wire → surroundings. Successful coating requires all three to be in balance.

### Step 1 — Wire Pre-heating
- Cold wire entering the die causes a steep temperature gradient, leading to **shrink marks**, **blow holes**, and **neck-down**.
- Wire is typically pre-heated to **80 – 120 °C** to reduce thermal shock and improve adhesion.

### Step 2 — Melt Conveying and Pressure Generation
- A single-screw extruder (L/D = 20:1 to 30:1) homogenises and pressurises the melt.
- Typical melt pressure at the die entry: **5 – 25 MPa**.

### Step 3 — Crosshead Die Flow
- The melt is split by the wire guide, flows around the mandrel, recombines on the downstream side at the **wire-melt interface**.
- The **land length ($L_{land}$)** typically equals **5 × to 10 ×** the coating thickness.

### Step 4 — Cooling and Sizing
- The coated wire enters a **water trough** (length 6 – 20 m) maintained at **15 – 60 °C**.
- Cooling rate must be controlled to avoid **amorphous orientation** and internal stresses.

### Step 5 — Haul-off and Quality Inspection
- A **dual-wheel capstan** (with caterpillar belts) pulls the wire at constant speed.
- In-line spark testers (≥ 3 kV for thin wires) and laser OD gauges ensure defect detection.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> This is the high-yield formula bank for KTU University Exam problems. Memorise these and their units.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \rho \cdot v_L \cdot A_c$ | Mass throughput = density × line speed × annular cross-section | kg/h |
| 2 | $A_c = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( D_f^2 - d_w^2 \right)$ | Annular area of the polymer sheath | mm² |
| 3 | $t_c = \dfrac{D_f - d_w}{2}$ | Radial coating thickness | mm |
| 4 | $DDR = \dfrac{D_{die}}{D_f}$ | Draw Down Ratio (die exit OD ÷ final OD) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $DDR_{turk} = \dfrac{D_{die}^2 - d_{tip}^2}{D_f^2 - d_w^2}$ | Turk's draw-down ratio accounting for mandrel | dimensionless |
| 6 | $v_{melt} = v_L \cdot DDR$ | Average melt velocity at die exit | m/min |
| 7 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{6 Q}{W \cdot H^2}$ | Shear rate in a rectangular die land (slit approximation) | s⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \ \mu \ Q \ L_{land}}{W \cdot H^3}$ | Pressure drop across die land (Poiseuille flow) | Pa |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{D_f - d_w}{D_f} \times 100$ | Eccentricity / insulation thickness uniformity (%) | % |
| 10 | $P_{haul} = \mu_b \cdot F_{normal}$ | Haul-off force balance (friction-based) | N |
| 11 | $Bi = \dfrac{h \cdot D_f}{2 k_p}$ | Biot number for cooling (h = HTC, $k_p$ = polymer conductivity) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{\rho_p c_p t_c (T_m - T_{water})}{h (T_{surface} - T_{water})}$ | Approximate cooling time | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRTICAL NOTATION NOTE** — In the KTU Formula Sheet above, the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ (absolute value) has been deliberately avoided inside markdown tables. For absolute-value expressions, write it as $\lvert x \rvert$ in your answer scripts to prevent table-formatting breakage during PDF generation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Wire coating is the **backbone of the global electrical infrastructure**. The process is used in:
- **House wiring & building cables** (PVC insulated, 1.5 – 6 mm² copper).
- **Automotive harnesses** (thin-wall PVC, crosslinked PE).
- **Underwater and submarine HV cables** (XLPE with metallic armoring).
- **Data and telecom cables** (foamed PE dielectric for low signal loss).
- **Aerospace & defence wiring** (PTFE, ETFE for high-temperature tolerance).
- **Medical device leads** (TPE / silicone-coated for biocompatibility).

> [!NOTE]
> The global wire and cable market exceeds **USD 200 billion annually**, with PVC and XLPE together accounting for over **70%** of polymeric insulation volume.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Throughput Equation (Mass Balance)

### Problem Statement
Derive the relation between extruder throughput, line speed, and final insulation dimensions for a wire-coating line.

### Given
- Final insulated wire outer diameter: $D_f$
- Bare wire diameter: $d_w$
- Line (haul-off) speed: $v_L$
- Polymer melt density at processing temperature: $\rho$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** In steady-state, the volumetric flow of polymer exiting the die must equal the annular volume of coating produced per unit time (conservation of mass, incompressible melt).

$$Q_{vol} = v_L \cdot A_c$$

where $A_c$ is the cross-sectional area of the polymer sheath.

**Step 2:** The annular cross-section is the area of the outer circle minus the area of the wire (the wire displaces the polymer at its exact diameter):

$$A_c = \frac{\pi D_f^2}{4} - \frac{\pi d_w^2}{4} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_f^2 - d_w^2 \right)$$

**Step 3:** Substituting back:

$$Q_{vol} = v_L \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_f^2 - d_w^2 \right)$$

**Step 4:** To convert volumetric flow to mass flow, multiply by density $\rho$:

$$Q_{mass} = \rho \cdot Q_{vol} = \rho \cdot v_L \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_f^2 - d_w^2 \right)$$

This is the **master throughput equation** for wire coating.

### Numerical Worked Example

**Given:** $D_f = 2.50 \ \text{mm}$, $d_w = 1.00 \ \text{mm}$, $v_L = 500 \ \text{m/min}$, $\rho = 1100 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ (PVC at 180 °C).

**Compute annular area:**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( 2.50^2 - 1.00^2 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( 6.25 - 1.00 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 5.25 = 4.124 \ \text{mm}^2$$

**Convert to SI:**

$$A_c = 4.124 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$v_L = \frac{500}{60} = 8.333 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Mass flow:**

$$Q_{mass} = 1100 \times 8.333 \times 4.124 \times 10^{-6} = 0.0378 \ \text{kg/s} = 136.0 \ \text{kg/h}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Draw Down Ratio (Turk's Method)

### Step 1
Define the geometric Draw Down Ratio (DDR) as the ratio of the die exit outer diameter to the final coated wire diameter:

$$DDR = \frac{D_{die}}{D_f}$$

**Step 2 — Turk's Correction (KTU High-Yield):**
The simple DDR ignores the mandrel (guide tip) diameter. Turk introduced a corrected ratio that includes the tip:

$$DDR_{Turk} = \frac{D_{die}^2 - d_{tip}^2}{D_f^2 - d_w^2}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Significance:**
- If $DDR_{Turk} > 1.0$: polymer is being **stretched** (neck-down possible).
- If $DDR_{Turk} = 1.0$: no stretching; equilibrium flow.
- If $DDR_{Turk} < 1.0$: polymer is being **accumulated** (die swell dominant, swelling visible at die exit).
- Practical range: $DDR_{Turk} = 1.0$ to $1.5$.

### Numerical Example
Given: $D_{die} = 6.0 \ \text{mm}$, $d_{tip} = 2.0 \ \text{mm}$, $D_f = 2.5 \ \text{mm}$, $d_w = 1.0 \ \text{mm}$.

$$DDR_{Turk} = \frac{6.0^2 - 2.0^2}{2.5^2 - 1.0^2} = \frac{36 - 4}{6.25 - 1.00} = \frac{32}{5.25} = 6.095$$

> [!WARNING]
> A DDR of 6.095 is **excessively high** and will cause excessive draw-down, surface fibrillation, and residual orientation stresses. Reduce $D_{die}$ or increase the wire diameter to bring DDR into the 1.0 – 1.5 range.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Drop in the Die Land (Poiseuille Flow Model)

### Step 1
Treat the die land as a thin rectangular slit of width $W$ (circumference of the die) and gap $H$ (radial die land clearance).

$$W \approx \pi \cdot D_{mean} = \pi \left( D_{die} + d_{tip} \right) / 2$$

**Step 2:** For fully developed, isothermal, laminar flow of a Newtonian melt (zero-order approximation; suitable for KTU numerical problems), the Hagen-Poiseuille equation applies:

$$\Delta P = \frac{12 \ \mu \ Q \ L_{land}}{W \cdot H^3}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example:**
Given: $\mu = 500 \ \text{Pa.s}$ (PVC melt), $Q = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $L_{land} = 0.020 \ \text{m}$, $W = 0.025 \ \text{m}$, $H = 0.001 \ \text{m}$.

$$\Delta P = \frac{12 \times 500 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.020}{0.025 \times (0.001)^3}$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-11}} = 4.8 \times 10^{7} \ \text{Pa} = 48 \ \text{MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, this 48 MPa far exceeds typical die-pressure ratings (15 MPa). Either reduce $Q$, increase $H$, or shorten $L_{land}$. This illustrates why die designers iteratively balance these four variables.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cooling Time Estimate

### Step 1
Heat balance on the coating (assuming lumped capacitance and 1-D radial conduction from outside to inside):

$$q = h \cdot A_s \cdot (T_s - T_{water})$$

**Step 2:** Heat to be removed to solidify the polymer (sensible + latent, for semi-crystalline materials):

$$q = \rho_p \cdot V \cdot [c_p (T_m - T_{water}) + \Delta H_f]$$

**Step 3:** Equating and simplifying for the cooling time:

$$t_{cool} = \frac{\rho_p \ c_p \ t_c \ (T_m - T_{water})}{h \ (T_{s,avg} - T_{water})}$$

### Numerical Example
PVC coating: $\rho_p = 1400 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1500 \ \text{J/kg.K}$, $t_c = 0.75 \ \text{mm} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}$, $T_m = 180 \ °C$, $T_{water} = 25 \ °C$, $h = 300 \ \text{W/m}^2\text{.K}$, $T_{s,avg} = 110 \ °C$.

$$t_{cool} = \frac{1400 \times 1500 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \times (180 - 25)}{300 \times (110 - 25)}$$

$$t_{cool} = \frac{1400 \times 1500 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 155}{300 \times 85}$$

$$t_{cool} = \frac{243.9}{25500} = 9.56 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{s} \approx 10 \ \text{ms}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This calculation is for the **surface-to-water** conduction only. Total trough length is determined by integrating cooling rate along the wire travel — typically requiring 6 – 20 m trough length depending on line speed.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Defect → Cause → Remedy

> [!TIP]
> High-yield table for KTU viva and short-answer questions. Memorise the **mechanism**, not just the symptom.

| Defect | Primary Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|
| Eccentric coating (off-centre) | Misaligned guide tip, worn die | Realign torpedo, replace die |
| Surface roughness / melt fracture | Excessive shear rate ($\dot{\gamma} > 500 \ \text{s}^{-1}$) | Increase melt temperature, reduce line speed |
| Blow holes / voids | Moisture in polymer, volatile additives | Pre-dry polymer (e.g., 80 °C, 4 h for PA) |
| Neck-down / thinning | DDR too high | Increase die land, reduce haul-off speed |
| Poor adhesion | Wire surface contamination, cold wire | Clean & pre-heat wire to > 80 °C |
| Die-lip buildup (drool) | Insufficient land length, low viscosity | Polish die lips, add land length |
| Sharkskin | Wall slip at high shear | Add process-aid, lower throughput |
| Sink marks on insulation | Non-uniform cooling | Adjust water trough temperature gradient |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Line Architecture — Wire Coating Extrusion Line

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the **functional block-level architecture** of a complete wire-coating line, from pay-off reel to take-up spool. It is rendered in Mermaid so it can be regenerated in any compatible viewer (GitHub, VS Code preview, Obsidian, etc.).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Pay-off Reel] --> A2[Wire Tension Control]
    A2 --> A3[Wire Pre-heater]
    A3 --> A4[Crosshead Die with Extruder]
    A4 --> A5[Primary Cooling Trough]
    A5 --> A6[Diameter Gauge Spark Tester]
    A6 --> A7[Dual Capstan Haul-off]
    A7 --> A8[Secondary Cooling Trough]
    A8 --> A9[Spark Tester HV]
    A9 --> A10[Take-up Spool]
    A4 --- E1[Hopper with Drying Unit]
    E1 --- E2[Screw Extruder L over D 25 to 1]
    E2 --- E3[Melt Pump and Filter]
    E3 --- A4
```

## 4.2 Crosshead Die — Internal Melt Flow Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block diagram shows the **internal melt path** inside a pressure-type crosshead die. The wire enters axially and the melt enters at 90° — this is the defining geometric feature of "crosshead" dies.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[Inlet Polymer Melt from Extruder 90 deg entry] --> B2[Distributor Manifold Channel]
    B2 --> B3[Conical Guide Tip Torpedo]
    B3 --> B4[Wire Inlet along axis]
    B4 --> B5[Melt flows around guide tip]
    B5 --> B6[Recombination point wire meets melt]
    B6 --> B7[Die Land annular channel]
    B7 --> B8[Die Exit coated wire]
    B8 --> B9[Cooling Trough]
    B2 -.annular flow.-> B7
```

## 4.3 Tubing (Sleeve) Coating Die — Collapsing Method Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Wire Inlet straight pass] --> C2[Crosshead Die extrudes larger tube]
    C2 --> C3[Air Gap sizing zone]
    C3 --> C4[Vacuum Sizing Chamber collapses tube onto wire]
    C4 --> C5[Collapsed Coating shrinks onto wire]
    C5 --> C6[Water Cooling Trough]
    C6 --> C7[Capstan Pull]
    C7 --> C8[Take-up Spool]
```

## 4.4 Control and Instrumentation Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> A modern wire-coating line has **closed-loop control** on line speed, melt temperature, melt pressure, and outer diameter. The block diagram below shows the feedback architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1[Setpoint: Line Speed Diameter] --> D2[PLC Controller]
    D2 --> D3[Capstan Drive VFD]
    D3 --> D4[Wire Motion]
    D4 --> D5[Laser OD Gauge Sensor]
    D5 --> D6[Feedback Signal]
    D6 --> D2
    D2 --> D7[Heater Bands]
    D7 --> D8[Melt Temperature]
    D8 --> D9[Thermocouple]
    D9 --> D6
```

## 4.5 Defect-Detection Subsystem — Spark Tester Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Insulated Wire Passes Through Beaded Chain Electrode] --> F2[High Voltage AC 3 to 30 kV]
    F2 --> F3{Pin-hole or Thin Spot present}
    F3 -- Yes --> F4[Spark Discharge Current Spike]
    F4 --> F5[Alarm and Mark Reject]
    F3 -- No --> F6[Normal Pass-through]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!TIP]
> These questions are calibrated to the **revised Bloom's Taxonomy** cognitive levels used in the KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE). Marks indicated in brackets correspond to ESE 2024 Scheme weightage.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper PCPOT503]**
*Compare the pressure-coating method and the tubing (sleeve) method of wire coating with respect to die geometry, adhesion, and typical applications.* (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Pressure Coating | Tubing / Sleeve |
|---|---|---|
| Die geometry | Crosshead, tight land | Crosshead, larger OD, vacuum |
| Polymer-wire contact | Forced pressure contact | Vacuum-induced collapse |
| Adhesion to wire | High (bonded) | Low (strippable) |
| Insulation thickness | Tight tolerance | Slightly looser |
| Typical applications | House wiring (PVC), signal cables | Cable jacketing, HV XLPE |

> **Marking allocation:** 1 mark for die geometry, 1 for adhesion difference, 1 for application example.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PCPOT503]**
*What is the function of a crosshead die in a wire coating line? Why is the wire pre-heated before entering the die?* (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **crosshead die** orients the polymer melt entry at 90° to the wire travel direction. The melt is split by a guide tip (mandrel), flows around the wire, recombines on the downstream side, and exits as a concentric annular coating. The die land controls back-pressure, thickness, and surface finish.

The wire is pre-heated to **80 – 120 °C** to:
1. **Prevent thermal shock** between the hot melt and cold metal, which causes voids, blow holes, and neck-down.
2. **Improve melt adhesion** by activating the metal surface and reducing viscosity at the wire-melt interface.
3. **Reduce differential shrinkage stresses** during cooling.

> **Marking allocation:** 1.5 marks for die function, 1.5 marks for the three pre-heating reasons.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, Module 2 carries a mandatory **internal choice** of two questions (a) and (b) — both 14 marks. We present TWO full alternative questions so you can practise both.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Describe with a neat schematic the construction and working of a crosshead (T-head) die used for pressure-type wire coating. Explain the role of the guide tip, die land, and die lip.*

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Construction (3 marks):** A crosshead die consists of a die body, an inlet port aligned perpendicular to the wire path, a conical guide tip (torpedo/mandrel), an annular die land of length $L_{land}$, and a die exit lip. The wire is fed axially through the centre of the die.

2. **Working (2 marks):** Molten polymer from the extruder enters the die inlet, flows through a distribution channel around the guide tip, and is forced under pressure around the moving wire. The polymer recombines at the **weld line** (also called the "knit line") on the far side of the guide tip and is drawn out as a concentric sleeve.

3. **Role of components (2 marks):**
   - **Guide tip (mandrel):** splits the melt flow, ensures concentricity, sets the inner diameter of the coating.
   - **Die land:** creates back-pressure, improves melt homogeneity, removes weld-line weakness by forcing polymer re-mixing.
   - **Die lip:** controls final surface finish; sharp lip = glossy surface; recessed lip = matt finish.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*For a PVC wire coating line, the following data is given: bare copper wire diameter = 1.20 mm, final insulated wire diameter = 2.40 mm, line speed = 600 m/min, melt density of PVC at 180 °C = 1080 kg/m³. Calculate: (i) the coating thickness, (ii) the volumetric throughput in cm³/s, and (iii) the mass throughput in kg/h.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $d_w = 1.20 \ \text{mm}$, $D_f = 2.40 \ \text{mm}$, $v_L = 600 \ \text{m/min}$, $\rho = 1080 \ \text{kg/m}^3$.

**(i) Coating thickness:**

$$t_c = \frac{D_f - d_w}{2} = \frac{2.40 - 1.20}{2} = \frac{1.20}{2} = 0.60 \ \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Volumetric throughput:**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_f^2 - d_w^2 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( 2.40^2 - 1.20^2 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( 5.76 - 1.44 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 4.32 = 3.393 \ \text{mm}^2$$

$$Q_{vol} = v_L \times A_c = \frac{600 \ \text{m/min}}{60} \times 3.393 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2 = 10 \ \text{m/s} \times 3.393 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2 = 3.393 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to cm³/s:

$$Q_{vol} = 3.393 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s} \times 10^6 \ \text{cm}^3/\text{m}^3 = 33.93 \ \text{cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**(iii) Mass throughput:**

$$Q_{mass} = \rho \times Q_{vol} = 1080 \ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \times 3.393 \times 10^{-5} \ \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{s}} = 0.03665 \ \text{kg/s}$$

Convert to kg/h:

$$Q_{mass} = 0.03665 \times 3600 = 131.9 \ \text{kg/h}$$

> **Final Answers:** $t_c = 0.60 \ \text{mm}$, $Q_{vol} = 33.93 \ \text{cm}^3/\text{s}$, $Q_{mass} \approx 132 \ \text{kg/h}$.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Explain the various types of defects encountered in polymer-insulated wires. Discuss their causes and remedies in detail.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Use the analytical matrix from Section 3.5 as the structural backbone of this answer.

1. **Eccentricity (off-centre insulation):** Caused by misaligned guide tip, worn die, or wire wandering in the capstan. *Remedy:* Realign torpedo, replace die, install dancer roller for tension control.

2. **Blow holes / voids:** Moisture in the polymer, or trapped air at the wire-melt interface. *Remedy:* Pre-dry the polymer (80 °C × 4 h for PA), use vented extruder, apply vacuum at die.

3. **Surface roughness / melt fracture:** Shear rate exceeds the critical value (typically $\dot{\gamma} > 500 \ \text{s}^{-1}$ for PVC). *Remedy:* Increase melt temperature, lower line speed, use processing aid.

4. **Neck-down / diameter reduction:** Draw Down Ratio too high; melt is over-stretched. *Remedy:* Increase die land, decrease haul-off speed, raise melt temperature.

5. **Poor adhesion:** Wire surface contamination, low melt temperature, insufficient pre-heat. *Remedy:* Clean wire (solvent wipe), pre-heat to > 80 °C, raise melt temperature.

6. **Sharkskin / stick-slip:** Wall slip at high shear stress; common in LLDPE. *Remedy:* Add fluoroelastomer process aid, lower throughput.

7. **Sink marks on insulation:** Non-uniform cooling or excessive post-extrusion crystallisation. *Remedy:* Gradual cooling trough (40 °C → 25 °C), reduce cooling rate.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*A wire coating line uses polyethylene of melt density 760 kg/m³ at 200 °C. The bare aluminium wire diameter is 0.80 mm and the final insulated diameter is 1.60 mm. The line speed is 800 m/min. If the DDR (Turk's method) is 1.20 and the guide tip diameter is 1.10 mm, calculate: (i) the die exit diameter, (ii) the mass throughput in kg/h, and (iii) the average melt velocity at the die exit.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $d_w = 0.80 \ \text{mm}$, $D_f = 1.60 \ \text{mm}$, $v_L = 800 \ \text{m/min}$, $\rho = 760 \ \text{kg/m}^3$, $DDR_{Turk} = 1.20$, $d_{tip} = 1.10 \ \text{mm}$.

**(i) Die exit diameter ($D_{die}$):**

$$DDR_{Turk} = \frac{D_{die}^2 - d_{tip}^2}{D_f^2 - d_w^2}$$

$$1.20 = \frac{D_{die}^2 - 1.10^2}{1.60^2 - 0.80^2} = \frac{D_{die}^2 - 1.21}{2.56 - 0.64} = \frac{D_{die}^2 - 1.21}{1.92}$$

$$D_{die}^2 - 1.21 = 1.20 \times 1.92 = 2.304$$

$$D_{die}^2 = 2.304 + 1.21 = 3.514$$

$$D_{die} = \sqrt{3.514} = 1.875 \ \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Mass throughput:**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_f^2 - d_w^2 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 1.92 = 1.508 \ \text{mm}^2 = 1.508 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$v_L = \frac{800}{60} = 13.33 \ \text{m/s}$$

$$Q_{vol} = 13.33 \times 1.508 \times 10^{-6} = 2.011 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q_{mass} = 760 \times 2.011 \times 10^{-5} = 0.01528 \ \text{kg/s} = 55.0 \ \text{kg/h}$$

**(iii) Average melt velocity at die exit:**

$$v_{melt} = v_L \times DDR_{Turk} = 13.33 \times 1.20 = 16.0 \ \text{m/s}$$

> **Final Answers:** $D_{die} = 1.875 \ \text{mm}$, $Q_{mass} = 55.0 \ \text{kg/h}$, $v_{melt} = 16.0 \ \text{m/s}$.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **1. Unit conversion trap (most common):** Students often forget to convert mm² to m² when computing volumetric flow. Always convert **before** multiplying. The examiner deducts **1 – 2 marks** for unit inconsistency even if the final answer is numerically correct.
>
> **2. DDR confusion:** Some students use the simple DDR $\frac{D_{die}}{D_f}$ instead of Turk's corrected form. If the question explicitly states "Turk's method" or provides $d_{tip}$, you **must** use the corrected formula. Penalty: **2 marks**.
>
> **3. Density-temperature distinction:** Wire coating exam problems sometimes provide density at room temperature. If not stated as "melt density," use room-temperature density (e.g., $\rho_{PVC} = 1400 \ \text{kg/m}^3$). Always read the **state of the material** in the problem.
>
> **4. Knock-out marking in PART B:** If your schematic is missing, expect a flat deduction of **2 – 3 marks** in part (a) of either Question A or B. Always draw a labelled crosshead die with all four key parts: body, inlet, guide tip, die land, die lip.
>
> **5. Eccentricity vs neck-down:** These are NOT the same defect. Eccentricity = non-uniform thickness around the circumference. Neck-down = uniform thinning along the wire length. Examiners test this distinction.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision checklist — read this twice before the exam.**

- ✅ Wire coating is a **continuous secondary extrusion process** using a **crosshead die**.
- ✅ Three industrial methods: **Pressure (T-head), Tubing (Sleeve/Collapsing), and Electrostatic** powder coating.
- ✅ The **guide tip (mandrel/torpedo)** splits the melt and sets the inner diameter of the coating.
- ✅ The **die land** creates back-pressure; typical length = **5 × to 10 × coating thickness**.
- ✅ Master throughput equation: $Q = \rho \ v_L \ \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_f^2 - d_w^2 \right)$.
- ✅ Coating thickness: $t_c = \frac{D_f - d_w}{2}$.
- ✅ **Turk's DDR**: $DDR_{Turk} = \frac{D_{die}^2 - d_{tip}^2}{D_f^2 - d_w^2}$ — preferred over simple DDR.
- ✅ **Pre-heating the wire** (80 – 120 °C) prevents blow holes and improves adhesion.
- ✅ **Cooling** is done in a water trough (15 – 60 °C), trough length 6 – 20 m.
- ✅ **Haul-off** uses dual-wheel capstan with caterpillar belts for slip-free pulling.
- ✅ **Quality control**: in-line laser OD gauge + spark tester (≥ 3 kV for thin wires).
- ✅ **Common defects**: eccentricity, blow holes, melt fracture, neck-down, poor adhesion, sharkskin, sink marks.
- ✅ **Common materials**: PVC (most), PE, XLPE, Nylon, PTFE, FEP, TPE/TPU.
- ✅ **Typical line speeds**: 50 – 2000 m/min; **typical extruder L/D**: 20:1 to 30:1.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme hot topics**: crosshead die schematic, throughput calculation, DDR (Turk's), defect-cause-remedy, comparison of pressure vs. tubing method.
- ✅ **Always convert units first**, label every numerical answer with units, and use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ notation (not pipes) for absolute values in any table.

---

*End of Module 2 – Wire Coating | PCPOT503 | KTU 2024 Scheme*

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Profile Extrusion** is a high-volume, continuous polymer processing operation in which a thermoplastic or elastomeric compound, in its molten/plasticized state, is forced under pressure through a shaped **profile die** to generate an endless product with a uniform, predetermined cross-sectional geometry. The semi-finished extrudate is subsequently subjected to **calibration (sizing)**, **controlled cooling**, **haul-off (traction)**, and **cutting/stacking** to yield finished products such as pipes, tubes, sheets, rods, window frames, and complex architectural/industrial profiles.

**Synonyms Used in KTU Examinations:** *Profile extrusion, contour extrusion, shape extrusion, continuous-section extrusion.*

**Standards & Reference Metrics:**
- **Output rate:** kg/h or m/min (line speed)
- **Tolerance class:** Typically **±0.1 mm to ±0.5 mm** depending on profile complexity
- **Haul-off force:** Newton (N)
- **Die pressure:** MPa (typically **5–35 MPa** for thermoplastics)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Conceptualization)

Imagine a giant **cake icing syringe** (like a piping bag used by bakers). You push soft butter-cream through a star-shaped nozzle, and out comes a continuous strip with star-shaped cross-section. As it emerges, it holds its shape because the cream cools. 

**Now scale this idea up:**
- The "syringe" is the **extruder barrel**,
- The "butter-cream" is **molten polymer** (PVC, PE, PP, etc.),
- The "star nozzle" is the **profile die**,
- The "cooling on the tray" is the **calibration + cooling tank**,
- The "table moving away" is the **haul-off caterpillar/caterpillar puller**,
- The "knife at the end" is the **cutting saw**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Examiners often begin profile-extrusion questions with *"Define profile extrusion and list any four products made by this process."* Memorize the formal definition word-for-word and the analogy above to score the easy 3 marks instantly.

## 1.3 Classification of Extrusion Profiles (KTU Board-Important)

| Profile Class | Typical Geometry | Common Examples | Die Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Solid profiles** | Rod, bar, flat strip, L/T/U channels | Rod stock, sealing strips | Low |
| **Hollow profiles** | Tubes, pipes, multi-channel sections | PVC pipes, medical tubing | Medium |
| **Open profiles** | Channels, frames, trays | Window/door frames | High |
| **Complex/technical profiles** | Multi-chamber, foamed, co-extruded | Refrigerator gaskets, decking | Very High |
| **Sheet & film profiles** | Wide flat sections (mono/die-layered) | PVC sheets, cling film | Specialized |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Sequential Process Stages

### Stage 1 — Plasticization (in the extruder)
- Solid polymer granules are conveyed, compressed, melted, and homogenized by a rotating screw.
- Screw rotation creates **drag flow** $Q_d$ and **pressure flow** $Q_p$.
- **Net output:** $Q_{net} = Q_d - Q_p$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *"Plasticization → Forming → Calibration → Cooling → Finishing"*. This sequence appears in nearly every KTU Module-2 question.

### Stage 2 — Profile Forming (in the die)
The melt is distributed through a **manifold** into the **land region** of the die.
- **Manifold** → balances flow velocity across the profile.
- **Die land** → final shape stabilization; length = $L_d$, cross-section $A_d$.
- **Compression ratio** of die = $\frac{A_{\text{manifold inlet}}}{A_{\text{land}}}$

### Stage 3 — Calibration (Sizing)
The still-warm extrudate passes through a **calibrator** (vacuum tank, sleeve, or plate) that fixes outer dimensions against **thermal shrinkage**.

### Stage 4 — Controlled Cooling
- Water spray, immersion bath, or air-cooling.
- Cooling rate is critical to avoid **internal stresses**, **voids**, and **warpage**.

### Stage 5 — Haul-off & Cutting
- **Caterpillar puller** (two opposing rubber belts) maintains constant linear velocity $v$.
- **Cutting saw / guillotine** produces finished lengths.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Governing Equation | Standard Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volumetric Output Rate** | $Q = v \cdot A$ | m³/s | Production capacity |
| 2 | **Mass Output Rate** | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = \rho \cdot v \cdot A$ | kg/s | Throughput planning |
| 3 | **Shear Rate (rectangular die)** | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{6Q}{w \cdot h^{2}}$ | s⁻¹ | Viscosity selection |
| 4 | **Shear Rate (circular die)** | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^{3}}$ | s⁻¹ | Pipe/tube dies |
| 5 | **Pressure Drop (Power-law fluid)** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 L \, \dot{\gamma}^{\,n+1}}{n+3}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{K}\right)^{\!1/n}$ | Pa | Die sizing |
| 6 | **Apparent Die Swell** | $B = \dfrac{D_{\text{extrudate}} - D_{\text{die}}}{D_{\text{die}}}$ | dimensionless | Tolerance compensation |
| 7 | **Puller Velocity** | $v_{\text{puller}} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{\text{cooled}}}$ | m/s | Line-speed setting |
| 8 | **Haul-off Force (friction model)** | $F = \mu \cdot N \cdot 2$ | N | Puller design |
| 9 | **Cooling Time (slab model)** | $t_{c} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}}\dfrac{T_{m}-T_{w}}{T_{e}-T_{w}}\right)$ | s | Cooling bath length |
| 10 | **Bending Stress in Profile** | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \, c}{I}$ | Pa | Post-cooling handling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule:** In the table above, the absolute value bars have been replaced by parentheses to preserve markdown table rendering. In your own notebook, write them as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$.

### 2.3 Variables & Symbols Glossary

- $Q$ — volumetric flow rate
- $v$ — linear velocity of extrudate
- $A$ — cross-sectional area
- $\rho$ — polymer melt density (≈ **750–1400 kg/m³** for thermoplastics)
- $w,\,h$ — width & height of rectangular channel
- $D$ — diameter (die or extrudate)
- $L$ — die land length
- $K$ — power-law consistency index
- $n$ — power-law flow behavior index (n ≈ **0.3–0.6** for polymer melts)
- $\mu$ — coefficient of friction at puller
- $N$ — normal clamping force
- $\alpha$ — thermal diffusivity (≈ **1 × 10⁻⁷ m²/s** for polymers)
- $T_m,\,T_w,\,T_e$ — melt, water and exit temperatures

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Drop in a Rectangular Die (Power-Law Fluid)

We start with the **general momentum equation** for steady, laminar, fully-developed flow in a thin rectangular channel (width $w \gg$ height $h$).

$$
0 = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y}
$$

where $y$ is the coordinate across the gap.

**Step 1.** Substitute the power-law viscosity model:

$$
\tau_{yx} = K \left(\dot{\gamma}\right)^{n} = K \left(-\dfrac{dv_x}{dy}\right)^{n}
$$

**Step 2.** Recognize that the pressure gradient is constant:

$$
\dfrac{d\tau_{yx}}{dy} = \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} = \text{constant} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}
$$

**Step 3.** Integrate once with respect to $y$:

$$
\tau_{yx}(y) = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\,y + C_1
$$

**Step 4.** Apply symmetry boundary condition at the centreline $y = 0$: shear stress is zero.

$$
C_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_{yx}(y) = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\,y
$$

**Step 5.** Equate to the power-law expression:

$$
-\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}\,y = -K\left(\dfrac{dv_x}{dy}\right)^{n}
$$

**Step 6.** Solve for the velocity gradient:

$$
\dfrac{dv_x}{dy} = \left(\dfrac{\Delta P \, y}{K \, L}\right)^{\!1/n}
$$

**Step 7.** Integrate from the wall ($y = h/2$, where $v_x = 0$) to a general $y$:

$$
v_x(y) = \int_{y}^{h/2}\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{K L}\right)^{\!1/n} y^{\,1/n}\, dy'
$$

**Step 8.** Perform the integration:

$$
v_x(y) = \left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{K L}\right)^{\!1/n}\!\dfrac{n}{n+1}\!\left[\left(\dfrac{h}{2}\right)^{\!\frac{n+1}{n}} - y^{\,\frac{n+1}{n}}\right]
$$

**Step 9.** Total volumetric flow (integrate across full gap):

$$
Q = w \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} v_x(y)\,dy
$$

**Step 10.** Evaluate the integral:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dfrac{2 w n}{n+1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{K L}\right)^{\!1/n}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2}\right)^{\!\frac{n+1}{n}}\!\!\int_{0}^{1}\!(1 - \zeta^{\,\frac{n+1}{n}})\,d\zeta \\
&= \dfrac{2 w n}{n+1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{K L}\right)^{\!1/n}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2}\right)^{\!\frac{n+1}{n}}\!\dfrac{n}{2n+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 11.** Solve for the **pressure drop** explicitly:

$$
\Delta P = \dfrac{2 L \, K}{(n+3)\,h^{\,n+1}}\!\left[\dfrac{(2n+1)(n+1) \, Q}{w \, n}\right]^{\!n}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Result:** The boxed form used by KTU examiners is $\boxed{\;\Delta P = \dfrac{2 L}{K^{1/n}}\!\left[\dfrac{(2n+1)Q}{n w h^{2}}\right]^{\!n}\dfrac{1}{(n+2n)}\;}$ — different textbooks re-arrange differently; the physics is identical.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Output Rate from Die Geometry

Given a finished profile of cross-section $A_f$ leaving the puller at velocity $v_p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= A_f \cdot v_p \\
\dot{m} &= \rho \cdot A_f \cdot v_p
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical worked example (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question):**

A rigid PVC window profile has cross-section $40 \text{ mm} \times 60 \text{ mm}$ and is pulled at $1.2 \text{ m/min}$. Density of PVC melt $\rho = 1.38 \times 10^{3} \text{ kg/m}^{3}$.

**Solution steps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_f &= 40 \times 60 = 2400 \text{ mm}^{2} = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} \\
v_p &= 1.2 \text{ m/min} = 0.02 \text{ m/s} \\
Q &= 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.02 = 4.8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s} \\
\dot{m} &= 1380 \times 4.8 \times 10^{-5} = 6.624 \times 10^{-2} \text{ kg/s} \\
\dot{m}_{\text{hourly}} &= 6.624 \times 10^{-2} \times 3600 \approx \mathbf{238.5 \text{ kg/h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This exact calculation has appeared in KTU ESE papers (Dec 2022 model). Practice with **two more random cross-sections** before the exam.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Process Parameters

| Profile Type | Typical Screw L/D | Die Pressure (MPa) | Haul-off Force (N/cm²) | Cooling Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rigid PVC pipe** | 25 : 1 | 15 – 25 | 0.5 – 1.5 | Vacuum water tank |
| **PE/PP pipe** | 30 : 1 | 10 – 20 | 0.3 – 1.0 | Spray + immersion |
| **Window profile (PVC)** | 22 : 1 | 12 – 22 | 0.8 – 2.0 | Calibration + spray |
| **Sheet (PS/PP/PET)** | 30 – 36 : 1 | 5 – 15 | 0.2 – 0.6 | Chill rolls + air |
| **Tubing (medical)** | 24 : 1 | 8 – 15 | 0.1 – 0.4 | Water bath |
| **Foamed profile** | 26 : 1 | 8 – 18 | 0.4 – 1.2 | Air + water mist |

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. Define profile extrusion. List four products manufactured by this process.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Profile extrusion** is a continuous polymer-processing operation in which a thermoplastic melt is forced through a shaped die to produce an extrudate of uniform cross-section, which is then calibrated, cooled, hauled-off and cut to length.

**Four products:** (i) PVC pipes, (ii) window/door frames, (iii) sealing gaskets, (iv) thermoplastic sheets & rods. *(Any four accepted.)*

---

### Q2. What is **die swell** (extrudate swell)? Write the formula.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Die swell is the increase in cross-sectional dimensions of an extrudate over the die exit dimensions, caused by elastic recovery of the viscoelastic polymer melt after it leaves the die.
>
> Formula: $\;B = \dfrac{D_{\text{extrudate}} - D_{\text{die}}}{D_{\text{die}}}$

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, adapted]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With a neat schematic, describe the **complete profile extrusion line**. Label every component from hopper to finished cut profile. State two functions of each of the following: (i) **breaker plate**, (ii) **die land**, (iii) **haul-off caterpillar**.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A rigid PVC pipe of outer diameter **63 mm** and wall thickness **5 mm** is extruded at a haul-off speed of **1.5 m/min**. The PVC melt density is **1.40 × 10³ kg/m³**. Calculate: (i) the volumetric output rate $Q$, (ii) the mass throughput in kg/h.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) — 7 marks**

**Schematic (block sequence — 2 marks):**
Hopper → Barrel & Screw → Screen pack + Breaker plate → Adapter → Profile die → Calibration tank → Cooling tank → Haul-off → Cutting saw → Stacking table.

**Functions (1.5 marks each):**

| Sub-part | Two Functions |
|---|---|
| **(i) Breaker plate** | 1. Converts **rotational (spiral) flow** into **axial (straight) flow**. 2. Holds the screen pack that filters contaminants and builds back-pressure. |
| **(ii) Die land** | 1. **Re-establishes fully-developed flow** before exit, removing memory of manifold irregularities. 2. **Determines die swell, pressure drop and final surface finish.** |
| **(iii) Haul-off** | 1. Provides **constant linear velocity**, setting the wall-thickness and output rate. 2. Generates **tensile stress** that aligns polymer chains, reducing post-extrusion shrinkage. |

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Given data:**
- $D_o = 63 \text{ mm} = 0.063 \text{ m}$
- $t = 5 \text{ mm} = 0.005 \text{ m}$
- Inner diameter: $D_i = D_o - 2t = 53 \text{ mm} = 0.053 \text{ m}$
- $v_p = 1.5 \text{ m/min} = 0.025 \text{ m/s}$
- $\rho = 1.40 \times 10^{3} \text{ kg/m}^{3}$

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of pipe (annulus):**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_o^{\,2} - D_i^{\,2}\right) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(0.063^{2} - 0.053^{2}\right)
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.003969 - 0.002809) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.001160) = 9.11 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate $Q$:**

$$
Q = A \cdot v_p = 9.11 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.025 = 2.278 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kg/h:**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 1.40 \times 10^{3} \times 2.278 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0319 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{hourly}} = 0.0319 \times 3600 = \mathbf{114.85 \text{ kg/h}}
$$

**Final boxed answers:**
$$
\boxed{Q = 2.28 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}} \qquad \boxed{\dot{m} = 114.85 \text{ kg/h}}
$$

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, model]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **classification of profile dies** with neat sketches for: (i) **plate die**, (ii) **spider die**, (iii) **coathanger die**. State one application of each.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **die-swell phenomenon** in polymer extrusion. Derive the formula for **apparent die swell** and discuss how it is controlled in industrial profile lines.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) — 7 marks**

| Die Type | Construction (2 marks each) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Plate die** | A simple flat plate with a machined opening; melt flows in one direction. | Sheet, flat strip, simple profiles |
| **Spider die** | A central mandrel supported by **2–4 spider legs**; forms a hollow section (e.g., pipe). | Pipes, tubes |
| **Coathanger die** | A **coathanger-shaped manifold** spreads melt across the die width; produces uniform flow over large widths. | Wide sheets, films, flat profiles |

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Phenomenon (2 marks):** When viscoelastic polymer melt exits a die, the **elastic strain energy stored during flow is recovered**. This causes the cross-section to enlarge — called **extrudate (die) swell**.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Let $D_d$ = die diameter, $D_e$ = extrudate diameter after free recovery.

**Definition:**

$$
B = \dfrac{D_e - D_d}{D_d}
$$

**Re-arrangement:**

$$
D_e = D_d (1 + B)
$$

**Common numerical bound:** For **HDPE** at typical extrusion shear rates, $B \approx 0.30$ – $0.60$.

**Industrial control methods (2 marks):**
1. **Increase die land length** → reduces swell (stress relaxation in land).
2. **Lower melt temperature** → reduces recoverable elastic strain.
3. **Lower shear rate** (larger die cross-section for same output).
4. **Use a sizing/calibration sleeve** immediately at the die exit (vacuum sizing).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently **omit units** in the die-swell derivation, write $D_e - D_d$ without dividing by $D_d$, or state $B$ in % without conversion. Always quote **$B$ as a dimensionless number** and write the answer in the form **$\;B = 0.45\;$ (i.e., 45 %)** — never as a single percentage. Loss of 1 mark in almost every attempt.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] Profile extrusion = **continuous shaping of a uniform cross-section** by forcing melt through a profile die.
- [x] Five-stage pipeline: **Plasticization → Die-forming → Calibration → Cooling → Haul-off/Cut**.
- [x] **Die swell** $B = \dfrac{D_e - D_d}{D_d}$ — caused by **elastic recovery** of viscoelastic melt.
- [x] Three major die families: **plate, spider, coathanger**.
- [x] Output equations: $Q = v \cdot A$ and $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$.
- [x] Pressure drop for power-law fluid in rectangular die: $\Delta P \propto L \cdot Q^{\,n} / h^{\,n+1}$.
- [x] **Shear rate in a circular die:** $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^{3}}$ — must appear in any viscosity discussion.
- [x] **Haul-off caterpillar** sets **line speed, wall thickness and molecular orientation**.
- [x] **Calibration** uses **vacuum or sleeve** to fix dimensions during cooling shrinkage.
- [x] **Cooling method** selection — vacuum tank for **rigid PVC pipe**; chill rolls for **sheet**.
- [x] Tolerances for engineering profiles: **±0.1 mm to ±0.5 mm**.
- [x] Always convert **m/min → m/s** before using SI output formulas (common 1-mark trap).
- [x] Standard screw L/D for profiles = **22 : 1 to 30 : 1**.
- [x] Die pressure range for thermoplastics = **5–35 MPa**.
- [x] Materials commonly extruded as profiles: **rPVC, uPVC, HDPE, LDPE, PP, ABS, PA, PC, TPE.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blown Films
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Blown Films

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blown Film Extrusion** is a continuous polymer processing technique in which a molten thermoplastic melt is extruded through an annular (circular) die to form a thin-walled tube, which is then inflated by internal air pressure, cooled, collapsed, and wound into flat film. The process simultaneously induces **biaxial orientation** (both machine direction and transverse direction) in the finished film.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Blown film is the most commercially dominant film-making process in the world, accounting for approximately **70%** of all polyethylene film production globally. It is the **single largest application** of extrusion technology by tonnage.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Soap Bubble" Model

Imagine blowing a **soap bubble** from a ring dipped in soap solution:
- The soap film is the **melt** (liquid polymer).
- The air you blow inside is the **internal bubble air**.
- The bubble expands, thins, and hardens as it rises — exactly analogous to how the molten polymer tube thins as it rises up the cooling tower.

**Plain English Explanation:**
A polymer is melted in an extruder (like a giant hot glue gun). The melt is pushed up through a circular die (like the tip of a balloon), forming a tube. Air is injected into the center of this tube, inflating it like a balloon into a giant bubble. Cold air blown from outside rapidly cools and solidifies the bubble. The bubble is then squeezed flat between rollers and wound onto a roll. The result is a continuous **thin plastic film** used in grocery bags, food packaging, cling wrap, and agricultural mulch films.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics (Bold Constants)

| Industrial Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Film thickness range** | **10 µm – 250 µm** | Range of commercial films |
| **Production speed** | **30 – 200 m/min** | Line throughput |
| **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)** | **1.5 – 4.0** | Degree of transverse stretching |
| **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR)** | **2 – 30** | Degree of machine-direction stretching |
| **Frost Line Height (FLH)** | **200 – 1500 mm** | Distance where melt solidifies |
| **Melt temperature (LDPE)** | **160 °C – 220 °C** | Processing window |

## 1.4 Historical Context & Relevance

The blown film process was first commercialized in the **late 1930s** and rapidly expanded during **World War II** for packaging military rations. Today, blown films dominate the **flexible packaging industry** because of:
- Superior mechanical balance (balanced biaxial orientation).
- Ability to produce wide films (up to **12 meters** lay-flat width on modern lines).
- Excellent optical properties (gloss, clarity).
- Lower capital cost vs. flat-die (cast film) extrusion.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always mention "biaxial orientation" when defining blown film — it is the **key distinguishing feature** compared to cast film (which is mostly uniaxially oriented).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Process Flow Logic

The blown film line consists of **seven sequential subsystems**:

1. **Feed & Melting Zone**
   - Polymer pellets enter the hopper.
   - A rotating screw (typically **L/D = 24:1 to 30:1**) conveys, compresses, shears, and melts the polymer.
   - A screen pack and breaker plate filter contaminants and remove melt pressure fluctuations.

2. **Annular Die (Spiral Mandrel Die)**
   - The melt is forced into a circular distribution system (spiral channels) to ensure uniform flow around the circumference.
   - The melt exits through a thin annular lip (die gap typically **0.5 – 2.0 mm**).
   - The annular lip defines the initial tube geometry.

3. **Bubble Inflation (Blow-Up)**
   - Air is injected through the die mandrel (via an air ring at the bottom or center of the die).
   - Internal pressure inflates the molten tube into a bubble.
   - The bubble diameter is controlled by adjusting internal air pressure.

4. **Air Ring Cooling (External Quench)**
   - High-velocity ambient or chilled air is directed from an air ring around the outside of the bubble.
   - Cooling air travels upward by the Coanda effect, creating a frost line where the polymer transitions from rubbery to glassy state.
   - Cooling rates of **50 °C/s – 100 °C/s** are typical for LDPE.

5. **Bubble Collapsing**
   - The bubble is guided through a collapsing frame (V-shaped wooden or aluminum guide).
   - At this point, the tube becomes flat (two-ply film).
   - Nip rolls pull the film down at the lay-flat width.

6. **Treatment & Winding**
   - The film may pass through corona or flame treatment for printability.
   - Final winding occurs on surface or center-driven winders.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Biaxial Orientation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does blown film produce superior mechanical properties?**
> During inflation, the melt simultaneously stretches in two perpendicular directions:
> - **MD (Machine Direction):** Drawing down by nip rolls — orients molecular chains longitudinally.
> - **TD (Transverse Direction):** Inflation by air pressure — orients molecular chains circumferentially.
>
> The resulting film has **balanced tensile strength, tear resistance, and impact strength** in both directions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BUR** | $BUR = \dfrac{D_b}{D_d}$ | dimensionless | Blow-Up Ratio (bubble / die diameter) |
| **DDR** | $DDR = \dfrac{V_{nip}}{V_{die}}$ | dimensionless | Draw-Down Ratio |
| **Film Thickness ($t_f$)** | $t_f = \dfrac{t_d}{BUR \times DDR}$ | µm | Final thickness from initial die gap |
| **Lay-flat Width ($W$)** | $W = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_b}{2}$ | mm | Width of collapsed flat film |
| **MDR (MD Stretch)** | $MDR = DDR$ | dimensionless | Equal to DDR by definition |
| **TDR (TD Stretch)** | $TDR = BUR$ | dimensionless | Equal to BUR by definition |
| **Total Stretch Ratio** | $\lambda_{total} = BUR \times DDR$ | dimensionless | Total biaxial deformation |
| **Throughput ($Q$)** | $Q = \rho \cdot v_{die} \cdot \pi \cdot D_d \cdot t_d$ | kg/h | Mass flow rate |
| **Specific Output** | $q = \dfrac{Q}{N \cdot D_s^2 \cdot L}$ | kg/h·rpm | Productivity index |
| **Nip Velocity** | $v_{nip} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_{nip} \cdot N_{nip}}{60}$ | m/min | Linear speed of nip rolls |
| **Bubble Velocity** | $v_b = \dfrac{Q}{2 \cdot \pi \cdot R \cdot t_f \cdot \rho}$ | m/s | Rising speed of bubble |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule (Anti-Syntax):** All pipe symbols in formulas use `\vert` or `\mid` — never raw `|` — to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Packaging Industry:** Grocery bags, bread bags, milk pouches, shrink films.
- **Agricultural Films:** Greenhouse covers, mulch films (LDPE / LLDPE blends).
- **Medical Films:** Sterile packaging (PET, PP blown films).
- **Industrial Films:** Liners, stretch wrap (LLDPE / mLLDPE).
- **Food Industry:** Co-extruded multi-layer films for vacuum packaging.

> [!TIP]
> **Why not just use cast film?** Cast film has higher clarity but lower tear strength. Blown film has **better puncture resistance and mechanical balance**, making it ideal for heavy-duty packaging.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Final Film Thickness Formula

### Setup
Let:
- $t_d$ = die gap (initial wall thickness of the molten tube), in mm.
- $D_d$ = die diameter, in mm.
- $D_b$ = bubble diameter (final frozen diameter), in mm.
- $v_{die}$ = melt velocity leaving the die, in mm/s.
- $v_{nip}$ = velocity of nip rolls (film take-up speed), in mm/s.

By **conservation of mass** (incompressible melt assumption):

$$
Q_{die} = Q_{film}
$$

### Step 1: Volumetric flow rate at the die exit

The die exit is an annulus (ring-shaped cross-section) of mean circumference $\pi \cdot D_d$ and gap $t_d$.

$$
Q_{die} = \pi \cdot D_d \cdot t_d \cdot v_{die}
$$

### Step 2: Volumetric flow rate at the frozen bubble

At the frost line, the bubble has diameter $D_b$, wall thickness $t_f$, and rises at velocity $v_b$.

$$
Q_{film} = \pi \cdot D_b \cdot t_f \cdot v_b
$$

### Step 3: Equating both expressions

$$
\pi \cdot D_d \cdot t_d \cdot v_{die} = \pi \cdot D_b \cdot t_f \cdot v_b
$$

Rearranging:

$$
t_f = \dfrac{D_d}{D_b} \cdot \dfrac{v_{die}}{v_b} \cdot t_d
$$

### Step 4: Substituting BUR and DDR definitions

By definition:

$$
BUR = \dfrac{D_b}{D_d} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{D_d}{D_b} = \dfrac{1}{BUR}
$$

$$
DDR = \dfrac{v_{nip}}{v_{die}} \approx \dfrac{v_b}{v_{die}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{v_{die}}{v_b} = \dfrac{1}{DDR}
$$

### Step 5: Final compact formula

Substituting both into Step 3:

$$
t_f = \dfrac{t_d}{BUR \cdot DDR}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The final thickness is reduced by the product of BUR and DDR relative to the die gap. To produce a **25 µm** film with **BUR = 2.5** and **DDR = 10**, you would need a die gap of:
>
> $t_d = 25 \times 2.5 \times 10 = 625 \;\mu m$ (or 0.625 mm) — a typical LDPE die gap.

## 3.2 Derivation of Lay-Flat Width

The bubble is a cylinder of circumference $\pi \cdot D_b$. When collapsed flat, it forms a double-layer film of width $W$:

$$
2W = \pi \cdot D_b \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_b}{2}
$$

Substituting $D_b = BUR \cdot D_d$:

$$
W = \dfrac{\pi \cdot BUR \cdot D_d}{2}
$$

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A blown film line uses a die of diameter $D_d = 200\;mm$ with a die gap $t_d = 1.2\;mm$. The bubble diameter is $D_b = 600\;mm$, and the nip velocity is 25 m/min. Calculate:
> 1. Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)
> 2. Draw-Down Ratio (DDR) — given die exit velocity $v_{die} = 0.5\;m/min$
> 3. Final film thickness
> 4. Lay-flat width

### Solution

**(1) Blow-Up Ratio:**

$$
BUR = \dfrac{D_b}{D_d} = \dfrac{600}{200} = 3.0
$$

**(2) Draw-Down Ratio:**

$$
DDR = \dfrac{v_{nip}}{v_{die}} = \dfrac{25}{0.5} = 50
$$

**(3) Final Film Thickness:**

$$
t_f = \dfrac{t_d}{BUR \cdot DDR} = \dfrac{1.2\;mm}{3.0 \times 50} = \dfrac{1.2}{150} = 0.008\;mm = 8\;\mu m
$$

**(4) Lay-Flat Width:**

$$
W = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_b}{2} = \dfrac{\pi \times 600}{2} = 300\pi \approx 942.5\;mm
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Lay-flat of 942.5 mm corresponds to a tube circumference of 1885 mm — equivalent to a bubble diameter of $\dfrac{1885}{\pi} = 600\;mm$. ✔ Consistent.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting Process Parameters

| Desired Property | Increase BUR? | Increase DDR? | Effect on Orientation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Higher transverse-direction tensile strength | ✓ Yes | ✗ No | More TD orientation |
| Higher machine-direction tensile strength | ✗ No | ✓ Yes | More MD orientation |
| Better tear resistance (balanced) | ✓ (to ~2.5) | ✓ (to ~10) | Balanced biaxial |
| Better optical clarity | ✗ Lower | ✗ Lower | Less orientation = more random chains |
| Higher stiffness | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes | More overall orientation |
| Higher impact strength | ✓ Yes | ✓ Yes | Higher crystallinity |
| Thinner film | ✗ | ✓ Yes | More downgauging |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** A balanced film typically has **BUR ≈ 2.5** and **DDR ≈ 10**, giving roughly equal MD and TD properties.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Blow-Up Ratio (BUR) and Draw-Down Ratio (DDR). Why are they critical in blown film extrusion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)** is defined as the ratio of the diameter of the inflated bubble to the diameter of the die:

$$
BUR = \dfrac{D_b}{D_d}
$$

**Draw-Down Ratio (DDR)** is the ratio of the take-up (nip) velocity to the melt velocity at the die exit:

$$
DDR = \dfrac{v_{nip}}{v_{die}}
$$

**Significance:** BUR governs **transverse direction (TD) orientation**, while DDR governs **machine direction (MD) orientation**. Together, they determine the final film thickness:

$$
t_f = \dfrac{t_d}{BUR \cdot DDR}
$$

and the balance of mechanical properties in the MD and TD directions.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the "frost line" in blown film extrusion? What happens at the frost line?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **frost line** (also called the **freeze line** or **crystallization line**) is a visible optical demarcation on the rising bubble, located at a certain distance above the die, where the molten polymer transitions from a **rubbery (amorphous melt) state** to a **glassy/crystalline solid state**.

At the frost line:
1. The polymer has cooled below its **crystallization temperature** ($T_c$) for semi-crystalline polymers like LDPE/HDPE.
2. The bubble becomes **dimensionally stable**, and no further thinning by inflation occurs.
3. The film's **optical properties** change from glossy/transparent (above frost line) to hazy/translucent (below frost line) due to surface crystallization.

The height of the frost line (**FLH**) is typically **200 mm – 1500 mm** above the die and is a critical process control parameter.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks with **internal choice**. You must answer **ONE** question out of two. Both sub-parts typically carry **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **complete blown film extrusion process**, including the function of each component from hopper to winder. Discuss the role of the **air ring** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A blown film line has the following specifications:
- Die diameter: $D_d = 150\;mm$
- Die gap: $t_d = 1.0\;mm$
- Bubble diameter: $D_b = 450\;mm$
- Melt density: $\rho = 0.92\;g/cm^3$
- Melt velocity at die: $v_{die} = 0.4\;m/min$
- Nip roll velocity: $v_{nip} = 20\;m/min$

Calculate: **(i)** BUR, **(ii)** DDR, **(iii)** Final film thickness in µm, **(iv)** Mass throughput in kg/h, **(v)** Lay-flat width. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) — Process Explanation [7 marks]

**The blown film extrusion process consists of the following stages:**

**1. Feeding & Melting (1 mark):**
Polymer pellets enter the hopper, which feeds a single-screw extruder (typically L/D = 24–30:1). The screw's three zones (feed, compression, metering) convey, compress, shear, and melt the polymer. A **screen pack** (mesh filters) and **breaker plate** remove contaminants and smooth out pulsations.

**2. Die System (1 mark):**
The melt enters a **spiral mandrel die**, where spiral channels distribute the melt uniformly around the annular circumference. The melt exits as a thin-walled tube through an annular gap (0.5–2.0 mm). A **side-fed** or **bottom-fed** die may also be used depending on the polymer.

**3. Bubble Inflation (1 mark):**
Internal air is injected via the die mandrel, inflating the molten tube into a bubble. Internal air pressure (typically **10–50 mbar** for LDPE) controls the bubble diameter. The **air ring** plays a crucial role here.

**4. Air Ring — Detailed Role (2 marks):**
The air ring is a critical cooling device positioned just above the die. It directs a high-velocity stream of ambient or chilled air tangentially onto the bubble's outer surface. The cooling air follows the **Coanda effect**, traveling upward along the bubble's surface in a laminar jet. This rapid quench:
- Cools the bubble from ~200 °C to below the **crystallization temperature** (~100 °C for LDPE).
- Establishes the **frost line height (FLH)**.
- Stabilizes the bubble against aerodynamic instabilities.
- Modern air rings (e.g., **dual-lip, IBC-compatible**) offer superior uniformity.

**5. Collapsing, Take-Up, and Winding (2 marks):**
The solidified bubble passes through a **collapsing frame** (V-shaped guide) that flattens it into a 2-ply film. **Nip rolls** pull the film down at controlled speed. The film then passes through optional **corona or flame treatment** to enhance surface energy (for printing), and finally winds onto a roll.

**Schematic Diagram:** (Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $D_d = 150\;mm$
- $t_d = 1.0\;mm$
- $D_b = 450\;mm$
- $\rho = 0.92\;g/cm^3 = 920\;kg/m^3$
- $v_{die} = 0.4\;m/min$
- $v_{nip} = 20\;m/min$

**(i) Blow-Up Ratio (BUR):**

$$
BUR = \dfrac{D_b}{D_d} = \dfrac{450}{150} = 3.0
$$

**(ii) Draw-Down Ratio (DDR):**

$$
DDR = \dfrac{v_{nip}}{v_{die}} = \dfrac{20}{0.4} = 50
$$

**(iii) Final Film Thickness:**

$$
t_f = \dfrac{t_d}{BUR \cdot DDR} = \dfrac{1.0\;mm}{3.0 \times 50} = \dfrac{1.0}{150}\;mm = 0.00667\;mm = 6.67\;\mu m
$$

**(iv) Mass Throughput:**

Volumetric flow rate at die:

$$
Q_v = \pi \cdot D_d \cdot t_d \cdot v_{die}
$$

Convert units: $D_d = 0.150\;m$, $t_d = 0.001\;m$, $v_{die} = 0.4/60 = 0.00667\;m/s$.

$$
Q_v = \pi \times 0.150 \times 0.001 \times 0.00667 = 3.142 \times 10^{-6}\;m^3/s
$$

Mass throughput:

$$
Q = Q_v \times \rho = 3.142 \times 10^{-6} \times 920 = 2.89 \times 10^{-3}\;kg/s
$$

Convert to kg/h:

$$
Q = 2.89 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 \approx 10.4\;kg/h
$$

**(v) Lay-Flat Width:**

$$
W = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_b}{2} = \dfrac{\pi \times 450}{2} \approx 706.86\;mm
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **biaxial orientation** in blown film. How does it influence the **mechanical and optical properties** of the film? Discuss the role of BUR and DDR in controlling this orientation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and briefly explain **any six common defects** observed in blown film extrusion. For each defect, mention its likely cause and one corrective action. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) — Biaxial Orientation [7 marks]

**Definition (2 marks):**
**Biaxial orientation** refers to the process by which polymer molecules are aligned in two perpendicular directions within the film plane:
- **Machine Direction (MD):** Along the direction of film travel.
- **Transverse Direction (TD):** Perpendicular to the film travel (around the bubble circumference).

In blown film extrusion, biaxial orientation occurs **simultaneously** due to:
- **MD stretching:** Caused by nip rolls pulling the film down at a velocity $v_{nip} > v_{die}$ (governed by DDR).
- **TD stretching:** Caused by internal air pressure inflating the bubble (governed by BUR).

**Influence on Mechanical Properties (3 marks):**
- **Tensile strength** increases in both MD and TD due to aligned molecular chains.
- **Tear resistance** improves (especially in the TD direction) with higher BUR.
- **Impact strength and puncture resistance** increase with higher overall orientation (higher BUR × DDR product).
- **Stiffness (modulus)** increases with orientation.

However, **excessive** orientation in one direction (e.g., very high DDR, low BUR) leads to **anisotropy** — the film becomes strong in MD but weak in TD, prone to "**MD tear propagation**".

**Role of BUR and DDR (2 marks):**
- **BUR controls TD properties.** Higher BUR = more TD stretching = higher TD tensile strength, better balance.
- **DDR controls MD properties.** Higher DDR = more MD stretching = thinner film, higher MD tensile strength, but reduced TD balance.
- **Optimal balance:** BUR ≈ 2.0–3.0, DDR ≈ 8–15 for balanced LDPE/LLDPE films.
- The product $\lambda = BUR \cdot DDR$ represents **total biaxial deformation**.

**Optical Properties:**
- Higher orientation reduces haze (chains align, fewer light-scattering crystallites).
- Balanced orientation produces **glossy, clear films** ideal for packaging.

##### Part (b) — Common Defects [7 marks]

| # | Defect | Likely Cause | Corrective Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Bubble Instability** (Helical or snake-like motion) | Improper air ring balance, uneven cooling, low melt strength | Adjust air ring for symmetric airflow; increase melt temperature; use stabilizing cage |
| **2** | **Gauge Banding** (Thickness variation around circumference) | Poor die lip adjustment, uneven die lip gap, uneven spiral flow distribution | Adjust die bolt for uniform gap; use auto-profile die |
| **3** | **Frost Line Variation** (Unstable FLH) | Fluctuating air pressure, melt temperature variation, ambient drafts | Stabilize melt temperature; use enclosed cooling cabinet |
| **4** | **Sharkskin / Melt Fracture** (Rough surface) | Melt shear rate exceeds critical value (low melt viscosity) | Increase melt temperature; use processing aids (e.g., fluoropolymer additives); reduce output |
| **5** | **Gel Formation** (Small hard specks in film) | Crosslinked polymer, contamination, degraded resin | Replace screen pack; purge extruder; check hopper for cross-contamination |
| **6** | **Blocking** (Layers stick together on roll) | Insufficient slip additive, high surface tack, high winding tension | Add slip/anti-block additives (e.g., erucamide); reduce winding tension; use corona treatment |
| **7** | **Drawdown Resonance** (Periodic thickness oscillation) | Process instability; often caused by melt flow fluctuations | Install melt pulsation dampener; stabilize screw speed; check screw wear |

(Any **six** of the above, properly explained, fetch full 7 marks.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in numericals:** Many students forget to convert mm to m, m/min to m/s, or g/cm³ to kg/m³. Always write **explicit unit conversions** before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the π factor in throughput:** $Q = \pi \cdot D_d \cdot t_d \cdot v_{die}$, NOT $D_d \cdot t_d \cdot v_{die}$.
> 3. **Conflating BUR with blow-up pressure:** BUR is a **geometric ratio** (dimensionless), while blow-up **pressure** is a measurable quantity (in mbar or Pa).
> 4. **Skipping the frost line concept:** Examiners often allocate 1–2 marks specifically for frost line. Always mention it in process descriptions.
> 5. **Confusing spiral mandrel die with side-fed die:** Be precise — mention "spiral mandrel" by name for LDPE/LLDPE; mention "bottom-fed" or "side-fed" for HDPE/PP.
> 6. **Not drawing diagrams:** Even rough block diagrams fetch 1–2 extra marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Blown Films**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ **Blown Film:** Continuous process; melt extruded through annular die → inflated tube → cooled → collapsed → wound.
- ☐ **Biaxial Orientation:** Simultaneous MD and TD stretching.
- ☐ **BUR** = $D_b / D_d$ — controls TD orientation.
- ☐ **DDR** = $v_{nip} / v_{die}$ — controls MD orientation.
- ☐ **Frost Line:** Boundary between molten and solidified polymer on bubble.

### Essential Formulas
- ☐ $t_f = t_d / (BUR \cdot DDR)$ — final thickness.
- ☐ $W = \pi \cdot D_b / 2$ — lay-flat width.
- ☐ $Q = \rho \cdot \pi \cdot D_d \cdot t_d \cdot v_{die}$ — throughput.
- ☐ Total deformation = BUR × DDR.

### Process Components
- ☐ Extruder (L/D = 24–30:1).
- ☐ Screen pack + breaker plate.
- ☐ Spiral mandrel annular die.
- ☐ Air ring (Coanda effect cooling).
- ☐ Collapsing frame + nip rolls.
- ☐ Corona/flame treatment (optional).
- ☐ Winder.

### Key Numerical Values to Remember
- ☐ BUR typical = **1.5 to 4.0**.
- ☐ DDR typical = **2 to 30**.
- ☐ Frost line height = **200–1500 mm**.
- ☐ Melt temp (LDPE) = **160–220 °C**.
- ☐ Die gap = **0.5–2.0 mm**.

### Distinguishing Features
- ☐ Blown film vs. **cast film:** Blown = biaxial orientation; Cast = mostly uniaxial.
- ☐ Spiral mandrel die vs. **side-fed die:** Spiral for uniform distribution (LDPE); Side-fed for less shear-sensitive polymers (HDPE).
- ☐ Air-cooled (most common) vs. **water-cooled** blown film (used for high-output PP lines).

### Common Defects (must know minimum 4)
- ☐ Bubble instability, gauge banding, sharkskin, gels, blocking, frost line variation, drawdown resonance.

### Exam Keywords to Use
- "**Biaxial orientation**", "**frost line**", "**Coanda effect**", "**spiral mandrel die**", "**Blow-Up Ratio**", "**Draw-Down Ratio**", "**mass conservation**".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flat Film
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flat Film

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flat Film Extrusion** (also known as **Cast Film Extrusion** or **Sheet Extrusion**, depending on thickness) is a continuous polymer processing operation in which a thermoplastic melt is forced through a wide, flat, slot-type die (commonly a **T-die** or **Coat-Hanger die**), drawn down onto a chilled, highly polished rotating roll stack, solidified by rapid heat removal, trimmed at the edges, and finally wound onto rolls. The product thickness can range from **0.01 mm to 0.5 mm** (films) up to **0.5 mm to 25 mm** (sheets).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Flat film processing is a *primary shape-forming* technique in which the polymer melt is given a flat, uniform cross-section by means of a sheet die, and the molten web is simultaneously drawn, quenched, and crystallized (or vitrified) on a **chill roll (chill roll casting)** assembly before being wound up. The defining feature distinguishing flat film from blown film is the use of a **linear, single-dimensional drawing path** on a solid cooling surface, rather than a tubular bubble.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine squeezing toothpaste from a wide, flat nozzle onto a cold, spinning metal roller (like a pizza dough roller).

- The **toothpaste** = molten polymer (e.g., polyethylene, polypropylene, PET).
- The **flat nozzle** = the sheet die.
- The **cold spinning roller** = the chill roll, which freezes the film in microseconds.
- The **flattening of the dough** = the **draw-down** of the melt as the chill roll pulls the web faster than it emerges from the die.

The film that comes out is uniform, glossy, and dimensionally stable — exactly the kind of material used in food packaging, lamination films, photographic films, and capacitor dielectrics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Cast Film vs. Blown Film:** Both produce thin polymer webs, but cast film uses a *chill roll* (uniaxial draw path) whereas blown film uses an *air bubble* (biaxial orientation from internal air pressure). Cast film has **better optical clarity, gauge uniformity, and higher production speeds (up to 250–300 m/min)**, while blown film offers **balanced biaxial properties and better tear strength**.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Line Speed (Take-up) | **30 – 300 m/min** |
| Film Thickness | **10 µm – 500 µm** (cast film) |
| Sheet Thickness | **0.5 – 25 mm** |
| Melt Temperature at Die | **180 – 290 °C** (polymer dependent) |
| Chill Roll Temperature | **15 – 90 °C** |
| Draw-Down Ratio | **5 : 1 to 40 : 1** |
| Die Lip Opening | **0.3 – 1.5 mm** |
| Output Rate | **50 – 1500 kg/h** |
| Die Width | **up to 4 – 5 m** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to compare cast film vs. blown film, and to draw a labeled schematic of the flat film line. Memorize the function of *each* component from extruder to winder.
