## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Flat Film Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The flat film line is a *serial* manufacturing line. Each station performs a specific thermodynamic or mechanical function:

1. **Feeding & Melting (Extruder):**
   - Polymer granules enter a single-screw extruder (typically with a barrier screw for high-output lines).
   - The screw plasticates, mixes, and meters the melt to the die at a constant pressure (10–35 MPa) and temperature (±1 °C).
   - A **screen pack** and **gear pump** are placed between the extruder and die to filter contaminants and provide *melt pulsation-free* flow.

2. **Distribution in the Die (Coat-Hanger or T-Die):**
   - Melt must be distributed across the entire width of the die with **uniform pressure, velocity, and residence time**.
   - A poorly designed die produces gauge bands (thickness variations across the width).
   - **Coat-hanger die** is the industry standard — its manifold is shaped like a coat hanger, with a tapered land that gives **uniform flow**.

3. **Die Exit — Draw-Down:**
   - As the melt leaves the die lips, it experiences:
     - **Die Swell (Extrudate Swell):** The extrudate thickens as elastic recovery occurs. Die swell ratio $\chi = \frac{h_{web}}{h_{lip}}$.
     - **Neck-In:** The width of the film *contracts* below the die width due to surface tension and draw stresses. Neck-in $N = W_{die} - W_{web}$.

4. **Quenching on the Chill Roll (Primary Cooling):**
   - The molten web is pinned against a large-diameter, chrome-plated, internally water-cooled steel roll.
   - An **air knife** blows air (or uses a **vacuum box**) to press the film against the roll for maximum heat transfer.
   - Typical heat transfer coefficient: $h = 1500 - 3000 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$.

5. **Secondary Cooling (Optional):**
   - A second chill roll or an air-cooled section completes solidification for thick sheets or slow lines.

6. **Edge Trimming & Gauging:**
   - The thickened "bead" edges are slit and recycled back to the extruder (regrind).
   - An online **β-ray or X-ray thickness gauge** controls the die lip bolts automatically.

7. **Surface Treatment & Winding:**
   - **Corona or flame treatment** improves printability/adhesion.
   - The film is wound on a **center-wind, dual-station turret winder** with tension control (typically 50–300 N/m of width).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are exam-critical and have appeared in KTU 2024 scheme questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = v \cdot w \cdot t$ | Volumetric output (throughput) | m³/s |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = \rho \cdot v \cdot w \cdot t$ | Mass output rate | kg/s |
| 3 | $DR = \dfrac{v_{take-up}}{v_{die-exit}}$ | Draw-down ratio (also called haul-off ratio) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $DR = \dfrac{h_{lip} \cdot W_{lip}}{t_{web} \cdot W_{web}}$ | Draw-down ratio in terms of cross-section (mass conservation) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $N = W_{die} - W_{web}$ | Neck-in (lateral contraction) | m |
| 6 | $\chi = \dfrac{h_{web}}{h_{lip}}$ | Die swell ratio (extrudate swell) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $v_{die} = \dfrac{Q}{W_{lip} \cdot h_{lip}}$ | Average melt velocity at die exit | m/s |
| 8 | $t_{c} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot C_{p} \cdot t \cdot (T_{m} - T_{roll})}{h \cdot (T_{s} - T_{roll})}$ | Approximate chill roll cooling time | s |
| 9 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \cdot \mu \cdot Q \cdot L}{W \cdot h^{3}}$ | Pressure drop in die land (slit flow, power-law fluid with n=1) | Pa |
| 10 | $h_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{\frac{1}{h_{air}} + \frac{\delta_{film}}{k_{film}} + \frac{\delta_{roll}}{k_{roll}} + \frac{1}{h_{water}}}$ | Composite heat transfer between melt and roll water | W/m²K |
| 11 | $B = \dfrac{W \cdot h^{n+1}}{(2n+1) \cdot \Delta P}$ | Power-law fluid flow rate in slit die | m³/s |
| 12 | $T_{Neck} = T_{m} \cdot \left[1 + \left(\frac{T_{ad}}{T_{m}}\right) \cdot \ln(DR)\right]$ | Adiabatic drawing temperature rise (fountain-flow heating) | °C or K |

> [!WARNING]
> **Avoid the pipe `|` symbol in markdown tables!** If a problem uses absolute values, render them as $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX or write them descriptively as "abs(x)" in the table cell.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Packaging Industry:** BOPP (biaxially oriented PP) and BOPET capacitor films are first made as cast films, then stretched.
- **Photographic & MRI Films:** Require ultra-flat, defect-free cast PET base film.
- **Laminations:** Multilayer cast film lines (up to 7 layers via feed-block or multi-manifold dies) make food packaging.
- **Solar Backsheets:** PVDF and PET cast films.
- **Hot-melt adhesives:** Cast directly onto release paper via flat film dies (slot coating).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Rate from Mass Conservation

**Statement:** For a steady-state flat film line, the volumetric flow rate through the die equals the volumetric rate at which film is taken up by the chill roll.

**Given:** A flat film of width $w$ and thickness $t$ is wound at linear velocity $v$. Polymer density is $\rho$.

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow rate from the take-up side.**
The take-up station pulls a rectangular web of cross-section $w \times t$ at speed $v$. In time $dt$, the length of film produced is $v \cdot dt$, so the volume produced is:

$$dV_{take-up} = (v \cdot dt) \cdot w \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate.**

$$Q = \frac{dV}{dt} = v \cdot w \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass flow rate using density $\rho$:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = \rho \cdot v \cdot w \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Apply conservation of mass at the die lip:**
The volumetric flow at the die exit must equal the volumetric flow at the take-up (incompressible melt + solid polymer of same density in the solid state is approximated):

$$Q = v_{die} \cdot W_{lip} \cdot h_{lip} = v \cdot w \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the take-up velocity $v$:**

$$v = \frac{v_{die} \cdot W_{lip} \cdot h_{lip}}{w \cdot t}$$

This equation is the foundation for setting the **haul-off speed** for a target film thickness.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Draw-Down Ratio in Terms of Geometry

**Statement:** Show that the draw-down ratio equals the ratio of die cross-section to final film cross-section.

**Step 1 — Mass conservation** (melt side, with subscripts "0" for die exit and "1" for solidified film):

$$\rho_0 \cdot v_0 \cdot W_{lip} \cdot h_{lip} = \rho_1 \cdot v_1 \cdot w_{film} \cdot t_{film}$$

**Step 2 — Define Draw-Down Ratio (DR):**

$$DR = \frac{v_1}{v_0}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange the mass balance:**

$$\frac{v_1}{v_0} = \frac{\rho_0 \cdot W_{lip} \cdot h_{lip}}{\rho_1 \cdot w_{film} \cdot t_{film}}$$

**Step 4 — Assume the polymer is in the same solid-state (below $T_g$ for amorphous, below $T_m$ for semi-crystalline) so $\rho_0 \approx \rho_1$ for the *melt* vs. *solid* case is replaced with a density correction factor:**

$$\frac{\rho_0}{\rho_1} = \frac{1}{\rho_{ratio}}$$

$$DR = \frac{W_{lip} \cdot h_{lip}}{\rho_{ratio} \cdot w_{film} \cdot t_{film}}$$

For a typical semi-crystalline polymer (e.g., PP) with $\rho_{melt}/\rho_{solid} \approx 0.73$, the density ratio correction is small but must be included in KTU numerical problems.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Pressure Drop in a Power-Law Fluid in a Slit Die Land

**Statement:** Derive the pressure drop-flow relationship for the die land region.

**Step 1 — Constitutive equation for a power-law fluid:**

$$\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

**Step 2 — For fully developed laminar flow in a slit of half-thickness $H = h/2$, the shear rate at the wall is:**

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{2n+1}{n} \cdot \frac{6Q}{W \cdot h^2}$$

For a Newtonian fluid ($n=1$), this reduces to $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dfrac{6Q}{W \cdot h^2}$.

**Step 3 — Wall shear stress:**

$$\tau_w = \frac{h \cdot \Delta P}{2L}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for the flow rate (the classical "power-law slit flow" equation):**

$$Q = \frac{W \cdot h^{2} \cdot \left(\frac{h \cdot \Delta P}{2KL}\right)^{1/n}}{2 \cdot \left(\frac{2n+1}{n}\right)}$$

Or, in the more common compact form used in KTU textbooks:

$$Q = \frac{n \cdot W \cdot h^{n+1}}{2(2n+1) \cdot K} \cdot \left(\frac{\Delta P}{2L}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 5 — For a Newtonian fluid ($n=1, K=\mu$):**

$$Q = \frac{W \cdot h^{3} \cdot \Delta P}{12 \cdot \mu \cdot L}$$

This is the **canonical slit-die equation** and is heavily tested.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A flat film line produces LDPE film. Die width $W_{lip} = 1.2$ m, lip opening $h_{lip} = 1.0$ mm, final film thickness $t = 50$ µm, final film width $w = 1.1$ m. The melt density is $770 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and the solid density is $920 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Calculate the take-up velocity if the line runs at $\dot{m} = 200$ kg/h.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert mass flow rate to SI units:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{200}{3600} = 0.0556 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate of solid film:**

$$Q_{solid} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{solid}} = \frac{0.0556}{920} = 6.04 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

**Step 3 — By mass conservation, this equals the volumetric take-up rate $Q = v \cdot w \cdot t$:**

$$v = \frac{Q_{solid}}{w \cdot t} = \frac{6.04 \times 10^{-5}}{1.1 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$v = \frac{6.04 \times 10^{-5}}{5.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.098 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to m/min for the operator panel:**

$$v = 1.098 \times 60 = 65.9 \text{ m/min}$$

**Step 5 — Draw-Down Ratio:**

$$v_{die} = \frac{Q}{W_{lip} \cdot h_{lip}} = \frac{6.04 \times 10^{-5}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0503 \text{ m/s}$$

$$DR = \frac{v_{take-up}}{v_{die}} = \frac{1.098}{0.0503} \approx 21.8 : 1$$

**Answer:** Take-up speed = **65.9 m/min**, Draw-down ratio ≈ **22 : 1**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cast Film vs. Blown Film

| Parameter | Cast (Flat) Film | Blown Film |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling | Chill roll (uniaxial) | Air bubble (biaxial) |
| Optical clarity | Excellent | Moderate |
| Gauge uniformity | Excellent (±2–5%) | Moderate (±5–10%) |
| Line speed | High (up to 300 m/min) | Lower (typically 60–120 m/min) |
| Orientation | Mostly MD (machine direction) | Balanced MD/TD |
| Tear strength | Lower (easy-tear MD) | Higher |
| Haze | Low | Higher |
| Thickness range | 10 µm – 25 mm (incl. sheet) | 10 – 200 µm typical |
| Die | Coat-hanger T-die | Annular spider die |
| Investment | High | Lower |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Flat Film Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hopper + Drying] --> B[Single Screw Extruder]
    B --> C[Screen Pack + Gear Pump]
    C --> D[Coat-Hanger T-Die]
    D --> E[Air Knife / Vacuum Box]
    E --> F[Primary Chill Roll]
    F --> G[Secondary Chill Roll]
    G --> H[Thickness Gauge]
    H --> I[Edge Trimmer]
    I --> J[Corona Treater]
    J --> K[Slitter]
    K --> L[Turret Winder]
    L --> M[Finished Roll]
    I -.Regrind.-> B
```

**Process flow narrative:** Hopper (1) → Extruder plasticates melt (2) → Screen pack filters; gear pump meters (3) → Coat-hanger die spreads melt uniformly (4) → Air knife presses web onto chill roll (5, 6, 7) → β-gauge measures thickness; feedback adjusts die-bolt heaters (8) → Edge trimmer cuts and recycles the bead (9) → Corona treat (10) → Slit to required widths (11) → Turret winder (12) → Shipped roll (13).

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Coat-Hanger Die Internals

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    In[Melt Inlet from Gear Pump] --> Man[Coat-Hanger Manifold]
    Man --> Pre[Pre-Land Restrictor Zone]
    Pre --> Land[Final Die Land Slot]
    Land --> Lip[Die Lip Adjusters]
    Lip --> Out[Web Exits Vertically Downward]
    Bolts[Die Bolt Heaters] -. Adjust Gap .-> Lip
    Lip -. Thickness Profile Feedback .-> Gauge[Online Gauge]
    Gauge -. Control Signal .-> Bolts
```

**Explanation:** Melt enters the central manifold shaped like a coat hanger. The cross-sectional area of the manifold is reduced progressively along the width so that *pressure* (and hence flow velocity) remains uniform across the entire die width. The restrictor zone (pre-land) further homogenizes the flow. The final land (10–25 mm long) damps any residual flow non-uniformity. Die bolts at the lips adjust the gap in 25–50 mm increments to fine-tune the thickness profile.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Cooling Zones on Chill Roll

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Contact: Web touches roll
    Contact --> AirKnife: Pinned by air/vacuum
    AirKnife --> PrimaryCool: 0 to 60 percent of roll wrap
    PrimaryCool --> Crystallize: T reaches Tc
    Crystallize --> SecondaryCool: 60 to 100 percent wrap
    SecondaryCool --> Strip: Web leaves roll
    Strip --> Trimmer: Bead removed
    Trimmer --> [*]
```

**Description:** The web undergoes **non-steady-state transient heat conduction** in the contact zone. At the "crystallization" transition (for semi-crystalline polymers), the cooling rate briefly plateaus because **latent heat of fusion** is released. The chill roll must be designed to *absorb* this heat without the surface temperature rising more than 2–5 °C.

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Architecture Flow — Control System for Flat Film Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sensors
        S1[Thickness Gauge Beta X-ray]
        S2[Melt Pressure Transducer]
        S3[Melt Temperature Pyrometer]
        S4[Chill Roll Surface RTD]
        S5[Line Speed Encoder]
    end
    subgraph PLC[Central PLC Controller]
        PID1[Melt Temp PID]
        PID2[Chill Roll PID]
        PID3[Line Speed PID]
        PID4[Thickness Profile PID]
    end
    subgraph Actuators
        A1[Die Heaters + Bolts]
        A2[Chill Roll Chiller Valve]
        A3[Pull Roll Drive Inverter]
        A4[Gear Pump RPM]
    end
    S1 --> PID4
    S2 --> PID4
    S3 --> PID1
    S4 --> PID2
    S5 --> PID3
    PID1 --> A1
    PID2 --> A2
    PID3 --> A3
    PID4 --> A1
    PID4 --> A4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid block diagrams?** Polymer processing equipment is a complex system of interacting mechanical, thermal, and control elements. A block-level functional architecture communicates the *interactions* (which is what KTU examiners test) more clearly than an attempt at a literal physical drawing, which would fail Mermaid's text-based rendering.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] (3 Marks)**
**"With the help of a neat sketch, explain the flat film extrusion process and list any four advantages of cast film over blown film."**

**Model Answer:**

Flat film extrusion is a continuous polymer shaping process in which a thermoplastic melt is extruded through a wide, flat, slot-type die (typically a **coat-hanger die**), drawn down onto a **chill roll assembly**, solidified, trimmed, and wound. The molten web is pinned to the polished, water-cooled roll by an **air knife or vacuum box**, which ensures intimate contact for high heat transfer coefficients (1500–3000 W/m²K).

**Four advantages of cast film over blown film:**
1. **Better optical clarity** (lower haze) because the film is quenched on a mirror-finish chrome roll.
2. **Tighter gauge tolerance** (±2–5%) due to precise die-bolt adjustment and β-gauge feedback.
3. **Higher line speeds** (up to 300 m/min) feasible because of efficient chill-roll cooling.
4. **Lower orientation in TD**, making the film easy to slit, laminate, and metallize.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often *omit* a labeled sketch, costing 1 mark. Always include the chill roll, air knife, take-up rolls, winder, and die — even in short answers.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model] (3 Marks)**
**"Define 'neck-in' and 'die swell' in flat film extrusion. State the factors affecting neck-in."**

**Model Answer:**

- **Neck-in (N):** The lateral contraction of the molten web as it travels from the die exit to the chill roll. Mathematically, $N = W_{die} - W_{web}$. It is caused by surface tension and tensile drawing stresses in the machine direction.

- **Die swell ($\chi$):** The increase in film thickness (and corresponding width reduction) at the die exit due to the elastic recovery of the polymer melt from the high-shear deformation in the die. $\chi = h_{web}/h_{lip}$.

**Factors affecting neck-in:**
1. **Melt draw-down ratio (DR):** Higher DR → more neck-in.
2. **Melt temperature:** Higher T → more neck-in.
3. **Die land length:** Longer land → less neck-in (more flow damping).
4. **Polymer rheology:** Higher elasticity (high $G'$, long relaxation time) → more neck-in.
5. **Line speed:** Higher take-up velocity → more neck-in.
6. **Air gap between die and chill roll:** Longer contact-free fall → more neck-in.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU Pattern: Each question is split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks]**

---

#### **QUESTION 1A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model] (14 Marks)**

**Q1A(a).** With the help of a labeled diagram, describe the **flat film extrusion line**. Explain the function of each major component from the extruder to the winder. **(7 Marks)**

**Q1A(b).** A coat-hanger die has the following specifications: Die width $W_{lip} = 1.5$ m, die lip opening $h_{lip} = 0.8$ mm. The line produces a film of thickness $t = 40$ µm and width $w = 1.42$ m at a take-up speed of $80$ m/min. The melt density is $760$ kg/m³ and the solid density is $940$ kg/m³.
- (i) Calculate the mass output rate in kg/h.
- (ii) Calculate the draw-down ratio.
- (iii) Calculate the average velocity of the melt at the die exit. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q1A(a):**

**Major Components and Functions:**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hopper / Dryer** | Stores and dries polymer granules (moisture < 50 ppm for PET, < 200 ppm for nylon) |
| 2 | **Single-Screw Extruder** | Melts, mixes, and meters the polymer; typically 24:1 to 30:1 L/D |
| 3 | **Screen Pack & Breaker Plate** | Filters contaminants; creates back-pressure; removes melt pulsations |
| 4 | **Gear Pump** | Provides *pulsation-free*, *pressure-stable* melt to the die; decouples extruder output from die pressure |
| 5 | **Coat-Hanger Die** | Spreads melt uniformly across the die width; delivers a flat, uniform web |
| 6 | **Air Knife / Vacuum Box** | Pins the molten web to the chill roll; enables rapid heat removal |
| 7 | **Primary Chill Roll** | Chrome-plated, internally water-cooled; solidifies the film |
| 8 | **Secondary Chill Roll** | Completes cooling for thick films/sheets |
| 9 | **Thickness Gauge** | β-ray or X-ray sensor; provides feedback to die-bolt heaters |
| 10 | **Edge Trimmer** | Removes the thickened "bead" at the edges; sends regrind to extruder |
| 11 | **Corona Treater** | Increases surface energy for printing/laminating (38–42 dyne/cm) |
| 12 | **Slitter** | Cuts the wide web into customer-specified roll widths |
| 13 | **Turret Winder** | Winds finished rolls with tension control; allows non-stop roll change |

**Diagram:** (Refer to Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes — always present a labeled sketch in your exam script.)

---

**Model Solution for Q1A(b):**

**Given Data:**
- $W_{lip} = 1.5$ m, $h_{lip} = 0.8 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $t = 40 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $w = 1.42$ m
- $v = 80$ m/min $= 80/60 = 1.333$ m/s
- $\rho_{melt} = 760$ kg/m³, $\rho_{solid} = 940$ kg/m³

**Step (i) — Mass output rate:**

Volumetric flow rate of solidified film:

$$Q_{solid} = v \cdot w \cdot t = 1.333 \times 1.42 \times 40 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$Q_{solid} = 7.57 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

Mass flow rate (using solid density):

$$\dot{m} = \rho_{solid} \cdot Q_{solid} = 940 \times 7.57 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\dot{m} = 0.0712 \text{ kg/s} = 0.0712 \times 3600$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = 256.3 \text{ kg/h}}$$

**Step (ii) — Draw-down ratio:**

Melt volumetric flow rate at die (using melt density):

$$Q_{melt} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho_{melt}} = \frac{0.0712}{760} = 9.37 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3\text{/s}$$

Average velocity at die exit:

$$v_{die} = \frac{Q_{melt}}{W_{lip} \cdot h_{lip}} = \frac{9.37 \times 10^{-5}}{1.5 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0781 \text{ m/s}$$

Draw-Down Ratio:

$$DR = \frac{v}{v_{die}} = \frac{1.333}{0.0781}$$

$$\boxed{DR \approx 17.07 : 1}$$

**Step (iii) — Average melt velocity at die exit:**

$$\boxed{v_{die} = 0.0781 \text{ m/s} = 7.81 \text{ cm/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students commonly forget to use **$\rho_{solid}$** in part (i) because the *solid* film is the one being wound, and the *melt* density in part (ii)/(iii) because the die is processing *molten* polymer. Mixing up densities can cost **2–3 marks** out of 7.

---

#### **QUESTION 1B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Alternative] (14 Marks)**

**Q1B(a).** Explain the **coat-hanger die** in detail. Discuss the design principles that ensure uniform film thickness across the width, and describe how die-bolt adjustment is used for profile control. **(7 Marks)**

**Q1B(b).** Derive the **pressure drop equation for a power-law fluid in a slit die land**. A LDPE melt with consistency $K = 1.5 \times 10^4$ Pa·s$^n$ and power-law index $n = 0.4$ flows through a slit die of width $W = 1.0$ m, half-gap $H = 0.5$ mm, and land length $L = 20$ mm. The volumetric flow rate is $Q = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s. Calculate the pressure drop across the land. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q1B(a):**

**Coat-Hanger Die — Design and Function:**

The coat-hanger die is the most widely used die for flat film and sheet extrusion. It is so named because its internal manifold has a shape resembling a coat hanger: a circular (or tear-drop) inlet pipe at the center, fanning out into a tapered slot manifold, ending in a uniform-width land.

**Design Principles for Uniform Thickness:**

1. **Constant Pressure at the Land Entry:** The manifold cross-sectional area is designed to *decrease* from the center (inlet) to the ends (die edges). This compensates for the longer flow path at the edges, so pressure is uniform across the entire width.

2. **Land Length Equalization:** The final die land has constant length $L$ across the width. Combined with the pre-land restrictor zone, it dampens any residual flow non-uniformity.

3. **Rheological Compensation:** For shear-thinning polymer melts, the die designer uses a finite-element or analytical model (e.g., 1-D carothers model, 2-D Newtonian approximation) to ensure the *shear rate* (and hence pressure drop) is the same at every cross-section.

4. **Land Height Gradation:** The pre-land (restrictor) height is graded from a maximum at the center to a minimum at the edges to fine-tune the flow.

5. **Choker Bar (older designs):** A movable bar across the manifold restricts flow and equalizes pressure.

**Die-Bolt Adjustment (Profile Control):**

- Each die has **20 to 40 die bolts** along the width, each controlling a 25–50 mm segment of the die lip.
- The bolt is heated by a small cartridge heater — heating the bolt locally *expands* the die land, *opening* the gap and increasing local thickness.
- A **β-ray thickness gauge** mounted after the chill roll measures the actual profile, and a **closed-loop PLC** adjusts the bolt heaters to maintain a target profile within ±1–2%.
- Modern dies use **piezoelectric or thermal actuator** bolts for sub-micron resolution.

**Sketch (in exam):** Draw a top-view of the die with: (i) circular inlet, (ii) tapered manifold, (iii) pre-land restrictor zone, (iv) uniform land, (v) die lip, (vi) bolts labeled at top, (vii) die width W.

---

**Model Solution for Q1B(b):**

**Derivation of Pressure Drop for Power-Law Fluid in Slit Die Land:**

The general slit-flow equation for a power-law fluid in a slit of half-gap $H$, width $W$, and length $L$ is:

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{2L \cdot K}{H} \cdot \left[\frac{(2n+1) \cdot Q}{2 \cdot n \cdot W \cdot H^2}\right]^{n}}$$

**Step-by-step derivation outline (must be shown in exam):**

1. Start with constitutive equation: $\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n$.
2. Apply momentum balance in the slit: $dP/dx = (1/H) \cdot d\tau/dz$.
3. Velocity profile: $v_z(y) = \left(\dfrac{n}{n+1}\right) \cdot \left[\dfrac{1}{K} \cdot \left\vert \dfrac{dP}{dx} \right\vert \right]^{1/n} \cdot \left[H^{(n+1)/n} - \vert y \vert^{(n+1)/n}\right]$.
4. Integrate over slit cross-section to get volumetric flow rate $Q$.
5. Solve for $\Delta P$ in terms of $Q$.

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:** $K = 1.5 \times 10^4$ Pa·s$^n$, $n = 0.4$, $W = 1.0$ m, $H = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $L = 20 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $Q = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s.

**Step 1 — Compute the bracketed term:**

$$A = \frac{(2n+1) \cdot Q}{2 \cdot n \cdot W \cdot H^2}$$

$$A = \frac{(2 \times 0.4 + 1) \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 0.4 \times 1.0 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

$$A = \frac{1.8 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.8 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$A = \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-5}}{2.0 \times 10^{-7}} = 450 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Raise to the power $n = 0.4$:**

$$A^{0.4} = 450^{0.4}$$

Using $\log_{10}(450) = 2.653$, $0.4 \times 2.653 = 1.061$, $10^{1.061} = 11.51$.

$$A^{0.4} \approx 11.5$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\Delta P$:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{2L \cdot K}{H} \cdot A^{0.4}$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \times 20 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5 \times 10^{4}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} \times 11.5$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{600}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \times 11.5$$

$$\Delta P = 1.2 \times 10^{6} \times 11.5$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P \approx 1.38 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 13.8 \text{ MPa}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall #1:** Many students mistakenly use $H = h/2$ as $h$ (the full gap) — losing 1 mark.
> **Examiner Pitfall #2:** Forgetting the $1/2$ factor in the bracketed term is the single most common error in this derivation. Re-derive carefully from $\int v \, dA$ if unsure.
> **Examiner Pitfall #3:** For Newtonian fluids ($n = 1$, $K = \mu$), the equation **reduces to** $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L Q}{W h^3}$ — make sure you mention this special case for full marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in "Flat Film" Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw a labeled diagram** in 7-mark questions → loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing die width with film width** when computing draw-down ratio.
> 3. **Using the wrong density** (melt vs. solid) when computing mass flow rate.
> 4. **Ignoring neck-in** in geometric calculations — when the problem gives both $W_{lip}$ and $w_{web}$, students often mistakenly set them equal.
> 5. **Mixing units** — die gap in mm, film thickness in µm, line speed in m/min. Always convert to SI (m, s) before substituting.
> 6. **Omitting edge-trim recycling logic** in process descriptions — examiners want to see that you understand the closed-loop regrind flow.
> 7. **Wrong equation for power-law slit flow** — memorize the *compact* form and the *Newtonian limiting case*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Flat Film" Module 2**

- **Definition:** Continuous shaping of molten polymer into a flat web via a coat-hanger (or T-) die, quenched on a chill roll, and wound.
- **Cast Film vs. Blown Film:** Cast = chill roll (uniaxial), blown = air bubble (biaxial). Cast is clearer, more uniform, faster.
- **Standard thickness range:** 10 µm – 500 µm (film); 0.5 – 25 mm (sheet).
- **Line speed:** 30 – 300 m/min.
- **Major components:** Extruder → Screen pack → Gear pump → Coat-hanger die → Air knife → Chill roll (primary) → Secondary chill roll → β-gauge → Edge trimmer → Corona treater → Slitter → Turret winder.
- **Chill roll heat transfer coefficient:** 1500 – 3000 W/m²K.
- **Output rate equation:** $Q = v \cdot w \cdot t$ and $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$.
- **Draw-down ratio:** $DR = v_{take-up} / v_{die}$.
- **Neck-in:** $N = W_{die} - W_{web}$.
- **Die swell:** $\chi = h_{web} / h_{lip}$.
- **Power-law slit die flow (non-Newtonian):** $Q = \dfrac{n \cdot W \cdot h^{n+1}}{2(2n+1) \cdot K} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{2L}\right)^{n}$.
- **Newtonian slit die flow:** $Q = \dfrac{W \cdot h^3 \cdot \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$ (or $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu L Q}{W h^3}$).
- **Fountain flow:** Melt flows outward from the center to the surfaces; responsible for surface orientation in cast film.
- **Surface treatment:** Corona or flame (38–42 dyne/cm surface energy).
- **Closed-loop control:** β-ray gauge → die bolt heaters (auto profile).
- **Regrind flow:** Edge trim and scrap are re-extruded through the same line.
- **Adiabatic drawing temperature rise:** $T_{Neck} = T_m \left[1 + (T_{ad}/T_m) \ln(DR)\right]$ — relevant for very high DR (e.g., BOPP, BOPET lines).

**One-line exam mnemonics:**
- "**Hopper → Extruder → Pump → Die → Chill → Trim → Treat → Wind**" (process order).
- "**C**oat-hanger gives **C**onstant pressure across the **C**ross-width" (uniformity).
- "**Draw-down makes the film thin; chill roll makes it solid; winder makes it saleable**" (function of each station).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sheets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sheets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Polymer Sheet:** A flat, continuous polymeric product with a thickness generally greater than **0.25 mm** (250 µm) and less than **25 mm**, produced by extrusion, calendering, or casting, characterized by uniform thickness, smooth surface finish, and a width-to-thickness ratio typically exceeding **100:1**.

A sheet is distinguished from a *film* primarily by its **thickness threshold**. In industrial practice, the demarcation line is:
- **Film:** thickness ≤ **0.25 mm** (250 µm)
- **Sheet:** thickness **> 0.25 mm** (up to 25 mm or beyond)
- **Plate:** thickness **> 25 mm**

The polymer melt is forced through a wide, slit-like die, calibrated for thickness, and subsequently cooled, dimensionally stabilized, surface-treated, and wound or cut into finished stock.

## 1.2 Classification of Polymer Sheets

| Class | Typical Thickness | Example Polymers | End-Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thin Sheets | 0.25 – 0.5 mm | PP, PS, PET | Lamination, stationery |
| Standard Sheets | 0.5 – 3 mm | HDPE, PP, PVC | Thermoforming, packaging |
| Thick Sheets | 3 – 12 mm | ABS, PC, PMMA | Industrial panels |
| Heavy Plates | > 12 mm | HDPE, PP | Chemical tanks, machining stock |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy 🍝

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition – "The Polymer Pasta Machine"**
> Imagine a baker forcing soft dough through a wide, flat slot. As the ribbon of dough emerges, it is stretched slightly thinner, draped over a set of cold metal rollers that harden it, and finally cut into sheets. Polymer sheet extrusion works on exactly this principle — molten polymer is the dough, a wide T-shaped die is the slot, the chill rolls are the cold rollers, and the haul-off unit determines how much the sheet is stretched before it solidifies.

The amount of stretching is governed by the **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR)** — the ratio of the die gap to the final sheet thickness — analogous to how thin you pull the dough.

## 1.4 Key Material Examples

- **Rigid Sheets:** Polypropylene (PP), Polystyrene (PS), Polyvinyl Chloride (uPVC), Polyethylene Terephthalate (APET), Acrylic (PMMA), Polycarbonate (PC), Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS)
- **Flexible Sheets:** Low-Density Polyethylene (LDPE), Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LLDPE), Plasticized PVC (pPVC), Ethylene Vinyl Acetate (EVA)
- **Engineering Sheets:** Polycarbonate (PC), Polyoxymethylene (POM), Polyamide (PA6, PA66)

## 1.5 Primary Applications

> [!NOTE]
> - **Packaging:** Thermoformed trays, blister packs, clamshells
> - **Construction:** Wall cladding, roofing, glazing, vapor barriers
> - **Automotive:** Interior trims, dashboards, door panels
> - **Stationery:** Folder, ID-card overlays
> - **Industrial:** Chemical-resistant liners, machine guards
> - **Advertising:** Sign boards, display panels
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sheet Extrusion Process – Stepwise Logic

The sheet extrusion line transforms polymer granules into a flat, dimensionally precise sheet via a sequence of tightly coupled operations:

### Step 1 — Feeding & Plasticating
Polymer granules (and additives) are gravity-fed from the hopper into a **single-screw extruder** (occasionally twin-screw for compounds). The screw's three functional zones perform:
1. **Feed Zone:** Conveys and compacts the solids
2. **Compression (Melt) Zone:** Shears and melts the polymer
3. **Metering Zone:** Homogenizes the melt and develops pressure

### Step 2 — Melt Pumping
A **gear pump (melt pump)** is often interposed between the extruder and the die. It decouples the extruder's pressure fluctuations from the die, ensuring **±0.5 %** flow uniformity — critical for gauge (thickness) accuracy.

### Step 3 — Filtration
A **breaker plate with screen pack** filters gels, carbonized particles, and agglomerates that would otherwise create surface defects or pinholes.

### Step 4 — Sheet Die
The die is the heart of the operation. The three most prevalent die architectures are:
- **T-die (Slit die):** Simplest construction; used for low-viscosity, narrow widths
- **Coat-hanger die:** Channel shaped like a coat-hanger; gives **uniform flow distribution** across wide widths — the industry standard
- **Fishtail die:** Channel diverges from a central point; used for very wide sheets and certain calender feeds

### Step 5 — Calibration & Cooling
A **chill roll stack** (typically a 3-roll "I" or "L" arrangement) simultaneously cools, calibrates the thickness via roll gap, and imparts surface finish (matte, gloss, embossed).

### Step 6 — Downstream Operations
**Haul-off → Edge trimming → Thickness gauging → Surface treatment (corona/flame) → Cutting/Slitting → Stacking/Winding**

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every key equation you are likely to encounter in KTU examinations on the *Sheets* topic. **Memorize the symbols and the dimensional logic** — not just the formulas.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volumetric Throughput | $Q$ | $Q = W_s \cdot t_s \cdot v_h$ | m³/s | Sets extruder size |
| 2 | Mass Throughput | $\dot{m}$ | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | kg/s | Plant capacity planning |
| 3 | Linear Throughput | $v$ | $v = \dot{m} / (\rho \cdot W_s \cdot t_s)$ | m/s | Line-speed determination |
| 4 | Draw-Down Ratio (linear) | $DDR$ | $DDR = h_d / t_s$ | dimensionless | Stretches & orients sheet |
| 5 | Area Draw-Down | $DDR_A$ | $DDR_A = (W_d h_d)/(W_s t_s)$ | dimensionless | Full area reduction |
| 6 | Draw Ratio (velocity) | $DR$ | $DR = v_h / v_d$ | dimensionless | Velocity stretching |
| 7 | Die Lip Land Length | $L$ | $L = (10 \text{ to } 20)\cdot h_d$ | mm | Reduces die swell |
| 8 | Die-Swell (Bagley) | $B$ | $B = (t_{sw} - t_s)/t_s$ | dimensionless | Edge bead control |
| 9 | Cooling Time (3-roll) | $t_c$ | $t_c = L_{arc}/v_h$ | s | Heat extraction window |
| 10 | Heat Flux | $q$ | $q = h_c(T_m - T_{roll})$ | W/m² | Cooling capacity |
| 11 | Biaxial Stress | $\sigma_{MD}$ | $\sigma = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa | Orientation prediction |
| 12 | Specific Energy | $E_{sp}$ | $E_{sp} = P/\dot{m}$ | J/kg | Process efficiency |
| 13 | Melt Pressure Drop (die) | $\Delta P$ | $\Delta P = 12\mu Q L / (W h^3)$ | Pa | Extruder power demand |
| 14 | Sag-Bead Width Fraction | $f_b$ | $f_b \le 0.10$ of $W_s$ | – | Material utilization |

Where:
- $W_s, t_s$ — sheet width and thickness
- $h_d$ — die lip opening
- $v_h, v_d$ — haul-off and die-exit velocity
- $L_{arc}$ — contact arc length on chill roll
- $\mu$ — polymer melt viscosity
- $h_c$ — convective heat-transfer coefficient

## 2.3 Why Draw-Down Matters

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why':** When the haul-off pulls the sheet faster than the melt exits the die, the sheet thins and the polymer chains are partially **oriented** in the machine direction. This **increases tensile strength and modulus along MD** but **reduces impact strength and ductility**, and can induce **neck-in** (width shrinkage) and **edge beads**. The DDR is the single most important design variable for balancing throughput, gauge, and mechanical anisotropy.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Coat-hanger manifold:** Enables > 4 m wide sheets with < 2 % gauge variation.
- **DDR tuning:** Allows a single line to produce a range of thicknesses without changing the die.
- **Chill roll polishing:** Controls surface gloss/roughness for downstream thermoforming, printing, or lamination.
- **Auto-gauge control:** Modern lines use β-ray or X-ray gauges feeding back to flex-lip bolts and dual-rotation screws for closed-loop control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Derivation — Volumetric & Mass Throughput of a Sheet Line

### Given
- Sheet thickness: $t_s = 2.0 \text{ mm} = 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- Sheet width: $W_s = 1200 \text{ mm} = 1.2 \text{ m}$
- Haul-off velocity: $v_h = 5 \text{ m/min}$
- Polymer (HDPE) density: $\rho = 950 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Die lip opening: $h_d = 3.0 \text{ mm} = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

### Required
(i) Volumetric throughput $Q$
(ii) Mass throughput $\dot{m}$
(iii) Draw-down ratio $DDR$
(iv) Die-exit velocity $v_d$
(v) Die-exit width $W_d$ (assuming no neck-in)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (i) — Convert haul-off velocity to SI units**

$$
v_h = 5 \;\frac{\text{m}}{\text{min}} = \frac{5}{60} \;\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}} = 0.0833 \;\text{m/s}
$$

*Logic:* Standard SI requires seconds; 1 min = 60 s.

**Step (ii) — Compute volumetric throughput**

$$
Q = W_s \cdot t_s \cdot v_h
$$

$$
Q = (1.2 \text{ m}) \cdot (2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}) \cdot (0.0833 \text{ m/s})
$$

$$
Q = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

*Logic:* Cross-sectional area of the finished sheet (width × thickness) multiplied by the velocity at which it is produced gives the volume extruded per second.

**Step (iii) — Compute mass throughput**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q
$$

$$
\dot{m} = (950 \;\text{kg/m}^3) \cdot (2.0 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s})
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.190 \;\text{kg/s} = 11.4 \;\text{kg/min}
$$

*Logic:* Mass is conserved; mass flow equals density multiplied by volumetric flow.

**Step (iv) — Compute the Draw-Down Ratio (linear thickness form)**

$$
DDR = \frac{h_d}{t_s} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}}{2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}}
$$

$$
DDR = 1.50
$$

*Logic:* A 3 mm die gap stretched to a 2 mm final sheet means the polymer is being drawn down by a factor of 1.5. Values typically lie between 1.2 and 6.0.

**Step (v) — Compute die-exit velocity using the principle of mass conservation**

For an incompressible melt: $A_d \cdot v_d = A_s \cdot v_h$

$$
v_d = v_h \cdot \frac{A_s}{A_d} = v_h \cdot \frac{W_s t_s}{W_d h_d}
$$

Assuming negligible width neck-in ($W_d \approx W_s$):

$$
v_d = v_h \cdot \frac{t_s}{h_d} = 0.0833 \cdot \frac{2.0}{3.0} = 0.0556 \;\text{m/s}
$$

*Logic:* A wider/thicker die cross-section means the melt must travel slower to satisfy continuity.

**Step (vi) — Compute the draw ratio (velocity form)**

$$
DR = \frac{v_h}{v_d} = \frac{0.0833}{0.0556} = 1.50
$$

*Logic:* Confirming that the velocity draw ratio equals the linear draw-down ratio for the ideal case of negligible neck-in.

### Summary of Numerical Results

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $v_h$ | **0.0833 m/s** |
| $Q$ | **2.0 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s** |
| $\dot{m}$ | **0.190 kg/s = 11.4 kg/min** |
| $DDR$ | **1.50** |
| $v_d$ | **0.0556 m/s** |
| $DR$ | **1.50** |

## 3.2 Secondary Derivation — Required Die Land Length

To minimize extrudate swelling (which causes edge beads and gauge variation), the die-land length $L$ must be sufficient to orient the melt and suppress elastic recovery.

$$
L = 15 \cdot h_d
$$

$$
L = 15 \cdot 3.0 \text{ mm} = 45 \text{ mm}
$$

*Logic:* The industry rule of thumb is **10 – 20 × gap**; 15 is a standard mid-value. Insufficient land causes the parison to swell, while excessive land causes melt degradation, residence-time issues, and pressure over-shoot.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operating Window for HDPE Sheet Extrusion

| Parameter | Symbol | Lower Limit | Upper Limit | Recommended | Failure if Exceeded |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Melt temperature | $T_m$ | 200 °C | 280 °C | **220 – 250 °C** | Degradation / sagging |
| Die pressure | $P_d$ | 5 MPa | 30 MPa | **10 – 20 MPa** | Surging / melt fracture |
| Draw-down ratio | $DDR$ | 1.1 | 6.0 | **1.3 – 2.5** | Neck-in / orientation |
| Chill-roll temp | $T_c$ | 30 °C | 90 °C | **60 – 80 °C** | Wrinkling / sticking |
| Line speed | $v_h$ | 1 m/min | 30 m/min | **3 – 12 m/min** | Instability / sag |
| Edge bead width | $f_b$ | — | 10 % | **< 5 %** | Material loss |
| Sheet gauge variation | $\Delta t$ | — | 5 % | **< 2 %** | Downstream rejection |

## 3.4 Heat-Transfer Sanity Check

The polymer enters the chill roll at $T_m \approx 230\;°\text{C}$ and must leave the third roll at $T \le 100\;°\text{C}$ for stable winding.

For a 2 mm HDPE sheet on three 0.8 m diameter rolls at 5 m/min line speed, contact arc per roll is approximated by:

$$
L_{arc} = \frac{2\pi R}{3} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 0.4}{3} = 0.838 \text{ m}
$$

Total contact time on the roll stack:

$$
t_{contact} = \frac{3 \cdot L_{arc}}{v_h} = \frac{3 \cdot 0.838}{0.0833} = 30.2 \text{ s}
$$

*Logic:* HDPE crystallization (peak at 115 °C) must occur while the sheet is still on the rolls to ensure flatness; a 30 s window is adequate for 2 mm gauge at 5 m/min.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Typical Sheet Extrusion Line

The following functional-architecture flowchart captures every key machine element from raw-material input to finished stacked sheets, including the feedback control loop for thickness.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[Raw Material Hopper and Dosing Unit] --> n2[Extruder Screw and Barrel]
    n2 --> n3[Breaker Plate and Screen Pack]
    n3 --> n4[Gear Melt Pump Optional]
    n4 --> n5[Adapter and Static Mixer]
    n5 --> n6[Coat Hanger Sheet Die]
    n6 --> n7[Flex Lip with Adjustable Bolts]
    n7 --> n8[Chill Roll Stack 3 Roll I or L]
    n8 --> n9[Air Knife and Edge Trimmer]
    n9 --> n10[Haul Off Unit with Rubber Rolls]
    n10 --> n11[Beta or X Ray Thickness Gauge]
    n11 --> n12[Surface Treatment Corona or Flame]
    n12 --> n13[Slitter and Sheet Cutter]
    n13 --> n14[Stacking Unit or Winder]
    n14 --> n15[Finished Sheet Stack]
    n11 -. Auto gauge feedback .-> n7
    n11 -. Line speed feedback .-> n10
```

## 4.2 Functional Grouping of Equipment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gA[MELT GENERATION\nHopper + Extruder + Screen] --> gB[MELT DISTRIBUTION\nGear Pump + Adapter + Die]
    gB --> gC[SOLIDIFICATION\nChill Roll Stack]
    gC --> gD[POST TREATMENT\nTrim + Haul + Gauge + Corona]
    gD --> gE[CONVERSION\nSlit + Cut + Stack + Wind]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of a Coat-Hanger Sheet Die

A coat-hanger die is the *de facto* standard for wide sheet extrusion. The internal manifold tapers from a circular inlet to a wide, flat, uniform-output slot. Polymer residence time across the width is held constant by tuning the manifold curvature.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    d1[Inlet from Extruder] --> d2[Circular Manifold Reservoir]
    d2 --> d3[Tapered Coat Hanger Channel]
    d3 --> d4[Preland Coffin Region]
    d4 --> d5[Land Region of Length L]
    d5 --> d6[Lip Opening of Gap h]
    d6 --> d7[Die Swell Edge Bead]
    d7 --> d8[Melt Curtain to Chill Rolls]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading:** The *coffin region* (or preland) damps the flow to remove circumferential velocity differences, while the *land* (length $L \approx 10$–$20 \times h_d$) suppresses extrudate swelling and yields a flat, uniform sheet curtain.

## 4.4 Process Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    c1[Set Point Sheet Thickness] --> c2[Thickness Gauge Sensor]
    c2 --> c3[PID Controller]
    c3 --> c4[Flex Lip Bolt Actuator]
    c4 --> c5[Die Lip Gap Adjusted]
    c5 --> c6[New Thickness Measured]
    c6 --> c2
```

*Interpretation:* The loop continuously tightens the **thickness deviation** until it is within tolerance (typically ±2 %).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — **Define a polymer sheet. How is it distinguished from a polymer film?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **polymer sheet** is a flat, continuous polymeric product with thickness **greater than 0.25 mm** and less than 25 mm, produced primarily by extrusion or calendering. It is distinguished from a **polymer film** by the thickness threshold: films are ≤ 0.25 mm, sheets exceed this value. Practically, sheets are stiffer, more dimensionally stable, and used in thermoforming, packaging, and structural applications, whereas films are flexible and used for wrapping, lamination, and bags.

> *Marker expects:* Clear thickness numbers, mention of extrusion/calendering process, application distinction. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for threshold, 1 mark for application difference.)*

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — **What is a coat-hanger die? Why is it preferred over a simple T-die for sheet extrusion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **coat-hanger die** is a sheet extrusion die whose internal manifold tapers from a circular inlet to a wide, flat, slit-like exit in the shape of a coat-hanger. It is preferred over the **T-die** because the tapered manifold:
1. Equalizes pressure and flow rate across the full width of the die, yielding a **uniform sheet thickness** (gauge variation < 2 %)
2. Minimizes **residence time difference** between the centre and edges, preventing degradation and warpage
3. Enables production of wide sheets (> 4 m) at uniform quality

> *Marker expects:* Sketch reference, three distinct advantages. *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for advantages.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently forget to **convert line speeds from m/min to m/s** — losing 1 full mark.
> - Neglecting **neck-in effects** when applying the draw-down ratio is a common mistake.
> - In die-design questions, students often skip the **land-length justification** — KTU marks this as a *process knowledge* point worth 1 mark.
> - Always state **assumptions** (incompressible melt, uniform flow, no width shrinkage) in derivations.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Modified)]

#### (a) — **With the help of a labelled schematic, explain the sheet extrusion process and the function of each component of the line. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The sheet extrusion line consists of the following components, each performing a specific function:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Hopper with volumetric/weight feeder** | Stores and meters the polymer granules |
| **Extruder (single-screw)** | Melts, mixes, and pressurizes the polymer |
| **Breaker plate + Screen pack** | Removes contaminants; creates back-pressure |
| **Gear pump (melt pump)** | Decouples extruder pulsation from die flow; improves gauge control |
| **Static mixer / Adapter** | Homogenizes melt temperature |
| **Coat-hanger die** | Distributes melt uniformly across the width |
| **Chill roll stack (3-roll)** | Cools, calibrates thickness, and imparts surface finish |
| **Air knife** | Removes surface air; improves heat transfer |
| **Edge trimmer** | Removes the high-edge-bead region |
| **Haul-off unit** | Pulls the sheet at a controlled velocity; sets DDR |
| **Thickness gauge (β / X-ray)** | Non-contact online measurement |
| **Corona / Flame treater** | Increases surface energy for printing/adhesion |
| **Slitter & cutter** | Converts wide sheet to required widths |
| **Stacking / winding unit** | Collects the finished product |

The polymer melt exits the die as a hot curtain, contacts the chrome-plated first chill roll, and is nipped between the roll stack where it solidifies. The haul-off pulls it forward at the calibrated line speed. The thickness gauge feeds back to the flex-lip actuators for **closed-loop gauge control**.

*(1 mark per major component discussed; 1 mark for overall process flow description; 1 mark for diagram referencing.)*

#### (b) — **A sheet extrusion line produces HDPE sheets of thickness 2.0 mm and width 1200 mm. The haul-off speed is 5 m/min. The density of HDPE is 950 kg/m³. The die gap is 3.0 mm. Calculate the volumetric throughput, mass throughput, draw-down ratio, and die-exit velocity. State clearly all assumptions. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Incompressible melt (ρ = constant)
2. No width shrinkage (neck-in negligible)
3. Steady-state, uniform flow
4. Die land length sufficient (no die swell correction needed)

**Calculations:**

$$
v_h = \frac{5}{60} = 0.0833 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
Q = W_s \cdot t_s \cdot v_h = 1.2 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.0833 = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 950 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-4} = 0.190 \text{ kg/s} = 11.4 \text{ kg/min}
$$

$$
DDR = \frac{h_d}{t_s} = \frac{3.0}{2.0} = 1.50
$$

$$
v_d = v_h \cdot \frac{t_s}{h_d} = 0.0833 \times \frac{2.0}{3.0} = 0.0556 \text{ m/s}
$$

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $v_h$ | 0.0833 m/s |
| $Q$ | 2.0 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s |
| $\dot{m}$ | 0.190 kg/s |
| $DDR$ | 1.50 |
| $v_d$ | 0.0556 m/s |

*(1 mark assumptions, 1 mark v_h conversion, 1.5 marks Q, 1 mark m-dot, 1.5 marks DDR, 1 mark v_d.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Modified)]

#### (a) — **With neat sketches, describe the different types of sheet dies used in polymer processing. Compare their flow distribution, residence time, and typical applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three principal die architectures are:

**1. T-die (Slit die):**
A simple rectangular channel with a constant cross-section. The melt enters through a central feed and flows symmetrically in two directions to reach the die exit.
- *Flow distribution:* Poor at the centre; the polymer stagnates near the centre and flows faster near the walls
- *Residence time:* Non-uniform; longer at the centre causes degradation
- *Applications:* Narrow sheets (< 1 m), low-viscosity polymers, low-cost applications

**2. Coat-hanger die:**
The manifold tapers from a circular inlet to a wide slit. The flow area decreases toward the ends, equalizing pressure.
- *Flow distribution:* Excellent; uniform gauge within ±2 %
- *Residence time:* Nearly uniform across the width
- *Applications:* The industry standard for wide sheets (> 4 m), packaging, and thermoforming stock

**3. Fishtail die:**
A wedge-shaped diverging channel; the flow area is symmetric about a central axis and increases linearly with distance from the feed.
- *Flow distribution:* Good for moderate widths; acceptable for low-viscosity polymers
- *Residence time:* Reasonable uniformity
- *Applications:* Wide sheets for calender feeds, certain film applications, lower-precision products

**4. Curtain die (specialty):**
A bottom-fed die where the melt emerges as a vertical curtain before being nipped onto a chill roll.
- *Flow distribution:* Good; used for coextrusion with high layer counts
- *Applications:* Multi-layer sheets, barrier packaging

**Comparison Table (relevant for 2 marks):**

| Property | T-die | Coat-hanger | Fishtail |
|---|---|---|---|
| Width range | < 1 m | up to 4–5 m | up to 3 m |
| Gauge uniformity | Poor | Excellent (±2 %) | Moderate |
| Residence-time uniformity | Poor | Excellent | Moderate |
| Cost | Low | High | Moderate |
| Typical use | Lab, narrow | Industrial standard | Calender feed |

*(1 mark per die type, 1 mark for sketch references, 1 mark for comparison table, 1 mark for application.)*

#### (b) — **Discuss the different cooling systems used in sheet extrusion. With a sketch, explain the 3-roll chill roll stack. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Cooling systems in sheet extrusion fall into three categories:

**1. Air cooling (immersion/forced):**
- Ambient or chilled air is blown across the sheet
- Suitable for thick sheets (≥ 6 mm) and polymers with low crystallization rate
- Slow line speed; very low cooling rate
- Used for acrylic and polycarbonate sheets

**2. Water bath / water cascade:**
- The sheet passes through a temperature-controlled water bath
- Higher heat-transfer coefficient than air
- Used for some HDPE and PP applications; less common for thin sheets

**3. Chill roll cooling (industry standard):**
- 2, 3, or 4 highly polished, internally water-cooled chrome-plated steel rolls
- Heat is extracted by conduction into the rolls
- The 3-roll stack is the most common

**3-Roll Chill Roll Stack — Sketch and Function:**

The three rolls are arranged in an "I" or "L" pattern. The melt curtain from the die enters the nip between Roll 1 and Roll 2, where it is nipped and cooled. It then wraps around Roll 2 and enters the second nip between Roll 2 and Roll 3. The sheet is cooled progressively as it travels around each roll.

- **Roll 1 (top):** Highly polished → imparts gloss
- **Roll 2 (middle):** Often polished or matte depending on product
- **Roll 3 (bottom):** Embossed or matte for texture/anti-block

**Process parameters:**
- Roll temperature: typically **30 – 90 °C** (set above dew point to avoid condensation marks)
- Roll diameter: 0.3 – 1.0 m
- Contact time: $\geq 5$ s for thin sheets; $20$–$30$ s for 2 mm HDPE
- Heat flux: 5 – 15 kW/m²

**Critical considerations:**
- The roll gap must match the desired sheet thickness within ±1 %
- Rolls must be parallel and synchronized at a uniform surface speed
- Air knives strip boundary-layer air and prevent entrapped bubbles
- For semi-crystalline polymers, crystallization should occur *on the roll* for flatness

*(2 marks for cooling categories, 3 marks for 3-roll stack description, 1 mark for sketch, 1 mark for parameters.)*

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam**

- ✅ A **sheet** has thickness **> 0.25 mm** but < 25 mm; **film** ≤ 0.25 mm; **plate** > 25 mm.
- ✅ The five major operations in sheet extrusion: **Melt → Distribute → Cool → Calibrate → Convert**.
- ✅ **Coat-hanger die** = industry standard; **T-die** = simple/narrow; **Fishtail** = calender feed.
- ✅ **Draw-down ratio** $DDR = h_d / t_s$ governs orientation, gauge, and neck-in.
- ✅ **Draw ratio** $DR = v_h / v_d$ — equal to DDR when neck-in is negligible.
- ✅ Throughput $Q = W_s t_s v_h$; Mass throughput $\dot{m} = \rho Q$.
- ✅ **Die land length** $L = 10$–$20 \times h_d$ to suppress extrudate swell.
- ✅ Convert **m/min to m/s** by dividing by 60 — examiners deduct a full mark if missed.
- ✅ **3-roll chill stack** is the standard cooling system; rolls are chrome-plated, internally water-cooled.
- ✅ Sheet must be cooled **below crystallization temperature** while still on rolls for flatness.
- ✅ Closed-loop **β-ray / X-ray gauge** controls thickness via flex-lip bolts.
- ✅ Applications: **thermoforming, packaging, construction panels, automotive trims, stationery, industrial liners**.
- ✅ Typical mass throughput for HDPE sheet: 10–20 kg/min on industrial lines.
- ✅ Polymers commonly sheet-extruded: **HDPE, LDPE, PP, PS, PVC, PET, PC, PMMA, ABS**.

> **End of Topic: Sheets (Module 2) — PCPOT503**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: FILAMENTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Filament**: A filament is a continuous, extremely long strand of polymeric material of finite cross-section (typically circular, but can be trilobal, hollow, or shaped) produced by forcing a viscous polymer melt or solution through a spinneret orifice, followed by solidification via cooling (melt spinning), evaporation of solvent (dry spinning), or precipitation in a non-solvent bath (wet spinning).

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, **filament manufacturing** is classified as a **continuous primary shaping process** (akin to extrusion) where the key distinguishing feature is that the product is a **single, endless fiber** rather than a discrete molded article. The unit of commerce is typically the **denier** (grams per 9000 meters) or **tex** (grams per 1000 meters).

> [!NOTE]
> **Spinneret**: A metal plate (typically stainless steel) containing precisely machined orifices (holes), analogous to a multi-hole die in conventional polymer extrusion. The spinneret determines filament count, cross-sectional shape, and initial diameter.

### 1.2 Classification of Filaments (Industry Standard)

| Type | Definition | Typical Diameter | Application Examples |
|------|-----------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Monofilament** | Single continuous strand from one spinneret hole | $0.1 \text{ mm}$ to $2 \text{ mm}$ | Fishing line, surgical sutures, bristles, brush filaments |
| **Multifilament** | Bundle of many fine filaments (typically 15–200) from a multi-hole spinneret | $5 \text{ μm}$ to $50 \text{ μm}$ per filament | Textile yarns, tire cord, industrial fabrics |
| **Tape/Yarn** | Flat or fibrillated polymer strip | Width $2–10 \text{ mm}$ | Woven sacks, carpet backing, geotextiles |
| **Staple Fiber** | Filament cut to short lengths ($20–150 \text{ mm}$) | Variable | Spun yarn, nonwovens, carpets |

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous Filament Yarn (CFY)** vs **Spun Yarn**: CFY is produced directly from spinnerets; spun yarn is made by twisting short staple fibers together. CFY generally exhibits superior tensile properties and uniformity.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **play-doh extruder gun** (the toy) with multiple small nozzles. As you push the handle, a continuous "rope" of play-doh emerges from each hole. Now imagine:
1. The play-doh is molten plastic (e.g., Nylon 6 at $260°\text{C}$).
2. The nozzle plate is a **spinneret** with $50$ tiny holes.
3. The room is a **chamber with cold air blowing** (the quench zone).
4. The play-doh solidifies mid-air and gets wound onto a bobbin at high speed.

**That entire process is filament spinning!**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why filaments are critical in polymer engineering**: They represent one of the highest-volume applications of synthetic polymers. Polyester, nylon, and polypropylene filaments collectively account for over **80 million metric tons/year** of global polymer consumption, making the spinning line the most economically important continuous processing equipment in the polymer industry.

### 1.4 Key Industrial Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Linear Density**: Measured in **denier** ($1 \text{ denier} = 1 \text{ g}/9000 \text{ m}$) or **tex** ($1 \text{ tex} = 1 \text{ g}/1000 \text{ m}$). $1 \text{ denier} = 0.1111 \text{ tex}$.
- **Tenacity**: Tensile strength expressed as **grams-force per denier (gpd)** or **cN/tex**.
- **Draw Ratio ($\lambda$)**: Ratio of final (drawn) linear velocity to initial (as-spun) linear velocity. Typical $\lambda = 3$ to $6$ for textile fibers.
- **Spin Draw Ratio (SDR)**: Ratio of take-up velocity to extrusion velocity at the spinneret face.
- **Birefringence ($\Delta n$)**: Optical measure of molecular orientation; increases with draw ratio.

### 1.5 Industrial Polymers Used for Filament Production

| Polymer | Abbreviation | Common Spinning Method | Key Application |
|---------|--------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Polyethylene terephthalate | **PET** | Melt spinning | Apparel, tire cord, bottle-grade (chips first) |
| Polyamide 6 / 6,6 | **PA-6 / PA-66** | Melt spinning | Hosiery, carpet, industrial cord |
| Polypropylene | **PP** | Melt spinning | Ropes, nonwovens, geotextiles |
| Polyacrylonitrile | **PAN** | Wet/Dry spinning (solution) | Carbon fiber precursor |
| Polyvinyl chloride | **PVC** | Dry spinning (solution) | Flame-resistant textiles |
| Cellulose acetate | **CA** | Dry spinning | Cigarette filters |
| Viscose rayon | — | Wet spinning | Apparel, hygiene |
| Aramid (Kevlar) | **PPTA** | Dry-jet wet spinning | Bulletproof vests, composites |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Principal Filament Spinning Processes

The classification of spinning processes is fundamentally based on the **physical state of the polymer** at the spinneret and the **solidification mechanism** downstream. This is a frequently tested concept in KTU Module 2.

#### 2.1.1 Melt Spinning
- **Polymer state at spinneret**: Molten (above melting point $T_m$).
- **Solidification mechanism**: Quenching in a cross-flow of cool air (typical quench air velocity: $0.3$ to $0.6 \text{ m/s}$).
- **No solvent recovery** required → most economical and environmentally clean.
- **Spin speeds**: Conventional $1000$–$4000 \text{ m/min}$; high-speed (POY) up to $6000 \text{ m/min}$.
- **Polymers**: PET, PA-6, PA-66, PP.

**Sequence**:
1. Polymer chips fed into extruder → melting zone ($T > T_m$).
2. Metering pump (gear pump) delivers constant throughput.
3. Filtration through **sand packs** or **metal filter media** (removes gels/spin finish residues).
4. Spinneret extrudes fine streams.
5. Quench chamber cools filaments below $T_g$ (glass transition) for amorphous polymers or $T_c$ (crystallization) for semi-crystalline.
6. Spin finish application (oiling).
7. Take-up winder.

#### 2.1.2 Dry Spinning
- **Polymer state at spinneret**: Dissolved in volatile solvent (e.g., PAN in DMF, PVC in DMF/THF).
- **Solidification mechanism**: Evaporation of solvent in a heated air column (spinning cell length: $4$–$8 \text{ m}$).
- **Solvent recovery** by adsorption/condensation is mandatory (cost-intensive).
- **Spin speeds**: $200$–$500 \text{ m/min}$ (slower than melt due to diffusion-limited drying).
- **Polymers**: PAN, PVC, cellulose acetate, spandex (segmented PU).

#### 2.1.3 Wet Spinning
- **Polymer state at spinneret**: Dissolved in non-volatile solvent.
- **Solidification mechanism**: Coagulation by **non-solvent** (chemical precipitation) in a coagulation bath.
- **Spinneret is submerged** in the bath.
- **Polymers**: Viscose rayon (cellulose xanthate in NaOH), PAN, aramid (some variants), acrylic.
- **Spin speeds**: Very low ($5$–$30 \text{ m/min}$) due to diffusion-limited coagulation.

#### 2.1.4 Dry-Jet Wet Spinning (Air Gap Spinning)
- **Hybrid process**: Polymer solution extruded into an **air gap** ($5$–$50 \text{ mm}$) before entering the coagulation bath.
- The air gap allows **molecular orientation** to develop under tension before coagulation locks in structure.
- **Polymers**: Aramid (Kevlar, Twaron), polybenzimidazole (PBI).
- Produces fibers with **exceptionally high modulus and tenacity** ($> 20 \text{ gpd}$).

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Process Physics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core physics**: Filament spinning is governed by the interplay of (a) **momentum transfer** (Newtonian/viscoelastic thinning), (b) **heat transfer** (cooling/solidification), and (c) **mass transfer** (solvent evaporation or diffusion). For melt spinning (simplest case), momentum and heat transfer dominate.

#### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Filament Formation

1. **Polymer preparation** → Homogeneous melt or solution with controlled rheology (melt viscosity $\eta$ typically $100$–$500 \text{ Pa·s}$ for PET at $285°\text{C}$).
2. **Metering** → Constant mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ delivered by gear pump (accuracy $\pm 0.5\%$).
3. **Filtration** → Removes particulate contaminants that would break filaments.
4. **Extrusion through spinneret** → Filaments emerge at velocity $V_0$.
5. **Attenuation (drawing-down)** → Filament velocity increases from $V_0$ to take-up velocity $V_L$. Diameter reduces from $D_0$ to $D_f$.
6. **Solidification** → Polymer transitions from liquid to solid (or gel to solid) state.
7. **Spin finish application** → Provides lubrication, antistatic, and cohesion properties.
8. **Winding** → Filaments wound on bobbins at controlled tension.

> [!NOTE]
> **Filtration media**: Stainless steel mesh or sintered metal powder (typically $10$–$40 \text{ μm}$ rated) is used as the final filter before the spinneret. Pressure drop is monitored — a rise indicates filter clogging (gel buildup).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS FOR KTU EXAM PREPARATION**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Linear density (denier) | $D = \dfrac{m}{L} \times 9000$ | denier | $m$ in grams, $L$ in meters |
| 2 | Linear density (tex) | $T = \dfrac{m}{L} \times 1000$ | tex | $m$ in grams, $L$ in meters |
| 3 | Tex-to-denier conversion | $1 \text{ denier} = 0.1111 \text{ tex}$ | — | $1 \text{ tex} = 9 \text{ denier}$ |
| 4 | Draw ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{V_f}{V_i} = \dfrac{A_i}{A_f} = \dfrac{D_i^2}{D_f^2}$ | dimensionless | $V$ = velocity, $A$ = cross-section |
| 5 | Spin draw ratio (SDR) | $\text{SDR} = \dfrac{V_L}{V_0}$ | dimensionless | $V_0$ = extrusion velocity at die |
| 6 | Mass flow per filament | $\dot{m}_{\text{fil}} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V$ | kg/s | $\rho$ = polymer density |
| 7 | Tenacity | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{T_{\text{tex}}}$ | N/tex or gpd | $F$ = breaking force |
| 8 | Elongation at break | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100\%$ | % | $L_f$ = length at break |
| 9 | Initial modulus (specific) | $E_{\text{sp}} = \dfrac{dF/d\varepsilon}{T_{\text{tex}}}$ | N/tex | Measure of stiffness |
| 10 | Birefringence (orientation) | $\Delta n = n_{\parallel} - n_{\perp}$ | dimensionless | $\Delta n \uparrow$ → orientation $\uparrow$ |
| 11 | Volume flow through spinneret | $Q = \dfrac{\pi D_0^4 \Delta P}{128 \eta L}$ | m³/s | Hagen-Poiseuille (for solution) |
| 12 | Filament diameter (as-spun) | $D_f = D_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{V_0}{V_L}}$ | m | $D_0$ = spinneret hole diameter |
| 13 | Crystallinity index | $X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_f^0} \times 100\%$ | % | From DSC analysis |
| 14 | Mass balance (filament line) | $\dot{m} = \rho_s A_s V_s = \rho_f A_f V_f$ | kg/s | Continuity equation |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Tire cord**: High-tenacity PET or PA-66 filament (tenacity $> 8 \text{ gpd}$) used as reinforcement in radial tires. Lower rolling resistance → better fuel economy.
- **Bulletproof vests**: Aramid (Kevlar) filaments produced by dry-jet wet spinning offer tenacity $> 22 \text{ gpd}$ and modulus $> 500 \text{ gpd}$.
- **Carbon fiber precursor**: PAN filaments are oxidized ($200$–$300°\text{C}$) and carbonized ($1000$–$1500°\text{C}$) to yield carbon fibers for aerospace composites.
- **Geotextiles**: Polypropylene tape yarns provide high tensile strength at low cost for soil reinforcement.
- **Surgical sutures**: Monofilaments of PA-66, PP, or PGA offer smooth passage through tissue.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Filament Diameter from Mass Throughput

**Problem statement**: A melt spinning line produces PA-6 filaments. The polymer melt density at the spinneret is $\rho = 950 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The spinneret has $N = 34$ holes, each of diameter $D_0 = 0.25 \text{ mm}$. The total mass throughput is $\dot{m} = 12 \text{ g/min}$. The take-up velocity is $V_L = 1000 \text{ m/min}$.

Find: (i) Extrusion velocity $V_0$ at the spinneret, (ii) Spin draw ratio (SDR), (iii) Final filament diameter $D_f$.

**Given data**:
- $\rho = 950 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 950 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/cm}^3 = 0.95 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $N = 34$ (number of holes)
- $D_0 = 0.25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ cm}$
- $\dot{m} = 12 \text{ g/min}$
- $V_L = 1000 \text{ m/min} = 10^5 \text{ cm/min}$

**Step 1**: Calculate total cross-sectional area of spinneret holes.
$$A_0 = N \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_0^2}{4}$$
$$A_0 = 34 \times \dfrac{\pi \times (0.025 \text{ cm})^2}{4}$$
$$A_0 = 34 \times \dfrac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}^2}{4}$$
$$A_0 = 34 \times 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}^2$$
$$A_0 = 1.6690 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2**: Calculate extrusion velocity $V_0$ from continuity equation.
$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_0 \cdot V_0$$
$$V_0 = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho \cdot A_0}$$
$$V_0 = \dfrac{12 \text{ g/min}}{(0.95 \text{ g/cm}^3)(1.6690 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm}^2)}$$
$$V_0 = \dfrac{12}{1.5856 \times 10^{-2}} \text{ cm/min}$$
$$V_0 = 756.85 \text{ cm/min} = 7.5685 \text{ m/min}$$

**Step 3**: Calculate Spin Draw Ratio (SDR).
$$\text{SDR} = \dfrac{V_L}{V_0} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ m/min}}{7.5685 \text{ m/min}} = 132.13$$

This is unusually high. **Interpretation**: A high SDR is characteristic of **high-speed melt spinning** where the filament undergoes massive attenuation between spinneret and winder.

**Step 4**: Calculate final filament diameter.
$$D_f = D_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{V_0}{V_L}} = D_0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\text{SDR}}}$$
$$D_f = 0.25 \text{ mm} \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{132.13}}$$
$$D_f = 0.25 \text{ mm} \times \dfrac{1}{11.495}$$
$$D_f = 0.25 \text{ mm} \times 0.08699$$
$$D_f = 0.02175 \text{ mm} = 21.75 \text{ μm}$$

**Sanity check**: Final denier per filament?
$$\text{Linear density per filament} = \rho \cdot A_f \cdot L = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_f^2}{4} \cdot 9000 \text{ m}$$

In consistent units: $D_f = 21.75 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$, $\rho = 950 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 0.95 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $L = 9000 \text{ m} = 9 \times 10^5 \text{ cm}$.
$$\text{denier/filament} = 0.95 \times \dfrac{\pi (21.75 \times 10^{-4})^2}{4} \times 9 \times 10^5$$
$$= 0.95 \times 3.715 \times 10^{-6} \times 9 \times 10^5$$
$$= 0.95 \times 3.344 = 3.18 \text{ denier/filament}$$

For $34$ filaments: $34 \times 3.18 = 108 \text{ denier}$ total. ✓ Realistic for fine textile yarn.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Draw Ratio Effect on Filament Properties (Semi-Quantitative)

**Problem statement**: PET filaments are produced by melt spinning. A filament with initial diameter $D_i = 200 \text{ μm}$ is drawn to a final diameter $D_f = 50 \text{ μm}$. Calculate (i) draw ratio, (ii) final linear density given initial denier = $35$, (iii) % reduction in cross-section.

**Step 1**: Draw ratio from diameter.
$$\lambda = \left(\dfrac{D_i}{D_f}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{200}{50}\right)^2 = (4)^2 = 16$$

Wait — this uses the **diameter-squared relationship** derived from volume conservation.

**Derivation of this relationship**:
$$V_i = V_f \text{ (incompressible)}$$
$$A_i L_i = A_f L_f$$
$$A_i / A_f = L_f / L_i = \lambda$$
$$\dfrac{\pi D_i^2/4}{\pi D_f^2/4} = \lambda$$
$$\left(\dfrac{D_i}{D_f}\right)^2 = \lambda$$

Hence: $D_f = D_i / \sqrt{\lambda}$.

**Step 2**: Final linear density from draw ratio.
$$\dfrac{T_f}{T_i} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$$
$$T_f = \dfrac{T_i}{\lambda} = \dfrac{35}{16} = 2.1875 \text{ denier}$$

In tex: $T_f = 2.1875 \times 0.1111 = 0.243 \text{ tex}$ per filament.

**Step 3**: Cross-section reduction.
$$\% \text{ reduction} = \dfrac{A_i - A_f}{A_i} \times 100\%$$
$$= \left(1 - \dfrac{A_f}{A_i}\right) \times 100\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\right) \times 100\%$$
$$= \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{16}\right) \times 100\% = 93.75\%$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Hagen-Poiseuille Flow Through Spinneret (Solution Spinning)

**For solution spinning (wet/dry)**, the polymer solution is a viscous fluid obeying laminar flow. The pressure-flow relation through a single cylindrical hole is:

$$Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$$

where $R$ is the hole radius, $\Delta P$ is the pressure drop across the spinneret, $\eta$ is the solution viscosity, and $L$ is the hole length.

**Step-by-step derivation** (cylindrical Poiseuille):

For a Newtonian fluid in steady, fully-developed laminar flow in a pipe, the Navier-Stokes equation reduces to:
$$\dfrac{1}{r} \dfrac{d}{dr} \left(r \dfrac{dv_z}{dr}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\eta} \dfrac{dP}{dz}$$

Integrate once:
$$r \dfrac{dv_z}{dr} = \dfrac{r^2}{2\eta} \dfrac{dP}{dz} + C_1$$

Apply boundary condition (no-slip, $v_z(R) = 0$):
$$C_1 = -\dfrac{R^2}{2\eta} \dfrac{dP}{dz}$$

Substituting and integrating again:
$$v_z(r) = \dfrac{1}{4\eta} \dfrac{dP}{dz} (r^2 - R^2)$$

Volumetric flow rate:
$$Q = \int_0^R v_z \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \dfrac{\pi R^4}{8\eta} \left(-\dfrac{dP}{dz}\right)$$

Since $dP/dz \approx -\Delta P/L$ (linear pressure drop along the hole of length $L$):
$$Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L}$$

For $N$ holes: $Q_{\text{total}} = N \times Q$.

**Numerical example**: PAN solution (15 wt%) is wet-spun through a spinneret with $N = 1000$ holes of diameter $D = 0.08 \text{ mm}$ ($R = 40 \text{ μm} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$), length $L = 0.3 \text{ mm} = 3 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$, viscosity $\eta = 50 \text{ Pa·s}$, pressure $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$.

**Step 1**: Single-hole flow rate.
$$Q = \dfrac{\pi (4 \times 10^{-5})^4 (5 \times 10^5)}{8 \times 50 \times (3 \times 10^{-4})}$$
$$= \dfrac{\pi \times 2.56 \times 10^{-17} \times 5 \times 10^5}{8 \times 50 \times 3 \times 10^{-4}}$$
$$= \dfrac{\pi \times 1.28 \times 10^{-11}}{0.12}$$
$$= \dfrac{4.021 \times 10^{-11}}{0.12} = 3.351 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2**: Total volumetric flow rate.
$$Q_{\text{total}} = 1000 \times 3.351 \times 10^{-10} = 3.351 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3**: Convert to mass flow rate ($\rho_{\text{soln}} \approx 1100 \text{ kg/m}^3$).
$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q_{\text{total}} = 1100 \times 3.351 \times 10^{-7} = 3.686 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s} = 22.1 \text{ g/min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rheological complication**: PAN and most polymer solutions are **non-Newtonian** (shear-thinning). The simple Hagen-Poiseuille law overestimates flow. A **power-law correction** is used: $Q = \dfrac{n \pi R^3}{(3n+1)} \left(\dfrac{R \Delta P}{2 L K}\right)^{1/n}$, where $n$ is the power-law index and $K$ is the consistency index.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Spinning Processes

| Parameter | Melt Spinning | Dry Spinning | Wet Spinning | Dry-Jet Wet |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|--------------|-------------|
| Polymer at die | Molten | Solution | Solution | Solution |
| Solidification | Cooling | Evaporation | Coagulation | Air-gap + coagulation |
| Spin speed (m/min) | $1000$–$6000$ | $200$–$500$ | $5$–$30$ | $50$–$200$ |
| Solvent recovery | None | Required | Required (partial) | Required |
| Filament fineness (denier/fil) | $1$–$10$ | $1$–$5$ | $1$–$15$ | $1$–$5$ |
| Tenacity (gpd) | $4$–$9$ | $2$–$5$ | $2$–$6$ | $15$–$30$ |
| Modulus (gpd) | $30$–$120$ | $20$–$50$ | $30$–$80$ | $300$–$1000$ |
| Capital cost | High | Very high | Medium-high | Very high |
| Operating cost | Low | High | Medium | High |
| Examples | PET, PA, PP | PAN, PVC, CA | Viscose, PAN | Aramid, PBI |

### 3.5 Mass Balance on a Filament Line (Melt Spinning)

For steady-state operation, mass in = mass out:
$$\dot{m}_{\text{pump}} = \dot{m}_{\text{winder}}$$

Across the spinneret: $\dot{m} = \rho_{\text{melt}} \cdot A_0 \cdot V_0$ (melt side).
At the winder: $\dot{m} = \rho_{\text{as-spun}} \cdot A_f \cdot V_L$ (solid side).

Equating:
$$\rho_m A_0 V_0 = \rho_s A_f V_L$$

Note: $\rho_m < \rho_s$ for polymers (density increases on solidification due to crystallization and reduced free volume). The relation therefore modifies the simple draw ratio formula. A more general form:
$$\dfrac{V_L}{V_0} = \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_s} \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} = \dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho_s} \cdot \text{SDR}_{\text{geometric}}$$

For PET: $\rho_m \approx 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$ at $285°\text{C}$, $\rho_s \approx 1.38 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (fully crystallized) or $1.34 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (amorphous). The ratio $\rho_m / \rho_s \approx 0.87$–$0.91$ — a small but significant correction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Generic Filament Spinning Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Hopper1["Polymer<br/>Hopper"] --> Extr1["Extruder<br/>Melt Zone"]
    Extr1 --> Meter1["Metering<br/>Pump"]
    Meter1 --> Filt1["Filter<br/>Pack"]
    Filt1 --> Spin1["Spinneret<br/>Multi-Hole Die"]
    Spin1 --> Qch1["Quench<br/>Chamber"]
    Qch1 --> Fin1["Spin Finish<br/>Applicator"]
    Fin1 --> Drw1["Drawing<br/>Godet Roll"]
    Drw1 --> Htr1["Heat<br/>Set Zone"]
    Htr1 --> Int1["Interlacing<br/>Jet"]
    Int1 --> Tn1["Tension<br/>Control"]
    Tn1 --> Wnd1["Take-Up<br/>Winder"]
    Wnd1 --> Bob1["Finished<br/>Bobbin"]
    style Hopper1 fill:#E8F4F8,stroke:#1F4E79
    style Spin1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#B8860B
    style Qch1 fill:#E0F8E0,stroke:#228B22
    style Drw1 fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#8B0000
    style Wnd1 fill:#E6E0F8,stroke:#4B0082
    style Bob1 fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#696969
```

**Functional Annotation**:
- **Extruder**: Melts and homogenizes polymer; $T = 240°\text{C}$ to $290°\text{C}$ depending on polymer.
- **Metering Pump**: Positive-displacement gear pump; throughput $1$–$200 \text{ kg/h}$ per position.
- **Filter Pack**: Sand + sintered metal; $10$–$40 \text{ μm}$ rating.
- **Spinneret**: $5$–$5000$ holes; hole diameter $0.1$–$0.5 \text{ mm}$.
- **Quench Chamber**: Cross-flow or radial-flow air; $T_{\text{air}} = 15$–$25°\text{C}$; RH $< 65\%$.
- **Draw Godet**: Heated/cooled rolls; rotates at $V_2 > V_1$ to impose draw.
- **Interlacing Jet**: Compressed air tangles filaments to prevent filamentation (separation).

### 4.2 Schematic: Comparison of the Four Spinning Processes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Melt["MELT SPINNING"]
        M1["Polymer Melt<br/>T greater than Tm"] -->|Extrude| M2["Spinneret"]
        M2 -->|Cool in air| M3["Quench<br/>Chamber"]
        M3 -->|Solidify| M4["Filament Bundle"]
    end
    subgraph Dry["DRY SPINNING"]
        D1["Polymer<br/>Solution"] -->|Extrude| D2["Spinneret"]
        D2 -->|Hot air| D3["Evaporation<br/>Cell"]
        D3 -->|Solvent out| D4["Filament Bundle"]
    end
    subgraph Wet["WET SPINNING"]
        W1["Polymer<br/>Solution"] -->|Extrude| W2["Spinneret<br/>Submerged"]
        W2 -->|Non-solvent| W3["Coagulation<br/>Bath"]
        W3 -->|Precipitate| W4["Filament Bundle"]
    end
    subgraph DJWS["DRY-JET WET SPINNING"]
        J1["Polymer<br/>Solution"] -->|Extrude| J2["Spinneret"]
        J2 -->|Air gap 5 to 50 mm| J3["Air<br/>Gap"]
        J3 -->|Coagulate| J4["Coagulation<br/>Bath"]
        J4 -->|Solidify| J5["High-Modulus<br/>Filament"]
    end
    style M1 fill:#FFD580
    style D1 fill:#A0E0E0
    style W1 fill:#B0E0A0
    style J1 fill:#FFA0A0
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Spinneret Hole Geometry Variations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SH["SPINNERET HOLE SHAPES"]
        A["Circular<br/>Hole<br/>Round Filament"] --> B["Trilobal<br/>Hole<br/>Triangular Filament"]
        B --> C["Hollow<br/>Ring<br/>Hollow Filament"]
        C --> D["Slot<br/>Hole<br/>Tape Yarn"]
    end
    style A fill:#FFE0E0
    style B fill:#E0FFE0
    style C fill:#E0E0FF
    style D fill:#FFF0E0
```

**Cross-Sectional Filament Shapes Produced**:
- **Circular** (standard): Used for apparel, hosiery.
