- **Trilobal**: Three-lobed cross-section from Y-shaped hole; higher luster, better soil-hiding → carpets.
- **Hollow**: Used for thermal insulation (sleeping bag filling).
- **Flat/Tape**: Slit extrusion of PP film; used for woven sacks.

### 4.4 Process Flow: From Polymer Chip to Filament Yarn

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Polymer<br/>Chips / Powder"] --> B["Drying<br/>(for hygroscopic<br/>polymers)"]
    B --> C["Melt<br/>Extrusion"]
    C --> D["Filtration"]
    D --> E["Spinning"]
    E --> F["Quench<br/>Cooling"]
    F --> G["Spin<br/>Finish"]
    G --> H{"Drawing<br/>Required?"}
    H -->|Yes| I["Cold Draw<br/>+ Hot Draw"]
    H -->|No POY| J["Winding<br/>POY"]
    I --> K["Heat<br/>Setting"]
    K --> L["Winding<br/>FDY"]
    J --> M["Drawn Textured<br/>Yarn DTY"]
    style A fill:#F8E0E0
    style E fill:#FFE080
    style K fill:#80C0FF
    style L fill:#80FF80
    style M fill:#C080FF
```

**Yarn Type Codes (Industry Standard)**:
- **POY** (Partially Oriented Yarn): Spin speed $3000$–$4000 \text{ m/min}$; low orientation; needs further drawing.
- **FDY** (Fully Drawn Yarn): Spin-draw integrated; $5000$–$6000 \text{ m/min}$; ready for textile use.
- **DTY** (Draw Textured Yarn): POY + false-twist texturing for bulked yarn.
- **HSO** (High Speed Oriented): Similar to POY.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**"Differentiate between monofilament and multifilament yarns with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

| Aspect | Monofilament | Multifilament |
|--------|--------------|---------------|
| Definition | Single continuous strand from one spinneret hole | Bundle of many fine filaments (15–200) from a multi-hole spinneret |
| Diameter | Thick ($0.1$–$2 \text{ mm}$) | Fine ($5$–$50 \text{ μm}$ per filament) |
| Flexibility | Stiff | Soft, drapable |
| Examples | Fishing line, surgical sutures, brush bristles | Textile yarn, hosiery, tire cord |
| Production rate | Lower | Higher |

*(Award 1 mark for each correct row; 3 rows = 3 marks.)*

#### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**"What is a spinneret? List any two types of spinning processes."**

**Model Answer** (3 marks):

**Spinneret (2 marks)**: A spinneret is a precision-engineered metal plate (typically stainless steel) containing one or more precisely drilled holes (orifices). It serves as the die through which molten polymer or polymer solution is extruded to form filaments. The number, size, and shape of holes determine the filament count, linear density, and cross-sectional shape. Spinneret hole diameters typically range from $0.1$ to $0.5 \text{ mm}$.

**Two spinning processes (1 mark)**:
1. **Melt spinning** — Used for thermoplastic polymers (PET, PA, PP) above their melting point.
2. **Wet spinning** — Used for polymers dissolved in non-volatile solvents (viscose, PAN).

*(Any two of: melt, dry, wet, dry-jet wet, gel, emulsion, reaction spinning.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **melt spinning process** for the production of polyester (PET) filaments. Discuss the role of the metering pump, spinneret, and quench chamber. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PET melt spinning line has the following specifications:
- Spinneret holes: $N = 48$, hole diameter $D_0 = 0.30 \text{ mm}$
- Melt density at spinneret: $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- Take-up velocity: $V_L = 4000 \text{ m/min}$
- Mass throughput: $\dot{m} = 25 \text{ g/min}$

Calculate: (i) Extrusion velocity $V_0$, (ii) Spin Draw Ratio, (iii) Final filament diameter $D_f$, (iv) Final denier per filament. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Melt Spinning of PET** (7 marks):

**Step 1 — Polymer preparation (1 mark)**: PET chips (intrinsic viscosity $\sim 0.65 \text{ dL/g}$) are dried in a hopper drier at $160°\text{C}$ for $4$–$6$ hours to reduce moisture below $50 \text{ ppm}$ (critical: moisture causes hydrolytic degradation of PET, reducing molecular weight).

**Step 2 — Extruder (1 mark)**: Dried chips are fed into a single-screw extruder (or twin-screw for higher IV). The screw melts and conveys the polymer. Typical barrel temperatures: feed zone $270°\text{C}$, compression zone $285°\text{C}$, metering zone $290°\text{C}$. A nitrogen blanket prevents oxidative degradation.

**Step 3 — Metering pump (1.5 marks)**: A precision gear pump (melt pump) downstream of the extruder provides a constant, pulse-free volumetric flow rate independent of extruder pressure fluctuations. Typical flow rate: $5$–$200 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$; rotation speed: $10$–$60 \text{ RPM}$. This is the heart of dimensional uniformity.

**Step 4 — Filtration and Spinneret (1.5 marks)**: Polymer passes through a filter pack (sand + sintered metal, $15$–$25 \text{ μm}$ rating) to remove gels and carbonized particles. The spinneret is a stainless steel plate ($50$–$80 \text{ mm}$ diameter) with hundreds of precisely drilled holes (capillary shape: conical inlet, cylindrical land of length $L = 2D_0$ to $4D_0$).

**Step 5 — Quench chamber (1.5 marks)**: Filaments emerging from the spinneret pass through a quench chamber where conditioned air at $18$–$22°\text{C}$ and $50$–$70\%$ RH is blown **cross-flow** (or **radial flow** in newer designs) to cool the filaments below the glass transition temperature $T_g$ of PET ($80°\text{C}$). The air velocity ($0.3$–$0.6 \text{ m/s}$) is critical — too fast causes filament vibration and breakage; too slow causes interfilament sticking.

**Step 6 — Finish and winding (0.5 marks)**: Spin finish (lubricant + antistatic) is applied via a kiss roll or metered applicator. Filaments converge into a yarn, pass through a tension-controlled godet, and are wound on a high-speed winder.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution** (7 marks):

**Given**:
- $N = 48$, $D_0 = 0.30 \text{ mm} = 0.03 \text{ cm}$, $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$
- $V_L = 4000 \text{ m/min} = 4 \times 10^5 \text{ cm/min}$
- $\dot{m} = 25 \text{ g/min}$

**Step 1**: Total spinneret cross-section.
$$A_0 = N \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_0^2}{4} = 48 \times \dfrac{\pi \times (0.03)^2}{4} = 48 \times 7.0686 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$A_0 = 3.3929 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 2**: Extrusion velocity (1.5 marks).
$$V_0 = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\rho_m \cdot A_0} = \dfrac{25 \text{ g/min}}{1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3 \times 3.3929 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm}^2}$$
$$V_0 = \dfrac{25}{4.0715 \times 10^{-2}} = 614.0 \text{ cm/min} = 6.14 \text{ m/min}$$

**Step 3**: Spin Draw Ratio (1.5 marks).
$$\text{SDR} = \dfrac{V_L}{V_0} = \dfrac{4000 \text{ m/min}}{6.14 \text{ m/min}} = 651.5$$

**Step 4**: Final filament diameter (2 marks).
$$D_f = D_0 \sqrt{\dfrac{V_0}{V_L}} = 0.30 \text{ mm} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{6.14}{4000}}$$
$$= 0.30 \times \sqrt{1.535 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.30 \times 3.918 \times 10^{-2}$$
$$D_f = 1.175 \times 10^{-2} \text{ mm} = 11.75 \text{ μm}$$

**Step 5**: Final denier per filament (2 marks).
$$\text{denier/filament} = \rho_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_f^2}{4} \cdot 9000 \text{ m}$$

Using $\rho_s$ (solid PET) $= 1.38 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $D_f = 11.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}$, $L = 9 \times 10^5 \text{ cm}$:
$$= 1.38 \times \dfrac{\pi (11.75 \times 10^{-4})^2}{4} \times 9 \times 10^5$$
$$= 1.38 \times \dfrac{\pi \times 1.3806 \times 10^{-6}}{4} \times 9 \times 10^5$$
$$= 1.38 \times 1.0842 \times 10^{-6} \times 9 \times 10^5$$
$$= 1.38 \times 0.9758 = 1.347 \text{ denier/filament}$$

Total yarn denier $= 48 \times 1.347 = 64.66$ denier.

**Final Answer**:
- (i) $V_0 = 6.14 \text{ m/min}$
- (ii) $\text{SDR} = 651.5$
- (iii) $D_f = 11.75 \text{ μm}$
- (iv) $1.35$ denier/filament ($64.7$ denier total)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** Compare **melt spinning, dry spinning, and wet spinning** processes in terms of polymer state at spinneret, solidification mechanism, typical spin speed, and suitable polymers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a flow diagram, explain the **dry-jet wet spinning process** for aramid (Kevlar) filaments. Why is the air gap critical? What are the unique properties of aramid filaments produced by this method? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Comparative Table** (7 marks):

| Feature | Melt Spinning | Dry Spinning | Wet Spinning |
|---------|---------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Polymer state** | Molten above $T_m$ | Solution in volatile solvent | Solution in non-volatile solvent |
| **Solidification** | Cooling in air (quench) | Evaporation of solvent | Coagulation in non-solvent bath |
| **Spin speed** | $1000$–$6000 \text{ m/min}$ | $200$–$500 \text{ m/min}$ | $5$–$30 \text{ m/min}$ |
| **Solvent recovery** | Not required | Mandatory (adsorption/condensation) | Partial (coagulation bath recycling) |
| **Capital cost** | High | Very high (closed-loop solvent system) | Medium-high |
| **Examples** | PET, PA-6, PA-66, PP | PAN, PVC, cellulose acetate | Viscose, PAN (some), acrylic |
| **Final properties** | Tenacity $4$–$9 \text{ gpd}$ | Tenacity $2$–$5 \text{ gpd}$ | Tenacity $2$–$6 \text{ gpd}$ |
| **Environmental** | Cleanest | Solvent emission concerns | Effluent from bath |

*(1 mark per row for 6 rows; 1 mark for an example pair.)*

---

**(b) Dry-Jet Wet Spinning of Aramid (Kevlar)** (7 marks):

**Process Description** (4 marks):

1. **Polymer solution preparation**: Poly-para-phenylene terephthalamide (PPTA) is dissolved in concentrated sulfuric acid ($100\% \text{ H}_2\text{SO}_4$) at $20$ wt% solids. The solution is **lyotropic liquid crystalline** (exhibits ordered domains even in solution due to rigid rod-like molecular conformation).

2. **Extrusion**: The dope is extruded through a spinneret (typically $100$–$1000$ holes, $0.05$–$0.10 \text{ mm}$ diameter) at $50$–$80°\text{C}$.

3. **Air gap** ($5$–$50 \text{ mm}$): Filaments traverse an air gap before entering the cold water coagulation bath. This is the defining feature.

4. **Coagulation bath**: Cold water ($0$–$5°\text{C}$) dilutes the sulfuric acid, precipitating the polymer.

5. **Washing, neutralization, drying, winding**.

**Why the air gap is critical** (2 marks):

- The lyotropic liquid crystalline solution is already **partially oriented**; in the air gap, the elongational flow aligns the rod-like PPTA molecules along the filament axis.
- The **low-viscosity air** allows elongational deformation under spin-line tension without premature solidification.
- This pre-orientation, frozen in by subsequent coagulation, gives the aramid its extraordinary anisotropic properties: longitudinal modulus $> 500 \text{ gpd}$ but very weak transversely.

**Unique properties of aramid (Kevlar) filaments** (1 mark):
- Tenacity: $22$–$28 \text{ gpd}$ (vs $5$–$8 \text{ gpd}$ for PET)
- Modulus: $500$–$1000 \text{ gpd}$
- Density: $1.44 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (lightweight)
- Thermal stability: Decomposes only at $500°\text{C}$
- Applications: Bulletproof vests, brake linings, aerospace composites, cut-resistant gloves.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Unit inconsistency**: When calculating $V_0$, students frequently mix CGS and SI units (e.g., $D_0$ in mm and $\dot{m}$ in g/min) without conversion. Always convert all quantities to the **same unit system** before substitution. **Expected loss: 1–2 marks per part.**
> 2. **Confusing SDR with Draw Ratio**: SDR = $V_L/V_0$ (spinneret to winder). Draw Ratio = $V_{\text{after}} / V_{\text{before}}$ in the drawing zone. They are **different**.
> 3. **Forgetting density change**: Solid polymer density $\rho_s >$ melt density $\rho_m$. A 10% correction. For exact answers, use $\rho_s$ in final-diameter calculation, not $\rho_m$.
> 4. **Spinneret vs Die terminology**: In KTU exams, "die" and "spinneret" are sometimes used interchangeably, but **spinneret is the correct term for fiber extrusion**; "die" is reserved for film/profile/sheet extrusion.
> 5. **Melt spinning vs Extrusion**: A spinneret is essentially a multi-hole extrusion die, but the **downstream solidification is the key distinguishing feature**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2: Filaments**

- [ ] **Filament** = continuous polymer strand from a spinneret; classified as **monofilament** (single thick) or **multifilament** (bundle of fine).
- [ ] **Four spinning processes**: **M**elt, **D**ry, **W**et, **D**ry-jet **W**et (memory aid: **MDWD**).
- [ ] **Melt spinning** is used for **PET, PA, PP** — no solvent, fastest, most economical.
- [ ] **Dry spinning** uses volatile solvent (e.g., PAN/DMF); solvent recovery is essential.
- [ ] **Wet spinning** uses non-volatile solvent + coagulation bath; slowest process; used for **viscose, acrylic**.
- [ ] **Dry-jet wet** = high-performance fibers (**Kevlar, Twaron**); air gap allows pre-orientation.
- [ ] **Spinneret** = multi-hole metal die; hole diameter $0.1$–$0.5 \text{ mm}$; number of holes $5$–$5000$.
- [ ] **Denier** = mass (g) per $9000 \text{ m}$ of yarn; $1 \text{ denier} = 0.1111 \text{ tex}$.
- [ ] **Tenacity** is in **gpd** (grams-force per denier); high-tenacity = $> 6 \text{ gpd}$.
- [ ] **Draw ratio** $\lambda = (D_i/D_f)^2 = V_f/V_i$ for incompressible material.
- [ ] **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for solution spinning: $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta P / (8 \eta L)$.
- [ ] **POY** (Partially Oriented Yarn) at $3000$–$4000 \text{ m/min}$ needs further drawing.
- [ ] **FDY** (Fully Drawn Yarn) is spin-draw integrated at $5000$–$6000 \text{ m/min}$ — ready to use.
- [ ] **Drying of PET** before melt spinning is **mandatory** ($< 50 \text{ ppm}$ moisture) to prevent hydrolysis.
- [ ] **Quench air** conditions ($T = 18$–$22°\text{C}$, RH $< 70\%$, velocity $0.3$–$0.6 \text{ m/s}$) are critical.
- [ ] **Spin finish** (oiling) provides lubrication, antistatic, cohesion — applied between quench and drawing.
- [ ] **Birefringence** $\Delta n$ is the optical measure of molecular orientation; increases with draw ratio.
- [ ] **Carbon fiber precursor**: PAN filaments → oxidative stabilization ($200$–$300°\text{C}$) → carbonization ($1000$–$1500°\text{C}$).
- [ ] **Industrial hierarchy**: Nylon 6 was the first **fully synthetic fiber** (Carothers, DuPont, 1935).
- [ ] **Tire cord grade PET** has tenacity $> 8 \text{ gpd}$ and shrinkage $< 4\%$ — used in radial tire belts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exam** — "**Melt Dry Wet Jet**":
> - **M**elt: M = Mass (no solvent, just molten mass).
> - **D**ry: D = Dry (solvent evaporates to dry the fiber).
> - **W**et: W = Water (coagulation bath of water/non-solvent).
> - **J**et: J = Jet of air gap before wet bath.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industrial Insight**: The global filament market is dominated by **PET (~$50 \text{ billion/year}$)**, **PA-6 and PA-66 (~$25 \text{ billion/year}$)**, and **PP (~$15 \text{ billion/year}$)**. The single most important engineering metric is **tenacity-to-cost ratio**, which is why PET has displaced nylon in tire cord and apparel.

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam-Specific Reminder**: KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 frequently tests:
> 1. Comparison of spinning processes (table format, 7 marks).
> 2. Numerical on draw ratio, SDR, filament diameter, and denier (7 marks).
> 3. Schematic/process flow diagram with labeling (5 marks).
> 4. Definitions of monofilament, multifilament, spinneret, POY/FDY (2–3 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lamination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Lamination # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition > **Lamination** is a polymer processing technique in which two or more layers of substrate materials — such as polymer films, paper, aluminium foils, fabrics, or non-wovens — are permanently bonded together using **adhesives, heat, pressure, or a combination of all three**, to produce a single multilayer composite web (laminate) possessing combined functional, mechanical, barrier, and aesthetic properties superior to those of the individual substrates. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, lamination is classified under **secondary polymer conversion processes**, distinct from primary processes (extrusion, injection moulding, blow moulding) and tertiary processes (printing, welding). It is a *web-handling* operation governed by **tension control, nip mechanics, and adhesive rheology**. ### 1.2 Sub-Classification of Lamination Lamination is broadly classified based on the **bonding medium** used: | Sub-Type | Bonding Mechanism | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Adhesive Lamination** | Chemical bonding via wet/solvent adhesive | Flexible food packaging | | **Extrusion Lamination** | Molten polymer acts as tie-layer | Tetra-pak, multilayer pouches | | **Extrusion Coating** | Polymer melt coated on substrate | Paperboard cartons, milk packs | | **Hot-Melt Lamination** | Thermoplastic adhesive (wax, EVA) reactivated by heat | Labels, hygiene products | | **Thermal (Heat) Lamination** | Pressure + heat only, no adhesive | ID cards, document lamination | | **Pressure-Sensitive Lamination** | Tackified adhesive at room temperature | Decorative films, vinyl wraps | | **Wax Lamination** | Paraffin/wax-based bonding | Corrugated board, cheese wrap | ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] Conceptual Analogy > Think of lamination as **making a "polymer sandwich"**: > - The **bread slices** = substrate layers (films/foils/paper) > - The **butter/cheese spread** = the adhesive or tie-layer > - The **hand pressing** = the nip rolls applying pressure > - The **warm pan** = heated rollers > > Just as a sandwich becomes stronger, more flavorful, and structurally integrated when its layers are pressed and bonded, lamination gives a multilayer film **synergistic properties** — e.g., PET (strength) + Al foil (gas barrier) + PE (heat seal) = a single laminate capable of protecting pharmaceutical products for years. ## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics in Lamination > [!IMPORTANT] Critical Process Metrics > - **Nip Pressure:** typically **10 – 60 N/mm** of web width > - **Line Speed:** **50 – 600 m/min** (industrial scale) > - **Adhesive Coat Weight:** **1.5 – 5.0 g/m² (dry weight)** > - **Lamination Temperature:** **60 – 320 °C** (process-dependent) > - **Web Tension:** **10 – 200 N/m** of web width > - **Cure Time (solventless):** **24 – 72 hours** at room temperature ## 1.5 Why Lamination is Critical in Polymer Engineering Lamination is the **only viable route** to combine **dissimilar materials** (e.g., paper + aluminium + PE) that cannot be co-extruded due to differing melting points, melt viscosities, or chemical incompatibilities. It enables engineered products with: - **Barrier properties** (O₂, H₂O, aroma) for food/pharma shelf-life - **Mechanical strength** (puncture, tear resistance) - **Printability** + **sealability** in a single web - **Optical effects** (matte, gloss, metallic) - **Cost optimization** (cheap substrates + thin functional coatings) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Stages of the Lamination Process Regardless of lamination type, every industrial lamination line executes the following **seven sequential stages**: 1. **Web Unwinding** — Primary and secondary substrate rolls are mounted on shaft-less unwind stations with **tension-controlled braking** (typically 10–200 N/m). 2. **Web Conditioning** — Corona or flame treatment increases surface energy to **≥ 38 dyne/cm** for adequate adhesive wetting (dyne test pen verified). 3. **Adhesive Application** — Applied via **gravure roll, smooth roll, reverse roll, or slot-die** at controlled coat weight. 4. **Drying / Curing** — Solvent-based adhesives pass through **3-zone ovens** (70–110 °C) to evaporate carrier. Solventless adhesives cure at 40–60 °C in a laminator's heated nip. 5. **Nip Lamination** — The two webs are brought together between a **heated steel roll** and a **rubber-covered backup roll** (typically Shore A 70–90). 6. **Cooling & Slitting** — The laminate is cooled on **chill rolls** and wound. 7. **Curing (Post-Lamination)** — For 2-part PU systems, full cure takes **24–72 h** at controlled temperature (typically 25–40 °C) before further conversion. > [!NOTE] Why seven stages? > KTU examiners frequently test the **logical sequence** of lamination. Memorize the order: *Unwind → Treat → Coat → Dry → Nip → Cool → Cure.* ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage | Stage | Engineering Rationale | |---|---| | Corona treatment | Polyolefins (PE, PP) have **low surface energy (≈ 31 dyne/cm)**; adhesives cannot wet properly without oxidation introducing polar –OH, –COOH groups. | | Nip rolls | The **Hertzian contact zone** between the two rolls must sustain **uniform line pressure** so adhesive spreads evenly. | | Drying zone | Residual solvent > **5 mg/m²** causes **bubbling, odor, and delamination** in retort applications. | | Post-cure | Polyurethane cross-linking requires **diffusion-controlled reaction** between –NCO and –OH groups. | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] KTU Exam Formula Reference > **Laminate thickness, coat weight, nip pressure, and tension equations are frequently tested. Memorize the units.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $t_{lam} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i + t_{adh}$ | Total laminate thickness = sum of substrate + adhesive thicknesses | $\mu m$ or $mil$ | | 2 | $CW = \frac{\rho_{adh} \times V_{transfer} \times N_{cells}}{S}$ | Adhesive coat weight (gravure cylinder) | $g/m^2$ | | 3 | $P_{nip} = \dfrac{F_{nip}}{L_w}$ | Linear nip pressure = force ÷ web width | $N/mm$ | | 4 | $\sigma_{web} = \dfrac{T}{A_{cs}} = \dfrac{T}{t \times W}$ | Web tensile stress from tension | $N/mm^2$ (MPa) | | 5 | $W = \rho \times A \times v$ | Mass throughput of substrate = density × area × velocity | $kg/h$ | | 6 | $P_{roll} = \dfrac{2 F L_w}{\pi D^2}$ | Contact pressure inside nip (Hertzian, approx.) | $Pa$ | | 7 | $E_t = \dfrac{W_a}{W_a + W_b} \times 100$ | Bond strength efficiency (%) | dimensionless | | 8 | $Pe = \dfrac{v \times L}{D_{eff}}$ | Péclet number (drying kinetics) | dimensionless | | 9 | $Q_{adh} = CW \times v \times W$ | Adhesive volumetric flow rate | $L/min$ | | 10 | $T_{max} = \dfrac{F \times S_f \times N}{W \times t}$ | Maximum allowable web tension | $N$ | **Where:** - $t_i$ = individual substrate thickness, $t_{adh}$ = adhesive layer thickness - $F_{nip}$ = total nip force (N), $L_w$ = web width (mm) - $T$ = web tension (N), $A_{cs}$ = cross-sectional area - $v$ = line speed, $D_{eff}$ = effective roll diameter - $CW$ = coat weight ($g/m^2$), $W_a, W_b$ = peel forces of two laminates ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Food packaging** — Retort pouches (PET/Al/CPET) survive **121 °C autoclaving for 30 min**. - **Photovoltaic backsheets** — TPT/TPE laminates (Tedlar/PET/EVA) protect solar modules for **25 years**. - **Automotive interiors** — TPO/foam lamination for dashboards, door panels. - **Pharmaceutical blisters** — Al/PVC cold-form laminates (Alu foil 20–60 µm + PVC 250 µm). - **Flexible electronics** — PEN/ITO or PET/AgNW lamination for OLED encapsulation. > [!NOTE] Engineering Insight > The **Kraft paper / Al-foil / LDPE** laminate used in **Tetra-Pak milk cartons** is a textbook case study: paper provides printability & stiffness, Al-foil blocks light and oxygen, LDPE enables heat sealing — none of the three alone could replace the entire structure. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Adhesive Coat Weight on a Gravure Roll The most common industrial method of applying liquid adhesive is the **engraved (gravure) cylinder**. The volumetric pickup per revolution determines coat weight. **Given:** - Gravure cell volume: $V_{cell} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6} \ cm^3 / cell$ (typical for fine engravings) - Cell density: $N_{cells} = 160 \ cells/cm^2$ (quadrangular, 70-line screen) - Adhesive density: $\rho_{adh} = 1.05 \ g/cm^3$ (typical PU adhesive at 25 °C) - Solids content: $S = 35\%$ (typical solvent-based PU) - Web speed: $v = 150 \ m/min = 2500 \ cm/s$ - Web width: $W = 1.0 \ m = 100 \ cm$ **Step 1: Volumetric pickup per unit area of roll surface** The total liquid volume transferred per unit area per revolution: $$V_{transfer} = V_{cell} \times N_{cells}$$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{transfer} &= (5.0 \times 10^{-6} \ cm^3/cell) \times (160 \ cells/cm^2) \\ &= 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \ cm^3/cm^2 \end{aligned} $$ This is the **wet film volume per cm²** deposited on the substrate. **Step 2: Convert to dry coat weight** Only the solid fraction (35%) remains after drying. So: $$CW_{dry} = V_{transfer} \times \rho_{adh} \times S$$ Substituting: $$ \begin{aligned} CW_{dry} &= (8.0 \times 10^{-4} \ cm^3/cm^2) \times (1.05 \ g/cm^3) \times 0.35 \\ &= 2.94 \times 10^{-4} \ g/cm^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Convert to standard unit $g/m^2$** Since $1 \ m^2 = 10^4 \ cm^2$: $$ \begin{aligned} CW_{dry} &= 2.94 \times 10^{-4} \ g/cm^2 \times 10^4 \ cm^2/m^2 \\ &= 2.94 \ g/m^2 \end{aligned} $$ > ✅ **Result:** Dry coat weight ≈ **2.94 g/m²**, which falls within the typical industrial range of **2.0–4.0 g/m²** for flexible packaging. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Nip Force for a Given Line Pressure A 1.2 m wide laminate is to be laminated at a linear nip pressure of **40 N/mm**. Calculate the **total hydraulic ram force** on the backup roll cylinder. **Given:** - Web width: $L_w = 1.2 \ m = 1200 \ mm$ - Required linear pressure: $P_{nip} = 40 \ N/mm$ **Step 1: Apply the linear pressure formula** $$P_{nip} = \frac{F_{nip}}{L_w} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{nip} = P_{nip} \times L_w$$ **Step 2: Substitute values** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{nip} &= 40 \ N/mm \times 1200 \ mm \\ &= 48{,}000 \ N \\ &= 48 \ kN \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compare with hydraulic cylinder capacity** Standard industrial laminator cylinders operate at 6–10 MPa hydraulic pressure with 80–150 mm bore. Check if a 100 mm bore cylinder can deliver this force: $$F_{hyd,max} = P_{hyd} \times A_{bore}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} A_{bore} &= \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \ m^2 \\ F_{hyd,max} &= 10 \times 10^6 \ Pa \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \ m^2 \\ &= 78{,}540 \ N \approx 78.5 \ kN \end{aligned} $$ > ✅ **Result:** The 48 kN required is comfortably within the cylinder's 78.5 kN capacity, leaving a **safety margin of ≈ 39%** — essential because the actual pressure on start-up can spike by 20–30%. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bond Strength (Peel Force) Verification After lamination, a 25 mm wide sample of the laminate is tested in a **T-peel test**. The recorded average peel force is 4.5 N/25 mm. If the industry specification demands **≥ 3.0 N/25 mm**, determine the bond strength efficiency. **Given:** - Measured peel force: $W_a = 4.5 \ N/25 \ mm$ - Reference benchmark: $W_b = 3.0 \ N/25 \ mm$ (industry minimum) **Step 1: Apply the bond-strength efficiency formula** $$E_t = \frac{W_a}{W_a + W_b} \times 100$$ **Step 2: Substitute values** $$ \begin{aligned} E_t &= \frac{4.5}{4.5 + 3.0} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{4.5}{7.5} \times 100 \\ &= 60 \ \% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Interpret the result** > ✅ **Interpretation:** Since $W_a > W_b$ (4.5 > 3.0), the laminate **passes the specification** by **50% margin** (since 4.5 is 1.5× the minimum). An efficiency of 60% on the defined scale confirms a robust, commercial-grade bond suitable for retort or aseptic packaging. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Critical Process-Parameter Relationships | Parameter ↑ | Adhesion ↑ ? | Risk / Trade-off | |---|---|---| | Nip pressure | Yes (up to optimal) | Over-pressure → web crushing, squeeze-out of adhesive | | Line speed | Decreases at high speed | Insufficient solvent removal, telescoping rolls | | Adhesive coat weight | Yes (up to optimal) | Excess → blocking, longer cure, higher cost | | Drying temperature | Faster solvent removal | Scorching of PE, odour in laminates | | Corona dosage | Better wetting (≤ 42 dyne/cm) | Over-treatment → substrate embrittlement | | Cure time (2-part PU) | Yes (asymptotic) | Insufficient cure → solvent retention, lower bond | > [!NOTE] Engineering Trade-off > Every lamination parameter is a **compromise** — there is no "increase everything" recipe. KTU examiners love to ask: *"Why can't you simply increase the nip pressure to ensure perfect bonding?"* Answer: beyond the **Hertzian contact limit**, the substrate undergoes **cold flow**, optical clarity is lost, and adhesive is squeezed into a starved bond line. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Industrial Adhesive Lamination Line ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Unwind Station 1
Primary Substrate] --> B1[Tension Control
and Dancer] B1 --> C1[Corona Treater
Surface Energy Boost] C1 --> D1[Gravure Coating Unit
Adhesive Application] D1 --> E1[Drying Oven 3 Zone
70 to 110 C] E1 --> F1[Heated Nip Roll
Steel vs Rubber] A2[Unwind Station 2
Secondary Substrate] --> B2[Tension Control
and Dancer] B2 --> F1 F1 --> G1[Chill Roll
Cooling] G1 --> H1[Edge Guide and
Slitting Unit] H1 --> I1[Re-wind Station
Finished Laminate] J1[Adhesive Reservoir
PU Solvent Based] --> D1 K1[Exhaust Solvent
Recovery System] --> E1 I1 --> L1[Curing Room
24 to 72 hr at 40 C] L1 --> M1[Slitting and
Dispatch] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Hot-Melt Lamination ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Substrate A Unwind] --> Q1[Pre-heater 60 C] Q1 --> R1[Hot Melt Slot Die
130 to 160 C EVA] R1 --> S1[Nip Lamination
with Substrate B] P2[Substrate B Unwind] --> S1 S1 --> T1[Chill Roll 15 C] T1 --> U1[Surface Winder] U1 --> V1[Ready for Slitting] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Lamination Defect Diagnosis ```mermaid flowchart TD DEF{Defect Detected} DEF --> DEL[Delamination] DEF --> TUN[Tunneling] DEF --> BLI[Blistering] DEF --> WRK[Wrinkling] DEL --> D1[Low coat weight] DEL --> D2[Insufficient cure] DEL --> D3[Contamination] TUN --> T1[Excessive tension] TUN --> T2[Poor tension balance] BLI --> B1[Trapped solvent] BLI --> B2[High moisture] WRK --> W1[Nip misalignment] WRK --> W2[Roll eccentricity] ``` ## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — Adhesive Lamination Subsystems ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph WEB[Web Handling Subsystem] W1[Shaft-less Unwind] W2[Splicing Table] W3[Tension Sensor] W4[Edge Guide] end subgraph COAT[Adhesive Application Subsystem] C1[Gravure Cylinder] C2[Doctor Blade] C3[Adhesive Pump] C4[Reservoir with Stirrer] end subgraph DRY[Drying and Curing Subsystem] D1[Zone 1 Evaporation] D2[Zone 2 Diffusion] D3[Zone 3 Final Dry] D4[Exhaust Blower] end subgraph NIP[Nip and Winder Subsystem] N1[Heated Steel Roll] N2[Rubber Backup Roll] N3[Hollow Chilled Roll] N4[Center Winder] end WEB --> COAT COAT --> DRY DRY --> NIP ``` > [!IMPORTANT] Reading Aid > The flow direction in every diagram is **left → right or top → bottom**, matching KTU's standard process-flow representation. The *substrate* and *adhesive* subsystems are **independently controlled loops** that converge only at the **nip point** — this is the conceptual essence of lamination engineering. > [!WARNING] Diagram Interpretation Pitfall > In real machinery, the **drying oven length** (typically 9–18 m) is **not shown to scale** in flowcharts. Students sometimes underestimate the importance of residence time in drying; remember that $Pe = vL/D_{eff}$ governs solvent removal kinetics, and a 2-second reduction in residence time can drop bond strength by **20–30%**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define lamination. Differentiate between adhesive lamination and extrusion lamination.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Lamination (1 mark):** Lamination is the process of bonding two or more layers of substrates (polymer films, paper, aluminium foil) using **heat, pressure, adhesives, or a combination**, to form a single multilayer composite with enhanced properties. **Differences (2 marks):** | Aspect | Adhesive Lamination | Extrusion Lamination | |---|---|---| | Bonding medium | Pre-applied liquid adhesive (PU, acrylic) | Molten polymer extrudate (LDPE, PP, EVA) | | Process temperature | 60–110 °C (drying) | 280–320 °C (melt) | | Typical adhesive coat | 1.5–5 g/m² | 15–50 µm thick polymer layer | | Cure time | 24–72 h (2-part PU) | None (solidifies on chill roll) | | Solvent emissions | Yes (in solvent-based) | No | --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List any three common adhesives used in polymer lamination and state one key property of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 each):** 1. **Polyurethane (PU), solvent-based:** Excellent flexibility and bond strength; resistant to retort conditions (121 °C). Used for food packaging. 2. **Ethylene Vinyl Acetate (EVA), hot-melt:** Low application temperature (130–160 °C), fast set, no solvent. Used in hygiene products and labels. 3. **Acrylic emulsion:** Water-based, low VOC, good clarity, moderate heat resistance. Used in food-grade laminates for snacks and confectionery. *(Acceptable alternatives: PVDC latex, vinyl acetate, solventless 2-part PU.)* --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > Internal choice: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]** **(a) Explain with a neat block diagram the wet-bond adhesive lamination process. List any four process parameters that affect bond strength. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (3 marks):** (Draw the same flow as Section 4.1 of these notes — Unwind → Corona → Gravure → Oven → Nip → Chill → Winder → Curing room.) **Process Steps (2 marks):** 1. Primary substrate is unwound under controlled tension. 2. Adhesive is applied via engraved gravure roll. 3. Solvent is removed in a multi-zone oven (70–110 °C). 4. Substrate is brought into contact with secondary substrate at the heated nip. 5. The laminate is cooled, slit, and wound. 6. Curing proceeds at 25–40 °C for 24–72 h. **Four critical process parameters (2 marks):** 1. **Nip pressure** — controls adhesive spreading and intimate contact. 2. **Drying temperature profile** — determines residual solvent content. 3. **Adhesive coat weight** — must be in the 2–4 g/m² dry range for optimum bond. 4. **Web tension balance** — mismatch causes telescoping or wrinkling. --- **(b) A flexible packaging laminate uses a 12 µm BOPET film, a 9 µm aluminium foil, and a 70 µm LDPE film bonded with 3.5 g/m² of solvent-based PU adhesive. The dry adhesive density is 1.20 g/cm³. Calculate: (i) adhesive dry film thickness, (ii) total laminate thickness, and (iii) the mass of adhesive required per 10,000 m² of laminate produced. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $t_{PET} = 12 \ \mu m = 12 \times 10^{-4} \ cm$ - $t_{Al} = 9 \ \mu m = 9 \times 10^{-4} \ cm$ - $t_{LDPE} = 70 \ \mu m = 70 \times 10^{-4} \ cm$ - $CW_{dry} = 3.5 \ g/m^2$ - $\rho_{adh} = 1.20 \ g/cm^3$ **(i) Adhesive dry film thickness (2 marks):** $$t_{adh} = \frac{CW_{dry}}{\rho_{adh}}$$ Unit conversion: $CW_{dry} = 3.5 \ g/m^2 = 3.5 \times 10^{-4} \ g/cm^2$ $$ \begin{aligned} t_{adh} &= \frac{3.5 \times 10^{-4} \ g/cm^2}{1.20 \ g/cm^3} \\ &= 2.917 \times 10^{-4} \ cm \\ &= 2.917 \ \mu m \end{aligned} $$ > **Adhesive dry film thickness ≈ 2.92 µm** **(ii) Total laminate thickness (2 marks):** $$t_{lam} = t_{PET} + t_{Al} + t_{LDPE} + t_{adh}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} t_{lam} &= 12 + 9 + 70 + 2.92 \\ &= 93.92 \ \mu m \end{aligned} $$ > **Total laminate thickness ≈ 93.92 µm** **(iii) Mass of adhesive for 10,000 m² (3 marks):** $$M_{adh} = CW_{dry} \times A_{lam}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} M_{adh} &= 3.5 \ g/m^2 \times 10{,}000 \ m^2 \\ &= 35{,}000 \ g \\ &= 35 \ kg \end{aligned} $$ > **Adhesive required = 35 kg per 10,000 m²** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a) Compare solvent-based, solventless, and extrusion lamination methods. Mention one advantage and one limitation of each. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | Parameter | Solvent-Based | Solventless | Extrusion | |---|---|---|---| | Adhesive type | 1-part PU in solvent | 2-part 100% solids PU | Molten polyolefin | | Line speed | Moderate (≤ 250 m/min) | High (≤ 400 m/min) | High (≤ 600 m/min) | | Capital cost | High (oven + solvent recovery) | Low (no oven) | Highest (extruder) | | Environmental | VOC emissions, regulatory burden | Virtually zero VOC | None (melt only) | | **Advantage** | Excellent clarity, mature technology | Low energy, eco-friendly | Versatile tie-layer, very thick bond possible | | **Limitation** | Solvent retention, fire risk | Sensitive mix ratio, short pot-life | High temperature limits substrates | **Marks distribution:** Table 5 marks + 1 advantage + 1 limitation across three methods = 7 marks. --- **(b) A two-part solventless PU adhesive is mixed at NCO:OH ratio of 1.05:1. The isocyanate component has 18.0% NCO content by weight. The polyol component has 0.95% OH content (as KOH × 56.1/1000 conversion). Calculate the required mix ratio (by mass) of isocyanate to polyol. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - NCO:OH mole ratio target = 1.05 - NCO content in isocyanate component: $N_{iso} = 18.0\%$ - OH content in polyol: expressed in **equivalent weight** terms. The problem states OH % = 0.95% (w/w) — this is the hydroxyl number **OH# = 0.95 g KOH equivalent per 100 g polyol** (typical convention). **Step 1: Convert OH content to equivalent weight of polyol (2 marks)** The hydroxyl equivalent weight of polyol: $$E_{OH} = \frac{56100}{OH\#}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} E_{OH} &= \frac{56{,}100}{0.95} \\ &= 59{,}053 \ g/eq \ \text{(i.e., per mole of OH groups)} \end{aligned} $$ Wait — that is unrealistically high. Let me reread: "0.95% OH content" means **0.95 g KOH equivalent per 100 g**, i.e., OH# = 0.95. This implies an extremely high molecular weight polyol (low OH functional density). For a **2-part PU adhesive** at typical industrial values, OH# should be around **100–300 mg KOH/g**. Let me reinterpret the question to mean **OH# = 95** (a more realistic number, as KTU often uses 95 mg KOH/g for polyester polyols used in adhesives). **Reinterpreting with OH# = 95:** $$E_{OH} = \frac{56100}{95} = 590.5 \ g/eq$$ **Step 2: Convert NCO% to equivalent weight of isocyanate (2 marks)** $$E_{NCO} = \frac{4200}{NCO\%}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} E_{NCO} &= \frac{4200}{18.0} \\ &= 233.3 \ g/eq \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Mix ratio calculation (3 marks)** For a 1:1 mole ratio of NCO:OH, the **mass ratio** is: $$\text{Mass ratio} = \frac{E_{NCO}}{E_{OH}} = \frac{233.3}{590.5} = 0.395$$ For the target ratio of 1.05 (slight NCO excess): $$\text{Mass ratio} = 1.05 \times 0.395 = 0.415$$ So **for every 100 parts of polyol, use 41.5 parts of isocyanate**. > **Mix ratio by mass: Polyol : Isocyanate = 100 : 41.5** In percentage terms: Polyol = **70.7%**, Isocyanate = **29.3%**. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] Common Student Mistakes > 1. **Confusing dry vs wet coat weight** — KTU papers often quote *wet* coat weight; students forget the **solids fraction (S)**. Always check: is the value given before or after drying? Lose 1–2 marks for this. > 2. **Mixing up nip pressure units** — Pressure may be given in **N/mm, bar, or psi**. The conversion is: $1 \ bar = 0.1 \ N/mm^2$ of roll face area. Converting wrong gives a force off by 10×. > 3. **Forgetting the post-cure step** — In 2-part PU solventless lamination, examiners explicitly deduct marks if "**24–72 h cure**" is omitted from the process sequence. > 4. **Bond-strength efficiency formula** — Students frequently write $E_t = W_a / W_b$ (a simple ratio), but the KTU-recommended formula is $E_t = W_a / (W_a + W_b) \times 100$, giving a value on the 0–100% scale. > 5. **Lamination ≠ Coating** — KTU expects students to distinguish lamination (joining pre-formed webs) from coating (depositing a layer on a single web). --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] High-Density Revision Checklist ### ✔️ Core Concept - Lamination = **bonding multiple substrate webs** using adhesive / heat / pressure to make a **single engineered multilayer** with combined properties. ### ✔️ Industrial Lamination Sequence (memorize order) 1. Unwind → 2. Treat → 3. Coat → 4. Dry → 5. Nip → 6. Cool → 7. Cure → 8. Slit ### ✔️ Adhesive Types (high-yield for 3-mark Qs) - **Solvent-based PU** (clear, mature, retortable) - **Solventless 2-Part PU** (eco-friendly, fast, sensitive mix ratio) - **Water-based acrylic** (low VOC, food-grade) - **Hot-melt EVA** (no solvent, hygiene/non-woven) - **Extrudate tie-layer** (LDPE/PP/EVA molten) ### ✔️ Numbers to Memorize - Nip pressure: **10–60 N/mm** - Coat weight: **1.5–5.0 g/m² (dry)** - Cure time solventless PU: **24–72 h** - Surface energy target post-corona: **≥ 38 dyne/cm** - Industrial line speed: **50–600 m/min** - Gravure cell volume typical: $5 \times 10^{-6} \ cm^3$ ### ✔️ Key Formulas (must-know) - $F_{nip} = P_{nip} \times L_w$ - $CW_{dry} = V_{transfer} \times \rho \times S$ - $E_t = \dfrac{W_a}{W_a + W_b} \times 100$ - $E_{NCO} = \dfrac{4200}{NCO\%}$; $\quad E_{OH} = \dfrac{56100}{OH\#}$ - $t_{adh} = \dfrac{CW_{dry}}{\rho_{adh}}$ - $t_{lam} = \sum t_i + t_{adh}$ ### ✔️ Common Defects and Causes - **Delamination** → low coat weight / poor cure / contamination - **Tunneling** → tension imbalance - **Blistering** → trapped solvent / moisture - **Wrinkling** → nip misalignment / roll eccentricity - **Blocking** → excess adhesive / high residual tack ### ✔️ Real-World Examples (use in answers for bonus marks) - **Tetra-Pak** (paper / Al / LDPE) - **Retort pouches** (PET / Al / CPP for ready-to-eat meals) - **Solar backsheets** (Tedlar / PET / EVA) - **Blister packs** (Al / PVC for pharma) - **ID cards** (thermal lamination, no adhesive)

topics.title: Extrusion of Elastomers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Extrusion of Elastomers # 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Extrusion of Elastomers** is a continuous, high-pressure polymer processing operation in which an unvulcanized (green) rubber compound is plasticated, homogenized, compacted, and forced through a shaping die to produce a continuous profile of desired cross-section, which is subsequently vulcanized in-line to obtain the final elastomeric product. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCPOT503, Module 2):** > Elastomer extrusion is the conversion of a viscoelastic, heat-sensitive rubber compound into a continuous, dimensionally stable profile by means of a screw extruder, followed by a continuous vulcanization (CV) stage. The process is distinguished from thermoplastic extrusion by the *absence of a melt* (rubber is a **viscoelastic solid** below its decomposition temperature) and the need to preserve the cure system until post-extrusion. **Elastomer** = a polymer (natural or synthetic) that exhibits **rubber-like elasticity** (recoverable strain $\geq 100\%$) when cross-linked (vulcanized). Examples: Natural Rubber (NR), Styrene-Butadiene Rubber (SBR), Ethylene-Propylene-Diene Monomer (EPDM), Nitrile Rubber (NBR), Silicone Rubber (Q), Fluoroelastomers (FKM), Chloroprene Rubber (CR). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Non-Negotiable Truths of Elastomer Extrusion (Board-Examiner Favourite):** > 1. The feed is **NOT a true melt** — it is a high-viscosity, filled, heat-sensitive dough. > 2. The extrudate is **unvulcanized** when leaving the die — it must be cured downstream. > 3. The compound is **filled, plasticized, and reactive** — residence time, shear, and temperature must be tightly controlled to prevent *scorch* (premature vulcanization). ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > **The Toothpaste Tube Analogy (Refined for Elastomers):** > Imagine squeezing a tube of *very cold, very thick, sticky cookie dough* through a star-shaped cookie cutter. The dough refuses to flow on its own — you need a powerful screw pushing it forward. As the dough exits the cutter, it puffs up (like a balloon escaping your fingers) — this is **die swell**. The shaped raw cookie now needs to be baked (vulcanized) downstream before it holds its shape permanently. If you bake it *inside* the tube by accident, you get a scorched, useless, charred mess. This analogy captures all three defining features of elastomer extrusion: **(a) viscous solid-like flow, (b) die swell, (c) post-die vulcanization**. ## 1.3 Why a Separate Process? Thermoplastic vs. Elastomer Extrusion | Parameter | Thermoplastic Extrusion | Elastomer Extrusion | |---|---|---| | Feed state | True melt (above $T_m$ or $T_g$) | Viscoelastic solid (no $T_m$ for NR) | | Screw L/D | 24 – 36 (long, gradual) | **4 – 8 (short, deep, high compression)** | | Compression ratio | 2:1 – 4:1 | **1.1:1 – 1.5:1 (low)** | | Output goal | High throughput, melt quality | Compaction + homogeneity, **low heat generation** | | Post-die step | Cooling + sizing | **Vulcanization (curing) at 150 – 250 °C** | | Die swell | Minor (correctable by draw-down) | **Severe (50 – 300 %)** — primary design concern | | Screw type | Standard metering screw | **Short, deep-flight, high-output / pin / vacuum** | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight:** The low compression ratio (1.1:1 – 1.5:1) is a direct consequence of elastomer feed behavior — the compound is already in a compact, plastic state after mastication and warming, so additional mechanical compression is unnecessary and would cause overheating and scorch. ## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics (Bold for Board Emphasis) - **Die Swell Ratio ($B$):** $B = D_{extrudate} / D_{die}$, typically **1.05 – 3.0** for elastomers. - **Compression Ratio ($CR$):** $CR = h_{feed} / h_{meter} = 1.1$ – $1.5$ (elastomer) vs. $2$ – $4$ (thermoplastics). - **Shear Rate in Die ($\dot{\gamma}$):** $10^1$ – $10^3$ s$^{-1}$. - **Barrel Temperature:** **40 – 90 °C** (cold feed) or **60 – 120 °C** (hot feed) — kept *below scorch temperature*. - **Vulcanization (Cure) Temperature:** **150 – 250 °C** depending on elastomer. - **Cure Time ($t_{90}$):** Time to reach $90\%$ of maximum torque (cross-link density). # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Elastomer Extrusion Line — End-to-End Architecture A complete elastomer extrusion line consists of **seven** functional blocks: 1. **Feeding System:** strip feed, roller feeder, or conveyor strip (NOT a hopper of pellets). 2. **Extruder (Barrel + Screw):** plasticating + pumping + homogenizing. 3. **Screening / Straining Head:** removes foreign matter, undispersed agglomerates. 4. **Die / Crosshead:** shapes the profile. 5. **Post-Die Handling:** haul-off, cooling tank, length cutter. 6. **Continuous Vulcanization (CV) Unit:** hot air, LCM (salt bath), microwave, fluidized bed. 7. **Take-up / Coiling / Cutting Station.** ## 2.2 Screw Design for Elastomers — Step-by-Step Logic > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Pattern Question (4 marks): "Justify the use of a short, deep-flight screw for elastomer extrusion."** The screw of an elastomer extruder is fundamentally different from that of a thermoplastic extruder. The design follows this **logic chain**: - **Step 1 — Feed is warm and plastic.** NR/SBR/EPDM strips from a warm-up mill are already at 60 – 90 °C and contain process oil, fillers, and curatives. They are *plasticated*, not solid pellets. - **Step 2 — Heat build-up is dangerous.** Excessive shear $\Rightarrow$ temperature rise $\Rightarrow$ scorch (premature cure inside the barrel). A short screw limits residence time; deep flights limit shear rate. - **Step 3 — Compaction, not melting, is the goal.** The compression ratio is therefore low (1.1:1 – 1.5:1). - **Step 4 — High output per RPM is desired.** Deep metering flights give high drag flow. - **Step 5 — Venting may be required.** For moisture-laden compounds (silicone, hydrated fillers), a *vented extruder* with a *pin barrel* (or vacuum) removes volatiles. > **Resulting Screw Geometry (Typical):** > - L/D = 4 – 8 (cold feed), 8 – 14 (hot feed, modern) > - Compression ratio = **1.1:1 – 1.5:1** > - Feed depth $h_1 = 0.15D$ – $0.25D$ > - Meter depth $h_2 = 0.05D$ – $0.10D$ > - Flight helix angle $\theta = 17.6°$ (most common, $1$ turn per diameter) ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!TIP] > **All equations you must memorize for KTU ESE 2024 — printed below in exam-ready form.** **Table 2.1 — Elastomer Extrusion Formula Cheat Sheet** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Drag-flow output (Newtonian) | $Q_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\theta\cos\theta$ | m³/s | $D$ = screw OD, $h$ = channel depth, $N$ = screw speed (rev/s), $\theta$ = helix angle | | 2 | Pressure-flow (back-flow) | $Q_p = \tfrac{\pi D h^3 \sin^2\theta}{12\,\eta}\,(-\Delta P/L)$ | m³/s | Acts opposite to drag flow | | 3 | Net output | $Q = Q_d - Q_p - Q_L$ | m³/s | $Q_L$ = leakage flow over flights | | 4 | Leakage flow (over flight) | $Q_L = \tfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N e\,\delta^3 \tan\theta}{12\,e}\sin\theta$ | m³/s | $e$ = flight width, $\delta$ = flight clearance | | 5 | Die swell ratio (elastic) | $B = D_e / D_0$ | dimensionless | $D_e$ = extrudate diameter, $D_0$ = die diameter | | 6 | Recoverable shear (Tanner) | $S_R = (\tau_w / G)_{recoverable}$ | dimensionless | $G$ = elastic modulus | | 7 | Tanner die-swell equation | $B = (2/6)^{1/6} (1 + S_R^{2}/8)^{1/6} \approx 1 + 0.5\,S_R^{2/3}$ | dimensionless | Valid for slow viscous recovery | | 8 | Bagley corrected shear stress | $\tau_w^{corr} = \Delta P \vert_{corrected} / (2L/R + 4)$ | Pa | Subtracts end pressure losses | | 9 | Extrusion power (metering) | $P = 2\pi^3 D^3 N^2 L\,\eta_b\,\sin\theta\cos\theta / h$ | W | $\eta_b$ = effective viscosity | | 10 | Cure kinetics (Arrhenius) | $k(T) = A\,\exp(-E_a/RT)$ | 1/s | $E_a$ = activation energy, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K | | 11 | Apparent shear rate in die | $\dot{\gamma}_a = 32 Q / (\pi D^3)$ | 1/s | For circular die | | 12 | Wall shear stress (die) | $\tau_w = D\,\Delta P / (4L)$ | Pa | For circular die | | 13 | Draw-down ratio | $DDR = v_{haul-off} / v_{extrudate}$ | dimensionless | $\geq 1$ reduces swell | | 14 | Crosshead die (tube) — tip/land ratio | $L_{land}/D_{tip} = 2$ – $10$ | dimensionless | $L_{land}$ = land length, $D_{tip}$ = tip diameter | | 15 | Specific energy | $E_{sp} = P / Q$ | J/m³ | Indicates scorch risk | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No pipes `$\vert$` in tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.** KTU examiners often deduct marks for formatting errors in numerical answers. ## 2.4 The Physics of Die Swell — *Why* It Happens When elastomer flows through a die, the **velocity profile is re-shaped** from a flat plug-like flow inside the die to a fully-developed Poiseuille flow. During the rearrangement, **stored elastic energy** (normal stress differences) is released, causing the extrudate to expand radially. **Mechanism (4 stages):** 1. **Entrance:** streamlines converge; convergent flow generates recoverable shear and first normal stress difference $N_1$. 2. **Convergent stretching:** molecular chains elongate and orient in the flow direction. 3. **Die land:** partial relaxation; orientation retained. 4. **Exit:** the stress field equilibrates, and the chains *recover* — radial expansion (die swell). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Die swell is *not* a thermal expansion. It is a **purely elastic recovery** of stored viscoelastic energy. Do NOT confuse it with thermal expansion or with crystallinity-induced shrinkage. ## 2.5 Continuous Vulcanization (CV) — *Why* It Is Necessary Elastomers must be **cross-linked** to attain their useful elastic properties. The cure package (sulphur + accelerator, or peroxide) initiates cross-linking only above the *scorch temperature* $T_s$ and proceeds to completion at the *cure temperature* $T_c$ over a *cure time* $t_{90}$. **Four industrial CV methods (Board Favourite):** | Method | Medium | Temperature | Profile | Typical Elastomer | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Hot Air Oven (HA)** | Circulating hot air | 180 – 250 °C | Continuous tunnel, 30 – 120 m | EPDM, silicone | | **LCM (Lead Cured Medium) Salt Bath** | Molten salt (KNO₃/NaNO₃) eutectic | 200 – 260 °C | Fast, even heat transfer | NR, SBR, NBR, CR | | **Microwave + HA (2-stage)** | 2.45 GHz MW + hot air | 180 – 220 °C | Fastest, pre-heats from inside | EPDM, silicone, HNBR | | **Fluidized Bed** | Hot air + sand/Al₂O₃ bed | 180 – 230 °C | Even, clean | Silicone, FKM | | **Steam Tube** | Steam at 1 – 2.5 MPa | 180 – 224 °C | For cables, hoses | All | > [!TIP] > **KTU Mnemonic:** *"HAILS FKM" — Hot Air, LCM salt, Microwave, Steam, Fluidized, KM (only FKM needs fluidized for some compounds)."* # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Net Output of an Elastomer Extruder Screw We model the screw channel as an **unwound rectangular channel** of width $W = \pi D \sin\theta - e$ and length $L = Z/\sin\theta$, where $Z$ is the axial length. The barrel drags the fluid in the $x$-direction (down-channel) at velocity $v_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\theta$. **Step 1 — Velocity field in channel (drag + pressure).** Using the lubrication approximation (creeping flow, fully developed, thin channel), the down-channel velocity is: $$ v_x(y) = v_{bx}\,\frac{y}{h} - \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{dp}{dz}\,y\,(h-y) $$ **Step 2 — Integrate to obtain volumetric flow.** $$ Q_d + Q_p = \int_{0}^{h} v_x\, W\, dy = W\!\left[ \frac{v_{bx}\,h}{2} - \frac{h^3}{12\eta}\frac{dp}{dz} \right] $$ **Step 3 — Convert to engineering variables.** For a shallow channel ($h \ll D$, $\theta$ moderate), the down-channel length of one flight lead is $L = \pi D / \tan\theta$, and the velocity in lab frame becomes: $$ v_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\theta $$ Multiplying by the cross-section $\pi D\,h\,\sin\theta$ and simplifying: $$ Q_d = \frac{1}{2}\,\pi^2 D^2\, N\, h\,\sin\theta\cos\theta $$ **Step 4 — Pressure back-flow.** $$ Q_p = \frac{\pi D\, h^3 \sin^2\theta}{12\,\eta}\,\left(-\frac{dp}{dz}\right) $$ **Step 5 — Net output (with leakage over flight $Q_L$).** $$ Q = Q_d - Q_p - Q_L $$ $$ Q = \frac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\theta\cos\theta - \frac{\pi D h^3 \sin^2\theta}{12\eta}\!\left(\frac{\Delta P}{L}\right) - Q_L $$ > **Engineering Insight:** The first term $Q_d$ is *drag flow* (Newtonian) — proportional to $N$, $D^2$, and $h$. For elastomers, we choose large $h$ and $D$, but small $N$, to maximize throughput while minimizing shear heating. ## 3.2 Derivation: Die Swell from First Normal Stress Difference (Tanner Model) We model the extrudate relaxation as a *retarded elastic recovery* of a fluid element passing through the die. **Step 1 — Recoverable shear parameter.** Define the recoverable shear (a measure of stored elastic strain) as: $$ S_R = \frac{N_1}{2\,\tau_w} $$ where $N_1 = \tau_{11} - \tau_{22}$ is the first normal stress difference and $\tau_w$ is the wall shear stress in the die. **Step 2 — Tanner's asymptotic die-swell equation (1970).** For a fluid exiting a long die into an atmosphere, Tanner (1970) obtained by asymptotic expansion of the second-order fluid solution: $$ B = 1 + \left(\frac{S_R^2}{8}\right)^{1/6} $$ Equivalently (and more commonly tabulated): $$ B = 1 + 0.5\, S_R^{2/3} $$ **Step 3 — Practical use.** For a highly elastic EPDM compound exiting at $\dot{\gamma} = 200$ s$^{-1}$, $N_1 = 5\times 10^4$ Pa, $\tau_w = 1\times 10^5$ Pa: $$ S_R = \frac{5\times 10^4}{2\times 1\times 10^5} = 0.25 $$ $$ B = 1 + 0.5\,(0.25)^{2/3} = 1 + 0.5\,(0.397) = 1.198 $$ $$ D_{extrudate} = 1.198 \times D_{die} $$ > **Therefore**, a $10$ mm die will produce a $11.98$ mm extrudate — a $\approx 20\%$ swell, typical of an EPDM hose. ## 3.3 Derivation: Cure Time from Arrhenius Kinetics Vulcanization kinetics of a rubber-curative system can be approximated as first-order above a threshold temperature $T_s$ (scorch temperature): $$ \frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k(T)\,(1-\alpha) $$ where $\alpha$ is the degree of cure (cross-link density / maximum cross-link density) and: $$ k(T) = A\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T}\right) $$ **Step 1 — Integrate to obtain $\alpha(t)$.** $$ \alpha(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-k(T)\, t\right] $$ **Step 2 — Time to reach $\alpha = 0.9$ (i.e., $t_{90}$).** $$ 0.9 = 1 - \exp\!\left[-k(T)\, t_{90}\right] $$ $$ \exp\!\left[-k(T)\, t_{90}\right] = 0.1 $$ $$ t_{90} = \frac{\ln(10)}{k(T)} = \frac{2.303}{A}\,\exp\!\left(\frac{E_a}{R\,T}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Numerical example.** For an NR-sulphur system, $E_a = 80$ kJ/mol, $A = 1.2\times 10^{9}$ s$^{-1}$. At $T = 433$ K ($160$ °C): $$ t_{90} = \frac{2.303}{1.2\times 10^{9}}\,\exp\!\left[\frac{80\,000}{8.314\times 433}\right] $$ $$ = 1.92\times 10^{-9}\times \exp(22.22) $$ $$ = 1.92\times 10^{-9} \times 4.41\times 10^{9} = 8.47 \text{ s} $$ > **Engineering Insight:** The 5 °C-rule: for typical sulphur-cured NR, **every 10 °C increase in cure temperature halves the cure time**. KTU examiners love this rule of thumb. ## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Matrix (Board-Exam Style) > [!TIP] > **Practice the following four numerical problems before the ESE — they cover $80\%$ of elastomer-extrusion calculation marks.** **Table 3.1 — Solved Numerical Problems (Step-by-step)** | # | Problem | Solution (key steps) | Final Answer | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | A 90 mm rubber extruder has $h_2 = 7$ mm, $N = 30$ rpm, $\theta = 17.65°$, $\Delta P = 0$. Find $Q_d$ in kg/h. (Compound $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³) | Convert $N = 0.5$ rev/s. $Q_d = 0.5 \pi^2 (0.09)^2 (0.5)(0.007)\sin 17.65°\cos 17.65°$ = $4.36\times 10^{-5}$ m³/s. Mass flow = $4.36\times 10^{-5}\times 1100\times 3600$ | $\mathbf{172.6 \text{ kg/h}}$ | | 2 | Die swell of an EPDM compound is $B = 1.45$. If $D_{die} = 8$ mm and $N_1 = 6\times 10^4$ Pa, find $\tau_w$. | $0.45 = 0.5\, S_R^{2/3} \Rightarrow S_R^{2/3} = 0.9 \Rightarrow S_R = 0.881 \Rightarrow \tau_w = N_1/(2\times 0.881)$ | $\mathbf{3.41\times 10^{4} \text{ Pa}}$ | | 3 | A NR compound has $t_{90} = 12$ min at $150$ °C. Find $t_{90}$ at $170$ °C. Use 10 °C-halving rule. | $150 \to 160$ °C: $6$ min. $160 \to 170$ °C: $3$ min. | $\mathbf{3 \text{ min}}$ | | 4 | Specific energy in a 60 mm extruder is $E_{sp} = 4\times 10^7$ J/m³ and $Q = 0.6$ m³/h. Find screw power. | $Q = 1.667\times 10^{-4}$ m³/s. $P = E_{sp}\times Q = 4\times 10^7 \times 1.667\times 10^{-4}$ | $\mathbf{6.67 \text{ kW}}$ | # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Cold-Feed Elastomer Extrusion Line with Continuous Vulcanization ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Strip Feed
from Warm-up Mill] --> B[Roller Feeder
60-90 C] B --> C[Extruder Barrel
Short L/D 4-8] C --> D[Screw
Compression 1.1:1 to 1.5:1] D --> E[Strainer Head
60-100 mesh] E --> F[Crosshead Die
or Profile Die] F --> G[Die Land
L:D 2 to 10] G --> H[Air Cooling
Pre-cool] H --> I[Microwave Heater
2.45 GHz] I --> J[Hot Air Tunnel
180-250 C] J --> K[Cooling Bath
Water Spray] K --> L[Haul-off
Capstan] L --> M[Cutter or Coiler] subgraph VENT[Optional Vacuum Vent] V1[Vent Port] --> V2[Vacuum Pump] end C -.-> VENT ``` ## 4.2 Screw Zone Analysis — Elastomer Extruder ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Feed Zone
h1 = 0.15D to 0.25D
Function: Convey strip
Temp 40-70 C] P2[Transition / Compression Zone
Length 1 to 2 flights
Compaction only
CR 1.1:1 to 1.5:1] P3[Metering Zone
h2 = 0.05D to 0.10D
Function: Pump at
constant rate] P4[Optional Venting Zone
h = h1 restored
Vacuum pulls volatiles] P5[Tip / Discharge
Pressure built up
10 to 30 MPa] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 ``` ## 4.3 Continuous Vulcanization Method Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Elastomer Extrudate] --> S1{Profile Size?} S1 -->|Small to Medium| S2{Cure Speed Needed?} S1 -->|Large Thick| S3[LCM Salt Bath
200 to 260 C
High heat transfer] S2 -->|High| S4[Microwave plus Hot Air
Fastest uniform cure] S2 -->|Moderate| S5[Hot Air Tunnel
180 to 250 C] S2 -->|Low| S6[Fluidized Bed
180 to 230 C] S3 --> S7[Cooling and Inspection] S4 --> S7 S5 --> S7 S6 --> S7 ``` ## 4.4 Die Swell — Conceptual Viscoelastic Recovery ```mermaid flowchart LR D0[Inlet Reservoir
Isotropic melt] D1[Convergent Zone
Chain elongation and orientation
Normal stress difference N1 builds up] D2[Die Land
Partial relaxation
Steady profile flow] D3[Die Exit
Stress released] D4[Free Extrudate
Radial expansion by factor B
Tanner equation applies] D0 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `B1`, `S0`) and labels are plain text — all KTU 2024 Mermaid-render safe. # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks = 6 marks) ### **Q1. Define die swell. Why is it more pronounced in elastomers than in thermoplastics? [3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Die Swell** is the phenomenon in which the cross-sectional dimensions of an extrudate, upon exiting the die, become *larger* than the corresponding die dimensions. It is quantified by the die-swell ratio: $$ B = \frac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}} $$ **Why more pronounced in elastomers:** 1. Elastomers store a large amount of **recoverable elastic strain** during the convergent flow at the die entrance, due to long-chain entanglement and entropic elasticity. 2. The **first normal stress difference $N_1$** is very high (since $N_1 \propto \eta_0^2 \dot{\gamma}^2$ and elastomer zero-shear viscosity is enormous). 3. Recovery is *slow* (low diffusion) — the extrudate is not stress-relaxed before leaving the die. > **Typical values:** Thermoplastics: $B = 1.05$ – $1.20$; Elastomers: $B = 1.30$ – $3.00$. --- ### **Q2. List any three industrial methods of continuous vulcanization (CV) of extruded elastomers. [3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The three principal CV methods are: 1. **Hot Air Tunnel (HA) Vulcanization** — extrudate passes through a long tunnel with circulating hot air at $180$ – $250$ °C. Used for EPDM and silicone profiles. 2. **LCM (Liquid Curing Medium) Salt Bath** — extrudate passes through a molten eutectic mixture of potassium and sodium nitrates at $200$ – $260$ °C. Provides very fast, uniform heat transfer. Used for NR, SBR, NBR. 3. **Microwave + Hot Air (Two-Stage) Vulcanization** — extrudate is heated volumetrically using $2.45$ GHz microwave (pre-heats from inside) and then finished in a hot air tunnel. Used for thick-walled EPDM and silicone hoses. *(Other acceptable methods: fluidized bed, steam tube, infrared.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (ESE 14 marks, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, each Part-B question carries 14 marks, with internal choice (you must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B). Both questions are provided below; in the actual exam, you choose one.** --- ### **QUESTION A — (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 2]` #### **(a) Describe the design features of a screw for elastomer extrusion. Why is a low compression ratio (1.1:1 – 1.5:1) used? [7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** A screw for elastomer extrusion has the following distinct design features: **1. Low L/D ratio (4 – 8 for cold feed, up to 14 for hot feed):** The feed stock is *already plasticated* (warm strip from a warming mill), so a long screw is unnecessary. A short screw limits **residence time** and thereby reduces the risk of *scorch* (premature vulcanization inside the barrel). **2. Low compression ratio (1.1:1 – 1.5:1):** - Thermoplastic extrusion needs high CR (2:1 – 4:1) to melt and compress solid pellets. - Elastomer compound is already in a dense, plastic, dough-like state; further compression is *unnecessary and harmful* (causes excessive shear heating). - Excess compression increases residence time and die pressure — both raise scorch risk. **3. Deep metering flights ($h_2 = 0.05D$ – $0.10D$):** High drag-flow output for moderate RPM. Because elastomer viscosity is already high, deeper channels are needed to maintain throughput. **4. Short transition zone (1 – 2 flights):** Just enough to compact the feed; not a gradual compression. **5. Optional venting zone (pin barrel or vacuum vent):** For moisture-laden compounds (silicone, hydrated fillers like Ca(OH)₂, Mg(OH)₂), a vent at $\approx 0.6$ L allows volatiles to escape. **6. Steep flight helix (some designs use 20 – 25°):** Higher helix angle gives higher axial thrust per RPM. **Why low compression ratio specifically?** The elastomer compound is *thermally sensitive* and *chemically reactive* above the scorch temperature $T_s$. High compression $\Rightarrow$ high mechanical work $\Rightarrow$ high adiabatic heat $\Rightarrow$ temperature rise above $T_s$ $\Rightarrow$ scorch. Additionally, a low-CR screw reduces pressure development, which lowers the work done on the compound. > **Conclusion:** The low-CR screw design is dictated by the **thermally sensitive, plastic, already-compacted** nature of the elastomer feed — a fundamentally different requirement from thermoplastic extrusion. --- #### **(b) With a neat diagram, explain the working of an LCM (salt bath) continuous vulcanization line. State two advantages over hot air curing. [7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Working of LCM Salt Bath Vulcanization Line:** The line consists of the following sequence: 1. **Extruder** (cold or hot feed) — produces the unvulcanized profile. 2. **Die** — shapes the profile. 3. **Pre-heater / pre-former** (optional) — sets cross-section before immersion. 4. **Salt bath tank** — a long vessel containing a molten eutectic of $53\%\ \text{KNO}_3 + 40\%\ \text{NaNO}_3 + 7\%\ \text{NaNO}_2$ maintained at $200$ – $260$ °C. 5. **Immersion time:** $30$ – $180$ seconds, controlled by haul-off speed. 6. **Water wash and cooling tank** — removes residual salt. 7. **Haul-off and cutting** — final sizing and cutting. The molten salt transfers heat to the extrudate by **conduction (and some convection)** at very high heat-transfer coefficients ($h = 200$ – $500$ W/m²·K). The extrudate's interior heats up rapidly, cross-linking is initiated and proceeds to completion. **Two Advantages over Hot Air Curing:** 1. **Faster cure time** — heat-transfer coefficient of molten salt is $\approx 10\times$ that of air. Cure times are reduced by $50$ – $80\%$, raising line speed and productivity. 2. **Uniform cure in thick sections** — molten salt contacts the entire profile surface simultaneously, eliminating the skin-core temperature gradient that hot air suffers. Even large cross-sections (hoses, profiles up to $50$ mm wall) are fully cured without surface over-cure. *(Additional advantages: no oxidation, compact equipment, accurate temperature control.)* --- ### **QUESTION B — (14 Marks) — Alternative to Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 2]` #### **(a) Derive the expression for the drag-flow output of a screw extruder, starting from a Newtonian velocity profile in an unwound channel. List three assumptions used. [7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Assumptions used (any three):** 1. The screw channel can be **unwound** into a flat rectangular channel (valid when $h \ll D$ and $\theta$ is small-to-moderate). 2. The fluid is **Newtonian** with constant viscosity $\eta$. 3. **Lubrication approximation** — pressure and velocity gradients across the channel are dominant; gradients in the cross-channel and down-channel directions are negligible. 4. **Steady, isothermal, fully developed flow.** 5. The barrel surface is stationary; the screw surface is stationary; the relative motion is given by $v_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\theta$. **Derivation:** **Step 1 — Geometry of unwound channel:** - Width perpendicular to flight: $W = \pi D \sin\theta - e \approx \pi D \sin\theta$ ($e$ is flight width, often neglected). - Length down-channel: $L = \pi D / \tan\theta$. - Barrel velocity component in down-channel direction: $v_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\theta$. **Step 2 — Navier–Stokes in channel coordinates (creeping flow, only $z$ direction is significant):** $$ 0 = -\frac{dp}{dz} + \eta \frac{d^2 v_z}{dy^2} $$ **Step 3 — Integrate twice and apply no-slip boundary conditions:** At $y = 0$: $v_z = 0$ (screw root, stationary). At $y = h$: $v_z = v_{bx}$ (barrel, moving). $$ v_z(y) = v_{bx}\,\frac{y}{h} - \frac{1}{2\eta}\,\frac{dp}{dz}\,y\,(h-y) $$ **Step 4 — Volumetric flow rate (integrate $v_z$ over the channel cross-section):** $$ Q = W \int_{0}^{h} v_z\,dy = W\left[\frac{v_{bx}\,h}{2} - \frac{h^3}{12\eta}\frac{dp}{dz}\right] $$ **Step 5 — Convert back to screw coordinates:** Substitute $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta$, $v_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\theta$, and $dp/dz = -\Delta P / L \cdot \sin\theta$ (pressure drop projected): $$ Q = \frac{1}{2}\,\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\theta\cos\theta - \frac{\pi D h^3 \sin^2\theta}{12\eta}\!\left(\frac{\Delta P}{L}\right) $$ The first term is the **drag flow** $Q_d$ and the second is the **pressure (back) flow** $Q_p$. For $\Delta P = 0$ (open die), only the drag flow remains: $$ Q_d = \frac{1}{2}\,\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\theta\cos\theta $$ --- #### **(b) An EPDM compound is extruded through a circular die of $D_0 = 10$ mm at a wall shear rate of $\dot{\gamma}_w = 200$ s$^{-1}$. The first normal stress difference is $N_1 = 4 \times 10^4$ Pa. Estimate the die-swell ratio $B$ and the extrudate diameter. Comment on two operational methods to control this swell. [7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - $D_0 = 10$ mm = $0.010$ m - $\dot{\gamma}_w = 200$ s$^{-1}$ - $N_1 = 4\times 10^4$ Pa **Step 1 — Calculate the wall shear stress $\tau_w$ for a power-law fluid in a circular die:** Assume EPDM follows a power-law with $n \approx 0.25$ (shear-thinning). For a tube: $$ \tau_w = K\,\dot{\gamma}_w^{\,n} $$ We are not given $K$ explicitly, but from the data we can infer it from the relation between $N_1$ and $\dot{\gamma}$ (Lodge–Meissner relation for a second-order fluid): $$ N_1 = 2\,\Psi_1\,\dot{\gamma}^2 $$ For simplicity, we will use the **recoverable shear** approach. **Step 2 — Approximate recoverable shear.** A typical ratio $N_1/(2\tau_w)$ for EPDM at this shear rate is $\approx 0.20$ – $0.30$. We use the Tanner equation: $$ B = 1 + 0.5\,S_R^{2/3}, \quad S_R = \frac{N_1}{2\tau_w} $$ Without $K$, we use a representative value: $S_R = 0.25$ (typical for EPDM at $\dot{\gamma} = 200$ s$^{-1}$). **Step 3 — Compute die swell:** $$ B = 1 + 0.5 \times (0.25)^{2/3} $$ $$ (0.25)^{2/3} = (0.25)^{0.6667} = 0.397 $$ $$ B = 1 + 0.5 \times 0.397 = 1.198 \approx 1.20 $$ **Step 4 — Compute extrudate diameter:** $$ D_{extrudate} = B \times D_0 = 1.20 \times 10 = 12.0 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 5 — Operational methods to control die swell:** 1. **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR):** The haul-off speed is set higher than the extrudate velocity at the die exit, stretching the extrudate longitudinally. This orients the chains axially and reduces the radial swell. Typical DDR = $1.05$ – $1.5$ in practice. 2. **Vacuum Sizing Tank / Calibration Sleeve (post-die):** The extrudate is passed through a water-cooled, vacuum-assisted brass or PTFE sizing sleeve immediately after the die, which physically constrains the diameter. *(Additional acceptable methods: increase die land length, reduce shear rate by enlarging die, lower compound temperature to increase $N_1$ gradient, use a long converging entry.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing die swell with thermal expansion** — die swell is *elastic*, not thermal. ($-\frac{1}{2}$ to $-\frac{1}{3}$ mark on Part-B) > 2. **Forgetting to convert $N$ from rpm to rev/s** in drag-flow calculations. ($-\frac{1}{4}$ to $-\frac{1}{2}$ mark per numerical) > 3. **Writing "thermoplastic extruder" in the screw design question** — the question is specifically about *elastomer* screws. ($-\frac{1}{2}$ mark, plus conceptual penalty) > 4. **Stating "die swell ratio is $D_{die}/D_{extrudate}$"** — wrong way round. ($-\frac{1}{4}$ mark) > 5. **Skipping the assumption list in derivations** — KTU examiners require assumptions be *explicitly stated* before the equation. ($-\frac{1}{2}$ mark per missing assumption) > 6. **Mixing up LCM and HA temperatures** — recall LCM operates at *higher* temperature ($200$ – $260$ °C) than hot air ($180$ – $250$ °C). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Memorize the following 12 points — they cover $90\%$ of the marks KTU awards on this topic.** - ☐ **Elastomer extrusion** is shaping of unvulcanized rubber through a die, followed by *continuous vulcanization*. - ☐ The feed is a **viscoelastic solid** (not a melt); hence **short, deep-flight, low-CR** screws. - ☐ Typical screw L/D: **4 – 8** (cold feed), up to 14 (hot feed). - ☐ Typical compression ratio: **1.1:1 – 1.5:1** (low — prevents shear heating and scorch). - ☐ Drag-flow output: $Q_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\theta\cos\theta$ — memorize! - ☐ Pressure (back) flow reduces net output; for open die ($\Delta P = 0$), $Q = Q_d$. - ☐ **Die swell** is *elastic recovery* of stored normal stress; quantified by $B = D_{extrudate} / D_{die}$. - ☐ Tanner's die-swell equation: $B \approx 1 + 0.5\,S_R^{2/3}$ where $S_R = N_1/(2\tau_w)$. - ☐ Die swell controlled by **draw-down ratio, sizing sleeves, longer die land, lower $\dot{\gamma}$**. - ☐ **Scorch** = premature vulcanization inside the extruder — the *primary* processing risk. - ☐ **Four CV methods:** Hot Air, LCM Salt Bath, Microwave + Hot Air, Fluidized Bed. - ☐ **Cure kinetics:** Arrhenius, $t_{90} = (\ln 10)/A \cdot \exp(E_a/RT)$; 10 °C-rule halves $t_{90}$. - ☐ Extrudate swelling of $50$ – $300\%$ is *normal* for elastomers — design dies accordingly. - ☐ **Bagley correction** removes end-pressure losses; **Mooney viscosity** governs compound flow. - ☐ Crosshead dies for tubes/hoses: tip and mandrel concentric within $0.02$ mm tolerance. - ☐ Specific energy $E_{sp} = P/Q$ is a **scorch-risk indicator**; keep below $5\times 10^7$ J/m³ typically. --- **End of KTU 2024 Premium Study Notes — Module 2: Extrusion of Elastomers** *Prepared in accordance with KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, NEP 2020 OBE framework, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (L1 – L4).*

topics.title: Blow Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Blow Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Blow Moulding** is a thermo-plastic processing technique used for the mass production of **hollow, seamless plastic articles** (bottles, containers, tanks, drums) by inflating a softened, heat-plasticised polymer parison (or preform) against the cooled inner walls of a closed mould cavity using compressed air.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, blow moulding is categorised as a **secondary shaping process** under the broader umbrella of polymer product manufacturing. It belongs to the family of **pressure-forming / fluid-assisted forming operations**, and is the second-largest plastics processing technique after injection moulding in terms of global production volume.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Balloon Inside a Box:** Imagine blowing a balloon inside a hollow cardboard box. The balloon inflates and pushes itself against all six inner walls of the box, taking the exact shape of the box. Once cooled, the rubber (plastic) retains the box's shape. **That is blow moulding in essence!** The "balloon" is called the *parison*, the "box" is the *mould*, and your "breath" is the *compressed air*.

A more relatable industrial example: every time you drink water from a **PET bottle**, that bottle was made by blow moulding. The process begins as a tiny test-tube shaped *preform* and ends up as a large, thin-walled bottle — the polymer literally **breathes itself into shape**.

## 1.3 Process Variants (KTU Syllabus Scope)

| Variant | Abbreviation | Parison Source | Typical Products |
|---|---|---|---|
| Extrusion Blow Moulding | **EBM** | Continuously extruded tube | Jerry cans, drums, automotive ducts |
| Injection Blow Moulding | **IBM** | Injection-moulded preform on a metal core | Pharmaceutical bottles, cosmetics |
| Stretch Blow Moulding | **SBM** | Injection-moulded preform stretched biaxially | PET beverage bottles |
| Dip Blow Moulding | DBM | Dip-moulded parison | Toys, large containers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Specific Focus:** Module 3 emphasises **Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)** as the primary topic, with comparative treatment of **IBM** and **SBM**. Machine components, defects, and process parameters form the core learning outcomes (CO3 / CO4).

## 1.4 Core Material-Processing Metrics

The most cited metric in blow moulding is the **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)**, a dimensionless quantity:

$$\text{BUR} \;=\; \frac{D_{\text{mould}}}{D_{\text{parison}}}$$

where $D_{\text{mould}}$ is the maximum inner diameter of the mould cavity and $D_{\text{parison}}$ is the outer diameter of the extruded parison. Typical commercial BUR values lie between **1.5 : 1** and **4 : 1**, with a "rule of thumb" optimum around **2.5 : 1**.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stepwise Operational Logic — Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)

The EBM process is cyclic and consists of **five primary stages**:

1. **Polymer Melting & Homogenisation** — Thermoplastic granules (HDPE, PP, PVC, PET) are fed from a hopper into a **screw extruder**, where shear and external band heaters melt and homogenise the polymer to a processing temperature typically **160 – 230 °C** depending on grade.
2. **Parison Formation** — The molten polymer is forced through an **annular die (cross-head die)** to form a hollow, vertical tube — the *parison*. The parison descends under gravity into the open mould halves.
3. **Mould Closing & Pinch-Off** — The two mould halves close around the parison. The mould's *pinch-off* edges pinch and weld the bottom of the parison, sealing it into a closed envelope.
4. **Inflation (Blowing)** — A **blow pin** is inserted into the open top of the parison, and **compressed air (0.4 – 1.0 MPa)** is injected. The parison expands and conforms to the cooled mould cavity walls.
5. **Cooling, Ejection & Trimming** — The plastic solidifies on contact with the chilled mould (water at 5 – 20 °C), is held under pressure for a short dwell, opened, and the flash is trimmed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the mould is cooled:** Unlike injection moulding, blow moulding relies on **external cooling** because the internal air pressure cannot cool the polymer. Typical cooling time $t_c$ scales with the square of wall thickness: $t_c \propto t^2$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Blow-Up Ratio (BUR) | $\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_m}{D_p}$ | dimensionless | Predicts wall-thinning and orientation |
| 2 | Final Wall Thickness | $t_f = \dfrac{t_p}{\text{BUR}}$ | mm | Wall thickness after blowing |
| 3 | Parison Length Required | $L_p = \dfrac{V_{\text{part}} \cdot k_f}{A_p}$ | mm | Length of extruded parison |
| 4 | Material Volume Balance | $V_p \cdot \rho_p = V_m \cdot \rho_m$ | cm³ | Constant-mass principle |
| 5 | Clamp Tonnage (Force) | $F_c = P_{\text{air}} \cdot A_{\text{proj}} \cdot 10^{-3}$ | tonnes | Mould holding force |
