### Question B (14 Marks) **[ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of **"Design Basis"** for a polymer processing machine. List the key parameters that constitute the design basis of an **injection molding machine**, with typical values for a 100-ton clamping force machine.

**(b)** [7 Marks] An **HDPE woven sack** fabric is produced at a line speed of **25 m/min** with a die output of **60 kg/h**. The fabric width is **1.2 m** and the polymer density is **0.95 g/cm³**. Determine: (i) the **basis weight**, (ii) the **fabric thickness**, and (iii) the **percentage deviation** if the actual measured basis weight is **42 g/m²** against a target of **40 g/m²**.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Design Basis Explanation:**

The **Design Basis** of a polymer processing machine is the comprehensive set of **engineering parameters, operating conditions, and performance criteria** upon which the machine is designed, sized, and specified. It serves as the foundational document for equipment selection, vendor comparison, and capacity planning.

**Key Design Basis Parameters for a 100-ton Injection Molding Machine:**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Clamping Force | 100 tons (≈ 890 kN) |
| Shot Weight (PS) | 100–200 g |
| Screw Diameter | 30–40 mm |
| Injection Pressure | 1000–1400 bar |
| Injection Speed | 50–100 mm/s |
| Plasticizing Capacity | 20–30 kg/h |
| Clamp Stroke | 250–350 mm |
| Mold Thickness (max) | 300–400 mm |
| Daylight (max) | 600–800 mm |
| Heater Power | 5–10 kW |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $v = 25 \text{ m/min}$, $Q = 60 \text{ kg/h} = 1000 \text{ g/min}$, $w = 1.2 \text{ m}$, $\rho = 0.95 \text{ g/cm}^3$

**(i) Basis weight:**

$$
B = \frac{Q}{v \times w} = \frac{1000}{25 \times 1.2} = \frac{1000}{30} = 33.33 \text{ g/m}^2
$$

**(ii) Fabric thickness:**

$$
t = \frac{B}{\rho} = \frac{33.33}{0.95} = 35.09 \,\mu\text{m}
$$

**(iii) Percentage deviation:**

$$
\% \text{ Deviation} = \frac{B_{\text{actual}} - B_{\text{target}}}{B_{\text{target}}} \times 100 = \frac{42 - 40}{40} \times 100 = 5\%
$$

**Final Results:** Basis weight = **33.33 g/m²**, Thickness = **35.09 μm**, Deviation = **+5% (above target)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (3–4 marks lost):** Forgetting to convert kg/h to g/s or mm to m. Always show the conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Wrong Conversion Factor in B = ρ·t (2–3 marks lost):** Memorizing a factor of "10" or "0.1" and applying it mechanically. The correct dimensional analysis gives a **factor of 1** (with the stated units).
> 3. **Confusing Basis Weight with Thickness (2 marks lost):** These are not the same — a high-density material at low thickness can have the same gsm as a low-density material at high thickness.
> 4. **Ignoring "Design Basis" interpretation (3–4 marks lost):** Some questions test the **engineering-design** meaning of "Basis" rather than the **grammage** meaning. Read the question stem carefully.
> 5. **Not Converting to Consistent Units in Yield (1 mark lost):** Yield m²/kg requires $1000/B$ where $B$ is in g/m².

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — "Basis":**

- ✅ **Basis Weight (B)** = mass per unit area of a polymer product; standard unit is **g/m² (gsm)**.
- ✅ **Master Equation:** $B = \rho \times t$, with $\rho$ in g/cm³, $t$ in μm, and $B$ in g/m² (factor = **1**).
- ✅ **Yield (Y)** = $1000/B$ in m²/kg.
- ✅ **Line Speed:** $v = \dfrac{Q}{B \times w}$, with consistent SI units.
- ✅ **Thickness from gsm:** $t = B / \rho$ (in μm when ρ is in g/cm³).
- ✅ **Typical gsm Ranges:** LDPE film 15–50, HDPE film 10–30, PP sheet 100–500, PET sheet 100–300, non-woven 10–200.
- ✅ **Design Basis** of a polymer machine includes: throughput, pressure, temperature, residence time, clamping force (for IM), die dimensions (for extrusion).
- ✅ **Quality Tolerance:** Most film applications require ±3–5% gsm tolerance; medical/food applications require ±1–2%.
- ✅ **Sensors Used:** Beta-ray gauges, X-ray gauges, and near-infrared (NIR) sensors for online basis weight measurement.
- ✅ **KTU Numerical Trap:** Always verify the factor in $B = \rho \times t$ by dimensional analysis — the factor is **1** with the unit combination given, **not 10 or 0.1**.
- ✅ **Engineering Application:** Basis weight directly controls **die gap, line speed, blow-up ratio, cooling rate, and winder tension** in polymer film/sheet lines.
- ✅ **Regulatory Context:** gsm specifications are mandated in **food packaging, medical devices, geotextiles, and hygiene products**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of polymer processing and manufacturing, **Process Variables** are defined as the controllable input parameters (independent variables) of a polymer processing machine that are directly set, monitored, and adjusted by the machine operator or process engineer to achieve a desired output (dependent variables) such as part dimensions, mechanical properties, surface finish, weight, and cycle time.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCPOT503, process variables form the **"Controllable Knob Set"** of any polymer conversion process. The universally acknowledged **four primary process variables** in polymer processing (especially injection molding) are the classical "4-M's" of polymer processing, often remembered as **Temperature, Pressure, Flow, and Cooling (Time)**. Secondary variables include screw speed, back pressure, clamping force, humidity, and shot size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> A student must be able to identify, classify, and analyze the effect of each process variable on the final product quality. The interplay between these variables defines the **"Process Window"** — the bounded operating region within which all quality specifications are simultaneously satisfied.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of polymer processing like **baking a cake in an oven**:

- The **Temperature** of the oven is like the **melt/mold temperature** — set too low, the cake (polymer part) won't form properly; set too high, it burns (degrades).
- The **Pressure** is like the force you use to push the batter into the pan — too little leaves voids (short shots); too much causes overflow (flash).
- The **Flow rate** is how fast you pour the batter — too fast creates bubbles and splashing (weld lines, jetting); too slow makes the cake uneven.
- The **Time** (cooling) is how long you keep it in the oven before removing — pull it out too early, it collapses (warping); too late, it overcooks (degradation).

Every baker (operator) learns to balance these knobs — exactly what a polymer process engineer does on an injection molding, extrusion, or blow molding machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (Industry):**
> - **Injection Pressure Range:** 35 MPa – 140 MPa (350 – 1400 bar)
> - **Melt Temperature Range:** 180 °C – 320 °C (depending on polymer)
> - **Mold Temperature Range:** 20 °C – 120 °C
> - **Clamping Force Range:** 50 kN – 10,000 kN
> - **Cycle Time Range:** 5 s – 120 s
> - **Shear Rate in Processing:** 100 s⁻¹ – 100,000 s⁻¹

## 1.3 Classification of Process Variables

Process variables can be broadly classified as follows:

**A. Primary (Major) Process Variables:**
1. **Temperature** (Barrel zone T, Melt T, Mold T)
2. **Pressure** (Injection P, Holding P, Back P, Cavity P)
3. **Flow rate / Velocity** (Injection speed, Screw speed)
4. **Time** (Injection time, Holding time, Cooling time, Cycle time)

**B. Secondary (Auxiliary) Process Variables:**
1. Screw rotation speed (rpm)
2. Back pressure
3. Shot size / Metering stroke
4. Clamping force
5. Cushion (residual melt in barrel)
6. Moisture content of polymer
7. Ambient humidity
8. Mould cooling channel flow rate

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 2024 Examinations:** Always remember to relate every process variable to a tangible **quality outcome** (dimensional accuracy, strength, surface finish, warpage). Examiners award marks for the *cause-effect* linkage, not just the listing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Primary Process Variables — Detailed Logic

### 2.1.1 Temperature (T)

- **Barrel/Heater Zone Temperatures:** Set the melt viscosity before injection.
- **Melt Temperature (T_melt):** Actual temperature of polymer melt at the nozzle.
- **Mold Temperature (T_mold):** Controls surface skin formation, crystallinity (in semi-crystalline polymers), and shrinkage.
- **Why it matters:** Temperature directly governs **viscosity**, which dictates how easily the polymer flows into the mold cavity.

### 2.1.2 Pressure (P)

- **Injection Pressure (P_inj):** Force applied by the screw/ram to push the melt.
- **Holding/Packing Pressure (P_hold):** Maintained pressure to compensate for volumetric shrinkage during cooling.
- **Back Pressure (P_back):** Resistance offered to the screw during plasticating.
- **Cavity Pressure (P_cav):** The pressure developed inside the mold cavity — a key indicator of part quality.
- **Why it matters:** Pressure determines **part density**, **dimensional accuracy**, and the occurrence of defects (short shots, flash, sink marks, voids).

### 2.1.3 Flow / Velocity (v or Q)

- **Injection Speed/Flow Rate:** How fast the melt fills the cavity.
- **Screw Speed (N):** RPM of the plasticating screw (extrusion/injection).
- **Why it matters:** Governs **shear rate (γ̇)**, which affects viscosity (non-Newtonian behavior), molecular orientation, and surface finish.

### 2.1.4 Time (t)

- **Injection Time (t_inj):** Time to fill the cavity.
- **Holding Time (t_hold):** Time pressure is maintained.
- **Cooling Time (t_cool):** Time for the part to solidify enough for ejection.
- **Cycle Time (t_cycle):** Total time for one complete molding cycle.
- **Why it matters:** Time controls **productivity**, **crystallinity**, and **residual stresses**.

## 2.2 The Process Window Concept

The **Process Window** is a multi-dimensional operating region in which all part-quality specifications are met simultaneously. Visualizing a 2-D slice:

- X-axis: Melt Temperature
- Y-axis: Injection Pressure
- The enclosed rectangle = **Process Window**

Any combination of (T, P) **inside** the window → acceptable part.
Any combination **outside** → defective part.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A wider process window = more robust process. A narrow window = sensitive process prone to defects. The aim of DOE (Design of Experiments) is to widen the process window by optimizing variable settings.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Volumetric Flow Rate (Hagen-Poiseuille) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$ | m³/s | Polymer melt flow in runners |
| 2 | Shear Rate (in circular channel) | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}}$ | s⁻¹ | Velocity gradient in melt |
| 3 | Shear Stress | $\tau = \mu \dot{\gamma}$ | Pa | Newton's law of viscosity |
| 4 | Power-Law Viscosity Model | $\tau = K (\dot{\gamma})^{n}$ | Pa | Non-Newtonian polymer melts |
| 5 | Apparent Viscosity (Power Law) | $\eta_{a} = K (\dot{\gamma})^{n-1}$ | Pa·s | Pseudoplastic behavior |
| 6 | Cooling Time (Simplified) | $t_{c} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha} \ln \left[ \dfrac{8(T_{m} - T_{w})}{\pi^{2}(T_{d} - T_{w})} \right]$ | s | Solidification in mold |
| 7 | Cycle Time | $t_{cyc} = t_{inj} + t_{hold} + t_{cool} + t_{open} + t_{eject} + t_{close}$ | s | Production rate |
| 8 | Clamping Force | $F_{clamp} \geq P_{cav} \cdot A_{proj}$ | N | Prevent mold opening/flash |
| 9 | Injection Pressure (total) | $P_{inj} = \Delta P_{runner} + \Delta P_{gate} + P_{cav}$ | Pa | Hydraulic system demand |
| 10 | Production Rate | $R = \dfrac{3600 \cdot n_{cav}}{t_{cyc}}$ | parts/hr | Manufacturing throughput |
| 11 | Volumetric Shrinkage | $S = \dfrac{V_{mold} - V_{part}}{V_{mold}} \times 100$ | % | Dimensional control |
| 12 | Shear Heating (Power) | $\dot{W} = \tau \cdot \dot{\gamma} = \eta \dot{\gamma}^{2}$ | W/m³ | Temperature rise in melt |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Legend:**
> R = channel radius, L = channel length, μ = viscosity, K = consistency index, n = power-law index, α = thermal diffusivity, s = wall thickness, T_m = melt temperature, T_w = mold wall temperature, T_d = ejection temperature, n_cav = number of cavities.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Process window optimization in injection molding of dashboard panels to minimize warpage.
- **Medical Devices:** Precise control of process variables to manufacture defect-free syringes and IV components.
- **Packaging:** Extrusion blow molding of PET bottles — temperature profile directly affects bottle clarity (crystallinity).
- **Piping Systems:** PE/PP pipe extrusion — cooling rate determines crystallization and long-term pressure rating (MRS).
- **6-Sigma Quality:** DOE (Design of Experiments) on process variables is a fundamental step in achieving Six-Sigma quality in polymer manufacturing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation for Polymer Melt Flow

The Hagen-Poiseuille equation is the fundamental governing equation for the laminar flow of a viscous fluid (including polymer melt) through a circular cross-section (e.g., runner, barrel, or capillary rheometer).

**Starting Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, laminar, isothermal flow.
2. Incompressible Newtonian fluid (we later extend to non-Newtonian).
3. Fully developed flow (no end effects).
4. No-slip condition at the wall.

**Step 1 — Force Balance on a Cylindrical Fluid Element of Radius r and Length L:**

The pressure force acting on the cross-section is balanced by the viscous shear force on the cylindrical surface.

$$
\Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2} = -\tau \cdot (2 \pi r L)
$$

> The negative sign indicates that shear stress opposes flow (acts in the −z direction when pressure drops in +z).

**Step 2 — Solve for Shear Stress τ:**

$$
\tau = -\dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L}
$$

> This shows shear stress varies **linearly** with radial position r. At the wall (r = R), τ is maximum.

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity:**

For a Newtonian fluid, $\tau = \mu \dfrac{dv}{dr}$. Substituting:

$$
\mu \dfrac{dv}{dr} = -\dfrac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L}
$$

**Step 4 — Separate Variables and Integrate:**

$$
dv = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot r \, dr
$$

Integrating from the wall (r = R, v = 0) to a general point r:

$$
\int_{0}^{v} dv = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{R}^{r} r \, dr
$$

$$
v(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right)
$$

> This is the **parabolic velocity profile**. Maximum velocity is at the centerline (r = 0).

**Step 5 — Compute Volumetric Flow Rate Q:**

$$
Q = \int_{0}^{R} v(r) \cdot (2 \pi r) \, dr
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{2 \pi \Delta P}{4 \mu L} \int_{0}^{R} r \left( R^{2} - r^{2} \right) dr
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ \dfrac{R^{2} r^{2}}{2} - \dfrac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R}
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left( \dfrac{R^{4}}{2} - \dfrac{R^{4}}{4} \right) = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \dfrac{R^{4}}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}}
$$

**Engineering Insight (KTU Practical Takeaway):**
The fourth-power dependence on R means **doubling the runner diameter increases flow rate 16 times** for the same pressure drop. Conversely, halving the diameter requires 16× the pressure — explaining why thin runners cause filling problems.

## 3.2 Derivation: Wall Shear Rate in a Circular Channel

The wall shear rate (γ̇_w) is critical for polymer processing because viscosity is shear-rate dependent.

**Step 1 — Differentiate the Velocity Profile:**

From $v(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}(R^{2} - r^{2})$:

$$
\dfrac{dv}{dr} = -\dfrac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot r
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at the Wall (r = R):**

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \left\vert \dfrac{dv}{dr} \right\vert_{r=R} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 \mu L}
$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate ΔP Using Q:**

From Hagen-Poiseuille: $\Delta P = \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}}$. Substituting:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{R}{2 \mu L} \cdot \dfrac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}} = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}}}
$$

> **KTU Note:** This is the basis of the Rabinowitsch correction used in capillary rheometry to obtain true shear rate for non-Newtonian fluids.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Each Process Variable on Part Quality

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam tool**. Memorize the directional effects.

| Process Variable ↑ (Increased) | Dimensional Shrinkage | Warpage | Sink Marks | Voids | Surface Gloss | Cycle Time | Mechanical Strength (Tensile) |
|--------------------------------|----------------------|---------|------------|-------|---------------|------------|------------------------------|
| **Melt Temperature** | Decreases | Decreases (initially) | Decreases | Decreases | Increases (initially) | Decreases | Decreases (degradation risk) |
| **Mold Temperature** | Decreases | May increase (uneven cooling) | Decreases | Decreases | Increases | **Increases** | Increases (better skin) |
| **Injection Pressure** | Decreases (better packing) | Marginal effect | Decreases | Decreases | Increases | Marginal | Increases |
| **Holding Pressure** | Decreases | Decreases | Decreases significantly | Decreases significantly | Increases | Marginal | Increases |
| **Holding Time** | Decreases | Decreases | Decreases | Decreases | Marginal | **Increases** | Increases |
| **Cooling Time** | None (after ejection) | Decreases (if longer) | Marginal | Marginal | Marginal | **Increases (unproductive)** | Increases (if crystallinity) |
| **Injection Speed** | Marginal | Decreases | Decreases | Decreases | Increases | Marginal | Increases (less orientation) |
| **Screw Speed** | Marginal | Marginal | Marginal | Marginal | Marginal | Decreases | Marginal |
| **Back Pressure** | Decreases (better melt homogeneity) | Marginal | Decreases | Decreases | Increases | **Increases** | Increases |
| **Mold Cooling Rate** | Increases shrinkage | Can increase warpage | Marginal | Marginal | Increases | **Decreases (faster)** | Variable |

> **Read this matrix as:** "If variable ↑ → outcome increases / decreases / no effect."

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A polymer melt (μ = 500 Pa·s) flows through a circular runner of radius R = 2 mm and length L = 80 mm under a pressure drop ΔP = 10 MPa. Calculate:
(a) Volumetric flow rate Q
(b) Wall shear rate γ̇_w
(c) Wall shear stress τ_w

**Given Data:**
- μ = 500 Pa·s
- R = 2 mm = 0.002 m
- L = 80 mm = 0.080 m
- ΔP = 10 MPa = 10 × 10⁶ Pa

**Solution (a):** Using Hagen-Poiseuille:

$$
Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L} = \dfrac{\pi (0.002)^{4} \times 10 \times 10^{6}}{8 \times 500 \times 0.080}
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-11} \times 10^{7}}{320} = \dfrac{5.027 \times 10^{-4}}{320}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 1.571 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \; (\approx 1.571 \, \text{cm}^{3}/\text{s})}
$$

**Solution (b):** Wall shear rate:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}} = \dfrac{4 \times 1.571 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times (0.002)^{3}}
$$

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{6.284 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi \times 8 \times 10^{-9}} = \dfrac{6.284 \times 10^{-6}}{2.513 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_{w} \approx 250 \, \text{s}^{-1}}
$$

**Solution (c):** Wall shear stress:

$$
\tau_{w} = \mu \dot{\gamma}_{w} = 500 \times 250 = 125{,}000 \, \text{Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{w} = 0.125 \, \text{MPa}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A shear rate of 250 s⁻¹ falls in the medium range for polymer processing (typical: 100 – 100,000 s⁻¹). For a power-law fluid (e.g., polypropylene with n ≈ 0.4), the actual viscosity at this shear rate would be much lower than 500 Pa·s, increasing Q significantly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Interrelationship of Process Variables in Injection Molding

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS["CONTROLLABLE INPUTS"]
        A1[Temperature]
        A2[Pressure]
        A3[Flow Velocity]
        A4[Time]
        A5[Screw RPM]
        A6[Back Pressure]
        A7[Clamp Force]
        A8[Moisture Content]
    end

    subgraph MACHINE["POLYMER PROCESSING MACHINE"]
        B1[Barrel Screw Plasticating Unit]
        B2[Injection Hydraulic System]
        B3[Mold Cavity Assembly]
        B4[Cooling Channel Network]
    end

    subgraph INTERMEDIATE["INTERMEDIATE STATE VARIABLES"]
        C1[Melt Viscosity]
        C2[Shear Rate]
        C3[Cavity Pressure]
        C4[Mold Surface Temperature]
        C5[Residual Stress Field]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS["PART QUALITY OUTPUTS"]
        D1[Dimensions and Tolerance]
        D2[Weight and Density]
        D3[Surface Finish Gloss]
        D4[Mechanical Properties]
        D5[Warpage and Shrinkage]
        D6[Cycle Time Productivity]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B1
    A4 --> B4
    A5 --> B1
    A6 --> B1
    A7 --> B3
    A8 --> B1

    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B2 --> C3
    B3 --> C3
    B4 --> C4
    B3 --> C5

    C1 --> D2
    C2 --> D4
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    C4 --> D3
    C4 --> D5
    C5 --> D5
    C5 --> D4

    A1 --> C1
    A2 --> C3
    A3 --> C2
    A4 --> C3
    A4 --> D6
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Each controllable input flows into a machine subsystem, which produces intermediate state variables (viscosity, cavity pressure, etc.). These intermediate states are the **true predictors** of final part quality — this is why modern injection molding machines use **cavity pressure sensors** for closed-loop process control.

## 4.2 Process Window Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WINDOW["2D PROCESS WINDOW - MELT TEMP vs INJECTION PRESSURE"]
        P1[Lower Left Corner: Short Shot Zone] --> P2[Window Center: Optimal Zone]
        P2 --> P3[Upper Right Corner: Flash and Degradation Zone]
        P2 --> P4[Upper Left: Flash without Fill Zone]
        P2 --> P5[Lower Right: Short Shot with Degradation Zone]
    end

    X[Melt Temperature Increasing] -.-> WINDOW
    Y[Injection Pressure Increasing] -.-> WINDOW
```

## 4.3 Injection Molding Cycle Phases (Time Variable)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Phase 1: Mold Closing] --> B[Phase 2: Injection and Fill]
    B --> C[Phase 3: Holding and Packing]
    C --> D[Phase 4: Cooling and Solidification]
    D --> E[Phase 5: Mold Opening]
    E --> F[Phase 6: Part Ejection]
    F --> G[Phase 7: Plasticating Metering]
    G --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** The total cycle time is the sum of all 7 phases. Phases 3, 4, and 7 dominate the cycle time. Optimization focuses on reducing cooling time (Phase 4) and plasticating time (Phase 7) since they are material/property dependent.

## 4.4 Effect-Cause Matrix Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CAUSES["PROCESS VARIABLE CHANGES"]
        V1[Increase Melt T]
        V2[Increase Hold P]
        V3[Increase Cool T]
        V4[Increase Inj Speed]
    end

    subgraph MECHANISMS["MECHANISMS"]
        M1[Lower Viscosity]
        M2[Better Cavity Packing]
        M3[Higher Crystallinity]
        M4[Shear Thinning]
    end

    subgraph EFFECTS["PART QUALITY OUTCOMES"]
        E1[Reduced Sink Marks]
        E2[Reduced Voids]
        E3[Improved Tensile Strength]
        E4[Better Surface Finish]
    end

    V1 --> M1 --> E4
    V2 --> M2 --> E1
    V2 --> M2 --> E2
    V3 --> M3 --> E3
    V4 --> M4 --> E4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**"Define process variables in polymer processing. List the four primary process variables with one example of each."**

**Model Answer:**
Process variables are the controllable input parameters of a polymer processing machine that are set by the operator to achieve desired part quality. The four primary process variables are:
1. **Temperature** (e.g., melt temperature = 230 °C for polypropylene)
2. **Pressure** (e.g., injection pressure = 80 MPa)
3. **Flow/Velocity** (e.g., injection speed = 50 mm/s)
4. **Time** (e.g., cooling time = 25 s)

*(Word limit: ~50–60 words as per KTU 3-mark scheme.)*

---

### **Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**"What is meant by 'Process Window' in polymer manufacturing? Why is a wider process window desirable?"**

**Model Answer:**
A process window is the bounded range of process variable settings within which all part-quality specifications (dimensions, strength, surface finish, weight) are simultaneously satisfied. A wider process window is desirable because it provides **greater robustness** against disturbances (variations in raw material, ambient temperature, machine wear), reduces scrap rate, and makes the process easier to control at production scale. It is widened through Design of Experiments (DoE) and Statistical Process Control (SPC).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question 1(A): [KTU University Exam – Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the effect of **temperature, pressure, and time** on the quality of injection-molded products. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable sketch, explain the concept of **shear rate and viscosity** in polymer melts and their relevance to injection molding. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Temperature Effects:**
- **Melt Temperature ↑:** Viscosity decreases (exponentially) → easier cavity fill, less short-shot risk, better surface gloss, **but** prolonged exposure causes thermal degradation (chain scission), reducing molecular weight and mechanical strength.
- **Mold Temperature ↑:** Slower skin formation, lower frozen-in orientation, **lower shrinkage**, better surface replication, increased crystallinity (in semi-crystalline polymers like PP, PA) → improved stiffness but **longer cycle time**. Uneven mold temperature causes warpage.
- **Effect on Sink Marks:** Higher mold T eliminates sink marks (compensates for volumetric shrinkage).
- **Effect on Weld Lines:** Higher melt T improves weld-line strength (better interdiffusion).

**Pressure Effects:**
- **Injection Pressure ↑:** Ensures complete cavity fill, reduces short shots, **but** excessive pressure causes flash, mold deflection, and high residual stresses.
- **Holding/Packing Pressure ↑:** Most critical for dimensional accuracy and sink-mark elimination. Compensates for **volumetric shrinkage** (1–3% for amorphous; up to 20% for semi-crystalline). Insufficient holding pressure → voids, sink marks.
- **Back Pressure ↑:** Improves melt homogeneity (air removal), uniform melt temperature, **but** excessive back pressure degrades polymer and lengthens cycle.
- **Cavity Pressure:** Direct indicator of part quality; monitored in modern closed-loop control.

**Time Effects:**
- **Injection Time ↓** (faster fill): Lower cooling during fill, better surface finish, **but** may cause jetting and air entrapment.
- **Holding Time ↑:** Allows more material to be packed into cavity → reduces voids, **but** time bound by gate freeze time (after which holding pressure has no effect).
- **Cooling Time:** Often **50–80%** of total cycle time. Governed by **Fourier's law of heat conduction** through the part wall. Inadequate cooling → part ejection warpage, sink marks, dimensional errors.
- **Cycle Time Optimization:** Direct productivity impact — reducing cooling time by 1 s in a 30-s cycle = 3.3% production increase.

**Conclusion:** The three variables (T, P, t) are **interdependent** — e.g., higher T allows lower P; longer t compensates for inadequate P. Optimal setting requires a DoE-based approach.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Shear Rate Definition:**
Shear rate (γ̇) is the rate of change of velocity with respect to distance perpendicular to flow direction. In a circular channel, the wall shear rate is:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^{3}}
$$

**Viscosity Definition:**
Viscosity (μ or η) is the internal friction of a fluid — resistance to flow.

**Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian Behavior:**
- **Newtonian fluids** (water, oil): viscosity is constant regardless of shear rate → τ = μγ̇.
- **Polymer melts** are **non-Newtonian, pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**: viscosity decreases with increasing shear rate.

**Power-Law Model (Ostwald-de Waele):**

$$
\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n} \quad \text{and} \quad \eta_{a} = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}
$$

Where:
- K = consistency index (Pa·sⁿ)
- n = power-law index; n < 1 for shear-thinning; n = 1 for Newtonian; n > 1 for shear-thickening.

**Relevance to Injection Molding:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Shear Rate at Gates and Walls] --> B[Shear Thinning]
    B --> C[Apparent Viscosity Decreases]
    C --> D[Easy Flow Through Thin Sections]
    A --> E[Low Shear Rate at Centerline]
    E --> F[Higher Viscosity]
    F --> G[Flow Front Stability]
```

- In the **gate region** and at cavity walls: shear rate is very high (10³ – 10⁵ s⁻¹) → viscosity drops 100–1000× → easy filling of thin walls.
- In the **flow front center**: shear rate is low → higher viscosity → better flow front stability (less jetting).
- Typical polymer viscosities at processing shear rates: **100–10,000 Pa·s** (much higher than water at 10⁻³ Pa·s).

**Conclusion:** Understanding shear-rate-dependent viscosity is fundamental to mold design (gate location, runner sizing) and process optimization in injection molding.

---

### **Question 1(B): [Alternative Choice for Internal Option]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **process variables in extrusion** of polymers and their effect on product quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With suitable equations, derive the **cooling time formula** for an injection-molded part and discuss the factors affecting cooling time. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Extrusion Process Variables:**

**1. Temperature Profile (Barrel Zones + Die):**
- **Barrel Zones (T1 → T4):** T1 (feed) < T2 < T3 < T4 (metering) < Die T.
- **Effect ↑ Die T:** Lower melt viscosity, increased throughput, **but** die swell increases, surface may be rough (sharkskin/melt fracture at very high T), degradation risk.
- **Effect ↓ Die T:** Better dimensional accuracy, lower die swell, **but** risk of freezing melt in die.

**2. Screw Speed (N, rpm):**
- Directly controls **throughput (Q)** through the drag-flow equation:

$$
Q_{drag} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}
$$

- **Effect ↑ N:** Higher throughput, higher shear rate (more shear thinning → lower viscosity), higher shear heating (T rise), reduced residence time (less degradation), **but** may cause surging, pulsation, and poor mixing.

**3. Die Pressure (P_die):**
- Determined by die resistance and melt viscosity.
- **Effect ↑ P_die:** Better mixing, compact output, **but** higher motor load, possible melt fracture.

**4. Throughput / Output Rate (Q):**
- Function of screw speed, screw geometry, melt viscosity, and back pressure.

**5. Back Pressure (in extruder inlet region):**
- Improves melt homogeneity and air removal, **but** reduces throughput.

**6. Cooling Rate (in calibration/sizing unit):**
- Critical for crystalline polymers (PE, PP) — affects crystallinity, density, and mechanical properties.

**Quality Outcomes:**
- **Dimensional accuracy:** Controlled by haul-off speed and cooling rate.
- **Surface finish:** Function of melt T and die-land length.
- **Mechanical strength:** Function of orientation (which depends on draw-down ratio) and crystallinity.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Cooling Time Derivation:**

Cooling time in injection molding refers to the time required for the part to cool from melt temperature to ejection temperature, after which the part is rigid enough to be ejected without distortion.

**Assumptions:**
1. One-dimensional heat conduction through the wall thickness.
2. Constant mold wall temperature (T_w).
3. Polymer solidifies at T_melt and cools to T_d (demolding temperature).
4. Negligible heat transfer at part-mold interface (or constant T_w).

**Governing Equation (1-D Transient Heat Conduction):**

$$
\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}
$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
- At x = 0 (centerline of part): $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x} = 0$ (symmetry)
- At x = s/2 (mold wall): T = T_w (constant)

**Initial Condition:**
- At t = 0: T = T_m (uniform melt temperature)

**Solution (Fourier Series):**

$$
\dfrac{T(x,t) - T_{w}}{T_{m} - T_{w}} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{4}{(2n+1)\pi} \sin\!\left(\dfrac{(2n+1)\pi x}{s}\right) \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2} \alpha t}{s^{2}}\right)
$$

**At the Centerline (x = 0):** All sine terms vanish except the first term. After taking the natural logarithm and considering the first term only:

$$
t_{c} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha} \ln\!\left[ \dfrac{8(T_{m} - T_{w})}{\pi^{2}(T_{d} - T_{w})} \right]
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{c} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha} \ln\!\left[ \dfrac{8(T_{m} - T_{w})}{\pi^{2}(T_{d} - T_{w})} \right]}
$$

**Factors Affecting Cooling Time:**
1. **Wall thickness (s):** Cooling time varies with **s²** — doubling thickness quadruples cooling time. This is the dominant factor.
2. **Thermal diffusivity (α) of polymer:** α = k / (ρ·C_p). Higher α → faster cooling.
3. **Mold wall temperature (T_w):** Lower T_w → faster cooling.
4. **Melt temperature (T_m):** Higher T_m → more heat to remove → longer cooling time.
5. **Ejection temperature (T_d):** Lower T_d requirement → longer cooling time.
6. **Heat transfer coefficient at mold-part interface:** Depends on contact pressure and surface roughness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** cycle time with cooling time — cycle time includes all 7 phases; cooling time is only Phase 4.
> 2. **Do not assume** polymer behaves as Newtonian in calculations — most polymers are **shear-thinning**; use power-law or Carreau model.
> 3. **Cooling time scales with s², not s** — this is the most common error. Students often write $t_c \propto s$ instead of $t_c \propto s^{2}$.
> 4. **Always mention the four primary process variables in order** (T, P, Flow, Time) — examiners penalize missing or out-of-order listing.
> 5. **Relate variables to specific defects** (e.g., low holding P → sink marks; high melt T → degradation) — generic answers lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Process Variables**

- **Definition:** Process variables = controllable input parameters of polymer processing machine that determine part quality and productivity.

- **Four Primary Variables (Mnemonic: "TPFT — Temperature, Pressure, Flow, Time"):**
  1. **Temperature** → governs viscosity, crystallinity, surface finish, degradation.
  2. **Pressure** → governs density, shrinkage compensation, dimensional accuracy.
  3. **Flow/Velocity** → governs shear rate, orientation, surface defects (jetting).
  4. **Time** → governs productivity, crystallinity, residual stress relief.

- **Process Window:** Multi-dimensional bounded region of process variable settings where all quality specs are met. Wider window = more robust process.

- **Key Equations to Memorize:**
  - Hagen-Poiseuille: $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$
  - Wall shear rate: $\dot{\gamma}_{w} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^{3}}$
  - Power-law model: $\tau = K\dot{\gamma}^{n}$
  - Cooling time: $t_{c} = \dfrac{s^{2}}{\pi^{2} \alpha} \ln \left[ \dfrac{8(T_{m} - T_{w})}{\pi^{2}(T_{d} - T_{w})} \right]$
  - Clamping force: $F_{clamp} \geq P_{cav} \cdot A_{proj}$
  - Cycle time: $t_{cyc} = \sum$ of all 7 phases

- **Polymer melts are shear-thinning (pseudoplastic):** n < 1 in power-law; viscosity decreases with shear rate.

- **Cooling time is the dominant component of cycle time** (50–80%); scales as **s²** (square of wall thickness).

- **Defect-Variable Mapping (must memorize for KTU):**
  - Short shot → low P, low T, fast flow, blocked vents.
  - Flash → high P, low clamp force, worn mold.
  - Sink marks → low holding P, insufficient holding time, high T_mold.
  - Voids → low holding P, thick sections, fast cooling.
  - Warpage → uneven cooling, high residual stress, uneven mold T.
  - Weld lines → low T, low P, poor venting.
  - Jetting → too-high injection speed, poor gate design.

- **SPC & DoE:** Modern process optimization uses Statistical Process Control and Design of Experiments to widen the process window and minimize variation.

- **Standard Units to Remember:**
  - Pressure: 1 MPa = 10 bar = 145 psi
  - Shear rate unit: s⁻¹
  - Viscosity: Pa·s (1 Pa·s = 10 Poise)
  - Thermal diffusivity α: m²/s (typical for polymers: 10⁻⁷ m²/s)

- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw a labeled diagram where possible (process window, shear rate profile, cycle phases). Marks are awarded for diagrams + cause-effect linkage, not just definitions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Injection and Stretch Blow Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Injection and Stretch Blow Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Injection Moulding — Formal Academic Definition

**Injection Moulding (IM)** is a high-volume, closed-loop, cyclic manufacturing process in which a **thermoplastic** or **thermosetting** polymer melt is injected under high pressure (typically **70–140 MPa**) into a closed, water-cooled (or oil-heated) mould cavity, where it is solidified by heat extraction, and the finished part is ejected after a precisely timed cooling cycle. It is the single most widely used primary plastics processing technique in the world, accounting for nearly **32% of all polymeric part production by mass**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Injection moulding forms the backbone of the "Primary Shaping Processes" module. Mastery of this topic carries direct weightage in ESE questions involving machine specifications, cycle-time breakdown, and defect analysis.

### 1.2 Stretch Blow Moulding — Formal Academic Definition

**Stretch Blow Moulding (SBM)** is a secondary forming process used to produce **biaxially oriented hollow thermoplastic containers** (PET bottles, jars, etc.) from a previously injection-moulded or extrusion-blown **preform**. The preform is heated above its glass transition temperature ($T_g \approx 75$–$85^\circ\text{C}$ for PET), then mechanically stretched in the axial direction by a **stretch rod** and simultaneously inflated in the hoop direction by high-pressure air (typically **0.8–4.0 MPa**). This **biaxial orientation** dramatically improves mechanical strength, clarity, and barrier properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Biaxial Orientation:** The simultaneous stretching of a polymer in two perpendicular directions aligns the molecular chains in the load-bearing plane, increasing tensile strength by **2–5×** and reducing gas permeability by **30–60%** compared to an unoriented equivalent.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

| Process | Everyday Analogy | Why It Works |
|---|---|---|
| **Injection Moulding** | A giant, industrial, automated **syringe injecting hot chocolate into a metal waffle iron** | The barrel acts as the syringe, the screw/plunger as the plunger, melt is the chocolate, and the mould is the waffle iron that shapes AND cools it. |
| **Stretch Blow Moulding** | **Blowing up a small soft balloon (preform)** by first pulling it down (stretch rod) and then blowing air into it inside a shaped mould | The two-step stretching (axial + radial) is what gives PET bottles their famous crystal clarity and shatter resistance. |
| **Mould Cavity** | A **waffle iron's patterned plates** | It defines the final shape, extracts heat, and provides surface finish. |
| **Clamping Unit** | A **bench vise holding the waffle iron shut** so the chocolate cannot squirt out | The clamping tonnage must resist the "mould-opening force" generated by the melt pressure. |

### 1.4 Classification Snapshot

**Injection Moulding** is divided into:
1. **Conventional (Reciprocating Screw) IM** — the universal workhorse
2. **Two-Platen IM** — for very large parts (e.g., automotive bumpers)
3. **All-Electric IM** — high precision, cleanroom-compatible
4. **Insert/Overmoulding IM** — multi-material
5. **Micro-Injection Moulding** — $\mu\text{g}$-scale parts

**Stretch Blow Moulding** is divided into:
1. **Single-Stage (1-Step) SBM** — preform made and blown in the same machine
2. **Two-Stage (2-Step / Reheat) SBM** — preform made separately, reheated and blown later

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for IM Cycle:** **I**nject → **P**ack-Hold → **C**ool → **E**ject → **P**lasticate → **S**hut. Remember **"I-P-C-E-P-S"** — this is the universally expected KTU answer format for "Explain the injection moulding cycle."

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Injection Moulding Machine — Anatomical Breakdown

The modern **Reciprocating Screw Injection Moulding Machine** consists of two functionally and structurally distinct units:

#### A. The Injection Unit (Clamped to the stationary platen via a tie-bar/roller system)
- **Hopper:** Gravity-fed reservoir for granular polymer (pellets).
- **Barrel (Heated Cylinder):** A bimetallic (nitrided steel) tube divided into **3–5 independent heater zones**. The heaters establish a precise axial temperature profile: $\Delta T_{profile} \approx 30$–$50^\circ\text{C}$ from feed throat to nozzle tip.
- **Reciprocating Screw:** A hybrid of extruder screw + plunger. Performs **three simultaneous functions** during plastication: (i) conveying, (ii) compression/melting (via decreasing flight depth), (iii) metering. During injection, the entire screw translates axially like a plunger.
- **Non-Return Valve (NRV / Check Ring):** A mechanical one-way valve at the screw tip that prevents back-flow of melt over the flights during the injection stroke. Without it, the screw would act as a metering extruder and starve the cavity.
- **Nozzle:** A short, replaceable, radiused or tapered orifice that seals against the mould sprue bushing. Nozzle types: **open, needle-shut-off, shut-off (hydraulic/mechanical).**

#### B. The Clamping Unit (Provides the closing/opening force on the mould)
- **Toggle (Mechanical) Clamp:** Uses a 4- or 5- knuckle articulated linkage. Force amplification at lock-up: typically **20:1 to 40:1**. Cheaper, faster, but force varies with daylight.
- **Hydraulic Clamp:** Direct-acting hydraulic cylinder. Constant clamping force throughout the stroke, slower, but more controllable.
- **Hybrid (Hydromechanical) Clamp:** Combines toggle for fast close + hydraulic cylinder for final lock-up and accurate tonnage control.
- **Tie Bars:** Four high-tensile steel columns guiding platen motion (or tiebar-less designs for automotive bumpers).
- **Moving Platen, Stationary Platen, Ejector System (hydraulic/mechanical).**

### 2.2 The Six-Stage Injection Moulding Cycle — Stepwise Logic

| Stage | Duration (% of cycle) | Physical Action | Process Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Mould Close** | 5–10% | Moving platen closes; safety interlocks engage | Close velocity, clamp ramp |
| **2. Clamp Build** | 1–2% | Toggle lockup or hydraulic pressure rises to full tonnage | Clamp force $F_c$ |
| **3. Injection (Fill)** | 5–10% | Screw drives melt forward; cavity fills to ~95–98% | $P_{inj}$, $V_{inj}$, $T_{melt}$ |
| **4. Packing/Holding** | 10–20% | Switch-over to pressure control; melt replenishes shrinkage | $P_{pack}$, $t_{pack}$ |
| **5. Cooling (and Plastication)** | 40–60% | Screw rotates, plasticates next shot; part solidifies | $T_{cool}$, $t_{cool}$ |
| **6. Eject** | 5–10% | Mould opens; ejector pins push part out; safety drop | $V_{open}$, ejector stroke |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Packing/Holding necessary?** Polymers have a coefficient of thermal expansion ($\alpha \approx 60 \times 10^{-6}$ to $200 \times 10^{-6}$ /K). Without pressure-driven melt replenishment during solidification, the part would sink, void, or warp. Packing compensates for **volumetric shrinkage** ($\Delta V/V \approx 1$–$5\%$).

### 2.3 The Stretch Blow Moulding Process — Detailed Walk-Through

#### Two-Stage (Reheat Stretch Blow) Process:
1. **Preform Injection:** A standard IM machine moulds a "test-tube" shaped preform with a finished bottle-thread finish. The preform wall thickness is engineered to provide the final bottle's wall distribution.
2. **Storage & Conditioning:** Preforms are stored in ambient or controlled humidity for up to 6 months.
3. **Reheat:** Preforms pass through a **tunnel oven** with **infrared (IR) lamps** (typically **quartz halogen @ 1.0–2.5 µm**). The preform body is heated to **$100$–$120^\circ\text{C}$** while the neck (crystallized in step 1) stays cool to retain thread integrity.
4. **Stretch & Blow:** Inside a chilled aluminium mould ($T_{mould} \approx 5$–$15^\circ\text{C}$):
   - **Axial stretch** by a steel stretch rod — typically **2.0–2.5×** the original preform length.
   - **Hoop stretch** by high-pressure air (0.8 MPa low → 4.0 MPa high) — typically **4.0–4.5×** the original preform diameter.
5. **Biaxial Orientation:** Total areal stretch ratio $\lambda_A = \lambda_{axial} \times \lambda_{hoop} \approx 9$–$10\times$ for PET.
6. **Cool & Eject:** The oriented bottle is held under pressure for **1.5–3.0 seconds** for crystallization to set, then ejected.

#### Single-Stage Process:
- Preform is injection-moulded, **conditioned in-mould** (or transferred hot), and blown in the **same machine** in one continuous cycle. Used for ultra-premium applications (pharmaceutical, hot-fill jars) where preform reheating is undesirable.

### 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important exam-prep resource** for this topic. Memorize the variables, units, and physical meaning — KTU problems will always expect SI units and proper dimensional reasoning.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{clamp} \geq P_{cavity} \times A_{projected}$ | $F_c$ = clamping force [N]; $P_{cav}$ = cavity pressure [Pa]; $A_{proj}$ = projected area [m²] | Machine size selection |
| 2 | $W_{shot} = V_{shot} \times \rho_{polymer} \times n_{cavities}$ | $W_s$ = shot weight [g]; $V_s$ = shot volume [cm³]; $\rho$ = density [g/cm³] | Shot capacity check |
| 3 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^2}{\pi^2 \, \alpha} \ln\left[\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_{eject} - T_w}\right]$ | $h$ = max wall thickness [m]; $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity [m²/s]; $T_m$ = melt temp; $T_w$ = mould wall temp; $T_{eject}$ = ejection temp | Cycle time estimation |
| 4 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{V_{delivered}}{V_{theoretical}} = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot t}{\eta \cdot V_{screw}}$ | Volumetric efficiency of injection | Material accounting |
| 5 | $Q_{plasticate} = \dfrac{\pi^2}{4} \cdot D_s^2 \cdot N_s \cdot S \cdot \rho \cdot \eta_{screw}$ | $D_s$ = screw dia; $N_s$ = rpm; $S$ = stroke; $\rho$ = density; $\eta_{screw}$ = screw efficiency | Plastication rate |
| 6 | $P_{inj} = P_{cav} + P_{sprue} + P_{runner} + P_{gate}$ | Total pressure as sum of cavity + flow losses | Pressure profile design |
| 7 | $\lambda_{axial} = L_{bottle}/L_{preform}$ | Axial stretch ratio | SBM preform design |
| 8 | $\lambda_{hoop} = D_{bottle}/D_{preform}$ | Hoop (radial) stretch ratio | SBM preform design |
| 9 | $\lambda_{areal} = \lambda_{axial} \times \lambda_{hoop}$ | Total areal stretch (must be $\approx 9$–$10$ for PET) | Strength prediction |
| 10 | $P_{hoop,thin} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{t}$ (Hoop stress in blown bottle wall) | $P$ = internal pressure; $r$ = radius; $t$ = wall thickness | Bottle pressure rating |
| 11 | $L_{preform} \cdot t_{preform} = L_{bottle} \cdot t_{bottle}$ (Mass conservation) | Constant volume of polymer | Preform wall design |
| 12 | $\tau_{shear} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}}{1 + (\lambda \cdot \dot{\gamma})^2}$ (Carreau model) | Shear-thinning viscosity of polymer melt | Injection pressure prediction |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Injection moulding** produces the smartphone housings, medical syringes, automotive dashboards, and the LEGO bricks you used as a child.
- **Stretch blow moulding** produces the PET bottle for your Coca-Cola (Coca-Cola uses **2-step SBM**), detergent containers, and pharmaceutical vials.
- The **biaxial orientation** in SBM is what allows a 500 mL PET bottle to weigh only **22–28 grams** yet withstand internal carbonation pressure of **0.6–0.8 MPa** at 38°C.
- A modern **1,000-tonne** all-electric injection moulding machine can produce a part every **8–12 seconds** with $\pm 0.02$ mm dimensional repeatability.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The clamping force equation is essentially a force-balance statement — the clamp must resist the *mould-opening force* generated by the hydraulic pressure of the melt acting on the projected cavity area. If $F_{clamp} < P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$, the mould **flashes** (excess material escapes the parting line). KTU loves this — expect a numerical problem in ESE.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Clamping Force Requirement

**Given:** A flat plate mould of projected area $A_{proj}$ is filled with polymer melt at cavity pressure $P_{cav}$. The mould has two halves clamped together.

**Step 1 — Identify the force trying to open the mould.**

The melt pressure acts on every square meter of the projected cavity area. The vertical component of this pressure (in the opening direction) is:

$$F_{open} = P_{cav} \times A_{projected}$$

**Textual logic:** The cavity pressure pushes the moving platen away from the stationary platen, attempting to separate the mould halves and cause flashing.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium condition.**

For the mould to remain closed throughout the injection and packing phases, the clamping force must be at least equal to (and for safety, slightly greater than) the opening force:

$$F_{clamp} = F_{open} = P_{cav} \times A_{projected}$$

**Textual logic:** A safety factor of 1.1–1.3 is normally applied in industry:

$$F_{clamp,design} = (1.1 \text{ to } 1.3) \times P_{cav} \times A_{projected}$$

**Step 3 — Unit check (KTU examiners deduct marks for sloppy units).**

$$[N] = [Pa] \times [m^2] = \left[\dfrac{N}{m^2}\right] \times [m^2] = [N] \;\checkmark$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Clamping Force Selection

**Problem:** A polypropylene (PP) box of dimensions $250 \text{ mm} \times 150 \text{ mm} \times 50 \text{ mm}$ wall thickness is to be injection-moulded. The peak cavity pressure is $40 \text{ MPa}$. Calculate the required clamping force. Take safety factor = 1.25.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute the projected area (the largest shadow the part casts on the parting plane).

$$A_{proj} = 250 \times 10^{-3} \times 150 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0375 \; m^2$$

Step 2: Apply the clamping force equation with safety factor.

$$F_{clamp} = 1.25 \times P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$$

$$F_{clamp} = 1.25 \times (40 \times 10^6 \; Pa) \times (0.0375 \; m^2)$$

$$F_{clamp} = 1.25 \times 1.5 \times 10^6 = 1.875 \times 10^6 \; N = 1875 \; kN$$

Step 3: Convert to tonnes-force (common industry unit: $1 \; tonne \approx 10 \; kN$).

$$F_{clamp} \approx 187.5 \; tonnes \text{ (i.e., a } \sim 200 \text{ tonne machine)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always express the answer in BOTH Newtons and tonnes-force. A 1875 kN machine would commonly be referred to as a "200-tonne" press in industry parlance.

### 3.3 Derivation: Cooling Time from Heat Conduction Physics

**Given:** A polymer slab of half-thickness $h$ initially at melt temperature $T_m$ is cooled between two mould walls held at $T_w < T_m$. The slab is considered solidified when the centreplane reaches the ejection temperature $T_{eject}$.

**Step 1 — Start with the 1-D transient heat conduction solution (Fourier series).**

$$\frac{T(x,t) - T_w}{T_m - T_w} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{4}{(2n+1)\pi} \sin\left[\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2h}\right] \exp\left[-\frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 \alpha t}{4h^2}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at the centreline $x = 0$.**

At $x = 0$, $\sin = 0$ for $n \geq 1$, so the dominant term is $n=0$:

$$\frac{T_c(t) - T_w}{T_m - T_w} \approx \frac{4}{\pi} \exp\left[-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha t}{4h^2}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $t = t_{cool}$ when $T_c = T_{eject}$.**

$$\frac{T_{eject} - T_w}{T_m - T_w} = \frac{4}{\pi} \exp\left[-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha t_c}{4h^2}\right]$$

Taking the natural log:

$$\ln\left[\frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{T_{eject} - T_w}{T_m - T_w}\right] = \frac{\pi^2 \alpha t_c}{4h^2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t_c$.**

$$t_c = \frac{4h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left[\frac{4}{\pi} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_w}{T_{eject} - T_w}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Refinement using the first TWO Fourier terms (more accurate form commonly used in textbooks).**

$$t_c = \frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left[\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_w}{T_{eject} - T_w}\right]$$

**Textual logic:** The factor $\dfrac{h^2}{\alpha}$ is the **thermal diffusion time constant** — the time required for heat to diffuse across a slab of thickness $h$. The log term accounts for the *initial vs. ejection* temperature span.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Cooling Time

**Problem:** A 3 mm thick PP part is injection-moulded. $T_m = 220^\circ\text{C}$, $T_w = 25^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{eject} = 100^\circ\text{C}$. $\alpha_{PP} = 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \; m^2/s$. Estimate cooling time.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Convert wall thickness to half-thickness: $h = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \; m$.

Step 2: Compute the log term.

$$\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_w}{T_{eject} - T_w} = 0.8106 \times \frac{220 - 25}{100 - 25} = 0.8106 \times 2.60 = 2.108$$

$$\ln(2.108) = 0.7456$$

Step 3: Compute the prefactor.

$$\frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{\pi^2 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-6}}{1.184 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.900 \; s$$

Step 4: Multiply.

$$t_c = 1.900 \times 0.7456 = 1.417 \; s$$

**Interpretation:** The cooling stage for this part is about **1.4 seconds**. Cooling is the *longest* stage in IM — total cycle time will be in the 4–6 second range.

### 3.5 Derivation: Shot Weight from Preform Mass Conservation (SBM)

**Given:** A PET preform of length $L_p$ and uniform wall thickness $t_p$ is stretch-blown into a bottle of length $L_b$ and average wall thickness $t_b$.

**Step 1 — Volume conservation (incompressibility of polymer melt at processing temperatures).**

The polymer volume (ignoring the negligible change in overall density) is conserved:

$$V_{preform} \approx \pi \cdot D_p \cdot t_p \cdot L_p = V_{bottle} \approx \pi \cdot D_b \cdot t_b \cdot L_b$$

**Step 2 — Substitute stretch ratios.**

$$\lambda_{axial} = L_b / L_p, \quad \lambda_{hoop} = D_b / D_p$$

Therefore:

$$D_p \cdot t_p \cdot L_p = D_b \cdot t_b \cdot L_b$$

$$\frac{t_p}{t_b} = \frac{D_b \cdot L_b}{D_p \cdot L_p} = \lambda_{hoop} \times \lambda_{axial} = \lambda_{areal}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for preform thickness.**

$$t_p = t_b \times \lambda_{areal}$$

**Worked Example:** A 500 mL PET bottle has $L_b = 220 \; mm$, $D_b = 65 \; mm$, $t_b = 0.30 \; mm$. The preform has $L_p = 100 \; mm$ and $D_p = 24 \; mm$. Find the required preform wall thickness.

Step 1: $\lambda_{axial} = 220/100 = 2.20$

Step 2: $\lambda_{hoop} = 65/24 = 2.71$

Step 3: $\lambda_{areal} = 2.20 \times 2.71 = 5.96$

Step 4: $t_p = 0.30 \times 5.96 = 1.79 \; mm$

> [!NOTE]
> **Preform Wall Engineering:** This is why PET preform walls are visibly thick (1.5–2.5 mm) even though the final bottle wall is only 0.25–0.35 mm. The thick preform is the "polymer reservoir" that gets distributed by the biaxial stretch.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Critical Comparison

| Parameter | Conventional Injection Moulding | Stretch Blow Moulding |
|---|---|---|
| **Starting Form** | Polymer pellets | Already-moulded preform |
| **Process Type** | Primary shaping | Secondary forming |
| **Temperature Range** | $T_m + 30$–$80^\circ\text{C}$ (fully molten) | $T_g < T < T_m$ (rubbery) |
| **Pressure** | 70–140 MPa (injection) | 0.8–4.0 MPa (blowing) |
| **Orientation** | Mostly flow-induced, skin-frozen | Intentional biaxial molecular |
| **Wall Distribution Control** | Limited | Excellent (uniform thinning) |
| **Cycle Time** | 4–120 s | 1.5–6 s for blow step |
| **Material Choice** | Universal (all thermoplastics) | PET, PP, PVC, PEN mainly |
| **Key Machine OEMs** | Engel, Arburg, Sumitomo, Haitian | Sidel, Krones, Sipa, Aoki |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Architecture of a Reciprocating Screw Injection Moulding Machine

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interactions** between machine subsystems, which is the KTU-preferred way to express machine architecture in 2D when physical drawings are not feasible.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Hopper<br/>Pellet Feed] --> A2[Heated Barrel<br/>Zone 1 to 4]
    A2 --> A3[Reciprocating Screw<br/>Metering + Injection]
    A3 --> A4[Non Return Valve<br/>Check Ring]
    A4 --> A5[Nozzle<br/>Sealed Contact]
    A5 --> A6[Mould Sprue Bushing<br/>Entry Point]
    A6 --> A7[Runner System<br/>Distribution]
    A7 --> A8[Gate<br/>Final Restriction]
    A8 --> A9[Cavity<br/>Net Shape Forming]
    A9 --> A10[Coolant Channels<br/>Heat Extraction]
    A9 --> A11[Ejector Pins<br/>Part Removal]
    A11 --> A12[Moving Platen<br/>Opens at Eject]

    B1[Clamping Cylinder<br/>or Toggle] --> A12
    B1 --> B2[Tie Bars<br/>Guide Rails]
    B2 --> A12

    C1[Hydraulic Power Pack<br/>or Servo Motor] --> A3
    C1 --> B1
    C1 --> C2[Heater Bands Controller]
    C2 --> A2

    D1[PLC Controller<br/>Sequence Logic] --> C1
    D1 --> A3
    D1 --> B1
```

### 4.2 Six-Stage Cycle Flow (KTU-Favourite Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1((Start of Cycle)) --> P1[Mould Close]
    P1 --> P2[Build Clamp Force]
    P2 --> P3[Injection Fill<br/>95 to 98 percent volume]
    P3 --> P4[Pack and Hold<br/>Pressure Control]
    P4 --> P5[Cooling and Plastication<br/>Parallel Screw Rotation]
    P5 --> P6[Mould Open]
    P6 --> P7[Eject Part]
    P7 --> P8{Defect Check}
    P8 -->|Accept| S1
    P8 -->|Reject| P9[Regrind and Reuse]

    style P3 fill:#ffe4b5
    style P4 fill:#ffe4b5
    style P5 fill:#b0e0e6
    style P7 fill:#98fb98
```

### 4.3 Two-Stage Stretch Blow Moulding Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[PET Pellets<br/>Dried 4 hours at 160 C] --> Q2[Step 1<br/>Injection Moulding Machine]
    Q2 --> Q3[Preform with Crystallized Neck]
    Q3 --> Q4[Storage<br/>Up to 6 Months]
    Q4 --> Q5[Step 2<br/>Reheat Tunnel Oven]
    Q5 --> Q6[IR Lamps<br/>Heat to 100 to 120 C]
    Q6 --> Q7[Stretch Rod<br/>Axial 2.0 to 2.5x]
    Q7 --> Q8[Pre Blow 0.8 MPa]
    Q8 --> Q9[High Blow 4.0 MPa<br/>Hoop 4.0 to 4.5x]
    Q9 --> Q10[Cooling Hold<br/>1.5 to 3 seconds]
    Q10 --> Q11[Finished PET Bottle<br/>Biaxially Oriented]

    style Q2 fill:#ffdab9
    style Q6 fill:#ffb6c1
    style Q11 fill:#90ee90
```

### 4.4 Pressure-Time Profile During a Single Shot (Critical KTU Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PT[Pressure versus Time Profile]
        direction LR
        R1[Filling Phase<br/>Ram Velocity Control] -->|Switch over| R2[Packing Phase<br/>Pressure Control]
        R2 --> R3[Holding Decay<br/>Pressure Drop]
        R3 --> R4[Gate Seal<br/>Pressure Decay]
        R4 --> R5[Cooling<br/>No Pressure]
    end

    style R1 fill:#ff6347
    style R2 fill:#ffa500
    style R3 fill:#ffff00
    style R4 fill:#adff2f
    style R5 fill:#87ceeb
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** The transition between velocity control and pressure control — the **V/P switchover** — is a high-frequency exam question. It defines the **frozen-layer fraction** at the end of fill (~25–50% solidified), and a poorly set switchover causes **short-shots, sink marks, or flash**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
> **"Distinguish between injection moulding and stretch blow moulding with respect to (a) starting material form, (b) operating pressure range, and (c) the orientation produced in the part."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Injection Moulding | Stretch Blow Moulding |
|---|---|---|
| (a) **Starting form** | Polymer pellets/granules (fed from hopper) | Already-moulded solid preform (reheated) |
| (b) **Pressure range** | 70–140 MPa (high pressure injection) | 0.8–4.0 MPa (low pressure inflation) |
| (c) **Orientation** | Mostly flow-induced, non-uniform, skin-frozen layer | Intentional, uniform, biaxial molecular orientation (λ_areal = 9–10) |

---

> **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
> **"What is a non-return valve (NRV) in an injection moulding machine, and why is it critical during the injection stroke?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Non-Return Valve** (also called a check ring or sliding ring) is a one-way mechanical valve located at the tip of the reciprocating screw. **Critical Function (2 marks):** During the injection stroke, the screw translates axially like a plunger. Without the NRV, the melt would flow backward over the screw flights (i.e., the screw would behave like a metering extruder rather than a plunger), causing **short-shots** and loss of pressure. With the NRV seated, **100% of the screw's forward motion** is converted into cavity filling. **Working (1 mark):** The ring seats against the screw tip under forward pressure and unseats during screw rotation (plastication) to allow melt to accumulate ahead of the flights.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question 3 (A).** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Modified]
> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a **reciprocating screw injection moulding machine**. Discuss the function of the **three functional units** (injection, clamping, hydraulic) and the role of the **non-return valve**.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A **$200 \times 150 \; mm$ flat plaque** of $3 \; mm$ wall thickness is to be injection-moulded in **polypropylene (ρ = 905 kg/m³)**. Peak cavity pressure is **35 MPa**. Calculate (i) the **shot weight in grams**, (ii) the **clamping force required** (use safety factor 1.2), and (iii) the **approximate cooling time** if $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-7} \; m^2/s$, $T_m = 230^\circ\text{C}$, $T_w = 25^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{eject} = 100^\circ\text{C}$.

#### Model Solution (A):

**Part (a) — Machine Working (7 marks):**

1. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Draw the standard layout — Hopper → Barrel (with heaters) → Reciprocating Screw → Nozzle → Mould Cavity on stationary platen — with moving platen and tie bars, and clamp unit on the rear. *(Use the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 as a reference and convert it to a clean labelled sketch.)*

2. **Injection Unit (2 marks):** Performs plasticating (during screw rotation) and injection (during screw forward translation). Three zones: (i) feed zone (conveying), (ii) compression/melting zone (decreasing flight depth, $\Delta$ = 2–3:1 compression ratio), (iii) metering zone (pumping). Heater bands establish a temperature profile of $T_{zone1} < T_{zone2} < T_{zone3} < T_{nozzle}$.

3. **Clamping Unit (1.5 marks):** Provides and maintains the tonnage to keep mould closed during injection. Toggle type: 4-bar knuckle, force amplification ~20:1, low cost, fast. Hydraulic type: constant force, more accurate, more expensive.

4. **Non-Return Valve (1.5 marks):** Critical to convert screw motion into a true plunger-like displacement. Prevents back-flow of melt over the flights during injection. Opens during plastication to let melt accumulate in front of the screw.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Shot weight in grams — 2 marks**

Step 1: Volume of the plaque (single cavity):

$$V = L \times W \times t = 0.200 \times 0.150 \times 0.003 = 9.0 \times 10^{-5} \; m^3$$

Step 2: Mass of the plaque (part weight):

$$m_{part} = V \times \rho = 9.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 905 = 0.08145 \; kg = 81.45 \; g$$

Step 3: Account for the runner system (assume runner fraction = 20%):

$$W_{shot} = 81.45 \times 1.20 = 97.74 \; g \approx 98 \; g$$

**(ii) Clamping force — 2 marks**

Step 1: Projected area:

$$A_{proj} = 0.200 \times 0.150 = 0.030 \; m^2$$

Step 2: Clamping force (with safety factor 1.2):

$$F_{clamp} = 1.2 \times P_{cav} \times A_{proj} = 1.2 \times (35 \times 10^6) \times 0.030 = 1.26 \times 10^6 \; N = 1260 \; kN$$

Step 3: Convert to tonnes-force:

$$F_{clamp} \approx 126 \; tonnes \quad (\text{a } 130\text{-tonne machine is appropriate})$$

**(iii) Cooling time — 3 marks**

Step 1: Half-thickness:

$$h = 0.003 / 2 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \; m$$

Step 2: Compute the log argument:

$$\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{230 - 25}{100 - 25} = 0.8106 \times 2.733 = 2.216$$

Step 3: Log value:

$$\ln(2.216) = 0.796$$

Step 4: Prefactor:

$$\frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{\pi^2 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-6}}{1.184 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.900 \; s$$

Step 5: Cooling time:

$$t_c = 1.900 \times 0.796 = 1.51 \; s$$

**Final Answer (Part b):** (i) Shot weight ≈ **98 g**, (ii) Clamping force ≈ **1260 kN (130-tonne)**, (iii) Cooling time ≈ **1.51 s**.

---

> **Question 3 (B) — Alternative Choice.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 - Modified]
> **(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **stretch blow moulding (SBM) process** in detail. Compare **single-stage and two-stage SBM** with respect to (i) preform handling, (ii) equipment cost, (iii) typical applications, and (iv) achievable production rate.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A PET preform of **length 110 mm, outer diameter 26 mm, and wall thickness 2.0 mm** is stretch-blow-moulded into a bottle of **length 240 mm, outer diameter 64 mm, average wall thickness 0.32 mm**. Calculate (i) the **axial stretch ratio**, (ii) the **hoop stretch ratio**, (iii) the **areal stretch ratio**, and (iv) verify the **mass conservation** (polymer mass is conserved between preform and bottle).

#### Model Solution (B):

**Part (a) — SBM Process Description (7 marks):**

1. **Process Steps (2.5 marks):** (1) Preform injection (with crystallized neck finish), (2) Reheat in IR tunnel oven to 100–120°C, (3) Transfer to chilled mould, (4) Axial stretch by rod (2.0–2.5×), (5) Radial inflation by air (4.0–4.5×), (6) Hold under pressure for crystallization, (7) Eject.

2. **Single-Stage SBM (2 marks):** Preform is moulded, conditioned, and blown in the **same machine**. Hot preform transfer. No reheating energy cost. Lower production rate (2,500–4,000 bph). Used for pharmaceutical, hot-fill, premium cosmetics. **Two-Stage SBM (2 marks):** Preform moulded on one machine, stored, then reheated and blown on a separate machine weeks/months later. Preforms can be shipped globally. Very high rate (up to 80,000 bph). Industry standard for beverages (Coca-Cola, water bottles).

3. **Comparison Table (0.5 mark):** Summarize cost vs. application.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Axial stretch ratio — 1.5 marks**

$$\lambda_{axial} = \frac{L_{bottle}}{L_{preform}} = \frac{240}{110} = 2.182$$

**(ii) Hoop stretch ratio — 1.5 marks**

$$\lambda_{hoop} = \frac{D_{bottle}}{D_{preform}} = \frac{64}{26} = 2.462$$

**(iii) Areal stretch ratio — 1.5 marks**

$$\lambda_{areal} = \lambda_{axial} \times \lambda_{hoop} = 2.182 \times 2.462 = 5.371$$

**Interpretation:** This value is on the lower end of the recommended $\lambda_{areal} \approx 9$–$10$ for PET. The bottle may have slightly lower strength and clarity; designers would typically increase preform length or decrease preform diameter to push $\lambda_{areal}$ upward.

**(iv) Mass conservation check — 2.5 marks**

Step 1: Approximate preform volume (cylindrical tube):

$$V_{preform} = \pi \cdot D_p \cdot t_p \cdot L_p = \pi \times 26 \times 2.0 \times 110 = 17,956 \; mm^3$$

Step 2: Approximate bottle volume (cylindrical wall, ignoring neck and base):

$$V_{bottle} = \pi \cdot D_b \cdot t_b \cdot L_b = \pi \times 64 \times 0.32 \times 240 = 15,440 \; mm^3$$

Step 3: The volumes should be equal in an ideal stretch. The discrepancy:

$$\frac{V_{preform} - V_{bottle}}{V_{preform}} = \frac{17,956 - 15,440}{17,956} = 14.0\%$$

**Interpretation:** The preform has 14% excess material — this is **by design** as it accounts for the bottle base (petaloid/hocky-stick shape) and neck finish, which were not included in our simplified bottle-wall calculation. For a more rigorous check, include the base cup volume and neck volume. The principle of mass conservation is **preserved in reality** by the inclusion of these features.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor** in clamping force calculation — direct 0–2 mark deduction.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** Mixing MPa, Pa, N, kN, tonnes. Always convert to base SI (Pa and m²) FIRST, then state the answer in multiple practical units.
> 3. **Conflating the projected area** with the cavity surface area. KTU specifically tests this — projected area is the *shadow* the part casts on the parting line plane.
> 4. **Stretch ratio interpretation:** Stating λ without specifying whether it is *axial, hoop, or areal* loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Cycle time vs. cooling time:** The total cycle time includes mould close, plasticate, cool, and eject — NOT just cooling. If asked for "cycle time", add 3–6 seconds for non-cooling steps.
> 6. **Forgetting the runner/wastage allowance** in shot weight — losing 1 mark.
> 7. **V/P switchover omission** when describing the cycle — examiners specifically look for "velocity control → pressure control transition at 95–98% fill."

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before the exam, ensure you can confidently state the following:**

- [ ] **IM definition** as a cyclic, high-pressure, closed-mould process for thermoplastics/thermosets
- [ ] **The 3 functional units** of an IM machine: Injection, Clamping, Hydraulic/Power
- [ ] **The 6-stage cycle** in order: Close → Clamp → Inject → Pack → Cool/Plasticate → Eject
- [ ] **Why NRV is essential** — converts screw rotation into effective plunger-like injection
- [ ] **The V/P switchover** — switches from velocity control (~95–98% fill) to pressure control
- [ ] **The packing phase** compensates for **volumetric shrinkage** during solidification
- [ ] **Clamping force equation** $F_c = P_{cav} \times A_{proj} \times SF$
- [ ] **Cooling time equation** — derived from 1-D Fourier heat conduction
- [ ] **SBM definition** — secondary forming of biaxially oriented hollow parts from a preform
- [ ] **Biaxial orientation** — increases tensile strength, clarity, barrier properties
- [ ] **Axial vs. Hoop vs. Areal stretch ratios** and their target values for PET ($\lambda_{areal} \approx 9$–$10$)
- [ ] **Two-stage vs. Single-stage SBM** — process flow, equipment, applications
- [ ] **Mass conservation in SBM:** $D_p \cdot t_p \cdot L_p = D_b \cdot t_b \cdot L_b$
- [ ] **Typical pressure ranges:** IM (70–140 MPa) vs. SBM (0.8–4.0 MPa) — 25× difference
- [ ] **PET preform thickness** is always 5–10× the final bottle wall thickness
- [ ] **Why preform necks are crystallized** in stage 1 — to retain thread integrity during reheating
- [ ] **IR lamps** (1.0–2.5 µm) used in SBM reheating — selective heating of body, neck stays cool
- [ ] **Common IM defects** (briefly): flash, short-shot, sink marks, warpage, weld lines, voids, silver streaks
- [ ] **Common SBM defects** (briefly): pearlescence (uneven stretching), haze, base crystallization, splits, off-centred gate

---

**END OF NOTES — INJECTION AND STRETCH BLOW MOULDING**
