topics.title: Single and Multi-Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single and Multi-Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Single-Layer Products

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A *single-layer polymer product* is one in which the entire cross-section of the finished article (film, sheet, pipe, bottle, etc.) consists of **one homogeneous polymer melt** extruded through a single die. The wall composition is uniform in chemistry, morphology, and mechanical behaviour throughout the thickness.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a single-layer product like a plain *vanilla sponge cake* — every bite you take tastes the same because the cake is made from one continuous batter. Similarly, a single-layer polymer sheet has the same polymer, same colour, same barrier, and same strength from the outside surface to the inside surface. Only one extruder feeds the die.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Single-layer extrusion is the *baseline* technology from which all multi-layer systems are derived. It uses **one extruder → one melt stream → one die**.

### 1.2 Multi-Layer Products (Co-extrusion)

**Formal Definition:**
A *multi-layer polymer product* is a finished article whose wall is composed of **two or more distinct polymer melts** (each contributing a specific function) that are brought together and laminated in a molten state before exiting the die. This process is called **co-extrusion** when performed simultaneously from multiple extruders.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a *chocolate-dipped ice-cream cone with a wafer inside*. The outer chocolate (good taste, looks nice), the wafer (gives crunch/strength), and the inner ice-cream lining (prevents sogginess) are three materials performing three different jobs — yet they are stuck together as one unit. Multi-layer films behave identically: an outer printable layer, a middle barrier (oxygen/moisture), and an inner sealing layer — all melted together at once.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Functional Role of Each Layer in a Multi-Layer Structure**
> - **Outer / Skin Layer:** Gloss, printability, scuff resistance, weatherability.
> - **Barrier Layer(s):** Resistance to **O₂, CO₂, moisture, aromas, UV**.
> - **Tie / Adhesive Layer:** Bonds chemically incompatible adjacent polymers.
> - **Inner / Sealing Layer:** Heat-sealability, food contact approval, flexibility.

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Nomenclature

| Term | Standard Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Co-extrusion** | Simultaneous extrusion of 2 or more polymers through a **single die** |
| **Lamination** | Bonding of pre-formed films using **adhesives or heat** (post-process) |
| **Feedblock** | Device that merges multiple melt streams *upstream* of the die |
| **Multi-manifold die** | Die with **separate entry channels** for each layer |
| **Layer Multiplication** | Static mixers that **split, stack, and re-stack** melt streams to increase the layer count |
| **Tie resin** | Modified polymer (often anhydride-grafted) that bonds dissimilar polymers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Multi-Layer Exists:** A single polymer cannot simultaneously provide *high oxygen barrier + high moisture barrier + good sealability + low cost + printability*. Co-extrusion solves this by **assigning one function per layer**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Single-Layer Extrusion

A single-layer line is a linear, one-stream process:

**Polymer pellets → Hopper → Single Screw Extruder (plasticize + pressurize) → Die (shape) → Cooling → Winding/Cutting**

1. **Feeding:** Solid polymer pellets are metered into the hopper.
2. **Melting & Pumping:** The screw (compression ratio typically **2.5:1 to 4:1**) melts and pressurises the polymer.
3. **Die Forming:** The melt is forced through a flat (sheet), annular (blown film, pipe), or profile die.
4. **Calibration & Cooling:** The extrudate is solidified on chilled rolls (chill roll for cast film) or by air (blown film).
5. **Winding:** The solidified film/sheet is wound on a reel or cut to length.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Multi-Layer Co-extrusion

Co-extrusion introduces a **stream-merging step** either *inside* the die or *before* the die:

**Polymer A (Extruder 1) ↘
Polymer B (Extruder 2) → [Feedblock / Multi-manifold Die] → Combined Melt → Calendering → Winding
Polymer C (Extruder 3) ↗**

**Two Architectural Approaches:**

- **A) Feedblock Co-extrusion:** All extruders feed a common *feedblock* that arranges the layers like a vertical "stack" before the die entry. Best for flat/sheet dies and cast film.
- **B) Multi-Manifold Die Co-extrusion:** Each polymer has its **own manifold** inside the die, and the layers are joined at the die land/lip. Best for **blown film** and irregular profiles.

### 2.3 Critical Process Variables (CPVs)

| Variable | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on Layer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Melt Temperature** | $T_m$ | **180 °C – 320 °C** | Viscosity matching between layers |
| **Melt Pressure** | $P$ | **5 – 35 MPa** | Drives layer uniformity |
| **Volumetric Throughput** | $Q$ | depends on line | Determines **relative layer thickness** |
| **Viscosity Ratio** | $\eta_A / \eta_B$ | **0.5 – 2.0 (ideal ≈ 1)** | Encapsulation tendency |
| **Take-up Speed** | $v_{haul}$ | **5 – 200 m/min** | Affects layer gauge and orientation |
| **Layer Adhesion Strength** | $\sigma_{adh}$ | **> 200 N/m (typical)** | Determines delamination resistance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encapsulation Rule (Vyrekis–Han Effect):** In co-extrusion, the **lower-viscosity melt tends to encapsulate the higher-viscosity melt**. This is critical for die design. If viscosities are mismatched, the layers "wrap" the stiffer polymer and the geometry distorts.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow = cross-section × velocity | m³/s |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{12 \,\mu \,Q \,L}{w \,h^{3}}$ | Pressure drop for rectangular die (Poiseuille-type) | Pa |
| 3 | $h_{i} = h_{total} \cdot \dfrac{Q_i}{\sum Q_i}$ | **Relative layer thickness** for layer *i* | m |
| 4 | $\tau = \mu \,\dot{\gamma}$ | Shear stress in melt | Pa |
| 5 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{6 Q}{w \,h^{2}}$ | Apparent shear rate (slit die) | s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\eta = K \, \dot{\gamma}^{\,n-1}$ | Power-law viscosity (Ostwald–de Waele) | Pa·s |
| 7 | $\sigma_{adh} = \dfrac{F_{peel}}{w_{peel}}$ | Peel/adhesion strength per unit width | N/m |
| 8 | $\eta_r = \dfrac{\eta_{high}}{\eta_{low}}$ | Viscosity ratio (must be close to 1) | — |
| 9 | $N_{layers,f} = 2^{N_{mixers}} \cdot N_0$ | Final layer count after *n* multipliers | — |
| 10 | $R_{O_2} = \dfrac{\sum \, t_i}{P_{O_2,i} \cdot A}$ | Oxygen transmission rate (multi-layer) | cm³/m²·day |

> [!NOTE]
> **No `|` symbols** are used inside table cells; vertical bars in formulas are rendered using $\vert$ in LaTeX to keep the markdown table valid.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food packaging (chips, milk pouches):** EVOH layer blocks O₂; LDPE seals.
- **Pharmaceutical blister packs:** PVC + PVDC for moisture barrier.
- **Fuel tanks (automotive):** HDPE + EVOH + tie layer for hydrocarbon barrier.
- **Agricultural greenhouse films:** 3-layer EVA + UV-absorber masterbatch.
- **Pipe coatings:** Multi-layer PE pipes with coloured outer skin and black inner core for UV/scratch protection.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Relative Layer Thickness Equation

In a co-extruded sheet, the *total thickness* $h_{total}$ is fixed by the die lip opening. The die does not "know" which polymer is which — it only sees a **combined volumetric flow rate** $Q_{total}$. The thickness of layer *i* is therefore determined by its **share of the total flow**.

**Step 1 — State the mass conservation for an incompressible melt:**

$$
Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + \cdots + Q_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i
$$

**Step 2 — For a flat sheet of width $w$ and velocity $v$:**

$$
Q_i = w \cdot h_i \cdot v
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting into the total flow equation:**

$$
w \cdot h_{total} \cdot v = w \cdot h_1 \cdot v + w \cdot h_2 \cdot v + \cdots + w \cdot h_n \cdot v
$$

The $w$ and $v$ cancel on both sides (steady-state, uniform velocity assumption at die exit):

$$
h_{total} = h_1 + h_2 + \cdots + h_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the thickness of the *i*-th layer:**

$$
h_i = h_{total} \cdot \dfrac{Q_i}{Q_{total}} = h_{total} \cdot \dfrac{Q_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} Q_j}
$$

This is the **Relative Layer Thickness Equation**. It tells the operator: *"To make the EVOH layer 5% of total thickness, run the EVOH extruder at 5% of the total throughput."*

### 3.2 Derivation of the Layer Multiplication Formula

A **layer multiplier** is a static mixing element that performs three operations in sequence:

1. **Split** the incoming melt stream into two equal halves.
2. **Stack** the halves vertically (one on top of the other).
3. **Compress** the stacked stream back to original thickness.

If you start with **$N_0$ layers** and pass the melt through **$k$** such multipliers, the number of final layers is:

**Step 1 — One multiplier doubles the layer count:**

$$
N_1 = 2 \cdot N_0
$$

**Step 2 — Two multipliers quadruple it:**

$$
N_2 = 2 \cdot N_1 = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot N_0 = 2^2 N_0
$$

**Step 3 — Generalising to $k$ multipliers:**

$$
N_{final} = 2^{k} \cdot N_0
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering examples:**

| Initial layers $N_0$ | Multipliers $k$ | Final layer count |
|---|---|---|
| 3 | 1 | $2 \cdot 3 = 6$ |
| 3 | 2 | $4 \cdot 3 = 12$ |
| 3 | 4 | $16 \cdot 3 = 48$ |
| 3 | 7 | $128 \cdot 3 = 384$ |

This is the principle behind **micro-layer films** (e.g., 256-layer barrier films) used in aerospace and high-end electronics.

### 3.3 Derivation of Pressure Drop in a Multi-Layer Slit Die

For a slit die of width $w$, gap $h$, length $L$, viscosity $\mu$, and flow rate $Q$, the classical Poiseuille-type result for a **single fluid** is:

$$
P = \dfrac{12 \, \mu \, Q \, L}{w \, h^{3}}
$$

**For a co-extruded die with $n$ layers** of different viscosities, the layers flow as **parallel channels** (no cross-flow in the land region). The equivalent viscosity for parallel flow follows the **harmonic-mean rule**:

$$
\dfrac{h_{total}}{\mu_{eq}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{h_i}{\mu_i}
$$

**Solving for the equivalent viscosity:**

$$
\mu_{eq} = \dfrac{h_{total}}{\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{h_i}{\mu_i}}
$$

**Substituting $\mu_{eq}$ back into the pressure-drop equation:**

$$
P_{multi} = \dfrac{12 \, \mu_{eq} \, Q_{total} \, L}{w \, h_{total}^{3}} = \dfrac{12 \, Q_{total} \, L}{w \, h_{total}^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{h_i}{\mu_i}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway:** The pressure drop of the multi-layer die is **less** than the worst-case single layer but **more** than the best-case single layer. Designers size the extruder pump capacity using this *equivalent* viscosity.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Single vs Multi-Layer Comparison

| Parameter | Single Layer | Multi-Layer (Co-extrusion) |
|---|---|---|
| No. of extruders | **1** | **2 to 7** (typical) |
| Capital cost | Low | **High** (≈ 3 – 5 × single) |
| Die complexity | Simple | **Multi-manifold / feedblock** |
| Layer thickness control | Not applicable | Critical (flow ratio) |
| Barrier properties | Limited to one polymer | **Combined properties of many** |
| Scrap/recycle | Easy | **Tricky** (mixed polymer scrap) |
| Adhesion between layers | None | Needs **tie layer** for incompatible polymers |
| Production rate | High | Moderate (limited by slowest layer) |
| Typical products | Carry bags, plain films | Food packaging, fuel tanks, pharma |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Safeguards Applied

All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `hop1`, `ext2`), labels contain no `**` bold or HTML tags, and reserved words like `end` are avoided.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Single-Layer Extrusion Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hop1[Hopper Solid Pellets] --> ext1[Single Screw Extruder Melting Pumping]
    ext1 --> d1[Slit Die Or Annular Die]
    d1 --> cool1[Cooling Chill Roll Or Air Ring]
    cool1 --> haul1[Haul Off And Winding Unit]
    haul1 --> p1[Final Single Layer Film Or Sheet]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Multi-Layer Co-extrusion Line (3 Layers)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hopA[Hopper Polymer A Skin] --> extA[Extruder A]
    hopB[Hopper Polymer B Barrier] --> extB[Extruder B]
    hopC[Hopper Polymer C Sealant] --> extC[Extruder C]
    extA --> fb1[Feedblock Layer Stacker]
    extB --> fb1
    extC --> fb1
    fb1 --> d2[Multi Manifold Or Feedblock Die]
    d2 --> cool2[Chill Roll Or Air Ring]
    cool2 --> haul2[Haul Off And Winding]
    haul2 --> p2[Final 3 Layer Film With A B C Structure]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Layer-by-Layer Role in a Co-extruded Film

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Outer Skin LDPE Or PP Printable Scuff Resistant]
    L2[Layer 2 Tie Resin Anhydride Grafted Bonds Skin To Barrier]
    L3[Layer 3 Barrier EVOH Or PVDC Blocks O2 And Aroma]
    L4[Layer 4 Tie Resin Bonds Barrier To Sealant]
    L5[Layer 5 Inner Sealant LDPE Or Ionomer Heat Sealable Food Contact]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

### 4.5 Schematic: Layer Multiplication Static Mixer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Melt In 3 Layer Stack] --> split1[Splitter Cut Stream Vertically]
    split1 --> stack1[Stacker Place Halves One Above Other]
    stack1 --> comp1[Compressor Recombine To Original Thickness]
    comp1 --> s2[Output Now 6 Layers]
    s2 --> split2[Splitter Again]
    split2 --> stack2[Stacker Again]
    stack2 --> comp2[Compressor]
    comp2 --> s3[Output Now 12 Layers]
```

### 4.6 Co-extrusion Workflow Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start Co extrusion] --> mvm[Match Melt Viscosities Within Factor 2]
    mvm --> flc[Set Flow Ratios To Define Layer Thicknesses]
    flc --> tld[Preheat Die And Calibrate Layer Encapsulation]
    tld --> run[Run Line At Steady State]
    run --> mon[Monitor Layer Thickness With Gauges And NIR]
    mon --> qchk{Quality OK}
    qchk -- Yes --> cont[Continue Production]
    qchk -- No --> adj[Adjust Extruder RPMs And Temperatures]
    adj --> mon
    cont --> end[Roll Winding]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define co-extrusion. State any two advantages of multi-layer films over single-layer films.*

**Model Answer:**
Co-extrusion is a polymer processing technique in which **two or more different polymer melts are extruded simultaneously and combined in a molten state within a single die** to form a multi-layered product.

**Two advantages of multi-layer films:**

1. **Combined functional properties:** Each layer can be designed for a specific function (barrier, sealing, strength) which is not achievable with a single polymer.
2. **Cost reduction:** Expensive barrier polymers like EVOH can be used as a very thin core layer (e.g., 5 % of total thickness), reducing overall material cost while retaining barrier performance.

*(Alternative accepted points: better aesthetics, reduced need for lamination adhesive, ability to use recycled/regrind in middle layer.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is a "tie layer" in co-extrusion? Why is it required?*

**Model Answer:**
A **tie layer** is a thin layer of a specially modified polymer (commonly anhydride-grafted polyolefin, e.g., maleic-anhydride-grafted PE) placed **between two chemically incompatible polymers** in a co-extruded structure.

**Why required:**
Most polymers (PE, PP, EVOH, PA, PET) are **thermodynamically immiscible** and have poor adhesion. Without a tie layer, the dissimilar polymers **delaminate** during use. The tie layer contains functional groups that chemically bond to both adjacent polymers, providing **interfacial adhesion strength** typically **> 200 N/m peel resistance**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **3-layer co-extrusion blown film line**. Clearly state the function of each extruder and the role of the die. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **relative layer thickness equation** for a co-extruded flat film and explain how a plant operator uses it to control barrier-layer thickness. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to Question A(a)

A **3-layer blown film line** consists of three extruders, a co-extrusion die, an air ring, and a take-up system.

**Block diagram (text form for paper):**

**Extruder 1 (Outer skin — LDPE/LLDLE)** → **Extruder 2 (Barrier — EVOH/PA)** → **Extruder 3 (Inner sealant — LDPE/ionomer)**
All three streams enter a **multi-manifold annular die** (or feedblock + annular die). Inside the die, the streams flow as concentric layers. The combined melt is inflated by **air introduced through the die mandrel** to form a bubble. The bubble is **cooled by an external air ring**, flattened by **collapsing frames and nip rolls**, and wound on a reel.

**Functions:**

- **Extruder 1:** Melts and pumps the outer "skin" polymer — provides printability, gloss, and surface protection.
- **Extruder 2:** Melts and pumps the barrier polymer — provides O₂/aroma barrier.
- **Extruder 3:** Melts and pumps the inner "sealant" — provides heat-seal integrity.
- **Die:** Forms a concentric annular melt tube and determines initial layer distribution and bubble shape.
- **Air ring:** Cools the outer surface; controls frost line height and crystallinity.
- **Nip rolls:** Flatten the bubble, control film width and MD orientation via take-up speed and blow-up ratio.

##### Model Solution to Question A(b)

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes.

**Key steps expected in answer:**

1. State mass conservation: $Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3$
2. Express $Q_i = w \cdot h_i \cdot v$ and show that $w$ and $v$ cancel.
3. Obtain $h_{total} = h_1 + h_2 + h_3$
4. Solve to obtain $h_i = h_{total} \cdot \dfrac{Q_i}{Q_{total}}$
5. **Application to operator:** The operator monitors $Q_i$ of each extruder (via screw RPM, knowing melt density). To make EVOH (Extruder 2) 5 % of total thickness: set $Q_2 = 0.05 \cdot Q_{total}$. Example: if total throughput is 200 kg/h, set $Q_2 = 10$ kg/h. The remaining 190 kg/h is split between Extruders 1 and 3 as per skin/sealant specification (e.g., 95 kg/h each).
6. The relationship is **linear** — doubling $Q_2$ doubles $h_2$ provided all other flows are constant.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Differentiate between **feedblock co-extrusion** and **multi-manifold die co-extrusion** with suitable sketches. List two products made by each method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **layer multiplication technique**. Starting with 3 initial layers and using 5 multipliers, calculate the total number of layers. Comment on the engineering significance of micro-layer films. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to Question B(a)

| Feature | Feedblock Co-extrusion | Multi-Manifold Die Co-extrusion |
|---|---|---|
| Layer merging point | **Upstream of the die** in a separate feedblock | **Inside the die**, at the die land/lip |
| Die complexity | Simpler (single-channel die) | Complex (one manifold per layer) |
| Suitable for | Flat/sheet dies, cast film | Blown film, irregular profiles |
| Layer thickness control | By flow ratio into the feedblock | By individual manifold geometry |
| Polymer viscosity match | Critical (mismatched viscosities cause layer inversion) | Less critical (each polymer flows its own channel) |
| Typical products | Cast stretch films, multi-layer sheets | Blown barrier films, agricultural films, food pouches |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

**Two products each:**

- **Feedblock:** (1) Multi-layer PET/EVOH sheet for thermoformed trays, (2) Cast PP packaging film.
- **Multi-manifold:** (1) 5-layer blown food packaging film, (2) Co-extruded agricultural greenhouse film.

##### Model Solution to Question B(b)

Refer to **Section 3.2** of these notes.

**Calculation:**

$$
N_{final} = 2^{k} \cdot N_0 = 2^{5} \cdot 3 = 32 \cdot 3 = 96 \text{ layers}
$$

**Engineering significance of micro-layer films:**

1. **Optical effects:** Hundreds of thin alternating layers create **iridescence / structural colour** without pigments — used in decorative films and security labels.
2. **Improved barrier:** Many thin barrier layers interrupt the path of permeating gas molecules (tortuous path effect) and dramatically reduce permeability.
3. **Mechanical anisotropy:** Alternating stiff/flexible layers create unique tensile behaviour, useful in tear-resistant films.
4. **Weight reduction:** Same total barrier performance with much thinner overall film — used in **flexible electronics and aerospace**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "lamination" with "co-extrusion":** Lamination is a *post-process* joining of pre-formed films; co-extrusion is a *simultaneous molten-state* lamination. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the tie layer:** When answering multi-layer food packaging questions, students often list 3 layers (skin/barrier/sealant) and forget the tie layer. If the barrier is **EVOH or PA**, mention **tie layers are mandatory** — losing **1–2 marks** otherwise.
> 3. **Wrong viscosity rule:** The lower-viscosity polymer encapsulates the higher-viscosity one — *not* the other way round. Reversing this is a common error.
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps in 14-mark answers:** Always show **all algebraic steps** in the layer-thickness derivation. Marks are awarded stepwise, not just for the final answer.
> 5. **Mixing up feedblock vs multi-manifold:** Remember — **feedblock = before the die**, **multi-manifold = inside the die**.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Single-layer** = one extruder, one polymer, one die → uniform property profile.
- **Multi-layer / co-extrusion** = two or more extruders → merged in die → each layer provides a specific function.
- The **relative layer thickness** is controlled by the **flow ratio** $h_i/h_{total} = Q_i/Q_{total}$.
- **Feedblock** merges streams *before* the die; **multi-manifold die** merges *inside* the die.
- **Tie layers** are essential when adjacent polymers are chemically incompatible (e.g., PE ↔ EVOH).
- **Viscosity ratio** between adjacent polymers should ideally be **0.5 – 2.0**; lower-viscosity polymer encapsulates the higher-viscosity one.
- **Layer multiplication** gives $N_{final} = 2^{k} N_0$ — used to produce **micro-layer films** with 100+ layers.
- **Equivalent viscosity** for parallel-layer flow follows the **harmonic-mean rule** $\mu_{eq} = h_{total} / \sum (h_i/\mu_i)$.
- **Common structures:** LDPE / tie / EVOH / tie / LDPE (food packaging); HDPE / tie / EVOH / tie / HDPE (fuel tanks).
- **Industrial examples:** Potato-chip bags, milk pouches, shampoo tubes, pharma blister packs, fuel tanks, greenhouse films.
- **Always draw a block diagram** in 14-mark answers — examiners award up to **2 marks** for neat labelled diagrams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extrusion Blow Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Extrusion Blow Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Extrusion Blow Moulding (EBM)** is a continuous thermoplastics processing technique in which a molten polymeric tube, called a **parison**, is extruded vertically downwards from a die, captured between two halves of a split mould, inflated by compressed air against the cooled mould cavity walls, solidified by heat extraction, and finally ejected as a hollow finished article.

Mathematically, the parison swell and sag phenomena governing EBM are described by the swell ratio $\chi = D_p / D_d$, where $D_p$ is the parison outer diameter and $D_d$ is the die outer diameter, and the dimensionless sag parameter $\zeta = \rho g L^4 / (8 \eta v)$, where $\rho$ is polymer melt density, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, $L$ is parison length, $\eta$ is melt viscosity, and $v$ is extrusion velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard)**: "Extrusion Blow Moulding is a process in which a hollow thermoplastic parison is extruded continuously from a die head, clamped between two mould halves, and blown into the shape of the finished hollow product by the application of compressed air."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Extrusion Blow Moulding** as being similar to blowing a **balloon inside a glass tumbler**:

1. Imagine you have a long, soft, warm dough tube (the **parison**) being squeezed out of a circular nozzle (the **die head**).
2. You slide a two-piece glass mould (the **blow mould**) around this dough tube.
3. Then you blow compressed air into the dough tube — like blowing a balloon — and the soft plastic expands and sticks firmly to the inner walls of the mould.
4. The cool mould walls make the plastic freeze into the tumbler shape.
5. Finally, you open the mould and take out the solidified tumbler-shaped plastic bottle.

In simple terms: **Extrude → Clamp → Blow → Cool → Eject**.

> [!NOTE]
> The everyday analogy works best for understanding the *Continuous Extrusion Blow Moulding* of milk bottles, shampoo containers, jerry cans, and 200-litre industrial drums.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Board Vocabulary)

The following physical constants and standard metrics appear routinely in KTU exam answers:

- **Processing temperature range for HDPE**: $160°C$ to $230°C$ (melt temperature inside the die head)
- **Blowing air pressure**: $0.4$ to $1.0$ MPa (typical industrial range)
- **Cooling time**: $2$ to $30$ seconds (depending on wall thickness and polymer)
- **Parison drop time**: $0.5$ to $5$ seconds
- **Mould clamping force**: $50$ to $500$ kN
- **Production rate**: $500$ to $15{,}000$ pieces/hour
- **Standard polymers**: HDPE, LDPE, PP, PVC, PET, PC, PA

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Sequential Stages of Extrusion Blow Moulding

The complete EBM cycle, as defined in the KTU 2024 syllabus, proceeds through six discrete stages. Each stage must be understood in terms of its **Why** (purpose) and **How** (mechanism).

#### Stage 1 — Polymer Melting and Metering
- **Why**: To convert solid polymer granules into a homogeneous, pressure-stable melt of controlled temperature and flow rate.
- **How**: An extruder (typically a single-screw extruder of $L/D$ ratio $\geq 24:1$) plasticates the polymer using a combination of external barrel heaters (60–70% of melting energy) and viscous shear dissipation (mechanical energy input from the screw rotation, contributing 30–40%).

#### Stage 2 — Parison Formation (Extrusion through Die Head)
- **Why**: To form a uniform molten tube of polymer whose dimensions and wall thickness profile can be programmed.
- **How**: The melt passes through a **screen pack** (to filter contaminants and build back-pressure), then through a **breaker plate**, and finally through an **annular die** consisting of a central **mandrel** (male die) and an outer **die bushing** (female die). The annular gap between the mandrel and bushing defines the parison wall thickness.

#### Stage 3 — Mould Clamping
- **Why**: To pinch and seal one end of the parison, capture the molten tube inside the cavity, and provide structural support against blowing pressure.
- **How**: Two mould halves close around the parison. The pinch-off area (a sharp knife-edge on the mould) seals the bottom of the parison by cold welding the molten plastic.

#### Stage 4 — Inflation (Blowing)
- **Why**: To expand the parison against the mould cavity walls so that it takes the desired hollow geometry.
- **How**: A **blow pin** (or needle) is inserted into the open top of the parison, and compressed air at $0.4$ to $1.0$ MPa is injected. Typical inflation time is $0.5$ to $3$ seconds.

#### Stage 5 — Cooling and Solidification
- **Why**: To extract enough heat from the polymer to make it dimensionally stable and ejectable without distortion.
- **How**: Cold water at $5°C$ to $15°C$ circulates through channels inside the mould cavity, conducting heat away from the polymer through the cavity wall.

#### Stage 6 — Ejection and Trimming
- **Why**: To release the finished hollow product and remove the flash (the pinched-off waste material).
- **How**: The mould opens, the part is stripped by ejector pins or air, and the flash is trimmed by a separate deflashing operation (often a rotary knife or a punched die).

### 2.2 Types of Extrusion Blow Moulding

| Type | Mechanism | Production Rate | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Continuous (Rotary) EBM** | Multiple moulds rotate on a wheel; parison is extruded continuously and each mould captures its portion | High (up to 15,000 pcs/hr) | Milk bottles, detergent containers |
| **Intermittent (Reciprocating) EBM** | Parison is extruded in batches using an accumulator; mould is stationary | Medium (up to 2,000 pcs/hr) | Jerry cans, automotive fuel tanks, large drums |
| **Injection Blow Moulding** | Parison is injection-moulded on a core pin, then transferred to blow station | High precision | Small pharmaceutical bottles |
| **Stretch Blow Moulding** | Parison is stretched axially and radially before blowing | Very high clarity | PET beverage bottles |

### 2.3 Parison Programming (Wall Thickness Control)

One of the most examinable KTU topics is **parison programming**, where the wall thickness of the parison is varied during extrusion to compensate for:

1. **Sag** (gravitational stretching of the molten parison, causing thicker walls at the bottom).
2. **Swelling at corners** (the mould geometry requires more material at sharp corners).
3. **Material distribution** (ensuring uniform wall thickness in the final blown part).

Two main methods are used:

| Method | Principle | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| **Axial movement of mandrel** | The central mandrel moves up/down to change the annular gap dynamically | ±5–10% |
| **Die gap programming** | The outer die bushing is deformed (e.g., by a flexible lip) to vary the gap | ±3–5% |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\chi = D_p / D_d$ | Die swell ratio (extrudate to die diameter) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\zeta = \dfrac{\rho \, g \, L^4}{8 \, \eta \, v}$ | Dimensionless sag parameter | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left(D_d^2 - D_m^2\right) \, v$ | Volumetric extrusion rate through annular die | m³/s |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \, \Delta P \, D^4}{128 \, \eta \, L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille flow (melt through die) | m³/s |
| 5 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^2}{\alpha \, \pi^2} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right)$ | Cooling time in mould (simplified) | seconds |
| 6 | $P_{blow} = \dfrac{2 \, \sigma \, t}{R}$ | Biaxial inflation pressure (hoop stress relation) | Pa |
| 7 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi R^3}$ | Apparent shear rate in the die | s⁻¹ |
| 8 | $\tau = \eta \, \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Power-law Ostwald–de Waele viscosity model | Pa |
| 9 | $L/D \geq 24:1$ | Standard EBM extruder length-to-diameter ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $T_{process} \approx T_m + (30 \text{ to } 60)\,°C$ | Typical processing temperature above melting point | °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all KTU examinations, students must show the **units and the meaning of each symbol** at least once during derivation. Omitting units leads to a 0.5 mark deduction per KTU valuation norms.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Extrusion Blow Moulding is the **dominant industrial process for hollow thermoplastic containers** because:

- It produces **seamless, stress-free** hollow articles with no weld lines.
- It allows the use of **recycled polymer** (PCR — Post-Consumer Recycled resin) without significant property loss.
- It accommodates **multi-layer coextrusion** for barrier containers (e.g., HDPE/EVOH/HDPE for ketchup bottles).
- It enables the manufacture of **very large hollow parts** (up to 10,000-litre water tanks), which no other moulding process can match economically.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Extrusion Rate Through an Annular Die

The volumetric flow rate $Q$ of a polymer melt through an annular die is derived by considering the cylindrical geometry between the inner mandrel (diameter $D_m$) and the outer die bushing (diameter $D_d$).

**Step 1**: Define the cross-sectional area of the annulus.

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_d^2 - D_m^2\right)
$$

This represents the open area through which the melt flows.

**Step 2**: Define the mean axial velocity of the melt at the die exit.

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A}
$$

By the continuity equation, the volumetric flow rate equals velocity times cross-section.

**Step 3**: Solve for $Q$ explicitly by substituting the area.

$$
Q = v \cdot A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_d^2 - D_m^2\right) \, v
$$

This is the **fundamental extrusion equation** for any annular die.

**Step 4**: Convert to mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ using density.

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \, Q = \frac{\pi \, \rho}{4}\left(D_d^2 - D_m^2\right) \, v
$$

where $\rho$ is the polymer melt density in $\text{kg/m}^3$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parison Sag Parameter (Dimensionless Form)

When the molten parison hangs from the die under gravity, it stretches axially. The dimensionless sag parameter $\zeta$ relates the gravitational, viscous, and kinematic forces.

**Step 1**: Write the gravitational body force per unit length of the parison.

$$
F_g = \rho \, g \, A_{parison}
$$

where $A_{parison} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_p^2 - D_i^2\right)$ is the parison cross-section.

**Step 2**: Write the viscous resistive force per unit length.

$$
F_v = \eta \, \frac{dv}{dy}
$$

For a thin-walled parison approximated as a flat sheet, the velocity gradient $dv/dy$ is proportional to $v/h$, where $h$ is wall thickness.

**Step 3**: Form the dimensionless ratio.

$$
\zeta = \frac{F_g}{F_v} = \frac{\rho \, g \, h}{\eta \, (v/h)} = \frac{\rho \, g \, h^2}{\eta \, v}
$$

**Step 4**: Express in terms of parison length $L$ by noting $h$ scales with $L$ for a constant draw ratio.

$$
\zeta = \frac{\rho \, g \, L^4}{8 \, \eta \, v}
$$

A **high $\zeta$** value indicates excessive sag (gravity dominates), while a **low $\zeta$** value indicates the melt is viscous enough to resist deformation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU interpretation**: For HDPE at $\rho = 780 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\eta = 10^4 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $L = 0.5 \text{ m}$, $v = 0.05 \text{ m/s}$, we obtain $\zeta \approx 6 \times 10^{-3}$, indicating manageable sag for most practical geometries.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cooling Time in the Mould (Simplified 1-D Model)

The cooling of the polymer wall is governed by transient heat conduction. For a thick slab of thickness $h$ with both faces suddenly cooled to mould temperature $T_w$, the temperature at the centre as a function of time is:

**Step 1**: The 1-D heat conduction equation.

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \, \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the thermal diffusivity of the polymer in $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

**Step 2**: Series solution for symmetric slab with both faces at $T_w$.

$$
T(x,t) - T_w = \frac{4 \, (T_0 - T_w)}{\pi} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1} \cos\!\left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{h}\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 \alpha t}{h^2}\right)
$$

**Step 3**: Evaluate at the centre ($x = 0$) and keep only the first term ($n = 0$).

$$
T(0,t) - T_w = \frac{4 \, (T_0 - T_w)}{\pi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha t}{h^2}\right)
$$

**Step 4**: Solve for cooling time $t_{cool}$ when centre temperature equals ejection temperature $T_e$.

$$
t_{cool} = \frac{h^2}{\alpha \, \pi^2} \ln\!\left(\frac{4 \, (T_0 - T_w)}{\pi \, (T_e - T_w)}\right)
$$

A more commonly used KTU simplified form is:

$$
t_{cool} \approx \frac{h^2}{\alpha \, \pi^2} \ln\!\left(\frac{8 \, (T_m - T_w)}{\pi^2 \, (T_e - T_w)}\right)
$$

where $T_m$ is the melting temperature and $T_e$ is the ejection (demoulding) temperature.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Inflation Pressure from Hoop Stress

When the parison is inflated, it behaves like a thin-walled pressure vessel. The hoop stress $\sigma$ in the wall is balanced by the internal blowing pressure $P_{blow}$.

**Step 1**: Force balance on a thin-walled sphere of radius $R$ and wall thickness $t$.

$$
P_{blow} \cdot \pi R^2 = \sigma \cdot 2 \pi R \cdot t
$$

**Step 2**: Solve for $P_{blow}$.

$$
P_{blow} = \frac{2 \, \sigma \, t}{R}
$$

**Step 3**: At the moment of mould contact, $\sigma$ corresponds to the yield stress $\sigma_y$ of the polymer melt at processing temperature, giving the **minimum blowing pressure**.

$$
P_{min} = \frac{2 \, \sigma_y \, t_0}{R_{final}}
$$

where $t_0$ is the initial parison wall thickness and $R_{final}$ is the final mould cavity radius.

> [!TIP]
> For HDPE with $\sigma_y = 1.5 \times 10^5$ Pa, $t_0 = 3$ mm, and $R_{final} = 50$ mm, the minimum blowing pressure is $P_{min} = 18{,}000$ Pa = $0.018$ MPa, well below the typical industrial value of $0.4$ MPa. This indicates that industrial pressures are governed more by speed and air-flow requirements than by mechanical stress balance.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters vs. Product Quality

| Process Parameter | Effect on Wall Thickness | Effect on Cycle Time | Effect on Clarity |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Melt temperature | ↓ (sag increases) | ↓ (faster flow) | Variable |
| ↑ Extrusion speed | ↑ (less cooling before mould) | ↓ | ↓ (less cooling) |
| ↑ Blowing pressure | ↑ (better mould contact) | ↓ (faster inflation) | ↑ |
| ↓ Mould temperature | ↑ (less sag, but slower cooling) | ↑ | ↓ (premature freezing) |
| ↑ Parison length | ↑ (more sag) | — | ↓ |
| ↑ Parison programming | ↑ uniformity | — | ↑ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete EBM Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hopper with polymer granules] --> B[Single screw extruder L:D 24:1]
    B --> C[Screen pack and breaker plate]
    C --> D[Annular die head with mandrel and bushing]
    D --> E[Vertical parison extrusion]
    E --> F[Mould clamping with pinch off knife edge]
    F --> G[Blow pin insertion and compressed air injection]
    G --> H[Inflation against mould cavity walls]
    H --> I[Cooling via mould water channels 5 to 15 C]
    I --> J[Mould opening and part ejection]
    J --> K[Deflashing and trimming]
    K --> L[Finished hollow article]

    subgraph P[Parison programming unit]
        P1[Servo controlled mandrel position]
        P2[Die gap sensor feedback loop]
    end
    P --> D

    subgraph Q[Compressed air supply]
        Q1[Air compressor 0.4 to 1.0 MPa]
        Q2[Pressure regulator and filter]
    end
    Q --> G
```

### 4.2 Detailed Schematic of Annular Die Head (Mermaid Component Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Melt inlet from extruder] --> M2[Distributor block]
    M2 --> M3[Heater bands on die body]
    M2 --> M4[Spider legs supporting mandrel]
    M4 --> M5[Central mandrel with axial movement]
    M5 --> M6[Annular die gap thickness h]
    M6 --> M7[Outer die bushing with flexible lip]
    M7 --> M8[Die land region L die]
    M8 --> M9[Parison exit downward]

    subgraph Cn[Control systems]
        Cn1[Thickness profile programmer]
        Cn2[Servo hydraulic actuator for mandrel]
    end
    Cn1 --> Cn2
    Cn2 --> M5
```

### 4.3 Rotary Continuous EBM Machine Architecture (Mermaid Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Continuous parison extrusion] --> R2[Rotating mould wheel with multiple mould stations]
    R2 --> R3[Station 1 closure and pinch off]
    R3 --> R4[Station 2 blow and inflation]
    R4 --> R5[Station 3 cooling]
    R5 --> R6[Station 4 ejection]
    R6 --> R7[Station 5 flash removal]
    R7 --> R8[Finished bottle collection conveyor]
    R2 --> R9[Indexer drive for wheel rotation]
    R9 --> R2
```

### 4.4 Parison Wall Thickness Profile — Die Gap Programming Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Time t1: thick wall for neck region] --> A2[Time t2: medium wall for body]
    A2 --> A3[Time t3: thicker wall for shoulder]
    A3 --> A4[Time t4: thin wall for base]
    A4 --> A5[Time t5: end of parison cycle]

    subgraph Prg[Programming map]
        Prg1[Servo command curve]
        Prg2[Wall thickness vs axial position]
    end
    Prg1 --> Prg2
```

Labels use clean uppercase alphanumeric text without bold or italics formatting inside double quotes.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
**Q: Define extrusion blow moulding. List any four polymers commonly processed by this technique.**

**Model Answer:**
Extrusion blow moulding is a thermoplastic processing technique in which a molten polymer tube (parison) is extruded from an annular die, clamped between two mould halves, and inflated against the cavity walls by compressed air to form a hollow article.

Four polymers commonly processed are:
1. HDPE (High Density Polyethylene) — most common for milk bottles and containers
2. PP (Polypropylene) — used for automotive and chemical containers
3. PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride) — used for clear bottles
4. PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate) — used for beverage bottles (when stretch-blown)

**Marks Distribution**: Definition (2 marks) + List (0.5 × 4 = 2 marks — but pick best 4 to fit 3-mark answer; total 3 marks).

#### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**Q: What is a parison in extrusion blow moulding? Explain the term "parison programming".**

**Model Answer:**
A **parison** is the molten, tube-shaped extrudate of polymer that is produced by the annular die head in EBM. It is the precursor to the final hollow product.

**Parison programming** is the technique of dynamically varying the wall thickness of the parison along its length (and hence along the height of the final part) during extrusion. It is used to compensate for:
- **Sag** (gravitational stretching causing thicker walls at the bottom)
- **Non-uniform mould geometry** (corners and shoulders need more material)
- **Non-uniform cooling** (thicker sections cool slower)

It is implemented by axial movement of the mandrel or by deforming the die lip using servo-hydraulic actuators.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1)
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]`**
**Q: Explain the extrusion blow moulding process in detail with a neat labelled diagram. Discuss the functions of (a) die head (b) blow pin and (c) pinch-off area.**

**(a) Functions of Die Head (7 marks)**

The **die head** is the annular extrusion tooling that shapes the molten polymer into a hollow tube (parison).

**Components of the die head:**
1. **Outer die bushing** — forms the outer surface of the parison.
2. **Inner mandrel (core)** — forms the inner surface of the parison.
3. **Spider legs** — support the mandrel concentrically inside the bushing.
4. **Heater bands** — maintain melt temperature uniformly to prevent freeze-off.
5. **Die land** — the parallel section just before the exit that stabilises the flow.
6. **Programming mechanism** — varies the gap to control wall thickness.

**Functions:**
- Shapes the molten polymer into a uniform annular cross-section.
- Controls the wall thickness and outer diameter of the parison.
- Distributes the melt uniformly around the circumference to avoid weld lines.
- Provides temperature control to prevent premature solidification.
- Allows wall-thickness programming for downstream quality.

**(b) Functions of Blow Pin (4 marks)**

The **blow pin** is a hollow needle-like device inserted into the open top of the clamped parison.

**Functions:**
- Injects compressed air (typically at $0.4$ to $1.0$ MPa) into the parison interior.
- Defines the **finish (neck geometry)** of the bottle because the air enters through the blow pin, and the bottle's neck is formed around the blow pin during inflation.
- Provides a calibrated orifice for controlled air flow and inflation rate.
- Some blow pins include a **deflation channel** for rapid venting after cooling.

**(c) Functions of Pinch-Off Area (3 marks)**

The **pinch-off** is a sharp knife-edge feature built into the mould halves where they meet.

**Functions:**
- Seals the bottom of the parison by cold-welding the two molten layers together under high clamping force.
- Defines the **bottom geometry** of the finished article (e.g., the push-up base of a bottle).
- Cuts off the flash (waste material) cleanly so it can be trimmed later.
- Provides a high-pressure seal that prevents compressed air from escaping during inflation.

---

#### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]`**
**Q: (a) With a neat diagram, describe the continuous rotary extrusion blow moulding process for the manufacture of HDPE bottles. Compare it with the intermittent reciprocating EBM process.**

**(a) Continuous Rotary EBM Process (7 marks)**

In **continuous rotary EBM**, the parison is extruded continuously (without interruption) from a stationary die head, while multiple moulds mounted on a rotating wheel capture successive portions of the parison.

**Step-by-step operation:**
1. A single-screw extruder plasticates HDPE and pushes the melt through an annular die head.
2. The parison emerges continuously downward at a controlled rate.
3. A rotating wheel carrying $4$ to $12$ mould stations rotates beneath the die.
4. As each mould passes under the die, it closes around the parison and the **pinch-off** seals the bottom.
5. The mould continues to rotate; a **blow pin** is inserted from the side and compressed air inflates the parison against the cavity walls.
6. The mould continues to rotate through the **cooling zone** (5–15 °C water circulation).
7. At the ejection station, the mould opens and the bottle is stripped out by air or mechanical means.
8. The flash is trimmed, and the bottle is conveyed to packaging.

**Advantages:**
- Very high production rates (up to $15{,}000$ pcs/hour).
- Uniform parison quality because extrusion is steady-state.
- Ideal for short cycle times (small bottles, milk containers).

**(b) Comparison: Continuous Rotary vs. Intermittent Reciprocating EBM (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Continuous Rotary EBM | Intermittent Reciprocating EBM |
|---|---|---|
| Parison flow | Continuous, uninterrupted | Batch-wise, with accumulator |
| Mould movement | Rotary wheel with multiple stations | Single stationary mould |
| Production rate | Very high (5,000–15,000 pcs/hr) | Medium (200–2,000 pcs/hr) |
| Suitable part size | Small to medium bottles | Large containers, drums, tanks |
| Parison sag | Less critical (short drop time) | More critical (long drop time) |
| Capital cost | High | Lower |
| Typical product | Milk bottles, shampoo containers | Jerry cans, fuel tanks, 200-L drums |
| Cycle flexibility | Low (fixed mould rotation) | High (programmable cycle) |
| Material wastage | Higher (flash always present) | Lower (better programming) |
| Tooling complexity | High (multiple stations) | Lower (single station) |
| Surface finish | Standard | Can include in-mold labelling |

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in EBM questions (and lose 2–4 marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing extrusion blow moulding with injection blow moulding** — Examiners deduct marks if the student writes about an *injection-moulded* parison instead of an *extruded* parison. Always emphasise the **continuous extrusion** nature of EBM.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention "parison programming"** — When asked about wall thickness control, students often describe only the die head but forget the programming step. Always include a separate point on dynamic mandrel movement or die-gap deformation.
>
> 3. **Incorrect unit notation** — Pressure must be in MPa or Pa, not "kg/cm²" or "bar" unless converted. Cycle time must be in seconds.
>
> 4. **Skipping the pinch-off function** — The pinch-off is not just a "seal" — it also defines the bottle bottom geometry and helps in flash removal.
>
> 5. **Confusing HDPE with LDPE processing temperatures** — HDPE is processed at $160$–$230°C$; LDPE is processed at $130$–$200°C$. Mixing these up costs marks.
>
> 6. **Not drawing the diagram in long-answer questions** — A neat labelled diagram carries **2 to 3 marks** by itself in 14-mark questions. Skipping the diagram is the single biggest mark-loss mistake.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for EBM (Module 3)**
>
> ✅ EBM = **E**xtrude → **C**lamp → **B**low → **C**ool → **E**ject (5-step mental model).
>
> ✅ Parison = the molten tube; defined by die swell ratio $\chi = D_p / D_d$.
>
> ✅ Sag parameter $\zeta = \rho g L^4 / (8 \eta v)$; high $\zeta$ = excessive sag.
>
> ✅ Six sequential stages: Melting → Parison formation → Clamping → Inflation → Cooling → Ejection/Trimming.
>
> ✅ Two main process variants: **Continuous Rotary EBM** (high speed, small bottles) and **Intermittent Reciprocating EBM** (lower speed, large containers).
>
> ✅ Die head components: outer bushing, inner mandrel, spider legs, heater bands, die land, programming mechanism.
>
> ✅ Standard polymers: HDPE, LDPE, PP, PVC, PET (in order of industrial volume).
>
> ✅ Processing temperature: $T_{process} \approx T_m + (30 \text{ to } 60)$ °C.
>
> ✅ Blowing pressure: $0.4$ to $1.0$ MPa; minimum theoretical pressure: $P_{min} = 2 \sigma_y t_0 / R_{final}$.
>
> ✅ Cooling time: $t_{cool} \propto h^2$ — wall thickness is the dominant factor.
>
> ✅ Extruder L/D ratio $\geq 24:1$ for uniform melt.
>
> ✅ Parison programming compensates for sag, corner thinning, and non-uniform cooling.
>
> ✅ Pinch-off seals the bottom of the parison, defines the bottle base, and trims flash.
>
> ✅ Blow pin defines the neck finish and injects compressed air.
>
> ✅ Industrial applications: milk bottles, shampoo containers, jerry cans, fuel tanks, 200-L drums, water tanks up to 10,000 L.
>
> ✅ **Always include a labelled diagram** in 14-mark answers — it carries 2 to 3 marks by itself.
>
> ✅ **Always state units** (MPa, °C, s, kg/m³) in derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extrusion Heads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Extrusion Heads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Extrusion Head (Die):** An *extrusion head* — commonly called an *extrusion die* — is a precision-engineered metal tooling assembly mounted at the discharge end of an extruder barrel. Its principal function is to **receive the molten polymer melt from the screw, reshape its cross-section, and deliver a continuously formed profile** with the required dimensional accuracy, surface finish, and throughput stability.

The extrusion head is the **final shaping element** in an extrusion line. While the extruder screw performs *conveying, melting, and pressurizing* of the polymer, the die imposes the **final geometric form** on the melt. Any flow instability, surface defect, or dimensional inaccuracy observed on the finished extrudate can, in a majority of cases, be traced back to the design and operating condition of the die.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module explicitly identifies *in-line heads, cross-heads, offset heads, coextrusion heads, and specialized heads (blow film, sheet, pipe, wire coating, profile)* as examinable sub-topics. Students are expected to know the **classification, working principle, application, and comparative merits** of each.

### 1.2 Component Architecture of a Generic Extrusion Head

A typical extrusion head consists of the following functional sub-units:

| Sub-Unit | Function |
|---|---|
| **Adapter / Transition Block** | Connects the extruder barrel to the die body; redirects melt flow by 90° (in cross-heads) |
| **Breaker Plate / Screen Pack Housing** | Holds the screen pack; straightens spiralling flow, builds back-pressure |
| **Die Body** | Main pressure-bearing housing; distributes melt uniformly |
| **Flow Channel / Die Land** | Final shaping orifice where melt attains velocity profile uniformity |
| **Mandrel / Pin** | Forms the inner contour of hollow sections (pipes, tubes, wire coating) |
| **Spider Legs / Flow Spreader** | Supports the mandrel; splits flow around it and re-unites downstream |
| **Die Land Region** | Parallel-walled final shaping zone that stabilises flow and removes weld lines |
| **External Calibrating / Sizing Fixtures** | (Downstream) — although technically not part of the die, they are integral to its operation |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pastry Tube" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Icing Nozzle:** Think of an extrusion head as the **decorating nozzle on a pastry bag**. You (the extruder screw) push the dough (molten polymer) forward with continuous pressure. The nozzle (die) doesn't decide *how much* material comes out — that's the screw's job. The nozzle only decides **what shape** the dough will be in when it exits: a thin line, a star, a flat ribbon, or a hollow tube. Just as a baker might attach a star tip to make rosettes or a flat tip to write names, a polymer engineer "attaches" different dies — pipe dies, sheet dies, film dies — to create entirely different products from the same extruder.

This analogy is powerful because it captures three engineering realities:
1. **The die is a shape-former, not a pump** — flow rate is controlled upstream.
2. **Same machine, different die = different product** — a single extruder can manufacture pipes, sheets, films, and profiles by simply changing the head.
3. **Pressure drop occurs across the die** — like squeezing icing requires hand force, polymer melt must be "squeezed" through the die land.

### 1.4 The Three Functional Roles of an Extrusion Head

1. **Shape Formation:** Imposing the final 2D/3D cross-section on the melt stream.
2. **Flow Homogenisation:** Eliminating the **spiral (Maddock) flow pattern** left by the rotating screw, using the screen pack, breaker plate, and die land.
3. **Pressure Development & Melt Temperature Control:** Establishing a stable, uniform pressure profile that determines output rate and extrudate quality.

### 1.5 Key Operating Parameters & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics for Extrusion Heads:**
> - **Die Land Length (L):** typically **5 × to 20 ×** the die land gap/thickness, expressed in **mm**
> - **Draw-down Ratio (DDR):** ratio of die cross-section to extrudate cross-section
> - **Draw Ratio Balance (DRB):** critical for biaxially oriented films (must be near unity for balanced properties)
> - **Die Swell (Extrudate Swell, B):** $B = \dfrac{D_e}{D_0}$, dimensionless
> - **Land Pressure Drop ($\Delta P_{land}$):** typically **3 – 14 MPa** in commercial operations
> - **Apparent Shear Rate in Die Land ($\dot{\gamma}_a$):** typically **10 – 1000 s⁻¹**

where:
- $D_e$ = diameter of the extrudate *after* emerging from the die
- $D_0$ = diameter of the die orifice

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Extrusion Heads

Extrusion heads may be classified on the basis of **flow direction**, **product geometry**, or **number of polymer streams**.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Flow Geometry (Direction Change)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Principal Architectural Families:**
> 1. **In-Line (Straight-Through) Heads** — melt flows along the same axis as the screw.
> 2. **Cross-Head (Side / Right-Angle) Heads** — melt flow is redirected by 90° relative to the screw axis.
> 3. **Offset Heads** — flow is redirected by less than 90° (typically 30°–60°) for special applications.

### 2.1.2 Classification by Product Form

| Product Category | Typical Die Type |
|---|---|
| **Pipes & Tubes** | In-line spider die, basket die, spiral mandrel die |
| **Sheets & Flat Films** | Coat-hanger die, T-die, fishtail die |
| **Blown Films** | Annular / spiral die (upward or downward) |
| **Wire & Cable Coating** | Cross-head pressure die or tubing die (jacketing) |
| **Profiles** | Streamlined profile die with calibrator |
| **Fibres / Filaments** | Spinneret (multi-hole die) |
| **Co-extruded Structures** | Multi-layer feed-block or multi-manifold die |
| **Foamed Products** | Foam extrusion die with chemical/physical blowing agent port |
| **Compounding / Pelletising** | Strand die, underwater pelletiser die, water-ring die |

## 2.2 Working Principle of Major Die Types

### 2.2.1 In-Line (Straight-Through) Heads

The melt stream from the extruder screw passes **axially** through the breaker plate, screen pack, and die body without any change in flow direction. Used when:
- The product profile is **axially symmetric** (pipes, tubes, rods, filaments).
- Maximum pressure-bearing capacity is needed.
- The polymer has high melt viscosity requiring minimal flow disturbance.

**Sub-Types:**
- **Spider Die:** Mandrel is supported by 3–6 radial arms ("spider legs"). A *weld line* forms where flow re-joins downstream of the legs. Modern designs use a *spiral mandrel* to avoid weld lines.
- **Basket Die (Strainer Die):** A conical porous mass replaces the spider legs; flow converges into the die land from a wide distribution area.
- **Coathanger / Side-Fed Die:** Used for flat products (sheets/films); the melt enters from one side and is distributed evenly through a *coathanger-shaped* manifold to a uniform width.

### 2.2.2 Cross-Head (Right-Angle) Heads

The melt is redirected by **90°** as it exits the extruder. Used primarily for:
- **Wire and cable insulation** (the conductor is fed in axially from the back of the cross-head, while the polymer enters from the side).
- **Jacketing / tubing over a substrate.**
- **Small profiles** where space constraints require compact tooling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The cross-head generates a **helical flow pattern** and a significant *weld line* on the inner surface. The "draw-down" or "core tube" length must be carefully designed to allow weld-line healing. For wire coating, the **tip-and-die** (also called "pressure die") and **tubing die** are the two main variants:
> - **Pressure Die:** Polymer melt contacts the conductor under pressure → better adhesion, thinner insulation, no vacuum needed.
> - **Tubing Die:** Polymer exits as a tube, is drawn down onto the conductor via vacuum → uniform coating, lower adhesion.

### 2.2.3 Co-extrusion Heads

A co-extrusion die combines **two or more polymer melt streams** into a single multi-layered extrudate. Two architectural approaches exist:

| Architecture | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedblock Co-extrusion** | Individual streams merged in a *feedblock* upstream of a single manifold die. | Flat films (food packaging) |
| **Multi-Manifold Co-extrusion** | Each layer has its own manifold; streams join near the die exit. | Sheets, blow films, pipes |
| **Combined / Modular** | Hybrid designs for complex layer sequences. | High-barrier packaging |

**Critical Design Variable — Viscosity Matching:** For stable layer distribution, the viscosities of the constituent melts at the die wall shear rate should be within a factor of **2×–3×**, otherwise the lower-viscosity layer migrates to the high-shear region and disrupts geometry.

### 2.2.4 Specialised Heads

#### (a) Blown Film Die (Annular / Spiral Die)
- An *annular* die with a central mandrel; the polymer exits as a thin-walled tube.
- Air is injected through the mandrel to inflate the tube into a "bubble" that is collapsed between nip rolls.
- The die is designed with a **spiral distributor** to ensure uniform circumferential flow.

#### (b) Sheet Extrusion Die (Coat-Hanger Die)
- A *T-shaped* or *coat-hanger* manifold distributes the melt laterally.
- The manifold pre-land length is computed such that pressure drop along the manifold equals the pressure drop across the die land, achieving **uniform flow across the width**.
- Typical manifold coefficients are tuned for a ±2% to ±5% gauge variation.

#### (c) Pipe Extrusion Die
- Spider-supported mandrel with calibrated sizing sleeve and vacuum or pressure calibrator downstream.
- A *bushing* on the outside and a *sizing mandrel* on the inside define the outer and inner diameters of the pipe.

## 2.3 Theoretical Foundation — Flow in the Die Land

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Die Land is where the polymer receives its final velocity profile.** The flow in the die land is analysed using the **Power Law (Ostwald–de Waele)** model of non-Newtonian behaviour.

For a power-law fluid flowing through a **slit die** of half-gap $h$ and width $W$ (with $W \gg h$):

$$\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

where $K$ is the consistency index and $n$ is the power-law index.

The **apparent shear rate** (Newtonian-equivalent) is:

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{6Q}{W h^2}$$

The **true shear rate at the wall** for a power-law fluid is:

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \dot{\gamma}_a \left(\frac{2n+1}{3n}\right)$$

The **pressure drop** across a die land of length $L$:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2L \dot{\gamma}_w}{D_h} \cdot K \dot{\gamma}_w^{n-1} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{n+1}\right) \cdot (n+1) = \frac{2 K L}{h} \left(\frac{2n+1}{3n}\right)^n \dot{\gamma}_a^n$$

The **die-swell ratio** (Bagley–Metzner-type estimation):

$$B \approx 0.5 + \left(\frac{\tau_w}{G}\right)^{0.5}$$

where $G$ is the elastic modulus of the melt. (Quantitative die-swell prediction requires viscoelastic constitutive models like the Phan-Thien–Tanner or Giesekus model.)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Apparent shear rate in slit die | $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{6Q}{W h^2}$ | s⁻¹; $Q$ in m³/s |
| 2 | Apparent shear rate in circular die | $\dot{\gamma}_a = \dfrac{32 Q}{\pi D^3}$ | s⁻¹ |
| 3 | True wall shear rate (Power Law) | $\dot{\gamma}_w = \dot{\gamma}_a \left(\dfrac{2n+1}{3n}\right)$ | s⁻¹ |
| 4 | Pressure drop in slit die (Power Law) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2KL}{h}\left(\dfrac{2n+1}{3n}\right)^n \dot{\gamma}_a^n$ | Pa; $K$ in Pa·sⁿ |
| 5 | Die swell ratio | $B = \dfrac{D_e}{D_0}$ | dimensionless, $B \in [1.0,\,1.5]$ typical |
| 6 | Draw-down ratio (DDR) | $\text{DDR} = \dfrac{A_{die}}{A_{extrudate}}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Draw ratio balance (DRB) | $\text{DRB} = \dfrac{\text{Machine direction stretch}}{\text{Transverse direction stretch}}$ | ≈ 1 for balanced film |
| 8 | Flow rate from extruder (Q) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi^2 D_s^3 N \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \dfrac{\pi D_s^3 (\sin\phi)^3}{12 \eta_e}\left(\dfrac{\Delta P}{L_s}\right)$ | m³/s (Newtonian) |
| 9 | Critical shear rate (sharkskin onset) | $\dot{\gamma}_{crit} \approx 10^4 - 10^5$ s⁻¹ for LLDPE | depends on polymer |
| 10 | Land L / gap h ratio | $\dfrac{L}{h} = 5 - 20$ | for stable flow & weld line healing |
| 11 | Manifold design (coathanger) | $\dfrac{dP_{manifold}}{dz} = -\dfrac{dP_{land}}{dz}$ | pressure gradients equalised |
| 12 | Volumetric flow in spiral die | $Q = \dfrac{\pi (D_o - D_i) \rho^2 \Delta P}{12 \mu L}$ | annular geometry |
| 13 | Co-extrusion viscosity match | $\eta_1 / \eta_2 \approx 1 - 3$ | at die wall shear rate |
| 14 | Wire coating line speed | $v_{line} = \dfrac{Q_{insulation}}{\pi (D_c + t_{wall}) t_{wall}}$ | m/min |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR TABLE:** The vertical pipe symbol (used in absolute-value expressions or "such that") is rendered as `\vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax. Example: $\vert \Delta P \vert$ instead of $| \Delta P |$ inside a table cell.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Extrusion heads are the **profit-defining components** of an extrusion line. Although the extruder is capital-intensive, the die determines:
- **Production speed** — well-designed dies run faster with less gauge variation.
- **Product quality** — surface finish, optical clarity, weld-line strength.
- **Material flexibility** — changeover between polymers without re-tooling.
- **Material savings** — tighter tolerances mean less polymer is wasted as edge trim or rejected product.

A poorly designed die is the **single largest source of rejects** in polymer processing — a 2% gauge variation in a 5 m wide film translates to tonnes of wasted polymer per year.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Drop of a Power-Law Fluid in a Slit Die

### Statement
Derive an expression for the pressure drop $\Delta P$ across a slit die of half-gap $h$, width $W$, and land length $L$ for a power-law fluid of consistency $K$ and power-law index $n$. Volumetric flow rate is $Q$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Governing Equation — Momentum Balance**

For steady, laminar, fully-developed flow of an incompressible fluid in a slit (assuming $W \gg h$, so edge effects are negligible), the Navier–Stokes equation reduces to:

$$\frac{dp}{dx} = \frac{d\tau}{dy}$$

where $x$ is the flow direction and $y$ is measured from the centreline.

**Step 2: Integrate Across the Gap**

Integrating from the centreline ($y = 0$, where $\tau = 0$ by symmetry) to a generic point $y$:

$$\int_0^y d\tau = \int_0^y \frac{dp}{dx} \, dy' \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau(y) = \frac{dp}{dx} \cdot y$$

**Step 3: Apply the Power-Law Constitutive Equation**

$$\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^n = K \left(-\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n$$

Equating the two expressions for $\tau$:

$$-K \left(\frac{du}{dy}\right)^n = \frac{dp}{dx} \cdot y$$

**Step 4: Solve for the Velocity Gradient**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = -\left(\frac{1}{K}\frac{dp}{dx}\right)^{1/n} y^{1/n}$$

**Step 5: Integrate Across the Gap to Find Velocity**

Integrating from the wall ($y = h$, $u = 0$) to a point $y$:

$$u(y) = \int_y^h \left(\frac{1}{K}\left(-\frac{dp}{dx}\right)\right)^{1/n} y'^{1/n} \, dy'$$

$$u(y) = \left(\frac{1}{K}\left(-\frac{dp}{dx}\right)\right)^{1/n} \cdot \frac{n}{n+1} \left(h^{(n+1)/n} - y^{(n+1)/n}\right)$$

**Step 6: Find the Volumetric Flow Rate**

Integrate $u(y)$ across the full cross-section (multiply by 2 for both halves, by $W$ for width):

$$Q = 2W \int_0^h u(y) \, dy$$

$$Q = 2W \left(\frac{1}{K}\left(-\frac{dp}{dx}\right)\right)^{1/n} \cdot \frac{n}{n+1} \cdot h^{(2n+1)/n} \cdot \frac{1}{(2n+1)/n}$$

$$Q = \frac{2n W h^{(2n+1)/n}}{2n+1} \left(\frac{1}{K}\left(-\frac{dp}{dx}\right)\right)^{1/n}$$

**Step 7: Invert to Find Pressure Gradient**

$$\left(-\frac{dp}{dx}\right) = K \left(\frac{(2n+1)Q}{2n W h^{(2n+1)/n}}\right)^n$$

**Step 8: Multiply by Land Length**

$$\Delta P = -\frac{dp}{dx} \cdot L = \frac{2 K L}{h} \left(\frac{2n+1}{2n}\right)^n \cdot \frac{Q^n}{(W h^2)^n} \cdot \frac{h}{2}$$

After algebraic simplification (using the apparent shear rate definition $\dot{\gamma}_a = 6Q/(Wh^2)$):

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{2 K L}{h} \left(\frac{2n+1}{3n}\right)^n \dot{\gamma}_a^{\, n}}$$

This is the **canonical die-land pressure drop formula** used in KTU-style numerical problems.

### Sample Numerical Application (Worked)

> **Problem:** HDPE melt ($K = 15000$ Pa·sⁿ, $n = 0.45$) flows through a slit die of half-gap $h = 1.5$ mm, width $W = 200$ mm, land length $L = 30$ mm. Volumetric flow rate $Q = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s. Compute the pressure drop and apparent shear rate.

**Solution:**

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{6 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{0.200 \times (0.0015)^2} = \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-4}}{4.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 2000 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

True wall shear rate correction:

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = 2000 \times \left(\frac{2(0.45)+1}{3(0.45)}\right)^{0.45} = 2000 \times (1.741)^{0.45} = 2000 \times 1.297 \approx 2594 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Pressure drop:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \times 15000 \times 0.030}{0.0015} \times (1.297)^{0.45} \times (2000)^{0.45}$$

Step by step:
- $2KL/h = 2 \times 15000 \times 0.030 / 0.0015 = 600{,}000$ Pa
- $(1.297)^{0.45} = 1.124$
- $(2000)^{0.45} = e^{0.45 \ln 2000} = e^{0.45 \times 7.601} = e^{3.420} = 30.59$
- $\Delta P = 600{,}000 \times 1.124 \times 30.59 = 2.063 \times 10^{7}$ Pa $\approx$ **20.6 MPa**

This is high but within range for some polyolefins at industrial throughputs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coathanger Manifold Pressure Distribution

### Statement
For a sheet extrusion die with a coathanger manifold, derive the manifold cross-sectional area $A_m(z)$ as a function of the lateral position $z$ such that the total flow is uniformly distributed into the die land.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define Geometry**

The coathanger die has a manifold of cross-sectional area $A_m(z)$ (varying with $z$), feeding a die-land of gap $h$, width $W$ (per unit lateral length), and land length $L$. The total sheet width is $B$.

**Step 2: Pressure Drop Condition**

The lateral pressure gradient inside the manifold must equal the pressure gradient required to push fluid out of the manifold into the die land at every $z$:

$$-\frac{dP_m}{dz} = \frac{\Delta P_{land}(z)}{L}$$

**Step 3: Flow Continuity**

The total flow at any point $z$ equals the integrated flow downstream of $z$:

$$Q_m(z) = \int_{0}^{z} q(z') \, dz' = \frac{Q_{total}}{B} (B - z)$$

where $q(z)$ is the per-unit-width flow into the die land.

**Step 4: Hagen–Poiseuille Type Equation for Manifold**

For the manifold (treated as a tapered channel of cross-section $A_m(z)$ and wetted perimeter $\mathcal{P}(z)$):

$$- \frac{dP_m}{dz} = \frac{2 K}{A_m^{n+1}} \left(\frac{(2n+1)Q_m}{2n \mathcal{P}}\right)^n$$

**Step 5: Equate and Solve**

Setting the manifold pressure gradient equal to the land requirement and solving the ODE for $A_m(z)$ yields the classic coathanger profile:

$$\boxed{A_m(z) = A_0 \left[1 - (1 - \lambda)\left(\frac{z}{B/2}\right)^{m}\right]^{1/n}}$$

where $\lambda = A_{tip}/A_0$ is the manifold area ratio and $m, n$ are design parameters dependent on rheology and flow distribution target.

This is why **real coathanger dies have a slightly tapered, "coat-hanger-shaped" manifold** — the area decreases from the centre (inlet) to the ends (lip).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of Die Types

| Criterion | In-Line Spider Die | Cross-Head Die | Coathanger Die | Spiral Mandrel Die | Co-extrusion (Feedblock) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Flow Direction** | Straight (0°) | 90° bend | Straight (T-fed) | Straight (axial) | Multi-stream merged |
| **Weld Line Risk** | Yes (downstream of spiders) | Yes (inner surface) | No (slot geometry) | Minimal (spiral mix) | Yes (between layers) |
| **Typical Product** | Pipes, tubes, rods | Wire insulation, jacketing | Sheets, flat films | Blown films, large pipes | Multi-layer films, sheets |
| **Pressure Drop** | Moderate | High (due to 90° turn) | Low (laminar slot) | Moderate | High (multiple streams) |
| **Cost & Complexity** | Low to moderate | Moderate | High (precision) | High | Very high |
| **Output Rate Limit** | High | Limited by weld-line healing | High (limited by gauge tolerance) | High | Moderate |
| **Suitability for Filled Polymers** | Good (large passages) | Moderate (abrasion at turn) | Poor (clogging risk) | Good | Limited |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Die Swell (Extrudate Swell) — Simplified

For an inelastic fluid (qualitative), die swell is dominated by **elastic recovery** of the polymer molecules after they leave the die. The most cited engineering correlation is:

$$B = 1 + \left[\left(\frac{\tau_w}{G}\right)^{0.5} - 0.5\right] \cdot \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{L}{h \cdot n}\right)\right]$$

where:
- $\tau_w$ = wall shear stress in the die
- $G$ = elastic modulus of the melt
- $L/h$ = land-to-gap ratio
- $n$ = power-law index

**Physical Interpretation:**
- $B \to 1$ as $L/h \to 0$ (no swelling when the melt is given no time to relax in the die).
- $B$ increases with $\tau_w$ (more elastic energy stored at higher shear).
- $B$ approaches a maximum as $L/h \to \infty$ (fully relaxed in die).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Note:** Die swell is the *principal reason* the die land gap is designed to be **smaller than the final extrudate thickness** in film/sheet operations. For example, a 1 mm thick film is often produced from a 0.7–0.8 mm die gap.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

> **Q1.** Define an *extrusion head*. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of an **in-line spider die** used for pipe extrusion. What is the role of the **die land** in this die?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **extrusion head** is the shaping tooling mounted at the discharge end of an extruder that receives molten polymer and forms it into a continuous profile of defined cross-section.

In an **in-line spider die**, the polymer melt from the extruder flows axially through a **breaker plate** (which eliminates the spiral flow induced by the screw rotation) and enters a die body. A central **mandrel** — supported by 3 to 6 radial **spider legs** — defines the inner profile of the pipe. The melt splits and flows around the spider legs, then re-converges in the **die land** (a parallel-walled final shaping zone) before exiting as a tubular section.

The **die land** plays three critical roles:
1. **Final dimensional accuracy** — establishes the precise outer/inner diameter of the pipe.
2. **Weld-line healing** — gives the melt sufficient time *post-spider* for molecular interdiffusion across the re-joining flow fronts, restoring mechanical strength.
3. **Velocity profile equilibration** — converts the non-uniform velocity profile downstream of the spiders into a near-uniform plug flow at the die exit.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **Q2.** Differentiate between a **pressure die** and a **tubing die** used in wire and cable insulation. Mention one application where each is preferred.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Pressure Die (Tip-and-Die) | Tubing Die |
|---|---|---|
| Melt contact with conductor | Polymer contacts the conductor under **pressure** | Polymer exits as a **tube**; collapses onto conductor via **vacuum** downstream |
| Adhesion to conductor | **High** (mechanical + chemical bonding) | **Low to moderate** (friction fit) |
| Insulation thickness control | Controlled by **die land geometry** and line speed | Controlled by **draw-down ratio** |
| Concentricity | Excellent at low speeds; requires precision tooling | Excellent at high speeds; less sensitive to die wear |
| **Preferred Application** | **Building wire, automotive wiring** (insulation must bond tightly) | **CATV coax, fibre optic jacketing** (insulation must slide over the dielectric) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question is internally optional — students answer *one* of the two choices (14 marks total). Sub-parts are typically 7 + 7.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

> **Q.A.** (a) Classify extrusion heads based on the direction of polymer flow. Describe the working and applications of **cross-head extrusion dies** used in wire coating. Add a note on **co-extrusion heads** and their design challenges. **(7 Marks)**
>
> (b) A high-density polyethylene (HDPE) melt with consistency $K = 2 \times 10^4$ Pa·sⁿ and power-law index $n = 0.4$ flows through a **slit die** of half-gap $h = 2$ mm, width $W = 300$ mm, and land length $L = 40$ mm. The volumetric flow rate is $Q = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s. Calculate the **apparent shear rate**, **true wall shear rate**, and **pressure drop** across the die. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Classification & Description (7 Marks)**

**Classification of Extrusion Heads by Flow Direction:**

1. **In-Line (Straight-Through) Heads:** Flow remains axial; used for symmetric products (pipes, tubes, rods).
2. **Cross-Head (Right-Angle) Heads:** Flow is redirected by 90°; used for wire coating, cable jacketing.
3. **Offset Heads:** Flow is redirected by an angle < 90°; used in compact tooling for small profiles.
4. **Co-extrusion Heads:** Multiple melt streams combined; used for multilayer films, sheets, pipes.
5. **Specialised Heads:** Coathanger, spiral mandrel, spinneret, foam, etc.

**Cross-Head Die for Wire Coating (Working):**

In a cross-head, the melt from the extruder enters **horizontally** into a tooling body and is **redirected 90°** so that it flows **axially** around a central conductor. The conductor (a copper wire, for example) is fed continuously through the **back of the cross-head**, passing through a **guide tip** that centres it. The melt stream envelopes the conductor in a region called the **coating zone**, where it solidifies under cooling. Two variants exist:
- **Pressure Die (Tip-and-Die):** Polymer is forced onto the conductor under high pressure. Insulation bonds strongly to the conductor; concentricity is excellent.
- **Tubing Die:** Polymer is extruded as a tube, then drawn down onto the conductor using vacuum. Lower adhesion, better for high-speed lines.

**Co-extrusion Heads — Design Challenges:**

A co-extrusion die combines multiple melt streams into a single multilayered structure. Two main architectures:
- **Feedblock + Single-Manifold Die:** Streams merged upstream of a standard die; simple, but limited to 3–5 layers.
- **Multi-Manifold Die:** Each layer has its own manifold; allows more layers (up to 9–11) and tighter layer control.

**Design challenges include:**
1. **Viscosity matching** — mismatched viscosities cause layer encapsulation, interfacial instability, or layer inversion. The viscosity ratio should ideally be within **1:3** at the die wall shear rate.
2. **Interfacial instability** — high shear between layers can cause "layer breakup" or encapsulation of the high-viscosity layer.
3. **Layer thickness control** — especially for very thin layers (e.g., tie-layers at < 5 µm).
4. **Polymer compatibility** — adhesion between adjacent layers depends on chemical compatibility; tie-layers are often needed.
5. **Thermal management** — different polymers have different optimal melt temperatures; the die must thermally isolate or balance them.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

Given:
- $K = 2 \times 10^4$ Pa·sⁿ
- $n = 0.4$
- $h = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $W = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3$ m
- $L = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04$ m
- $Q = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s

**Step 1: Apparent Shear Rate**
