$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{6Q}{W h^2} = \frac{6 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{0.3 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

$$\dot{\gamma}_a = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.3 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 1000 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: True Wall Shear Rate (Power-Law Correction)**

$$\dot{\gamma}_w = \dot{\gamma}_a \left(\frac{2n+1}{3n}\right) = 1000 \times \left(\frac{2(0.4)+1}{3(0.4)}\right) = 1000 \times \left(\frac{1.8}{1.2}\right) = 1000 \times 1.5 = 1500 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Pressure Drop**

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 K L}{h} \left(\frac{2n+1}{3n}\right)^n \dot{\gamma}_a^{\, n}$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2 \times 2 \times 10^4 \times 0.04}{2 \times 10^{-3}} \times (1.5)^{0.4} \times (1000)^{0.4}$$

Compute each factor:
- $\frac{2KL}{h} = \frac{2 \times 2 \times 10^4 \times 0.04}{0.002} = 8 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $(1.5)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 1.5} = e^{0.4 \times 0.4055} = e^{0.1622} = 1.176$
- $(1000)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \times 6.908} = e^{2.763} = 15.85$

Therefore:

$$\Delta P = 8 \times 10^5 \times 1.176 \times 15.85 = 1.49 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \approx 14.9 \text{ MPa}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **Apparent Shear Rate:** $\dot{\gamma}_a = 1000$ s⁻¹
- **True Wall Shear Rate:** $\dot{\gamma}_w = 1500$ s⁻¹
- **Pressure Drop:** $\Delta P \approx 14.9$ MPa

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $W$ (width) and $h$ (half-gap) in the apparent shear rate formula.** The formula is $\dot{\gamma}_a = 6Q/(Wh^2)$ for a *slit* die — students frequently write $6Q/(2W h^2)$ or use the *full gap* $2h$ instead of half-gap $h$. This is a **2-mark deduction per error**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Power-Law correction.** Using $\Delta P = 12 \mu L Q / (W h^3)$ (Newtonian formula) on a shear-thinning polymer is a **3-mark error** and signals poor understanding of polymer rheology.
> 3. **Not stating units explicitly in the final answer.** Always write s⁻¹ and Pa / MPa in the boxed answer.
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps.** Even if the final answer is correct, KTU examiners deduct marks for "no work shown" on numerical problems. Always show the substituted equation before evaluating.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> **Q.B.** (a) With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of a **coathanger die** used for sheet extrusion. Discuss how **uniformity of flow** across the die width is achieved. **(7 Marks)**
>
> (b) Explain the phenomenon of **die swell** in polymer extrusion. A circular die of diameter $D_0 = 4$ mm produces an extrudate of diameter $D_e = 4.6$ mm. Calculate the **die swell ratio**. If the same extrudate is drawn at a haul-off speed such that the final diameter is $D_f = 3.8$ mm, calculate the **draw-down ratio (DDR)**. Comment on the effect of the land length ($L/h$) on the die swell value. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Coathanger Die (7 Marks)**

A **coathanger die** is the most common die for flat-sheet and cast-film extrusion. It consists of:

1. **Inlet Port** — receives melt from the extruder at a single point (centre of the die width).
2. **Coathanger Manifold** — a tapered channel whose cross-section *decreases* from the centre to the ends. The tapering is designed to **maintain constant pressure** along the manifold, despite the lateral outflow of melt.
3. **Pre-land** — a short, often converging channel downstream of the manifold that further equalises the velocity profile.
4. **Die Land / Lip** — the final parallel-walled region that determines the sheet thickness.
5. **Lip Adjustment Bolts** — allow fine-tuning of the local gap (and hence the local thickness).

**Working Principle:**
- Melt enters the die at the centre via the inlet port.
- It flows **laterally** in both directions through the coathanger manifold.
- As it moves laterally, a portion of the melt leaks out (downward) through the pre-land and die land.
- The **manifold cross-section decreases** toward the ends, which **increases the local velocity** and **maintains the pressure drop** equal at all lateral positions.

**Uniformity of Flow — Design Equation:**
The fundamental design rule is:

$$- \left(\frac{dP}{dz}\right)_{\text{manifold}} = \frac{\Delta P_{\text{land}}}{L_{\text{land}}}$$

This ensures the pressure at the die exit is uniform across the entire width, producing a sheet of **constant thickness**.

**Part (b) — Die Swell & Draw-Down (7 Marks)**

**Die Swell Definition:**
*Die swell* (also called *extrudate swell* or *Barus effect*) is the phenomenon where the cross-sectional dimensions of the polymer extrudate *exceed* the corresponding dimensions of the die orifice from which it emerged. It is caused by the **elastic recovery** of polymer chains that were deformed during flow through the die.

**Calculations:**

Given:
- $D_0 = 4$ mm (die diameter)
- $D_e = 4.6$ mm (free extrudate diameter)
- $D_f = 3.8$ mm (final drawn diameter)

**Die Swell Ratio:**

$$B = \frac{D_e}{D_0} = \frac{4.6}{4.0} = 1.15$$

**Draw-Down Ratio (DDR):**

$$\text{DDR} = \frac{A_{\text{die}}}{A_{\text{final}}} = \left(\frac{D_0}{D_f}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4.0}{3.8}\right)^2 = (1.0526)^2 = 1.108$$

(For circular cross-sections, DDR is the square of the diameter ratio.)

**Effect of Land Length ($L/h$) on Die Swell:**

- At **$L/h \to 0$** (no die land), the polymer has **no residence time** in the die to relax, so **die swell approaches 1.0** (no swelling).
- As $L/h$ **increases**, the polymer molecules have more time to relax their elastic strain, so die swell **increases** and asymptotically approaches a **maximum value** characteristic of the polymer.
- The asymptotic value is given approximately by:

$$B_{\max} = 1 + \left(\frac{\tau_w}{G}\right)^{0.5}$$

- For $L/h$ in the range **5–20** (typical industrial die design), die swell is at or near its maximum.
- This is why **die land geometry is critical** in calibrating the final extrudate dimensions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Die Swell Problems:**
> 1. **Using $D_e/D_0$ vs $D_f/D_0$ interchangeably.** Die swell uses the *free extrudate* diameter, not the *drawn* diameter. Conflating them is a 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Forgetting to square the diameter ratio for area-based DDR.** DDR is defined on *area* — students who write $D_0/D_f$ directly lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Stating the wrong direction of $L/h$ effect on $B$.** Some students think longer lands *reduce* die swell because they "compress" the melt. In fact, longer lands *increase* the time for elastic recovery, hence *increase* swell.
> 4. **Confusing "die swell" with "draw-down."** Die swell is the *unconstrained expansion*; draw-down is the *forced thinning* during haul-off. They are opposite phenomena.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Extrusion Heads (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Extrusion head = die = shaping tooling at the end of the extruder.
- **Three primary functions:** Shape formation, flow homogenisation, pressure/temperature control.
- **Three flow-direction families:** In-line, cross-head, offset.
- **Product-specific families:** Pipe (spider/spiral mandrel), sheet (coathanger), film (coathanger / annular blown), wire (cross-head pressure / tubing), profile (custom die + calibrator).
- **In-line spider die:** Mandrel supported by 3–6 spider legs; **weld lines** form and must heal in the die land.
- **Cross-head die:** 90° redirection; used for wire/cable; two variants — **pressure die** (good adhesion) and **tubing die** (good concentricity at high speed).
- **Coathanger die:** Tapered manifold → uniform pressure across width → uniform sheet thickness. Manifold area decreases from centre to ends.
- **Co-extrusion die:** Multiple polymer streams combined; **feedblock** or **multi-manifold** designs; viscosity ratio must be ≤ 3:1.
- **Blown film die:** Annular/spiral die; air injected through mandrel inflates the tube; key parameter is **blow-up ratio (BUR)**.
- **Die land length:** Typically $L/h = 5$ to $20$ for stable flow and weld-line healing.
- **Apparent shear rate (slit):** $\dot{\gamma}_a = 6Q/(Wh^2)$.
- **Apparent shear rate (circular):** $\dot{\gamma}_a = 32Q/(\pi D^3)$.
- **Power-law pressure drop:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{2KL}{h}\left(\dfrac{2n+1}{3n}\right)^n \dot{\gamma}_a^{\, n}$.
- **Die swell ratio:** $B = D_e / D_0 \in [1.0, 1.5]$ typical; due to elastic recovery.
- **Draw-down ratio:** $\text{DDR} = A_{die} / A_{extrudate} = (D_0 / D_f)^2$ for circular sections.
- **Draw Ratio Balance (DRB):** For balanced biaxial film, DRB ≈ 1.
- **Sharkskin / Melt Fracture:** Onset typically at $\dot{\gamma}_{crit} \sim 10^4$–$10^5$ s⁻¹ for polyolefins; can be mitigated by die-land design or polymer additives.
- **Screen pack + breaker plate:** Remove contaminants, eliminate spiral flow, build back-pressure.
- **Weld line:** Region downstream of spider legs or layer mergers where flow re-converges; strength depends on molecular interdiffusion time (∝ land length).
- **Co-extrusion viscosity matching:** $\eta_1 / \eta_2 \le 3$ at wall shear rate.
- **Blow-up ratio (BUR):** $\text{BUR} = D_{bubble} / D_{die}$; typical 2:1 to 4:1 for HDPE/LDPE films.
- **Sizing / Calibration:** Downstream of the die, it freezes the extrudate dimensions via vacuum or pressure; not strictly part of the die but integral to the extrusion head's performance.
- **Manifold design rule:** $\left(-dP/dz\right)_{manifold} = \Delta P_{land} / L_{land}$ at every $z$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "I COPE Spiral Down" for Die Classification:**
> - **I**n-line (pipes, rods)
> - **C**ross-head (wire, cable)
> - **O**ffset (small profiles)
> - **P**rofile dies (3D, custom)
> - **E**xtrusion-coat (lamination dies)
> - **Spiral** (blown film)
> - **Down**stream — coathanger for sheet/cast film

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Controls for Blow Moulding Machine
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process Controls for Blow Moulding Machine

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Controls for Blow Moulding Machine** refer to the integrated, closed-loop and open-loop regulation systems that monitor, command, and stabilize the critical parameters — *temperature, pressure, time, position, flow, and velocity* — governing the conversion of thermoplastic melt into a dimensionally accurate, defect-free hollow product. These controls span the **extruder (plasticating unit)**, **die head (parison formation)**, **mold clamping system**, **blowing station**, and **cooling/ejection station**, and are implemented through a hierarchy of sensors, controllers (PID, PLC, DCS), and actuators (hydraulic, pneumatic, servo-electric).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, blow moulding process controls are classified under **Machine-Directed Process Parameters (MDPP)** and **Material-Directed Process Parameters (MDPP)** — the former relating to screw speed, die gap, clamp tonnage, and blow pressure, and the latter relating to melt viscosity, shear rate, parison swell, and cooling rate.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bakery Cake Injector"

Imagine you are using a piping bag to fill a cake mould. To get a perfect shape, you must control:
- **The temperature of the cream** (too cold = hard to pipe, too warm = messy) — this is the **melt temperature control** of the extruder.
- **How hard you squeeze** — this is the **screw speed / extruder pressure**.
- **How much cream you release before the mould closes** — this is the **parison length and wall thickness control**.
- **The burst of air that makes it puff into shape** — this is the **blow pressure and blow flow rate**.
- **How long you wait before opening the mould** — this is the **cooling time / cycle time**.

Just as a baker instinctively adjusts all these, a blow moulding machine uses sensors and controllers to make these adjustments automatically, hundreds of times per hour, with micron-level precision.

## 1.3 Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]
> The modern blow moulding machine is a **mechatronic system** — its quality, repeatability, and energy efficiency are dictated not by the mechanics alone, but by the intelligence of the control loops. Industry 4.0-ready blow moulding lines integrate **OPC-UA, MQTT, and cloud-based SCADA** for predictive process control.

Key standard metrics (bolded for emphasis):
- **Melt Temperature Window:** **180 °C – 240 °C** (polyolefins); **200 °C – 270 °C** (PET, PC).
- **Blow Pressure Range:** **0.4 MPa – 1.0 MPa** (low pressure for shaping); **2.0 MPa – 4.0 MPa** (high pressure for detail).
- **Cycle Time:** **5 s – 60 s** depending on part weight and wall thickness.
- **Parison Die Swell Ratio:** **1.10 – 1.60** depending on shear rate and polymer rheology.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Control Architecture Hierarchy

A modern blow moulding machine has a **five-tier control hierarchy**:

| Tier | Level | Components | Function |
|------|-------|------------|----------|
| **L1** | Field Instrumentation | Thermocouples, pressure transducers, linear variable differential transformers (LVDTs), flow meters | Sense actual process state |
| **L2** | Local Loop Control | PID controllers, servo drives, hydraulic proportional valves | Maintain setpoints via closed-loop feedback |
| **L3** | Machine Supervisory Control | PLC (e.g., Siemens S7-1500, Allen-Bradley CompactLogix) | Sequence logic, interlock, recipe management |
| **L4** | Cell / Line Control | HMI, SCADA, MES interface | Operator interface, batch traceability |
| **L5** | Enterprise / Cloud | ERP, IIoT gateways | Production analytics, predictive maintenance |

## 2.2 Sub-Systems and Their Controlled Variables

### 2.2.1 Extruder (Plasticating Unit) Control
- **Barrel Zone Temperatures (T₁, T₂, T₃, T₄):** controlled via band heaters and PID with thermocouple feedback.
- **Melt Temperature (Tm) and Melt Pressure (Pm):** measured at the adapter/die inlet using a **melt thermocouple + pressure transducer** (often combined as Dynisco® or Gefran® sensors).
- **Screw Speed (N):** controlled via inverter-driven AC motor or servo motor; range **10 – 120 rpm**.
- **Throughput (Q):** related to screw speed via the melt conveying equation.

### 2.2.2 Parison Die Head Control
- **Die Gap / Wall Thickness Profile:** Modern machines use **axial profile control** (up to **100 axial points** along parison length) and **circumferential profile control** to counter die swell and sag.
- **Parison Length (Lp):** controlled by shot size (stroke of accumulator or extruder).
- **Die Swell (B):** B = Dpar / Ddie, where Dpar is the parison diameter just outside the die and Ddie is the die exit diameter.

### 2.2.3 Clamp / Mold Carriage Control
- **Mold Closing Speed (vc):** typically fast approach → slow clamp-in to prevent mold damage.
- **Clamp Tonnage (Fcl):** Hydraulic or toggle-electric; must overcome blowing pressure times projected area.

### 2.2.4 Blowing Station Control
- **Pre-blow / Needle-in Delay:** typically **50 – 200 ms** after mold close.
- **Low-pressure Air (PL):** **0.2 – 0.5 MPa** for initial expansion.
- **High-pressure Air (PH):** **0.6 – 4.0 MPa** for final shaping and detail replication.
- **Blow Flow Rate (Qair):** measured and controlled via mass flow controllers.
- **Exhaust:** vacuum-assisted deflation for cooling efficiency.

### 2.2.5 Cooling & Ejection Control
- **Mold Temperature (Tmo):** **5 °C – 60 °C** depending on polymer; controlled via chilled water circulation.
- **Cooling Time (tc):** governed by heat conduction through wall; calculated from 1D transient conduction.
- **Cycle Time (tcycle):** tcycle = tclose + tblow + tcool + topen + teject.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are essential for KTU Part B (14-mark) derivations and numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}$ | Extruder throughput (drag flow) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $v = \frac{\pi D N \cos\phi - \frac{P_m \, h^3 \sin\phi}{12 \eta L}}{1}$ | Net melt velocity in metering zone | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ |
| 3 | $B = \frac{D_{par}}{D_{die}} = 1 + \lambda (\dot{\gamma}^n - \dot{\gamma}_0^n)$ | Die swell ratio (simplified) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $P_b \cdot A = F_{cl}$ | Blow pressure × projected area = clamp force | $\text{N}$ |
| 5 | $t_c = \frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left(\frac{8(T_m - T_{mo})}{\pi (T_e - T_{mo})}\right)$ | Cooling time (1D transient conduction) | $\text{s}$ |
| 6 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID controller output (continuous) | control signal |
| 7 | $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum e[k] + K_d (e[k] - e[k-1])$ | PID output (discrete, KTU favourite) | control signal |
| 8 | $R_z = \frac{T_{max} - T_{final}}{T_{final} - T_{setpoint}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage overshoot definition | % |
| 9 | $\dot{\gamma}_{apparent} = \frac{32 Q}{\pi D^3}$ | Apparent shear rate in die land | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 10 | $\eta_a = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Power-law viscosity (Ostwald-de Waele) | $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ |

> **Where the variables denote:**
> $D$ = screw diameter, $N$ = screw speed, $h$ = flight depth, $\phi$ = helix angle, $P_m$ = melt pressure, $\eta$ = viscosity, $L$ = metering length, $s$ = wall thickness, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, $T_m$ = melt temperature, $T_{mo}$ = mold temperature, $T_e$ = ejection temperature, $K_p, K_i, K_d$ = PID gains, $e(t)$ = error signal, $K$ = consistency index, $n$ = power-law index.

## 2.4 The "Why & How" of Parison Profile Control

The **Why:** A parison extruded at uniform wall thickness will exhibit *gravity sag* and *non-uniform cooling*, producing asymmetric wall distribution in the final article — leading to weak spots, warpage, and excessive material use.

**The How:**
1. The die gap is varied in real time via a **servo-driven mandrel or die lip** as the parison is extruded.
2. The axial profile is pre-programmed (typically a **pulse pattern of 20 – 100 axial points**) to compensate for sag.
3. A **wall thickness monitoring system** (often using capacitive or ultrasonic sensors) measures actual wall thickness in the blow-moulded part at multiple stations and feeds the data back to the PLC, which adjusts the next shot's profile — this is **closed-loop profile control**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **axial-circumferential parison programming** is one of the **most frequently tested topics** in KTU Module 3 — students must be able to draw the pulse pattern diagram and explain its purpose.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Melt Pressure–Throughput Relationship in the Metering Zone

Consider the metering section of a single-screw extruder (length $L_m$, channel depth $H$, screw diameter $D$, helix angle $\phi$). The flow is the superposition of **drag flow** and **pressure flow** in a shallow rectangular channel.

**Step 1: Drag flow rate** (Newtonian fluid, no pressure gradient):
The melt is dragged by the moving barrel at velocity $v_b = \pi D N$. The volumetric flow per unit width is:
$$q_{drag} = \frac{v_b \cdot H}{2} = \frac{\pi D N H}{2}$$

**Step 2: Pressure flow rate** (back flow due to die restriction):
For a pressure gradient $\partial P / \partial z$ along the channel of depth $H$ and width $W = \pi D \sin\phi$:
$$q_{pressure} = -\frac{H^3 \, W}{12 \eta} \cdot \frac{\partial P}{\partial z}$$

**Step 3: Net flow rate** (combining and substituting $W = \pi D \sin\phi$, $H = h$):
$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= q_{drag} + q_{pressure} \\
Q &= \frac{\pi D N h \sin\phi}{2} - \frac{\pi D h^3 \sin\phi}{12 \eta} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L_m} \\
Q &= \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} - \frac{\pi D h^3 \sin\phi}{12 \eta L_m} \cdot \Delta P
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Express in compact form** (drop subscript $m$ for metering):
$$Q = A \cdot N - B \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\eta}$$
where $A = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}$ and $B = \dfrac{\pi D h^3 \sin\phi}{12 L_m}$.

> This is the **famous extruder characteristic equation** — it shows that throughput $Q$ depends on screw speed $N$ and back pressure $\Delta P$. In blow moulding, $\Delta P$ is dictated by the **die restrictor / accumulator / head pressure**, which is why **die head pressure is a primary control variable**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Cooling Time (1D Transient Conduction)

**Step 1: Governing equation** for a polymer slab of half-thickness $s$, initially at $T_m$, suddenly cooled at both faces to mold temperature $T_{mo}$:
$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 2: Apply separation of variables** with boundary conditions:
- $T(0,t) = T_{mo}$ (mold wall, $x = 0$)
- $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big|_{x=s} = 0$ (symmetry at centerline)
- $T(x,0) = T_m$ (initial condition)

**Step 3: Series solution**:
$$T(x,t) - T_{mo} = \frac{4(T_m - T_{mo})}{\pi} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1} \cos\!\left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2s}\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 \alpha t}{4 s^2}\right)$$

**Step 4: At the centerline ($x = s$)**, only odd cosine terms with $(-1)^n$ survive, giving:
$$T_c(t) - T_{mo} = \frac{4(T_m - T_{mo})}{\pi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha t}{4 s^2}\right)$$

**Step 5: Solve for time $t_c$ at which centerline reaches ejection temperature $T_e$**:
$$\begin{aligned}
T_e - T_{mo} &= \frac{4(T_m - T_{mo})}{\pi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha t_c}{4 s^2}\right) \\
\exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha t_c}{4 s^2}\right) &= \frac{\pi (T_e - T_{mo})}{4 (T_m - T_{mo})} \\
-\frac{\pi^2 \alpha t_c}{4 s^2} &= \ln\!\left(\frac{\pi (T_e - T_{mo})}{4 (T_m - T_{mo})}\right) \\
t_c &= \frac{4 s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\frac{4 (T_m - T_{mo})}{\pi (T_e - T_{mo})}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** Cooling time is proportional to the **square of the wall thickness** — doubling the wall thickness quadruples the cooling time. This is why **parison thickness control** is so critical for productivity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PID Controller Output (Velocity / Positional Form)

The KTU 2024 examiner loves the **discrete PID form**. Starting from the continuous form:
$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 1: Discretize** with sample time $\Delta t$, current sample $k$:
$$u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] \Delta t + K_d \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 2: Positional form** (output sent directly to actuator):
$$u[k] = K_p \left[ e[k] + \frac{\Delta t}{T_i} \sum e[j] + \frac{T_d}{\Delta t}\big(e[k] - e[k-1]\big) \right]$$
where $T_i = K_p / K_i$ and $T_d = K_d / K_p$.

**Step 3: Velocity form** (incremental — preferred in PLC implementations to prevent **integral windup**):
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta u[k] &= u[k] - u[k-1] \\
\Delta u[k] &= K_p \Big[ (e[k] - e[k-1]) + \frac{\Delta t}{T_i} e[k] + \frac{T_d}{\Delta t}(e[k] - 2e[k-1] + e[k-2]) \Big]
\end{aligned}$$

> This incremental form is what is implemented in the **Siemens FB 41 "CONT_C"** block used in real blow moulding machines.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Set-ups

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Equation |
|--------------|-------|------|--------------|
| Extruder output | $D=60$ mm, $h=3$ mm, $N=60$ rpm, $\phi=17.7°$ | $Q$ (m³/s) | Equation 1 |
| Cooling time | $s=2$ mm, $\alpha = 10^{-7}$ m²/s, $T_m=200°C$, $T_{mo}=30°C$, $T_e=80°C$ | $t_c$ | Step 5 of §3.2 |
| Clamp tonnage | $P_b=0.8$ MPa, $A_{proj}=0.05$ m² | $F_{cl}$ | Equation 4 |
| Shear rate in die | $Q=2\times10^{-5}$ m³/s, $D=0.02$ m | $\dot{\gamma}$ | Equation 9 |
| PID tuning effect | Given $K_p, T_i, T_d$, step setpoint | $R_z$ (% overshoot) | Ziegler–Nichols table |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Process Control Architecture for a Blow Moulding Machine

```mermaid
graph TD
    SP[Setpoint e.g. Melt Temp 200C] --> SUM[1 Summing Junction]
    PV[Process Variable e.g. Melt Temp Measured] --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR[Error Signal e t]
    ERR --> PID[2 PID Controller Kp Ki Kd]
    PID --> ACT[3 Actuator Heater or Servo Valve]
    ACT --> PROC[4 Blow Moulding Process Extruder Die Clamp Blow]
    PROC --> SENSOR[5 Sensor Thermocouple LVDT Pressure Transducer]
    SENSOR --> PV
    FB[Feedback Path] -.-> PV
    HMI[6 HMI SCADA Recipe Management] --> SP
    HMI --> PID
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer dashed loop** is the classical feedback loop (SP → error → controller → actuator → process → sensor → PV).
- The **HMI/SCADA block** is the supervisory layer that downloads recipes, logs trends, and provides operator override.
- Every block in a real machine is implemented as a **structured text (ST) or ladder logic (LD) function block** in the PLC.

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Parison Programming & Wall Thickness Control

```mermaid
graph TD
    RECIPE[1 Recipe Memory 100 Axial Points] --> PROFC[2 Profile Generator PLC]
    PROFC --> SERVO[3 Servo Motor Drive]
    SERVO --> DIE[4 Die Lip Mandrel]
    DIE --> PAR[5 Parison Extrusion]
    PAR --> CAP[6 Capacitive Wall Sensor]
    CAP --> PLCFB[7 Closed Loop Feedback to PLC]
    PLCFB --> PROFC
    PAR --> MOLD[8 Closed Mold + Blow Pin]
    MOLD --> PART[9 Blow Moulded Part]
    PART --> QC[10 Post Mold Thickness Check]
    QC --> PLCFB
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Steps 1 → 5 are the **forward path** of the profile-control loop.
- Step 6 → 7 is the **in-process feedback** that corrects the next parison.
- Steps 9 → 10 is the **post-process correction loop** used in Industry 4.0 plants.

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram — Blow Moulding Machine Cycle

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[State S0 Idle Mold Open] --> S1[State S1 Mold Closing]
    S1 --> S2[State S2 Parison Extrusion]
    S2 --> S3[State S3 Mold Closed Needle In]
    S3 --> S4[State S4 Low Pressure Pre Blow]
    S4 --> S5[State S5 High Pressure Final Blow]
    S5 --> S6[State S6 Hold Cooling]
    S6 --> S7[State S7 Exhaust and Deflation]
    S7 --> S8[State S8 Mold Opening]
    S8 --> S9[State S9 Part Ejection]
    S9 --> S0
    S6 -.->|tc reached| S7
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each state transition is gated by a **condition** (e.g., position reached, pressure achieved, timer elapsed) — this is implemented as a **sequential function chart (SFC / Grafcet)** in IEC 61131-3 PLC programming, which is the standard for KTU lab viva questions.

## 4.4 Sensor & Actuator Mapping Table

| Process Variable | Sensor | Signal Type | Actuator | Control Algorithm |
|------------------|--------|-------------|----------|-------------------|
| Barrel temperature | Type-J/K thermocouple | mV → 4–20 mA | Band heater + SSR | PID with auto-tune |
| Melt pressure | Strain-gauge transducer (Dynisco) | 4–20 mA / 0–10 V | Screw speed (VFD) | Cascade to screw RPM |
| Parison length | LVDT / encoder | 0–10 V | Accumulator / shot timer | Time-based + position |
| Wall thickness | Capacitive / ultrasonic | 0–10 V | Servo die gap | Profile lookup table |
| Clamp force | Pressure transducer in hydraulic line | 4–20 mA | Proportional valve | Pressure feedback |
| Blow pressure | Pressure transducer in blow line | 4–20 mA | Blow valve (proportional) | Two-stage PI |
| Mold temperature | RTD Pt100 | Resistance | Chilled water valve | PID with dead-band |
| Cycle time | PLC timer | Internal | Sequence sequencer | SFC / state machine |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1.** Define **die swell** in extrusion blow moulding. Mention two factors affecting it.

**Model Answer:**
Die swell is the phenomenon where the extrudate (parison) diameter/ thickness at the die exit is greater than the die land dimension. Mathematically, $B = D_{par}/D_{die} > 1$. It occurs due to **(i) elastic recovery** of the viscoelastic polymer melt upon exiting the high-shear die land, and **(ii) molecular orientation relaxation**.
**Factors:** (1) Shear rate (higher $\dot{\gamma}$ → higher swell), (2) Melt temperature (higher $T$ → lower swell), (3) Die land length, (4) Polymer molecular weight / MFI.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2.** What is the function of a **parison programmer** in an extrusion blow moulding machine?

**Model Answer:**
A parison programmer is a **closed-loop servo-hydraulic / servo-electric control system** that varies the **die gap (mandrel position) as a function of parison length** during extrusion. Its purpose is to **compensate for parison sag and non-uniform cooling** by increasing wall thickness in regions (e.g., shoulder, base) that experience higher stretch. Modern programmers control **up to 100 axial points** plus **circumferential profile** to achieve uniform wall thickness in the final product, reducing material usage by 10–20%.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Repeated Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **closed-loop PID control system** used for **melt temperature regulation** in an extruder. Derive the discrete PID algorithm and discuss the effect of each term ($K_p, K_i, K_d$) on system response. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-screw extruder has diameter **D = 75 mm**, flight depth in metering zone **h = 2.5 mm**, helix angle **ϕ = 17.65°**, screw speed **N = 70 rpm**, and melt viscosity **η = 200 Pa·s**. If the metering length **L = 10D** and the die pressure drop is **ΔP = 5 MPa**, calculate the **(i) drag flow**, **(ii) pressure flow**, and **(iii) net throughput** of the extruder. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram** (Refer to §4.1 of these notes for the equivalent schematic). The components are:
- **Setpoint (SP):** Desired melt temperature (e.g., 200 °C) entered via HMI.
- **Sensor:** Type-K thermocouple inserted in the adapter / die body.
- **Transmitter:** Converts mV to standard 4–20 mA signal.
- **PID Controller:** Implemented in PLC as a function block (e.g., Siemens FB 41).
- **Actuator:** Solid-state relay (SSR) switching the band heater, or a PID-controlled SCR power controller.
- **Process:** Polymer melt in barrel zone whose temperature depends on heater input, screw shearing, and conduction.

**Step 2 — Discrete PID derivation** (as in §3.3 of these notes):
$$u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] \Delta t + K_d \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 3 — Effect of each term:**
- **$K_p$ (Proportional):** Reduces rise time, increases responsiveness, but causes steady-state error (offset) if used alone. Higher $K_p$ → faster response but more overshoot.
- **$K_i$ (Integral):** Eliminates steady-state error by accumulating past errors. Causes **integral wind-up** if not anti-windup protected; slows response.
- **$K_d$ (Derivative):** Acts on rate of change of error — provides **damping**, reduces overshoot, improves stability. Sensitive to noise (requires filtering).

**Step 4 — Tuning methods (mention in 2–3 lines):**
Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop: increase $K_p$ until sustained oscillation → set $K_u$ and $T_u$ → apply $K_p = 0.6 K_u, T_i = 0.5 T_u, T_d = 0.125 T_u$.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $D = 0.075$ m, $h = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\phi = 17.65°$, $N = 70/60 = 1.1667$ rps, $\eta = 200$ Pa·s, $L = 10D = 0.75$ m, $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^6$ Pa.

**Step 1 — Compute geometric constants:**
$\sin\phi = \sin 17.65° = 0.3032$
$\cos\phi = \cos 17.65° = 0.9529$

**Step 2 — Drag flow rate** (Equation 1):
$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{drag} &= \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2} \\
Q_{drag} &= \frac{\pi^2 (0.075)^2 (1.1667)(2.5 \times 10^{-3})(0.3032)(0.9529)}{2} \\
Q_{drag} &= \frac{(9.8696)(5.625 \times 10^{-3})(1.1667)(2.5 \times 10^{-3})(0.2890)}{2} \\
Q_{drag} &= \frac{9.8696 \times 5.625 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.1667 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2890}{2} \\
Q_{drag} &= \frac{9.8696 \times 5.625 \times 10^{-3}}{1} = 5.551 \times 10^{-2} \text{ (intermediate)} \\
Q_{drag} &= 5.551 \times 10^{-2} \times 1.1667 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.619 \times 10^{-4} \\
Q_{drag} \times 0.2890 &= 4.679 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s (intermediate)} \\
Q_{drag} &= \frac{4.679 \times 10^{-5}}{2} = 2.339 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure flow rate** (magnitude, opposes drag flow):
$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{pressure} &= \frac{\pi D h^3 \sin\phi \cdot \Delta P}{12 \eta L} \\
Q_{pressure} &= \frac{\pi (0.075)(2.5 \times 10^{-3})^3 (0.3032)(5 \times 10^6)}{12 \times 200 \times 0.75} \\
Q_{pressure} &= \frac{(0.2356)(1.5625 \times 10^{-8})(0.3032)(5 \times 10^{6})}{1800} \\
Q_{pressure} &= \frac{0.2356 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-8}}{1} = 3.681 \times 10^{-9} \\
Q_{pressure} \times 0.3032 &= 1.116 \times 10^{-9} \\
Q_{pressure} \times 5 \times 10^6 &= 5.580 \times 10^{-3} \\
Q_{pressure} &= \frac{5.580 \times 10^{-3}}{1800} = 3.10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Net throughput:**
$$Q_{net} = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure} = 2.339 \times 10^{-5} - 3.10 \times 10^{-6} = 2.029 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to practical units:**
$$Q_{net} = 2.029 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \times \frac{10^6 \text{ cm}^3}{1 \text{ m}^3} = 20.29 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} \approx 1217 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

> **Final Answer:** $Q_{drag} = 23.39$ cm³/s, $Q_{pressure} = 3.10$ cm³/s, **$Q_{net} = 20.29$ cm³/s ≈ 73.0 kg/h (assuming HDPE melt density ~1000 kg/m³)**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Repeated Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the **parison programming and wall thickness control system** in a modern extrusion blow moulding machine with a block diagram. Explain how the system compensates for parison sag. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **minimum cooling time** for a blow-moulded container of wall thickness **s = 3 mm** made of HDPE. Given: melt temperature **T_m = 210 °C**, mold temperature **T_mo = 25 °C**, ejection temperature **T_e = 90 °C**, and thermal diffusivity **α = 9.2 × 10⁻⁸ m²/s**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Block Diagram** (Refer to §4.2 of these notes).

**Operating principle:**
1. The recipe stored in PLC memory contains a **profile table** of (axial position, wall thickness setpoint) — typically 30 to 100 points.
2. As the extruder pushes melt through the die, a **servo motor** drives a **conical mandrel or die pin** that varies the annular die gap in real time.
3. The die gap at position $z$ along the parison, $h(z)$, follows the lookup table pre-compensated for **sag** (which is greatest at the bottom of the parison for vertical extrusion).
4. Sag compensation formula (empirical): $h_{compensated}(z) = h_{nominal}(z) \cdot [1 + k_s \cdot (1 - z/L_p)^2]$, where $k_s$ is a sag coefficient (typically 0.05 – 0.20) and $L_p$ is the parison length.
5. A **capacitive wall thickness sensor** (mounted on a post-cooling station) measures the actual thickness of the blow-moulded part, and the **closed-loop feedback** corrects the next shot's profile.
6. **Circumferential profile control** uses segmented die lips (typically 8–16 segments) to correct ovality and weld-line thinning.

**Why sag occurs:** A molten parison hangs under gravity; the lower portions stretch, thinning the wall. The programmer increases the die gap for the lower portions so that, after sag, the wall thickness is uniform.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Use the cooling time equation** derived in §3.2:
$$t_c = \frac{4 s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\frac{4 (T_m - T_{mo})}{\pi (T_e - T_{mo})}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Plug in values:**
- $s = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ m, so $s^2 = 9 \times 10^{-6}$ m².
- $T_m - T_{mo} = 210 - 25 = 185$ °C.
- $T_e - T_{mo} = 90 - 25 = 65$ °C.
- $\alpha = 9.2 \times 10^{-8}$ m²/s.

**Step 3 — Compute the logarithmic term:**
$$\frac{4 (T_m - T_{mo})}{\pi (T_e - T_{mo})} = \frac{4 \times 185}{\pi \times 65} = \frac{740}{204.2} = 3.624$$

$$\ln(3.624) = 1.287$$

**Step 4 — Compute the prefactor:**
$$\frac{4 s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} = \frac{4 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}}{\pi^2 \times 9.2 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-5}}{9.079 \times 10^{-7}} = 39.65 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Final cooling time:**
$$t_c = 39.65 \times 1.287 = 51.03 \text{ s}$$

> **Final Answer: $t_c \approx 51$ seconds** (with safety factor 1.2 – 1.5, the actual cycle cooling time used in production is ~60–75 s).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **In the extruder throughput derivation, do NOT confuse the channel width $W$ with screw diameter $D$.** Most students write $Q = \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}$ and **forget to subtract the pressure flow** — losing 2–3 marks. Always state $Q_{net} = Q_{drag} - Q_{pressure}$.
> 2. **In PID, do not write the P, I, D terms in series and assume they are independent.** The examiner expects the **discrete (sum) form** for a PLC and explanation of **anti-windup** (clamping the integral term between $u_{min}$ and $u_{max}$).
> 3. **In cooling time, students often forget the "4" in the numerator of the log argument** — it should be 4, not π. This single error costs 1 full mark.
> 4. **Parison programmer question:** Many students describe it as a "timer" — it is **NOT** a timer; it is a **servo-driven profile controller**. Examiners specifically deduct marks for this misnomer.
> 5. **Always show units and final numerical answer in both SI and engineering units** (e.g., cm³/s and kg/h) for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Process Controls for Blow Moulding Machine:**

- ☐ **Five-tier control hierarchy:** Field instrumentation → Local loop (PID) → Machine supervisory (PLC) → Cell/HMI → Enterprise/cloud.
- ☐ **Melt pressure** is the master variable that ties extruder output to die head back-pressure — measured at the **adapter** using a strain-gauge transducer.
- ☐ **Extruder characteristic equation:** $Q = A \cdot N - B \cdot \Delta P / \eta$ — control $N$ via VFD, control $\Delta P$ via die restrictor or accumulator position.
- ☐ **Die swell** $B = D_{par}/D_{die}$ depends on shear rate, melt temperature, residence time, and polymer viscoelasticity.
- ☐ **Parison programmer** = servo-controlled die-gap variation; **compensates for sag**; uses 30–100 axial points + circumferential segments.
- ☐ **PID discrete form (PLC-implementable):** $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \sum e[j]\Delta t + K_d (e[k]-e[k-1])/\Delta t$.
- ☐ **Anti-windup** is mandatory for integral term in temperature control (clamping the integrator).
- ☐ **Blow pressure stages:** low pressure (0.2–0.5 MPa) for pre-blow / shaping, high pressure (0.6–4.0 MPa) for detail and final wall packing.
- ☐ **Clamp tonnage:** $F_{cl} = P_b \cdot A_{proj} + \text{safety factor}$ — uses hydraulic or toggle-electric clamp.
- ☐ **Cooling time:** $t_c = \dfrac{4 s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4 (T_m - T_{mo})}{\pi (T_e - T_{mo})}\right)$ — proportional to $s^2$.
- ☐ **Cycle time** = $t_{close} + t_{blow} + t_{cool} + t_{open} + t_{eject}$; cooling dominates (>60% of total).
- ☐ **Sequence control** is implemented as **SFC / Grafcet** per IEC 61131-3 — standard for lab viva.
- ☐ **Industry 4.0 features** in modern machines: OPC-UA, MQTT, IIoT gateways, predictive maintenance via vibration/temperature analytics.
- ☐ **Standard polymer windows:** HDPE 180–230 °C, PP 200–250 °C, PET 240–280 °C, PC 270–310 °C.
- ☐ **Sensors to remember:** Type-K thermocouple (T), Pt100 RTD (T), Dynisco strain gauge (P), LVDT (position), capacitive (wall thickness), mass flow controller (air).
- ☐ **Two numbers that almost always appear in 14-mark numericals:** D = 60–90 mm extruder, h = 2–4 mm flight depth, ΔP = 3–8 MPa die back pressure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process and Product Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Process and Product Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Process and Product Controls?

In polymer processing machinery (extruders, injection moulding machines, blow moulders, etc.), two intertwined but distinct layers of control govern the manufacturing outcome:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Control** is the real-time regulation of machine *operating variables* (temperature, pressure, screw speed, flow rate, residence time) so that the polymer melt is produced and shaped under stable, repeatable conditions.
> **Product Control** is the verification and regulation of the *output part's* quality attributes (dimensions, weight, surface finish, mechanical properties) so that the finished article conforms to specification.

**Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Process control refers to the closed-loop regulation of processing parameters in polymer converting equipment using feedback sensors, controllers, and actuators to maintain set-points (SP) of barrel zones, melt temperature, injection pressure, screw RPM, and throughput. Product control refers to the post-moulding (and in-mould) monitoring of part quality through dimensional inspection, gravimetric checks, and statistical process control (SPC) techniques to ensure conformance to design tolerances and customer specifications.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Kitchen Chef" Model

Imagine a chef cooking a curry on a gas stove:

| Kitchen Element | Polymer Processing Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Gas knob (actuator) | Heater band / Hydraulic valve |
| Thermometer on the pan (sensor) | Thermocouple / Pressure transducer |
| Chef's brain (decision) | PID controller / PLC |
| Recipe book (set-point) | Mould specification / Processing window |
| Tasting the curry (feedback) | In-mould pressure / Part weight check |
| Final plated dish | Moulded part ready for shipment |

The chef *controls the process* by adjusting the flame, and *controls the product* by tasting. Both are continuous loops. If the curry is too salty, the chef changes the recipe next time — this is **Statistical Process Control (SPC)**. Polymer manufacturing applies the exact same philosophy, only at industrial scale with **response times in milliseconds**.

## 1.3 Why Both Controls are Mandatory

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standards / Metrics Used in Industry:**
> - **Cpk ≥ 1.33** (Process Capability Index — six-sigma quality benchmark)
> - **Melt Temperature Tolerance: ± 2 °C** (typical for engineering plastics)
> - **Part Weight Tolerance: ± 0.5 %** (automotive sector)
> - **Cavity Pressure Peak Tolerance: ± 5 bar** (injection moulding)
> - **Standard Reference:** ISO 9001, ISO/TS 16949 (now IATF 16949), VDI 2017

Without process control, polymer parts suffer from **short shots, warpage, flash, sink marks, voids, and molecular degradation**. Without product control, the manufacturer ships defective parts undetected.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of a Control System

### A. Open-Loop Control (Feed-Forward Only)
The controller applies an input to the process without measuring the output. Common in older extrusion lines where the screw RPM is set manually.

- **Advantage:** Simple, cheap, fast response.
- **Disadvantage:** Cannot correct for disturbances (resin batch variation, ambient temperature drift).

### B. Closed-Loop Control (Feedback)
The controller compares the *measured* process variable (PV) with the *set-point* (SP) and generates a corrective action through an *actuator*.

**Logical Steps of a Closed-Loop Action:**

1. **Sensor** measures the live value (e.g., thermocouple reads 198 °C).
2. **Signal conditioner** filters and linearises the raw mV signal.
3. **Controller** (PID / PLC) computes the error: e(t) = SP − PV.
4. **Actuator** (heater / cooler valve) is driven by the controller output u(t).
5. **Process** responds, and the cycle repeats continuously at the **sampling rate** (typically 10–100 ms in polymer machinery).

## 2.2 The PID Controller — Heart of Polymer Process Control

The **Proportional–Integral–Derivative (PID)** controller is the workhorse of polymer machinery. Its continuous-time form is:

$$u(t) \;=\; K_{p}\,e(t) \;+\; K_{i}\!\!\int_{0}^{t}\! e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_{d}\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Where the error signal is:

$$e(t) \;=\; \text{SP} \;-\; \text{PV}(t)$$

- **P-term (Proportional):** Responds to the *magnitude* of the error. Larger Kp → faster response, but if too high, causes oscillation.
- **I-term (Integral):** Eliminates *steady-state offset* by accumulating past errors.
- **D-term (Derivative):** Predicts *future* error from its rate of change; damps overshoot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In modern polymer machinery, the controller is **digital**, so the discrete-time (velocity-form) PID is used to avoid *integral wind-up*:

$$\Delta u[k] \;=\; K_{p}\bigl(e[k]-e[k-1]\bigr) \;+\; K_{i}\,e[k]\,\Delta t \;+\; \frac{K_{d}}{\Delta t}\bigl(e[k]-2e[k-1]+e[k-2]\bigr)$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 2.1 — Critical Equations for Process and Product Control**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $u(t) = K_{p}e(t) + K_{i}\!\int e\,dt + K_{d}\frac{de}{dt}$ | PID controller output | % or V |
| 2 | $e(t) = \text{SP} - \text{PV}(t)$ | Control error | Same as PV |
| 3 | $G_{cl}(s) = \frac{G_c(s)G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)G_p(s)H(s)}$ | Closed-loop transfer function | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL}-\bar{x}}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\bar{x}-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process capability index | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N \sin\phi\cos\phi}{2} - \frac{h^{3}\sin^{2}\phi}{12\eta}\frac{\Delta P}{L}$ | Extruder throughput (Newtonian) | m³/s |
| 6 | $\text{Specific Energy} = \frac{P}{Q}$ | Mechanical energy per unit mass of polymer | J/kg |
| 7 | $P_{cavity} \approx P_{inj}\cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | Cavity pressure decay during packing | bar |
| 8 | $\dot{\gamma}_{app} = \frac{6Q}{W H^{2}}$ | Apparent shear rate in rectangular channel | s⁻¹ |
| 9 | $T_{melt} = T_{set} + \Delta T_{diss}$ | Melt temperature (set + viscous dissipation) | °C |
| 10 | $\text{Yield} = \frac{\text{Good Parts}}{\text{Total Parts}} \times 100\%$ | First-pass yield | % |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Tight Cpk values on bumper fascia and dashboard panels are mandated by IATF 16949.
- **Medical Devices:** Syringes and catheters require validated process windows per **ISO 13485** — every process parameter must be *controlled, monitored, and recorded*.
- **Packaging:** Blown film lines use **gravimetric control** to hold film thickness within ± 2 µm to avoid wastage.
- **Real-time SPC dashboards:** Modern Industry 4.0 plants stream every cycle's data (peak pressure, cushion position, cooling time) to MES systems for *predictive* quality control using machine-learning models.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the PID Controller Output

We start from the requirement that the controller must drive the error e(t) to zero. A general linear controller can be written in the Laplace domain as:

$$U(s) \;=\; G_{c}(s)\cdot E(s)$$

**Step 1 — Define the proportional action.**
The simplest controller reacts to the current error:

$$U_{p}(s) \;=\; K_{p}\cdot E(s)$$

**Step 2 — Add integral action to remove steady-state offset.**
The integral of error in time domain becomes division by s in the Laplace domain:

$$U_{i}(s) \;=\; \frac{K_{i}}{s}\cdot E(s)$$

**Step 3 — Add derivative action to anticipate future error.**
Differentiation in time domain becomes multiplication by s in the Laplace domain:

$$U_{d}(s) \;=\; K_{d}\,s\cdot E(s)$$

**Step 4 — Superpose all three actions (linearity of Laplace transform).**

$$U(s) \;=\; \left(K_{p} + \frac{K_{i}}{s} + K_{d}\,s\right)E(s)$$

Hence the **PID transfer function** is:

$$G_{c}(s) \;=\; \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} \;=\; K_{p} + \frac{K_{i}}{s} + K_{d}\,s$$

**Step 5 — Convert back to time domain (inverse Laplace).**

$$u(t) \;=\; K_{p}\,e(t) + K_{i}\!\!\int_{0}^{t}\! e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_{d}\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

This is the canonical PID law used in every polymer machine controller. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Closed-Loop Transfer Function — Full Derivation

Consider a standard unity-feedback block diagram with controller $G_c(s)$, process $G_p(s)$, and sensor $H(s) = 1$.

**Step 1 — Forward-path transfer function:**

$$G_{fwd}(s) \;=\; G_{c}(s)\cdot G_{p}(s)$$

**Step 2 — Feedback signal equals the output (unity feedback):**

$$Y_{fb}(s) \;=\; H(s)\cdot Y(s) \;=\; Y(s)$$

**Step 3 — Error signal:**

$$E(s) \;=\; R(s) - Y_{fb}(s) \;=\; R(s) - Y(s)$$

**Step 4 — Output expressed using the forward path:**

$$Y(s) \;=\; G_{fwd}(s)\cdot E(s) \;=\; G_{c}(s)G_{p}(s)\bigl[R(s) - Y(s)\bigr]$$

**Step 5 — Solve algebraically for $Y(s)/R(s)$:**

$$Y(s) \;=\; G_{c}(s)G_{p}(s)\,R(s) - G_{c}(s)G_{p}(s)\,Y(s)$$

$$Y(s)\bigl[1 + G_{c}(s)G_{p}(s)\bigr] \;=\; G_{c}(s)G_{p}(s)\,R(s)$$

$$\boxed{\;G_{cl}(s) \;=\; \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{G_{c}(s)\,G_{p}(s)}{1 + G_{c}(s)\,G_{p}(s)}\;}$$

This is the **closed-loop transfer function** used to analyse stability (via Routh–Hurwitz or Bode plots) of barrel-temperature or injection-velocity loops. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation of Extruder Throughput (Newtonian Fluid)

For a single-screw extruder with metering section of depth h, screw diameter D, helix angle φ, screw speed N, and pressure gradient $\Delta P/L$:

**Step 1 — Drag-flow component (no pressure):**
Fluid is dragged forward by the relative motion of the barrel over the screw channel:

$$Q_{d} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,v_{bz}\,W\,h \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}D^{2}N\sin\phi\cos\phi}{2}\,h$$

When the channel width $W = \pi D \sin\phi - e$ and the flight width $e$ is small, this simplifies to:

$$Q_{d} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}D^{2}N\sin\phi\cos\phi}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Pressure-flow component (opposing flow):**

$$Q_{p} \;=\; -\frac{Wh^{3}\sin^{2}\phi}{12\eta}\frac{\Delta P}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Net throughput (drag minus pressure):**

$$\boxed{\;Q \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}D^{2}N\sin\phi\cos\phi}{2} \;-\; \frac{h^{3}\sin^{2}\phi}{12\eta}\frac{\Delta P}{L}\;}$$

This equation is fundamental when *controlling* extruder output by manipulating N or the die restriction (and hence $\Delta P$). $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Process Capability (Cpk) — Worked Example

**Problem:** A moulded part has specification LSL = 19.8 mm and USL = 20.2 mm. After 50 samples, $\bar{x} = 20.05$ mm and $\sigma = 0.04$ mm. Calculate Cpk.

**Step 1 — Calculate upper capability:**

$$C_{pu} \;=\; \frac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3\sigma} \;=\; \frac{20.2 - 20.05}{3(0.04)} \;=\; \frac{0.15}{0.12} \;=\; 1.25$$

**Step 2 — Calculate lower capability:**

$$C_{pl} \;=\; \frac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \;=\; \frac{20.05 - 19.8}{3(0.04)} \;=\; \frac{0.25}{0.12} \;=\; 2.083$$

**Step 3 — Cpk is the minimum:**

$$C_{pk} \;=\; \min(1.25,\; 2.083) \;=\; 1.25$$

**Interpretation:** $C_{pk} = 1.25$ is *borderline* (target ≥ 1.33). The process mean is shifted toward USL — the operator should *re-centre* the process by adjusting holding pressure. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Closed-Loop Process Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spNode[Set Point SP] --> sumNode[Summing Junction]
    sumNode --> errNode[Error e of t]
    errNode --> ctrlNode[PID Controller]
    ctrlNode --> actNode[Actuator Heater or Valve]
    actNode --> procNode[Polymer Process Melt Zone]
    procNode --> sensNode[Sensor Thermocouple or Transducer]
    sensNode --> fbNode[Feedback Signal]
    fbNode --> sumNode
    procNode --> outNode[Process Output]
```

*Figure 4.1 — Universal feedback loop applied to barrel-zone temperature, melt pressure, or screw speed control.*

## 4.2 Injection Moulding Machine — Multi-Loop Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    hmiNode[HMI Touchscreen] --> plcNode[PLC Master Controller]
    plcNode --> loop1[Temperature Loop]
    plcNode --> loop2[Pressure Loop]
    plcNode --> loop3[Position Loop]
    plcNode --> loop4[Speed Loop]
    loop1 --> zone1[Barrel Zone 1]
    loop1 --> zone2[Barrel Zone 2]
    loop1 --> zone3[Barrel Zone 3]
    loop1 --> zone4[Nozzle Zone]
    loop2 --> injPress[Injection Pressure Sensor]
    loop2 --> cavPress[Cavity Pressure Sensor]
    loop3 --> clampPos[Mould Clamp Position]
    loop4 --> screwRPM[Screw Rotation Speed]
    injPress --> plcNode
    cavPress --> plcNode
    clampPos --> plcNode
    screwRPM --> plcNode
    plcNode --> dbNode[Production Database MES]
```

*Figure 4.2 — Multi-loop control hierarchy in a modern injection moulding machine. Each loop operates independently at its own sampling rate.*

## 4.3 Product Quality Control — From Mould to Shipment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Moulded Part] --> visCheck[Visual Inspection Camera]
    visCheck --> wtCheck[Gravimetric Weight Check]
    wtCheck --> dimCheck[CMM Dimensional Check]
    dimCheck --> mechCheck[Mechanical Property Test]
    mechCheck --> decNode{Within Specification}
    decNode -- Yes --> okNode[Pack and Ship]
    decNode -- No --> rejNode[Reject Bin or Rework]
    wtCheck --> spcNode[SPC Chart Xbar R]
    spcNode --> decNode
    dimCheck --> spcNode
```

*Figure 4.3 — In-line and off-line product control workflow with Statistical Process Control (SPC) feedback to the process.*

## 4.4 PID Tuning Sequence for a Barrel Heater

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    initNode[Start with Ki equals 0 and Kd equals 0] --> incKp[Increase Kp until oscillation appears]
    incKp --> setKp[Set Kp to 50 percent of oscillation value]
    setKp --> incKi[Increase Ki slowly to remove offset]
    incKi --> addKd[Add small Kd to damp overshoot]
    addKd --> valNode[Validate with set-point step change]
    valNode --> docNode[Document Kp Ki Kd in machine recipe]
```

*Figure 4.4 — Ziegler–Nichols-style tuning workflow routinely used during polymer machine commissioning.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between process control and product control in polymer processing. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
Process control refers to the regulation of machine operating parameters (temperature, pressure, screw speed) during polymer processing to maintain stable melt conditions. *Example:* Maintaining barrel zone 3 temperature at 220 °C ± 2 °C through PID-controlled heater bands.
Product control refers to the verification of the finished moulded part's quality attributes (weight, dimensions, mechanical strength) against design specifications. *Example:* Checking the weight of an injection-moulded gear to be within 0.5 % of nominal using an automatic check-weigher.
Process control governs the *means*; product control governs the *end*. Both are essential for zero-defect manufacturing.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is a PID controller? State the function of the integral and derivative terms.**

**Model Answer:**
A PID (Proportional–Integral–Derivative) controller is a closed-loop feedback controller widely used in polymer machinery to maintain process variables (temperature, pressure, flow) at their set-points.
- **Integral (I) term:** Accumulates past errors over time and eliminates **steady-state offset** so the process variable exactly equals the set-point in the long run.
- **Derivative (D) term:** Responds to the rate of change of error and provides a **damping action**, reducing overshoot and improving transient stability — particularly valuable in fast injection-velocity loops.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often confuse the *time-domain* PID equation with its *Laplace-domain* transfer function. Write both clearly to earn full marks.
> - In Cpk problems, forgetting to take the **minimum** of $C_{pu}$ and $C_{pl}$ is the most common error leading to loss of 2 marks.
> - In diagram questions, examiners deduct marks for missing the *feedback path* from sensor back to summing junction.

---

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] — Choice 1

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the closed-loop control of barrel-zone temperature in a single-screw extruder. Identify the sensor, controller, and actuator used.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A polyethylene melt is processed at 200 °C set-point. The actual melt temperature is found to fluctuate between 195 °C and 205 °C with a mean of 201 °C and standard deviation of 1.8 °C. The specification is 200 °C ± 4 °C. Calculate the process capability index Cpk and comment on the process.

**Model Solution (a):**
The closed-loop temperature control of an extruder barrel zone is implemented as follows:

1. **Set-Point (SP):** The desired zone temperature (e.g., 200 °C) is entered via the HMI.
2. **Sensor:** A **J-type or K-type thermocouple** inserted into the barrel wall (or into the melt via a thermowell) generates a millivolt signal proportional to temperature.
3. **Signal Conditioner:** Amplifies and linearises the mV signal into a standard 4–20 mA or 0–10 V signal.
4. **Controller (PID):** A PLC or dedicated temperature controller computes the error $e(t) = SP - PV$ and generates a control output $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$.
5. **Actuator:** A **thyristor-controlled heater band** (or a solid-state relay) modulates electrical power to the heater. A cooling fan is switched on for the cooling half of the cycle.
6. **Feedback:** The thermocouple continuously measures the new temperature and closes the loop.

Block diagram (refer to *Figure 4.1* in Section 4): Set-Point → Comparator → PID → SSR/Heater → Barrel Zone → Thermocouple → Comparator.

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $\text{LSL} = 196\,°C$, $\text{USL} = 204\,°C$, $\bar{x} = 201\,°C$, $\sigma = 1.8\,°C$.

$$C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3\sigma} = \frac{204 - 201}{3(1.8)} = \frac{3}{5.4} = 0.556$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{201 - 196}{3(1.8)} = \frac{5}{5.4} = 0.926$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.556,\; 0.926) = 0.556$$

**Comment:** Since $C_{pk} < 1$, the process is **incapable** and will produce out-of-specification parts. The mean is shifted toward the USL — the controller needs to be tuned to lower the average temperature, and the variation ($\sigma$) must be reduced by stabilising heater-band performance.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — Choice 2

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the working of a closed-loop control system for injection moulding cavity pressure. Why is cavity pressure considered the most reliable indicator of part quality?

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a single-screw extruder having screw diameter $D = 60$ mm, screw speed $N = 60$ rpm, channel depth $h = 3$ mm, helix angle $\phi = 17.65°$, viscosity $\eta = 500$ Pa·s, and pressure gradient $\Delta P/L = 5 \times 10^{6}$ Pa/m, calculate the extruder throughput.

**Model Solution (a):**
In modern injection moulding, a **piezoelectric pressure sensor** is mounted behind a pin protruding into the mould cavity. The closed-loop works as follows:

1. The **set-point** is a pressure–time profile (e.g., switch from velocity-control to pressure-control at 600 bar cavity pressure).
2. As the polymer melt fills the cavity, the **sensor** measures the instantaneous pressure $P_{cav}(t)$.
3. The **controller** compares $P_{cav}$ to the set-point profile and modulates the **hydraulic valve** (actuator) controlling injection ram velocity.
4. The **ram/screw** responds, changing the fill rate, and the loop closes.

Cavity pressure correlates directly with part density, shrinkage, and dimensional accuracy because it reflects the packing and cooling history *inside* the mould — far more reliable than external hydraulic pressure, which is affected by line losses and machine wear.

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $D = 0.06$ m, $N = 60/60 = 1$ rev/s, $h = 0.003$ m, $\phi = 17.65°$, $\eta = 500$ Pa·s, $\Delta P/L = 5 \times 10^{6}$ Pa/m.

**Step 1 — Drag-flow component:**

$$Q_{d} = \frac{\pi^{2}D^{2}N\sin\phi\cos\phi}{2}$$

$$\sin(17.65°) \approx 0.3032, \quad \cos(17.65°) \approx 0.9529$$

$$Q_{d} = \frac{\pi^{2}(0.06)^{2}(1)(0.3032)(0.9529)}{2} = \frac{9.8696 \times 0.0036 \times 0.2890}{2}$$

$$Q_{d} = \frac{0.01027}{2} = 5.13 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \times (\text{channel-width factor})$$

**Step 2 — Pressure-flow component:**

$$Q_{p} = \frac{h^{3}\sin^{2}\phi}{12\eta}\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{(0.003)^{3}(0.3032)^{2}}{12(500)}(5 \times 10^{6})$$

$$Q_{p} = \frac{2.7 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.0919}{6000} \times 5 \times 10^{6} = \frac{2.481 \times 10^{-9}}{6000} \times 5 \times 10^{6}$$

$$Q_{p} = 2.07 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Net throughput:**

$$Q = Q_{d} - Q_{p} \approx 5.13 \times 10^{-3} - 2.07 \times 10^{-6} \approx 5.11 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

Converting: $Q \approx 5.11 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 \approx 18.4\,\text{m}^{3}/\text{h}$, which is physically reasonable for a 60 mm extruder.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**
> - **Process vs Product Control:** Process = machine parameters; Product = part attributes. Both essential.
> - **Closed-Loop Components:** Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Controller (PID) → Actuator → Process → Feedback.
> - **PID Equation (time):** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$.
> - **PID Transfer Function (Laplace):** $G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$.
> - **Closed-Loop TF:** $G_{cl} = \frac{G_c G_p}{1 + G_c G_p H}$.
> - **Ziegler–Nichols Tuning:** Start with P-only, increase until sustained oscillation, then back off.
> - **Common Sensors in Polymer Machines:** Thermocouple (J/K), Pressure transducer, LVDT, Load cell, IR pyrometer.
> - **Common Actuators:** Heater band, SSR, Cooling fan, Hydraulic/servo valve, VFD for screw drive.
> - **Cpk Threshold:** ≥ 1.33 = capable, < 1 = incapable. Always take the minimum of $C_{pu}$ and $C_{pl}$.
> - **Extruder Throughput (Newtonian):** $Q = \frac{\pi^{2}D^{2}N\sin\phi\cos\phi}{2} - \frac{h^{3}\sin^{2}\phi}{12\eta}\frac{\Delta P}{L}$.
> - **Cavity Pressure** is the most reliable in-mould quality indicator — superior to hydraulic pressure.
> - **SPC Charts:** X̄-R chart for variable data; p-chart for attribute data; Cp/Cpk for capability.
> - **Industry 4.0 Trend:** MES-integrated, real-time SPC with predictive models replacing classical post-hoc inspection.
> - **Safety Reminder:** Always include **anti-windup** logic in digital PID for heater zones to prevent overshoot after cold starts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trouble Shooting in Blow Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Trouble Shooting in Blow Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Blow Moulding Troubleshooting** is defined as the systematic identification, diagnosis, and rectification of process, equipment, and material-related defects that occur during the three primary stages of blow moulding — *extrusion, parison formation, and inflation/moulding* — to produce dimensionally accurate, defect-free hollow plastic articles (bottles, containers, tanks, automotive ducts).

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the term encompasses:

> **Trouble Shooting** = Defect identification (WHAT) + Root-cause analysis (WHY) + Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA — HOW).

The three principal process variants in scope are:

| Variant | Process Reference |
|---|---|
| **EBM** | Extrusion Blow Moulding |
| **IBM** | Injection Blow Moulding |
| **SBM** | Stretch Blow Moulding (PET) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine blowing a soap bubble from a soap film wand. If you dip the wand too shallow, the film is too thin and the bubble bursts (**blow-out / hole**). If you hold it too long without blowing, gravity pulls the film down and you get a lopsided blob (**parison sag**). If you blow too gently, the bubble stays small (**short shot / under-inflation**). If you blow too hard, the bubble pops (**splits**). If the wand is dirty, the bubble gets streaks (**die-lip build-up / contamination**). If the air escapes from the seam, you get a leaky edge (**weld-line / pinch-off failure**).

Blow moulding of plastics works on the exact same physics — only the soap film is a *hot molten thermoplastic parison*, the wand is a *die head*, and your lungs are the *blow pin / needle* delivering compressed air at **0.5–1.0 MPa** (typical).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** Nearly **70% of EBM bottle defects** are traceable to *parison programming, die geometry, or mould cooling* — NOT to the resin itself. Always check process parameters BEFORE blaming the material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metric:** Industry-standard acceptable defect rate (AQL) for blow-moulded packaging is **≤ 0.65%** (Acceptable Quality Limit per MIL-STD-105E). Automotive fuel tanks demand **≤ 0.10%**.

## 1.3 Classification of Blow Moulding Defects (Master Map)

Defects are categorised by their **stage of origin**:

1. **Extrusion / Die Stage** — Parison irregularities (sag, swelling, melt fracture, die-lip build-up).
2. **Inflation / Mould-Closure Stage** — Short shots, blow-outs, weld/flow lines, pinchoff issues.
3. **Cooling / Ejection Stage** — Warpage, sink marks, sticking, post-blow shrinkage.
4. **Surface / Optical Stage** — Streaks, haze, orange peel, burn marks, contamination.
5. **Dimensional / Structural** — Wall-thickness variation, ovality, flash, holes, thinning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics-of-Failure Approach to Troubleshooting

Effective KTU-grade troubleshooting follows the **"4-M + E"** model — a standard industry framework:

- **M₁ — Material:** Resin grade, MFI, moisture content, regrind %, contamination.
- **M₂ — Machine:** Die geometry, screw/barrel condition, clamp tonnage, air pressure, mould temperature uniformity.
- **M₃ — Method:** Parison programming, cycle time, blow-up ratio (BUR), cooling time, pinch-off design.
- **M₄ — Man/Operator:** Settings, purging procedure, changeover discipline.
- **E — Environment:** Ambient humidity, ambient temperature, plant vibration.

## 2.2 The 15 Most Common Defects — Comprehensive Diagnostic Matrix

> The following is the **KTU Board Examiner's Go-To Table**. Memorise this; it appears in 90% of troubleshooting questions.

### DEFECT 1 — Parison Sag (Extrudate Drop)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Molten parison stretches/drops under gravity between die exit and mould close. |
| **Visible Symptom** | Thin bottom, thick top, asymmetric bottle. |
| **Root Causes** | Excessive melt temperature, high MFI resin, long parison length, slow mould-closure time. |
| **Remedy** | Lower melt temp by 5–10 °C; use higher-viscosity grade; reduce cycle time; use **parison programming** (thicker wall where material will sag most). |

### DEFECT 2 — Die Swell (Extrudate Swell / Barus Effect)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Parison diameter larger than die exit diameter after exiting. |
| **Cause** | Viscoelastic recovery of polymer chains upon release of pressure. |
| **Magnitude Formula** | $B = \dfrac{D_{\text{parison}}}{D_{\text{die}}}$ (typically 1.10–1.40) |
| **Remedy** | Increase land length of die, reduce shear rate, use lower-MFR resin, cool die lips. |

### DEFECT 3 — Short Shot / Incomplete Inflation

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Parison does not fully inflate; corners/sharp features remain unfilled. |
| **Causes** | Low air pressure, blocked air inlet, low melt strength, mould too cold, excessive blow-up ratio. |
| **Remedy** | Increase air pressure to **0.6–0.8 MPa**; check blow pin; raise mould temp to 20–40 °C; pre-blow delay. |

### DEFECT 4 — Blow-Out / Holes / Pinholes

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Wall rupture during inflation; small holes appear in finished article. |
| **Causes** | Excessive air pressure, low melt strength, contamination, worn die lips, pinch-off failure. |
| **Remedy** | Reduce air pressure; replace resin if contaminated; inspect pinch-off geometry (typical land 1.5–3 mm). |

### DEFECT 5 — Weld Line (Knit Line)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Visible line where two parison halves meet at pinch-off. |
| **Cause** | Insufficient fusion at pinch-off land; cold mould; low melt temperature. |
| **Remedy** | Increase pinch-off land length, raise melt temp, increase mould-closure force, optimise pinch-off knife angle (typical 15°–30°). |

### DEFECT 6 — Flash / Burrs (Excess Material at Parting Line)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Thin plastic sheet at mould parting line. |
| **Causes** | Excessive clamp tonnage, worn mould parting surface, low melt viscosity, over-inflation. |
| **Remedy** | Reduce clamp pressure; regrind/replace mould surfaces; tighten mould halves. |

### DEFECT 7 — Warpage (Post-Demould Distortion)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Article distorts after ejection from mould. |
| **Causes** | Non-uniform cooling, insufficient cooling time, residual stresses, ejection too hot. |
| **Remedy** | Balance cooling channels; extend cooling time; use chilled water at **10–15 °C**; orient cooling lines per part geometry. |

### DEFECT 8 — Surface Streaks / Drag Lines

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Longitudinal marks on parison surface. |
| **Causes** | Melt fracture, dirty die, degraded resin, screw/barrel scoring. |
| **Remedy** | Lower screw RPM, increase die land length, clean die, replace resin, polish barrel. |

### DEFECT 9 — Burn Marks / Discolouration

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Yellow/brown/black marks on inner or outer surface. |
| **Causes** | Excessive melt temp, residence time too long, screw back-flow, mould-vent blockage. |
| **Remedy** | Lower barrel temps by 5–15 °C, purge barrel, clean vents, reduce cycle. |

### DEFECT 10 — Haze / Loss of Transparency (PET Bottles)

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Bottle appears cloudy instead of crystal clear. |
| **Causes** | Inadequate preform temperature, low stretch ratio, fast cooling, contamination. |
| **Remedy** | Maintain preform temp at **100–115 °C** for PET; optimise stretch ratio 2.4–2.6 axial, 4.0–4.5 hoop. |

### DEFECT 11 — Sink Marks

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Localised depressions on surface. |
| **Causes** | Thick sections, insufficient cooling, low mould pressure. |
| **Remedy** | Increase cooling; reduce section thickness; use cooling cores. |

### DEFECT 12 — Part Sticking in Mould

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Article does not release from mould. |
| **Causes** | Insufficient draft angle, over-cooling, surface roughness, vacuum. |
| **Remedy** | Apply mould release (silicone, semi-permanent); check draft ≥ 1°; warm mould slightly. |

### DEFECT 13 — Ovality / Cross-Section Distortion

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Bottle is not perfectly round in cross-section. |
| **Causes** | Uneven parison wall, asymmetric cooling, mould misalignment. |
| **Remedy** | Re-calibrate parison programmer; check mould alignment; balance cooling. |

### DEFECT 14 — Fish Eyes / Gel Particles

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Small unfused particles visible on surface. |
| **Causes** | Contamination, cross-linked resin, high MFI variation. |
| **Remedy** | Purge machine thoroughly; check incoming resin; use finer screen pack (60–100 mesh). |

### DEFECT 15 — Post-Blow Shrinkage / Dimensional Drift

| Aspect | Detail |
|---|---|
| **What is it?** | Part shrinks more than predicted after demould. |
| **Causes** | Insufficient cooling, mould temp too high, semi-crystalline resin cooling cycle too short. |
| **Remedy** | Increase cooling time; use chilled water; for HDPE allow **1.5–3%** design shrinkage compensation. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. Every KTU exam question on this module uses at least one of these equations.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_{\text{mould}}}{D_{\text{parison}}}$ | Blow-Up Ratio (B) | 1.5 : 1 to 4 : 1 |
| 2 | $\text{Swell Ratio } B = \dfrac{D_p}{D_d}$ | Die Swell | 1.10 – 1.40 |
| 3 | $t_{\text{wall}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{parison}}}{2 \pi R_{\text{mould}}}$ | Wall thickness (cylindrical bottle) | 0.3 – 6 mm |
| 4 | $P_{\text{blow}} \cdot V_{\text{air}} = n R T$ | Compressed-air equation (ideal gas) | 0.4–1.0 MPa |
| 5 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2 t}$ | Hoop stress in inflated parison | MPa |
| 6 | $t_{\text{cool}} \propto \dfrac{s^2}{\alpha}$ | Cooling time ~ (thickness)²/thermal diffusivity | 4–20 s |
| 7 | $L_p = \dfrac{L_{\text{part}}}{\text{BUR}}$ | Parison length needed | mm |
| 8 | $\% \text{Sag} = \dfrac{\Delta h}{h_0} \times 100$ | Parison sag percentage | 0–25% |
| 9 | $S_{\text{design}} = 0.015 \text{ to } 0.030$ | Shrinkage factor (HDPE) | 1.5–3.0% |
| 10 | $\text{Output} = \dfrac{\pi D_s^2}{4} \cdot N \cdot \rho \cdot 60$ | Extruder throughput (kg/h) | kg/h |

> **Note:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` to avoid markdown table breaks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Troubleshooting competence is the single highest-leverage skill in any polymer processing plant. A senior process engineer in a **PET bottle plant** typically saves the company **₹15–40 lakh per month** by reducing rejection rate from 4% to 1%. In **automotive fuel-tank EBM** lines, an undetected weld-line defect can lead to field failures costing **millions in recalls**. KTU graduates entering the polymer industry are expected to apply the **4-M + E** diagnostic tree on Day 1 of their job.
