# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wall-Thickness Prediction for a Cylindrical Bottle

This is the **single most repeated numerical in KTU Module 3 exams.** The model is:

> Given a parison of mass $M$ and density $\rho$, inflated into a cylindrical bottle of radius $R$ and height $H$, find the average wall thickness $t$.

### Step 1 — Parison Volume

The parison (hollow tube) has outer diameter $D_o$ and inner diameter $D_i$, and length $L_p$. Its volume is:

$$V_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( D_o^2 - D_i^2 \right) L_p$$

> *Text: The parison is an annular cylinder. Volume = cross-sectional area × length. The cross-section is $\frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)$ because the parison is hollow.*

### Step 2 — Mass Conservation

The mass of the parison equals the mass of the bottle (no material lost, ignoring flash). Therefore:

$$M = \rho \cdot V_p = \rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( D_o^2 - D_i^2 \right) L_p$$

> *Text: Density × Volume = Mass. We use this as the anchor equation.*

### Step 3 — Bottle Volume (Wall Only)

After inflation, the bottle wall forms a thin cylindrical shell of radius $R$, height $H$, and thickness $t$. Its volume is:

$$V_b = 2 \pi R H t$$

> *Text: Imagine unrolling the cylinder — it becomes a flat sheet of length $2\pi R$, width $H$, and thickness $t$. Volume = length × width × thickness.*

### Step 4 — Equate Masses (Bottle = Parison)

$$\rho \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} \left( D_o^2 - D_i^2 \right) L_p = \rho \cdot 2 \pi R H t$$

### Step 5 — Simplify

Cancel $\rho$ and divide both sides by $\pi$:

$$\dfrac{1}{4} \left( D_o^2 - D_i^2 \right) L_p = 2 R H t$$

### Step 6 — Solve for Wall Thickness

$$\boxed{\,t = \dfrac{L_p \left( D_o^2 - D_i^2 \right)}{8 R H}\,}$$

> *Text: This is the final closed-form expression. It is the workhorse equation used to program die gap and parison length to achieve a target wall thickness in production.*

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Air Pressure for Full Inflation

### Given
- Bottle inner radius $R = 40 \text{ mm}$
- Wall thickness $t = 1.2 \text{ mm}$
- Material: HDPE at room temperature
- Allowable hoop stress $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 10 \text{ MPa}$

### Step 1 — Hoop-Stress Equation (Thin-Walled Pressure Vessel)

$$\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{t}$$

> *Text: Standard mechanical-engineering derivation. Force balance on half-cylinder: internal pressure pushes the wall outward; wall resists via hoop tension.*

### Step 2 — Solve for Pressure

$$P = \dfrac{\sigma_h \cdot t}{R}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Values

$$P = \dfrac{10 \text{ MPa} \times 1.2 \text{ mm}}{40 \text{ mm}}$$

> *Text: Note that mm cancels dimensionally since both t and R are in mm; MPa = N/mm², so the units work out.*

$$P = \dfrac{10 \times 1.2}{40} \text{ MPa} = \dfrac{12}{40} \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\,P = 0.30 \text{ MPa} = 3.0 \text{ bar}\,}$$

### Step 4 — Practical Fudge Factor

Industry adds a **1.5× to 2× safety factor** to overcome friction, surface tension, and cooling-induced stiffness:

$$P_{\text{set}} \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.6 \text{ MPa}$$

> *Text: This is the typical operating point. A reading of 0.3 MPa is the *theoretical minimum*; 0.5–0.6 MPa is what the operator sets.*

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Defect → Cause → Action (Quick-Reference for Exam)

| Defect | Most Likely Cause | First Action | Second Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Short shot | Low air pressure | Increase blow pressure | Check blow pin for blockage |
| Blow hole | Excessive air pressure | Reduce pressure | Inspect pinch-off land |
| Sag | High melt temp | Lower barrel temp | Use higher-viscosity resin |
| Weld line | Cold mould | Raise mould temp | Increase clamp force |
| Warpage | Uneven cooling | Balance cooling channels | Extend cooling time |
| Haze (PET) | Low preform temp | Raise preform temp | Slow stretch rod |
| Flash | High clamp force | Reduce tonnage | Check mould parting wear |
| Burn marks | Long residence | Purge barrel | Lower rear-zone temp |
| Streaks | Melt fracture | Lower screw RPM | Increase die land length |
| Sink marks | Thick wall | Add cooling core | Reduce cycle time |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Sag Compensation in Parison Programming

A 1 L HDPE bottle has a target average wall thickness of **0.9 mm**. The parison length is **280 mm**. Without programming, the operator measures the bottom wall = **0.7 mm** and top wall = **1.1 mm**.

### Question
What parison wall-thickness distribution should the programmer command to achieve uniform **0.9 mm** throughout?

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the sag pattern.** Bottom thin (0.7) means material has *flowed downward* during the **0.8-second parison hang time**. Bottom receives less material; top receives more.

**Step 2 — Programme adjustment.** Programme a *thicker wall at the TOP* of the parison (the part that will become the bottle bottom after inflation) and *thinner wall at the BOTTOM* of the parison (the part that becomes the bottle neck/top).

**Step 3 — Numerical correction.**
Target = 0.9 mm everywhere.
- Top of parison → bottle bottom: needs **0.9 + 0.2 = 1.1 mm** command.
- Middle: **0.9 mm** command.
- Bottom of parison → bottle neck: needs **0.9 − 0.2 = 0.7 mm** command.

**Step 4 — Die-gap profile.** Modern programmable dies use **20–50 zones** along the die mandrel. Set zone commands accordingly:

$$\text{Top zone} = 1.1 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{Mid} = 0.9 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{Bottom} = 0.7 \text{ mm}$$

> *Text: This 20% over/under-programming compensates the empirical sag. Refinement is iterative based on cut-and-weigh tests on the first 50 samples.*

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    C -- Dimensional --> D[Wall thickness too low]
    C -- Surface --> E[Optical/Aesthetic issue]
    C -- Structural --> F[Crack/Hole/Blowout]
    D --> G{Check parison programming}
    G -- OK --> H[Raise melt strength]
    G -- NOT OK --> I[Re-program 20+ zones]
    E --> J{Material related?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Dry resin / check MFI]
    J -- No --> L[Adjust mould temp / cooling]
    F --> M{Reduce air pressure?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Inspect pinch-off land]
    M -- No --> O[Lower melt temp / increase clamp]
    H --> P[Quality Pass]
    I --> P
    K --> P
    L --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```


# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**"List any six common defects in extrusion blow moulding with one-line causes."**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **Short shot** → Insufficient air pressure or blocked blow pin.
2. **Blow hole / blowout** → Excessive air pressure or worn pinch-off.
3. **Weld line** → Cold mould or insufficient pinch-off land.
4. **Parison sag** → High melt temperature or low-viscosity resin.
5. **Flash** → Excessive clamp tonnage or worn mould parting line.
6. **Warpage** → Non-uniform cooling or insufficient cooling time.
7. *(Bonus)* **Haze (PET)** → Low preform temperature or fast cooling.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "bad material" as the cause for every defect. This is a *lazy answer* and attracts **only 1 of 3 marks**. Always identify the **specific** process parameter.

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**"Define Blow-Up Ratio (BUR). What is its typical range for HDPE bottles? What happens if BUR exceeds 4:1?"**

**Model Answer:**

- **Definition:** $\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_{\text{mould}}}{D_{\text{parison}}}$
- **Typical range (HDPE):** 1.5 : 1 to 3 : 1 (4 : 1 is the upper limit).
- **If BUR > 4:1:**
  1. Wall becomes very thin → risk of **blow-out**.
  2. **Material orientation** is excessive → part becomes brittle.
  3. **Surface roughness** ("orange peel") develops.
  4. Neck-finish becomes difficult to trim cleanly.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice (A or B). Two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option A**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the phenomenon of **parison sag** in extrusion blow moulding. Discuss the **material, process, and equipment parameters** that affect sag, and describe the **parison programming technique** used to compensate for it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 500 mL HDPE bottle has mould diameter 60 mm, parison outer diameter 18 mm, parison inner diameter 14 mm, and parison length 200 mm. Calculate the **average wall thickness** of the bottle. If the parison **sags 15%** during the hang time, recalculate the **bottom-wall thickness** assuming all sagged material accumulates at the top.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option B**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **15 most common defects in extrusion blow moulding** in a tabular format covering defect, cause, and remedy. Highlight the **two defects** that are most influenced by the **cooling system**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 1 L PET bottle is stretch-blow-moulded. Preform diameter = 26 mm; final bottle diameter = 70 mm; axial stretch ratio = 2.5. Calculate the **hoop stretch ratio**, the **overall stretch ratio**, and the **resulting wall thickness** if the preform wall is 3.5 mm. State whether the resulting wall thickness satisfies the **20–25 µm barrier requirement** for carbonated soft drinks.

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions to PART B

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Parison Sag: Explanation (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Parison sag is the *downward gravitational stretching* of the molten parison between the die exit and the moment of mould closure, causing **non-uniform wall distribution** in the finished article. Mathematically:

$$\% \text{Sag} = \dfrac{h_0 - h(t)}{h_0} \times 100$$

**Affected Parameters (3 marks):**

| Category | Parameter | Effect on Sag |
|---|---|---|
| Material | MFI / MFR | Higher MFI → lower melt strength → MORE sag |
| Material | Density | Lower density (LDPE) sags more than HDPE |
| Process | Melt temperature | Higher temp → lower viscosity → MORE sag |
| Process | Hang time (drop time) | Longer → MORE sag |
| Process | Parison length | Longer parison → MORE sag |
| Equipment | Die land length | Longer land → better orientation → LESS sag |
| Equipment | Accumulator head | Reduces cycle → LESS sag |

**Parison Programming (3 marks):**
Modern EBM machines use **programmable dies** with **20–50 control zones** along the die mandrel. The die gap is varied during extrusion to deliver a *pre-shaped* parison:

- **Thicker material at the parison top** (the part that will become the *bottle bottom*).
- **Thinner material at the parison bottom** (the part that will become the *bottle shoulder/neck*).
- This compensates the gravitational sag, giving **uniform bottle wall distribution**.

Adjustment range is typically **±30%** around nominal wall thickness. Modern systems use **servo-hydraulic or piezo-electric** actuators for **response times of 5–20 ms per zone**.

---

#### Part (b) — Wall Thickness Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of parison (1 mark):**

$$A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(18^2 - 14^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(324 - 196)$$

$$A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 128 = 32 \pi \text{ mm}^2 \approx 100.53 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Parison volume (1 mark):**

$$V_p = A_p \times L_p = 100.53 \times 200 = 20{,}106 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Bottle volume (wall only) (1 mark):**

$$V_b = 2 \pi R H t$$

For a 500 mL bottle, approximate cylinder dimensions are $D = 60$ mm → $R = 30$ mm; $H \approx 350$ mm (rough). But the question asks **average wall thickness** directly. Use the **mass conservation** shortcut:

$$A_p \cdot L_p = 2 \pi R H \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to find $t$ (1 mark):**

$$t = \dfrac{A_p \cdot L_p}{2 \pi R H}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute (1 mark):**

$$t = \dfrac{100.53 \times 200}{2 \pi \times 30 \times 350} = \dfrac{20{,}106}{65{,}973}$$

$$t \approx 0.305 \text{ mm} \approx 0.30 \text{ mm}$$

> *Note to examiner:* If the student uses the original derivation $t = \dfrac{L_p(D_o^2 - D_i^2)}{8RH}$, the same answer is obtained: $t = \dfrac{200 \times 128}{8 \times 30 \times 350} = \dfrac{25{,}600}{84{,}000} \approx 0.305$ mm. ✓

**Step 6 — Sag Compensation (2 marks):**

If 15% of material sags from top to bottom, the top of the parison (which becomes the bottle bottom) loses material.

Volume of sagged material: $V_{\text{sag}} = 0.15 \times V_p = 0.15 \times 20{,}106 = 3{,}016 \text{ mm}^3$

This volume accumulates at the bottom of the parison (bottle top).

If we assume the *effective top section* is shortened by 15% in length (and its material moves to the bottom), the top section length is:

$$L_{\text{top, eff}} = 200 \times 0.85 = 170 \text{ mm}$$

Effective volume of material making the bottle bottom:

$$V_{\text{bottom}} = 100.53 \times 170 = 17{,}090 \text{ mm}^3$$

Assuming the bottom half of the bottle (in terms of the wall segment near the bottle base) uses this material distributed over half the bottle surface:

$$t_{\text{bottom}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{bottom}}}{\pi R H} = \dfrac{17{,}090}{\pi \times 30 \times 350}$$

$$t_{\text{bottom}} \approx 0.518 \text{ mm}$$

> *Examiner Note:* Mark distribution: 1 mark for setting up; 1 mark for arithmetic; 1 mark for sag interpretation. Full credit for any reasonable physical interpretation.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Defect Table (7 marks)

The student is expected to draw a **15-row table** with the columns: **Defect | Cause | Remedy**.

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| Short shot | Low air pressure | Raise to 0.6–0.8 MPa |
| Blow hole | Excessive pressure | Reduce pressure |
| Weld line | Cold pinch-off | Heat mould, increase land |
| Parison sag | Low melt strength | Lower temp, use higher MFR resin |
| Flash | High clamp force | Reduce tonnage |
| Warpage | Uneven cooling | Balance cooling channels |
| Haze (PET) | Low preform temp | Heat to 100–115 °C |
| Burn marks | Long residence | Purge, lower temp |
| Streaks | Melt fracture | Lower screw RPM |
| Sink marks | Thick wall | Add cooling core |
| Sticking | Insufficient draft | Apply release agent |
| Ovality | Parison asymmetry | Re-program die |
| Fish eyes | Contamination | Purge, sieve resin |
| Post-blow shrinkage | Insufficient cooling | Extend cooling time |
| Orange peel | High BUR | Reduce BUR to 2.5–3.0 |

**Two defects most influenced by cooling system (1 mark):**
1. **Warpage** – direct result of non-uniform cooling.
2. **Post-blow shrinkage** – direct result of insufficient cooling time.

(Honourable mention: **Sink marks**, **part sticking**.)

#### Part (b) — PET Stretch Ratios (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Preform diameter $D_p = 26$ mm
- Final bottle diameter $D_b = 70$ mm
- Axial stretch ratio $\lambda_a = 2.5$
- Preform wall thickness $t_p = 3.5$ mm

**Step 1 — Hoop (transverse) stretch ratio (1.5 marks):**

$$\lambda_h = \dfrac{D_b}{D_p} = \dfrac{70}{26} \approx 2.69$$

**Step 2 — Overall (planar) stretch ratio (1.5 marks):**

$$\lambda_{\text{total}} = \lambda_a \times \lambda_h = 2.5 \times 2.69 = 6.73$$

> *Note: In PET bottle-making, the standard metric is sometimes the **square root** (for biaxial orientation characterisation):*
> $$\lambda_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\lambda_a \cdot \lambda_h} = \sqrt{6.73} \approx 2.59$$
> *Acceptable if the student uses the convention taught in class.*

**Step 3 — Wall thickness reduction (2 marks):**

For **incompressible plastic flow**, the wall thickness scales inversely with the *area* stretch ratio:

$$t_b = \dfrac{t_p}{\lambda_a \cdot \lambda_h} = \dfrac{3.5}{2.5 \times 2.69}$$

$$t_b = \dfrac{3.5}{6.73} \approx 0.520 \text{ mm} = 520 \text{ \mu m}$$

**Step 4 — CSD barrier requirement check (2 marks):**

- Required for carbonated soft drinks: **20–25 µm** barrier layer (typically an **EVOH** or **nylon** layer in multi-layer PET).
- But the **total PET wall** for a 1 L CSD bottle is typically **250–350 µm** (12–35 mil).
- The 520 µm calculated wall is **too thick** for a 1 L CSD bottle — indicates either:
  - Preform wall too heavy, **OR**
  - Stretch ratios insufficient.
- Industry-correct values for a 1 L CSD bottle: $\lambda_a = 2.5, \lambda_h = 2.5, \lambda_{\text{total}} = 6.25 → t_b = 0.56$ mm — still too thick. So usually the **preform wall is 3.0 mm or less** and the final wall is **0.30–0.40 mm**.

> *Examiner Note:* Award full credit if the student clearly:
> (1) Calculates hoop ratio ✓
> (2) Calculates overall ratio ✓
> (3) Calculates wall thickness using $t_p / \lambda_{\text{total}}$ ✓
> (4) Compares to barrier spec and concludes "wall is too thick" ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout (VERY IMPORTANT)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to mention units** in final answers (e.g., writing 0.30 instead of 0.30 mm) → **−0.5 mark**.
> 2. **Skipping the "diagnosis first, action second"** pattern in troubleshooting answers → 1 mark deducted because it shows the student has memorised but not understood.
> 3. **Confusing BUR with Stretch Ratio** → BUR is for EBM (parison → bottle diameter), Stretch Ratio is for SBM (preform → bottle). Mixing them up is a **−2 mark** classic trap.
> 4. **Writing "increase temperature" as a universal fix** → Specificity matters. Always say *which zone* (rear, middle, front, melt).
> 5. **In sag problems, forgetting that parison TOP becomes bottle BOTTOM** → This directional confusion is the #1 mistake.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Memorise Before Exam)**

- [ ] Blow moulding has **3 variants** (EBM, IBM, SBM); EBM dominates by volume.
- [ ] **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)** = mould diameter / parison diameter; range **1.5:1 to 4:1**.
- [ ] **Die Swell** = parison OD / die OD; range **1.10–1.40**; compensated by **die land length**.
- [ ] **Parison sag** is gravity-driven; controlled by **melt temperature, MFI, hang time, parison programming**.
- [ ] **Wall thickness** formula: $t = \dfrac{A_p \cdot L_p}{2 \pi R H}$.
- [ ] **Hoop stress** in inflated parison: $\sigma = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2 t}$.
- [ ] **Blow pressure** typical: **0.5–0.8 MPa** (5–8 bar); theoretical minimum derived from hoop stress.
- [ ] **15 master defects** — know at least 10 by name, cause, and remedy.
- [ ] **Cooling** is the most time-consuming stage (~50–70% of cycle).
- [ ] **PET stretch ratios** — axial 2.4–2.6, hoop 4.0–4.5; preform temp 100–115 °C.
- [ ] **4-M + E** diagnostic framework (Material, Machine, Method, Man, Environment) is the universal troubleshooting template.
- [ ] **HDPE design shrinkage** = **1.5–3.0%**; compensate in mould design.
- [ ] **Pinch-off land length** = **1.5–3 mm**; knife angle = **15°–30°**.
- [ ] **Weld line** = parison halves fuse at pinch-off; fix via pinch-off land, not air pressure.
- [ ] **Warpage** = cooling asymmetry; fix via coolant flow balancing, not temperature reduction.
- [ ] **Haze in PET** = low preform temperature; fix by heating preforms.
- [ ] **Burn marks** = residence time too long; fix by purging and lowering rear-zone temp.
- [ ] Always use the **diagnose → root-cause → CAPA** structure in written answers.
- [ ] In KTU ESE, **diagrams get 1–2 marks extra** — always draw a labelled sketch.
- [ ] Numerical problems almost always involve **mass conservation** or **hoop stress** equations.
- [ ] Mention **units** in every numerical final answer (mm, MPa, %, °C).
- [ ] Use **\[\]** for display equations; **$ $** for inline; never mix them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermoforming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermoforming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermoforming** is a secondary plastics processing (fabrication) technique in which a flat thermoplastic sheet (extruded, cast, or calendered) is heated above its **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** for amorphous polymers or near the **melting point ($T_m$)** for semi-crystalline polymers, until it reaches a soft, rubbery, and formable state. The softened sheet is then forced against the contours of a mold (male or female) by means of **vacuum, positive air pressure, mechanical force, or matched dies**. After cooling below the softening temperature, the formed part is removed and trimmed from the original sheet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Thermoforming is a sheet-forming process in which a thermoplastic sheet pre-heated to its softening point is forced against the contours of a mold by the application of vacuum, pressure, or mechanical force, and the part is allowed to cool and solidify in the desired geometry before trimming."

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Sheet thickness range | **0.4 mm to 6 mm** (standard), up to 12 mm for heavy gauge |
| Forming temperature (PS) | $120^{\circ}\text{C} - 180^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Forming temperature (PP) | $150^{\circ}\text{C} - 170^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Forming temperature (PMMA) | $140^{\circ}\text{C} - 180^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Molds | Aluminum, epoxy, wood, plaster (low-cost tooling) |
| Typical cycle time | $10 \text{ s} - 180 \text{ s}$ (thin gauge: high speed) |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: "The Pizza Base" 🍕**
>
> Imagine you take a cold, rigid, round pizza base (the **thermoplastic sheet**). If you place it on a hot plate, it becomes soft, stretchy, and droopy (this is **heating above $T_g$**). Now, if you press a bowl upside down onto this soft base and gently push, the base will stretch and wrap itself around the bowl, taking the bowl's exact shape (this is **forming against a mold**). Once it cools down, it hardens into the bowl's shape, and you can peel it off and trim the excess edges (this is **cooling, demolding, and trimming**).
>
> That, in essence, is **Thermoforming** — molding a softened polymer sheet over a shape.

---

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (KTU 2024 Module-3 Focus Areas)

- **Thin-gauge thermoforming** (Sheet thickness $\le$ 2 mm) → Disposable cups, blister packs, trays.
- **Heavy-gauge thermoforming** (Sheet thickness $>$ 2 mm) → Automotive panels, refrigerator liners, bathtubs.
- **Roll-fed thermoforming** → Continuous inline systems.
- **Sheet-fed thermoforming** → Batch-type shuttle or rotary systems.

> [!TIP]
> The fundamental physics of all variants remains the same: **HEAT → FORM → COOL → TRIM**. Only the forcing mechanism (vacuum, pressure, plug, etc.) and the sheet-handling system change.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

Thermoforming is governed by **thermo-mechanical stretching of a viscoelastic polymer sheet above its softening point**. The polymer transitions from a **glassy, elastic solid** to a **rubbery, viscous fluid** (for amorphous polymers) or to a near-molten state (for semi-crystalline polymers), enabling large-scale plastic deformation under relatively low stress ($\sigma \approx 0.1 - 1 \text{ MPa}$).

The process is essentially a **free-surface forming operation** — the sheet is unsupported on one side while being forced against the mold on the other. This results in a characteristic **wall-thickness distribution** that is fundamentally different from injection molding (where uniform thickness is achievable).

---

## 2.2 The Five Sequential Process Steps (KTU Board Favorite)

1. **Clamping:** The thermoplastic sheet is rigidly clamped in a frame (called a *picture frame* or *clamp frame*) to prevent slippage during forming.
2. **Heating:** The clamped sheet is moved into a heating station (infra-red ceramic heaters, calrod heaters, or forced-convection ovens) and brought uniformly to the **forming temperature** (typically $T_g + 20^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $T_g + 60^{\circ}\text{C}$ for amorphous grades).
3. **Forming (Shaping):** The softened sheet is deformed over or into the mold using one of the forcing methods (vacuum, pressure, mechanical plug, or matched dies).
4. **Cooling:** The formed part is held in contact with the mold (which is temperature-controlled, usually $40^{\circ}\text{C} - 80^{\circ}\text{C}$) until it cools below its **Heat Distortion Temperature (HDT)** and becomes dimensionally stable.
5. **Trimming:** The excess material (called *web* or *skeleton*) is removed by die cutting, routing, or CNC knife cutting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Steps 1, 2, and 5 are **pre/post-forming operations**, while steps 3 and 4 are the **actual forming operations** that define the geometry.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Classification of Thermoforming Techniques

| S.No. | Technique | Forcing Mechanism | Typical Application |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Vacuum Forming (Straight Vacuum)** | Atmospheric pressure pushes sheet against mold (vacuum drawn behind sheet) | Disposable cups, packaging trays |
| 2 | **Drape Forming** | Sheet draped over a male mold, then vacuum applied | Aircraft canopies, large enclosures |
| 3 | **Pressure Forming (Compressed Air)** | Compressed air (0.3 - 1.0 MPa) pushes sheet into female mold | Refrigerator door liners |
| 4 | **Mechanical Forming (Matched Die)** | Two mold halves mechanically compress the sheet | Tubs, larger structural parts |
| 5 | **Plug-Assist Forming (Plug & Ring)** | A male plug first stretches sheet, then vacuum completes forming | Deep-draw containers (yogurt cups) |
| 6 | **Free Forming (Bubble Forming)** | Air pressure forms a free bubble, no mold | Curved canopies, skylights |
| 7 | **Twin-Sheet Forming** | Two sheets are formed simultaneously and welded | Hollow parts (pallets, cases) |
| 8 | **Billow Forming (Snap-back)** | Air pressure bulges the sheet, then male mold enters | Dome-shaped covers |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU Board-Exam ready. Memorize units carefully.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_f = t_0 \left(\dfrac{A_0}{A_f}\right)$ | $t_f$ = final thickness, $t_0$ = initial thickness, $A_0$ = original area, $A_f$ = formed area | **Constant volume** wall-thickness prediction |
| 2 | $\text{Draw Ratio (DR)} = \dfrac{H}{W}$ | $H$ = mold depth, $W$ = mold width (smaller lateral dim.) | Predicts wall thinning (DR $< 1$ is good) |
| 3 | $\text{Depth-of-Draw (DoD)} = \dfrac{H}{W}$ | Same as DR (alternate form) | KTU 2024 preferred term |
| 4 | $t_f = t_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{1 + \text{DR}}\right)$ | For cylindrical mold approximation | Quick DR-based thickness estimate |
| 5 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q$ = heat energy (J), $m$ = mass (kg), $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K), $\Delta T$ = temperature rise | Heater energy sizing |
| 6 | $P_{\text{vac}} = P_{\text{atm}} \cdot A = 101.325 \cdot A \text{ kN}$ | $P_{\text{vac}}$ = vacuum forming force, $A$ = sheet area ($\text{m}^2$) | Forming force calculation |
| 7 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{P_{\text{atm}} \cdot A_{\text{mold}}}{t_0 \cdot W}$ | $W$ = perimeter, $t_0$ = sheet thickness | Bending stress at clamped edge |
| 8 | $t = \dfrac{1}{\alpha} \ln\left(\dfrac{T_0 - T_{\text{mold}}}{T_f - T_{\text{mold}}}\right)$ | $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, $T$ = temperatures | Cooling time estimation |
| 9 | $T_f = T_g + (20 \text{ to } 60)^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Empirical forming window | Amorphous polymer forming temp. |
| 10 | $\text{Stretch Ratio} = \dfrac{l_f}{l_0}$ | Final length / original length | Local material thinning |

### 2.4.1 Critical Note on Forming Temperature Windows

| Polymer | $T_g$ ($^{\circ}$C) | $T_m$ ($^{\circ}$C) | Recommended Forming $T$ ($^{\circ}$C) |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Polystyrene (PS) | 100 | — | 130 – 160 |
| HIPS | 100 | — | 130 – 165 |
| Polypropylene (PP) | -10 | 165 | 150 – 170 |
| PVC (rigid) | 80 | — | 100 – 140 |
| PET (amorphous) | 70 | — | 100 – 130 |
| PMMA (Acrylic) | 105 | — | 140 – 180 |
| ABS | 105 | — | 150 – 175 |
| Polycarbonate (PC) | 150 | — | 180 – 210 |

> [!TIP]
> For semi-crystalline polymers like **PP**, the forming window is **narrow** (only $\approx 5 - 10^{\circ}\text{C}$ wide above $T_m$) because the material cools and re-crystallizes rapidly — this is a common KTU viva question.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Interior door panels, dashboard fascias, bumper fascias (using heavy-gauge ABS, TPO, PP sheets).
- **Packaging Industry:** Disposable cups, blister packs, clamshells, egg trays (PS, PET, PVC, HIPS).
- **Medical Industry:** Sterile packaging trays, medical device housings, dental aligners (PETG, PP).
- **Construction:** Shower enclosures, skylight domes, refrigerator liners (PMMA, ABS, HIPS).
- **Aviation:** Aircraft window canopies (stretched acrylic, drape forming).
- **Signage and Display:** Illuminated sign faces, 3D lettering (PMMA, PC).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Wall-Thickness in a Formed Part (Constant-Volume Principle)

### 3.1.1 The Underlying Assumption

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** Polymer melt and softened polymer are essentially **incompressible**. Hence, the **volume of the sheet is conserved** during thermoforming:
> $$V_{\text{before forming}} = V_{\text{after forming}}$$

### 3.1.2 Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the initial volume of the flat sheet:**

The original sheet is a flat rectangle of area $A_0$ and uniform thickness $t_0$:

$$V_0 = A_0 \cdot t_0$$

**Step 2 — Write the final volume of the formed part:**

The formed part has a complex 3D surface of area $A_f$ (the total surface area of the mold cavity including all walls) and an average wall thickness $t_f$:

$$V_f = A_f \cdot t_f$$

**Step 3 — Apply the constant-volume principle:**

$$V_0 = V_f$$

$$A_0 \cdot t_0 = A_f \cdot t_f$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the average final wall thickness $t_f$:**

$$\boxed{t_f = t_0 \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation:**

- Since $A_f > A_0$ always (a 3D shape has more surface area than its flat projection), it follows that $\mathbf{t_f < t_0}$.
- The **maximum thinning** occurs at corners, edges, and the deepest draw regions of the part.
- The **thickness is non-uniform** — this is a fundamental limitation of thermoforming (unlike injection molding).

> [!TIP]
> This is the **single most important equation** for any KTU 2024 thermoforming numerical problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Thickness in a Cylindrical Cup (Draw-Ratio Method)

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A circular sheet of initial thickness $t_0$ and diameter $D_0$ is vacuum-formed into a cylindrical cup of depth $H$ and diameter $D$ (where $D = D_0$ for a clamped-sheet scenario).

**Step 1 — Initial surface area (the flat disk):**

$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_0^2$$

**Step 2 — Final surface area (bottom + cylindrical wall of the cup):**

$$A_f = \underbrace{\dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2}_{\text{bottom}} + \underbrace{\pi D H}_{\text{cylindrical wall}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply constant volume principle:**

$$t_f = t_0 \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{A_f} = t_0 \cdot \dfrac{\dfrac{\pi}{4} D_0^2}{\dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 + \pi D H}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by canceling $\pi$ and assuming $D = D_0$ (clamped sheet, no lateral stretching):**

$$t_f = t_0 \cdot \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{4} D_0^2}{\dfrac{1}{4} D_0^2 + D_0 H}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $D_0^2$:

$$t_f = t_0 \cdot \dfrac{0.25}{0.25 + \dfrac{H}{D_0}}$$

**Step 5 — Define the draw ratio $\text{DR} = \dfrac{H}{D_0}$:**

$$\boxed{t_f = t_0 \cdot \dfrac{0.25}{0.25 + \text{DR}} = \dfrac{t_0}{1 + 4\,\text{DR}}}$$

### 3.2.2 Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 2 mm thick PS sheet of diameter 200 mm is vacuum-formed into a cylindrical cup of depth 80 mm. Find (a) the average final wall thickness of the cup, and (b) the draw ratio.

**Given:**
- $t_0 = 2 \text{ mm}$
- $D_0 = 200 \text{ mm}$
- $H = 80 \text{ mm}$
- $D = D_0 = 200 \text{ mm}$ (clamped)

**Part (a) — Draw ratio:**

$$\text{DR} = \dfrac{H}{D_0} = \dfrac{80}{200} = 0.4$$

**Part (b) — Average final thickness:**

$$t_f = \dfrac{t_0}{1 + 4\,\text{DR}} = \dfrac{2}{1 + 4(0.4)} = \dfrac{2}{1 + 1.6} = \dfrac{2}{2.6}$$

$$\boxed{t_f \approx 0.769 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The 2 mm sheet becomes $\approx$ 0.77 mm in the formed cup. The bottom of the cup is thicker (closer to $t_0$) while the side walls are thinner (approaching $t_f$ value). The **maximum thinning** would be near the top rim where the draw ratio is locally highest.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Required to Heat the Sheet

### 3.3.1 Setup

A sheet of polymer with mass $m$ must be raised from room temperature $T_{\text{room}}$ (assume $25^{\circ}\text{C}$) to the forming temperature $T_f$. We wish to compute the energy $Q$ required.

**Step 1 — Sensible heat energy (raising temperature):**

$$Q_1 = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_f - T_{\text{room}})$$

**Step 2 — For semi-crystalline polymers, add the latent heat of fusion $\Delta H_f$:**

$$Q_2 = m \cdot \Delta H_f$$

**Step 3 — Total energy for semi-crystalline polymers:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_f - T_{\text{room}}) + m \cdot \Delta H_f$$

**Step 4 — Required heater power (with cycle time $t_{\text{cycle}}$):**

$$P = \dfrac{Q_{\text{total}}}{t_{\text{cycle}} \cdot \eta}$$

where $\eta$ is the heater efficiency (typically $0.5$ to $0.7$ for IR heaters, $0.7$ to $0.85$ for calrod/convection).

### 3.3.2 Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A 1.5 mm thick PP sheet of area $0.5 \text{ m} \times 0.5 \text{ m}$ must be heated from $25^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $165^{\circ}\text{C}$ in 30 seconds. Density of PP = $905 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1925 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $\Delta H_f = 165 \text{ kJ/kg}$, heater efficiency $\eta = 0.6$. Find the heater wattage.

**Step 1 — Mass of the sheet:**

$$m = \rho \cdot V = 905 \cdot (0.5 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.0015) = 905 \cdot 0.000375$$

$$m = 0.3394 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Sensible heat:**

$$Q_1 = 0.3394 \cdot 1925 \cdot (165 - 25) = 0.3394 \cdot 1925 \cdot 140$$

$$Q_1 = 91{,}440 \text{ J} \approx 91.4 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3 — Latent heat (since PP is semi-crystalline):**

$$Q_2 = 0.3394 \cdot 165{,}000 = 56{,}000 \text{ J} = 56.0 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Total heat:**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 91.4 + 56.0 = 147.4 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Required heater power:**

$$P = \dfrac{147{,}400}{30 \cdot 0.6} = \dfrac{147{,}400}{18} = 8189 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 8.2 \text{ kW}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Problem Patterns

| # | Problem Type | Key Formula to Use | Common Trap |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wall thickness after forming | $t_f = t_0 (A_0 / A_f)$ | Forgetting to include side walls |
| 2 | Draw ratio check | $\text{DR} = H / W$ | Using wrong dimension (use smallest lateral dim.) |
| 3 | Cycle time from cooling | Fourier conduction equation | Use $T_{\text{mold}}$ correctly |
| 4 | Vacuum forming force | $F = P_{\text{atm}} \cdot A$ | Unit conversion to kN or N |
| 5 | Heater power sizing | $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ | Ignoring $\Delta H_f$ for crystalline polymers |
| 6 | Material selection | Forming temp vs degradation temp | Choosing material with narrow forming window |
| 7 | Sheet area calculation | $A_0 = A_f \cdot (t_f / t_0)$ | Forgetting trim allowance (add 5-10%) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Complete Thermoforming Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Clamp Sheet in Frame] --> B[Move Sheet to Heating Station]
    B --> C[Heat Sheet to Forming Temperature Tg + 20 to 60 °C]
    C --> D{Temperature Reached Uniformly?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Move Sheet to Forming Station]
    E --> F[Select Forming Method]
    F --> F1[Vacuum Forming]
    F --> F2[Pressure Forming]
    F --> F3[Plug Assist Forming]
    F --> F4[Drape Forming]
    F --> F5[Matched Die Forming]
    F1 --> G[Force Sheet Against Mold]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    G --> H[Hold and Cool Below HDT]
    H --> I[Release Vacuum or Pressure]
    I --> J[Demold the Formed Part]
    J --> K[Trim Excess Web Material]
    K --> L[Inspect Part for Defects]
    L --> M[End: Finished Thermoformed Part]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading aid:** The node "F" (decision diamond) acts as a **method selector** — this represents the engineer's choice of forcing mechanism. Every path converges at node G because, regardless of the forming method chosen, the physical act of *forcing the sheet against the mold* is identical.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Vacuum Forming Machine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Section1[Heating Zone]
        H1[IR Heater Bank Top]
        H2[IR Heater Bank Bottom]
    end
    subgraph Section2[Forming Zone]
        C[Clamp Frame with Softened Sheet]
        M[Mold on Pneumatic Platen]
        V[Vacuum Pump and Reservoir]
        VA[Vacuum Line to Mold Cavity]
    end
    subgraph Section3[Control Zone]
        PLC[PLC Controller with HMI]
        TC[Thermocouples on Sheet]
        PS[Pressure Sensor Vacuum Line]
    end
    PLC --> H1
    PLC --> H2
    TC --> PLC
    H1 --> C
    H2 --> C
    C --> M
    V --> VA
    VA --> M
    PS --> PLC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Explanation:**
> - **Heating Zone** contains the IR heaters that radiate thermal energy to the clamped sheet.
> - **Forming Zone** contains the moving mold platen and the vacuum reservoir that creates the pressure differential.
> - **Control Zone** contains the **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** that orchestrates the entire sequence by reading thermocouples (sheet temperature) and pressure sensors (vacuum level).
> - The **signal flow** is bi-directional: sensors feed the PLC, and the PLC commands actuators (heaters, vacuum valves, platen motion).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Defect Tree — Common Thermoforming Defects and Root Causes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Thermoformed Part Defect]
    D --> D1[Web Bridging]
    D --> D2[Sagging Sheet]
    D --> D3[Mark Off]
    D --> D4[Blistering]
    D --> D5[Poor Definition]
    D1 --> R1[Insufficient Vacuum: Inadequate Venting]
    D2 --> R2[Overheating: Tg Exceeded by >30 °C]
    D3 --> R3[Mold Surface Polish Issue: Scratches on Mold]
    D4 --> R4[Trapped Air: Insufficient Vacuum Time]
    D5 --> R5[Under Heating or Low Forming Pressure]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This defect-cause tree is **KTU viva favorite** — memorize at least 4 of these defect-cause pairs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 3 marks each]**

### Question 1: Define Thermoforming. Mention any two differences between thermoforming and injection molding.

**Model Answer:**

**Thermoforming** is a sheet-forming process in which a thermoplastic sheet is heated to its softening temperature and deformed against a mold using vacuum, pressure, or mechanical force, followed by cooling and trimming.

| Parameter | Thermoforming | Injection Molding |
|---|---|---|
| Starting material | **Pre-formed sheet** | **Pellets / granules** |
| Tooling cost | **Very low** (aluminum, epoxy molds) | **Very high** (hardened steel) |
| Wall thickness uniformity | **Non-uniform** (thinning at corners) | **Uniform and controllable** |
| Part complexity | Limited (no undercuts without plugins) | Very high (complex 3D) |
| Production rate | Lower (except thin-gauge inline) | Very high |
| Suitable for | Large, shallow parts | Small, complex, mass-produced parts |

---

### Question 2: Why is the forming temperature window of polypropylene (PP) narrower than that of polystyrene (PS)?

**Model Answer:**

Polypropylene is a **semi-crystalline polymer** with a sharp melting point at $T_m \approx 165^{\circ}\text{C}$. Below $T_m$, PP exists as a tough, semi-rigid solid; above $T_m$, it rapidly becomes a low-viscosity melt. The crystalline regions melt over a narrow temperature range of $5 - 10^{\circ}\text{C}$, giving a **narrow processing window**.

In contrast, polystyrene is an **amorphous polymer** that gradually softens over a wide temperature range above its $T_g$ ($\approx 100^{\circ}\text{C}$), giving a **wide forming window** of $50 - 80^{\circ}\text{C}$ where it remains in a workable rubbery state without flowing excessively.

Hence, PP must be precisely temperature-controlled during thermoforming, while PS tolerates broader temperature variations.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam Pattern: 14-Mark Question with Two Sub-Parts, Internal Choice Between Q-A and Q-B]**

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model, 14 marks]**

**"Explain in detail the vacuum forming process for plastics. With neat sketches, describe the various stages involved. Also discuss the advantages, limitations, and major applications of vacuum forming."**

#### Part (a) — Process Description and Stages [7 marks]

**Definition:** Vacuum forming is a thermoforming process in which atmospheric pressure ($\approx 101.325 \text{ kPa}$) is used to force a pre-heated thermoplastic sheet against a mold by creating a partial vacuum (typically $50 - 90 \text{ kPa}$ absolute) on the opposite side of the sheet.

**Stages of Vacuum Forming (with sketch explanation):**

**Stage 1 — Clamping:**
The flat thermoplastic sheet is rigidly held in a **clamp frame** (also called a *picture frame*). The clamp prevents sheet slippage and ensures airtight sealing for the subsequent vacuum step.

**Stage 2 — Heating:**
The clamped sheet is moved into a **heating station** equipped with **infrared (IR) ceramic heaters** above and below the sheet. The sheet is heated uniformly to the forming temperature:
$$T_f = T_g + (20 \text{ to } 60)^{\circ}\text{C}$$

For amorphous PS: $T_f \approx 150^{\circ}\text{C}$; for PP: $T_f \approx 160^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**Stage 3 — Indexing (Sheet Transfer):**
The heated, softened sheet is moved over the **mold cavity** (mounted on a movable platen). The mold is typically made of aluminum, wood, or epoxy.

**Stage 4 — Vacuum Application:**
The vacuum pump evacuates air from the sealed mold cavity. The pressure differential pushes the sheet tightly against the mold surface, replicating its contours. The forming pressure is:
$$P_{\text{form}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vac}} \approx 80 \text{ kPa}$$

**Stage 5 — Cooling:**
The sheet remains in contact with the mold (which is water-cooled, typically at $40 - 60^{\circ}\text{C}$) until the polymer cools below its **Heat Distortion Temperature (HDT)**, gaining sufficient rigidity for demolding.

**Stage 6 — Demolding and Trimming:**
The part is ejected, and the excess flange material (the *web* or *skeleton*) is trimmed using a **die cutter, CNC router, or steel-rule die**.

#### Part (b) — Advantages, Limitations, and Applications [7 marks]

**Advantages:**

1. **Low tooling cost** (1/10 to 1/50 of injection mold cost).
2. **Short lead time** for prototype development.
3. Suitable for **large parts** (up to 6 m × 2.4 m in some industrial systems).
4. **Wide material selection** (PS, PP, PET, PVC, ABS, PMMA, PC, etc.).
5. **Low scrap rate** (excess material can be re-ground and re-extruded).

**Limitations:**

1. **Non-uniform wall thickness** — thinning at corners and deep-draw regions.
2. **Limited part complexity** — undercuts and sharp internal corners are difficult.
3. **Trimming is an additional secondary operation**, increasing cycle time and cost.
4. **Material wastage** is higher than injection molding for simple parts.

**Major Applications:**

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| Packaging | Yogurt cups, blister packs, egg cartons, clamshells |
| Automotive | Dashboard prototypes, door panels, headlamp housings |
| Medical | Sterile packaging trays, instrument enclosures |
| Consumer goods | Refrigerator liners, washing-machine top covers, suitcases |
| Signage | Illuminated sign faces, 3D letterforms |

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[Alternative Choice, 14 marks]**

**"Describe plug-assist thermoforming in detail. Explain its working principle, design features of the plug, and compare it with straight vacuum forming. Derive the wall-thickness distribution equation for a cup formed by plug-assist."**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle and Plug Design [7 marks]

**Working Principle:**

Plug-assist thermoforming is a **two-stage forming process** designed to overcome the **uneven wall-thickness distribution** of straight vacuum forming, especially in **deep-draw parts**. The process works as follows:

1. A **male plug** (made of aluminum, polymer, or wood) is pushed into the **pre-heated and clamped sheet** from above, **partially stretching it downward** into the cavity. This pre-stretch distributes the material more uniformly.
2. After the plug reaches its lowest position (typically $70 - 80\%$ of the mold depth), vacuum is applied to the mold cavity, **drawing the pre-stretched sheet fully against the mold walls**.

**Key Design Features of the Plug:**

| Feature | Specification |
|---|---|
| Material | Aluminum (most common), polymer, wood, syntactic foam |
| Surface finish | Highly polished (to avoid sheet marking) |
| Temperature | Pre-heated to $80 - 120^{\circ}\text{C}$ (to prevent chill-marking the sheet on contact) |
| Shape | Tapered or rounded edges; slightly smaller than the mold cavity |
| Speed | $100 - 500 \text{ mm/s}$ penetration speed |
| Travel | $70 - 80\%$ of mold depth |

#### Part (b) — Comparison and Wall-Thickness Derivation [7 marks]

**Comparison: Plug-Assist vs. Straight Vacuum Forming**

| Parameter | Straight Vacuum | Plug-Assist |
|---|---|---|
| Material distribution | Poor (heavy thinning at corners) | Excellent (uniform thickness) |
| Maximum draw ratio | DR $\leq 0.5$ | DR up to $1.0 - 1.5$ |
| Part depth | Shallow to medium | Deep-draw capable |
| Cycle time | Faster | Slightly slower |
| Equipment cost | Lower | Higher (extra plug mechanism) |
| Suitable for | Trays, lids, blister packs | Yogurt cups, deep containers, bathtubs |

**Wall-Thickness Distribution Derivation:**

**Step 1 — After plug pre-stretch:**
The plug pushes the sheet into a hemispherical or tapered shape. The stretched area $A_1$ of the partially bulged sheet is approximately:
$$A_1 \approx 2 \pi R_p h_p$$
where $R_p$ is the plug radius and $h_p$ is the plug penetration depth.

**Step 2 — After vacuum completion:**
The total formed surface area is $A_f = A_{\text{bottom}} + A_{\text{wall}}$, and the average thickness is:
$$t_f = t_0 \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{A_f}$$

**Step 3 — Local thickness at the side wall (post plug-stretch):**
Since the plug distributes the material more evenly, the side-wall thickness is approximately:
$$t_{\text{wall}} \approx t_0 \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{2 A_f}$$

**Step 4 — Local thickness at the bottom (less stretched):**
The bottom of the cup experiences less stretching and retains more thickness:
$$t_{\text{bottom}} \approx t_0 \cdot \dfrac{A_0}{A_0 + 0.5 A_f}$$

**Step 5 — Key engineering insight:**
The plug-assist method achieves a **bottom-to-wall thickness ratio closer to unity** (often $0.85 - 0.95$), which is critical for parts requiring **structural strength**, such as automotive interior panels and deep food containers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Thermoforming Questions (KTU 2024 Board):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $T_g$ and $T_m$:** Students often use $T_g$ for crystalline polymers. KTU examiners deduct 1-2 marks for this. **Always state: Amorphous → form above $T_g$; Semi-crystalline → form above $T_m$.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to include side walls in $A_f$:** When computing $A_f$ for a cup, students often use only the bottom area, forgetting the cylindrical wall. This causes an inflated $t_f$ value, losing 2-3 marks in numericals.
>
> 3. **Wrong units in vacuum force calculation:** Atmospheric pressure $101.325 \text{ kPa}$ must be multiplied by area in $\text{m}^2$ to get force in **kN**, not N. Convert carefully.
>
> 4. **Not mentioning mold materials:** Examiner expects at least one line on mold materials (aluminum, epoxy, wood). Vague answer = 1 mark deduction.
>
> 5. **Skipping the cooling stage in process descriptions:** Always mention **HDT (Heat Distortion Temperature)** as the cutoff for cooling.
>
> 6. **Writing "vacuum" without specifying pressure range:** A complete answer must state the vacuum level (typically $50 - 90 \text{ kPa}$ absolute).
>
> 7. **Confusing draw ratio with depth-of-draw:** These are the same quantity, but students sometimes invert the formula as $W/H$. Use $\text{DR} = H / W_{\min}$ consistently.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Thermoforming (Module 3)**

- ☐ **Definition:** Sheet heating + force against mold + cooling + trimming.
- ☐ **Five sequential steps:** Clamp → Heat → Form → Cool → Trim.
- ☐ **Forming temperature rule (Amorphous):** $T_f = T_g + (20 - 60)^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- ☐ **Forming temperature rule (Semi-crystalline):** $T_f = T_m + (5 - 10)^{\circ}\text{C}$ — **narrow window**.
- ☐ **Constant-volume principle:** $t_0 \cdot A_0 = t_f \cdot A_f$ → $t_f < t_0$ always.
- ☐ **Draw Ratio:** $\text{DR} = H / W_{\min}$ → $\text{DR} < 1$ preferred; $\text{DR} \leq 0.5$ for straight vacuum.
- ☐ **Atmospheric pressure used for forming force:** $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ → 0.1 MPa.
- ☐ **Mold materials (cheap to expensive):** Plaster → Wood → Epoxy → Aluminum → Steel.
- ☐ **Major techniques:** Vacuum, Pressure, Plug-assist, Drape, Matched-die, Twin-sheet.
- ☐ **Plug-assist = best for uniform wall thickness in deep-draw parts.**
- ☐ **Thin-gauge (≤ 2 mm) → packaging; Heavy-gauge (> 2 mm) → automotive, construction.**
- ☐ **Energy equation:** $Q = m c_p \Delta T + m \Delta H_f$ (include $\Delta H_f$ for crystalline polymers).
- ☐ **Cooling cutoff temperature:** $T_{\text{demold}} < \text{HDT}$ (Heat Distortion Temperature).
- ☐ **Defects to memorize:** Web bridging, sagging, mark-off, blistering, poor definition.
- ☐ **Key polymers:** PS, HIPS, PP, PET, PVC, PMMA, ABS, PC.
- ☐ **Advantages:** Low tooling cost, short lead time, large parts possible, wide material range.
- ☐ **Limitations:** Non-uniform wall thickness, limited complexity, secondary trimming needed.

> 🎯 **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Always draw a **labeled process block diagram** when describing any thermoforming technique. Examiners allocate **1-2 marks** specifically for the diagram, even in descriptive answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Polymer Product Manufacturing – Foundational Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 Focus (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This module introduces the **secondary shaping processes** used to convert polymer raw materials (pellets, powders, sheets, or melts) into finished or semi-finished end-use products. The following definitions form the conceptual backbone for all machinery and process discussions that follow in this module.

---

### 1.2 Primary vs. Secondary Processing of Polymers

> **Primary Processing:** Operations that convert raw monomers/polymers into usable stock forms such as pellets, sheets, films, rods, or profiles. Examples: **polymerization, compounding, extrusion, and mixing**.

> **Secondary Processing (Scope of Module 3):** Operations that reshape the primary stock into final products using heat, pressure, or mechanical deformation. Examples: **blow molding, rotational molding, thermoforming, compression molding, transfer molding, and casting**.

---

### 1.3 Definitions of Key Polymer Product Manufacturing Processes

#### A. Blow Molding

> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> Blow molding is a **secondary thermoplastic processing** technique in which a heated thermoplastic parison (a molten, tube-like preform) or preform is inflated with compressed air against the cooled walls of a closed mold cavity, taking the shape of the mold to form a hollow plastic product.

- **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine blowing up a balloon inside a glass bottle. The balloon (parison) expands and presses against the walls of the bottle (mold), taking its exact shape. Once cooled, you have a solid hollow object that is the bottle's negative.

- **Standard Process Categories:** Extrusion Blow Molding (EBM), Injection Blow Molding (IBM), Stretch Blow Molding (SBM).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Product Examples:** PET bottles, HDPE containers, fuel tanks, toys, drums, and large industrial containers.

---

#### B. Rotational Molding (Rotomolding)

> **Formal Definition:**
> Rotational molding is a **low-pressure, high-temperature** polymer processing technique in which a measured quantity of polymer powder (or liquid plastisol) is placed inside a closed, hollow mold. The mold is rotated biaxially (about two perpendicular axes) in an oven, causing the powder to melt, coat, and fuse uniformly onto the inner mold walls. The mold is then cooled to solidify the part.

- **Conceptual Analogy:** Picture slowly rotating a sealed can filled with sand over a fire. As you rotate it, the sand coats the entire inner surface. When the can is cooled, the inside surface is lined with a solid layer. Rotomolding uses polymer powder instead of sand and controlled ovens instead of fire.

- **Process Variants:** **3-axis rotation (rock-and-roll), shuttle molding, clamshell molding**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Product Examples:** Water storage tanks, kayaks, playground equipment, fuel tanks, road barriers, and large hollow containers with no weld lines.

---

#### C. Thermoforming

> **Formal Definition:**
> Thermoforming is a **secondary thermoplastic sheet-processing** technique in which a thermoplastic sheet (extruded or cast) is heated above its glass transition temperature ($T_g$) for amorphous polymers or above its melting point ($T_m$) for semi-crystalline polymers, then forced against a single-sided mold by vacuum, pressure, or mechanical force, and cooled to retain the new shape.

- **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of heating a thin plastic sheet (like a piece of cling wrap) over a small bowl. As you press it down, it takes the bowl's shape. Once cooled, it stays molded.

- **Standard Variants:** **Vacuum thermoforming, pressure thermoforming, mechanical (matched-die) thermoforming, twin-sheet thermoforming**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Product Examples:** Disposable cups, blister packs, refrigerator liners, bathtubs, automotive dashboards, and clamshell packaging.

---

#### D. Compression Molding

> **Formal Definition:**
> Compression molding is a **high-pressure thermoset (and limited thermoplastic) processing** technique in which a measured charge of molding compound (e.g., BMC, SMC, phenolic resin, melamine, or rubber) is placed directly into an open, heated mold cavity. The mold is then closed, applying high pressure, which forces the material to flow and fill the cavity. Heat and pressure trigger curing (crosslinking), after which the part is ejected as a solid, rigid product.

- **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine making a sandwich in a waffle iron. You place the batter (compound) on the bottom plate, close the top plate, apply pressure, and heat cures the batter into the waffle shape. Compression molding follows the same logic for polymers.

- **Standard Variants:** **Cold compression molding, hot compression molding, sheet molding compound (SMC), bulk molding compound (BMC)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Product Examples:** Electrical switchgear, automotive panels (SMC), melamine dinnerware, brake pads, rubber seals, and phenolic handles.

---

#### E. Transfer Molding

> **Formal Definition:**
> Transfer molding is a variant of compression molding in which the polymer charge is **preheated in a transfer pot (plunger chamber)** above the mold and then forced through a sprue/runner system into a closed, heated mold cavity where curing occurs. This decouples the plasticating/charging step from the cavity-filling step.

- **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of using a syringe to inject cake batter into a closed cake mold. The batter is pre-loaded in the syringe (transfer pot), then injected into a closed mold where it solidifies, allowing for more intricate shapes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Product Examples:** Encapsulation of electronic components, intricate rubber gaskets, and thermoset parts with delicate inserts.

---

#### F. Casting (Polymer Casting)

> **Formal Definition:**
> Polymer casting is a **low-pressure, ambient or mildly elevated temperature** shaping process in which a liquid polymer system (monomer + hardener, resin, or liquid plastisol) is poured into an open or closed mold and allowed to solidify by chemical reaction (curing), cooling, or solvent evaporation without application of significant external pressure.

- **Conceptual Analogy:** Pouring Jell-O into a bowl and letting it set in the refrigerator. The liquid takes the shape of the container and solidifies without any applied pressure.

- **Standard Variants:** **Open casting, centrifugal casting, investment casting, reaction injection molding (RIM), slush casting, dip casting**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Product Examples:** Polyurethane (PU) elastomer rollers, epoxy electrical insulators, acrylic sheets (PMMA), PVC flooring, and polyurethane foam parts.

---

### 1.4 Summary of Standard Process Metrics (KTU Reference Values)

| Process | Typical Pressure Range | Typical Temperature | Material Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Blow Molding** | **0.3 – 1.0 MPa** (Inflation air) | **150 – 230 °C** (Melt) | Thermoplastics |
| **Rotational Molding** | **Gravitational (≈ 0 Pa external)** | **200 – 350 °C** (Oven) | Thermoplastics (powder) |
| **Thermoforming** | **Atmospheric – 0.3 MPa** | **120 – 200 °C** (Sheet) | Thermoplastic sheets |
| **Compression Molding** | **10 – 100 MPa** | **150 – 200 °C** | Thermosets / Rubbers |
| **Transfer Molding** | **5 – 70 MPa** | **140 – 200 °C** | Thermosets / Rubbers |
| **Casting** | **Gravitational** | **RT – 120 °C** | Liquid resins / Elastomers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Conceptual Takeaway:** The selection of a polymer product manufacturing process depends on four key parameters: **(1) Material type (thermoplastic/thermoset/elastomer), (2) Production volume, (3) Geometric complexity, and (4) Tolerances required.**

---

### 1.5 Industry Significance

> These six processes collectively account for **more than 80%** of all secondary polymer product manufacturing worldwide. Each process has dedicated machinery (extruders, blow stations, rotational armatures, thermoformers, hydraulic presses, casting lines) that are detailed in subsequent topics of Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework for Classifying Manufacturing Processes

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme requires students to be able to **classify and justify** the selection of a polymer processing technique based on material, geometry, volume, and cost. The following analytical framework forms the basis for KTU Board exam questions.

---

### 2.2 Classification Logic – Step-by-Step

1. **Step 1 – Identify the Material State:**
   - **Thermoplastic** (re-meltable) → Blow molding, Rotomolding, Thermoforming
   - **Thermoset** (crosslinks irreversibly) → Compression, Transfer, Casting
   - **Elastomer/Rubber** → Compression, Transfer, Casting (often with vulcanization)

2. **Step 2 – Identify the Product Geometry:**
   - **Hollow, single-piece, openable/closed containers** → Blow molding
   - **Large, seamless, hollow 3D parts** → Rotomolding
   - **Thin-walled shells, packaging, large area** → Thermoforming
   - **High-strength, load-bearing, structural parts** → Compression molding (SMC/BMC)
   - **Intricate parts with embedded inserts** → Transfer molding
   - **Large, low-stress, prototype parts** → Casting

3. **Step 3 – Evaluate Production Volume:**
   - **High Volume (>10,000 parts/year):** Injection blow molding, compression molding
   - **Medium Volume (1,000–10,000):** Extrusion blow molding
   - **Low Volume (<1,000) or Large Parts:** Rotomolding, Casting

4. **Step 4 – Cost and Tolerance Considerations:**
   - **Tight tolerances + high surface finish:** Injection blow molding, compression molding
   - **Loose tolerances + economic tooling:** Rotomolding, casting, thermoforming

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are essential for solving numerical and theoretical KTU 2024 ESE questions on process selection, cycle time, and process capability.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bottle Surface Area (Cylinder Approximation)** | $A = \pi d h + 2 \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | $d$ = diameter (m), $h$ = height (m) | Blow mold parison sizing |
| 2 | **Parison Length Required** | $L_p = \frac{V_{part}}{A_{cs}}$ | $L_p$ = parison length (m), $V_{part}$ = part volume (m³), $A_{cs}$ = cross-sectional area (m²) | EBM process design |
| 3 | **Inflation Pressure (Blow Molding)** | $P_{infl} = \frac{2 \sigma t}{r}$ | $P_{infl}$ (Pa), $\sigma$ = hoop stress (Pa), $t$ = wall thickness (m), $r$ = radius (m) | Bottle wall stress analysis |
| 4 | **Rotomolding Heating Index** | $HI = T_{oven} - T_{melt}$ | $T_{oven}$ (°C), $T_{melt}$ (°C) | Material selection |
| 5 | **Thermoforming Sag Depth** | $H_{sag} = f(T_{sheet}, \text{time})$ | Empirical, function of sheet temperature and heating time | Process control |
| 6 | **Compression Mold Clamping Force** | $F_c = P_{mold} \cdot A_{cav}$ | $F_c$ (N), $P_{mold}$ (Pa), $A_{cav}$ = projected cavity area (m²) | Press selection |
| 7 | **Cycle Time (Compression)** | $t_{cyc} = t_c + t_{cur} + t_o$ | $t_c$ = closing, $t_{cur}$ = curing, $t_o$ = opening (s) | Productivity analysis |
| 8 | **Transfer Pot Capacity** | $V_{pot} = n \cdot V_{cavity} + V_{runner}$ | $V_{pot}$ (m³), $n$ = cavities, $V_{cav}$ (m³) | Mold design |
| 9 | **Casting Cure Shrinkage** | $S\% = \frac{L_{mold} - L_{part}}{L_{mold}} \times 100$ | $L$ = dimensions (m) | Mold dimensioning |
| 10 | **Heat Transfer (Rotomolding)** | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q$ (J), $m$ = mass (kg), $c_p$ (J/kg·K), $\Delta T$ (K) | Energy balance |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Note:** Absolute value expressions (e.g., $\vert x - \bar{x} \vert$) use `\vert` to prevent markdown table parsing errors. Do not use plain `|` inside any cell.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> These formulas and definitions are not academic exercises — they are used daily in:
> - **Packaging industry** (PET bottle stress design using inflation pressure equation)
> - **Automotive industry** (SMC body panel mold force calculation)
> - **Water storage industry** (rotomolding tank wall thickness uniformity)
> - **Electronics industry** (transfer molding of IC chips using EMC epoxy)
> - **Furniture and flooring industry** (casting of PU foam using reaction kinetics)

The classification framework above allows practicing engineers to **justify process selection in design reviews**, which is a critical course outcome in KTU 2024 (CO3: *Apply appropriate polymer processing technique for a given product requirement*).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 – Parison Length for Extrusion Blow Molding (EBM)

This is a high-frequency KTU numerical problem. We derive the parison length required to manufacture a hollow part of given volume.

**Given:**
- A cylindrical bottle of diameter $d$ and height $h$
- Wall thickness $t$ (assumed uniform)

**Required:** Length of parison $L_p$ to be extruded before mold close.

**Step 1 – Define the volume of the bottle wall (hollow cylinder):**

The bottle wall volume equals the part volume minus the internal cavity volume. Since the wall is thin, we can approximate:

$$V_{part} = A_{wall} \cdot h_{equiv}$$

where $A_{wall}$ is the annular cross-sectional area of the wall:

$$A_{wall} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[ d_o^2 - d_i^2 \right]$$

where $d_o$ = outer diameter and $d_i = d_o - 2t$ is the inner diameter.

**Step 2 – Substitute and simplify:**

$$A_{wall} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[ d_o^2 - (d_o - 2t)^2 \right]$$

Expanding the inner term:

$$(d_o - 2t)^2 = d_o^2 - 4 d_o t + 4 t^2$$

Therefore:

$$A_{wall} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[ d_o^2 - d_o^2 + 4 d_o t - 4 t^2 \right]$$

$$A_{wall} = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[ 4 d_o t - 4 t^2 \right]$$

$$A_{wall} = \pi t (d_o - t)$$

**Step 3 – Since $t \ll d_o$, simplify:**

$$A_{wall} \approx \pi t \cdot d_o = \pi d_o t$$

**Step 4 – Relate to parison cross-section:**

The parison is a thin tube of outer diameter $D_p$ and wall thickness $t_p$:

$$A_{cs} = \pi D_p t_p$$

**Step 5 – Volume balance (parison = bottle wall):**

$$A_{cs} \cdot L_p = A_{wall} \cdot h_{equiv}$$

$$L_p = \frac{A_{wall} \cdot h_{equiv}}{A_{cs}}$$

Substituting the simplified wall area:

$$L_p = \frac{\pi d_o t \cdot h_{equiv}}{\pi D_p t_p}$$

**Step 6 – Final form:**

$$\boxed{L_p = \frac{d_o \cdot t \cdot h_{equiv}}{D_p \cdot t_p}}$$

> **Physical Meaning:** The parison length scales linearly with bottle dimensions and inversely with parison geometry. This is critical for **parison programming** in continuous EBM machines.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 – Compression Mold Clamping Force

**Given:**
- Projected cavity area $A_{cav}$
- Molding pressure (cavity pressure) $P_{mold}$

**Required:** Clamping force $F_c$ needed to keep the mold closed.

**Step 1 – Pressure acts on the projected area:**

The cavity pressure exerts a separating force on the mold halves equal to the pressure times the projected area.

$$F_{separating} = P_{mold} \cdot A_{cav}$$

**Step 2 – Clamping force must balance this separating force (with safety factor):**

$$F_c \geq P_{mold} \cdot A_{cav}$$

Including a safety factor $n$ (typically $n = 1.2$ to $1.5$):

$$\boxed{F_c = n \cdot P_{mold} \cdot A_{cav}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU style):**

A SMC compression mold has a projected area of $0.05 \text{ m}^2$. The cavity pressure is **8 MPa**. Calculate the required clamping force with a safety factor of **1.25**.

$$F_c = 1.25 \times 8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \times 0.05 \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_c = 1.25 \times 400{,}000 \text{ N}$$

$$F_c = 500{,}000 \text{ N} = 500 \text{ kN}$$

> **Note:** This 500 kN is within the typical capacity of a **500-ton hydraulic press** commonly used in KTU laboratory setups.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Process Selection Justification

| Engineering Requirement | Recommended Process | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Hollow, sealed, lightweight container (e.g., shampoo bottle)** | Blow Molding (EBM/IBM) | Inherent hollow geometry, high production rate, low scrap |
| **Large 5000 L water tank, seamless, low volume** | Rotational Molding | Seamless hollow part, low tooling cost, stress-free |
| **Disposable cup, thin wall, low cost** | Thermoforming | Short cycle, cheap tooling, large area |
| **High-strength electrical switchgear (flame retardant)** | Compression Molding (Phenolic) | Thermoset, high $T_g$, dimensional stability, insulation |
| **Electronic IC encapsulation (delicate wire bonds)** | Transfer Molding (EMC) | Low pressure on inserts, no flash, repeatable |
| **Large PU elastomer roller, prototype batch** | Casting (PU) | Low pressure, simple tooling, large parts possible |
| **Brake pad / friction material** | Compression Molding | High $T$, high pressure, fiber-reinforced thermoset |
| **Acrylic (PMMA) display panel** | Casting (cell cast) | Optical clarity, no internal stress |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "which process for product X?", always cite **(a) material class, (b) geometry, (c) volume, (d) tolerance/cost** in your justification to score full marks.

---

### 3.4 Process Capability Comparison Matrix (Numerical)

| Parameter | Blow Molding | Rotomolding | Thermoforming | Compression | Transfer | Casting |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wall thickness range (mm)** | 0.3 – 6 | 3 – 25 | 0.1 – 6 | 1 – 50 | 1 – 25 | 1 – 100 |
| **Tolerance (mm)** | ±0.1 – ±0.3 | ±0.4 – ±0.8 | ±0.2 – ±0.5 | ±0.05 – ±0.2 | ±0.05 – ±0.2 | ±0.5 – ±1.0 |
| **Cycle time (s)** | 10 – 60 | 1800 – 3600 | 5 – 30 | 60 – 600 | 60 – 300 | 600 – 7200 |
| **Tooling cost** | High | Low | Low–Medium | High | High | Low |
| **Material waste** | Low (flash) | None | High (trim) | Low | Medium (runner) | None |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow – Polymer Product Manufacturing Process Classification

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Polymer Raw Material] --> B{Material State?}
    
    B -->|Thermoplastic Pellets| C[Melt Processing Line]
    B -->|Thermoplastic Sheet| D[Sheet Forming Line]
    B -->|Thermoplastic Powder| E[Powder Processing Line]
    B -->|Thermoset Granular| F[Press Curing Line]
    B -->|Liquid Resin| G[Liquid Casting Line]
    B -->|Elastomer Compound| H[Vulcanization Line]
    
    C -->|Melt + Air| P1[Blow Molding]
    C -->|Melt + Injection| P2[Injection Molding]
    
    D -->|Vacuum/Pressure| P3[Thermoforming]
    
    E -->|Biaxial Rotation| P4[Rotational Molding]
    
    F -->|Direct Heat+Pressure| P5[Compression Molding]
    F -->|Preheated Transfer| P6[Transfer Molding]
    
    G -->|Gravity Pour| P7[Casting]
    G -->|Centrifuge| P8[Centrifugal Casting]
    
    H -->|Open Mold Cure| P9[Rubber Molding]
    H -->|Transfer Pot| P10[Rubber Transfer]
    
    P1 --> O1[Hollow Containers / Bottles]
    P3 --> O2[Packaging / Panels]
    P4 --> O3[Tanks / Large Hollow Parts]
    P5 --> O4[Structural Parts / SMC Panels]
    P6 --> O5[Encapsulated Electronics]
    P7 --> O6[Rollers / Sheets / Prototypes]
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O5 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style O6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Interpretation:** The diagram shows the complete decision tree for selecting a polymer product manufacturing process. It begins with **raw material state** (KTU CO3: process selection), proceeds through the **process family** (KTU CO2: process knowledge), and ends with **typical products** (KTU CO4: industrial application).

---

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram – Compression Molding Cycle (KTU High-Yield Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Charge Placement<br/>in Open Mold] --> B[Mold Closing<br/>Top Halve Descends]
    B --> C[Pressure Application<br/>10-100 MPa]
    C --> D[Heating and Curing<br/>150-200 C]
    D --> E[Pressure Hold<br/>Cure Completion]
    E --> F[Mold Opening<br/>Top Halve Lifts]
    F --> G[Part Ejection<br/>Using Ejector Pins]
    G --> H[Trimming and Inspection]
    H --> A
    
    style A fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Engineering Insight:** The four shaded blocks (orange) represent the **pressurized, heated phase** — this is the actual shaping time and is the primary contributor to **cycle time** $t_{cyc}$. The two green blocks are auxiliary operations that can be parallelized for productivity.

---

### 4.3 Rotational Molding Block Architecture (KTU High-Yield)

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1[Charging Station<br/>Powder Loaded] --> R2[Mold Enters Oven]
    R2 --> R3[Biaxial Rotation Begins<br/>2 axes simultaneously]
    R3 --> R4[Polymer Melts and Coats<br/>Inner Mold Wall]
    R4 --> R5[Mold Exits Oven]
    R5 --> R6[Cooling Chamber<br/>Air or Water Spray]
    R6 --> R7[Solidification Phase]
    R7 --> R8[Mold Unloading]
    R8 --> R9[Demolding Station]
    R9 --> R1
    
    style R3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R4 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R6 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R7 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Insight:** The unique feature of rotomolding is the **biaxial rotation** (Step R3) which eliminates weld lines and produces seamless, stress-free parts. The total cycle time is dominated by **oven residence** + **cooling residence**, which is why rotomolding is reserved for low-to-medium production volumes.

---

### 4.4 Thermoforming Process Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1[Sheet Clamping<br/>in Frame] --> T2[Heating Zone<br/>IR or Convection]
    T2 --> T3[Sheet Reaches<br/>Forming Temperature]
    T3 --> T4[Mold Moves to Sheet]
    T4 --> T5[Vacuum/Pressure Applied<br/>0 to 0.3 MPa]
    T5 --> T6[Sheet Conforms to Mold]
    T6 --> T7[Cooling in Contact]
    T7 --> T8[Mold Retracts]
    T8 --> T9[Part Trimming and Stack]
    
    style T3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T5 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Note:** Thermoforming is asymmetric (one mold half only), unlike blow molding which uses two halves. This makes thermoforming tooling very economical for large-area parts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*Define blow molding. List its three main variants with one typical product of each.*

**Model Answer:**

> Blow molding is a thermoplastic processing technique in which a heated parison or preform is inflated with compressed air against the walls of a closed, cooled mold to form a hollow product. The three main variants and their products are:
> 1. **Extrusion Blow Molding (EBM):** Used to produce **HDPE milk bottles** from an extruded parison.
> 2. **Injection Blow Molding (IBM):** Used to produce **PET pharmaceutical bottles** with high dimensional accuracy.
> 3. **Stretch Blow Molding (SBM):** Used to produce **PET carbonated drink bottles** with biaxial orientation for improved strength and clarity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Distinguish between compression molding and transfer molding with respect to (a) charge placement and (b) pressure on the cavity.*

**Model Answer:**

> | Aspect | Compression Molding | Transfer Molding |
> |---|---|---|
> | **(a) Charge Placement** | Compound placed **directly into the open mold cavity**. | Compound preheated in a **separate transfer pot** and forced through a sprue into the closed cavity. |
> | **(b) Cavity Pressure** | Pressure is applied to **force material to flow within the cavity itself** from the bottom up. | Pressure is applied in the **transfer pot/plunger**, and material enters the cavity at a **lower, controlled pressure** after plasticating. |
>
> Transfer molding is preferred for parts with **delicate inserts** (e.g., electronic components), while compression molding is preferred for **large, thick, structural parts** (e.g., SMC automotive panels).

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** within each module. Students answer **ONE out of TWO** in the ESE. Below are two parallel 14-mark questions with complete sub-part solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Define rotational molding. With a neat block diagram, explain the steps involved in the rotomolding process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare rotational molding with blow molding on the basis of (i) pressure required, (ii) mold cost, (iii) cycle time, (iv) part thickness uniformity, and (v) typical products. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and Block Diagram (7 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Rotational molding (rotomolding) is a low-pressure, high-temperature polymer processing technique in which a measured charge of polymer powder is placed inside a closed, hollow mold. The mold is rotated biaxially (about two perpendicular axes) inside an oven, allowing the polymer to melt, sinter, and uniformly coat the inner surface of the mold. After sufficient heating, the mold is transferred to a cooling chamber where the polymer solidifies. The part is then demolded.

> **Steps (4 marks) – matching the diagram in Section 4.3:**
> 1. **Charging (1 mark):** A pre-weighed quantity of polymer powder (e.g., LLDPE, MDPE, PVC plastisol, nylon) is loaded into the open half of the mold. The mold is then closed and clamped.
> 2. **Heating and Rotation (1 mark):** The mold is transferred to a preheated oven (typically 200–350 °C) and rotated biaxially. Common ratios are **4:1 (major to minor axis)**. The polymer powder melts and progressively fuses onto the inner wall.
> 3. **Cooling (1 mark):** The mold is moved to a cooling chamber (forced air or water spray) while rotation continues. The polymer solidifies into a uniform wall.
> 4. **Demolding (1 mark):** The mold is opened, and the finished part is removed. The mold is recharged and the cycle repeats.

**Block Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.3 (Rotational Molding Block Architecture) of these notes.

---

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks – 1.4 marks per key point, full table):**

| Parameter | Rotational Molding | Blow Molding |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Pressure Required** | Essentially **gravitational / zero external pressure**; powder fuses by gravity and surface tension. | **Compressed air at 0.3 – 1.0 MPa** to inflate the parison. |
| **(ii) Mold Cost** | **Low** (thin sheet metal molds, no high-pressure clamping). | **High** (CNC-machined steel/aluminum molds, blow stations). |
| **(iii) Cycle Time** | **Long (30 – 60 minutes)** due to oven and cooling residence. | **Short (10 – 60 seconds)** per part. |
| **(iv) Part Thickness Uniformity** | **Excellent and easily controllable** by charge weight; no weld lines. | **Variable**; requires parison programming for uniformity. |
| **(v) Typical Products** | Large hollow tanks (up to 50,000 L), kayaks, toys, road barriers. | PET bottles, HDPE containers, fuel tanks (mid-size). |

> **Examiner's note:** Students often confuse "no pressure" with "no force" in rotomolding. Be clear that there is **no externally applied shaping pressure**; shaping occurs via gravity and centrifugal force from rotation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Define thermoforming. Explain the vacuum thermoforming process with a labelled schematic. List any four thermoplastic materials commonly thermoformed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A compression mold has a projected cavity area of **0.04 m²** and operates at a cavity pressure of **10 MPa**.
