Calculate: (i) the required clamping force, and (ii) the parison length needed to produce a bottle of diameter 0.1 m, height 0.25 m, and wall thickness 2 mm, given a parison outer diameter of 25 mm and wall thickness of 4 mm. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition, Vacuum Thermoforming, and Materials (7 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Thermoforming is a secondary thermoplastic processing technique in which a thermoplastic sheet is heated above its forming temperature ($T_g$ or $T_m$), then forced against a single-sided mold using vacuum, pressure, or mechanical force, and cooled to retain the molded shape.

> **Vacuum Thermoforming Process (3 marks):**
> 1. A thermoplastic sheet is clamped in a frame above the mold.
> 2. The sheet is heated (infrared heaters) until it sags uniformly — confirming forming temperature.
> 3. The mold is raised into the heated sheet (or the sheet is lowered onto the mold).
> 4. A vacuum is applied beneath the sheet, drawing atmospheric pressure (~0.1 MPa) on the upper side. This pressure differential forces the sheet to conform to the mold surface.
> 5. The formed part is cooled in contact with the mold (water channels in the mold).
> 6. The mold retracts, the clamp releases, and the formed sheet is removed for trimming.

> **Schematic Reference:** Section 4.4 of these notes (Thermoforming Block Architecture).

> **Four Thermoplastic Materials (2 marks – 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Polystyrene (PS)** – disposable cups, yogurt containers.
> 2. **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)** – blister packaging, clamshells.
> 3. **Polypropylene (PP)** – refrigerator liners, automotive interior parts.
> 4. **Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET)** – clear packaging trays, blister packs.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Clamping Force Calculation (3 marks):**

Using the formula from Section 3.2:

$$F_c = n \cdot P_{mold} \cdot A_{cav}$$

Assuming safety factor $n = 1.25$ (standard KTU assumption):

$$F_c = 1.25 \times 10 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \times 0.04 \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_c = 1.25 \times 400{,}000 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_c = 500{,}000 \text{ N} = 500 \text{ kN}}$$

This corresponds to a **500 kN (≈ 50-ton) hydraulic press**.

---

**(ii) Parison Length Calculation (4 marks):**

Using the derived formula from Section 3.1:

$$L_p = \frac{d_o \cdot t \cdot h_{equiv}}{D_p \cdot t_p}$$

Given:
- $d_o = 0.1$ m (bottle outer diameter)
- $t = 0.002$ m (bottle wall thickness = 2 mm)
- $h_{equiv} = 0.25$ m (bottle height)
- $D_p = 0.025$ m (parison outer diameter = 25 mm)
- $t_p = 0.004$ m (parison wall thickness = 4 mm)

Substituting:

$$L_p = \frac{0.1 \times 0.002 \times 0.25}{0.025 \times 0.004}$$

$$L_p = \frac{0.00005}{0.0001}$$

$$\boxed{L_p = 0.5 \text{ m} = 500 \text{ mm}}$$

> **Physical interpretation:** The parison extruder must deliver 500 mm of parison tube before the mold closes, blow pin inserts, and compressed air inflates the parison to fill the mold cavity.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Wrong Units in Numerical Answers:** Writing "500 kN" instead of "500,000 N" (or omitting units entirely) — **lose 1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing Transfer Molding with Injection Molding:** Transfer molding uses **pot/plunger** and is for **thermosets**; injection molding uses a **screw/barrel** and is for **thermoplastics**. Mixing these definitions loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Omitting Justification in Process Selection:** Just naming a process without stating **material + geometry + volume** loses 2 marks (KTU 2024 emphasizes "Apply" level of Bloom's taxonomy).
> 4. **Missing the "Biaxial" in Rotomolding:** Writing only "the mold rotates" without specifying **two perpendicular axes** loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting Safety Factor in Clamping Force:** A 1.25 safety factor is **mandatory** in KTU-style problems; omitting it loses 1 mark.
> 6. **In Thermoforming Definitions:** Not specifying that the sheet is heated **above $T_g$ or $T_m$** (depending on amorphous/semi-crystalline) is a partial definition; full marks require this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Blow Molding:** Hollow thermoplastic parts; uses compressed air to inflate a parison; three variants: **EBM, IBM, SBM**.
- **Rotational Molding:** Biaxial rotation, zero external pressure, long cycle, low tooling cost; **seamless** large hollow parts; powder-based.
- **Thermoforming:** Single-sided mold, vacuum/pressure, **above $T_g$ or $T_m$**, large-area parts, asymmetric tooling.
- **Compression Molding:** High pressure + heat; thermosets and rubbers; **charge directly in cavity**; SMC/BMC, phenolic, melamine parts.
- **Transfer Molding:** Preheated in **transfer pot**, then injected into closed cavity; for **delicate inserts and intricate parts**.
- **Casting:** No pressure; liquid resin poured and cured; PU, epoxy, PMMA sheets; large parts and prototypes.
- **Process Selection Decision:** Always consider **material, geometry, volume, tolerance, cost**.
- **Key Formulae:** Parison length $L_p = \frac{d_o t h}{D_p t_p}$; Clamping force $F_c = n P A$; Cycle time $t_{cyc} = t_c + t_{cur} + t_o$.
- **Industry Relevance:** PET bottles, SMC car panels, rotomolded water tanks, blister packs, IC encapsulation, PU rollers.
- **Standard KTU Numbers to Memorize:** Blow pressure 0.3–1.0 MPa; Compression pressure 10–100 MPa; Compression temp 150–200 °C; Rotomolding oven 200–350 °C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When answering "define and explain" questions, always follow the three-part structure: **(1) Definition (formal), (2) Process steps (sequence), (3) Typical applications / products**. This structure consistently scores full marks in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Forming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Methods of Forming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Methods of Forming** in polymer processing refers to a comprehensive family of manufacturing techniques wherein a polymeric material (in the form of powder, granules, sheet, melt, or dispersion) is shaped into a finished or semi-finished product of desired geometry by the application of **heat**, **pressure**, and **mechanical force**, with or without a phase transition. These methods are classified under **primary shaping processes** and form the backbone of the plastics and rubber product manufacturing industry.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCPOT503, Module 3), the methods of forming encompass the transformation of thermoplastic and thermosetting raw materials into usable products through processes such as **Compression Molding**, **Transfer Molding**, **Injection Molding**, **Blow Molding**, **Thermoforming**, **Rotational Molding**, and **Casting**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students are expected to understand the *principle of operation, equipment, process parameters, advantages, limitations, and applications* of each forming method. Marks are frequently allotted for neat labelled diagrams and comparative tables.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef in a bakery** with multiple cooking techniques available:

| Bakery Analogy | Polymer Forming Method | Why it Works |
|---|---|---|
| Pressing dough into a cookie cutter | **Compression Molding** | Direct pressure shapes the material in a closed mold |
| Pouring batter into a waffle iron | **Transfer Molding** | Material transferred into a closed cavity |
| Injecting cream into a pastry bag | **Injection Molding** | Molten polymer is forcibly injected under high pressure |
| Blowing glass with breath | **Blow Molding** | A soft parison is expanded by air pressure |
| Shaping melted cheese on bread | **Thermoforming** | A heated sheet is pulled/vacuumed over a mold |
| Spinning a rotisserie chicken | **Rotational Molding** | Powder tumbles inside a rotating heated mold |
| Pouring jelly into a mould to set | **Casting** | Liquid resin is poured and allowed to cure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Every polymer forming method essentially answers the same four questions:
> 1. **How** will heat be supplied to the polymer?
> 2. **How** will the polymer be forced into the desired shape?
> 3. **How** will the shape be solidified (cooling/curing)?
> 4. **How** will the part be ejected without damage?

## 1.3 Classification of Forming Methods

The methods of forming can be systematically classified as follows:

### A. Based on Material State During Forming
- **Solid-State Forming:** Thermoforming, cold forming
- **Melt-State Forming:** Injection molding, extrusion, blow molding
- **Liquid-State Forming:** Casting, RIM (Reaction Injection Molding)
- **Powder-State Forming:** Rotational molding, powder coating

### B. Based on Pressure Application
- **High-Pressure Processes** (> 7 MPa): Injection molding, compression molding
- **Low-Pressure Processes** (< 1 MPa): Thermoforming, rotational molding
- **No-Pressure Processes:** Casting

### C. Based on Mould Type
- **Open Mould:** Hand lay-up (composites), some casting methods
- **Closed Mould:** Injection, compression, transfer, blow molding

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

For Module 3 calculations, the following standard metrics are used:

- **Typical Compression Molding Pressure:** **7 – 35 MPa** (100 – 5000 psi)
- **Typical Injection Molding Pressure:** **70 – 140 MPa** (10,000 – 20,000 psi)
- **Typical Blow Molding Pressure:** **0.4 – 1.0 MPa** (60 – 150 psi)
- **Rotational Molding Speed:** **4 – 20 rpm** (variable axis)
- **Mold Cavity Temperature (Thermoforming):** **80 °C – 150 °C**
- **Standard Platen Sizes:** 200 mm × 200 mm to 1500 mm × 2000 mm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Density of common polymers (must be memorized for KTU problems):**
> - Polyethylene (PE): **910 – 960 kg/m³**
> - Polypropylene (PP): **905 kg/m³**
> - Polystyrene (PS): **1050 kg/m³**
> - PVC: **1380 – 1400 kg/m³**
> - PMMA: **1180 kg/m³**
> - Polycarbonate (PC): **1200 kg/m³**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Compression Molding

### Principle
A pre-weighed charge of thermosetting polymer (or thermoplastic in SMC/BMC form) is placed directly in the open cavity of a heated mold. The mold is then closed, applying **pressure** and **heat** simultaneously. The material flows, fills the cavity, cures (thermosets) or solidifies (thermoplastics), and the part is ejected.

### Operational Logic Steps
1. **Pre-heating / Pre-forming:** The powder or preform is preheated to reduce cycle time.
2. **Charge Placement:** A measured charge (by weight or volume) is placed in the lower mold half.
3. **Mold Closing:** The press brings the mold halves together under controlled velocity.
4. **Pressure Application:** Hydraulic pressure (typically **7 – 35 MPa**) is applied; the material flows and fills the cavity.
5. **Curing / Cooling:** Thermosets cure (crosslink); thermoplastics solidify.
6. **Mold Opening & Ejection:** The press opens; ejector pins push the part out.

### Material Flow During Compression
The flow behavior is governed by the **Spencer-Denny equation** and modified **Cox-Merkz rule** for polymers. The compressive flow resembles a parallel-plate plastometer:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{app} = \frac{v \cdot h}{H^2}$$

where $v$ is the closing velocity and $H$ is the instantaneous gap height.

## 2.2 Transfer Molding

### Principle
A variation of compression molding where the polymer charge is first heated in a **transfer pot** (separate from the mold cavity) and then forced through a **sprue and runner system** into a closed cavity. Used primarily for thermosets with intricate geometries and embedded inserts.

### Sub-Types
- **Pot Transfer Molding:** The transfer pot is an integral part of the mold.
- **Plunger Transfer Molding:** Uses a separate plunger and transfer cylinder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why transfer molding?** In compression molding, the flow distance is large for deep parts, causing non-uniform cure. Transfer molding reduces flow distance, giving better dimensional control.

## 2.3 Injection Molding

### Principle
Thermoplastic (or specially formulated thermoset) pellets are melted in a heated barrel, then **injected under high pressure** into a closed, water-cooled mold cavity. After solidification, the mold opens and the part is ejected.

### Cycle Stages
1. **Plastication (Melting):** Screw rotates, polymer melts and accumulates in front of screw.
2. **Injection (Filling):** Screw acts as a plunger, forcing melt into cavity.
3. **Packing / Holding:** Pressure maintained to compensate for shrinkage.
4. **Cooling:** Part solidifies inside cavity.
5. **Ejection:** Mold opens, ejector pins release the part.

### Key Engineering Relationships
- **Clamping Force:** $F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \times A_{projected}$
- **Shot Weight:** $W_{shot} = \rho \times V_{shot} \times n_{cav}$
- **Injection Pressure (Poiseuille flow):** $P_{inj} = \dfrac{8 \eta L Q}{\pi r^4}$

## 2.4 Blow Molding

### Principle
A molten **parison** (hollow tube of polymer) is enclosed in a cooled mold, then inflated with compressed air to take the shape of the cavity. Used for hollow plastic products (bottles, containers, tanks).

### Sub-Types
- **Extrusion Blow Molding (EBM):** Parison is extruded, then clamped and blown.
- **Injection Blow Molding (IBM):** Parison is injection-molded onto a core, transferred, then blown.
- **Stretch Blow Molding (SBM):** Axial + biaxial stretching for PET bottles.

### Wall Thickness Equation
For a parison of diameter $D_p$ and length $L_p$ being inflated to a bottle of diameter $D_b$ and length $L_b$:

$$t_{bottle} = t_{parison} \times \left(\frac{D_p}{D_b}\right) \times \left(\frac{L_p}{L_b}\right)$$

## 2.5 Thermoforming

### Principle
A thermoplastic sheet is heated to its **forming temperature** (above $T_g$ for amorphous, near $T_m$ for semi-crystalline), then forced against a mold surface by **vacuum**, **pressure**, or **mechanical plug assist**.

### Forming Techniques
- **Vacuum Forming:** Vacuum applied behind the sheet.
- **Pressure Forming:** Compressed air pushes the sheet.
- **Mechanical Forming:** Direct plug pushes the sheet.

### Sag Phenomenon
When heated, the sheet sags under gravity before forming. The sag depth is:

$$h_{sag} = \frac{\rho g L^4}{32 E I}$$

## 2.6 Rotational Molding

### Principle
A measured charge of polymer powder is placed in a hollow, thin-walled mold. The mold is heated while being **rotated biaxially** (about two perpendicular axes). The powder tumbles, melts, coats the interior surface, and fuses. The mold is then cooled and the part removed.

### Centripetal Force on Powder
The force keeping the powder against the wall:

$$F_c = m \omega^2 r$$

where $\omega$ is the angular velocity and $r$ is the radial distance from the rotation axis.

## 2.7 Casting

### Principle
A liquid polymer system (resin + hardener for thermosets, or molten polymer for thermoplastics like PMMA) is poured into a mold and allowed to **cure or solidify** without applied pressure. Used for thick sheets, rods, encapsulations, and acrylic (PMMA) products.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **exam-critical formulas**. Master these for guaranteed marks in KTU 2024 Scheme questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clamping Force (Injection Molding) | $F_c = P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$ | $P_{cav}$ in Pa, $A_{proj}$ in m², $F_c$ in N | Press selection |
| 2 | Shot Weight | $W_s = \rho \cdot V_s \cdot n_c$ | $\rho$ in kg/m³, $V_s$ in m³, $n_c$ is cavity count | Material cost, machine size |
| 3 | Injection Pressure (Poiseuille) | $P_{inj} = \dfrac{8 \eta L Q}{\pi r^4}$ | $\eta$ in Pa·s, $L$ in m, $Q$ in m³/s, $r$ in m | Pressure loss in runners |
| 4 | Cooling Time (Simplified) | $t_c = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_e - T_w}\right)$ | $s$ = wall thickness, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, $T$ in K | Cycle time estimation |
| 5 | Parison Wall Thickness | $t_b = t_p \cdot \dfrac{D_p}{D_b} \cdot \dfrac{L_p}{L_b}$ | All lengths in mm | Blow molding design |
| 6 | Sag Depth (Thermoforming) | $h_{sag} = \dfrac{\rho g L^4}{32 E I}$ | $E$ in Pa, $I$ in m⁴, $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Sheet sagging prediction |
| 7 | Centripetal Force (Rotomolding) | $F_c = m \omega^2 r$ | $\omega$ in rad/s, $r$ in m | Powder adhesion check |
| 8 | Heat Conduction (Fourier) | $q = \dfrac{k A \Delta T}{x}$ | $k$ in W/m·K, $A$ in m², $\Delta T$ in K, $x$ in m | Mold heating/cooling |
| 9 | Compressive Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $F$ in N, $A$ in m², $\sigma$ in Pa | Press force sizing |
| 10 | Bulk Modulus (Curing Shrinkage) | $\Delta V = V_0 (1 - \beta \Delta P)$ | $\beta$ in Pa⁻¹, $\Delta P$ in Pa | Shrinkage compensation |
| 11 | Power for Plastication | $P = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + Q_{losses}$ | $P$ in W, $\dot{m}$ in kg/s, $c_p$ in J/kg·K | Heater sizing |
| 12 | Reynolds Number (Polymer Flow) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\eta}$ | $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter | Flow regime check |
| 13 | Apparent Shear Rate (Compression) | $\dot{\gamma}_{app} = \dfrac{v h}{H^2}$ | $v$ = closing speed, $h$ = charge height | Flow characterization |
| 14 | Curing Time (Thermoset) | $t_{cure} = \dfrac{t_{gel}}{\text{Cure kinetic function}(T)}$ | $T$ in K | Mold dwell time |
| 15 | Cycle Time (Total) | $t_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{eject}$ | All in seconds | Production planning |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used daily in the polymer industry for **process design** (selecting press size), **cycle time optimization** (reducing cooling time via conformal cooling), **defect diagnosis** (calculating expected shrinkage), and **cost estimation** (shot weight × material cost per kg).

## 2.9 Comparative Analysis: Why Different Methods?

| Method | Best For | Pressure | Cycle Time | Tooling Cost | Typical Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Compression | Large, bulky thermoset parts | High | Long | Low–Medium | Electrical switchgear, brake pads |
| Transfer | Intricate thermosets with inserts | High | Medium | Medium | Connectors, encapsulated coils |
| Injection | High-volume thermoplastics | Very High | Short | High | Toys, bottle caps, housings |
| Blow | Hollow containers | Low | Medium | Medium | Bottles, jerry cans, tanks |
| Thermoforming | Large thin-wall parts | Low | Short | Low | Trays, refrigerator liners |
| Rotational | Large hollow seamless parts | None | Long | Low | Water tanks, kayaks, bins |
| Casting | Thick clear sheets, encapsulations | None | Long | Very Low | Acrylic sheets, resin art, prototypes |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme marks distribution:** Derivations and numerical problems are usually **7 marks** in Part B. The following three derivations appear most frequently in KTU model papers and previous year questions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Clamping Force in Injection Molding

### Statement
**Derive the expression for the clamping force required in an injection molding machine to prevent mold opening during injection.**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the cavity pressure.**
When the polymer melt is injected into the cavity, it exerts a hydrostatic pressure $P_{cav}$ on the mold walls. This pressure is highest near the gate and decreases along the flow path due to viscous losses.

**Step 2 — Define the projected area.**
The projected area $A_{proj}$ is the area of the shadow (silhouette) of the cavity when projected onto a plane parallel to the mold parting line. It is the area on which the internal pressure acts to push the mold halves apart.

**Step 3 — Apply force balance on the mold half.**
For the mold to remain closed, the clamping force $F_{clamp}$ applied by the press must balance the separating force exerted by the polymer.

$$\sum F_{vertical} = 0$$

$$F_{clamp} - F_{separating} = 0$$

$$F_{clamp} = F_{separating}$$

**Step 4 — Express the separating force.**
The separating force equals the cavity pressure times the projected area:

$$F_{separating} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$$

**Step 5 — Combine to obtain the result.**

$$\boxed{F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj}}$$

**Step 6 — Add a safety factor (industrial practice).**
In practice, a safety factor of $1.1$ to $1.3$ is applied to account for pressure spikes:

$$F_{clamp,design} = (1.1 \text{ to } 1.3) \times P_{cav,max} \times A_{proj}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A polypropylene mould has a projected cavity area of **$400 \text{ cm}^2$**. The average cavity pressure during filling is **$30 \text{ MPa}$**. Calculate the minimum clamping force required. Take safety factor = **1.2**.

**Solution:**

$$A_{proj} = 400 \text{ cm}^2 = 400 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.04 \text{ m}^2$$

$$P_{cav} = 30 \text{ MPa} = 30 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$F_{clamp,design} = 1.2 \times P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$$

$$F_{clamp,design} = 1.2 \times (30 \times 10^6) \times 0.04$$

$$F_{clamp,design} = 1.2 \times 1.2 \times 10^6$$

$$\boxed{F_{clamp,design} = 1.44 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 1440 \text{ kN} = 144 \text{ tonnes}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shot Weight Calculation for Multi-Cavity Mould

### Statement
**Derive the expression for shot weight in a multi-cavity injection mould and calculate the material required per shot.**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Volume of one cavity.**
The volume of polymer required to fill one cavity (part + runner + gate) is denoted $V_c$ in m³.

**Step 2 — Total volume per shot.**
For a mould with $n_c$ identical cavities plus a runner system of volume $V_r$:

$$V_{shot} = n_c \times V_c + V_r$$

**Step 3 — Convert volume to mass using density.**
The mass of polymer per shot is the volume times the density of the polymer melt $\rho$ (or solid density for material accounting):

$$W_{shot} = \rho \times V_{shot}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 2 into Step 3.**

$$\boxed{W_{shot} = \rho \times (n_c \cdot V_c + V_r)}$$

**Step 5 — For a single-cavity mould** ($n_c = 1$, $V_r$ still present):

$$W_{shot} = \rho \times (V_c + V_r)$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A 4-cavity injection mould produces a polypropylene spoon. Each cavity has a volume of **$8 \text{ cm}^3$**. The runner system volume is **$6 \text{ cm}^3$**. Calculate the shot weight. Take $\rho_{PP} = 905 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

**Solution:**

$$V_{shot} = (4 \times 8) + 6 = 32 + 6 = 38 \text{ cm}^3 = 38 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

$$W_{shot} = 905 \times 38 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$W_{shot} = 34{,}390 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{W_{shot} = 0.0344 \text{ kg} = 34.39 \text{ g per shot}}$$

**Useful Part Calculation:**

$$W_{part} = \rho \times V_c = 905 \times 8 \times 10^{-6} = 7.24 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} = 7.24 \text{ g per spoon}$$

**Number of shots per kg of PP:**

$$N_{shots} = \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{34.39 \text{ g/shot}} \approx 29.08 \text{ shots/kg}$$

**Number of spoons per kg:**

$$N_{spoons} = 4 \times 29.08 = 116.3 \text{ spoons/kg}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Wall Thickness of Blow-Molded Bottle

### Statement
**Derive the relationship between the wall thickness of a blow-molded bottle and the parison dimensions.**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Conservation of mass (volume).**
The polymer material in the parison is the same as that in the final bottle. Neglecting any sag or thinning during transfer:

$$V_{parison} = V_{bottle}$$

**Step 2 — Express the parison volume.**
The parison is a thin-walled hollow cylinder of diameter $D_p$, length $L_p$, and average wall thickness $t_p$:

$$V_{parison} = \pi \cdot D_p \cdot L_p \cdot t_p$$

**Step 3 — Express the bottle volume (cylindrical approximation).**
The bottle is approximated as a hollow cylinder of diameter $D_b$, length $L_b$, and wall thickness $t_b$:

$$V_{bottle} = \pi \cdot D_b \cdot L_b \cdot t_b$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two volumes.**

$$\pi \cdot D_p \cdot L_p \cdot t_p = \pi \cdot D_b \cdot L_b \cdot t_b$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the bottle wall thickness.**

$$\boxed{t_b = t_p \times \frac{D_p}{D_b} \times \frac{L_p}{L_b}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A parison of diameter **$25 \text{ mm}$**, length **$200 \text{ mm}$**, and wall thickness **$3 \text{ mm}$** is blow-molded into a bottle of diameter **$100 \text{ mm}$** and length **$180 \text{ mm}$**. Calculate the final bottle wall thickness.

**Solution:**

$$t_b = 3 \times \frac{25}{100} \times \frac{200}{180}$$

$$t_b = 3 \times 0.25 \times 1.1111$$

$$\boxed{t_b \approx 0.833 \text{ mm}}$$

**Interpretation:** The bottle wall is thinner than the parison because the polymer is stretched over a much larger surface area during inflation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Forming Method

> [!NOTE]
> This decision matrix is **exam-favorite** for KTU. Practice drawing it from memory.

| Selection Criterion | Compression | Transfer | Injection | Blow | Thermoforming | Rotational | Casting |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Material type | TS, SMC, BMC | TS | TP, TS | TP (hollow) | TP (sheet) | TP powder | TS, acrylic |
| Part size | Medium–Large | Small–Medium | Small–Large | Small–Large | Large | Large | Small–Large |
| Wall thickness | Thick | Medium | Uniform thin | Thin | Thin | Thick | Variable |
| Production volume | Low–Medium | Medium | Very High | High | Low–Medium | Low–Medium | Very Low |
| Tooling cost | Low | Medium | Very High | Medium | Low | Low | Very Low |
| Surface finish | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Good (one side) | Good | Excellent |
| Insert molding | Difficult | Excellent | Good | Not possible | Not possible | Not possible | Possible |
| Dimensional accuracy | Good | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Moderate | Moderate | Good |

**Legend:** TS = Thermoset, TP = Thermoplastic, SMC = Sheet Moulding Compound, BMC = Bulk Moulding Compound.

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Differentiate between Compression Molding and Transfer Molding
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Compression Molding | Transfer Molding |
|---|---|---|
| **Material placement** | Charge placed directly in the open cavity | Charge placed in a separate transfer pot |
| **Flow path** | Material flows radially from the center of the cavity | Material flows through a sprue and runners into a closed cavity |
| **Pressure required** | 7 – 35 MPa (lower) | 7 – 70 MPa (slightly higher) |
| **Curing uniformity** | Less uniform; depends on charge placement | More uniform due to pre-heating in transfer pot |
| **Insert molding** | Difficult (inserts may shift) | Easy (inserts placed in closed cavity) |
| **Cycle time** | Longer (curing is heat-transfer limited) | Shorter (pre-heated resin cures faster) |
| **Material waste** | Minimal flash | More waste due to sprue and runner |
| **Applications** | Large flat parts, electrical switchgear | Intricate parts with metal inserts, electronic encapsulations |

---

### Question 2: Explain the Principle of Rotational Molding. List Four Advantages.
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
A measured quantity of polymer powder (typically polyethylene or PVC plastisol) is loaded into a thin-walled, hollow metal mold. The mold is then closed, mounted on a rotating arm, and rotated **biaxially** (about two perpendicular axes simultaneously) at speeds of **4 – 20 rpm**. The mold is heated (typically by hot air or molten salt bath at **250 °C – 350 °C**). As the mold rotates, the powder tumbles, melts, and gradually coats the inner surface in a uniform layer. After complete fusion, the mold is transferred to a cooling chamber, where it is cooled by air and/or water spray. The solidified part is then removed.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Seamless hollow parts** with uniform wall thickness can be produced in a single operation.
2. **Low tooling cost** compared to injection or blow molding (no high-pressure molds required).
3. **No stress** in the finished part, as there is no shear-induced molecular orientation.
4. **Large parts** (up to several meters) can be made, suitable for water tanks, kayaks, and storage bins.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Explain the Injection Molding Process with a Neat Labelled Diagram. Discuss its Advantages, Limitations, and Applications. (7 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Injection molding is a high-volume manufacturing process in which molten thermoplastic (or thermoset) polymer is injected under high pressure into a closed, water-cooled mold cavity, where it solidifies into the desired shape.

**Working Principle (refer to SECTION 4.7 diagram):**
1. **Hopper loading:** Polymer pellets are fed from the hopper into the heated barrel.
2. **Plastication:** The rotating screw (driven by a hydraulic motor) conveys, compresses, and melts the polymer through external heater bands and shear heating.
3. **Injection:** The screw acts as a plunger and pushes the melt through the nozzle into the closed mold cavity under pressure of **70 – 140 MPa**.
4. **Packing/Holding:** Additional melt is packed into the cavity to compensate for volumetric shrinkage during cooling.
5. **Cooling:** Water channels in the mold extract heat; the part solidifies.
6. **Ejection:** The mold opens, and ejector pins push the part out.
7. **Cycle repeats.**

**Labelled Diagram:** *(See Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.7 above)*

**Key Process Parameters:**
- Injection pressure: 70 – 140 MPa
- Clamping force: 500 – 8000 kN
- Barrel temperature: 150 °C – 300 °C
- Mold temperature: 20 °C – 80 °C
- Cycle time: 5 – 120 seconds

**Advantages:**
1. **High production rate** (cycle times of seconds).
2. **Excellent dimensional accuracy** and repeatability.
3. **Complex geometries** with undercuts, threads, and inserts are possible.
4. **Minimal post-processing** (no trimming of flash in most cases).
5. **Wide material range** — thermoplastics, thermosets, elastomers, composites.
6. **Low labor cost** due to automation.

**Limitations:**
1. **High initial tooling cost** (molds can cost ₹5 lakh – ₹50 lakh).
2. **High machine cost** and energy consumption.
3. **Not economical for small production runs** (typically < 10,000 parts).
4. **Wall thickness limitations** (very thick parts are difficult to fill without voids).
5. **Design constraints** on draft angles, radii, and parting lines.

**Applications:**
- Consumer goods: bucket, mug, chair, toys (Legos)
- Automotive: dashboards, bumpers, interior trim
- Medical: syringes, petri dishes, surgical instrument handles
- Electronics: mobile phone housings, laptop cases, connectors
- Packaging: bottle caps, closures, containers

---

#### Part (b) — Derive the Expression for Clamping Force in Injection Molding. A Mould has a Projected Area of 320 cm². If the Cavity Pressure is 35 MPa, Calculate the Clamping Force Required. Use a Safety Factor of 1.2. (7 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:** *(See Section 3.1 for the full derivation)*

$$\boxed{F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj}}$$

With safety factor:

$$F_{clamp,design} = SF \times P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$$

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Projected area, $A_{proj} = 320 \text{ cm}^2 = 320 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.032 \text{ m}^2$
- Cavity pressure, $P_{cav} = 35 \text{ MPa} = 35 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- Safety factor, $SF = 1.2$

**Calculation:**

$$F_{clamp,design} = 1.2 \times (35 \times 10^6) \times 0.032$$

$$F_{clamp,design} = 1.2 \times 1.12 \times 10^6$$

$$\boxed{F_{clamp,design} = 1.344 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 1344 \text{ kN} \approx 137 \text{ tonnes}}$$

**Conclusion:** An injection molding machine with a clamping force capacity of at least **1344 kN** (≈ **137 tonnes**) is required to safely produce the part.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — Explain the Blow Molding Process with a Neat Labelled Diagram. Discuss its Types, Advantages, Limitations, and Applications. (7 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Blow molding is a manufacturing process used to produce **hollow plastic parts** by inflating a hot, soft polymer parison (or preform) inside a closed mold using compressed air.

**Process Steps** *(refer to Section 4.4 diagram):*
1. **Melt extrusion:** Polymer is melted in an extruder (for EBM) or injection molded into a preform (for IBM/SBM).
2. **Parison formation:** A hollow tube of molten polymer is extruded vertically.
3. **Mold clamping:** A two-piece mold closes around the parison, pinching one end.
4. **Inflation:** Compressed air (0.4 – 1.0 MPa) is blown through a needle or blow pin, inflating the parison to conform to the mold cavity.
5. **Cooling:** The mold walls (cooled by water) extract heat; the polymer solidifies.
6. **Ejection:** The mold opens, and the hollow part is ejected.
7. **Trimming:** Excess flash is removed.

**Types of Blow Molding:**

| Type | Description | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Extrusion Blow Molding (EBM)** | Parison is continuously extruded, then blown | Milk bottles, detergent containers |
| **Injection Blow Molding (IBM)** | Parison is injection-molded on a core, then transferred and blown | Small bottles, pharmaceutical containers |
| **Stretch Blow Molding (SBM)** | Parison is axially stretched by a stretch rod AND blown — gives biaxial orientation | PET beverage bottles (Coca-Cola, water) |

**Advantages:**
1. **Low mold cost** compared to injection molding.
2. **High production rate** for hollow parts.
3. **Seamless hollow products** with no joints.
4. **Wide range of sizes** (a few ml to several thousand liters).
5. **Biaxial orientation** (in SBM) improves barrier properties and strength.

**Limitations:**
1. Limited to **hollow parts** only.
2. **Wall thickness variation** in deep-draw parts.
3. **Material wastage** from flash and trim.
4. Not suitable for very **complex internal geometries**.

**Applications:**
- Beverage bottles (PET), water jugs
- Fuel tanks (HDPE)
- Toys (hollow dolls, balls)
- Industrial drums and containers
- Medical containers (IV bottles)

---

#### Part (b) — Derive the Expression for Wall Thickness of a Blow-Molded Bottle. A Parison of Diameter 30 mm and Length 250 mm with Wall Thickness 4 mm is Blown into a Bottle of Diameter 110 mm and Length 200 mm. Calculate the Final Wall Thickness. (7 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:** *(See Section 3.3 for the full derivation)*

Applying conservation of volume (mass) of polymer between parison and bottle:

$$V_{parison} = V_{bottle}$$

$$\pi D_p L_p t_p = \pi D_b L_b t_b$$

$$\boxed{t_b = t_p \times \frac{D_p}{D_b} \times \frac{L_p}{L_b}}$$

**Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Parison diameter, $D_p = 30 \text{ mm}$
- Parison length, $L_p = 250 \text{ mm}$
- Parison wall thickness, $t_p = 4 \text{ mm}$
- Bottle diameter, $D_b = 110 \text{ mm}$
- Bottle length, $L_b = 200 \text{ mm}$

**Calculation:**

$$t_b = 4 \times \frac{30}{110} \times \frac{250}{200}$$

$$t_b = 4 \times 0.2727 \times 1.25$$

$$t_b = 4 \times 0.3409$$

$$\boxed{t_b \approx 1.36 \text{ mm}}$$

**Conclusion:** The bottle wall thickness is **1.36 mm**, which is much thinner than the original parison (**4 mm**), as the polymer has been stretched over a larger area. The bottle's diameter is 3.67× the parison diameter, leading to significant thinning.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (Module 3 — Methods of Forming):**
>
> 1. **Missing diagram labels:** A "neat labelled diagram" in KTU 2024 Scheme is worth **2 marks minimum**. If a student draws the diagram without labels (e.g., barrel, screw, hopper, nozzle, mold, ejector pins), the examiner deducts **1–1.5 marks**. **Always label at least 6 components.**
>
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** A very common mistake is using $A_{proj}$ in cm² directly with $P_{cav}$ in MPa without converting to SI units. Result: a clamping force answer that is **off by a factor of 10,000**. Always convert: $\text{cm}^2 \to \text{m}^2$ by multiplying by $10^{-4}$, and $\text{MPa} \to \text{Pa}$ by multiplying by $10^6$.
>
> 3. **Confusing compression and transfer molding:** Students frequently mix up the two and write the wrong principle. Memorize the key difference: **compression = material in cavity; transfer = material in pot, then forced in**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to state the formula before substitution:** KTU examiners want the formula written explicitly before plugging in numbers. A bare calculation with no formula = **–1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the safety factor in clamping force:** If the question says "use safety factor 1.2", not including it = **–1 mark** in Part B.
>
> 6. **Writing one-word answers in Part A:** A 3-mark question needs **at least 4–5 points** in tabular or bulleted form. A one-line answer gets a maximum of **1 mark**.
>
> 7. **Confusing $T_g$ and $T_m$:** In thermoforming, amorphous polymers are heated **above $T_g$** (glass transition), semi-crystalline polymers are heated **near $T_m$** (melting). Wrong temperature cited = **–0.5 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision — read this section 3 times before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Compression Molding:** High pressure, heated mold, thermosets, large parts, low tooling cost.
- ✅ **Transfer Molding:** Material first heated in pot, then forced into closed cavity; good for inserts.
- ✅ **Injection Molding:** Highest pressure (70–140 MPa), fastest cycle, highest tooling cost, complex parts, thermoplastics.
- ✅ **Blow Molding:** Hollow parts (bottles, tanks); types are EBM, IBM, SBM; **conservation of volume** gives wall thickness formula.
- ✅ **Thermoforming:** Heated sheet + vacuum/pressure; large thin-wall parts (trays, refrigerator liners); low tooling cost.
- ✅ **Rotational Molding:** Powder + biaxial rotation + no pressure; large seamless parts (kayaks, tanks); long cycle.
- ✅ **Casting:** Liquid resin poured into mould, no pressure, cures/solidifies; thick clear parts (acrylic sheets).
- ✅ **Master Formula 1:** $F_{clamp} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$ (× safety factor in industry).
- ✅ **Master Formula 2:** $W_{shot} = \rho \times (n_c \cdot V_c + V_r)$.
- ✅ **Master Formula 3:** $t_b = t_p \times \dfrac{D_p}{D_b} \times \dfrac{L_p}{L_b}$ (blow molding).
- ✅ **Master Formula 4:** $h_{sag} = \dfrac{\rho g L^4}{32 E I}$ (thermoforming sheet sag).
- ✅ **Master Formula 5:** $F_c = m \omega^2 r$ (rotational molding centripetal force).
- ✅ **Standard Densities to Memorize:** PE = 910–960, PP = 905, PS = 1050, PVC = 1380, PMMA = 1180, PC = 1200 kg/m³.
- ✅ **Unit Conversions:** $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$; $1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ Pa}$; $1 \text{ tonne} = 9.81 \text{ kN}$.
- ✅ **Cycle Time Components:** $t_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{eject}$.
- ✅ **Always convert all quantities to SI units** (m, kg, s, Pa, N, J) before any calculation.
- ✅ **Always state the formula, then substitute, then solve** — KTU valuation key requires this structure.
- ✅ **Always draw a labelled diagram** for process-based questions (worth 2–3 marks).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermoforming Machinery
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermoforming Machinery

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermoforming** is a secondary thermoplastic processing technique in which a flat polymer sheet (typically 0.025 mm to 6.35 mm thick) is heated above its glass transition temperature ($T_g$) for amorphous polymers or near its melting point ($T_m$) for semi-crystalline polymers, then forced by pneumatic (vacuum/pressure) or mechanical (plug/die) means to conform to the contours of a mold, where it is subsequently cooled, solidified, and trimmed to yield the finished product.

**Thermoforming Machinery** refers to the integrated mechanical assembly comprising the sheet feeding/loading station, clamping frame, heating plenum, mold carriage, vacuum and pressure generation systems, cooling arrangement, trimming station, and Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) based automation unit — all orchestrated to convert a flat thermoplastic sheet into a three-dimensional shaped article in a single semi-continuous operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU PCPOT503 syllabus categorizes thermoforming machinery under **"Secondary Forming / Shaping Equipment"** (Module 3) and the evaluation typically focuses on (a) machine classification, (b) operating sequence, (c) heating and vacuum systems, and (d) design considerations for the mold (male vs. female).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a slice of sandwich bread:
- **Step 1 — Heating:** When you toast bread, it becomes soft, pliable, and rubbery. A polymer sheet behaves identically when heated in a thermoforming machine — it loses its rigidity and becomes formable.
- **Step 2 — Forming:** If you press that warm, flexible bread slice down onto a small bowl with your palm, the bread stretches and takes the bowl's shape. This is exactly what a vacuum, compressed air, or a plug does in a thermoforming machine — it pushes the softened sheet onto a mold.
- **Step 3 — Cooling & Setting:** As the bread cools, it hardens and "remembers" the bowl's shape permanently. The polymer sheet, upon cooling below its softening point, likewise freezes in the mold's geometry.

This "soften-shape-harden" cycle is the philosophical foundation of every thermoforming process, from blister packaging for medicines to refrigerator inner liners and disposable cups.

## 1.3 Standard Operating Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Operating Parameters for Board Examination:**
> - **Forming Temperature:** 120 °C – 200 °C (amorphous: 20–40 °C above $T_g$)
> - **Vacuum Pressure:** **0.1 MPa** (i.e., one full atmosphere ≈ **101.325 kPa**)
> - **Pneumatic Forming Pressure:** 0.3 – 1.0 MPa
> - **Sheet Thickness Range:** **0.025 mm – 6.35 mm** (standard industrial)
> - **Cycle Time:** 2 – 60 seconds (depending on part geometry)
> - **Draw Ratio (DR):** $DR = \dfrac{\text{Mold Depth}}{\text{Mold Width (or Diameter)}}$ — typically limited to **0.5 – 1.0**
> - **Material Utilization (Yield):** Often only **60 – 80 %** (excess is trimmed and recycled)

## 1.4 Primary Industrial Applications

| Application Sector | Typical Product Examples |
|---|---|
| Packaging | Blister packs, clamshells, disposable cups, trays |
| Automotive | Door panels, dashboard skins, headliner substrates |
| Refrigeration | Inner-door liners, freezer cabinets |
| Sanitaryware | Bathtub surrounds, shower stalls |
| Medical | Disposable Petri dishes, blister packs |
| Signage | Illuminated sign faces, vending machine panels |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Principle — The Soften–Form–Harden Triad

Thermoforming is governed by three sequential physical transformations:

1. **Thermal Softening Stage:** Radiant (IR) or convective energy elevates the sheet above its formability window. Heat transfer follows the **Fourier conduction law**:
$$Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{x}$$
where $Q$ = heat flux (W), $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K), $A$ = sheet area (m²), $\Delta T$ = temperature gradient (K), and $x$ = sheet thickness (m).

2. **Mechanical Forming Stage:** The pressure differential $\Delta P$ across the softened sheet produces a forming stress that obeys Newton's second law applied to the fluid-like polymer melt. The driving pressure can be vacuum, compressed air, or mechanical plug force.

3. **Solidification Stage:** The formed part is cooled (typically by forced convection or water spray) until its temperature drops below $T_g$ (amorphous) or $T_m$ (crystalline), locking in the geometry.

## 2.2 Classification of Thermoforming Machinery

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Question Frequency:** "Classify thermoforming machines" or "Differentiate between vacuum forming and pressure forming" appears almost every semester. Master the table below verbatim.

| Type | Driving Force | Max. Pressure | Mold Type | Typical Products | Surface Quality |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Vacuum Forming** | Vacuum (suction) | ≤ 0.1 MPa | Female (negative) | Trays, blisters | Moderate |
| **Pressure Forming** | Compressed air | 0.3 – 1.0 MPa | Female or Male | Detailed panels | High detail |
| **Drape Forming** | Mechanical (matched die) | Variable | Male (positive) | Aircraft canopies | Excellent exterior |
| **Plug-Assist Forming** | Mechanical plug + vacuum | 0.1 MPa vacuum | Female | Deep-draw cups | Uniform thickness |
| **Twin-Sheet Forming** | Vacuum / pressure on both sides | 0.3 MPa typical | Two opposing molds | Hollow parts | Excellent |
| **Mechanical / Matched-Die Forming** | Hydraulic press | 5 – 25 MPa | Male + Female pair | Thick structural parts | Very high |

## 2.3 Key Subsystems of a Thermoforming Machine

A modern thermoforming line consists of **seven integrated sub-assemblies**:

- **(A) Sheet Loading Station** — Roll feed or cut-sheet magazine with index rollers.
- **(B) Clamping Frame** — Pneumatically/hydraulically actuated jaws that grip sheet edges to form a hermetic seal.
- **(C) Heating System** — Radiant emitters (ceramic, quartz halogen, or calrod); often dual-zone (top and bottom) for symmetric heating.
- **(D) Mold Carriage** — Mounting platform for the aluminum, epoxy, or wood mold with vacuum/pressure channels.
- **(E) Vacuum System** — Vacuum pump (typically 30–100 m³/h), vacuum reservoir tank, and solenoid valves.
- **(F) Pressure System** — Compressed air reservoir (0.5 – 1.0 MPa) and pressure control valves.
- **(G) Trimming Station** — In-line steel rule die cutting, CNC router, or laser cutter for edge finishing.

## 2.4 Operating Sequence (Sequential Logic for KTU Board Answer)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard 7-Step Thermoforming Cycle:**
> 1. **Loading** — Thermoplastic sheet is fed and clamped.
> 2. **Heating** — Sheet is brought to forming temperature (IR or convection).
> 3. **Indexing** — Heated sheet is transferred over the mold cavity.
> 4. **Forming** — Vacuum / pressure / plug applied to deform the sheet.
> 5. **Cooling** — Mold and part are cooled; part solidifies.
> 6. **Trimming** — Excess flash is removed.
> 7. **Unloading** — Finished part is ejected; cycle repeats.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable. Memorize the variables, units, and physical meaning.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vacuum}$ | Pressure differential driving forming | Vacuum-forming force calculation |
| 2 | $F_{form} = \Delta P \times A$ | Forming force (N) | Clamp force sizing |
| 3 | $t_{avg} = \dfrac{t_0 \times A_0}{A_f}$ | Volume conservation | Thickness prediction |
| 4 | $DR = \dfrac{h}{w}$ | Draw ratio (depth ÷ width) | Mold design constraint |
| 5 | $Q_{req} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat energy required to soften sheet | Heater sizing |
| 6 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{h \cdot \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{h_c \cdot \Delta T_s}$ | Transient cooling time | Cycle-time optimization |
| 7 | $P_{heater} = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{t_{heat} \times \eta}$ | Heater power rating (W) | Machine specification |
| 8 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{2 \cdot t}$ | Hoop stress in a thermoformed dome | Failure / burst analysis |
| 9 | $\eta_{mat} = \dfrac{A_{part}}{A_{sheet}} \times 100\%$ | Material utilization % | Cost estimation |
| 10 | $\dot{V}_{pump} = \dfrac{V_{mold}}{t_{vac}}$ | Vacuum pump flow rate (m³/s) | Vacuum system sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Unit Conversion:** $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 0.101325 \text{ MPa} \approx 14.7 \text{ psi}$. Always quote in **SI units** for KTU 2024 board examinations.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Thermoforming machinery is the **workhorse of low-to-medium volume plastic part production**. Unlike injection molding, thermoforming tools are inexpensive (often 5–10 % the cost of an injection mold), enabling rapid prototyping and short production runs. The capital cost of a thermoforming line is also 30–50 % lower than injection molding equipment of equivalent throughput, making it indispensable for **packaging, signage, and large-area parts** where injection molding would be technically or economically infeasible.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forming Force on a Vacuum-Formed Sheet

**Problem Setup:** A circular polymer sheet of diameter $d = 500$ mm is vacuum-formed over a female mold. Atmospheric pressure acts on one side; the vacuum side approaches $0$ kPa gauge. Calculate the **total forming force** pushing the sheet into the mold.

**Given:**
- Sheet diameter: $d = 500$ mm = 0.5 m
- Atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa = 101,325 N/m²
- Vacuum pressure (gauge): $P_{vac} = 0$ Pa (ideal vacuum)

**Step 1 — Define the pressure differential.**
The pressure difference driving the forming action is the difference between atmospheric pressure and the mold-side vacuum pressure:
$$\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vac}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$\Delta P = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa} - 0 \text{ Pa}$$
$$\Delta P = 101{,}325 \text{ N/m}^2$$

This is why **vacuum forming is pressure-limited to 0.1 MPa** — you cannot create a pressure differential greater than one full atmosphere under Earth conditions.

**Step 3 — Compute the projected mold area.**
The sheet forms over a circular footprint:
$$A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.5)^2}{4}$$
$$A = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.25}{4} = 0.19635 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's second law (force = pressure × area).**
$$F_{form} = \Delta P \times A$$
$$F_{form} = 101{,}325 \text{ N/m}^2 \times 0.19635 \text{ m}^2$$
$$\boxed{F_{form} \approx 19{,}894 \text{ N} \approx 19.9 \text{ kN}}$$

**Interpretation:** Even for a modest 500 mm circular part, vacuum forming delivers a forming force of nearly **20 kN** — sufficient to deform most commercial thermoplastic sheets in the 0.5–3 mm range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Sheet Thickness After Forming (Volume Conservation)

**Problem Setup:** A flat PS (polystyrene) sheet of original thickness $t_0 = 2.0$ mm and area $A_0 = 0.5 \text{ m} \times 0.4 \text{ m} = 0.20$ m² is vacuum-formed into a cup. The final cup's total surface area (bottom + sides) is $A_f = 0.36$ m². Calculate the **average wall thickness** of the formed cup.

**Step 1 — State the principle of volume conservation.**
Thermoforming does not appreciably compress the polymer melt. The volume of polymer before forming equals the volume after forming:
$$V_0 = V_f$$
$$A_0 \cdot t_0 = A_f \cdot t_{avg}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for the average thickness.**
$$t_{avg} = \dfrac{A_0 \cdot t_0}{A_f}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$t_{avg} = \dfrac{0.20 \text{ m}^2 \times 2.0 \text{ mm}}{0.36 \text{ m}^2}$$
$$t_{avg} = \dfrac{0.40 \text{ m}^2 \cdot \text{mm}}{0.36 \text{ m}^2}$$
$$\boxed{t_{avg} \approx 1.11 \text{ mm}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** The cup walls are on average **~45 % thinner** than the original sheet. In practice, walls are **non-uniform** — corners and bases are thinner than sidewalls, leading to a "**corner-thinning**" defect.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Heater Power Requirement for Sheet Heating

**Problem Setup:** A 1.5 mm thick HIPS sheet of mass 0.5 kg must be heated from 25 °C to 160 °C in 30 seconds. Compute the heater power required (assume heater efficiency $\eta = 0.7$).

**Given:**
- Specific heat of HIPS: $c_p = 1,400$ J/(kg·K)
- Mass: $m = 0.5$ kg
- Temperature rise: $\Delta T = 160 - 25 = 135$ °C (= 135 K)
- Heating time: $t_{heat} = 30$ s
- Heater efficiency: $\eta = 0.7$

**Step 1 — Calculate the heat energy required.**
Using the sensible heat equation:
$$Q_{req} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$
$$Q_{req} = 0.5 \times 1{,}400 \times 135$$
$$Q_{req} = 94{,}500 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the theoretical power input.**
$$P_{theoretical} = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{t_{heat}} = \dfrac{94{,}500}{30} = 3{,}150 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Account for heater efficiency.**
Real heaters lose energy via convection, conduction to frames, and radiation to surroundings. The rated heater power must exceed the theoretical value:
$$P_{heater} = \dfrac{P_{theoretical}}{\eta} = \dfrac{3{,}150}{0.7}$$
$$\boxed{P_{heater} \approx 4{,}500 \text{ W} = 4.5 \text{ kW}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Draw Ratio (DR) for Design Validation

**Problem Setup:** A mold has a depth $h = 100$ mm and a width $w = 200$ mm. Verify whether it falls within the safe draw-ratio limit for thermoforming.

**Step 1 — Recall the standard draw ratio formula.**
$$DR = \dfrac{h}{w}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**
$$DR = \dfrac{100 \text{ mm}}{200 \text{ mm}} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Compare with KTU-recommended limit.**
The recommended safe draw ratio for most thermoplastics is **DR ≤ 0.5 – 1.0**.
$$\boxed{DR = 0.5 \;\;\text{(Within safe limit; molding feasible)}}$$

**Extension:** If $h = 250$ mm and $w = 200$ mm, then $DR = 1.25$ — this would require **plug-assist forming** to prevent excessive thinning and material rupture.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Thermoforming Variant

| Application Need | Recommended Forming Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Shallow tray, low cost | Vacuum Forming | 0.1 MPa is sufficient |
| Sharp detail, texture | Pressure Forming | Higher $\Delta P$ (0.3–1.0 MPa) |
| Thick-walled structural | Matched-Die / Mechanical | High tonnage available |
| Deep-draw cup, uniform wall | Plug-Assist | Plug pre-stretches sheet |
| Hollow double-walled tank | Twin-Sheet | Two sheets form simultaneously |
| Aircraft canopy, smooth exterior | Drape Forming | Male mold preserves surface |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Thermoforming Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sheet Loading Magazine] --> B[Pneumatic Clamping Frame]
    B --> C[IR Heating Plenum - Top Zone]
    B --> D[IR Heating Plenum - Bottom Zone]
    C --> E[Mold Carriage Indexing]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Vacuum Pump Reservoir]
    E --> G[Compressed Air Manifold]
    E --> H[Plug Assist Actuator]
    F --> I[Forming Chamber]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Cooling Fan / Water Spray Bank]
    J --> K[In-line Trimming Station - Steel Rule Die]
    K --> L[Finished Part Ejection]
    L --> M[PLC Control Cabinet]
    M --> A
```

**Description of Architecture Flow:**
- The **Sheet Loading Magazine** feeds cut sheets or continuous roll stock.
- The **Pneumatic Clamping Frame** grips the sheet edges to form a hermetic seal against atmospheric leakage.
- **IR Heating Plenums** (top and bottom) raise the sheet to forming temperature using ceramic, quartz, or halogen emitters.
- The **Mold Carriage** indexes the heated sheet precisely over the forming station.
- Three parallel sub-systems (Vacuum / Compressed Air / Plug Assist) deliver the forming energy depending on the part specification.
- The **Cooling Station** removes heat to solidify the part.
- The **Trimming Station** removes flash; the finished part is ejected.
- The **PLC Control Cabinet** sequences the entire cycle, providing feedback on temperature, pressure, and stroke position.

## 4.2 Process Sequence State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Loading
    Loading --> Clamping
    Clamping --> Heating
    Heating --> Indexing
    Indexing --> Forming
    Forming --> Cooling
    Cooling --> Trimming
    Trimming --> Ejection
    Ejection --> Loading
    Ejection --> [*]
```

**State Explanation:**
- **Loading** — Fresh sheet indexed into the machine.
- **Clamping** — Sheet edges secured pneumatically.
- **Heating** — Radiant energy raises sheet temperature.
- **Indexing** — Sheet is moved into position above the mold.
- **Forming** — Vacuum / pressure / plug applied; sheet deforms.
- **Cooling** — Part solidifies below $T_g$ or $T_m$.
- **Trimming** — Excess material (flash) is removed.
- **Ejection** — Finished part is unloaded; the cycle restarts.

## 4.3 Subsystem Interconnection Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWERHOUSE["Power & Utility Feed"]
        A1[Electric Supply 415V AC]
        A2[Compressed Air 0.6 MPa]
        A3[Cooling Water 25C]
    end

    subgraph COREUNITS["Core Machine Units"]
        B1[PLC and HMI Panel]
        B2[IR Heater Bank]
        B3[Vacuum Pump Unit]
        B4[Clamp Cylinder]
        B5[Trimming Press]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Stage"]
        C1[Finished Thermoformed Part]
        C2[Regrind Flash]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A2 --> B3
    A2 --> B4
    A3 --> B2
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B1 --> B4
    B1 --> B5
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    B5 --> C2
```

**Description:** This schematic illustrates the three-tier architecture: **Power & Utility Feed** supplies energy and media; the **Core Machine Units** perform the actual thermoforming operations under PLC coordination; and the **Output Stage** separates the finished part from the regrind flash.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the principle of vacuum forming in thermoforming. State any two advantages and one limitation of vacuum forming.**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
Vacuum forming is a thermoforming technique in which a heated thermoplastic sheet is drawn into a female mold cavity by evacuating the air beneath the sheet. Atmospheric pressure (~0.1 MPa) on the upper side of the sheet provides the forming force. The pressure differential $\Delta P = P_{atm} - P_{vacuum}$ drives the softened sheet to conform to the mold surface, where it is cooled and solidified.

**Advantages (0.5 mark each):**
1. **Low tooling cost** — Molds can be made from wood, epoxy, or cast aluminum, costing only 5–10 % of an equivalent injection mold.
2. **Fast prototyping** — Lead time for mold fabrication is typically 1–2 weeks.
3. **Simple machine design** — No high-tonnage hydraulic press is required.

**Limitation (0.5 mark):**
1. **Limited forming pressure (≤ 0.1 MPa)** restricts the achievable detail, sharpness of features, and depth-to-width ratio. Pressure forming overcomes this drawback.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six components of a thermoforming machine and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sheet Loading Magazine / Roll Feeder** | Stores and indexes fresh thermoplastic sheet into the machine. |
| 2 | **Clamping Frame** | Holds the sheet edges rigidly during heating and forming; creates an airtight seal. |
| 3 | **IR Heating Plenum** | Radiates infrared energy to bring the sheet above its softening point. |
| 4 | **Mold** | Defines the final geometry of the formed part (male or female). |
| 5 | **Vacuum Pump** | Evacuates air from beneath the sheet to create forming pressure differential. |
| 6 | **Cooling System** | Fans or water sprays that remove heat to solidify the formed part. |
| 7 | **Trimming Station** | Removes excess flash from the formed part. |
| 8 | **PLC Control Panel** | Sequences the entire cycle and monitors process parameters. |

*(Any six components with correct functions fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **"vacuum forming"** with **"pressure forming"** — remember, vacuum is limited to **0.1 MPa**; pressure forming uses **compressed air up to 1.0 MPa**.
> - When asked to "explain the operating sequence," always give the **sequence in chronological order** (Loading → Clamping → Heating → Indexing → Forming → Cooling → Trimming → Ejection) — examiners award marks for **correct ordering**, not just listing steps.
> - Do **not** confuse **draw ratio** with **stretch ratio** — DR is mold depth ÷ mold width (a design parameter), while stretch ratio is the actual elongation of the polymer.
> - Always state **units** in numerical problems. KTU examiners frequently deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing SI units.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) With neat schematic, explain the working of a vacuum forming machine. List the main subsystems and the operating sequence in detail. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Schematic Description (2 marks):**
A vacuum forming machine consists of (i) a sheet clamping frame at the top, (ii) IR heaters above the sheet, (iii) a mold mounted on a movable table below, (iv) a vacuum pump connected to the mold cavity through a reservoir and solenoid valves, and (v) a PLC-based control panel. A block-level architecture was shown in Section 4.1 of these notes.

**Main Subsystems (3 marks):**
1. **Sheet Feeding & Clamping System** — Pneumatically actuated jaws that grip the sheet edges to provide a hermetic seal.
2. **Heating System** — Top and bottom IR heaters (ceramic, quartz halogen, or nichrome) controlled by zone-wise temperature controllers.
3. **Mold System** — Female (negative) mold typically made of cast aluminum, with vacuum channels machined into the back.
4. **Vacuum System** — Vacuum pump (typically 30–100 m³/h capacity), a vacuum reservoir tank (to hold vacuum), and quick-acting solenoid valves.
5. **Cooling & Trimming System** — Forced air fans or water mist cooling followed by in-line steel-rule die trimming.
6. **Control System** — PLC and HMI panel for cycle programming and process monitoring.

**Operating Sequence (2 marks):**
1. Sheet is loaded into the clamping frame.
2. The frame closes pneumatically, sealing the sheet edges.
3. IR heaters raise the sheet temperature to the forming range.
4. Heaters retract; the mold carriage indexes into position.
5. Vacuum is applied; atmospheric pressure forces the sheet into the mold cavity.
6. The part cools and solidifies.
7. Vacuum is released; the part is removed and trimmed.

---

**(b) A flat HIPS sheet of dimensions 600 mm × 400 mm × 2 mm is vacuum formed into a tray of final surface area 0.42 m². Calculate: (i) the average wall thickness, (ii) the draw ratio if mold depth is 80 mm and mold width is 300 mm, and (iii) the forming force if the effective forming area is 0.24 m² and the pressure differential is 0.1 MPa. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Original sheet: $L = 600$ mm, $W = 400$ mm, $t_0 = 2$ mm
- $A_0 = 0.6 \times 0.4 = 0.24$ m²
- Final part area: $A_f = 0.42$ m²
- Mold depth: $h = 80$ mm
- Mold width: $w = 300$ mm
- Forming area: $A = 0.24$ m²
- Pressure differential: $\Delta P = 0.1$ MPa = 100,000 N/m²

**Part (i) — Average Wall Thickness (3 marks):**
Using volume conservation: $A_0 \cdot t_0 = A_f \cdot t_{avg}$
$$t_{avg} = \dfrac{A_0 \cdot t_0}{A_f} = \dfrac{0.24 \times 2}{0.42}$$
$$\boxed{t_{avg} \approx 1.143 \text{ mm}}$$

**Part (ii) — Draw Ratio (2 marks):**
$$DR = \dfrac{h}{w} = \dfrac{80}{300} \approx \boxed{0.267}$$
*(DR is well within the safe limit of 0.5–1.0.)*

**Part (iii) — Forming Force (2 marks):**
$$F_{form} = \Delta P \times A = 100{,}000 \times 0.24$$
$$\boxed{F_{form} = 24{,}000 \text{ N} = 24 \text{ kN}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Compare vacuum forming and pressure forming. Discuss the role of plug-assist in deep-draw thermoforming. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Vacuum Forming | Pressure Forming |
|---|---|---|
| Driving Force | Vacuum (suction) | Compressed air (push) |
| Maximum Pressure | ≤ 0.1 MPa (1 atm) | 0.3 – 1.0 MPa |
| Surface Detail | Moderate | High (sharp corners, textures) |
| Mold Type | Usually female | Female or male |
| Cost | Lower | Slightly higher (air system) |
| Typical Parts | Trays, blisters, cups | Detailed panels, dashboards |
| Cycle Time | Faster | Slightly slower |
| Equipment Complexity | Simple | More complex |

**Role of Plug-Assist in Deep-Draw Forming (3 marks):**
In deep-draw applications (e.g., disposable cups), excessive material thinning occurs at the corners and base when only vacuum is used. A **plug-assist** is a male tool (typically heated and made of aluminum or syntactic foam) that is pushed into the softened sheet *before* vacuum is applied. It performs **mechanical pre-stretching**, which:
1. Distributes the polymer mass more uniformly across the mold cavity.
2. Reduces corner thinning by 30–50 %.
3. Enables higher draw ratios (up to 1.5–2.0) compared to pure vacuum forming.
4. Improves wall-thickness uniformity and reduces material consumption.

**Sequence:** Sheet is heated → plug descends and stretches the sheet → plug retracts → vacuum is applied to complete forming against the mold walls.

---

**(b) A thermoforming machine must heat 0.8 kg of ABS sheet from 30 °C to 175 °C in 25 seconds. The heater efficiency is 65 %. Compute the rated heater power. Given: $c_p$ of ABS = 1,500 J/(kg·K). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Mass: $m = 0.8$ kg
- Initial temperature: $T_i = 30$ °C
- Final temperature: $T_f = 175$ °C
- Temperature rise: $\Delta T = 175 - 30 = 145$ K (note: $\Delta T$ in K = $\Delta T$ in °C)
- Specific heat: $c_p = 1,500$ J/(kg·K)
- Heating time: $t_{heat} = 25$ s
- Heater efficiency: $\eta = 0.65$

**Step 1 — Compute the required heat energy (2 marks):**
$$Q_{req} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$
$$Q_{req} = 0.8 \times 1{,}500 \times 145$$
$$Q_{req} = 174{,}000 \text{ J} = 174 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the theoretical power (2 marks):**
$$P_{theoretical} = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{t_{heat}} = \dfrac{174{,}000}{25}$$
$$P_{theoretical} = 6{,}960 \text{ W} \approx 6.96 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the heater efficiency factor (2 marks):**
$$P_{heater} = \dfrac{P_{theoretical}}{\eta} = \dfrac{6{,}960}{0.65}$$
$$\boxed{P_{heater} \approx 10{,}708 \text{ W} \approx 10.71 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification and Interpretation (1 mark):**
For a commercial 11 kW-rated heater, the cycle time of 25 seconds is feasible. If $\eta$ were higher (e.g., 0.85), the rated power required would drop to 8.19 kW — illustrating why modern thermoforming machines use **ceramic IR emitters with reflectors** to maximize $\eta$.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam — Thermoforming Machinery**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- Thermoforming is a **secondary shaping** process (not a primary one like extrusion or injection molding).
- The process operates on the **soften → form → harden** principle.
- Maximum vacuum pressure = **0.1 MPa** (1 atmosphere); pressure forming extends this to **1.0 MPa**.
- Material is heated to **20–40 °C above $T_g$** (amorphous) or **near $T_m$** (semi-crystalline).

### 🛠️ Machine Subsystems (Remember all 7)
1. Sheet loading/feeding
2. Clamping frame
3. Heating system (IR)
4. Mold carriage
5. Vacuum system
6. Pressure system
7. Trimming station + PLC

### 📐 High-Yield Formulas
- $F_{form} = \Delta P \times A$
- $t_{avg} = \dfrac{A_0 \cdot t_0}{A_f}$ (volume conservation)
- $DR = \dfrac{h}{w}$ (safe limit: 0.5–1.0)
- $Q_{req} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$
- $P_{heater} = \dfrac{Q_{req}}{t_{heat} \times \eta}$

### ⚠️ Common Exam Traps
- Confusing **vacuum** vs. **pressure** forming pressure limits.
- Forgetting to apply **heater efficiency** in power calculations.
- Listing operating steps in the wrong order (sequence matters!).
- Using **gauge pressure** vs. **absolute pressure** incorrectly.
- Forgetting SI units in numerical answers (deducted marks).

### 🎯 Material Choices
- **PS, HIPS, PVC, PET, PP, ABS** are the most common thermoforming polymers.
- Semi-crystalline polymers (PP, PET) have a **narrower forming window** than amorphous polymers (PS, PVC, ABS).

### 💡 Industrial Edge
- Thermoforming is preferred for **large-area, low-volume, low-cost** parts.
- Tooling cost is **5–10 %** of equivalent injection molds.
- Material utilization is often only **60–80 %**; the flash is **reground and recycled**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heating of Sheet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heating of Sheet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Heating of Sheet** in polymer thermoforming refers to the controlled thermal conditioning of a thermoplastic sheet (extruded or calendered) from its ambient/storage temperature to a homogeneous **forming temperature** at which the polymer exhibits rubbery, ductile behavior suitable for deformation against a mould. The sheet must be heated uniformly — both laterally (across the area) and transversely (through the thickness) — before it can be stretched, sagged, or vacuum/pressure formed without inducing residual stresses, orientation gradients, or surface defects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 — Thermoforming):**
> Heating of the plastic sheet is the **first and most critical step** in the thermoforming cycle. An improperly heated sheet leads to *uneven wall thickness*, *webbing*, *incomplete part detail*, *brittle fracture*, or *material degradation* during forming. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to identify heater types, justify heater zoning, calculate heating time, and compute total energy requirements.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a **chapati (flatbread) on a tawa (griddle)**. If you only heat one side, the surface touching the tawa chars while the top remains raw — the heat has not penetrated the full thickness. To make a uniform, pliable chapati, you must give it *time* so that heat *conducts inward*, and you must *flip* it so both faces reach the soft, cooked state simultaneously.

A polymer sheet behaves identically:
- **The "tawa"** = radiant/convective/conductive heater
- **The "chapati"** = polymer sheet
- **"Cooking both sides"** = heating from **both faces** (top and bottom heater banks)
- **"Soft, pliable state"** = the polymer's **rubbery plateau** (above $T_g$ for amorphous, above $T_m$ for semi-crystalline polymers)
- **"Cooking time"** = the **heating time $t_h$**, which depends on sheet thickness, thermal diffusivity, and heater surface temperature

## 1.3 Why Heating Governs Part Quality

| Phenomenon | Cause (Heating-Related) | Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Uneven wall thickness | Non-uniform surface temperature across the area | Local over-stretching → thin spots / rupture |
| Hazy / blotchy surface | Sheet center still below $T_g$ while surface is molten | Residual crystallinity or stress whitening |
| Sheet sag & drape problems | Slow heating → crystallite reorganization in semi-crystalline sheet | Loss of dimensional control |
| Thermal degradation | Overheating or excessive residence time at high $T$ | Yellowing, odour, molecular weight drop |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Typical Process Window:**
> - **Forming temperature** for amorphous polymers (PS, PMMA, PVC, ABS): $\mathbf{T_g + 40 \text{ to } 120~°C}$
> - **Forming temperature** for semi-crystalline polymers (PP, PE, PET, PA): $\mathbf{T_m + 10 \text{ to } 40~°C}$
> - **Thermal diffusivity** $\alpha$ of typical thermoplastics: $\mathbf{1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ to } 2 \times 10^{-7}~m^2/s}$
> - **Specific heat capacity** $c_p$: $\mathbf{1200 \text{ to } 2500~J/(kg\cdot K)}$
> - **Mass density** $\rho$: $\mathbf{900 \text{ to } 1450~kg/m^3}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanisms of Heat Transfer to the Sheet

Three primary modes transfer heat from the heater to the polymer sheet:

### (a) Radiation Heating (Most common in thermoforming)
- Uses **infrared (IR) emitters** — ceramic, quartz, halogen, or nichrome wire elements.
- Heat flux follows the **Stefan–Boltzmann law**:
$$q'' = \varepsilon \sigma \left( T_h^4 - T_s^4 \right)$$
- Where $\varepsilon$ is emissivity of the heater, $\sigma$ is the Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\mathbf{(5.67 \times 10^{-8}~W/m^2K^4)}$, and $T_h$, $T_s$ are heater and sheet surface temperatures in Kelvin.
- **Heater temperature** typically **400–700 °C**; sheet surface reaches forming temperature by absorption.
- **Advantage:** Fast, non-contact, easy zoning.
- **Disadvantage:** Heater-on / heater-off response is slow (thermal inertia of element).

### (b) Convection Heating (Hot-air ovens)
- Uses **recirculated hot air** at controlled temperature.
- Heat flux:
$$q'' = h \left( T_\infty - T_s \right)$$
- Where $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient $\mathbf{(15 \text{ to } 100~W/m^2K)}$ for forced convection in thermoforming ovens.
- **Advantage:** Very uniform heating, gentle on sheet.
- **Disadvantage:** Slow — long cycle times.

### (c) Conduction Heating (Contact / Platen)
- Sheet pressed against **heated aluminium platens**.
- Heat flux:
$$q'' = \frac{k}{L}\left( T_p - T_s \right)$$
- **Advantage:** Very efficient, fast.
- **Disadvantage:** Surface marking, limited to flat sheets, difficult for thin films.

## 2.2 Heater Zoning & Lateral Uniformity

To counteract **edge heat losses** (edges of the sheet radiate and convect to the surrounding frame), heaters are arranged in **zones** — typically **3 to 5 zones** across the width.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is zoning necessary?**
> The corners and edges of the sheet lose heat to the clamping frame faster than the centre. Without zoning, the centre becomes over-heated while the edges remain cold. Zoning uses **higher power input at the edges** and **lower power input at the centre**, sometimes called the **"smile curve"** of power distribution.

## 2.3 Transverse (Through-Thickness) Temperature Profile

Because polymer sheets have **low thermal conductivity** ($k \approx 0.1$–$0.3~W/mK$), the surface heats far faster than the centre. The temperature distribution across thickness during heating follows the **transient heat conduction equation** (one-dimensional):

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

with initial condition $T(x,0) = T_i$ and boundary conditions representing heater flux (or temperature) on both faces.

The solution for a sheet of thickness $L$ heated symmetrically from both sides reduces (after sufficient time, neglecting edge effects) to the series:

$$\theta^* = \frac{T(x,t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} C_n \cos\left( \frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{L} \right) \exp\left( - (2n+1)^2 \frac{\pi^2 \alpha t}{L^2} \right)$$

The dominant first-term approximation gives the **bulk-to-surface temperature lag** during heating.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities below must be expressed in **SI units**. The vertical bar symbol is rendered as `\vert` to avoid breaking table formatting.

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical SI Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Density of polymer sheet | $kg/m^3$ |
| $c_p$ | Specific heat capacity at constant pressure | $J/(kg \cdot K)$ |
| $k$ | Thermal conductivity | $W/(m \cdot K)$ |
| $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ | Thermal diffusivity | $m^2/s$ |
| $L$ | Sheet thickness | $m$ |
| $h$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient | $W/(m^2 \cdot K)$ |
| $T_s$ | Sheet surface temperature | $°C$ or $K$ |
| $T_b$ | Sheet bulk (centre) temperature | $°C$ or $K$ |
| $T_i$ | Initial sheet temperature | $°C$ or $K$ |
| $T_\infty$ | Ambient or effective heater temperature | $°C$ or $K$ |
| $t_h$ | Total heating time | $s$ |
| $Bi = hL/k$ | Biot number | dimensionless |
| $Fo = \alpha t / L^2$ | Fourier number | dimensionless |
| $A$ | Heated sheet area | $m^2$ |
| $m$ | Mass of sheet | $kg$ |
| $Q$ | Total heat energy required | $J$ |
| $P$ | Heater power input | $W$ |
| $\varepsilon$ | Emissivity | dimensionless |
| $\sigma$ | Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $5.67 \times 10^{-8}~W/(m^2 \cdot K^4)$ |

### Master Equations

| # | Equation | Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \, c_p \, (T_f - T_i)$ | Sensible heat to raise sheet from $T_i$ to forming $T_f$ |
| 2 | $t_h = \dfrac{\rho \, c_p \, L^2}{4 \, k \, \pi^2} \ln\left[\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_b - T_\infty}{T_s - T_\infty}\right]$ | Heating time for symmetric two-sided heating |
| 3 | $\theta^* = \dfrac{T(x,t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty}$ | Dimensionless temperature |
| 4 | $Bi = hL/k$ | Lumped vs distributed heating criterion |
| 5 | $Fo = \alpha t / L^2$ | Dimensionless time |
| 6 | $q'' = \varepsilon \sigma (T_h^4 - T_s^4)$ | Radiative heat flux (IR heaters) |
| 7 | $q'' = h (T_\infty - T_s)$ | Convective heat flux |
| 8 | $P = (Q/t_h) + P_{loss}$ | Heater power with loss term |
| 9 | $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ | Definition of thermal diffusivity |

### Real-World Utility

- **Industrial thermoforming lines (ILLIG, Kiefel, GN Thermoforming):** these formulas are embedded in PLCs to compute heater zoning profiles in real time.
- **Solar absorber design & IR drying ovens:** the same radiation equation governs paint drying and polymer film orientation lines (BOPP, BOPET).
- **Food packaging sheet lines (PS, PP, PET):** heating uniformity determines whether a yoghurt cup wall-thickness meets the ±5% tolerance demanded by customers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Heating Time Equation (Equation 2)

The sheet is treated as an infinite slab of thickness $L$ heated **symmetrically from both faces**. Heat enters at $x = \pm L/2$; the centre is at $x = 0$.

### Step 1 — Governing PDE
The transient 1-D heat conduction equation (no internal heat generation) is:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \, \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

### Step 2 — Initial & Boundary Conditions
**Initial condition (IC):**
$$T(x, 0) = T_i \quad \text{for } -L/2 \leq x \leq L/2$$

**Boundary conditions (BC) — constant surface temperature (Dirichlet):**
$$T(\pm L/2, t) = T_s \quad \text{for } t > 0$$

### Step 3 — Non-Dimensionalisation
Define dimensionless temperature and coordinate:

$$\theta^* = \frac{T(x,t) - T_s}{T_i - T_s}, \quad X = \frac{x}{L}$$

Then $\theta^*(X, 0) = 1$ and $\theta^*(\pm 1/2, t) = 0$, with the PDE becoming:

$$\frac{\partial \theta^*}{\partial Fo} = \frac{\partial^2 \theta^*}{\partial X^2}$$

where the Fourier number $Fo = \alpha t / L^2$.

### Step 4 — Separation of Variables
Assume $\theta^*(X, Fo) = F(X) \cdot G(Fo)$. Substituting and dividing by $F \cdot G$:
