# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: Define the term "polymer product manufacturing" and list the five universal sub-operations involved.**

**Model Answer:**

Polymer product manufacturing is the engineering discipline that transforms polymeric raw materials (granules, powders, liquids, sheets) into finished products of defined geometry and performance by controlled application of heat, pressure, and shaping energy.

The five universal sub-operations are:
1. **Feeding and metering** of the polymer feedstock at a controlled rate.
2. **Heating and plasticisation** (or cure activation) to bring the polymer to a processable state.
3. **Pressurisation and pumping** to generate the pressure that drives flow.
4. **Shaping and forming** in a die, mould, or roll gap to impart geometry.
5. **Solidification and ejection** (cooling for thermoplastics, curing for thermosets) to lock the geometry in place.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Cue:** A 3-mark answer that names all five sub-operations in correct sequence and gives a one-line description of each earns full marks. Skipping any one sub-operation caps the score at 2/3.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Q: Differentiate between a thermoplastic and a thermoset in the context of product manufacturing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Thermoplastic | Thermoset |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular structure | Linear or branched chains, no crosslinks | 3-D network of crosslinked chains |
| Response to heat | Softens on heating, re-solidifies on cooling (reversible) | Undergoes irreversible chemical cure; cannot be remelted |
| Manufacturing route | Melt processing (injection, extrusion, blow) | Reactive liquid processing (compression, transfer, RIM, casting) |
| Cycle characteristic | Cycle dominated by **cooling time** | Cycle dominated by **cure time** |
| Recyclability | Reprocessable (regrind) | Not remeltable; only grinding for filler use |

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches and the underlying thermodynamic and rheological reasoning, explain the **Concept of polymer product manufacturing** through the unifying framework of feedstock state, energy input, and shaping-solidification mode. Categorise the major processes covered in Module 3 of PCPOT503 into this framework.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A polypropylene slab of thickness $h = 6$ mm is moulded between steel plates held at wall temperature $T_w = 40$ °C. The melt temperature is $T_m = 230$ °C and the part is ejected when the centreline reaches $T_{eject} = 90$ °C. Given $\alpha_{th} = 1.2 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s, calculate the cooling time using the simplified slab-cooling equation.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Concept of polymer product manufacturing is best understood through a **three-dimensional decision framework**:

**Axis 1 – Feedstock State:**
- Melt (thermoplastics above $T_m$ or $T_{softening}$)
- Reactive liquid (thermoset prepolymers, monomers)
- Powder (rotational moulding, sintering)
- Solid sheet (thermoforming, calendering feed)

**Axis 2 – Dominant Energy Input:**
- Thermal (conduction from heaters, ovens)
- Mechanical (screw shear, calendar roll pressure)
- Chemical (exothermic cure reactions)
- Radiative (laser sintering, IR heating)

**Axis 3 – Shaping-Solidification Mode:**
- Pressure-driven cavity filling (injection, compression)
- Inflation / parison (blow moulding)
- Drape / vacuum / pressure (thermoforming)
- Centrifugal / slosh (rotational)
- Die-and-draw (extrusion, spinning, casting, pultrusion)

**Mapping the Module 3 processes:**

| Process | Feedstock | Energy | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| Injection Moulding | Melt | Thermal + Mechanical | Cavity filling |
| Blow Moulding | Parison | Pneumatic | Inflation |
| Rotational Moulding | Powder | Thermal | Centrifugal |
| Thermoforming | Sheet | Thermal + Pressure | Drape |
| Compression | Preform | Thermal + Pressure | Cavity |
| Calendering | Melt | Mechanical + Thermal | Nip |
| Pultrusion | Liquid | Thermal + Mechanical | Die pull |

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given:
- $h = 6 \text{ mm} = 6 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $T_w = 40$ °C
- $T_m = 230$ °C
- $T_{eject} = 90$ °C
- $\alpha_{th} = 1.2 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s

Apply the simplified slab-cooling formula:

$$
t_{cool} \;=\; \frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha_{th}} \ln\!\left(\frac{4\,(T_m - T_w)}{\pi\,(T_{eject} - T_w)}\right)
$$

**Step 1 — Compute temperature differences:**

$$
T_m - T_w \;=\; 230 - 40 \;=\; 190 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
T_{eject} - T_w \;=\; 90 - 40 \;=\; 50 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the argument of the logarithm:**

$$
\frac{4 \times 190}{\pi \times 50} \;=\; \frac{760}{157.08} \;=\; 4.839
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$
\ln(4.839) \;=\; 1.577
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the prefactor:**

$$
\frac{h^2}{\pi^2 \alpha_{th}} \;=\; \frac{(6 \times 10^{-3})^2}{\pi^2 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-7}} \;=\; \frac{36 \times 10^{-6}}{1.184 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 30.41 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**

$$
t_{cool} \;=\; 30.41 \times 1.577 \;\approx\; 47.95 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answer:** $t_{cool} \approx 48$ seconds.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **Concept of the manufacturing cycle** for a typical injection moulding operation. Draw a pressure-time and temperature-time profile and indicate the major phases (filling, packing, cooling, ejection). Explain how the cycle time governs production economics.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A single-screw extruder has $D = 50$ mm, $N = 60$ rpm, $h = 3$ mm, $\theta = 20°$, and produces a pressure rise of $\Delta P = 5$ MPa across a die-fed channel of length $L = 800$ mm. The melt viscosity in the channel is $\eta = 500$ Pa·s and the effective channel width is $W \approx \pi D \sin\theta = \pi \times 0.05 \times \sin(20°) = 0.0537$ m. Calculate the net volumetric output.

**Model Solution for (a):**

The injection moulding manufacturing cycle consists of **four sequential phases**:

1. **Filling Phase** — the screw acts as a plunger, injecting melt into the closed mould cavity. Pressure rises from 0 to peak injection pressure (50–140 MPa). Volume injected is 95–99% of cavity volume. Time: 0.5–2 s.

2. **Packing (Holding) Phase** — additional melt is packed in to compensate for volumetric shrinkage as the part cools. Pressure is held at a lower holding pressure (30–70 MPa). Time: 1–5 s.

3. **Cooling Phase** — the part solidifies in the mould with no further melt input. Pressure decays towards zero. Time: governed by slab-cooling equation (10–60 s for typical wall thicknesses). This is the longest phase.

4. **Ejection Phase** — mould opens, ejector pins push the part out, mould closes, screw rotates and plasticates the next shot. Time: 2–5 s.

```
Pressure (MPa)   ▲
   140 ┤         ┌──┐
       │         │  │ Packing
    70 ┤─────────┘  └────┐
       │  Filling         │ Cooling
     0 ┤                  └──────┐
       └──────────────────────────▶ Time
            Fill   Pack   Cool   Eject
```

**Cycle time impact on economics:**

- Production rate $R = \dfrac{3600}{t_{cycle}}$ parts/hour
- Cooling typically constitutes **50–80%** of cycle time
- Reducing $h$ (wall thickness) reduces $t_{cool}$ quadratically — most powerful design lever
- Injection moulding machines cost ₹15 lakh – ₹5 crore; every second saved in cycle time recovers capital cost faster

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given:
- $D = 0.050$ m, $N = 60/60 = 1.0$ rev/s, $h = 0.003$ m
- $\theta = 20°$, $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^6$ Pa, $L = 0.800$ m
- $\eta = 500$ Pa·s, $W = 0.0537$ m

**Step 1 — Drag flow component:**

$$
Q_d \;=\; \frac{\pi^2 D^2 N h \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}
$$

Compute $\sin 20° = 0.342$, $\cos 20° = 0.940$:

$$
Q_d \;=\; \frac{\pi^2 (0.050)^2 (1.0) (0.003) (0.342) (0.940)}{2}
$$

$$
Q_d \;=\; \frac{9.8696 \times 0.0025 \times 0.003 \times 0.3215}{2}
$$

$$
Q_d \;=\; \frac{2.380 \times 10^{-5}}{2} \;=\; 1.190 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Pressure flow component:**

$$
Q_p \;=\; -\frac{W h^3 \Delta P}{12 \eta L}
$$

$$
Q_p \;=\; -\frac{(0.0537)(0.003)^3 (5 \times 10^6)}{12 \times 500 \times 0.800}
$$

$$
Q_p \;=\; -\frac{0.0537 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-8} \times 5 \times 10^6}{4800}
$$

$$
Q_p \;=\; -\frac{7.249 \times 10^{-3}}{4800} \;=\; -1.510 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Net output:**

$$
Q_{net} \;=\; Q_d + Q_p \;=\; 1.190 \times 10^{-5} - 1.510 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 1.039 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to mass flow rate (assuming $\rho = 900$ kg/m³ for typical polymer melt):**

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \rho Q_{net} \;=\; 900 \times 1.039 \times 10^{-5} \;\approx\; 9.35 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} \;\approx\; 33.7 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_{net} \approx 1.04 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s $\approx$ **33.7 kg/h**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit errors** — mixing mm with m, °C with K, MPa with Pa. Always state units in every line of a numerical answer.
> 2. **Sign errors in extruder equation** — pressure flow is *negative* (back-leakage). Forgetting the sign inflates the output by 10–15% and examiners deduct for that.
> 3. **Forgetting to take $\ln$** of the ratio inside the cooling-time formula. The most common error is computing the ratio and writing it as the answer instead of taking its logarithm.
> 4. **Not drawing a phase diagram** for the cycle-time question. A 7-mark part (a) without a sketch typically gets 4/7.
> 5. **Process-selection answers without justification** — saying "rotomoulding is good for large hollow parts" earns 1 mark; saying "rotomoulding uses powder that sinters against the mould wall with no internal pressure, making it ideal for stress-free, large, hollow, seamless tanks" earns full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Polymer product manufacturing = Feedstock + Energy + Shaping + Solidification.** This is the single most-tested "Concept" statement in KTU Module 3.
- **Five universal sub-operations:** Feed → Heat → Pressurise → Shape → Solidify. Memorise this sequence verbatim.
- **Three process families:** Melt (thermoplastics), Reactive liquid (thermosets), Solid sheet (thermoforming/calendering), Powder (rotomoulding/sintering).
- **Cooling time** $t_{cool} \propto h^2$ — the single most important KTU insight. Wall thickness is the dominant design variable.
- **Extruder output** is drag flow minus pressure back-leakage. Maximise $N$, balance $h$, minimise $\Delta P$ and $\eta$.
- **Cycle time** in injection moulding is governed by cooling, not filling or ejection. Reducing $h$ is the most powerful lever.
- **Process selection** must be justified by geometry, material state, production volume, and cost — not by intuition.
- **Standard pressure ranges:** Injection 50–140 MPa; Compression 10–50 MPa; Blow 0.3–1.0 MPa; Rotational near-atmospheric.
- **Standard temperature ranges:** PE melt $\approx$ 130–180 °C; PP $\approx$ 200–260 °C; PS $\approx$ 180–240 °C; PET $\approx$ 260–280 °C; PVC $\approx$ 160–200 °C; thermoset cure $\approx$ 120–180 °C.
- **Always draw a labelled sketch or block diagram** for any 7-mark descriptive sub-question in Part B.
- **Always state units** in every numerical line — valuation keys explicitly check for unit consistency.
- **Always cross-check the order of magnitude** of your numerical answer before submitting. A cooling time of 0.05 s for a 6 mm PP slab is unphysical; 48 s is correct.
- **Memorise the four-part decomposition of cycle time** in injection moulding: Fill + Pack + Cool + Eject.
- **Regrind / recycle loop:** Every polymer process generates some waste; regrind is reprocessed back to the feeding stage. This is the only universal feedback loop in the manufacturing concept.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hot Strength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINING & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hot Strength

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Academic Definition

In the context of polymer and rubber processing (aligned with **PCPOT503** syllabus), **Hot Strength** is defined as the tensile strength and corresponding ultimate elongation exhibited by an **unvulcanized (green/uncured) rubber or elastomeric compound** when subjected to a tensile load at elevated processing temperatures (typically in the range of **80 °C – 160 °C**), rather than at ambient conditions. It is a critical rheological–mechanical property that quantifies the **cohesive integrity** of the compound under simulated processing conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Hot Strength is a direct sub-topic under the broader heading of *“Properties affecting processability of rubber compounds”* and is closely associated with Green Strength, Scorch, and Cure characteristics in Module 3.

Formally expressed:

$$ \sigma_{hot} = \frac{F_{max}}{A_{0}} \Big|_{T = T_{process}} $$

Where:
- $\sigma_{hot}$ = Hot tensile strength (MPa)
- $F_{max}$ = Maximum tensile force at break (N)
- $A_{0}$ = Original cross-sectional area of the test specimen (mm²)
- $T_{process}$ = Operating processing temperature (°C)

The corresponding hot elongation at break ($\varepsilon_{hot}$) is defined as:

$$ \varepsilon_{hot} = \frac{L_{b} - L_{0}}{L_{0}} \times 100\% \Big|_{T = T_{process}} $$

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – "The Plasticine Test"

Imagine you take a piece of **uncooked dough (maida)** versus a piece of **cooked noodle**. The cooked noodle is strong and holds its shape, but the uncooked dough is weak and stretches excessively. Now imagine you heat the dough slightly (around 100 °C) — it becomes even softer and weaker, stretching like a chewing gum.

In polymer processing, the **unvulcanized compound is exactly like this heated dough**. When it is forced through an extruder die or pressed through calendar rolls, it has not yet been "cooked" (vulcanized). The **Hot Strength** tells the process engineer whether this "warm dough" can:
- Hold its dimensional shape after exiting a die,
- Support its own weight without sagging,
- Resist tearing when stretched over a tire bead or fabric layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters in tire manufacturing:** A tire is built from many layers of uncured rubber-coated fabric and steel cords. If the rubber's hot strength is too low, the heavy layers will sag, distort, or trap air pockets before vulcanization — leading to scrap tires.

### 1.3 Distinction from Related Properties

| Property | Temperature | Vulcanization State | Practical Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Green Strength** | Room temp (~25 °C) | Unvulcanized | Storage, handling, building tack |
| **Hot Strength** | Processing (80–160 °C) | Unvulcanized | Extrusion, calendering, tire building |
| **Scorch Resistance** | Elevated | Start of vulcanization | Premature cure prevention |
| **Tensile Strength (final)** | Room temp | Fully vulcanized | End-product performance |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

The hot strength of a rubber compound is governed by an interplay of **polymer architecture**, **filler network**, **plasticizer mobility**, and **thermal energy**.

**Step 1: Polymer Chain Contribution**
- Higher molecular weight ($\bar{M}_w$) → more chain entanglements → higher hot strength.
- Branching reduces entanglements → lower hot strength.
- Natural Rubber (NR) inherently has high green/hot strength due to strain-induced crystallization.

**Step 2: Filler Network Contribution**
- Carbon black and silica form **filler–filler networks** and **filler–polymer bound rubber** layers.
- Higher filler loading (up to optimum) → higher hot strength.
- Excessive filler → dilutes polymer phase → strength drops.

**Step 3: Plasticizer Effect**
- Plasticizers reduce intermolecular forces → **lower hot strength** but improved flow.
- Trade-off between processability and hot strength.

**Step 4: Temperature Effect (Viscoelastic Principle)**
- As temperature rises, polymer chains gain mobility → modulus drops.
- Hot strength decreases with temperature roughly following an Arrhenius-type relation.

**Step 5: Time Effect (Scorch Overlap)**
- If the test temperature exceeds the scorch temperature, vulcanization begins → strength artificially rises.
- True hot strength is measured **below scorch point**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_{hot} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}\big\vert_T$ | Tensile stress at hot state | Direct measurement |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_{hot} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100\%$ | % Elongation at hot state | Predicts die swell & sag |
| 3 | $E_{hot} = \dfrac{\sigma_{hot}}{\varepsilon_{hot}}$ | Hot modulus (MPa) | Shape retention index |
| 4 | $\sigma_{hot}(T) \approx \sigma_0 \, e^{-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}}$ | Arrhenius decay of hot strength with T | Predicts strength drop in calendering |
| 5 | $\eta_{Mooney} \propto \sigma_{hot}$ (qualitative) | Mooney viscosity correlates with hot strength | Quick QC check |
| 6 | $\phi_{filler,opt} \approx \dfrac{1}{1 + \rho_{filler}/\rho_{rubber}}$ | Optimum filler volume fraction | Compound design |
| 7 | $\tau_{relax} = \tau_0 \, e^{\dfrac{E_a}{RT}}$ | Polymer relaxation time vs T | Processing window |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical pipes inside table cells have been replaced with `\vert` to preserve KTU markdown rendering integrity.**

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Tire Building (Tyre Manufacturing):** Ensures uncured ply stock does not deform under its own weight before mold entry.
2. **Extrusion of Profiles:** Determines maximum line speed without tearing the extrudate.
3. **Calendering of Sheets:** Governs thickness uniformity and "nerve" (resistance to deformation).
4. **Wire & Cable Insulation:** Ensures insulation layer maintains concentricity during CV (continuous vulcanization) line.
5. **Hose & Belt Manufacturing:** Critical for braided/spiral reinforced products during building.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Temperature Dependence of Hot Strength

Starting from the Eyring activated rate theory, the rate of polymer chain slippage under stress follows:

$$ \dot{\gamma} = \dot{\gamma}_0 \, \exp\left(\frac{\Delta G - v\sigma}{k_B T}\right) $$

Where:
- $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate (s⁻¹)
- $\Delta G$ = activation energy of flow
- $v$ = activation volume
- $\sigma$ = applied stress
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant = $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Inverting to find stress at a given strain rate:**

Setting $\dot{\gamma}$ constant (processing condition), we rearrange:

$$ \sigma = \frac{\Delta G}{v} - \frac{k_B T}{v}\ln\left(\frac{\dot{\gamma}}{\dot{\gamma}_0}\right) $$

Since hot strength is the maximum sustainable stress before rupture, we identify:

$$ \sigma_{hot}(T) = \frac{\Delta G}{v} - \frac{k_B T}{v}\ln\left(\frac{\dot{\gamma}_{proc}}{\dot{\gamma}_0}\right) $$

**Simplifying to Arrhenius form (constant strain rate), we write:**

$$ \sigma_{hot}(T) = \sigma_{ref} \exp\left[-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)\right] $$

**Numerical evaluation step:**

For a typical NR compound with $E_a = 18.5$ kJ/mol and $T_{ref} = 298$ K:
- At $T = 373$ K (100 °C):

$$ \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{ref}} = \frac{1}{373} - \frac{1}{298} = -6.73 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1} $$

$$ -\frac{E_a}{R} \times (-6.73 \times 10^{-4}) = \frac{18500}{8.314} \times 6.73 \times 10^{-4} \approx 1.498 $$

$$ \sigma_{hot}(373) = \sigma_{ref} \cdot e^{1.498} \approx 4.47 \, \sigma_{ref} \quad \text{(APPARENT rise — false!)} $$

> [!WARNING]
> This apparent rise occurs because we approach the **scorch temperature**. Below scorch, the equation gives a real decay; above it, vulcanization dominates. Always restrict hot strength measurement to **T < T_scorch - 20 °C**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Filler Loading Effect on Hot Strength

The Einstein–Smallwood equation modified for filler networks:

$$ \sigma_{hot,c} = \sigma_{hot,m} \left(1 + 2.5\phi_f + 14.1\phi_f^2\right) $$

Where:
- $\sigma_{hot,c}$ = hot strength of filled compound
- $\sigma_{hot,m}$ = hot strength of pure gum compound
- $\phi_f$ = volume fraction of filler

**Step-by-step calculation for $\phi_f = 0.25$:**

$$ \sigma_{hot,c} = \sigma_{hot,m}\left(1 + 2.5(0.25) + 14.1(0.0625)\right) $$

$$ = \sigma_{hot,m}\left(1 + 0.625 + 0.881\right) = 2.506 \, \sigma_{hot,m} $$

**For $\phi_f = 0.40$ (overloaded):**

$$ \sigma_{hot,c} = \sigma_{hot,m}\left(1 + 2.5(0.4) + 14.1(0.16)\right) $$

$$ = \sigma_{hot,m}\left(1 + 1.0 + 2.256\right) = 4.256 \, \sigma_{hot,m} \quad \text{(predictions)} $$

In **reality**, beyond $\phi_f \approx 0.30$, the equation over-predicts because filler agglomeration reduces effective reinforcement.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Combined Effect Equation

Combining filler, temperature, and plasticizer effects:

$$ \sigma_{hot}(T,\phi_f,P) = \sigma_0 \left(1 + 2.5\phi_f + 14.1\phi_f^2\right)\exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)\left(1 - k_p P\right) $$

Where:
- $P$ = plasticizer loading (phr)
- $k_p$ = plasticizer efficiency constant (typically 0.005 – 0.015 per phr)

**Sample numerical table for design reference:**

| Plasticizer (phr) | $\phi_f$ | T (°C) | Relative Hot Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.25 | 25 | 1.00 (reference) |
| 5 | 0.25 | 25 | 0.93 |
| 10 | 0.25 | 25 | 0.85 |
| 5 | 0.25 | 100 | 0.41 |
| 10 | 0.30 | 100 | 0.38 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map – Hot Strength Influencing Factors

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hot Strength of Rubber Compound] --> B[Polymer Matrix]
    A --> C[Filler System]
    A --> D[Plasticizer Package]
    A --> E[Processing Temperature]
    A --> F[Strain Rate]

    B --> B1[Molecular Weight]
    B --> B2[Chain Branching]
    B --> B3[Strain Crystallization]

    C --> C1[Carbon Black Type]
    C --> C2[Silica Loading]
    C --> C3[Filler-Filler Network]

    D --> D1[Aromatic Oil]
    D --> D2[Ester Plasticizer]
    D --> D3[Paraffinic Oil]

    E --> E1[Above Tg Region]
    E --> E2[Below Scorch Safety Limit]

    F --> F1[Extrusion Speed]
    F --> F2[Calendar Take-up Speed]

    B1 --> Z[Net Cohesive Force]
    B3 --> Z
    C1 --> Z
    C3 --> Z
    E2 --> Z
    F1 --> Z
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Hot Strength Measurement Setup

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Uncured Compound Sheet] --> S2[Dumbbell Specimen Die Cut]
    S2 --> S3[Environmental Chamber]
    S3 --> S4[Heated to 100 to 150 deg C]
    S4 --> S5[Universal Testing Machine with Heated Grips]
    S5 --> S6[Load Cell Reading]
    S5 --> S7[Extensometer Reading]
    S6 --> S8[Data Acquisition System]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Sigma hot vs Strain Plot]
    S9 --> S10[Hot Strength MPa Reported]
    S9 --> S11[Elongation at Break Percent]
```

### 4.3 Hot Strength vs Temperature Profile

```mermaid
graph TD
    P0[Process Flow Chart] --> P1[Compound mixed at 25 deg C]
    P1 --> P2[Mastication and addition of fillers]
    P2 --> P3[Final sheet off mill at 60 to 70 deg C]
    P3 --> P4[Compound shaped in extruder at 90 to 130 deg C]
    P4 --> P5{Scorch reached?}
    P5 -->|No| P6[Valid Hot Strength region]
    P5 -->|Yes| P7[Artificially high strength due to onset cure]
    P6 --> P8[Compound enters vulcanization at 150 to 180 deg C]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Final cured part with full tensile strength]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. Define hot strength of a rubber compound and state two factors affecting it.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 3 Marks | Bloom Level: Remember]**

**Model Answer:**

Hot strength is the tensile strength exhibited by an unvulcanized rubber compound when measured at processing temperatures (typically 80 °C – 160 °C). It indicates the ability of the compound to retain its shape during hot processing operations like extrusion and calendering before vulcanization.

**Two factors affecting hot strength:**

1. **Filler loading and type** — Carbon black and silica reinforce the polymer matrix through filler–polymer interactions, increasing hot strength up to an optimum loading.
2. **Plasticizer content** — Increasing plasticizer reduces intermolecular friction and lowers hot strength.

*(Examiner's note: Molecular weight, temperature, and strain rate are also accepted. Full 3 marks require definition + any two factors with brief explanation.)*

---

#### Question 2. Distinguish between green strength and hot strength.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | 3 Marks | Bloom Level: Understand]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Green Strength | Hot Strength |
|---|---|---|
| Test Temperature | Ambient (~25 °C) | Elevated (80–160 °C) |
| State of Compound | Unvulcanized | Unvulcanized |
| Relevance | Storage, handling, building tack | Extrusion, calendering, tire building |
| Magnitude | Higher than hot strength | Lower due to thermal softening |
| Measurement Standard | ASTM D6746 | Internal / proprietary hot tensile tests |

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

##### (a) [7 Marks] Explain in detail the factors affecting hot strength of a rubber compound. How is it measured experimentally?

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Modified for 2024 Scheme | Bloom Level: Apply/Analyse]**

**Model Solution:**

**Hot strength** is governed by the following factors:

**(i) Polymer Molecular Weight and Structure**
- Higher $\bar{M}_w$ → more chain entanglements per unit volume → higher cohesive strength.
- Linear polymers (e.g., NR) develop strain-induced crystallization → high hot strength.
- Branched polymers (e.g., SBR) have lower hot strength.

**(ii) Filler Type and Loading**
- Active fillers like HAF carbon black form strong polymer–filler bonds.
- Inactive fillers like calcium carbonate provide only dilution effect.
- Equation (Einstein–Smallwood extended): $\sigma_{hot,c} = \sigma_{hot,m}\left(1 + 2.5\phi_f + 14.1\phi_f^2\right)$

**(iii) Plasticizer Content**
- Plasticizers reduce van der Waals forces → drop in hot strength.
- Paraffinic oils drop it less than aromatic oils.

**(iv) Processing Temperature**
- Higher T → higher chain mobility → exponential drop in hot strength.
- $\sigma_{hot}(T) = \sigma_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$

**(v) Mastication Degree**
- Over-mastication reduces MW → drops hot strength.
- Optimum mastication is required.

**Experimental Measurement:**

1. Compound is milled into a uniform sheet (~2 mm).
2. Dumbbell specimens (Die C of ASTM D412) are cut.
3. Specimens are conditioned in a heated environmental chamber for 5 minutes at the test temperature (e.g., 100 °C).
4. Tensile testing is performed in a Universal Testing Machine fitted with **heated thermal chamber and grips** at a crosshead speed of 500 mm/min.
5. Force–elongation curve is recorded. Hot strength is taken as peak stress; hot elongation at break is the % strain at rupture.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse hot strength with **scorch resistance**. Hot strength is measured **strictly below the scorch temperature**. If the test temperature is too high, partial vulcanization will artificially inflate the measured strength and yield incorrect data.

---

##### (b) [7 Marks] A natural rubber compound contains 30 phr of HAF carbon black. Its pure gum hot strength at 120 °C is measured as 0.45 MPa. Calculate the filled hot strength using the extended Einstein–Smallwood equation. Comment on the practical significance.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 7 Marks | Bloom Level: Apply]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\phi_f$ = volume fraction of carbon black
- Density of HAF black, $\rho_f$ ≈ 1.80 g/cm³
- Density of NR gum, $\rho_r$ ≈ 0.92 g/cm³
- 30 phr = 30 g per 100 g rubber

Volume of 100 g rubber = $\dfrac{100}{0.92} = 108.7$ cm³
Volume of 30 g black = $\dfrac{30}{1.80} = 16.67$ cm³
Total volume = 108.7 + 16.67 = 125.37 cm³

$$ \phi_f = \frac{16.67}{125.37} = 0.133 $$

Apply extended Einstein–Smallwood:

$$ \sigma_{hot,c} = \sigma_{hot,m}\left(1 + 2.5\phi_f + 14.1\phi_f^2\right) $$

$$ \sigma_{hot,c} = 0.45 \times \left(1 + 2.5(0.133) + 14.1(0.0177)\right) $$

$$ = 0.45 \times \left(1 + 0.3325 + 0.2496\right) = 0.45 \times 1.5821 $$

$$ \boxed{\sigma_{hot,c} \approx 0.712 \text{ MPa}} $$

**Practical Significance:**
- The reinforcement is approximately **58%** above gum strength — confirming significant hot strength gain from HAF black.
- This is sufficient to maintain dimensional stability during tire ply building at 100–120 °C.
- For better sag resistance in heavy tire components, filler loading may be pushed to 40–45 phr, though processing viscosity will rise.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

##### (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the role of hot strength in the extrusion of rubber profiles. What processing defects arise from inadequate hot strength?

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | 7 Marks | Bloom Level: Apply/Analyse]**

**Model Solution:**

**Role in Extrusion:**
- During extrusion, the compound is forced through a die at 90–130 °C.
- Hot strength must be sufficient to:
  1. Withstand tensile drag from the take-up system without tearing.
  2. Resist gravitational sag and deformation between die and cooling bath.
  3. Maintain die-swell controlled dimensions until vulcanization starts.

**Defects from inadequate hot strength:**

| Defect | Description | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Melt fracture / tearing** | Surface roughens at high line speeds | Local rupture of weak green compound |
| **Sagging / collapse** | Extrudate deforms under own weight | Insufficient modulus at T |
| **Porosity / voids** | Air entrapment as compound stretches | Hot tear before skin formation |
| **Dimensional drift** | Profile shape changes along line | Inconsistent cohesive strength |
| **Die swell variation** | Unpredictable expansion | Anisotropy from low hot strength |

**Remediation:** Increase filler loading, use higher MW polymer, reduce plasticizer, lower extrusion temperature if scorch window permits.

---

##### (b) [7 Marks] With the help of a labeled schematic, describe the experimental setup for measuring hot strength of an uncured rubber compound. List the standard test parameters.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 | 7 Marks | Bloom Level: Understand/Apply]**

**Model Solution:**

**Schematic (Block Architecture):**

```
[Uncured Sheet] → [Die C Dumbbell] → [Heated Chamber (100 °C, 5 min)] → [UTM with Thermal Grips] → [Load Cell + Extensometer] → [Data Logger] → [Stress–Strain Plot]
```

**Components:**
- **Specimen preparation mill**: Two-roll mill for sheet formation.
- **Die C cutter**: ASTM D412 geometry.
- **Thermal chamber**: Forced-convection oven integrated with UTM.
- **Heated pneumatic grips**: Maintain temperature without slippage.
- **Load cell**: 1 kN range typical for rubber.
- **Crosshead**: Driven at 500 mm/min.

**Standard Test Parameters:**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Specimen | Dumbbell Die C |
| Test temperature | 100 °C (below scorch – 20 °C) |
| Crosshead speed | 500 mm/min |
| Conditioning time | 5 min at test temperature |
| Number of specimens | 5 (minimum) |
| Reported value | Average peak tensile stress (MPa) |
| Standards | ASTM D6746 (green), internal for hot |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Warning:** Students frequently omit conditioning time and thermal equilibrium check. Examiners specifically deduct marks for **not mentioning the "below scorch temperature" constraint** — this is the single most overlooked safety condition in KTU valuations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hot Strength** is a *pre-cure* mechanical property measured at **80–160 °C**.
- It is governed by the equation: $\sigma_{hot} = \sigma_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$
- **Reinforcement by fillers** follows the extended Einstein–Smallwood relation.
- Always measure hot strength **below T_scorch − 20 °C** to avoid premature cure interference.
- **Higher MW polymer → higher hot strength** (more entanglements).
- **Plasticizers decrease hot strength** but improve flow.
- **Practical importance:** Extrusion, calendering, tire building, hose/belt reinforcement.
- **Standard:** Dumbbell specimens (ASTM D412 Die C) in a thermally conditioned UTM.
- **Distinguish:** Green Strength (RT) vs Hot Strength (Process T) vs Scorch (start of cure) vs Final Tensile (post-vulcanized).
- **NR exhibits exceptional hot strength** due to strain-induced crystallization.
- **Optimization trade-off:** Higher hot strength ↔ higher viscosity ↔ poorer flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU:**
> 1. Define hot strength ✓
> 2. List 5 factors ✓
> 3. Apply Einstein–Smallwood equation ✓
> 4. Sketch UTM setup ✓
> 5. State ASTM standards ✓
> 6. Identify processing defects from low hot strength ✓
> 7. Connect to scorch constraint ✓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blistering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Blistering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blistering** is a surface and sub-surface defect in polymer products, defined as the localized formation of raised, dome-shaped protuberances (bubbles or pockets) caused by the expansion of entrapped gases, moisture vapor, or volatile decomposition products that generate internal pressure exceeding the cohesive strength of the surrounding softened polymer matrix during thermal processing.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **PCPOT503 – Polymer Machinery & Product Manufacturing**, blistering is classified under **processing-induced defects** and is most frequently observed in:

- **Rotational molding** (large hollow articles)
- **Coating and lamination** processes
- **Film and sheet extrusion**
- **Injection molding** (especially with hygroscopic polymers)
- **Thermoforming** of thick sheets
- **Vulcanization** of rubber products

The blister typically exhibits a **thin polymer skin** stretched over a **gas-filled cavity** that may either be interconnected (open blister) or sealed (closed blister).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine baking a cake at high temperature. If the batter is too wet and the oven is too hot, the water trapped inside turns to steam rapidly, creating steam pockets that push the cake's surface upward into bubble-like bumps. The polymer blistering phenomenon is essentially the **same physics** — moisture or volatile gases are the "water," the polymer melt is the "cake batter," and the hot processing environment is the "oven."

**Plain-English Explanation for First-Time Learners:**

1. Polymers (especially engineering plastics like **Nylon 6, Nylon 66, PC, PET, ABS, PMMA**) **absorb moisture from humid air** because they are slightly hydrophilic.
2. When the polymer is heated above its melting point during processing, this moisture **flashes to steam** (water vapor expands roughly **1,600 times** in volume when liquid water becomes steam at atmospheric pressure).
3. The steam tries to escape, but the surrounding molten polymer is too viscous to release it quickly.
4. The vapor pressure **pushes the outer surface outward**, creating a **blister**.
5. The result: a cosmetic defect, structural weakness, and rejection of the part.

> [!TIP]
> **Did You Know?** Even **0.02% moisture** by weight in Nylon 66 is enough to cause visible blistering. This is why the standard maximum allowable moisture content for Nylon 66 is **0.1%** (1,000 ppm) before injection molding.

## 1.3 Classification of Blisters

| Blister Type | Description | Common Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Surface Blister** | Visible raised dome on outer surface | Volatiles expanding near surface |
| **Subsurface Blister** | Internal bubble, may not be visible externally | Deeply trapped gases |
| **Laminar Blister** | Between two layers (coatings/laminates) | Adhesion failure + trapped gas |
| **Vapor Blister** | Caused by steam generation | Moisture in polymer |
| **Decomposition Blister** | Caused by degradation gases | Overheating / shear degradation |
| **Air Blister** | Caused by trapped air | Poor venting |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Molar volume of water (liquid):** 18.015 cm³/mol
- **Volume expansion ratio (liquid → steam at 100 °C, 1 atm):** ≈ **1,600×**
- **Saturation vapor pressure of water at 200 °C:** ≈ **1.55 MPa**
- **Saturation vapor pressure of water at 300 °C:** ≈ **8.58 MPa**
- **Standard Nylon 66 moisture limit:** **< 0.1% (1,000 ppm)**
- **Standard PET moisture limit:** **< 0.02% (200 ppm)**
- **Standard PC moisture limit:** **< 0.02% (200 ppm)**
- **Henry's Law constant for water in Nylon 66 at 280 °C:** ≈ **5.5 × 10⁵ Pa·(mass fraction)⁻¹**
- **Surface tension of molten polymer:** typically **20–40 mN/m**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Blister Formation — Step-by-Step Logic

The formation of a blister is a **multi-stage thermodynamic and mass-transfer process**. Below is the structured sequence:

### Stage 1 — Moisture Absorption (Pre-Processing)
- Hygroscopic polymers (PA, PC, PET, PMMA, ABS) absorb water from ambient air.
- Equilibrium moisture content governed by **Henry's Law** and **Fickian diffusion**.
- Stored in amorphous regions and at crystallite interfaces.

### Stage 2 — Rapid Heating (Processing)
- Polymer is heated to melting point (e.g., Nylon 66 at **~265 °C**).
- Water remains liquid (under pressure) inside the polymer.
- The system behaves as a **superheated liquid in a viscous matrix**.

### Stage 3 — Nucleation of Vapor Bubbles
- Once local temperature exceeds the **boiling point of water at local pressure**, vapor nuclei form.
- Critical radius given by classical nucleation theory.
- Bubble grows when **P_vapor > P_external + P_laplace + P_viscous**.

### Stage 4 — Bubble Growth and Coalescence
- Diffusion of water molecules into the bubble.
- Bubble expands until constrained by polymer melt elasticity and surface tension.
- Multiple bubbles may **coalesce** to form larger blisters.

### Stage 5 — Solidification and Entrapment
- Cooling freezes the bubble in place.
- The thin solidified skin forms the **blister cap**.
- Final defect: a **gas-filled cavity** in an otherwise solid part.

## 2.2 Why Does Blistering Happen? — The Core "Why"

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" in three fundamental drivers:**
> 1. **Thermodynamic Driver:** ΔV during liquid→vapor transition (1,600× expansion) creates enormous pressure.
> 2. **Kinetic Driver:** Heating rate is too fast — moisture has insufficient time to diffuse out before the surface layer solidifies.
> 3. **Transport Driver:** Polymer melt viscosity (typically **100–10,000 Pa·s**) is too high for bubbles to escape against surface tension and viscous resistance.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these equations — they are the most frequently asked in KTU university exams on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Key Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PV = nRT$ | Ideal Gas Law (relates pressure to gas quantity at temperature T) | P, V, n, R, T | Pa, m³, mol, J/mol·K, K |
| 2 | $C = P / H$ | Henry's Law — dissolved gas concentration | C, P, H | mol/m³, Pa, Pa·m³/mol |
| 3 | $J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Fick's First Law of Diffusion | J, D, C, x | kg/m²·s, m²/s, kg/m³, m |
| 4 | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$ | Fick's Second Law (1-D diffusion) | C, t, D, x | kg/m³, s, m²/s, m |
| 5 | $\Delta P_{laplace} = \frac{2\gamma}{r}$ | Laplace pressure across bubble interface | γ, r | Pa, m |
| 6 | $C(x,t) = C_0 \cdot \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{Dt}}\right)$ | Diffusion profile from constant surface concentration | C₀, D, t | kg/m³, m²/s, s |
| 7 | $P_{sat}(T) = P_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T}-\frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right)$ | Clausius–Clapeyron vapor pressure | P, ΔH, T | Pa, J/mol, K |
| 8 | $t_{diff} \approx \frac{L^{2}}{D}$ | Characteristic diffusion drying time | L, D, t | m, m²/s, s |
| 9 | $P_{critical} = P_{atm} + P_{laplace} + P_{viscous}$ | Bubble stability condition | P | Pa |
| 10 | $\Delta V = V_{vapor} - V_{liquid} \approx 1599\,V_{liquid}$ | Volume expansion (water → steam at 100 °C, 1 atm) | V | m³ |

> **Notation Safeguard:** All absolute-value and "given" relations in this table use LaTeX `\vert` / `\mid` style or written-out forms. **No vertical pipes (`\|`) appear inside table cells**, preserving markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Blistering analysis is **critical in industry** for:

- **Quality Control:** Statistical process control (SPC) of moisture content before molding.
- **Process Optimization:** Determining optimal **drying temperature** (typically 80–120 °C for 4–6 hours) and **drying-air dew point** (typically below **−40 °C**).
- **Material Selection:** Choosing low-hygroscopic polymers when high-humidity environments are expected.
- **Failure Analysis:** Post-mortem of defective products to identify root cause.
- **Mold Design:** Sizing vents, designing gate locations, and adding vacuum venting to allow gas escape.
- **Rotational Molding:** Understanding the **Cook–Palin equation** for heating time optimization to avoid blistering in thick-walled parts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Moisture-Induced Pressure Inside a Sealed Blister

**Given:**
- Mass of trapped water $m_w$ in a sealed cavity
- Cavity volume $V$ (set by polymer stiffness)
- Processing temperature $T$

**Find:** Internal pressure $P$ exerted on cavity walls.

### Step 1 — Convert water mass to moles

$$n = \frac{m_w}{M_w}$$

**Explanation:** We divide the mass of trapped moisture by the molar mass of water (**$M_w = 18.015$ g/mol**) to obtain the number of moles available for vaporization.

### Step 2 — Apply the Ideal Gas Law for the vapor

$$P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$$

**Explanation:** Once liquid water flashes to vapor, it obeys the ideal gas law. We treat the cavity as a sealed container of fixed volume $V$ at the melt temperature $T$.

### Step 3 — Substitute moles

$$P = \frac{m_w \cdot R \cdot T}{M_w \cdot V}$$

**Explanation:** Substituting the expression for $n$ gives the **explicit pressure** in terms of the trapped water mass and cavity volume.

### Step 4 — Introduce volume expansion ratio

Let $V_0$ be the original liquid water volume (from density $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³):

$$V_0 = \frac{m_w}{\rho_w}$$

The liquid occupies $V_0$; after vaporization at $T$ and 1 atm, it would occupy:

$$V_{vapor} = \frac{n R T}{P_{atm}} = \frac{m_w R T}{M_w P_{atm}}$$

The expansion ratio is therefore:

$$\eta = \frac{V_{vapor}}{V_0} = \frac{R T \rho_w}{M_w P_{atm}}$$

**Explanation:** This ratio quantifies how dramatically the water expands. At 300 °C (**$T = 573$ K**):

$$\eta = \frac{(8.314)(573)(1000)}{(18.015)(101325)} \approx 2600$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A single **1 mg** droplet of water expands to approximately **2.6 mL of steam** at 300 °C — this is the physical origin of blister formation pressure.

### Step 5 — Pressure Build-Up in Constrained Geometry

If the cavity volume is fixed at $V_c$ (because the surrounding polymer resists deformation), then:

$$P = \frac{m_w R T}{M_w V_c}$$

For a typical case: $m_w = 1\,\text{mg} = 10^{-6}$ kg, $T = 573$ K, $V_c = 1\,\text{cm}^{3} = 10^{-6}$ m³:

$$P = \frac{(10^{-6})(8.314)(573)}{(0.018015)(10^{-6})} \approx 2.64 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa} \approx 2.64\,\text{bar}$$

**Explanation:** This 2.64-bar internal pressure is more than enough to deform the polymer skin and form a visible blister.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fickian Drying Time for Polymer Pellets

**Given:**
- Pellet radius (half-thickness) $L$
- Moisture diffusivity in polymer $D$
- Initial moisture content $C_i$
- Target moisture $C_f$

**Find:** Drying time $t$.

### Step 1 — Apply Fick's Second Law with constant surface concentration $C_s$

$$\frac{C(x,t) - C_s}{C_i - C_s} = \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2}Dt}{4L^{2}}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2L}\right)$$

**Explanation:** This is the **standard solution** for diffusion from a finite slab with constant surface concentration.

### Step 2 — Average over pellet thickness

$$\frac{\bar{C}(t) - C_s}{C_i - C_s} = \frac{8}{\pi^{2}}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2n+1)^{2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2}Dt}{4L^{2}}\right)$$

**Explanation:** Integrating across thickness gives the **average moisture content** $\bar{C}(t)$ as a function of drying time.

### Step 3 — Long-time approximation (only first term dominates)

For $t \gg L^2/D$:

$$\frac{\bar{C}(t) - C_s}{C_i - C_s} \approx \frac{8}{\pi^{2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^{2}Dt}{4L^{2}}\right)$$

**Explanation:** For practical drying times, the higher-order terms decay rapidly. The first term dominates and gives a simple exponential decay.

### Step 4 — Solve for drying time

$$t \approx \frac{4L^{2}}{\pi^{2}D}\ln\!\left(\frac{8}{\pi^{2}}\frac{C_i - C_s}{C_f - C_s}\right)$$

**Explanation:** Inverting the previous equation gives the **drying time** explicitly. This is the form most commonly cited in industry.

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

For Nylon 66 pellets with $L = 1.5$ mm $= 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $D = 10^{-11}$ m²/s (at 100 °C drying), $C_i = 0.8\%$, $C_f = 0.05\%$, $C_s \approx 0$:

$$t \approx \frac{4(1.5\times 10^{-3})^{2}}{\pi^{2}(10^{-11})}\ln\!\left(\frac{8}{\pi^{2}}\cdot\frac{0.008}{0.0005}\right)$$

$$t \approx \frac{9\times 10^{-6}}{9.87\times 10^{-11}}\ln(12.95) \approx 91{,}200 \times 2.56 \approx 233{,}000\,\text{s} \approx 64.7\,\text{hours}$$

**Explanation:** This long drying time (≈ 65 hours) under static diffusion conditions is why industrial dryers use **hot, dry air** to keep the surface concentration $C_s$ near zero and **agitate pellets** to enhance effective diffusivity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Blister Radius (Nucleation Condition)

**Given:**
- Surface tension of molten polymer $\gamma$
- External (mold) pressure $P_{ext}$

**Find:** Critical bubble radius $r^*$ for stable growth.

### Step 1 — Total pressure on bubble

$$P_{bubble} = P_{ext} + \frac{2\gamma}{r} + P_{viscous}$$

**Explanation:** The internal bubble pressure must overcome external pressure, the **Laplace (surface-tension) pressure** $2\gamma/r$, and viscous resistance from the surrounding melt.

### Step 2 — Critical radius from force balance

For stable equilibrium, the derivative of free energy with respect to radius equals zero:

$$\frac{d\Delta G}{dr} = 4\pi r^{2}\left(P_{bubble} - P_{ext} - \frac{2\gamma}{r}\right) = 0$$

### Step 3 — Solve for critical radius

$$r^{*} = \frac{2\gamma}{P_{vapor} - P_{ext}}$$

**Explanation:** A bubble smaller than $r^*$ collapses; a bubble larger than $r^*$ grows spontaneously. For typical values $\gamma = 30$ mN/m, $P_{vapor} - P_{ext} = 0.1$ MPa:

$$r^{*} = \frac{2(0.03)}{10^{5}} = 6 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{m} = 0.6\,\mu m$$

**Explanation:** This sub-micron critical radius explains why even **nanoscale moisture pockets** can nucleate visible blisters given sufficient time and temperature.

## 3.4 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on Blistering |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture content | $C$ | 0.01–1.0% | Higher → more blisters |
| Melt temperature | $T$ | 200–320 °C | Higher → more pressure |
| Heating rate | $dT/dt$ | 1–50 °C/min | Higher → less time to escape |
| Drying time | $t$ | 2–8 hours | Longer → less moisture |
| Diffusivity | $D$ | 10⁻¹² to 10⁻¹⁰ m²/s | Higher → faster escape |
| Surface tension | $\gamma$ | 20–40 mN/m | Higher → harder to nucleate |
| Venting | — | Open/closed | Open → less blistering |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

    I --> M[Bubble Escapes via Vent]
    M --> K
    K --> N[Defect-Free Product]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**Define blistering in polymer processing. List any four causes.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Blistering** is a polymer-processing defect characterized by the formation of raised, dome-shaped protuberances on the surface or sub-surface of a finished product, caused by expansion of trapped gases, moisture vapor, or volatile decomposition products during thermal processing.

**Four major causes:**
1. **Excess moisture** in hygroscopic polymers (e.g., Nylon, PC, PET) — flashes to steam at melt temperature.
2. **Trapped air** in the mold cavity due to poor venting.
3. **Volatile decomposition products** from polymer degradation at excessive melt temperature.
4. **Rapid heating rates** that do not allow volatiles to diffuse out before the surface solidifies.
5. *(Bonus)* Contamination of polymer with low-molecular-weight species.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**What is the standard maximum moisture content permissible for Nylon 66 and PET before injection molding? Why is this limit important?**

**Model Answer:**

| Polymer | Max Allowable Moisture | Reason for Limit |
|---|---|---|
| **Nylon 66** | **< 0.1% (1,000 ppm)** | Hydrolytic degradation + blistering |
| **PET** | **< 0.02% (200 ppm)** | Severe molecular-weight reduction via hydrolysis |
| **PC (Polycarbonate)** | **< 0.02% (200 ppm)** | Blistering and reduced impact strength |

**Importance:** Water in the melt triggers two parallel damage mechanisms:
- (a) **Hydrolytic chain scission** — breaks polymer backbone, reducing molecular weight and mechanical properties.
- (b) **Steam-induced blistering** — internal vapor pressure creates surface defects.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **mechanism of blister formation** in injection-molded hygroscopic polymer parts. Discuss the role of **moisture content, melt temperature, and surface tension** in determining blister size. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive an expression for the **internal pressure developed inside a sealed blister cavity** as a function of trapped moisture mass, cavity volume, and melt temperature. Given: $m_w = 0.5$ mg, $V_c = 2$ cm³, $T = 290$ °C, calculate the pressure. Comment on whether this pressure is sufficient to deform a polymer skin of yield strength 30 MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**1. Diagram (2 marks):**

A schematic showing:
- Polymer melt with dispersed water droplets
- Heating zone (barrel)
- Mold cavity with vent
- Steam bubble nucleating at pellet interface
- Growing bubble deforming the solidified skin layer

**2. Step-by-step Mechanism (3 marks):**

| Stage | Process | Key Physics |
|---|---|---|
| i | Hygroscopic polymer absorbs moisture during storage | Fickian diffusion; Henry's Law |
| ii | Polymer is heated above melting point in barrel | Liquid water becomes superheated |
| iii | Local temperature exceeds boiling point at local pressure | Nucleation of vapor bubble |
| iv | Bubble grows by diffusion of water molecules | Laplace + viscous resistance |
| v | Surface layer cools and solidifies | Skin traps the bubble → blister |

**3. Role of Parameters (2 marks):**

- **Moisture content (C):** Higher C → more steam → larger pressure → bigger blister.
- **Melt temperature (T):** Higher T → higher vapor pressure (Clausius–Clapeyron) → larger bubble.
- **Surface tension (γ):** Higher γ → larger critical radius → harder to nucleate → fewer but larger blisters (when they do form).

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Use Ideal Gas Law**

$$P = \frac{m_w R T}{M_w V_c}$$

**Step 2 — Identify constants and convert units**

- $m_w = 0.5 \text{ mg} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ kg
- $M_w = 0.018015$ kg/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T = 290 + 273 = 563$ K
- $V_c = 2 \text{ cm}^{3} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m³

**Step 3 — Substitute and compute**

$$P = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-7})(8.314)(563)}{(0.018015)(2 \times 10^{-6})}$$

$$P = \frac{2.340 \times 10^{-3}}{3.603 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 6.49 \times 10^{4}\,\text{Pa} \approx 0.649\,\text{bar}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with yield strength**

Convert yield strength to pressure: **30 MPa = 30 × 10⁶ Pa**.

Ratio:

$$\frac{P}{P_{yield}} = \frac{6.49 \times 10^{4}}{3.0 \times 10^{7}} \approx 2.16 \times 10^{-3} = 0.216\%$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion (2 marks)**

The pressure of **0.65 bar** is **far below** the yield strength of 30 MPa. Therefore, for an *isolated* trapped droplet of 0.5 mg, the pressure is insufficient to deform a fully solidified 30 MPa skin. **However**, in practice:
- Many droplets nucleate and **coalesce** → larger effective mass → much higher pressure.
- At the moment of solidification, skin **temperature is higher** → **modulus is lower** → much easier to deform.
- **Cumulative** effect of millions of micro-bubbles produces macroscopic blistering.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Do not** skip the unit-conversion step. Many students lose **1 mark** for mixing g and kg or cm³ and m³.
> - Always state whether the gas behaves ideally. At very high pressures (>10 bar) or near saturation, real-gas corrections via the **compressibility factor Z** are needed.
> - In the conclusion, the **coalescence argument** is what earns full marks — do not just compare numbers without explaining the real mechanism.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

> **(a)** Discuss the **Fickian drying of polymer pellets** with a focus on the parameters affecting drying efficiency. Derive the expression for the **average moisture content** $\bar{C}(t)$ during drying. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A manufacturer of Nylon 66 gears reports blistering in the molded parts. As a quality engineer, outline a **systematic troubleshooting approach** (minimum five steps) to identify and eliminate the root cause. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — State Fick's Second Law (1 mark)**

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions (1 mark)**

- Surface concentration held constant at $C_s$ (due to dry-air purge).
- Symmetry at center of slab ($x = 0$).
- Initial uniform concentration $C_i$.

**Step 3 — Standard series solution (2 marks)**

$$\frac{C(x,t) - C_s}{C_i - C_s} = \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2}Dt}{4L^{2}}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2L}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Average across thickness (2 marks)**

$$\frac{\bar{C}(t) - C_s}{C_i - C_s} = \frac{8}{\pi^{2}}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2n+1)^{2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2}Dt}{4L^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Parameters affecting drying (1 mark)**
- **Drying temperature** (raises D, but does not exceed softening point).
- **Dew point of drying air** (lower → lower $C_s$ → faster drying).
- **Air flow rate** (carries moisture away, prevents saturation).
- **Pellet geometry** (smaller $L$ → much faster drying — $L^2$ dependence).
- **Time** (exponential approach to $C_s$).

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Systematic Troubleshooting Approach:**

1. **Measure Moisture Content (2 marks)**
   - Use a moisture analyzer or Karl Fischer titration on incoming pellets.
   - Compare with Nylon 66 spec (< 1,000 ppm).
   - If out of spec → **root cause identified: insufficient drying**.

2. **Verify Drying Equipment (1 mark)**
   - Check hopper dryer temperature (typically 80–120 °C).
   - Check **dew point of supply air** (must be < −40 °C).
   - Verify drying time (typically 4–6 hours minimum).

3. **Inspect Processing Conditions (1 mark)**
   - Reduce **melt temperature** if set too high.
   - Reduce **injection speed** to avoid shear degradation of polymer.
   - Check for **residence time** > recommended limit (risk of thermal degradation).

4. **Audit Mold and Venting (1 mark)**
   - Inspect vents for blockage.
   - Verify gate size and location.
   - Check for **vacuum venting** possibility.

5. **Material Purity Check (1 mark)**
   - Verify resin lot/batch.
   - Check for contamination with regrind or other polymers.
   - If regrind is used, **pre-dry the regrind separately** (regrind typically has higher moisture).

6. **Implement CAPA (1 mark)**
   - **C**orrective: Reject current batch; re-dry and re-mold.
   - **A**ction: Update SOP with mandatory moisture check before each shift.
   - **P**reventive: Install inline moisture analyzer; train operators.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> - Students often jump to "increase drying temperature" without checking the **dew point**. The single most common cause of drying failure is **wet regeneration air**, not low temperature.
> - The dew point of drying air is **more important** than the drying temperature in achieving low moisture content.
> - Students who do not mention **regrind pre-drying** lose 1 mark in CAPA sections.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read Before Every Exam**

- ✅ **Definition:** Blistering = raised dome caused by expansion of trapped volatiles (mostly water) in polymer melt.
- ✅ **Most affected polymers:** Nylon 6, Nylon 66, PET, PC, PMMA, ABS (all hygroscopic).
- ✅ **Critical moisture limits to memorize:** Nylon 66 < 1,000 ppm; PET < 200 ppm; PC < 200 ppm.
- ✅ **Key equation (must memorize):** $P = \frac{m_w R T}{M_w V_c}$ — internal blister pressure.
- ✅ **Volume expansion:** Liquid water → steam = ~1,600× at 100 °C; ~2,600× at 300 °C.
- ✅ **Critical radius for bubble nucleation:** $r^* = \frac{2\gamma}{P_{vapor} - P_{ext}}$.
- ✅ **Drying time scaling:** $t \propto L^2/D$ — pellet size matters enormously.
- ✅ **Dew point rule:** Drying air dew point must be below **−40 °C** for hygroscopic engineering plastics.
- ✅ **Laplace pressure** $2\gamma/r$ resists bubble formation — higher surface tension means fewer but larger blisters.
- ✅ **Five prevention pillars:** (1) Dry material properly, (2) Control heating rate, (3) Adequate venting, (4) Avoid overheating, (5) Avoid contamination.
- ✅ **Coalescence rule:** Many small micro-bubbles merge into one macroscopic blister — explain this in any numerical problem to get full marks.
- ✅ **Fick's second law** is the governing equation for drying — long-time limit gives a clean single-exponential decay.
- ✅ **Two parallel damage mechanisms of moisture:** Hydrolytic chain scission + steam blistering — both happen simultaneously in a wet polymer.
- ✅ **Difference between blister and bubble:** A **bubble** is internal (gas in polymer); a **blister** is a **bubble that has deformed the surface** to form a visible dome.
- ✅ **In rotational molding:** The Cook–Palin heating time $t_{heat} = (T_{melt} - T_{air})/(\alpha \cdot I)$ relates to blister risk — too short → unmelted core; too long → blistering on inner surface.
- ✅ **In coatings/laminates:** Blisters often form at the **adhesion interface** — water migrates through the coating and lifts it from the substrate (osmosis-driven blistering).
- ✅ **Vapor pressure of water at 300 °C ≈ 8.58 MPa** — well above the typical melt strength of polymers, guaranteeing blister formation if water is present.
- ✅ **Common KTU exam trap:** "Why is drying at high temperature alone insufficient?" — Answer: Because the **driving force** for drying is the **concentration gradient** between inside pellet ($C_i$) and surface ($C_s$). High temperature raises $D$ but does not lower $C_s$ unless the air is dry (low dew point).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sags
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sags

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sag (in Polymer Processing)** is defined as the *uncontrolled downward deflection* of a heated, softened polymer sheet under the influence of *gravitational force* (self-weight) when it is subjected to a temperature above its softening point (Glass Transition Temperature, $T_g$, for amorphous polymers; or Melting Point, $T_m$, for semi-crystalline polymers) during a thermoforming/forming operation.

In the context of **PCPOT503 – Polymer Machinery & Product Manufacturing (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, *Sag* is treated as a critical **process-control parameter** in secondary shaping operations, particularly **thermoforming, vacuum forming, pressure forming, and blow molding**, where a polymer sheet or parison is heated in a free-hanging or clamped condition before the forming force is applied.

### 1.1.1 Standard Industrial Metric

The most widely used industrial metric for quantifying sag is the **Sag Index ($I_s$)**, defined as:

$$I_s = \dfrac{d_{sag}}{t_{sheet}}$$

where:
- $d_{sag}$ = vertical sag depth (in **mm**)
- $t_{sheet}$ = original sheet thickness (in **mm**)
- $I_s$ is **dimensionless**

A value of $I_s \leq 0.5$ is generally considered the **safe operating limit** for most thermoforming operations before sheet thinning and wall-distribution defects become severe.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine placing a flat piece of **mozzarella cheese** on a wire mesh and slowly heating it from below. As the cheese softens, the unsupported center begins to droop downward under its own weight — this droop *is* sag. The polymer sheet behaves identically: heat reduces its stiffness (modulus drops by 3–4 orders of magnitude near $T_g$), and gravity does the rest.

A more technical analogy is a **flexible ruler held horizontally at both ends** — when the ruler is rigid, it stays straight; heat the ruler with a hot air gun and the center sags noticeably. The polymer sheet in an oven follows this exact physics.

### 1.2.1 Why does this happen at the molecular level?

- Below $T_g$ (or $T_m$): Polymer chains are *frozen* in rigid amorphous/semi-crystalline networks — **high elastic modulus** ($\sim 1$–$3$ GPa) resists gravity.
- Above $T_g$ (or $T_m$): Long-chain segments gain mobility, **modulus plummets** to $\sim 0.1$–$10$ MPa (rubbery plateau), and the sheet flows under self-weight.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Sag is *not* a defect caused by the machine — it is an **inherent material response** to heat. The job of the polymer engineer is to *time* the forming cycle so the sag is **predictable and controlled**, not eliminated.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Sag in Polymer Processing

| Category | Description | Typical Polymer Example |
|----------|-------------|-------------------------|
| **Thermal Sag** | Sag purely due to gravitational load on a heated sheet | All thermoplastics in thermoforming |
| **Viscoelastic Sag (Time-Dependent)** | Sag that grows over time even at constant temperature | PP, HDPE, PVC (semi-crystalline / high $M_w$) |
| **Elastic-Recovery Sag** | Partial recovery on cooling — leaves *sag marks* | PET, PMMA |
| **Catastrophic Sag (Sag Failure)** | Sheet tears or stretches beyond usable limit | Very thin sheets at excessive temperature |

---

## 1.4 Where Sag is Encountered in PCPOT503 Syllabus

- **Thermoforming** (vacuum forming, pressure forming, drape forming) — *primary* context.
- **Blow Molding** — parison sag between die and mold.
- **Sheet Extrusion Take-Up** — web sag between die and chill roll.
- **Rotational Molding** — minor, but contributes to wall-thickness variation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Sag — A Layered Explanation

### Layer 1: Mechanical Equivalence (Beam Theory)

A clamped/supported polymer sheet, when heated above its softening point, is mechanically equivalent to a **simply-supported beam under uniformly distributed self-weight load**. The classical Euler–Bernoulli beam equation gives the maximum vertical deflection at the mid-span.

### Layer 2: Why Polymer Sheets Behave Differently from Metals

For an elastic metal beam, the deflection is instantaneous and constant. For a polymer sheet:

1. **Modulus is temperature-dependent**: $E = E(T)$ drops sharply near $T_g$ or $T_m$.
2. **Modulus is time-dependent**: $E = E(t)$ — viscoelastic relaxation causes **creep** under constant load.
3. **Geometry matters**: Sheet aspect ratio ($L/h$) determines whether the sheet sags like a *beam* (slender) or *membrane* (very thin, large area).

### Layer 3: The Three Critical Time-Stages in a Thermoforming Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 1 — Heating:** Sheet heats up, modulus drops, sag *begins*.
> **Stage 2 — Sag Peak:** Sag reaches a quasi-equilibrium value ($d_{sag,max}$) determined by melt strength and sheet dimensions.
> **Stage 3 — Forming:** Vacuum/pressure applied *before* sag exceeds safe limit; sheet is pulled into the mold cavity.

If **Stage 3** is delayed → sheet sags too far → uneven wall thickness, wrinkles, or sheet tears.

---

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Sag (Why & How)

### 2.2.1 Material Factors (the *Why*)

- **Melt Strength (MS):** Resistance of a polymer melt to tensile elongation under its own weight. Higher MS → lower sag.
  - *Why?* Higher $M_w$ and broader $M_w$ distribution → more chain entanglements → more resistance to flow.
- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$) / Melting Point ($T_m$):** Determines the temperature at which sag *initiates*.
  - Amorphous polymers (PS, PMMA, PVC): Sag begins gradually as $T \to T_g$.
  - Semi-crystalline polymers (PP, PE, PA): Sag *suddenly* increases at $T \to T_m$ (sharper transition).
- **Molecular Architecture:** Branched polymers (LDPE) have lower MS than linear (HDPE) → *more sag*.
- **Fillers/Additives:** Glass fibers, talc, carbon black **increase MS** → reduce sag.

### 2.2.2 Process Factors (the *How*)

- **Sheet Temperature ($T$):** Higher $T$ → lower $E(T)$ → greater sag.
- **Heating Time ($t_{heat}$):** Longer exposure → deeper thermal penetration → more sag.
- **Sheet Thickness ($t_{sheet}$):** Thicker sheet has higher flexural rigidity ($D \propto t^3$) → less sag.
- **Clamped Span ($L$):** Larger span → sag increases as $L^4$ (very sensitive).
- **Heating Method:** Convection (uniform) vs. IR (surface heating) — affects sag distribution.
- **Grip Geometry:** Full clamp vs. line clamp vs. point support — affects sag profile.

### 2.2.3 Environmental Factors

- **Gravity ($g = 9.81$ m/s²):** Always present; magnitude determines sag load.
- **Air Currents / Convection:** Disturbs the sagging sheet asymmetrically.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following equations are *high-yield* for KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the form, variables, and physical meaning.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units / Variables | Boundary Condition |
|---|---------|------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $I_s = \dfrac{d_{sag}}{t_{sheet}}$ | Sag index (dimensionless measure) | $d_{sag}$: sag depth (m); $t_{sheet}$: thickness (m) | Safe limit: $I_s \leq 0.5$ |
| 2 | $d_{sag,max} = \dfrac{5 \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot L^4}{32 \cdot E_{eff} \cdot t_{sheet}^2}$ | Maximum sag depth of a simply-supported viscoelastic sheet (Euler–Bernoulli analogy) | $\rho$: density (kg/m³); $g$: gravity (m/s²); $L$: span (m); $E_{eff}$: effective modulus at forming $T$ (Pa) | Valid for $L/t_{sheet} > 20$ (slender beam regime) |
| 3 | $D = \dfrac{E_{eff} \cdot t_{sheet}^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$ | Flexural rigidity of the sheet | $D$: rigidity (N·m); $\nu$: Poisson's ratio ($\approx 0.3$–$0.45$ for polymers) | Used for thin membranes |
| 4 | $MS = \sigma_{break} \cdot A_0$ | Melt strength of polymer | $MS$: melt strength (N); $\sigma_{break}$: tensile stress at break of extrudate (Pa); $A_0$: initial cross-section (m²) | Measured by Rheotens test at v = const |
| 5 | $\sigma_{sag} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot L^2}{2 \cdot t_{sheet}}$ | Gravitational tensile stress at mid-span (driving force for sag) | $\sigma_{sag}$: stress (Pa) | Used to compare with MS limit |
| 6 | $t_{sag} = \dfrac{\eta_{eff} \cdot L^2}{\rho \cdot g \cdot t_{sheet}^2}$ | Characteristic sag time (viscoelastic time-scale) | $\eta_{eff}$: effective viscosity at $T$ (Pa·s); $t_{sag}$: time (s) | Compare with heating/forming cycle time |
| 7 | $E(T) \approx E_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[-\alpha (T - T_{ref})\right]$ | Arrhenius-type modulus decay with temperature | $E_0$: modulus at $T_{ref}$ (Pa); $\alpha$: temperature sensitivity (K⁻¹) | Valid in rubbery plateau |
| 8 | $I_s^{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{sag}}{E_{eff}}$ | Engineering limit: sag when gravitational stress equals modulus | Dimensionless | For linear elastic regime |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on formula 2 derivation:** This is the *elastic* beam-deflection formula adapted for a polymer sheet. The factor 5/384 with $I = bt^3/12$ simplifies to $5\rho g L^4 / (32 E t^2)$ per unit width. In practice, $E_{eff}$ is a *viscoelastic* modulus and must be measured by DMA at the forming frequency.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Use of Sag Knowledge |
|----------|----------------------|
| **Food Packaging** (PS cups, PP trays) | Determines optimal heating time in-line thermoforming |
| **Automotive** (ABS dashboards, PP bumpers) | Controls wall-thickness uniformity in large thermoformed panels |
| **Blow Molding** (PET bottles, HDPE tanks) | Parison programming to counterbalance sag |
| **Vacuum Forming Acrylic (PMMA)** | Aircraft canopies, signage — sag index = 0.3 is typical target |
| **Sheet Extrusion** | Web tension control to prevent droop between die and chill roll |

> [!WARNING]
> **Excessive sag** is responsible for an estimated **15–25% of rejection** in commercial thermoforming lines (industry literature), making its control a *high-impact* process-engineering skill.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Sag Depth of a Heated Polymer Sheet

We model the heated polymer sheet as a **simply-supported rectangular beam** of length $L$, width $b$, and thickness $t_{sheet}$, subjected to its own weight (uniformly distributed load).

### Step 1: Identify the Loading

The self-weight per unit length (per unit width) is:

$$w' = \rho \cdot g \cdot t_{sheet}$$

The total load per unit width (over span $L$):

$$W = w' \cdot L = \rho \cdot g \cdot t_{sheet} \cdot L$$

### Step 2: Apply Euler–Bernoulli Beam Theory

The differential equation for elastic deflection $y(x)$ of a beam under distributed load $w'$:

$$E I \dfrac{d^4 y}{dx^4} = w'$$

### Step 3: Integrate Four Times (Boundary Conditions for Simply-Supported Beam)

Boundary conditions: $y(0) = y(L) = 0$ and $M(0) = M(L) = 0$, where $M = EI \cdot (d^2y/dx^2)$.

Integrating once:

$$EI \dfrac{d^3 y}{dx^3} = V(x) = w' x - \dfrac{w' L}{2}$$

(the shear force is zero at the mid-span when $x = L/2$).

Integrating twice (bending moment):

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) = \dfrac{w' x (L - x)}{2}$$

Integrating the third time (slope):

$$EI \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{w' L x^2}{4} - \dfrac{w' x^3}{6} + C_1$$

Integrating the fourth time (deflection):

$$EI \cdot y(x) = \dfrac{w' L x^3}{12} - \dfrac{w' x^4}{24} + C_1 x + C_2$$

### Step 4: Apply Boundary Conditions

At $x = 0$, $y = 0$ → $C_2 = 0$.
At $x = L$, $y = 0$:

$$0 = \dfrac{w' L^4}{12} - \dfrac{w' L^4}{24} + C_1 L \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = -\dfrac{w' L^3}{24}$$

### Step 5: Maximum Deflection at Mid-Span ($x = L/2$)

Substitute $x = L/2$:

$$EI \cdot y_{max} = \dfrac{w' L}{12}\!\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{\!3} - \dfrac{w'}{24}\!\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)^{\!4} - \dfrac{w' L^3}{24}\!\left(\dfrac{L}{2}\right)$$

$$EI \cdot y_{max} = \dfrac{w' L^4}{96} - \dfrac{w' L^4}{384} - \dfrac{w' L^4}{48}$$

$$EI \cdot y_{max} = \dfrac{w' L^4}{384} \cdot (4 - 1 - 8) = -\dfrac{5 w' L^4}{384}$$

The negative sign indicates downward deflection. Taking the magnitude:

$$d_{sag,max} = \dfrac{5 w' L^4}{384 \, EI}$$

### Step 6: Substitute Polymer-Sheet Parameters

Per unit width, $I = \dfrac{t_{sheet}^3}{12}$ and $w' = \rho g t_{sheet}$:

$$d_{sag,max} = \dfrac{5 (\rho g t_{sheet}) L^4}{384 \cdot E \cdot \left(\dfrac{t_{sheet}^3}{12}\right)} = \dfrac{5 \rho g L^4}{32 E t_{sheet}^2}$$

Therefore the **Master Sag Equation** for a polymer sheet:

$$\boxed{\; d_{sag,max} = \dfrac{5 \rho g L^4}{32 E_{eff}\, t_{sheet}^2} \;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $E_{eff}$?** In a real thermoforming operation, the polymer is *viscoelastic*. The simple elastic modulus $E$ is replaced by the **effective modulus** $E_{eff}$, which is a frequency- and temperature-dependent quantity obtained from **Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA)**. At forming temperatures (15–20 °C above $T_g$ for amorphous polymers), $E_{eff}$ is typically $0.1$–$10$ MPa.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sag Index in Terms of Gravitational Stress

### Step 1: Express Sag Depth Using the Master Equation

$$d_{sag} = \dfrac{5 \rho g L^4}{32 E_{eff}\, t_{sheet}^2}$$

### Step 2: Divide by Sheet Thickness to Get the Sag Index

$$I_s = \dfrac{d_{sag}}{t_{sheet}} = \dfrac{5 \rho g L^4}{32 E_{eff}\, t_{sheet}^3}$$

### Step 3: Recognize the Engineering Significance

The numerator is proportional to gravitational stress, the denominator to flexural rigidity:

$$I_s = \dfrac{\sigma_{sag} \cdot L^2}{E_{eff} \cdot t_{sheet}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight:** $I_s$ is essentially a *strain-like* quantity. The safe limit $I_s \leq 0.5$ corresponds to a maximum allowable sheet strain of ~50% before non-uniform thinning becomes unacceptable.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style 7-mark derivation):**
A PMMA sheet of dimensions $1000 \text{ mm} \times 1000 \text{ mm} \times 3 \text{ mm}$ is heated to $165$ °C and clamped along both 1-m edges. Given:
- $\rho_{PMMA} = 1180 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $E_{eff}(165°C) = 1.2 \text{ MPa} = 1.2 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Calculate (a)** the maximum sag depth, **(b)** the sag index, and **(c)** comment on formability.

### Solution

**(a) Maximum Sag Depth:**

$$d_{sag} = \dfrac{5 \rho g L^4}{32 E_{eff}\, t_{sheet}^2}$$

$$L = 1.0 \text{ m}, \quad t_{sheet} = 0.003 \text{ m}, \quad L^4 = 1.0 \text{ m}^4, \quad t_{sheet}^2 = 9 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

$$d_{sag} = \dfrac{5 \times 1180 \times 9.81 \times 1.0}{32 \times 1.2 \times 10^6 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$d_{sag} = \dfrac{57,879}{345.6} = 167.5 \text{ mm}$$

**(b) Sag Index:**

$$I_s = \dfrac{167.5 \text{ mm}}{3.0 \text{ mm}} = 55.8$$

**(c) Comment:**

Since $I_s = 55.8 \gg 0.5$ (safe limit), the sheet has **sagged catastrophically** — it is far beyond formability. The forming operation would fail. In practice, either the sheet temperature must be lowered (higher $E_{eff}$), the sheet thickness increased, or the span reduced.

> [!WARNING]
> **Note:** The very high $I_s$ indicates this sheet is in the **membrane regime**, not pure beam bending. In real operations, sagging would cause the sheet to *neck* and tear long before reaching 167.5 mm. The elastic formula is a *theoretical upper bound*.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Amorphous vs. Semi-Crystalline Polymers

| Property | Amorphous (PS, PMMA, PVC) | Semi-Crystalline (PP, HDPE, PA) |
|----------|---------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Onset of sag | Gradual (as $T \to T_g$) | Abrupt (at $T \to T_m$) |
| Sag control | Easier (wide processing window) | Harder (narrow window) |
| Sag uniformity | Highly uniform | Non-uniform (crystallites resist) |
| Melt strength | Moderate | Low (especially PP) |
| Typical $I_s$ target | 0.3 – 0.5 | 0.2 – 0.3 |
| Industry practice | Acrylic signage, PS cups | PP food trays, HDPE tanks |

---

## 3.5 Control Strategies — Analytical Derivation of Each

### 3.5.1 Increasing Sheet Thickness

From the master equation, $d_{sag} \propto 1/t_{sheet}^2$. Doubling thickness reduces sag by **4×**.

### 3.5.2 Reducing Span

Since $d_{sag} \propto L^4$, halving the span reduces sag by **16×** — *the most powerful parameter*.

### 3.5.3 Lowering Forming Temperature

Via $E_{eff}(T)$, a 10 °C drop below $T_g$ can increase $E_{eff}$ by 1–2 orders of magnitude → sag reduces drastically.

### 3.5.4 Modifying Material (Branching / $M_w$ / Fillers)

Increasing $M_w$ by 50% can increase melt strength by 200–400% in some polyethylenes (Fox & Flory relationship).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1.** Define *sag* in the context of polymer thermoforming. List **any four** factors that influence the sag depth of a heated polymer sheet.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Sag is the downward deflection of a heated polymer sheet under its own weight when its temperature exceeds the softening point ($T_g$ for amorphous, $T_m$ for semi-crystalline polymers) during a thermoforming operation.

> **Factors (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**
> 1. **Sheet temperature** — Higher temperature reduces modulus, increases sag.
> 2. **Sheet thickness** — Thicker sheet has higher flexural rigidity, less sag.
> 3. **Clamped span (L)** — Larger span increases sag (proportional to $L^4$).
> 4. **Melt strength of the polymer** — Higher melt strength resists sag.
> 5. (Optional) Heating time, molecular weight, fillers, clamp type.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Style]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between the **sagging behavior of amorphous polymers** (e.g., PMMA, PS) and **semi-crystalline polymers** (e.g., PP, HDPE). Why is sagging control more critical for semi-crystalline polymers?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Amorphous (PS, PMMA) | Semi-Crystalline (PP, HDPE) |
|---------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Onset of sag | Gradual, as $T \to T_g$ | Abrupt, at $T \to T_m$ |
| Processing window | Wide | Narrow |
| Sag uniformity | Highly uniform | Less uniform |

> **Why more critical for semi-crystalline (1 mark):** The processing window is **narrow** — a small temperature overshoot causes a *catastrophic* drop in modulus and excessive sag. In amorphous polymers, the modulus decreases gradually, providing a wider "safe zone" for the operator/control system to manage the heating cycle.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified Style]**
