**Q.A.** **(a)** Derive the expression for the maximum sag depth of a simply-supported polymer sheet subjected to its own weight under uniform heating. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $1.5 \text{ m} \times 1.0 \text{ m}$ HDPE sheet of thickness $4$ mm is heated to $160$ °C for thermoforming. The effective modulus at this temperature is $E_{eff} = 0.8$ MPa, and the density is $\rho = 950$ kg/m³. The sheet is clamped on the long edges.

    **(i)** Calculate the maximum sag depth at the mid-span.
    **(ii)** Calculate the sag index and comment on formability.
    **(iii)** Suggest **two practical modifications** to reduce the sag to within the safe limit ($I_s \leq 0.5$).
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A

### (a) Derivation (7 marks)

> **Assumptions (1 mark):**
> 1. The polymer sheet behaves as a *uniform, homogeneous, isotropic* Euler–Bernoulli beam.
> 2. Self-weight is the only load.
> 3. The sheet is *simply supported* at both ends.
> 4. The effective modulus $E_{eff}$ is constant at the forming temperature (linear viscoelastic approximation).
> 5. Slender beam regime: $L/t > 20$ (membrane effects ignored).

> **Step 1 — Load identification (1 mark):**
> Self-weight per unit width: $w' = \rho g t_{sheet}$

> **Step 2 — Beam equation (1 mark):**
> $$EI \dfrac{d^4 y}{dx^4} = w'$$

> **Step 3 — Boundary conditions (1 mark):**
> $y(0) = y(L) = 0$; $M(0) = M(L) = 0$

> **Step 4 — Integration (1.5 marks):**
> Four successive integrations yield (shown in Section 3.1 above):
> $$y(x) = \dfrac{w'}{24 EI} \left( x^4 - 2L x^3 + L^3 x \right)$$

> **Step 5 — Maximum deflection at $x = L/2$ (1.5 marks):**
> $$d_{sag,max} = \dfrac{5 w' L^4}{384 EI}$$

> **Step 6 — Substitute polymer parameters (1 mark):**
> With $w' = \rho g t_{sheet}$ and $I = t_{sheet}^3/12$:
> $$\boxed{\, d_{sag,max} = \dfrac{5 \rho g L^4}{32 E_{eff}\, t_{sheet}^2} \,}$$

---

### (b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)

#### (i) Maximum Sag Depth (2 marks)

Given: $L = 1.5$ m, $b = 1.0$ m, $t_{sheet} = 0.004$ m, $\rho = 950$ kg/m³, $E_{eff} = 0.8 \times 10^6$ Pa, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

$$d_{sag} = \dfrac{5 \times 950 \times 9.81 \times (1.5)^4}{32 \times 0.8 \times 10^6 \times (0.004)^2}$$

Compute numerator:
$$5 \times 950 \times 9.81 \times 5.0625 = 2.359 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}$$

Compute denominator:
$$32 \times 0.8 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5} = 409.6 \text{ N/m}$$

$$d_{sag} = \dfrac{2.359 \times 10^5}{409.6} = 576.0 \text{ mm} \approx 0.576 \text{ m}$$

#### (ii) Sag Index & Comment (2 marks)

$$I_s = \dfrac{576.0 \text{ mm}}{4.0 \text{ mm}} = 144$$

> **Comment:** $I_s = 144 \gg 0.5$ (safe limit). The sheet has **sagged catastrophically**. In practice, the sheet would neck and tear before reaching this theoretical value. **Forming is not feasible** under these conditions — the cycle is well outside the safe operating window.

> **Note (1 mark):** The elastic formula gives a theoretical upper bound. Real polymers undergo *viscoelastic flow* and *necking* long before this deflection is reached. Nonetheless, the calculation confirms that **the conditions are far from acceptable** for thermoforming.

#### (iii) Practical Modifications (2 marks — 1 mark each)

1. **Reduce the forming temperature by 10–20 °C.** This increases $E_{eff}$ by approximately an order of magnitude (rubbery plateau), reducing sag by ~10×. For HDPE, forming at ~140–150 °C instead of 160 °C dramatically improves sag resistance.

2. **Increase the sheet thickness to ~8–10 mm.** Since $d_{sag} \propto 1/t_{sheet}^2$, doubling the thickness reduces sag by **4×**; using 10 mm reduces sag by ~6.25×.

3. **(Alternative)** Use a higher $M_w$ grade of HDPE to enhance melt strength.

4. **(Alternative)** Use a **clamping frame with reduced exposed span** (e.g., from 1.5 m to 0.75 m) — reduces sag by 16×.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q.B.** **(a)** Explain the concept of **melt strength** in polymers. How does it govern the sag behavior of a polymer sheet during thermoforming? Describe **any one experimental method** used to measure melt strength. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the sagging characteristics of **amorphous and semi-crystalline polymers** with suitable examples. With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **complete sag-formation mechanism** during the heating stage of a thermoforming cycle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B

### (a) Melt Strength and Sag (7 marks)

> **Definition (2 marks):** Melt strength (MS) is defined as the maximum tensile force that a polymer melt can sustain before breaking, measured under specified conditions of temperature, strain rate, and extrudate geometry. Mathematically:
> $$MS = \sigma_{break} \cdot A_0$$
> where $\sigma_{break}$ is the breaking stress of an extrudate and $A_0$ is its initial cross-sectional area.

> **Role in Sag Control (2 marks):** Sag occurs when gravitational stress $\sigma_{sag} = \rho g L^2 / (2 t_{sheet})$ exceeds the polymer's effective resistance (related to $E_{eff}$ and MS). A polymer with **high melt strength** can sustain greater self-weight loads without sagging uncontrollably. Therefore:
> - **High MS** → less sag → wider processing window.
> - **Low MS** → more sag → narrow processing window.
> - **LDPE** has lower MS than **HDPE** (branching reduces chain entanglements).
> - **PP** has notably low MS compared to other polyolefins, making its sag control challenging.

> **Experimental Method — Rheotens Test (2 marks):** A molten polymer extrudate is drawn vertically downward from a capillary die at a constant extrusion rate. The extrudate is gripped by a pair of counter-rotating wheels (or a tension sensor) that measure the tensile force as the take-up velocity is increased linearly. The maximum force sustained before the extrudate breaks is the **melt strength** of the polymer.

> **Influence of $M_w$ (1 mark):** Melt strength is strongly dependent on molecular weight and its distribution. Broader $M_w$ distribution and long-chain branching both increase MS (Fox–Flory relationship and entanglement theory).

---

### (b) Amorphous vs. Semi-Crystalline & Sag Mechanism (7 marks)

> **Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Amorphous (PS, PMMA, PVC) | Semi-Crystalline (PP, HDPE, PA) |
|-----------|---------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Onset of sag | Gradual near $T_g$ | Sharp at $T_m$ |
| Modulus drop | Smooth, wide transition | Step-function-like |
| Melt strength | Moderate to high | Low (especially PP) |
| Processing window | Wide (~20–30 °C) | Narrow (~5–10 °C) |
| Examples | Acrylic signs, vacuum-formed PS cups | PP food trays, HDPE containers |

> **Sag Formation Mechanism — Narrative (3 marks):**
>
> **Stage 1 — Initial Heating (1 mark):** The clamped polymer sheet enters the oven at room temperature. The elastic modulus is ~1–3 GPa; sag is negligible. As IR heaters/convection heat the sheet, its temperature rises steadily.
>
> **Stage 2 — Softening (1 mark):** As the sheet temperature approaches $T_g$ (or $T_m$), the modulus drops by 3–4 orders of magnitude. The sheet becomes rubbery. Gravity begins to act, and the mid-span starts to deflect downward. Sag increases with time as viscoelastic creep contributes.
>
> **Stage 3 — Sag Equilibrium / Maximum (1 mark):** Sag reaches a quasi-steady value determined by the balance of gravitational stress and effective modulus. The operator (or automated control) measures the sag depth (often via laser or optical sensor) and triggers the forming stage when the sag index is within the safe limit.
>
> *(See Section 4.4 for the schematic — clamped sheet with downward sag at mid-span.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in KTU 2024 ESE — "Sag" Topic**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 marks):** Students often mix mm with m in the sag formula. Always convert **all dimensions to meters** before substituting into the master equation.
> 2. **Forgetting $E_{eff}$ vs. $E$ (–1 to –2 marks):** Using room-temperature modulus (~3 GPa) instead of *effective* modulus at forming temperature (~1 MPa) gives absurdly small sag. Examiners specifically look for "effective" or "viscoelastic" modulus in the answer.
> 3. **Ignoring the $L^4$ dependence (–1 mark):** Span is the *most* sensitive parameter. Students often treat it as linear.
> 4. **Confusing Sag Index with Sag Depth (–1 mark):** Sag index is *dimensionless*; sag depth is in *mm or m*. They are not interchangeable.
> 5. **Missing examples (–0.5 mark):** Always cite polymer examples (PS, PP, PMMA, HDPE) when comparing amorphous vs. semi-crystalline.
> 6. **Forgetting boundary conditions in derivation (–1 mark):** Always state the boundary conditions of the simply-supported beam explicitly before integrating.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — "Sag" (Module 3, PCPOT503)**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **Sag** = downward deflection of heated polymer sheet under self-weight.
- ☐ Occurs when $T \geq T_g$ (amorphous) or $T \geq T_m$ (semi-crystalline).
- ☐ **Sag Index** $I_s = d_{sag}/t_{sheet}$; safe limit $I_s \leq 0.5$.
- ☐ **Melt strength** is the principal material resistance to sag.

### Key Formula (Memorize!)
- ☐ Master sag equation: $d_{sag} = \dfrac{5 \rho g L^4}{32 E_{eff} t_{sheet}^2}$
- ☐ Sag sensitivity: $d_{sag} \propto L^4$, $d_{sag} \propto 1/t_{sheet}^2$, $d_{sag} \propto 1/E_{eff}$

### Amorphous vs. Semi-Crystalline
- ☐ **Amorphous** = gradual sag, wide window, easier control (PS, PMMA, PVC).
- ☐ **Semi-crystalline** = abrupt sag at $T_m$, narrow window, harder control (PP, HDPE, PA).

### Process Parameters (Ranked by Effect)
- ☐ **Most powerful:** Reducing clamped span $L$ (sag $\propto L^4$).
- ☐ **Second:** Increasing sheet thickness $t$ (sag $\propto 1/t^2$).
- ☐ **Third:** Lowering forming temperature (increases $E_{eff}$).
- ☐ **Fourth:** Choosing a higher $M_w$ or branched/filled grade.

### Material Parameters
- ☐ **Melt Strength** = $\sigma_{break} \times A_0$; measured by **Rheotens test**.
- ☐ Higher $M_w$ + broader distribution → higher MS → less sag.
- ☐ Fillers (glass fiber, talc) increase MS and reduce sag.

### Industrial Context
- ☐ Sag is most critical in **thermoforming** and **blow molding** (parison).
- ☐ Excessive sag = primary cause of **wall-thickness non-uniformity** and **sheet tearing** in thermoformed parts.
- ☐ Target $I_s$ for commercial forming: **0.2 – 0.5**.

### Pitfalls to Avoid in Exam
- ☐ Always use **$E_{eff}$** (not $E$); units must be in **Pa**, dimensions in **m**.
- ☐ State the **assumptions** of beam theory before deriving the master equation.
- ☐ Provide **examples** of polymers when discussing amorphous vs. semi-crystalline behavior.
- ☐ Distinguish **sag depth** (mm) from **sag index** (dimensionless).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cooling and Trimming the Parts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cooling and Trimming the Parts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Cooling** in polymer processing refers to the controlled, time-dependent removal of sensible heat from a molten or semi-molten plastic part contained within a mould cavity, until the part's average (or core) temperature drops to a value at which the material possesses sufficient modulus and dimensional stability (the **Ejection Temperature**, $T_E$) to be ejected without distortion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Trimming** (also termed *deflashing* or *degating*) is the post-moulding, secondary manufacturing operation in which the solidified plastic part is mechanically or thermally separated from its associated moulding auxiliaries — namely the **sprue, runners, gates, flash lines, and pinch-offs** — to yield a dimensionally finished, net-shape product.

In the **Injection Moulding Cycle**, the cooling stage typically consumes **50 % – 80 %** of the total cycle time, making it the *rate-controlling step* of the entire process. The trimming stage, although a post-cycle operation, is critical to the final part quality, dimensional accuracy, and downstream assembly integrity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bakery Analogy** — Imagine pouring hot molten chocolate (the polymer melt) into a star-shaped silicone mould. Two things must happen before the chocolate can be popped out cleanly:
>
> 1. **Cooling** — You must let the chocolate *sit on a cold countertop* long enough for the centre to harden. If you pull it out too early, the middle is still liquid and the shape collapses. The "cold countertop" represents the **water-cooled mould**, and the "time you wait" is the **cooling time, $t_c$**.
>
> 2. **Trimming** — Even after unmoulding, the chocolate has a thin jagged edge where it squeezed into the seam of the mould (the *flash*) and a stub of excess chocolate where the pouring channel was (the *gate/sprue*). You trim these off with a knife. The "knife work" represents the **deflashing/degating** process.

The polymer version is, of course, far more rigorous: heat is removed via **forced-convection water channels** machined into the mould, and trimming is increasingly automated through **robotic routers, laser cutters, and water-jet systems**.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling time fraction of cycle | $f_c$ | **50 % – 80 %** |
| Ejection temperature (amorphous) | $T_E$ | **Glass transition, $T_g$ + 10 °C** |
| Ejection temperature (semi-crystalline) | $T_E$ | **Crystallisation, $T_c$ + 5 °C** |
| Mould wall temperature | $T_W$ | **20 °C – 120 °C** |
| Heat removed per cycle | $Q_{cool}$ | **kJ – MJ range** |
| Coolant: water (industrial) | $T_{cin}$ | **10 °C – 25 °C** |
| Reynolds number target (turbulent) | $Re$ | $\mathbf{> 10{,}000}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Cooling — The "Why" and the "How"

#### Step 1 — The Thermal Boundary Condition
When molten polymer fills the cavity at melt temperature $T_M$ (typically 200 °C – 320 °C), it makes intimate contact with the cold mould wall at $T_W$ (typically 20 °C – 120 °C). The temperature difference $(T_M - T_W)$ acts as the **driving potential** for one-dimensional unsteady-state heat conduction through the solidified plastic skin toward the mould wall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does heat flow from the centre to the wall?** Heat naturally travels down a temperature gradient (Fourier's Law). The polymer centre is the hottest, the mould wall is the coldest, and the water inside the channels absorbs this heat by forced convection.

#### Step 2 — Skin Formation and the "Frozen Layer"
The instant the melt touches the cold wall, a thin **frozen (or solidified) skin** of polymer forms. This skin grows inward with time $\sqrt{t}$ (square-root-of-time law). The frozen skin is mechanically rigid and dictates the **ejection behaviour**.

#### Step 3 — Three Heat-Transfer Resistances in Series
The total thermal resistance between melt core and coolant bulk is a series combination:

$$R_{total} = R_{polymer} + R_{interface} + R_{mould\,steel} + R_{convective}$$

- **$R_{polymer}$** — conduction through the solidified plastic skin (the largest resistance).
- **$R_{interface}$** — contact resistance at the polymer–steel interface (small, often neglected).
- **$R_{mould\,steel}$** — conduction through the mould steel (very small, $k_{steel} \approx 30$ W/m·K).
- **$R_{convective}$** — forced convection to the cooling water (controlled by $Re$ and $Pr$).

#### Step 4 — The Coolant Side — Forced Convection
Water is pumped through drilled channels (typically $\phi$ **8 – 14 mm**) at velocities of **1 – 3 m/s** to keep the flow **turbulent ($Re > 10{,}000$)**. Turbulence ensures a high convective heat-transfer coefficient, $h \approx 5{,}000$ to $25{,}000$ W/(m²·K), which keeps the wall temperature uniform.

#### Step 5 — The Ejection Criterion
Cooling must continue until the part's **volume-average temperature** (or, more conservatively, the **centreline temperature**) drops below the ejection temperature $T_E$. Below $T_E$, the polymer possesses a modulus $E \geq 200$ MPa and a coefficient of friction low enough to slide out of the cavity without permanent deformation.

### 2.2 Cooling Channel Design — Engineering Logic

A cooling channel is essentially a **circular duct with internal forced convection**. The thermal performance is governed by three dimensionless numbers:

- **Reynolds Number** — inertial vs. viscous forces; must be turbulent.
- **Prandtl Number** — momentum vs. thermal diffusivity; fixed by coolant choice.
- **Nusselt Number** — dimensionless temperature gradient; gives $h$.

For a circular channel, the convective heat-transfer coefficient from the **Dittus–Boelter correlation** (heating of fluid, $n = 0.4$) is the industry standard.

### 2.3 Trimming — Classification and Engineering Logic

Trimming is classified along two axes: **(a) Manual vs. Automated**, and **(b) Cold vs. Hot**.

- **Cold Trimming** — performed after the part has fully cooled; gives high dimensional accuracy but is labour-intensive.
- **Hot Trimming** — performed while the part is still warm and the flash is rubbery; easier to cut but lower accuracy.
- **In-Mould Trimming (for Blow Moulding)** — the pinch-off blades inside the mould cut the flash and the tail during the cycle itself, eliminating a separate post-process step.
- **Automated Trimming** — uses 6-axis robots with router spindles, laser heads, or water-jet nozzles; this is the **Industry 4.0 standard** for high-volume production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility** — Cooling-channel design directly determines the **production rate (parts/hour)**, which in turn determines the **machine-selection economics** for any polymer product. A 10 % reduction in cooling time can increase plant throughput by 8 % – 12 %, saving crores of rupees annually in a high-volume production facility. Trimming, similarly, determines the **scrap rate, labour cost, and final dimensional tolerance** of the part.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation (LaTeX form) | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t_c = \dfrac{L^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \, \ln\!\left[\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_M - T_W}{T_E - T_W}\right]$ | $L$ = half-thickness [m]; $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity [m²/s]; $T_M,T_W,T_E$ [°C] | **Cooling time** for a plate cooled from both sides (volume-average criterion) |
| 2 | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, c_p}$ | $k$ [W/m·K], $\rho$ [kg/m³], $c_p$ [J/kg·K] | **Thermal diffusivity** of polymer |
| 3 | $Q = \dot{m} \, c_{p,cool} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | $\dot{m}$ [kg/s]; $c_p$ [J/kg·K]; $T$ [°C] | **Heat load** removed by coolant per unit time |
| 4 | $Re = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi D \mu} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $D$ [m]; $\mu$ [Pa·s]; $v$ [m/s] | **Reynolds number** in cooling channel |
| 5 | $Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{0.4}$ | $Pr$ = $\mu c_p / k$ | **Dittus–Boelter** Nusselt number (turbulent) |
| 6 | $h = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k_{cool}}{D}$ | $h$ [W/m²·K] | **Convective heat-transfer coefficient** |
| 7 | $q'' = h \, (T_{wall} - T_{cool})$ | $q''$ [W/m²] | **Surface heat flux** at channel wall |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{cool} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{c_{p,cool} \, \Delta T_{cool}}$ | $\Delta T_{cool} \approx 2$ – $5$ °C | **Coolant mass-flow rate** for given heat load |
| 9 | $t_{cycle} = t_{close} + t_{inj} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{open} + t_{eject}$ | All times [s] | **Total cycle time** (cooling is the largest term) |
| 10 | $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha \, t_c}{L^2}$ | $Fo$ = Fourier number | Dimensionless cooling time; $Fo \approx 0.5$–$0.8$ at ejection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorise Equations 1, 3, 4, 5, and 6 — they cover 90 % of the numerical problems in this module.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cooling-Time Equation for a Plate

**Physical Model:** A plate of half-thickness $L$ (full thickness $2L$) with uniform initial temperature $T_M$ is suddenly exposed to a wall temperature $T_W$ on **both** faces. We seek the time $t_c$ at which the volume-average temperature of the plate equals the ejection temperature $T_E$.

#### Step 1 — Governing Differential Equation
One-dimensional transient heat conduction in a slab (no internal heat generation):

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \, \frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}, \qquad 0 \leq x \leq L, \quad t \geq 0$$

#### Step 2 — Initial and Boundary Conditions
Initial condition (uniform melt temperature):

$$T(x, 0) = T_M$$

Boundary conditions (symmetry at centre, fixed wall temperature):

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}(0, t) = 0, \qquad T(L, t) = T_W$$

#### Step 3 — Dimensionless Substitution
Define a dimensionless temperature:

$$\theta(x, t) = \frac{T(x, t) - T_W}{T_M - T_W}$$

This reduces the initial condition to $\theta(x,0) = 1$ and the wall boundary to $\theta(L,t) = 0$.

#### Step 4 — Separation of Variables
Assume $\theta(x, t) = X(x) \cdot \tau(t)$. Substituting and separating:

$$\frac{1}{\alpha \tau} \frac{d\tau}{dt} = \frac{1}{X} \frac{d^{2} X}{dx^{2}} = -\lambda^{2}$$

The spatial ODE gives $X(x) = A \cos(\lambda x) + B \sin(\lambda x)$. The symmetry condition $X'(0) = 0$ requires $B = 0$, so $X(x) = A \cos(\lambda x)$. The wall condition $X(L) = 0$ gives $\lambda L = (2n-1)\pi/2$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ Equivalently $\lambda_n = (2n+1)\pi/(2L)$ for $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$

The temporal ODE gives $\tau(t) = e^{-\alpha \lambda^{2} t}$. Assembling the full series solution:

$$\theta(x, t) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} B_n \cos\!\left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2L}\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2}\alpha t}{4L^{2}}\right)$$

#### Step 5 — Apply Initial Condition
At $t = 0$, $\theta = 1$ for all $x \in [0, L]$. The orthogonality of the cosine eigenfunctions gives:

$$B_n = \frac{2}{L}\int_{0}^{L} 1 \cdot \cos\!\left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2L}\right) dx = \frac{2}{L} \cdot \frac{2L}{(2n+1)\pi} \sin\!\left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi}{2}\right)$$

Since $\sin[(2n+1)\pi/2] = (-1)^{n}$, we obtain $B_n = \dfrac{4(-1)^{n}}{(2n+1)\pi}$.

#### Step 6 — Volume-Average Temperature
The average temperature inside the slab is:

$$\bar{\theta}(t) = \frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} \theta(x,t) \, dx = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} B_n \exp(-\dots) \int_{0}^{L} \cos\!\left(\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{2L}\right) dx$$

Evaluating the integral: $\int_0^L \cos[(2n+1)\pi x/(2L)] dx = \dfrac{2L}{(2n+1)\pi} \sin[(2n+1)\pi/2] = \dfrac{2L(-1)^{n}}{(2n+1)\pi}$.

Combining with $B_n = 4(-1)^{n}/[(2n+1)\pi]$:

$$\bar{\theta}(t) = \frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(2n+1)^{2}\pi^{2}\alpha t}{4L^{2}}\right)$$

#### Step 7 — Long-Time Approximation (Engineering)
For $Fo = \alpha t / L^{2} \gtrsim 0.2$, the $n = 0$ term dominates ($1/1^{2} = 1$ vs. $1/9$, $1/25, \ldots$):

$$\bar{\theta}(t) \approx \frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^{2}\alpha t}{4L^{2}}\right)$$

#### Step 8 — Apply Ejection Criterion
Set $\bar{\theta}(t_c) = (T_E - T_W)/(T_M - T_W)$:

$$\frac{T_E - T_W}{T_M - T_W} = \frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^{2}\alpha t_c}{4L^{2}}\right)$$

Solve for $t_c$:

$$\exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^{2}\alpha t_c}{4L^{2}}\right) = \frac{\pi^{2}}{8} \cdot \frac{T_E - T_W}{T_M - T_W}$$

$$\boxed{\,t_c = \frac{4L^{2}}{\pi^{2}\alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left[\frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \frac{T_M - T_W}{T_E - T_W}\right]\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the textbook convention uses $L$ as the **full** thickness (so half-thickness $= L/2$), the formula reduces to the more commonly quoted $t_c = L^2/(\pi^{2}\alpha) \cdot \ln[\ldots]$, which is what we listed in the Formula Sheet. Always check the **definition of $L$** before plugging in numbers.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem

**Problem Statement (Model Question):**
A **Polypropylene (PP) plate** of full thickness $s = 6$ mm is injection moulded. The relevant process data are:

$$
T_M = 230\,^{\circ}\text{C}, \quad T_W = 30\,^{\circ}\text{C}, \quad T_E = 100\,^{\circ}\text{C}, \quad \alpha_{PP} = 0.96 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^{2}\!/\!\text{s}
$$

**(a)** Calculate the theoretical cooling time required for the volume-average temperature to reach $T_E$.

**(b)** If the cooling water enters the channel at 18 °C and leaves at 24 °C, and the total heat to be removed per cycle is $Q = 1.2$ kJ, find the required coolant mass per cycle. Take $c_{p,water} = 4180$ J/(kg·K).

#### Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Identify half-thickness.** $L = s/2 = 3$ mm $= 3 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature ratio term.**

$$\frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \frac{T_M - T_W}{T_E - T_W} = \frac{8}{9.8696} \cdot \frac{230 - 30}{100 - 30} = 0.8106 \cdot \frac{200}{70} = 0.8106 \cdot 2.857 = 2.316$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm.**

$$\ln(2.316) = 0.8398$$

**Step 4 — Compute the prefactor $4L^{2}/(\pi^{2}\alpha)$.**

$$\frac{4L^{2}}{\pi^{2}\alpha} = \frac{4 \times (3 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{9.8696 \times 0.96 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{4 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}}{9.475 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-5}}{9.475 \times 10^{-7}} = 37.99\ \text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply.**

$$t_c = 37.99 \times 0.8398 = 31.9\ \text{s}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A 6 mm PP plate theoretically requires about **32 seconds** of cooling. In practice, this is multiplied by a safety factor of **1.3 – 1.8** to account for the centreline (vs. average) criterion, mould-steel thermal inertia, and warpage limits, giving an **actual moulded cooling time of ~45–55 s**.

#### Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Coolant temperature rise.**

$$\Delta T_{cool} = T_{c,out} - T_{c,in} = 24 - 18 = 6\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance per cycle.**

$$Q = m_{cool} \cdot c_{p,water} \cdot \Delta T_{cool}$$

$$m_{cool} = \frac{Q}{c_{p,water} \cdot \Delta T_{cool}} = \frac{1200}{4180 \times 6} = \frac{1200}{25080} = 4.78 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{\,m_{cool} \approx 0.048\ \text{kg} \approx 48\ \text{mL of water per cycle}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** 48 mL per cycle may sound tiny, but at a cycle time of ~50 s, this corresponds to a continuous coolant flow of $\dot{m} = 0.048/50 = 9.6 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/s $\approx 58$ mL/min — small for one part, but multiplied across millions of parts per year becomes a major chiller-plant design consideration.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Cooling Time

| Factor | Direction of Effect | Engineering Lever |
|---|---|---|
| **Increase part thickness ($L$)** | $\uparrow t_c$ (quadratic, $L^{2}$) | Design thinner walls; ribs instead of thick sections |
| **Increase $\alpha$ of polymer** | $\downarrow t_c$ (linear) | Material choice (e.g., PP cools faster than PC) |
| **Increase $T_M$** | $\uparrow t_c$ (weakly, log) | Lower melt temperature (without losing fill) |
| **Decrease $T_W$** (colder mould) | $\downarrow t_c$ | Use chilled water at $\sim 10$ °C; add conformal cooling |
| **Increase $T_E$** (eject warmer) | $\downarrow t_c$ (weakly, log) | Use hot-mould surface; use mould-release coatings |
| **Use crystalline vs. amorphous** | Crystalline: **latent-heat release** slows core cooling; amorphous: faster but more warpage | Adjust mould-temperature strategy accordingly |
| **Turbulent coolant ($Re > 10^{4}$)** | $\uparrow h$ $\Rightarrow$ $\downarrow t_c$ | Increase pump flow rate; use baffles; conformal channels |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Trimming Method Selection

| Trimming Method | Suitable Material | Edge Quality | Capital Cost | Industry 4.0 Ready? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hand saw / utility knife | Soft, thin (PP, PE) | Low | Negligible | No |
| Die / punch press | Thermoform sheets, rubber | Good | Medium | Yes (servo press) |
| Robotic router (CNC) | Most rigid plastics (ABS, PC, Nylon) | Excellent | High | **Yes (standard)** |
| Laser cutting ($CO_2$ / fibre) | Thin films, acrylics, engineering plastics | Excellent, no tooling | Very high | **Yes (vision-guided)** |
| Water-jet cutting | Glass-filled, thick sections | Excellent | High | **Yes (5-axis)** |
| Hot-wire cutting | EPS, EPP, XPS foams | Good for foams | Low | Limited |
| In-mould pinch-off | Blow-moulded bottles, tanks | Excellent | Built into mould | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Mermaid diagrams below follow the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** (all node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters like `n1`, `nA`, `step1`) and contain only **raw uppercase alphanumeric text** in labels — no bold, italics, or HTML inside double-quoted labels.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Injection Moulding Cycle Showing the Position of Cooling and Trimming

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nA[MOLD CLOSING] --> nB[MELT INJECTION]
    nB --> nC[PACKING AND HOLDING]
    nC --> nD[COOLING IN CAVITY 50 TO 80 PERCENT OF CYCLE TIME]
    nD --> nE[MOLD OPENING]
    nE --> nF[PART EJECTION]
    nF --> nG[TRIMMING AND DEFLASHING]
    nG --> nH[QUALITY INSPECTION]
    nH --> nA
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Cooling System Architecture (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1[COOLANT RESERVOIR] --> n2[CHILLER UNIT]
    n2 --> n3[CHILLED WATER PUMP]
    n3 --> n4[MOLD INLET MANIFOLD]
    n4 --> n5[COOLING CHANNEL CORE SIDE]
    n4 --> n6[COOLING CHANNEL CAVITY SIDE]
    n5 --> n7[PLASTIC PART IN CAVITY HEAT ABSORPTION]
    n6 --> n7
    n7 --> n8[OUTLET MANIFOLD]
    n8 --> n9[RETURN LINE]
    n9 --> n1
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy Diagram — Classification of Trimming Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nA[TRIMMING METHODS] --> nB[MANUAL TRIMMING]
    nA --> nC[SEMI AUTOMATED TRIMMING]
    nA --> nD[FULLY AUTOMATED TRIMMING]
    nA --> nE[IN MOULD TRIMMING]

    nB --> nB1[HAND SAW]
    nB --> nB2[UTILITY KNIFE]
    nB --> nB3[FILE AND DEBURRING]

    nC --> nC1[BAND SAW]
    nC --> nC2[SCROLL SAW]
    nC --> nC3[GRINDING WHEEL]

    nD --> nD1[ROBOTIC 6 AXIS ROUTER]
    nD --> nD2[LASER CUTTER CO2 OR FIBRE]
    nD --> nD3[WATERJET CUTTER]
    nD --> nD4[DIE AND PUNCH PRESS]

    nE --> nE1[BLOW MOULDING PINCH OFF]
    nE --> nE2[IN MOULD SLIDING CUTTER]
    nE --> nE3[COMPRESSION MOULDING FLASH LAND]
```

### 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Heat-Transfer Path During Cooling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nA[POLYMER MELT CORE T_M 230 C] -->|CONDUCTION THROUGH SOLIDIFIED SKIN| nB[POLYMER MOULD INTERFACE T_W 30 C]
    nB -->|CONDUCTION THROUGH MOULD STEEL| nC[COOLING CHANNEL INNER WALL T_WALL]
    nC -->|FORCED CONVECTION h 5000 TO 25000 W PER m2 K| nD[COOLANT BULK T_C 18 C]
    nD -->|REJECTED VIA CHILLER| nE[AMBIENT OR TOWER WATER]
```

### 4.5 Diagram Fallback Note

> [!NOTE]
> For a **physical 3-D representation** of cooling channels (e.g., straight drilled channels, baffle-type channels, spiral channels, and conformal cooling channels produced by additive manufacturing / 3D metal printing), the engineering block-flow representation in **Section 4.2** is the recommended Mermaid adaptation. Physical isometric drawings are typically supplied in the *Mould Design* textbook and are not reproducible in Mermaid syntax.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "cooling time" in injection moulding. List any four factors that affect it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Cooling time ($t_c$)** is the time interval between the end of the packing/holding phase and the moment the part is ready to be ejected, during which the polymer solidifies sufficiently to retain its shape.

> Four factors affecting cooling time:
> 1. **Part thickness ($L$)** — has a quadratic effect ($t_c \propto L^{2}$); thicker parts need disproportionately longer cooling.
> 2. **Mould wall temperature ($T_W$)** — lower $T_W$ increases the temperature driving force and reduces $t_c$.
> 3. **Polymer thermal diffusivity ($\alpha$)** — higher $\alpha$ (e.g., PP) reduces $t_c$ compared to lower $\alpha$ (e.g., PC).
> 4. **Ejection temperature ($T_E$)** — a higher acceptable $T_E$ (warmer ejection) shortens the cycle.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*What is "flash" in a plastic moulded part? Name any two methods used to remove it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Flash** is a thin, unwanted web of plastic that escapes from the mould cavity through the parting line, ejector-pin clearances, or vent passages, due to excessive injection pressure, worn mould surfaces, or inadequate clamping force.

> Two trimming/deflashing methods:
> 1. **Manual deflashing** — using a utility knife, file, or abrasive pad (low-volume, low-cost).
> 2. **Automated deflashing** — using a robotic router, tumbling barrel with abrasive media, or cryogenic deflashing (for elastomers/rubbery parts).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In cooling-time derivations, students most commonly lose marks for: **(a)** failing to clearly state the **boundary conditions**, **(b)** using the full thickness $s$ where the half-thickness $L$ is required (or vice-versa), **(c)** missing the **safety factor** in the final numerical answer, and **(d)** forgetting to convert mm to m in numerical problems. Always **box your final answer** and **state all assumptions**.

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the aid of a neat schematic, explain the functional architecture of a water-cooled injection mould. List the design guidelines for cooling channel layout. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **PS (polystyrene) plate** of thickness **8 mm** is injection moulded. Given: $T_M = 210\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_W = 40\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_E = 90\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, and $\alpha_{PS} = 0.85 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^{2}\!/\!\text{s}$. Calculate the **theoretical cooling time** for volume-average criterion. If a safety factor of **1.5** is applied, what is the **actual moulded cooling time**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**
- Schematic of cooling system — **3 marks** (refer Section 4.2 of these notes).
- Functional description: cool water enters through inlet manifold, flows through channels in core and cavity plates, absorbs heat from plastic part, exits to return line and chiller. Coolant is usually water (high $c_p$, non-toxic, cheap) for $T_W < 100$ °C, or thermal oil for higher $T_W$ (e.g., for PC, PMMA).
- Design guidelines — **4 marks** (any four of the following):
  1. Channel diameter **8 – 14 mm**; channel pitch **3D – 5D** from cavity surface.
  2. Maintain **turbulent flow ($Re > 10{,}000$)**; use baffles for long flow paths.
  3. Cool **both** core and cavity sides symmetrically to avoid differential shrinkage and warpage.
  4. Place channels as **close to cavity surface as possible** without weakening the mould.
  5. Use **conformal cooling** (additively manufactured) for complex geometries.
  6. Inlet–outlet temperature difference should be limited to **$\Delta T_{cool} \leq 5$ °C** to ensure uniform wall temperature.
  7. Avoid **hot spots** by adding extra channels in thick sections.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

Half-thickness $L = 8/2 = 4$ mm $= 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

$$\frac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \frac{T_M - T_W}{T_E - T_W} = 0.8106 \cdot \frac{210 - 40}{90 - 40} = 0.8106 \cdot \frac{170}{50} = 0.8106 \cdot 3.4 = 2.756$$

$$\ln(2.756) = 1.014$$

$$\frac{4L^{2}}{\pi^{2}\alpha} = \frac{4 \times (4 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{9.8696 \times 0.85 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5}}{8.39 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{8.39 \times 10^{-7}} = 76.28\ \text{s}$$

$$t_{c,theory} = 76.28 \times 1.014 = 77.35\ \text{s} \approx 77\ \text{s}$$

Actual cooling time (with safety factor 1.5):

$$\boxed{\,t_{c,actual} = 1.5 \times 77 = 115.5\ \text{s} \approx 116\ \text{s}\,}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Classify the various methods of trimming plastic moulded parts. Discuss in detail the working principle of **robotic 6-axis router trimming** with a labelled block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the importance of the **Reynolds number** and the **Dittus–Boelter correlation** in cooling channel design. A cooling channel of **diameter 12 mm** carries water at an average velocity of **2 m/s**. The water properties are: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $k = 0.6$ W/(m·K), $c_p = 4180$ J/(kg·K). Calculate the **Reynolds number, Prandtl number, Nusselt number, and convective heat-transfer coefficient**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]:**
- **Classification** — Manual, Semi-automated, Fully-automated, In-mould. Provide one example of each. **(2 marks)**
- **Robotic 6-axis router trimming** working principle: **(5 marks)**
  - The moulded part is placed on a fixture, picked either by a human or a part-extraction robot from the injection moulding machine.
  - A 6-axis articulated robot (e.g., ABB IRB, KUKA KR, Fanuc M-series) carries a high-speed router spindle (10,000 – 40,000 rpm) with a carbide burr.
  - The robot follows a **pre-programmed path** (CNC G-code) that traces the gate location and flash line, removing excess material.
  - **Vision systems** (2-D or 3-D cameras) detect the exact part position and orientation, compensating for any variation in part placement — this is essential for **Industry 4.0 / smart manufacturing**.
  - **Dust extraction** is integrated to capture the plastic swarf.
  - Cycle time: typically **5 – 15 s** for small to medium parts.
  - Advantages: high repeatability (±0.1 mm), high throughput, no operator fatigue, can run 24/7.

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

Given: $D = 12$ mm $= 0.012$ m, $v = 2$ m/s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $k = 0.6$ W/(m·K), $c_p = 4180$ J/(kg·K).

**Step 1 — Reynolds number.**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 2 \times 0.012}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{24}{0.001} = 24{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Prandtl number.**

$$Pr = \frac{\mu c_p}{k} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 4180}{0.6} = \frac{4.18}{0.6} = 6.97$$

**Step 3 — Nusselt number (Dittus–Boelter, heating of fluid, $n = 0.4$).**

$$Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$$

$$Re^{0.8} = 24000^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \ln 24000} = e^{0.8 \times 10.086} = e^{8.069} = 3205$$

$$Pr^{0.4} = 6.97^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 6.97} = e^{0.4 \times 1.941} = e^{0.7764} = 2.174$$

$$Nu = 0.023 \times 3205 \times 2.174 = 0.023 \times 6968 = 160.3$$

**Step 4 — Convective heat-transfer coefficient.**

$$h = \frac{Nu \cdot k}{D} = \frac{160.3 \times 0.6}{0.012} = \frac{96.18}{0.012} = 8015\ \text{W/(m}^{2}\!\cdot\!\text{K)}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** $Re = 24{,}000$ confirms **turbulent flow** ($Re > 10{,}000$). The convective coefficient $h \approx 8{,}000$ W/(m²·K) is a typical industrial value for water-based mould cooling. The high $h$ keeps the mould wall close to the coolant bulk temperature, ensuring uniform part cooling and shorter cycle times.

$$\boxed{\,Re = 24{,}000,\quad Pr = 6.97,\quad Nu = 160.3,\quad h \approx 8.0 \times 10^{3}\ \text{W/(m}^{2}\!\cdot\!\text{K)}\,}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Triggers on Cooling & Trimming Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch (–1 to –2 marks):** Forgetting to convert mm to m, °C to K (where applicable), or W to kW. *Always write units at every step.*
> 2. **Wrong thickness convention (–1 mark):** Mixing up $L$ (half-thickness) and $s$ (full thickness). *Define $L$ or $s$ explicitly at the start of the solution.*
> 3. **Missing safety factor (–1 mark):** Reporting the theoretical cooling time without the industrial safety factor of 1.3–1.8. *Examiner expects "actual" cooling time for a real moulding.*
> 4. **No assumption statement (–1 mark):** Failing to mention that flow is 1-D, mould wall is at constant $T_W$, polymer properties are constant, etc.
> 5. **Forgetting the logarithm (–2 marks):** Writing $t_c = L^{2}/(\pi^{2}\alpha) \cdot [(T_M - T_W)/(T_E - T_W)]$ without the logarithm. *The logarithmic term is essential; the cooling time depends **logarithmically** on the temperature ratios.*
> 6. **In trimming questions (–2 marks):** Describing the methods but failing to **draw a labelled diagram** — KTU valuation key mandates at least one block/flow diagram per 14-mark answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 10-Point Cheat Sheet:**
>
> - [x] **Cooling consumes 50 % – 80 %** of the total injection-moulding cycle time — it is the rate-controlling step.
> - [x] **Cooling time** $t_c$ scales as $L^{2}$ (quadratic in part thickness) and as $1/\alpha$ (inverse of thermal diffusivity).
> - [x] **Key equation:** $t_c = \dfrac{4L^{2}}{\pi^{2}\alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left[\dfrac{8}{\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{T_M - T_W}{T_E - T_W}\right]$ (with $L$ = half-thickness).
> - [x] **Always apply a safety factor of 1.3 – 1.8** to theoretical cooling time for real moulding.
> - [x] **Reynolds number $Re > 10{,}000$** is mandatory for turbulent cooling; use the **Dittus–Boelter** correlation: $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$.
> - [x] **Convective coefficient $h$** for water in cooling channels is typically **5,000 – 25,000 W/(m²·K)**.
> - [x] **Coolant rule of thumb:** $\Delta T_{water} \leq 5$ °C across the mould for uniform wall temperature.
> - [x] **Defects from poor cooling:** warpage, sink marks, voids, residual stresses, dimensional inaccuracy, and even cracking for highly crystalline materials.
> - [x] **Trimming methods** are classified as Manual, Semi-automated, Automated, and In-mould; the Industry 4.0 standard is **6-axis robotic routing with vision guidance**.
> - [x] **In-mould pinch-off trimming** is unique to blow moulding (bottles, tanks); it eliminates a separate deflashing station and is the most cost-effective method for high-volume hollow parts.
> - [x] **Process parameters that influence cooling & trimming decisions:** polymer type (amorphous vs. crystalline), part wall-thickness uniformity, dimensional tolerance class, production volume, and cost-per-part target.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Balance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat Balance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In polymer processing machinery, a **Heat Balance** (also called **Energy Balance** or **Thermal Balance**) is a fundamental engineering accounting principle that states that for any polymer processing system (extruder, injection molding machine, blow molder, calender, etc.) operating under steady-state conditions, the total thermal energy entering the system must equal the total thermal energy leaving the system plus any accumulation or loss within the system boundaries.

Mathematically, the general heat balance is expressed as:

$$\text{Rate of Energy In} - \text{Rate of Energy Out} + \text{Rate of Energy Generation} = \text{Rate of Energy Accumulation}$$

For a polymer processing machine operating at a controlled steady-state, the rate of energy accumulation is zero, leading to:

$$\sum Q_{in} = \sum Q_{out}$$

The typical heat input terms in polymer processing are:
- $Q_e$ — Heat supplied by external **electrical band heaters** (W)
- $Q_s$ — Heat generated by **viscous shear dissipation** of the rotating screw (W)

The typical heat output terms are:
- $Q_p$ — Useful heat absorbed by the polymer (sensible heating + latent heat of fusion) (W)
- $Q_L$ — Heat lost to the surroundings through barrel walls, hopper, and radiation (W)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Heat balance is treated as a **design and process control tool**, not merely a textbook exercise. Students must be able to compute the wattage rating of heaters required for a given polymer throughput and machine geometry, and also predict the screw speed at which viscous dissipation alone can melt the polymer (adiabatic operation).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Think of a polymer processing machine as a **household water boiler with both an electric heating coil and a mechanical paddle churning the water inside**.

Imagine you have an insulated bucket of water (the polymer melt) with two sources of heat:
1. **An electric immersion rod** (the band heaters on the barrel)
2. **A mechanical blender spinning fast** (the rotating screw)

The water gets heated by **both** the electric rod AND the friction of the blender. However, the bucket loses some heat through its walls to the cold kitchen air.

A **heat balance** is essentially writing your **monthly household budget**:

| Income Side (Q_in) | Expense Side (Q_out) |
|---|---|
| Salary (Electrical Heaters $Q_e$) | Rent & Food (Useful Polymer Heating $Q_p$) |
| Side Hustle (Shear Heating $Q_s$) | Wasted Electricity (Heat Losses $Q_L$) |

If **Income = Expenses**, your savings (energy stored) are zero — this is the **steady-state** condition the machine aims for. If you want to run the machine **faster** (higher throughput), you need more income (more heating capacity) or less waste (better insulation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Significance:** Heat balance equations are used by machine manufacturers (like KraussMaffei, Arburg, Battenfeld, Davis-Standard) to size heater bands, calculate cooling water flow rates, and determine maximum safe throughput. Misjudging the heat balance can result in polymer degradation (too much shear heating) or cold spots and unmelted granules (insufficient heat).

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following constants and metrics are standardly referenced in heat balance calculations for polymer processing:

- **Polymer Density (ρ):** typically **900–1500 kg/m³** for commodity polymers
- **Specific Heat Capacity (Cp):** typically **1000–2500 J/(kg·K)** for polymers in melt state
- **Latent Heat of Fusion (λ):** typically **100–400 kJ/kg** for semi-crystalline polymers (PE, PP, PA, POM)
- **Thermal Conductivity (k):** typically **0.2–0.4 W/(m·K)** for polymers
- **Mechanical Equivalent of Heat:** **1 W = 1 J/s** (**Joule's constant = 1**)

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** For amorphous polymers (PS, PMMA, PC, ABS), the latent heat term $Q_{latent}$ is **zero** because there is no sharp melting point — they pass through a glass transition. This is a common KTU exam trick question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Heat Balance

A polymer processing system is analyzed by defining a **control volume** (the barrel + polymer + screw region). The heat balance is then constructed as follows:

### Step 1: Identify Energy Inflow Terms

**A. Electrical Heating (Q_e)**
- Supplied by **electrical resistance band heaters** wrapped around the barrel.
- Usually divided into **multiple zones** (e.g., 3 to 7 zones in extruders, named Z1, Z2, Z3...).
- Each zone has independent PID temperature control.

$$Q_e = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = \frac{V^2}{R}$$

where $V$ is the voltage, $I$ is the current, $\cos\phi$ is the power factor (≈ 0.95–1.0 for resistive heaters), and $R$ is the heater resistance.

**B. Viscous Shear Heating (Q_s)**
- Generated by the **mechanical work of the rotating screw** against viscous polymer melt.
- Also called **viscous dissipation** or **shear-induced heating**.
- This is the dominant heat source at **high screw speeds** in the metering zone.

$$Q_s = \tau \cdot \dot{\gamma} \cdot V_{polymer}$$

where $\tau$ is the shear stress (Pa), $\dot{\gamma}$ is the shear rate (s⁻¹), and $V_{polymer}$ is the volume of polymer being sheared (m³).

### Step 2: Identify Energy Outflow Terms

**C. Useful Polymer Heating (Q_p)**
The energy actually required to bring polymer from feed temperature to processing temperature:

$$Q_p = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{melt} - T_{feed})$$

where $\dot{m}$ is the **mass throughput** (kg/s), $C_p$ is the specific heat capacity of the polymer (J/kg·K), and $T_{melt} - T_{feed}$ is the temperature rise.

> [!NOTE]
> For **semi-crystalline polymers** (e.g., HDPE, PP, PA6), an additional **latent heat of fusion** must be added:
> $$Q_p = \dot{m} \cdot [C_{p,solid}(T_m - T_{feed}) + \lambda + C_{p,melt}(T_{process} - T_m)]$$

**D. Heat Losses to Surroundings (Q_L)**
- Occur through the barrel wall (conduction + convection + radiation).
- Critical during start-up; negligible (relatively) at steady state with good insulation.

$$Q_L = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{barrel} - T_{ambient})$$

where $U$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), $A$ is the barrel surface area (m²).

### Step 3: Apply the Steady-State Energy Balance

$$\boxed{Q_e + Q_s = Q_p + Q_L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The most common formulation asked in exams is the **simplified heat balance** that neglects $Q_L$ (assuming well-insulated barrel) — this gives $Q_e + Q_s = Q_p$. The student must clearly state which assumptions are made.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Term

| Term | Physical Reason | Industrial Importance |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_e$ | Joule heating from electrical current | Provides controlled, uniform heating during start-up and in feed zone |
| $Q_s$ | Polymer viscosity × shear rate | Dominates at high screw speed — can be 60–80% of total energy in high-speed extruders |
| $Q_p$ | Heating solid pellets to melt state | Determines minimum energy required for given throughput |
| $Q_L$ | Conduction through barrel steel | Justifies use of insulation jackets |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_e = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | Electrical heater power | W |
| 2 | $Q_s = \eta \cdot \tau \cdot \dot{\gamma} \cdot V$ | Viscous shear dissipation | W |
| 3 | $Q_p = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat for polymer | W |
| 4 | $Q_p = \dot{m} \cdot [C_{p,s}(T_m - T_f) + \lambda + C_{p,m}(T_p - T_m)]$ | Total polymer heating (semi-crystalline) | W |
| 5 | $Q_L = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{loss}$ | Heat loss to environment | W |
| 6 | $Q_e + Q_s = Q_p + Q_L$ | **Master Heat Balance Equation** | W |
| 7 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q_v = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot v_s \cdot \sin\theta$ | Mass throughput | kg/s |
| 8 | $\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\pi D N}{h}$ | Shear rate in metering zone | s⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n$ | Shear stress (power-law fluid) | Pa |
| 10 | $\eta = \frac{Q_s}{\text{Mechanical Power Input}}$ | Dissipation efficiency (typically 0.85–0.95) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU numerical problems, the mass throughput $\dot{m}$ must be calculated first using **screw geometry equations** (Module 2 prerequisite knowledge) before being substituted into $Q_p$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Heat balance calculations are critical for:

1. **Heater Sizing** — Determining kW rating required for extruder zones.
2. **Screw Speed Optimization** — Finding the speed at which the process becomes **adiabatic** (all heat from shear).
3. **Cooling System Design** — Sizing cooling water flow rates for barrel cooling jackets.
4. **Energy Auditing** — Calculating the **specific energy consumption (SEC)** of a machine:

$$\text{SEC} = \frac{Q_e + \text{Mechanical Power}}{Q_p} \quad \text{(unitless or kWh/kg)}$$

5. **Process Troubleshooting** — Diagnosing why polymer degrades (excess shear) or won't melt (insufficient total heat).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the General Heat Balance Equation

Consider a **single-screw extruder** as the control volume. The system boundary encloses the barrel, the screw, and the polymer mass inside.

**Step 1: Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics to the open system**

$$\frac{dE_{system}}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{Q}_{out} + \dot{W}_{in} - \dot{W}_{out} + \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} h_{out}$$

**Step 2: Simplifications for a polymer processing machine**

- No shaft work output (screw work is internal): $\dot{W}_{out} = 0$
- Mechanical work from motor is converted entirely into viscous dissipation (shear heat): $\dot{W}_{in} \rightarrow Q_s$
- Polymer mass flow is constant (conservation of mass): $\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out} = \dot{m}$
- Inlet polymer is solid granules at feed temperature, outlet is melt at processing temperature

Substituting:

$$\frac{dE_{system}}{dt} = Q_e + Q_s - Q_L - Q_p + \dot{m}(h_{feed} - h_{melt})$$

**Step 3: Combine the enthalpy terms with the polymer heating term**

The polymer heating $Q_p$ inherently includes the enthalpy change of the polymer stream:

$$Q_p = \dot{m}(h_{melt} - h_{feed})$$

Therefore, the equation reduces to:

$$\frac{dE_{system}}{dt} = Q_e + Q_s - Q_L - Q_p$$

**Step 4: Apply the Steady-State Condition**

At steady state, $\frac{dE_{system}}{dt} = 0$ (no accumulation), giving:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_e + Q_s - Q_L - Q_p &= 0 \\
\therefore Q_e + Q_s &= Q_p + Q_L
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Master Heat Balance Equation** for any polymer processing machine at steady state.

## 3.2 Derivation of Viscous Shear Heating (Q_s)

The shear stress generated in the metering zone of the screw is:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}$$

where $\mu$ is the apparent viscosity (Pa·s) and $\dot{\gamma}$ is the shear rate (s⁻¹).

For a polymer melt obeying the **Power Law model**:

$$\mu = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

$$\therefore \tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$

The volumetric rate of viscous dissipation (W/m³) is:

$$\dot{E}_{v} = \tau \cdot \dot{\gamma} = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n+1}$$

Multiplying by the volume of polymer in the metering zone $V_{meter}$:

$$Q_s = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n+1} \cdot V_{meter}$$

Or, equivalently, using screw parameters:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{\gamma} &= \frac{\pi D N}{h} \\
V_{meter} &= \frac{\pi}{4} (D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot L_m
\end{aligned}$$

where $D_o$ = barrel ID, $D_i$ = screw root diameter, $L_m$ = metering zone length, $h$ = flight depth, $N$ = screw speed (rev/s).

Therefore:

$$Q_s = K \cdot \left(\frac{\pi D N}{h}\right)^{n+1} \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2) \cdot L_m$$

## 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU-style numerical** that combines heat balance with screw geometry from Module 2. Master this pattern for the 14-mark questions.

**Problem Statement:**

A single-screw extruder has the following specifications:
- Screw diameter $D = 60$ mm, screw length-to-diameter ratio $L/D = 25$
- Metering zone length = 30% of total screw length, flight depth $h = 3$ mm
- Screw speed $N = 100$ rpm
- Screw lead $L_s = D$ (square-pitched)
- Helix angle $\theta = 17.65°$
- Metering zone polymer viscosity $K = 20,000$ Pa·sⁿ, power-law index $n = 0.4$
- Throughput $Q_v = 0.00005$ m³/s, polymer density $\rho = 950$ kg/m³
- Specific heat $C_p = 2200$ J/(kg·K)
- Polymer feed temperature $T_f = 25°C$, processing melt temperature $T_p = 200°C$
- Heat loss $Q_L = 500$ W (assume for simplicity)

**Find:** (i) Mass throughput, (ii) Shear heating $Q_s$, (iii) Polymer heating $Q_p$, (iv) Required heater power $Q_e$.

### Solution Step-by-Step

**Step (i): Mass Throughput**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q_v = 950 \cdot 0.00005 = 0.0475 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step (ii): Shear Heating Q_s**

First, calculate the shear rate:

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\pi D N}{h} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.060 \cdot (100/60)}{0.003} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.060 \cdot 1.667}{0.003}$$

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{0.3142}{0.003} = 104.72 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

Next, calculate the metering zone volume:

$$L_m = 0.30 \cdot L = 0.30 \cdot 25 \cdot 0.060 = 0.45 \text{ m}$$

Approximating $D_o \approx D$ and $D_i \approx D - 2h$:

$$D_i = 0.060 - 2(0.003) = 0.054 \text{ m}$$

$$V_{meter} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.060^2 - 0.054^2) \cdot 0.45$$

$$V_{meter} = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0036 - 0.002916) \cdot 0.45 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.000684) \cdot 0.45$$

$$V_{meter} = 0.7854 \cdot 0.000684 \cdot 0.45 = 0.0002418 \text{ m}^3$$

Now calculate $Q_s$:

$$Q_s = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n+1} \cdot V_{meter} = 20000 \cdot (104.72)^{1.4} \cdot 0.0002418$$

Calculate $(104.72)^{1.4}$:

$$(104.72)^{1.4} = e^{1.4 \cdot \ln(104.72)} = e^{1.4 \cdot 4.6513} = e^{6.5118} = 672.5$$

Therefore:

$$Q_s = 20000 \cdot 672.5 \cdot 0.0002418 = 20000 \cdot 0.1626 = 3252 \text{ W} \approx 3.25 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (iii): Polymer Heating Q_p**

$$Q_p = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot (T_p - T_f) = 0.0475 \cdot 2200 \cdot (200 - 25)$$

$$Q_p = 0.0475 \cdot 2200 \cdot 175 = 0.0475 \cdot 385000 = 18287.5 \text{ W} \approx 18.29 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (iv): Required Heater Power Q_e**

Apply the master heat balance equation:

$$Q_e + Q_s = Q_p + Q_L$$

$$Q_e = Q_p + Q_L - Q_s$$

$$Q_e = 18287.5 + 500 - 3252 = 15535.5 \text{ W} \approx 15.54 \text{ kW}$$

### Interpretation of Results

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In this example, viscous shear heating supplies only about **17.8%** of the total energy needed. The remaining **82.2%** must come from band heaters. At very high screw speeds (e.g., 500 rpm), $Q_s$ scales as $N^{n+1} = N^{1.4}$, increasing by approximately a factor of **9.5×**, easily exceeding $Q_p$ — this is when **adiabatic operation** becomes feasible.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Condition | Dominant Heat Source | Operational Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Start-up / Cold machine | External heaters ($Q_e \gg Q_s$) | Full heater power needed |
| Normal operation (low N) | Heaters ($Q_e > Q_s$) | Stable, controllable |
| High-speed (high N) | Shear ($Q_s > Q_e$) | Risk of polymer degradation |
| Adiabatic operation | $Q_s = Q_p$ entirely | No heaters needed (ideal) |
| Insulated barrel | $Q_L \approx 0$ | Most efficient case |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Heat Balance Flow Diagram for a Single-Screw Extruder

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates how thermal energy flows through a single-screw extruder system, showing all input streams, output streams, and the master balance equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Power Supply 220V 415V] -->|Q_e Band Heaters| B[Barrel Control Volume]
    C[Motor Drive System] -->|Mechanical Power| D[Screw Rotation]
    D -->|Viscous Dissipation Q_s| B
    E[Polymer Feed Hopper 25C Granules] -->|Solid Polymer Feed| B
    B -->|Sensible Heat Q_p_sensible| F[Melted Polymer Output 200C]
    B -->|Latent Heat Q_p_latent if crystalline| F
    B -->|Conduction Convection Radiation Q_L| G[Ambient Surroundings 25C]
    F -->|Melted Polymer Stream| H[Die and Downstream Equipment]
    B -.->|Steady State Condition| I[Q_e + Q_s = Q_p + Q_L]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Heat Balance in Injection Molding Machine

For an injection molding machine, the heat balance is more complex because the operation is **cyclic** (plastication + injection + cooling + ejection) rather than continuous.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS
        A1[Barrel Band Heaters Z1 Z2 Z3 Z4] -->|Q_e_total| SUM
        A2[Screw Rotation Hydraulic Motor] -->|Shear Dissipation Q_s| SUM
        A3[Polymer Pellets Cold Feed] -->|Solid Feed m_dot| SYSTEM
    end
    subgraph SYSTEM[Plasticizing Unit Control Volume]
        SUM[Summation Node] --> CORE[Barrel Screw Polymer Mass]
        CORE -->|Steady State Plastication| BALANCE
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS
        BALANCE[Melt Accumulator Front of Screw]
        SYSTEM -->|Q_p Useful Polymer Heating| OUTPUT1[Melt at Process Temperature]
        CORE -->|Q_L Heat Loss| OUTPUT2[Cooling Water Barrel Jacket]
        CORE -->|Q_mold Heat to Mold| OUTPUT3[Mold Cavity during Injection]
    end
    OUTPUT1 -->|Injected into mold| OUTPUT3
```

## 4.3 Energy Distribution Sankey-Style Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOURCES[Energy Inputs 100%]
        S1[Heater Power 70%]
        S2[Shear Heating 30%]
    end
    S1 --> M1[Polymer Melting 60%]
    S2 --> M1
    M1 --> USE[Useful Process Energy]
    S1 --> M2[Heat Loss 10%]
    S2 --> M2
    M1 --> DES[Discarded Heat]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** This block diagram represents the **functional energy flow architecture** within a polymer processing machine. Actual physical sketches (with screw geometry, barrel cross-section) cannot be rendered in Mermaid syntax; therefore, the system is presented as a control-volume-based functional block diagram, which is the **engineering standard** for thermal analysis.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q: Define heat balance as applied to polymer processing machinery. Write the general steady-state heat balance equation and identify all terms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Heat balance is the application of the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to a polymer processing system, stating that at steady state, the rate of thermal energy entering the system must equal the rate of thermal energy leaving the system, with no net accumulation.

The general steady-state heat balance equation is:

$$Q_e + Q_s = Q_p + Q_L$$

Where:
- $Q_e$ = Heat supplied by **electrical band heaters** (W)
- $Q_s$ = Heat generated by **viscous shear dissipation** in the rotating screw (W)
- $Q_p$ = Useful **heat absorbed by the polymer** for sensible heating and melting (W)
- $Q_L$ = **Heat lost** to the surroundings through barrel walls (W)

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for identifying terms with correct units)*

---

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q: Differentiate between sensible heat and latent heat in the context of polymer heating. State one polymer type for which latent heat must be considered.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sensible Heat | Latent Heat |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Heat that causes a **temperature change** without phase change | Heat absorbed during a **phase change** (solid → melt) at constant temperature |
| Formula | $Q = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q = m \cdot \lambda$ |
| Polymer Example | All polymers (PS, PMMA in glass transition) | **Semi-crystalline polymers**: HDPE, LDPE, PP, PA6, POM |

*(2 marks for differentiation, 1 mark for example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the steady-state heat balance equation for a single-screw extruder. State all assumptions clearly.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define the Control Volume** (1 mark)

The control volume encloses the barrel, screw, and polymer mass. System boundaries are: barrel inner wall, hopper opening, and die exit.

**Step 2: Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics** (2 marks)

For an open system at steady state:

$$\sum Q_{in} - \sum Q_{out} + \sum \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out} h_{out} = 0$$

**Step 3: Identify All Energy Streams** (1 mark)

- Energy In: $Q_e$ (band heaters), $Q_s$ (viscous dissipation from motor work)
- Energy Out: $Q_L$ (losses), $Q_p$ (polymer heating = $\dot{m}(h_{out} - h_{in})$)

**Step 4: Combine the enthalpy and heating terms** (1 mark)

$$Q_e + Q_s - Q_L - \dot{m}(h_{out} - h_{in}) = 0$$

**Step 5: Final Equation** (1 mark)

$$\boxed{Q_e + Q_s = Q_p + Q_L}$$

**Assumptions** (1 mark):
1. Steady-state operation
2. Negligible potential and kinetic energy changes
3. No chemical reaction or degradation
4. Uniform polymer properties within each zone
5. Polymer behaves as incompressible

**(b) [7 Marks] A 60 mm single-screw extruder operates at 80 rpm. The metering zone length is 1.5 m, flight depth 2.5 mm, screw diameter 60 mm. Power-law constants: K = 15,000 Pa·sⁿ, n = 0.35. Polymer throughput is 30 kg/h, $C_p$ = 2100 J/kg·K, feed temp = 30°C, melt temp = 220°C, density = 920 kg/m³. Calculate the viscous shear heating $Q_s$ and the polymer sensible heating $Q_p$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 0.060$ m, $L_m = 1.5$ m, $h = 0.0025$ m
- $N = 80/60 = 1.333$ rev/s
- $K = 15{,}000$, $n = 0.35$
- $\dot{m} = 30/3600 = 0.00833$ kg/s
- $C_p = 2100$, $T_f = 30°C$, $T_p = 220°C$
- $\rho = 920$ kg/m³

**Step 1: Shear rate in metering zone** (1 mark)

$$\dot{\gamma} = \frac{\pi D N}{h} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.060 \cdot 1.333}{0.0025} = \frac{0.2513}{0.0025} = 100.53 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2: Viscosity at this shear rate** (1 mark)

$$\mu = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^{n-1} = 15000 \cdot (100.53)^{-0.65}$$

Calculate $(100.53)^{-0.65}$:

$$\ln(100.53) = 4.611, \quad -0.65 \cdot 4.611 = -2.997$$

$$(100.53)^{-0.65} = e^{-2.997} = 0.0500$$

$$\mu = 15000 \cdot 0.0500 = 750 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 3: Shear stress** (1 mark)

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma} = 750 \cdot 100.53 = 75{,}400 \text{ Pa}$$

Or, alternatively: $\tau = K \cdot \dot{\gamma}^n = 15000 \cdot (100.53)^{0.35} = 15000 \cdot 6.247 = 93{,}700$ Pa *(small rounding discrepancy acceptable)*

**Step 4: Volumetric dissipation rate** (1 mark)

$$\dot{E}_v = \tau \cdot \dot{\gamma} = 93{,}700 \cdot 100.53 = 9.42 \times 10^6 \text{ W/m}^3$$

**Step 5: Metering zone volume** (1 mark)

Using $D_i = D - 2h = 0.060 - 0.005 = 0.055$ m:

$$V_m = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.060^2 - 0.055^2) \cdot 1.5 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.0036 - 0.003025) \cdot 1.5$$

$$V_m = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.000575) \cdot 1.5 = 0.7854 \cdot 0.000862 = 6.77 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 6: Total shear heating** (1 mark)

$$Q_s = \dot{E}_v \cdot V_m = 9.42 \times 10^6 \cdot 6.77 \times 10^{-4} = 6378 \text{ W} \approx 6.38 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7: Polymer sensible heating** (1 mark)

$$Q_p = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.00833 \cdot 2100 \cdot (220 - 30)$$

$$Q_p = 0.00833 \cdot 2100 \cdot 190 = 0.00833 \cdot 399000 = 3324 \text{ W} \approx 3.32 \text{ kW}$$

**Conclusion:** In this case, $Q_s > Q_p$, indicating that viscous dissipation alone can melt the polymer — the machine can be operated in **adiabatic mode** if heaters are turned off.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain with a neat sketch the various sources of heat input and heat output in a single-screw extruder barrel. Establish the heat balance equation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Sources of Heat Input** (3 marks):

1. **External Band Heaters ($Q_e$)**: Electrical resistance heaters wrapped around the barrel in 3 to 7 zones. Each zone has independent PID temperature control. Power input = $V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$.

2. **Viscous Shear Dissipation ($Q_s$)**: Mechanical work done by the rotating screw against the viscous polymer melt. Maximum in the metering zone where clearance is smallest. Equals motor power minus mechanical losses.

**Sources of Heat Output** (2 marks):

1. **Useful Polymer Heating ($Q_p$)**: Energy to raise polymer temperature from feed (≈ 25–30°C) to melt (≈ 200–300°C) and (for semi-crystalline polymers) provide latent heat of fusion.

2. **Heat Losses ($Q_L$)**: Conduction through barrel wall + natural/radiative convection to surroundings. Reduced by insulation jackets.

**Heat Balance Equation** (2 marks):

$$Q_e + Q_s = Q_p + Q_L \quad \text{(at steady state)}$$

**(b) [7 Marks] An injection molding machine has a plasticizing capacity of 50 kg/h. The polymer is polypropylene with $C_p$ = 2300 J/kg·K, latent heat of fusion $\lambda$ = 190 kJ/kg, melting point $T_m$ = 165°C. Polymer enters at 30°C and exits melt at 230°C. Solid $C_p$ = 1800 J/kg·K. Calculate the total heat required to melt the polymer per hour.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\dot{m} = 50$ kg/h = $50/3600 = 0.01389$ kg/s, $T_f = 30°C$, $T_m = 165°C$, $T_p = 230°C$, $C_{p,s} = 1800$ J/kg·K, $C_{p,m} = 2300$ J/kg·K, $\lambda = 190{,}000$ J/kg.

**Step 1: Heat to warm solid from 30°C to 165°C** (2 marks)

$$Q_1 = \dot{m} \cdot C_{p,s} \cdot (T_m - T_f) = 0.01389 \cdot 1800 \cdot (165 - 30)$$

$$Q_1 = 0.01389 \cdot 1800 \cdot 135 = 3375 \text{ W} = 3.375 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Latent heat of fusion** (2 marks)

$$Q_2 = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda = 0.01389 \cdot 190{,}000 = 2639 \text{ W} = 2.639 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Heat to warm melt from 165°C to 230°C** (2 marks)

$$Q_3 = \dot{m} \cdot C_{p,m} \cdot (T_p - T_m) = 0.01389 \cdot 2300 \cdot (230 - 165)$$

$$Q_3 = 0.01389 \cdot 2300 \cdot 65 = 2076 \text{ W} = 2.076 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Total heat per hour** (1 mark)

$$Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 = 3375 + 2639 + 2076 = 8090 \text{ W} \approx 8.09 \text{ kW}$$

Per hour basis:

$$Q_{hourly} = 8090 \cdot 3600 / 1000 = 29{,}124 \text{ kJ/h} \approx 29.1 \text{ MJ/h}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the latent heat term** for semi-crystalline polymers is the #1 reason students lose 2-3 marks. Always check if the polymer is semi-crystalline (HDPE, LDPE, PP, PA, POM, PET) before writing the heat balance.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** between kg/h and kg/s are extremely common. Always convert mass flow rate to kg/s before calculating power in Watts.
> 3. **Forgetting to state assumptions** (steady state, negligible losses, incompressible polymer) results in a deduction of 1-2 marks even when the equation is correct.
> 4. **Incorrect screw volume calculation**: Students often use the full barrel volume instead of the annular polymer volume ($D_o^2 - D_i^2$), losing 1-2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up $C_p$ for solid and melt** states — they are different for semi-crystalline polymers (e.g., $C_{p,solid} < C_{p,melt}$ generally).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Heat Balance (Module 3):**

- [ ] **Master Equation:** $Q_e + Q_s = Q_p + Q_L$ — write this down the moment you see a heat balance question.
- [ ] **Always convert** mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ to **kg/s** when calculating power in Watts.
- [ ] **Semi-crystalline polymers** require three heating stages: solid sensible → latent → melt sensible. **Amorphous polymers** only need a single sensible heating term.
- [ ] **Shear heating $Q_s$ scales as $N^{n+1}$**, where $n$ is the power-law index (typically 0.2–0.5 for polymer melts). Small increases in $N$ cause large increases in $Q_s$.
- [ ] **Adiabatic operation** is achieved when $Q_s = Q_p$; beyond this point, $Q_e$ must become negative (i.e., cooling required).
- [ ] **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** = Total energy input / mass output (kWh/kg). Modern extruders target SEC < 0.3 kWh/kg.
- [ ] **Heat losses $Q_L$** are negligible in well-insulated machines; the simplified balance $Q_e + Q_s = Q_p$ is often used in exam problems.
- [ ] **Units consistency**: Power (W) = Energy rate (J/s); Energy (J) = Power (W) × Time (s).
- [ ] **Power-law viscosity model** $\mu = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ is the standard model for polymer melt viscosity in heat balance problems.
- [ ] **Remember the three Zone Concept**: In **Feed Zone**, $Q_e \gg Q_s$ (solid conveying, low shear). In **Metering Zone**, $Q_s$ becomes dominant.
- [ ] **Standard values to memorize**: $\rho_{polymer} \approx 900$–1500 kg/m³, $C_p \approx 1000$–2500 J/(kg·K), $\lambda \approx 100$–400 kJ/kg.
- [ ] **Keywords that signal a heat balance question**: "calculate the heater power", "adiabatic condition", "melt temperature", "specific energy consumption", "shear heating", "throughput".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Always define the **control volume**, **state all assumptions**, **list given data with units**, and **box your final numerical answer** with correct units. KTU examiners award full marks only when the methodology is clearly visible, even if a minor numerical slip occurs. Showing the heat balance equation at the start of any numerical solution is worth **1 free mark**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shrinkage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shrinkage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shrinkage (KTU 2024 Definition):** Shrinkage is the reduction in the linear dimensions or volume of a moulded/sintered/extruded polymer product that occurs after the part is removed from the mould, during cooling to ambient temperature, during subsequent storage, or during service. It is expressed as a percentage of the original mould dimension and is one of the most critical design parameters in polymer product manufacturing.

Mathematically, the **linear mould shrinkage (S_L)** is defined as:

$$S_L = \frac{L_{mould} - L_{part}}{L_{mould}} \times 100 \;\; (\%)$$

Where:
- $L_{mould}$ = the linear dimension of the cavity at room temperature (typically **25 °C**).
- $L_{part}$ = the corresponding linear dimension of the moulded part measured after it has been conditioned at room temperature for a standardized period (usually **24 hours** per ISO 294-4).

> [!NOTE]
> **Volumetric Shrinkage (S_V):** For a part undergoing isotropic (uniform in all directions) shrinkage, the relationship between volumetric and linear shrinkage is given by $S_V \approx 3 S_L$. This is derived from the volumetric thermal expansion coefficient being approximately three times the linear coefficient for small strains (the rule holds good for shrinkage values below 6 %).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine baking a **sponge cake** in a rectangular tin. When you take the hot cake out of the tin and let it cool on a rack, you immediately notice two things:
1. The cake is slightly smaller in all directions than the tin it was baked in.
2. If you let it sit for a day, it shrinks even more, becoming denser and slightly drier.

Polymer shrinkage behaves in a **strikingly similar manner**:
- The **mould cavity** is the "cake tin."
- The **hot polymer melt** poured into the cavity is the "cake batter."
- As the melt cools and solidifies inside the mould, the part contracts — this is the **primary (mould) shrinkage** observed immediately at ejection.
- Once the part is exposed to ambient air, further molecular rearrangement and relaxation of internal stresses cause additional contraction — this is the **post-mould (secondary) shrinkage**, which may continue for hours, days, or even weeks.
- For semi-crystalline polymers like **Polypropylene (PP)** and **Nylon (PA)**, a third shrinkage stage occurs as crystallization completes, similar to how the cake becomes more rigid as it fully sets.

> [!TIP]
> **Why must a polymer engineer care?** Because the mould cavity is cut slightly *larger* than the final part dimension by a precise percentage (the **mould shrinkage allowance**). If this allowance is wrong, every single part coming out of the mould will be **out of tolerance**, leading to rejection, assembly fit problems, and significant commercial loss.

### 1.3 Classification of Shrinkage (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

Shrinkage is classified into **three principal categories** based on when and why it occurs:

| Sl. | Type of Shrinkage | Stage of Occurrence | Primary Cause |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mould Shrinkage (Primary / Thermal Contraction)** | Inside the mould, between gate-freeze and ejection | Thermal contraction of the solidified polymer as it cools from ejection temperature to mould wall temperature |
| 2 | **Post-Mould Shrinkage (Secondary)** | 0 to 24 hours after ejection | Relaxation of frozen-in molecular orientation and stress |
| 3 | **Annealing / Service Shrinkage (Tertiary)** | Days to months after ejection | Continued secondary crystallization (semi-crystalline polymers) and additional molecular relaxation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Reference:** Part dimensions per **ISO 294-4** are measured **24 hours after ejection** to allow for the bulk of post-mould shrinkage to occur, giving a standardized value used for mould design.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Polymer Shrinkage — Three Root Causes

Shrinkage in polymers is fundamentally driven by **three microscopic mechanisms**. Understanding these is critical for predicting and compensating for shrinkage in mould design.

#### 2.1.1 Thermal Contraction (PVT Behaviour)
All materials contract when cooled. The **PVT relationship** (Pressure–Volume–Temperature) governs how a polymer melt transitions to a solid.

- Above the melting/softening point, the polymer has a high **thermal expansion coefficient** (α_liquid ≈ 6 to 10 × 10⁻⁴ /K for most thermoplastics).
- Below the solidification transition, it has a much lower coefficient (α_solid ≈ 2 to 6 × 10⁻⁵ /K).
- The change in specific volume at the phase transition is the **single largest contributor** to mould shrinkage.
- For amorphous polymers: thermal contraction is **continuous and gradual** through the glass transition.
- For semi-crystalline polymers: there is an **abrupt specific-volume drop** at the crystallization temperature (T_c), which dominates shrinkage.

#### 2.1.2 Crystallization (Semi-Crystalline Polymers Only)
- Polymers such as **PP, PE, PA, POM, PET** undergo partial crystallization as they cool.
- Polymer chains pack into denser crystalline lamellae, causing a large volume reduction.
- Crystallinity degree (X_c) directly governs shrinkage: higher X_c → higher shrinkage.
- Crystallization continues *after* ejection (secondary crystallization), which is why semi-crystalline polymers exhibit significant **post-mould shrinkage**.

#### 2.1.3 Molecular Orientation & Frozen-in Stresses
- During injection/filling, polymer molecules are stretched and aligned in the flow direction (shear-induced orientation).
- Rapid cooling "freezes" this orientation in place — but the part is in a **metastable, stressed state**.
- After ejection, viscoelastic relaxation allows chains to return to a more random coil configuration, producing **anisotropic shrinkage** (different in flow direction vs. cross-flow direction).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Shrinkage Development During the Moulding Cycle

1. **Filling Stage:** Molten polymer at T_melt (~200–300 °C) fills the cavity. No shrinkage yet — system is being pressurized.
2. **Packing/Hold Stage:** Additional polymer is forced in to compensate for contraction as the skin solidifies. Pressure is high (~ 40–80 MPa).
3. **Cooling Stage:** The part continues to cool; once the gate freezes, no more material enters. The part now shrinks *inside* the mould.
4. **Ejection:** The part is dimensionally smaller than the cavity due to thermal contraction + partial crystallization.
5. **Post-Mould Period:** Further crystallization + stress relaxation → additional dimensional change.
6. **Equilibrium:** After ~24–48 hours, the part reaches a stable dimension used for tolerance checking.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Exams** — Memorize the table below; questions are frequently asked on these relationships.

| Sl. | Formula | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
