| 1 | $S_L = \dfrac{L_m - L_p}{L_m} \times 100$ | **Linear Mould Shrinkage (%)** | Mould design, dimension calculation |
| 2 | $S_V \approx 3 S_L$ | Volumetric-to-linear shrinkage (isotropic) | Volume change estimation |
| 3 | $\Delta L = L_m \cdot S_L$ | Absolute linear contraction | Cavity dimension calculation |
| 4 | $L_{cavity} = L_{part}(1 + S_L/100)$ | **Mould cavity size from part size** | Core design calculation |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \alpha_V \cdot \Delta T$ | Volumetric thermal contraction | Thermal shrinkage estimate |
| 6 | $S_{total} = S_{mould} + S_{post} + S_{anneal}$ | **Total shrinkage (additive)** | Tolerance stack-up |
| 7 | $\rho_{solid} > \rho_{melt}$ (always true) | Density increases on solidification | PVT design logic |
| 8 | $\alpha_{liquid} \gg \alpha_{solid}$ | Liquid expansion >> solid expansion | Why packing pressure matters |
| 9 | $S_{flow} \neq S_{transverse}$ | Anisotropy statement | Predicting warp |
| 10 | $S_{\%} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_p} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_m} \times \rho_m \times 100$ | Shrinkage from density | Specialized calculation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Caveat on Equation 6 (additive rule):** The "additive" rule $S_{total} = S_{mould} + S_{post} + S_{anneal}$ is valid only when each shrinkage stage is measured and reported as a percentage of the *original mould dimension* $L_m$. In some KTU problems, students confuse this and get incorrect answers.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mould Cavity Design:** The single most important application. A mould for a PP part of nominal 100 mm dimension with $S_L = 1.8\%$ requires a cavity of $L_{cavity} = 100(1 + 0.018) = 101.8$ mm.
- **Tolerance Allocation:** Knowing each shrinkage component lets designers allocate tighter tolerances to critical features and looser ones to non-critical ones.
- **Predicting Warpage:** Anisotropic shrinkage (flow vs. transverse) is the leading cause of warpage. Predicting the *direction* of warp requires the $S_{flow}$ vs. $S_{transverse}$ values.
- **Material Selection:** Materials with low and isotropic shrinkage (e.g., **filled grades, ABS**) are chosen for precision parts; high-shrinkage materials are avoided.
- **Process Optimization:** Holding pressure, holding time, mould temperature, and cooling rate are tuned to *minimize variability* in shrinkage.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cavity Dimension from Desired Part Dimension

**Problem Statement:** You need to mould a rectangular plate of nominal length **150 mm** using **Polypropylene (PP)** which has a linear mould shrinkage of **1.8 %** (per ISO 294-4). What should the corresponding mould cavity length be?

**Step 1 — Identify the relationship.**
By definition:
$$S_L = \frac{L_m - L_p}{L_m} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the knowns.**
$S_L = 1.8$, $L_p = 150$ mm, $L_m = ?$

$$1.8 = \frac{L_m - 150}{L_m} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Divide by 100.**
$$0.018 = \frac{L_m - 150}{L_m}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply both sides by $L_m$.**
$$0.018 \cdot L_m = L_m - 150$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to isolate $L_m$.**
$$L_m - 0.018 \cdot L_m = 150$$

$$L_m (1 - 0.018) = 150$$

$$L_m (0.982) = 150$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $L_m$.**
$$L_m = \frac{150}{0.982} = 152.749 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7 — State the final answer with appropriate precision.**
$$\boxed{L_m \approx 152.75 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The direct formula $L_m = L_p/(1 - S_L/100)$ is faster than the verbose derivation. Always show the derivation for full marks in KTU 14-mark problems, but for time-saving, this closed form is excellent for Part-A 3-mark problems.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Shrinkage from Density Data

**Problem Statement:** A polymer is moulded at a melt density $\rho_m = 0.78$ g/cm³ and has a solid density $\rho_p = 0.85$ g/cm³. Calculate the volumetric shrinkage in %.

**Step 1 — Apply the volumetric shrinkage formula.**
$$S_V = \frac{V_m - V_p}{V_m} \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{V_p}{V_m}\right) \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute volumes using mass conservation (mass m is constant).**
Since $V = m/\rho$:
$$\frac{V_p}{V_m} = \frac{m/\rho_p}{m/\rho_m} = \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_p}$$

**Step 3 — Plug into the shrinkage formula.**
$$S_V = \left(1 - \frac{\rho_m}{\rho_p}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{\rho_p - \rho_m}{\rho_p}\right) \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$S_V = \left(\frac{0.85 - 0.78}{0.85}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{0.07}{0.85}\right) \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Compute the result.**
$$S_V = 0.08235 \times 100 = 8.235 \;\; \approx \;\; 8.24 \;\;\%$$

**Step 6 — Convert to linear shrinkage using the isotropic relation.**
$$S_L \approx \frac{S_V}{3} = \frac{8.24}{3} = 2.75 \;\;\%$$

$$\boxed{S_V \approx 8.24\%, \quad S_L \approx 2.75\%}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Contraction Component

**Problem Statement:** A 200 mm PP bar is moulded at a melt temperature of **230 °C** and ejected at a mould wall temperature of **60 °C**. Given: $\alpha_{solid} = 7 \times 10^{-5}$ /K (below T_g ≈ -10 °C for PP atactic; we use the average solid value). Estimate the thermal linear contraction in mm.

**Step 1 — Recall the linear thermal strain formula.**
$$\frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \alpha_L \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Delta T$.**
$$\Delta T = T_{eject} - T_{final} = 60 - 25 = 35 \text{ °C}$$

(NB: We use ejection temperature, not melt temperature, because shrinkage initiates only after the polymer solidifies; melt contraction is compensated by packing pressure.)

**Step 3 — Compute the linear strain.**
$$\frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = 7 \times 10^{-5} \times 35 = 2.45 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Compute absolute contraction.**
$$\Delta L = L_0 \times 2.45 \times 10^{-3} = 200 \times 2.45 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta L \approx 0.49 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Express as a percentage.**
$$S_{thermal} = 2.45 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 = 0.245 \;\;\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Check:** Pure thermal contraction contributes only ~0.25 % of the 1.8 % total mould shrinkage of PP. The remaining ~1.55 % comes from **crystallization**. This highlights why semi-crystalline polymers shrink far more than amorphous ones.

### 3.4 KTU Numerical-Problem Type — Analytical Decision Matrix

| Given Quantity | Required Quantity | Governing Equation | Typical KTU Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L_m$, $L_p$ | $S_L$ | $S_L = \dfrac{L_m - L_p}{L_m} \times 100$ | 3 marks |
| $L_p$, $S_L$ | $L_m$ | $L_m = \dfrac{L_p}{1 - S_L/100}$ | 7 marks |
| $\rho_m$, $\rho_p$ | $S_V$, $S_L$ | $S_V = \left(1 - \rho_m/\rho_p\right) \times 100$ | 7 marks |
| $\alpha_L$, $\Delta T$ | $\Delta L$ | $\Delta L = L_0 \alpha_L \Delta T$ | 3–7 marks |
| $S_{mould}$, $S_{post}$, $S_{anneal}$ | $S_{total}$ | $S_{total} = S_{mould} + S_{post} + S_{anneal}$ | 3 marks |

### 3.5 KTU-Style Worked Numerical — Full Solution

**Question:** A mould cavity is **200.5 mm** long at 25 °C. After moulding **Polypropylene** and conditioning for 24 hours, the part measures **196.9 mm** at 25 °C.
(a) Calculate the linear mould shrinkage percentage.
(b) If a design change requires the final part to be exactly **198.0 mm**, what is the new required cavity length?
(c) Comment on whether the post-mould shrinkage component is significant for PP.

**Solution:**

**(a) Linear Mould Shrinkage:**

$$S_L = \frac{200.5 - 196.9}{200.5} \times 100 = \frac{3.6}{200.5} \times 100 = 1.7955 \;\;\%$$

$$\boxed{S_L \approx 1.80 \;\;\%}$$

**(b) New cavity length for a 198.0 mm part:**

Using $L_m = L_p/(1 - S_L/100)$:
$$L_m = \frac{198.0}{1 - 0.018} = \frac{198.0}{0.982} = 201.629 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{L_m \approx 201.63 \text{ mm}}$$

**(c) Comment on post-mould shrinkage for PP:**

Polypropylene is a **semi-crystalline polymer** with typical crystallinity of 50–70 %. Secondary crystallization continues for **days to weeks** after ejection, and the post-mould shrinkage can be a significant fraction of the total (commonly **0.2–0.5 %** additional linear shrinkage). For precision PP parts, mould designers usually quote the *total* shrinkage (mould + post-mould, measured at 24 h or 48 h) and design the cavity accordingly, rather than relying on the in-mould value alone. The 1.80 % value reported above is already a *post-mould-included* value per ISO 294-4 if measured at 24 h.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Causes, Types, and Compensation of Shrinkage

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete conceptual landscape of shrinkage as a **functional architecture flow**, showing causes, manifestation, and engineering controls.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[POLYMER MELT INJECTED INTO CAVITY] --> B[STAGE 1: PACKING and COOLING]
    B --> C[Gate Freezes]
    C --> D[STAGE 2: IN-MOULD CONTRACTION]
    D --> D1[Thermal Contraction]
    D --> D2[Crystallization In-Mould]
    D --> D3[Frozen-in Orientation Stress]
    D1 --> E[MOULD SHRINKAGE S1 at Ejection]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[PART EJECTED at 60 to 90 C]
    F --> G[STAGE 3: POST-MOULD PERIOD 0 to 48 hr]
    G --> G1[Stress Relaxation]
    G --> G2[Secondary Crystallization]
    G --> G3[Moisture Loss for PA / PET]
    G1 --> H[POST-MOULD SHRINKAGE S2]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I[STAGE 4: LONG-TERM SERVICE]
    I --> I1[Annealing at Elevated T]
    I --> I2[Continued Crystallization over Weeks]
    I1 --> J[ANNEALING SHRINKAGE S3]
    I2 --> J
    J --> K[FINAL EQUILIBRIUM DIMENSION]
    E --> K
    H --> K
    K --> L[SHAPE DEVIATIONS]
    L --> L1[Warpage from Sflow not equal Stransverse]
    L --> L2[Sink Marks from Localized Thick Sections]
    L --> L3[Voids from Internal Shrinkage]
    K --> M[COMPENSATION METHODS]
    M --> M1[Add Shrinkage Allowance to Cavity]
    M --> M2[Use Filler or Fibre Reinforcement]
    M --> M3[Optimize Packing Pressure and Time]
    M --> M4[Control Mould Temperature Uniformity]
    M --> M5[Anneal Parts for Dimensional Stability]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Process Variable Influence Map

The following Mermaid block shows how the *input process variables* in injection moulding influence the *output shrinkage* — a classic KTU-style 7-mark analytical question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Mould Temperature] -->|Higher T: slower cooling, higher crystallinity| S[Mould Shrinkage SL]
    P2[Melt Temperature] -->|Higher T: more thermal contraction potential| S
    P3[Packing Pressure] -->|Higher P: more material, less shrinkage| S
    P4[Holding Time] -->|Longer t: more compensation| S
    P5[Cooling Time] -->|Affects crystallinity and frozen stress| S
    P6[Wall Thickness] -->|Thicker: more crystallinity, more shrinkage| S
    P7[Glass Fibre Content] -->|Higher GF: lower SL, more isotropic| S
    P8[Flow Direction vs. Transverse] -->|Anisotropy: causes warpage| S
    S --> Q[Part Dimensional Accuracy]
    S --> R[Warpage and Sink Marks]
    S --> T[Assembly Fit Issues]
```

### 4.3 Mould Shrinkage Values Reference Table (Textual Diagram)

The following block-diagram-style table summarizes typical linear mould shrinkage ranges for common engineering polymers, which is **high-yield exam content** for KTU students.

```
+-------------------------+----------------+----------------------+
| Polymer                 |  Shrinkage %   |  Classification      |
+-------------------------+----------------+----------------------+
| Polypropylene (PP)      |  1.0 to 2.5    |  Semi-crystalline    |
| Polyethylene (PE)       |  1.5 to 3.0    |  Semi-crystalline    |
| Nylon 6,6 (PA 66)       |  1.0 to 2.0    |  Semi-crystalline    |
| Polyoxymethylene (POM)  |  1.8 to 2.5    |  Semi-crystalline    |
| Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET) | 0.2 to 0.5 | Semi-crystalline |
| Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS) | 0.4 to 0.7 | Amorphous |
| Polystyrene (PS)        |  0.2 to 0.6    |  Amorphous           |
| Polycarbonate (PC)      |  0.5 to 0.7    |  Amorphous           |
| PMMA                   |  0.2 to 0.8    |  Amorphous           |
| PP + 30 percent GF      |  0.3 to 0.7    |  Fibre-reinforced    |
+-------------------------+----------------+----------------------+
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Amorphous polymers generally have shrinkage < 1 % because they lack the abrupt density change at crystallization. Semi-crystalline polymers have shrinkage in the 1–3 % range. Fibre reinforcement (e.g., glass-filled PP) **drastically reduces** shrinkage and makes it more isotropic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define linear mould shrinkage. State the ISO standard used for its measurement.**

**Model Answer:**
Linear mould shrinkage is defined as the percentage reduction in a linear dimension of a moulded part compared to the corresponding linear dimension of the mould cavity, both measured at room temperature (25 °C) after a standardized conditioning period.

$$S_L = \frac{L_m - L_p}{L_m} \times 100 \;\; (\%)$$

The ISO standard used for the determination of mould shrinkage is **ISO 294-4**, which specifies conditioning of test specimens for **24 hours** at **23 °C / 50 % RH** before measurement. **Self-Adhesive Tag:** [3 marks — Definition 1.5 + Formula 1.0 + Standard 0.5]

#### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**List any four factors affecting mould shrinkage in injection moulded thermoplastic parts.**

**Model Answer:**
The four factors are:
1. **Mould temperature** — Higher mould temperature leads to higher crystallinity (in semi-crystalline polymers) and thus higher shrinkage.
2. **Packing pressure and holding time** — Insufficient packing produces higher shrinkage and sink marks.
3. **Polymer type and crystallinity** — Semi-crystalline polymers shrink more than amorphous ones.
4. **Filler and fibre content** — Glass-fibre-reinforced grades exhibit much lower and more isotropic shrinkage.

(Self-Adhesive Tag: 4 × 0.75 = 3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1)

#### [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]
**Question A:**
(a) **Explain the three stages of shrinkage** observed in a moulded thermoplastic part, with reference to their underlying molecular mechanisms. **[7 marks]**
(b) **A mould cavity of length 250 mm is used to mould an ABS part. If the linear mould shrinkage of ABS is 0.6 %, calculate:**
   (i) The length of the moulded part. **[3 marks]**
   (ii) The new cavity length required to obtain a part of length 200 mm using the same ABS grade. **[4 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Three Stages of Shrinkage [7 marks]

**Stage 1 — Mould Shrinkage (In-Mould Thermal Contraction):**
This occurs as the polymer solidifies and cools inside the mould cavity from the solidification temperature down to the ejection temperature. It is governed primarily by:
- **Thermal contraction** of the solidified polymer (governed by α_solid and ΔT).
- **In-mould crystallization** for semi-crystalline polymers (volume drop at T_c).
- **Packing-pressure-induced density** — higher pressure packs more polymer into the cavity, reducing this stage.

**Stage 2 — Post-Mould Shrinkage (0–24 hours after ejection):**
After ejection, the part is no longer constrained. The mechanisms are:
- **Viscoelastic relaxation** of frozen-in molecular orientation and internal stresses. The chains re-coil to a more entropically favourable state, causing contraction in the flow direction and expansion in the cross direction.
- **Secondary crystallization** in semi-crystalline polymers (PP, PA, POM) where smaller crystallites form in the amorphous regions over hours.
- **Moisture loss** in hygroscopic polymers like PA 6,6 and PET (these absorb water during processing which later evaporates).

**Stage 3 — Annealing / Service Shrinkage (Days to Months):**
At ambient or slightly elevated service temperatures:
- **Tertiary crystallization** continues very slowly over weeks.
- The part may shrink further if exposed to temperatures above its previous service history (e.g., a PP part in a hot car interior may shrink more).
- This is the most stable end-state, and design for dimensional stability targets this equilibrium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Three Stages Matter in Mould Design:** A mould designed only for Stage-1 shrinkage will produce parts that subsequently *shrink further* after ejection, falling out of tolerance. The 24-hour ISO 294-4 measurement is a deliberate compromise to capture Stage 1 + most of Stage 2.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]

**Part (b)(i): Length of the moulded part from a 250 mm cavity, $S_L = 0.6$ %**

From the definition:
$$L_p = L_m (1 - S_L/100) = 250 (1 - 0.006) = 250 \times 0.994$$

$$\boxed{L_p = 248.5 \text{ mm}}$$

(3 marks — Formula 1, Substitution 1, Answer 1)

**Part (b)(ii): New cavity length for $L_p = 200$ mm**

Rearranging:
$$L_m = \frac{L_p}{1 - S_L/100} = \frac{200}{1 - 0.006} = \frac{200}{0.994}$$

$$\boxed{L_m = 201.207 \text{ mm} \approx 201.21 \text{ mm}}$$

(4 marks — Formula 1, Substitution 1, Calculation 1, Units 1)

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2)

#### [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]
**Question B:**
(a) **Discuss the effect of the following process parameters on mould shrinkage of an injection-moulded polypropylene part:**
   (i) Mould temperature
   (ii) Packing pressure
   (iii) Fibre reinforcement **[7 marks]**
(b) **The volumetric shrinkage of a polymer during moulding is 6.0 %. Estimate its linear shrinkage, assuming isotropic behaviour. If the part is required to be 120 mm long, determine the cavity dimension.** **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Effect of Process Parameters on Shrinkage [7 marks]

**(i) Mould Temperature (2.5 marks):**
Higher mould temperature slows the cooling rate of the polymer inside the cavity. For **semi-crystalline PP**, a slower cooling rate allows more time for polymer chains to organize into crystalline lamellae, **increasing the overall crystallinity** of the part. Since crystallization is a major contributor to shrinkage, higher mould temperature leads to **higher mould shrinkage**. Conversely, a cold mould produces an amorphous-rich skin and a less crystalline (but more isotropic) core.

**(ii) Packing Pressure (2.5 marks):**
Packing pressure is applied *after* the cavity is filled to compensate for shrinkage as the skin solidifies. **Higher packing pressure** forces additional polymer melt into the cavity before the gate freezes, increasing the **bulk density** of the solidified part. This directly **reduces mould shrinkage** and eliminates sink marks in thick sections. Insufficient packing pressure is the most common cause of high shrinkage and sink marks in production.

**(iii) Fibre Reinforcement (2 marks):**
Adding glass or carbon fibres to PP drastically **reduces shrinkage** (e.g., from ~1.8 % to ~0.3–0.7 %) because the rigid fibres physically restrain the polymer matrix from contracting. Furthermore, fibres align in the flow direction, making shrinkage **anisotropic** (lower in flow, higher transverse) — this can be a design benefit (predictable) or a drawback (warpage if flow geometry is unbalanced).

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Convert volumetric to linear shrinkage (isotropic assumption).**
$$S_L = \frac{S_V}{3} = \frac{6.0}{3} = 2.0 \;\;\%$$

**Step 2 — Calculate cavity dimension for $L_p = 120$ mm.**

$$L_m = \frac{L_p}{1 - S_L/100} = \frac{120}{1 - 0.02} = \frac{120}{0.98}$$

**Step 3 — Final value.**
$$\boxed{L_m = 122.449 \text{ mm} \approx 122.45 \text{ mm}}$$

(7 marks — Step 1: 3 marks, Step 2 formula: 1 mark, Step 2 substitution: 1 mark, Step 2 answer: 1 mark, Units: 1 mark)

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Shrinkage Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Always express $S_L$ as a *percentage* in the formula, OR convert to a *decimal* before plugging in. Mixing the two is the #1 error.
>    - **Correct:** $S_L = 1.8$, then $1 - 0.018$, OR $S_L = 1.8\%$, then $1 - 0.018$.
>    - **Wrong:** $1 - 1.8 = -0.8$ (treats % as raw number).
>
> 2. **Inverting the shrinkage formula (–2 marks):** The shrinkage formula subtracts part from mould ($L_m - L_p$), not the other way around. If a part measures *larger* than the cavity, the value is *negative* (overpack/expansion) — note this and state it.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to sum shrinkage components (–1 to –2 marks):** When asked for *total* dimensional change, use $S_{total} = S_{mould} + S_{post} + S_{anneal}$. Do not average them or pick only one.
>
> 4. **Confusing volumetric and linear shrinkage (–2 marks):** $S_V \approx 3 S_L$ is valid *only* for isotropic shrinkage and small strains. For highly crystalline or fibre-filled polymers, state the assumption explicitly.
>
> 5. **Ignoring mould vs. part temperature mismatch (–1 mark):** The shrinkage formula assumes both are at the *same* reference temperature (25 °C). Always state this when writing the formula.
>
> 6. **Numerical precision:** KTU allows ±0.01 mm / ±0.01 % tolerance in final answers. Round to 2–3 significant figures.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Shrinkage (Module 3, PCPOT503):**
>
> - **Definition:** Shrinkage = % reduction in part dimension relative to mould cavity, measured at 25 °C after 24 h conditioning (ISO 294-4).
> - **Master Formula:** $S_L = \dfrac{L_m - L_p}{L_m} \times 100$ [%].
> - **Inverse Formula (cavity from part):** $L_m = \dfrac{L_p}{1 - S_L/100}$.
> - **Volumetric ↔ Linear:** $S_V \approx 3 S_L$ (isotropic).
> - **Three Stages:**
>   1. **Mould shrinkage** (in-mould, thermal + crystallization) — largest, ~70 % of total.
>   2. **Post-mould shrinkage** (0–24 h, stress relaxation + secondary crystallization) — 20–25 % of total.
>   3. **Annealing shrinkage** (days–weeks, tertiary crystallization) — 5–10 % of total.
> - **Three Root Causes:** Thermal contraction (PVT), crystallization (semi-crystalline polymers), molecular orientation & frozen-in stress.
> - **High-shrinkage polymers:** PP, PE, POM, PA, PET (all semi-crystalline) — typically 1–3 %.
> - **Low-shrinkage polymers:** PS, ABS, PC, PMMA (amorphous) — typically 0.2–0.8 %.
> - **Fibre reinforcement drastically reduces shrinkage** and changes its anisotropy.
> - **Process levers to reduce shrinkage:** ↑ packing pressure, ↑ holding time, ↑ mould temperature (for amorphous), ↓ mould temperature (for crystalline, to limit crystallinity), use fillers/fibres.
> - **Anisotropy:** $S_{flow} \neq S_{transverse}$ is the *root cause of warpage*.
> - **Practical rule for KTU 14-mark problems:** Always state the assumption of *isotropic* shrinkage when using $S_V \approx 3 S_L$; always state the reference temperature (25 °C) when defining $L_m$ and $L_p$.
> - **Memorize shrinkage ranges** for at least: PP (1.0–2.5 %), ABS (0.4–0.7 %), PC (0.5–0.7 %), POM (1.8–2.5 %).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trimming Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERINE & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Trimming Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trimming Operation:** Trimming is a fundamental **secondary (post-molding/finishing) manufacturing process** in polymer product manufacturing wherein excess polymeric material—typically in the form of **flash, sprues, runners, gates, parting line projections, or edge burrs**—is systematically removed from a molded, extruded, or cast plastic component to achieve the **desired final geometry, dimensional tolerances, surface finish, and functional aesthetics**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, trimming is classified under **finishing and deflashing operations**, forming an indispensable link between primary shaping processes (injection molding, compression molding, blow molding, thermoforming, rotational molding, etc.) and downstream operations like assembly, decoration, or packaging.

The **primary sources of excess material** that necessitate trimming include:

- **Flash** – Thin sheets of polymer that escape from the parting line of a mold.
- **Sprue** – The channel through which molten polymer enters the mold cavity.
- **Runner** – Distribution channels carrying polymer to multiple cavities.
- **Gate** – The narrow restriction at the cavity entrance.
- **Burrs and parting-line projections** – Resulting from mold wear or misalignment.
- **Excess edge material** – From thermoforming, blow molding, or rotational molding parisons.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation (Real-World Analogy):**
> Imagine you have just baked a cake in a fancy silicone mold. When you remove the cake, there are rough, uneven edges hanging off the sides—the cake's "flash." Before you can frost and serve it, you carefully run a knife around the edges to clean it up, then use a fork to gently scrape off any remaining imperfections.
>
> **Polymer trimming is EXACTLY this process—just much more precise, often automated, and sometimes done at temperatures colder than Antarctica or hotter than your oven!**
>
> - The **cake** = Your molded plastic part.
> - The **rough edges** = Flash, sprues, and runners.
> - The **knife/fork** = Trimming tools (mechanical, thermal, abrasive, or cryogenic).
> - The **clean cake** = A finished, dimensionally accurate, market-ready product.

## 1.3 Classification Metrics & Standard Parameters

The following **engineering metrics** govern the selection and effectiveness of a trimming operation:

- **Tensile Strength of Material ($\sigma_{TS}$):** Typically **30 – 100 MPa** for commodity polymers (PP, PE, PS) and up to **200+ MPa** for engineering polymers (PA, POM, PC).
- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$):** Critical threshold for thermal/cryogenic trimming.
- **Deflection Temperature Under Load (HDT):** Indicates material softening behavior.
- **Impact Strength (Izod/Charpy):** Determines brittleness during deflashing.
- **Flash Thickness Tolerance:** Usually maintained at **0.10 mm – 0.30 mm** in well-maintained molds.
- **Production Rate:** Trimming throughput in **parts/hour** or **parts/minute**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Trimming operations are evaluated as a critical finishing step, with emphasis on **method selection, mechanism of material removal, machine specifications, and process parameters** for different polymer families.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Trimming Operations

Trimming operations are systematically classified based on the **mechanism of material removal** and the **energy domain** employed:

### 2.1.1 Manual Trimming (Hand Operations)
- **Hand Knives / Scissors / Shears:** Used for soft polymers and large parts.
- **Hand Files and Rasps:** For flash on rigid thermoset parts.
- **Hand-Held Rotary Tools:** Die grinders with cutting wheels.

### 2.1.2 Mechanical Trimming
- **Deflashing Machines (Tumbling / Barrel Finishing):** Rotating barrels with abrasive media.
- **Deflashing by Hand-Press with Dies:** Abrasive belt or roll contact.
- **Sanding and Grinding Wheels:** Off-hand grinding for large flash.
- **Power Brushes / Buffing Wheels:** For surface defects.

### 2.1.3 Thermal Trimming
- **Hot-Air/Flame Trimming:** Direct heat application softens flash.
- **Hot-Roll Trimming:** Roller contact heats and sears flash.
- **Resistance Wire Trimming:** Heated wire cuts and seals simultaneously.

### 2.1.4 Cryogenic Trimming
- **Cryogenic Tumbling/Deflashing:** Immersion in **Liquid Nitrogen (LN₂)** at **−196 °C (77 K)** embrittles flash, which is then removed by tumbling.
- **Cryogenic Belt Deflashing:** Parts carried on a belt through an LN₂ chamber.

### 2.1.5 Advanced/CNC-Based Trimming
- **CNC Routing:** For thermoformed and rotationally molded parts.
- **Robotic Trimming (6-axis):** Automated milling/cutting.
- **Laser Trimming:** CO₂ or Nd:YAG laser cuts via localized vaporization.
- **Water-Jet Trimming:** High-pressure water (up to **400 MPa**) cuts soft polymers.
- **Abrasive Water-Jet (AWJ):** Water + garnet abrasive for harder polymers.

### 2.1.6 Die Cutting & Stamping
- **Steel-Rule Dies (Clicker Dies):** For flat sheets (gaskets, seals, thin films).
- **Matched Metal Dies:** For high-volume precision cutting.

## 2.2 Operating Principles — The "Why & How"

### Step 1: Identification of Excess Material
- Visual inspection and dimensional gauging identify flash locations.
- **Why?** Different molding processes generate flash at predictable locations (parting lines for compression/injection; pinch-offs for blow molding; mold seams for rotational molding).

### Step 2: Selection of Trimming Method
- Depends on **polymer type** (thermoplastic vs. thermoset), **flash thickness**, **part geometry**, **production volume**, and **tolerance requirements**.

### Step 3: Application of Removal Energy
- Mechanical (kinetic + friction), Thermal (heat conduction), Cryogenic (embrittlement), or Radiative (laser).

### Step 4: Verification of Final Geometry
- CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine) or optical comparator for critical dimensions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Parameter** | **Expression** | **Application / Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| Flash Thickness Estimation | $t_f = \dfrac{P \cdot w^3}{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L}$ | $P$ = molding pressure (Pa), $w$ = gap width, $\eta$ = polymer viscosity, $L$ = flow length |
| Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $MRR = v_f \cdot a_p \cdot d$ | $v_f$ = feed rate, $a_p$ = depth of cut, $d$ = width of cut (mm³/min) |
| Cutting Force (Milling) | $F_c = K_s \cdot a_p \cdot f_z \cdot z$ | $K_s$ = specific cutting energy (N/mm²), $z$ = number of teeth |
| Brittleness Index | $B_i = \dfrac{\sigma_{TS}}{\epsilon_f}$ | Ratio of tensile strength to elongation at fracture |
| Cryogenic Embrittlement Temp. | $T_{br} \approx 0.6 \cdot T_g$ (K) | Approximate brittle-ductile transition (Kelvin) |
| Laser Power Requirement | $P_L = \dfrac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + L_v \cdot m}{t \cdot \eta_{abs}}$ | Mass, specific heat, latent heat of vaporization |
| Tumbling Cycle Time | $t_{cyc} = f(\text{media size}, \text{RPM}, \text{load factor})$ | Empirical; typically 5–30 min |
| Abrasive Wear Rate | $W_r = K \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot v_s}{H_v}$ | $P$ = load, $v_s$ = sliding speed, $H_v$ = hardness |
| Trim Tolerance | $\Delta t = \pm 0.1 \text{ to } \pm 0.5 \text{ mm}$ | Standard industry tolerance range |
| Shear Stress in Cutting | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A_c}$ | $F$ = cutting force, $A_c$ = cross-sectional area |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note for Tables:** Vertical bars in formulas use the word "absolute" or are written as $\vert x \vert$ outside table syntax to avoid markdown parsing errors. In the table above, no absolute value operators were needed, ensuring clean rendering.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Trimming Systems

Trimming operations are indispensable in:

- **Automotive Industry:** Trimmed bumper fascias, dashboards, and door panels.
- **Medical Devices:** Flash-free syringes, IV components, surgical instruments.
- **Packaging:** Containers, caps, closures, and thin-film products.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Phone cases, laptop housings, cable insulation.
- **Aerospace:** Composite trim of CFRP/thermoset panels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Flash Thickness in a Compression Mold

The flash thickness is critical because it determines the trimming effort required. Consider a compression mold where a polymer charge is placed in a heated cavity. The excess material escapes through the parting line gap.

### Assumptions:
- Steady-state viscous flow (Hagen-Poiseuille type).
- Constant viscosity ($\eta$) and temperature.
- The flash gap is rectangular with width $w$ and length $L$ (the perimeter).

### Step 1: Volumetric Flow Rate Through Gap
The volumetric flow rate $Q$ of a viscous polymer through a rectangular slit of gap thickness $t_f$ is given by:

$$
Q = \dfrac{w \cdot t_f^3 \cdot \Delta P}{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L}
$$

**Logic:** This is the classical **Hagen-Poiseuille equation for rectangular cross-sections**, derived from the Navier-Stokes equations under the assumptions of:
- Laminar flow.
- No-slip boundary conditions at mold walls.
- Newtonian behavior (modified by viscosity models for non-Newtonian polymers).

### Step 2: Relate Flow Rate to Platen Velocity
The platen closing velocity $v_p$ pushes material out, so:

$$
Q = v_p \cdot A_c = v_p \cdot w \cdot L
$$

**Logic:** The product of platen velocity and the projected cavity area equals the volumetric displacement that must escape as flash.

### Step 3: Equate the Two Expressions
By continuity, the material leaving the cavity must equal the material escaping as flash:

$$
v_p \cdot w \cdot L = \dfrac{w \cdot t_f^3 \cdot \Delta P}{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L}
$$

**Logic:** Mass conservation demands that the rate at which material is displaced by the advancing platen equals the rate of flash extrusion.

### Step 4: Solve for Flash Thickness $t_f$

$$
t_f^3 = \dfrac{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L^2 \cdot v_p}{\Delta P}
$$

$$
t_f = \left( \dfrac{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L^2 \cdot v_p}{\Delta P} \right)^{1/3}
$$

**Logic:** Cube-rooting both sides gives the final flash thickness. This shows that flash thickness is **most sensitive to viscosity** (cube-root dependence) and **inversely proportional to molding pressure**.

### Step 5: Final Working Equation

$$
\boxed{t_f = \left( \dfrac{12 \cdot \eta \cdot v_p \cdot L^2}{\Delta P} \right)^{1/3}}
$$

**Logic:** This is the key engineering equation used in KTU problems. For a given polymer, higher closing speeds and longer flow paths increase flash thickness, while higher pressures suppress it.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A phenolic resin is being compression molded. The parting line perimeter is $L = 0.40$ m, the platen closing velocity is $v_p = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s, the melt viscosity is $\eta = 10^4$ Pa·s, and the cavity pressure is $\Delta P = 15$ MPa. Estimate the flash thickness.

### Step 1: Identify Given Values
- $L = 0.40$ m
- $v_p = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s
- $\eta = 10^4$ Pa·s
- $\Delta P = 15 \times 10^6$ Pa

### Step 2: Substitute into the Flash Equation

$$
t_f = \left( \dfrac{12 \cdot (10^4) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.40)^2}{15 \times 10^6} \right)^{1/3}
$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Numerator

$$
\text{Numerator} = 12 \times 10^4 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.16
$$

$$
= 12 \times 10^4 \times 0.0008
$$

$$
= 96
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Fraction

$$
\dfrac{96}{15 \times 10^6} = 6.4 \times 10^{-6}
$$

### Step 5: Take the Cube Root

$$
t_f = (6.4 \times 10^{-6})^{1/3}
$$

$$
t_f \approx 1.86 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 0.186 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 6: Interpret the Result

> **Result:** The flash thickness is approximately **0.19 mm**. This is within the **typical industry range of 0.1 – 0.3 mm** and would be classified as **moderate flash**, requiring either mechanical deflashing or cryogenic trimming depending on the part's tolerance requirements.

## 3.3 Derivation: Material Removal Rate (MRR) in CNC Trimming

For a trimming operation using a milling cutter, the MRR determines the cycle time.

### Step 1: Define the Engagement Geometry
- $a_p$ = axial depth of cut (mm)
- $a_e$ = radial depth of cut (mm)
- $v_f$ = feed rate (mm/min)
- $z$ = number of teeth
- $f_z$ = feed per tooth (mm/tooth)

### Step 2: Volumetric Material Removed Per Revolution

$$
V_{rev} = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot f_z \cdot z
$$

### Step 3: Spindle Speed

$$
N = \dfrac{v_c}{\pi \cdot D}
$$

where $v_c$ = cutting speed and $D$ = tool diameter.

### Step 4: MRR Formula

$$
\boxed{MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot v_f}
$$

**Logic:** This is the universal MRR formula. Substituting $v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$:

$$
MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot f_z \cdot z \cdot N
$$

### Step 5: Cycle Time

$$
t_{cycle} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{MRR}
$$

where $V_{part}$ is the total volume of material to be removed.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Trimming Methods

| **Method** | **Material Suitability** | **Flash Thickness Range** | **Production Volume** | **Capital Cost** | **Surface Finish** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Manual (Knife/File) | Thermosets, soft rubbers | > 0.5 mm | Low | Very Low | Variable |
| Tumbling (Room Temp) | Rigid plastics, die-cast | 0.1 – 0.5 mm | Medium-High | Low | Matte |
| Cryogenic Tumbling | Elastomers, rubber, PP | 0.05 – 0.3 mm | High | Medium | Smooth |
| Abrasive Belt | Thermosets, rigid plastics | 0.2 – 1.0 mm | Medium | Medium | Matte |
| Hot Roll / Hot Wire | Thermoplastics, films | 0.1 – 0.5 mm | High | Low-Medium | Sealed Edge |
| CNC Routing | Thermoformed, rotational | 0.5 – 5.0 mm | Low-Medium | High | Excellent |
| Laser Trimming | All polymers | 0.05 – 0.5 mm | Medium | Very High | Sealed Edge |
| Water-Jet | Soft-medium polymers | 0.5 – 3.0 mm | Medium | High | Clean Cut |
| Die Cutting (Clicker) | Sheets, films, gaskets | Full sheet cut | Very High | Medium | Sharp Edge |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define "flash" in polymer molding. List four sources of excess material that require trimming.**

**Model Answer:**
Flash is a thin, unwanted layer of polymer that escapes from the mold cavity through the parting line, ejector pin clearances, or other gaps during the molding cycle. It is essentially excess material that has flowed outside the intended part geometry.

**Four sources of excess material requiring trimming:**
1. **Parting-line flash** – escapes at the mold split line.
2. **Sprue and runner system** – the channels that feed polymer into cavities.
3. **Gate vestiges** – small projections at the gate location.
4. **Ejector-pin marks and burrs** – from the ejection mechanism.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: What is cryogenic deflashing? State the temperature range at which it operates.**

**Model Answer:**
Cryogenic deflashing is a trimming process in which molded rubber or thermoplastic parts are cooled to extremely low temperatures using **Liquid Nitrogen (LN₂)**, causing the thin flash to become brittle. The brittle flash is then removed by tumbling, brushing, or shot-blasting.

**Operating temperature:** Approximately **−196 °C (77 K)**, the boiling point of liquid nitrogen at atmospheric pressure. The parts are typically pre-cooled to around **−80 °C** before LN₂ immersion to avoid thermal shock.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Jul 2024]

**Q: (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **cryogenic deflashing machine**. Discuss its process parameters, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **polypropylene part** is produced by injection molding with a flash thickness of 0.25 mm along a 350 mm parting line perimeter. The polymer viscosity at flash temperature is $8 \times 10^3$ Pa·s, the cavity pressure is 18 MPa, and the platen velocity is $4 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s. **Estimate and verify whether the flash thickness falls within the recommended industry range for mechanical deflashing (0.1 – 0.5 mm).** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**Construction of a Cryogenic Deflashing Machine:**

A cryogenic deflashing machine consists of the following subsystems:

1. **Insulated Tumbling Chamber:** A double-walled, vacuum-insulated rotating barrel where parts and abrasive media are loaded.
2. **LN₂ Supply System:** Includes a storage tank, insulated piping, and a precision flow control valve.
3. **Pre-cooling Zone:** A secondary chamber where parts are gradually cooled before LN₂ immersion to prevent thermal shock cracking.
4. **Drive System:** Variable-speed motor (typically **10 – 60 RPM**) to rotate the chamber.
5. **Media Separator:** Vibratory or magnetic separator to separate parts from the abrasive media.
6. **Exhaust System:** Vents cold nitrogen gas safely to the atmosphere.
7. **Control Panel:** PLC-based controller for temperature, RPM, and cycle time.

**Working Principle:**

- Molded parts are loaded into the tumbling chamber along with **abrasive media** (e.g., polycarbonate pellets, aluminum oxide chips, or walnut shells).
- The chamber is rotated while LN₂ is injected, cooling the parts to approximately **−196 °C**.
- At this temperature, the thin flash becomes **brittle** (because it is below the polymer's glass transition or secondary transition temperature), while the bulk part retains sufficient toughness.
- The rotating media mechanically **impacts and breaks off** the embrittled flash.
- After the cycle (typically **5 – 20 minutes**), the parts are separated from the media, allowed to warm to room temperature, and inspected.

**Process Parameters:**

- **LN₂ temperature:** −196 °C
- **Chamber rotation speed:** 20 – 40 RPM
- **Cycle time:** 5 – 20 min
- **Media-to-part ratio:** 2:1 to 4:1
- **Media type and size:** Polycarbonate pellets (2 – 4 mm), or aluminum oxide (1 – 3 mm)

**Advantages:**

- Excellent for elastomers and tough thermoplastics.
- No thermal damage to the bulk part.
- High production rate; suitable for **10,000+ parts/day**.
- Consistent and repeatable flash removal.

**Limitations:**

- High operational cost (LN₂ consumption).
- Not suitable for parts with **very thin walls** that could become brittle.
- Requires strict safety protocols for cryogenic handling.
- Capital cost is moderate to high.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Given Data:**
- Flash thickness (given/observed): $t_f^{obs} = 0.25$ mm $= 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m
- Parting line perimeter: $L = 0.350$ m
- Viscosity: $\eta = 8 \times 10^3$ Pa·s
- Cavity pressure: $\Delta P = 18 \times 10^6$ Pa
- Platen velocity: $v_p = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s

**Formula (derived in Section 3.1):**

$$
t_f = \left( \dfrac{12 \cdot \eta \cdot v_p \cdot L^2}{\Delta P} \right)^{1/3}
$$

**Step 1: Compute the Numerator**

$$
12 \cdot \eta \cdot v_p \cdot L^2 = 12 \cdot (8 \times 10^3) \cdot (4 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.350)^2
$$

$$
= 12 \times 8000 \times 0.004 \times 0.1225
$$

$$
= 12 \times 8000 \times 0.00049
$$

$$
= 47.04
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Ratio**

$$
\dfrac{47.04}{18 \times 10^6} = 2.613 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 3: Take the Cube Root**

$$
t_f = (2.613 \times 10^{-6})^{1/3}
$$

$$
t_f \approx 1.379 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 0.138 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Compare with Industry Range**

$$
t_f^{predicted} = 0.138 \text{ mm} \quad \text{vs.} \quad t_f^{observed} = 0.25 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Verification Against Mechanical Deflashing Range**

The recommended industry range for mechanical deflashing is **0.1 mm – 0.5 mm**.

$$
0.138 \text{ mm} \in [0.1, 0.5] \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The calculated flash thickness of **0.138 mm** falls **well within the recommended range (0.1 – 0.5 mm)** for mechanical deflashing operations. The part is therefore suitable for **room-temperature tumbling, abrasive belt, or cryogenic deflashing** without any special handling. The slight deviation from the observed 0.25 mm can be attributed to non-Newtonian flow behavior and mold-wear effects, which are not captured by the simplified Newtonian model.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Jul 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**Q: (a)** Classify the various **trimming methods** used in polymer product manufacturing. Compare **mechanical, thermal, and cryogenic trimming** with respect to working principle, applicable polymers, surface finish, and production volume. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **thermoformed ABS tray** has a peripheral flash of 8 mm width. The cutting tool is a 6 mm diameter end mill with 4 teeth, running at 12,000 RPM. The feed per tooth is 0.05 mm/tooth, and the depth of cut is 4 mm. The total cut length is 1.2 m. **Calculate the Material Removal Rate (MRR) and the cycle time for trimming.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

**Classification of Trimming Methods (as per Section 2.1):**

1. Manual Trimming
2. Mechanical Trimming (tumbling, belt, grinding)
3. Thermal Trimming (hot wire, flame, hot roll)
4. Cryogenic Trimming (LN₂ tumbling, cryogenic belt)
5. Advanced Trimming (CNC, robotic, laser, water jet)
6. Die Cutting / Stamping

**Comparison Table:**

| **Criterion** | **Mechanical** | **Thermal** | **Cryogenic** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Abrasion, impact, cutting at room temperature | Localized heating softens/melts flash | LN₂ embrittles flash; mechanical impact removes it |
| **Applicable Polymers** | Rigid thermosets, hard thermoplastics | Thermoplastics with sharp melting points | Elastomers, rubber, tough thermoplastics |
| **Surface Finish** | Matte to semi-gloss | Sealed, slightly discolored edge | Smooth, clean edge |
| **Production Volume** | Medium to High | High (continuous operation) | High (batch process) |
| **Capital Cost** | Low to Medium | Low to Medium | Medium to High |
| **Energy Source** | Electrical (motor) | Electrical (resistive heating) / Gas | LN₂ + Electrical |
| **Limitations** | Dust, media wear, dimensional variation | Heat-affected zone (HAZ), discoloration | LN₂ cost, not for thin parts |

**Discussion:**

- **Mechanical trimming** is the most versatile and economical for a wide range of polymers, but it may leave tool marks or dust.
- **Thermal trimming** is excellent for **thermoplastic films and sheets** where a sealed edge is desired (e.g., PVC banners, PE packaging).
- **Cryogenic trimming** is the **gold standard for elastomeric and rubber parts**, offering superior finish with no thermal degradation of the bulk part.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Given Data:**
- Cutter diameter: $D = 6$ mm
- Number of teeth: $z = 4$
- Spindle speed: $N = 12{,}000$ RPM
- Feed per tooth: $f_z = 0.05$ mm/tooth
- Depth of cut: $a_p = 4$ mm
- Total cut length: $L_c = 1.2$ m $= 1200$ mm

**Step 1: Calculate the Feed Rate $v_f$**

$$
v_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N
$$

$$
v_f = 0.05 \times 4 \times 12{,}000
$$

$$
v_f = 2400 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Radial Width of Cut $a_e$**

The peripheral flash is 8 mm wide, and the cutter diameter is 6 mm. Assuming the cutter engages the full flash width in one pass (or is stepped across), take the effective width of cut as $a_e = 6$ mm (single pass) or the full 8 mm. We use $a_e = 6$ mm for one full-diameter pass.

**Step 3: Calculate the Material Removal Rate (MRR)**

$$
MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot v_f
$$

$$
MRR = 4 \times 6 \times 2400
$$

$$
MRR = 57{,}600 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Volume to be Removed**

Assuming the flash cross-section is approximated as a rectangle of 8 mm width × 4 mm depth:

$$
V_{flash} = L_c \cdot w \cdot d
$$

$$
V_{flash} = 1200 \times 8 \times 4 = 38{,}400 \text{ mm}^3
$$

(Note: Since the cutter diameter is 6 mm and flash width is 8 mm, the cut is performed in two passes of 6 mm each, or one pass at 8 mm engagement. For this calculation, we use 8 mm as the width to reflect total material removed.)

**Step 5: Calculate the Cycle Time**

$$
t_{cycle} = \dfrac{V_{flash}}{MRR}
$$

$$
t_{cycle} = \dfrac{38{,}400}{57{,}600} = 0.667 \text{ min} = 40 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 6: Interpret the Result**

> **Result:** The Material Removal Rate is **57,600 mm³/min**, and the cycle time for trimming the peripheral flash is approximately **40 seconds**. This is highly suitable for **medium-volume production (200 – 500 parts/hour)** with excellent dimensional control and surface finish.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Always convert all values to SI units (Pa, m, s) before substituting into the flash thickness equation. A common error is mixing mm and m.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Cube Root:** The flash equation yields $t_f^3$, not $t_f$. Students often forget to take the cube root and lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing MRR Formulas:** The MRR formula $MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot v_f$ is for milling. Do not confuse it with the turning formula $MRR = v_f \cdot a_p \cdot \pi D \cdot N$ (different geometry).
>
> 4. **Newtonian Assumption:** The flash derivation assumes Newtonian flow. In reality, polymers are non-Newtonian (shear-thinning). Mention this as a limitation for full marks.
>
> 5. **Skipping Diagrams:** In 7-mark questions, a neat labeled diagram is **mandatory**. Examiners often allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the sketch.
>
> 6. **Missing Process Parameters:** In descriptive questions, always list **temperature, RPM, cycle time, and media type** for deflashing questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Trimming Operations:**

- **Definition:** Trimming is the removal of excess polymer material (flash, sprue, runner, gate) from molded parts to achieve final geometry and tolerance.

- **Sources of Excess Material:** Parting-line flash, sprue, runner, gate, ejector-pin burrs, and pinch-off remnants.

- **Flash Thickness Equation (Critical Formula):**

$$
t_f = \left( \dfrac{12 \cdot \eta \cdot v_p \cdot L^2}{\Delta P} \right)^{1/3}
$$

  - $t_f$ increases with $\eta$, $v_p$, and $L$.
  - $t_f$ decreases with increasing $\Delta P$.

- **Six Major Trimming Categories:**
  1. Manual
  2. Mechanical (tumbling, belt, grinding)
  3. Thermal (hot wire, flame)
  4. Cryogenic (LN₂ at −196 °C)
  5. Advanced (CNC, laser, water jet)
  6. Die Cutting (clicker, matched dies)

- **Cryogenic Trimming:**
  - Uses **Liquid Nitrogen (LN₂) at −196 °C (77 K)**.
  - Best for **elastomers, rubber, and tough thermoplastics** (PP, PE, NBR, EPDM).
  - Embrittles only the thin flash; bulk part remains tough.

- **MRR Formula for CNC Trimming:**

$$
MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot v_f = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot f_z \cdot z \cdot N
$$

- **Cycle Time:**

$$
t_{cycle} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{MRR}
$$

- **Industry Flash Range:** **0.1 – 0.3 mm** is typical for well-maintained molds.

- **Method Selection Factors:** Polymer type, flash thickness, part geometry, tolerance, production volume, surface-finish requirement, and cost.

- **KTU Hot Tip:** For **14-mark questions**, always pair a **diagram + theory + numerical example**. This is the KTU 2024 valuation key pattern, and students who follow it consistently score **12+ marks** on descriptive questions.

- **Safety Note:** Cryogenic trimming requires **PPE (cryogenic gloves, face shield)** and well-ventilated areas for LN₂ gas venting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rotational Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Rotational Moulding # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Rotational Moulding (Rotomoulding / Rotational Casting)** is a *gravity-assisted, low-pressure thermoplastic processing technique* in which a measured charge of **liquid, powder, or plastisol** polymer is loaded into a **hollow, biaxially-rotating mould**, heated externally in an oven until the polymer fuses onto the inner mould surface, and subsequently cooled before demoulding to yield a **seamless, hollow, stress-free finished product** with uniform wall thickness. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition Box** > Rotational moulding is classified under the broader family of *secondary shaping processes* (post-extrusion/post-moulding) and is uniquely characterized by **zero internal mould pressure** (≈ 0 to 1 bar gauge) compared to injection moulding (700–1400 bar) and blow moulding (2–10 bar). > [!NOTE] > **Synonyms accepted in KTU exam answers:** *Rotomoulding*, *Roto Casting*, *Rotational Casting*, *Centrifugal Casting* (historically, though strictly centrifugal casting is for metals/liners). Use "Rotational Moulding" for full marks. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pulao Pot on a Tawa" Imagine you have a sealed, hollow **aluminium handi (cooking pot)** containing uncooked rice grains inside its walls. You place this handi on a **rotating spit** that slowly turns it on two axes simultaneously (like a chicken tandoor turning on the rotisserie). You bring a low flame (oven heat) close — the inner wall warms up and the rice grains stuck to the inner wall **cook and stick together** forming a uniform cooked layer. After the rice is cooked, you remove the flame and let the handi cool. **Key parallels:** | Cooking Analogy | Rotational Moulding | |---|---| | Cooking pot (aluminium handi) | Mould (aluminium/steel) | | Rice grains | Polymer powder (PE/PVC/Nylon) | | Low flame from tandoor | Hot air convection oven (260–400 °C) | | Two-axis rotation of spit | Biaxial rotation of mould arms | | Cooked rice layer fused on wall | Sintered polymer wall | | Cooling the handi before opening | Forced/ambient cooling cycle | ## 1.3 Position in the Plastics Processing Family > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU:** Examiners often ask *"Why is rotomoulding preferred over blow moulding for large tanks?"* — The answer lies in the **mould pressure** (rotomoulding ≈ 0 bar; blow moulding needs 4–6 bar which requires heavy steel moulds for large parts). The polymer processing family chart: $$ \text{Plastics Processing} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{High Pressure (700–1400 bar)} & \rightarrow \text{Injection Moulding} \\ \text{Medium Pressure (4–10 bar)} & \rightarrow \text{Blow Moulding, Thermoforming} \\ \text{Low Pressure (0–1 bar)} & \rightarrow \text{Rotational Moulding, Casting} \\ \text{No Pressure (extrusion)} & \rightarrow \text{Profile/Sheet Extrusion} \end{cases} $$ ## 1.4 Standard Process Metrics (bold for emphasis) - **Mould temperatures:** 260 °C to 400 °C - **Mould rotation ratio (Major : Minor axis):** 1 : 2 to 1 : 8 (most common is **1 : 4**) - **Heating cycle time:** 5 to 30 minutes (depending on wall thickness) - **Cooling cycle time:** 5 to 30 minutes - **Typical wall thickness:** 1 mm to 50 mm - **Mould materials:** Cast aluminium (most common), fabricated steel, electroformed nickel - **Most used polymer:** **Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LLDPE)** — ~ 85% of all rotomoulded products worldwide ## 1.5 What Makes a Product a Good Candidate for Rotomoulding? A KTU favourite 2-mark question. The five-pointer rule: 1. **Hollow** with **no internal stress** (e.g., water tanks, fuel tanks). 2. **Large size** (typically > 1 m³) where injection moulding becomes uneconomical. 3. **Complex shape** with curves, recesses, and varying cross-sections. 4. **Low-to-medium volume production** (100s to 10,000s per year). 5. **Single-piece, seamless construction** required (no welded joints that can leak). # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Sequential Process Stages The complete rotomoulding cycle consists of **four stages**. KTU frequently asks: *"Explain the stages of rotomoulding with a temperature–time diagram."* — Master this section thoroughly. ### Stage 1 — **Loading (Charging)** [≈ 30–90 seconds] - A predetermined **charge mass** of polymer powder (or liquid plastisol) is introduced into the open mould cavity. - The powder particle size is critical: typically **35 mesh (500 µm) to 8 mesh (2.4 mm)**. - Mould is then **clamped shut** (clamshell type) or sealed (split moulds). - No rotation occurs during this stage. $$ m_{charge} = \rho_p \cdot V_{cavity} $$ where $\rho_p$ = polymer powder bulk density (kg/m³), $V_{cavity}$ = mould internal volume (m³). ### Stage 2 — **Heating (Fusion Stage)** [≈ 5–30 minutes] - The sealed mould is mounted onto the **arm/spider** of the rotational moulding machine. - The arm rotates the mould on **two perpendicular axes** simultaneously. - Mould is rolled into a **hot air convection oven** (gas or electric) at 260–400 °C. - Powder particles contact the hot inner wall, **sinter** (fuse together) layer by layer. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sintering mechanism (KTU high-yield):** Heat transfers from the mould wall → through the deposited powder layer → to the inner surface. The powder melts from the **outside-in** (mould side melts first), then fuses inward as time progresses. This is **opposite to injection moulding** where the skin freezes first. The temperature evolution at the inner mould surface: $$ T_{wall}(t) = T_{oven} - \left(T_{oven} - T_{initial}\right) \cdot e^{-t/\tau} $$ where $\tau$ = thermal time constant of the mould-polymer system. ### Stage 3 — **Cooling (Crystallization Stage)** [≈ 5–30 minutes] - The mould is moved into the **cooling chamber** (forced air, water spray, or mist). - Biaxial rotation is **maintained** throughout cooling to prevent sagging. - For semi-crystalline polymers like PE, **crystallization kinetics** (Avrami equation) determines part quality. $$ X_c(t) = 1 - \exp(-K \cdot t^n) $$ where $X_c$ = crystalline fraction, $K$ = crystallization rate constant, $n$ = Avrami exponent (typically 2–4 for PE). > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU mistake:** Students often write that the mould stops rotating during cooling. **This is wrong** — the mould must keep rotating during cooling to maintain uniform wall thickness and avoid part deformation. ### Stage 4 — **Demoulding (Unloading)** [≈ 30–90 seconds] - Mould is opened (clamshell) or the part is extracted (split mould). - Part is allowed to equilibrate to room temperature. - Mould is cleaned, treated with **mould release agent** (silicone, wax-based), and recharged. ## 2.2 Biaxial Rotation — The Heart of Rotomoulding The mould rotates on **two axes simultaneously**: - **Major axis (primary)** — horizontal arm rotation - **Minor axis (secondary)** — perpendicular to major, on the mould carriage The **rotation ratio** is critical: $$ R = \frac{N_{major}}{N_{minor}} $$ Typical ratios: $$ \begin{array}{|l|c|l|} \hline \textbf{Product Shape} & \textbf{Ratio (Major:Minor)} & \textbf{Application} \\ \hline \text{Cylindrical tank} & 1 : 4 & \text{Vertical water tanks} \\ \hline \text{Rectangular box} & 1 : 2 & \text{Storage containers} \\ \hline \text{Sphere/ball} & 1 : 1 & \text{Toys, road marker balls} \\ \hline \text{Long thin tube} & 1 : 8 & \text{Road cones, kayaks} \\ \hline \end{array} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 14-Mark Favourite:** "Why is the ratio not 1:1 for most products?" — Answer: A 1:1 ratio produces uniform wall thickness in all directions, but for cylindrical tanks, we need MORE rotation on the axis perpendicular to the cylinder's length to ensure the powder coats the cylindrical wall evenly. ## 2.3 Powder Behaviour Inside the Rotating Mould — 4 Phenomena As the mould rotates, the powder inside experiences: 1. **Tumbling/Cascading** — Powder slides and tumbles against the mould wall (low speed regime). 2. **Falling/Cataracting** — Powder falls freely through the air (medium speed regime). 3. **Centrifuging** — Powder sticks to wall due to centrifugal force (high speed regime). 4. **Coating** — Powder adheres to mould wall by surface tension after melting. > [!NOTE] > **Operating window for rotomoulding:** The mould speed must be high enough to **tumble and coat** the powder, but low enough to avoid **centrifuging** (which prevents powder from falling onto all surfaces). The critical speed to avoid centrifuging is given by: $$ N_{critical} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{2g}{D}} $$ where $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $D$ = mould internal diameter (m). For $D = 1$ m: $N_{critical}$ ≈ 0.7 rev/s ≈ 42 RPM. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table for Part A and Part B calculations.** $$ \begin{array}{|l|l|c|l|} \hline \textbf{Quantity} & \textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Units} & \textbf{Application} \\ \hline \text{Charge mass} & m = \rho_p \cdot V & \text{kg} & \text{Material loading} \\ \hline \text{Wall thickness (est.)} & t_w = \dfrac{m}{\rho_s \cdot A_s} & \text{m} & \text{Design check} \\ \hline \text{Rotational ratio} & R = N_{maj}/N_{min} & \text{dimensionless} & \text{Process setup} \\ \hline \text{Linear speed at wall} & v = \pi D N & \text{m/s} & \text{Speed verification} \\ \hline \text{Heat required (sensible)} & Q_s = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T & \text{J} & \text{Oven sizing} \\ \hline \text{Heat required (latent)} & Q_l = m \cdot L_f & \text{J} & \text{Fusion energy} \\ \hline \text{Total cycle time} & t_{cycle} = t_h + t_c + t_{load} + t_{unload} & \text{s} & \text{Production rate} \\ \hline \text{Production rate} & PR = \dfrac{3600}{t_{cycle}} & \text{parts/hr} & \text{Planning} \\ \hline \text{Critical RPM} & N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2g}{D}} & \text{rev/s} & \text{Speed limit} \\ \hline \text{Oven heat balance} & Q_{oven} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot \Delta T_{air} & \text{W} & \text{Utility sizing} \\ \hline \end{array} $$ > **Note on notation:** $\rho_p$ = powder bulk density, $\rho_s$ = solid polymer density, $A_s$ = internal mould surface area, $c_p$ = specific heat, $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility of Rotomoulding in Industry > [!NOTE] > **For 14-mark questions requiring industry context:** - **Storage tanks** — 200 L to 50,000 L chemical and water tanks (Clover, Sintex, Harvel). - **Marine products** — Kayaks, buoys, pontoons, boat hulls (Rotax, Bell). - **Automotive** — Fuel tanks, ducting, dashboards, air spoilers. - **Material handling** — Pallets, garbage bins, road barriers, traffic cones. - **Toys and recreation** — Playhouses, rocking horses, balls, doll bodies. - **Medical and defense** — Insulated containers, ballistic panels (UHMWPE reinforced). The process is **inherently sustainable** because: 1. There is **zero flash, zero sprue, zero runner** — no material wastage. 2. Moulds are **100% reusable** for decades. 3. Trimmings and rejected parts can be **reground and reused** (closed-loop recycling). 4. **No volatile emissions** because there is no melt pool exposed to atmosphere. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Charge Mass Required to Achieve a Target Wall Thickness This is the **most commonly asked numerical in KTU Module 4** under rotational moulding. We derive the relation: $$ t_w = \frac{m_{charge}}{\rho_s \cdot A_s} $$ ### Derivation Walkthrough **Step 1 — Define the system.** The mould is a hollow closed cavity of internal volume $V$ and internal surface area $A_s$. We load a charge mass $m$ of polymer powder having bulk density $\rho_p$. **Step 2 — Equate volumes before and after fusion.** The bulk volume occupied by the powder (loose) is $V_p = m / \rho_p$. After melting and fusing, the polymer collapses to its solid density $\rho_s$, and the polymer now coats the inner mould wall uniformly to a thickness $t_w$: $$ V_{polymer,solid} = \frac{m}{\rho_s} $$ **Step 3 — Relate coated volume to surface area and thickness.** The polymer forms a thin shell over the inner mould surface. The shell volume equals: $$ V_{shell} = A_s \cdot t_w $$ **Step 4 — Set solid volume equal to shell volume.** Conservation of polymer mass (assuming 100% densification; rotomoulded PE has ~ 2–5% microvoids, but we ignore for this derivation): $$ A_s \cdot t_w = \frac{m}{\rho_s} $$ **Step 5 — Solve for charge mass required to achieve a given wall thickness.** $$ m_{charge} = \rho_s \cdot A_s \cdot t_w $$ **Step 6 — Solve for expected wall thickness for a given charge mass.** $$ t_w = \frac{m_{charge}}{\rho_s \cdot A_s} $$ ### Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style) > **Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme pattern):** A cylindrical water tank has internal diameter $D = 1.2$ m and internal height $H = 1.8$ m. The target wall thickness is $t_w = 6$ mm. The polymer used is HDPE with solid density $\rho_s = 950$ kg/m³. Calculate: (a) the required charge mass, (b) the production rate if cycle time is 28 minutes. **Solution:** (a) **Internal surface area of the cylinder** (bottom + side wall + top — but as an open-top tank only bottom + side): $$ A_s = \pi D H + \frac{\pi D^2}{4} $$ Substitute values: $$ A_s = \pi (1.2)(1.8) + \frac{\pi (1.2)^2}{4} $$ $$ A_s = 6.786 + 1.131 $$ $$ A_s = 7.917 \; \text{m}^2 $$ **Charge mass:** $$ m = \rho_s \cdot A_s \cdot t_w $$ $$ m = 950 \times 7.917 \times 0.006 $$ $$ m = 45.13 \; \text{kg} $$ (b) **Production rate:** $$ PR = \frac{60}{t_{cycle}} = \frac{60}{28} = 2.14 \; \text{tanks/hr} $$ $$ \boxed{PR \approx 2.14 \text{ tanks/hr, or } 128 \text{ tanks per 8-hour shift}} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation — Critical Rotational Speed (Centrifuging Limit) The condition for powder to start centrifuging on the inner wall of a rotating cylinder is when the centrifugal acceleration equals gravitational acceleration: $$ \omega^2 \cdot r \geq g $$ where $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $r$ = radius of the cylinder (m). **Step 1 — Solve for the threshold angular velocity:** $$ \omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{r}} $$ **Step 2 — Convert to rotational speed in revolutions per second:** $$ N_c = \frac{\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{r}} $$ **Step 3 — Express in revolutions per minute (RPM):** $$ N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{r}} = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{r}} $$ **Step 4 — For diameter form (using $r = D/2$):** $$ N_c = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2g}{D}} $$ ### Worked Example > **Problem:** A spherical toy mould has internal diameter $D = 0.4$ m. Calculate the maximum safe rotational speed to avoid centrifuging. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **Solution:** $$ N_c = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.81}{0.4}} $$ $$ N_c = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{19.62}{0.4}} $$ $$ N_c = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{49.05} $$ $$ N_c = \frac{30}{\pi} \times 7.00 $$ $$ N_c = 66.86 \; \text{RPM} $$ $$ \boxed{N_c \approx 66.9 \; \text{RPM (major axis)}} $$ The minor axis speed (assuming ratio 1:4) is: $$ N_{minor} = 4 \times 66.9 = 267.5 \; \text{RPM} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world check:** Actual rotomoulding speeds are typically 4–20 RPM on the major axis — well below the centrifuging limit. This is because we WANT the powder to fall, not stick to the wall. ## 3.3 Derivation — Heat Required in the Heating Stage The polymer powder must be heated from room temperature ($T_i \approx 25$ °C) to the melting point ($T_m$) and then fully fused. The total energy is: $$ Q_{total} = Q_{sensible,polymer} + Q_{latent,polymer} + Q_{sensible,mould} $$ **Step 1 — Sensible heat of polymer (solid to melting point):** $$ Q_{s,p} = m_p \cdot c_{p,solid} \cdot (T_m - T_i) $$ For LLDPE: $c_{p,solid} \approx 2300$ J/(kg·K), $T_m \approx 125$ °C. **Step 2 — Latent heat of fusion (at melting point):** $$ Q_{l,p} = m_p \cdot L_f $$ For LLDPE: $L_f \approx 230,000$ J/kg = 230 kJ/kg. **Step 3 — Sensible heat of polymer (melt to oven equilibrium — small contribution):** $$ Q_{s2,p} = m_p \cdot c_{p,melt} \cdot (T_{oven} - T_m) $$ **Step 4 — Sensible heat of the aluminium mould:** $$ Q_{s,mould} = m_{mould} \cdot c_{p,Al} \cdot (T_{oven} - T_i) $$ For aluminium: $c_{p,Al} = 900$ J/(kg·K). A 50 kg mould from 25 °C to 300 °C needs: $$ Q_{s,mould} = 50 \times 900 \times 275 = 12.375 \; \text{MJ} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** The mould heat-up dominates the energy budget (60–80% of oven energy) because aluminium is dense and has a high specific heat. This is why **insulated moulds** and **high-emissivity mould coatings** are used to reduce energy consumption. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Process Parameters > [!NOTE] > **Use this matrix in 14-mark questions to demonstrate process-engineering thinking.** $$ \begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|} \hline \textbf{Parameter} & \textbf{Small Parts} & \textbf{Medium Parts} & \textbf{Large Parts} \\ & \text{(toys)} & \text{(containers)} & \text{(tanks)} \\ \hline \text{Oven temp (°C)} & 320–360 & 280–320 & 260–300 \\ \hline \text{Heat time (min)} & 5–10 & 10–20 & 20–35 \\ \hline \text{Cool time (min)} & 5–10 & 10–20 & 15–30 \\ \hline \text{Major axis RPM} & 8–12 & 5–8 & 2–5 \\ \hline \text{Ratio (Maj:Min)} & 1:4 & 1:4 & 1:5–1:8 \\ \hline \text{Wall thickness (mm)} & 1.5–3 & 4–8 & 6–15 \\ \hline \text{Mould material} & \text{Ni electroform} & \text{Al cast} & \text{Al/Steel} \\ \hline \end{array} $$ # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Rotational Moulding Machine (Carousel Type) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Operator Console
PLC HMI Panel] --> B[Central Carousel Hub

and Inspection] F --> N[Regrind and Reuse

While the mould in Station 2 is heating, the mould in Station 3 is cooling, and so on. This is the **most common industrial configuration** because it maximises oven utilisation. ## 4.2 Block Architecture — Process Flow Within a Single Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step A: Loading

Gas Burner / Heater] -->|Convection Q conv| HAA[Hot Air Stream
T oven 300 degC] HAA -->|Conduction through air gap Q cond 1| MW[Mould Outer Wall
Aluminium Shell] MW -->|Conduction through Al Q cond 2| MI[Mould Inner Wall
T inner 250 degC] MI -->|Conduction through powder Q cond 3| PL[Powder Layer
Partially Sintered] PL -->|Conduction through melt Q cond 4| IS[Inner Surface
Polymer Melt T melt 130 degC] IS -->|Convection Q conv free| AIR[Internal Air
Trapped Gas] AIR -->|Radiation back Q rad| IS ``` > [!NOTE] > **Series thermal resistance network** — The total resistance from oven to polymer centre is the sum of four conduction resistances and two convection resistances. The **powder layer** is typically the largest resistance (low thermal conductivity ≈ 0.15 W/m·K) — this is why heating time increases dramatically with wall thickness. ## 4.4 Block Architecture — Machine Types Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TD MT[Rotational Moulding Machines] --> M1[Clamshell Type

Boat Hulls

Long Parts
Single Axis plus Sweep] M1 --> APP1[Toys, Samples] M2 --> APP2[Containers, Bins] M3 --> APP3[Tanks, Road Barriers] M4 --> APP4[Kayaks, Tubs] M5 --> APP5[Pipes, Conveyor Covers] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **Define rotational moulding. Mention any two advantages of the process.** **Model Answer:** Rotational moulding is a low-pressure polymer processing technique in which a measured charge of polymer powder is loaded into a closed, hollow mould. The mould is heated while being rotated biaxially, causing the powder to sinter and fuse uniformly onto the inner mould surface. After cooling, a seamless, hollow plastic part is demoulded. **Two advantages (any two, 1.5 marks each):** 1. **Moulds are inexpensive** compared to injection moulding — cast aluminium moulds cost 10–30% of equivalent injection moulds. 2. **Seamless, stress-free parts** with uniform wall thickness and no weld lines are produced. 3. **Zero material wastage** — no flash, sprue, or runner. 4. **Large hollow parts** (up to 50,000 L) can be made in a single piece. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Why is rotational moulding classified as a "low-pressure" process? What is the typical mould rotation ratio for a cylindrical water tank?** **Model Answer:** Rotational moulding is low-pressure (≈ 0 to 1 bar gauge) because the polymer is melted and fused purely by **heat transfer from the mould wall** — there is no external force pushing the melt into the cavity as in injection moulding. The only pressure on the polymer is the **centrifugal force from rotation**, which is very small. For a **cylindrical water tank**, the typical rotation ratio is **1 : 4** (major : minor axis), meaning the minor axis rotates four times faster than the major axis. This ensures even coating of the cylindrical wall. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **carousel-type rotational moulding machine**. List the four stations of the cycle and state the function of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A spherical tank of internal diameter $D = 2$ m is to be rotomoulded using HDPE powder. The target wall thickness is $t_w = 8$ mm. The solid density of HDPE is $\rho_s = 954$ kg/m³. Calculate: (i) The charge mass of powder required. (ii) The production rate if the cycle time is 35 minutes. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A **(a) Carousel-Type Rotational Moulding Machine** A carousel machine consists of a **central rotating hub** with **3 or 4 radial arms**, each carrying a mould. The hub indexes (rotates) by 90° (4-arm) or 120° (3-arm) at the end of each station's cycle, so that all stations operate **simultaneously and continuously**. **The Four Stations:** | Station | Name | Function | |---|---|---| | **Station 1** | **Loading Bay** | Mould is opened, charge of powder is loaded, release agent sprayed, mould clamped. | | **Station 2** | **Heating Oven** | Mould is heated to 260–400 °C with biaxial rotation; powder sinters onto inner wall. | | **Station 3** | **Cooling Chamber** | Mould is cooled (forced air + water mist) while still rotating to crystallize and set the part. | | **Station 4** | **Demoulding** | Mould is opened, part is extracted, trimmed, and inspected. | **Key advantages of carousel over shuttle type:** Continuous oven utilisation, higher production rate, lower labour per part. --- **(b) Numerical Solution** **(i) Charge Mass Calculation** For a sphere, the **internal surface area** is: $$ A_s = \pi D^2 $$ Substitute $D = 2$ m: $$ A_s = \pi (2)^2 = 4\pi = 12.566 \; \text{m}^2 $$ The **internal volume** of the spherical shell (approximately equal to surface area times thickness for thin shells): $$ V_{polymer} = A_s \cdot t_w = 12.566 \times 0.008 = 0.1005 \; \text{m}^3 $$ The **charge mass**: $$ m = \rho_s \cdot V_{polymer} = 954 \times 0.1005 $$ $$ \boxed{m = 95.91 \; \text{kg} \approx 96 \; \text{kg of HDPE powder}} $$ **(ii) Production Rate** $$ PR = \frac{60}{t_{cycle}} = \frac{60}{35} $$ $$ \boxed{PR \approx 1.71 \; \text{tanks/hr, or about 102 tanks per 10-hour shift}} $$ --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the **stages of rotational moulding** with the help of a temperature–time diagram. Discuss the importance of biaxial rotation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare **rotational moulding with blow moulding** in terms of mould cost, part size, wall thickness uniformity, material wastage, and typical applications. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B **(a) Stages of Rotational Moulding** The complete cycle consists of four sequential stages, with biaxial rotation maintained during stages 2 and 3: | Stage | Duration | Action | |---|---|---| | **1. Loading** | 30–90 s | Powder poured into open mould; clamped shut. | | **2. Heating** | 5–30 min | Mould biaxially rotated in oven at 260–400 °C; powder sinters onto inner wall. | | **3. Cooling** | 5–30 min | Mould moved to cooling station; rotation maintained; polymer crystallizes. | | **4. Demoulding** | 30–90 s | Mould opened; part removed; flash trimmed. | **Temperature–Time Diagram Description:** The x-axis is time (minutes), y-axis is temperature (°C). Three curves are plotted: - **Oven temperature** — flat at ≈ 300 °C during heating stage. - **Mould inner wall temperature** — rises with a time constant, plateaus near oven temp, then falls during cooling. - **Polymer inner surface temperature** — lags behind mould wall, rises through $T_m$ (≈ 125 °C for LLDPE) during fusion, plateaus briefly due to latent heat absorption, then falls. **Importance of Biaxial Rotation:** 1. Ensures **uniform wall thickness** in all directions of the part. 2. Prevents **localised thinning or pooling** of polymer. 3. Distributes powder **evenly** across complex mould geometries. 4. The **rotation ratio** (1:2 to 1:8) is tuned to the part geometry. 5. Continued rotation during **cooling** prevents sagging and maintains dimensional accuracy. --- **(b) Comparison: Rotational Moulding vs Blow Moulding** | Parameter | Rotational Moulding | Blow Moulding | |---|---|---| | **Mould cost** | Low (cast Al, ₹50K–5L) | High (steel/Al with cooling channels) | | **Part size** | Up to 50,000 L | Typically < 5000 L | | **Wall thickness uniformity** | Excellent (gravity-driven) | Moderate (parison sag) | | **Material wastage** | Zero (closed-mould) | Flash + tail (5–15% waste) | | **Mould pressure** | ≈ 0 bar (gravity) | 4–6 bar (compressed air) | | **Tooling lead time** | 4–8 weeks | 8–16 weeks | | **Typical polymers** | PE, PVC, Nylon | PET, PE, PP, PVC | | **Applications** | Water tanks, kayaks, bins | Bottles, jerry cans, tanks | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deductions in Rotational Moulding Answers (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > > 1. **Confusing "sintering" with "melting"** — The process is sintering (particle-to-particle fusion below the bulk melt pool). Examiners deduct 1 mark if you claim the polymer forms a continuous melt pool like in injection moulding. > > 2. **Forgetting to maintain rotation during cooling** — This is a frequent error. Many students write "mould is cooled, then demoulded" — but the mould MUST keep rotating during cooling. > > 3. **Wrong rotation ratio for tank geometry** — Saying "1:1 ratio for all parts" loses marks. The correct answer depends on geometry (1:4 for cylinders, 1:1 for spheres). > > 4. **Confusing bulk density with solid density** — In numerical problems, use **solid density** $\rho_s$ for the wall thickness formula, not powder bulk density. Bulk density is used only for hopper/feeder design. > > 5. **Missing the "low pressure" justification** — When asked "why low pressure?", students often skip the explanation and write only "no pressure needed".
