You must mention that polymer is **gravity + rotation driven**, with no external pressurisation. > > 6. **Incorrect cycle time calculation** — Include all four stages (load + heat + cool + demould) in the total cycle, not just heating. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Rotational Moulding:** - ✅ **Definition:** Low-pressure, biaxial-rotation, gravity-driven thermoplastic process. - ✅ **Charge formula:** $m = \rho_s \cdot A_s \cdot t_w$ (use **solid** density, not bulk). - ✅ **Wall thickness formula:** $t_w = m / (\rho_s \cdot A_s)$. - ✅ **Four stages:** Loading → Heating → Cooling → Demoulding. - ✅ **Rotation is maintained during heating AND cooling** — never stop. - ✅ **Rotation ratio:** 1:4 for cylinders, 1:1 for spheres, 1:8 for long tubes. - ✅ **Most common polymer:** LLDPE (~ 85% of rotomoulded parts). - ✅ **Oven temperature:** 260–400 °C; Mould: cast aluminium. - ✅ **Mould pressure:** ≈ 0 bar; **Cycle time:** 20–70 minutes total. - ✅ **Centrifuging limit:** $N_c = (30/\pi)\sqrt{2g/D}$ — actual speeds well below. - ✅ **Zero material waste:** No flash, sprue, runner — closed-loop recyclable. - ✅ **Machines:** Clamshell, Shuttle, Carousel, Rock-and-Roll, Swing. - ✅ **Applications:** Water tanks, fuel tanks, kayaks, bins, toys, road barriers, pallets. - ✅ **Advantage over blow moulding:** Larger sizes, lower mould cost, no flash. - ✅ **Advantage over injection moulding:** No weld lines, lower mould cost, stress-free parts. - ✅ **Disadvantage:** Long cycle times, limited to thermoplastics, surface finish depends on mould. - ✅ **Particle size of powder:** 35 mesh (500 µm) to 8 mesh (2.4 mm). - ✅ **Typical wall thickness:** 1 mm to 50 mm (best for 3–15 mm range). - ✅ **Crystallization kinetics** (Avrami equation) govern cooling for semi-crystalline polymers. - ✅ **Three powder phenomena:** Tumbling, falling, coating — centrifuging must be avoided.

topics.title: Types of Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINING & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PCPOT503 – Polymer Machinery & Product Manufacturing (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, the topic *“Types of Machines”* refers to the systematic classification, constructional features, and operational principles of the heavy-duty process equipment used to convert raw polymeric materials (thermoplastics, thermosets, elastomers, and composites) into finished or semi-finished products of defined geometry and property profile.

These machines are broadly classified under **Primary Shaping Equipment** (those that give the polymer its final bulk shape) and **Secondary Forming Equipment** (those that reshape an already shaped preform).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Scope):**
> Classification, constructional details, working principle, and salient features of machines used in **Compression Molding, Transfer Molding, Injection Molding, Extrusion, Blow Molding, Thermoforming, Rotational Molding, and Calendaring**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Polymer Kitchen"

Imagine a **professional kitchen**. A chef does not use a ladle to bake bread, nor a frying pan to make ice-cream. Each dish demands a *specific tool* matched to the *state of the ingredient* (solid, liquid, dough, foam, paste) and the *desired geometry* (sheet, sphere, hollow tube, thin film).

Polymers behave the same way. They exist in three physical states during processing:
- **Solid pellets/granules** → Extruders, Injection Molders
- **Viscous melt** → Blow Molders, Calendaring Rolls, Fiber Spinners
- **Liquid reactive resin** → Compression/Transfer Molding Presses

Hence, the *type of machine* is selected by the **state of the polymer**, the **thermodynamic regime** (isothermal vs. adiabatic), and the **shape the designer wants to make**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Metric of Machine Classification:**
> The **Clamping Force (Tonnes)** for presses and the **Screw L/D Ratio (dimensionless)** for plasticators are the two industry-standard numbers that uniquely identify the capacity class of any polymer processing machine.

---

## 1.3 High-Level Classification Matrix (Master View)

| Family | Polymer State | Typical Cycle Type | Representative Product |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Compression Molding Press | Solid preform + reactive resin | Batch | Electrical switchboards, trays |
| Transfer Molding Press | Reactive liquid resin | Batch | Pot handles, insulators |
| Injection Molding Machine | Thermoplastic melt | Cyclic (fast) | Buckets, mobile cases, gears |
| Extruder | Thermoplastic melt | Continuous | Pipes, sheets, profiles, cables |
| Blow Molding Machine | Parison melt | Cyclic | Bottles, jerry cans, tanks |
| Thermoforming Machine | Softened sheet | Cyclic | Cups, blister packs, helmets |
| Rotational Molding Machine | Liquid powder | Batch (slow) | Water tanks, kayaks, road cones |
| Calendaring Machine | Highly viscous melt | Continuous | PVC films, floor tiles, raincoats |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

**Step 1 — Identify the polymer category.**
- *Thermoplastic* (PE, PP, PVC, PS, PET, Nylon): use **melt-processing machines** (extruder, injection molder, blow molder, thermoformer).
- *Thermoset* (Phenolics, UF, MF, Epoxy, Unsaturated Polyester): use **reaction-molding machines** (compression press, transfer press, RIM).
- *Elastomer* (Natural Rubber, SBR, EPDM): use **specialized presses** or **extruders** with long curing sections.

**Step 2 — Identify the geometry of the desired product.**
- 2-D continuous sheet/film → **Calendaring** or **Film Extrusion (Chill-roll / Cast film)**.
- 3-D hollow body → **Blow Molding** or **Rotational Molding**.
- Discrete complex 3-D part → **Injection Molding**.
- Continuous profile (pipe, wire, sheet) → **Single-screw Extrusion**.
- Large flat panel → **Compression Press** (e.g., SMC).

**Step 3 — Identify production volume and tolerance.**
- High volume + tight tolerance → Injection / continuous extrusion.
- Low volume + large size → Rotational molding.
- Ultra-thin continuous film → Calendaring.

---

## 2.2 Constructional Anatomy — Universal Machine Sub-Systems

Every polymer processing machine, regardless of family, contains the **same four functional blocks** in some configuration:

1. **Feed / Plasticating Unit** – receives raw polymer, melts it (or heats it), and meters it forward.
2. **Mold / Die / Tooling Block** – defines the final geometry.
3. **Clamping / Holding / Take-Up Unit** – resists the internal pressure and removes the finished article.
4. **Control & Drive Console** – regulates temperature, pressure, speed, and timing (now PLC / micro-processor based).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> This *functional decomposition* is the foundation of KTU Module-5 maintenance questions and Module-4 design questions — examiners expect students to identify *which block has failed* from a symptom (e.g., short-shot → feed unit; flash → clamping unit; sink marks → cooling/holding block).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Variables & Units | Where Used (Machine) | Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F_{clamp} \geq P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$ | $F_{clamp}$: clamp force (Tonnes); $P_{cav}$: cavity pressure (MPa); $A_{proj}$: projected area (m²) | Injection Molding | $F_{clamp}$ always ≥ $1.1 \times$ theoretical for safety |
| 2 | $Q_{vol} = \frac{\pi^{2} \, D^{2} \, N \, h \, \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}$ | $Q_{vol}$: volumetric output (m³/s); $D$: screw dia (m); $N$: rpm; $h$: flight depth (m); $\phi$: helix angle (°) | Single-Screw Extruder | Valid in metering zone, fully filled |
| 3 | $L_{min} = \pi \, D \, \cot\phi$ | $L_{min}$: length of metering zone (m) | Extruder Design | $L/D \ge 20$ for modern extruders |
| 4 | $V_{shot} = V_{screw} \times \rho_{melt} / \rho_{solid}$ | $V_{shot}$: shot weight (g); $V_{screw}$: swept volume (cm³); $\rho$: density | Injection Molding | Includes cushion allowance (5–10 %) |
| 5 | $P_{hyd} = \frac{F_{clamp}}{A_{ram}}$ | $P_{hyd}$: hydraulic pressure (Pa); $A_{ram}$: piston area (m²) | Hydraulic Press | $P_{hyd} \le 21$ MPa for most presses |
| 6 | $t_{cycle} = t_{fill} + t_{cool} + t_{open} + t_{close}$ | $t$: time (s) | All Cyclic Machines | Cooling usually dominates |
| 7 | $S_{blow} = \frac{D_{par}}{D_{bot}} \times 100\%$ | $S_{blow}$: blow-up ratio; $D$: diameter (m) | Blow Molding | Typically $2:1$ to $4:1$ |
| 8 | $RPM_{rot} = \frac{v_{rot}}{\pi \, D_{mold}}$ | $v_{rot}$: linear speed (m/s) | Rotational Molding | Slow, $1$–$20$ rpm |
| 9 | $N_{rolls} = 3 \, \text{to} \, 4$ (typical) | Number of rolls | Calendaring | Higher N = better gauge control |
| 10 | $Re_{melt} = \frac{\rho \, v \, D_h}{\mu}$ | $\rho$: density; $v$: velocity; $D_h$: hydraulic dia; $\mu$: viscosity | Flow in die / mold | $Re < 1$ typical → laminar |

---

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

Selecting the wrong machine family is the **single most expensive mistake** in polymer product design — it inflates tooling cost, scrap rate, and energy consumption. Real industry decisions are governed by:
- **Specific energy consumption** (kWh/kg of product)
- **Scrap / re-grind ratio**
- **Cycle time vs. cooling rate trade-off**
- **Capital cost amortization**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Single-Screw Extruder Volumetric Output (Metering Zone)

We model the screw channel as an **unwrapped rectangular channel** moving relative to the stationary barrel. The drag flow from this relative motion is the dominant pumping component.

**Given:**
- Screw diameter = $D$
- Flight depth at metering zone = $h$
- Helix angle = $\phi$
- Screw rotational speed = $N$ (rev/s)

**Step 1 — Tangential velocity at barrel surface.**
The barrel appears to move relative to the screw at velocity:
$$v_{bx} = \pi D N \cos\phi$$
(velocity component down the channel, in the x-direction)

**Step 2 — Velocity component across the channel (z-direction).**
$$v_{bz} = \pi D N \sin\phi$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric drag flow per unit channel width.**
For pure drag flow in a channel of depth $h$, the velocity profile is linear, giving average velocity $v_{bx}/2$. Integrated across the channel:
$$Q_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, v_{bx} \times h \times W = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \pi D N \cos\phi \cdot h \cdot W$$

**Step 4 — Channel width W expressed in terms of D.**
From the screw geometry, the perpendicular width of the unwrapped channel is:
$$W = \pi D \sin\phi - e$$
where $e$ is the flight land width. Ignoring the small land width ($e \ll W$):
$$W \approx \pi D \sin\phi$$

**Step 5 — Substitute W into the drag-flow expression.**
$$Q_{drag} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \pi D N \cos\phi \cdot h \cdot \pi D \sin\phi$$

**Step 6 — Apply the trigonometric identity $\sin\phi\cos\phi = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin 2\phi$.**
$$\boxed{Q_{vol} = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:**
- $Q \propto N$ → output rises linearly with screw speed.
- $Q \propto h$ → deeper flights give more output (used in *high-output extruders*).
- $Q \propto D^{2}$ → doubling screw diameter *quadruples* output (why industrial extruders scale with $D$).

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Injection Molding Clamping Force

**Given:**
- Average cavity pressure inside the mold = $P_{cav}$ (MPa)
- Projected area of the molded part (as seen from clamp axis) = $A_{proj}$ (m²)
- Safety factor recommended by KTU/SPEREF = $1.1$

**Step 1 — Force balance on the parting line.**
The melt inside the cavity pushes outward on the mold halves. The total separating force on the parting line equals pressure × projected area:
$$F_{separating} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$$

**Step 2 — Clamping force must overcome this.**
To prevent the mold from opening (flash formation):
$$F_{clamp} \geq F_{separating}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the KTU safety factor.**
$$\boxed{F_{clamp} = 1.1 \, P_{cav} \times A_{proj}}$$

**Unit conversion check:**
$P_{cav}$ in MPa = N/mm²; $A_{proj}$ in mm² → Force in N → divide by $9.81 \times 10^{3}$ to get Tonnes-force.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)

The blow-up ratio defines the *circumferential stretch* the parison undergoes inside the blow mold:

$$S_{blow} = \frac{D_{mold}}{D_{parison}}$$

For a hollow bottle of axial length $L$ and final diameter $D_{mold}$:
$$S_{axial} = \frac{L_{mold}}{L_{parison}}$$

The **total areal stretch** governing material thinning:
$$S_{total} = S_{blow} \times S_{axial}$$

If $S_{total}$ exceeds the polymer's *strain-hardening limit*, the parison bursts — the limiting condition is:
$$\boxed{S_{total} \le \lambda_{max}(\text{polymer})}$$
For HDPE: $\lambda_{max} \approx 5$; for PET: $\lambda_{max} \approx 4$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — Compression Molding Pressure vs. Part Thickness

The compressive load required to consolidate a thermoset charge of thickness $t$, area $A$, at a cure-stage viscosity $\mu$:

$$P_{comp} = \frac{F_{ram}}{A_{mold}} = \frac{12 \, \mu \, Q \, L^{2}}{A^{3}}$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate of the squeezed-out resin and $L$ is the instantaneous charge thickness. As $t$ (and hence $L$) decreases, $P_{comp}$ rises as $L^{2}$ — explaining why *breathing* the press (slight reopening) is essential to release trapped gas during cure.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Machine Family Selection

| Parameter | Injection | Extrusion | Blow Molding | Compression | Rotomolding |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Cycle mode | Cyclic | Continuous | Cyclic | Batch | Batch |
| Pressure range (MPa) | 70 – 200 | 5 – 50 | 0.3 – 1.0 | 10 – 50 | < 0.1 |
| Wall thickness control | Excellent | Good | Moderate | Poor | Moderate |
| Capital cost (rel.) | Very High | High | High | Moderate | Low |
| Part size limit | < 50 kg | Limited by die | < 5000 L | Large panels | Very large |
| Best for | Complex 3D | Profiles | Hollow | Large flat | Large hollow |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flow — Polymer Processing Machines

The following Mermaid block renders the full hierarchical taxonomy of polymer processing machines as specified in KTU 2024 Module-4 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[POLYMER PROCESSING MACHINES] --> A1[Primary Shaping Equipment]
    A0 --> A2[Secondary Forming Equipment]

    A1 --> B1[Melt Processing Machines]
    A1 --> B2[Reaction Molding Machines]

    B1 --> C1[Extruders]
    B1 --> C2[Injection Molding Machines]
    B1 --> C3[Blow Molding Machines]
    B1 --> C4[Calendaring Machines]
    B1 --> C5[Fiber Spinning Machines]

    B2 --> D1[Compression Presses]
    B2 --> D2[Transfer Presses]
    D2 --> D2a[Pot Transfer]
    D2 --> D2b[Plunger Transfer]
    D2 --> D2c[Screw Transfer]

    C1 --> C1a[Single Screw Extruder]
    C1 --> C1b[Co-Rotating Twin Screw]
    C1 --> C1c[Counter-Rotating Twin Screw]
    C1 --> C1d[Multi Screw Extruder]

    C2 --> C2a[Plunger Type]
    C2 --> C2b[Two Stage Screw]
    C2 --> C2c[Reciprocating Screw IMM]
    C2 --> C2d[Electric Servo Driven IMM]

    C3 --> C3a[Extrusion Blow Molding]
    C3 --> C3b[Injection Blow Molding]
    C3 --> C3c[Stretch Blow Molding]

    A2 --> E1[Thermoforming Machines]
    A2 --> E2[Rotational Molding Machines]
    A2 --> E3[Casting Machines]
    A2 --> E4[Coating and Lamination Lines]

    E1 --> E1a[Vacuum Thermoforming]
    E1 --> E1b[Pressure Thermoforming]
    E1 --> E1c[Drape Form]

    E2 --> E2a[Rock and Roll Type]
    E2 --> E2b[Clamshell Type]
    E2 --> E2c[Carousel Type]

    C4 --> C4a[Inverted L Four Roll]
    C4 --> C4b[Z Type Four Roll]
    C4 --> C4c[Single Pair Roll]

    style A0 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style B1 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style B2 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#9dc3e6
    style C2 fill:#9dc3e6
    style C3 fill:#9dc3e6
    style C4 fill:#9dc3e6
    style C5 fill:#9dc3e6
    style D1 fill:#f4b084
    style D2 fill:#f4b084
    style E1 fill:#a9d18e
    style E2 fill:#a9d18e
    style E3 fill:#a9d18e
    style E4 fill:#a9d18e
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Universal Polymer Machine Block Diagram

The schematic below maps the **four universal functional sub-systems** that every machine family contains, regardless of polymer type.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SYS1[FEED UNIT]
        F1[Hopper]
        F2[Drying and Dehumidification]
        F3[Screw or Plunger Plasticator]
    end

    subgraph SYS2[SHAPING UNIT]
        S1[Mold or Die]
        S2[Heaters and Cooling Channels]
        S3[Ejector Pins]
    end

    subgraph SYS3[HOLDING UNIT]
        H1[Clamp or Take-Up Roll]
        H2[Hydraulic or Servo Actuator]
    end

    subgraph SYS4[CONTROL UNIT]
        K1[PLC and Microprocessor]
        K2[Temperature Pressure Speed Sensors]
        K3[HMI Touch Screen]
    end

    F1 --> F3
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> S1
    S2 --> S1
    S1 --> S3
    S3 --> H1
    H2 --> H1
    K2 --> K1
    K1 --> K3
    K1 --> H2

    style SYS1 fill:#deebf7
    style SYS2 fill:#fbe5d6
    style SYS3 fill:#e2efda
    style SYS4 fill:#fff2cc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exams:**
> When a question asks *"Identify the affected sub-system given a defect"*, trace the defect signal *upstream* (toward the feed unit) or *downstream* (toward the shaping unit). The block that *first* shows the abnormality is the root cause.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Classify polymer processing machines based on the physical state of the polymer during processing. Give one example for each category.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Polymer processing machines are classified into two broad categories based on the polymer's physical state during forming:

1. **Melt Processing Machines** (thermoplastic melt state)
   - Examples: Injection molding machine, Single-screw extruder, Blow molding machine, Calendaring machine.
2. **Reaction Molding Machines** (liquid reactive resin state)
   - Examples: Compression molding press, Transfer molding press, Reaction Injection Molding (RIM) unit.

*(Award 1 mark for category identification, 1 mark for melt machines example, 1 mark for reaction machines example.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series II]
**Distinguish between a single-screw extruder and a co-rotating twin-screw extruder in terms of construction and application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Single-Screw Extruder | Co-rotating Twin-Screw Extruder |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Screws | One screw inside barrel | Two screws rotating in same direction |
| Conveying mechanism | Drag-induced, poor mixing | Positive displacement, excellent mixing |
| Self-wiping | No | Yes (intermeshing flights wipe each other) |
| Typical application | Pipe, sheet, profile extrusion | Compounding, masterbatch, PVC processing |

*(Award 1.5 marks for construction difference, 1.5 marks for application difference.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**(a)** With a neat labelled block diagram, describe the working of a **Reciprocating Screw Injection Molding Machine (IMM)**. Discuss the functions of the *plasticating, injection, and clamping units*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermoplastic part has a projected area of $320 \, \text{cm}^{2}$ and the average cavity pressure developed is $35 \, \text{MPa}$. Estimate the required clamping force (in Tonnes) using a safety factor of $1.1$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block Diagram (text rendering):**

```
   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │     HOPPER          │
   └──────────┬──────────┘
              │ pellets
   ┌──────────▼──────────┐
   │  PLASTICATING UNIT  │ (barrel + reciprocating screw + heaters)
   └──────────┬──────────┘
              │ melt
   ┌──────────▼──────────┐
   │   INJECTION UNIT    │ (hydraulic ram pushes screw forward)
   └──────────┬──────────┘
              │ shot
   ┌──────────▼──────────┐
   │      MOLD           │ (cavity + cooling channels)
   └──────────┬──────────┘
              │ solidified part
   ┌──────────▼──────────┐
   │   CLAMPING UNIT     │ (tie bars + moving platen + toggle or hydraulic)
   └──────────┬──────────┘
              │ ejected part
   ┌──────────▼──────────┐
   │    CONTROLLER       │ (PLC + HMI)
   └─────────────────────┘
```

**Working Description (5 marks for description + 2 marks for diagram):**

1. **Plasticating Unit:** Polymer pellets from the hopper are conveyed forward by the rotating screw. External heaters and internal shear raise the temperature to $T_{melt} + 30^{\circ}\text{C}$. A non-return valve at the screw tip prevents back-flow during injection.
2. **Injection Unit:** After sufficient melt accumulates ahead of the screw (measured by shot-volume sensor), rotation stops. The screw now acts as a plunger; hydraulic pressure (typically $70$ – $200$ MPa) drives it forward, injecting melt into the closed mold through the nozzle and sprue.
3. **Clamping Unit:** The clamping unit (toggle or direct hydraulic) holds the two mold halves together against the cavity pressure developed during injection. Clamp tonnage is selected as $1.1 \, P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$.
4. **Cooling and Ejection:** The part cools in the mold. After the cooling time elapses, the clamp opens, ejector pins push the part out, and the cycle repeats.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $A_{proj} = 320 \, \text{cm}^{2} = 320 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^{2} = 0.0320 \, \text{m}^{2}$
- $P_{cav} = 35 \, \text{MPa} = 35 \times 10^{6} \, \text{N/m}^{2}$
- Safety factor $K = 1.1$

**Step 1 — Separating force:**
$$F_{sep} = P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$$
$$F_{sep} = 35 \times 10^{6} \times 0.0320$$
$$F_{sep} = 1.12 \times 10^{6} \, \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Apply safety factor:**
$$F_{clamp} = 1.1 \times 1.12 \times 10^{6}$$
$$F_{clamp} = 1.232 \times 10^{6} \, \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Tonnes-force:**
$$F_{clamp} = \frac{1.232 \times 10^{6}}{9.81 \times 10^{3}}$$
$$\boxed{F_{clamp} \approx 125.6 \, \text{Tonnes}}$$

*(Award 2 marks for formula, 3 marks for substitution and arithmetic, 2 marks for unit conversion and final answer.)*

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**(a)** Explain with a schematic the working principle of a **Calendaring Machine** used for producing PVC film. Discuss the role of the *fourth (reverse) roll*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-screw extruder has a screw diameter of $90 \, \text{mm}$, flight depth in the metering zone of $4 \, \text{mm}$, helix angle of $17.6^{\circ}$, and operates at $60 \, \text{rpm}$. Calculate the theoretical volumetric output using the metering-zone equation. Comment on whether this is suitable for processing PVC pipe. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Schematic (text rendering):**

```
   TOP ROLL (1)         ────► polymer melt enters
        │
        ▼
   ROLL 2               (small gap = final gauge)
        │
        ▼
   ROLL 3  (offset)     
        │
        ▼
   ROLL 4  (reverse direction)
        │
        ▼
   Take-up roll → wind-up
```

**Working Principle (5 marks):**

1. The compounded and pre-heated PVC dough (B-form compound) is fed between the first two counter-rotating rolls.
2. The polymer melt sticks to the faster roll and is carried through successive nips between the rolls.
3. Each successive nip gap is smaller than the previous one — this *progressive gap reduction* ensures uniform thinning without stressing the polymer in a single step.
4. The **fourth roll** rotates in the *reverse* direction relative to the third roll. Its function is:
   - To control the *stripping* of the film from the third roll.
   - To enable *gauge profiling* and tension adjustment.
   - To permit *embossing* if a textured finish is desired.
5. The film then passes over chill rolls and is wound up.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $D = 90 \, \text{mm} = 0.090 \, \text{m}$
- $h = 4 \, \text{mm} = 0.004 \, \text{m}$
- $\phi = 17.6^{\circ}$
- $N = 60 \, \text{rpm} = 1.0 \, \text{rev/s}$

**Step 1 — Compute trigonometric values.**
$$\sin 17.6^{\circ} \approx 0.3024$$
$$\cos 17.6^{\circ} \approx 0.9532$$
$$\sin\phi \cos\phi \approx 0.3024 \times 0.9532 \approx 0.2883$$

**Step 2 — Apply the metering-zone drag-flow equation.**
$$Q_{vol} = \frac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**
$$Q_{vol} = \frac{(9.8696)(0.090)^{2}(1.0)(0.004)(0.2883)}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Arithmetic step-by-step:**
- $D^{2} = 0.0081 \, \text{m}^{2}$
- $D^{2} N h = 0.0081 \times 1.0 \times 0.004 = 3.24 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ per channel-width factor
- Multiplying by $\pi^{2}$: $9.8696 \times 3.24 \times 10^{-5} = 3.198 \times 10^{-4}$
- Multiplying by $\sin\phi\cos\phi = 0.2883$: gives $9.220 \times 10^{-5}$
- Divide by 2:
$$Q_{vol} \approx 4.61 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to common industrial units:**
$$Q_{vol} = 4.61 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \times 10^{6} \, \text{cm}^{3}/\text{m}^{3} = 46.1 \, \text{cm}^{3}/\text{s}$$
$$\boxed{Q_{vol} \approx 46.1 \, \text{cm}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 166 \, \text{L/hr}}$$

**Step 6 — Suitability comment for PVC pipe:**
This output (~166 L/hr) is **modest**, suitable for *small-diameter thin-walled PVC pipes* (up to ~63 mm OD). For large-diameter thick-walled PVC pressure pipes, the output is insufficient — such applications need either a *larger barrel diameter* ($D \ge 150 \, \text{mm}$) or a *counter-rotating twin-screw extruder*, since PVC is shear- and heat-sensitive and benefits from positive displacement feeding.

*(Award 2 marks for formula, 3 marks for calculation, 2 marks for engineering comment.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Places Students Lose Marks on this Topic:**
> 1. **Mixing units** — writing pressure in MPa without converting to Pa in force formulas. KTU deducts **1 mark** for dimensional inconsistency.
> 2. **Confusing Screw Diameter (D) vs. Flight Depth (h)** — students sometimes substitute the barrel ID for $h$. Mark: **–1**.
> 3. **Forgetting the safety factor (1.1)** in clamping-force calculations.
> 4. **Calling the screw *plunger* in injection molding** — the IMM screw is a *reciprocating screw*, *not* a plunger. This costs 1 mark in part-(a) descriptions.
> 5. **Not labelling axes** in the calendaring or blow-molding schematic — examiners explicitly demand *labelled* diagrams for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] Polymer processing machines are classified by **polymer state**: melt vs. reaction.
- [x] **Eight major families** are in KTU Module 4 syllabus: Compression, Transfer, Injection, Extrusion, Blow, Thermoforming, Rotational, Calendaring.
- [x] Every machine has **four universal blocks**: Feed → Shape → Hold → Control.
- [x] **Reciprocating Screw IMM** is the most versatile machine — dominant in commercial plastic product manufacturing.
- [x] **Clamping force formula:** $F_{clamp} = 1.1 \, P_{cav} \times A_{proj}$ — always convert $A_{proj}$ to **m²** and $P_{cav}$ to **Pa**.
- [x] **Single-screw extruder output:** $Q_{vol} = \tfrac{\pi^{2} D^{2} N h \sin\phi \cos\phi}{2}$ — valid in metering zone.
- [x] **Twin-screw extruders** are *positive displacement* — used for compounding, PVC, and masterbatch.
- [x] **Blow-up ratio** $S_{blow} = D_{mold}/D_{parison}$ is the key design parameter for blow molding.
- [x] **Calendaring** uses **3–4 rolls** with progressive gap reduction; the **fourth roll** runs in reverse for stripping and embossing.
- [x] **Rotational molding** is *low-pressure, large-part, slow-cycle* — used for tanks and kayaks.
- [x] **Thermoforming** reshapes a *pre-extruded sheet* — separate machine from extruder.
- [x] **Compression molding** is dominant for **thermosets** and **SMC/BMC composites**.
- [x] **Transfer molding** = compression + injection hybrid — pot-type vs. plunger-type vs. screw-type.
- [x] Always cross-check **specific energy consumption** (kWh/kg) and **scrap ratio** when justifying machine selection.
- [x] For KTU board exams, **draw block diagrams with labels** — a labelled diagram alone can earn 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moulds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Moulds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Mould in Polymer Processing?

A **mould** (also spelled *mold* in American English) is a precision-engineered, hollow, hardened steel tooling block containing a negative impression of the desired plastic part. It is the heart of any polymer shaping process such as **injection moulding**, **compression moulding**, **transfer moulding**, and **blow moulding**. The mould receives molten or softened polymer, shapes it under pressure, cools (or cures) it, and ejects the finished article.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A mould is a tooling system consisting of a fixed half (cavity plate) and a moving half (core plate) that are clamped together under high tonnage to define the geometry of a polymer product. The mould incorporates feeding systems (sprue, runner, gate), thermal regulation (cooling/heating channels), and an ejection mechanism.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Waffle Iron of Plastics"

Imagine a **waffle iron**. You pour liquid batter in, close the lid, apply heat, wait for the batter to solidify, and then open the iron to pop out a perfectly shaped waffle. A polymer mould works on exactly the same principle, but:
- Instead of batter, you inject **molten plastic** at high pressure (often 700–1400 bar for injection moulding).
- Instead of cooking with direct flame, you actively **circulate temperature-controlled water or oil** through internal channels.
- Instead of a simple hinge, moulds are held in a **clamping unit** delivering **50 to 8000 tonnes** of force to keep the two halves sealed against the internal pressure of the injected polymer.

### 1.3 Classification of Moulds (Master Overview)

Moulds are classified in two principal ways — **by process type** and **by construction type**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification Table**

| S.No | Classification Basis | Type | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **By Process** | Injection Mould | Thermoplastic & thermoset parts |
| 2 | By Process | Compression Mould | Phenolic, melamine, BMC, rubber |
| 3 | By Process | Transfer Mould | Thermosets with inserts |
| 4 | By Process | Blow Mould | Hollow bottles, tanks |
| 5 | By Process | Rotational Mould | Large hollow items (kayaks, tanks) |
| 6 | **By Construction** | Two-Plate Mould | Single-cavity, simple parts |
| 7 | By Construction | Three-Plate Mould | Multi-cavity, automatic degating |
| 8 | By Construction | Hot-Runner Mould | Zero-waste, high-quality parts |
| 9 | By Construction | Family / Multi-Cavity Mould | Mass production |
| 10 | By Construction | Stack Mould | Double output per cycle |
| 11 | By Construction | Insert Mould | Moulded parts with metal inserts |
| 12 | By Construction | Unscrewing Mould | Threaded closures (bottle caps) |

### 1.4 Key Physical / Engineering Constants in Mould Design

- **Steel grade hardness for cavity/core: 58–62 HRC** (P20, H13, S7, 420 SS).
- **Typical injection pressure inside cavity: 700–1400 bar** (≈ 70–140 MPa).
- **Clamping tonnage rule of thumb: 2.5–5 tonnes per cm² of projected area** (≈ 4–8 tons/in²).
- **Cooling water inlet temperature: 25–35 °C**; mould surface target ≈ 40–80 °C.
- **Polished surface roughness for optical lenses: Ra ≤ 0.05 μm**.
- **Standard ejection stroke: 25–150 mm** depending on part depth.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick:** If a question asks for the *typical clamp tonnage required for a part of 250 cm² projected area*, immediately recall the **4–5 tonnes/cm²** rule — that gives 1000–1250 tonnes clamping force, well within a standard 1500-tonne press.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Functional Subsystems of a Modern Injection Mould

Every polymer mould, regardless of complexity, is composed of **eight functional subsystems**. Understanding these is the single most important takeaway for KTU Module 4.

### 2.1.1 The Moulding Cavity (Cavity & Core)
- The **cavity** is the concave female half that forms the outer surface of the part.
- The **core** is the convex male half that forms the inner surface.
- Cavity and core together define the **parting line** — the visible line on the finished part where the two halves meet.
- Made from **P20 (pre-hardened)**, **H13 (hot-work tool steel)**, or **S7 (shock-resistant)**.

### 2.1.2 The Feeding System (Sprue, Runner, Gate)
- **Sprue**: The direct channel from the machine nozzle into the mould. Usually tapered at **1°–2°** per side to allow easy release.
- **Runner**: The horizontal channel that distributes melt from the sprue to each cavity.
- **Gate**: The narrow final restriction that controls melt flow into the cavity.
- **Runner layout types**: 2-plate, 3-plate, hot-runner, and cold-runner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the gate the smallest cross-section?** Because it freezes first after the cavity is filled, sealing the cavity pressure and preventing back-flow — this is the principle of **pressure maintenance** during packing.

### 2.1.3 The Cooling / Heating System
- Bored channels (typically **Ø 8–12 mm**) carry temperature-controlled fluid.
- **Cooling circuit layout patterns**: straight drilled, baffle, bubbler, conformal (copper insert).
- For crystalline polymers, **uniform cooling is critical** to prevent warpage and differential shrinkage.
- The cooling time often dominates the **total cycle time (typically 60–80 %)**.

### 2.1.4 The Ejection System
- **Ejector pins** (the most common), **ejector sleeves** (for cylindrical bosses), **stripper plates** (for thin parts), and **air poppets** (for delicate parts).
- Located on the **moving (core) half** to avoid operator-side interference.
- Standard layout: pins placed on thick sections and bosses; minimum **Ø 1.5 mm** for steel pins.

### 2.1.5 The Parting Line & Venting
- **Parting line** must be placed considering:
  - Aesthetics (hidden edge preferred).
  - Draft (typically **0.5°–2°**).
  - Ejection direction.
- **Vents**: 0.02–0.05 mm deep grooves at the parting line — allow trapped air and gas to escape, preventing **diesel effect** (compressed-air burn marks).

### 2.1.6 The Guide & Alignment System
- **Leader pins and bushings** (4-pin pattern; 2 of unequal diameter to prevent mis-match).
- **Support pillars** to prevent core deflection under injection pressure.
- **Wear plates** on the moving platen side.

### 2.1.7 The Clamping & Support System
- The mould is mounted between the machine platens via a **clamping frame** with **eye-bolts, clamps, and a locating ring**.
- **Locating ring** aligns the sprue bushing with the machine nozzle.

### 2.1.8 The Interchangeable Insert System
- **Mould inserts** allow the cavity/core inserts to be replaced without re-machining the entire mould base.
- Massively reduces maintenance cost and downtime.

## 2.2 Two-Plate vs Three-Plate Mould — The Core Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic — Frequently Asked**

| Feature | Two-Plate Mould | Three-Plate Mould |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of parting lines | 1 | 2 |
| Runner system | Cold runner (one side) | Cold runner between plates 1 & 2 |
| Gate type | Edge / sprue gate | Pin-point (submarine) gate |
| Runner wastage | Yes (manual trim) | Automatic degating |
| Parting line quality | Cleaner | Slight witness at gate |
| Cost | Lower | ~30–50 % higher |
| Cavity layout | Single row | Multi-row possible |
| Best suited for | Large parts, few cavities | Small precision parts, many cavities |

## 2.3 Hot Runner vs Cold Runner System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question**

| Parameter | Cold Runner | Hot Runner |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Runner state at ejection | Solidified, scrap | Molten, recirculated |
| Material wastage | 15–40 % of shot weight | < 1 % |
| Cycle time | Longer (runner cooling) | Shorter |
| Initial mould cost | Lower | 2–3× higher |
| Operating cost | Low | Heating element power + maintenance |
| Part quality | Gate vestige, weld lines | Cleaner gate, fewer weld lines |
| Colour change | Easy (no heated zones) | Difficult (purge required) |
| Best for | Short runs, frequent colour change | Long runs, high volume |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas use SI units unless otherwise stated. Keep this table for quick revision.**

| S.No | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Projected area | $A_p = L \times W$ | $\text{m}^2$ or $\text{cm}^2$ |
| 2 | Clamping force | $F_c = A_p \times P_{inj} \times k$ | k = safety factor (1.1–1.3) |
| 3 | Injection pressure | $P_{inj} = \dfrac{4F_{ram}}{\pi D_{ram}^2}$ | Pa (ram force ÷ ram area) |
| 4 | Cavity pressure | $P_{cav} \approx 0.4 \text{ to } 0.7 \times P_{inj}$ | MPa |
| 5 | Cooling time (thick part, simple) | $t_c = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_{ej} - T_{wall}}\right)$ | s |
| 6 | Number of cavities (economic) | $n = \dfrac{N_{req} \times t_c}{3600 \times \eta}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Heat to be removed per cycle | $Q = m \left[c_p (T_{inj} - T_{ej}) + h_f\right]$ | J (m = shot mass) |
| 8 | Cooling water flow rate | $\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{Q}{c_w (T_{wo} - T_{wi})}$ | kg/s |
| 9 | Volumetric shrinkage compensation | $V_{cav} = V_{part} (1 + S_v)$ | $\text{m}^3$ |
| 10 | Ejector pin diameter (min) | $d_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 F_{eject}}{\pi \sigma_{allow}}}$ | m |
| 11 | Deflection of cantilever core | $\delta = \dfrac{P L^4}{8 E I}$ | m |
| 12 | Gate diameter (round) | $d_g = \sqrt[4]{\dfrac{32 \mu L Q}{\pi \Delta P}}$ | m (Poiseuille flow) |
| 13 | Pressure drop in runner | $\Delta P = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$ | Pa |
| 14 | Total cycle time | $t_{cycle} = t_{inj} + t_{pack} + t_{cool} + t_{ej}$ | s |
| 15 | Mould cost (rough estimate) | $C_{mould} = C_{base} + n \times C_{cav}$ | ₹ / USD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion / Boundary Conditions Used in Mould Design**
> - 1 tonne (clamp) ≈ 10 kN
> - 1 bar = 0.1 MPa
> - Typical moulding shrinkage of PP ≈ **1.5–2.5 %**
> - Typical moulding shrinkage of ABS ≈ **0.4–0.7 %**
> - Typical moulding shrinkage of Nylon 6,6 ≈ **1.5–2.0 %**
> - Thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ for steel ≈ $2.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Mould Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| Automotive | Bumpers, dashboards, headlamp housings (multi-cavity stack moulds) |
| Medical | Syringes, IV components (cleanroom hot-runner moulds) |
| Packaging | Bottle caps, PET preforms (unscrewing + hot-runner moulds) |
| Electronics | Mobile phone housings (high-gloss, conformal cooling) |
| Consumer goods | Toys, kitchenware (two-plate cold-runner) |
| Aerospace | Carbon-fibre reinforced PEEK parts (heated moulds to 200 °C) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Clamping Force Required for a Mould

The clamping force must overcome the **injection cavity pressure** acting on the **projected area** of the part. The derivation proceeds in five explicit steps.

> **Given:**
> - Projected part area $A_p = 250 \text{ cm}^2 = 250 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
> - Cavity pressure $P_{cav} = 50 \text{ MPa} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
> - Safety factor $k = 1.2$

**Step 1: Convert projected area to SI units.**

$$A_p = 250 \text{ cm}^2 \times \left(\frac{1 \text{ m}}{100 \text{ cm}}\right)^2 = 250 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.025 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the opening force on the mould cavity.**

The cavity pressure acts on the projected area pushing the two halves apart. The opening force is therefore:

$$F_{open} = P_{cav} \times A_p$$

Substituting the numbers:

$$F_{open} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \times 0.025 \text{ m}^2 = 1.25 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Convert Newtons to tonnes-force for machine selection.**

Since $1 \text{ tonne-force} = 1000 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 = 9810 \text{ N}$, we get:

$$F_{open} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^6}{9810} \approx 127.4 \text{ tonnes-force}$$

**Step 4: Apply the safety factor (accounting for pressure peaks, machine wear, and operator error).**

$$F_{clamp} = F_{open} \times k = 127.4 \times 1.2 \approx 152.9 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 5: Select the next commercially available machine tonnage (typically rounded up to nearest 50 tonnes).**

$$F_{clamp,\,selected} = 200 \text{ tonnes (machine size)}$$

> **Final Answer: A 200-tonne injection moulding machine must be selected.**

## 3.2 Derivation — Cooling Time (Simplified Analytical Model)

For a plate-like plastic part of thickness $s$ cooling between two mould walls, the one-dimensional transient heat conduction with **step-change boundary conditions** gives the following classic Chato / Engelmann result.

> **Given:**
> - Wall thickness $s = 3 \text{ mm} = 0.003 \text{ m}$
> - Melt temperature $T_m = 230 \text{ °C}$ (Polypropylene)
> - Ejection temperature $T_{ej} = 90 \text{ °C}$ (above PP glass-transition, below melting)
> - Mould wall temperature $T_{wall} = 40 \text{ °C}$
> - Thermal diffusivity of PP $\alpha = 0.9 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

**Step 1: Write the analytical cooling time expression for a slab of finite thickness with both sides cooled.**

$$t_c = \frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_{ej} - T_{wall}}\right)$$

**Step 2: Compute the temperature ratio inside the logarithm.**

First, the numerator (initial driving force):

$$T_m - T_{wall} = 230 - 40 = 190 \text{ °C}$$

Then, the denominator (residual driving force at ejection):

$$T_{ej} - T_{wall} = 90 - 40 = 50 \text{ °C}$$

So the inner ratio is:

$$\frac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_{ej} - T_{wall}} = \frac{190}{50} = 3.8$$

**Step 3: Apply the geometric coefficient.**

$$\frac{8}{\pi^2} \approx \frac{8}{9.8696} \approx 0.8106$$

So the entire argument of the logarithm becomes:

$$0.8106 \times 3.8 \approx 3.080$$

**Step 4: Take the natural logarithm.**

$$\ln(3.080) \approx 1.1247$$

**Step 5: Compute the prefactor $s^2 / (\pi^2 \alpha)$.**

$$s^2 = (0.003)^2 = 9 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

$$\pi^2 \alpha = 9.8696 \times 0.9 \times 10^{-7} = 8.883 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$\frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} = \frac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{8.883 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 10.13 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6: Multiply the two pieces.**

$$t_c = 10.13 \times 1.1247 \approx 11.39 \text{ s}$$

> **Final Answer: Cooling time ≈ 11.4 seconds** — this is the dominant contribution to a typical 14–16 s cycle.

## 3.3 Derivation — Number of Cavities from Production Requirement

> **Given:**
> - Required production $N_{req} = 1{,}00{,}000$ parts/month
> - Cooling time $t_c = 11.4 \text{ s}$
> - Working days = 25/month, 2 shifts of 8 h each ⇒ 400 h/month
> - Machine efficiency $\eta = 0.85$

**Step 1: Convert monthly production to parts/hour target.**

$$N_{hourly} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{400 \times 0.85} \approx 294.1 \text{ parts/hour}$$

**Step 2: Compute cycle time (cooling time dominates).**

$$t_{cycle} \approx t_c = 11.4 \text{ s} \Rightarrow \text{shots/hour} = \frac{3600}{11.4} \approx 315.8$$

**Step 3: Number of cavities per mould (must be an integer ≥ 1).**

$$n = \frac{N_{hourly}}{315.8} = \frac{294.1}{315.8} \approx 0.93$$

Since $n$ must be a positive integer and we need at least the target output, we select:

$$n = 1 \text{ cavity (a single-cavity mould is sufficient)}.$$

> **Conclusion: A 1-cavity mould is acceptable, but a 2-cavity mould gives ~ 65 % spare capacity, useful for machine downtime and quality checks.** KTU answer: design for **n = 1** if cost-optimization is the goal, **n = 2** if flexibility is needed.

## 3.4 Derivation — Ejector Pin Diameter (Strength Check)

> **Given:**
> - Required ejection force per pin $F_{eject} = 200 \text{ N}$ (typical for a small PP part)
> - Allowable stress for ejector pin (hardened steel, HRC 60) $\sigma_{allow} = 1500 \text{ MPa}$
> - Safety factor $n_s = 3$

**Step 1: Derive the pin cross-section from the axial load formula.**

The pin carries pure compression under the ejection force:

$$\sigma = \frac{F_{eject}}{A_p} \leq \frac{\sigma_{allow}}{n_s}$$

$$A_p \geq \frac{F_{eject} \times n_s}{\sigma_{allow}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$A_p \geq \frac{200 \times 3}{1500 \times 10^6} = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2 = 0.4 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Convert to pin diameter.**

$$d_p = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_p}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.4}{\pi}} = \sqrt{0.5093} \approx 0.714 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Apply the standard minimum pin diameter (manufacturing constraint).**

Because ejector pins smaller than **1.5 mm** are fragile, bend easily, and frequently break during ejection of glass-filled or semi-crystalline parts, the **practical selection** is:

$$d_p = 1.5 \text{ mm (manufacturing standard)}$$

> **Final Answer: Use a 1.5 mm ejector pin.** The strength calculation is satisfied, but the manufacturing constraint governs.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Gate Type Selection

| Gate Type | Best Material | Part Geometry | Cycle Effect | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sprue gate | General thermoplastics | Cylindrical, thick | No wastage at gate | Manual trim needed |
| Edge (side) gate | General thermoplastics | Flat parts | Cold-runner | Simple, low cost |
| Pin-point (submarine) | Engineering plastics | Small precision parts | Auto-degated | Standard for 3-plate |
| Diaphragm gate | Optical lenses | Circular thin parts | Concentric gate | Eliminates weld lines |
| Tunnel (banana) gate | PP, PE | Box-shaped | Buried under surface | Auto-degated |
| Fan gate | Sheets, panels | Flat, large area | Weld-line control | Wide thin gate |
| Ring gate | Cylindrical bushings | Hollow parts | Uniform fill | Concentric |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Two-Plate Injection Mould

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start([Molten polymer from machine nozzle]) --> sprue[Sprue Bushing]
    sprue --> runner[Cold Runner Channel]
    runner --> gate1[Gate 1]
    runner --> gate2[Gate 2]
    runner --> gate3[Gate 3]
    gate1 --> cavA[Cavity A - Part 1]
    gate2 --> cavB[Cavity B - Part 2]
    gate3 --> cavC[Cavity C - Part 3]
    cavA --> coolA[Cooling Channel A]
    cavB --> coolB[Cooling Channel B]
    cavC --> coolC[Cooling Channel C]
    coolA --> ejA[Ejector Pins A]
    coolB --> ejB[Ejector Pins B]
    coolC --> ejC[Ejector Pins C]
    ejA --> partLine[Parting Line]
    ejB --> partLine
    ejC --> partLine
    partLine --> mouldOpen[Mould Opens - Moving Half Retracts]
    mouldOpen --> eject[Ejection Stroke]
    eject --> partOut[Finished Part Released]
    partOut --> runnerWaste[Runner Scrap - Recycle]
    runnerWaste --> start
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The diagram shows how melt enters through the sprue, splits at the runner into three gates, fills three identical cavities, gets cooled by three independent cooling channels, and is then ejected through pins on the moving half. The runner solidifies and is ejected as scrap for recycling.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Three-Plate Mould Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nozzle[Machine Nozzle] --> sprueA[Sprue - Plate 1 to Plate 2]
    sprueA --> runnerA[Runner - Between Plate 2 and Plate 3]
    runnerA --> gateP1[Pin Point Gate 1]
    runnerA --> gateP2[Pin Point Gate 2]
    gateP1 --> cav1[Cavity 1 in Plate 3]
    gateP2 --> cav2[Cavity 2 in Plate 3]
    cav1 --> coolR1[Cooling Circuit 1]
    cav2 --> coolR2[Cooling Circuit 2]
    coolR1 --> ejP1[Auto Ejection at Plate Separation]
    coolR2 --> ejP2[Auto Ejection at Plate Separation]
    ejP1 --> p1[Part 1 - Gate Free]
    ejP2 --> p2[Part 2 - Gate Free]
    ejP1 -.-> scrap[Runner Plate 1 - Plate 2 drop]
    ejP2 -.-> scrap
```

> **Reading the diagram:** In a three-plate mould, the second parting line occurs between Plate 2 and Plate 3. The runner and sprue separate from the parts automatically at this second parting line, so no manual degating is required. This is essential for small, high-precision parts in 16-, 32- or 64-cavity configurations.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hot-Runner System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    meltIn[Polymer Melt from Nozzle] --> manifold[Heated Manifold Block]
    manifold --> nozzleTip1[Heated Nozzle Tip 1]
    manifold --> nozzleTip2[Heated Nozzle Tip 2]
    manifold --> nozzleTip3[Heated Nozzle Tip 3]
    manifold --> nozzleTip4[Heated Nozzle Tip 4]
    nozzleTip1 --> gateH1[Hot Gate 1]
    nozzleTip2 --> gateH2[Hot Gate 2]
    nozzleTip3 --> gateH3[Hot Gate 3]
    nozzleTip4 --> gateH4[Hot Gate 4]
    gateH1 --> cavH1[Cavity 1]
    gateH2 --> cavH2[Cavity 2]
    gateH3 --> cavH3[Cavity 3]
    gateH4 --> cavH4[Cavity 4]
    cavH1 --> coolH1[Conformal Cooling 1]
    cavH2 --> coolH2[Conformal Cooling 2]
    cavH3 --> coolH3[Conformal Cooling 3]
    cavH4 --> coolH4[Conformal Cooling 4]
    coolH1 --> partH1[Part 1 - Zero Waste]
    coolH2 --> partH2[Part 2 - Zero Waste]
    coolH3 --> partH3[Part 3 - Zero Waste]
    coolH4 --> partH4[Part 4 - Zero Waste]
    manifold -.Heater Bands.-> heatCtrl[PID Temperature Controller]
    heatCtrl -.Feedback.-> tc1[Thermocouple 1]
    heatCtrl -.Feedback.-> tc2[Thermocouple 2]
    heatCtrl -.Feedback.-> tc3[Thermocouple 3]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Unlike cold-runner systems, the hot-runner manifold and nozzle tips are maintained at melt temperature (typically 220–280 °C) by heater bands and PID controllers with thermocouple feedback. The polymer never freezes in the runner, eliminating runner scrap. The mould is closed-loop thermally regulated, so colour change requires careful purging.

## 4.4 Schematic — Heat Flow Path Through Mould Wall

```
   +-------------------+      ← Plastic Part (T_part)
   |  Plastic Part     |
   +-------------------+
   |  Mould Cavity     |      ← Mould Wall (k = 29 W/m·K for steel)
   |  Surface          |
   +-------------------+
   |  Cooling Channel  |      ← Water at T_wi
   |  (8-12 mm dia)    |
   +-------------------+
   |  Mould Body       |
   +-------------------+
```

> **Engineering interpretation:** Heat generated by the hot polymer flows by conduction through the steel mould wall and is then convected away by the cooling water. The heat flux $q$ (W/m²) is given by $q = U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ where $U$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient. This is the foundation of cooling system design.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Mould Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Part Specification] --> mat[Material Type]
    mat --> absM[Amorphous]
    mat --> semiM[Semi-crystalline]
    mat --> thermoset[Thermoset]
    absM --> tolCheck[Tight Tolerance]
    semiM --> tolCheck
    thermoset --> comp[Compression / Transfer Mould]
    tolCheck --> highT[High Tolerance]
    tolCheck --> lowT[Loose Tolerance]
    highT --> hotR[Hot Runner Mould]
    lowT --> coldR[Cold Runner Mould]
    hotR --> threeP[3-Plate for Small Parts]
    hotR --> twoP[2-Plate for Larger Parts]
    coldR --> twoP
    threeP --> multCav[Multi Cavity Layout]
    twoP --> multCav
    multCav --> family[Family Mould for Variety]
    multCav --> stackC[Stack Mould for High Output]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This decision tree guides a mould designer from the part specification through material, tolerance, and quantity decisions, ending in a specific mould architecture. KTU exam questions often present a scenario and expect the student to walk through this logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define a mould. List the eight functional subsystems of an injection mould.**

**Model Answer:**

A mould is a precision tooling device used in polymer processing that shapes molten or softened plastic into a finished part by means of a cavity and core held together under high tonnage.

The eight functional subsystems are:
1. **Moulding cavity & core** (defines part geometry)
2. **Feeding system** (sprue, runner, gate)
3. **Cooling / heating system** (water channels, heaters)
4. **Ejection system** (pins, sleeves, stripper plates)
5. **Parting line & venting** (separates halves, releases gases)
6. **Guide & alignment system** (leader pins, bushings)
7. **Clamping & support frame** (locating ring, clamps)
8. **Interchangeable insert system** (replaceable cavity/core inserts)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between a two-plate and a three-plate mould with two points each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Two-Plate Mould | Three-Plate Mould |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Parting lines | Single parting line | Two parting lines |
| Gate type | Edge gate or sprue gate | Pin-point (submarine) gate |
| Runner system | One cold runner on one side | Runner between the two middle plates |
| Degating | Manual trimming required | Automatic degating at second parting |
| Cost | Lower initial cost | ~30–50 % higher due to extra plate |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **Mould Design Calculations**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

> An injection moulding machine is to be selected for producing a flat rectangular polypropylene tray of dimensions **200 mm × 150 mm × 3 mm** (length × width × thickness).
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **clamping force** required if the cavity pressure is **50 MPa** and the safety factor is **1.25**.
> **(b)** Calculate the **cooling time** for the part. Take: melt temperature = 230 °C, ejection temperature = 95 °C, mould wall temperature = 40 °C, thermal diffusivity of PP = 0.9 × 10⁻⁷ m²/s.
> **(c)** State **two design considerations** for placement of the **parting line** for this tray.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Clamping Force Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute projected area of the part.**

$$A_p = 200 \text{ mm} \times 150 \text{ mm} = 30{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 = 300 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.03 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute opening force (cavity pressure × projected area).**

$$F_{open} = P_{cav} \times A_p = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \times 0.03 \text{ m}^2 = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Convert to tonnes-force.**

$$F_{open} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{9810} \approx 152.9 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 4: Apply safety factor.**

$$F_{clamp} = 152.9 \times 1.25 = 191.1 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 5: Select next standard machine tonnage.**

$$\boxed{F_{clamp,\,selected} = 200 \text{ tonnes (next standard size)}}$$

**(b) Cooling Time Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Use the one-dimensional slab cooling formula:**

$$t_c = \frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{T_m - T_{wall}}{T_{ej} - T_{wall}}\right)$$

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values.**

- $s = 0.003 \text{ m}$, so $s^2 = 9 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$
- $\alpha = 0.9 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$
- $T_m - T_{wall} = 230 - 40 = 190 \text{ °C}$
- $T_{ej} - T_{wall} = 95 - 40 = 55 \text{ °C}$

**Step 3: Compute the prefactor.**

$$\frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} = \frac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{9.8696 \times 0.9 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{8.883 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 10.13 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Compute the log argument.**

$$\frac{8}{\pi^2} \times \frac{190}{55} = 0.8106 \times 3.4545 = 2.799$$

**Step 5: Take the logarithm and multiply.**

$$\ln(2.799) \approx 1.029$$

$$t_c = 10.13 \times 1.029 \approx 10.42 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t_c \approx 10.4 \text{ seconds}}$$

**(c) Parting Line Design Considerations (2 marks embedded in part b):**

1. **Aesthetic placement:** Parting line should be placed on the **longest edge** of the tray, hidden on the bottom surface to avoid visible flash.
2. **Draft angle:** Both cavity and core surfaces must incorporate a **draft angle of 1°–2°** perpendicular to the parting line to allow easy ejection.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Mould Subsystems and Hot vs Cold Runner**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Describe the **ejection system** in an injection mould with a neat labelled sketch. List **four types of ejectors** used.
> **(b)** Compare the **hot-runner and cold-runner moulding systems** with **six points**. Justify which system is preferred for a **medical syringe barrel** requiring annual production of 50 million parts.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ejection System Description (7 marks):**

The ejection system is located on the **moving (core) half** of the mould and is actuated by the machine's ejector plate (hydraulic or mechanical knock-out). After the mould opens, the ejector plate pushes the solidified part out of the core.

**Components:**
- **Ejector plate** (retainer plate and backing plate)
- **Return pins** (to retract the ejector plate when mould closes)
- **Ejector pins** (push the part off the core)
- **Guide bushings** (for smooth motion)

**Four types of ejectors:**

1. **Ejector pin** (straight cylindrical, most common)
2. **Ejector sleeve** (for cylindrical bosses and threaded cores)
3. **Stripper plate** (for thin, flat parts to avoid pin marks)
4. **Air poppet / valve ejector** (for delicate parts with no visible witness)

```
   +-------------------+
   |   Backing Plate   |
   +-------------------+
   |  Retainer Plate   |
   +-------------------+
   |   Ejector Plate   |  ←  (carries pins)
   +-------------------+
   |    Parting Line   |
   +-------------------+
   |   Cavity (Fixed)  |
   +-------------------+
   |  Mould Base (Top) |
   +-------------------+

   +-------------------+
   | Mould Base (Btm)  |
   +-------------------+
   |    Core Plate     |
   +-------------------+
   |  Cavity / Cavities|
   +-------------------+
   |  Ejector Pins  ↕  |  ←  (push the part up)
   +-------------------+
   |  Ejector Plate    |
   +-------------------+
   |  Retainer Plate   |
   +-------------------+
   |  Backing Plate    |
   +-------------------+
```

**(b) Hot vs Cold Runner Comparison (7 marks):**

| S.No | Parameter | Hot Runner | Cold Runner |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Runner state at ejection | Molten (kept hot) | Solidified (becomes scrap) |
| 2 | Material wastage | < 1 % | 15–40 % of shot weight |
| 3 | Cycle time | Shorter (no runner cooling) | Longer (runner must solidify) |
| 4 | Initial mould cost | 2–3× higher (manifold + heater controls) | Lower |
| 5 | Part quality | Cleaner gate, fewer weld lines | Gate vestige, possible weld lines |
| 6 | Colour / material change | Difficult (purge required) | Easy |

**Justification for Medical Syringe Barrel:**

A medical syringe barrel requires:
- **50 million parts/year** — extremely high volume, justifying the higher initial cost.
- **Zero material contamination** — hot runner eliminates runner regrind, which is critical for medical-grade PP.
- **Dimensional consistency** — hot runner provides stable shot weight and fewer weld lines.
- **Cycle time reduction** — saves hours of production per week.

$$\boxed{\text{Hot runner mould is strongly preferred for the medical syringe barrel.}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Students frequently forget to convert cm² → m² before applying the pressure-area formula. Always write down the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing thermal and mould temperatures:** Confusing the melt temperature ($T_m$) with the mould wall temperature ($T_{wall}$) inside the cooling-time formula is the #1 mark-deduction reason.
> 3. **Forgetting the safety factor in clamping force:** Always multiply by k ≥ 1.1 to account for pressure peaks.
> 4. **Confusing hot-runner and cold-runner definitions:** A hot runner is a runner that is **maintained above the polymer's melting/solidification point** so it stays molten — students often say "hot runner" without explaining *why* it is hot.
> 5. **Not drawing sketches:** Part A and Part B both benefit from a labelled block diagram — without it, 1–2 marks are lost even if the description is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module 4 — Moulds Revision Sheet**

- A **mould** is a precision tooling with a **cavity and core** held under clamping force.
- The **eight subsystems** are: cavity/core, feeding system, cooling system, ejection system, parting & venting, guides, clamping frame, inserts.
- **Clamping force formula:** $F_c = P_{cav} \times A_p \times k$. Always use SI units.
- **Cooling time** for a slab: $t_c = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_m - T_w}{T_{ej} - T_w}\right)$ — this **dominates cycle time**.
- **Two-plate mould:** single parting, edge/sprue gate, manual degating.
- **Three-plate mould:** two partings, pin-point gate, automatic degating.
- **Hot runner** = molten runner, zero waste, high initial cost.
- **Cold runner** = solid runner, 15–40 % scrap, low cost.
- **Parting line** = mould meeting line; place for aesthetics + draft + ejection.
- **Vents** = 0.02–0.05 mm deep grooves to release trapped air and prevent diesel effect.
- **Draft angle** = 0.5°–2° to allow ejection without damage.
- **Ejector pin** standard min diameter = **1.5 mm** (manufacturing constraint).
- **Cavity pressure ≈ 0.4–0.7 × injection pressure** (e.g., 50 MPa cavity from 100 MPa machine).
- **Mould material grades:** P20 (general), H13 (high pressure), S7 (shock), 420 SS (corrosion).
- **Rule of thumb:** 4–5 tonnes of clamp tonnage per cm² of projected area.
- **Number of cavities:** economic calculation balances machine capacity, cooling time, and demand.
- **Ejector system on moving half** — to keep operator side clean and avoid safety hazards.
- **Cooling channel diameter:** 8–12 mm typical; water inlet 25–35 °C.
- **Moulding shrinkage compensation:** cavity volume = part volume × (1 + S_v).
- **Family mould:** multiple part geometries in one mould — for variety, not volume.
- **Stack mould:** two parting lines in one mould — **doubles output per cycle**.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Aid — "8 C's of a Mould":**
> 1. **C**avity
> 2. **C**ore
> 3. **C**ooling channels
> 4. **C**lamp frame
> 5. **C**ompensation (shrinkage)
> 6. **C**onnection (sprue/runner/gate)
> 7. **C**onstraint (guides, leader pins)
> 8. **C**leanout (ejection & venting)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Materials

# Module 4 — Materials for Polymer Processing Machinery, Molds, and Dies

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> In the context of **PCPOT503 – Polymer Machinery & Product Manufacturing**, *Materials* refers to the **engineering metals, alloys, and surface treatments** selected for the construction of polymer processing equipment (extruders, injection molding machines, blow molders) and the toolings (molds, dies, screws, barrels) that come into direct contact with molten polymers. Selection is governed by **mechanical strength, wear resistance, thermal conductivity, corrosion resistance, machinability, polishability, and cost**.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Analogy"

Imagine a **commercial kitchen** where hot chocolate is made every day:
- The **cooking pot** that holds the hot liquid must withstand heat, scraping, and chemical attack from cocoa → this is exactly like the **barrel of an extruder or injection molding machine**.
- The **whisk/agitator** that mixes and shears the liquid must be hard, wear-resistant, and not contaminate the food → this is exactly like the **screw and check-ring of an injection unit**.
- The **mold into which chocolate is poured** must release the solid cleanly, conduct heat away rapidly, and survive thousands of cycles → this is exactly like a **polymer injection mold**.

Just as a chef would never use a soft aluminum pot for daily cooking, a polymer engineer cannot use mild steel for a screw that must survive **millions of cycles** in a polymer melt. Hence, **material selection is the silent hero** that determines tool life, part quality, and production economics.

### 1.2 Why Materials Engineering is Critical in Polymer Processing

> [!NOTE]
> **The Polymer Processing Environment is Brutal:**
> - **Temperature:** $200$ – $400\ ^\circ\text{C}$ (steady-state melt contact).
> - **Pressure:** $35$ – $140\ \text{MPa}$ (injection cylinder).
> - **Shear Rate:** $10^2$ – $10^4\ \text{s}^{-1}$ (viscous drag on metal surfaces).
> - **Chemistry:** Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) liberates $\text{HCl}$; polyamides release moisture; polyolefins carry abrasive fillers.
> - **Cyclic Loading:** Thermal fatigue + mechanical fatigue simultaneously.

A single material cannot optimize all of these — therefore, **engineered combinations** (e.g., nitrided screw inside a bimetallic barrel) are standard practice in industry.

### 1.3 Classification of Materials (Top-Level Overview)

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups machinery materials into the following families:

1. **Constructional / Structural Steels** — for machine frames, platens, tie-bars, clamping units.
2. **Barrel Materials** — plain carbon steel, nitrided steel, bimetallic (centrifugally cast).
3. **Screw Materials** — nitrided alloy steel, tool steel, powder-metallurgy (PM) grades.
4. **Tool Steels for Molds & Dies** — pre-hardened (P20, P21), through-hardening (H13, S7, S136, 420 SS).
5. **Non-Ferrous Mold Materials** — beryllium copper ($\text{CuBe}$), aluminum alloys, zinc alloys.
6. **Surface Treatments & Coatings** — nitriding, hard chrome, TiN, PVD, CVD, DLC, electroless nickel.
7. **Specialty Materials** — ceramics, tungsten carbide, superalloys (Inconel).

![Hierarchy of Materials Used in Polymer Processing Equipment](attachment://placeholder)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Engineering Requirements of Every Polymer Tool Material

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A material used for a mold, die, screw, or barrel must satisfy **five simultaneous requirements**:

1. **Mechanical Strength** — to resist clamping force, injection pressure, and bending.
2. **Wear Resistance** — to survive millions of cycles against abrasive polymer and glass/carbon fibers.
3. **Thermal Stability & Conductivity** — to maintain dimensional accuracy and remove heat efficiently.
4. **Corrosion Resistance** — to resist acidic by-products (e.g., $\text{HCl}$ from PVC) and cooling water.
5. **Manufacturability** — machinability, polishability, weldability, and EDM-ability.

If any one of these is compromised, the tool fails prematurely.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Material Selection Logic

- **Step 1 — Identify the dominant failure mode:**
   * *Abrasive wear* → choose high-hardness tool steel with carbide reinforcement.
   * *Thermal fatigue / heat-checking* → choose hot-work tool steel (H13) with high thermal conductivity.
   * *Corrosion* → choose stainless tool steel (S136, 420) or apply hard chrome.
   * *Rapid prototyping / short runs* → choose aluminum or beryllium copper for fast heat removal.
- **Step 2 — Match the production volume:**
   * $<10{,}000$ parts → aluminum, $\text{Zn}$ alloy, epoxy.
   * $10{,}000$ – $1{,}000{,}000$ parts → pre-hardened P20.
   * $>1{,}000{,}000$ parts → through-hardening H13 / S136.
- **Step 3 — Account for the polymer chemistry:**
   * PVC / flame-retardant grades → stainless or nitrided + coated.
   * Glass-filled polymers → fully hardened + nitrided screw.
- **Step 4 — Account for surface finish requirements:**
   * Optical / lens-grade parts → ESR-re-melted S136 polished to mirror finish.
- **Step 5 — Account for cost and lead-time:**
   * Pre-hardened steels skip the heat-treatment step → faster delivery.

### 2.3 Key Material Properties — The "Big Five"

| Property | Symbol | Why it Matters in Polymer Tooling | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hardness (Rockwell) | $\text{HRC}$ | Wear resistance of cavity and screw | $28$ – $62\ \text{HRC}$ |
| Thermal Conductivity | $k$ | Heat removal rate from cavity | $20$ – $230\ \text{W/m·K}$ |
| Coefficient of Thermal Expansion | $\alpha$ | Dimensional change with temperature | $(8$ – $24) \times 10^{-6}\ /\ ^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Tensile Strength | $\sigma_u$ | Resistance to cavity deflection | $500$ – $2000\ \text{MPa}$ |
| Corrosion Resistance | — | Survival against PVC by-products | qualitative |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **the most frequently tested formulas** in Module 4 — Materials. Memorize them as a complete set.

$$
q = -k \, A \, \frac{dT}{dx}
$$

> **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction** (1-D steady state). Used to size cooling channels in molds.

$$
\dot{Q} = \frac{2 \pi k L \, (T_{\text{core}} - T_{\text{wall}})}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_o}{r_i}\right)}
$$

> **Radial heat flow through a cylindrical cooling channel.** $r_i$ and $r_o$ are inner and outer radii, $L$ is the channel length.

$$
\Delta L = \alpha \, L_0 \, \Delta T
$$

> **Thermal expansion of mold dimension** — directly affects part tolerance.

$$
\bar{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{\pi D N}{h} - \frac{v_\text{ext}}{h}
$$

> **Average shear rate in extruder/injection screw channel** (Newtonian approximation). The **screw** is selected to withstand the resulting viscous drag.

$$
P_\text{visc} = \mu \, \bar{\dot{\gamma}}^2 \, V
$$

> **Viscous dissipation power in the screw channel.** $V$ is the channel volume, $\mu$ is polymer melt viscosity. Screw material must not soften under this heating load.

$$
t_\text{cool} = \frac{s^2}{\pi^2 \, \alpha_\text{diff}}}
\qquad \text{where} \qquad
\alpha_\text{diff}} = \frac{k}{\rho \, c_p}
$$

> **Characteristic cooling time** in a mold cavity of half-thickness $s$, with $\alpha_\text{diff}$ being the polymer's thermal diffusivity. Drives the choice of **high-$k$ mold material** (e.g., $\text{CuBe}$) for short cycles.

$$
\text{Hardness conversion: } \text{HRC} \approx \frac{\text{HB}}{10}
$$

> **Approximate Brinell-to-Rockwell-C conversion** (valid in mid-range). Used in viva questions comparing different steel grades.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> - In **PET preform molding**, mold steel with $k \geq 30\ \text{W/m·K}$ is mandatory to achieve cycle times below $6$ seconds.
> - In **PVC extrusion**, screws are made of **nitrided 38CrMoAl** (AISI 4140 modified) to resist $\text{HCl}$ attack; barrels are **bimetallic** (iron-boride particles in a nickel matrix).
> - In **micro-molding** of medical parts, **powder-metallurgy tool steel** with carbide homogeneity is used to avoid pitting at sub-mm features.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Cooling Channel Heat Extraction

**Problem Statement:**
A polypropylene (PP) mold cavity surface must be maintained at $T_s = 40\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Cooling water at $T_w = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$ flows through a single straight cooling channel of **diameter** $D = 10\ \text{mm}$, placed at a distance of $20\ \text{mm}$ from the cavity surface. The channel length is $L = 200\ \text{mm}$. The mold steel is H13 with thermal conductivity $k = 30\ \text{W/m·K}$. Calculate the steady-state heat extraction rate $\dot{Q}$.

**Given:**
- Cavity surface temperature: $T_s = 40\ ^\circ\text{C} = 313\ \text{K}$
- Coolant bulk temperature: $T_w = 25\ ^\circ\text{C} = 298\ \text{K}$
- Channel inner radius: $r_i = D/2 = 5\ \text{mm} = 0.005\ \text{m}$
- Outer radius (taken as half-channel-spacing): $r_o \approx 2 \cdot r_i = 10\ \text{mm} = 0.010\ \text{m}$
- Length: $L = 0.200\ \text{m}$
- Thermal conductivity: $k = 30\ \text{W/m·K}$

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**
For radial conduction from a cavity into a cylindrical cooling channel, we use:

$$
\dot{Q} = \frac{2 \pi k L \, (T_s - T_w)}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\dot{Q} = \frac{2 \pi \cdot (30) \cdot (0.200) \cdot (313 - 298)}{\ln(0.010 / 0.005)}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the temperature difference.**

$$
\Delta T = 313 - 298 = 15\ \text{K}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the natural-logarithm term.**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{0.010}{0.005}\right) = \ln(2) = 0.6931
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify the numerator.**

$$
2 \pi \cdot 30 \cdot 0.200 \cdot 15 = 2 \pi \cdot 90 = 180 \pi = 565.49
$$

**Step 6 — Divide to obtain the heat flow.**

$$
\dot{Q} = \frac{565.49}{0.6931} \approx 815.9\ \text{W}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q} \approx 816\ \text{W}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A single $10\ \text{mm}$ channel extracts almost **1 kW** of heat — this is why mold designers use **multiple channels, high-conductivity beryllium-copper inserts (with $k \approx 230\ \text{W/m·K}$)** at hot spots, and turbulent coolant flow to boost convection. With $\text{CuBe}$ in place of H13, $\dot{Q}$ rises to $6{,}260\ \text{W}$ — a **$7.7\times$ improvement**, cutting cycle time dramatically.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Thermal Expansion of a Mold Cavity

**Problem Statement:**
A $300\ \text{mm}$-long P20 steel mold cavity (current dimension measured at $25\ ^\circ\text{C}$) is operated at a steady surface temperature of $180\ ^\circ\text{C}$. P20 has a coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha = 11.5 \times 10^{-6}\ /\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Find the dimensional change and the resulting oversized part dimension.

**Given:**
- $L_0 = 300\ \text{mm}$
- $\alpha = 11.5 \times 10^{-6}\ /\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- $\Delta T = 180 - 25 = 155\ ^\circ\text{C}$

**Step 1 — Governing equation.**

$$
\Delta L = \alpha \, L_0 \, \Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
\Delta L = (11.5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (300) \cdot (155)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
\Delta L = 11.5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 46{,}500 = 0.5348\ \text{mm}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta L \approx 0.535\ \text{mm}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is huge!** A $0.5\ \text{mm}$ expansion over a $300\ \text{mm}$ cavity is unacceptable for a part with a $\pm 0.05\ \text{mm}$ tolerance. KTU question-framing trick: students often forget to subtract room-temperature reference and lose 2 marks. Always report **both** the **expanded dimension** ($300.535\ \text{mm}$) and the **change** ($0.535\ \text{mm}$).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Material Choices

| Material | $\text{HRC}$ (typical) | $k$ $(\text{W/m·K})$ | $\alpha \times 10^{-6}\ /\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | Corrosion Resistance | Cost Index | KTU 2024 Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel (AISI 1020) | $<20$ | $52$ | $11.6$ | Low | $1\times$ | Machine frames, platens |
| P20 (pre-hardened) | $30$ – $36$ | $34$ | $11.5$ | Low | $3\times$ | General-purpose injection mold |
| H13 (hot-work) | $48$ – $54$ | $30$ | $11.0$ | Medium | $5\times$ | High-pressure molds, die-casting dies |
| S136 / 420 SS | $48$ – $52$ | $25$ | $10.5$ | **High** | $8\times$ | PVC, medical, optical parts |
| Beryllium Copper ($\text{CuBe}$) | $35$ – $42$ | $230$ | $17.0$ | Medium | $25\times$ | Inserts, hot-spot cooling |
| Aluminum 7075 | $15$ – $18$ | $130$ | $23.0$ | Medium | $4\times$ | Prototype / short-run molds |
| 38CrMoAl (nitrided screw) | $60$ – $65$ (surface) | $40$ | $12.0$ | Medium | $6\times$ | Extruder / injection screws |
| Tungsten Carbide | $85$ – $92$ (HRA) | $84$ | $5.5$ | High | $50\times$ | Wear inserts, mixer tips |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the matrix:**
> - For **high wear + corrosion**, choose **S136**.
> - For **high thermal cycling**, choose **H13**.
> - For **rapid prototyping / quick turnover**, choose **aluminum**.
> - For **localized hot spots**, use **$\text{CuBe}$ inserts** inside a P20 or H13 mold body.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Material Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Polymer & Part Specification] --> B[Identify Dominant Failure Mode]
    B --> C{Abrasive Wear?}
    B --> D{Thermal Fatigue?}
    B --> E{Corrosion Attack?}
    B --> F{Short Run / Prototype?}
    C -->|Yes| G[Select Hard Tool Steel H13 / S136]
    D -->|Yes| H[Select Hot-Work Steel H13 / S7]
    E -->|Yes| I[Select Stainless S136 / 420]
    F -->|Yes| J[Select Aluminum 7075 / CuBe]
    G --> K{Production Volume}
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K -->|< 10,000| L[Soft Tool: Al / Zn / Epoxy]
    K -->|10k - 1M| M[Pre-Hardened P20]
    K -->|> 1M| N[Through-Hardened H13 / S136]
    L --> O[Apply Surface Treatment]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Final Validation: Polish / EDM / Trial]
    P --> Q[Approved Material for Production]
```

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Materials Used in Polymer Processing Equipment

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Materials for Polymer Processing] --> nodeB[Constructional Steels]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Barrel Materials]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Screw Materials]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Mold and Die Materials]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Surface Treatments]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Frame Platens Tie-bars]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Mild Steel AISI 1020]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Plain Carbon Steel]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Nitrided Steel]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Bimetallic Centrifugally Cast]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[38CrMoAl Nitrided]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Tool Steel H13]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[PM Tool Steel]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Pre-hardened P20]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Through-hardening H13]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Stainless S136 / 420]
    nodeE --> nodeE4[Non-ferrous CuBe Al7075]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Nitriding]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Hard Chrome Plating]
    nodeF --> nodeF3[PVD TiN CrN Coating]
    nodeF --> nodeF4[Electroless Nickel]
```

### 4.3 Function-Level Block Diagram — Heat Path in an Injection Mold

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Hot Melt in Cavity] --> M2[Mold Steel Wall]
    M2 --> M3[Coolant Channel Wall]
    M3 --> M4[Cooling Water Flow]
    M4 --> M5[Chiller / Heat Exchanger]
    M5 -.->|Recirculation| M4
    M1 -.->|Conduction + Convection| M2
    M2 -.->|Conduction| M3
    M3 -.->|Convection| M4
    M4 -.->|Convection| M5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **rate-limiting step** is almost always the **mold steel conductivity** ($k$) and the **convective heat-transfer coefficient** $h$ on the water side.
