Designers use **high-$k$ CuBe inserts** to bypass the low $k$ of standard tool steel at hot spots, and **turbulent flow ($\text{Re} > 10{,}000$)** to maximize $h$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`**
**List any three pre-hardened tool steels used in polymer injection molds and state one reason why pre-hardened steels are preferred for medium-volume production.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribute as 1 + 1 + 1):**

> [!NOTE]
> - **Three examples (1 mark):** P20 (AISI P20, $\sim 30$–$36\ \text{HRC}$), P21 (pre-hardened Ni–Al–Cu alloy steel), and DIN 1.2738 (modified P20 with higher toughness).
> - **Reason (1 mark):** Pre-hardened steels are supplied at the working hardness directly from the steel mill, **eliminating the post-machining heat-treatment step** — this **prevents distortion and cracking** of complex cavity shapes.
> - **Conclusion (1 mark):** They offer the best balance of machinability, polishability, and tool life for production volumes of $10^4$ – $10^6$ parts, which represents the bulk of commercial polymer parts.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`**
**Why is beryllium copper ($\text{CuBe}$) used as an insert material in injection molds, even though it is more expensive than tool steel?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribute as 1 + 1 + 1):**

> [!NOTE]
> - **Property advantage (1 mark):** Beryllium copper has a thermal conductivity $k \approx 230\ \text{W/m·K}$, which is **roughly $7$ – $8$ times higher** than P20 or H13 tool steel.
> - **Functional advantage (1 mark):** When placed at **hot spots** (thin sections, near gates, or high-aspect-ratio cores), CuBe inserts **remove heat $7$ – $8\times$ faster** than the surrounding steel, **eliminating weld lines, sink marks, and warpage**.
> - **Cycle-time advantage (1 mark):** The improved heat removal shortens the cooling time $t_\text{cool}$ (per $\alpha_\text{diff}}$ relation), giving **shorter cycle times** and higher productivity, which more than offsets the material cost over a high-volume production run.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

---

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model QP]`**

**(a)** Compare the properties of **P20, H13, and S136** tool steels for injection mold applications. Discuss the role of each alloying element (C, Cr, Mo, V, Ni) in determining the final properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-cavity injection mold for a polypropylene (PP) container has a **rectangular cavity of length $250\ \text{mm}$ and width $100\ \text{mm}$**. The cavity surface is maintained at $40\ ^\circ\text{C}$. A cooling channel of **$8\ \text{mm}$ diameter** is placed at a depth of $18\ \text{mm}$ below the cavity surface. Water at $25\ ^\circ\text{C}$ flows at a sufficient rate to give a bulk water temperature equal to its inlet temperature. The mold is made of H13 steel, $k = 30\ \text{W/m·K}$. Estimate the **heat extraction rate per unit length** of the channel and comment on whether a beryllium-copper insert would significantly improve the cooling. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (a) — 7 marks**

**Allocation:**
- Tabular comparison → **3 marks**
- Alloying-element role → **3 marks**
- Selection conclusion → **1 mark**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Property | P20 | H13 | S136 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Pre-hardened cold-work | Hot-work tool steel | Stainless mold steel |
| $\text{HRC}$ (working) | $30$ – $36$ | $48$ – $54$ | $48$ – $52$ |
| $k\ (\text{W/m·K})$ | $34$ | $30$ | $25$ |
| $\alpha \times 10^{-6}$ | $11.5$ | $11.0$ | $10.5$ |
| Corrosion | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Polishability | Very good | Good | **Mirror-grade** |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Use | General-purpose | High-pressure hot-runner | Optical, medical, PVC |

**Role of Alloying Elements (3 marks):**
- **Carbon ($\sim 0.35$ – $0.45\%$)** — forms carbides with Cr, Mo, V; provides **hardness and wear resistance**. *(1 mark)*
- **Chromium ($\sim 5$ – $13\%$)** — forms hard **Cr-carbides (M$_7$C$_3$, M$_{23}$C$_6$)**; provides **wear resistance, hardenability, and (in S136) corrosion resistance** through passivation. *(1 mark)*
- **Molybdenum ($\sim 1$ – $2\%$)** — refines the grain, suppresses **temper embrittlement**, improves **hot-strength and thermal fatigue resistance** — critical in H13. *(0.5 mark)*
- **Vanadium ($\sim 0.3$ – $1\%$)** — forms very hard **V-carbides (VC)** that resist grain growth, improving **toughness and wear life**. *(0.5 mark)*

**Selection Conclusion (1 mark):**
- **General purpose** → **P20** (lowest cost, fastest delivery).
- **High-pressure / hot-runner / high-cycle** → **H13** (best toughness + thermal fatigue).
- **Corrosive polymer / optical / medical** → **S136** (only grade that can be mirror-polished and survive $\text{HCl}$ from PVC).

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Cavity temperature: $T_s = 40\ ^\circ\text{C} = 313\ \text{K}$
- Water temperature: $T_w = 25\ ^\circ\text{C} = 298\ \text{K}$
- $\Delta T = 15\ \text{K}$
- Channel inner radius: $r_i = 4\ \text{mm} = 0.004\ \text{m}$
- Distance from cavity to channel center: $18\ \text{mm}$, so outer radius $r_o = 18\ \text{mm} = 0.018\ \text{m}$
- $k_\text{steel} = 30\ \text{W/m·K}$
- $k_\text{CuBe} = 230\ \text{W/m·K}$

**Step 1 — Governing equation:**

$$
\frac{\dot{Q}}{L} = \frac{2 \pi k \, (T_s - T_w)}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the natural-log term.**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{0.018}{0.004}\right) = \ln(4.5) = 1.5041
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute for H13 steel.**

$$
\frac{\dot{Q}_\text{steel}}{L} = \frac{2 \pi \cdot 30 \cdot 15}{1.5041} = \frac{2827.4}{1.5041} \approx 1880\ \text{W/m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the channel length of one perimeter route.**
Assuming a perimeter channel around the cavity: $L = 2(250 + 100) = 700\ \text{mm} = 0.700\ \text{m}$.

$$
\dot{Q}_\text{steel} = 1880 \cdot 0.700 \approx 1316\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 5 — Repeat the calculation for a CuBe insert at the same location.**

$$
\frac{\dot{Q}_\text{CuBe}}{L} = \frac{2 \pi \cdot 230 \cdot 15}{1.5041} = \frac{21{,}677}{1.5041} \approx 14{,}413\ \text{W/m}
$$

**Step 6 — Total for CuBe insert over $0.700\ \text{m}$.**

$$
\dot{Q}_\text{CuBe} = 14{,}413 \cdot 0.700 \approx 10{,}089\ \text{W}
$$

**Step 7 — Improvement factor.**

$$
\frac{\dot{Q}_\text{CuBe}}{\dot{Q}_\text{steel}} = \frac{230}{30} = 7.67\times
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion (1 mark):** A beryllium-copper insert improves the heat extraction by a factor of **$7.7\times$**, which would dramatically shorten cycle time for the PP container. The improvement is recommended only if the **cycle-time savings (in rupees/hour) exceed the insert cost** — i.e., for high-volume production (typically $> 200{,}000$ parts/year).

---

### **Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`**

**(a)** Explain the **construction of a bimetallic barrel** used in plasticating extruders. Discuss the centrifugal casting process, the role of alloying elements in the lining alloy, and the typical base-steel composition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An injection-molding screw of **diameter $D = 50\ \text{mm}$** rotates at $N = 120\ \text{rpm}$ in a $h = 4\ \text{mm}$ deep channel. The polymer melt has a viscosity $\mu = 500\ \text{Pa·s}$. The screw length is $L = 1.2\ \text{m}$ and the helix angle is small. Compute the **average shear rate** and the **viscous power dissipation** in the screw channel. Comment on whether screw-material selection (nitrided 38CrMoAl vs. PM tool steel) is a more significant factor than viscosity reduction. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a) — 7 marks**

**Allocation:**
- Process description → **3 marks**
- Lining alloy composition → **2 marks**
- Base steel composition → **1 mark**
- Advantages → **1 mark**

**Bimetallic Barrel Construction (3 marks):**
A bimetallic barrel is produced by **centrifugal casting**: a horizontal cylindrical steel mold is spun at high speed, and a **molten alloy lining** (typically nickel-based or iron-based with carbide particles) is poured in. Centrifugal force pushes the denser alloy to the outer wall, producing a **uniform $1.5$ – $3\ \text{mm}$ thick lining metallurgically bonded** to a pre-placed outer **carbon-steel sleeve** (the shrink-fit backing). After cooling, the bore is honed and polished. *(3 marks)*

**Lining Alloy Composition (2 marks):**
- **Nickel matrix** with **iron-boride / chromium-carbide / tungsten-carbide particles**.
- Typical: $50$ – $60\%\ \text{Ni}$, $15$ – $20\%\ \text{Cr}$, $5$ – $10\%\ \text{B}$, $4$ – $8\%\ \text{Si}$, balance Fe and C.
- The **borides ($\text{FeB}$, $\text{CrB}$)** and **carbides** give surface hardness up to **$60$ – $65\ \text{HRC}$**. *(2 marks)*

**Base Steel (1 mark):**
- Mild / medium-carbon steel ($\sim 0.20$ – $0.30\%\ \text{C}$), cast or forged, providing the **mechanical strength and pressure containment**. *(1 mark)*

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Combines **wear-resistant lining** with **tough, low-cost backing**.
- Service life of **$10^6$ – $10^7\ \text{kg}$ of polymer processed**, $5$ – $10\times$ longer than nitrided barrels.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $D = 0.050\ \text{m}$, $N = 120\ \text{rpm} = 2.0\ \text{rev/s}$, $h = 0.004\ \text{m}$
- $\mu = 500\ \text{Pa·s}$, $L = 1.2\ \text{m}$
- Helix angle small $\Rightarrow$ $v_\text{ext} \approx 0$

**Step 1 — Screw surface (linear) speed.**

$$
v_\text{ext} \approx \pi D N = \pi \cdot 0.050 \cdot 2.0 = 0.314\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Average shear rate (Newtonian, shallow channel).**

$$
\bar{\dot{\gamma}} = \frac{\pi D N}{h} = \frac{0.314}{0.004} \approx 78.5\ \text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Screw channel volume** (approximated as a thin cylindrical shell of thickness $h$ and length $L$):

$$
V = \pi D h L = \pi \cdot 0.050 \cdot 0.004 \cdot 1.2 = 7.54 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Viscous dissipation power.**

$$
P_\text{visc} = \mu \, \bar{\dot{\gamma}}^2 \, V = 500 \cdot (78.5)^2 \cdot 7.54 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate.**

$$
P_\text{visc} = 500 \cdot 6162 \cdot 7.54 \times 10^{-4} \approx 500 \cdot 4.65 \approx 2323\ \text{W}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\bar{\dot{\gamma}} \approx 78.5\ \text{s}^{-1}, \qquad P_\text{visc} \approx 2.32\ \text{kW}}
$$

**Step 6 — Discussion (1 mark):**
- $\sim 2.3\ \text{kW}$ is dissipated as heat per screw — raising melt temperature along the channel.
- **Material selection** (nitrided 38CrMoAl, $\text{HRC} \geq 60$ at the surface) matters because **the screw must not soften or gall** at this shear rate and temperature.
- A **PM tool steel** (e.g., ASP 2060) with finer and harder carbide distribution can be used for **glass-fiber-reinforced polymers** where abrasive wear dominates.
- **Viscosity reduction** (via higher temperature or better slip additives) reduces the dissipation in proportion to $\mu$, but it **does not eliminate the wear mechanism**. Hence, **material selection is the more fundamental design lever** in aggressive-processing applications.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where Students Lose Marks in Module 4 — Materials**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in Fourier's law — it is **not** a sign-error but a **physical meaning** (heat flows from hot to cold). Use the **absolute value** when computing magnitudes.
> 2. **Confusing $k$ (thermal conductivity) with $\alpha$ (thermal diffusivity or CTE).** $k$ has units $\text{W/m·K}$; $\alpha$ is dimensionless only when referring to CTE (otherwise $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$). Examiners *test* this.
> 3. **Using $D$ instead of $r$ in the cylindrical conduction equation.** Half the heat-conduction problems in KTU are radial — a single $D$ vs. $r$ slip loses **3 marks**.
> 4. **Omitting the room-temperature reference** in thermal-expansion problems (must subtract $25\ ^\circ\text{C}$ or $293\ \text{K}$). Costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Stating "stainless steel" without specifying the grade.** Always say **S136, AISI 420, or 17-4 PH**. KTU examiners treat generic "stainless steel" as **incomplete**.
> 6. **Confusing "HRC" with "HV" or "HB".** Conversion formulas (e.g., $\text{HRC} \approx \text{HB}/10$) are tested.
> 7. **Forgetting units** in final answers — a $1.316\ \text{kW}$ answer with no unit is treated as incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Module 4 — Materials: One-Page Summary**
> - **Five engineering requirements** of any polymer tool material: **Strength, Wear, Thermal, Corrosion, Manufacturability** (Mnemonic: **S-W-T-C-M**).
> - **Constructional steel:** Mild steel AISI 1020 — for frames, platens, tie-bars.
> - **Screw materials:** Nitrided 38CrMoAl (surface HRC 60+), PM tool steel for filled polymers, H13 for high-pressure.
> - **Barrel materials:** Plain carbon steel, nitrided steel, bimetallic (centrifugally cast Ni-Fe-B lining) for abrasive and corrosive polymers.
> - **Mold steels:** P20 (general), H13 (hot-runner, high-pressure), S136 / 420 (corrosive, optical, medical).
> - **Non-ferrous inserts:** Beryllium copper ($\text{CuBe}$) for hot-spot cooling ($k \approx 230\ \text{W/m·K}$); aluminum 7075 for prototype / short-run molds.
> - **Surface treatments:** Nitriding (gas/salt/plasma), hard chrome, PVD TiN/CrN, DLC, electroless nickel — all improve wear and release.
> - **Key formulas to memorize (verbatim):**
>   * $q = -k A \, dT / dx$
>   * $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{2 \pi k L (T_s - T_w)}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}$
>   * $\Delta L = \alpha L_0 \, \Delta T$
>   * $\bar{\dot{\gamma}} = \pi D N / h$
>   * $P_\text{visc} = \mu \, \bar{\dot{\gamma}}^2 \, V$
> - **Critical numerical values:** H13 $k = 30\ \text{W/m·K}$; CuBe $k = 230\ \text{W/m·K}$; $\alpha$ for tool steel $\approx 11 \times 10^{-6}\ /\ ^\circ\text{C}$.
> - **Selection rule of thumb:** Volume $< 10^4 \Rightarrow$ Al/Zn; $10^4$ – $10^6 \Rightarrow$ P20; $>10^6 \Rightarrow$ H13/S136.
> - **Why CuBe?:** $7$ – $8\times$ faster cooling at hot spots; use only when **cycle-time savings > insert cost**.
> - **Why bimetallic barrel?:** Carbide/boride lining on tough backing — life of $10^6$ – $10^7\ \text{kg}$ of processed polymer.
> - **Exam traps:** Generic "stainless steel" loses marks; missing units loses marks; $D$ vs. $r$ in radial conduction loses 3 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Part Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Part Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Part Design** in polymer processing refers to the **systematic engineering discipline** of designing plastic/molded components such that they can be manufactured economically, function reliably, and be assembled efficiently — while satisfying structural, aesthetic, and dimensional requirements dictated by the chosen polymer processing method (injection molding, blow molding, extrusion, thermoforming, rotational molding, etc.).

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPOT503 – Module 4)** syllabus, Part Design encompasses:

- **Geometric design rules** for molded parts (wall thickness, ribs, bosses, draft, radii).
- **Process-specific design considerations** for injection molding, blow molding, thermoforming, and rotational molding.
- **Shrinkage allowances and dimensional tolerance** stack-up analysis.
- **Design for Manufacturability (DFM)** and **Design for Assembly (DFA)**.
- **Gating, weld-line, and knit-line** predictions from the part design perspective.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Clay Pot & Mould" Metaphor**
> Imagine you are a potter shaping wet clay over a wooden mould. If your clay wall is **thick on one side and thin on the other**, the thick side dries slower → it warps and cracks. If your clay has **undercuts** (a shape that locks into the mould), you can never separate the clay from the mould without breaking it. If your walls have **no taper (draft)**, friction grips the clay and tears it. If you have **sharp internal corners**, stress concentrates and the pot cracks under the slightest thermal shock.
>
> **Polymer part design follows the EXACT same logic** — except the "clay" is molten/softened thermoplastic at **180 °C–320 °C**, the "mould" is hardened tool-steel at **~25 °C–80 °C**, and the cycle time is measured in **seconds**, not days. The "drying" happens during **cooling & solidification**, governed by **Crystallinity (Xc)** and **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)**.

A well-designed plastic part is one that:
1. Fills uniformly (no short shots).
2. Cools uniformly (no warpage).
3. Ejects cleanly (no drag marks).
4. Performs its function at end-use conditions (mechanical + thermal + chemical).
5. Costs the least to produce, assemble, and inspect.

## 1.3 Critical Physical & Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wall thickness | $t$ | **0.5 mm – 6 mm** (injection) | Affects cycle time, shrinkage, sink marks |
| Draft angle | $\theta$ | **0.5° – 2°** (typical) | Ejection without scoring |
| Corner radius | $R$ | **0.5t – 0.75t** | Stress distribution, melt flow |
| Shrinkage allowance | $S$ | **0.2 % – 3.0 %** | Mould cavity sizing |
| Dimensional tolerance | $T$ | **±0.05 mm – ±0.5 mm** | Based on ISO 20457 / DIN 16901 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 4 explicitly covers *part-design rules specific to injection molding, blow molding, thermoforming, and rotational molding* — the notes below treat each as a sub-topic for exam-focused clarity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Design Rules (Common to All Molding Processes)

### 2.1.1 Uniform Wall Thickness
- **Why:** Non-uniform walls → differential cooling rates → differential volumetric shrinkage → **residual stresses, warpage, sink marks, voids**.
- **Rule of thumb:** Variation in wall thickness should not exceed **±10 %** of nominal $t$.
- **Engineering principle:** The thicker section acts as a **heat reservoir** (thermal mass), cooling slower and shrinking more, pulling material from adjacent thinner areas.

### 2.1.2 Draft Angle ($\theta$)
- **Why:** During ejection, the plastic part shrinks onto the core. A taper is required to overcome **frictional adhesion** and **vacuum effect**.
- **Empirical formula (per side):**

$$
\theta = \text{Draft per mm depth} \times \text{Depth of cavity}
$$

- **Standards (KTU reference):** Textured surfaces require an **additional 1° per 0.025 mm** of texture depth.

### 2.1.3 Fillet and Radius ($R$)
- **Why:** Sharp corners → **stress concentration factor $K_t \to \infty$** in theory, finite but very high in practice → crack initiation.
- **Rule:** Internal radius $R_i = 0.5\,t$ to $0.75\,t$; external radius $R_o = 1.5\,R_i$.

### 2.1.4 Ribs (Stiffening Ribs)
- **Purpose:** Increase section modulus $I$ (second moment of area) without increasing bulk wall thickness.
- **Rule:** Rib thickness $t_r = 0.5\,t$ to $0.75\,t$ at the base.
- **Height:** $H_r \le 3\,t$ to avoid sink marks on the opposite wall.

### 2.1.5 Bosses
- **Purpose:** Locating features for screws, fasteners, mating parts.
- **Rule:** Boss outer diameter $D_{bo} \le 2.5\,D_{screw}$; boss root radius $\ge 0.25\,t$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in KTU Part-B questions almost every year.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{mould} = L_{part}(1 + S)$ | Cavity dimension from part dimension + shrinkage | Mould cavity machining |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{\rho_m - \rho_p}{\rho_m}$ | Volumetric shrinkage definition | Material data lookup |
| 3 | $\Delta L = L_p \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Linear thermal expansion (post-mould ejection) | Tolerance stack-up |
| 4 | $C_{tol} = \sqrt{C_{tool}^2 + C_{proc}^2}$ | Combined tolerance (RSS method) | Quality planning |
| 5 | $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{R}}$ | Stress concentration factor (sharp notch) | Failure analysis |
| 6 | $I_{ribbed} = I_{wall} + n \cdot I_{rib}$ | Effective stiffness from ribs | Stiffness design |
| 7 | $t_{cool} \propto t^2$ | Cooling time scales with square of wall thickness | Cycle-time estimation |
| 8 | $\theta_{min} = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\mu \cdot \Delta r}{h}\right)$ | Minimum draft from friction & shrink-on | Ejection analysis |
| 9 | $X_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta H_{f,100\%}}$ | Crystallinity (DSC method) | Shrinkage prediction |
| 10 | $P_{blow} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t}{D}$ | Hoop-stress based parison inflation pressure | Blow molding design |

> **Notation Safeguard:** All absolute-value / modulus notations in the table use $\vert$ or explicit symbols to avoid markdown-table breakage.

## 2.3 Why These Equations Matter (Engineering Utility)

- **Equations 1–3** → directly used in mould drawing, costing, and **PPAP (Production Part Approval Process)** documents supplied to KTU industry projects.
- **Equation 5** → governs **fatigue-life** prediction of living hinges and snap-fits.
- **Equation 7** → the **single most cited rule** in moulding — doubling wall thickness **quadruples** cooling time, directly impacting **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)**.
- **Equation 10** → critical for designing **blow-molded bottles & tanks** subjected to internal pressure.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shrinkage Allowance & Cavity Dimension

**Problem statement:** A polypropylene (PP) part has a nominal length $L_p = 200\,\text{mm}$ at room temperature. The volumetric mould shrinkage for PP is $S = 1.8\%$. Determine the corresponding **mould cavity length** $L_m$ at **mould temperature** (i.e., the dimension the mould maker must machine on the tool).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define shrinkage.**
Volumetric shrinkage $S$ is defined as the relative reduction in volume from the molten state inside the cavity to the solidified state at room temperature.

$$
S = \dfrac{V_{cavity} - V_{part}}{V_{cavity}}
$$

For isotropic, low-shrinkage plastics, volumetric shrinkage is **approximately 3× the linear shrinkage** for engineering hand-calculations. However, mould-drawing practice uses the **linear shrinkage** $S_L$ directly:

$$
S_L \approx \dfrac{S}{3} \quad \text{(rule of thumb for semi-crystalline polymers)}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the linear shrinkage relationship.**

$$
S_L = \dfrac{L_m - L_p}{L_m}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $L_m$ (cavity dimension).**

$$
L_m - L_p = S_L \cdot L_m
$$

$$
L_m(1 - S_L) = L_p
$$

$$
\boxed{L_m = \dfrac{L_p}{1 - S_L}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**
Given $L_p = 200\,\text{mm}$ and $S = 1.8\,\% \Rightarrow S_L \approx 0.018 / 3 = 0.006$.

$$
L_m = \dfrac{200}{1 - 0.006} = \dfrac{200}{0.994}
$$

$$
L_m \approx 201.207\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification (alternate closed-form).**
Using $L_m \approx L_p(1 + S_L)$ (valid for small $S_L$):

$$
L_m \approx 200 \times (1 + 0.006) = 201.200\,\text{mm}
$$

Both results agree to within **0.007 mm** (negligible machining tolerance).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** KTU students often forget to **divide volumetric shrinkage by 3** when applying to linear dimensions. The mould cavity is always **LARGER** than the final part dimension.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Draft Angle from Friction Model

**Problem statement:** A cylindrical core has diameter $D_c = 50\,\text{mm}$, depth $h = 80\,\text{mm}$, and the part is **PS (Polystyrene)** with linear shrinkage $S_L = 0.4\%$. The friction coefficient between PS and polished tool-steel is $\mu = 0.25$. Determine the **minimum draft angle** $\theta_{min}$ required for clean ejection.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometric interference (shrink-on).**
The part shrinks onto the core. The radial interference (shrink-fit) is:

$$
\Delta r = \dfrac{S_L \cdot D_c}{2} = \dfrac{0.004 \times 50}{2} = 0.100\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Force balance for ejection.**
For the part to slide axially without rotation locking, the **axial component** of the normal force at the tapered wall must exceed the **frictional resistance**.

Considering an elemental strip of the core wall at height $y$ (from the open end), with local radius $r(y) = r_0 + y \tan\theta$, the normal pressure $p$ is uniform (shrink-fit) and the frictional force per unit area is $\mu p$. Summing axial components across the full depth $h$:

$$
N \sin\theta \ge \mu N \cos\theta
$$

where $N$ is the total normal force on the tapered surface.

**Step 3 — Solve for the limiting angle.**

$$
\tan\theta_{min} = \mu
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$
\theta_{min} = \tan^{-1}(0.25) = 14.04°
$$

**Step 5 — Refine for shrink-on interference.**
A more rigorous model accounts for the **lateral stiffness** of the part. The corrected KTU-recommended relation is:

$$
\boxed{\theta_{min} = \tan^{-1}\left(\mu + \dfrac{\Delta r}{h}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(0.25 + \dfrac{0.100}{80}\right)}
$$

$$
\theta_{min} = \tan^{-1}(0.25 + 0.00125) = \tan^{-1}(0.25125) \approx 14.10°
$$

**Step 6 — Practical recommendation.**
Standard mould-drawing practice rounds up to the next half-degree: $\theta_{min} = 14.5°$. For **deep-cored, high-shrinkage polymers (PP, PA66)**, designers use **1°–2°** for production safety.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cooling Time (Moulding Cycle Predictor)

**Problem statement:** Estimate the cooling time $t_{cool}$ for a PP flat plaque of thickness $t = 3\,\text{mm}$, mould surface temperature $T_w = 30°\text{C}$, and ejection temperature $T_e = 90°\text{C}$. Melt temperature $T_m = 230°\text{C}$. Thermal diffusivity $\alpha_p = 9.0 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the 1-D transient heat-conduction (plane sheet) solution.**

For a plate of half-thickness $x = t/2$, the dimensionless temperature $\theta^*$ at ejection is:

$$
\theta^* = \dfrac{T_e - T_w}{T_m - T_w} = \dfrac{90 - 30}{230 - 30} = \dfrac{60}{200} = 0.30
$$

**Step 2 — Use the Fourier-number solution for an infinite slab.**

$$
\theta^* = \dfrac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\dfrac{(-1)^n}{2n+1}\exp\left[-\dfrac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 Fo}{4}\right]
$$

where Fourier number $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha_p \cdot t_{cool}}{x^2} = \dfrac{4\alpha_p\, t_{cool}}{t^2}$.

**Step 3 — Solve iteratively for $Fo$.**
For $\theta^* = 0.30$, the first-term approximation gives:

$$
0.30 \approx \dfrac{4}{\pi}\exp\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 Fo}{4}\right)
$$

$$
\exp\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 Fo}{4}\right) \approx \dfrac{0.30 \times \pi}{4} = 0.2356
$$

$$
-\dfrac{\pi^2 Fo}{4} = \ln(0.2356) = -1.445
$$

$$
Fo = \dfrac{4 \times 1.445}{\pi^2} = \dfrac{5.780}{9.870} \approx 0.586
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for cooling time.**

$$
t_{cool} = \dfrac{Fo \cdot t^2}{4\,\alpha_p} = \dfrac{0.586 \times (0.003)^2}{4 \times 9.0 \times 10^{-8}}
$$

$$
t_{cool} = \dfrac{0.586 \times 9.0 \times 10^{-6}}{3.6 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{5.274 \times 10^{-6}}{3.6 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{cool} \approx 14.65\,\text{s}}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering approximation formula.**

The **KTU-recommended shortcut** (valid when $T_w$ is approximately the mean of $T_m$ and $T_e$):

$$
t_{cool} \approx \dfrac{t^2}{\pi^2 \alpha_p}\ln\left[\dfrac{4(T_m - T_w)}{\pi(T_e - T_w)}\right]
$$

$$
t_{cool} \approx \dfrac{(0.003)^2}{\pi^2 \times 9.0 \times 10^{-8}}\ln\left[\dfrac{4 \times 200}{\pi \times 60}\right]
$$

$$
t_{cool} \approx \dfrac{9.0 \times 10^{-6}}{8.88 \times 10^{-7}}\ln(4.244) = 10.14 \times 1.445 \approx 14.65\,\text{s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The quadratic scaling $t_{cool} \propto t^2$ is the most-cited rule in moulding. **Doubling wall thickness quadruples cooling time** — drive your design philosophy toward **minimum uniform thickness**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process-Specific Design Rules

| Design Feature | Injection Molding | Blow Molding | Thermoforming | Rotational Molding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Typical $t$ (mm)** | 0.5 – 6 | 0.5 – 6 (parison) | 0.2 – 6 (sheet) | 2 – 25 |
| **Draft angle $\theta$** | 0.5° – 2° | 0° – 1° (split mould) | 2° – 5° (male) | 1° – 3° |
| **Corner radius $R$** | 0.5t – 0.75t | 1.0t – 1.5t | ≥ 1.5t | ≥ 2.0t |
| **Shrinkage $S$** | 0.2 % – 3 % | 0.1 % – 0.8 % | 0.1 % – 0.5 % | 1.5 % – 4 % |
| **Critical defect** | Weld lines, sink | Parison sag | Webbing, thinning | Pinholes, voids |
| **Rib ratio $t_r / t$** | 0.5 – 0.75 | Not applicable | Limited | Not applicable |
| **Undercuts** | Side-actions / lifters | Split moulds | Drape only | Easily accommodated |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Part Design Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Product Requirement Specification PRS] --> B{Process Selection}
    B -->|High volume, complex| C1[Injection Molding]
    B -->|Hollow parts| C2[Blow Molding]
    B -->|Large thin panels| C3[Thermoforming]
    B -->|Large hollow, low volume| C4[Rotational Molding]

    C1 --> D1[Define Wall Thickness t]
    C2 --> D2[Define Parison Die Gap]
    C3 --> D3[Define Sheet Thickness]
    C4 --> D4[Define Mold Charge Mass]

    D1 --> E1{Uniform Thickness?}
    D2 --> E2{Parison Programming?}
    D3 --> E3{Mold Material Male or Female?}
    D4 --> E4{Mold Rotational Symmetry?}

    E1 -->|Yes| F1[Apply Draft theta 0.5 to 2 deg]
    E1 -->|No| F1a[Redesign: add coring or ribs]

    E2 -->|Yes| F2[Apply Split Mould Draft]
    E3 -->|Male| F3[Use positive draft 2 to 5 deg]
    E3 -->|Female| F3a[Use negative draft allowance]
    E4 -->|Yes| F4[Single axis rotation]

    F1 --> G1[Add Fillets R 0.5t to 0.75t]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1

    G1 --> H1[Add Ribs t_r 0.5t to 0.75t]
    H1 --> I1[Add Bosses for Fasteners]
    I1 --> J1[Calculate Shrinkage Allowance]
    J1 --> K1[Define Mould Cavity Dimension Lm]
    K1 --> L1[Verify DFM and DFA Checklist]
    L1 --> M1[Issue Mould Drawing for Fabrication]
```

flowchart LR
    A[Wall Thickness t mm] --> B[Heat Path x = t/2]

    C --> D{t cool requirement}
    D -->|t 1 mm| E1[Approx 1.6 seconds]
    D -->|t 2 mm| E2[Approx 6.5 seconds]
    D -->|t 3 mm| E3[Approx 14.6 seconds]
    D -->|t 4 mm| E4[Approx 26.0 seconds]
    D -->|t 6 mm| E5[Approx 58.5 seconds]
    E1 --> F[Cycle Time Impact]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G[Production Cost per Part]
    G --> H[Design Rule: Keep t Minimum and Uniform]
```

flowchart TD
    A[Part Cross Section Schematic] --> B[Nominal Wall t]
    A --> C[Rib t_r = 0.6 t]
    A --> D[Boss D_bo = 2.0 D_screw]
    A --> E[Internal Fillet R_i = 0.5 t]
    A --> F[External Fillet R_o = 1.5 R_i]
    A --> G[Draft Angle theta on Core Side]

    B --> K[Heat Removal Symmetry Check]

    D --> K

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define the term "draft angle" in plastic part design. Why is it necessary, and what is its typical range for injection-molded parts?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Draft angle** is the small taper provided on the walls of a moulded plastic part, parallel to the direction of mould opening, to facilitate easy ejection of the part from the mould cavity or core. **(1 mark)**

> During cooling, the plastic part **shrinks onto the core**, creating a mechanical lock. Without a draft angle, the frictional and vacuum forces would make ejection impossible without damaging the part surface. **(1 mark)**

> The typical range is **0.5° to 2°** per side for smooth surfaces; for textured surfaces, an additional **1° per 0.025 mm** of texture depth is added. **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**State the rule for rib design in injection-molded thermoplastic parts. Why is the rib thickness kept lower than the nominal wall thickness?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **rib thickness** $t_r$ at its base should be **0.5t to 0.75t** where $t$ is the nominal wall thickness. Rib height should not exceed **3t** to avoid sink marks. **(1.5 marks)**

> Rib thickness is kept lower than the wall thickness to (i) prevent **sink marks** on the opposite (cosmetic) wall caused by local thickening, (ii) allow uniform cooling, and (iii) avoid warpage from differential shrinkage. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students **lose 2–3 marks** by quoting shrinkage values without specifying whether they are **volumetric or linear** — always state both.
> - Drawing the **rib boss intersection** incorrectly costs heavily. The boss must **merge smoothly** with the rib via a **generous fillet (R ≥ 0.25t)**.
> - Forgetting the **ejection-side draft direction** in sketches → 2-mark penalty. Draft is **always measured from the mould-open direction**.
> - Numerical problems on cooling time: forgetting to **square the wall thickness** in the $t_{cool} \propto t^2$ rule → full-mark loss.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the various **design guidelines for injection-molded plastic parts** with neat sketches. Cover the rules for: (i) uniform wall thickness, (ii) draft angle, (iii) ribs and bosses, (iv) radii and fillets, and (v) gate location. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **acrylonitrile butadiene styrene (ABS)** part has a nominal length of **150 mm**. The linear mould-shrinkage for ABS is **0.5 %**. The part is moulded in a single-cavity mould at a melt temperature of **240 °C** and ejected at **80 °C**. The mould-wall temperature is **60 °C**. If the coefficient of linear thermal expansion (CLTE) of ABS in the mould-open direction is **$8.0 \times 10^{-5}\,/\text{K}$**, calculate:
1. The required cavity dimension. **(2 marks)**
2. The dimensional change in the part between ejection and end-use at **25 °C**. **(2 marks)**
3. The **final part length at 25 °C**. **(1 mark)**
4. State **two reasons** why ABS shrinks less than polypropylene (PP). **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Q-A):**

**(a) Injection-Molded Plastic Part Design Guidelines (7 marks):**

| # | Design Rule | Sketch Description | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Uniform wall thickness** | Show part with arrows pointing to equal $t$ at all locations | Avoids differential cooling, warpage, sink marks |
| 2 | **Draft angle 0.5° – 2°** | Tapered wall from mould-open direction | Eases ejection, prevents scoring & vacuum lock |
| 3 | **Ribs $t_r = 0.5t$–$0.75t$, $H_r \le 3t$** | Cross-section: thin rib attached to wall | Increases stiffness without sink marks |
| 4 | **Bosses $D_{bo} \le 2.5\,D_{screw}$** | Cylindrical boss with internal hole | Self-tapping screw retention |
| 5 | **Fillet $R_i = 0.5t$–$0.75t$** | Rounded internal corner | Reduces $K_t$ from $\infty$ to finite value |
| 6 | **Gate at thickest section** | Runner ending at boss/rib junction | Minimises weld-line visibility, sink marks |

**Award: 1 mark per guideline (0.5 for rule, 0.5 for reason/safety).**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Required cavity dimension** (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution):

$$
L_m = \dfrac{L_p}{1 - S_L} = \dfrac{150}{1 - 0.005} = \dfrac{150}{0.995}
$$

$$
\boxed{L_m = 150.754\,\text{mm}}
$$

**Step 2 — Dimensional change from ejection to 25 °C** (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for answer):

$$
\Delta L = L_p \cdot \alpha \cdot (T_{eject} - T_{use}) = 150 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-5} \times (80 - 25)
$$

$$
\Delta L = 150 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-5} \times 55 = 0.660\,\text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Final part length at 25 °C** (1 mark):

$$
L_{final} = L_p - \Delta L = 150 - 0.660 = \boxed{149.340\,\text{mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Why ABS shrinks less than PP (2 marks):**

1. **ABS is amorphous** (no crystallization peak), whereas PP is **semi-crystalline** (crystallinity $X_c \approx 50\%$). Crystallization causes **additional densification shrinkage** beyond the thermal contraction. **(1 mark)**
2. **ABS has a higher glass-transition temperature $T_g \approx 105°\text{C}$** and lower free-volume relaxation in the moulded state, leading to a denser, less-shrinkage-prone morphology than PP. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Compare the **part-design rules for injection molding, blow molding, thermoforming, and rotational molding** in a **tabular format** covering wall thickness, draft angle, shrinkage, and typical defects. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **high-density polyethylene (HDPE)** container of nominal capacity **5 litres** is blow-moulded. The parison die has an outer diameter $D_o = 25\,\text{mm}$ and inner diameter $D_i = 22\,\text{mm}$. The final blown container has an average wall thickness of $t = 1.2\,\text{mm}$ and a maximum diameter of $D_{max} = 180\,\text{mm}$ at the equator. If the parison is extruded at length $L_p = 280\,\text{mm}$ and the final container height is $H = 240\,\text{mm}$, calculate:
1. The **axial stretch ratio**. **(2 marks)**
2. The **hoop (circumferential) stretch ratio**. **(2 marks)**
3. The **blow pressure** required if the hoop tensile strength of HDPE at the blowing temperature is $\sigma_h = 12\,\text{MPa}$. **(2 marks)**
4. **State one design reason** why blow-moulded parts are limited in **internal-undercut complexity** compared to injection-moulded parts. **(1 mark)**

---

**Model Solution (Q-B):**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):** Use the **Analytical Matrix from Section 3.4** as the answer (already derived above). Award **0.5 mark per correct cell × 16 cells = 8 → cap at 7 marks** by emphasizing key differences.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Axial stretch ratio** (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for answer):

$$
\lambda_{axial} = \dfrac{H}{L_p} = \dfrac{240}{280} = \boxed{0.857}
$$

(If parison length is the **original** and final container height is the **deformed** height, axial stretch < 1 indicates **axial draw-down** during parison sagging.)

**Step 2 — Hoop (circumferential) stretch ratio** (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for answer):

Initial parison outer circumference $C_i = \pi D_o = \pi \times 25 = 78.54\,\text{mm}$.

Final equatorial circumference $C_f = \pi D_{max} = \pi \times 180 = 565.49\,\text{mm}$.

$$
\lambda_{hoop} = \dfrac{C_f}{C_i} = \dfrac{565.49}{78.54} = \boxed{7.20}
$$

**Step 3 — Blow pressure** (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for answer):

$$
P_{blow} = \dfrac{2 \sigma_h \, t}{D_{max}} = \dfrac{2 \times 12 \times 1.2}{180}
$$

$$
P_{blow} = \dfrac{28.8}{180} = 0.16\,\text{MPa} = \boxed{1.6\,\text{bar}}
$$

**Step 4 — Why blow moulds cannot have complex internal undercuts (1 mark):**
Blow-moulded parts are **expanded inside a closed two-piece mould** with no side-actions or lifters. The mould halves must **separate along a single parting line**, so any **internal undercut (e.g., a snap-fit internal rib or a hollow boss with cross-hole) cannot be formed** because the parison cannot be wrapped around an internal core. Injection moulding, by contrast, uses **side-action cams and collapsible cores** to release such features.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (must-memorize for KTU 2024 ESE)**

- ✅ **Uniform wall thickness** is the **#1 rule** — variation ≤ ±10 % of nominal.
- ✅ **Draft angle** always measured from **mould-open direction**: 0.5°–2° (injection), 0°–1° (blow), 2°–5° (thermoforming male).
- ✅ **Fillet $R_i = 0.5t$ – $0.75t$** reduces stress concentration factor $K_t$.
- ✅ **Ribs $t_r = 0.5t$ – $0.75t$, $H_r \le 3t$**; rib-boss intersection must have a **generous fillet**.
- ✅ **Cavity dimension = Part dimension ÷ (1 − $S_L$)** — always **LARGER** than the part.
- ✅ **Cooling time scales as $t^2$** — halving wall thickness **quarters cycle time**.
- ✅ **Amorphous polymers (ABS, PC, PS)** shrink **less** (0.2–0.7 %) than **semi-crystalline (PP, PA, POM)** (1.0–3.0 %) because crystallization causes **additional densification shrinkage**.
- ✅ **Blow molding** is limited to **hollow, open-ended, axisymmetric parts** with **no internal undercuts**; uses **bifurcated moulds** with a single parting line.
- ✅ **Thermoforming** stretches a **pre-extruded sheet** → wall thickness varies as $t \propto 1/\text{stretch ratio}$ — **deep draws cause severe thinning**.
- ✅ **Rotational molding** is **process-limited to large, hollow, low-volume parts** with no internal pressures; powder fusion requires **biaxial rotation in two axes**.
- ✅ **Shrinkage + CTE contribution** must both be added when predicting final part dimensions from cavity.
- ✅ **Gate location** should be at the **thickest section** to minimize weld lines, sink marks, and air entrapment.
- ✅ **Snap-fits** must be designed with **H ≤ 2t × strain capacity** of the material to avoid stress-whitening or failure.
- ✅ **Weld lines** form when two melt fronts meet → place gate so weld line falls on a **non-critical, non-cosmetic surface**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Calendering** is a continuous, high-volume polymer processing operation in which a thermoplastic compound (most commonly Polyvinyl Chloride - PVC) is forced through a series of precisely spaced, counter-rotating, heated rolls (called *calender rolls*) to produce a continuous film, sheet, or coated substrate of uniform thickness, smooth surface finish, and controlled gauge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPOT503 – Module 4):**
> Calendering is treated as a *post-compounding / post-mixing* conversion process. The feed material entering the calender is a pre-plasticized, homogeneous, hot polymer mass (often called a "bank" or "rolling bank") supplied by an extruder, batch mixer, or continuous mixer.

Mathematically, calendering can be viewed as a **squeezing flow problem** between two rotating cylinders, governed by the **lubrication approximation** of the Navier–Stokes equations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Rolling-Pin vs. Dough" Analogy:**
> Imagine rolling out a piece of dough with a heavy kitchen rolling pin to make chapati. You push the dough forward, the rolling pin flattens it to a precise thickness, and it comes out as a thin, smooth sheet. Now scale that up — replace the rolling pin with **giant, heated, polished steel cylinders** turning at exact speeds, replace the dough with **hot, plasticized PVC**, and feed the material continuously instead of in batches. That industrial "rolling-pin" is the **calender**.

**Why is it heated?** — To lower the polymer's melt viscosity so it flows easily under the squeezing action of the rolls.
**Why multiple rolls?** — To progressively thin the material, improve surface finish, and control the final gauge.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Roll Crown (Camber)** | Slight convexity machined into the roll to compensate for elastic bending under load. Typically **0.05 mm to 0.20 mm**. |
| **Bite / Nip** | The narrowest gap between two adjacent rolls where the polymer is squeezed. |
| **Bank** | The reservoir of polymer melt accumulated just before the nip. |
| **Gauge** | Final thickness of the calendered sheet (typically **0.05 mm to 1.0 mm**). |
| **Stripping Roll** | Final roll that peels the finished sheet off the preceding roll. |
| **Take-Up Speed** | Linear velocity of the finished sheet at the wind-up station, typically **30 to 90 m/min** for rigid PVC, up to **200 m/min** for plasticized PVC. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Industry Application:** Calendering produces ~70% of all flexible PVC film/sheet used globally — for products like inflatable pools, raincoats, credit cards, flooring, automotive dashboards, and blood bags.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sequence of Operations in a Calendering Line

A complete calendering line consists of **seven functional zones** executed in series:

1. **Compounding / Pre-plasticization:** PVC resin, plasticizer, stabilizer, filler, and pigment are mixed in a Banbury mixer or continuous mixer to form a homogeneous hot dough-like mass at **140–170 °C**.
2. **Feeding (Feed Conveyor / Metal Detector):** A belt conveyor delivers the mass to the *first nip* of the calender; a metal detector guards against ferrous contamination that could damage roll surfaces.
3. **Melt Banking:** A rotating, screw-fed *bank* forms in front of the first nip, ensuring uniform feeding.
4. **Squeezing / Calendering:** The melt passes through **2, 3, 4, or 5 nips** in sequence, with the gap progressively reduced.
5. **Gauging / Embossing:** An embossing roll may texture the surface (e.g., leather-grain for upholstery).
6. **Cooling:** The hot sheet passes over a series of chilled drums (typically 4–8) to reduce temperature from ~160 °C to ~40 °C.
7. **Trimming, Tensioning, and Winding:** Edge-trim slitters cut the sheet to width; tension-controlled winders spool the product.

## 2.2 Roll Arrangements (Roll Configurations)

The arrangement of the rolls defines the machine's name and operational characteristics:

| Configuration | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **I-Type (2-roll)** | Two rolls stacked vertically; only one nip. | Lab-scale or thin-gauge coating. |
| **L-Type (3-roll)** | Three rolls forming an L; one bank. | Coating of fabrics. |
| **Inverted L (3-roll)** | Smaller roll below two larger rolls. | Thin films. |
| **F-Type (4-roll)** | Two nips, two banks; very common. | Rigid & flexible PVC sheets. |
| **Z-Type (4-roll)** | Rolls offset in a zig-zag; each roll independent. | High-speed, wide films. |
| **Inverted L (4-roll)** | Compact; lower building height. | Flooring manufacture. |
| **5-Roll / 6-Roll** | Added roll = extra nip = finer gauge control. | Premium optical films. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The number of nips equals the number of *gauge reductions*. A 4-roll F-type calender has 2 nips; a 5-roll machine has 3 nips. Each successive nip reduces the thickness by **40–70%**.

## 2.3 The Calendering Pressure — Archard's Equation

The pressure developed in the nip is the core engineering quantity. For two counter-rotating rolls of radius **R₁, R₂**, surface speed **U₁, U₂**, with a viscosity **η** and minimum gap **2h₀**, the **maximum pressure at the nip center** is given by:

$$P_{max} = \eta \cdot \frac{(U_1 + U_2) \cdot \pi}{4} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{R_1 + R_2}{2 h_0^3}}$$

For equal rolls of radius **R** and equal surface speed **U**, this simplifies to:

$$P_{max} = \frac{\pi \eta U}{4} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{R}{2 h_0^3}}$$

The total **separating force (roll-separating force)** on one roll is obtained by integrating this pressure over the contact arc:

$$F = \frac{\pi \eta U R L}{4 h_0}$$

where **L** is the roll face length (width of the sheet).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** This force can range from **50 kN to 500 kN per meter of roll width** in industrial calenders. It governs the required roll strength, bearing capacity, and the *deflection compensation* (crown) needed to keep the sheet flat.

## 2.4 Roll Deflection Compensation Methods

The roll-separating force causes the rolls to **bend (deflect) elastically**, increasing the nip gap in the middle relative to the edges. Compensation methods include:

1. **Roll Crown (Camber):** Pre-machined convex profile (typically **+0.05 to +0.20 mm**).
2. **Cross-Axis (Skewing):** Tilting one roll axis by a small angle (typically **0.5° to 1.5°**) creates a tapered gap.
3. **Roll Bending:** Hydraulic jacks apply counter-moments at the roll journals to push the roll center upward.
4. **Counter-Bending (Anti-Bending):** Opposite-direction bending to over-compensate and allow fine tuning.
5. **Roll Crossing (Axial Offset):** Shifting one roll along its axis relative to the other, creating a converging/diverging wedge.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{U}{h_0}$ | Average shear rate in the nip | s⁻¹ |
| 2 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{\pi \eta U}{4}\sqrt{\dfrac{R}{2h_0^3}}$ | Peak nip pressure | Pa (N/m²) |
| 3 | $F = \dfrac{\pi \eta U R L}{4 h_0}$ | Roll-separating force | N |
| 4 | $\delta = \dfrac{F L_{shaft}^3}{48 E I}$ | Elastic deflection of roll | m |
| 5 | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | Linear surface speed of roll | m/s |
| 6 | $Q = U \cdot L \cdot (2h_0)$ | Volumetric throughput (per nip) | m³/s |
| 7 | $T_{frictional} = \eta \cdot \dfrac{U}{h_0} \cdot A_{contact}$ | Viscous friction torque | N·m |
| 8 | $P_{motor} = 2 \pi N \cdot T_{frictional}$ | Required motor power | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always carry **η** (melt viscosity, Pa·s) and **U** (roll speed, m/s) in **SI units**. KTU board exams penalize mixed units (e.g., cP with m/min).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Nip Pressure Between Two Equal Counter-Rotating Rolls

### Statement of the Problem
Two identical rolls of radius **R** rotate in opposite directions with equal surface speed **U**. The melt is a Newtonian fluid of viscosity **η** undergoing steady, laminar, isothermal, creeping flow. The minimum gap between the rolls is **2h₀**. Determine the maximum pressure developed at the nip.

### Step 1 — Geometry of the Nip Region
Define a coordinate system with **x** measured along the roll surface and **y** perpendicular to it. The half-gap **h(x)** varies with axial position:

$$h(x) = h_0 \left(1 + \frac{x^2}{2 R h_0}\right)$$

For a small nip region, we adopt the **lubrication approximation**:
- Inertial terms $\rho (\vec{v} \cdot \nabla) \vec{v}$ are negligible.
- Pressure is constant across the gap: $\partial p / \partial y = 0$.
- The flow is locally Couette-like: $v_y = 0$, $v_x = v_x(x, y)$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Navier–Stokes Equation in the x-direction

$$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \eta \frac{\partial^2 v_x}{\partial y^2}$$

Integrating twice with respect to **y**, with no-slip boundary conditions $v_x(+h) = U$ and $v_x(-h) = -U$:

$$v_x(x, y) = \frac{1}{2\eta}\frac{dp}{dx}(y^2 - h^2) + U \frac{y}{h}$$

### Step 3 — Volumetric Flow Rate
Integrate the velocity profile across the gap to get the volumetric flow per unit width **q**:

$$q = \int_{-h}^{h} v_x \, dy = -\frac{2h^3}{3\eta}\frac{dp}{dx} + 2 U h$$

### Step 4 — Continuity (Steady State)
Since flow is steady and incompressible, **q is constant** along **x** (and equal to the delivery rate of melt to the nip):

$$q = 2 U h_0$$

Setting $q = 2U h_0$ in the integrated flow expression:

$$2 U h_0 = -\frac{2h^3}{3\eta}\frac{dp}{dx} + 2 U h$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Pressure Gradient

$$\frac{dp}{dx} = \frac{3\eta U}{h^3}(h - h_0)$$

### Step 6 — Substitute h(x) and Integrate

Using $h = h_0 \left(1 + \dfrac{x^2}{2 R h_0}\right)$, substitute into the integral:

$$p(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} \frac{3\eta U}{h(\xi)^3}\bigl[h(\xi) - h_0\bigr] \, d\xi$$

By symmetry, the pressure reaches its **maximum at the nip center (x = 0)**. Solving the integral (a standard result from Gaskell / Middleman):

$$P_{max} = \frac{\pi \eta U}{4}\sqrt{\frac{R}{2 h_0^3}}$$

### Step 7 — Interpret the Result

$$\boxed{\,P_{max} = \frac{\pi \eta U}{4}\sqrt{\frac{R}{2 h_0^3}}\,}$$

> **Observations:**
> - $P_{max} \propto \eta^{1}$ → more viscous melts need higher forces.
> - $P_{max} \propto U^{1}$ → higher line speed requires stronger rolls and bearings.
> - $P_{max} \propto h_0^{-3/2}$ → the most sensitive parameter; halving the gap increases pressure by **2.83×**.

### Step 8 — Derive the Total Separating Force
Integrate $P_{max}$ over the contact arc length, approximated as $2\sqrt{R h_0}$:

$$F = \int_{nip} P \, dA \approx P_{max} \cdot L \cdot 2\sqrt{R h_0}$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$\boxed{\,F = \frac{\pi \eta U R L}{4 h_0}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the formula examiners love to test.** The KTU pattern: *"A PVC calender with roll radius 200 mm, face width 1500 mm, surface speed 0.5 m/s, viscosity 500 Pa·s, and gap 0.5 mm is producing a sheet. Calculate the roll-separating force."*

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Calendering vs. Extrusion vs. Injection Molding

| Parameter | Calendering | Film Extrusion | Injection Molding |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Form of product** | Continuous sheet/film | Continuous film | Discrete part |
| **Driving force** | Squeezing between rolls | Screw pressure + drag | Screw injection |
| **Typical thickness** | 0.05–1.0 mm | 0.01–0.25 mm | 1–10 mm (wall) |
| **Throughput** | Very high | High | Low–Medium |
| **Gauge accuracy** | ±1% (with compensation) | ±3–5% | ±2% |
| **Best polymer** | PVC, rubber | PE, PP, PET | All thermoplastics |
| **Tooling cost** | Very high (rolls) | Low (die) | High (mold) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Calendering Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Banbury Mixer / Continuous Mixer] --> B[Feed Conveyor + Metal Detector]
    B --> C[First Nip - Calender Rolls 1 & 2]
    C --> D[Second Nip - Rolls 2 & 3]
    D --> E[Third Nip - Rolls 3 & 4]
    E --> F[Embossing / Lamination Station]
    F --> G[Cooling Drums - Multi-stage]
    G --> H[Edge Trimmers + Thickness Gauge]
    H --> I[Tension Control + Windup Roll]
    I --> J[Finished Sheet / Film]
```

> **Figure 4.1:** Sequential flow of polymer through a 4-roll F-type calendering line, from pre-plasticized feed to spooled sheet.

## 4.2 Roll Configuration Schematic (F-Type, 4-Roll Calender)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph F_Type["F-Type 4-Roll Calender"]
        R1[Roll 1 - Top] -->|First Nip| R2[Roll 2]
        R2 -->|Second Nip| R3[Roll 3]
        R3 -->|Third Nip| R4[Roll 4 - Bottom Stripping]
    end
    FEED[Feed Bank] --> R1
    R4 --> SHEET[Calendered Sheet]
```

> **Figure 4.2:** Schematic of a 4-roll F-type calender showing three sequential nips and one stripping roll.

## 4.3 Nip Region — Pressure Distribution and Velocity Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Polymer Bank Entry] -->|Approaching x = -infinity| B[Narrowing Gap Region]
    B -->|Pressure Rising| C[Maximum Pressure at Nip Center x = 0]
    C -->|Pressure Falling| D[Expanding Gap Region]
    D -->|Exit as Sheet| E[Calendered Film at h0 thickness]
```

> **Figure 4.3:** Conceptual pressure profile through the nip, showing the peak at the narrowest point.

## 4.4 Roll Deflection Compensation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DEF[Roll-Separating Force Causes Deflection] --> M1[Method 1: Pre-Machined Crown]
    DEF --> M2[Method 2: Cross-Axis Skewing]
    DEF --> M3[Method 3: Hydraulic Roll Bending]
    DEF --> M4[Method 4: Roll Crossing Axial Offset]
    M1 --> OUT[Uniform Sheet Thickness Achieved]
    M2 --> OUT
    M3 --> OUT
    M4 --> OUT
```

> **Figure 4.4:** Four primary methods used in industry to compensate for roll bending, ensuring flat sheet profile.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define calendering. List any four major products manufactured by the calendering process.**

**Model Answer:**
Calendering is a continuous polymer processing technique in which a pre-plasticized thermoplastic mass is passed through a series of counter-rotating, heated rolls to produce a continuous sheet or film of uniform thickness.

**Four major products:**
1. Flexible PVC film (raincoats, inflatables)
2. PVC flooring sheets
3. PVC-coated fabrics (tarpaulins, conveyor belts)
4. Rubber sheets and tire components

*(CO2, Bloom's Level: Remember — 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the function of a "bank" in a calendering operation? Why are calender rolls heated?**

**Model Answer:**
A **bank** is the reservoir of polymer melt that accumulates immediately before the nip. It ensures a continuous, uniform supply of material to the rolls and prevents air entrainment.

**Rolls are heated** to:
- Reduce melt viscosity → lower separating force.
- Maintain a uniform processing temperature.
- Improve surface gloss and fusion quality of the sheet.

*(CO2, Bloom's Level: Understand — 3 marks)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Module 4]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] With the aid of a neat schematic, describe the **construction and working of a 4-roll F-type calender**. Discuss the function of each roll and explain how the polymer feed bank is established.

> **(b)** [7 marks] Explain the **roll deflection problem** in calendering. Describe in detail **any two methods** used to compensate for roll deflection and ensure uniform sheet thickness.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Construction and Working of 4-Roll F-Type Calender:**

The F-type calender consists of **four rolls** arranged such that three nips are formed in series. The pre-plasticized polymer (typically PVC dough at 150–170 °C) is fed from a Banbury mixer or extruder via a conveyor belt.

- **Roll 1 (Top Roll):** Receives the feed bank; primary function is to receive the melt and initiate the first gauge reduction.
- **Roll 2:** Receives material from the first nip, conducts a second squeezing to further thin the sheet.
- **Roll 3:** Third nip, providing the final gauge reduction and imparting smooth surface finish.
- **Roll 4 (Bottom Stripping Roll):** Operates at a slightly higher speed than Roll 3, "stripping" the sheet cleanly off Roll 3.

The **feed bank** is established by the controlled delivery of melt at a rate slightly exceeding the nip's intake rate; the excess accumulates as a rotating mass called the *bank*. This bank ensures continuous, air-free feeding.

**Process Parameters:**
- Roll surface temperature: 160–190 °C
- Roll surface speed: 30–60 m/min
- Nip pressure: 5–15 MPa
- Sheet thickness: 0.1–1.0 mm

*(CO2, Bloom's Level: Understand / Apply — 7 marks)*

**(b) Roll Deflection and Compensation:**

**Problem Statement:** When the polymer melt is squeezed in the nip, the roll-separating force (50–500 kN/m) causes the rolls to bend elastically. This widens the gap at the center, producing a sheet that is **thicker in the middle and thinner at the edges** — known as "profile variation."

**Method 1 — Roll Crown (Camber):**
The roll surface is machined with a slight convex profile (typically +0.05 to +0.20 mm). Under load, the roll flattens out, yielding a parallel nip. This is a *static* compensation and is fixed at design time.

**Method 2 — Cross-Axis Skewing:**
The axis of one roll is tilted (skewed) by a small angle (0.5°–1.5°) relative to the other. This creates a wedge-shaped nip, with one end narrower than the other. By adjusting the skew, the operator can tune the local gauge and counteract any residual thickness variation.

**Other methods (for completeness):** Roll Bending (hydraulic jacks apply counter-moments at the journals), and Roll Crossing (axial offset to alter the nip profile).

*(CO3, Bloom's Level: Apply / Analyze — 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Module 4]**

> **(a)** [7 marks] Derive an expression for the **maximum pressure developed at the nip** of a calender, assuming Newtonian fluid behavior. Clearly state all assumptions used in the derivation.

> **(b)** [7 marks] A 4-roll PVC calender has the following specifications:
> - Roll radius, R = 0.20 m
> - Face width, L = 1.5 m
> - Surface speed, U = 0.5 m/s
> - Melt viscosity, η = 500 Pa·s
> - Minimum nip gap, 2h₀ = 0.5 mm
>
> **Calculate:**
> (i) Maximum pressure at the nip
> (ii) Total roll-separating force
> (iii) Motor power required to overcome viscous friction, assuming the torque is 5% higher than the theoretical value.

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of Maximum Nip Pressure:**

*(The complete derivation is presented in Section 3.1 of these notes. Key assumptions: Newtonian fluid, steady, laminar, isothermal, creeping flow, lubrication approximation, equal roll radii, equal surface speeds.)*

**Final Result:**
$$P_{max} = \frac{\pi \eta U}{4}\sqrt{\frac{R}{2 h_0^3}}$$

*(CO3, Bloom's Level: Apply / Analyze — 7 marks)*

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- R = 0.20 m
- L = 1.5 m
- U = 0.5 m/s
- η = 500 Pa·s
- 2h₀ = 0.5 mm → h₀ = 0.25 × 10⁻³ m = 2.5 × 10⁻⁴ m

**(i) Maximum Pressure:**

$$P_{max} = \frac{\pi \times 500 \times 0.5}{4} \times \sqrt{\frac{0.20}{2 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-4})^3}}$$

Step 1 — Compute the leading factor:

$$\frac{\pi \times 500 \times 0.5}{4} = \frac{250 \pi}{4} \approx 196.35 \, \text{Pa·s/m}$$

Step 2 — Compute the term under the square root:

$$\frac{0.20}{2 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-4})^3} = \frac{0.20}{2 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-11}} = \frac{0.20}{3.125 \times 10^{-11}} = 6.4 \times 10^{9} \, \text{m}^{-2}$$

Step 3 — Take the square root:

$$\sqrt{6.4 \times 10^{9}} = 8.0 \times 10^{4} \, \text{m}^{-1}$$

Step 4 — Multiply:

$$P_{max} = 196.35 \times 8.0 \times 10^{4} \approx 1.57 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa} = 15.7 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} \approx 15.7 \, \text{MPa}}$$

**(ii) Total Roll-Separating Force:**

$$F = \frac{\pi \eta U R L}{4 h_0} = \frac{\pi \times 500 \times 0.5 \times 0.20 \times 1.5}{4 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$F = \frac{\pi \times 75}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{235.62}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.36 \times 10^{5} \, \text{N} = 236 \, \text{kN}$$

$$\boxed{F \approx 236 \, \text{kN}}$$

**(iii) Motor Power Required:**

First, the average shear rate in the nip:

$$\dot{\gamma}_{avg} = \frac{U}{h_0} = \frac{0.5}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2000 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

The viscous friction force on each roll surface (approximated):

$$F_{fric} = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma} \cdot A_{contact} = 500 \times 2000 \times (L \times 2\sqrt{R h_0})$$

$$A_{contact} = 1.5 \times 2 \times \sqrt{0.20 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.5 \times 2 \times \sqrt{5.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.5 \times 2 \times 7.07 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$A_{contact} \approx 0.0212 \, \text{m}^2$$

$$F_{fric} = 500 \times 2000 \times 0.0212 = 21,200 \, \text{N} = 21.2 \, \text{kN}$$

Torque on a single roll:

$$T = F_{fric} \times R = 21,200 \times 0.20 = 4240 \, \text{N·m}$$

Roll rotational speed:

$$N = \frac{U}{2\pi R} = \frac{0.5}{2\pi \times 0.20} = 0.398 \, \text{rev/s} = 23.9 \, \text{rpm}$$

Power for one roll:

$$P_{one} = 2\pi N T = 2\pi \times 0.398 \times 4240 \approx 10,605 \, \text{W}$$

For 4 rolls (sum), with a 5% increase factor:

$$P_{total} = 4 \times 10,605 \times 1.05 \approx 44,540 \, \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{motor} \approx 44.5 \, \text{kW} \, (\approx 60 \, \text{HP})}$$

*(CO4, Bloom's Level: Apply / Evaluate — 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Converting m/s to cm/s or h₀ in mm without squaring properly. *Always convert to SI first.*
> 2. **Forgetting h₀ is HALF-gap:** The question states "nip gap = 0.5 mm" — students often plug 0.5 mm as h₀ instead of 0.25 mm. *Read the symbol definition carefully.*
> 3. **Missing the 5% factor** in motor power calculation → loses 1 mark.
> 4. **No sketch in part (a)** → loses up to 2 marks. *Always draw the F-type configuration with labels.*
> 5. **Conflating "roll crown" and "roll bending":** They are *different* compensation methods. Examiners test this distinction.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Calendering** = continuous squeezing flow between heated counter-rotating rolls to form sheet/film.
- [x] **Primary polymer:** PVC (flexible & rigid); also rubber and certain thermoplastics.
- [x] **Roll arrangements:** I, L, inverted L, F, Z, 5-roll — each adds one nip and finer gauge control.
- [x] **Number of nips** = Number of gauge reductions.
- [x] **Bank:** the rotating melt reservoir just before each nip; ensures air-free, uniform feeding.
- [x] **Key formula 1:** $P_{max} = \dfrac{\pi \eta U}{4}\sqrt{\dfrac{R}{2h_0^3}}$ — peak pressure at the nip center.
- [x] **Key formula 2:** $F = \dfrac{\pi \eta U R L}{4 h_0}$ — total roll-separating force (drives bearing selection & roll stiffness).
- [x] **Key formula 3:** $\dot{\gamma} = U/h_0$ — average shear rate in the nip.
- [x] **Roll deflection compensation methods:** (1) Crown, (2) Cross-axis, (3) Roll bending, (4) Roll crossing.
- [x] **Typical operating window:** 30–200 m/min line speed, 0.05–1.0 mm gauge, 5–15 MPa nip pressure, 50–500 kN/m separating force.
- [x] **Cooling:** 4–8 chilled drums reduce sheet from ~160 °C to ~40 °C before winding.
- [x] **Industry dominance:** Calendering produces ~70% of all flexible PVC film/sheet worldwide.
- [x] **Newtonian assumption** is the exam default; in reality, PVC melts are shear-thinning (pseudoplastic).
- [x] **Lubrication approximation** is the key simplifying assumption that makes the derivation tractable.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Calenders and Strainer
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Calenders and Strainer

---

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PCPOT503 / Module 4):** *Calendering machinery — classification of calenders (2-roll, 3-roll, 4-roll, multi-roll), roll arrangements (I, L, inverted L, Z, S), roll deflection and crown compensation, and ancillary equipment — strainers and screen changers.*

---

## 1.1 What is a Calender?

A **calender** is a precision machine consisting of a series of large, heavy, internally heated/cooled rolls (also called *bowls* or *rolls*) arranged in a specific configuration. The polymer compound (most commonly **PVC plastisol**, **rubber compounds**, or **thermoplastic blends**) is fed into the *nip* (the converging gap between two adjacent rolls) and is squeezed, sheared, and pressed into a continuous **film, sheet, or coated substrate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A calender is a machine used in polymer processing to produce continuous sheets or films (typically **0.05 mm to 1.0 mm** thickness) of uniform gauge by passing an elastomeric or thermoplastic compound through a series of counter-rotating, precision-machined rolls operating at carefully controlled speeds, temperatures, and pressures.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Industrial Mangle"

Think of an old-fashioned **clothes mangle** (the wringer your grandmother used) — two large heavy rollers you feed wet laundry through to squeeze out water. Now imagine:
- The rolls are **heated** like a giant waffle iron,
- The laundry is replaced by a **doughy PVC/rubber mass**,
- Instead of two rolls, you have **three, four, or even seven rolls in a row**, each squeezing the material thinner and thinner,
- The speed of each roll is **slightly different** to create shearing (just like rubbing your palms together heats them up).

That is exactly what a calender does — it is the polymer industry's high-precision, heated, multi-stage mangle for turning thick polymer dough into micron-thin films used in **raincoats, vinyl flooring, blood bags, conveyor belts, and pool liners**.

---

## 1.2 What is a Strainer?

A **strainer** (also called an *extrusion strainer* or *screen changer*) is an ancillary device installed between the melting/mixing equipment and the calender (or extruder die) to **filter out contaminants, gels, unmelted polymer particles, carbon specks, and foreign debris** from the polymer melt before it enters the precision calender rolls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A strainer is a polymer-processing machine comprising a heated barrel, a hydraulically or mechanically driven ram/screw, a die plate, a breaker plate, and a multi-mesh screen pack that forces a polymer melt through the screen pack under high pressure to remove solid contaminants, ensuring a defect-free, homogeneous feedstock for downstream calendering or extrusion.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Sieve"

If your calender is a precision pasta-rolling machine, your **strainer is the fine-mesh kitchen sieve** you pour the flour or sauce through to catch lumps before it hits the final mould. In a rubber/PVC line, even a single speck of dirt, hair, charred polymer, or metal flake can:
- **Puncture** a 0.1 mm film (causing a hole in a blood bag),
- **Scratch** the polished calender roll surface (ruining a ₹50 lakh chrome roll),
- **Trigger** a downstream quality rejection.

Hence, straining is non-negotiable in industrial calendering lines.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

> [!TIP]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme has shifted toward **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. For Module 4, the expected Course Outcomes (COs) typically include:
> - **CO4.1:** Classify calender types and explain roll arrangements.
> - **CO4.2:** Compute roll separating force, roll deflection, and crown compensation.
> - **CO4.3:** Describe strainer design and screen-changer operation.
>
> Questions on this module usually appear for **7 to 14 marks** in the University Exam (Part B), and frequently test the **roll configurations** diagram plus the **separating force equation**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Calenders

Calenders are classified based on the **number of rolls** and the **geometric arrangement** of those rolls.

### 2.1.1 Based on Number of Rolls

| Calender Type | Roll Count | Typical Use | Production Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| **2-Roll Calender** | 2 | Laboratory sheeting, pre-forming, simple rubber sheets | **Low (10–50 kg/h)** |
| **3-Roll Calender** | 3 | Rubber frictioning, coating, basic PVC sheets | **Medium (50–300 kg/h)** |
| **4-Roll Calender** | 4 | Industrial PVC film/sheet, floor tiles, coated fabrics | **High (300–2500 kg/h)** |
| **5-Roll Calender** | 5 | Precision thin films, optical-grade sheets | **Very High** |
| **7-Roll Calender** | 7 | Ultra-precision capacitor films, photographic film base | **Specialty** |

### 2.1.2 Based on Roll Arrangement (The KTU Hot Topic)

The **4-roll calender** is the industrial workhorse and has **five standard roll arrangements** that KTU examiners love to draw. Each letter (I, L, Z, S) describes the *shape of the polymer path* between the rolls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Naming Convention:** Trace the path of the polymer from the **feed (entry) roll** to the **take-off roll**. The shape traced by this path resembles the English alphabet letter used to name the configuration.

| Configuration | Polymer Path | Roll Layout (Feed → Take-off) | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I-Type** (Straight) | Vertical | All 4 rolls in a vertical line | Small lab/pilot calenders |
| **L-Type** | L-shape | Two lower rolls, two upper rolls offset | Rubber coating |
| **Inverted L-Type** | ⌐ shape | Top two rolls + lower offset roll | PVC floor tiles |
| **Z-Type** | Z-shape | Alternating up-down-up-down (zig-zag) | **Most common for PVC film** |
| **S-Type** (Inclined Z) | S-shape | Tilted Z, all 4 rolls at different heights | Modern high-speed PVC calenders |

### 2.1.3 Why Different Arrangements?

The arrangement determines:
1. **Accessibility** for the operator to thread the polymer web,
2. **Length of the production line** (Z and S types are most compact),
3. **Roll separating force direction** (gravity assists in L-type),
4. **Cooling/heating efficiency**,
5. **Polymer residence time and shear history**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** A common 7-mark question asks *"With a neat sketch, explain the Z-type 4-roll calender."* Always draw the rolls, label them (1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th from feed), indicate **roll rotation direction** (alternating CW/CCW), mark the **polymer entry (nip) and take-off**, and mention **roll temperatures decreasing from 1st to last roll** (since polymer cools progressively).

---

## 2.2 Key Operating Parameters of a Calender

A calender is a precision machine. Every parameter is tightly controlled:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Roll Diameter** | $D$ or $2R$ | **400 mm to 1000 mm** | Larger $D$ = lower roll deflection |
| **Roll Face Length** | $L$ | **1000 mm to 3000 mm** | Determines sheet width |
| **Surface Speed** | $v$ | **20 to 120 m/min** | Affects production rate |
| **Nip Gap (Film thickness)** | $h_0$ | **0.05 to 1.0 mm** | Final product thickness |
| **Operating Temperature** | $T$ | **140 °C to 200 °C** (PVC) | Controls fusion and viscosity |
| **Roll Separating Force** | $F$ | **50 to 800 kN** | Drives roll deflection |
| **Friction Ratio** | $v_{\text{fast}}/v_{\text{slow}}$ | **1.0 to 1.5** | Creates shear for mixing |

---

## 2.3 Roll Deflection and Crown Compensation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **#1 KTU high-yield sub-topic** from Module 4. A 14-mark Part B question is almost guaranteed.

### The Problem
A calender roll is a long, slender cylinder supported only at its two ends. When the polymer is squeezed in the nip, it exerts a **huge separating force** ($F$) on the roll along its length. This force tries to **bend the roll in the middle**, making the roll slightly **concave** (like a banana). The consequence: the polymer sheet is **thicker in the middle and thinner at the edges** — unacceptable for a uniform product.

### The Solution — Crown Compensation
Three classical techniques are used to pre-compensate for this bending:

1. **Mechanical Crown (Camber):** The roll is ground with a slightly **convex profile** (diameter at centre is 0.05 to 0.20 mm larger than at the ends). When loaded, the roll flexes to flat.
2. **Cross-Axis (Roll Crossing):** The two rolls in a nip are deliberately set with their axes **slightly non-parallel** (crossed by a small angle, typically **0.5° to 2°**). The crossed-nip shape compensates for deflection.
3. **Roll Bending (Counter-bending):** An additional hydraulic force is applied at the roll bearings to *counter-bend* the roll in the opposite direction of natural deflection.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every equation the KTU examiner can ask in **Part A (3-mark)** or **Part B (14-mark)** questions on this topic. Memorize the units!

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Roll Separating Force per unit width** (Newtonian) | $\dfrac{F}{W} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot v \cdot R}{h_0} \cdot 2 \cdot \cos\theta_1$ | $F$=force, $W$=roll width, $\mu$=viscosity, $v$=roll velocity, $R$=roll radius, $h_0$=film thickness, $\theta_1$=nip entry angle | $\text{N/m}$ |
| 2 | **Simplified Roll Separating Force** | $F = \dfrac{2 \mu \cdot v \cdot R \cdot W}{h_0}$ | Same as above | $\text{N}$ |
| 3 | **Power-Law (Non-Newtonian) Correction** | $F = \mu \cdot v \cdot R \cdot W \cdot \dfrac{(2\pi R / h_0)^{(1-1/n)}}{n+1}$ | $n$ = power-law index, $\mu$ = consistency | $\text{N}$ |
| 4 | **Roll Deflection (Simply Supported, UDL)** | $y_{\max} = \dfrac{5 F L^3}{384 \, E \, I}$ | $y$=deflection, $L$=roll length between bearings, $E$=Young's modulus, $I$ = moment of inertia | $\text{m}$ |
| 5 | **Moment of Inertia (Solid Roll)** | $I = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}$ | $d$ = roll diameter | $\text{m}^4$ |
| 6 | **Residence Time in Nip** | $t_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{1.38\, R\, h_0}{v\, h_1}$ | $h_1$ = entry bank thickness, $v$ = roll velocity | $\text{s}$ |
| 7 | **Pressure at Nip Centre** (Newtonian) | $p_{\max} = \dfrac{\mu v R}{h_0} \left(2 \cos\theta_1 - 1 - \cos^2\theta_1\right)$ | — | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 8 | **Production Rate** | $Q = 2 \, v \, W \, h_0$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 9 | **Heat Generation in Nip** | $P = F \cdot v$ | Power dissipated as heat | $\text{W}$ |
| 10 | **Crown Compensation Ratio** | $\dfrac{y_{\max}}{L} = \dfrac{5 F L^2}{384 \, E \, I}$ | Dimensionless | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule:** When you write `|x|` or `|F|` (absolute value) in your exam answer, **always use** $\vert x \vert$ — never the plain pipe symbol — because it can break Markdown table rendering in many KTU digital evaluation systems.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Calender Application | Strainer's Role |
|---|---|---|
| **PVC Floor Tiles** | 4-roll L-type calender | Removes carbon specks, gel particles |
| **Pharmaceutical Blister Packs** | 3 or 4-roll calender | Filters metallic contaminants (GMP compliance) |
| **Conveyor Belts & Hoses** | 3-roll calender (rubber) | Removes fibre clumps, dirt |
| **Blood Bags (PVC Film)** | 5 or 7-roll calender | Filters every microgel (life-critical) |
| **Vinyl Wallpaper** | 4-roll Z-type calender + coating | Removes pinholes-causing particles |
