# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations follow the standard **Tadmor & Gogos / Middleman** approach used in polymer-processing textbooks and are the most-tested derivations in KTU exams.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Roll Separating Force (Newtonian Fluid)

**Objective:** Derive an expression for the horizontal force $F$ that the polymer exerts on a calender roll per unit width, when two counter-rotating rolls of radius $R$ and surface speed $v$ squeeze a film of final thickness $h_0$ from a polymer bank of thickness $H$.

### Step 1 — Set Up the Geometry

Place the origin at the **centre of the nip** (the line joining the two roll centres). A point on the roll surface at angular position $\theta$ (measured from the centre line) is at:

$$
x = R \sin\theta \qquad \text{and} \qquad y = R \cos\theta
$$

The **local nip gap** at angle $\theta$ is therefore:

$$
h(\theta) = h_0 + 2R(1 - \cos\theta) = h_0 + 2R - 2R\cos\theta
$$

For small $\theta$ (near the nip), $1 - \cos\theta \approx \theta^2/2$, giving:

$$
h(\theta) \approx h_0 + R\theta^2
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Newtonian Viscosity Law

The shear stress in the nip is:

$$
\tau_{xy} = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}
$$

Assuming a linear velocity profile (lubrication approximation) across the nip thickness $h(\theta)$ and using the relative velocity $2v$ between the two roll surfaces:

$$
\dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{2v}{h(\theta)} = \dfrac{2v}{h_0 + R\theta^2}
$$

Hence:

$$
\tau_{xy} = \dfrac{2\mu v}{h_0 + R\theta^2}
$$

### Step 3 — Integrate to Get Pressure

Apply the **Reynolds lubrication equation** in its simplified form (steady-state, Newtonian, no inertial effects):

$$
\dfrac{dp}{d\theta} = -\sqrt{2R\,h_0 + R^2\theta^2} \cdot \dfrac{d\tau_{xy}}{d\theta}
$$

After mathematical manipulation (using the substitution $u = \tan(\theta/2)$) the pressure distribution in the nip is:

$$
p(\theta) = \dfrac{2\mu v R}{h_0} \cdot \dfrac{\cos^2\theta - \cos^2\theta_1}{\left(1 + \dfrac{R\theta^2}{h_0}\right)^2}
$$

where $\theta_1$ is the **entry angle** at which the polymer bank first touches the roll.

### Step 4 — Boundary Condition (Atmospheric Pressure at Entry)

The pressure must equal atmospheric (gauge pressure = 0) at $\theta = \theta_1$:

$$
p(\theta_1) = 0
$$

This gives the **maximum pressure** at the centre of the nip ($\theta = 0$):

$$
p_{\max} = \dfrac{2\mu v R}{h_0} \left(1 - \cos^2\theta_1\right) = \dfrac{2\mu v R}{h_0} \sin^2\theta_1
$$

### Step 5 — Integrate Pressure to Get Separating Force

The total horizontal separating force per unit width is:

$$
\dfrac{F}{W} = \int_{-\theta_1}^{+\theta_1} p(\theta) \cos\theta \cdot R \, d\theta
$$

After integration and simplification:

$$
\dfrac{F}{W} = \dfrac{2\mu v R}{h_0} \left[\cos\theta_1 - \dfrac{1}{2}\left(1 + \cos^2\theta_1\right)\right] \cdot 2\theta_1
$$

For the **practical case** where $\theta_1$ is small (typical calender conditions), the term in brackets reduces approximately to $\theta_1/2$, and using the geometric relation $\theta_1 \approx \sqrt{H/R}$ (where $H$ is bank thickness), the final textbook form becomes:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{F}{W} = \dfrac{2 \mu v R}{h_0} \cdot \cos\theta_1 \approx \dfrac{\mu \pi v R}{h_0}\;}
$$

or in expanded form (using $\cos\theta_1 \approx 0.5\pi$ for the upper-limit of integration):

$$
\boxed{\;F = \dfrac{2 \mu v R W}{h_0}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that $F$ is **inversely proportional to $h_0$**. Halving the film thickness **doubles the separating force**. This is why producing very thin films (< 50 µm) requires gigantic calenders with massive rolls, and why the **roll deflection problem** becomes critical at low $h_0$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Roll Deflection

**Objective:** Find how much a calender roll of length $L$ (between bearings), diameter $d$, Young's modulus $E$, subjected to a uniformly distributed load $F$ (over the central contact zone) bends at its centre.

### Step 1 — Model the Roll as a Simply Supported Beam

Standard Euler-Bernoulli beam theory applies. The roll is treated as a cylindrical beam with:

- **Distributed load** $w = F/L$ (N/m) over its central portion (an excellent engineering approximation for the calender contact zone, which is small compared to the roll length),
- **Boundary conditions:** Deflection $y = 0$ and bending moment $M = 0$ at both ends ($x = 0$ and $x = L$).

### Step 2 — Differential Equation

$$
E I \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)
$$

where $I$ is the second moment of area. For a solid cylindrical roll:

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}
$$

### Step 3 — Standard Solution for a Simply Supported Beam with UDL

From any strength-of-materials reference, the **maximum deflection occurs at mid-span** and is:

$$
\boxed{\;y_{\max} = \dfrac{5 w L^4}{384 \, E\, I} = \dfrac{5 F L^3}{384 \, E\, I}\;}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $I$ and Rearrange

$$
y_{\max} = \dfrac{5 F L^3 \cdot 64}{384 \, E \, \pi d^4} = \dfrac{5 F L^3}{6 \pi E d^4}
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Example (Typical Industrial Values)

Suppose: $F = 4 \times 10^5$ N, $L = 2.5$ m, $E = 2 \times 10^{11}$ Pa (cast iron), $d = 0.6$ m.

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi (0.6)^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 0.1296}{64} \approx 6.36 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
y_{\max} = \dfrac{5 \times 4 \times 10^5 \times (2.5)^3}{384 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 6.36 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
y_{\max} = \dfrac{5 \times 4 \times 10^5 \times 15.625}{4.886 \times 10^{11}} \approx \dfrac{3.125 \times 10^7}{4.886 \times 10^{11}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;y_{\max} \approx 6.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 0.064 \text{ mm}\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Problem Pattern:** If $y_{\max} = 0.064$ mm and the film thickness target is $h_0 = 0.20$ mm, then the **thickness variation across the sheet width** due to roll bending would be **$\sim 32\%$** — totally unacceptable. This is why every calender roll has a **crown of 0.05 to 0.20 mm** machined into it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Crown Compensation (Quick Approximation)

**Objective:** Calculate the required crown height $C$ to be ground onto a calender roll so that, under load, the roll surface becomes perfectly flat (parallel to the other roll).

### Step 1 — Deflection Profile

The deflected shape of a simply supported beam under UDL is a **4th-order parabola**:

$$
y(x) = \dfrac{w \, x \, (L^3 - 2L x^2 + x^3)}{24 E I}
$$

### Step 2 — Required Crown Profile

The roll manufacturer grinds the roll surface in the **opposite (convex) shape** to the deflection. The required crown is therefore the **difference between the undeflected cylindrical surface and the desired flat working surface**:

$$
C(\xi) = y_{\max} - y(\xi)
$$

where $\xi$ is the position along the roll length. The maximum crown is at the centre:

$$
C_{\max} = y_{\max} = \dfrac{5 F L^3}{384 E I}
$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Check

Using the values from the worked example:

$$
\boxed{\;C_{\max} \approx 0.064 \text{ mm} = 64 \,\mu\text{m}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **exactly the amount** a skilled calender roll grinder will add to the centre of the roll. Real industrial crowns are typically in the range of **0.05 mm to 0.20 mm** for $L \approx 2$ m rolls.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Production Rate of a Calender

The polymer leaves the final nip at thickness $h_0$ and width $W$ at velocity $v$ (assuming no slip). Since the sheet is being formed from **two rolls** (one for each face), the total volumetric flow is:

$$
Q = 2 \, v \, W \, h_0 \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s)}
$$

The **mass throughput** is:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \, Q = 2 \, \rho \, v \, W \, h_0 \quad \text{(kg/s)}
$$

where $\rho$ is the polymer density (e.g., rigid PVC: $\rho \approx 1400$ kg/m³, plasticised PVC: $\rho \approx 1250$ kg/m³).

---

> [!TIP]

| Property | I-Type | L-Type | Inverted L | Z-Type | S-Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Polymer Path Length** | Shortest | Medium | Medium | Longest | Longest |
| **Gravity Assistance** | Strong | Strong | Strong | None | None |
| **Operator Accessibility** | Poor | Good | Good | Excellent | Excellent |

| **Production Rate** | Low | Medium | Medium | **High** | **Highest** |
| **Floor Space Required** | Tall | Medium | Medium | Compact | Compact |
| **Typical Use** | Pilot plant | Rubber sheet | PVC tiles | **PVC film** | Modern PVC film |
| **Shear History of Polymer** | Minimal | Low | Low | High | High |

```mermaid
    A6["4-ROLL Z-TYPE<br/>CALENDER"]:::cal
    A7["COOLING<br/>DRUMS"]:::cool
    A8["HAUL-OFF<br/>AND WINDER"]:::wind
    A9["FINISHED<br/>PVC FILM"]:::final

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7 --> A8 --> A9

    classDef raw fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#8a6d00,color:#000
    classDef mixer fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef cooler fill:#b5e0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef str fill:#e0c3fc,stroke:#4b0082,color:#000
    classDef cal fill:#d2f8d2,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef cool fill:#b5d8ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef wind fill:#cccccc,stroke:#333333,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Context:** This is the **complete calendering line** seen in any PVC film factory. The strainer sits between the extruder and the calender — it is the **last line of defence** against contaminants reaching the precision calender rolls.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions follow the **exact KTU 2024 Scheme pattern** (Part A: 3 marks, Part B: 14 marks with **internal choice** between Question A and Question B). Each carries a **simulated KTU past-year tag**.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a 2-roll and a 4-roll calender. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | 2-Roll Calender | 4-Roll Calender |
|---|---|---|
| Number of rolls | 2 | 4 (arranged in I/L/Z/S) |
| Number of nips | 1 | 3 |
| Sheet thickness control | Coarse | Very fine (multi-stage reduction) |
| Production rate | Low (10–50 kg/h) | High (300–2500 kg/h) |
| Applications | Lab work, pre-forming | Industrial PVC films, coated fabrics |
| Roll deflection issue | Less critical | **Major design constraint** |
| Cost | Low | High (multi-roll, complex drives) |

A 2-roll calender has a single nip, so the polymer is reduced in thickness in **one step**, giving poor gauge uniformity. A 4-roll calender reduces the polymer in **three successive nips**, with each nip having a smaller gap, giving **superior thickness uniformity** and much higher production rates — making it the industrial workhorse for PVC film and sheet manufacturing.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the function of a strainer in a polymer processing line? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **strainer** is an ancillary filtration device installed in a polymer processing line (typically between the extruder/mixer and the calender or die) to:

1. **Remove solid contaminants** such as carbon specks, metal particles, dust, fibres, charred polymer, and foreign matter from the polymer melt.
2. **Remove gels and unmelted polymer particles** that would otherwise cause surface defects, pinholes, or weak spots in the final film.
3. **Generate back-pressure** in the line, which improves melt homogeneity and degassing.

It consists of a **heated barrel, a die plate, a breaker plate, and a multi-mesh screen pack** through which the melt is forced. In PVC calendering lines, it is essential for producing **defect-free, high-clarity films** used in medical and food packaging.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**Question A (14 marks):**
**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Z-type 4-roll calender** for PVC film production. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Derive the expression for the **roll separating force** in a calender for a Newtonian fluid and explain how it is used in **crown compensation**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a) — Z-Type 4-Roll Calender (7 marks)

**Construction (3.5 marks):**

A Z-type 4-roll calender consists of **four precision-machined, internally heated/cooled steel rolls** (Roll 1, 2, 3, 4) arranged in a zig-zag path. The polymer entry is at the top (Roll 1–2 nip) and exit at the bottom (Roll 3–4 nip).

Key components:
1. **Rolls:** Hollow steel rolls (diameter 400–900 mm, face length 1000–3000 mm), internally cored for circulation of hot oil/steam for heating and chilled water for cooling. Surface finish: mirror-polished, hardened to **60–65 HRC**, often chrome-plated.
2. **Bearings:** Heavy-duty anti-friction bearings mounted in **chocks** that can be hydraulically adjusted for gap setting and roll crossing.
3. **Heating/Cooling System:** Each roll has independent temperature control. Typical temperature profile: Roll 1 = 175 °C, Roll 2 = 170 °C, Roll 3 = 160 °C, Roll 4 = 145 °C (gradual cooling).
4. **Drives:** Each roll is driven by an **individual DC motor** with gearbox, allowing independent speed control (friction ratio 1.0–1.5).
5. **Crown Compensation:** Mechanical crown, cross-axis, and roll-bending systems.
6. **Auxiliaries:** Polymer feed from strainer/extruder, embossing unit, cooling drums, thickness gauge (β-ray or X-ray), and winder.

**Working (3.5 marks):**

1. The polymer compound (e.g., plasticised PVC plastisol pre-gelled) is fed into the **first nip** between Roll 1 and Roll 2.
2. The polymer is squeezed to ~0.5 mm and adheres to Roll 2.
3. It transfers to the **second nip** (Roll 2–3) where thickness reduces to ~0.2 mm.
4. It then enters the **third nip** (Roll 3–Roll 4) to reach the **final gauge** (e.g., 0.10 mm).
5. The thin film is stripped off Roll 4, passed over cooling drums, edge-trimmed, and wound up.
6. Speed differential between rolls creates **shear**, generating heat that fuses the compound.

> **Advantage of Z-type:** Operator can **easily thread** the polymer web through the three nips, the line is **compact**, and polymer **runs horizontally** — no gravity issues.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b) — Roll Separating Force & Crown Compensation (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):** *(See Section 3.1 above for full derivation. Key steps to write in exam:)*

1. **Geometric setup:** Gap $h(\theta) = h_0 + R\theta^2$.
2. **Shear rate:** $\dot{\gamma} = 2v/(h_0 + R\theta^2)$.
3. **Newtonian shear stress:** $\tau = 2\mu v/(h_0 + R\theta^2)$.
4. **Apply Reynolds equation** to get pressure distribution $p(\theta)$.
5. **Apply boundary condition** $p(\theta_1) = 0$ to get $p_{\max}$.
6. **Integrate pressure** to get the **separating force** per unit width:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{F}{W} = \dfrac{2 \mu v R}{h_0} \cdot \cos\theta_1\;}
$$

7. **Final simplified form:**

$$
\boxed{\;F = \dfrac{2 \mu v R W}{h_0}\;}
$$

**Application to Crown Compensation (3 marks):**

The roll separating force $F$ pushes the two rolls apart, bending each roll. For a simply supported roll of length $L$ and diameter $d$:

$$
y_{\max} = \dfrac{5 F L^3}{384 \, E \, I} \quad \text{where} \quad I = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{64}
$$

To prevent **thickness variation** across the sheet (centre thicker, edges thinner), the roll is pre-ground with a **convex crown** of magnitude equal to $y_{\max}$. When loaded, the roll deflects to a **flat working surface**, ensuring **uniform film thickness** across the full sheet width $W$.

For example, with $F = 4 \times 10^5$ N, $L = 2.5$ m, $E = 2 \times 10^{11}$ Pa, $d = 0.6$ m:

$$
y_{\max} \approx 0.064 \text{ mm}
$$

So a crown of **~0.06 mm** is required.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**Question B (14 marks):**
**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **different roll arrangements** in a 4-roll calender and discuss the advantages of the **Z-type** configuration. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the **construction, working, and types of strainers** used in a polymer calendering line, with emphasis on the **screen-pack design**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a) — Roll Arrangements (7 marks)

**The five standard arrangements of a 4-roll calender:**

1. **I-Type (Straight Vertical):** All 4 rolls in a vertical line. Polymer enters at top, exits at bottom. Simple design but **poor operator accessibility** and large floor-to-ceiling height needed.

2. **L-Type:** Two lower rolls, two upper rolls offset horizontally. Polymer follows an **L-shaped path**. Good for **rubber coating** operations.

3. **Inverted L-Type:** Two upper rolls, one lower offset roll. Polymer follows an **inverted-L (⌐) path**. Common for **PVC floor tile** production.

4. **Z-Type:** Rolls arranged in a zig-zag, alternating up-down. Polymer traces a **Z-shape**. Most popular for **PVC film and sheet**. Operator can easily thread the web; compact layout; horizontal polymer flow.

5. **S-Type (Inclined Z):** Tilted version of Z-type. **All 4 rolls are at different heights**, with the whole calender tilted slightly. Polymer traces an **S-shape**. Used in **modern high-speed PVC calenders** for the best possible thickness profile.

**Advantages of Z-type (key points for 3 marks):**
- **Easy polymer threading** through all three nips.
- **Compact footprint** — line is short and horizontal.
- **No gravity-induced sag** of the thin film.
- **Independent roll temperature and speed control** is straightforward.
- **High production rates** (up to 2500 kg/h for PVC film).

---

### Model Solution — Part (b) — Strainer Construction & Working (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**

A strainer consists of:
1. **Heated Barrel:** Cylindrical steel barrel, jacketed for heating (steam/oil), typically maintained at **160–180 °C** for PVC.
2. **Ram / Gear Pump:** Hydraulic ram (in batch-type) or gear pump (in continuous-type) to push the melt through the screens under high pressure.
3. **Breaker Plate:** Perforated steel plate supporting the screens; also helps straighten flow and generate back-pressure.
4. **Screen Pack:** Multiple layers of woven wire mesh (typically **20 / 60 / 100 / 200 mesh**, fine on the bottom).
5. **Die Plate:** Final exit plate with central hole.
6. **Pressure & Temperature Sensors:** To monitor filter clogging.

**Working (2 marks):**

The polymer melt is forced (by ram or pump) through the **multi-mesh screen pack**. Larger contaminants are trapped by the **coarse screen** at the top; progressively smaller particles are caught by the **finer screens** below. The clean polymer exits through the die plate to the calender. As contaminants accumulate, the pressure drop across the screen rises; when it exceeds a setpoint (~150–200 bar), the screens are **replaced** (batch-type) or **automatically switched** (continuous-type).

**Types of Strainers (2 marks):**

| Type | Mechanism | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual / Batch Strainer** | Operator replaces screens on pressure alarm | Small-scale rubber/PVC |
| **Hydraulic Slide-Plate Strainer** | Slide plate with two screen positions, hydraulic shift | Medium-scale |
| **Automatic / Continuous Strainer** | Rotating disc or bolt-type screen, self-cleaning | **High-speed calendering** |
| **Gear-Pump Strainer** | Built-in gear pump + screen pack | Modern PVC lines |

> **Screen-pack design rule:** Mesh sequence from **coarse-to-fine** is essential to prevent premature blinding of the finest mesh. Typical sequence: **20 → 60 → 100 → 200 mesh**, with the **200 mesh** being the **backing** (fine layer is the most downstream).

---

## 5.4 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing roll configurations:** Students often draw the **L-type** when asked for the **Z-type** (or vice versa). Always trace the polymer path and check the letter shape.
> 2. **Forgetting roll rotation arrows:** In every calender diagram, **alternate rolls must rotate in opposite directions** (CW, CCW, CW, CCW). Missing arrows = **1 mark lost**.
> 3. **Mixing up separating force and pressure:** The roll separating force $F$ (Newtons) is the **integrated effect of pressure** $p$ (Pascals) over the contact area. Writing $F = p$ (without integration) loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Ignoring units in the formula sheet:** Writing $F = 2\mu v R W / h_0$ without stating units of $\mu$ (Pa·s), $v$ (m/s), $R$ (m), $W$ (m), $h_0$ (m) gives the right **number** but loses a **1-mark unit-consistency mark**.
> 5. **Omitting the boundary condition** $p(\theta_1) = 0$ in the derivation = **2 marks** lost.
> 6. **Crown value not given numerically:** Always quote a **typical crown** (e.g., 0.05–0.20 mm) — the examiner wants a number.
> 7. **Strainer confusion:** Some students describe a *filter press* or *centrifuge* in the strainer answer. Stick to the **screen-pack + die-plate** definition.
> 8. **Skipping the purpose of the breaker plate:** It is **not** just a support — it also **straightens flow** and **generates back pressure**. Both points needed for full marks.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Calenders & Strainers**

### Calender Basics
- Calendering = squeezing polymer compound through **counter-rotating heated rolls** to make thin films/sheets.
- Used mainly for **PVC, rubber, and some thermoplastics**.
- 4-roll Z-type calender is the **industrial workhorse** for PVC films.

### Roll Configurations (4-Roll)
- **I, L, Inverted L, Z, S** — named by the **shape of the polymer path**.
- **Z-type** is the most common; **S-type** is the most modern.
- Each successive nip has **smaller gap** and **higher separating force**.

### Key Equations
- **Separating force:** $F = 2\mu v R W / h_0$
- **Maximum pressure:** $p_{\max} = (2\mu v R / h_0) \sin^2\theta_1$
- **Roll deflection:** $y_{\max} = 5 F L^3 / (384 E I)$
- **Moment of inertia:** $I = \pi d^4 / 64$
- **Production rate:** $Q = 2 v W h_0$

### Crown Compensation
- **Mechanical crown:** grind roll convex, $\Delta = 0.05$–$0.20$ mm.
- **Cross-axis:** skew rolls by **0.5°–2°**.
- **Roll bending:** hydraulic counter-bending force at bearings.
- All three aim to make the deflected roll **flat under load**.

### Strainer Basics
- Strainer = **filtering device** between extruder and calender.
- Removes **carbon specks, gels, unmelted, metal particles**.
- Components: **barrel, ram/pump, breaker plate, screen pack, die plate**.
- Screen pack: **coarse to fine** (e.g., 20 → 60 → 100 → 200 mesh).
- **Automatic screen changers** are used in modern high-speed lines.

### Industrial Context
- A complete line: **Mixer → Cooling mixer → Extruder → Strainer → Calender → Cooling drums → Winder**.
- Calender throughput for rigid PVC film: **up to 2500 kg/h**.
- Roll chrome plating is **essential** (0.05–0.1 mm thick) to prevent corrosion and scratching.

### KTU-Specific Exam Tips
- Always draw the calender with **labels, rotation arrows, and temperature profile**.
- For derivations, **state the boundary condition** $p(\theta_1) = 0$ explicitly.
- Quote **typical industrial values** (roll diameter 600 mm, speed 30 m/min, F = 400 kN, crown = 0.1 mm) — examiners love numbers.
- Mention **two or more methods** of crown compensation for full marks.
- In strainer answers, mention **screen-pack design rule** (coarse-to-fine).

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Calenders & Strainer** ✅
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, OBE-aligned Bloom's Taxonomy (L1–L5).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embosser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Embosser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Embosser** (also called an **Embossing Machine** or **Texturing Unit**) is a downstream polymer processing machine used to impart a permanent, three-dimensional surface pattern — either raised, recessed, or a combination of both — onto a thermoplastic film, sheet, or web by forcing the softened polymer surface against an engraved (patterned) roller under controlled temperature, pressure, and line speed conditions.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCPOT503 syllabus (Module 4)**, the Embosser is classified under **Post-Extrusion / Secondary Polymer Processing Equipment** and is typically positioned immediately after the chill-roll / cast-roll stack and before the winder in a **Cast Film / Sheet Extrusion Line**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCPOT503 Module 4):** Embossers are studied as part of "Downstream Equipment for Polymer Product Manufacturing." Students must be able to (i) identify the constructional features, (ii) explain the working principle, (iii) classify types, and (iv) state applications and process parameters.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an Embosser like a **rolling pin with a textured surface** that you press down on a soft slab of dough:

- The **textured rolling pin** = the **embossing roller** (steel roller engraved with the desired pattern).
- The **soft dough** = the **molten / softened thermoplastic film** exiting the extrusion die or chill roll.
- The **downward pressing force** = the **nip pressure** applied between the embossing roller and the backup (rubber) roller.
- The **final patterned biscuit** = the **embossed polymer film/sheet** with permanent surface texture.

Just like the dough retains the rolling pin's pattern once it cools and hardens, the polymer film "remembers" the embossed pattern once it passes the nip and cools below its softening point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Printing:** Embossing creates **three-dimensional** physical texture (depth/height), whereas printing only applies a **two-dimensional** ink layer on the surface.

## 1.3 Engineering Metrics & Standard Definitions

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Range (Industrial) | Unit |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------------|------|
| Line Speed | $v$ | **5 – 150** | m/min |
| Nip Pressure | $P_n$ | **10 – 80** | N/mm (line load) |
| Embossing Temperature | $T_e$ | **80 – 160** | °C |
| Pattern Depth | $d_p$ | **5 – 200** | µm |
| Roller Hardness (Steel) | HRC | **58 – 65** | Rockwell C |
| Backup Roller Shore A | $S_A$ | **60 – 90** | Shore A |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The embossing operation is governed by the simultaneous application of **heat, pressure, and time** at a precisely controlled nip:

1. **Pre-conditioning of the web:** The extruded polymer film/sheet (LDPE, LLDPE, PP, PVC, PET, etc.) is maintained at a temperature slightly **above its Vicat softening point** but **below its melting point**. This ensures the surface is pliable enough to deform but stable enough to retain shape.

2. **Nip engagement:** The web enters the **nip** — the converging contact zone between the **embossing roller** (top, engraved steel) and the **backup roller** (bottom, rubber-covered or steel).

3. **Pattern replication:** Hydraulic or pneumatic loading forces the rollers together. The engraved cavities on the embossing roller locally displace polymer into the pattern cavities, producing raised features on the polymer side (mirror image).

4. **Thermal setting / cooling:** Immediately after the nip, the embossed surface contacts a **chilled embossing roller** (internally water-cooled) or a separate **cooling roller**, dropping the temperature below the **Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT)** to "freeze" the pattern permanently.

5. **Release and tension control:** The web is released under controlled **winder tension** ($T_w$) to avoid distortion of the freshly embossed pattern.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a rubber backup roller?** A rigid steel-on-steel nip would crush the polymer and damage the engraving. The compliant rubber roller (typically **Shore A 70–85**) conforms to local pressure variations and provides uniform line contact pressure across the web width.

## 2.2 Constructional Components

1. **Embossing Roller (Pattern Roller):**
   - Material: Hard-chrome plated carbon steel or tool steel.
   - Surface: Chemically etched, laser-engraved, or mechanically knurled with the desired pattern.
   - Internal: Double-walled with spiral water channels for temperature control.
   - Surface finish: Polished to **Ra 0.1 – 0.4 µm** for fine patterns.

2. **Backup Roller (Counter Roller):**
   - Material: Steel core covered with silicone rubber, EPDM, or polyurethane (Shore A 60–90).
   - Function: Provides elastic counter-pressure; protects the engraved roller.

3. **Nip Loading System:**
   - Pneumatic cylinders, hydraulic rams, or electromechanical screw jacks.
   - Load cell feedback for closed-loop pressure control.

4. **Heating System (for the embossing roller):**
   - Internal hot-oil circulation or electrical band heaters.
   - Maintains roller surface at $T_e$.

5. **Cooling System:**
   - Chilled water circulation (typically **5 – 15 °C**) through embossing roller core.
   - Optional external air knives for accelerated cooling.

6. **Web Handling System:**
   - Idler rollers, edge guides, tension controllers, and anti-static bars.

7. **Drive System:**
   - AC vector motor with encoder feedback for **synchronization** with upstream extrusion line speed.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $v = \pi \cdot D_e \cdot N_e$ | Tangential speed of embossing roller (D = diameter, N = RPM) | m/min |
| 2 | $P_n = \dfrac{F_n}{L_w}$ | Nip line pressure (Force / Web width) | N/mm |
| 3 | $T_{res} = \dfrac{L_{nip}}{v}$ | Residence / contact time in the nip | seconds |
| 4 | $L_{nip} \approx 2 \cdot \sqrt{R_e \cdot \Delta R}$ | Hertzian nip length approximation | mm |
| 5 | $\sigma_{nip} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_n \cdot E^*}{\pi \cdot L_w \cdot R_{eq}}}$ | Maximum Hertzian contact stress | MPa |
| 6 | $\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{R_e} + \dfrac{1}{R_b}$ | Equivalent radius for steel-on-rubber contact | mm⁻¹ |
| 7 | $Q_{cool} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat removal rate of chilled roller (m = mass flow, $C_p$ = specific heat) | kW |
| 8 | $h_{pattern} = k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_n \cdot t}{\eta}}$ | Pattern penetration depth (Stefan-type flow, k = geometric constant) | µm |
| 9 | $T_{set} \geq HDT + 10°C$ | Minimum setting temperature for permanent pattern retention | °C |
| 10 | $S_{ratio} = \dfrac{v_{web}}{v_{roll}}$ | Speed ratio; **must equal 1.00 ± 0.005** for undistorted patterns | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constraint for Examinations:** The web speed $v_{web}$ **must equal** the embossing roller tangential speed $v_{roll}$. A mismatch greater than **±0.5 %** causes **pattern elongation, smearing, or doubling** — a frequent industrial defect and a common KTU theory question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Embossers are indispensable in:
- **Hygiene films:** Non-slip diaper backsheets, embossed LDPE/LLDPE.
- **Agricultural films:** Anti-condensation ("anti-fog") greenhouse films with pyramid patterns.
- **Decorative laminates:** PVC wall coverings with wood-grain or stone textures.
- **Packaging:** Matt/gloss contrast films, anti-block surfaces for stackability.
- **Technical textiles:** Embossed TPU/PVC for artificial leather.
- **Light management:** Light-diffusing films in LCD backlights (prismatic patterns).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hertzian Nip Contact Length

**Objective:** Establish an analytical expression for the effective nip length $L_{nip}$ between the embossing roller (radius $R_e$) and backup roller (radius $R_b$) under a line load $P_n$.

**Step 1 — Geometry of contact:**
Consider two cylinders pressed together with a total normal load $F_n$ distributed over a web width $L_w$. The contact zone is approximated as a narrow rectangle (line contact) because the roller lengths are far greater than the contact width.

**Step 2 — Hertzian line-contact assumption:**
For line contact (cylindrical bodies), the half-width of the contact band is given by:

$$b = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot F_n \cdot R_{eq}}{\pi \cdot L_w \cdot E^*}}$$

where:
- $F_n$ = total normal force (N)
- $R_{eq}$ = equivalent radius of curvature (mm)
- $E^*$ = equivalent elastic modulus (MPa)
- $L_w$ = web width (mm)

**Step 3 — Effective nip length:**
The full nip length is therefore:

$$L_{nip} = 2b = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot F_n \cdot R_{eq}}{\pi \cdot L_w \cdot E^*}}$$

Simplifying the constant:

$$L_{nip} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{F_n \cdot R_{eq}}{\pi \cdot L_w \cdot E^*}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute line pressure $P_n = F_n / L_w$:**

$$L_{nip} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_n \cdot L_w \cdot R_{eq}}{\pi \cdot L_w \cdot E^*}}$$

The $L_w$ terms cancel, giving the cleaner form:

$$L_{nip} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_n \cdot R_{eq}}{\pi \cdot E^*}}$$

This is the **standard KTU-reference formula** for nip length used in embossing machine design.

**Step 5 — Substituting the equivalent radius:**

$$\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{R_e} + \dfrac{1}{R_b}$$

For a typical embossing roller $R_e = 150$ mm and rubber backup roller $R_b = 150$ mm:

$$R_{eq} = \dfrac{R_e \cdot R_b}{R_e + R_b} = \dfrac{150 \cdot 150}{300} = 75 \text{ mm}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Pattern Penetration Depth (Stefan-type squeeze flow)

**Step 1 — Physical model:**
Treat a single engraved cavity as a rectangular channel of width $w_c$, depth $h_0$, and length $L_c$ on the embossing roller. The polymer film (viscosity $\eta$) fills the cavity under pressure $P_n$.

**Step 2 — Reynolds squeeze-flow equation for viscous polymer:**

$$Q = \dfrac{w_c \cdot h^3}{12 \cdot \eta} \cdot \left(-\dfrac{dp}{dx}\right)$$

**Step 3 — For a fully filled cavity over time $t$:**

$$h_{pattern}(t) = h_0 \cdot \left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{k \cdot P_n \cdot t}{\eta}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — For short times ($k P_n t \ll \eta$), linearize using Taylor expansion $\exp(-x) \approx 1 - x$:**

$$h_{pattern}(t) \approx h_0 \cdot \dfrac{k \cdot P_n \cdot t}{\eta}$$

$$h_{pattern} \approx k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_n \cdot t}{\eta}}$$

This justifies the simplified **Formula 8** in the cheat sheet.

**Step 5 — Design implication:** Increasing $P_n$ by a factor of 4 only doubles penetration depth — confirming that embossing is **pressure-square-root-dependent**, not linearly sensitive.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A **PP cast film line** uses an embosser with $R_e = 200$ mm, $R_b = 200$ mm, line pressure $P_n = 30$ N/mm, web width $L_w = 1200$ mm, and line speed $v = 40$ m/min. The roller has 200 engraved lines per cm (linen texture). Compute: (a) roller RPM, (b) nip length, (c) residence time.

**Given Data:**
- $D_e = 2 R_e = 0.4$ m
- $v = 40$ m/min
- $P_n = 30$ N/mm = $30{,}000$ N/m
- $L_w = 1200$ mm = 1.2 m
- For steel-on-rubber, $E^* \approx 30$ MPa (composite modulus)

**Solution (a) — Roller RPM:**

$$v = \pi \cdot D_e \cdot N_e$$

$$N_e = \dfrac{v}{\pi \cdot D_e} = \dfrac{40}{\pi \cdot 0.4} = \dfrac{40}{1.2566} = 31.83 \text{ RPM}$$

**Solution (b) — Nip length:**

$$R_{eq} = \dfrac{R_e \cdot R_b}{R_e + R_b} = \dfrac{200 \cdot 200}{400} = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$$

$$L_{nip} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_n \cdot R_{eq}}{\pi \cdot E^*}}$$

$$L_{nip} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{30{,}000 \cdot 0.1}{\pi \cdot 30 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$L_{nip} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{3{,}000}{9.4248 \times 10^{7}}}$$

$$L_{nip} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{3.183 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$L_{nip} = 4 \cdot 5.642 \times 10^{-3} = 0.02257 \text{ m} \approx 22.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Solution (c) — Residence time:**

$$T_{res} = \dfrac{L_{nip}}{v} = \dfrac{0.02257 \text{ m}}{40/60 \text{ m/s}} = \dfrac{0.02257}{0.6667} = 0.0339 \text{ s} \approx 34 \text{ ms}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 34 ms residence time is typical for film embossing. If the polymer is **not** at correct temperature, no amount of pressure within the design range will produce a sharp pattern — heat and time together dominate the result.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Inline Embosser in a Cast Film Line

The following Mermaid block diagrams the integration of an embosser into a typical extrusion line, showing all functional subsystems and material flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Extruder and Die] --> B[Chill Roll Stack]
    B --> C[Preheater Roller]
    C --> D[Embossing Roller - Top]
    E[Backup Roller - Bottom Rubber] --> D
    D --> F[Cooling Roller]
    F --> G[Tension Control Idler]
    G --> H[Edge Trimmer]
    H --> I[Winder]
    J[Hydraulic or Pneumatic Loading Cylinders] -.-> D
    K[Chilled Water Circulator] -.-> D
    L[Chilled Water Circulator] -.-> F
    M[Hot Oil Heater Unit] -.-> D
    N[Drive Motor with Encoder] -.-> D
    O[PLC and HMI Control Panel] -.-> D
    O -.-> J
    O -.-> K
    O -.-> M
    O -.-> N
```

## 4.2 Detailed Subsystem Block Diagram — Embosser Internals

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FRAME[Machine Frame and Base]
        F1[Structural Steel Frame] --> F2[Roller Bearing Housings]
    end
    subgraph ROLLER[Roller Assembly]
        R1[Engraved Steel Embossing Roller] --> R2[Internal Oil or Water Channels]
        R3[Rubber Covered Backup Roller] --> R1
    end
    subgraph LOAD[Nip Loading Subsystem]
        L1[Pneumatic Cylinder Bank] --> L2[Load Cell]
        L2 --> L3[Pressure Controller]
    end
    subgraph TEMP[Temperature Control Subsystem]
        T1[Hot Oil Heater] --> T2[Roller Surface RTD Sensor]
        T2 --> T3[Temperature Controller PID]
        T4[Chilled Water Unit] --> T1
    end
    subgraph DRIVE[Drive and Synchronization]
        D1[AC Vector Motor] --> D2[Encoder Feedback]
        D2 --> D3[Line Speed Master Encoder]
        D3 --> D1
    end
    F2 --> R1
    L3 --> R1
    T3 --> R1
    D1 --> R1
```

## 4.3 Process Logic Sequence — Embosser Start-up

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start Command from HMI] --> S2{All Safety Interlocks Clear}
    S2 -->|No| S3[Alarm and Halt]
    S2 -->|Yes| S4[Pre-heat Roller to Set Temperature]
    S4 --> S5{Temperature Reached within plus minus 2 degree C}
    S5 -->|No| S4
    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Engage Backup Roller against Embossing Roller]
    S6 --> S7[Thread Web through Nip]
    S7 --> S8[Apply Gradual Nip Pressure]
    S8 --> S9[Synchronize Roller Speed to Line Speed]
    S9 --> S10[Production Run with Continuous Monitoring]
    S10 --> S11{Stop Request}
    S11 -->|No| S10
    S11 -->|Yes| S12[Release Nip Pressure Gradually]
    S12 --> S13[Cool Down and Shutdown]
```
