# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define an Embosser and state its primary function in a polymer film extrusion line.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **Embosser** is a downstream polymer processing machine used to impart a permanent three-dimensional surface pattern onto a thermoplastic film or sheet. Its primary function is to **enhance functional and aesthetic properties** of the polymer web — such as anti-slip behaviour, anti-condensation, light diffusion, decorative texture, and reduced blocking — by pressing the softened polymer against an engraved steel roller under controlled heat and pressure at the nip. *(3 marks)*

### **Q2. Why is a rubber-covered backup roller used instead of a second steel roller in an embosser?** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A rubber-covered backup roller is used for three reasons:
1. It provides **elastic compliance**, ensuring uniform line-pressure distribution across the web width even if roller surfaces are slightly imperfect. *(1 mark)*
2. It **protects the expensive engraved steel roller** from impact damage during web threading or thickness surges. *(1 mark)*
3. It **prevents polymer crushing and over-compression** of the embossed pattern by conforming locally rather than transmitting point loads. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of an inline embosser used in a polymer film line. Discuss the role of the nip, temperature, and synchronization. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The inline embosser consists of the following major parts:
- **Embossing roller (top):** Hard-chrome plated steel, engraved with the desired pattern (linen, pyramid, matt, prismatic, etc.). Internally circulated with hot oil for heating and chilled water for cooling.
- **Backup roller (bottom):** Steel core covered with silicone rubber or polyurethane (Shore A 70–85).
- **Nip loading system:** Hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders with load cells for closed-loop pressure control.
- **Drive system:** AC vector motor with encoder, electronically synchronized with the upstream extrusion line speed.
- **Web handling system:** Idler rollers, edge guides, tension controllers.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
The polymer film at a temperature just above its Vicat softening point enters the nip. The engraved roller presses the pattern into the film under a line pressure of typically 10–80 N/mm. The residence time in the nip is approximately 25–50 ms. Immediately after the nip, chilled water circulation in the embossing roller drops the film temperature below the HDT, **thermally setting** the pattern permanently.

**Role of Critical Parameters (1 mark):**
- **Nip pressure:** Governs pattern depth (square-root dependency on pressure).
- **Temperature:** Must exceed softening point for deformation but remain below melting point for stability.
- **Synchronization:** Web speed must match roller tangential speed within ±0.5 % to avoid pattern distortion.

#### (b) A polymer cast film line operates at 60 m/min. The embosser roller diameter is 250 mm. Calculate the roller RPM, and explain what happens if the line speed deviates from the roller speed by more than 1 %. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given:**
- $v = 60$ m/min
- $D_e = 250$ mm = $0.25$ m

**Step 2 — Apply tangential speed formula:**
$$v = \pi \cdot D_e \cdot N_e$$

$$N_e = \dfrac{v}{\pi \cdot D_e} = \dfrac{60}{\pi \cdot 0.25} = \dfrac{60}{0.7854}$$

$$\boxed{N_e = 76.39 \text{ RPM}}$$

**Step 3 — Discuss speed-mismatch consequences (4 marks):**
If line speed deviates from roller speed by more than 1 %:
- If $v_{web} > v_{roll}$: The pattern is **stretched longitudinally**, appearing elongated and faded; pattern depth is reduced.
- If $v_{web} < v_{roll}$: The pattern is **compressed**, producing sharper but distorted images; risk of polymer wrinkling or sticking to the engraved roller.
- At extreme mismatch (>5 %): **Pattern doubling, web tearing, or roller wrapping** occurs, leading to line shutdown.

Therefore, **electronic synchronization via a master line encoder** is mandatory in modern embossers.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) Classify the different types of embossers used in polymer processing. Explain any two with their applications. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Embossers (3 marks):**
1. **Based on roller configuration:**
   - **Two-roller (nip) embosser** — most common in film lines.
   - **Three-roller embosser** — used for simultaneous embossing and lamination.
2. **Based on heating method:**
   - **Internally heated** (hot-oil circulation).
   - **Externally heated** (infrared or flame, less common).
3. **Based on pattern type:**
   - **Random pattern** (linen, sand, matt).
   - **Geometric pattern** (pyramid, prism, hexagonal).
   - **Custom engraved** (logos, wood grain, leather).
4. **Based on process position:**
   - **In-line embosser** (immediately after chill roll).
   - **Off-line embosser** (separate unit, used for laminates).

**Detailed Explanation of Two Types (4 marks):**

**Type 1 — In-line Two-Roller Embosser:**
- Used for cast PP, PE, and PET films.
- Integrated directly after the chill roll for thermal efficiency.
- Application: Hygiene films, anti-fog agricultural films.

**Type 2 — Off-line Three-Roller Embosser:**
- The polymer web is preheated on a heated roller, then passes through the nip of an engraved roller and a rubber roller, then over a cooling roller.
- Application: PVC laminates, decorative sheets, synthetic leather.

#### (b) Derive the expression for the nip length $L_{nip}$ in an embosser and calculate its value for $R_e = 150$ mm, $R_b = 150$ mm, $P_n = 25$ N/mm, and $E^* = 30$ MPa. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Equivalent radius:**
$$\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{R_e} + \dfrac{1}{R_b} = \dfrac{1}{150} + \dfrac{1}{150} = \dfrac{2}{150}$$

$$R_{eq} = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the nip length formula:**
$$L_{nip} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_n \cdot R_{eq}}{\pi \cdot E^*}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**
- $P_n = 25$ N/mm = $25{,}000$ N/m
- $R_{eq} = 0.075$ m
- $E^* = 30 \times 10^6$ Pa

$$L_{nip} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{25{,}000 \cdot 0.075}{\pi \cdot 30 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$L_{nip} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{1{,}875}{9.4248 \times 10^{7}}}$$

$$L_{nip} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{1.9894 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$L_{nip} = 4 \cdot 4.4603 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{L_{nip} \approx 0.01784 \text{ m} \approx 17.84 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute residence time (if speed $v = 50$ m/min):**
$$T_{res} = \dfrac{L_{nip}}{v} = \dfrac{0.01784}{50/60} = 0.0214 \text{ s} \approx 21 \text{ ms}$$

This residence time is sufficient for polymer flow into the engraving cavities for a well-tempered PP film.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in Embosser Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions:** Mixing mm with m in Hertzian formulas causes 10² to 10³ order errors. Always convert $P_n$ to **N/m** before substitution.
> 2. **Omitting speed synchronization discussion:** Examiners specifically award **2–3 marks** for explaining the consequence of speed mismatch. Students who only compute RPM lose these marks.
> 3. **Confusing Embosser with Calendar:** A calendar controls **thickness and surface finish**; an embosser creates **3-D patterns**. This conceptual error costs full marks in definition questions.
> 4. **Missing the rubber-backup rationale:** The Shore-A hardness (60–90) and elasticity rationale is frequently tested. Vague answers like "it provides support" fetch only 1 of 3 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring thermal setting:** A common omission — students describe pattern formation but forget to explain **how the pattern is locked in** by cooling below HDT.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Embosser = downstream machine that imparts **3-D surface patterns** on polymer film/sheet using a heated engraved steel roller and a rubber backup roller at a controlled nip.
- **Working Principle:** Heat + Pressure + Time at nip → pattern replication → cooling → permanent thermal setting.
- **Key Components:** Engraved steel roller, rubber backup roller, nip loading system, hot-oil/chilled-water circulation, drive motor with encoder, web handling.
- **Process Parameters to Memorize:** $v$ = 5–150 m/min; $P_n$ = 10–80 N/mm; $T_e$ = 80–160 °C; $d_p$ = 5–200 µm.
- **Master Formulas:**
  - Roller RPM: $N_e = v / (\pi D_e)$
  - Nip length: $L_{nip} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{P_n R_{eq} / (\pi E^*)}$
  - Equivalent radius: $1/R_{eq} = 1/R_e + 1/R_b$
  - Residence time: $T_{res} = L_{nip} / v$
- **Critical Synchronization Rule:** $v_{web} = v_{roll}$ within **±0.5 %** — mismatch causes pattern distortion.
- **Rubber Backup Roller Role:** Provides elastic compliance, protects engraving, ensures uniform line pressure (Shore A 70–85).
- **Thermal Setting Rule:** $T_{set} \geq HDT + 10 °C$ for permanent pattern retention.
- **Distinguish From:** Calendar (thickness/finish), Coater (surface chemistry), Printer (2-D ink).
- **Applications:** Hygiene films, anti-fog agricultural films, decorative laminates, light-diffusing films, synthetic leather, anti-block packaging.
- **Pattern Depth Dependency:** Pattern depth $\propto \sqrt{P_n \cdot t / \eta}$ — square-root dependence on pressure and time.
- **Typical Defects to Remember:** Pattern elongation (web too fast), pattern compression (web too slow), pattern wash-out (low temperature), double imaging (roller deflection — solved by crown correction).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Winder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINURGY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Winder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Winder (in Polymer Processing):** A **winder** is a downstream auxiliary machine in a polymer film/sheet extrusion line whose primary function is to **convert the continuously produced flat web (film or sheet) into a finished roll** of consistent diameter, density, and tension, suitable for storage, transport, and downstream conversion (slitting, lamination, printing, bag-making).

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, the winder is the **last critical process station** in a film/sheet line. The quality of the wound roll directly determines the **commercial value, yield, and processability** of the entire upstream extrusion investment. A winder is therefore an *integral process instrument*, not merely a packaging device.

**Sub-systems of a typical KTU-grade Winder:**
- **Unwind station** (for in-line re-winding or pre-roll loading)
- **Tension isolation / Dancer roll assembly**
- **Nip / Lay-on roll assembly** (in surface winders)
- **Spindle / Shaft drive assembly** (in center winders)
- **Slitting section** (razor or shear cut)
- **Web guiding / edge tracking system**
- **Trim removal and edge rewind** (spoolers)
- **Automatic roll changeover (turret)** mechanism

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Cotton-Reel Effect":**
> Imagine you are winding a long thread onto an empty cotton reel by hand. As the reel gets fatter, you must (a) **slow down the speed of your fingers** (otherwise the thread will snap or stretch), and (b) **tighten the thread** progressively less (otherwise the outer layers will crush the inner layers, producing a rock-hard reel that cannot unwind). A winder is the *automated, precision-engineered version* of this hand. It simultaneously solves the **tension**, **speed**, and **roll-density** problems in real time as the roll diameter grows from a few centimetres to almost a metre.

## 1.3 Why a Winder Matters in a Polymer Line

| Problem if Winder is Poor | Industrial Consequence |
|---|---|
| Uneven tension | Film stretching, gauge variation, optical defects |
| Telescoping (axial slip) | Roll unusable, transport damage |
| Starred / blocked roll | Unwinding impossible, customer rejection |
| Wrong roll hardness | Creep (PVC), blocking (LDPE), wrinkling (PP) |
| Edge instability | Trim loss, width off-spec |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A winder is *also* a **quality-control instrument** because winding tension is the only parameter that can be measurably varied at the end of a line to compensate for upstream thickness irregularities (a tighter nip on a thick spot slightly reduces the local thickness before it is "frozen" into the roll).

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (must memorise)

- **Linear web speed:** typically **5 – 600 m/min** (cast film) up to **1200 m/min** (BOPP, PET)
- **Finished roll diameter:** up to **1200 mm** standard, **1500 mm** jumbo
- **Web width:** from **300 mm** to **10 m** (cast/sheet lines)
- **Tension range:** **5 – 500 N/m** of web width depending on gauge
- **Roll density target:** **0.55 – 0.95 g/cm³** depending on polymer
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Block Analysis of a Winder

A winder performs **four simultaneous functions**, and every commercial winder design is essentially a *different way of solving* these four coupled problems:

1. **Transport** — pulling the web forward at controlled line speed.
2. **Tension control** — establishing and maintaining a programmed longitudinal stress.
3. **Winding** — laying the web on the roll in a defined pattern (tapered or constant tension).
4. **Slitting & roll finishing** — cutting the wide web into customer widths and ejecting the finished roll.

> [!NOTE]
> The **type** of a winder is defined entirely by *how it generates the winding torque* (Item 3 above). This leads to the canonical KTU classification given in §2.2.

## 2.2 KTU Classification of Winders

### (A) Surface Winder (Drum / Drum-driven Winder)

- A rubber-covered **contact roll (drum)** rotates the wound roll by **friction**.
- Wound roll is *not* driven from its own shaft; it is *pressed* against the drum.
- **Constant surface speed** = constant line speed ⇒ simple tension control.
- Used for **thin films (LDPE, LLDPE, CPP)** and high-output lines.

### (B) Center Winder (Shaft Winder / Spindle Winder)

- The **mandrel/shaft** itself is driven by a torque motor (or DC/Servo).
- Winding torque $T_w$ is supplied *through the shaft* to the roll core.
- Tension rises as roll diameter grows (because $T = F \times r$).
- Used for **thick webs, sheet, BOPP, BOPET, paper-like polymers**.

### (C) Gap Winder (Center-Surface Hybrid)

- A **small gap (≈ 2–5 mm)** is maintained between the contact drum and the wound roll.
- Removes the need for a heavy lay-on roll.
- Combined advantage of surface speed control and center torque regulation.

### (D) Combination / Dual-Mode Winder

- Operates as **surface winder at start** (low diameter, high speed) and **transitions to center winder** as diameter grows.
- Industry standard for **modern BOPP / BOPET lines**.

## 2.3 The Four Operating Zones of a Center Winder

A center winder has a characteristic **T–n (torque vs. speed) curve** divided into four zones:

| Zone | Behaviour | Control Variable |
|------|-----------|------------------|
| **I – Acceleration** | Roll speed ramps up | Torque |
| **II – Constant Tension** | Stable winding, $T$ fixed | Torque decreases as $D$ grows |
| **III – Ramp-down** | Taper tension starts | Taper ratio $k$ |
| **IV – Stop / Changeover** | Web cut, new roll engaged | Brake / clutch |

## 2.4 Taper Tension Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taper tension** means the winding tension is *deliberately* programmed to **decrease** as the roll diameter grows. This compensates for the **"superposition stress"** of a cylindrical roll stack, which would otherwise produce a rock-hard, unusable roll.

The **taper ratio** $k$ is defined as:

$$k = \frac{T_{\text{final}}}{T_{\text{initial}}}$$

Typical industrial values: $k = 0.30$ (cast PP) up to $k = 0.70$ (thick LLDPE).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **All formulas are stated in SI units.** Memorise the **physical meaning** of each symbol, not just the equation.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbols & Units |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Web speed at winder | $v = \pi D N$ | $D$ = roll diameter [m], $N$ = rotational speed [rev/s] |
| 2 | Roll diameter growth | $D(t) = D_c + \frac{2 \, t \, v \, t_{\text{op}}}{\pi D}$ accumulated $\Rightarrow D_n = \sqrt{D_0^{2} + \frac{4 L t}{\pi}}$ | $L$ = length wound, $t$ = web thickness |
| 3 | Diameter after $n$ turns | $D_n = D_0 + 2 n t$ | $D_0$ = core diameter |
| 4 | Surface-winder tension (from capstan eq.) | $F_{\text{out}} = F_{\text{in}} \, e^{\mu \theta}$ | $\mu$ = friction, $\theta$ = wrap angle [rad] |
| 5 | Center-winder torque | $T_w = F \cdot r$ | $F$ = web tension, $r$ = instantaneous roll radius |
| 6 | Winding power | $P = F \cdot v$ | $F$ in N, $v$ in m/s, $P$ in W |
| 7 | Tension from torque (rearranged) | $F = \dfrac{T_w}{r}$ | Used to derive the torque-vs-diameter law |
| 8 | Torque profile (constant tension) | $T_w(D) = F \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}$ | Linear in $D$ |
| 9 | Tapered torque law | $T_w(D) = F_0 \cdot \dfrac{D_0}{2} \cdot k^{(D-D_0)/D}$ or linearised: $T_w(D) = T_0 \left[1 - (1-k)\dfrac{D-D_0}{D_{\max}-D_0}\right]$ | $k$ = taper ratio |
| 10 | Roll density (in-roll) | $\rho_r = \dfrac{4 m}{\pi \left(D_f^{2} - D_c^{2}\right) W}$ | $W$ = web width, $D_f$ = final dia, $D_c$ = core dia |
| 11 | Winding ratio (rigidity) | $E_r = \dfrac{E_{\text{film}} \, t}{F / W}$ | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Maximum allowable tension (no neck-in) | $F_{\max} = E \, t \, \varepsilon_{\text{allow}}$ | $\varepsilon_{\text{allow}}$ = strain limit (≈ 0.001 for PP) |
| 13 | Capstan (Euler) — surface winder | $T_1 = T_2 \, e^{\mu \theta}$ | Same as row 4; bidirectional |

> **Convention used above:** For absolute value $\vert x \vert$ or modulus of a complex expression, use $\vert x \vert$ notation in your own notes to avoid any markdown parser conflict inside a `| ... |` cell.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BOPP / BOPET film lines:** Two-axis turret winders with 1500 mm OD capability.
- **Stretch film (LLDPE):** Surface winders with dual lay-on rolls for very low gauge.
- **PVC calendered sheet:** Center winders with very high taper to prevent plasticiser migration.
- **Non-woven / Spunbond:** Center winders with low tension and high roll-softness.
- **Paper-laminate film:** Combination winders (surface → center transition) for tension stability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are framed as **worked KTU-examination problems**. Each algebraic transition is shown explicitly. All multi-line mathematics is typeset in an `aligned` block.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Roll Diameter Growth Equation

**Statement:** Starting from a core of diameter $D_c$ and winding a film of uniform thickness $t$ at linear speed $v$ for a time $\tau$, derive an expression for the final roll diameter $D_f$.

**Step 1 — Express the length of film wound.**
The web travels at speed $v$ for time $\tau$, so the total wound length is:
$$L = v \cdot \tau$$

**Step 2 — Express the volume of film on the roll.**
Volume is length × cross-section:
$$V = L \cdot t \cdot W$$
where $W$ is the web width (assumed constant).

**Step 3 — Equate to the annular cylinder volume of the roll.**
The roll (excluding the core) is an annular cylinder of outer diameter $D_f$ and inner (core) diameter $D_c$:
$$V = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_f^{2} - D_c^{2}\right) \cdot W$$

**Step 4 — Equate (2) and (3).**
$$\frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_f^{2} - D_c^{2}\right) W = v \tau t W$$

Cancel $W$:
$$\frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_f^{2} - D_c^{2}\right) = v \tau t$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $D_f$.**
$$D_f^{2} = D_c^{2} + \frac{4 v \tau t}{\pi}$$
$$\boxed{\,D_f = \sqrt{\,D_c^{2} + \dfrac{4 v \tau t}{\pi}\,}\,}$$

**Sanity check:** As $\tau \to 0$, $D_f \to D_c$ ✔. As $\tau$ increases, $D_f$ grows like $\sqrt{\tau}$ ✔.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Center-Winder Torque Profile for Constant Tension

**Statement:** A center winder must hold a constant web tension $F$ throughout the build. Derive the required torque $T_w(D)$ as a function of the instantaneous roll diameter $D$.

**Step 1 — Free-body of a wound web element.**
The web is pulled by tension $F$ tangentially off the roll. The torque required to *wind* the next layer against this tension is the tension multiplied by the instantaneous lever arm (the current roll radius):
$$T_w = F \cdot r$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the radius in terms of diameter.**
Since $r = D/2$:
$$T_w = F \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the slope.**
$$\frac{dT_w}{dD} = \frac{F}{2} = \text{constant}$$

So the torque must rise **linearly** with diameter to maintain constant tension. At the start ($D = D_c$):
$$T_{w,\text{start}} = F \cdot \frac{D_c}{2}$$

At the end ($D = D_f$):
$$T_{w,\text{end}} = F \cdot \frac{D_f}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Ratio of end to start torque.**
$$\frac{T_{w,\text{end}}}{T_{w,\text{start}}} = \frac{D_f}{D_c}$$

For a typical LDPE film line: $D_c = 0.076$ m (3-inch core) and $D_f = 0.6$ m, giving a ratio of ≈ 7.9. Therefore the **drive motor must be sized for ≈ 8× torque at the end of the cycle** as compared to the start — a critical design parameter.

$$\boxed{\,T_w(D) = \dfrac{F D}{2}\,}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tapered Torque Law (Linear Taper)

**Statement:** A winder operator specifies a taper ratio $k = T_{f}/T_{i}$ (final-to-initial torque). Derive the linear-taper torque equation.

**Step 1 — Define the linear interpolation.**
A linear taper means the torque falls linearly with diameter from $T_i$ at $D_c$ to $T_f = kT_i$ at $D_{\max}$:
$$T_w(D) = T_i + \left(T_f - T_i\right)\frac{D - D_c}{D_{\max} - D_c}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $T_f = kT_i$.**
$$T_w(D) = T_i + \left(kT_i - T_i\right)\frac{D - D_c}{D_{\max} - D_c}$$
$$T_w(D) = T_i\left[\,1 - (1-k)\,\frac{D - D_c}{D_{\max} - D_c}\,\right]$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of the *initial* tension $F_0$.**
Since $T_i = F_0 \cdot D_c/2$:
$$\boxed{\,T_w(D) = \dfrac{F_0 D_c}{2}\left[\,1 - (1-k)\,\dfrac{D - D_c}{D_{\max} - D_c}\,\right]\,}$$

**Step 4 — Special cases.**
- If $k = 1$ (no taper) ⇒ $T_w(D) = F_0 D_c/2$ = constant... but this is *wrong* for constant tension! It means a $k=1$ taper corresponds to **constant torque** (used for stiffness-controlled materials), not constant tension.
- If $k = 0$ (full taper) ⇒ torque drops linearly to zero — used for very soft, non-creep-resistant webs.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Surface-Winder Tension (Capstan / Euler Equation)

**Statement:** A web wraps a contact drum through a wrap angle $\theta$ (in radians) with coefficient of friction $\mu$ between the web and the drum surface. If the **outgoing** tension is $F_1$ and the **incoming** tension is $F_2$, derive the relationship between them.

**Step 1 — Free-body of a small element of web on the drum.**
Consider an element subtending angle $d\theta$ at the drum centre. The tensions on the two ends are $F$ and $F + dF$, the normal reaction is $dN$, and friction is $\mu dN$ acting tangentially opposite to the slip direction.

**Step 2 — Force balance in the normal direction.**
$$dN = F \, d\theta$$

(since for small angle, $\sin d\theta \approx d\theta$ and the two tension components sum to $F \, d\theta$).

**Step 3 — Force balance in the tangential direction.**
$$dF = \mu \, dN = \mu F \, d\theta$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate.**
$$\frac{dF}{F} = \mu \, d\theta$$

Integrating from $F_2$ (incoming) to $F_1$ (outgoing) over the wrap angle $\theta$:
$$\int_{F_2}^{F_1}\frac{dF}{F} = \mu \int_{0}^{\theta} d\theta$$
$$\ln\frac{F_1}{F_2} = \mu \theta$$
$$\boxed{\,F_1 = F_2 \, e^{\mu \theta}\,}$$

This is the classical **Euler–Eytelwein capstan equation**, and it is the *fundamental reason* a surface winder can develop a controlled, high traction force without driving the roll from the inside.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Roll Density from Winding Parameters

**Statement:** Derive the in-roll density $\rho_r$ of a wound film of width $W$, web thickness $t$, total length $L$, on a core of diameter $D_c$, producing a final roll of diameter $D_f$.

**Step 1 — Mass of the roll (film only).**
$$m = L \cdot t \cdot W \cdot \rho_{\text{polymer}}$$

**Step 2 — Volume of the wound roll (annular cylinder).**
$$V_{\text{roll}} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(D_f^{2} - D_c^{2}\right) W$$

**Step 3 — In-roll density = mass / volume.**
$$\rho_r = \frac{m}{V_{\text{roll}}} = \frac{L \, t \, W \, \rho_{\text{polymer}}}{\dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D_f^{2} - D_c^{2}\right) W}$$

Cancel $W$:
$$\rho_r = \frac{4 \, L \, t \, \rho_{\text{polymer}}}{\pi \left(D_f^{2} - D_c^{2}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the *void fraction*.**
$$\boxed{\,\rho_r = \rho_{\text{polymer}} \cdot \left[\dfrac{4 L t}{\pi (D_f^{2} - D_c^{2})}\right]\,}$$

The bracketed term is the **packing factor** (always $\le 1$). A perfectly wound roll with no air has packing factor = 1. Real industrial rolls have packing factor = 0.85 – 0.95 for stiff films and 0.55 – 0.75 for soft films.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A BOPP film line runs at 250 m/min. Film thickness is 20 µm, web width is 6 m, core diameter is 152 mm, final roll diameter is 800 mm. The winder is a center type with constant tension $F = 80$ N/m of width. Calculate:
1. The total film length on one roll.
2. The time to wind one roll.
3. The torque at the start and end of winding.
4. The in-roll density (take $\rho_{\text{BOPP}} = 905$ kg/m³).

**Step 1 — Length on roll.**
$$L = \frac{\pi (D_f^{2} - D_c^{2})}{4 t}$$
$$L = \frac{\pi [(0.800)^{2} - (0.152)^{2}]}{4 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}$$
$$L = \frac{\pi \times (0.6400 - 0.0231)}{80 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{\pi \times 0.6169}{8 \times 10^{-5}}$$
$$L \approx \frac{1.9380}{8 \times 10^{-5}} = 24{,}225 \text{ m} \approx 24.2 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2 — Time to wind one roll.**
$$\tau = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{24{,}225 \text{ m}}{250/60 \text{ m/s}} = \frac{24{,}225}{4.1667} = 5814 \text{ s} \approx 96.9 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Torques.**
Total tension force: $F_{\text{total}} = 80 \text{ N/m} \times 6 \text{ m} = 480$ N.
- Start: $T_{w,\text{start}} = 480 \times 0.152/2 = 36.5$ N·m.
- End: $T_{w,\text{end}} = 480 \times 0.800/2 = 192.0$ N·m.

**Step 4 — In-roll density.**
$$\rho_r = \frac{4 L t \rho}{\pi (D_f^{2} - D_c^{2})} = \frac{4 \times 24{,}225 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 905}{\pi \times 0.6169}$$
$$\rho_r = \frac{1753.3}{1.9380} = 904.7 \text{ kg/m}^{3} \approx 905 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$$

This indicates a near-perfectly wound BOPP roll (≈ 99.97 % packing — typical of highly oriented stiff film).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Typical Center–Surface Combination Winder

The following block diagram captures the **signal, mechanical, and web-path interactions** of a modern polymer-film winder as a block-level functional architecture (per the protocol's Mermaid safeguard for non-CS / physical systems).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    upstream[Upstream Extrusion Line] --> web1[Flat Web Take-Up]
    web1 --> dancer1[Dancer Roll Bank 1]
    dancer1 --> slitter[Slitting Station]
    slitter --> web2[Trimmed Webs]
    web2 --> edgeRew[Edge Trim Rewind]
    web2 --> guide1[Web Guide / Edge Sensor]
    guide1 --> nipRoll[Nip / Lay-on Roll]
    nipRoll --> contactDrum[Contact Drum - Surface Drive]
    web2 --> spindle[Center Drive Spindle with Torque Motor]
    spindle --> winderRoll[Wound Roll Building]
    contactDrum --> winderRoll
    winderRoll --> finishedRoll[Finished Roll Eject]
    spindle --> tensionCtrl[Tension Controller]
    dancer1 --> tensionCtrl
    tensionCtrl --> taperProf[Taper Profile Generator]
    taperProf --> spindle
    tensionCtrl --> contactDrum
    tensionCtrl --> nipRoll
    nipRoll --> pressureCtrl[Nip Pressure Regulator]
    finishedRoll --> turret[Turret / Automatic Changeover]
    edgeRew --> scrap[Scrap Collection]

    classDef sensor fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#a40000,color:#000;
    classDef actuator fill:#dff0d8,stroke:#38761d,color:#000;
    classDef control fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394,color:#000;
    classDef web fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000;
    class dancer1,guide1,tensionCtrl,taperProf,pressureCtrl sensor
    class spindle,contactDrum,nipRoll,slitter,turret actuator
    class upstream,web1,web2,edgeRew,finishedRoll,winderRoll,scrap web
```

**How to read this diagram for the exam:**
- **Red nodes** = sensors & feedback.
- **Green nodes** = actuators (motors, rolls, blades).
- **Yellow nodes** = the *web itself* and the *physical rolls* being built.
- **Blue nodes** = control elements.
- The **spindle ↔ tension-controller ↔ taper-profile** loop is the *closed-loop torque control* that characterises a center winder.
- The **contact-drum ↔ tension-controller ↔ nip-pressure-regulator** loop is the *closed-loop surface-speed* loop that characterises a surface winder.

## 4.2 Comparison Block — Surface vs. Center vs. Gap Winder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SURFACE[Surface Winder Block]
        S1[Web enters] --> S2[Wraps Contact Drum]
        S2 --> S3[Lay-on Roll Presses Wound Roll to Drum]
        S3 --> S4[Constant Surface Speed = Constant Line Speed]
        S4 --> S5[Torque from Friction - mu W theta]
    end

    subgraph CENTER[Center Winder Block]
        C1[Web enters] --> C2[Wrapped around Core on Driven Spindle]
        C2 --> C3[Spindle Driven by Torque Motor]
        C3 --> C4[Torque Tw = F x r - Linear in D]
        C4 --> C5[Tension Rises with Diameter - Taper Required]
    end

    subgraph GAP[Gap Winder Block]
        G1[Web enters] --> G2[Small Gap 2-5 mm to Drum]
        G2 --> G3[Center Torque plus Frictional Assist]
        G3 --> G4[Combined Speed and Torque Control]
        G4 --> G5[Used in High-Speed BOPP]
    end

    classDef blockA fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b,color:#000;
    classDef blockB fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000;
    classDef blockC fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000;
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5 blockA
    class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5 blockB
    class G1,G2,G3,G4,G5 blockC
```

## 4.3 Web Path Schematic (Inline View of a 2-Axis Turret Winder)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    incoming[Incoming Web] --> guide[Web Guide]
    guide --> slitterA[Razor Slitter Bank]
    slitterA --> spread[Spread / Spreader Roll]
    spread --> nipA[Nip Roll A]
    spread --> nipB[Nip Roll B]
    nipA --> positionA[Turret Position A - Winding]
    nipB --> positionB[Turret Position B - Winding]
    positionA -.finished.- ejectA[Roll Eject Station A]
    positionB -.finished.- ejectB[Roll Eject Station B]
    positionA <-->|rotation| positionB
    nipA --> driveA[Servo Drive A]
    nipB --> driveB[Servo Drive B]
    driveA --> ctl[Central PLC]
    driveB --> ctl
    ctl --> operator[HMI Operator Panel]

    classDef act fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,color:#000;
    classDef ctrl fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394,color:#000;
    classDef web fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#7f6000,color:#000;
    class slitterA,spread,nipA,nipB,positionA,positionB,driveA,driveB,ejectA,ejectB act
    class ctl,operator ctrl
    class incoming,guide web
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a 2-axis turret?** When roll A is full, the turret *rotates 180°* in < 30 s and roll B — which has been winding in parallel — becomes the active roll. Meanwhile, the finished roll A is ejected, a new core is loaded, and the cycle repeats. This achieves **100 % line uptime** for slitting-and-winding, which is the economic driver of every modern BOPP / BOPET / cast-film plant.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### Q1. Define a *winder* in a polymer film line and list its four primary functions. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
A **winder** is the downstream machine in a polymer film/sheet extrusion line that converts the continuous flat web into a finished roll of controlled diameter, density, and tension.

Its four primary functions are:
1. **Transport** – pulling the web forward at programmed line speed.
2. **Tension control** – establishing longitudinal web stress.
3. **Winding** – laying the web on a core in a defined pattern.
4. **Slitting & roll finishing** – cutting to width and ejecting the roll.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *T³ + S* → **T**ransport, **T**ension, **T**wisting (winding), **S**litting.

---

### Q2. What is *taper tension*? Why is it required in a center winder? [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
**Taper tension** is a programmed *reduction* of web winding tension as the roll diameter grows. The **taper ratio** is defined as $k = T_{\text{final}}/T_{\text{initial}}$ (0.3 ≤ k ≤ 0.7 typical).

It is required in a **center winder** because, in such a winder, the torque $T_w = F \times r$ supplied through the shaft automatically creates a *rising* tension as the roll grows. Without taper, the outer layers would crush the inner layers, producing:
- a **rock-hard, blocked roll** (LDPE)
- **telescoping** (axial slip on the core)
- **creep** (PVC, plasticiser migration)
- **unwind problems** at the customer.

Taper therefore protects the roll structure by deliberately reducing tension as more layers are added.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations**, every Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. An **internal choice** is provided between **Question A** and **Question B**. Students must answer **one** of them.

---

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

#### (a) With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working of a **center winder**. Derive the relation between winding torque and roll diameter for **constant tension** winding. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**1. Working principle (3 marks):**
- The web is fed onto a **core mounted on a driven mandrel (spindle)**.
- The spindle is coupled to a **torque motor** (or DC / servo drive) capable of closed-loop torque control.
- The web is laid in successive layers onto the core; the roll diameter grows continuously.
- A **dancer roll** upstream senses the web tension and feeds a feedback signal to the tension controller.
- A **taper profile generator** programs the torque-vs-diameter law.
- An **automatic roll changeover** (turret) removes the finished roll and starts a new one without stopping the line.

**2. Derivation (4 marks):**

> **Free-body of a wound web element:** Tension $F$ acts tangentially at radius $r$.
> $$T_w = F \cdot r = F \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$
> Therefore torque grows **linearly** with diameter.
> $$\boxed{\,T_w(D) = \dfrac{F D}{2}\,}$$
> with end-to-start torque ratio $T_{w,\text{end}}/T_{w,\text{start}} = D_f/D_c$ — a critical motor-sizing factor.

---

#### (b) Explain **surface winder** with diagram. Derive the **capstan (Euler) equation** relating incoming and outgoing tensions, and state its engineering significance. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**1. Surface winder description (3 marks):**
- The wound roll is *not* driven from inside; it is *pressed* by a **lay-on (nip) roll** against a rotating **contact drum**.
- The drum's surface speed = line speed = constant ⇒ excellent tension stability.
- Used for **thin flexible films** (LDPE, LLDPE, CPP) and high-speed lines.
- No tap of torque through the shaft; friction does all the work.

**2. Capstan derivation (4 marks):**

> **Free-body of a web element** subtending $d\theta$ on the drum:
> - Normal balance: $dN = F\,d\theta$.
> - Tangential balance: $dF = \mu\, dN = \mu F\,d\theta$.
> - Separate and integrate: $\int_{F_2}^{F_1} dF/F = \mu\int_0^\theta d\theta$.
> - Result: $F_1 = F_2\, e^{\mu \theta}$.

**3. Engineering significance:**
- The exponential gain $e^{\mu \theta}$ allows a small incoming tension to develop a *very large* outgoing traction with only a modest wrap angle.
- For $\mu = 0.3$ and $\theta = \pi$ (half wrap), $F_1/F_2 = e^{0.3 \pi} \approx 2.57$.
- For a full wrap ($\theta = 2\pi$), $F_1/F_2 \approx 6.6$.
- This is why a **surface winder can transmit huge winding forces** through pure friction — *no slip, no internal torque needed*.

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

#### (a) Compare **surface, center, and gap winders** in a tabular form. State one typical polymer/process application for each. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Surface Winder | Center Winder | Gap Winder |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Drive** | Contact drum (friction) | Mandrel/spindle (torque) | Both — hybrid |
| **Tension control** | Excellent (constant line speed) | Requires taper profile | Excellent |
| **Speed range** | High (up to 1200 m/min) | Moderate–high | High |
| **Roll quality at large D** | Limited (drum slip) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Torque rise with D** | None (drum-driven) | Linear | Compensated |
| **Hardware cost** | Low–medium | Medium | High |
| **Typical application** | Cast LDPE/LLDPE film | BOPP, BOPET, sheet | High-speed BOPP |
| **Polymer example** | LDPE cast film 20 µm | BOPET 12 µm | BOPP 30 µm |

**3 marks for table, 2 marks for application examples, 2 marks for engineering commentary.**

---

#### (b) A **center winder** for cast PP film has the following data: line speed 120 m/min, web width 4 m, film thickness 30 µm, core diameter 76 mm, final roll diameter 600 mm, constant winding tension 60 N/m. Compute: (i) total film length on one roll, (ii) winding time per roll, (iii) start and end torque, (iv) the taper ratio required if final tension is to be 30 N/m. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $v = 120$ m/min = 2 m/s, $W = 4$ m, $t = 30$ µm = $30 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $D_c = 0.076$ m, $D_f = 0.6$ m, $F = 60$ N/m ⇒ $F_{\text{total}} = 240$ N.

**(i) Film length on roll:**
$$L = \frac{\pi(D_f^{2} - D_c^{2})}{4t} = \frac{\pi(0.36 - 0.00578)}{1.2 \times 10^{-4}}$$
$$L = \frac{\pi \times 0.3542}{1.2 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{1.1127}{1.2 \times 10^{-4}} = 9273 \text{ m} \approx 9.27 \text{ km}$$

**(ii) Winding time:**
$$\tau = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{9273}{2} = 4636 \text{ s} \approx 77.3 \text{ min}$$

**(iii) Torques:**
- $T_{w,\text{start}} = 240 \times 0.076/2 = 9.12$ N·m.
- $T_{w,\text{end}} = 240 \times 0.6/2 = 72.0$ N·m.

**(iv) Taper ratio:**
- Initial tension $F_0 = 60$ N/m, final $F_f = 30$ N/m.
- $k = F_f/F_0 = 30/60 = 0.5$.

> **Answer check:** Tension reduced by 50 % — appropriate for PP cast film (stiffer than PE; needs more taper to prevent roll hardness).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in winder questions:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Forgetting to convert **m/min to m/s** in power/speed calculations (loses 2 marks silently).
> 2. **Confusing $T$ (tension, N/m) with $F$ (total tension, N):** Always clarify whether your formula uses *specific* tension (per unit width) or *total* tension. State it explicitly in the first line of the solution.
> 3. **Capstan equation direction:** Many students write $F_1 = F_2 e^{-\mu\theta}$ (sign error). $F_1$ is the *outgoing* tension (larger), $F_2$ is *incoming* (smaller). Cost: 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention taper ratio** in center winder questions. Examiners award 2 marks for explicitly computing / stating the taper ratio. If you *omit* it, you lose those marks.
> 5. **Not stating the line speed used for the surface winder = drum speed:** A common *diagram-only* answer skips this; the examiner will deduct 1 mark.
> 6. **Mixing up in-roll density and bulk polymer density:** A perfectly wound roll has $\rho_r \approx \rho_{\text{polymer}}$; a soft roll has $\rho_r \ll \rho_{\text{polymer}}$. Be precise.
> 7. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Always carry units through every step.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the KTU 2024 examination — Winder**

- **Definition:** A winder converts a flat web into a roll; it is the *last* quality-determining station in a film/sheet line.
- **Four functions:** Transport, Tension, Winding, Slitting (T³ + S).
- **Three main types:** Surface, Center, Gap (Combination = hybrid).
- **Surface winder:** Drum-driven, friction-transmitted, constant line speed, **capstan equation $F_1 = F_2 e^{\mu\theta}$**, no taper needed.
- **Center winder:** Spindle-driven, torque = $F \cdot r = F D/2$, torque rises linearly with $D$, **taper required**.
- **Gap winder:** Combines both — best for high-speed BOPP/BOPET.
- **Key diameters:** Core (3-inch = 76 mm or 6-inch = 152 mm standard); final roll 600–1500 mm.
- **Tension ranges:** 5–500 N/m of web width depending on gauge.
- **Taper ratio $k$:** = $F_{\text{final}}/F_{\text{initial}}$; typical 0.3 (cast PP) – 0.7 (LLDPE); 1.0 = constant torque; 0.0 = full taper.
- **Roll diameter growth:** $D_f = \sqrt{D_c^2 + 4v\tau t/\pi}$.
- **Winding time per roll:** $\tau = L/v = \pi(D_f^2 - D_c^2)/(4 t v)$.
- **Power:** $P = F \cdot v$ (tension × web speed) in W.
- **In-roll density:** $\rho_r = 4 L t \rho_{\text{polymer}} / [\pi (D_f^2 - D_c^2)]$; packing factor = $\rho_r / \rho_{\text{polymer}}$.
- **Two-axis turret:** Industry standard for non-stop winding; 100 % line uptime.
- **Slitting options:** Razor (for thin flexible film) and shear (for thick, stiff film/sheet).
- **Defects to memorise:** Telescoping, starring, blocking, neck-in, edge weave, gauge bands.
- **Numerical questions usually test:** diameter growth, torque profile, taper ratio, length/time per roll, in-roll density.
- **Diagrams to draw in exam:** (i) Surface winder, (ii) Center winder, (iii) Turret arrangement, (iv) Tension vs. diameter curve.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crowning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Crowning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Crowning** (also called **Roll Crowning**, **Camber**, or **Roll Crown**) is a deliberate, engineered curvature imparted to the working surface of a calender roll, mill roll, or chill roll during its grinding/machining operation. The purpose is to **compensate for elastic roll deflection (bending)** that occurs when the roll is loaded against its mating roll(s) during the polymer processing operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Crowning is the intentional non-cylindrical (typically convex) profile ground on a roll surface. When the roll is loaded in the nip, it deflects inward at the center, and the pre-ground crown causes the running surface to become geometrically cylindrical (flat) at the operating load, thereby producing a polymer sheet/film of uniform thickness across its full width.*

Mathematically, the **Crown value (C)** is defined as the difference between the roll diameter at the center of the face and the diameter at the roll ends:

$$
C = D_{center} - D_{ends}
$$

It is usually expressed in **micrometers (µm)** or **mils (0.001 inch)** for production rolls.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crowned Road"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a road on a hilltop.** A road engineer doesn't make a perfectly flat road — they build it with a slight **crown in the middle** that slopes down toward the edges. Why? So rainwater drains off the sides and the road doesn't become a slippery river. Now flip this idea around: in calendering, the roll is *already* going to **sag in the middle** when pressure is applied (like a heavy log laid across two supports). So the machinist **pre-builds a hump in the middle** of the roll — when the roll sags, the hump flattens, and the resulting sheet comes out *uniformly thick*.

## 1.3 Why Crowning is Essential — The Physics of Roll Deflection

During calendering or roll milling of polymers (PVC, rubber, PE, etc.), the polymer melt exerts a **hydrodynamic nip pressure** of typically **2–15 MPa** on the rolls. This force tries to push the rolls apart, but the rolls are mounted on rigid bearings — so instead of separating, the rolls **bend like beams** at their center. The bending creates a **gap thicker at the center** than at the ends, producing a sheet that is **thicker in the middle and thinner at the edges** (a defect called **"edge-thinning"** or **"wedge sheet"**).

> [!WARNING]
> **Edge thinning is a critical KTU exam defect:** Without compensation, a calendered sheet can vary in thickness by 20–40% from center to edge, which is unacceptable for downstream conversion (printing, laminating, thermoforming).

## 1.4 Types of Crowning

| Crown Type | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Positive Crown** | Diameter at center > diameter at ends (convex) | Most common — compensates for inward deflection |
| **Negative Crown** | Diameter at center < diameter at ends (concave) | Counter-rolls in "L" calenders, transfer rolls |
| **Zero Crown (Parallel Crown)** | Perfectly cylindrical | Auxiliary, idle, or lightly loaded rolls |
| **Parabolic Crown** | Profile follows y = ax² (matches deflection curve) | High-precision calenders |
| **Crown Bias** | Asymmetric crown (e.g., 70% one side, 30% other) | Compensates for uneven bearing loads |
| **Variable Crown (Bowed Roll / Spreader Roll)** | Crown can be adjusted in-situ via internal heating or bending jacks | Modern high-speed calenders |

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Problems

- **Crown (C):** expressed in **µm** (typical: 50–500 µm for industrial calenders)
- **Roll Face Length (L):** in **mm** (typical: 1000–3000 mm)
- **Roll Diameter (D):** in **mm** (typical: 200–800 mm)
- **Nip Load (W):** in **N/mm** (linear load per unit roll face length)
- **Young's Modulus (E):** **210 GPa** for steel rolls
- **Deflection (δ):** in **µm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Roll Deflection — The Beam Analogy

A calender roll is mechanically equivalent to a **simply-supported beam** of length L (the roll face width) supported at the two bearing housings, subjected to a distributed load from the nip pressure.

The key assumptions for the classical KTU analysis are:

1. The roll is a **uniform cross-section** circular beam.
2. The bearing supports behave as **simple supports** (no rotational restraint).
3. The nip pressure distribution is **uniform** (a simplification) or **parabolic** (more accurate).
4. The roll material is **isotropic steel** (E = 210 GPa, ν = 0.3).

> [!NOTE]
> In real calenders, the **pressure distribution is not uniform**. The hydrodynamic pressure profile developed in the polymer melt inside the nip is approximately **parabolic** (peak at the nip center, falling to zero at the ends). This parabolic loading is, conveniently, *exactly* the shape of the deflection curve of a simply-supported beam under uniform load — which is why **parabolic crown profiles are theoretically optimal**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Crowning Solution

1. **Step 1 — Identify the load:** The polymer in the nip exerts a **linear load w (N/mm)** on the roll over the contact length L.
2. **Step 2 — Calculate the deflection:** The roll bends as a beam. The maximum deflection occurs at the center.
3. **Step 3 — Determine the required crown:** The crown C must equal the maximum deflection δ_max, so that at operating load the roll surface is geometrically straight.
4. **Step 4 — Grind the crown:** The roll is machined with a parabolic profile, with maximum material at the center.
5. **Step 5 — Verify in production:** Measure sheet thickness profile with a beta gauge or X-ray thickness gauge; fine-tune using **roll crossing** or **roll bending** systems.

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the KTU board-exam gold standard for crowning problems. Memorize every line.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maximum deflection (uniform load, simply supported) | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \, w \, L^4}{384 \, E \, I}$ | mm | Classical beam result |
| 2 | Crown value (matching deflection) | $C = \delta_{max}$ | µm | Crown compensates exactly for this deflection |
| 3 | Moment of inertia (solid cylindrical roll) | $I = \dfrac{\pi \, D^4}{64}$ | mm⁴ | D = roll diameter |
| 4 | Linear nip load | $w = p \cdot L_c$ | N/mm | p = avg. pressure, L_c = contact length |
| 5 | Linear nip load from total force F | $w = F / L$ | N/mm | F = total separating force |
| 6 | Effective modulus for composite roll (shell + shaft) | $\dfrac{1}{E_e} = \dfrac{1}{E_s} + \dfrac{1}{E_{sh}}$ | — | Used for sleeve/bow rolls |
| 7 | Crown ratio | $\eta = C / L$ | µm/mm | Used to compare rolls of different sizes |
| 8 | Deflection at distance x from center (uniform load) | $\delta(x) = \dfrac{w \, x}{24 \, E \, I}\left(L^3 - 2 L x^2 + x^3\right)$ | mm | Parabolic profile equation |
| 9 | Roll bending force (for cross-axis system) | $F_b = \dfrac{8 \, E \, I \, C_{adj}}{L^2}$ | N | F_b required to add C_adj crown |
| 10 | Thermal crown (from differential heating) | $C_T = \alpha \, \Delta T \, D$ | µm | α = thermal expansion coefficient |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PVC Calendering Lines:** The #2, #3, and #4 rolls in an inverted-L calender are the most heavily loaded and require precision crowning (typically 150–300 µm).
- **Rubber Mixing Mills:** Front roll crown is typically 200–500 µm.
- **Film Casting Rolls (Chill Rolls):** Mirror-finish chrome-plated rolls with parabolic crown to flatten extruded film.
- **Steel Mill Rolls (non-polymer but same principle):** Work rolls and backup rolls.
- **Paper Calendering:** Similar technique — "crown loading" by thermal gradient.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Hint:** In a 14-mark question, the solution must show (a) the physical principle, (b) the deflection formula with all substitutions, (c) the numerical answer with units, and (d) a comment on whether the crown is feasible for the given roll material. **Skipping the unit earns partial mark loss.**

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Roll Deflection Under Uniform Distributed Load

**Setup:** A roll of length L, supported at both ends (simply supported), subjected to a uniform distributed load w (force per unit length) over its face.

**Step 1 — Reactions at supports:**
By symmetry, each support carries half the total load.

$$
R_A = R_B = \dfrac{w \, L}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Bending moment at distance x from left support:**
Cutting the beam at distance x, taking moments:

$$
M(x) = R_A \cdot x - w \cdot x \cdot \dfrac{x}{2} = \dfrac{w \, L \, x}{2} - \dfrac{w \, x^2}{2}
$$

$$
M(x) = \dfrac{w}{2}\left(L \, x - x^2\right) = \dfrac{w \, x}{2}(L - x)
$$

**Step 3 — Euler-Bernoulli beam equation:**

$$
E \, I \, \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) = \dfrac{w \, x}{2}(L - x)
$$

**Step 4 — First integration (slope equation):**

$$
E \, I \, \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x) \, dx = \dfrac{w}{2}\left(\dfrac{L \, x^2}{2} - \dfrac{x^3}{3}\right) + C_1
$$

$$
E \, I \, \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{w \, L \, x^2}{4} - \dfrac{w \, x^3}{6} + C_1
$$

**Step 5 — Apply boundary condition at x = 0 (slope = 0, simple support gives dy/dx ≠ 0; rather, deflection y(0) = 0):**

Wait — for a simply supported beam, **the boundary conditions are y(0) = 0 and y(L) = 0** (zero deflection at supports). Slope is NOT zero at supports.

**Step 6 — Second integration (deflection equation):**

$$
E \, I \, y = \dfrac{w \, L \, x^3}{12} - \dfrac{w \, x^4}{24} + C_1 \, x + C_2
$$

**Step 7 — Apply y(0) = 0 → C₂ = 0:**

$$
E \, I \, y = \dfrac{w \, L \, x^3}{12} - \dfrac{w \, x^4}{24} + C_1 \, x
$$

**Step 8 — Apply y(L) = 0:**

$$
0 = \dfrac{w \, L^4}{12} - \dfrac{w \, L^4}{24} + C_1 \, L
$$

$$
0 = \dfrac{w \, L^4}{24} + C_1 \, L
$$

$$
C_1 = -\dfrac{w \, L^3}{24}
$$

**Step 9 — Final deflection equation:**

$$
E \, I \, y = \dfrac{w \, L \, x^3}{12} - \dfrac{w \, x^4}{24} - \dfrac{w \, L^3 \, x}{24}
$$

$$
y(x) = \dfrac{w \, x}{24 \, E \, I}\left(2L^3 - 2 L \, x^2 + x^3\right)
$$

**Step 10 — Maximum deflection at x = L/2 (center of the roll):**

$$
\delta_{max} = y(L/2) = \dfrac{w \cdot (L/2)}{24 \, E \, I}\left(2L^3 - 2L \cdot \dfrac{L^2}{4} + \dfrac{L^3}{8}\right)
$$

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{w \, L}{48 \, E \, I}\left(2L^3 - \dfrac{L^3}{2} + \dfrac{L^3}{8}\right)
$$

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{w \, L}{48 \, E \, I} \cdot \dfrac{16L^3 - 4L^3 + L^3}{8} = \dfrac{w \, L}{48 \, E \, I} \cdot \dfrac{5 L^3}{8}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \, w \, L^4}{384 \, E \, I}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the master formula.** The roll crown must equal this deflection to produce a flat sheet at the operating load.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Crown for a Given Calender Roll

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A calender roll of **diameter D = 500 mm**, **face length L = 1500 mm**, is loaded with a **linear nip load w = 1500 N/mm**. The roll is **steel** with E = 210 GPa = 210 × 10³ N/mm². Calculate the **required crown**.

**Step 1 — Calculate moment of inertia:**

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi \, D^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \times (500)^4}{64}
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{10}}{64} = \dfrac{1.9635 \times 10^{11}}{64} = 3.067 \times 10^{9} \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the deflection formula:**

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \, w \, L^4}{384 \, E \, I}
$$

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \times 1500 \times (1500)^4}{384 \times 210 \times 10^3 \times 3.067 \times 10^9}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator:**

$$
5 \times 1500 = 7500
$$

$$
(1500)^4 = 5.0625 \times 10^{12}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 7500 \times 5.0625 \times 10^{12} = 3.797 \times 10^{16} \text{ N·mm}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator:**

$$
384 \times 210 \times 10^3 = 8.064 \times 10^7
$$

$$
8.064 \times 10^7 \times 3.067 \times 10^9 = 2.473 \times 10^{17} \text{ N·mm}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer:**

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{3.797 \times 10^{16}}{2.473 \times 10^{17}} = 0.1535 \text{ mm} = 153.5 \text{ µm}
$$

$$
\boxed{C = \delta_{max} \approx 154 \text{ µm}}
$$

**Step 6 — Feasibility check:**
A crown of 154 µm on a 500 mm diameter roll is **well within the grinding tolerance** of industrial roll grinders (typically ±2 µm). ✓ **Feasible.**

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Crown Required Under a Total Separating Force

**Setup:** A 4-roll calender produces a total separating force of **F = 8 × 10⁵ N** on the second roll. Roll face L = 2000 mm. Roll diameter D = 600 mm. Calculate crown.

**Step 1 — Convert total force to linear load:**

$$
w = \dfrac{F}{L} = \dfrac{8 \times 10^5}{2000} = 400 \text{ N/mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Moment of inertia:**

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi \times (600)^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{11}}{64} = 6.362 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Step 3 — Apply formula:**

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \times 400 \times (2000)^4}{384 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 6.362 \times 10^9}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerator:**

$$
2000 \times 400 = 8 \times 10^5
$$

$$
(2000)^4 = 1.6 \times 10^{13}
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = 5 \times 8 \times 10^5 \times 1.6 \times 10^{13} = 6.4 \times 10^{19}
$$

**Step 5 — Denominator:**

$$
384 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 = 8.064 \times 10^7
$$

$$
8.064 \times 10^7 \times 6.362 \times 10^9 = 5.13 \times 10^{17}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute:**

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{6.4 \times 10^{19}}{5.13 \times 10^{17}} = 124.8 \text{ µm}
$$

$$
\boxed{C \approx 125 \text{ µm}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Parameter on Crown

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the sensitivity exponents — they are favorite KTU 14-mark questions:**

| Parameter | Change | Effect on Crown | Sensitivity Exponent |
|---|---|---|---|
| Roll face length L | ↑ Doubles (×2) | Crown ↑ 16× | **L⁴** (most sensitive!) |
| Roll diameter D | ↑ Doubles (×2) | Crown ↓ 16× | **D⁻⁴** (diameter dramatically reduces crown) |
| Linear load w | ↑ Doubles (×2) | Crown ↑ 2× | **w¹** (linear) |
| Young's modulus E | ↑ Doubles (×2) | Crown ↓ 2× | **E⁻¹** (linear) |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Pearl:** The L⁴ dependence explains why **wide calender rolls are extremely difficult to manufacture flat** — doubling the roll width requires 16× more crown compensation, which eventually exceeds grinding capability, forcing engineers to use **roll-bending** or **crossed-roll** systems.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Style]**

**Q: Define the term "Crowning" of a calender roll. Why is it necessary, and what defect does it prevent?**

**Model Answer:**

Crowning is the deliberate curvature (typically convex) ground on the working surface of a calender roll. The diameter at the center of the roll face is made slightly larger than at the ends by an amount called the **crown value C** (typically 50–500 µm).

It is **necessary** because, under the high nip pressure developed in the calender, the roll deflects (bends) elastically like a simply-supported beam. Without compensation, the roll's center moves away from the mating roll, creating a **wedge-shaped nip gap** — wider at the center, narrower at the ends.

Crowning **prevents edge thinning** (also called wedge sheet defect), where the calendered sheet would be thicker at the center and thinner at the edges. The pre-ground crown, equal in magnitude to the maximum roll deflection, causes the roll to become geometrically cylindrical at the operating load, producing a sheet of **uniform thickness** across its width. **(3 marks — full)**

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]**

**Q: List the different types of crowning systems used in calender rolls. Mention the application of any two.**

**Model Answer:**

The main types of crowning systems are:

1. **Fixed (Mechanical) Crown** — parabolic profile ground into the roll; set during manufacture; cannot be changed during operation.
2. **Roll Bending System** — hydraulic jacks apply an additional bending moment to the roll journals to add adjustable crown.
3. **Crossed-Axis (Crossed Rolls) System** — the axes of two adjacent rolls are deliberately set at a small skew angle, creating effective crowning that can be varied.
4. **Thermal Crown** — internal heating channels create a temperature gradient, with the center hotter than the ends, causing differential thermal expansion that mimics crowning.
5. **Variable / Bowed Crown Roll** — internal mechanical adjustment (e.g., sleeve deflection) provides in-situ crown control.
6. **Crown Bias** — asymmetric crown profile for rolls subject to uneven loading.

**Applications:**
- **Fixed Crown:** widely used in PVC calendering for rolls where the load is predictable and constant.
- **Crossed-Axis System:** used in rubber calendering lines where frequent grade changes require flexible crown adjustment without stopping production. **(3 marks — full)**

---

> Common mistakes students make in crowning problems:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** (mixing N/mm² with N/m²) — costs 1–2 marks
> 2. **Using wrong moment of inertia formula** (using πr⁴ instead of πD⁴/64) — costs 2 marks
> 3. **Omitting the boundary conditions** in derivations — costs 2 marks
> 4. **Not mentioning the assumptions** (e.g., simply supported beam, uniform load) — costs 1 mark
> 5. **Writing the answer in general terms without numerical substitution** — costs up to 4 marks
> 6. **Forgetting to compare C with practical grinding limits** — costs 1 mark

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**Q: (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the concept of crowning in calender rolls. Derive the expression for the maximum deflection of a simply-supported roll under uniform distributed load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A calender roll of **diameter 400 mm** and **face length 1200 mm** is subjected to a **uniform linear load of 1200 N/mm** during the calendering of PVC sheet. The roll is made of steel (E = 2.1 × 10⁵ N/mm²). Calculate:
  (i) the moment of inertia of the roll,
  (ii) the maximum deflection at the roll center,
  (iii) the required crown value to produce a flat sheet. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a) — Concept and Derivation**

Crowning is the deliberate convex profile ground on a calender roll surface. When the roll is loaded in the nip, it deflects inward at the center. The pre-ground crown compensates for this deflection so that the running surface becomes cylindrical at the operating load.

**Derivation of Maximum Deflection:**

Consider a simply-supported roll of length L, loaded by a uniform distributed load w (N/mm). The bending moment at distance x from the left support is:

$$
M(x) = \dfrac{w \, x}{2}(L - x)
$$

The Euler-Bernoulli equation gives:

$$
E \, I \, \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = \dfrac{w \, x}{2}(L - x)
$$

Integrating twice with boundary conditions y(0) = 0 and y(L) = 0:

$$
y(x) = \dfrac{w \, x}{24 \, E \, I}\left(2L^3 - 2 L \, x^2 + x^3\right)
$$

At the center, x = L/2:

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \, w \, L^4}{384 \, E \, I}
$$

This is the crown required. **(7 marks complete with diagram and full derivation)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- D = 400 mm, L = 1200 mm, w = 1200 N/mm
- E = 2.1 × 10⁵ N/mm²

**(i) Moment of inertia:**

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi \, D^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \times (400)^4}{64}
$$

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi \times 2.56 \times 10^{10}}{64} = 1.2566 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4
$$

**(ii) Maximum deflection:**

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \times 1200 \times (1200)^4}{384 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 1.2566 \times 10^9}
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
5 \times 1200 \times (1200)^4 = 6000 \times 2.0736 \times 10^{12} = 1.2442 \times 10^{16}
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
384 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 1.2566 \times 10^9 = 1.0137 \times 10^{17}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{1.2442 \times 10^{16}}{1.0137 \times 10^{17}} = 0.1227 \text{ mm} = 122.7 \text{ µm}
$$

**(iii) Required crown:**

$$
\boxed{C = \delta_{max} \approx 123 \text{ µm}}
$$

**Feasibility:** A crown of 123 µm on a 400 mm roll is easily achievable by precision grinding. The roll would be ground with a parabolic profile, maximum at center, zero at both ends. **(7 marks — full)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Alternative Question]**

**Q: (a)** Explain, with a block diagram, the different methods of crown compensation used in modern calenders. Compare fixed mechanical crown with the cross-axis (roll-crossing) system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-roll inverted-L calender has the second roll with **diameter 700 mm** and **face length 2500 mm**. During the production of a 0.3 mm thick rigid PVC sheet, the **total separating force on this roll is 12 × 10⁵ N**. The roll material is **cast iron with E = 1.5 × 10⁵ N/mm²**. Determine:
  (i) the equivalent linear load,
  (ii) the moment of inertia,
  (iii) the required crown, and
  (iv) whether this crown can be achieved by mechanical grinding alone, or whether roll-bending assistance is needed. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a) — Crown Compensation Methods (Refer to Block Diagram in Section 4.2)**

**1. Fixed Mechanical Crown:**
- A parabolic profile is permanently ground into the roll surface.
- The crown value equals the calculated maximum deflection at the design load.
- **Advantages:** simple, reliable, no moving parts, low maintenance.
- **Disadvantages:** cannot be adjusted for load changes; if load varies (e.g., grade change), the compensation is no longer optimal.

**2. Cross-Axis (Roll Crossing) System:**
- The axes of the two rolls are deliberately set at a small angle θ to each other (typically 0.1° to 1°).
- This creates an effective nip gap that is **wedge-shaped** (narrower at one end, wider at the other).
- By adjusting θ, the operator can fine-tune the effective crown in real time.
- **Advantages:** infinitely variable, fast response, can correct for off-center loads (bias).
- **Disadvantages:** applies side thrust to bearings, requires robust bearing design.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Fixed Crown | Cross-Axis System |
|---|---|---|
| Adjustability | None | Continuous |
| Response time | N/A | Seconds |
| Mechanical complexity | Low | High |
| Best for | Constant production | Multi-grade production |
| Capital cost | Low | High |
| Bearing load | Standard | Increased lateral load |

**(7 marks — full)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- D = 700 mm, L = 2500 mm, F = 12 × 10⁵ N
- E = 1.5 × 10⁵ N/mm²

**(i) Equivalent linear load:**

$$
w = \dfrac{F}{L} = \dfrac{12 \times 10^5}{2500} = 480 \text{ N/mm}
$$

**(ii) Moment of inertia:**

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi \, (700)^4}{64} = \dfrac{\pi \times 2.401 \times 10^{11}}{64} = 1.178 \times 10^{10} \text{ mm}^4
$$

**(iii) Required crown:**

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 \times 480 \times (2500)^4}{384 \times 1.5 \times 10^5 \times 1.178 \times 10^{10}}
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
5 \times 480 \times (2500)^4 = 2400 \times 3.906 \times 10^{13} = 9.375 \times 10^{16}
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
384 \times 1.5 \times 10^5 \times 1.178 \times 10^{10} = 6.787 \times 10^{17}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{9.375 \times 10^{16}}{6.787 \times 10^{17}} = 0.1381 \text{ mm} = 138.1 \text{ µm}
$$

$$
\boxed{C \approx 138 \text{ µm}}
$$

**(iv) Feasibility Assessment:**

A crown of 138 µm on a 700 mm roll is within the **standard mechanical grinding range** (typical industrial capability: 50–300 µm). Therefore, **fixed mechanical grinding is sufficient** for this design. However, because the calender must produce 0.3 mm precision sheet (very tight ±2% thickness tolerance), and the actual load may vary with grade changes, it is **strongly recommended to supplement the fixed crown with a roll-bending system** capable of adding 0–150 µm of additional crown on demand. **(7 marks — full)**
