
---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A (3-Mark Short-Answer Questions)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term **die swell** in polymer extrusion. State its cause and one method to reduce it.

> **Model Answer:**
> Die swell (or extrudate swell) is the phenomenon in which the diameter of a polymer extrudate emerging from a die is greater than the die diameter. It is quantified by the **die swell ratio $B$**:
> $$B = \frac{D_{extrudate}}{D_{die}}$$
> Typically $B$ ranges from **1.1 to 1.5** for most thermoplastics.
> **Cause:** Elastic recovery of polymer chains from the deformation imposed during flow inside the die. The viscoelastic melt stores entropic energy, which is released as the melt exits the die, causing the diameter to expand.
> **Methods to reduce die swell:** (1) Increase the die land length (gives more relaxation time inside the die), (2) Lower the extrusion rate / screw speed, (3) Increase melt temperature (reduces elasticity), (4) Reduce molecular weight and narrow MWD.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
List any **four** polymer manufacturing methods used for thermoplastics, with one example product of each.

> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Injection Molding** — Used for TV remote housings, bottle caps, automotive dashboards.
> 2. **Extrusion** — Used for PVC pipes, polyethylene films, and wire insulation.
> 3. **Blow Molding** — Used for PET bottles and HDPE jerry cans.
> 4. **Thermoforming** — Used for disposable cups, blister packaging, and refrigerator liners.
> 5. **Rotational Molding** — Used for water storage tanks, kayaks, and road barriers.

---

### PART B (14-Mark Long-Answer Questions — Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, describe the working of a **single-screw extruder** and derive the expression for its **output rate**.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Explain the complete **injection molding cycle** with a labeled schematic. List the key process parameters and state the empirical relation between cooling time and wall thickness.

---

#### **Model Answer for Question A:**

**(a) Working of a Single-Screw Extruder and Output Rate Derivation:**

**Working Principle:**
A single-screw extruder consists of a **rotating screw** inside a **heated barrel**. Polymer pellets enter from the hopper into the feed throat. As the screw rotates, the pellets are conveyed forward, compacted, melted, and finally pumped through a shaping die as a continuous profile.

**The screw has three functional zones:**

1. **Feed Zone (~25% of length):** Conveys solid pellets from the hopper into the barrel. The channel depth is constant and maximum. Heat is supplied mainly by the barrel heaters.

2. **Compression / Transition Zone (~50% of length):** The channel depth decreases progressively. The polymer is compacted, air is squeezed out, and melting occurs due to a combination of external heat and frictional shear.

3. **Metering Zone (~25% of length):** The channel depth is constant and minimum. The fully molten polymer is metered (pumped) at a uniform rate to the die. Pressure builds up here to overcome die resistance.

**Schematic:** (Refer to Block Diagram 4.3 above for the flow architecture.)

**Derivation of Output Rate:**

The output of a single-screw extruder in the metering zone is the superposition of two flow components:

**(i) Drag Flow** — Melt is dragged forward by the relative motion of the barrel over the channel.

$$Q_d = \frac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N D \sin\phi \cos\phi$$

**(ii) Pressure Flow** — The die back-pressure $\Delta P$ drives a reverse (leakage) flow opposing the drag flow.

$$Q_p = -\frac{H^3 W \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$$

**Net Output Rate (Newtonian Superposition):**

$$Q = \frac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N D \sin\phi \cos\phi - \frac{H^3 W \Delta P}{12 \eta L}$$

**Maximum Output** occurs at $\Delta P = 0$ (open discharge):
$$Q_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \pi^2 D^2 N D \sin\phi \cos\phi$$

> (Full derivation steps are provided in Section 3.1 above.)

**(b) Injection Molding Cycle and Cooling Time Relation:**

**Stages of the Injection Molding Cycle:**

1. **Mold Closing:** The two mold halves close and lock under high clamp tonnage.

2. **Injection / Filling:** The screw acts as a plunger and injects the melt into the cavity at high pressure (~700–1400 bar). The cavity is filled to ~95% volume.

3. **Packing / Holding:** Additional melt is forced in (at lower pressure) to compensate for volumetric shrinkage as the part cools. This phase determines the final density and minimizes sink marks.

4. **Cooling / Solidification:** The part remains in the mold under no pressure while internal heat is removed by the mold cooling channels. The part must reach ejection temperature $T_{ej}$ (usually $T_g$ + 20°C for amorphous polymers) before ejection.

5. **Mold Opening:** The clamp retracts, and the mold halves separate.

6. **Ejection:** Ejector pins push the part out of the cavity.

7. **Plasticizing:** The screw rotates, drawing in fresh pellets from the hopper and melting them for the next cycle. This occurs **simultaneously** with cooling of the previous part (overlapping cycle time savings).

**Key Process Parameters:**

| Parameter | Typical Range |
|-----------|---------------|
| Melt Temperature | 180°C – 320°C |
| Mold Temperature | 20°C – 120°C |
| Injection Pressure | 35 – 140 MPa |
| Clamp Tonnage | 50 – 4000 tons |
| Cooling Time | 5 – 60 s |
| Cycle Time | 10 – 120 s |

**Empirical Relation for Cooling Time:**

$$t_{cool} \propto s^2$$

where $s$ = wall thickness. The rigorous form (from Section 3.2):

$$t_{cool} = \frac{4 s^2}{\alpha \pi^2} \ln\left[\frac{4 (T_{melt} - T_{mold})}{\pi (T_{ej} - T_{mold})}\right]$$

> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** $t_{cool} \approx 1.5 \times s^2$ (for PS), $2.5 \times s^2$ (for PE), $3.0 \times s^2$ (for PP), where $s$ is in mm and $t_{cool}$ is in seconds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> 1. Many students forget to state the **assumptions** before deriving the extruder output equation. This costs 1 mark. Always state: (a) Newtonian fluid, (b) isothermal flow, (c) shallow channel, (d) steady-state.
> 2. Students often confuse **drag flow direction** with **pressure flow direction**. Mark the negative sign explicitly when writing the pressure flow term.
> 3. In injection molding, do **not** skip the **overlapping of plasticizing with cooling**. This overlap is the key to cycle time reduction and is a favorite KTU question.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **blow molding process** in detail. With a labeled schematic, describe the **extrusion blow molding (EBM)** technique and state the key differences from **injection blow molding (IBM)**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is **rotational molding**? Explain its working principle, advantages, and limitations. State the typical cycle time and list four major applications.

---

#### **Model Answer for Question B:**

**(a) Blow Molding — Extrusion Blow Molding (EBM):**

Blow molding is a process used to manufacture **hollow thermoplastic parts** by inflating a heated polymer tube (parison) inside a closed mold using compressed air.

**Steps of Extrusion Blow Molding (EBM):**

1. **Parison Extrusion:** A molten polymer tube (parison) is extruded vertically downwards from an annular die.

2. **Mold Clamping:** A two-piece split mold closes around the parison, pinching off the top and bottom to seal it.

3. **Blowing:** Compressed air (0.3 – 1.0 MPa) is injected through a blow pin into the parison, inflating it against the cooled mold walls.

4. **Cooling:** The blown part is held in contact with the cold mold walls until it solidifies.

5. **Mold Opening & Ejection:** The mold opens, and the part is ejected. The pinched-off top and bottom (called "flash") are trimmed and recycled.

**Schematic of EBM:** (See block diagram 4.1 above — D4 node.)

**Comparison Table: EBM vs IBM:**

| Feature | Extrusion Blow Molding (EBM) | Injection Blow Molding (IBM) |
|---------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Parison Formation | Extruded continuously | Injection-molded preform |
| Process Type | Continuous (interrupted) | Fully cyclic |
| Production Volume | Medium–High | Very High |
| Container Sizes | Wide range (10 mL to 1000 L) | Mostly small bottles (PET) |
| Wall Thickness Control | Less uniform (axial variation possible) | Highly uniform (bottle-to-bottle) |
| Material Waste | Higher (flash + pinch-off) | Lower (no flash; just neck trim) |
| Tooling Cost | Lower | Higher (two tools: preform + blow mold) |
| Common Resins | HDPE, LDPE, PP, PVC | PET, PP, PS |
| Neck Finish Quality | Moderate | Excellent (precise neck threads) |
| Typical Application | Detergent bottles, drums, tanks | Soft drink bottles, pharma bottles |

**(b) Rotational Molding:**

**Definition:** Rotational molding (rotomolding) is a **batch process** for manufacturing large, hollow, seamless thermoplastic parts by rotating a closed mold biaxially in a heated oven while the polymer powder or liquid melts and coats the inner surface.

**Working Principle (Four Phases):**

1. **Charging:** A pre-weighed quantity of polymer powder (or liquid plastisol) is placed inside an open mold.

2. **Heating + Biaxial Rotation:** The mold is closed, moved into a heated oven (300–400°C), and rotated about two perpendicular axes simultaneously. Centrifugal force pushes the polymer to coat the inner mold walls uniformly as it melts.

3. **Cooling:** The mold is moved to a cooling chamber while still rotating. Cooling is slow (air + water spray) to prevent warpage and internal stresses.

4. **Demolding:** Rotation stops, the mold is opened, and the hollow part is removed.

**Total Cycle:** Typically **30–120 minutes** depending on wall thickness.

**Advantages:**

- Very **low tooling cost** compared to injection/blow molding.
- Suitable for **very large parts** (up to 50,000 L tanks).
- **Uniform wall thickness** with no weld lines, knit lines, or flow marks.
- Minimal material waste (~95% material utilization).
- **Stress-free parts** (no flow-induced orientation).
- Easy to mold complex shapes with undercuts.

**Limitations:**

- Long cycle times → low production volume.
- Limited to **thermoplastics** that can be powdered (mainly PE family).
- **Poor dimensional accuracy** and surface finish.
- Not suitable for very thin walls (<1 mm) or very thick walls (>25 mm).
- High energy consumption per part.

**Major Applications:**
1. Water storage tanks (1000 L – 25,000 L).
2. Septic tanks and chemical containers.
3. Kayaks, canoes, and small boats.
4. Road barriers, traffic cones, and playground equipment.
5. Automotive fuel tanks and air ducts.
6. Refrigerator inner liners and insulated containers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. In EBM vs IBM, students often forget to mention that **IBM gives better neck finish** (since the preform mold has the neck finish), which is a favorite KTU point.
> 2. For rotomolding, students commonly write that it is suitable for "any polymer" — **this is wrong**. It is mainly for PE, PP, PVC plastisol, and nylons. Mark loss here.
> 3. Many students confuse the axis of rotation. Always state **biaxial rotation about two perpendicular axes**, not uniaxial.
> 4. The relation $t_{cool} = M/(\rho A)$ for wall thickness is often requested in follow-up parts — memorize it.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ☐ **Compression Molding:** Oldest process, mainly for **thermosets** and rubber. Cure time depends on wall thickness (2 min/mm rule). Material utilization is low due to flash.
- ☐ **Injection Molding:** Dominant for **thermoplastics**. Cooling time $t_{cool} \propto s^2$. Clamp force = cavity pressure × projected area.
- ☐ **Extrusion:** Continuous process. **Output equation** $Q = Q_d + Q_p$. **Die swell** is due to elastic recovery.
- ☐ **Blow Molding:** For hollow parts. **EBM** = extruded parison; **IBM** = injection-molded preform; **SBM** = biaxial stretch blow.
- ☐ **Thermoforming:** Sheet heating + vacuum/pressure. **Thin-walled** products (cups, trays).
- ☐ **Rotomolding:** Biaxial rotation. Suitable for **large hollow tanks**. Long cycle, low tooling cost.
- ☐ **RIM:** Two reactive streams mixed at impingement. Low pressure (~0.5 MPa).
- ☐ **Pultrusion:** Continuous FRP profiles; fiber + resin pulled through heated die.
- ☐ **Cooling time formula** is the **single most tested derivation** in this module.
- ☐ **Output rate of extruder** derivation — must state assumptions, derive drag flow, then pressure flow, then superpose.
- ☐ **Selection of process** depends on: polymer type, geometry, volume, tolerance, cost, MUR.
- ☐ Always give **at least one example product** for every method in descriptive answers.
- ☐ Write all equations inside `$$ ... $$` with proper units stated beside them.
- ☐ Draw **block diagrams** for all process descriptions — it earns easy 1–2 marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:**
> KTU examiners consistently reward **labeled diagrams** (block diagrams are acceptable and easier to draw). For 7-mark questions, aim to include: (1) block diagram, (2) one equation with units, (3) three advantages/limitations, and (4) one example product. This is a near-guaranteed 6/7 score.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Polymer Machinery & Product Manufacturing**, **Application Methods** refer to the systematic engineering techniques, processes, and machinery employed to deposit, convert, or transform polymeric materials (in the form of melts, solutions, dispersions, emulsions, powders, or pre-formed sheets) onto a substrate, into a mold cavity, or onto a moving web in order to produce a finished or semi-finished polymeric product with the desired geometry, thickness, surface finish, and functional properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCPOT503 / Module 4):**
> *Application methods encompass the unit operations by which raw polymers (thermoplastics, thermosets, elastomers, paints, adhesives, coatings, and composite matrices) are converted into usable industrial artifacts. The classification includes coating methods, laminating methods, molding methods, and specialized deposition techniques such as extrusion coating, calendering, and additive manufacturing.*

The discipline integrates **fluid mechanics, heat transfer, mass transfer, rheology, surface science, and machine design** to control parameters such as film thickness, coating uniformity, adhesion, drying/curing rate, and production throughput.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Wall-Painting Toolbox"**
>
> Imagine you need to apply a layer of paint onto a wall. You instinctively choose a tool based on the desired outcome:
> - **Small detailed area?** → You use a *brush*.
> - **Large smooth surface?** → You use a *roller*.
> - **Complex shape with crevices?** → You use a *spray gun*.
> - **Dip the object itself into a paint bath?** → You use a *dipping tank*.
> - **Pour paint like a waterfall onto the surface?** → You use a *curtain coater*.
>
> Polymer application methods follow the **exact same logic**. Different end-product geometries, substrate types, viscosity ranges, and production speeds require different "tools" — which in engineering are called *application methods*. Each method is essentially a controlled way of placing a precisely metered quantity of polymer onto a target surface or into a defined space.

### 1.3 Industrial Significance

Application methods are responsible for converting **> 40 % of all manufactured polymers** into their final usable form. Industries that critically depend on these techniques include:

| Industry | Typical Application Method Used |
|---|---|
| Packaging (food, pharma) | Extrusion coating, Lamination, Cast film |
| Automotive | Spray coating, Electrostatic powder coating, Reaction Injection Molding |
| Construction | Calendering, Extrusion coating, Spray-on insulation |
| Electronics (PCB, displays) | Spin coating, Slot-die coating, Curtain coating |
| Adhesives & Tapes | Hot-melt coating, Gravure coating, Slot-die |
| Medical Devices | Dip coating (catheters), Compression molding (tablets) |
| Paints & Decorative Coatings | Air spray, Airless spray, HVLP, Dip coating |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Industry

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are *universally applied* across all application methods in industry and are **expected** in KTU 2024 numerical questions:
>
> - **Coating weight (gsm):** grams of polymer deposited per square meter of substrate. Typical range: **5–500 gsm**.
> - **Wet film thickness (WFT):** **10 µm – 2 mm**.
> - **Dry film thickness (DFT):** **1 µm – 500 µm**.
> - **Line speed (web speed):** **5 m/min – 1500 m/min** in modern coating lines.
> - **Viscosity range of applicable polymer:** **0.001 Pa·s (solvents) – 10⁵ Pa·s (pastes)**.
> - **Throughput:** measured in **kg/hr** or **m²/min**.

---

## 1.5 Broad Classification of Application Methods

For the KTU 2024 scheme, the methods are broadly classified as follows:

1. **Liquid-applied coating methods** — Spray, Dip, Brush, Flow, Curtain, Spin
2. **Roll-applied methods** — Direct, Reverse, Kiss, Gravure, Mayer rod
3. **Melt-applied methods** — Extrusion coating, Calendering, Hot-melt coating
4. **Lamination methods** — Adhesive lamination, Extrusion lamination, Thermal lamination
5. **Molding methods (product manufacturing)** — Injection, Blow, Compression, Rotational, Casting, Thermoforming
6. **Advanced/Specialized methods** — Electrostatic spray, Plasma coating, 3D Printing (Additive Manufacturing)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Major Application Methods — Operational Logic

### 2.1.1 SPRAY COATING

**Principle:** Polymer liquid (solution, dispersion, or melt) is atomized into fine droplets and projected onto the substrate by a pressure differential.

**Atomization Mechanisms:**
- **Conventional (air) spray:** Compressed air (3–6 bar) shears the liquid stream into droplets. Droplet size: **20–200 µm**.
- **Airless spray:** Liquid is forced at very high pressure (100–500 bar) through a small orifice (0.2–1.0 mm). Droplet size: **80–500 µm**. No air mixing → less overspray.
- **HVLP (High Volume Low Pressure):** Air pressure 0.5–1.0 bar, high volume. Transfer efficiency **>65 %** (vs 30 % for conventional).
- **Electrostatic spray:** Droplets are charged (–30 to –100 kV) and attracted to the grounded substrate. Transfer efficiency **>90 %**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reynolds Number of the Spray Jet:**
> $$Re = \frac{\rho \, v \, D_h}{\mu}$$
> where $D_h$ is the nozzle hydraulic diameter. Atomization is efficient when $Re > 10^4$.

---

### 2.1.2 DIP COATING

**Principle:** The substrate is immersed in a polymer liquid bath and withdrawn at a controlled speed. A thin liquid film adheres to the substrate and is subsequently dried/cured.

**Steps:**
1. **Immersion** — Substrate lowered at speed $U_i$ (typically $\geq$ withdrawal speed to avoid dripping).
2. **Dwelling** — Held in liquid for $t_d$ (5 s – 5 min) to allow equilibration.
3. **Withdrawal** — Withdrawn at constant speed $U$ (0.1 – 100 cm/min).
4. **Drainage & Drying** — Excess drains; film dries to a solid coating.

> [!NOTE]
> **Landau–Levich–Derjaguin (LLD) Film Thickness Equation** — the cornerstone of dip-coating theory (derived in Section 3):
> $$h = 0.944 \left( \frac{\eta \, U}{\rho \, g} \right)^{1/2} \cdot \left( \frac{\eta \, U}{\gamma_{LV}} \right)^{1/6}$$
> where $\gamma_{LV}$ is the liquid–vapor surface tension, $\eta$ is dynamic viscosity, $U$ is withdrawal speed, $\rho$ is liquid density, $g$ is gravitational acceleration.

---

### 2.1.3 ROLL COATING

**Principle:** Polymer is metered and transferred onto the substrate by a series of rotating rolls.

**Major Sub-types:**

| Type | Roll Configuration | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Direct (forward) roll | Two rolls rotating in same direction as web | Adhesives, paints |
| Reverse roll | Top roll rotates opposite to web direction | Precision coatings, photographic film |
| Kiss roll | Single roll transfers by surface tension | Lacquers, release coatings |
| Gravure roll | Engraved roll with micro-cells | Inks, adhesives, decorative laminates |
| Mayer rod (wire-wound rod) | Wire wound rod meters excess | Lab-scale coatings, paper coatings |

**Operating Window:** Roll speeds **5–1500 m/min**, line pressures **0.5–50 kN/m**.

---

### 2.1.4 EXTRUSION COATING

**Principle:** A thermoplastic melt is extruded through a flat (slot) die as a thin film and laminated onto a moving substrate (paper, board, foil, fabric) inside a nip between the die-lip and a chilled roll.

**Process Schematic (conceptual):** Polymer pellets → Extruder → Slot die → Molten curtain → Nip + Chill roll → Laminated substrate.

**Process Variables:**
- Melt temperature: **200–330 °C** (LDPE, LLDPE, PP, PET, EVOH).
- Line speed: **100–600 m/min** (modern lines exceed **1000 m/min**).
- Coating weight: **5–100 gsm**.
- Air gap: **100–300 mm** between die and nip.
- Adhesion promoted by **ozone treatment, flame treatment, or primer coating** on substrate.

---

### 2.1.5 CURTAIN COATING

**Principle:** Polymer liquid is pumped through a slot die to form a continuous, free-falling liquid "curtain" (typically 0.5–2 mm thick) through which the substrate passes horizontally.

**Used for:** Photographic film, glossy paper, photoresists, water-based paints, high-solids coatings.

**Key requirement:** The substrate must be **rigid or supported on a carrier** (cannot be a flimsy web — the curtain applies a tensile force).

**Advantage over slot-die:** Coating thickness is **independent of line speed** to first order (the curtain is steady and gravity-driven).

---

### 2.1.6 BRUSH / MANUAL APPLICATION

**Principle:** Manual deposition using brushes, rollers, or squeegees.

**Used in:** Decorative paints, touch-up, small-batch products, repair work.

**Parameters controlled by operator:** WFT, uniformity, edge coverage.

**Industrial variant:** *Pad coating* — a felt or rubber pad transfers the polymer. Used in textile printing, PCB solder mask.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it contains the 8 most examination-relevant equations for Module 4 Application Methods.**

| # | Equation / Rule | Description | Variables & Units | Applicable Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h = 0.944 \left( \dfrac{\eta U}{\rho g} \right)^{1/2} \left( \dfrac{\eta U}{\gamma_{LV}} \right)^{1/6}$ | Landau–Levich–Derjaguin film thickness on a flat plate withdrawn from a liquid bath | $h$ (m), $\eta$ (Pa·s), $U$ (m/s), $\rho$ (kg/m³), $g$ (m/s²), $\gamma_{LV}$ (N/m) | Dip coating |
| 2 | $Ca = \dfrac{\eta U}{\gamma_{LV}}$ | Capillary number — ratio of viscous to capillary forces (dimensionless) | All variables in SI | Dip, slot-die, curtain |
| 3 | $Q = C_d \, A_n \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Volumetric flow rate through a nozzle under pressure | $Q$ (m³/s), $C_d$ (–), $A_n$ (m²), $\Delta P$ (Pa), $\rho$ (kg/m³) | Spray (airless) |
| 4 | $D_{32} = A \left( \dfrac{Q}{\Delta P} \right)^{B}$ | Sauter mean droplet diameter (empirical) — $A, B$ are constants ($A \approx 0.3$–0.5, $B \approx 0.3$–0.5) | $D_{32}$ (µm), $Q$ (L/min), $\Delta P$ (bar) | Spray atomization |
| 5 | $CR = \dfrac{60 \, Q_g \, N}{v \, W}$ | Coverage rate of a spray line | $CR$ (m²/min), $Q_g$ (L/min per gun), $N$ (guns), $v$ (m/min), $W$ (m) | Spray, slot-die |
| 6 | $h_{wet} = \dfrac{Q}{v \, W}$ | Wet film thickness from volumetric flow | $h_{wet}$ (m), $Q$ (m³/min), $v$ (m/min), $W$ (m) | All continuous methods |
| 7 | $\dot{m} = \rho \, v \, W \, h$ | Mass flow rate of deposited polymer | $\dot{m}$ (kg/min), $\rho$ (kg/m³), $v$ (m/min), $W$ (m), $h$ (m) | All continuous methods |
| 8 | $C_{gsm} = \dfrac{\rho \, h_{wet}}{S}$ | Coating weight in grams per square meter (S = solids fraction) | $C_{gsm}$ (g/m²), $S$ (–) | All liquid methods |
| 9 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D_h}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number of the flow | Dimensionless | Spray, curtain, slot-die |
| 10 | $T_{dry} = \dfrac{\rho_s \, h_{wet}^2 \, \lambda}{k \, \Delta T}$ | Characteristic drying time of a wet film (rough scaling) | $T_{dry}$ (s), $\lambda$ (J/kg), $k$ (W/m·K) | Drying/curing of coatings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value expressions inside tables (e.g., $\vert T - T_\infty \vert$) to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.** In this cheat sheet, no vertical bars were needed.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

| Method | Typical Polymer | Substrate | Industrial Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spray | Polyurethane, epoxy, acrylic lacquer | Metal car body, aircraft fuselage | Automotive paint, anti-corrosion coatings |
| Dip | Latex, PVC plastisol | Glass, metal, catheter tubing | Medical gloves, dipped rubber goods |
| Roll | Water-based adhesive, PVA, silicone | Paper, film, foil | Adhesive tapes, photographic film |
| Extrusion | LDPE, LLDPE, PP, EVOH, PET | Paper, board, aluminum foil | Milk cartons, Tetra Pak, barrier packaging |
| Curtain | Photographic emulsion, water-based paint | Paper, rigid board | Photo paper, high-gloss magazine covers |
| Brush | Enamel, varnish, distemper | Wall, wood, metal | Decorative paints, touch-up, art |

The **selection logic** in industry is governed by a decision matrix involving:
- **Viscosity of polymer** (0.001 – 10⁵ Pa·s)
- **Required film thickness** (1 µm – 5 mm)
- **Production speed** (1 m/min – 1500 m/min)
- **Substrate geometry** (flat, 3D, flexible, rigid)
- **Required precision** (precision < ±1 µm for electronics to ±50 µm for construction)
- **Capital cost** (₹50 lakh for a manual booth vs ₹50 crore for a 1500 m/min extrusion coating line)
- **Environmental constraints** (VOC emission, overspray, solvent recovery)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Landau–Levich–Derjaguin Equation for Dip Coating Film Thickness

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** This is the *single most-tested* analytical derivation in polymer coating modules across all leading KTU 2024-scheme textbooks. A 7-mark question on this derivation appears in **≥ 1 past-paper every academic year**.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A flat vertical plate of width $W$ is withdrawn from a Newtonian liquid bath at a steady speed $U$. The entrainment of liquid by the plate produces a thin film of thickness $h$ on the plate (measured at a height sufficiently far above the bath such that the film is steady and uniform).

We assume:
- Steady-state, incompressible, laminar flow.
- Newtonian liquid with viscosity $\eta$ and density $\rho$.
- Capillary number $Ca = \eta U / \gamma_{LV} \ll 1$ (gravity-dominated regime).
- The film is thin: $h \ll \ell_c$ where $\ell_c$ is the capillary length $\sqrt{\gamma_{LV}/(\rho g)}$.

### 3.1.2 Governing Equation

In a coordinate system attached to the plate (so the bath surface moves downward at speed $U$ at infinity), the steady-state 2D Navier–Stokes equation in the film reduces to a balance between **viscous shear**, **gravitational body force**, and the **stream-wise pressure gradient**:

$$\rho \left( u \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right) = -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \eta \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \rho g$$

where $x$ is the vertical direction (upward) and $y$ is perpendicular to the plate.

### 3.1.3 Simplification — Lubrication Approximation

For a thin film ($h$ very small) the convective inertia terms are negligible compared to viscous and gravity terms. The flow is locally parallel and the pressure is approximately constant across the film thickness:

$$\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} \approx \rho g + \eta \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$$

### 3.1.4 Boundary Conditions

1. **No-slip at the plate** (located at $y = 0$):
$$u(y=0) = U$$

2. **Zero shear at the free surface** (located at $y = h$):
$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\bigg|_{y=h} = 0$$

3. **Capillary pressure at the free surface** (curvature of the free surface):
$$p(h) = p_{atm} - \gamma_{LV} \dfrac{d^2 h}{d x^2}$$

### 3.1.5 Pressure Distribution in the Film

Integrating the simplified momentum equation once with the zero-shear condition at the free surface:

$$\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \dfrac{1}{\eta} \left( \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} - \rho g \right) (y - h)$$

Integrating again with the no-slip condition at $y = 0$:

$$u(y) = U + \dfrac{1}{2\eta} \left( \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} - \rho g \right) (y^2 - 2 h y)$$

### 3.1.6 Volumetric Flow Rate Per Unit Width

The total volumetric flow per unit width of the plate is:

$$q = \int_0^h u(y) \, dy$$

Substituting $u(y)$ and integrating:

$$q = U h + \dfrac{1}{2\eta} \left( \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} - \rho g \right) \left( \dfrac{h^3}{3} - h^3 \right)$$

$$q = U h - \dfrac{h^3}{3\eta} \left( \dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} - \rho g \right)$$

In a **steady, uniform film far from the meniscus**, $\partial p / \partial x = 0$ and gravity drives the flow. However, in the **meniscus region** (the curved transition between the bath and the film), capillary pressure dominates and the flow redistributes itself. In the asymptotic matching of the inner (meniscus) and outer (steady film) solutions (Landau & Levich, 1942; Derjaguin, 1943):

- Outer (gravity-dominated) film thickness scales as:
$$h \sim \left( \dfrac{\eta U}{\rho g} \right)^{1/2}$$

- Capillary correction (matching to the static meniscus) introduces a multiplicative factor of order $Ca^{1/6}$, where $Ca = \eta U / \gamma_{LV}$.

### 3.1.7 Final Assembled Equation

Combining the two contributions via matched asymptotics, the **Landau–Levich–Derjaguin equation** is:

$$\boxed{\, h = 0.944 \left( \dfrac{\eta \, U}{\rho \, g} \right)^{1/2} \left( \dfrac{\eta \, U}{\gamma_{LV}} \right)^{1/6} \,}$$

Or in compact form:

$$h = 0.944 \, \ell_c \, Ca^{2/3} \quad \text{where} \quad \ell_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma_{LV}}{\rho g}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of the equation:**
> - $h$ increases with $\eta$ (thicker liquid → thicker film).
> - $h$ increases with $U$ (faster withdrawal → thicker film).
> - $h$ decreases with $\rho$, $g$, $\gamma_{LV}$.
> - The **Capillary number exponent of 1/6** shows that surface tension has only a weak influence on film thickness.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Spray Gun Flow Rate (Continuity + Bernoulli)

For an **airless spray gun** with a circular orifice of diameter $d$ and area $A_n = \pi d^2 / 4$, the liquid is pressurized to $\Delta P$ above atmospheric. Applying Bernoulli's equation between the liquid reservoir (at rest, pressure $P_0 + \Delta P$) and the exit plane (atmospheric pressure, velocity $v$):

$$P_0 + \Delta P = P_0 + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2$$

Solving for exit velocity:

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

The **volumetric flow rate** corrected by a discharge coefficient $C_d \approx 0.6$ (accounts for viscous losses and vena contracta):

$$Q = C_d \, A_n \, v = C_d \, A_n \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

For typical spray operation:
- $d = 0.5$ mm → $A_n = 1.96 \times 10^{-7}$ m²
- $\Delta P = 200$ bar = $2 \times 10^{7}$ Pa
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ (water-based paint)
- $C_d = 0.6$

$$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 2 \times 10^7}{1000}} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^4} = 200 \; \text{m/s}$$

$$Q = 0.6 \times 1.96 \times 10^{-7} \times 200 = 2.35 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 1.41 \; \text{L/min}$$

This validates the operating range of a single airless spray gun.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Model:** These are the *exact type* of numerical problems asked in 14-mark questions. Practice each one.

### 3.3.1 Example 1 — Dip Coating Film Thickness

**Problem:** A vertical glass plate is withdrawn at $U = 5$ cm/min from a PVC plastisol bath. The plastisol has $\eta = 0.5$ Pa·s, $\rho = 1100$ kg/m³, and $\gamma_{LV} = 0.04$ N/m. Calculate the wet film thickness.

**Step 1: Convert units to SI.**
$$U = 5 \; \text{cm/min} = \dfrac{0.05}{60} \; \text{m/s} = 8.33 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute the gravity term.**
$$\dfrac{\eta U}{\rho g} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 8.33 \times 10^{-4}}{1100 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{10791} = 3.86 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{m}^2$$

$$\left( \dfrac{\eta U}{\rho g} \right)^{1/2} = 1.965 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}$$

**Step 3: Compute the capillary term.**
$$Ca = \dfrac{\eta U}{\gamma_{LV}} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 8.33 \times 10^{-4}}{0.04} = 0.0104$$

$$Ca^{1/6} = 0.0104^{0.1667} = 0.479$$

**Step 4: Apply the LLD equation.**
$$h = 0.944 \times 1.965 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.479 = 8.88 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m} = 88.8 \; \mu m$$

**Result:** The wet film thickness is approximately **89 µm** — typical for dip-coated plastisol films.

---

### 3.3.2 Example 2 — Spray Coverage Rate

**Problem:** A coating line uses 4 spray guns, each delivering $0.4$ L/min, on a web moving at $v = 6$ m/min. The spray width is $W = 0.5$ m. Calculate:
(i) Coverage rate per minute.
(ii) Wet film thickness.

**Step (i): Coverage rate.**
$$CR = \dfrac{60 \, Q_g \, N}{v \, W} = \dfrac{60 \times 0.4 \times 4}{6 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{96}{3} = 32 \; \text{m}^2/\text{min}$$

**Step (ii): Wet film thickness.**
$$h_{wet} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{v \, W} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3/\text{min}}{6 \times 0.5} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 5.33 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m} = 533 \; \mu m$$

**Result:** Coverage rate = **32 m²/min**, WFT = **533 µm**.

---

### 3.3.3 Example 3 — Roll Coating Film Thickness (Forward Roll Coater)

**Problem:** A forward roll coater has rolls of radius $R_1 = R_2 = 15$ cm, both rotating at $\omega = 30$ rpm. The gap is set such that the geometric fill fraction is $f/G = 0.5$. Estimate the film thickness using the simplified Pearson formula:

$$h = \dfrac{1}{2} R \left( \dfrac{f}{G} \right)^{1/2}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute values.
$$h = 0.5 \times 0.15 \times (0.5)^{0.5} = 0.075 \times 0.707 = 0.053 \; \text{m} = 53 \; \text{mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Note:** The Pearson formula above gives a *geometric* (rough) estimate. Real roll coaters use empirically calibrated *Bland–Newman* or *Pitts–Greille* charts that account for capillary number, roll speeds, and surface tension. KTU 2024 questions typically provide the simplified form.

---

### 3.3.4 Example 4 — Extrusion Coating Throughput

**Problem:** A slot die has a width $W = 1.2$ m, lip opening $h = 0.5$ mm, and the LDPE melt has $\eta = 1000$ Pa·s. The pressure across the die is $\Delta P = 5 \times 10^6$ Pa. The web speed is $v = 200$ m/min. Estimate:
(i) Volumetric flow rate.
(ii) Coating weight in gsm ($\rho_{LDPE} = 920$ kg/m³).

**Step (i): Volumetric flow rate (Poiseuille flow in slot die).**
$$Q = \dfrac{W \, h^3 \, \Delta P}{12 \, \eta \, L}$$

Assuming a standard die land length $L = 50$ mm = 0.05 m:

$$Q = \dfrac{1.2 \times (5 \times 10^{-4})^3 \times 5 \times 10^6}{12 \times 1000 \times 0.05} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-10} \times 5 \times 10^6}{600}$$

$$Q = \dfrac{7.5 \times 10^{-4}}{600} = 1.25 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 4.5 \; \text{L/hr}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **A more realistic approach in industry** uses the simpler output equation:
> $$\dot{m} = \rho \, Q = 920 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-6} = 1.15 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg/s} = 4.14 \; \text{kg/hr}$$

**Step (ii): Coating weight.**

Per unit area coated per minute = $v \times W = 200 \times 1.2 = 240 \; \text{m}^2/\text{min}$

$$C_{gsm} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \; (\text{in g/min})}{v \times W} = \dfrac{1.15 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times 1000}{240} = \dfrac{69}{240} = 0.288 \; \text{g/m}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The above value is unrealistically low** because the simplified Poiseuille estimate used a small pressure. In practice, slot dies operate at $Q$ 100–1000× higher with larger $h$. KTU exam questions usually provide numerical values that yield a **reasonable gsm (10–50 gsm)**. The mathematical *methodology* is what is being tested.

---

### 3.3.5 Example 5 — Electrostatic Spray — Deposition Efficiency

**Problem:** A conventional air spray gun has a transfer efficiency of 35 % (i.e., 65 % of paint becomes overspray). An electrostatic spray gun has a transfer efficiency of 85 %. The required paint on the substrate is 50 L. How much paint must be loaded in each case?

**Conventional:** Paint loaded = $\dfrac{50}{0.35} = 142.9$ L
**Electrostatic:** Paint loaded = $\dfrac{50}{0.85} = 58.8$ L

**Savings:** $142.9 - 58.8 = 84.1$ L per batch, or **59 % reduction** in paint consumption.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **electrostatic coating is mandated** in modern automotive OEM plants and why it forms a 7-mark question in KTU every year.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define polymer application methods. List any four major application methods used in polymer product manufacturing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Polymer application methods are the engineering techniques and unit operations used to deposit, coat, laminate, or mold polymeric materials onto substrates or into cavities to obtain products with defined geometry, thickness, and functional properties.
- **List of four methods (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
  1. **Spray coating** (conventional, airless, electrostatic).
  2. **Dip coating**.
  3. **Roll coating** (direct, reverse, kiss, gravure).
  4. **Extrusion coating** (or any of: curtain coating, lamination, injection molding, blow molding, calendering).

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**What is electrostatic spray coating? State any two of its advantages over conventional air spray.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Electrostatic spray coating is an application technique in which the polymer droplets are electrically charged (typically –30 to –100 kV) as they leave the spray gun. The charged droplets are attracted along the field lines to the grounded (or oppositely charged) substrate, producing a uniform wrap-around coating.
- **Two advantages (0.75 each):**
  1. **High transfer efficiency (85–95 %)** versus 30–40 % for conventional air spray, reducing paint wastage and VOC emissions.
  2. **Wrap-around effect** — coating reaches the sides and back of the substrate, eliminating the need for rotating fixtures.
  3. *(Optional third, if asked)* Uniform film thickness, less overspray, lower air consumption, suitable for automation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Skipping a neat sketch (loses 2–3 marks).** Every Part B answer *must* include a labelled diagram. Verbal description alone scores at most 60 % of marks.
> - **Confusing "direct" and "reverse" roll coaters** is a top reason for 2-mark deductions. Direct = same direction as web; Reverse = opposite.
> - **In dip-coating derivations, omitting the Capillary number term** ($Ca^{1/6}$) costs a full mark. The LLD equation has *two* factors — both must be present.
> - **Listing advantages without quantitative support** loses marks. Always state numerical transfer efficiency, line speed, or film thickness.
> - **Mixing units (cm/min, mm, bar)** without conversion is a 1-mark penalty per occurrence.

---

### Question B-1 (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of **extrusion coating** with a neat block diagram. List the major process variables and the typical range of line speeds. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **spray coating, dip coating, and roll coating** based on (i) principle, (ii) typical viscosity range, (iii) production speed, (iv) film thickness control, and (v) suitable applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Principle (2 marks):**
Extrusion coating is a melt-applied method in which a thermoplastic polymer (LDPE, LLDPE, PP, EVOH, PET) is melted in an extruder, homogenized by a melt pump, and forced through a wide **slot die** to form a thin molten curtain. The curtain is drawn down by a moving substrate web and pressed against a **chill roll** at a nip. The substrate is unwound, often surface-treated (corona, ozone, or flame) to improve adhesion, passes through the nip, and is then trimmed, edge-cut, and wound up.

**Block diagram (2 marks — must be drawn, refer to Section 4.2 above):**
The path is: Hopper → Extruder → Melt Pump → Slot Die → Air Gap → Nip + Chill Roll → Trimmer → Winder. The substrate is fed in parallel and combined at the nip.

**Process variables (2 marks):**
- **Melt temperature:** 200–330 °C (LDPE ~ 280 °C, PP ~ 290 °C, PET ~ 310 °C).
- **Line speed:** **100–600 m/min** standard; modern lines reach **1000–1500 m/min**.
- **Coating weight:** 5–100 gsm.
- **Air gap (die-to-nip distance):** 100–300 mm.
- **Nip pressure:** 20–100 kN/m.
- **Chill roll temperature:** 15–30 °C.

**Applications (1 mark):** Milk cartons (Tetra Pak), juice boxes, pharma blister foil, snack packaging, pet food bags, multi-layer barrier films.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

| Criterion | Spray Coating | Dip Coating | Roll Coating |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Principle** | Atomize liquid into droplets; project onto substrate | Immerse substrate, withdraw at controlled speed; film forms by entrainment | Meter polymer through gap between rotating rolls; transfer to substrate |
| **(ii) Viscosity range** | 0.001 – 1 Pa·s (thin) | 0.05 – 5 Pa·s (medium) | 0.1 – 100 Pa·s (broad) |
| **(iii) Production speed** | 1 – 30 m/min (manual); up to 100 m/min (automated) | 0.1 – 10 m/min | 5 – 1500 m/min |
| **(iv) Film thickness control** | Moderate (±10 %); depends on operator/gun settings | Good (±2 %); governed by LLD equation | Excellent (±1 %); calibrated by roll gap and speed |
| **(v) Typical applications** | Automotive paint, aircraft finish, anti-corrosion coatings | Medical gloves, dipped rubber goods, plastisol films | Adhesive tapes, photographic film, paper coatings, packaging laminates |

(Allocate ~1.4 marks per row × 5 rows = 7 marks.)

---

### Question B-2 (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Describe the working of **direct, reverse, and gravure roll coaters** with neat sketches. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a diagram, explain the **electrostatic spray coating process**. State its **advantages** and **limitations**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**Direct Roll Coater (2 marks):**
- Two rolls, R₁ (applicator) and R₂ (backing), rotate in the *same* direction as the moving web.
- The polymer liquid is fed into the nip between the rolls. The web passes around the lower roll and picks up the polymer.
- **Limitation:** Film thickness is non-uniform; suitable for rough substrates.
- **Application:** Adhesive coating on paper, simple paint jobs.

**Reverse Roll Coater (2.5 marks):**
- Top applicator roll rotates *opposite* to the web direction.
- A metering roll controls the amount of polymer on the applicator roll. A backing roll supports the web.
- The doctor blade on the applicator roll ensures a precise, uniform film.
- **Film thickness:** $h = (1/2)(f/G)^{1/2} \cdot R$ (Pearson approximation, where $f$ is film thickness, $G$ is gap).
- **Application:** Photographic film, magnetic tape, precision coatings.

**Gravure Roll Coater (2.5 marks):**
- A chrome-plated steel roll with **engraved micro-cells** (typically 50–500 cells/cm², cell depth 5–50 µm).
- The engraved roll dips into a polymer bath; excess is wiped by a **doctor blade**, leaving a metered quantity in the cells.
- The roll contacts the substrate, transferring the polymer in a pattern.
- **Cell volume** (in cm³/m²) directly determines the coating weight.
- **Application:** Decorative laminates, printing inks, pressure-sensitive adhesives, packaging films.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Diagram (2 marks — refer to functional description below):**
The setup consists of:
- A **paint reservoir / cup** at the spray gun.
- An **electrode** (sharp needle at the gun nozzle) connected to a **high-voltage DC source** (–30 to –100 kV).
- The **substrate** mounted on a grounded metal rack or conveyor.
- An **air atomizer** that breaks the liquid into droplets, immediately charged by the electrode.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Liquid paint is atomized into droplets (5–100 µm).
- The high-voltage electrode imparts a negative charge to each droplet (charge-to-mass ratio ~ 1–10 mC/kg).
- The charged droplets travel along electric field lines toward the grounded substrate.
- The charged paint "wraps around" the back of the part, ensuring uniform coverage even on complex geometries.
- After deposition, the part enters a curing oven (IR, convection, or UV) for cross-linking.

**Advantages (1.5 marks — list 3 of the following):**
1. **High transfer efficiency (85–95 %)** — drastically reduces paint wastage and VOC emissions.
2. **Wrap-around effect** — uniform coating on edges and complex shapes.
3. **Lower booth maintenance** — less overspray on walls, ducts, filters.
4. **Automation-friendly** — easily integrated into robotic lines.
5. **Reduced health hazard** — less airborne paint mist.

**Limitations (1.5 marks — list 3 of the following):**
1. **Faraday cage effect** — coating difficulty in deep recesses and cavities.
2. **Grounding required** — non-conductive substrates need a conductive primer.
3. **High-voltage safety** — risk of spark ignition with flammable solvents (requires intrinsically safe designs).
4. **Initial capital cost** — higher than conventional spray equipment.
5. **Not suitable for highly resistive paints** (must have at least some conductivity).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4: Application Methods**

### A. Definitions (must memorize verbatim)
- **Application method** — engineering technique to deposit/coat/mold polymer onto a substrate or into a cavity.
- **Capillary number** — $Ca = \eta U / \gamma_{LV}$ — ratio of viscous to surface-tension forces.
- **Transfer efficiency** — fraction of polymer that actually deposits on the substrate (not lost as overspray).
- **LLD film thickness** — the theoretical film thickness in dip coating given by $h = 0.944 (\eta U / \rho g)^{1/2} (Ca)^{1/6}$.

### B. The Big Six Application Methods — One-line recall
1. **Spray** — atomize + project. Droplet size 20–500 µm.
2. **Dip** — immerse + withdraw. Film governed by LLD equation.
3. **Roll** — meter + transfer. Sub-types: direct, reverse, kiss, gravure, Mayer rod.
4. **Extrusion** — melt + extrude + nip. 100–1500 m/min.
5. **Curtain** — free-falling liquid film. Independent of line speed.
6. **Brush** — manual. Low capital cost, low precision.

### C. The Top 5 Equations
1. **LLD film thickness:** $h = 0.944 (\eta U / \rho g)^{1/2} (\eta U / \gamma_{LV})^{1/6}$
2. **Capillary number:** $Ca = \eta U / \gamma_{LV}$
3. **Spray flow rate:** $Q = C_d A_n \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$
4. **Coverage rate:** $CR = 60 \, Q_g \, N / (v \, W)$
5. **Wet film thickness:** $h_{wet} = Q / (v \, W)$

### D. Numerical Constants to Remember
- Acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$**.
- Airless spray pressure: **100–500 bar**.
- HVLP pressure: **0.5–1.0 bar**.
- Electrostatic spray voltage: **–30 to –100 kV**.
- Conventional air spray transfer efficiency: **30–40 %**.
- Electrostatic spray transfer efficiency: **85–95 %**.
- Modern extrusion coating line speed: **up to 1500 m/min**.
- LLD prefactor: **0.944** (sometimes written as 0.93 or 0.95 in different textbooks — do not lose marks by mixing them up; the 0.944 value is most accepted).

### E. Common Mistakes to Avoid
- Do **not** confuse *film thickness* (h) with *coating weight* (gsm) — they are related by density: $gsm = \rho \cdot h$.
- Do **not** state the LLD equation without both the gravity term *and* the capillary term.
- Do **not** omit the **neat sketch** in any 7-mark sub-part.
- Do **not** confuse **roll coater** sub-types — pay attention to *direction of rotation*.
- Do **not** apply dip-coating theory to **highly viscous** polymers (LLD is valid only in the capillary-number regime $Ca < 1$).

### F. Industry-to-Syllabus Quick Map

| Industry Need | KTU Application Method |
|---|---|
| Milk carton inner coating | Extrusion coating (LDPE) |
| Car body paint | Electrostatic spray |
| Surgical gloves | Dip coating (latex) |
| Magazine cover gloss | Curtain coating |
| Scotch tape adhesive | Hot-melt roll / gravure |
| PVC pipe fittings | Injection molding (specialized) |
| Bullet-proof glass interlayer | Cast film / lamination |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final exam tip:** Out of a 100-mark KTU 2024 question paper, expect **15–20 marks** to be directly drawn from Module 4 (Application Methods). Of this, **at least one 7-mark question** will require a labelled diagram and **at least one 7-mark question** will require a numerical solution. The two model answers above (Q-B1 and Q-B2) are the **most representative templates** for your preparation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Powder Coating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Types of Powder Coating ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Powder Coating** is defined as a **100% solids, dry-finish coating process** in which finely ground particles of pigmented resin are electrostatically or fluidized-bed applied to a substrate, then fused and cured under heat to form a continuous, durable polymeric film — *without the use of solvent carriers*. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPOT503 – Module 4)**, powder coatings are broadly classified on **two primary axes**: 1. **Chemistry of the binder resin** → Thermoplastic vs. Thermosetting 2. **Method of application** → Electrostatic Spray, Fluidized Bed, Electrostatic Fluidized Bed, and Flame Spray > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:** Students are *specifically* expected to differentiate the **mechanism of film formation** between thermoplastic (physical melt-fusion) and thermosetting (chemical crosslinking) powder coatings, and identify the application method suited to each. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding Imagine you are decorating a **metal fence** with color. With **liquid paint**, you have a wet chemical soup of pigment + resin + solvent — the solvent evaporates leaving the color. The smell, dripping, and drying time are unavoidable. Now, picture instead that you have a **dry, flour-like powder** that you gently throw at the fence. The powder *clings* because of static electricity (like a balloon rubbed on wool). You then **heat the fence in an oven**, and the powder melts, flows, and hardens into a smooth, glossy skin — much like how a **cheese slice melts and solidifies on a hot pizza**. That is **powder coating** in a single breath. The "no solvent" nature makes it a **Green / Sustainable Coating** process — **VOC (Volatile Organic Compound) emissions ≈ 0 g/L**, compared to **~300–500 g/L** in conventional liquid paints. > [!TIP] > **Industry Fact:** The global powder coating market exceeds **USD 15 billion** and is the fastest-growing industrial finishing technology because it eliminates VOC emissions and recovers **up to 95–98% of overspray**. --- ### 1.3 Key Industrial Metrics (Bold Constants & Standards) | Parameter | Standard Value | |---|---| | Average Particle Size | **30 – 50 μm** | | Curing Temperature Range | **160 °C – 220 °C** | | Curing Time | **10 – 25 minutes** | | Film Thickness | **50 – 120 μm** | | Transfer Efficiency | **> 95%** (electrostatic) | | VOC Content | **0 g/L** | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Master Classification Tree of Powder Coatings ``` POWDER COATINGS | __________________|__________________ | | THERMOPLASTIC THERMOSETTING (Physical Fusion) (Chemical Crosslink) | | -- No chemical reaction -- Irreversible cure -- Reversible melt -- 3D network forms -- High film build possible -- Better chemical resistance -- Thick films (250-500 μm) -- Thin films (50-80 μm) | | Examples: Examples: - PVC, Nylon-11, - Epoxy Polyethylene, - Polyester Polypropylene, - Epoxy-Polyester (Hybrid) PVDF - Acrylic, Polyurethane ``` --- ### 2.2 Type I — Thermoplastic Powder Coatings #### 2.2.1 Operational Principle In thermoplastic systems, **no chemical reaction** occurs during film formation. The powder is simply **melted, flowed, and re-solidified**. Because the polymer chains are not crosslinked, the film is **re-meltable** and **recyclable**. #### 2.2.2 Film Formation Steps (Why & How) - **Step 1 — Preheat Substrate:** The metal is heated above the polymer melting point (e.g., **Nylon-11 melts at ~186 °C**). This is necessary so the powder will fuse *on contact*. - **Step 2 — Powder Application:** The powder is applied via **fluidized bed** (preferred for thick films) or electrostatic spray. - **Step 3 — Melt Fusion:** Contact with the hot substrate causes the powder to **melt, coalesce, and flow**, eliminating voids. - **Step 4 — Cool & Solidify:** The substrate is cooled, and the polymer **re-crystallizes or vitrifies** into a continuous film. > [!NOTE] > **Why no curing oven?** Because no crosslinking is required — only physical phase change. This dramatically **reduces energy cost** for thick-film applications. #### 2.2.3 Common Thermoplastic Powder Resins | Resin | Melting Point (°C) | Key Properties | Typical Applications | |---|---|---|---| | **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride)** | 160 – 180 | Chemical resistance, flexibility | Chain-link fencing, dishwasher racks | | **Nylon-11 / Nylon-12** | 180 – 190 | Abrasion resistance, low friction | Sprockets, gears, marine hardware | | **Polyethylene (HDPE / LDPE)** | 120 – 130 | Low cost, food contact safe | Toy parts, pipe coatings | | **Polypropylene** | 160 – 170 | Toughness, chemical resistance | Battery cases, automotive | | **PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride)** | 165 – 170 | UV stability, weatherability | Architectural panels, chimneys | --- ### 2.3 Type II — Thermosetting Powder Coatings #### 2.3.1 Operational Principle Thermosetting systems rely on an **irreversible chemical crosslinking reaction** between the **base resin**, a **hardener (crosslinker)**, and optionally a **catalyst**. Once cured, the coating **cannot be re-melted** — it forms a 3D crosslinked network. #### 2.3.2 Film Formation Steps (Why & How) - **Step 1 — Substrate Preparation:** Degreasing, phosphating, or chromating to ensure adhesion. - **Step 2 — Cold Electrostatic Spray:** Powder is sprayed on a **cold or pre-heated** substrate using corona or tribo charging. - **Step 3 — Melt Phase:** Substrate enters a **curing oven** at **160 – 220 °C**. Powder melts and flows to form a continuous wet film. - **Step 4 — Crosslinking (Cure):** A chemical reaction between resin and hardener creates a **3D molecular network** — this is the *irreversible* step. - **Step 5 — Cooling & Hardening:** The film cools to a hard, solvent-resistant finish. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** *Always* distinguish — Thermoplastic = **physical fusion only**, Thermosetting = **physical fusion + chemical crosslinking**. Examiners love this contrast question. #### 2.3.3 The Four Major Thermosetting Chemistries **A) Epoxy Powder Coatings** - **Base Resin:** Bisphenol-A epoxy - **Hardener:** Dicyandiamide (DICY), phenolic, or anhydride - **Reactivity:** Cures at **180 – 200 °C** for 10 – 15 min - **Properties:** Excellent **chemical and corrosion resistance**, poor UV stability (chalk on outdoor exposure) - **Uses:** Electrical insulation, pipelines, automotive underbody, primer coats **B) Polyester Powder Coatings** - **Base Resin:** Carboxyl- or hydroxyl-terminated polyester - **Hardener:** TGIC (Triglycidyl Isocyanurate) or HAA (Hydroxyalkyl Amide) - **Reactivity:** 180 – 200 °C for 10 – 15 min - **Properties:** Excellent **weatherability (UV)**, good mechanical properties - **Uses:** Architectural facades, outdoor furniture, garden equipment - **KTU Note:** Modern HAA systems replace toxic TGIC — **HAA = TGIC-free green chemistry** **C) Epoxy-Polyester Hybrid (Most Widely Used)** - **Composition:** 50:50 to 70:30 ratio of epoxy to polyester - **Hardener:** No external hardener — the polyester acid groups react with epoxy - **Properties:** Combines epoxy's chemical resistance with polyester's flexibility; better overbake resistance - **Uses:** Domestic appliances, office furniture, shelving, radiators - **Market Share:** **~50% of all thermoset powder coatings produced** **D) Polyurethane Powder Coatings** - **Base Resin:** Hydroxyl-functional polyester - **Hardener:** **Blocked Isocyanate (IPDI or TDI blocked)** - **Properties:** Excellent flow, smooth finish, good chemical & UV resistance - **Uses:** Automotive clear coats, farm equipment, lawn mowers - **Mechanism:** Heat *unblocks* the isocyanate, freeing it to react with —OH groups **E) Acrylic Powder Coatings** - **Hardener:** Epoxy or DICY - **Properties:** Excellent clarity, UV resistance, transparency - **Uses:** **Clear coats for automotive (clear-over-base systems)** --- ### 2.4 Application Methods (Sub-classification) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Expectation:** Every powder coating type is paired with an *appropriate* application technique. Students must justify *why* a particular method is used. #### Method 1: Electrostatic Spray (Most Common — ~80% of all powder applied) - **Working Principle:** Powder particles pass through a high-voltage corona field (**30 – 100 kV**) and acquire a **negative charge**. - **Deposition:** The charged powder is attracted to the **grounded (earthed) workpiece**. - **Equipment:** Corona gun or tribo gun. - **KTU Equation — Coulombic Attraction Force:** $$ F = \frac{k \cdot Q_1 \cdot Q_2}{d^2} $$ - where **F** = force of attraction, **k** = Coulomb's constant, **Q₁, Q₂** = charges on particle and substrate, **d** = gap distance. - **Best For:** Thin-film thermoset coatings on conductive substrates. #### Method 2: Fluidized Bed Coating - **Working Principle:** Powder is suspended in air inside a porous-bottomed tank, behaving like a **boiling liquid**. The **preheated** substrate is dipped into the bed. - **Process Temperature:** Substrate is heated to **above the powder melting point** (e.g., 200 – 350 °C). - **Film Thickness:** Very thick (200 – 500 μm) in a single dip. - **Best For:** Thermoplastic coatings (Nylon, PE, PVC) on large/heavy parts. #### Method 3: Electrostatic Fluidized Bed (Hybrid) - **Working Principle:** Combines fluidization + electrostatic charging. Electrodes in the bed charge the powder; the substrate picks up charged particles even *without* preheating. - **Best For:** Small parts, electrical insulators, complex geometries. #### Method 4: Flame Spray - **Working Principle:** Powder is fed through a **flame (oxy-fuel)** that melts and projects it onto the substrate. - **Best For:** Field repairs, large structures (bridges, tanks) where oven curing is impractical. --- ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) | # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Coulomb's Force on Powder Particle | $F = \dfrac{k Q_1 Q_2}{d^2}$ | Newton (N) | Determines deposition efficiency in electrostatic spray | | 2 | Particle Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law) | $v = \dfrac{g \, d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_a)}{18 \mu}$ | m/s | Predicts fluidized bed behavior | | 3 | Curing Time (Arrhenius Approx.) | $\ln t_c = \dfrac{E_a}{RT} + \ln A$ | seconds | Estimate cure schedule | | 4 | Transfer Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{\text{deposited}}}{W_{\text{sprayed}}} \times 100$ | % | Process economics | | 5 | Film Build (Theoretical) | $T_f = \dfrac{C \cdot \rho_p \cdot d_p}{\rho_f}$ | μm | Coating thickness control | | 6 | Oven Heat Load | $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ | kJ | Energy required to heat substrate | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for Thermoset Cure Sequence:** *"Cure My Friend Make My Network"* — **Coat → Melt → Flow → Mix → Network**. This 5-step mental model is the *exact* sequence examiners expect in 14-mark answers. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Comparative Analytical Matrix — The Most Important KTU Table > [!IMPORTANT] > **This matrix is a recurring 14-mark question in KTU exams.** Memorize the contrast points. | Property | Thermoplastic | Thermosetting | |---|---|---| | **Mechanism** | Physical melt-fusion | Chemical crosslinking | | **Reversibility** | Reversible (re-meltable) | Irreversible (3D network) | | **Curing Oven Required?** | Not always (substrate heat suffices) | Yes — mandatory | | **Typical Film Thickness** | 200 – 500 μm (thick) | 50 – 80 μm (thin) | | **Chemical Resistance** | Moderate | Excellent | | **UV / Weatherability** | Variable (PVDF best) | Polyester / Acrylic best | | **Recyclability of Powder** | Yes | No (cured rejects cannot be re-used as film) | | **Overspray Recovery** | Yes, easy | Yes, can be re-blended up to 30% | | **Common Application** | Fluidized bed | Electrostatic spray | | **Energy Intensity** | Lower | Higher (oven cure) | | **Cost per kg** | Higher (engineering resins) | Lower (commodity resins) | | **Example Applications** | Sprockets, fences | Appliances, automotive | | **Shelf Life of Powder** | Indefinite (if dry) | 6 – 12 months | --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Why Electrostatic Spray Gives >95% Transfer Efficiency Let us derive the **theoretical transfer efficiency** $\eta$ of an electrostatic powder spray process. #### Step 1 — Define the charge acquired by a powder particle A spherical powder particle of diameter $d_p$ in a corona field of strength $E$ acquires a saturation charge $Q_s$ given by the **Pauthenier equation**: $$ Q_s = \left(1 + \frac{2 \epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2}\right) \pi \epsilon_0 E d_p^{\,2} $$ #### Step 2 — Force balance on a charged particle The electrostatic force pulling the particle to the substrate must overcome gravitational force: $$ F_{\text{electrostatic}} \geq F_{\text{gravity}} $$ $$ Q_s \cdot E_{\text{field}} \geq m_p g $$ where $m_p = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{\,3} \rho_p$ is the particle mass, and $\rho_p$ is the powder density. #### Step 3 — Substitute and simplify $$ \left(1 + \frac{2 \epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2}\right) \pi \epsilon_0 E^2 d_p^{\,2} \geq \frac{\pi}{6} d_p^{\,3} \rho_p g $$ Dividing both sides by $\pi d_p^{\,2}$: $$ \left(1 + \frac{2 \epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2}\right) \epsilon_0 E^2 \geq \frac{1}{6} d_p \, \rho_p \, g $$ #### Step 4 — Solve for minimum particle size that will be retained $$ d_p^{\min} = \frac{6 \epsilon_0 E^2 \left(1 + \frac{2 \epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2}\right)}{\rho_p \, g} $$ #### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation For typical values: - $E = 5 \times 10^5$ V/m - $\epsilon_r = 4$ (typical polymer) - $\rho_p = 1500$ kg/m³ - $\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m - $g = 9.81$ m/s² $$ d_p^{\min} = \frac{6 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 25 \times 10^{10} \times \left(1 + \frac{7}{6}\right)}{1500 \times 9.81} $$ $$ d_p^{\min} = \frac{6 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.5 \times 10^{11} \times 2.1667}{14715} $$ $$ d_p^{\min} = \frac{6 \times 8.854 \times 2.1667 \times 10^{-1}}{14715} \approx 7.8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} $$ $$ d_p^{\min} \approx 7.8 \ \mu m $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion of Derivation:** Any powder particle **larger than ~8 μm** will be electrostatically retained on the substrate. Since commercial powders are **30 – 50 μm**, virtually the entire spray reaches the part — explaining the **>95% transfer efficiency**. This derivation is a KTU-favorite for **14-mark analytical questions**. --- ### 3.3 Selection Logic — How to Choose the Right Powder Type Use the following decision chain: $$ \text{Substrate conductive?} \rightarrow \text{Yes: Electrostatic Spray} $$ $$ \text{Substrate conductive + Thick film?} \rightarrow \text{Fluidized Bed (Thermo)} $$ $$ \text{Outdoor exposure?} \rightarrow \text{Polyester or Acrylic} $$ $$ \text{Chemical exposure?} \rightarrow \text{Epoxy or Hybrid} $$ $$ \text{Food contact?} \rightarrow \text{PE or Nylon-11} $$ $$ \text{UV transparency required?} \rightarrow \text{Acrylic clear coat} $$ $$ \text{Heat-sensitive substrate?} \rightarrow \text{Low-cure polyester (140 °C) or UV-cure powder} $$ --- ### 3.4 Worked Example — Calculating Oven Heat Load for a Steel Substrate **Given:** Steel panel, mass $m = 5$ kg, specific heat $c_p = 0.49$ kJ/(kg·K), enters oven at $T_i = 25\ ^\circ C$, curing temperature $T_f = 180\ ^\circ C$. **Find:** Heat load $Q$ in kJ. **Solution:** $$ Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T $$ $$ Q = 5 \times 0.49 \times (180 - 25) $$ $$ Q = 5 \times 0.49 \times 155 $$ $$ Q = 379.75 \ \text{kJ} $$ For a production line of 200 panels/hour: $$ Q_{\text{hourly}} = 200 \times 379.75 = 75{,}950 \ \text{kJ/h} \approx 21 \ \text{kWh} $$ > [!TIP] > This is **why thermoset powder coating is energy-intensive** — energy cost is a major component of unit economics. Engineers select low-temperature-cure (LTC) polyesters (140 °C) to reduce this load by ~25%. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Powder Coating Line ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Metal Substrate] --> B[Pre-Treatment Tank
Degrease + Phosphating] B --> C[Drying Oven 80 C] C --> D[Masking Station] D --> E[Powder Application Booth] E --> F1[Corona Spray Gun] E --> F2[Fluidized Bed Tank] E --> F3[Tribo Spray Gun] F1 --> G[Recovery Cyclone] F2 --> G F3 --> G G --> H[Powder Re-blend Silo] H --> E E --> I[Melting Zone 120 C] I --> J[Curing Zone 180 200 C] J --> K[Cooling Tunnel Forced Air] K --> L[Inspection and QC] L --> M[Finished Coated Part] style A fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca style M fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d style E fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207 style J fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c ``` ### 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Choosing a Powder Type ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start: Choose Powder Type]) --> Q1{Application Film Thickness?} Q1 -->|Thick 200 to 500 um| Q2{Substrate Pre-heated?} Q1 -->|Thin 50 to 80 um| Q3{End-use Environment?} Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Thermoplastic + Fluidized Bed] Q2 -->|No| R2[Electrostatic Fluidized Bed + Thermo] Q3 -->|Outdoor UV| R3[Polyester TGIC or HAA] Q3 -->|Indoor + Chemical| R4[Epoxy Polyester Hybrid] Q3 -->|Pure Corrosion| R5[Epoxy] Q3 -->|Automotive Clear| R6[Acrylic] Q3 -->|High Impact| R7[Polyurethane] R1 --> End([Selected]) R2 --> End R3 --> End R4 --> End R5 --> End R6 --> End R7 --> End style Start fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8 style End fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534 style R1 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c style R2 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#c2410c style R3 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207 style R4 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207 style R5 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207 style R6 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207 style R7 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207 ``` ### 4.3 Schematic — Curing Reaction Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TP[THERMOPLASTIC: Physical Fusion] A1[Powder Particle] --> A2[Heat Above Tm] A2 --> A3[Melt and Flow] A3 --> A4[Cool and Solidify] A4 --> A5[Linear Chains Reversible] end subgraph TS[THERMOSETTING: Chemical Crosslink] B1[Resin + Hardener + Pigment] --> B2[Heat 160 to 200 C] B2 --> B3[Melt and Flow] B3 --> B4[Crosslinking Reaction] B4 --> B5[3D Network Irreversible] end style A5 fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#047857 style B5 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c ``` ### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Electrostatic Spray System ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Powder Feeder Hopper] --> V[Venturi Air Mix] V --> G[Corona Spray Gun 30 to 100 kV] G -->|Charged powder cloud| W[Grounded Workpiece] W -->|Adhesion by Coulomb force| H[Heated to 160 to 200 C] H --> O[Curing Oven] O --> C[Cooling Zone] C --> Q[Quality Control] OV[Overspray] --> RC[Recovery Cyclone] RC --> S[Sieving] S --> P style P fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a style G fill:#fde68a,stroke:#a16207 style W fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534 style O fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c style RC fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#7e22ce ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between thermoplastic and thermosetting powder coatings with two points of distinction.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | S.No. | Thermoplastic Powder Coating | Thermosetting Powder Coating | |---|---|---| | 1 | Film formation by **physical melt-fusion** only; no chemical reaction. | Film formation by **chemical crosslinking** between resin and hardener. | | 2 | The film is **reversible** — can be re-melted by reheating. | The film is **irreversible** — once cured, cannot be re-melted. | | 3 | Usually applied by **fluidized bed** for thick films (200–500 μm). | Usually applied by **electrostatic spray** for thin films (50–80 μm). | *Marking Key:* 1 mark for each valid point of difference. **Total = 3 marks.** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **List any three commonly used thermosetting powder coating resins.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three most common thermosetting powder coating chemistries are: 1. **Epoxy** — based on Bisphenol-A resin and DICY hardener; offers excellent chemical and corrosion resistance; used in pipelines and electrical insulation. *(1 mark)* 2. **Polyester** — based on carboxyl/hydroxyl polyester with TGIC or HAA hardener; offers excellent UV and weather resistance; used for architectural and outdoor applications. *(1 mark)* 3. **Epoxy-Polyester Hybrid** — 50:50 to 70:30 blend; combines epoxy's chemical resistance with polyester's flexibility; widely used in domestic appliances and furniture. *(1 mark)* *(Optional additional: Polyurethane with blocked isocyanate hardener; Acrylic.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]** **(a)** Classify powder coatings based on the **chemistry of the binder resin**. Explain the mechanism of film formation in **thermosetting powder coatings** with a neat flow diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **electrostatic spray coating process** with a labeled diagram. Explain why the **transfer efficiency exceeds 95%** in this process. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks **1. Classification of powder coatings (2 marks):** Powder coatings are classified into two broad categories based on binder chemistry: - **Thermoplastic powder coatings** (e.g., PVC, Nylon, PE, PVDF) - **Thermosetting powder coatings** (e.g., Epoxy, Polyester, Hybrid, Polyurethane, Acrylic) **2. Mechanism of film formation in thermosetting coatings (4 marks):** The film formation occurs in **four sequential stages**: - **Stage 1 — Application:** Powder is electrostatically sprayed onto a *cold* or *slightly pre-heated* substrate. The charged powder adheres by Coulombic attraction. *(1 mark)* - **Stage 2 — Melting & Flow:** The coated substrate enters a curing oven at **160 – 220 °C**. The powder melts, its viscosity drops, and it **flows out** to form a continuous wet film, eliminating surface texture from the original powder particles. *(1 mark)* - **Stage 3 — Crosslinking (Cure):** A chemical reaction begins between the **base resin functional groups** (e.g., —OH, —COOH, epoxy) and the **hardener** (e.g., DICY, TGIC, HAA, blocked isocyanate). A **3D crosslinked network** forms, providing: - Insolubility - Infusibility - High chemical resistance *(1 mark)* - **Stage 4 — Cooling & Hardening:** The film cools; the crosslinked network is now **irreversible** and the part is ready for inspection. *(1 mark)* **3. Flow diagram (1 mark):** ``` Powder Spray → Melt → Flow → Crosslink → Cool → Hard Film ``` --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks **1. Process description (3 marks):** - Powder is fluidized in a feed hopper and pneumatically conveyed to a **corona discharge gun** operating at **30 – 100 kV DC**. - Powder particles pick up a **negative charge** as they pass through the ionized field at the gun electrode. - The charged powder is projected toward the **grounded (earthed) workpiece**, where it deposits. - After application, the part enters a **curing oven (160 – 220 °C)** for **10 – 25 min** to melt, flow, and crosslink the film. - **Overspray** is recovered in a cyclone separator, sieved, and blended with virgin powder (up to 30%) for re-use. **2. Why transfer efficiency > 95% (4 marks):** Using the **Coulomb force equation** $F = \dfrac{k Q_1 Q_2}{d^2}$, the force on a charged particle in the spray is enormous. As derived in Section 3.2: - For a typical powder of size $d_p = 30 \ \mu m$ and $\rho_p = 1500 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ in a field of $E = 5 \times 10^5$ V/m, the **Pauthenier saturation charge** is: $$ Q_s = \left(1 + \frac{2 \epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2}\right) \pi \epsilon_0 E d_p^{\,2} \approx 3.2 \pi \epsilon_0 E d_p^{\,2} $$ - The electrostatic force $F = Q_s \cdot E$ exceeds the gravitational force $F_g = m_p g$ by a factor of **> 1000**. - Hence, **particles that miss the part on the first pass wrap around due to the field lines** and are still attracted — the "wrap-around" or "self-searching" effect. - This explains the **> 95% transfer efficiency**, far superior to liquid paint (~30–60%). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – July 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain the **fluidized bed coating process** with its working principle, key process parameters, and suitable applications. Compare it with **electrostatic spray coating**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **four major thermosetting chemistries** (Epoxy, Polyester, Epoxy-Polyester Hybrid, Polyurethane) used in powder coatings. Mention the **hardener used** and the **typical application** for each. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks **1. Fluidized Bed Coating — Principle (3 marks):** - A **fluidized bed** is a tank with a **porous membrane at the bottom** through which compressed air is forced upward. - The upward airflow suspends the powder particles, making them behave like a **boiling fluid**. - The **pre-heated substrate** (above the powder melting point, typically **200 – 350 °C**) is dipped into the fluidized powder. - Powder **melts on contact**, fuses, and forms a **thick continuous film (200 – 500 μm)** in a single dip. - The part is withdrawn, allowed to cool, and the film solidifies. **2. Key Process Parameters (2 marks):** - Substrate pre-heat temperature (must exceed polymer Tm) - Dip time (typically 2 – 10 seconds) - Air flow rate (controls fluidization quality) - Powder particle size (typically 100 – 250 μm for fluidized bed, larger than spray) **3. Comparison with Electrostatic Spray (2 marks):** | Parameter | Fluidized Bed | Electrostatic Spray | |---|---|---| | Film Thickness | Thick (200–500 μm) | Thin (50–80 μm) | | Substrate Heating | Required (pre-heated) | Optional | | Resin Type | Thermoplastic (most common) | Thermoset (most common) | | Powder Size | Coarse (100–250 μm) | Fine (30–50 μm) | | Application | Large/heavy parts | Decorative, complex shapes | --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks | # | Chemistry | Hardener / Crosslinker | Cure Schedule | Key Properties | Application | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Epoxy** | DICY / Phenolic / Anhydride | 180–200 °C / 10–15 min | Excellent chemical & corrosion resistance; poor UV | Electrical insulation, pipelines, primers | | 2 | **Polyester** | TGIC or HAA | 180–200 °C / 10–15 min | Excellent UV & weatherability | Architectural panels, outdoor furniture, fencing | | 3 | **Epoxy-Polyester Hybrid** | No external hardener (epoxy + COOH of polyester) | 180–200 °C / 10–15 min | Balanced properties; good flow & flexibility | Domestic appliances, office furniture | | 4 | **Polyurethane** | Blocked Isocyanate (IPDI/TDI) | 180–200 °C / 15–20 min | Excellent flow, smoothness, weatherability | Automotive clear coats, lawn mowers, farm equipment | *Each row ≈ 1.5–2 marks; mention **resin**, **hardener**, **cure schedule**, and **application**.* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in this topic:** > 1. **Confusing thermoplastic with thermoset** film formation — examiners specifically test the *"physical vs. chemical"* mechanism. Writing *"melting occurs in both"* gets **zero marks**. > 2. **Forgetting to name the hardener/crosslinker** in thermoset answers — always write *"DICY, TGIC, HAA, or blocked isocyanate"*. > 3. **Wrong application method pairing** — Nylon/PE is *fluidized bed*, not electrostatic. Polyester is *electrostatic spray*, not fluidized bed. > 4. **Not mentioning "VOC = 0"** when asked *"why powder coating is eco-friendly"*. This is a **3-marker line item** always expected. > 5. **Missing the 3D crosslinked network** concept in thermoset answers — without this phrase, an examiner cannot award full marks on the "irreversibility" question. > 6. **Skipping units** in numerical derivations (e.g., $d_p^{\min}$ in μm). > 7. **Confusing TGIC with HAA** — TGIC is being phased out due to toxicity (mutagenic); HAA is the modern TGIC-free alternative. KTU 2024 expects awareness of this regulatory shift. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision — 10 Bullet Points** - [x] **Two master types:** Thermoplastic (physical) vs. Thermosetting (chemical crosslinking). - [x] **Thermoplastic examples:** PVC, Nylon-11/12, PE, PP, PVDF → applied by **fluidized bed** for thick films. - [x] **Thermosetting examples:** Epoxy, Polyester, Hybrid, Polyurethane, Acrylic → applied by **electrostatic spray** for thin films. - [x] **Hardeners you MUST memorize:** DICY (epoxy), TGIC/HAA (polyester), Blocked Isocyanate (PU). - [x] **Cure schedule:** **160 – 220 °C for 10 – 25 minutes** is the standard oven window. - [x] **Electrostatic gun voltage:** **30 – 100 kV DC** (corona charging). - [x] **Coulomb force governs adhesion:** $F = \dfrac{k Q_1 Q_2}{d^2}$ — this is why transfer efficiency > 95%. - [x] **Hybrid (epoxy-polyester) is the volume leader** — about 50% of thermoset powder coating market. - [x] **No VOC emissions, 95%+ powder recovery** = powder coating is the **greenest industrial finishing technology**. - [x] **Modern shift:** TGIC → HAA (regulatory-driven), Low-Temperature Cure (LTC) polyesters at 140 °C (energy-driven), UV-cure powders (speed-driven).

topics.title: Finishing and Machining of Plastics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Finishing and Machining of Plastics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
