> **Finishing of Plastics** refers to the secondary manufacturing operations performed on moulded, extruded, or otherwise formed plastic products to improve their surface quality, dimensional accuracy, aesthetic appearance, and functional performance. It encompasses a wide range of processes including deflashing, sanding, buffing, polishing, painting, metallizing, printing, and surface texturing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machining of Plastics** is defined as the controlled material removal process applied to plastic workpieces (thermoplastics, thermosets, or composites) using conventional or specialized machine tools. The objective is to achieve tighter dimensional tolerances, complex geometries, drilled holes, threaded features, or mating surfaces that cannot be obtained directly from the primary forming operation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just baked a clay sculpture (analogous to a moulded plastic part). The raw sculpture has rough edges (flash), uneven surfaces, and may not fit perfectly with another piece. To make it presentable and functional, you would:
- **Trim off the excess clay** (deflashing/trimming) — analogous to deflashing
- **Smooth the rough surfaces** with sandpaper (sanding) — analogous to sanding and grinding
- **Apply a glossy coat** (painting/polishing) — analogous to surface coating
- **Carve fine details** with a knife (machining) — analogous to CNC milling of plastics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Plastics behave fundamentally differently from metals during machining. They are **soft, viscoelastic, and have low thermal conductivity** (typically **0.1–0.5 W/m·K** versus **45 W/m·K** for steel). This means heat generated during cutting does not dissipate quickly, leading to localized melting, gumming, and poor surface finish if cutting parameters are not properly chosen.

### 1.3 Classification of Plastics for Machining Purposes

| Plastic Category | Examples | Machinability | Key Characteristic |
|------------------|----------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Thermoplastics (Amorphous)** | PMMA, PS, ABS, PC | Good | Transparent, isotropic |
| **Thermoplastics (Semi-crystalline)** | PE, PP, POM, Nylon | Moderate | Tougher, but gummy |
| **Thermoplastics (Engineering)** | POM, PA, PEEK, PTFE | Good | High strength, expensive |
| **Thermosets** | Phenolic, Epoxy, Melamine | Excellent (when reinforced) | Rigid, brittle |
| **Fiber-Reinforced Composites** | GFRP, CFRP, Phenolic laminates | Difficult | Abrasive to tools |
| **Foamed Plastics** | PU Foam, EPS, XPS | Easy | Very soft, low density |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Machinability Metrics (Industry Benchmarks):**
> - Cutting Speed: **30–300 m/min** (much higher than metals)
> - Feed Rate: **0.05–0.5 mm/rev**
> - Depth of Cut: **0.5–3 mm**
> - Tool Material: **High-Speed Steel (HSS)** or **Polycrystalline Diamond (PCD)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Finishing is Required in Plastics

Plastics require finishing for several engineering and commercial reasons:

1. **Removal of Flash and Mould Defects:** In injection moulding and compression moulding, excess material (flash) escapes at the parting line. This must be trimmed off to meet dimensional and aesthetic standards.
2. **Improved Surface Finish:** The as-moulded surface roughness (typically **Ra 0.4–3.2 µm**) may be insufficient for optical, sealing, or decorative applications. Finishing reduces this to **Ra 0.05–0.4 µm**.
3. **Dimensional Accuracy:** Mould shrinkage (often **0.3–3%**) causes deviations from nominal dimensions. Machining corrects these.
4. **Functional Requirements:** Holes, threads, slots, and mating surfaces often require secondary machining.
5. **Decorative and Branding Needs:** Painting, printing, hot stamping, and metallization enhance marketability.

### 2.2 Major Finishing Operations

#### 2.2.1 Deflashing Operations
- **Manual Deflashing:** Hand trimming with knives/scissors for low-volume parts.
- **Mechanical Deflashing (Tumbling):** Plastic parts are rotated in a barrel with abrasive media (e.g., walnut shells, ceramic chips).
- **Cryogenic Deflashing:** Parts are cooled to below the glass transition temperature (Tg) using liquid nitrogen (**−196°C**), making flash brittle, then tumbled.
- **Abrasive Deflashing:** Use of abrasive belts or brushes for elastomers and flexible plastics.

#### 2.2.2 Surface Finishing Operations
- **Sanding:** Uses abrasive papers (grit size **P60–P2000**). Wet sanding prevents heat buildup.
- **Buffing and Polishing:** Rotating cloth wheels with polishing compounds (e.g., Tripoli, rouge).
- **Flame Polishing:** Brief exposure to flame melts the surface layer (**~10–50 µm**), producing optical clarity in PMMA and PS.
- **Solvent Polishing:** Application of solvent vapors (e.g., methylene chloride for ABS/PS) that smooth surface micro-irregularities.
- **Hot Plate/Wire Polishing:** Contact with heated element (**120–180°C**) for edge finishing of acrylic sheets.

#### 2.2.3 Decorative Finishing
- **Painting:** Spray painting, dip coating, or roller coating. Surface preparation often requires corona or plasma treatment to increase surface energy (must be **>38 dynes/cm** for good ink adhesion).
- **Vacuum Metallization:** Deposition of thin metal film (typically **0.05–0.1 µm** of aluminum) under vacuum (**10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ Torr**).
- **Electroplating:** Requires conductive coating (e.g., electroless copper/nickel layer of **0.5–2 µm**).
- **Hot Stamping:** Transfer of metallic or pigmented foil using heated dies (**80–150°C**).
- **Printing:** Pad printing, screen printing, and digital printing for graphics and labels.

### 2.3 Major Machining Operations for Plastics

| Operation | Typical Application | Common Plastics |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **Turning (Lathe)** | Cylindrical parts, rods | POM, Nylon, PTFE |
| **Milling** | Slots, pockets, complex shapes | PC, ABS, Acrylic |
| **Drilling** | Holes for fasteners | All rigid plastics |
| **Sawing** | Cutting sheets, blocks | PMMA, PC, Phenolic |
| **Threading/Tapping** | Internal/external threads | Nylon, POM |
| **Grinding** | Precision surfaces | Hard thermosets, composites |
| **Routing** | Signage, sheet cutting | Acrylic, foam boards |
| **Laser Cutting** | Intricate 2D shapes | PMMA, ABS, thin films |
| **Waterjet Cutting** | Thick sections, composites | All types |
| **CNC Machining** | Complex 3D parts | Engineering plastics |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Machining of Plastics**

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|-------|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Cutting Speed (V)** | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | D = tool diameter (mm), N = spindle speed (rpm) |
| 2 | **Spindle Speed (N)** | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | |
| 3 | **Feed Rate (f_r)** | $f_r = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | f = feed per revolution (mm/rev) |
| 4 | **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $MRR = f \cdot d \cdot V \cdot \dfrac{1000}{60}$ | mm³/s | d = depth of cut (mm) |
| 5 | **Cutting Force (F_c)** | $F_c = K_c \cdot A_c$ | N | K_c = specific cutting energy (N/mm²), A_c = chip area (mm²) |
| 6 | **Specific Cutting Energy (K_c)** | $K_c = \dfrac{F_c}{A_c}$ | N/mm² | Plastics: **0.1–1.5 N/mm²**; Metals: 1000–4000 N/mm² |
| 7 | **Cutting Power (P_c)** | $P_c = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \times 1000}$ | kW | V in m/min, F_c in N |
| 8 | **Heat Generation Rate (Q)** | $Q = P_c \cdot (1 - R)$ | W | R = proportion of heat carried by chip |
| 9 | **Surface Roughness (R_a)** | $R_a \approx \dfrac{f^2}{8 \cdot R}$ | µm | Theoretical for turning, R = nose radius (mm) |
| 10 | **Tool Life (Taylor's Equation)** | $V \cdot T^n = C$ | m/min × min | T = tool life, n, C = constants |
| 11 | **Shrinkage Compensation** | $D_{cavity} = D_{part} \cdot (1 + S)$ | mm | S = mould shrinkage (decimal) |
| 12 | **Cutting Temperature (T)** | $T = T_0 + \dfrac{\beta \cdot V \cdot f}{k \cdot \rho \cdot c}$ | °C | β = fraction of heat into workpiece, k = thermal conductivity, ρ = density, c = specific heat |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Difference from Metal Machining:** The **specific cutting energy (K_c)** for plastics is roughly **1000–4000 times lower** than for metals. This means cutting forces are minimal, but the heat generated per unit volume is enormous due to the low thermal conductivity. This is the central reason why plastics require **high cutting speeds, low feed rates, and sharp tools with high rake angles**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Machined PEEK and PPS components for structural and thermal applications.
- **Medical:** CNC-machined UHMWPE for joint replacements; machined PMMA for intraocular lenses.
- **Automotive:** Trim components, dashboards, and decorative panels requiring finishing.
- **Electronics:** Drilling and routing of PCBs, machining of enclosures.
- **Signage and Architecture:** Laser-cut and routed acrylic for lighting and decorative panels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Spindle Speed and Cutting Speed

The **cutting speed (V)** is the linear speed at which the cutting edge of the tool moves relative to the workpiece surface. For a rotating tool of diameter D rotating at N revolutions per minute:

**Step 1:** The circumference traversed by the tool per revolution is:

$$C = \pi \cdot D$$

where C is in mm (when D is in mm).

**Step 2:** The distance covered per minute is the circumference multiplied by the number of revolutions:

$$\text{Distance per minute} = N \cdot \pi \cdot D \text{ (mm/min)}$$

**Step 3:** Since V is conventionally expressed in m/min, we divide by 1000:

$$V = \dfrac{N \cdot \pi \cdot D}{1000} \text{ (m/min)}$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging for N:

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} \text{ (rpm)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **frequently tested in KTU examinations** under Module 4 machining problems. Always remember to use **D in mm** and convert V to m/min before applying the formula.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cutting Force for Plastics

For orthogonal cutting, the cutting force F_c is related to the uncut chip cross-sectional area A_c and the specific cutting energy K_c.

**Step 1:** Define the uncut chip area:

$$A_c = f \cdot d$$

where f = feed per revolution (mm/rev) and d = depth of cut (mm).

**Step 2:** The cutting force is proportional to the chip area:

$$F_c = K_c \cdot A_c$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the area expression:

$$F_c = K_c \cdot f \cdot d$$

**Step 4:** For plastics, K_c is empirically determined. Example values:
- PMMA: **K_c ≈ 0.3 N/mm²**
- POM: **K_c ≈ 0.5 N/mm²**
- HDPE: **K_c ≈ 0.2 N/mm²**
- PTFE: **K_c ≈ 0.15 N/mm²**

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Unlike metals where K_c is a material property, for plastics it is strongly dependent on cutting temperature, tool geometry, and strain rate. Always cite the cutting condition when comparing values.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Power Required for Turning a Nylon Rod

**Problem:** A nylon rod of diameter 60 mm is being turned on a lathe. The cutting speed is 200 m/min, feed is 0.2 mm/rev, and depth of cut is 2 mm. If the specific cutting energy of nylon under these conditions is 0.4 N/mm², calculate:
- (a) Spindle speed
- (b) Material removal rate
- (c) Cutting force
- (d) Cutting power

**Solution:**

**Step (a) — Spindle Speed:**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 200}{\pi \cdot 60}$$

$$N = \dfrac{200000}{188.496} = 1061.03 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step (b) — Material Removal Rate:**

$$MRR = f \cdot d \cdot V \cdot \dfrac{1000}{60}$$

$$MRR = 0.2 \cdot 2 \cdot 200 \cdot \dfrac{1000}{60} = \dfrac{80000}{60} = 1333.33 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step (c) — Cutting Force:**

$$F_c = K_c \cdot f \cdot d = 0.4 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 2 = 0.16 \text{ N}$$

**Step (d) — Cutting Power:**

$$P_c = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \times 1000} = \dfrac{0.16 \cdot 200}{60000} = 5.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kW} = 0.533 \text{ W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** The cutting power required is **less than 1 Watt**! This is because plastics are extremely soft. However, the **heat concentration is critical** because plastics have poor thermal conductivity. The same mass of plastic can reach 200°C during cutting, even at such low power levels.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Cutting Tools for Plastics

| Plastic Type | Tool Material | Rake Angle (γ) | Clearance Angle (α) | Cutting Speed (m/min) | Coolant |
|--------------|--------------|----------------|---------------------|----------------------|---------|
| PMMA | HSS or Carbide | 5°–15° | 10°–15° | 100–300 | Air blast or water-soluble |
| PE / PP | HSS, sharp edge | 20°–30° | 10°–15° | 200–500 | Air blast preferred |
| POM (Acetal) | HSS or PCD | 10°–20° | 8°–12° | 150–400 | Dry or air blast |
| Nylon (PA) | HSS, polished | 10°–20° | 10°–15° | 200–500 | Coolant to reduce gumming |
| PTFE | Sharp HSS | 15°–25° | 10°–12° | 100–300 | Dry (low friction) |
| PC (Polycarbonate) | Carbide | 5°–10° | 10°–12° | 100–250 | Air blast |
| Phenolic (Bakelite) | Carbide | 0°–5° (negative) | 8°–10° | 50–150 | Coolant required (dust) |
| GFRP / CFRP | Diamond-coated carbide | 5°–10° | 8°–12° | 30–100 | Coolant + dust extraction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow: Finishing Operations for Injection-Moulded Parts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Moulded Plastic Part] --> B[Deflashing Operation]
    B --> C{Quality Check}
    C -->|Pass| D[Surface Preparation]
    C -->|Fail| E[Rejection or Rework]
    D --> F[Sanding or Buffing]
    F --> G[Cleaning]
    G --> H[Decorative Process Selection]
    H --> I[Painting]
    H --> J[Vacuum Metallization]
    H --> K[Hot Stamping]
    H --> L[Printing]
    I --> M[Final Inspection]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Packaging and Dispatch]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Machining Process Architecture for Plastics

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Input_Stage[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        A1[Plastic Material Type]
        A2[Workpiece Geometry]
        A3[Tolerance Requirement]
    end
    
    subgraph Process_Stage[MACHINING PROCESS]
        B1[Tool Selection]
        B2[Cutting Parameter Setting]
        B3[Coolant/Lubricant Selection]
        B4[Machine Operation]
    end
    
    subgraph Output_Stage[OUTPUT QUALITY]
        C1[Dimensional Accuracy]
        C2[Surface Finish Ra]
        C3[Tool Wear Status]
        C4[Heat Affected Zone]
    end
    
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    B1 --> B4
    B2 --> B4
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    B4 --> C3
    B4 --> C4
    C3 --> B1
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow: Selection of Finishing Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Plastic Part Ready for Finishing]) --> Q1{Excess Flash Present?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R1[Deflashing: Manual/Tumble/Cryogenic]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Surface Roughness < Required?}
    R1 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Decorative Coating Required?}
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Sanding/Buffing/Polishing]
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Painting/Metallization/Hot Stamping]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Optical Clarity Required?}
    R2 --> Q4
    Q4 -->|Yes| R4[Flame/Solvent Polishing]
    Q4 -->|No| End([Finished Part])
    R3 --> End
    R4 --> End
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Machining Setup for Plastics

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Workpiece_Fixture[WORKPIECE SIDE]
        W1[Plastic Rod/Block] --> W2[Fixture or Chuck]
    end
    
    subgraph Tool_Side[TOOL SIDE]
        T1[Cutting Tool HSS/Carbide/PCD] --> T2[Sharp Cutting Edge]
        T2 --> T3[High Rake Angle Geometry]
    end
    
    subgraph Process_Zone[CUTTING ZONE]
        P1[Heat Generation] --> P2[Heat Dissipation]
        P2 --> P3{Coolant or Air Blast}
    end
    
    W2 --> P1
    T3 --> P1
    P3 --> P2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**"Differentiate between the machinability of thermoplastics and thermosets with suitable examples."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Thermoplastics | Thermosets |
|--------|----------------|------------|
| **Structure** | Linear/branched chains, weak secondary bonds | Cross-linked 3D network, strong covalent bonds |
| **Behavior on heating** | Soften and melt reversibly | Do not melt; decompose at high T |
| **Machinability** | Softer, prone to gumming and melting due to low Tg | Hard and brittle, but produce dust |
| **Examples** | PE, PP, PMMA, POM, Nylon | Phenolic, Melamine, Epoxy, Silicone |
| **Tool Wear** | Low to moderate | High (due to abrasive fillers) |
| **Coolant Need** | Air blast usually sufficient | Coolant mandatory (dust hazard) |

**Key Point for Valuation:** *(1 mark each for structure, behavior, and example)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Question]**
**"List any three decorative finishing operations used for plastics. Explain any one in brief."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The three decorative finishing operations are:
1. **Painting** (1 mark)
2. **Vacuum Metallization** (1 mark)
3. **Hot Stamping** (1 mark)

**Explanation of Vacuum Metallization:**
In vacuum metallization, the plastic part is first coated with a base lacquer for surface smoothness. It is then placed in a vacuum chamber (**10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ Torr**), where aluminum is evaporated from a resistance-heated tungsten filament. The vapor condenses on the part surface, forming a thin metallic film (**0.05–0.1 µm**). A top lacquer is then applied for protection. This produces a mirror-like metallic appearance used in automotive trim, lamp reflectors, and decorative packaging.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the various **finishing operations** carried out on plastic products with their purpose and industrial applications. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **POM (Acetal) rod of diameter 80 mm** is being turned on a lathe. The cutting speed is **250 m/min**, feed is **0.15 mm/rev**, and depth of cut is **1.5 mm**. The specific cutting energy of POM is **0.45 N/mm²**. Calculate the **(i) spindle speed**, **(ii) material removal rate**, **(iii) cutting force**, and **(iv) cutting power**. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Finishing Operations on Plastics

The major finishing operations performed on plastic products are:

**1. Deflashing:** *(1.5 marks)*
- Purpose: To remove excess material (flash) from the parting line, gate locations, and ejector pin marks of moulded parts.
- Methods: Manual trimming, tumble deflashing (rotating parts with media in a barrel), cryogenic deflashing (cooling below Tg using liquid nitrogen at **−196°C**), and abrasive deflashing.
- Application: Rubber gaskets, O-rings, injection-moulded enclosures.

**2. Sanding and Grinding:** *(1.5 marks)*
- Purpose: To improve surface finish and remove mould marks, sink marks, and surface defects.
- Methods: Wet sanding using abrasive papers (grit **P60 to P2000**), belt grinding, and disc grinding.
- Application: Automotive interior parts, acrylic displays, prototype models.

**3. Polishing and Buffing:** *(1 mark)*
- Purpose: To achieve high-gloss, optical-quality surfaces.
- Methods: Mechanical buffing with cloth wheels and polishing compounds (Tripoli, rouge), flame polishing using brief flame exposure, and solvent polishing using vapors of methylene chloride.
- Application: Transparent acrylic displays, lenses, decorative articles.

**4. Painting and Coating:** *(1 mark)*
- Purpose: To add color, gloss, and protection.
- Methods: Spray painting, dip coating, and powder coating.
- Pre-treatment: Corona or plasma treatment to raise surface energy above **38 dynes/cm**.
- Application: Automotive panels, consumer electronics, helmets.

**5. Metallization:** *(1 mark)*
- Purpose: To create a metallic appearance or improve barrier properties.
- Methods: Vacuum metallization (aluminum deposition) and electroplating (with prior electroless coating).
- Application: Lamp reflectors, decorative trims, EMI shielding enclosures.

**6. Printing and Hot Stamping:** *(1 mark)*
- Purpose: To apply graphics, branding, and decorative patterns.
- Methods: Pad printing, screen printing, digital printing, and hot stamping (transfer foil using heated die at **80–150°C**).
- Application: Bottles, caps, nameplates, and promotional items.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given Data:**
- Workpiece diameter, D = 80 mm
- Cutting speed, V = 250 m/min
- Feed, f = 0.15 mm/rev
- Depth of cut, d = 1.5 mm
- Specific cutting energy, K_c = 0.45 N/mm²

**(i) Spindle Speed (N):**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 250}{\pi \cdot 80}$$

$$N = \dfrac{250000}{251.327} = 994.72 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Material Removal Rate (MRR):**

$$MRR = f \cdot d \cdot V \cdot \dfrac{1000}{60}$$

$$MRR = 0.15 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 250 \cdot \dfrac{1000}{60} = \dfrac{56250}{60} = 937.5 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**(iii) Cutting Force (F_c):**

$$F_c = K_c \cdot f \cdot d = 0.45 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 1.5 = 0.10125 \text{ N} \approx 0.101 \text{ N}$$

**(iv) Cutting Power (P_c):**

$$P_c = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \times 1000} = \dfrac{0.10125 \cdot 250}{60000}$$

$$P_c = 4.22 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kW} = 0.422 \text{ W}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students frequently lose marks for:
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — Forgetting to convert V to m/min or diameter to mm.
> 2. **Sign error in π·D calculation** — Always use D in mm for the formula N = 1000V/πD to give rpm directly.
> 3. **Skipping intermediate steps** — Show the substitution clearly.
> 4. **Not including units in the final answer.**
> 5. **Confusing feed per revolution with feed per minute** — Use f in mm/rev for F_c and f_r (mm/min) for feed rate.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern, Module 4 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the various **machining operations** used for plastic products. Explain the **key differences in machinability between plastics and metals**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **tool materials, tool geometry, and cutting parameters** recommended for machining of plastics. *(7 marks)*

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Machining Operations and Key Differences

**Major Machining Operations for Plastics:** *(3 marks)*

1. **Turning:** Performed on lathes to produce cylindrical parts, rods, and bushings. Common for POM, Nylon, and PTFE.
2. **Milling:** Used for slots, pockets, and complex 3D contours. Done on vertical/horizontal milling machines and CNC machining centers.
3. **Drilling:** For producing holes using twist drills. Critical to use proper point angle (**60°–90°** for plastics vs. 118° for metals) to avoid cracking and heat buildup.
4. **Sawing:** For cutting sheets, blocks, and rods using band saws or circular saws.
5. **Routing:** High-speed cutting of sheet materials like acrylic, foam boards, and composites using CNC routers.
6. **Grinding:** Precision finishing using abrasive wheels, mainly for thermosets and fiber-reinforced plastics.
7. **Non-Conventional Methods:** Laser cutting, waterjet cutting, and EDM (for conductive composites).

**Key Differences in Machinability: Plastics vs. Metals:** *(4 marks)*

| Parameter | Plastics | Metals |
|-----------|----------|--------|
| **Cutting Force** | Very low (0.1–1.5 N/mm² specific energy) | High (1000–4000 N/mm²) |
| **Heat Dissipation** | Poor; heat concentrates at cut zone | Good; heat carried by chip |
| **Tool Wear** | Low (for unreinforced); high for composites | Significant |
| **Chip Formation** | Continuous (gummy) or powdery | Continuous, segmented, or discontinuous |
| **Surface Finish** | Can be excellent (Ra < 0.1 µm possible) | Limited by tool marks |
| **Spring-back** | High (elastoplastic recovery) | Negligible |
| **Coolant** | Often dry; air blast sufficient | Flood coolant usually required |
| **Cutting Speed** | 100–500 m/min | 30–200 m/min (for steel) |
| **Temperature Sensitivity** | Critical (T > Tg causes melting) | Less critical |
| **Anisotropy** | Reinforced plastics are highly anisotropic | Mostly isotropic |

---

#### Part (b) — Tool Materials, Geometry, and Cutting Parameters

**Tool Materials:** *(2.5 marks)*

1. **High-Speed Steel (HSS):** General purpose, low cost, suitable for soft plastics. Easily resharpened. Limited tool life at high speeds.
2. **Carbide (WC-Co):** Higher wear resistance, suitable for abrasive plastics and composites. Used at higher cutting speeds.
3. **Polycrystalline Diamond (PCD):** Best for highly abrasive composites (CFRP, GFRP) and filled plastics. Longest tool life but expensive.
4. **Diamond-Coated Tools:** Cost-effective alternative to PCD for abrasive applications.

**Tool Geometry:** *(2.5 marks)*

- **Rake Angle (γ):** Should be **high (10°–30°)** to reduce cutting forces and shearing action. Positive rake is preferred for soft thermoplastics; zero or negative rake for hard thermosets and composites.
- **Clearance Angle (α):** Standard **8°–15°** to prevent rubbing of flank face.
- **Cutting Edge:** Must be **sharp and polished** (mirror finish) to prevent crack initiation and reduce friction.
- **Point Angle (for drills):** Use **60°–90°** instead of 118° to reduce thrust force and prevent cracking.
- **Helix Angle (for drills):** **15°–30°** for efficient chip evacuation.
- **Nose Radius:** Small (0.4–0.8 mm) to reduce surface roughness and chatter.

**Recommended Cutting Parameters:** *(2 marks)*

| Parameter | Soft Thermoplastics | Engineering Plastics | Reinforced Plastics |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Cutting Speed | 200–500 m/min | 100–300 m/min | 30–150 m/min |
| Feed | 0.1–0.5 mm/rev | 0.05–0.3 mm/rev | 0.05–0.2 mm/rev |
| Depth of Cut | 1–3 mm | 0.5–2 mm | 0.5–1.5 mm |
| Coolant | Air blast | Air blast / water-soluble | Coolant + dust extraction |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Common mistakes students make in this question:
> 1. **Confusing rake angle values** for plastics vs. metals. Plastics need **higher** positive rake angles, not lower.
> 2. **Using standard drill point angle (118°)** for plastics, which causes **cracking and high thrust force**.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention surface treatment of tools** (e.g., TiN, diamond coating).
> 4. **Omitting chip evacuation strategy** — Plastics produce gummy chips that clog flutes.
> 5. **Not justifying the parameter selection** with reference to thermal properties of plastics.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Plastics differ from metals** in machining primarily due to **low thermal conductivity**, **low elastic modulus**, and **low glass transition temperature (Tg)**.
- **Specific cutting energy (K_c) for plastics is 1000–4000× lower** than for metals, resulting in very low cutting forces.
- **Heat dissipation is the central challenge** in plastic machining — use **sharp tools, high rake angles, and air blast cooling**.
- **Cutting speeds are 2–10× higher** than for metals, but **feeds and depths of cut are lower**.
- **Tool materials:** HSS for general use, Carbide for abrasive plastics, PCD for composites.
- **Drill point angle for plastics: 60°–90°** (NOT 118°).
- **Standard formula for spindle speed:** $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ (V in m/min, D in mm).
- **MRR formula:** $MRR = f \cdot d \cdot V \cdot \dfrac{1000}{60}$ mm³/s.
- **Cutting power** is typically in **fractions of a Watt** for plastics — but **heat concentration is critical**.
- **Finishing operations** include deflashing, sanding, polishing, painting, metallization, hot stamping, and printing.
- **Cryogenic deflashing** uses liquid nitrogen at **−196°C** to embrittle flash before tumbling.
- **Vacuum metallization** occurs at **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ Torr** with aluminum film thickness of **0.05–0.1 µm**.
- **Surface treatment (corona/plasma)** is essential before painting to raise surface energy above **38 dynes/cm**.
- **Reinforced plastics** (CFRP, GFRP) are highly **abrasive** — diamond tools are recommended.
- **Glass transition temperature (Tg)** is the critical threshold below which plastics should be cooled for cryogenic deflashing.
- **Recycling consideration:** Plastic chips and dust from machining should be collected and recycled, especially for thermoplastics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Joining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Joining** in polymer processing refers to the family of secondary manufacturing processes used to permanently or semi-permanently unite two or more plastic components — either to similar polymers (homo-joining) or dissimilar polymers/materials (hetero-joining) — without altering the bulk geometry of the parent parts. It encompasses **welding (thermal fusion), adhesive bonding, solvent cementing, mechanical fastening, and hybrid techniques**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *"Joining is the process of creating a molecular, mechanical, or chemical bond between two polymer surfaces using thermal energy, chemical solvents, adhesives, or mechanical fasteners to achieve structural integrity equivalent to, or approaching, the parent material."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you have two pieces of butter. You cannot glue them back together permanently with most ordinary glues — but if you warm them slightly until they soften, push them together, and let them cool, they fuse into a single lump. **This is exactly how polymer welding works at the molecular level.** 

Now imagine trying to glue a Lego brick to a wooden table. You would use a special adhesive (cyanoacrylate / super glue) that chemically grabs both surfaces. **This mirrors adhesive bonding of polymers.**

Finally, imagine screwing two pieces of wood together with a bolt. **This is mechanical fastening** — no molecular union, just a physical interlock.

All three analogies operate at the heart of polymer joining technology.

## 1.3 Why Joining is Critical in Polymer Manufacturing

| Industrial Driver | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Design flexibility** | Enables fabrication of complex hollow parts (fuel tanks, ducts) from simple molded halves |
| **Material economy** | Avoids moulding large, thick parts (reduces warpage, sink marks) |
| **Multi-material assemblies** | Allows polymer-metal, polymer-ceramic, polymer-composite combinations |
| **Repair & refurbishment** | Damaged plastic components (bumpers, pipes) can be salvaged instead of replaced |
| **Cost reduction** | Joining semi-finished mouldings is cheaper than investment in complex tooling |

## 1.4 Classification of Polymer Joining Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical classification (as per KTU Module 4 syllabus):**

1. **Thermal / Fusion Welding**
   - Hot-plate (contact) welding
   - Hot-gas (air) welding
   - Extrusion welding
   - Ultrasonic welding
   - Vibration welding
   - Spin (friction) welding
   - Laser welding
   - Induction welding
   - Radio-frequency (RF) / dielectric welding

2. **Solvent Cementing (Solvent Bonding)**
   - Solvent welding of PVC, ABS, polystyrene

3. **Adhesive Bonding**
   - Epoxy, polyurethane, cyanoacrylate, acrylic adhesives

4. **Mechanical Fastening**
   - Screws, rivets, snap-fits, threaded inserts

5. **Hybrid / Emerging Methods**
   - Friction stir welding, microwave welding, infrared welding

## 1.5 Fundamental Bonding Principles

Every joining method ultimately relies on one or more of these fundamental adhesion mechanisms:

- **Adsorption / Wetting Theory** — Adhesive flows into surface micro-cavities.
- **Mechanical Interlocking** — Adhesive flows into pores and solidifies.
- **Chemical Bonding** — Covalent/ionic bonds form across the interface.
- **Diffusion Theory** — Polymer chains interdiffuse across the interface (basis of thermal welding of thermoplastics).
- **Electrostatic Attraction** — Charge transfer between dissimilar surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **thermal welding of thermoplastics**, the **diffusion theory ( reptation model by de Gennes)** is the dominant mechanism — polymer chain ends "snake" across the molten interface and entangle on cooling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Welding Window Concept

For any thermal welding operation on a thermoplastic, the material must be heated to a temperature between its **secondary transition temperature (T<sub>g</sub> for amorphous polymers)** or **melting temperature (T<sub>m</sub> for semi-crystalline polymers)** and its **degradation temperature (T<sub>d</sub>)**.

$$\text{Welding Window} = T_d - T_m \quad \text{(or } T_d - T_g\text{ for amorphous)}$$

A **wide welding window** (e.g., PP, PE, PVC) makes a polymer easy to weld.
A **narrow welding window** (e.g., PET, PA6) makes welding technically challenging.

## 2.2 Reptation Model (de Gennes — Foundation of Polymer Welding Theory)

The depth to which polymer chains interdiffuse during welding follows the **reptation time (t<sub>r</sub>)**:

$$D(t) = D_\infty \left( \frac{t}{t_r} \right)^{1/4}$$

where:
- $D(t)$ = depth of chain interdiffusion at time $t$
- $D_\infty$ = equilibrium diffusion depth
- $t_r$ = reptation time (time for a chain to fully escape its initial tube)

For full weld strength:

$$t_{\text{weld}} \geq t_r \implies \text{Strength} \to \text{Parent Material Strength}$$

## 2.3 Heat Transfer Analysis (Hot-Plate Welding)

The interface temperature evolution for two semi-infinite polymer slabs brought into contact with a heated platen:

$$T(x,t) = T_s + (T_0 - T_s)\, \text{erf}\!\left( \frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} \right)$$

where:
- $T(x,t)$ = temperature at depth $x$ and time $t$
- $T_s$ = surface (platen) temperature
- $T_0$ = initial polymer bulk temperature
- $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity of polymer
- $\text{erf}$ = error function

The **heat-affected zone (HAZ) depth** after heating time $t_h$:

$$x_{HAZ} \approx 2\sqrt{\alpha\, t_h}$$

## 2.4 Ultrasonic Welding — Energy & Power Relations

The mechanical energy delivered per unit joint area:

$$E = \frac{P \cdot t}{A} = \frac{F \cdot d}{A}$$

where:
- $P$ = ultrasonic power (W)
- $t$ = weld time (s)
- $A$ = joint area (m²)
- $F$ = applied axial force (N)
- $d$ = displacement amplitude (m)

Typical operating parameters:
- **Frequency:** 20–40 kHz (most common: 20 kHz)
- **Amplitude:** 10–50 µm
- **Pressure:** 0.2–1.5 MPa
- **Weld time:** 0.5–3.0 s

## 2.5 Laser Welding — Beer-Lambert Absorption

For transmission laser welding (the most common variant — *through-transmission laser welding, TTIW*):

$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\beta x}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident laser intensity
- $\beta$ = absorption coefficient of the absorbing polymer (typically filled with carbon black or laser-absorbing dye)
- $x$ = depth into material

The laser is transmitted through the upper (transparent) layer and absorbed at the interface, generating the fusion heat.

## 2.6 Spin Welding — Frictional Heat Generation

$$Q = \mu \cdot F \cdot v \cdot t = \mu \cdot F \cdot \omega \cdot r \cdot t$$

where:
- $Q$ = heat generated (J)
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction at interface
- $F$ = axial (clamping) force (N)
- $v = \omega \cdot r$ = relative surface velocity (m/s)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
- $r$ = mean radius of the interface (m)
- $t$ = welding time (s)

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula / Symbol | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Welding Window | $\Delta T = T_d - T_m$ | K or °C | Material selection for welding |
| 2 | Reptation Diffusion | $D(t) = D_\infty (t/t_r)^{1/4}$ | m | Time to reach full weld strength |
| 3 | HAZ Depth (hot plate) | $x_{HAZ} \approx 2\sqrt{\alpha t_h}$ | m | Optimising heat soak time |
| 4 | Ultrasonic Energy Density | $E = Pt/A$ | J/m² | Energy input per joint |
| 5 | Laser Attenuation | $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\beta x}$ | W/m² | Laser welding depth |
| 6 | Spin Weld Heat | $Q = \mu F \omega r t$ | J | Frictional heat generated |
| 7 | Vibration Weld Heat | $Q = \mu F \cdot 2\pi f a \cdot t$ | J | Linear frictional welding |
| 8 | Adhesive Shear Strength | $\tau = F/A$ | N/m² or MPa | Bond strength testing |
| 9 | Bolted Joint Clamp Load | $F_{clamp} = k \cdot T_{bolt}$ | N | Pre-load in fasteners |
| 10 | Pressure in Hot-Gas Welding | $P_{\text{gas}} = 0.03$–$0.1$ MPa | MPa | Hot-gas welding gun |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive bumpers & dashboards** → Ultrasonic & vibration welding (fast cycle times, hermetic seals).
- **Medical disposables** (IV bags, blood filters) → RF/dielectric & solvent bonding (no contamination).
- **Piping systems** (PVC, PP, PE) → Hot-plate & socket fusion (pressure-rated leak-proof joints).
- **Electronic enclosures** → Ultrasonic welding + threaded inserts (mechanical + welded hybrid).
- **Aircraft interiors** → Adhesive bonding (lightweight, fatigue-resistant).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ) Depth in Hot-Plate Welding

**Starting point:** 1-D transient heat conduction in a semi-infinite solid.

For a semi-infinite polymer slab initially at $T_0$ with surface suddenly raised to $T_s$ at $t = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

**Boundary conditions:**
- $T(x, 0) = T_0$ (initial uniform temperature)
- $T(0, t) = T_s$ (platen contact at surface)

**Initial condition:** $T(x, 0) = T_0$ for $x \geq 0$.

**Solution** (Carslaw & Jaeger, *Conduction of Heat in Solids*):

$$T(x,t) - T_0 = (T_s - T_0)\, \text{erfc}\!\left( \frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} \right)$$

**Step 1:** Define the melting isotherm $T_m$ at depth $x_{HAZ}$. Set $T(x_{HAZ}, t_h) = T_m$:

$$T_m - T_0 = (T_s - T_0)\, \text{erfc}\!\left( \frac{x_{HAZ}}{2\sqrt{\alpha t_h}} \right)$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the complementary error function argument:

$$\text{erfc}\!\left( \frac{x_{HAZ}}{2\sqrt{\alpha t_h}} \right) = \frac{T_m - T_0}{T_s - T_0}$$

**Step 3:** Invert using the inverse erfc function:

$$\frac{x_{HAZ}}{2\sqrt{\alpha t_h}} = \text{erfc}^{-1}\!\left( \frac{T_m - T_0}{T_s - T_0} \right)$$

**Step 4:** Final expression for HAZ depth:

$$x_{HAZ} = 2\sqrt{\alpha\, t_h}\; \text{erfc}^{-1}\!\left( \frac{T_m - T_0}{T_s - T_0} \right)$$

**Practical approximation** (when $T_s \gg T_m$ and $T_0$ is ambient, the erfc inverse ≈ 1):

$$x_{HAZ} \approx 2\sqrt{\alpha\, t_h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For HDPE ($\alpha \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s) heated for 15 s: $x_{HAZ} \approx 2\sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-7} \times 15} \approx 3.3$ mm — this sets the necessary molten layer thickness.

## 3.2 Derivation — Reptation Time Scaling with Molecular Weight

de Gennes' reptation theory predicts that the time for a polymer chain to disengage from its initial "tube" scales as:

$$t_r \propto M^3$$

where $M$ is the molecular weight.

**Step 1:** For a flexible polymer chain, the longest relaxation time (Rouse time) is:

$$\tau_R \propto \frac{\zeta\, b^2}{k_B T}\, \frac{M}{M_0}$$

**Step 2:** Reptation time scales as the time to diffuse one tube length:

$$t_r \propto \tau_R \left(\frac{L}{\sqrt{N}\, b}\right)^2 \propto M^3$$

**Step 3:** Engineering implication — doubling the molecular weight increases the required weld time by **8×**:

$$\frac{t_{r,2}}{t_{r,1}} = \left(\frac{M_2}{M_1}\right)^3 = 2^3 = 8$$

This is why **high-MFI (low-MW) grades weld more easily but give lower strength**, while **low-MFI (high-MW) grades need longer weld times but produce stronger joints**.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Joining Methods

| Parameter | Hot-Plate | Ultrasonic | Vibration | Spin | Laser | Adhesive |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cycle time | 10–60 s | 0.5–3 s | 1–5 s | 1–5 s | 1–10 s | Minutes–hours |
| Joint strength | Excellent (90–100%) | 70–90% | 80–95% | 80–90% | 85–95% | 40–80% |
| Material thickness | Thick (1–50 mm) | Thin (< 6 mm) | Medium-large | Small parts | Thin–medium | All |
| Surface prep | Minimal | Energy director required | Minimal | Minimal | Tight fit | Critical (cleaning) |
| Dissimilar polymers | Limited | Limited | Good | Limited | Good | Excellent |
| Capital cost | Low | Medium | Medium | Low | High | Very low |
| Typical polymers | PP, PE, PVC, PA | ABS, PP, PS, PA | PP, PA, PBT | Circular: PP, PA | PP, PA, PC, ABS | All + composites |

## 3.4 Solvent Welding — Solubility Parameter (Hildebrand) Criterion

For successful solvent welding, the solvent's Hildebrand solubility parameter must match that of the polymer:

$$\Delta \delta = \vert \delta_{polymer} - \delta_{solvent} \vert \leq 2 \; (\text{MPa})^{1/2}$$

| Polymer | δ (MPa)<sup>1/2</sup> | Typical Solvent |
|---|---|---|
| PVC | 19.2–19.8 | THF, MEK, cyclohexanone |
| ABS | 18.4–20.0 | MEK, methylene chloride |
| Polystyrene | 17.4–18.6 | Toluene, xylene |
| PMMA | 18.6–19.4 | Acetone, MEK |
| Polycarbonate | 19.4–20.0 | Methylene chloride (limited) |
| HDPE | 15.8–17.1 | No good solvent — cannot be solvent welded |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Polyolefins (PE, PP) have **no practical solvent** at room temperature and therefore **cannot be solvent welded** — they must be thermally welded.

## 3.5 Energy Director Design (Ultrasonic Welding)

An **energy director** is a triangular protrusion (typically 60° apex, 0.3–0.5 mm height) moulded onto one of the joint surfaces. It concentrates ultrasonic energy, melts first, and initiates fusion.

$$h_{ED} \geq 0.3 \;\text{mm}, \quad \theta_{apex} \approx 60°, \quad \text{Sharp tip required}$$

**Failure mode if missing:** Bulk melting of entire joint surface → flash, weak weld, part distortion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Polymer Joining Process Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Joining Methods] --> B[Thermal Fusion Welding]
    A --> C[Solvent Cementing]
    A --> D[Adhesive Bonding]
    A --> E[Mechanical Fastening]

    B --> B1[Hot-Plate Welding]
    B --> B2[Ultrasonic Welding]
    B --> B3[Vibration Welding]
    B --> B4[Spin Welding]
    B --> B5[Laser Welding]
    B --> B6[Hot-Gas Welding]
    B --> B7[RF Dielectric Welding]

    B1 --> X1[Heating Phase]
    B1 --> X2[Change-Over Phase]
    B1 --> X3[Joining Phase]
    B1 --> X4[Holding Cooling Phase]

    B2 --> Y1[Converter 20kHz]
    B2 --> Y2[Booster]
    B2 --> Y3[Horn Sonotrode]
    B2 --> Y4[Energy Director Melts]

    B5 --> Z1[Transparent Top Layer]
    B5 --> Z2[Absorbing Bottom Layer]
    B5 --> Z3[Interface Fusion]

    C --> C1[Solvent Application]
    C --> C1a[Dissolution of Surfaces]
    C --> C2[Chain Interdiffusion]
    C --> C3[Evaporation Solvent Recovery]

    D --> D1[Surface Preparation]
    D --> D2[Adhesive Application]
    D --> D3[Curing]

    E --> E1[Screws and Bolts]
    E --> E2[Snap Fits]
    E --> E3[Threaded Inserts]
    E --> E4[Rivets and Standoffs]
```

## 4.2 Hot-Plate Welding Process — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Match Surfaces Against Heated Platen] --> P2[Step 2: Heat Soak till Surface Melts]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Retract Platen - Change Over]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Press Molten Faces Together]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Hold under Pressure during Cooling]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Solidified Weld Joint]
```

## 4.3 Ultrasonic Welding System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PSU[20 kHz Power Supply] --> CONV[Piezoceramic Converter]
    CONV --> BOO[Booster Amplifier]
    BOO --> HORN[Horn Sonotrode]
    HORN --> PART1[Top Plastic Part With Energy Director]
    PART1 --> PART2[Bottom Plastic Part on Anvil]
    PART2 --> ANVIL[Stationary Anvil with Clamp Pressure]
    ANVIL --> WELD[Melted Energy Director creates Weld]
```

## 4.4 Through-Transmission Laser Welding (TTLW) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Diode Laser 808-940 nm] --> BEAM[Collimated Beam]
    BEAM --> LENS1[Focussing Lens]
    LENS1 --> TOP[Transparent Polymer Layer]
    TOP --> INTER[Interface With Absorbing Carbon Black Filler]
    INTER --> BOTTOM[Absorbing Polymer Layer]
    BOTTOM --> POOL[Localized Melt Pool at Interface]
    POOL --> SEAM[Continuous Weld Seam as Laser Scans]
```

## 4.5 Decision Logic — Selecting a Joining Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the Polymer?} --> Q1a[Amorphous e.g. ABS PC PS] --> Q1b{Part Size?}
    Q1 --> Q1c[Semi-Crystalline e.g. PP PE PA] --> Q1d{Part Geometry?}

    Q1b --> Q1b1[Small Thin] --> M1[Ultrasonic]
    Q1b --> Q1b2[Medium Large] --> M2[Vibration or Hot Plate]

    Q1d --> Q1d1[Circular Symmetric] --> M3[Spin Welding]
    Q1d --> Q1d2[Large Flat] --> M4[Hot Plate or Extrusion]
    Q1d --> Q1d3[Complex 3D] --> M5[Laser Welding]

    Q1a --> M6[Solvent or Adhesive if PE PP then only Thermal]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for board exam:** **"HUVS-LAS"** — **H**ot-plate, **U**ltrasonic, **V**ibration, **S**pin, **L**aser, **A**dhesive, **S**olvent — the seven most-tested joining methods.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "welding window" of a thermoplastic and explain its significance in selecting a polymer for thermal welding. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **welding window** of a thermoplastic is the temperature range between the polymer's melting temperature (T<sub>m</sub>, for semi-crystalline) or glass transition temperature (T<sub>g</sub>, for amorphous) at the lower end and its degradation temperature (T<sub>d</sub>) at the upper end:

$$\Delta T_{weld} = T_d - T_m \;\;\; \text{(or } T_d - T_g\text{)}$$

A **wide welding window** (e.g., HDPE: T<sub>m</sub> ≈ 130 °C, T<sub>d</sub> > 300 °C, giving ΔT ≈ 170 °C) means the polymer can be heated well above its melting point without decomposing, making it easy to weld. A **narrow welding window** (e.g., PVC: T<sub>m</sub> ≈ 100 °C, T<sub>d</sub> ≈ 200 °C, ΔT ≈ 100 °C) requires very precise temperature control. Polymers with overlapping processing and degradation ranges (e.g., PET, PVDC) are difficult or impossible to thermally weld.

**(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — equation; 1 mark — example and engineering significance)**

---

### Question 2: What is an "energy director" in ultrasonic welding of plastics? Why is it essential? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **energy director** is a small triangular protrusion (typically with a 60° apex angle and 0.3–0.5 mm height) moulded onto one of the joint surfaces in an ultrasonic welding assembly.

**Role and necessity:**
1. It **concentrates the ultrasonic energy** into a small area, raising local temperature above T<sub>m</sub> rapidly.
2. The sharp tip melts first, generating a thin molten layer that flows across the interface.
3. Without it, ultrasonic energy would be distributed across the entire joint, causing bulk melting, flash, weak joints, and part distortion.
4. It reduces cycle time and ensures reproducible weld quality.

**(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — geometry; 1 mark — role in fusion)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> • Students often write *only* definitions in 14-mark answers — you MUST include **process description, parameters, equations, advantages/limitations, and applications** for full marks.
> • Sketches (even hand-drawn block diagrams) carry **2–3 marks**. Always include at least one schematic.
> • For comparison-type questions, a **markdown table** saves time and is examiner-friendly.

---

### Question A: `**[14 Marks]**` — Hot-Plate Welding `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the hot-plate (heated tool) welding process for thermoplastics with a neat block diagram. Discuss the four stages of the welding cycle and the role of the heated platen temperature and heating time on joint strength.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A HDPE pipe of wall thickness 8 mm is welded using a hot-plate welder. The platen temperature is 200 °C, the ambient temperature is 25 °C, and the heating time is 12 s. The thermal diffusivity of HDPE is 1.8 × 10⁻⁷ m²/s and T<sub>m</sub> = 130 °C. Estimate the heat-affected zone (HAZ) depth using the error-function solution for 1-D transient heat conduction.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Process Description (2 marks):**
Hot-plate (heated tool) welding is a thermal fusion process where two polymer surfaces are simultaneously brought into contact with a heated metal platen, melted, then pressed together under pressure to form a permanent weld.

**2. Four Stages (3 marks):**
| Stage | Description | Typical Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| (i) **Match / Heat Soak** | Surfaces pressed against platen (P ≈ 0.1–0.3 MPa) | T<sub>platen</sub> = 180–230 °C; t = 5–30 s |
| (ii) **Change-Over** | Parts retracted, platen removed | t = 1–3 s (minimize cooling) |
| (iii) **Joining / Forging** | Molten faces pressed together | P = 0.3–0.7 MPa; t = 2–10 s |
| (iv) **Cooling / Holding** | Joint held under pressure until solidified | t = 5–30 s |

**3. Effect of T<sub>platen</sub> and t on strength (2 marks):**
- Higher T<sub>platen</sub> → thicker molten layer → higher weld strength (until degradation).
- Longer t → more melting → better chain interdiffusion → strength ↑.
- Excess t → polymer degradation, flash, poor aesthetics.

**4. Block Diagram (1 mark):**
```
[Part A] ↔ [Heated Platen] ↔ [Part B]
   ↓             ↓               ↓
Heated       Retracts       Pressed together
phase        in stage 2     in stage 3
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- T<sub>m</sub> = 130 °C (melting)
- T<sub>s</sub> = 200 °C (platen)
- T<sub>0</sub> = 25 °C (ambient)
- t<sub>h</sub> = 12 s
- α = 1.8 × 10⁻⁷ m²/s

**Step 1 — Set up the erfc equation (2 marks):**

$$T(x,t) - T_0 = (T_s - T_0)\, \text{erfc}\!\left( \frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}} \right)$$

At the HAZ boundary, T(x<sub>HAZ</sub>, t<sub>h</sub>) = T<sub>m</sub>:

$$T_m - T_0 = (T_s - T_0)\, \text{erfc}\!\left( \frac{x_{HAZ}}{2\sqrt{\alpha t_h}} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (2 marks):**

$$130 - 25 = (200 - 25)\, \text{erfc}\!\left( \frac{x_{HAZ}}{2\sqrt{\alpha t_h}} \right)$$

$$105 = 175 \cdot \text{erfc}(\xi)$$

$$\text{erfc}(\xi) = \frac{105}{175} = 0.600$$

where $\xi = \dfrac{x_{HAZ}}{2\sqrt{\alpha t_h}}$.

**Step 3 — Invert erfc (1 mark):**

$$\xi = \text{erfc}^{-1}(0.600) \approx 0.3716$$

**Step 4 — Compute the argument (1 mark):**

$$2\sqrt{\alpha t_h} = 2\sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{-7} \times 12} = 2\sqrt{2.16 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$2\sqrt{\alpha t_h} = 2 \times 1.470 \times 10^{-3} = 2.940 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 2.94\,\text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for x<sub>HAZ</sub> (1 mark):**

$$x_{HAZ} = 0.3716 \times 2.94\,\text{mm} \approx 1.09\,\text{mm}$$

**Result:** The heat-affected zone depth ≈ **1.09 mm**, which is well below the wall thickness (8 mm) — adequate fusion without burning through.

---

### Question B: `**[14 Marks]**` — Ultrasonic Welding `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the ultrasonic welding process for thermoplastics with a neat block diagram. Explain the function of each component (converter, booster, horn) and state the role of the energy director.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare ultrasonic, vibration, spin, and laser welding methods for plastics with respect to: (i) principle of heat generation, (ii) cycle time, (iii) typical joint strength, (iv) suitable polymers and part geometries, and (v) limitations.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. Process Principle (2 marks):**
Ultrasonic welding uses **high-frequency mechanical vibrations (20–40 kHz)** combined with moderate clamping pressure to generate frictional heat at the joint interface. Heat melts the polymer locally, and after vibration ceases, the molten layer solidifies under pressure to form a weld.

**2. Components (3 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Power Supply (20 kHz)** | Converts 50 Hz mains to 20 kHz electrical signal |
| **Piezoceramic Converter** | Converts electrical signal to mechanical vibration (PZT crystals) |
| **Booster** | Amplifies (or attenuates) vibration amplitude; mechanical impedance matcher |
| **Horn (Sonotrode)** | Transmits vibration to the workpiece; tuned to a specific resonance mode |
| **Anvil** | Supports the lower part; provides reaction force |
| **Energy Director** | Moulded triangular protrusion that concentrates energy and initiates melting |

**3. Block Diagram (1 mark):**
```
[Power Supply] → [Converter] → [Booster] → [Horn]
                                              ↓ vibrates
                                    [Part A with Energy Director]
                                              ↓ contacts
                                    [Part B on Anvil]
                                              ↓
                                    [Molten Layer → Weld]
```

**4. Energy Director Role (1 mark):** Concentrates ultrasonic energy, melts first, ensures localized fusion without bulk melting of the joint surface.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Comparison Table (7 marks — distribute 1.4 marks per criterion):**

| Criterion | Ultrasonic | Vibration | Spin | Laser |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (i) **Heat generation** | Friction from 20 kHz vibration | Friction from linear vibration @ 100–240 Hz | Friction from rotational motion (≈ 1000 rpm) | Absorption of laser light at interface |
| (ii) **Cycle time** | 0.5–3 s | 1–5 s | 1–5 s | 1–10 s |
| (iii) **Joint strength** | 70–90% of parent | 80–95% of parent | 80–90% of parent | 85–95% of parent |
| (iv) **Best for** | Small thin parts in PS, ABS, PP, PA | Large flat/irregular parts in PP, PA, PBT | Circular/rotational parts in PP, PA | Precision 3D parts in PC, PA, ABS |
| (v) **Limitations** | Limited to < 6 mm thickness; needs energy director | Larger equipment; vibration noise | Only circular joints; flash at rim | High capital cost; safety hazard; needs absorbent layer |

**Conclusion (optional 1 mark):**
Selection depends on **part geometry, polymer type, production volume, and strength requirements**. For high-volume small parts → ultrasonic; for large structural parts → vibration; for circular assemblies → spin; for precision/clean welds → laser.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiners' perspective):**
> 1. **Confusing T<sub>g</sub> with T<sub>m</sub>** — amorphous polymers use T<sub>g</sub> in the welding window; semi-crystalline use T<sub>m</sub>. Mixing these loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — every numerical must carry units (mm, °C, MPa, J). Missing units ⇒ −1 mark.
> 3. **Omitting the energy director** in ultrasonic welding — examiners specifically test this. Always mention its geometry (60°, 0.3–0.5 mm).
> 4. **Stating that PE/PP can be solvent-welded** — this is FALSE. Polyolefins have no room-temperature solvent and require thermal methods only.
> 5. **Skipping diagrams in 14-mark answers** — always include at least one labelled schematic, even if hand-drawn style.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Joining**

### 🔹 Definitions to Memorise
- **Welding window:** ΔT = T<sub>d</sub> − T<sub>m</sub> (or T<sub>d</sub> − T<sub>g</sub>)
- **Reptation time (t<sub>r</sub>):** time for a polymer chain to escape its initial tube; scales as M³
- **Energy director:** 60° apex triangular protrusion, 0.3–0.5 mm height
- **HAZ depth:** $x_{HAZ} \approx 2\sqrt{\alpha t_h}$
- **Beer-Lambert law:** $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\beta x}$

### 🔹 Seven Major Welding Methods (Mnemonic: **HUVS-LAS**)
- **H**ot-plate — slow, robust, for large parts (pipes, tanks)
- **U**ltrasonic — fast (1–3 s), needs energy director, thin parts
- **V**ibration — for large flat/irregular parts, 100–240 Hz
- **S**pin — for circular parts only (one part rotates)
- **L**aser — precision, TTIW with absorbent lower layer
- **A**dhesive — universal, slow, surface prep critical
- **S**olvent — only for amorphous polar polymers (PVC, ABS, PS, PMMA)

### 🔹 Polymer-Specific Joining Facts
- **PE / PP** → only thermal welding; no solvent; adhesives require surface treatment (corona, plasma, primers).
- **PVC** → solvent (THF, MEK), hot-gas, hot-plate all work.
- **PMMA / PC** → solvent bonding or adhesive; ultrasonic with care.
- **PA (Nylon)** → hygroscopic — must be dried before thermal welding.

### 🔹 Numbers to Remember (typical industrial values)
- Ultrasonic frequency: **20 kHz** (also 35, 40 kHz)
- Ultrasonic amplitude: **10–50 µm**
- Ultrasonic pressure: **0.2–1.5 MPa**
- Vibration frequency: **100–240 Hz**, amplitude **1–5 mm**
- Spin speed: **500–3000 rpm**
- Hot-platen temperature for HDPE: **200–230 °C**
- Laser wavelength (TTIW): **808–940 nm** (diode)

### 🔹 Typical Exam Traps
- "Which method for PE?" → Only **thermal** (hot-plate, extrusion, ultrasonic).
- "Which method for circular parts?" → **Spin welding**.
- "Why energy director?" → Concentrates energy, ensures localized fusion.
- "Why no solvent welding for PP?" → No room-temperature solvent exists; solubility parameter mismatch too large.
- "Define reptation in one line" → Snakelike diffusion of polymer chains along their contour, enabling weld strength development.

### 🔹 One-Line Takeaway
> *Joining of polymers is governed by the **diffusion of chains across interfaces** (reptation) for thermal methods, by **chemical/electrostatic adhesion** for bonded joints, and by **mechanical interlock** for fastened joints — the choice depends on polymer type, geometry, strength requirement, and production volume.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT503 - POLYMER MACHINERY & PRODUCT MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Welding and Assembling of Plastics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Welding of Plastics?

**Welding of Plastics** is a fusion-based joining process in which two thermoplastic surfaces are brought to a molten (or viscoelastic) state and held together under controlled pressure until molecular interdiffusion and solidification occur, producing a homogeneous, leak-tight joint. Unlike metal welding, plastic welding does not involve phase change through melting–solidification of identical chemistry; instead, the polymer chains across the interface interpenetrate (autohesion) to form a continuous matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> *"Welding is a process of joining two thermoplastic components by the application of heat (with or without pressure), causing localized fusion of the polymer at the interface, followed by cooling and solidification to form an integral joint."*

**Key Pre-conditions for Plastic Welding:**
1. The base material **must be a thermoplastic** (thermosets cannot be re-melted).
2. The two parts must be **chemically compatible** (same polymer or miscible blends).
3. Sufficient **heat input** must raise the polymer above its softening/melt temperature.
4. **Pressure** must be applied to ensure intimate contact and chain interdiffusion.
5. **Welding time** must allow for interdiffusion of polymer chains across the interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
> Imagine you have two pieces of cold butter. If you press them together, they don't stick. But if you warm them slightly so they become soft (but not liquid), and then press them together, they fuse into a single piece as they cool. Plastic welding works on the same principle — heat softens the polymer chains, pressure brings them into intimate contact, and as they cool, the chains "intertwine" and form a permanent bond. The only difference is that polymers need *much* higher temperatures than butter!

---

## 1.2 What is Assembling of Plastics?

**Assembling of Plastics** is a broader term that encompasses **all methods of joining plastic components to each other or to other materials** (metals, ceramics, glass, etc.). It includes welding, but also covers:
- Mechanical fastening (screws, rivets, snaps)
- Adhesive bonding
- Solvent cementing
- Insert molding
- Press/Interference fits

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
> If welding is like "gluing" two pieces of butter with heat, then assembling is the entire toolbox of ways you can connect two plastic parts — like Lego blocks (snap fits), sewing (mechanical stitching with screws), or using double-sided tape (adhesives). Each method has its own pros and cons, and the engineer must pick the right one based on the application.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Plastic Joining Methods (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies plastic joining into **three primary categories**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **I. Welding (Fusion-Based)**
> - Hot Gas Welding (HGW)
> - Hot Plate/Contact Welding
> - Ultrasonic Welding (USW)
> - Vibration Welding (VW)
> - Spin Welding
> - Radio Frequency (RF) / Dielectric Welding
> - Induction Welding
> - Laser Welding
> - Friction Welding
>
> **II. Chemical / Solvent-Based Joining**
> - Solvent Welding / Cementing
> - Adhesive Bonding
>
> **III. Mechanical Assembly**
> - Self-tapping Screws
> - Threaded Inserts
> - Rivets
> - Snap Fits (Cantilever, Annular, Torsional)
> - Press/Interference Fits
> - Heat Staking

**Standard Industrial Metrics (Bold for Emphasis):**
- Welding temperatures typically range from **200 °C to 400 °C** depending on polymer.
- Ultrasonic welding frequency: **20 kHz to 40 kHz** (above human hearing).
- Vibration welding frequency: **100 Hz to 240 Hz** (audible, requires noise control).
- RF welding frequency: **27.12 MHz** (ISM band).
- Weld strength is typically **60 % to 90 %** of the parent material strength.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Mechanism of Plastic Welding

The bonding process in plastic welding occurs in **four sequential stages**:

| Stage | Physical Event | Duration |
|-------|---------------|----------|
| **1. Heating** | Heat source raises polymer above glass transition (T_g) for amorphous or above melting point (T_m) for semi-crystalline | 0.1 – 10 s |
| **2. Fusion / Interdiffusion** | Polymer chains (reptation) cross the interface | 0.5 – 5 s |
| **3. Consolidation** | Pressure squeezes out air and voids; intimate contact | 0.1 – 2 s |
| **4. Solidification** | Joint cools below T_g or T_m under pressure | 1 – 30 s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Each Stage Matters:**
> 1. **Heating** without sufficient temperature will not mobilize the chains.
> 2. **Interdiffusion** follows the *reptation model* (de Gennes, 1971) — chains "snake" through the interface.
> 3. **Consolidation** is critical for void-free joints (voids act as crack initiators).
> 4. **Solidification** under pressure prevents shrinkage voids and residual stress.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Welding Method

### 2.2.1 Hot Gas Welding (HGW)

**Operating Principle:** A stream of pre-heated inert gas (nitrogen or air) is directed at the joint. A filler rod of the same polymer is fed into the joint and melts along with the parent material, forming a weld bead.

**Process Parameters:**
- **Gas temperature:** 200 – 400 °C (material dependent)
- **Gas velocity:** 15 – 25 m/s
- **Weld speed:** 50 – 200 mm/min (manual), up to 2000 mm/min (automatic)
- **Welding rod diameter:** 3, 4, 5 mm (round); various triangular profiles
- **Joint gap:** 0.5 – 1.0 mm (V-groove, 60° included angle typical)

**Sequence (Why it works):**
1. Hot gas softens parent material surface to ~200 °C.
2. Filler rod, simultaneously heated, becomes molten.
3. Manual trowel or automatic shoe presses filler into the V-groove.
4. On cooling, weld bead solidifies and fuses with parent material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Materials Suited:** PVC, PP, PE, PVDF, ECTFE — *NOT* suitable for amorphous polymers like PMMA, PC (low thermal conductivity causes degradation).

---

### 2.2.2 Ultrasonic Welding (USW)

**Operating Principle:** High-frequency (20–40 kHz) electrical signal is converted by a *piezoelectric transducer* into mechanical vibrations. These are amplified by a *booster* and transmitted through a *horn* (sonotrode) to the workpiece. Friction at the joint interface generates localized heat, melting the polymer in 0.5–2 seconds.

**Components of an Ultrasonic Welder:**
- **Generator:** 50/60 Hz → 20 kHz, 2–3 kW power
- **Transducer:** Piezoelectric (Lead Zirconate Titanate – PZT)
- **Booster:** Amplifies vibration amplitude
- **Horn (Sonotrode):** Delivers vibration to workpiece (titanium or aluminum)
- **Anvil:** Supports the bottom part

**Process Variables:**
- **Frequency:** 20 kHz (large parts) to 40 kHz (small, precise parts)
- **Amplitude:** 20 – 50 µm (peak-to-peak)
- **Weld pressure:** 0.2 – 1.0 MPa
- **Weld time:** 0.3 – 2.0 s
- **Hold time:** 0.5 – 1.5 s (under pressure after vibration stops)

**Why the joint heats up:** The relative velocity at the interface (v = 2π·f·A) is very high. With f = 20 kHz and A = 30 µm, v ≈ 3.77 m/s. The friction between the two surfaces dissipates kinetic energy as heat:

$$Q = F \cdot \mu \cdot v \cdot t$$

where μ is the coefficient of friction and F is the clamping force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Types of Ultrasonic Welding:**
> 1. **Near-field welding** — Horn within 6 mm of joint (best for rigid plastics).
> 2. **Far-field welding** — Horn > 6 mm from joint (requires energy directors).
> 3. **Transmission welding** — Horn on top, anvil with textured surface.
> 4. **Spot welding** — Staked inserts or localized welds.
> 5. **Shear welding** — Shear-mode vibration reduces flash.

**Energy Director:** A triangular ridge (typically 0.2–0.5 mm height, 60° apex) molded onto one part initiates melting at a precise location.

---

### 2.2.3 Vibration Welding

**Principle:** Two parts are rubbed together under pressure at 100–240 Hz with 1–5 mm amplitude. Friction melts the interface; alignment is achieved; then vibration stops and parts cool under pressure.

**Process Window:**
- Frequency: 100 – 240 Hz
- Amplitude: 1 – 5 mm (peak-to-peak)
- Weld pressure: 0.5 – 2.0 MPa
- Weld time: 1 – 10 s (material dependent)

**Advantages over USW:**
- Suitable for large, irregular parts.
- No energy director required.
- Lower equipment cost.

> [!WARNING]
> **Disadvantage:** Audible noise (100–240 Hz) requires sound-dampening enclosures.

---

### 2.2.4 Spin Welding

**Principle:** One part is rotated at high speed (RPM) while pressed against a stationary part. Friction melts the interface; rotation stops, parts realign, and the joint solidifies.

**Process Parameters:**
- Rotational speed: 500 – 4000 RPM
- Axial pressure: 0.3 – 1.5 MPa
- Weld time: 0.5 – 3 s
- Suitable for: **circular/axisymmetric** parts only.

**Applications:** Automotive filters, fluid containers, ball valves.

---

### 2.2.5 Hot Plate (Contact / Tool) Welding

**Principle:** A heated metal plate (200 – 300 °C) is pressed against the joint faces until they melt, then retracted. The molten faces are brought into contact and held under pressure until solidification.

**Process Sequence:**
1. **Match phase:** Parts contact the hot plate (3–10 s).
2. **Soak phase:** Heat soaks into the polymer (5–20 s).
3. **Changeover:** Hot plate retracts (1–3 s) — *critical*: melt must not cool.
4. **Forge phase:** Parts pressed together (5–30 s).
5. **Cool/hold:** Solidification under pressure (5–20 s).

> [!NOTE]
> **For semi-crystalline polymers** (PP, PE, PA), the changeover phase is critical — premature cooling yields weak, porous welds. This is why *non-contact hot plate* and *infrared* variants exist.

---

### 2.2.6 Radio Frequency (RF / Dielectric) Welding

**Principle:** The plastic is placed between two electrodes; an alternating electric field (27.12 MHz) causes polar molecules to oscillate, generating heat through dielectric losses.

**Suitable Materials:** Polymers with high dielectric loss factor — **PVC, polyurethane (PU), polyamide, EVA.** Not suitable for non-polar polymers (PE, PP, PTFE).

**Applications:** Medical pouches, inflatable products, rainwear, blister packs.

---

### 2.2.7 Laser Welding

**Principle:** A laser beam (typically **diode laser at 800–1000 nm** or **CO₂ at 10.6 µm**) passes through a transparent part and is absorbed by the lower part, generating heat at the interface.

**Two Common Modes:**
1. **Clear-on-dark:** Top part transparent, bottom part carbon-black filled.
2. **Mask welding:** Laser passes through a mask to weld complex 2D patterns in one shot.

**Advantages:** Non-contact, high precision, clean (no flash), hermetic seals. Used in medical devices, automotive sensors, micro-fluidics.

---

### 2.2.8 Induction Welding

**Principle:** A metal insert (susceptor) is placed at the joint. High-frequency AC (1–30 MHz) induces eddy currents in the metal, which heat and melt the surrounding polymer. Pressure consolidates the joint.

**Applications:** Embedding metal threaded inserts in automotive panels, large pipe joints.

---

## 2.3 Assembling Methods — Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 Solvent Welding / Cementing

**Principle:** A solvent (e.g., MEK for PVC, toluene for polystyrene) softens the polymer surface. Parts are pressed together; the solvent diffuses and evaporates, leaving a fusion joint.

**Mechanism in 3 Steps:**
1. Solvent penetrates the surface, swelling the polymer.
2. Polymer chains disentangle and diffuse across the interface.
3. Solvent evaporates; chains re-entangle in a continuous matrix.

| Polymer | Common Solvent |
|---------|----------------|
| PVC | MEK, THF, cyclohexanone |
| ABS | MEK, acetone, methylene chloride |
| Polystyrene | Toluene, xylene |
| Acrylic (PMMA) | Dichloromethane, chloroform |
| Polycarbonate | Methylene chloride (limited) |

> [!WARNING]
> Solvent welding is **NOT** suitable for polyolefins (PE, PP) because they are chemically resistant to common solvents.

---

### 2.3.2 Adhesive Bonding

**Types of Adhesives Used:**
- **Epoxies** — high strength, room-temperature cure.
- **Cyanoacrylates (super-glue)** — fast cure, brittle.
- **Polyurethanes** — flexible, good for dissimilar materials.
- **Acrylics** — moderate strength, fast cure.
- **Silicones** — flexible, temperature resistant.

**Surface Preparation (Critical for Strength):**
1. Degrease (isopropanol wipe).
2. Abrade (sandpaper, plasma, or corona treatment).
3. Apply primer (if required).
4. Apply adhesive (controlled bondline thickness 0.1–0.2 mm).
5. Clamp and cure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surface Energy Rule (KTU High-Yield Concept):** Most plastics have low surface energy (PE ≈ 31 mN/m, PP ≈ 30 mN/m). Adhesives with surface tension *lower* than the polymer's critical surface energy will wet it. Corona or plasma treatment raises surface energy to > 50 mN/m, improving adhesion dramatically.

---

### 2.3.3 Mechanical Fastening

| Method | Description | Typical Application |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Self-tapping screws** | Screw threads cut into plastic | Enclosures, panels |
| **Threaded inserts** | Metal insert molded or heat-staked | Removable joints |
| **Rivets** | Plastic or metal | Permanent joints |
| **Snap fits** | Cantilever beam deflects and locks | Toys, enclosures |
| **Press fits** | Interference between shaft and hole | Bearings, hubs |

---

### 2.3.4 Snap Fits — Detailed Design

**Three Main Types:**
1. **Cantilever snap fit** — Most common; a beam deflects and engages a hook.
2. **Annular (ring) snap fit** — A ring expands and locks; suited for rotational parts.
3. **Torsional snap fit** — A torsion spring provides the locking force.

**Cantilever Snap Fit Geometry:**
- Beam length: L
- Beam width: b
- Beam thickness: h
- Deflection: δ (must exceed the hook height)
- Undercut: u (locking feature)

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule (KTU):** Maximum strain at the base of the cantilever must be below the allowable strain for the polymer. For most thermoplastics, ε_allow ≈ 5 % (short-term) to 2 % (long-term, creep-loaded).

---

## 2.4 🔥 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Legend | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $Q = F \cdot \mu \cdot v \cdot t$ | Q = heat (J), F = force (N), μ = friction coeff., v = velocity (m/s), t = time (s) | Heat generated in friction/spin/vibration welding |
| 2 | $P = 2\pi f A F$ | P = power (W), f = frequency (Hz), A = amplitude (m), F = force (N) | Ultrasonic welding power input |
| 3 | $v_{rel} = 2\pi f A$ | Relative velocity at joint interface | Velocity in friction welding |
| 4 | $\delta = \dfrac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}$ | δ = deflection (m), L = length (m), E = modulus (Pa), I = second moment of area (m⁴) | Cantilever snap fit deflection |
| 5 | $I = \dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}$ | I for rectangular cross-section | Moment of inertia for snap fit |
| 6 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6 F L}{b h^{2}}$ | σ_max = max bending stress | Cantilever stress at the base |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} = \dfrac{6 F L}{E b h^{2}}$ | ε = strain at the base | Cantilever strain (must be < ε_allow) |
| 8 | $\delta = \dfrac{\sigma L^{2}}{E h}$ | Strain-related deflection | Re-expressed in terms of stress |
| 9 | $F_{snap} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y} \, b \, h^{2}}{6 L}$ | F_snap = snap-in force | Force at which snap engages |
| 10 | $t_{cool} = \dfrac{h^{2}}{\alpha}$ | t_cool ∝ h²/α; α = thermal diffusivity (m²/s) | Cooling time in hot plate welding |
| 11 | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, c_{p}}$ | k = thermal conductivity, ρ = density, c_p = specific heat | Thermal diffusivity |
| 12 | $\eta_{weld} = \dfrac{\sigma_{weld}}{\sigma_{parent}} \times 100\,\%$ | Weld efficiency | Joint strength ratio |
| 13 | $E_{bond} = \gamma \cdot A_{weld}$ | E_bond = bond energy, γ = surface energy | Thermodynamic work of adhesion |
| 14 | $T_{g}$ correlation with welding temp | $T_{weld} \approx T_{g} + (50 \text{ to } 100)\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Welding temperature for amorphous polymers |
| 15 | Interference (press fit) | $\delta_{i} = \dfrac{D_{shaft} - D_{hole}}{D_{hole}} \times 100\,\%$ | % interference for press fit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember the Order of Magnitude:**
> - Ultrasonic amplitude: **20–50 µm** (smaller than a human hair).
> - Vibration amplitude: **1–5 mm** (visible to the eye).
> - Spin welding RPM: **500–4000** (high speed).
> - RF frequency: **27.12 MHz** (ISM band, no license required).
> - Typical weld efficiency: **70–85 %** for hot gas, **85–95 %** for ultrasonic/hot plate.
