# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power and Heat in Ultrasonic Welding

We start with the basic definition of mechanical power and substitute the harmonic motion of the horn.

**Step 1: Definition of Power**

For a force F applied over a relative displacement, the rate of work (power) is:

$$P = F \cdot v_{rel}$$

**Step 2: Relative Velocity in Sinusoidal Motion**

The horn moves with simple harmonic motion: $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$.

Differentiating once to get velocity:

$$v_{rel} = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = 2\pi f A \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 3: Peak (Amplitude) Velocity**

The peak (RMS-relevant) velocity is the amplitude of the cosine term:

$$v_{peak} = 2\pi f A$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the Power Equation**

$$P = F \cdot v_{peak} = 2\pi f A F$$

This is the **peak power** delivered by the horn to the joint interface.

**Step 5: Energy Delivered over Welding Time t_w**

$$E = P \cdot t_{w} = 2\pi f A F \cdot t_{w}$$

**Step 6: Numerical Example**

Given: f = 20 kHz = 2 × 10⁴ Hz, A = 30 µm = 3 × 10⁻⁵ m, F = 50 N.

$$v_{peak} = 2\pi \times (2 \times 10^{4}) \times (3 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$v_{peak} = 2\pi \times 6 \times 10^{-1} = 3.77 \text{ m/s}$$

$$P = 50 \times 3.77 = 188.5 \text{ W}$$

For a weld time of 1 second:

$$E = 188.5 \times 1 = 188.5 \text{ J}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even a modest 50 N force at 20 kHz and 30 µm delivers ~188 W of frictional power. This is why ultrasonic welding is so fast (under 2 seconds typically) and so localized.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cantilever Snap Fit — Deflection, Stress, and Snap Force

**Given:** A cantilever beam of rectangular cross-section, length L, width b, thickness h, made of polymer with Young's modulus E and yield stress σ_y.

### 3.2.1 Deflection at the Free End

The standard Euler–Bernoulli beam equation for a cantilever with a tip load F:

$$\delta = \dfrac{F L^{3}}{3 E I}$$

For a rectangular cross-section:

$$I = \dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}$$

Substituting:

$$\delta = \dfrac{F L^{3}}{3 E \cdot \dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}} = \dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{E b h^{3}}$$

### 3.2.2 Maximum Bending Stress

The bending moment at the fixed end is M = F·L. The maximum bending stress is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{F L \cdot \dfrac{h}{2}}{\dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}} = \dfrac{6 F L}{b h^{2}}$$

### 3.2.3 Maximum Strain

Using Hooke's law:

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{E} = \dfrac{6 F L}{E b h^{2}}$$

### 3.2.4 Deflection Re-expressed via Strain

Since ε = 6FL/(Ebh²), we can solve for F and substitute back into δ:

$$F = \dfrac{\varepsilon E b h^{2}}{6 L}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{4 L^{3}}{E b h^{3}} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon E b h^{2}}{6 L} = \dfrac{4 \varepsilon L^{2}}{6 h} = \dfrac{2 \varepsilon L^{2}}{3 h}$$

A more common engineering approximation (for short beams) is:

$$\delta \approx \dfrac{\varepsilon L^{2}}{h}$$

### 3.2.5 Snap-In Force

The snap engages when the strain reaches the yield strain (or allowable strain):

$$\varepsilon_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{E}$$

Substituting:

$$F_{snap} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y} \, b \, h^{2}}{6 L}$$

### 3.2.6 Worked Example

**Problem:** Design a cantilever snap fit for an ABS enclosure. Given: L = 15 mm, b = 6 mm, h = 2 mm. Material: ABS (E = 2.3 GPa, σ_y = 45 MPa, ε_allow = 5 %).

**Step A — Maximum allowable deflection:**

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{allow} L^{2}}{h} = \dfrac{0.05 \times (15 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 225 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{11.25 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.625 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\delta_{max} = 5.625 \text{ mm}$$

**Step B — Snap-in force:**

$$F_{snap} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y} \, b \, h^{2}}{6 L} = \dfrac{(45 \times 10^{6}) \times (6 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{6 \times (15 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$F_{snap} = \dfrac{45 \times 10^{6} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{90 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$F_{snap} = \dfrac{1.08}{90 \times 10^{-3}} = 12 \text{ N}$$

**Step C — Verification (using the exact equation):**

$$\delta = \dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{E b h^{3}} = \dfrac{4 \times 12 \times (15 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}{2.3 \times 10^{9} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^{3}}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{4 \times 12 \times 3.375 \times 10^{-6}}{2.3 \times 10^{9} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\delta = \dfrac{1.62 \times 10^{-4}}{1.104 \times 10^{-1}} = 1.467 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.47 \text{ mm}$$

This is below the allowable deflection (5.625 mm), confirming that the snap can deflect 1.47 mm before reaching the allowable stress. So the *undercut height* should be **less than 1.47 mm** to avoid permanent deformation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Takeaway:** The thickness h has the strongest influence (cubic in deflection, quadratic in stress). Doubling h reduces δ by 8× but increases snap force by 4×. The designer must balance "soft snap" vs. "strong lock."

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cooling Time in Hot Plate Welding

Heat transfer through a polymer of thickness h, after the heat source is removed, follows Fourier's law. The characteristic cooling time scales as:

$$t_{cool} \sim \dfrac{h^{2}}{\alpha}$$

where α is the thermal diffusivity:

$$\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho \, c_{p}}$$

**Proof Sketch:** The heat equation is:

$$\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \dfrac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}}$$

For boundary layer-type cooling, the relevant length scale is h, and the diffusion time over that length is h²/α.

**Example:** For PP, k = 0.1 W/(m·K), ρ = 905 kg/m³, c_p = 1920 J/(kg·K):

$$\alpha = \dfrac{0.1}{905 \times 1920} = \dfrac{0.1}{1.738 \times 10^{6}} = 5.75 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

For h = 3 mm:

$$t_{cool} \sim \dfrac{(3 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{5.75 \times 10^{-8}} = \dfrac{9 \times 10^{-6}}{5.75 \times 10^{-8}} = 156 \text{ s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Implication:** This is why hot plate welding of thick-walled parts is slow. For 6 mm PP, t_cool ~ 625 s. This drives the use of *non-contact hot plate* or *infrared* pre-heat methods to reduce the changeover cooling loss.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Welding Methods

| Method | Best For | Cycle Time | Weld Efficiency | Capital Cost | Limitation |
|--------|----------|------------|-----------------|--------------|------------|
| Hot Gas | Large PP/PE/PVC parts, tanks | 1–10 min | 60–80 % | Low | Manual skill dependent |
| Hot Plate | Large flat parts, pipes | 10–60 s | 85–95 % | Medium | Slow for thick walls |
| Ultrasonic | Small rigid parts, mass production | 0.5–2 s | 85–95 % | Medium | Limited to small parts |
| Vibration | Large automotive parts | 2–10 s | 85–95 % | High | Noise, large parts only |
| Spin | Circular parts, filters | 1–3 s | 85–90 % | Low | Axisymmetric only |
| RF | PVC films, PU garments | 1–5 s | 80–90 % | High | Polar materials only |
| Laser | Precision medical/electronics | 0.5–5 s | 90–98 % | Very high | One-part-transparent required |
| Induction | Metal-plastic joints | 3–15 s | 80–90 % | High | Needs metal insert |

## 4.2 Hot Gas Welding — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    inA[Gas Supply - Air or N2] --> inB[Heater Element]
    inB --> inC[Heated Nozzle 200 to 400 C]
    inC --> inD[Weld Groove - V Joint]
    nodeE[Filler Rod Same Polymer] --> inC
    inC --> inF[Weld Bead]
    inD --> inF
    inF --> inG[Solidified Weld]
    
    inB -. heat path .-> inC
    inC -. melt .-> inE
    inC -. melt .-> inD
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Gas flows from supply → heater → nozzle.
- The nozzle directs hot gas at the **joint groove** *and* at the **filler rod**.
- Both melt simultaneously, fuse, and solidify to form a **weld bead**.

---

## 4.3 Ultrasonic Welding System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    usA[AC Mains 50 or 60 Hz] --> usB[RF Generator 20 kHz]
    usB --> usC[Piezoelectric Transducer PZT]
    usC --> usD[Booster Amplifier]
    usD --> usE[Horn Sonotrode]
    usE --> usF[Top Workpiece]
    usG[Bottom Workpiece] --> usH[Anvil]
    usF --> usI[Energy Director]
    usG --> usI
    usI --> usJ[Heat Melt 0.5 to 2 s]
    usJ --> usK[Solidified Weld]
    usH --> usK
```

**Block-wise function:**
1. **Generator** converts 50 Hz mains → 20 kHz high-frequency AC.
2. **Transducer** converts electrical → mechanical vibrations (piezoelectric effect).
3. **Booster** amplifies amplitude (tunable gain: 1:1, 1:2, 1:3).
4. **Horn** delivers vibration to the workpiece at the joint.
5. **Anvil** supports the lower part and may carry a textured surface (transmission mode).
6. **Energy director** localizes melting.

---

## 4.4 Cantilever Snap Fit — Geometric Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    snapA[Fixed End at Housing] --> snapB[Beam Length L]
    snapB --> snapC[Beam Thickness h]
    snapB --> snapD[Beam Width b]
    snapC --> snapE[Free End with Hook]
    snapE --> snapF[Undercut u]
    snapF --> snapG[Engages with Mating Lip]
    snapH[Deflection delta during assembly] --> snapE
    
    snapI[Stress Concentration at Base] --> snapA
    snapJ[Allowable Strain 2 to 5 percent] --> snapI
```

**Reading:**
- Stress is **maximum at the fixed end** (root of the cantilever).
- Strain is **most critical** for long-term performance (creep).
- The undercut `u` must be **less than δ_max** to avoid permanent deformation.

---

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define hot gas welding of plastics. List any four of its advantages.**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Hot gas welding is a fusion welding process in which a stream of preheated gas (air or nitrogen, typically at 200–400 °C) is directed at the joint between two thermoplastic parts. A filler rod of the same base polymer is simultaneously melted and pressed into the V-groove joint using a trowel, forming a continuous weld bead upon solidification.
>
> **Advantages (4 × 0.375 = 1.5 marks):**
> 1. Low capital cost and portable equipment.
> 2. Suitable for field repairs and large structures (tanks, ducts, pipes).
> 3. Works on a wide range of thermoplastics (PVC, PP, PE, PVDF).
> 4. No requirement for complex fixturing.
> 5. Suitable for both manual and automated production.

---

### **Q2. What is solvent welding? Mention two polymer–solvent pairs commonly used.**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Solvent welding is a chemical joining process in which a volatile solvent is applied to the surfaces of two thermoplastic parts. The solvent diffuses into the polymer, causing surface swelling and chain disentanglement. When the parts are pressed together, the polymer chains interdiffuse across the interface. As the solvent evaporates, the chains re-entangle to form a permanent, continuous joint.
>
> **Polymer–Solvent Pairs (2 × 0.75 = 1.5 marks):**
> 1. **PVC + Tetrahydrofuran (THF) or Methyl Ethyl Ketone (MEK).**
> 2. **ABS + Acetone or Methylene Chloride.**
> 3. **Polystyrene + Toluene.**
> 4. **Acrylic (PMMA) + Dichloromethane (DCM).**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### ✏️ **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Explain the working principle, equipment, and process parameters of ultrasonic welding of plastics with a neat sketch. (7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
Ultrasonic welding uses high-frequency mechanical vibrations (20–40 kHz) to generate frictional heat at the joint interface of two thermoplastic parts held under pressure. The heat melts a thin layer of polymer at the interface, and after vibration stops, the parts solidify under pressure to form a hermetic, high-strength weld.

**Equipment (2.5 marks):**
1. **Generator:** Converts 50/60 Hz mains AC into 20 kHz (or 40 kHz) high-frequency electrical signal, typically 1–3 kW.
2. **Piezoelectric Transducer:** Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT) crystals convert the electrical signal into mechanical vibrations via the *inverse piezoelectric effect*.
3. **Booster:** Mechanical amplifier that increases the vibration amplitude (gain 1:1 to 1:3, tunable).
4. **Horn (Sonotrode):** Titanium or aluminum tool that delivers vibration to the workpiece, often with a 1:1 gain or further amplification.
5. **Anvil:** Rigid support for the lower part, often with textured surface for transmission welding.
6. **Pneumatic Press:** Applies 0.2–1.0 MPa clamping force.

**Process Parameters (2 marks):**
- Frequency: 20 kHz (large) – 40 kHz (small precise).
- Amplitude: 20–50 µm peak-to-peak.
- Weld pressure: 0.2–1.0 MPa.
- Weld time: 0.3–2.0 s.
- Hold time: 0.5–1.5 s.
- Energy director height: 0.2–0.5 mm with 60° apex.

**Neat Sketch (0.5 marks):** *(See the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.3 for the equivalent schematic.)*

---

#### **(b) Describe the different types of snap fits used in plastic assembly. Derive the formula for the maximum deflection and stress in a cantilever snap fit. (7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

**Types of Snap Fits (3 marks):**
1. **Cantilever Snap Fit:** A flexible beam that bends to engage a mating lip. Most common, simple, and economical.
2. **Annular (Ring) Snap Fit:** A ring or cylindrical section expands radially to engage a groove. Suited for rotational/circular parts.
3. **Torsional Snap Fit:** A torsion spring rotates by an angle to lock the joint. Used where lateral space is limited.
4. **U-Shaped Snap Fit:** Two parallel cantilevers provide a balanced, high-force engagement.
5. **Latching Snap Fit:** A flexible latch that engages under an overhang.

**Cantilever Snap Fit — Derivation (4 marks):**

*Setup:* Rectangular cantilever of length L, width b, thickness h, with tip load F. Material modulus E.

*Step 1 — Moment of inertia:*
$$I = \dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}$$

*Step 2 — Tip deflection:*
$$\delta = \dfrac{F L^{3}}{3 E I} = \dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{E b h^{3}}$$

*Step 3 — Maximum bending stress at the fixed end:*
$$\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{6 F L}{b h^{2}}$$

*Step 4 — Maximum strain (Hooke's law):*
$$\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{6 F L}{E b h^{2}}$$

*Step 5 — Re-express deflection in terms of strain:*
$$\delta = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{max} L^{2}}{h}$$

*Step 6 — Design rule:* The undercut height `u` of the hook must satisfy:

$$u \leq \delta_{max} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{allow} L^{2}}{h}$$

Typical ε_allow for short-term loading: **5 %**; for long-term/creep: **2 %**.

---

### ✏️ **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Discuss hot plate welding and spin welding in detail, comparing their process parameters, advantages, and limitations. (7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

**Hot Plate Welding (3 marks):**

*Principle:* A heated metal platen (200–300 °C) is pressed against the joint faces of the two parts until a molten layer of ~0.1–0.5 mm forms. The platen is retracted, and the parts are brought together under pressure (forge phase). On cooling, a strong, hermetic joint forms.

*Process Phases:*
1. **Match Phase:** Parts contact the platen (3–10 s).
2. **Soak Phase:** Heat conducts into the bulk (5–20 s).
3. **Changeover:** Platen retracts (1–3 s).
4. **Forge Phase:** Parts pressed together (5–30 s).
5. **Hold/Cool:** Solidification (5–20 s).

*Advantages:* High weld strength (up to 95 % of parent), suitable for large flat parts (pipes, sheets, automotive tanks). No filler material required.

*Limitations:* Slow cycle time. Not suitable for highly crystalline polymers with sharp melting points unless non-contact variant is used.

**Spin Welding (3 marks):**

*Principle:* One part is rotated at high speed (500–4000 RPM) while pressed against a stationary counterpart. Friction at the interface melts the polymer; rotation stops, and the parts solidify under pressure.

*Process Parameters:*
- Rotational speed: 500–4000 RPM.
- Axial pressure: 0.3–1.5 MPa.
- Weld time: 0.5–3 s.
- Material loss: 0.5–3 mm flash on the perimeter.

*Advantages:* Fast cycle, low cost, hermetic seal. Ideal for circular parts (filters, fluid reservoirs, ball valves).

*Limitations:* Only **circular/axisymmetric** joints. Generates flash that may need machining.

**Comparison (1 mark):**

| Criterion | Hot Plate | Spin |
|-----------|-----------|------|
| Joint geometry | Any flat profile | Circular only |
| Cycle time | 10–60 s | 1–3 s |
| Weld strength | 85–95 % | 85–90 % |
| Capital cost | Medium | Low |
| Flash produced | Minimal | Significant |

---

#### **(b) Explain the various mechanical fastening methods used for assembly of plastic components. Discuss the design of a cantilever snap fit with a numerical example. (7 marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanical Fastening Methods (3.5 marks):**
1. **Self-tapping screws:** Cut threads into a pre-molded or drilled hole. Common in enclosures. *Limitation:* stress concentration at the threads can cause cracking in brittle plastics.
2. **Threaded inserts (molded-in or heat-staked):** Metal inserts provide reusable threads. Higher strength and reliability.
3. **Rivets:** Plastic or metal rivets used for permanent joints. Common in large structures.
4. **Snap fits:** Cantilever, annular, torsional types — see Section 2.3.4.
5. **Press/Interference fits:** Shaft is slightly larger than the hole, creating a permanent friction fit. Typical interference 0.5–2 % of the diameter.
6. **Heat staking:** A molded post is heated and reformed to capture a mating component (e.g., PCB to plastic housing).

**Cantilever Snap Fit Design — Numerical Example (3.5 marks):**

*Given:* ABS cantilever, L = 12 mm, b = 5 mm, h = 1.8 mm. ABS properties: E = 2.3 GPa, σ_y = 45 MPa, ε_allow = 5 %.

*Step 1 — Allowable deflection:*
$$\delta_{allow} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{allow} L^{2}}{h} = \dfrac{0.05 \times (12 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{1.8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\delta_{allow} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 144 \times 10^{-6}}{1.8 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{7.2 \times 10^{-6}}{1.8 \times 10^{-3}} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 4 \text{ mm}$$

*Step 2 — Snap-in force:*
$$F_{snap} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y} \, b \, h^{2}}{6 L} = \dfrac{(45 \times 10^{6}) \times (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1.8 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{6 \times (12 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$F_{snap} = \dfrac{45 \times 10^{6} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.24 \times 10^{-6}}{72 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$F_{snap} = \dfrac{0.729}{72 \times 10^{-3}} = 10.1 \text{ N}$$

*Step 3 — Design check:*
If the undercut `u` of the hook is 1.5 mm, then since 1.5 mm < δ_allow = 4 mm, the design is safe. The snap will engage and lock without permanent deformation.

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit errors in E and σ_y:** Always check that E is in **Pa** and dimensions in **m**. A common mistake is using mm and GPa together without conversion.
> 2. **Confusing σ_y with σ_allow:** For long-term/creep-loaded snaps, use σ_allow ≈ 0.4 × σ_y (reduction factor), NOT the full yield strength.
> 3. **Forgetting the undercut check:** The undercut `u` MUST be smaller than the allowable deflection; otherwise, the snap will plastically deform and not return.
> 4. **Neglecting the radius at the base of the cantilever:** Sharp corners cause stress concentration; KTU expects a fillet radius of at least **0.5 × h** to be mentioned.
> 5. **In welding questions, students often forget the *hold time* parameter**, but it is critical for joint quality.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Welding and Assembling of Plastics**

**A. Welding Methods (Fusion):**
- ☐ **Hot Gas Welding (HGW):** 200–400 °C gas + filler rod; for PVC/PE/PP; low cost, manual skill needed.
- ☐ **Hot Plate Welding:** Heated platen melts faces, then parts forged together; for flat joints; 85–95 % efficiency.
- ☐ **Ultrasonic Welding (USW):** 20–40 kHz, 20–50 µm amplitude, 0.2–1.0 MPa, 0.5–2 s; needs energy director.
- ☐ **Vibration Welding:** 100–240 Hz, 1–5 mm amplitude; for large irregular parts; noisy.
- ☐ **Spin Welding:** 500–4000 RPM; only for circular joints; flash generated.
- ☐ **RF / Dielectric Welding:** 27.12 MHz; only for polar polymers (PVC, PU).
- ☐ **Laser Welding:** Diode 800–1000 nm; clear-on-dark; high precision, no flash.
- ☐ **Induction Welding:** Uses metal susceptor; embeds metal inserts.

**B. Chemical Joining:**
- ☐ **Solvent Welding:** Solvent softens, chains interdiffuse, solvent evaporates. NOT for PE/PP.
- ☐ **Adhesive Bonding:** Epoxies, cyanoacrylates, PUs; surface preparation is critical; corona/plasma treatment for polyolefins.

**C. Mechanical Assembly:**
- ☐ **Self-tapping screws:** Easy but stress-concentrated.
- ☐ **Threaded inserts:** Reusable, high strength.
- ☐ **Snap fits — Cantilever:** δ = 4FL³/(Ebh³); σ = 6FL/(bh²); ε_allow ≤ 5 % short-term, 2 % long-term.
- ☐ **Snap fits — Annular:** Radial expansion; for circular parts.
- ☐ **Snap fits — Torsional:** Torsion spring; for compact spaces.
- ☐ **Press fits:** Interference 0.5–2 %; permanent.
- ☐ **Heat staking:** Heated post captures mating part.

**D. Critical Numerical Constants to Memorize:**
- ☐ Ultrasonic frequency: **20 kHz** (large) to **40 kHz** (small).
- ☐ RF welding frequency: **27.12 MHz**.
- ☐ Hot gas temperature: **200–400 °C**.
- ☐ Hot plate temperature: **200–300 °C**.
- ☐ Typical weld efficiency: **70–95 %**.
- ☐ Allowable strain for snap fits: **2–5 %**.
- ☐ Cantilever moment of inertia: I = **bh³/12**.

**E. KTU 2024 Frequently Asked Concepts:**
- ☐ Why ultrasonic welding needs an **energy director**.
- ☐ Difference between **near-field** and **far-field** USW.
- ☐ The **changeover phase** in hot plate welding.
- ☐ Why **RF welding** doesn't work on PE/PP.
- ☐ The **reptation model** for polymer interdiffusion.
- ☐ The **undercut design rule** for cantilever snap fits.
- ☐ Why **surface treatment** is essential for adhesive bonding of polyolefins.
